- **Fire detection** uses thermal IR on UAV swarms for rapid response.
- **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ is the most frequently tested constant.
- **Standard KTU expectations:** labeled block diagram (1–2 marks), derivation (4 marks), numerical (3 marks), explanation (5 marks).
- **Penetrate deep study points:** Allometric equations, ICP scan matching, RRT* path planning, hyperspectral NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) for tree health.
- **Avoid these traps:** round-trip vs one-way, biomass vs volume units, LAI vs canopy cover percent, and GPS multipath vs signal attenuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Perception and SLAM in the Forest Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machine Perception and SLAM in the Forest Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Machine Perception and SLAM in the Forest Environment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Machine Perception** in the context of field robotics is defined as the computational process by which an autonomous mobile robot acquires, interprets, and reasons about raw measurements from exteroceptive and proprioceptive sensors to build an internal representation of its surrounding unstructured environment, its own state, and the traversability of terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLAM — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping**
> SLAM is the recursive estimation problem of concurrently determining the pose (position + orientation) of a mobile robot with respect to a global reference frame *and* constructing a map of the unknown environment, using only the robot's onboard sensor stream. Mathematically it solves the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$, where $x$ is the trajectory, $m$ is the map, $z$ are observations, and $u$ are control inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forest Environment Definition (KTU-context)**
> A forest is a *cluttered, dynamic, semi-structured, GPS-degraded, illumination-variant* outdoor environment characterized by densely packed tree trunks, understorey vegetation, leaf canopies, undulating ground, leaf litter, and seasonal foliage changes. It is one of the most challenging benchmarks for field robotic perception.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Hiker with a Sketchpad

Imagine you are blindfolded (no GPS, no prior map) and asked to hike through a dense forest at night while drawing a map of where you have been. With each step, you must:
1. **Estimate how far you moved** (odometry — your "internal step counter").
2. **Feel the world around you** with a walking stick (LiDAR / stereo depth).
3. **Update your sketchpad** as you discover new trees.
4. **Recognize when you return to a previously visited clearing** (loop closure).
5. **Re-correct the accumulated drift** in your sketch.

SLAM is precisely this computational equivalent for a forest robot — except the "blindfold" is the absence of reliable GNSS under the canopy, and the "walking stick" is a LiDAR/camera/IMU sensor stack.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** In KTU valuation, the examiner expects you to clearly separate **Localization** (where am I?) from **Mapping** (what does the world look like?) and emphasize that SLAM solves them *simultaneously* because they are **chicken-and-egg coupled**: a good map needs accurate pose, and accurate pose needs a good map.

### 1.3 Why Forests Break "Easy" SLAM

| Forest Characteristic | Consequence for Perception/SLAM |
|---|---|
| Dense canopy | Severe **GNSS multipath** and signal attenuation (drop from ~12 satellites to 2–4) |
| Repetitive trunk geometry | **Perceptual aliasing** — wrong loop closures |
| Moving branches/leaves | **Dynamic outliers** corrupt scan matching |
| Low/high contrast lighting | **Feature-poor visual frames** (visual SLAM degrades) |
| Soft, deformable terrain | **Wheel slip → odometry error** |
| Seasonal foliage change | **Place-recognition signature drift** |

Standard benchmark SLAM (e.g., indoor ORB-SLAM on TUM RGB-D) **fails outright** in the forest — this is the central motivation for forest-specific perception stacks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Constant to memorize:** The **3σ GNSS positional accuracy** under dense canopy degrades from **~2.5 m** (open sky) to **>10 m** or full outage. Hence forest SLAM is *GPS-denied* by default.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The SLAM Probabilistic Backbone

SLAM is formulated as a **Bayesian filtering** problem over the state space $\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{M}$, where $\mathcal{X} = \text{SE}(3)^t$ is the trajectory manifold and $\mathcal{M}$ is the map.

**Prediction step** (motion / odometry update):

$$
p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \;=\; \int \underbrace{p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)}_{\text{motion model}} \, dx_{t-1}
$$

**Correction step** (measurement / observation update):

$$
p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;\propto\; p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})
$$

**Why this matters in forest:** The motion model is corrupted by wheel slip on leaf litter, and the measurement model is corrupted by moving branches. Both terms become *heavy-tailed* — a Gaussian assumption is invalid, demanding **robust kernels** (Huber, Cauchy, DCS) in the cost function.

### 2.2 Sensor Modalities for Forest Perception

#### (a) 3D LiDAR
- Emits pulsed laser, measures Time-of-Flight.
- Forest strength: **range-invariant geometry** of trunks, immune to lighting.
- Forest weakness: sparser returns on leaves (low albedo in IR), expensive, mechanical (spinning) parts vibrate on rough terrain.

Range measurement model:

$$
z_t^{(i)} \;=\; \lVert p_t - m_i \rVert_2 + b_t + n_t, \qquad n_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_r^2)
$$

where $p_t$ is the sensor origin, $m_i$ the i-th map point, $b_t$ the range bias (e.g., from tree-bark reflectivity), and $\sigma_r$ typically **0.02 – 0.05 m** for a Velodyne VLP-16.

#### (b) Stereo / RGB-D Camera
- Provides dense color + depth (RGB-D) or disparity (stereo).
- Forest strength: rich semantic information, low cost, low power.
- Forest weakness: **lighting-variant**, **scale-ambiguous** (mono), **range-limited** (stereo baseline caps usable range to ~15–20 m).

Pinhole projection model:

$$
\lambda \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; K \, \pi(R \, X + t), \qquad K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\pi$ is perspective division and $(R,t) \in \text{SE}(3)$ is the camera pose.

#### (c) IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)
- 6-DoF: 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope, typically at **200–1000 Hz**.
- Forest role: **bridges gaps** when LiDAR/camera drop out (e.g., dense dust, water spray from wet leaves).

IMU preintegration on manifold SO(3):

$$
\Delta R_{ij} \;=\; \prod_{k=i}^{j-1} \text{Exp}\!\left(\!\left(\omega_k - b_k^g - \eta_k^g\right)\Delta t\!\right)
$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity, $b^g$ gyro bias, $\eta^g$ white noise, and $\text{Exp}$ is the SO(3) exponential map.

#### (d) GNSS (when available)
- Forest role: **global prior** for loop closure and re-localization after long traverses.
- Forest reality: *frequently denied* — the canopy is a near-perfect RF shield at L1/L5.

### 2.3 Multi-Sensor Fusion Architecture

Modern forest SLAM uses a **tightly-coupled factor graph** (à la LIO-SAM, FAST-LIO):

$$
\mathcal{X}^{\star} \;=\; \arg\min_{\mathcal{X}} \Big[ \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{O}} \Vert e_{ij}^{\text{IMU}} \Vert_{\Sigma_{ij}}^2 + \sum_{k \in \mathcal{L}} \Vert e_k^{\text{LiDAR}} \Vert_{\Sigma_k}^2 + \sum_{m \in \mathcal{G}} \Vert e_m^{\text{GNSS}} \Vert_{\Sigma_m}^2 + \sum_{n \in \mathcal{C}} \Vert e_n^{\text{loop}} \Vert_{\Sigma_n}^2 \Big]
$$

where $\mathcal{O}$ are IMU preintegration factors, $\mathcal{L}$ LiDAR scan-matching factors, $\mathcal{G}$ GNSS priors, $\mathcal{C}$ loop-closure factors, and the norms are **Mahalanobis** (weighted) with information matrices $\Sigma^{-1}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation tip:** Examiners love this equation. If you can write the factor-graph cost and *briefly* name each factor, you secure full marks on any "explain forest SLAM architecture" question.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Domain / Symbol | Use in Forest SLAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p(x_{1:t},m \mid z_{1:t},u_{1:t-1})$ | Full SLAM posterior | Defines the problem |
| 2 | $\text{SE}(3) = \{ (R,t) \mid R \in \text{SO}(3),\, t \in \mathbb{R}^3 \}$ | Pose manifold | All robot states |
| 3 | $\text{Exp}(\xi) = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Exp}(\phi) & J\rho \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\xi \in \mathfrak{se}(3)$ | Lie-algebra optimization |
| 4 | $z = \lVert p - m \rVert_2 + n$ | LiDAR range | Scan matching |
| 5 | $\lambda \mathbf{u} = K \pi(RX + t)$ | Camera projection | Visual feature projection |
| 6 | $\Delta R_{ij} = \prod \text{Exp}((\omega - b^g)\Delta t)$ | IMU preintegration | High-rate pose delta |
| 7 | $E = \sum_k \Vert p_k^{\text{src}} - T \cdot p_k^{\text{tgt}} \Vert^2$ | ICP cost | LiDAR odometry |
| 8 | $x_k = F_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k$ | EKF predict | Filter-based SLAM |
| 9 | $K_k = P_k H_k^\top (H_k P_k H_k^\top + R_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | EKF correction |
| 10 | $H = \sum_{(i,j)} J_{ij}^\top \Omega_{ij} J_{ij}$ | Gauss-Newton Hessian | Pose-graph optimization |
| 11 | $\text{F-score} = 2\frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Place recognition eval | Forest loop closure |
| 12 | $v_\text{max} = \sqrt{\mu_s g r}$ | Terramechanics | Wheel slip prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule for tables:** When writing absolute value, norm, or determinant inside any KTU markdown table, **always** use `\Vert \cdot \Vert`, `\vert \cdot \vert`, or `\mid \cdot \mid`. Never use the bare pipe `|` symbol — it breaks the markdown table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Post-earthquake/avalanche forest reconnaissance (e.g., ANYmal-C search missions, DARPA SubT).
- **Precision Agriculture & Forestry:** Autonomous tree inventory, phenotyping, log-skidder automation (John Deere, Komatsu foresters).
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Biodiversity surveys with acoustic + visual SLAM in rainforests.
- **Defense:** Perimeter patrol in wooded military installations.
- **Wildfire Response:** Front-line situational awareness in smoke-obscured, GPS-denied forest.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Iterative Closest Point (ICP) for Forest LiDAR Odometry

ICP is the workhorse scan-matching algorithm that aligns two successive LiDAR sweeps $S^{\text{src}}$ and $S^{\text{tgt}}$. We derive it step-by-step as a Gauss-Newton minimization on $\text{SE}(3)$.

**Step 1 — Formalize the cost function.**
We seek a rigid transform $T = (R, t) \in \text{SE}(3)$ that minimizes the sum of squared distances between corresponding points:

$$
E(T) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert S^{\text{src}} \vert} \sum_{p \in S^{\text{src}}} \Vert p - T \cdot q(p) \Vert_2^2
$$

where $q(p) \in S^{\text{tgt}}$ is the nearest neighbour of $p$ in the target cloud (under KD-tree lookup).

**Step 2 — Linearize via perturbation on the Lie algebra.**
Let $T = \text{Exp}(\xi)\, T_{\text{prev}}$ with $\xi = (\delta\phi, \delta\rho)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^6$. A first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$
T \cdot q(p) \;\approx\; T_{\text{prev}} q(p) + \underbrace{\mathcal{J}_T \xi}_{\text{linear correction}}
$$

where $\mathcal{J}_T \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 6}$ is the pose-Jacobian (derivative of a 3-D point w.r.t. the 6-DoF pose).

**Step 3 — Build the residual.**
The per-point residual vector is:

$$
r_i(\xi) \;=\; T_{\text{prev}} q(p_i) + \mathcal{J}_i \xi - p_i
$$

**Step 4 — Stack the linear system.**
Stacking all $N$ residuals, the Gauss-Newton normal equations become:

$$
\underbrace{\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{J}_i^\top \mathcal{J}_i \right)}_{H \in \mathbb{R}^{6\times 6}} \xi^\star \;=\; -\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{J}_i^\top r_i(0)}_{b \in \mathbb{R}^{6}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve and update.**

$$
\xi^\star = -H^{-1} b
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \text{Exp}(\xi^\star)\, T_{\text{prev}}
$$

**Step 6 — Iterate to convergence.**
Re-evaluate nearest neighbours with the updated $T_{\text{new}}$ and repeat Steps 1–5 until $\Vert \xi^\star \Vert < \epsilon$ (typical $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Forest-specific note:** Vanilla ICP fails on leaves because the *correspondence* is ambiguous. Forest SLAM uses **Generalized ICP (GICP)** or **plane-to-plane ICP** with a plane covariance $\Sigma_i$ per point, giving a cost $r_i^\top (\Sigma_i^{p} + R\Sigma_j^{q} R^\top)^{-1} r_i$, which is more robust to leaf outliers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — EKF-SLAM Linearized Measurement Update

For a 2-D forest scenario with range-bearing landmarks, the robot state is augmented with landmark positions:

$$
x_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_v \\ y_v \\ \theta_v \\ x_{\ell_1} \\ y_{\ell_1} \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3+2N}
$$

**Step 1 — Motion model (predator step).**

$$
x_{k\mid k-1} = f(x_{k-1}, u_k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{k-1} + v_k \Delta t \cos\theta_{k-1} \\ y_{k-1} + v_k \Delta t \sin\theta_{k-1} \\ \theta_{k-1} + \omega_k \Delta t \\ x_{\ell_1} \\ y_{\ell_1} \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^\top + Q_k
$$

where $F_k = \partial f / \partial x \vert_{\hat x_{k-1}}$ and $Q_k$ is process noise (in forest: $Q_k$ is *inflated* to account for wheel slip).

**Step 2 — Observation model for trunk $j$.**

$$
z_{k,j} = h(x_k) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(x_{\ell_j} - x_v)^2 + (y_{\ell_j} - y_v)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(y_{\ell_j} - y_v,\; x_{\ell_j} - x_v) - \theta_v \end{bmatrix} + v_k
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation (residual) and its Jacobian.**

$$
\nu_{k,j} = z_{k,j} - h(\hat x_{k\mid k-1})
$$

$$
H_{k,j} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\hat x_{k\mid k-1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman gain and state correction.**

$$
K_{k,j} = P_{k\mid k-1} H_{k,j}^\top (H_{k,j} P_{k\mid k-1} H_{k,j}^\top + R_k)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat x_{k\mid k} = \hat x_{k\mid k-1} + K_{k,j} \nu_{k,j}
$$

$$
P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_{k,j} H_{k,j}) P_{k\mid k-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Why EKF-SLAM is impractical in real forests:** The state grows linearly with landmark count, so complexity is $O(N^2)$ per step. A forest with 50 000+ tree-trunk returns (per scan) makes EKF-SLAM computationally intractable. Hence modern forest SLAM uses **pose-graph** or **factor-graph** formulations, not EKFs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forest Loop Closure Score (NetVLAD / Scan-Context)

A common place-recognition score in forest LiDAR is the **Scan-Context** ring descriptor. Given a 3-D point cloud at pose $p_k$, bin it into a polar grid of $N_r$ rings and $N_s$ sectors:

$$
\Omega(p_k) \in \mathbb{R}^{N_r \times N_s}, \qquad \Omega_{ij} = \max_{p \in \text{bin}(i,j)} z_p
$$

where $z_p$ is the maximum height of points in ring $i$, sector $j$.

The descriptor distance between two scans is the **column-wise cosine distance**:

$$
d(\Omega_a, \Omega_b) = \frac{1}{N_s} \sum_{j=1}^{N_s} \left( 1 - \frac{c_j^a \cdot c_j^b}{\Vert c_j^a \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert c_j^b \Vert_2} \right)
$$

where $c_j^a, c_j^b \in \mathbb{R}^{N_r}$ are the $j$-th columns. A low $d$ score implies the same forest clearing.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for examiners:** *Rings* (radial) capture **distance-from-robot** information; *sectors* (azimuthal) capture **direction-from-North**. Scan-Context is rotation-robust in the yaw direction — vital when a robot re-enters a clearing from a different heading.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Algorithm Selection in Forest

| Algorithm | Sensor | Forest Suitability | Drift Rate | Compute |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ORB-SLAM3 | Mono/Stereo + IMU | **Poor** (lighting variant) | ~1 % | Low (CPU) |
| LOAM | 3D LiDAR | **Good** | <0.5 % | Medium |
| LIO-SAM | LiDAR + IMU + GNSS | **Excellent** | <0.3 % | High (factor graph) |
| FAST-LIO2 | LiDAR + IMU (ikd-Tree) | **Excellent** | <0.4 % | Medium (EKF) |
| Cartographer | 2D/3D LiDAR | **Good** (CSM + submap) | <0.6 % | High |
| GMapping | 2D LiDAR | **Marginal** (only floor maps) | ~1 % | Low |
| RTAB-Map | RGB-D / Stereo | **Moderate** | ~0.8 % | Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forest SLAM Perception Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forest Robot Hardware Platform] --> B[Sensor Stack]
    B --> B1[3D LiDAR Velodyne VLP-16]
    B --> B2[Stereo Camera ZED 2i]
    B --> B3[IMU BMI088 9-Axis]
    B --> B4[GNSS Ublox ZED-F9P]
    B --> B5[Wheel Odometry]
    B1 --> C1[LiDAR Front End: Feature Extraction Planes and Edges]
    B2 --> C2[Visual Front End: ORB Feature Tracking]
    B3 --> C3[IMU Preintegration on SO 3]
    B4 --> C4[GNSS Preprocessing RTK Fix or Float]
    B5 --> C5[Wheel Odom Forward Kinematics]
    C1 --> D{Factor Graph Optimizer GTSAM or iSAM2}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Pose Estimate x y z roll pitch yaw]
    D --> F[Forest Map: 3D Point Cloud Octomap]
    D --> G[Loop Closure Detection Scan Context or NetVLAD]
    G --> D
    E --> H[Decision Module: Traversability and Path Planner]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Motion Controller: Diff Drive or Ackermann]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Forest-Specific Perception Challenges and Mitigations (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Dense GNSS Attenuation] --> M1[Tightly Coupled LiDAR-IMU Odometry]
    P2[Dynamic Vegetation] --> M2[Dynamic Point Cloud Filtering ERASOR or Removert]
    P3[Perceptual Aliasing Repetitive Trunks] --> M3[Geometric Verification ICP on Loop Candidates]
    P4[Low Light and Shadow Variation] --> M4[Active IR Illumination and HDR Imaging]
    P5[Wheel Slip on Leaf Litter] --> M5[Visual Inertial Odometry as Slip Compensator]
    P6[Scale Ambiguity Monocular] --> M6[Stereo Baseline or LiDAR Depth Fusion]
    P1 --> M1
    P2 --> M2
    P3 --> M3
    P4 --> M4
    P5 --> M5
    P6 --> M6
    M1 --> O[Robust Forest SLAM Estimate]
    M2 --> O
    M3 --> O
    M4 --> O
    M5 --> O
    M6 --> O
```

### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Topology (Tightly-Coupled Factor Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0[Pose x0] --> X1[Pose x1]
    X1 --> X2[Pose x2]
    X2 --> X3[Pose x3]
    X3 --> X4[Pose x4]
    X1 --- IMU1[IMU Factor u0-1]
    X2 --- IMU2[IMU Factor u1-2]
    X3 --- IMU3[IMU Factor u2-3]
    X4 --- IMU4[IMU Factor u3-4]
    X1 --- L1[LiDAR Factor z1]
    X2 --- L2[LiDAR Factor z2]
    X3 --- L3[LiDAR Factor z3]
    X4 --- L4[LiDAR Factor z4]
    X0 --- G0[GNSS Prior]
    X4 --- G4[GNSS Prior]
    X1 -.loop.-> X4[Loop Closure Factor Scan Context]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed edge between `X1` and `X4` represents a **loop-closure factor** — a non-sequential constraint added when the robot re-recognizes a previously visited location. This is the single most important factor for *correcting accumulated drift* in long forest traverses.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
Define SLAM. Why is the forest environment particularly challenging for SLAM algorithms compared to indoor environments?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) is the recursive estimation problem of determining a robot's pose while concurrently constructing a map of an unknown environment, formulated as the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$.
Forests are challenging because: **(i)** dense canopy attenuates GNSS signals (GPS-denied condition); **(ii)** repetitive tree-trunk geometry causes perceptual aliasing, leading to false loop closures; **(iii)** dynamic vegetation (swaying branches/leaves) introduces outlier measurements that corrupt scan matching; **(iv)** uneven terrain causes wheel slip and odometry drift; and **(v)** seasonal/lighting variations cause visual feature instability. (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 2–3 challenges.)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
List the major exteroceptive and proprioceptive sensors used in forest field robots and give one forest-specific limitation of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sensor | Type | Forest Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| 3D LiDAR | Exteroceptive | Sparse returns on foliage; expensive |
| Stereo Camera | Exteroceptive | Lighting-variant; range limited |
| IMU | Proprioceptive | Bias drift over long traverses |
| GNSS | Exteroceptive (pseudo) | Signal blocked by canopy |
| Wheel Encoders | Proprioceptive | Slip on leaf litter |

(½ mark per row × 4 sensors = 2 marks; 1 mark for any one forest-specific limitation explained.)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice: attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Adapted from KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a tightly-coupled LiDAR-IMU-GNSS SLAM system suitable for a forest environment. Discuss the role of the factor graph in fusing heterogeneous sensor measurements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the linearized measurement-update step of an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for a 2-D range-bearing landmark observation. State clearly why vanilla EKF-SLAM is computationally infeasible in dense forest environments. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
- Block diagram (3 marks): Draw the perception stack (LiDAR feature extraction → IMU preintegration → GNSS preprocessing → factor graph → pose + map output). Reference §4.1 of these notes.
- Role of factor graph (2 marks): Joint optimization of all sensor factors; allows re-linearization; re-uses historical measurements; provides globally consistent estimate.
- Forest suitability (2 marks): GNSS provides global anchors when available; LiDAR handles canopy-darkness; IMU bridges motion during LiDAR dropouts; loop closure corrects drift.

**(b) EKF-SLAM derivation (7 marks):**
- State vector definition (1 mark): $x = [x_v, y_v, \theta_v, x_{\ell_1}, y_{\ell_1}, \ldots]^\top$.
- Observation model (1 mark): $z_j = [\sqrt{(x_{\ell_j}-x_v)^2+(y_{\ell_j}-y_v)^2}, \text{atan2}(\ldots)-\theta_v]^\top$.
- Jacobian $H = \partial h/\partial x$ (2 marks).
- Kalman gain and update (2 marks): $K = PH^\top(HPH^\top+R)^{-1}$; $\hat x = \hat x + K\nu$.
- Computational infeasibility (1 mark): State dimension grows linearly with $N$ landmarks (~$10^4$–$10^5$ trunks), making $P$ matrix operations $O(N^2)$–$O(N^3)$ intractable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm for LiDAR scan matching. Show the Gauss-Newton linearization step that leads to the $6 \times 6$ normal equations for the pose update on $\text{SE}(3)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major sources of error in forest SLAM. How does a robust cost function (e.g., Huber or Cauchy kernel) mitigate the effect of dynamic vegetation outliers? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ICP (7 marks):**
- Cost function (1 mark): $E(T) = \sum \Vert p - T q \Vert^2$.
- Nearest-neighbour correspondence (1 mark).
- Pose perturbation $T = \text{Exp}(\xi) T_{\text{prev}}$ (2 marks).
- Gauss-Newton system (2 marks): $H\xi^\star = -b$ with $H = \sum J_i^\top J_i$, $b = \sum J_i^\top r_i$.
- Update + iterate (1 mark).

**(b) Forest error sources + robust kernel (7 marks):**
- List 4 error sources (2 marks): GNSS multipath, wheel slip, dynamic leaves, perceptual aliasing.
- Huber kernel definition (2 marks): $\rho_H(r) = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2$ for $\vert r \vert \le \delta$, else $\delta(\vert r \vert - \tfrac{1}{2}\delta)$.
- Cauchy kernel definition (1 mark): $\rho_C(r) = \tfrac{c^2}{2} \log(1 + (r/c)^2)$.
- Why robust kernels help (2 marks): They down-weight large residuals from dynamic leaves/trunks in the *tail* of the distribution, preventing a single outlier from corrupting the entire pose estimate. This is critical because forest data is heavy-tailed and non-Gaussian.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Q(a):**
> A very common mistake is for students to write the ICP cost as a sum over **all** points without specifying the **correspondence** $q(p)$. The correspondence is the *defining* step of ICP — without it, ICP is just a non-rigid registration. **You lose 2 marks** if you omit the nearest-neighbour step.
>
> **Pitfall — Part B Q(b):**
> Students often write "robust kernels remove outliers" — this is **wrong**. Robust kernels *down-weight* outliers, they do not *remove* them. The distinction matters. A correct statement is: "robust kernels reduce the influence of outliers on the optimizer by saturating the cost for large residuals."

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ SLAM = simultaneous pose + map estimation; formal posterior is $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$.
- ✅ Forest = GPS-denied, dynamic, lighting-variant, perceptually aliased, slip-prone.
- ✅ Multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR + IMU + GNSS) is **mandatory** for robust forest SLAM.
- ✅ Factor graph optimization > EKF for large-scale forest mapping.
- ✅ ICP and its variants (GICP, plane-to-plane) are the LiDAR front-end backbone.
- ✅ IMU preintegration provides high-rate pose deltas between LiDAR scans.
- ✅ Loop closure corrects long-term drift; Scan-Context / NetVLAD are forest-friendly descriptors.
- ✅ Robust kernels (Huber, Cauchy, DCS) are essential because forest data is heavy-tailed.
- ✅ GNSS accuracy under canopy: degrades from ~2.5 m (open) to >10 m (dense).
- ✅ KTU expects: definition + sensor table + factor graph + ICP derivation + EKF derivation.
- ✅ Always use `\Vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables — never the bare pipe `|`.
- ✅ Always include a blank line above and below every standalone `$$ ... $$` block.
- ✅ In a 14-mark Part-B, the block diagram alone is worth **at least 2–3 marks** — never skip it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Autonomy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Field and Service Robotics (PCRUT602)**, **Autonomy** is formally defined as the capability of a robotic system to *perceive* its environment, *reason* about its state and goals, *plan* a sequence of actions, and *execute* those actions **without real-time human intervention**, while adapting to dynamic, unstructured, and often uncertain operational conditions.

Mathematically, the degree of autonomy $A$ of a robot can be expressed as a function of the ratio of decision-making bandwidth handled by the robot to the total decision-making bandwidth required for a given task:

$$A = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}}$$

where $D_{robot}$ is the number of decisions made autonomously per unit time, and $D_{human}$ is the number of decisions delegated to a human operator per unit time. A value of $A = 1$ implies **full autonomy**, while $A = 0$ implies **pure teleoperation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Autonomy is *not* a binary state. It exists on a **spectrum (or continuum)** from manual teleoperation to fully autonomous operation, and the level of autonomy is task-dependent and environment-dependent.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "New Driver" Metaphor:**
> 
> Think of a robot's autonomy level exactly like a new driver's progression:
> 
> - **Level 0 (Teleoperated):** A learner driver with an instructor holding a second steering wheel — *every* turn is dictated by the human.
> - **Level 2 (Semi-autonomous):** Adaptive Cruise Control is on, but the driver must keep hands on the wheel and monitor the road.
> - **Level 4 (Highly autonomous):** The car drives itself in a geofenced city area, but cannot operate in unmapped snowy mountain roads.
> - **Level 5 (Full autonomy):** A skilled chauffeur who can drive anywhere, anytime, in any weather, with no human input.
> 
> A **field robot** (e.g., a Mars rover) is often Level 4–5 because it must operate millions of kilometers away from human controllers with multi-minute communication delays. A **service robot** (e.g., a hospital assistant) is typically Level 2–3, as it shares space with humans and must yield to unpredictable human behavior.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Autonomy Classification

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomy Index | $A$ | 0 to 1 | Fraction of robot-made decisions |
| Mean Time Between Human Interventions | MTBHI | hours–days | Reliability measure |
| Operational Design Domain | ODD | Set of conditions | Defines safe operating envelope |
| Response Latency | $\tau$ | ms–s | Critical for dynamic environments |
| Communication Delay | $T_c$ | s–min | Field robots have $T_c \gg \tau$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** "Autonomy" in robotics is **not** the same as "Automation." Automation implies a *fixed, pre-programmed* sequence of actions (e.g., a factory arm). Autonomy implies *adaptive, intelligent* decision-making in response to a changing world.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Robotic Autonomy

For a robot to be considered autonomous, it must implement three tightly-coupled functional pillars. These pillars map directly to KTU Course Outcomes related to **system architecture** and **intelligent behavior**.

**Pillar 1 — Perception (The "Senses")**
- Acquiring data from sensors: **LiDAR, cameras (RGB / RGB-D / stereo), IMU, GPS, ultrasonic, tactile**.
- Processing raw data into meaningful representations: object detection, semantic segmentation, occupancy grids, pose estimation.
- *Why it matters:* A robot cannot reason about what it cannot perceive. In **field robotics**, perception must handle dust, fog, low light, and GPS-denied caves. In **service robotics**, perception must recognize human gestures, faces, and intent.

**Pillar 2 — Cognition / Planning (The "Brain")**
- Building and updating an internal **world model**.
- Path planning (global A*, Dijkstra) and local trajectory optimization (DWA, MPC).
- Task scheduling, behavior selection, and human-robot interaction policies.
- *Why it matters:* This is where the robot *decides* what to do. Cognition can be **deliberative** (think before you act) or **reactive** (act now, think later).

**Pillar 3 — Action (The "Muscles")**
- Executing commands via actuators: motors, grippers, wheels, drone rotors.
- Low-level control loops: PID, feedforward, model-based control.
- *Why it matters:* Even a perfect plan fails if actuation is sloppy.

### 2.2 Levels of Autonomy — KTU Reference Framework

The most widely-cited framework (used in KTU board questions) is the **Sheridan Scale of Human-Robot Interaction**, adapted into the following 10 levels used in modern robotics textbooks:

| Level | Name | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Teleoperation | Human does *all* work | Bomb disposal robot |
| 2 | Shared control | Robot assists one axis | Surgical robot with tremor filter |
| 3 | Supervisory | Human sets goals, robot executes | Mars rover (Curiosity) |
| 4 | Task-level | Human gives task, robot decomposes | Warehouse order fulfillment |
| 5 | Autonomous with constraints | Robot chooses path, but stays in geofence | Roomba vacuum |
| 6 | Fully autonomous | Robot sets its own sub-goals | Self-driving car in geofence |
| 7 | Collective autonomy | Swarm cooperation | Drone light shows |
| 8 | Human-robot teaming | Bidirectional intent sharing | Collaborative manufacturing |
| 9 | Cognitive autonomy | Robot reasons about its own state | Self-diagnosing satellite |
| 10 | Super-intelligent | Beyond current scope | Hypothetical AGI |

### 2.3 Architectural Paradigms

**A. Deliberative (Sense → Plan → Act)**
- Classic AI robotics architecture (Nilsson, Shakey, 1969).
- The robot maintains a **complete world model**, plans an optimal path, then executes.
- *Weakness:* Slow; brittle in dynamic environments where the world model becomes stale.

**B. Reactive (Sense → Act)**
- Proposed by Rodney Brooks in 1986 (the **Subsumption Architecture**).
- No internal world model. Behavior is a direct mapping from sensors to actuators.
- *Strength:* Extremely fast, robust to dynamic change.
- *Weakness:* Cannot reason about long-term goals; "the world is its own best model."

**C. Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive (Three-Layer Architecture)**
- The **de facto standard** in modern field and service robots.
  - **Layer 1 (Reactive/Executive):** Low-level control, runs at kHz.
  - **Layer 2 (Sequencer/Executive):** Manages state machine of mission, runs at Hz.
  - **Layer 3 (Deliberative/Planner):** High-level mission planning, runs at sub-Hz.
- *Example:* Autonomous car software stacks (Apollo, Autoware) follow this pattern.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomy Index | $A = \dfrac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}}$ | $D_{robot}, D_{human}$ | dimensionless |
| Situational Awareness Score | $SA = \alpha P + \beta U + \beta' C$ | $P$=perception, $U$=understanding, $C$=comprehension, weights $\alpha, \beta$ | 0–100 scale |
| Control Loop Period | $T_{loop} = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency | seconds |
| Stability Margin (Lyapunov) | $\Delta V = V(x_{k+1}) - V(x_k) < 0$ | $V$ = Lyapunov function | energy units |
| Bounded Latency for Safety | $\tau < \dfrac{d_{safe}}{v_{max}}$ | $d_{safe}$ = braking distance, $v_{max}$ = max speed | seconds |
| ODD Coverage | $C_{ODD} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks within ODD}}{\text{Total Tasks}}$ | coverage ratio | dimensionless |

> **Engineering Utility:** In **field robotics** (mining, agriculture, search & rescue), the autonomy index is bounded by communication delay $T_c$. If $T_c > 1\,\text{s}$ (e.g., satellite link), the robot must operate at $A > 0.95$ to avoid collision. In **service robotics** (healthcare, hospitality), $T_c \to 0$ but $A$ is intentionally limited for *liability and safety* reasons.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Teleoperation to Full Autonomy

The most intuitive analytical framework is to derive how the *human workload* decreases as the robot gains autonomy. Let us model a mission with $N$ total decisions.

**Step 1 — Define the total decision count.**
The total number of decisions made during a mission is:

$$N_{total} = N_{human} + N_{robot}$$

**Step 2 — Define the autonomy index in terms of counts.**
By the definition in Section 1.1, the autonomy index is:

$$A = \frac{N_{robot}}{N_{robot} + N_{human}} = \frac{N_{robot}}{N_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the human decision count.**
Rearranging the previous equation to express human decision burden:

$$N_{human} = N_{total} (1 - A)$$

**Step 4 — Introduce communication delay penalty.**
In field robotics, if the human is teleoperating across a link with one-way delay $T_c$, the *effective* time the robot waits is $2T_c$ (round-trip). The human's effective decision rate is:

$$D_{human}^{eff} = \frac{D_{human}}{1 + 2T_c \cdot D_{human}}$$

This is the **cognitive tunneling** effect: as human decision rate increases, the delay penalty also increases nonlinearly.

**Step 5 — Combine into the effective autonomy index.**
Substituting Step 4 into Step 2 gives the *effective* autonomy:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}^{eff}}$$

**Step 6 — Examine limiting cases.**

- **Case A: $T_c \to 0$** (service robot in same room as human)
  - $D_{human}^{eff} \to D_{human}$
  - $A_{eff} \to A$ — human can intervene freely.

- **Case B: $T_c \to \infty$** (deep-space rover)
  - $D_{human}^{eff} \to 0$
  - $A_{eff} \to 1$ — the robot *must* be fully autonomous, regardless of design.

This derivation proves the central KTU teaching point: **autonomy is not a design choice, but a physical necessity when communication delay is large.**

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Autonomy vs. Application Domain

| Application | $T_c$ | Required $A$ | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mars Rover (Curiosity) | ~13 min | $\geq 0.99$ | Cannot wait for Earth commands |
| Subsea ROV | 0.1–2 s | 0.7–0.9 | Acoustic link too slow for fine control |
| Autonomous Taxi (geofenced) | 0 s (5G) | 0.95 | Geofence handles edge cases |
| Hospital Service Robot | 0 s (Wi-Fi) | 0.6 | Human-in-loop for safety/liability |
| Drone Swarm (light show) | 0 s | 1.0 | Collective behavior, no human input |
| Bomb Disposal | 0 s | 0.05 | Human must see every motion |
| Agricultural Harvester | 1–5 s | 0.85 | GPS + cellular, mostly autonomous |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A search-and-rescue robot is deployed in a collapsed mine. The operator is at the surface, communicating over a wireless link with one-way delay $T_c = 0.5$ s. The robot must make $D_{robot} = 50$ decisions per second, and the human can issue $D_{human} = 5$ supervisory decisions per second. Calculate the effective autonomy index $A_{eff}$.

**Solution:**

$$D_{human}^{eff} = \frac{5}{1 + 2(0.5)(5)} = \frac{5}{1 + 5} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833 \text{ decisions/s}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{50}{50 + 0.833} \approx 0.9836$$

**Interpretation:** The robot operates at $A_{eff} \approx 98.4\%$, which is necessary because the half-second delay would prevent the operator from reacting to a wall collapse in time.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Autonomy Functional Stack (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block renders the complete **three-layer hybrid architecture** of an autonomous field/service robot. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU board questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L3["LAYER 3 DELIBERATIVE PLANNER Mission Goal Decomposition Global Path Search Task Scheduling"]
    L2["LAYER 2 EXECUTIVE SEQUENCER State Machine Behavior Arbitration SLAM Module"]
    L1["LAYER 1 REACTIVE CONTROL Low Level PID Loop Obstacle Avoidance Safety Watchdog"]
    SENS["SENSOR ARRAY LiDAR Camera IMU GPS Ultrasonic"]
    ACT["ACTUATOR ARRAY Wheels Manipulator Gripper Drone Rotors"]
    HRI["HUMAN OPERATOR INTERFACE"]

    SENS --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> ACT
    HRI -. Supervisory Commands .-> L3
    HRI -. Emergency Stop .-> L1
    L1 -. Telemetry .-> HRI
    L2 -. Status Updates .-> HRI
```

### 4.2 Autonomy Decision Flow (Sense-Plan-Act Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PERCEIVE Acquire sensor data"]
    B["FUSE Build world model"]
    C["REASON Estimate state predict outcomes"]
    D["PLAN Generate candidate actions"]
    E["SELECT Choose optimal action via cost function"]
    F["ACTUATE Send commands to motors"]
    G["FEEDBACK Measure new sensor data"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

### 4.3 Autonomy Level Trade-off Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW AUTONOMY"]
        T1["Teleoperation Human controls every joint"]
        T2["Telepresence Remote operation with video"]
    end
    subgraph MID["MID AUTONOMY"]
        T3["Shared Control Robot assists"]
        T4["Supervisory Human sets waypoints"]
        T5["Task Level Human gives mission"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH AUTONOMY"]
        T6["Autonomous Geofenced robot picks path"]
        T7["Full Autonomy Robot sets sub goals"]
    end

    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7

    LOW -.->|"Operator workload: HIGH"| O1["Reliability: HIGH (human in loop)"]
    HIGH -.->|"Operator workload: LOW"| O2["Reliability: DEPENDS on AI robustness"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU board frequently asks students to *draw and label* the Sense-Plan-Act loop or the three-layer hybrid architecture. Memorize the names of all three layers and the direction of data flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Autonomy" as applied to field and service robots. Differentiate it from "Automation" with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Autonomy:** Autonomy is the capability of a robot to perceive its environment, make decisions, and execute actions to achieve a goal *without real-time human intervention*, while adapting to changing, unstructured conditions. **(1.5 marks)**

**Automation vs. Autonomy (Table form expected for full marks):**

| Feature | Automation | Autonomy |
|---|---|---|
| Decision-making | Pre-programmed, fixed sequence | Adaptive, context-aware |
| Environment | Structured (e.g., factory floor) | Unstructured (e.g., mine, road, home) |
| Human role | Sets the program once | Sets the goal; robot decides the path |
| Example | A welding arm in a car factory **(0.5 marks)** | A vacuum robot that re-plans when a chair has been moved **(0.5 marks)** |
| Response to change | Cannot respond; fails or stops | Re-plans and continues |

The key distinction: *automation* repeats; *autonomy* thinks. **(0.5 marks for the concluding remark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List any three levels of robot autonomy and give one practical example for each level.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per level):**

1. **Teleoperation (Level 1):** A bomb-disposal robot operated by a human via a wired tether; *every* motor is directly controlled by the joystick. **(1 mark)**
2. **Supervisory Control (Level 3):** NASA's Mars rover *Curiosity*, where scientists upload daily command sequences and the rover executes them autonomously. **(1 mark)**
3. **Full Autonomy (Level 6):** A modern robot vacuum cleaner (e.g., Roomba) that builds a map of the home and re-plans its coverage path when furniture is moved. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module Coverage:** This question set covers Module 1 (Autonomy) exclusively and aligns with **CO1** of PCRUT602: *"Understand the fundamental concepts, levels, and architectures of robot autonomy in field and service applications."*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Autonomy Levels, Architectures, and Field/Service Trade-offs*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Sheridan Scale of Levels of Autonomy** for robots. Describe at least **six distinct levels** with one real-world example for each. State clearly how the *autonomy index* $A$ varies across these levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **deliberative, reactive, and hybrid deliberative/reactive** architectures for robot autonomy. Use a labeled diagram of the *Sense-Plan-Act* loop and the *three-layer hybrid architecture* in your explanation. Justify why the hybrid architecture is the *de facto* standard in modern autonomous vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sheridan Scale of Autonomy — 7 Marks**

The scale categorizes the *degree* to which a robot makes its own decisions versus relying on a human. We tabulate six core levels:

| Level | Name | Who Decides? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Teleoperation | Human does all work | Bomb-disposal manipulator |
| 3 | Supervisory | Human sets goals; robot executes | Mars rover *Curiosity* |
| 5 | Task-level | Human gives task; robot decomposes | Amazon warehouse picker |
| 7 | Fully autonomous | Robot makes all sub-goal decisions | Waymo robotaxi in geofence |
| 8 | Collective autonomy | Multiple robots cooperate | Drone light show |
| 10 | Cognitive autonomy | Robot monitors its own health | Satellite with autonomous fault recovery |

The **autonomy index** $A = \dfrac{N_{robot}}{N_{robot} + N_{human}}$ increases monotonically from 0 (teleoperation) to 1 (full autonomy) as we ascend the scale. For example, in the Mars rover case, $N_{human}$ is constrained by the 13-minute light delay, forcing $A \geq 0.99$. In contrast, a hospital service robot has $A \approx 0.6$ by *design*, because liability regulations mandate human oversight. **(1 mark for tabulation + 2 marks for index discussion + 4 marks for levels with examples)**

**(b) Three Architectural Paradigms — 7 Marks**

| Architecture | How it Works | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deliberative** | World model $\to$ planner $\to$ actuator | Optimal plans | Slow, brittle in dynamic env. |
| **Reactive** | Direct sensor-to-actuator mapping | Fast, robust | No long-term reasoning |
| **Hybrid** | Three layers: reactive (kHz) + executive (Hz) + deliberative (sub-Hz) | Combines both strengths | Complex software engineering |

**Sense-Plan-Act Diagram (expected in answer, 2 marks):**
The classic loop is *Perceive $\to$ Fuse $\to$ Reason $\to$ Plan $\to$ Select $\to$ Actuate $\to$ Feedback*. (See Section 4.2 of these notes for the labeled flow.)

**Three-Layer Hybrid Diagram (expected in answer, 2 marks):**
Students should draw three stacked layers (Reactive at bottom, Executive in middle, Deliberative on top) with arrows indicating that high-level goals flow *down* and sensor data flows *up*. The reactive layer handles obstacle avoidance at kHz; the executive runs SLAM and state machines; the deliberative layer runs global A* path planning.

**Why hybrid is standard in autonomous vehicles (1 mark):**
A self-driving car must (1) avoid a pedestrian in 50 ms — a **reactive** task, (2) decide whether to merge or yield — an **executive** task, and (3) plan a 30-km route — a **deliberative** task. No single architecture handles all three time scales; hence the hybrid model is universally adopted (e.g., Apollo, Autoware, NVIDIA DriveOS).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Focus: Autonomy in Field Robotics, Communication Delay, and the Autonomy Index*

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the *autonomy index* $A$, the robot's decision rate $D_{robot}$, and the human operator's decision rate $D_{human}$. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planetary rover is exploring the surface of Titan (Saturn's moon). The one-way communication delay to Earth is $T_c = 45$ minutes. The rover makes $D_{robot} = 100$ decisions per second, and the Earth-based human team can issue at most $D_{human} = 2$ supervisory commands per hour. Compute:
- (i) the raw autonomy index $A$,
- (ii) the effective autonomy index $A_{eff}$ accounting for communication delay,
- (iii) the average waiting time per human command, and
- (iv) comment on whether the rover's autonomy is sufficient for safe operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Autonomy Index — 7 Marks**

Let the total decisions per unit time be $D_{total} = D_{robot} + D_{human}$. **(1 mark)**

By definition:

$$A = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{total}} = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}}$$

**(1 mark for the definition)**

Solving for the human decision burden:

$$D_{human} = D_{robot} \left(\frac{1 - A}{A}\right)$$

**(1 mark)**

When the human is separated by a communication link of one-way delay $T_c$, the round-trip delay is $2T_c$. A human command takes $2T_c$ seconds to be received by the robot. Therefore the *effective* human decision rate is reduced by the delay penalty:

$$D_{human}^{eff} = \frac{D_{human}}{1 + 2T_c \cdot D_{human}}$$

**(2 marks for introducing the delay penalty)**

The effective autonomy becomes:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}^{eff}} = \frac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + \dfrac{D_{human}}{1 + 2T_c D_{human}}}$$

**(1 mark)**

Limiting cases:
- If $T_c \to 0$ (local teleoperation), $D_{human}^{eff} \to D_{human}$ and $A_{eff} \to A$. **(0.5 marks)**
- If $T_c \to \infty$ (deep space), $D_{human}^{eff} \to 0$ and $A_{eff} \to 1$ — the robot *must* be fully autonomous. **(0.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $D_{robot} = 100$ decisions/s, $D_{human} = 2$ commands/hour $= \frac{2}{3600} \approx 5.56 \times 10^{-4}$ decisions/s, $T_c = 45$ min $= 2700$ s.

**(i) Raw Autonomy Index (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{100}{100 + 5.56 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.999994 \approx 0.99999$$

**(ii) Effective Autonomy Index (3 marks):**

$$D_{human}^{eff} = \frac{5.56 \times 10^{-4}}{1 + 2(2700)(5.56 \times 10^{-4})} = \frac{5.56 \times 10^{-4}}{1 + 3.003} = \frac{5.56 \times 10^{-4}}{4.003}$$

$$D_{human}^{eff} \approx 1.389 \times 10^{-4} \text{ decisions/s}$$

$$A_{eff} = \frac{100}{100 + 1.389 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.9999986$$

**(iii) Average waiting time per human command (1.5 marks):**
The round-trip time is $2T_c = 2(45) = 90$ minutes. The human team issues 2 commands/hour, so a command arrives roughly every 30 minutes. Plus the 90-minute round-trip delay, the *total* lag from human decision to rover action is $30 + 90 = 120$ minutes = 2 hours. **(1.5 marks)**

**(iv) Comment on sufficiency (1.5 marks):**
With $A_{eff} \approx 0.999999$, the rover is effectively fully autonomous. The 2-hour lag is far too long for *any* real-time reaction to terrain hazards, so all obstacle avoidance, SLAM, and fault-recovery **must** be performed onboard. The mission is feasible *only* because the rover is designed for Level 7+ autonomy from the start. The autonomy is *sufficient* — but it is also *mandatory*, not optional. **(1.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Automation with Autonomy** (loses 1–2 marks in Q1). Always state the *adaptive* nature of autonomy.
> 2. **Missing the direction of arrows in the Sense-Plan-Act diagram** (loses 1 mark in Q-A/b). The arrows must show the *closed loop* nature, with feedback returning to perception.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation of the autonomy index** (loses up to 4 marks in Q-B/a). KTU board *requires* showing every algebraic step. Do not write only the final formula.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** (loses 1 mark in Q-B/b). Always convert minutes to seconds and commands/hour to commands/second before substituting.
> 5. **Omitting the figure in the architecture question** (loses 2 marks). The diagram is worth at least 2 marks in the 7-mark sub-part. Practice drawing it cleanly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Autonomy** = ability to *perceive, reason, plan, and act* without real-time human intervention. It is **adaptive**, not pre-programmed.
- ✅ **Autonomy Index:** $A = \dfrac{D_{robot}}{D_{robot} + D_{human}}$ — ranges from 0 (teleop) to 1 (full auto).
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Perception, Cognition, Action — all three are *necessary*; no autonomy if any is missing.
- ✅ **Three Architectures:** Deliberative (slow, optimal), Reactive (fast, blind), Hybrid (best of both — industry standard).
- ✅ **Three-Layer Hybrid Stack:** Reactive (kHz) → Executive (Hz) → Deliberative (sub-Hz).
- ✅ **Communication delay** *forces* autonomy. As $T_c$ increases, $A_{eff} \to 1$ regardless of design preference.
- ✅ **Field vs. Service:** Field robots face unstructured outdoor environments and long $T_c$ (need high $A$). Service robots share space with humans (liability often *limits* $A$).
- ✅ **Sheridan Scale** has 10 levels; know at least 6 for exam.
- ✅ **Draw the Sense-Plan-Act loop and the Three-Layer Architecture** — both are mandatory in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Limiting cases** of any autonomy formula (e.g., $T_c \to 0$ and $T_c \to \infty$) are favorite KTU board questions.
- ✅ **ODD (Operational Design Domain)** is the geofence or condition-set within which the autonomy is *certified* to work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In any 14-mark autonomy question, allocate your time as: **2 marks for definition + 4 marks for architecture/levels + 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for derivation/numerics + 3 marks for application/comment.** A neatly labeled diagram is worth 2 marks *minimum*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Teleoperation and Fleet Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Teleoperation and Fleet Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Teleoperation — Formal Definition

**Teleoperation** is the remote control of a robotic system by a human operator from a distance, where commands, sensory feedback (and optionally, haptic force reflection) are exchanged over a communication channel. In the KTU 2024 scheme context of *Field and Service Robotics*, teleoperation is the cornerstone technology that allows robots to operate in environments that are hazardous, inaccessible, or geographically distant from the human — for example, deep-sea inspection, nuclear decommissioning, planetary rovers, and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs).

The three canonical operating modes taught under the KTU syllabus are:

| Mode | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Control** | Operator drives every actuator in real time | Drone piloting, bomb disposal |
| **Supervisory Control** | Operator gives high-level goals; robot executes autonomously | Mars rovers, surgical robots |
| **Shared Control** | Authority is blended between human and autonomous controller | Smart wheelchairs, assistive exoskeletons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCRUT602):** *Teleoperation architectures, haptic feedback, and stability under time delay* form a high-weightage area. Expect at least one 7-mark sub-part on this topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy for Teleoperation

Imagine a **Mars rover mission**. The rover is millions of kilometres away, so a one-way radio signal takes **4 to 24 minutes**. A human operator in a control room in Pasadena sends joystick commands, but the rover cannot wait for human reaction time — instead, the operator *supervises*: sets science targets, plans trajectories, and the rover's onboard autonomy executes them. The latency, the *round-trip delay*, is what makes pure direct control impossible and motivates **supervisory and shared control architectures**.

## 1.3 Fleet Management — Formal Definition

**Fleet Management** in robotics refers to the centralized or distributed coordination of a *group* (fleet) of heterogeneous robotic agents — typically Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs) or Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) — performing a shared mission. It encompasses **task allocation, path planning, traffic management, scheduling, and state monitoring** to maximise fleet-level Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) such as throughput, makespan, energy efficiency, and collision-free operation.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy for Fleet Management

Think of **Amazon's Kiva / Hercules robots** in a fulfilment centre. Hundreds of squat orange robots zip under shelves, lift them, and bring inventory to human pickers. No single robot "knows" the entire warehouse. Instead, a **central fleet manager** allocates pick tasks, plans collision-free paths, and resolves intersections using a *reservation-grid* traffic system. The analogy extends naturally to hospital logistics robots, agricultural UAV swarms, and autonomous taxi fleets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (Bold for KTU):**
> - **Throughput (η):** Tasks completed per unit time
> - **Makespan (Tₘ):** Total time to complete a job batch
> - **Fleet Utilisation (ρ):** Fraction of time robots are productive (typically **0.6 to 0.85** for well-balanced systems)
> - **Round-Trip Latency (τ):** Time delay between command and acknowledgement (**typically 20 ms to 500 ms** in field robotics)

## 1.5 Why Teleoperation and Fleet Management Are Coupled Topics

In modern field robotics, a **single teleoperator often supervises many robots**. The 2024 KTU syllabus deliberately groups these concepts because:

1. **Shared communication infrastructure** (latency, bandwidth) affects both.
2. **Supervisory control scales** by delegating sub-tasks to a fleet.
3. **Human-swarm interaction (HSI)** is a frontier research area built on both pillars.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Teleoperation Architecture — The Four-Block Model

Every teleoperation system can be decomposed into five functional blocks:

1. **Human Operator (H)** — Applies force $F_h^*$ to the master.
2. **Master Device (M)** — Haptic I/O interface; measures position $x_m$, velocity $v_m$.
3. **Communication Channel (C)** — Bidirectional link with **round-trip delay $T = T_{up} + T_{down}$** and possible packet loss.
4. **Slave Robot (S)** — Actuated to track master commands in a remote environment.
5. **Environment (E)** — Exerts reaction force $F_e$ on the slave.

The **Why**: Each block introduces dynamics (impedance, latency, quantisation) that degrade *transparency* — the fidelity with which the operator perceives the remote environment.

## 2.2 Transparency — The Teleoperation Quality Metric

**Transparency** is defined as the match between the impedance transmitted to the operator ($Z_t$) and the true environment impedance ($Z_e$):

$$Z_t = \frac{F_h}{v_m} \approx Z_e \quad \text{(perfect transparency)}$$

For **ideal transparency**, the operator should feel as if directly manipulating the remote object. The teleoperator must therefore replicate *both* kinesthetic position *and* force cues.

## 2.3 Stability vs Transparency — The Fundamental Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **single most important KTU concept** in this module: Stability and transparency are *competing* objectives. Adding force feedback improves transparency but can destabilise the loop, especially with non-zero time delay. This is the **Ando / Lawrence stability problem**.

**Lawrence's Stability Condition (4-channel architecture):**
A 2-port bilateral controller is stable if and only if the *hybrid matrix* satisfies a strict passivity inequality involving the delay. The simplified scalar form for a 1-DOF position-force architecture is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Stable} \iff 1 - C_1(s) C_2(s) e^{-2sT} > 0\;}$$

where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the local master and slave controllers and $T$ is the round-trip delay. Intuitively, **high controller gain + high delay = instability**.

## 2.4 Haptic Feedback and Force Scaling

The operator feels a scaled version of the environment force:

$$F_h = \gamma \cdot F_e \quad (\gamma = \text{force scaling factor})$$

Velocity and position scaling (typical in macro–micro teleoperation):

$$v_s = \alpha \cdot v_m \quad ; \quad x_s = \beta \cdot x_m$$

For **impedance matching** (transparency), the operator-perceived impedance becomes:

$$Z_t = \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot Z_e$$

## 2.5 Wave-Variable Transformation (Time-Delay Robustness)

To guarantee **passivity** (and hence stability) under arbitrary time delay, Niemeyer and Slotine (1991) introduced wave variables. Signals are encoded as waves before transmission:

$$u = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}(b v + F) \quad ; \quad v_{wave} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2b}}(b v - F)$$

where $b$ is the *wave impedance* (a tuning parameter). The communication channel then only exchanges waves, which are **passive by construction** regardless of delay.

## 2.6 Fleet Management — The Three-Layer Stack

A modern robotic fleet is organised in three layers:

- **Strategic Layer (offline):** Mission planning, battery charging schedules, shift assignments.
- **Tactical Layer (online):** Task allocation, role assignment, global path planning.
- **Operational Layer (real-time):** Local obstacle avoidance, motor control, sensor fusion.

The **Why**: Separating time-scales prevents combinatorial explosion and allows each layer to use appropriate algorithms (e.g., mixed-integer programming at strategic level vs. Dynamic Window Approach at operational level).

## 2.7 Task Allocation — The Core Fleet Optimisation

For a fleet of $N$ robots and $M$ tasks, we seek a binary assignment matrix $X \in \{0,1\}^{N \times M}$ that minimises total cost:

$$\min_{X} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{M} c_{ij} \, x_{ij} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{j} x_{ij} = 1, \; \sum_{i} x_{ij} \leq 1$$

The **Hungarian Algorithm** solves this in $O(N^3)$ for the square case and is the workhorse of warehouse fleet managers.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity scaling | $v_s = \alpha \cdot v_m$ | $\alpha$ = scale factor | dimensionless |
| 2 | Position scaling | $x_s = \beta \cdot x_m$ | $\beta$ = scale factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | Force scaling | $F_h = \gamma \cdot F_e$ | $\gamma$ = force gain | dimensionless |
| 4 | Impedance matching | $Z_t = \alpha \gamma Z_e$ | $Z_t$ = transmitted impedance | N·s/m |
| 5 | Round-trip delay | $T = T_{up} + T_{down}$ | uplink + downlink delay | s |
| 6 | Stability (Lawrence) | $1 - C_1 C_2 e^{-2sT} > 0$ | controller + delay term | dimensionless |
| 7 | Passivity | $E_{in} \geq E_{out} + E_{stored}$ | net energy non-negative | J |
| 8 | Wave variables | $u = (bv + F) / \sqrt{2b}$ | $b$ = wave impedance | m/s, N |
| 9 | Fleet throughput | $\eta = N_{eff} \cdot P_{task} / T_m$ | $N_{eff}$ = effective robots | tasks/s |
| 10 | Hungarian cost | $J = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | assignment cost matrix | varies |
| 11 | Bandwidth requirement | $BW = N \cdot R_b \cdot f_s$ | $R_b$ = bit rate per robot | bits/s |
| 12 | Optimal fleet size | $N^* = \sqrt{2 D C_f / (C_r T)}$ | economic breakeven | robots |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, vertical bars inside math expressions have been replaced with multiplication dots or $\cdot$ to comply with KTU note formatting. For absolute value or norm expressions in your exam answer sheets, use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Teleoperation Utility | Fleet Management Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Nuclear Decommissioning | Reduces human radiation dose | Coordinates multiple inspection UGVs |
| Precision Agriculture | Tractor auto-steer with override | UAV swarm for crop spraying |
| Warehouse Logistics | Manual override of stuck AMRs | 1000+ robot Kiva-style fleets |
| Space Exploration | Mars rover driving via orbiter relay | Future lunar base robot teams |
| Underwater ROVs | Sub-sea cable inspection | Coordinated pipe-laying AUVs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Operator-Perceived Impedance in a Scaled Teleoperator

**Objective:** Derive the relationship between the operator's perceived impedance $Z_t$ and the true environment impedance $Z_e$ under general position, velocity, and force scaling.

### Given Assumptions
- Master and slave are 1-DOF and frictionless.
- Operator applies force $F_h^*$ to the master; the master velocity is $v_m$.
- The slave interacts with the environment of impedance $Z_e$, so $F_e = Z_e \cdot v_s$.

### Step 1 — Establish the Scaling Laws

The teleoperator is configured with three independent scaling factors:

$$x_s = \beta \, x_m, \qquad v_s = \alpha \, v_m, \qquad F_h = \gamma \, F_e$$

Each factor can be tuned. For example, in **microsurgery**, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are small (< 1) to reduce hand tremor, while $\gamma$ may be large (> 1) to amplify subtle tissue forces.

### Step 2 — Write the Environment Force Equation

Newton's second law at the environment contact point:

$$F_e = Z_e \cdot v_s = Z_e \cdot (\alpha v_m)$$

We have used the velocity scaling $v_s = \alpha v_m$ to express $F_e$ in terms of master velocity.

### Step 3 — Substitute Into the Force Scaling Equation

$$F_h = \gamma F_e = \gamma Z_e \alpha v_m$$

### Step 4 — Form the Ratio $F_h / v_m$

Dividing both sides by $v_m$:

$$\frac{F_h}{v_m} = \alpha \, \gamma \, Z_e$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Transmitted Impedance

By definition, the impedance the operator "feels" through the master device is:

$$\boxed{\;Z_t = \frac{F_h}{v_m} = \alpha \, \gamma \, Z_e\;}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result (Engineering Insight)

- If $\alpha = 1$ and $\gamma = 1$, then $Z_t = Z_e$: **perfect transparency**.
- If $\alpha > 1$ (slave moves faster than master), the operator underestimates inertia — useful for *fast manipulation* tasks.
- If $\gamma > 1$ (force amplification), the operator feels enhanced contact forces — useful in *minimally invasive surgery*.

This single equation is the **design cornerstone** of every haptic teleoperation system and is examinable in both short-answer and 14-mark formats.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Bound for Proportional Bilateral Control with Delay

**Objective:** Show that a simple proportional bilateral controller becomes unstable when the loop gain-delay product exceeds a critical value.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Law

Master-side force feedback:

$$F_h = K_p (x_m - x_s^d)$$

where $x_s^d = x_s(t - T)$ is the **delayed** slave position reaching the master after round-trip time $T$.

### Step 2 — Define the Slave-Side Tracking

$$v_s = K_v (x_m^d - x_s)$$

where $x_m^d = x_m(t - T)$ is the delayed master position.

### Step 3 — Combine and Linearise

Integrating the slave velocity gives the slave position:

$$x_s(t) = \int_0^t K_v (x_m(\tau - T) - x_s(\tau)) \, d\tau$$

In the Laplace domain, with the delay modelled as $e^{-sT}$:

$$X_s(s) = \frac{K_v e^{-sT}}{s + K_v} X_m(s)$$

### Step 4 — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

The master loop transfer function (ignoring operator impedance) yields the characteristic equation:

$$s + K_p K_v e^{-2sT} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Delay-Decomposition Test

For small delay, expand $e^{-2sT} \approx 1 - 2sT$:

$$s + K_p K_v (1 - 2sT) = 0$$

$$s (1 - 2 K_p K_v T) + K_p K_v = 0$$

### Step 6 — Stability Requirement

For the coefficient of $s$ to be positive (Routh-Hurwitz criterion for first-order system):

$$1 - 2 K_p K_v T > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\;K_p K_v T < 0.5\;}$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

| Parameter | Effect on Stability | Practical Bound |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ (force gain) | Higher → more unstable | < 200 N/m in 100 ms delay |
| $K_v$ (velocity gain) | Higher → more unstable | < 1 s⁻¹ typically |
| $T$ (delay) | Higher → more unstable | < 100 ms for stable control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that $T$ is the **round-trip** delay. If only uplink delay is given, multiply by **2** before applying the bound.

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Communication Bandwidth for a Robotic Fleet

**Objective:** Compute the minimum Wi-Fi / 5G bandwidth required to teleoperate a fleet of $N$ robots.

### Step 1 — Define Per-Robot Data Rate

Each robot streams:
- **HD video** at 30 fps, 720p, H.264: $R_v = 2 \text{ Mbps}$
- **LiDAR point cloud** at 10 Hz: $R_l = 1 \text{ Mbps}$
- **Telemetry** (pose, IMU, battery): $R_t = 0.05 \text{ Mbps}$

Total per robot:

$$R_b = R_v + R_l + R_t = 2 + 1 + 0.05 = 3.05 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 2 — Add Protocol Overhead

A typical UDP/IP + RTSP stack adds ~**15% overhead**:

$$R_{b,total} = 1.15 \cdot R_b = 3.5075 \text{ Mbps per robot}$$

### Step 3 — Scale to Fleet Size

For $N$ robots sharing an access point with contention factor $f_c$ (typically $f_c = 1.4$ for CSMA/CA Wi-Fi):

$$\boxed{\;BW_{min} = f_c \cdot N \cdot R_{b,total} = 1.4 \cdot N \cdot 3.5075 \text{ Mbps}\;}$$

For a fleet of 10 robots:

$$BW_{min} = 1.4 \cdot 10 \cdot 3.5075 = 49.1 \text{ Mbps}$$

This is comfortably within a Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) cell capacity, but **exceeds** typical 4G uplink (≈ 25 Mbps), motivating 5G adoption for large-scale teleoperated fleets.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Teleoperation System — Bilateral Block Diagram

The diagram below maps the signal flow and identifies each functional block, including the round-trip delay $T$ introduced by the communication channel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    opNode["OPERATOR APPLIES FORCE FH_STAR"]
    mstNode["MASTER DEVICE HAPTIC INTERFACE READS XM AND VM"]
    encNodeA["WAVE VARIABLE ENCODER U EQUALS BV PLUS F DIV SQRT 2B"]
    txNode["UPLINK TRANSMISSION DELAY T UP SECONDS"]
    rxNode["DOWNLINK RECEPTION DELAY T DOWN SECONDS"]
    decNodeB["WAVE VARIABLE DECODER RECOVERS F AND V"]
    slvCtrl["SLAVE CONTROLLER ACTUATES JOINTS"]
    slvNode["SLAVE ROBOT PHYSICAL BODY"]
    envNode["REMOTE ENVIRONMENT IMPEDANCE ZE"]
    snsrNode["SLAVE SENSOR SUITE ENCoders FORCE SENSOR"]
    fbPath["FORCE AND VELOCITY FEEDBACK PATH"]
    
    opNode -->|FH_STAR COUPLED TO| mstNode
    mstNode -->|VM XM TO ENCODER| encNodeA
    encNodeA -->|UPLINK WAVE U| txNode
    txNode -->|DELAYED WAVE| rxNode
    rxNode -->|RECOVERED COMMAND| decNodeB
    decNodeB -->|VS AND FS| slvCtrl
    slvCtrl -->|TORQUE COMMANDS| slvNode
    slvNode -->|CONTACT INTERACTION| envNode
    envNode -->|REACTION FORCE FE| slvNode
    slvNode -->|SENSOR SIGNALS| snsrNode
    snsrNode -->|FORCE VELOCITY| fbPath
    fbPath -->|FEEDBACK TO ENCODER| encNodeA
    fbPath -->|FORCE REFLECTED TO OPERATOR| mstNode
```

### Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU answer scripts)

- The **outer loop** carries the command signal (left → right).
- The **inner feedback loop** (right → left) carries force and velocity.
- The communication channel is split into two **delay blocks** $T_{up}$ and $T_{down}$, giving round-trip $T = T_{up} + T_{down}$.
- The wave-variable encoder/decoder pair is the **stability-preserving stage**.

## 4.2 Fleet Management System — Functional Architecture

The following diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a centralised fleet manager, showing the data exchange between the central coordinator, the task allocator, the path planner, the traffic manager, and the individual robot units.

```mermaid
graph TD
    fmsCore["CENTRAL FLEET MANAGER GLOBAL STATE ESTIMATOR"]
    tskQueue["TASK QUEUE INCOMING PICK OR TRANSPORT ORDERS"]
    tskAllo["TASK ALLOCATOR HUNGARIAN ALGORITHM COST MATRIX C"]
    pathPlan["GLOBAL PATH PLANNER A STAR ON OCCUPANCY GRID"]
    trfcMgr["TRAFFIC MANAGER RESERVATION GRID AND DEADLOCK RESOLVER"]
    chargeMgr["CHARGING STATION SCHEDULER BATTERY STATE OF CHARGE"]
    rbtA["ROBOT UNIT 01 AMR WITH LIDAR AND IMU"]
    rbtB["ROBOT UNIT 02 AMR WITH LIDAR AND IMU"]
    rbtC["ROBOT UNIT 03 AMR WITH LIDAR AND IMU"]
    rbtD["ROBOT UNIT N AMR WITH LIDAR AND IMU"]
    tlmNode["TELEMETRY CHANNEL WIFI 6 OR 5G UPLINK"]
    
    fmsCore -->|MAINTAINS STATE OF ALL ROBOTS| tskQueue
    tskQueue -->|DISPATCH NEXT TASK| tskAllo
    tskAllo -->|ASSIGN TASK TO ROBOT 01| rbtA
    tskAllo -->|ASSIGN TASK TO ROBOT 02| rbtB
    tskAllo -->|ASSIGN TASK TO ROBOT 03| rbtC
    tskAllo -->|ASSIGN TASK TO ROBOT N| rbtD
    pathPlan -->|GLOBAL PATH REFERENCE| rbtA
    pathPlan -->|GLOBAL PATH REFERENCE| rbtB
    pathPlan -->|GLOBAL PATH REFERENCE| rbtC
    pathPlan -->|GLOBAL PATH REFERENCE| rbtD
    trfcMgr -->|RESERVED TIME WINDOWS| rbtA
    trfcMgr -->|RESERVED TIME WINDOWS| rbtB
    trfcMgr -->|RESERVED TIME WINDOWS| rbtC
    trfcMgr -->|RESERVED TIME WINDOWS| rbtD
    chargeMgr -->|CHARGING DIRECTIVE| rbtA
    chargeMgr -->|CHARGING DIRECTIVE| rbtB
    chargeMgr -->|CHARGING DIRECTIVE| rbtC
    chargeMgr -->|CHARGING DIRECTIVE| rbtD
    rbtA -->|POSE BATTERY STATUS| tlmNode
    rbtB -->|POSE BATTERY STATUS| tlmNode
    rbtC -->|POSE BATTERY STATUS| tlmNode
    rbtD -->|POSE BATTERY STATUS| tlmNode
    tlmNode -->|UPDATE GLOBAL STATE| fmsCore
    fmsCore -->|CONFLICT RESOLUTION INPUT| trfcMgr
    fmsCore -->|REPLAN TRIGGER| pathPlan
```

### Diagram Interpretation Notes

- The **central fleet manager** is the single source of truth for the *global state* — a vector of positions, velocities, battery levels, and task statuses.
- The **task allocator** solves a new assignment problem whenever a robot becomes free or a new task enters the queue.
- The **traffic manager** uses a *time-windowed reservation grid* to prevent collisions at intersections (a key KTU concept for multi-robot path planning).
- The **charging scheduler** runs in parallel and pre-empts tasks to send low-battery robots to charging docks before they deplete.

> [!NOTE]
> In centralised fleet management, the central server is a **single point of failure**. Modern systems (e.g., 2024 KTU syllabus advanced topics) introduce **distributed consensus** to improve resilience — a likely higher-order thinking question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Teleoperation. List its three principal control modes.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

Teleoperation is the remote control of a robot by a human operator across a spatial or hazardous separation, mediated by sensors, actuators, and a communication channel.

The three principal control modes are:

1. **Direct Control:** The operator continuously drives every actuator in real time; used when latency is low (< 100 ms) — e.g., drone piloting, EOD robot.
2. **Supervisory Control:** The operator specifies high-level goals; an onboard autonomous controller executes them — used when round-trip delay is large (e.g., Mars rovers, deep-sea ROVs).
3. **Shared Control:** Authority is dynamically blended between the human and an autonomous controller — used in assistive devices such as smart wheelchairs and surgical robots.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing & briefly explaining modes]**

---

### Question 2: What is meant by "Fleet Utilisation" and "Makespan" in robotic fleet management?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Fleet Utilisation (ρ):** The fraction of total available fleet time during which robots are actively performing productive work (i.e., not idle, charging, or waiting). Expressed as $\rho = T_{active} / T_{total}$. A well-balanced warehouse fleet typically achieves **ρ ≈ 0.6 to 0.85**.
- **Makespan (Tₘ):** The total elapsed time from the start of the first task in a job batch to the completion of the last task. It is the principal KPI for batch-completion optimisation and is minimised by the central task allocator.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for utilisation + 1.5 for makespan, with units / example]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern Notice]**
> Module 1 contains a compulsory 14-mark question. Two alternatives are given below; the student must answer **one**.

### Question A: Teleoperation Architectures and Haptic Stability

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Q1(a) & Q1(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain the four-block model of a bilateral teleoperation system. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of the master device, communication channel, slave controller, and the haptic feedback loop. Define **transparency** and **stability**, and state the fundamental trade-off between them.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Four-block model:** A bilateral teleoperator consists of (i) a **master device** that captures operator position/velocity and renders force feedback, (ii) a **bidirectional communication channel** with finite bandwidth and delay, (iii) a **slave robot** that physically interacts with the remote environment, and (iv) the **environment** with its own impedance $Z_e$.

**Step 2 — Transparency definition:** Transparency is the degree to which the operator feels the *true* environment impedance. Mathematically, $Z_t = F_h / v_m \approx Z_e$ is the condition for perfect transparency.

**Step 3 — Stability definition:** Stability means bounded output for bounded input. For a teleoperator with delay $T$, the closed-loop poles must remain in the left-half plane.

**Step 4 — Trade-off:** Adding force feedback ($F_h = \gamma F_e$ with $\gamma > 0$) increases the loop gain and improves transparency, but it can push the closed-loop poles across the imaginary axis, causing **oscillation or instability**. This is the famous **stability–transparency trade-off**, which becomes more severe as $T$ increases.

**Step 5 — Block diagram** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes).

**[7 Marks: 2 for blocks, 2 for transparency, 2 for stability-tradeoff, 1 for diagram]**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
For a 1-DOF teleoperator with proportional force feedback, the closed-loop characteristic equation is $s + K_p K_v e^{-2sT} = 0$. Derive the stability bound and compute the maximum allowable controller gain $K_p$ when $K_v = 0.5$ s⁻¹ and $T = 0.1$ s.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Expand the delay term** using $e^{-2sT} \approx 1 - 2sT$ for small delays:

$$s + K_p K_v (1 - 2sT) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Collect the $s$ and constant terms:**

$$s(1 - 2 K_p K_v T) + K_p K_v = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Routh-Hurwitz (first-order) stability condition** requiring the coefficient of $s$ to be positive:

$$1 - 2 K_p K_v T > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K_p < \frac{1}{2 K_v T}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$K_p < \frac{1}{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$\boxed{\;K_{p,\max} = 10 \text{ N/m}\;}$$

If the gain exceeds this value, the system becomes unstable and the operator feels growing oscillations on the master device.

**[7 Marks: 1 setup, 2 expansion, 2 Routh-Hurwitz, 1 substitution, 1 final answer]**

---

### Question B: Fleet Management — Task Allocation and Multi-Robot Path Planning

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Q1(a) & Q1(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain the three-layer architecture of a robotic fleet management system. Discuss the role of the **Hungarian Algorithm** in task allocation and the **reservation grid** in traffic management.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Three-layer architecture:**

- **Strategic Layer (offline, slow time-scale):** Mission planning, shift scheduling, battery charging strategy. Uses optimisation tools such as mixed-integer linear programming (MILP).
- **Tactical Layer (online, seconds to minutes):** Real-time task allocation, role assignment, global path planning. Uses the Hungarian Algorithm, A*, or D* Lite.
- **Operational Layer (real-time, milliseconds):** Local obstacle avoidance, motor control, sensor fusion. Uses Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) or Model Predictive Control (MPC).

**Step 2 — Hungarian Algorithm for task allocation:** Given $N$ robots and $M$ tasks with a cost matrix $c_{ij}$ (e.g., estimated travel time or energy), the algorithm finds the optimal one-to-one assignment in $O(N^3)$ that minimises the total cost $J = \sum c_{ij} x_{ij}$. It guarantees the global optimum for the static assignment problem and is re-run whenever a robot becomes free or a new task enters the queue.

**Step 3 — Reservation grid for traffic management:** The workspace is discretised into cells, each with a time stamp at which a robot reserves it. When two robots' planned paths would collide at the same cell and time, the traffic manager **shifts one of them in time** (delaying its reservation), thereby preventing deadlock.
