<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

### **Q1. Define Robot Milking. List its three major subsystems.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Robot Milking, also called **Automatic Milking System (AMS)**, is a fully autonomous robotic system that performs the complete milking process — cow identification, udder localization, teat cleaning, teat-cup attachment, milk extraction, and post-milking disinfection — without human intervention.

Three major subsystems:
1. **Perception subsystem** — RFID reader, 3D camera, laser sensor, force/torque sensor.
2. **Manipulation subsystem** — Multi-DOF robotic arm with teat-cup gripper and vacuum pulsation system.
3. **Decision/Data subsystem** — Herd management software, AI-based health analytics, and farmer dashboard.

---

### **Q2. What is a Voluntary Milking System (VMS)? How does it differ from conventional milking?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Voluntary Milking System (VMS)** is a mode of robot milking in which cows self-decide when to be milked, 24 hours a day, by walking into the milking robot stall. The system uses RFID to identify the cow, checks the last milking time, and either permits milking or redirects the cow.

**Differences from conventional milking:**

| Parameter | Conventional | VMS Robot Milking |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Farmer-scheduled (2×/day) | Cow-initiated (2.5–3×/day) |
| Labor | 2 workers | 0 (autonomous) |
| Operating hours | 4–6 hrs/day | 21–22 hrs/day |
| Hygiene | Manual | Automated CIP + per-cow sanitize |
| Data capture | Paper records | Real-time cloud analytics |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choose ONE — 14 Marks)

> `[KTU ESE Model – Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Robotics-Focused**

**(a) Explain the architecture of a robotic milking system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the 3D vision system in teat localization.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A robotic milking system comprises the following functional blocks:

1. **Cow Identification Module:** RFID reader at the entry gate reads the cow's ear-tag and queries the central herd database.
2. **Feeding Module:** Concentrate feed is dispensed as a positive reward, encouraging the cow to stand still.
3. **Pre-Cleaning Module:** Rotating brushes and iodine-based sanitizer clean the udder.
4. **Vision Module (Teat Localization):** A Time-of-Flight 3D camera captures a depth image of the udder. A CNN-based detector segments the four teats and outputs their 3D coordinates $(x, y, z)$ in the camera frame.
5. **Robotic Arm:** A 4–6 DOF articulated arm uses inverse kinematics to pick up a teat cup and attach it to each teat sequentially.
6. **Vacuum & Milk Line:** A pulsation vacuum pump extracts milk; inline sensors monitor flow, conductivity, and color.
7. **Post-Milking Module:** Auto-detach on low flow, followed by teat-dip disinfection.
8. **Herd Management Software:** Cloud-based dashboard for the farmer with yield, health alerts, and reproduction insights.

**Role of 3D Vision in Teat Localization:**
- Generates a **3D point cloud** of the udder (~50,000 points).
- **Color segmentation** isolates the pink udder region from the background stall.
- A **cylindrical-primitive fitting** algorithm identifies the four teat cylinders.
- **End-point detection** computes the $(x,y,z)$ of each teat tip.
- Coordinate transform ${}^{B}\!T_{C}$ maps camera coordinates to robot base frame.
- Output is fed to the inverse kinematics solver of the robotic arm.

**(b) Derive the camera-to-robot base coordinate transformation used for teat positioning. Show one numerical example.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The transformation is given by:

$$\mathbf{P}_B^{teat} = {}^{B}\!T_{W} \cdot {}^{W}\!T_{C} \cdot \mathbf{P}_C^{teat}$$

where ${}^{B}\!T_{W}$ (world-to-robot) and ${}^{C}\!T_{W}$ (world-to-camera) are determined during hand-eye calibration. Composing them:

$${}^{B}\!T_{C} = {}^{B}\!T_{W} \cdot ({}^{C}\!T_{W})^{-1}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Camera mounted at height 1.2 m, tilted 25° about Y-axis.
- Robot base offset by 0.6 m in +X direction.
- Teat detected at $\mathbf{P}_C^{teat} = (0.05, 0.02, 0.80)$ m.

Applying the derived transform (as shown in Section 3.1, Step 6):

$$\mathbf{P}_B^{teat} = (0.815, 0.020, -0.341)\ \text{m}$$

The robot's IK solver computes joint angles to position the end-effector at this 3D location, achieving attachment within ±2–5 mm accuracy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — System & Control-Focused (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) Describe the seven-phase operational workflow of a robotic milking cycle. List the typical cycle time and the engineering KPIs for each phase.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

The seven phases are:

| Phase | Function | Time | KPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Entry & ID | RFID reads ear-tag | 10 s | ID accuracy > 99.9% |
| 2. Feed Dispense | Concentrate reward | 15 s | Cow stillness < 2 cm drift |
| 3. Pre-Cleaning | Brush + sanitizer | 30 s | Bacterial load < $10^4$ CFU |
| 4. Vision Scan | 3D teat detection | 15–20 s | Localization error < 5 mm |
| 5. Cup Attachment | Robotic arm places 4 cups | 20–40 s | First-attempt success > 95% |
| 6. Milk Extraction | Vacuum + inline sensors | 4–7 min | Flow rate 2–3 L/min |
| 7. Detach & Exit | Drop on low flow + teat dip | 15 s | Post-dip coverage 100% |

**Total cycle time: ~6–9 minutes per cow.**

**(b) Explain how inline sensors and AI are used for mastitis detection and herd health monitoring in a robot milking system.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Inline Sensors Used:**
- **Electrical Conductivity Sensor:** Healthy milk = 4.0–4.5 mS/cm; mastitis > 5.5 mS/cm (due to Na⁺/Cl⁻ leakage from inflamed tissue).
- **Color (RGB) Sensor:** Detects blood/red tint → alerts for udder injury.
- **Optical Fluorescence SCC Sensor:** Counts somatic cells (threshold > 200,000 cells/mL = sub-clinical mastitis).
- **Temperature Sensor:** Fever indicator.
- **Coriolis Flow Meter:** Sudden drop in yield = early disease.

**AI Integration:**
- A **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** or **LSTM** processes time-series sensor data per cow.
- A **Random Forest classifier** outputs a *Mastitis Risk Score* (0–100).
- Alerts sent to the **farmer's mobile app** within 1 minute.
- Historical data enables **predictive analytics** for the entire herd — flagging subclinical issues 2–3 days before clinical symptoms appear.
- This **closed-loop AI** improves animal welfare, reduces antibiotic use, and boosts milk quality premiums.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Milking Robot" with "Dairy Automation"** — the robot is a *manipulator + perception system*, not the entire barn automation.
> 2. **Forgetting the coordinate frame** — students often write ${}^{B}\!T_{C}$ without defining frames; always define $\{B\}, \{C\}, \{W\}, \{T\}$ first.
> 3. **Skipping the biological constraints** — KTU examiners award marks for mentioning *mastitis, somatic cell count, teat tissue compliance, and animal welfare* — not just kinematics.
> 4. **Using `|x|` in markdown tables** — breaks formatting; always use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX.
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Robot Milking = **AMS / VMS** — autonomous cow identification, teat localization, cup attachment, milking, monitoring.
- ✅ Classified as a **Service Robot (ISO 8373)** operating in a **semi-structured biological environment**.
- ✅ Core subsystems: **RFID, 3D vision, robotic arm (4–6 DOF), pulsation vacuum, inline sensors, DMS cloud, CIP cleaning.**
- ✅ Standard cycle = **7 phases, ~6–9 minutes per cow**, herd capacity **50–70 cows/robot**.
- ✅ Teat localization accuracy: **±2–5 mm**, success rate: **> 95%**.
- ✅ Key formula: $\mathbf{P}_B^{teat} = {}^{B}\!T_{C} \cdot \mathbf{P}_C^{teat}$ (eye-to-hand transform).
- ✅ Compliant control: **Impedance law** $F = Ms^2 + Bs + K$ ensures soft-tissue safety.
- ✅ Milk flow (Hagen–Poiseuille analogy): $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L} \cdot D_{ratio} \approx 2.4$ L/min.
- ✅ Mastitis detection via **conductivity (> 5.5 mS/cm)**, **SCC (> 200k/mL)**, **color**, **temperature**, **flow drop**.
- ✅ KPI: **attachment success > 95%, milking 21–22 hrs/day, yield gain 10–15% over manual**.
- ✅ Engineering spin-off: **soft-tissue compliant manipulation** used in medical robotics.
- ✅ Kerala context: relevant to **MILMA, Kudumbashree, and dairy cooperative modernization**.
- ✅ Draw **state-machine + functional-block diagrams** in exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ Always state **units** and define **coordinate frames** before writing transforms.
- ✅ Mention **biological constraints** (udder softness, mastitis, cow behavior) — KTU loves this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sheep Shearing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sheep Shearing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Robotic Sheep Shearing** is a classic field-robotics application in which an autonomous or semi-autonomous manipulator, equipped with a cutting end-effector, perception sensors, and compliant control, removes fleece (wool) from a live, non-rigid, deformable, biological subject (the sheep) without breaching the integument (skin). It belongs to the broader class of *Agricultural Field Robotics* and is formally classified by **ISO 18497** (Agricultural machinery and robotics – Safety) and the older **ISO 10218-1** (Industrial robot safety) when used in research/manipulator contexts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Hook:** Sheep shearing is repeatedly cited in Module 1 of PCRUT602 as the *canonical* example of a field task that is **non-repetitive**, performed in an **unstructured environment**, on a **moving deformable object**, with **mixed force/position control** requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a sheep-shearing robot as a **"barber robot for a fidgety, ever-changing client"**:
- The "client" (sheep) is a living, breathing, soft, non-rigid body — it is never the same shape twice.
- The "barber" (robot arm) must glide a sharp blade millimeters above the skin, cutting through wool thousands of fibers thick, but **never puncturing skin that is only 1–2 mm thick**.
- Unlike a factory welding robot that follows a fixed path, the shearing robot must **react in real time** to bumps, breathing motion, wool clumping, and skin folds.
- It is a *field* robot because it operates in a farm environment (dust, light variation, animal welfare constraints), not a structured factory cell.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Biological Constants (Standard Values)
| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Adult sheep live weight | **60 – 100** | kg |
| Skin thickness | **1 – 2** | mm |
| Wool fiber diameter | **15 – 40** | µm |
| Wool tensile strength | **100 – 200** | MPa |
| Wool Young's modulus | **1 – 5** | GPa |
| Skin piercing force (safe limit) | **≤ 10** | N |
| Wool growth rate | **10 – 12** | mm/month |
| Shearing frequency | **1 – 2** | per year |
| Human shearer throughput | **120 – 200** | sheep/day |
| Robotic manipulator DOF required | **≥ 6** | DOF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** The **10 N skin-piercing limit** versus the **>30 N cutting force** for dense wool means the robot's force bandwidth must be controlled to *sub-Newton* accuracy — a hallmark of advanced compliance control.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Sheep Shearing is Hard (The "Why" Behind the Robot)
- **Non-rigidity of the workpiece:** Unlike a metal panel, a sheep's body deforms locally (subcutaneous fat, muscle, ribs). The robot cannot rely on a CAD model.
- **Unstructured, varying environment:** Wool density, dirt, skin folds, and posture differ per animal.
- **Mixed control paradigm:** Position control alone will either *miss wool* (blade too high) or *cut the sheep* (blade too low). The right paradigm is **hybrid force/position control** or **impedance control**.
- **Safety-critical & welfare-critical:** Animal-welfare legislation (e.g., *Australian Animal Welfare Standards*) and ISO 18497 demand fail-safe end-effectors.
- **Productivity-critical:** Shearing is seasonal; downtime is uneconomical.

### 2.2 The "How" — Operational Logic Steps
1. **Restrain & present the sheep** in a known pose (cradle, conveyor, or robotic manipulator on the sheep).
2. **Scan the body surface** using a vision/depth sensor (RGB-D, stereo, structured light).
3. **Segment wool vs. skin vs. background** using image processing / learned models.
4. **Generate a 2.5-D shearing path** parallel to the local skin surface, offset by a safe stand-off distance (typically **2–5 mm**).
5. **Execute the path under impedance/admittance control** so the blade behaves like a mechanical admittance: stiff in the tangential direction, soft in the normal direction.
6. **Monitor force/torque** continuously; if a force spike exceeds a threshold (e.g., 8 N normal), retract and re-plan.
7. **Quality check** post-shearing; repeat for next body region (belly, side, back, neck, britch).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = \tau_y \cdot A$ | Cutting force = shear yield stress × cross-section of wool bundle | N |
| 2 | $\sigma_w = \dfrac{F}{A_{fiber}}$ | Tensile stress in a single wool fiber (failure $\approx 150$ MPa) | Pa |
| 3 | $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + K(x - x_d) = F_{ext}$ | **Impedance control** law (target dynamics) | N |
| 4 | $J(q)\,\dot{q} = \xi$ | Manipulator Jacobian relating joint to Cartesian velocities | — |
| 5 | $\tau = J^T F_{ext}$ | Joint torque from external Cartesian force | N·m |
| 6 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \dot{T} \times \ddot{T} \vert}{\vert \dot{T} \vert^3}$ | Path curvature (T = tangent vector) — for blade trajectory smoothness | 1/m |
| 7 | $F_{pierce} \le 10$ N | Safety threshold for skin piercing | N |
| 8 | $v_{blade} \approx 0.3 - 0.6$ m/s | Typical blade traverse speed | m/s |
| 9 | $\delta_{skin} = 1$–$2$ mm | Skin stand-off safe distance | m |
| 10 | $\text{DOF} \ge 6$ | Minimum DOF for body-conforming shearing | — |
| 11 | $F_{normal} = K_n (x - x_d)$ | Normal-direction stiffness law (compliance) | N/m |
| 12 | $P_{cut} = F_c \cdot v_{blade}$ | Cutting power dissipation | W |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables the symbol $\vert x \vert$ is written using **\vert** or **\mid** (never the bare pipe `|`) so the table layout is not broken.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
The same algorithms pioneered for sheep shearing are now used in:
- **Robotic surgery** (compliance on soft tissue, vision-based deformable tracking)
- **Robotic fruit picking** (vision + compliant grasping of deformable produce)
- **Hide/skin processing in meat works** (force-controlled cutting on carcasses)
- **Automated lawn mowing / turf cutting** (geometry + impedance)
- **Robotic massage and physiotherapy** (force-controlled contact on a soft human body)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Wool-Cutting Force Model

We model the wool bundle as a parallel array of cylindrical fibers of radius $r_f$ and number density $n$ (fibers per $\text{m}^2$). The bundle has an effective shear yield stress $\tau_y$ for the cut.

**Step 1 — Define the cut cross-sectional area of a single fiber:**
$$
A_f = \pi r_f^{\,2}
$$

**Step 2 — The number of fibers in a strip of width $w$ and depth $d$ (the bundle cross-section):**
$$
N_f = n \cdot w \cdot d
$$

**Step 3 — Total cross-section of wool actually being sheared:**
$$
A = N_f \cdot A_f = n \cdot w \cdot d \cdot \pi r_f^{\,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the shear-yield cutting model (engineering shearing of a fibre bundle):**
$$
F_c = \tau_y \cdot A = \tau_y \cdot n \cdot w \cdot d \cdot \pi r_f^{\,2}
$$

**Step 5 — Insert typical numerical values to verify magnitude.**
Let $\tau_y = 80$ MPa, $n = 2 \times 10^{6}$ fibers/$\text{m}^2$, $w = 0.05$ m, $d = 0.01$ m, $r_f = 20\,\mu\text{m}$:
$$
A_f = \pi (20\times10^{-6})^{2} = 1.256 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2
$$
$$
A = 2\times10^{6} \times 0.05 \times 0.01 \times 1.256\times10^{-9} = 1.256\times10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2
$$
$$
F_c = 80\times10^{6} \times 1.256\times10^{-6} = 100.5\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** A dense wool bundle needs ~100 N to cut. This is **10× the skin-piercing limit of 10 N**, so the controller must use *tangential* cutting motion (skimming along the skin) so the *normal* force component on the skin is far smaller than 10 N.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering implication:** the robot cannot "press" the blade into the wool vertically; it must **skim** tangentially. This is the geometric reason for **impedance control** (stiff tangential, soft normal).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Impedance Control Law for Safe Contact

The goal: make the end-effector behave like a mass-spring-damper in contact with the skin.

**Step 1 — Define a desired mass matrix $M_d$, damping $B_d$, stiffness $K_d$.** These are diagonal, anisotropic matrices (different values in normal vs. tangential direction).

**Step 2 — Define position error $e = x - x_d$ and velocity error $\dot{e} = \dot{x} - \dot{x}_d$.**

**Step 3 — Write Newton's 2nd law for the *target* admittance:**
$$
M_d\,\ddot{e} + B_d\,\dot{e} + K_d\,e = F_{ext}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the desired Cartesian acceleration:**
$$
\ddot{x}_d^{\text{cmd}} = \ddot{x}_d + M_d^{-1}\bigl(F_{ext} - B_d\,\dot{e} - K_d\,e\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to joint-space command using the inverse Jacobian $J^{-1}(q)$:**
$$
\dot{q}^{\text{cmd}} = J^{-1}(q)\,\xi^{\text{cmd}}
$$
where $\xi^{\text{cmd}}$ is the Cartesian twist built from $[\dot{x}_d^{\text{cmd}},\ \omega_d^{\text{cmd}}]^T$.

**Step 6 — Compute joint torque via computed-torque control:**
$$
\tau = M(q)\ddot{q}^{\text{cmd}} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + J(q)^T F_{ext}
$$
where $M(q)$ is the inertia matrix, $C$ the Coriolis term, and $G$ gravity.

**Step 7 — Anisotropic selection for shearing** (decoupling normal/tangential):
$$
M_d = \text{diag}(m_t, m_t, m_n),\quad
B_d = \text{diag}(b_t, b_t, b_n),\quad
K_d = \text{diag}(k_t, k_t, k_n)
$$
with **$k_n \ll k_t$** and **$b_n \ll b_t$** so the blade "floats" on the skin surface and resists lateral motion.

**Step 8 — Safety clamp on the normal force:** if the measured $F_{ext,n} \ge F_{pierce}^{\max} = 10$ N, override the controller with an emergency lift-off:
$$
x_n^{\text{cmd}} \leftarrow x_n^{\text{meas}} + \Delta x_{\text{safe}},\quad \Delta x_{\text{safe}} = 5\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This two-layer architecture (impedance + safety clamp) is the *de-facto* standard taught at UWA, UNSW, and the University of Sydney for sheep-shearing robots.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Blade Trajectory Curvature Bound

The blade must follow the sheep's contour smoothly; sharp curvature causes the skin to tent and the blade to dig in.

**Step 1 — Parametrize the path as $T(s)$ with arc-length $s$.**

**Step 2 — Curvature magnitude:**
$$
\kappa(s) = \frac{\vert T'(s) \times T''(s) \vert}{\vert T'(s) \vert^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — For a circular arc of radius $R$, $\kappa = 1/R$.**

**Step 4 — Empirically safe bound for sheep body (ribs, hip):**
$$
\kappa_{\max} = \frac{1}{R_{\min}} = \frac{1}{0.15\ \text{m}} \approx 6.67\ \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — If the planned path has $\kappa > \kappa_{\max}$, insert a smoothing spline of length $L_s \ge v_{blade}/\kappa_{\max}$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the exam:** examiners often ask "Why use a 6-DOF robot and not a Cartesian gantry?" — the answer is the curvature constraint above: a sheep's body has features with $R \approx 0.15$ m that demand a manipulator with a 0.3–0.5 m reach and dexterity, which a gantry cannot provide.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robotic Sheep-Shearing Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sheep Restraint Cradle] --> B[Body Pose Estimator]
    C[RGB-D Camera Array] --> D[Vision Processing Unit]
    D --> E[Wool and Skin Segmentation]
    E --> F[3D Body Surface Reconstruction]
    F --> G[Shearing Path Planner]
    B --> G
    G --> H[Trajectory Generator]
    H --> I[Robot Motion Controller]
    J[6 DOF Manipulator Arm] --> I
    I --> J
    K[Force Torque Sensor Wrist] --> J
    K --> L[Impedance and Safety Module]
    L --> I
    M[Cutting End Effector] --> J
    N[Operator HMI and E stop] --> I
    L --> N
    O[Safety Light Curtain] --> I
    P[Data Logger and Welfare Audit] --> I
```

### 4.2 Impedance-Control Loop (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Desired Pose xd] --> B[Error e equals x minus xd]
    B --> C[Impedance Law M ddot e plus B dot e plus K e]
    C --> D[Cartesian Acceleration Command]
    D --> E[Inverse Jacobian J inverse]
    E --> F[Joint Command q cmd]
    F --> G[Robot and Cutter Dynamics]
    G --> H[Contact with Sheep Skin]
    H --> I[Force Torque Sensor F ext]
    I --> J[Force Observer and Clamp]
    J --> C
    H --> K[Measured Pose x]
    K --> B
```

### 4.3 Data / Signal Flow (Perception → Action)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[RGB Image] --> M1[CNN Wool Detection]
    S2[Depth Map] --> M2[TSDF Body Reconstruction]
    M1 --> F1[Fused 2.5D Wool Mask]
    M2 --> F1
    F1 --> P1[Parallel Offset Path at 3 mm]
    P1 --> P2[Smoothed Spline Trajectory]
    P2 --> P3[Impedance Controller]
    P3 --> P4[Robot Arm]
    P4 --> P5[Cutter Force Feedback]
    P5 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid safety rules followed:** all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `M1`, `P2`); no reserved keyword (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) is used as a node name; labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only, no markdown formatting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*"Why is robotic sheep shearing classified as a non-repetitive, unstructured field-robotics task?"* (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Non-repetitive** — the path is regenerated per sheep because body shape, wool density, and posture vary each time (1 mark).
2. **Unstructured** — natural outdoor/yard lighting, dust, animal motion, no fixed jigs (1 mark).
3. **Deformable workpiece** — the sheep's body changes shape under contact, unlike a rigid factory part (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*"State the role of a force/torque sensor in a robotic sheep-shearing system."* (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. It measures the **contact force** between the cutter and the sheep's body in real time (1 mark).
2. It provides the **$F_{ext}$ input** to the impedance controller, enabling safe normal-force clamping below the **10 N skin-piercing limit** (1 mark).
3. It enables **fault detection** — a sudden force spike triggers emergency retraction and protects the animal (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, with internal choice)

> **Internal choice rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the **major challenges** in automating sheep shearing, covering **sheep anatomy, wool properties, and the unstructured environment**. Give numerical values where relevant. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
1. *Anatomical challenges:* skin thickness only **1–2 mm**, piercing limit **≤10 N**; sheep breathes and shifts; deformable body (1.5 marks).
2. *Wool-property challenges:* non-uniform density, fiber diameter **15–40 µm**, tensile strength up to **200 MPa**, ~100 N cutting force vs. 10 N skin limit → tangential skimming mandatory (1.5 marks).
3. *Unstructured environment:* variable light, dust, fleece clumping, dirt, no CAD model, real-time perception required (1.5 marks).
4. *Engineering consequences:* need for hybrid force/position control, 6+ DOF arm, vision, and ISO 18497-compliant safety (1.5 marks).
5. *Welfare & economics:* must be at least as humane as a human shearer; throughput target ≥100 sheep/day (1 mark).

**(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, describe the **architecture of a robotic sheep-shearing system** and explain the function of each block. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
1. *Restraint and pose-estimation block* (1 mark) — presents the sheep and reads its pose.
2. *Perception block (RGB-D + CNN)* (1.5 marks) — segments wool and reconstructs a 2.5-D body surface.
3. *Path-planning block* (1 mark) — generates a **2–5 mm offset, tangential-constrained** trajectory.
4. *Impedance / admittance controller* (1.5 marks) — anisotropic stiffness, $k_n \ll k_t$, normal-force clamp at 10 N.
5. *Manipulator + cutter + F/T sensor* (1 mark).
6. *Safety block* (ISO 18497, E-stop, light curtain) (0.5 mark).
7. *HMI and audit logger* (0.5 mark).

*(Use the diagrams from Section 4 as the basis for the block diagram in your answer sheet.)*

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of machine vision and force feedback** in autonomous sheep-shearing systems. How do they complement each other? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
1. *Vision role — Geometry:* RGB-D camera + CNN yields a 2.5-D wool mask and 3-D body surface (1.5 marks).
2. *Vision role — Perception:* distinguishes wool, skin, dirt, and staples (1.5 marks).
3. *Force role — Safety:* F/T sensor measures normal/tangential force, feeds $F_{ext}$ to the impedance law (1.5 marks).
4. *Force role — Compliance:* enables the blade to "float" on the skin via $k_n \ll k_t$ (1 mark).
5. *Complementarity:* vision plans the path (open-loop geometry); force closes the loop to handle unmodeled deformation, breathing, and contact transients (1.5 marks).

**(b)** With **relevant derivation**, explain the **impedance-control strategy** used to keep the contact force between the cutting tool and the sheep skin below the safe threshold. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**
1. State the impedance law: $M_d\ddot{e} + B_d\dot{e} + K_d e = F_{ext}$ (1 mark).
2. Define position error $e = x - x_d$ and velocity error (0.5 mark).
3. Solve for the commanded Cartesian acceleration $\ddot{x}_d^{\text{cmd}}$ (1.5 marks).
4. Map to joint command via $J^{-1}(q)$ (0.5 mark).
5. Compute torque $\tau = J(q)^T F_{ext} + M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G$ (1 mark).
6. Anisotropic choice: $k_n \ll k_t$ so blade skims tangentially (1 mark).
7. Safety clamp: if $F_{ext,n} \ge 10$ N, retract by 5 mm (1.5 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of force (N) vs. pressure (Pa).** A common mark-loss is writing $F_c$ in pascals. Always check units.
> 2. **Confusing position control with impedance control.** Examiners explicitly deduct marks if the answer says "the robot is position-controlled to keep a fixed offset." It must be **impedance / admittance / hybrid force-position** controlled.
> 3. **Omitting the safety threshold (10 N).** Any derivation of cutting force that does not reference the **safe skin-piercing limit of 10 N** is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Using a fixed-CAD assumption.** Writing "the robot follows a pre-taught path" loses 1–2 marks in 2024 scheme because the syllabus mandates deformable-object handling.
> 5. **Skipping the role of the F/T sensor and Jacobian.** Many students derive impedance but forget to map it to joint torques via $J^T F_{ext}$ — this loses a full sub-mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sheep shearing is the canonical non-repetitive, deformable-object, field-robotics task** in KTU Module 1.
- The sheep is a **soft, moving, non-rigid body**; skin is **1–2 mm thick**; piercing limit is **≤ 10 N**.
- Wool fibers are **15–40 µm in diameter**, with tensile strength **100–200 MPa**; cutting force on a dense bundle is ~**100 N**, so **tangential skimming** is mandatory.
- Required robot: **≥ 6 DOF manipulator**, dexterous, with **F/T sensor at the wrist** and a **cutter end-effector**.
- Core control paradigm: **impedance / admittance control**, anisotropic stiffness ($k_n \ll k_t$), with a **normal-force safety clamp at 10 N**.
- Key equations to memorize:
  - $F_c = \tau_y \cdot A$ (cutting force)
  - $M_d\ddot{e} + B_d\dot{e} + K_d e = F_{ext}$ (impedance)
  - $\tau = J(q)^T F_{ext}$ (force-to-torque mapping)
  - $\kappa_{\max} \approx 6.67\ \text{m}^{-1}$ (curvature bound for body conformity)
- Standards: **ISO 18497** (agricultural robots safety), **ISO 10218-1** (industrial robot safety), and **animal-welfare regulations** govern design.
- Vision system: **RGB-D + CNN** for wool segmentation; **TSDF or NURBS** for body reconstruction.
- Productivity benchmark: a human shearer does **120–200 sheep/day**; the robot must match or exceed this to be commercially viable.
- Historical context: the **University of Western Australia (Billingsley, 1980s)** built the first prototype; modern work uses **deep-learning vision and admittance control**.
- Common exam traps: (i) confusing force with pressure units, (ii) treating the path as fixed, (iii) omitting the safety clamp, (iv) forgetting $J^T F_{ext}$ in the torque equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Livestock Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Livestock Inspection # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Livestock Inspection** in the context of Field and Service Robotics refers to the deployment of **autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic platforms** equipped with multimodal perception systems (vision, thermal, acoustic, LiDAR, IMU) to perform non-invasive, continuous, and systematic monitoring of farm animals (cattle, sheep, pigs, poultry) within structured or semi-structured agricultural environments. The robotic system is expected to perform three canonical inspection tasks aligned with **precision livestock farming (PLF)** principles: 1. **Localization & Navigation** — traversing barns, pastures, and feedlots without human intervention. 2. **Perception & Detection** — identifying individual animals, estimating **Body Condition Score (BCS)**, detecting lameness, mastitis, fever, or distress behavior. 3. **Actuation & Alerting** — generating actionable data streams to the farmer or automatically triggering interventions (e.g., sorting gates, alarms). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Livestock Inspection falls under the *Application Domains of Field Robots* unit. Examiners frequently test the mapping between **sensor modalities** and **animal welfare metrics**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are a **veterinarian visiting a dairy farm of 200 cows**. You cannot watch every cow 24/7, so you hire a **patrolling security guard with a thermal camera and a notebook**. This guard walks the barn, scans each cow, notes the ones with hot udders, limping gait, or low feed intake, and reports back to you. A **livestock inspection robot is exactly that smart guard**, but it never sleeps, never gets distracted, and logs data in a structured digital format that a machine-learning model can analyze for early disease prediction. > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea (in one line):** *A mobile sensing platform that physically replaces the human "eye-walk" in animal husbandry with quantitative, repeatable, and continuous observation.* ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Livestock Robotics The following metrics are used to evaluate a livestock inspection robot's effectiveness: - **Inspection Coverage Ratio (ICR):** The fraction of the target herd successfully scanned per mission. - **Detection Sensitivity ($S_d$):** Probability of correctly identifying a sick/injured animal. - **False Alarm Rate (FAR):** Frequency of falsely flagging a healthy animal. - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Operational reliability, typically **> 50 hours** for commercial units. - **Animal Disturbance Index (ADI):** A dimensionless score quantifying how much the robot startles the herd (target: ADI < 0.2). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Frame:** Most livestock robots operate in the **ENU (East-North-Up) world frame** with the robot's onboard **IMU** providing the body frame $\{B\}$ orientation via roll-pitch-yaw $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ Euler angles. --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Stack of a Livestock Inspection Robot A typical system is decomposed into four interacting layers: ### Layer 1 — Mechanical Substrate - **Differential-drive or 4-wheel skid-steer** chassis (preferred for tight barn corridors of width $\geq 1.2$ m). - **IP65-rated enclosure** to resist ammonia, humidity, and dust. - **Suspension system** with spring constant typically in the range **$k = 2000$–$4000$ N/m** to handle uneven earthen floors. ### Layer 2 — Sensing Suite - **RGB-D Camera** (e.g., Intel RealSense D435) — animal silhouette, BCS estimation. - **Thermal Infrared Camera** (FLIR Boson, 8–14 µm) — udder temperature, fever screening. - **2D/3D LiDAR** — barn mapping, obstacle avoidance. - **Microphone Array** — cough detection, vocalization analysis. - **IMU + Wheel Encoders** — dead reckoning between GPS dropouts (rooftops of barns block GNSS). ### Layer 3 — Perception & Cognition - **Object Detection:** YOLOv8 / Faster R-CNN trained on custom herd datasets. - **Tracking:** DeepSORT or ByteTrack with Re-Identification (Re-ID) embeddings. - **Health Inference:** CNN/LSTM models mapping thermal + behavioral features to health states. ### Layer 4 — Mission Planning & HRI - **Coverage Path Planning (CPP):** Boustrophedon or spiral patterns. - **Mission Scheduler:** Multi-day planning with charging dock integration. - **Farmer Dashboard:** Cloud-based alerting (e.g., MQTT over 4G/LoRaWAN). ## 2.2 Why Each Layer Matters — The Engineering Rationale - **Differential drive** is chosen because the **non-holonomic constraint** simplifies kinematic equations while still permitting zero-radius turning in narrow alleys. - **Multimodal fusion** is mandatory because **no single sensor** can robustly detect all welfare indicators (e.g., thermal cameras miss lameness; RGB cameras miss subclinical mastitis). - **Coverage path planning** is non-trivial because the environment is **dynamic** (animals move) and **cluttered** (feed troughs, water buckets, milking machines). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Unit** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---|---| | $v, \omega$ | Linear & angular velocity of robot | $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | m/s, rad/s | Differential drive kinematics | | $R_{turn}$ | Instantaneous turning radius | $R_{turn} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | m | L = wheelbase | | $\sigma_{IMU}$ | IMU bias instability | Allan variance at $\tau = 1$ s | deg/h | Drift benchmark | | $P_d$ | Probability of detection (sonar/Radar) | $P_d = Q\left(\frac{\lambda - \text{SNR}}{\sigma_n}\right)$ | dimensionless | Used in herd counting | | $mAP$ | Mean Average Precision | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$ | [0, 1] | Vision detection metric | | $S_d$ | Detection sensitivity | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | [0, 1] | Recall in confusion matrix | | $F_1$ | F1-score | $\frac{2 P \cdot S_d}{P + S_d}$ | [0, 1] | Harmonic mean of P, S_d | | $T_{core}$ | Core body temperature (derived) | $T_{core} \approx T_{eye} + 0.8 \, \Delta T$ | °C | $\Delta T$: eye-skin gradient | | $\eta_{cov}$ | Coverage efficiency | $\frac{A_{scanned}}{A_{total}} \cdot \frac{T_{mission}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Penalties for missed scans | | $E_{bat}$ | Battery state of charge | $\int_{0}^{t} P(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Wh | Used for auto-dock mission | | $f_{cough}$ | Cough frequency (audio) | $\frac{N_{coughs}}{\Delta t}$ | events/min | Audio monitoring metric | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all LaTeX expressions above, the absolute value / norm notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the unescaped `|` pipe), preserving the markdown table integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dairy Industry:** Companies like **Lely (Vector, Juno)** and **DeLaval (DeLaval VMS)** deploy robots that not only milk but also inspect udder health. - **Poultry:** **Octopus Poultry Robot** autonomously walks broiler houses detecting dead birds. - **Open Pasture:** Boston Dynamics' **Spot** has been experimentally fitted with thermal payloads to detect cattle fever in rangelands. - **Academic:** Projects like **PIGFLOW** (EU) and **GDBRO** (UK) pioneered multi-robot bovine monitoring. --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential-Drive Kinematic Model The robot has two independently driven wheels (left L, right R) of radius $r$, separated by wheelbase $L$. Each wheel has an angular velocity $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$. **Step 1 — Linear velocity of each wheel contact point:** The contact point of each wheel moves with linear speed given by the product of wheel radius and angular velocity. $$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L \qquad v_R = r \cdot \omega_R$$ **Step 2 — Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR):** For a rigid body in pure rolling, the ICR lies on the line connecting the two wheel contact points. The angular velocity of the chassis $\omega$ is determined by the difference in wheel speeds divided by the wheelbase. $$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Linear velocity of robot's centre of mass:** The forward speed of the robot is the arithmetic mean of the two wheel speeds (the centre moves at the average tangential velocity). $$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Turning radius:** The turning radius is the ratio of linear velocity to angular velocity. Substituting the above expressions and rearranging: $$R_{turn} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$ **Step 5 — Special cases:** - *Straight line:* $\omega_L = \omega_R \Rightarrow R_{turn} = \infty$ (no rotation). - *Pure rotation:* $\omega_L = -\omega_R \Rightarrow R_{turn} = 0$ (in-place pivot, useful in tight barns). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coverage Path Planning (Boustrophedon Decomposition) The barn is modelled as a 2D polygon $\mathcal{P} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ discretized into cells. The objective is to find a path $\mathcal{C}$ that minimizes total traversal length while covering every cell at least once. **Step 1 — Cell decomposition:** Sweep a vertical line $x = s$ from $x_{min}$ to $x_{max}$. At each $s$, the intersection of the sweep line with $\mathcal{P}$ forms intervals of "free" cells. **Step 2 — Detect critical points:** A critical point $s^*$ occurs when the topology of the free interval changes (an obstacle begins or ends). **Step 3 — Decompose polygon into strips:** Between two consecutive critical points, the polygon forms a single monotone strip. **Step 4 — Boustrophedon coverage within a strip:** Within each strip, the robot traces parallel back-and-forth lines spaced by a distance $d$, where $d$ is the sensor footprint diameter (for a downward-facing camera at height $h$ with FOV $2\alpha$): $$d = 2 h \tan(\alpha)$$ **Step 5 — Total path length:** For a barn of length $X$ and width $Y$: $$L_{total} = X \left\lceil \frac{Y}{d} \right\rceil + d \left( \left\lceil \frac{Y}{d} \right\rceil - 1 \right)$$ The first term is the horizontal scan lines, and the second term is the vertical transition moves between adjacent lanes. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Body Condition Score from Image Features The **Body Condition Score (BCS)** is a 1–5 scale quantifying subcutaneous fat. We approximate it from the cow's top-line curvature using a depth image. **Step 1 — Acquire depth image:** Let $D(x, y)$ be the depth at pixel $(x, y)$ in the dorsal (back) region of the cow. **Step 2 — Fit a quadratic surface:** Model the back as $z = a x^2 + b y^2 + c x + d y + e$. Solve via least squares: $$\min_{a,b,c,d,e} \sum_{(x,y) \in \mathcal{R}} \left( D(x,y) - (a x^2 + b y^2 + c x + d y + e) \right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute curvature:** The Gaussian curvature $\kappa_G$ of the fitted surface is: $$\kappa_G = \frac{4 a b - (0)^2}{(1 + c^2 + d^2)^2} = \frac{4 a b}{(1 + c^2 + d^2)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Map curvature to BCS:** Empirically calibrated linear regression yields: $$BCS = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \kappa_G + \beta_2 P_{fat}$$ where $P_{fat}$ is the proportion of pixels in the hip-pin region classified as "fat" by the CNN. **Step 5 — Validation target:** Validation against expert veterinary scoring should achieve a **Mean Absolute Error (MAE) ≤ 0.25 BCS points** and a **Pearson correlation $r \geq 0.85$**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor-to-Task Mapping | **Animal Welfare Task** | **Primary Sensor** | **Algorithm** | **Key Output** | **Acceptable Performance** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Lameness detection | RGB-D camera | Pose estimation (HRNet) | Gait asymmetry index | $S_d \geq 0.80$ | | Mastitis screening | Thermal IR | ROI thresholding | Udder $\Delta T > 1.5°C$ | $S_d \geq 0.85$ | | BCS estimation | Depth camera | Quadratic surface fit | BCS ∈ [1, 5] | MAE ≤ 0.25 | | Cough / respiratory | Microphone array | CNN on Mel-spectrogram | Cough event count | $S_d \geq 0.75$ | | Heat / estrus detection | Accelerometer collar + robot IMU | Activity index variance | Mounting/standing events | $S_d \geq 0.90$ | | Counting / ID | RGB camera | YOLOv8 + DeepSORT | Per-animal trajectory | $mAP \geq 0.85$ | | Dead-bird detection (poultry) | RGB camera + thermal | YOLOv8 + thermal fusion | Mortality alert | $S_d \geq 0.95$ | | Feed intake monitoring | RGB-D above feed bunk | Volumetric change | kg/day per animal | $\pm 0.5$ kg error | --- # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Livestock Inspection Robot ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Power Management Unit
Li-ion 48V 60Ah] --> B[Motor Controller
BLDC Driver] B --> C[Drive Chassis
Differential 4WD] A --> D[Onboard Computer
NVIDIA Jetson Orin] D --> E[Sensor Fusion Hub
ROS 2 Node] E --> F1[RGB-D Camera
RealSense D435] E --> F2[Thermal Camera
FLIR Boson 640] E --> F3[2D LiDAR
RPLIDAR S3] E --> F4[IMU + Wheel Odometry
VectorNav VN-100] E --> F5[Microphone Array
ReSpeaker 4-Mic] D --> G[Perception Engine
YOLOv8 + DeepSORT] D --> H[Health Inference
CNN-LSTM Model] D --> I[Mission Planner
Boustrophedon CPP] I --> J[Behavior Tree
nav2 BT] J --> B G --> K[Cloud Dashboard
MQTT / LoRaWAN] H --> K K --> L[Farmer Mobile App
Alerts and Reports] F1 --> G F2 --> H F5 --> H F3 --> I F4 --> I style A fill:#FFD700 style D fill:#87CEEB style G fill:#90EE90 style H fill:#FFA07A style K fill:#DDA0DD style L fill:#F0E68C ``` ## 4.2 Mission State Machine for Autonomous Inspection Patrol ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> SelfCheck: Mission Trigger SelfCheck --> Localization: All Sensors OK SelfCheck --> Fault: Sensor Failure Fault --> Maintenance: Human Intervention Maintenance --> SelfCheck Localization --> Mapping: Map Not Built Mapping --> PathPlanning: SLAM Complete Localization --> PathPlanning: Map Exists PathPlanning --> Navigating: CPP Computed Navigating --> Inspecting: Reached Scan Pose Inspecting --> Navigating: Animal Detected and Logged Inspecting --> Alerting: Health Anomaly Detected Alerting --> Navigating: Alert Logged Navigating --> Docking: Battery SOC Below 20 Percent Docking --> Charging: Dock Contact Charging --> Idle: SOC Above 95 Percent Alerting --> CloudSync: 4G Available CloudSync --> Navigating Navigating --> EmergencyStop: E-Stop Pressed EmergencyStop --> Idle ``` ## 4.3 Sensor-to-Task Allocation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Animal in Sensor FOV] --> B{Detection Module} B -->|RGB| C1[Identity and Pose] B -->|Thermal| C2[Health Thermal Map] B -->|Depth| C3[BCS and Morphology] B -->|Audio| C4[Vocalization and Cough] C1 --> D[Feature Vector Builder] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D D --> E[Health Classifier
Multi Modal CNN] E -->|Healthy| F[Continue Patrol] E -->|Suspect| G[Flag and Log] E -->|Critical| H[Trigger Alert
Sound and Cloud Push] style E fill:#FFB6C1 style H fill:#FF6347 style F fill:#98FB98 ``` ## 4.4 Spatial Coverage Schematic (Boustrophedon Pattern) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BARN [Barn Polygon 30m x 12m] direction LR L1[Lane 1: y = 1.0 m] L2[Lane 2: y = 3.5 m] L3[Lane 3: y = 6.0 m] L4[Lane 4: y = 8.5 m] L5[Lane 5: y = 11.0 m] T1[Transition Move 1] T2[Transition Move 2] T3[Transition Move 3] T4[Transition Move 4] end L1 --> T1 T1 --> L2 L2 --> T2 T2 --> L3 L3 --> T3 T3 --> L4 L4 --> T4 T4 --> L5 style BARN fill:#FAFAD2 style T1 fill:#FFA07A style T2 fill:#FFA07A style T3 fill:#FFA07A style T4 fill:#FFA07A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `L1`, `T1`, `F1`). Reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` are not used as standalone node names. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags. --- # 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define "Precision Livestock Farming (PLF)" and list any three key robotic sensing modalities used in livestock inspection.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** Precision Livestock Farming (PLF) is the application of sensing, information, and communication technologies to provide continuous, automated monitoring of individual animals to optimize productivity, health, and welfare. - **Three Sensing Modalities (2 Marks):** 1. **RGB-D Vision** — for body condition scoring and gait analysis. 2. **Thermal Infrared Imaging** — for non-contact temperature screening (mastitis, fever). 3. **Acoustic Sensing (Microphone Arrays)** — for cough detection and respiratory health monitoring. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *Explain the significance of the "Animal Disturbance Index (ADI)" in the deployment of livestock inspection robots.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 Mark):** ADI is a dimensionless score that quantifies the level of behavioural disruption (e.g., flight distance, heart rate elevation, milk-let-down inhibition) caused by a robot's presence among animals. - **Significance (2 Marks):** 1. **Welfare Compliance:** A high ADI indicates the robot is stressing the herd, defeating the purpose of inspection. KTU commercial benchmarks target **ADI < 0.2**. 2. **Design Constraint:** A low ADI mandates design choices like **slow approach velocities** ($\leq 0.5$ m/s), **non-threatening morphology** (rounded, low-profile), and **predictable trajectories** (no sudden moves). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Skipping the "Why":** Students often list sensors without justifying why each is chosen. Always link sensor → metric → welfare parameter. > 2. **Units Missing:** The board penalizes answers that omit units (e.g., "10" without "m" or "rad/s"). Always state units. > 3. **Confusing BCS with weight:** BCS is a *visual* morphological score, not body mass. Do not equate them. > 4. **Forgetting Non-Holonomic Constraints:** When writing kinematics, mention that differential-drive robots are *non-holonomic* — they cannot move sideways. ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Past Year Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an autonomous livestock inspection robot. Explain the role of **sensor fusion** in achieving robust health monitoring. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A differential-drive inspection robot has wheel radius $r = 0.15$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.6$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s, respectively. Calculate: (i) The linear velocity of the robot. **(2 Marks)** (ii) The angular velocity of the robot. **(2 Marks)** (iii) The turning radius. **(1 Mark)** (iv) Comment on the manoeuvrability of this configuration inside a barn corridor of width 1.5 m. **(2 Marks)** **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Architecture and Sensor Fusion (7 Marks) **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw a four-layer stack: 1. **Mechanical Layer:** IP65 chassis, differential drive, suspension. 2. **Sensing Layer:** RGB-D, Thermal, LiDAR, IMU, Microphone. 3. **Perception Layer:** Object detection (YOLO), tracking (DeepSORT), health inference (CNN-LSTM). 4. **Decision Layer:** Mission planner, navigation (Nav2), farmer dashboard. **Role of Sensor Fusion (4 Marks):** - **Robustness:** A single sensor can fail in dusty, low-light, or occluded conditions. Fusion (e.g., Kalman filter) maintains state estimates even when one modality degrades. - **Complementarity:** Thermal detects fever but cannot identify the individual; RGB identifies but cannot read temperature. Fusion yields a *labelled thermal map*. - **Reduced Uncertainty:** Bayesian fusion of $N$ sensors with individual variances $\sigma_i^2$ yields a fused variance: $$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2} \right)^{-1}$$ demonstrating that $N$ independent sensors always outperform a single sensor. - **Behavioural Context:** Audio (coughing) + Motion (inactivity) + Thermal (fever) together pinpoint respiratory disease, which a single sensor cannot. #### Part (b) — Kinematic Calculations (7 Marks) **Given:** $r = 0.15$ m, $L = 0.6$ m, $\omega_L = 4$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 6$ rad/s. **(i) Linear velocity (2 Marks):** $$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.15 \cdot (6 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.15 \cdot 10}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Angular velocity (2 Marks):** $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.15 \cdot (6 - 4)}{0.6} = \frac{0.15 \cdot 2}{0.6} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(iii) Turning radius (1 Mark):** $$R_{turn} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.75}{0.5} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ **(iv) Manoeuvrability comment (2 Marks):** The robot's turning radius is **1.5 m**, which is comparable to the corridor width of **1.5 m**. This means the robot can **just barely complete a U-turn** within the corridor with very little margin. In practice, a margin of $\geq 0.3$ m on either side is recommended for safety against animal-induced disturbances. Hence, the corridor width is **marginally adequate** and a wider barn ($\geq 2$ m) is preferred. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Past Year Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **Boustrophedon Coverage Path Planning (CPP)** algorithm as applied to a rectangular barn. A barn is **30 m long and 12 m wide**, and the robot's downward camera has a field of view $2\alpha = 60°$ at a flying height $h = 2.5$ m. Compute: (i) The lane spacing $d$. **(2 Marks)** (ii) The number of lanes required. **(2 Marks)** (iii) The total horizontal scan length. **(1 Mark)** (iv) Discuss the limitations of Boustrophedon CPP in a *dynamic* livestock environment. **(2 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the procedure to estimate the **Body Condition Score (BCS)** of a cow from a depth image. What is the acceptable Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for an automated BCS system? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Boustrophedon CPP (7 Marks) **Algorithm Explanation (Mark allocations embedded below):** The Boustrophedon algorithm (literally "as the ox plows") sweeps a vertical line across the polygon, decomposing it into strips at *critical points* where the free-space topology changes. Within each strip, the robot moves back and forth in parallel lanes spaced by the sensor footprint. **(i) Lane spacing (2 Marks):** The footprint diameter on the ground from a camera at height $h$ with half-FOV $\alpha$ is: $$d = 2 h \tan(\alpha) = 2 \cdot 2.5 \cdot \tan(30°) = 5 \cdot 0.5774 = 2.887 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Number of lanes (2 Marks):** $$N_{lanes} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{d} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{12}{2.887} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.156 \rceil = 5 \text{ lanes}$$ **(iii) Total horizontal scan length (1 Mark):** $$L_{scan} = N_{lanes} \cdot L_{barn} = 5 \cdot 30 = 150 \text{ m}$$ (Transition moves are $d \cdot (N_{lanes}-1) = 2.887 \cdot 4 = 11.55$ m, giving a grand total of $161.55$ m.) **(iv) Limitations in dynamic environments (2 Marks):** 1. **Static Assumption:** Boustrophedon assumes a fixed map; animals moving into the path cause *unmodelled occlusions* and may require replanning. 2. **No Priority:** It treats all cells equally, but a cow exhibiting distress (high-priority welfare event) should be inspected *first*, not on a fixed sweep schedule. 3. **Re-visit Problem:** Animals entering the corridor after the robot has passed are missed until the next sweep cycle. #### Part (b) — BCS Estimation Procedure (7 Marks) **Procedure (5 Marks):** 1. **Image Acquisition (1 Mark):** Capture a depth image of the cow's dorsal region (back and hip area) using a top-mounted RGB-D camera as the robot passes beneath or alongside the animal. 2. **Segmentation (1 Mark):** Apply a semantic segmentation network (e.g., DeepLabV3+) to isolate the cow's body from the background and other animals. 3. **Surface Fitting (2 Marks):** Fit a quadratic surface $z = ax^2 + by^2 + cx + dy + e$ to the depth values within the segmented region using least-squares minimization. 4. **Curvature and Feature Extraction (1 Mark):** Compute the Gaussian curvature $\kappa_G$ and extract additional features (hip-pin width, tail-head depression, hook-bone visibility) using a CNN backbone. 5. **Regression to BCS (1 Mark):** A pre-trained regressor (e.g., XGBoost or a small MLP) maps the feature vector to a continuous BCS value in the range [1.0, 5.0]. **Acceptable MAE (2 Marks):** The acceptable **Mean Absolute Error** is **MAE ≤ 0.25 BCS points** on a 1–5 scale, with a **Pearson correlation $r \geq 0.85$** against expert veterinary scoring. Systems exceeding MAE = 0.5 are considered clinically unreliable. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Livestock Inspection (Module 1)** - ✅ **Definition:** Use of mobile robotic platforms with multimodal sensors for non-invasive animal monitoring in PLF contexts. - ✅ **Three Pillars of Robotic PLF:** *Localize* the animal → *Perceive* its state → *Act/Alert* on anomalies. - ✅ **Mandatory Sensors:** RGB-D (BCS), Thermal (fever/mastitis), LiDAR (mapping), Microphone (cough), IMU (odometry). - ✅ **Key Kinematic Formulas:** - $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ - $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ - $R_{turn} = \frac{v}{\omega}$ - ✅ **Boustrophedon CPP:** Lane spacing $d = 2h\tan(\alpha)$; total scan length = $N_{lanes} \cdot X$. - ✅ **BCS Target:** MAE ≤ 0.25, $r \geq 0.85$. - ✅ **ADI Target:** $< 0.2$ (animal welfare compliance). - ✅ **Non-Holonomic Constraint:** Differential drive cannot translate sideways — always state this in kinematics answers. - ✅ **F1-Score:** $F_1 = \frac{2 P \cdot S_d}{P + S_d}$ — know when to use F1 vs mAP. - ✅ **Thermal Threshold:** Udder $\Delta T > 1.5°C$ above baseline indicates subclinical mastitis. - ✅ **Sensor Fusion Benefit:** $\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left(\sum 1/\sigma_i^2\right)^{-1}$ — independent sensors always reduce uncertainty. - ✅ **GNSS-Denied Operation:** Barns block GPS — robot must rely on **LiDAR-SLAM + IMU + wheel odometry**. - ✅ **Standards:** IP65 enclosure; commercial MTBF > 50 hours. - ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Don't confuse BCS (visual score 1–5) with body weight (kg). - ✅ **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Kinematics calculation (numerical), CPP lane spacing, sensor-task mapping table, and "why fusion" reasoning.

topics.title: Robotic Animals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotic Animals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Robotic Animal** (also termed a **Bio-Inspired Robot** or **Biomimetic Robot**) is a class of field and service robotic system whose mechanical structure, locomotion strategy, and control architecture are systematically derived from the morphology, kinematics, and behavioural patterns of biological organisms. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme definition for PCRUT602, robotic animals constitute engineered mechatronic platforms that emulate **terrestrial, aquatic, or aerial locomotion** of fauna to traverse unstructured environments where conventional wheeled or tracked platforms are inefficient or infeasible.

The design philosophy is governed by **Biomimetics** — the discipline of translating biological principles into engineered solutions — and is mathematically grounded in the dimensionless analysis of animal locomotion, namely the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, **Froude Number (Fr)**, and **Strouhal Number (St)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Robotic Animals form the foundational pillar of Module 1, establishing the bio-inspired design paradigm that propagates through all subsequent modules (manipulation, navigation, SLAM) of PCRUT602.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are asked to design a vehicle that can climb over fallen logs, scramble across loose gravel, and wade through shallow streams in a disaster zone. A traditional wheeled robot would fail spectacularly at the first log. Now observe a **mountain goat** doing exactly this effortlessly — it has four legs, a low centre of mass, and adaptive hooves. A robotic animal simply asks the question: *"Why not just copy what evolution already perfected over 400 million years?"*

A **robotic goat** would have four articulated legs, force-sensitive hooves, and a balance controller mimicking the goat's cerebellum. Extending this logic:
- **Legged animals → Quadruped/Hexapod robots** (Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYbotics ANYmal)
- **Snakes → Serpentine robots** (modular hyper-redundant manipulators)
- **Fish → Aquatic robots** (MIT RoboTuna, Festo AquaPenguin)
- **Birds/Insects → Aerial flappers** (Festo SmartBird, DelFly)
- **Dogs/Horses → Quadruped pack robots** (BigDog, Cheetah)

> [!NOTE]
> The unifying engineering principle is **Energy Efficiency per unit distance (Specific Resistance)**. Biological locomotion routinely achieves specific resistance values **5–10× lower** than wheeled vehicles on soft, uneven terrain — this is the single most important reason robotic animals are preferred in field robotics.

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Pillars of Robotic Animal Design

1. **Morphological Fidelity** — Matching the geometric structure (limbs, joints, body segments) of the animal archetype.
2. **Kinematic Equivalence** — Reproducing the degrees of freedom (DOF) and joint trajectories of the biological gait cycle.
3. **Dynamic Equivalence** — Matching the inertial, force, and energy profiles — quantified using **St, Re, Fr** numbers.

## 1.4 Classification Matrix (At a Glance)

| Animal Archetype | Robot Class | Locomotion Medium | Canonical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quadruped Mammal | 4-Legged Robot | Terrestrial | BigDog, Spot, ANYmal, Cheetah |
| Hexapod Insect | 6-Legged Robot | Terrestrial | RHex, LAURON, Messor |
| Serpent | Snake Robot | Terrestrial / Aquatic | ACM-R5, Modular Snake |
| Fish | Fish Robot | Aquatic | RoboTuna, GhostSwimmer |
| Bird / Bat | Ornithopter | Aerial | SmartBird, RoboBat |
| Insect | Micro Air Vehicle | Aerial | DelFly, Robobee |
| Humanoid | Biped Robot | Terrestrial | ASIMO, Atlas, H1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Locomotion Dimensionless Numbers — Conceptual Decomposition

The behaviour of any robotic animal — terrestrial, aquatic, or aerial — is governed by a small set of dimensionless parameters that capture the *ratio of competing physical forces*. Mastering these is essential for KTU 2024 Scheme exam performance.

### 2.1.1 The Reynolds Number (Re) — Inertial vs. Viscous Forces

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, U \, L}{\mu}$$

- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $U$ = characteristic velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the medium (Pa·s)

**Physical Meaning:** Re is the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid. It dictates whether the flow regime around a swimming/flying robot is **laminar** (Re < 1, dominated by viscosity — typical for micro-robots and bacteria) or **turbulent** (Re > 10⁴, dominated by inertia — typical for fish, birds, and large vehicles).

**Engineering Utility:** When designing a robotic fish intended to operate in the same regime as a biological tuna (Re ≈ 10⁶ to 10⁷), the tail-beat kinematics must be scaled so that viscous boundary layer effects are negligible. Conversely, a micro-swimmer (Re ≈ 10⁻²) must use entirely different propulsion strategies (non-Newtonian flagellar motion, as in sperm-cell-inspired robots).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The Reynolds Number does NOT apply to legged terrestrial robots since they are not immersed in a fluid continuum. It is exclusively for aquatic and aerial bio-inspired platforms.

### 2.1.2 The Froude Number (Fr) — Inertial vs. Gravitational Forces

$$\text{Fr} = \frac{v^{2}}{g \, L}$$

- $v$ = forward velocity of the robot (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $L$ = hip height or leg length (m)

**Physical Meaning:** Fr is the ratio of kinetic energy to gravitational potential energy per unit mass. It is the **single most important parameter** for legged robotic animals because it classifies gait transitions observed in biological quadrupeds.

| Froude Number Range | Gait Regime | Biological Analog |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \leq \text{Fr} < 0.5$ | Walk | Human walking, elephant stroll |
| $0.5 \leq \text{Fr} < 2.0$ | Trot | Horse trotting, dog jogging |
| $\text{Fr} \geq 2.0$ | Gallop / Run | Horse galloping, cheetah sprint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact:** The transition from walk to trot in biological quadrupeds occurs at a **dynamically similar** Froude number ($\text{Fr} \approx 0.5$) regardless of body size — a horse, a dog, and a mouse all transition gaits at the same Fr. This is the principle of **Dynamic Similarity** in bio-inspired robot design.

### 2.1.3 The Strouhal Number (St) — Oscillatory Propulsion Efficiency

$$\text{St} = \frac{f \, A}{U}$$

- $f$ = tail-beat or wing-flap frequency (Hz)
- $A$ = peak-to-peak tail-beat or wing-flap amplitude (m)
- $U$ = forward cruising velocity (m/s)

**Physical Meaning:** St is the ratio of the characteristic speed of the oscillating appendage to the forward translation speed. **Alexander's Theorem (1999)** states that efficient cruising locomotion in fish, birds, bats, and insects universally occurs within the band:

$$0.2 \leq \text{St} \leq 0.4$$

This band is *species- and size-invariant* — a robotic fish or ornithopter achieving cruising motion must be designed to operate within this Strouhal band to maximise propulsive efficiency ($\eta_{\text{prop}} > 0.7$).

## 2.2 Gait Analysis for Quadruped Robotic Animals

A **gait** is the periodic sequence of foot-fall events that defines the locomotion cycle. Two parameters fully characterise any gait:

**Duty Factor (β):**
$$\beta = \frac{t_{\text{stance}}}{T}$$
where $t_{\text{stance}}$ = time a foot is on the ground, $T$ = total gait cycle period.

**Stride Length (λ):**
$$\lambda = v \, T = \frac{v}{f}$$

| Gait | Duty Factor β | Number of Feet in Stance | Stability Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Walk** | β > 0.75 | ≥ 3 always | Static (no motion needed) |
| **Dynamic Walk** | 0.5 < β ≤ 0.75 | 2-3 | Quasi-static |
| **Trot** | β = 0.5 | 2 (diagonal pairs) | Dynamic (ZMP critical) |
| **Pace** | β = 0.5 | 2 (lateral pairs) | Dynamic (less stable) |
| **Gallop** | β < 0.5 | 0-2 | Highly dynamic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Paradigm:** Walk gaits with β ≥ 0.75 (e.g., RHex hexapod) maintain **Static Stability** — the centre of mass projection always lies within the support polygon. Trot and gallop gaits require **Dynamic Stability** via Zero Moment Point (ZMP) control or Model Predictive Control (MPC).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application Domain | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Fish robots, ornithopters, MAVs | Dimensionless; $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\mu$ in Pa·s |
| 2 | $\text{Fr} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{g L}$ | Quadrupeds, hexapods, bipeds | $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L$ = hip height |
| 3 | $\text{St} = \dfrac{f A}{U}$ | Fish robots, flapping-wing MAVs | Optimal band: $0.2 \leq \text{St} \leq 0.4$ |
| 4 | $\beta = \dfrac{t_{\text{stance}}}{T}$ | All legged robots | Dimensionless; $\beta \in [0, 1]$ |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | All periodic gait systems | metres per cycle |
| 6 | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{P}{m g v}$ | Energy efficiency metric | Specific Resistance (dimensionless) |
| 7 | $\text{SSM} = \min \; d(\text{COM}_{\text{proj}}, \partial \mathcal{P})$ | Static stability | metres; $\mathcal{P}$ = support polygon |
| 8 | $\tau_{i} = \sum_{j} M_{ij} \ddot{q}_{j} + h_{i} + g_{i}$ | Joint dynamics | Standard Euler-Lagrange form |

> **Critical Notation Note (Anti-Break Safeguard):** All absolute-value and division operations in the table use $\vert$ or $\mid$ written as \vert/\mid in LaTeX. The vertical pipe character has been deliberately replaced to preserve the markdown table boundary integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Search & Rescue** | Quadrupeds (Spot) traverse earthquake rubble inaccessible to wheels |
| **Precision Agriculture** | Hexapods monitor crop rows without compacting soil |
| **Underwater Inspection** | Fish-robots inspect ship hulls and pipelines silently |
| **Defence Reconnaissance** | Snake robots penetrate pipe networks and caves |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Ornithopters survey forests and coastlines with low acoustic signature |
| **Planetary Exploration** | Legged robots (LEMUR, ANYmal) traverse Martian regolith |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Strouhal Number and Propulsive Efficiency

We derive why cruising animals — and the robotic animals that emulate them — universally select $\text{St} \approx 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Define the propulsion problem.** A robotic fish oscillates its tail with frequency $f$ and peak-to-peak amplitude $A$, while cruising at velocity $U$ through water of density $\rho_{w}$.

**Step 2 — Express the characteristic speed of the oscillating tail.** The tail's maximum lateral velocity is:

$$v_{\text{tail}} = 2 \pi f A$$

(The factor $2\pi$ arises from sinusoidal motion with peak-to-peak amplitude $A$, giving single-sided amplitude $A/2$ and peak velocity $\omega \cdot A/2 = 2\pi f \cdot A/2$.)

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio of tail speed to cruise speed.** This is the *propulsive Strouhal Number*:

$$\text{St} = \frac{f A}{U}$$

**Step 4 — Connect to the thrust coefficient.** The time-averaged thrust produced by an oscillating foil is approximately:

$$\bar{T} \approx \frac{1}{2} \rho_{w} \, U^{2} \, S \, C_{T}(\text{St})$$

where $S$ is the foil planform area and $C_{T}$ is the thrust coefficient that peaks when $\text{St} \in [0.2, 0.4]$.

**Step 5 — State the Froude Efficiency.** The Froude propulsive efficiency of an oscillating appendage is:

$$\eta_{F} = \frac{\bar{T} \, U}{\bar{T} \, U + \bar{P}_{\text{profile}} + \bar{P}_{\text{induced}}}$$

where $\bar{P}_{\text{profile}}$ is the profile-drag power loss and $\bar{P}_{\text{induced}}$ is the kinetic-energy loss in the wake.

**Step 6 — Maximum efficiency condition.** Taylor (1952) and Lighthill (1971) independently proved via variational analysis that the Froude efficiency is maximised for:

$$\boxed{\text{St}_{\text{opt}} = \frac{1}{\pi} \approx 0.318}$$

Thus, any robotic fish designed for efficient cruising must be tuned so that its $f$, $A$, and $U$ satisfy $fA/U \approx 0.3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication for Engineers:** If a robotic tuna is required to cruise at $U = 1.5$ m/s with tail amplitude $A = 0.15$ m, then the required tail-beat frequency is $f = (0.3 \times 1.5) / 0.15 = 3.0$ Hz. This is a KTU-style numerical problem that appears regularly in Part B question papers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gait Transition Condition from the Froude Number

We derive the critical Froude number at which a quadruped robot (and its biological counterpart) transitions from a walk to a trot.

**Step 1 — Define the kinetic and potential energy of the COM.** Treating the robot as an inverted pendulum of effective leg length $L$ (hip height), the COM kinetic energy during stance is:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

The potential energy at apex is:

$$V = m g L \left(1 - \cos\theta_{0}\right)$$

where $\theta_{0}$ is the half-angle of leg swing.

**Step 2 — Apply energy conservation in the ballistic regime.** At gait transition, the energy ratio $T/V$ becomes comparable to unity. Setting $T = V$ (the energy break-even condition):

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = m g L \left(1 - \cos\theta_{0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Froude Number.** Substituting $\text{Fr} = v^{2}/(gL)$ and the small-angle approximation $1 - \cos\theta_{0} \approx \theta_{0}^{2}/2$:

$$\text{Fr} = \theta_{0}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply empirical biological data.** Biologists observe that quadrupeds consistently switch gaits at $\theta_{0} \approx 0.7$ rad. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Fr}_{\text{trans}} \approx 0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering consequence.** A quadruped robot's gait scheduler must be programmed to switch from walk to trot at the velocity $v_{\text{trans}}$ satisfying:

$$v_{\text{trans}} = \sqrt{0.5 \, g L} = \sqrt{4.905 \, L}$$

For a Spot-class robot with $L = 0.6$ m, this gives $v_{\text{trans}} \approx 1.72$ m/s — closely matching Spot's published walk-to-trot transition velocity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Zero Moment Point (ZMP) Stability Criterion for Dynamic Gaits

For a quadruped in a trot gait, only two diagonal feet are on the ground at any instant. The robot has only **2-contact-point support**, so static stability fails; we must enforce dynamic stability.

**Step 1 — Define the generalised coordinates.** Let the COM be at position $(x_{\text{com}}, y_{\text{com}}, z_{\text{com}})$ in the world frame.

**Step 2 — Write the ZMP formula.** The ZMP $(p_{x}, p_{y})$ on the ground plane is the point where the net moment of inertial and gravitational forces vanishes:

$$p_{x} = x_{\text{com}} - \frac{z_{\text{com}}}{g} \ddot{x}_{\text{com}}$$

$$p_{y} = y_{\text{com}} - \frac{z_{\text{com}}}{g} \ddot{y}_{\text{com}}$$

**Step 3 — State the stability condition.** The robot does not tip over if and only if the ZMP remains strictly inside the support polygon $\mathcal{P}$ defined by the contact feet:

$$\boxed{(p_{x}, p_{y}) \in \text{int}(\mathcal{P})}$$

**Step 4 — Trot-gait polygon.** During a trot, the support polygon is a quadrilateral formed by the two diagonal stance feet and the line segments between them. The minimum margin $d_{\min} = \min \; d(\text{ZMP}, \partial \mathcal{P})$ is the **Dynamic Stability Margin** and is the controlled variable in MPC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern quadruped robots (ANYmal, Spot) use **Model Predictive Control** with a 200–500 ms horizon to predict the ZMP trajectory and pre-emptively adjust foot placement, achieving robust dynamic stability even on slippery or uneven terrain.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Static Stability Margin (SSM) for Hexapod Walking

**Step 1 — Define the support polygon.** For a hexapod in a tripod gait, three feet form a triangle; the COM projection must lie inside this triangle.

**Step 2 — Compute the signed distance from a point to an edge.** For triangle vertices $A$, $B$, $C$ and COM projection $G$:

$$d_{AB} = \frac{\vert (B_{x} - A_{x})(A_{y} - G_{y}) - (A_{x} - G_{x})(B_{y} - A_{y}) \vert}{\sqrt{(B_{x} - A_{x})^{2} + (B_{y} - A_{y})^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Static stability margin is the minimum such distance:**

$$\text{SSM} = \min\{d_{AB}, d_{BC}, d_{CA}\}$$

**Step 4 — Stability condition:**

$$\boxed{\text{SSM} > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G \in \text{int}(\triangle ABC)}$$

This is *the* classical stability condition for statically stable walking robots (RHex, LAURON, Messor) and a frequent topic in KTU Part A questions.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Locomotion Modes

| Metric | Wheeled | Tracked | Quadruped (Trot) | Hexapod (Tripod) | Snake (Serpentine) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Resistance (soft terrain) | 0.4 | 0.3 | 0.15 | 0.18 | 0.20 |
| Max Obstacle Height | $r_{\text{wheel}}$ | $0.5 L_{\text{track}}$ | $\geq L_{\text{leg}}$ | $\geq L_{\text{leg}}$ | $\geq 0.3 L_{\text{body}}$ |
| Static Stability | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (dynamic) | ✓ (tripod) | Marginal |
| Turning Radius | $> L_{\text{veh}}$ | $> L_{\text{track}}$ | $\approx 0$ (spin) | $\approx 0$ (spin) | $\approx 0$ (loco) |
| Energy Efficiency on Flat | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| Mobility on Rough Terrain | Poor | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Good (narrow gaps) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A robotic tuna is to cruise at $U = 2.0$ m/s. The tail oscillates with peak-to-peak amplitude $A = 0.20$ m. Compute (i) the optimal tail-beat frequency, and (ii) verify whether the design falls within Alexander's optimal Strouhal band.

**Solution:**

(i) Using $\text{St}_{\text{opt}} = 0.30$:

$$f = \frac{\text{St}_{\text{opt}} \cdot U}{A} = \frac{0.30 \times 2.0}{0.20} = 3.0 \text{ Hz}$$

(ii) Compute the actual Strouhal number: $\text{St} = fA/U = (3.0)(0.20)/2.0 = 0.30$.

Since $0.2 \leq 0.30 \leq 0.4$, the design is within the optimal band. ✓
