Example: *ISO Group Sweeper, Priva Kompano.* **(c) Aerial Service Robots (UAV class):** Limited use in greenhouses due to downwash turbulence; restricted to large Venlo-type structures and for canopy mapping. **Robotic Subsystems Required:** - *Perception:* RGB-D cameras, multispectral, hyperspectral, LiDAR, thermal imaging. - *Localization:* 2D SLAM using QR/ArUco fiducials on the floor, wheel odometry, UWB beacons. - *Manipulation:* Soft grippers for delicate fruit, vacuum end-effectors for seedlings. - *Decision-Making:* Edge-AI accelerators (NVIDIA Jetson, Google Coral) running plant disease CNNs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustiveness Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is written out. No step is skipped. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD) from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the VPD equation used in robotic irrigation triggers. **Step 1 — Saturation Vapor Pressure (Antoine / Tetens form):** The Clausius–Clapeyron relation gives the temperature dependence of saturation vapor pressure over liquid water. Integrating it with the empirical Antoine constants tuned for water over 0–50 °C yields the Tetens equation: $$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right) \quad [\text{kPa}]$$ where $T$ is the dry-bulb temperature in degrees Celsius, and $0.6108$ kPa is the saturation vapor pressure at 0 °C. **Step 2 — Actual Vapor Pressure from RH:** Relative humidity is, by definition, the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor $e_a$ to the saturation value at the same temperature: $$RH = \frac{e_a}{e_s(T)} \times 100\%$$ Solving for $e_a$: $$e_a = \frac{RH}{100}\,e_s(T)$$ **Step 3 — Define VPD:** VPD is the difference between the saturation and actual vapor pressures. It represents the *drying power* of the air—the driving force for transpiration. $$VPD = e_s(T) - e_a$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:** $$VPD = e_s(T) - \frac{RH}{100}\,e_s(T) = e_s(T)\left(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\right)$$ Or equivalently, using the *()* and a clean latex form: $$VPD = e_s(T)\,\Bigl(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\Bigr)$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Suppose $T = 25\,°C$ and $RH = 70\%$. Compute: $$e_s(25) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.6497) = 0.6108 \times 5.2046 \approx 3.179 \text{ kPa}$$ $$VPD = 3.179 \times (1 - 0.70) = 3.179 \times 0.30 = 0.9537 \text{ kPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A VPD of 0.95 kPa at 25 °C is ideal for tomato cultivation. The robotic controller compares this to a target band of **0.8 – 1.2 kPa** and dispatches foggers or vents accordingly. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Greenhouse Heating Load **Step 1 — Start from steady-state conductive heat loss through the greenhouse envelope:** $$Q_{heat} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_o) \quad [\text{W}]$$ where: - $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient of the cover (W/m²·K). - $A$ is the total surface area of the envelope (m²). - $T_i$ and $T_o$ are internal and external temperatures (K or °C, same ΔT). **Step 2 — Inject a typical Venlo greenhouse with the following design parameters:** $$U = 6.5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K},\quad A = 1200 \text{ m}^2,\quad T_i = 21\,°C,\quad T_o = 4\,°C$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$Q_{heat} = 6.5 \times 1200 \times (21 - 4) = 7800 \times 17 = 132{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 132.6 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Add a 25% safety margin for infiltration and edge effects:** $$Q_{design} = 1.25 \times 132{,}600 = 165{,}750 \text{ W} \approx 166 \text{ kW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is precisely the value the heating system (gas boiler + finned pipe network, or heat pump) must deliver, and that the SCADA system must verify is being delivered in real time. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Penman–Monteith Reference Evapotranspiration (FAO-56 Form) The FAO-56 Penman–Monteith equation for a reference crop (grass, height 0.12 m, surface resistance 70 s/m, albedo 0.23) is: $$ET_o = \frac{0.408\,\Delta(R_n - G) + \gamma\,\frac{900}{T+273}\,u_2(e_s - e_a)}{\Delta + \gamma\,(1 + 0.34\,u_2)}$$ **Derivation Logic:** 1. Start from the energy balance at the canopy surface: net radiation $R_n$ supplies latent heat $λE$, sensible heat $H$, and ground flux $G$. So $R_n = λE + H + G$, hence $λE = R_n - G - H$. 2. The combination equation unifies the *energy term* ($R_n - G$, in mm/day equivalent) and the *aerodynamic term* (driven by vapor pressure deficit and wind speed $u_2$). 3. The constant **900** in the aerodynamic term comes from $\frac{\rho_a\,c_p}{\varepsilon \lambda \cdot 70}$ where $\rho_a$ is air density, $c_p$ specific heat, and 70 s/m the bulk canopy resistance. 4. $\Delta = \frac{de_s}{dT}$ is the slope of the saturation vapor pressure curve at temperature $T$. 5. $\gamma = 0.665 \times 10^{-3}\,P$ is the psychrometric constant (in kPa/°C at standard atmospheric pressure). **Step-by-step numerical example:** Assume $T = 20\,°C$, $RH = 60\%$, $u_2 = 2$ m/s, $R_n = 12$ MJ/m²/day, $G = 2$ MJ/m²/day. (i) Compute $e_s(20) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 20 / (20+237.3)} = 0.6108 \cdot e^{1.3430} = 0.6108 \times 3.831 = 2.339$ kPa. (ii) $e_a = 0.60 \times 2.339 = 1.403$ kPa; so $e_s - e_a = 0.936$ kPa. (iii) $\Delta = \frac{4098 \cdot e_s}{(T+237.3)^2} = \frac{4098 \times 2.339}{(257.3)^2} = \frac{9585.1}{66203.3} \approx 0.1448$ kPa/°C. (iv) $\gamma = 0.665 \times 10^{-3} \times 101.3 = 0.0674$ kPa/°C. (v) Plug into Penman–Monteith: $$ET_o = \frac{0.408 \times 0.1448 \times (12-2) + 0.0674 \times \frac{900}{293} \times 2 \times 0.936}{0.1448 + 0.0674 \times (1 + 0.34 \times 2)}$$ Compute numerator: $0.408 \times 0.1448 \times 10 = 0.5908$. Aerodynamic term: $0.0674 \times 3.071 \times 2 \times 0.936 = 0.3874$. Numerator = $0.9782$. Denominator: $0.1448 + 0.0674 \times 1.68 = 0.1448 + 0.1132 = 0.2580$. $$ET_o = \frac{0.9782}{0.2580} \approx 3.79 \text{ mm/day}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Robotic Utility:** A greenhouse irrigation robot multiplies $ET_o$ by the crop coefficient $K_c$ (0.6 for young tomato, 1.15 at peak) and the canopy area to obtain daily water demand in liters. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Photosynthesis Light-Response (Rectangular Hyperbola) **Step 1:** The Michaelis–Menten style saturation model is widely accepted for leaf-level light response: $$A = \frac{A_{max}\,I}{K_m + I} - R_d$$ where $A$ is net photosynthesis (µmol CO₂/m²/s), $I$ is incident PPFD, $A_{max}$ is light-saturated rate, $K_m$ is half-saturation constant, and $R_d$ is dark respiration. **Step 2:** At compensation point $I_c$, $A = 0$: $$0 = \frac{A_{max}\,I_c}{K_m + I_c} - R_d \;\Rightarrow\; I_c = \frac{K_m\,R_d}{A_{max} - R_d}$$ **Step 3:** Numerical: with $A_{max} = 20$, $K_m = 200$, $R_d = 1.5$: $$I_c = \frac{200 \times 1.5}{20 - 1.5} = \frac{300}{18.5} \approx 16.22 \text{ µmol/m}^2\text{s}$$ This is the **light compensation point**—the minimum PAR below which the plant loses biomass. Robotic lighting systems ensure $I \gg I_c$ at all times. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Equations to Robotic Decisions | Equation | Robotic Decision Triggered | Actuator Dispatched | |---|---|---| | VPD > upper band | Humidify | Misting nozzle valve | | VPD < lower band | Dehumidify | Roof vent, dehumidifier | | $T_i > T_{set}$ | Cool | Exhaust fans, evaporative pad | | $T_i < T_{set}$ | Heat | Boiler circulation, heat pump | | $CO_2 < C_{set}$ & lights ON | Enrich | CO₂ solenoid valve | | Soil moisture < θ_{crit} | Irrigate | Dripper pump | | $ET_o \times K_c$ daily | Schedule | Master irrigation controller | | Light < PPFD_{min} | Supplement | LED array dimmer | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Greenhouse The diagram below captures the **complete closed-loop cyber-physical architecture** of a modern automated greenhouse—from physical processes, through sensors, robotic agents, edge intelligence, and back to actuation. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PLANT CANOPY
Photosynthesis and Transpiration"] -->|Heat Mass and Vapor| B["GREENHOUSE ENVELOPE
Glazing and Screens"] B -->|Solar Gain and Infiltration| A H["EXTERNAL WEATHER
Sun, Wind, Rain"] -->|Disturbance| B A --> S1["TEMP SENSOR
PT100 RTD"] A --> S2["HUMIDITY SENSOR
Capacitive Polymer"] A --> S3["CO2 SENSOR
NDIR Module"] A --> S4["PAR SENSOR
Quantum Meter"] A --> S5["SOIL MOISTURE
TDR Probe"] A --> S6["RGB-D CAMERA
Plant Inspection"] S1 --> MUX["DATA ACQUISITION
and TIME SYNC"] S2 --> MUX S3 --> MUX S4 --> MUX S5 --> MUX S6 --> MUX MUX --> EDGE["EDGE COMPUTE UNIT
NVIDIA Jetson Orin"] EDGE -->|PID and ML Inference| CTRL["CENTRAL CONTROLLER
PLC plus SCADA"] EDGE -->|Image Classification| VISION["VISION AI PIPELINE
YOLOv8 Disease Detect"] CTRL --> ACT1["HEAT PUMP and BOILER"] CTRL --> ACT2["EXHAUST FAN and PAD"] CTRL --> ACT3["ROOF VENT MOTOR"] CTRL --> ACT4["MISTING VALVE"] CTRL --> ACT5["CO2 SOLENOID"] CTRL --> ACT6["LED SUPPLEMENT ARRAY"] CTRL --> ACT7["DRIP IRRIGATION PUMP"] MOB1["MOBILE INSPECTION ROBOT
Wheeled Rover"] -->|Mission Dispatch| CTRL MOB1 -->|Inspection Data| EDGE MAN1["GANTRY MANIPULATOR
6DOF Arm"] -->|Pick Place Task| CTRL MAN1 -->|Force Torque Feedback| EDGE CTRL --> CLOUD["CLOUD DASHBOARD
Yield Analytics"] CLOUD -->|Daily Plan| CTRL ``` ## 4.2 Robotic Task State Machine for Harvesting Cycle This state machine represents the **logical sequence** a harvesting manipulator follows inside the greenhouse—a high-yield KTU question on robotics state machines. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Localize : SLAM and fiducial scan Localize --> ScanRow : move to next plant row ScanRow --> DetectFruit : vision inference DetectFruit --> Approach : inverse kinematics solve Approach --> Grasp : soft gripper close Grasp --> Cut : stem cut by end effector Cut --> PlaceInBin : lift and transport PlaceInBin --> ScanRow : continue row ScanRow --> Recharge : battery low Recharge --> Idle : dock complete Grasp --> Reattempt : slip detected Reattempt --> Grasp : retry once Reattempt --> LogFault : double fail LogFault --> Idle : human alert PlaceInBin --> Done : row complete Done --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Mapping Matrix (Visual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PERCEPTION_LAYER P1["Temperature"] P2["Humidity"] P3["CO2"] P4["Light PAR"] P5["Soil Moisture"] P6["Camera RGB-D"] end subgraph DECISION_LAYER D1["PID T Controller"] D2["PID RH Controller"] D3["Threshold Logic CO2"] D4["On Off Light Logic"] D5["Fuzzy Irrigation"] D6["CNN Disease Detector"] end subgraph ACTUATION_LAYER A1["Heater and Fan"] A2["Vent and Misting"] A3["CO2 Injector"] A4["LED Lamp"] A5["Drip Valve"] A6["Sprayer Robot"] end P1 --> D1 --> A1 P2 --> D2 --> A2 P3 --> D3 --> A3 P4 --> D4 --> A4 P5 --> D5 --> A5 P6 --> D6 --> A6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Always label your diagrams in plain uppercase text inside `[]` to comply with Mermaid safety rules—markdown formatting inside labels is rejected by the parser. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **Q: Define greenhouse farming. List any four key environmental parameters that must be controlled in a greenhouse.** **Model Answer:** Greenhouse farming is a method of controlled environment agriculture in which crops are grown inside transparent enclosures where the microclimate is precisely regulated to optimize growth, yield, and resource use efficiency. The four key parameters are: 1. **Temperature** (typically 20 – 25 °C daytime). 2. **Relative Humidity** (60 – 80 %). 3. **CO₂ concentration** (800 – 1200 ppm enriched). 4. **Light intensity / PAR** (300 – 800 µmol·m⁻²·s⁻¹). *(Other acceptable parameters: soil moisture, pH, EC, air velocity.)* --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **Q: What is Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD)? Why is it important for robotic irrigation decisions?** **Model Answer:** VPD is the difference between the saturation vapor pressure at a given temperature and the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the air, expressed in kPa. $$VPD = e_s(T)\left(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\right)$$ It is important because it directly governs plant transpiration rate. A VPD outside the optimal band (typically 0.8 – 1.2 kPa) indicates either disease-favorable humid conditions or excessive drying stress. Robotic irrigation and ventilation systems use the live VPD reading as the primary trigger for actuating drip valves, misting nozzles, and roof vents. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With the aid of a block diagram, explain the architecture of an automated greenhouse robotics system. List the major sensors and actuators used.** **Model Solution:** The architecture consists of three layered subsystems: **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing:** - PT100 RTDs for air temperature. - Capacitive polymer sensors for RH. - NDIR sensors for CO₂. - Quantum PAR sensors for light. - TDR probes for soil moisture / EC. - RGB-D cameras and multispectral imagers mounted on mobile robots for plant phenotyping and disease detection. **Layer 2 — Edge Computing and Decision:** - PLC / industrial controller running SCADA. - NVIDIA Jetson-class edge AI for vision inference (CNN-based disease classification). - Communication via MQTT / RS-485 / LoRa to cloud dashboard. **Layer 3 — Actuation:** - Heat pump and finned-pipe network (heating). - Exhaust fans and evaporative cooling pads. - Roof vent motors. - Misting / fogging valves. - CO₂ solenoid valves connected to compressed CO₂ cylinders. - LED supplementary lighting arrays. - Drip and drip-fertigation pumps. - Mobile inspection rovers and gantry manipulators. **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.)* The system is **closed-loop**: sensor → edge AI → controller → actuator → plant → sensor. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A Venlo-type glass greenhouse has a glazing area of 1200 m², overall heat transfer coefficient U = 6.5 W/m²·K, and the desired internal temperature is 21 °C when the outside temperature is 4 °C. Calculate the design heating load, including a 25 % safety margin for infiltration. If a heat pump with a COP of 3.5 is used, what is the electrical input power required?** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Conductive heat loss:** $$Q_{cond} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_o) = 6.5 \times 1200 \times (21 - 4) = 132{,}600 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2 — Apply 25 % safety margin:** $$Q_{design} = 1.25 \times 132{,}600 = 165{,}750 \text{ W} \approx 165.75 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Electrical input to heat pump:** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) is defined as: $$COP = \frac{Q_{design}}{P_{el}} \;\Rightarrow\; P_{el} = \frac{Q_{design}}{COP}$$ $$P_{el} = \frac{165{,}750}{3.5} = 47{,}357 \text{ W} \approx 47.4 \text{ kW}$$ **Conclusion:** The greenhouse requires a heat pump drawing approximately **47.4 kW** of electrical power to maintain 21 °C internally during a 4 °C ambient spell. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]* #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Derive the Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD) expression from the Clausius–Clapeyron relation. Compute the VPD for T = 25 °C and RH = 70 %.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Clausius–Clapeyron Integration:** The Clausius–Clapeyron relation for water vapor is: $$\frac{de_s}{dT} = \frac{\lambda\,e_s}{R_v T^2}$$ Integrating from a reference point ($T_0 = 0\,°C = 273.15$ K, $e_{s0} = 0.611$ kPa) gives the Tetens form used in greenhouse engineering: $$e_s(T) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right) \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — VPD Definition:** Relative humidity is defined as: $$RH = 100 \cdot \frac{e_a}{e_s(T)} \;\Rightarrow\; e_a = \frac{RH}{100}\,e_s(T)$$ VPD is the drying power of the air: $$VPD = e_s(T) - e_a = e_s(T)\left(1 - \frac{RH}{100}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Numerical Calculation:** $$e_s(25) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3}\right) = 0.6108 \cdot \exp(1.6497) = 3.179 \text{ kPa}$$ $$VPD = 3.179 \times (1 - 0.70) = 3.179 \times 0.30 = 0.954 \text{ kPa}$$ **Result:** VPD ≈ **0.95 kPa**, which lies inside the optimal 0.8 – 1.2 kPa band for tomatoes—no irrigation or venting action is needed. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the role of mobile robots and robotic manipulators in a modern greenhouse. Describe the typical sensors, end-effectors, and localization methods used.** **Model Solution:** **Mobile Inspection Robots:** - Wheeled / rail-mounted rovers traverse plant rows. - **Sensors:** RGB-D cameras, multispectral imagers, thermal cameras, LiDAR for obstacle avoidance, IMU + wheel encoders for odometry. - **Localization:** 2D SLAM combined with ArUco / AprilTag fiducials embedded in the floor grid; UWB beacons for cm-level accuracy. - **Tasks:** Disease scouting, ripeness assessment, phenotyping, autonomous spraying of biopesticides. **Robotic Manipulators (Gantry / Suspended):** - Multi-DOF (often 6-DOF) arms ride on overhead gantries. - **End-effectors:** Soft silicone pneumatic grippers for delicate fruit, vacuum grippers for seedling trays, shear cutters for selective pruning, camera-in-gripper for visual servoing. - **Sensors:** Force–torque transducers, tactile skins, embedded depth cameras. - **Tasks:** Grafting, de-leafing, selective harvesting, transplanting. **Localization and Mapping:** - Fiducial-based localization is preferred over SLAM in cluttered canopies. - UWB trilateration provides ± 5 cm accuracy. - Wheel odometry fused with IMU via Extended Kalman Filter. **Centralized Fleet Management:** - A central scheduler assigns tasks (e.g., "harvest row 4 block B") to idle robots using a job-shop scheduling algorithm, minimizing cycle time and energy. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > 1. **Unit errors:** Writing VPD in % instead of kPa. Always state units in the *final boxed answer*. > 2. **Confusing "heat pump COP" with "efficiency":** COP can exceed 1; efficiency cannot. Never write η > 1. > 3. **Forgetting the safety margin in heating load calculations:** Examiners explicitly test whether students apply the 20 – 30 % infiltration factor. Skipping it = -1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up transpiration and evaporation:** Transpiration is plant-driven (stomatal); evaporation is purely physical. Penman–Monteith $ET_o$ is a *reference* evapotranspiration, not crop ET. > 5. **Drawing robot architecture as plain arrows:** Examiners expect labeled blocks for *perception*, *decision*, and *actuation* layers. Always use a layered block diagram. > 6. **Writing "AI controls greenhouse"** without naming the actual algorithms (PID, MPC, fuzzy logic, CNN). Vague answers get zero credit in 14-mark questions. > 7. **Forgetting to justify numeric assumptions:** Every numerical problem must justify which formula is used and which constants are plugged in. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Greenhouse Farming):** - ☐ Greenhouse = **Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA)** + robotics + AI. - ☐ Six controlled parameters: **T, RH, CO₂, PAR, soil moisture/EC, air velocity**. - ☐ **VPD** is the master index for transpiration-triggered robotics. - ☐ **Tetens equation** gives saturation vapor pressure; integrate to get VPD. - ☐ **Penman–Monteith (FAO-56)** is the global ET standard; uses $R_n, G, T, RH, u_2$. - ☐ **Heating load** = $U A \Delta T$; always add 20–30 % safety margin. - ☐ **Heat pump COP** = $Q_{heat}/P_{el}$ — can exceed 1, unlike thermal efficiency. - ☐ **Photosynthesis** follows a rectangular hyperbola: $A = \frac{A_{max}I}{K_m + I} - R_d$. - ☐ **Compensation point** $I_c$ = light level where photosynthesis = respiration. - ☐ **Robotic triad:** Mobile rover (inspection) + Gantry arm (manipulation) + Edge AI (vision). - ☐ **Localization** in greenhouses: fiducials + UWB + wheel odometry + EKF fusion. - ☐ **Sensors:** PT100, capacitive RH, NDIR CO₂, quantum PAR, TDR soil, RGB-D cameras. - ☐ **Actuators:** heat pump, fan, vent, fogger, CO₂ valve, LED array, drip pump, robot fleet. - ☐ Standard targets: **T = 20–25 °C**, **RH = 60–80 %**, **CO₂ = 800–1200 ppm**, **PPFD = 300–800 µmol/m²/s**, **DLI = 12–20 mol/m²/day**. - ☐ **Energy balance** is *Q_solar = Q_cond + Q_conv + Q_rad + Q_ET*. - ☐ Robotics converts CEA from manual art to **data-driven, repeatable industrial process**. - ☐ Mermaid rule: **alphanumeric node IDs only**; **no markdown inside node labels**.

topics.title: Seeding and Harvesting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Seeding and Harvesting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Agricultural Robotics in Seeding & Harvesting

**Seeding and Harvesting** are the two foundational end-effectors applications of **Field Robotics** in precision agriculture. In the KTU 2024 framework, this topic falls under *Agricultural Field Robotics*, where autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems perform critical agronomic operations — replacing conventional mechanized tractors with intelligent, sensor-driven machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Seeding robotics* refers to the deployment of autonomous or teleoperated robotic platforms equipped with precise seed-metering mechanisms, GPS-guided navigation, and computer-vision-based furrow detection to place seeds at optimal depth, spacing, and soil coverage. *Harvesting robotics* refers to autonomous systems that identify mature crops via multi-spectral or RGB-D vision, manipulate end-effectors (grippers, cutters, suction cups), and collect produce without human intervention.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Farmhand"

Imagine a **traditional farm worker** who walks the field with a **sack of seeds** and a **hoe**. They bend down, dig a small hole, drop 2–3 seeds, cover it, and move to the next spot — doing this hundreds of times a day. The worker uses *eyes* to see spacing, *hands* to place seeds, and *legs* to move systematically.

A **seeding robot** is the *robotic version* of this farm worker. The:
- **Eyes** = RGB cameras, LiDAR, GPS-RTK for navigation
- **Hands** = Pneumatic or servo-driven seed-metering discs
- **Legs** = Wheeled or tracked mobile base with autonomous path planning

Similarly, the **harvesting robot** is the farm worker who **inspects ripe fruits** by looking, **picks them gently** without bruising, and **places them in a basket** — repeated thousands of times with uniform quality.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following metrics are **industry-standard benchmarks** in agricultural robotics:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Seed spacing accuracy** | ±**2 cm** | $\sigma_s$ |
| **Working width per pass** | **3–12 m** | $W_p$ |
| **Operating speed (seeding)** | **5–15 km/h** | $v_s$ |
| **Operating speed (harvesting)** | **0.5–2 m/s** | $v_h$ |
| **Vision detection accuracy** | ≥**95%** | $\eta_d$ |
| **Bruise/damage rate (harvesting)** | <**5%** | $R_d$ |
| **RTK-GPS positional accuracy** | ±**2.5 cm** | $\sigma_g$ |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 expects students to know that **RTK-GPS (Real-Time Kinematic GPS)** is the de-facto navigation standard for modern seeding/harvesting robots, offering centimeter-level accuracy critical for row-following and inter-row weeding.

### 1.4 Classification of Seeding & Harvesting Robots

**By Autonomy Level:**
- **Tractor-mounted autonomous systems** (retrofit kits like John Deere AutoTrac)
- **Standalone mobile robots** (e.g., Naio Technologies Oz, Ecorobotix AVO)
- **Swarm-based micro-robots** (research stage, e.g., Fendt MARS project)

**By Crop Type:**
- **Row crops** (maize, wheat, soy) — geometric row patterns
- **Orchard crops** (apples, oranges, mangoes) — 3D canopy navigation
- **Specialty crops** (strawberries, asparagus) — delicate handling needed
- **Grains** (rice, barley) — combine harvester style

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Seeding Robots — Operational Architecture

A precision seeding robot operates in a closed-loop **Sense → Decide → Actuate** cycle:

### Step 1: Field Mapping & Path Planning
- The robot scans the field boundaries using **GNSS-RTK** and generates an **optimal Boustrophedon (lawn-mower) path**.
- Path width equals the robot's **working envelope** $W_p$.
- Overlap percentage is typically set to **5–10%** to prevent missed rows.

### Step 2: Row Detection & Localisation
- Downward-facing **RGB cameras** or **LiDAR** detect existing furrow lines.
- A **PID controller** corrects lateral offset using steering actuation.
- Localization is fused via **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** combining GPS + IMU + odometry.

### Step 3: Seed Metering & Placement
- A **rotating seed disc** (pneumatic or mechanical) picks individual seeds from a hopper.
- Seeds are dropped through a **tube** into a **furrow opened by a coulter blade**.
- **Depth control** uses load cells on gauge wheels feeding back to a hydraulic actuator.

### Step 4: Coverage Verification
- Seed-flow sensors (often optical) confirm every seed is actually placed.
- Coverage map is updated in real-time for **prescription seeding** (variable rate).

## 2.2 Harvesting Robots — Operational Architecture

### Step 1: Crop Detection & Maturity Estimation
- **Multi-spectral cameras** (NIR + RGB) compute vegetation indices like **NDVI**:

$$NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$$

- NDVI > **0.7** typically indicates mature, harvest-ready crop.

### Step 2: 3D Localization of Produce
- **RGB-D cameras** (Intel RealSense, Azure Kinect) generate point clouds.
- A **trained CNN** (e.g., YOLOv8, Mask R-CNN) detects individual fruits with bounding boxes.
- **6-DoF pose estimation** aligns the end-effector with the fruit stem.

### Step 3: Manipulation
- End-effector types:
  - **Soft grippers** (silicone fingers with pneumatic actuation) — for delicate fruits
  - **Suction-based end-effectors** — for smooth surfaces (apples, tomatoes)
  - **Cutting mechanisms** — for stem-severance (asparagus, broccoli)

### Step 4: Post-Harvest Handling
- Conveyor belts or robotic arms transfer produce to **onboard bins**.
- Weight sensors, vision-based **quality grading** (size, color, defect) occur in-transit.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_s = \frac{D_s}{T_s}$ | Seeding speed = spacing / time-between-drops | m/s |
| 2 | $R_d = \frac{N_{bruised}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Damage rate in harvesting | % |
| 3 | $NDVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$ | Crop maturity index | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Seed spacing standard deviation | cm |
| 5 | $W_{eff} = W_p \cdot (1 - O_f)$ | Effective coverage width with overlap factor | m |
| 6 | $\eta_{cov} = \frac{A_{covered}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Field coverage efficiency | % |
| 7 | $T_{cycle} = t_{detect} + t_{approach} + t_{pick} + t_{place}$ | Harvesting cycle time | s |
| 8 | $P_{throughput} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} \cdot \eta_{pick}$ | Picks per hour | fruits/h |
| 9 | $F_{traction} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Traction force on soft soil | N |
| 10 | $D_{seed} = f(\text{crop type}, \text{soil moisture})$ | Seed depth function | cm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** The most commonly tested concept is the **NDVI formula** and its application in autonomous harvesting maturity detection. Students often lose marks by confusing NDVI with EVI (Enhanced Vegetation Index).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Seeding/harvesting robots are revolutionizing:
- **Labor-shortage agriculture** (Japan, Europe, USA)
- **Precision farming** (variable rate seeding saves 15–20% seeds)
- **24/7 harvesting** (no fatigue, no daylight dependency when using thermal/LiDAR)
- **Data-driven yield prediction** (per-plant geo-tagging)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Seed Spacing Standard Deviation

A precision seeder must maintain uniform seed-to-seed spacing. Let $x_i$ be the actual spacing of the $i$-th seed and $\bar{x}$ the mean target spacing.

**Step 1: Define the mean spacing.**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$

**Step 2: Compute deviation of each measurement.**
$$\Delta x_i = x_i - \bar{x}$$

**Step 3: Square the deviations to remove sign.**
$$(\Delta x_i)^2 = (x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**Step 4: Sum all squared deviations.**
$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**Step 5: Normalize by population size $N$ to get variance.**
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**Step 6: Take the square root for standard deviation.**

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}$$

> Conversion logic: This is the population standard deviation (dividing by $N$, not $N-1$). In agricultural robotics, where $N$ is large (thousands of seeds per hectare), the bias correction is negligible.

## 3.2 Derivation: NDVI as a Maturity Index

**Step 1:** Incident light $I_0$ strikes the leaf. Chlorophyll strongly absorbs **Red (R)** light for photosynthesis.

**Step 2:** Healthy/mesophyll-rich leaves strongly reflect **Near-Infrared (NIR)** due to internal cell scattering.

**Step 3:** Define the two reflectances:
- $\rho_{NIR}$ = fraction of NIR reflected
- $\rho_R$ = fraction of Red reflected

**Step 4:** Combine into a normalized ratio.
$$NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_R}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_R}$$

**Step 5:** Bound analysis:
- Bare soil: $\rho_{NIR} \approx \rho_R$ → $NDVI \approx 0$
- Dense vegetation: $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_R$ → $NDVI \to 1$
- Water/snow: $\rho_{NIR} < \rho_R$ → $NDVI < 0$

**Step 6:** For harvesting, set decision threshold $\tau$:
$$\text{If } NDVI \geq \tau, \text{ mark as harvest-ready}$$

Typical values: $\tau = 0.6$ to $0.75$ depending on crop.

## 3.3 Derivation: Coverage Efficiency with Overlap

**Step 1:** Let $W_p$ be the physical working width of the implement.

**Step 2:** Adjacent passes overlap by a factor $O_f$ (e.g., 0.08 = 8%).

**Step 3:** Effective width per pass:
$$W_{eff} = W_p \cdot (1 - O_f)$$

**Step 4:** For a rectangular field of length $L$ and width $B$:
- Number of passes $n = \lceil B / W_{eff} \rceil$
- Total path length $\ell = n \cdot L$

**Step 5:** Time to cover:
$$T_{cover} = \frac{\ell}{v_s} = \frac{n \cdot L}{v_s}$$

**Step 6:** Coverage efficiency (accounting for turning at row ends):
$$\eta_{cov} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{useful} + T_{turn}} \times 100\%$$

> Conversion logic: A typical $\eta_{cov}$ is **80–88%** in real field conditions; the remaining time is consumed in headland turns (U-turns at field edges).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: Seeding vs Harvesting

| Parameter | Seeding Robot | Harvesting Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary sensor** | RTK-GPS, IMU | RGB-D, multi-spectral |
| **End-effector** | Seed disc, coulter | Gripper, cutter, suction |
| **Cycle time** | Continuous (no discrete) | Discrete (per fruit) |
| **Accuracy metric** | $\sigma_s$ (spacing) | $\eta_d$ (detection), $R_d$ (damage) |
| **Speed** | High (5–15 km/h) | Low (0.5–2 m/s) |
| **Main challenge** | Soil variability, slip | Occlusion, varied ripeness |
| **Economic driver** | Seed cost savings | Labor cost savings |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A seeding robot plants maize at a target spacing of **20 cm** at a forward speed of **8 km/h**. If the seed disc has **24 holes** and rotates at **$\omega = 10$ rad/s**, verify the spacing.

**Solution:**

Convert speed:
$$v = 8 \text{ km/h} = \frac{8 \times 1000}{3600} = 2.222 \text{ m/s}$$

Seed drop frequency from rotating disc:
$$f = \frac{\omega \cdot n_h}{2\pi} = \frac{10 \times 24}{2\pi} = 38.197 \text{ seeds/s}$$

Spacing achieved:
$$s = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{2.222}{38.197} = 0.0582 \text{ m} = 5.82 \text{ cm}$$

This is **not equal to 20 cm**, indicating a design mismatch. The disc must rotate at:
$$\omega_{new} = \frac{2\pi \cdot v}{n_h \cdot s} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.222}{24 \times 0.20} = 2.908 \text{ rad/s}$$

> **Conclusion:** KTU expects students to recognize that **disc rotational speed must be synchronized to forward velocity** — this is the foundation of *seed-metering control*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Seeding Robot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Boundary Input] --> B[RTK-GPS + IMU Fusion via EKF]
    B --> C[Path Planner Boustrophedon]
    C --> D[Row Tracking Controller PID]
    D --> E[Seed Disc Motor Drive]
    D --> F[Depth Actuator Hydraulic]
    E --> G[Seed Flow Sensor Optical]
    F --> H[Coulter Blade Ground Engagement]
    G --> I[Coverage Map Update]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Variable Rate Seeding Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Harvesting Decision Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RGB-D Camera Captures Plant] --> B[CNN Object Detection YOLOv8]
    B --> C{Mature Check NDVI}
    C -->|No| D[Skip to Next Plant]
    C -->|Yes| E[6-DoF Pose Estimation]
    E --> F[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    F --> G[End Effector Approach]
    G --> H[Soft Gripper Closure with FBG Force Sensor]
    H --> I[Stem Cut Suction Assist]
    I --> J[Place in Bin Conveyor]
    J --> K[Update Yield Map Geo Tagged]
    K --> A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Full System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION_LAYER
        P1[RGB Camera]
        P2[LiDAR 2D]
        P3[Multi Spectral NIR Red]
        P4[IMU 9 Axis]
    end

    subgraph DECISION_LAYER
        D1[Localization EKF]
        D2[Path Planner A Star]
        D3[Maturity Classifier CNN]
        D4[Coverage Mapper]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION_LAYER
        AC1[Steering Motor BLDC]
        AC2[Seed Disc Servo]
        AC3[Robot Arm 6 DoF]
        AC4[Gripper Pneumatic]
    end

    P1 --> D1
    P2 --> D1
    P3 --> D3
    P4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> AC1
    D3 --> AC4
    D2 --> AC2
    D4 --> AC1
    D1 --> AC3
    AC3 --> AC4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence: Seed-to-Harvest Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Operator
    participant FieldMap
    participant Seeder
    participant Crop
    participant Harvester

    Operator->>FieldMap: Upload field boundary
    FieldMap->>Seeder: Generate Boustrophedon path
    Seeder->>Crop: Plant seeds at 20 cm spacing
    Note over Crop: 90-120 days growth
    Crop->>Harvester: Multi-spectral scan
    Harvester->>Crop: Detect ripe fruits via NDVI
    Harvester->>Harvester: Pick and bin
    Harvester->>Operator: Yield report uploaded
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define NDVI and explain its role in autonomous harvesting. (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) is a spectral index defined as:

$$NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_R}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_R}$$

where $\rho_{NIR}$ and $\rho_R$ are near-infrared and red band reflectances. In autonomous harvesting robots, multi-spectral cameras compute NDVI pixel-by-pixel; values ≥ **0.6–0.75** indicate mature, harvest-ready fruits. The harvesting robot uses this threshold to decide whether to actuate its end-effector, enabling selective picking and avoiding immature produce.

---

### **Q2. List three end-effector types used in harvesting robots and state one crop suitable for each. (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Soft pneumatic gripper** — strawberries, tomatoes (delicate skin, requires gentle contact force < **2 N**).
2. **Suction-cup end-effector** — apples, mangoes (smooth, firm surface allows vacuum grip).
3. **Cutting/scissor mechanism** — asparagus, broccoli, lettuce (requires stem severance close to the base).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of an autonomous seeding robot. Discuss the role of RTK-GPS and PID control in row tracking. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An autonomous seeding robot consists of three major subsystems:

**1. Perception Subsystem:**
- **RTK-GPS** provides absolute position with **±2.5 cm** accuracy by correcting satellite errors using a fixed base station.
- **IMU** measures orientation (roll, pitch, yaw) and angular rates.
- **Downward LiDAR or RGB camera** detects existing furrow lines for local row alignment.

**2. Decision Subsystem:**
- An **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses GPS, IMU, and wheel odometry to estimate pose $(x, y, \theta)$.
- A **Boustrophedon path planner** generates parallel tracks spaced by the working width $W_{eff}$.

**3. Actuation Subsystem:**
- **Steering motor** (BLDC) corrects lateral deviation.
- **Seed-metering disc** driven by a servo synchronizes seed drops with forward speed.
- **Hydraulic depth actuator** maintains constant seed depth.

**Role of PID in Row Tracking:**
The lateral error $e(t)$ between actual and desired row position is fed to a PID controller:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- $K_p$ provides proportional correction.
- $K_i$ eliminates steady-state drift.
- $K_d$ damps oscillations on uneven terrain.

RTK-GPS supplies the **global reference**, while PID closes the **local feedback loop** for centimeter-level row tracking.

---

**(b)** Derive the seed spacing achieved by a rotating disc seeder with $n_h$ holes, disc angular velocity $\omega$, and forward speed $v$. A maize seeder must achieve 20 cm spacing at 8 km/h using a 24-hole disc. Calculate the required disc speed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**
The disc makes one revolution in time:
$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

During this time, $n_h$ seeds are dropped, so time between drops:
$$\Delta t = \frac{2\pi}{n_h \omega}$$

Distance traveled by robot in $\Delta t$:
$$s = v \cdot \Delta t = \frac{2\pi v}{n_h \omega}$$

**Given:** $v = 8$ km/h, $s = 0.20$ m, $n_h = 24$.

Convert speed:
$$v = 8 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 2.222 \text{ m/s}$$

Solve for $\omega$:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi v}{n_h \cdot s} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.222}{24 \times 0.20}$$

$$\omega = \frac{13.962}{4.8} = 2.908 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to rpm:
$$N = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 2.908 \times 9.549 = 27.77 \text{ rpm}$$

**Required disc speed ≈ 2.91 rad/s (≈ 27.8 rpm).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the working principle of an autonomous fruit-harvesting robot. Explain the role of multi-spectral imaging, CNN-based detection, and 6-DoF pose estimation with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

An autonomous fruit-harvesting robot operates in a four-stage pipeline:

**Stage 1: Multi-Spectral Imaging**
- Cameras capture Red and NIR bands. NDVI is computed per pixel.
- Mature fruits exhibit NDVI > 0.6 in surrounding canopy regions or color signatures in the visible band.

**Stage 2: CNN-Based Detection**
- A pre-trained **YOLOv8 or Mask R-CNN** model identifies fruit bounding boxes $(x, y, w, h)$ in real time.
- Confidence threshold $C > 0.85$ filters false positives.

**Stage 3: 6-DoF Pose Estimation**
- An **RGB-D camera** (e.g., Intel RealSense D435) generates a 3D point cloud of the detected fruit.
- The fruit's centroid $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, z)$ and orientation $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ are computed.
- Inverse kinematics solves joint angles $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^6$ for the robot arm to reach the fruit.

**Stage 4: Manipulation**
- The end-effector (gripper/suction) closes with force feedback from **FBG (Fiber Bragg Grating) sensors** to prevent bruising.
- Stem is cut (if required), and fruit is placed in an onboard bin.

**Block diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.2 above.

---

**(b)** A harvesting robot completes one pick in $T_{cycle} = 4.2$ s with a success rate of $\eta_{pick} = 88\%$. Calculate the throughput in fruits/hour. Comment on the economic feasibility vs. a human picker who picks at 60 fruits/hour with 95% success. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Robot Throughput:**
$$P_{robot} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} \times \eta_{pick} = \frac{3600}{4.2} \times 0.88$$

$$P_{robot} = 857.14 \times 0.88 = 754.28 \text{ fruits/hour}$$

**Human Throughput (effective):**
$$P_{human} = 60 \times 0.95 = 57 \text{ fruits/hour}$$

**Comparison:**
$$\frac{P_{robot}}{P_{human}} = \frac{754.28}{57} \approx 13.23 \times$$

**Economic Feasibility Comment:**
- The robot achieves **~13× the throughput** of a human picker.
- However, capital expenditure (CAPEX) for a harvesting robot is typically **\$200,000–\$500,000**, while a human costs ~**\$15/hour**.
- Break-even is reached after **~3–5 years** of continuous operation in labor-scarce regions (Japan, California, Western Europe).
- Additional benefits: **24/7 operation**, **geo-tagged yield data**, and **uniform quality**.

**Conclusion:** Robotic harvesting is economically viable in regions with high labor costs and labor shortages.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** between km/h and m/s in speed problems (causes **−2 marks**).
> 2. **Confusing NDVI with EVI** — EVI includes a soil-adjustment factor; do not interchange.
> 3. **Skipping the block diagram** in seeding/harvesting questions — KTU awards **1–2 marks** specifically for a labeled diagram.
> 4. **Writing "robot picks faster" without numerical comparison** — examiners expect explicit throughput calculation.
> 5. **Not mentioning RTK-GPS accuracy** (±2.5 cm) — this is a frequently tested exact figure.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Seeding robots** use **rotating seed discs** synchronized to forward speed via the relation $s = \frac{2\pi v}{n_h \omega}$.
- ✅ **Harvesting robots** rely on **multi-spectral NDVI** (threshold ~0.6) for maturity detection.
- ✅ **RTK-GPS** offers **±2.5 cm** accuracy — the gold standard for field robot navigation.
- ✅ **Boustrophedon (lawn-mower) paths** are the default coverage pattern for row crops.
- ✅ **PID control** maintains row tracking; EKF fuses multi-sensor localization.
- ✅ **Soft grippers** with **FBG force sensors** are preferred for delicate produce to keep $R_d < 5\%$.
- ✅ **Coverage efficiency** $\eta_{cov} = 80$–$88\%$ in real fields (rest is headland turning).
- ✅ **CNN architectures** (YOLOv8, Mask R-CNN) are industry-standard for fruit detection.
- ✅ **NDVI formula** is **commonly asked** — memorize: $NDVI = \frac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$.
- ✅ **Economic viability** depends on labor cost, crop value, and operating hours per year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aerial Robots and Ground Robots for Agriculture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aerial Robots and Ground Robots for Agriculture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Agricultural Robotics** is a specialized branch of field and service robotics that integrates **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)**, **Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs)**, and hybrid autonomous platforms with precision agriculture technologies to perform farming operations such as crop monitoring, spraying, seeding, weeding, harvesting, and soil analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRUT602 – Module 1):**
> *"Aerial and ground robots for agriculture refer to autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems (UAVs/drones and UGVs) equipped with sensors, actuators, and intelligent control algorithms, designed to operate in agricultural environments for surveying, mapping, crop health assessment, targeted input application, and mechanical field operations."*

**Aerial Robots (Agricultural Drones / UAVs)** are powered, aerial vehicles that use aerodynamic forces to provide lift, capable of sustained flight, and operate without an onboard human pilot. They are classified by KTU/ISO standards into:
- **Multirotor UAVs** (Quadcopter, Hexacopter, Octocopter) – 4 to 8 rotors.
- **Fixed-wing UAVs** – Conventional airplane architecture.
- **Hybrid VTOL UAVs** – Vertical takeoff + fixed-wing cruise.

**Ground Robots (Agricultural UGVs / Field Robots)** are mobile robotic platforms that navigate agricultural terrain (soil, crops, orchards) on wheels, tracks, or legs to perform field tasks autonomously or via teleoperation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - *Aerial Robot* = Operates in the **air column** above crops → best for **monitoring, mapping, spraying**.
> - *Ground Robot* = Operates **on or in the field** → best for **physical intervention** (weeding, harvesting, soil work).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of an **aerial robot** as a *"flying eye and a flying sprayer"* — like a tireless, hyper-accurate crop doctor that flies over the field and instantly tells the farmer "this patch of wheat is stressed, and that patch needs pesticide." It is the **scout** of precision agriculture.

Think of a **ground robot** as a *"mechanical farmhand on wheels"* — like an autonomous tractor's smaller, smarter cousin. While the drone sees the problem from above, the ground robot walks into the field and **physically fixes it** (pulls the weed, prunes the leaf, harvests the fruit).

Together, they form a **complementary duo**:
- The **aerial robot** = *eyes in the sky* (perception layer).
- The **ground robot** = *hands on the ground* (action layer).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants):**
> - **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW):** Typically **5–25 kg** for spray drones; up to **150 kg** for industrial UAVs.
> - **Flight Endurance:** Multirotor ≈ **15–30 min**; Fixed-wing ≈ **60–120 min**.
> - **Ground Robot Operating Speed:** **0.5–2.5 m/s** in field conditions.
> - **Spray Swath Width:** **3–7 m** (drone) vs. **12–36 m** (tractor boom).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Agricultural Aerial Robots

**A. Multirotor UAVs (Most common in KTU syllabus):**
- **Quadcopter:** 4 rotors — *simplest, lowest payload (~2–5 kg)*.
- **Hexacopter:** 6 rotors — *higher stability, redundancy, payload up to 10 kg*.
- **Octocopter:** 8 rotors — *heavy lift, payload up to 25 kg*, used in industrial spraying.
- **Advantages:** Hover capability, vertical takeoff/landing, precise hovering over a point.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited endurance, high energy consumption.

**B. Fixed-Wing UAVs:**
- Use a single forward propeller + wings for lift.
- **Advantages:** Long endurance (up to 2 hours), large area coverage (>500 ha/day).
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot hover; needs runway/launcher; lower spray precision.

**C. Hybrid VTOL:**
- Combines multirotor takeoff with fixed-wing cruise.
- Used in **large-scale precision agriculture mapping**.

### 2.2 Classification of Agricultural Ground Robots

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheeled UGV** | 4WD / Ackermann / Differential drive | Open-field navigation, spraying, mowing |
| **Tracked UGV** | Tank-like tracks | Soft, uneven, or muddy terrain (paddy, vineyards) |
| **Legged UGV** | 4 or 6 legs | Orchard navigation under irregular canopies |
| **Rail/Cable UGV** | Moves on installed rails | Greenhouse and vertical farming |
| **Hybrid (Wheel-Leg)** | Articulated wheels with legs | Steep slopes, terraced farming |

### 2.3 Key Subsystems of Agricultural Robots (Why & How)

- **Perception Stack:** RGB camera, **multispectral sensor** (Red, Green, Blue, NIR, Red Edge), **LiDAR**, **thermal IR**, **hyperspectral imager**.
  - *Why?* To compute vegetation indices like **NDVI = (NIR − Red)/(NIR + Red)** for crop health.
- **GNSS + RTK Module:** Provides cm-level positioning (RTK = Real-Time Kinematic; accuracy **±2.5 cm**).
  - *How?* Receives corrections from a base station to remove atmospheric errors.
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** 9-DoF (3-axis accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer) — used for attitude control.
- **Flight/Motion Controller:** Pixhawk, Raspberry Pi + NAVIO2, or custom ARM Cortex boards running **PX4** or **ArduPilot** firmware.
- **Payload Interface:** Centrifugal/electrostatic nozzles (spraying), seed dispensers, grippers (harvesting), manipulator arms (weeding).
- **Communication:** **2.4 GHz / 5.8 GHz** RF (control), **4G/5G telemetry** (data), **900 MHz** (long-range).
- **Power Source:** **Lithium-Polymer (LiPo)** or **Lithium-ion (Li-ion)** battery with energy density of **150–250 Wh/kg**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Spraying:** Reduces pesticide use by **30–50%** by spot-application.
- **Crop Monitoring:** Generates orthomosaics and **NDVI maps** for variable-rate fertilization.
- **Labour Shortage Mitigation:** Solves the rising cost and scarcity of farm labour in Kerala, Japan, USA, and Europe.
- **Yield Prediction:** Multispectral imaging predicts harvest yield 4–6 weeks in advance.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU exam-relevant equations for Agricultural Robotics. Memorize these units carefully!**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{cov} = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | Coverage area, velocity, swath width, time | m², m/s, m, s |
| 2 | $t_{flight} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | Flight time, battery capacity, voltage, total power | s, mAh, V, W |
| 3 | $R_{spray} = \dfrac{Q_{nozzle}}{v \cdot w}$ | Spray rate per unit area, nozzle flow, velocity, swath | L/ha, L/min, m/s, m |
| 4 | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}$ | Normalized Difference Vegetation Index | dimensionless, range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 5 | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | Ground Sampling Distance, height, pixel size, focal length | cm/pixel, m, µm, mm |
| 6 | $Thrust_{req} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{n}$ | Required total thrust, mass, gravity, number of rotors | N, kg, m/s², unitless |
| 7 | $P_{hover} = m \cdot g \cdot v_{ind}$ | Hover power, mass, gravity, induced velocity at rotor | W, kg, m/s², m/s |
| 8 | $E_{field} = \dfrac{A_{field}}{A_{cov\_per\_flight}}$ | Number of flights needed, total field area, area per flight | unitless, ha, ha |
| 9 | $V_{actuator} = K_m \cdot u_a$ | Motor voltage constant × applied voltage | rad/s, V |
| 10 | $F_{track} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$ | Traction force, friction coefficient, mass, gravity | N, unitless, kg, m/s² |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Flight Time of a Multirotor Agricultural Drone

The flight time is the most critical performance parameter in KTU problems. We derive it from first principles.

**Given:**
- Battery capacity $C_{bat}$ (in mAh) — typical value: **10000 mAh = 10 Ah**.
- Battery nominal voltage $V_{bat}$ (V) — typical value: **22.2 V** (6S LiPo).
- Total power consumption of the drone $P_{total}$ (W) — typical value: **1500 W** at hover with payload.

**Step 1:** Convert battery capacity from mAh to Ah.

$$
C_{Ah} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{1000}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert battery capacity to coulombs (or directly to Wh for energy-based calc).

$$
E_{bat} = C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat} \quad \text{[Watt-hours]}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for usable depth-of-discharge (DoD). A LiPo battery should not be discharged below 80% to prevent damage. So usable energy is:

$$
E_{usable} = E_{bat} \cdot DoD
$$

**Step 4:** Convert usable energy to seconds of operation by dividing by total power.

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{E_{usable} \cdot 3600}{P_{total}} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

> The factor **3600** converts watt-hours to watt-seconds (joules), since $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A hexacopter spray drone uses a 6S 22000 mAh LiPo battery. At full payload, the total power draw is 2400 W. Find the flight time assuming 80% DoD.

$$
C_{Ah} = \dfrac{22000}{1000} = 22 \text{ Ah}
$$

$$
E_{bat} = 22 \times 22.2 = 488.4 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
E_{usable} = 488.4 \times 0.8 = 390.72 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{390.72 \times 3600}{2400} = \dfrac{1406592}{2400} \approx 586.08 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
t_{flight} \approx 9.77 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** This matches real-world hexacopter spray drones (DJI Agras T40 class), confirming the validity of the model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Thrust per Rotor for a Multirotor

For a multirotor in **steady hover**, the total upward thrust must balance the weight:

$$
T_{total} = m \cdot g
$$

For an $n$-rotor system (e.g., $n=4$ for quadcopter, $n=6$ for hexacopter), the thrust per rotor is:

$$
T_{rotor} = \dfrac{m \cdot g}{n}
$$

**Worked Example:**
An octocopter ($n = 8$) has a total mass (drone + battery + payload + pesticide) of $m = 28 \text{ kg}$. Find thrust per rotor. Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

$$
T_{total} = 28 \times 9.81 = 274.68 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_{rotor} = \dfrac{274.68}{8} = 34.335 \text{ N}
$$

**Safety Margin Check (KTU Examiner's favourite):**
Each motor/propeller combo must be rated to provide at least **2× this thrust** for safety and maneuverability:

$$
T_{rated} \geq 2 \times T_{rotor} = 68.67 \text{ N}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) for Aerial Survey

**GSD** is the actual ground distance represented by one pixel in an aerial image. Critical for mapping accuracy.

$$
GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}
$$

Where:
- $H$ = flying height above ground (m)
- $p$ = pixel pitch of the camera sensor (µm)
- $f$ = focal length of the lens (mm)

**Unit Conversion Trick (Most common KTU error):**
Since $p$ is in µm and $f$ is in mm, convert $f$ to µm: $f_{\mu m} = f_{mm} \times 1000$.

$$
GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f_{\mu m}} \quad \text{[m/pixel]}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A drone flies at $H = 120 \text{ m}$ with a camera having $p = 3.76 \text{ µm}$ and $f = 8.8 \text{ mm}$. Find GSD in cm/pixel.

$$
f_{\mu m} = 8.8 \times 1000 = 8800 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
GSD = \dfrac{120 \times 3.76}{8800} = \dfrac{451.2}{8800} = 0.05127 \text{ m/pixel}
$$

$$
GSD \approx 5.13 \text{ cm/pixel}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: NDVI from Multispectral Imagery

**NDVI** quantifies vegetation vigor:

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}
$$

**Worked Example:**
A pixel in a multispectral image has NIR = 0.45 and Red = 0.10.

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.10}{0.45 + 0.10} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.55} \approx 0.636
$$

> NDVI = 0.636 indicates **dense, healthy vegetation** (typical for mature paddy in Kerala).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Platform

| Parameter | Multirotor UAV | Fixed-Wing UAV | Wheeled UGV | Tracked UGV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coverage Rate (ha/h) | 4–10 | 100–500 | 1–5 | 0.5–3 |
| Precision | **High** | Medium | **Very High** | High |
| Payload Capacity | 5–25 kg | 5–15 kg | 50–500 kg | 100–1000 kg |
| Operating Cost (₹/ha) | **Low** | Very Low | Medium | High |
| Terrain Adaptability | Air-borne (best) | Air-borne | Flat fields | **Uneven/Muddy** |
| Best Application | Spot spraying, mapping | Large-area mapping | Seeding, weeding | Paddy, wet fields |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Agricultural Drone System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[GNSS RTK Module] --> node2[Flight Controller PX4]
    node3[IMU 9 DoF] --> node2
    node4[Barometer] --> node2
    node5[Magnetometer] --> node2
    node2 --> node6[ESC Electronic Speed Controller]
    node6 --> node7[Brushless Motor x n]
    node7 --> node8[Propeller Assembly]
    node2 --> node9[Telemetry Radio 2.4 GHz]
    node2 --> node10[Payload Controller]
    node10 --> node11[Multispectral Camera]
    node10 --> node12[Spray Pump and Nozzle Bank]
    node10 --> node13[Seed Dispenser]
    node2 --> node14[LiPo Battery 22.2 V]
    node14 --> node6
    node14 --> node10
    node9 --> node15[Ground Control Station GCS]
    node15 --> node16[Path Planning Software]
```

### 4.2 Ground Robot Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Power Pack Li ion 48V] --> nodeB[Motor Controller]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Sensor Stack]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Compute Unit NVIDIA Jetson]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Drive Motors 4WD]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Wheels or Tracks]
    nodeC --> nodeG[LiDAR 2D]
    nodeC --> nodeH[RGB D Camera]
    nodeC --> nodeI[IMU and Wheel Encoders]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[GNSS RTK Rover]
    nodeD --> nodeK[SLAM Algorithm]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Path Planner A Star]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Mission Logic ROS2]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Manipulator Arm Weeding Harvesting]
    nodeM --> nodeO[End Effector Gripper Cutter]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Wireless Link 4G 5G]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Cloud Dashboard]
```

### 4.3 Operational Workflow: Drone-to-Ground Robot Handoff

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart((Mission Start)) --> node1[Aerial Survey Multispectral]
    node1 --> node2[Generate NDVI Map]
    node2 --> node3{Anomaly Detected}
    node3 -- No --> nodeEnd1((Mission End Report))
    node3 -- Yes --> node4[Geo Tag Anomaly Zone]
    node4 --> node5[Transmit Coordinates to UGV]
    node5 --> node6[UGV Navigates to Zone]
    node6 --> node7[Visual Servoing Approach]
    node7 --> node8[Execute Intervention Weeding Spraying Harvesting]
    node8 --> nodeEnd1
```

### 4.4 Sensor-to-Decision Pipeline (Aerial)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Sunlight Reflectance] --> node2[Multispectral Sensor 5 Bands]
    node2 --> node3[Radiometric Calibration]
    node3 --> node4[Orthomosaic Stitching]
    node4 --> node5[NDVI GNDVI NDRE Computation]
    node5 --> node6[Classification Healthy Stressed Diseased]
    node6 --> node7[Variable Rate Map Generation]
    node7 --> node8[Spray Prescription Upload to Drone]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** 
*Define an agricultural UAV. List any four sensors commonly used in agricultural drones.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **agricultural UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle)** is a powered aerial robotic platform capable of sustained flight without an onboard human pilot, equipped with sensors and actuators for performing farming operations such as crop monitoring, mapping, and spraying.

Four sensors used are:
1. **Multispectral camera** (for NDVI computation).
2. **RGB camera** (for visual orthomosaics).
3. **LiDAR** (for terrain and canopy height).
4. **GNSS/RTK module** (for cm-level geotagging).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for sensor list — 4 items, write at least 4 for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is NDVI? Write the formula and state its typical range for healthy vegetation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** is a quantitative indicator of vegetation health derived from multispectral remote sensing data, using the difference between near-infrared (NIR) and red band reflectance.

Formula:

$$
NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - R}{NIR + R}
$$

**Range:** $[-1, 1]$. Healthy, dense vegetation typically has NDVI values between **0.6 and 0.9**. Bare soil gives values near **0**, and water gives **negative values**.

*(1 mark NDVI definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark range interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a multirotor agricultural drone**. List and describe the function of **at least six major subsystems**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hexacopter agricultural spray drone has the following specifications:
- Total mass (drone + battery + full pesticide tank) = **22 kg**.
- 6 rotors.
- Each rotor must provide thrust at hover with a **safety factor of 2.0**.
- Battery: 6S 16000 mAh LiPo, nominal voltage = 22.2 V.
- Average power draw at hover with full payload = **2000 W**.
- Depth of Discharge (DoD) limit = **80%**.

Calculate:
(i) Thrust required per rotor at hover.
(ii) Total usable battery energy in Wh.
(iii) Maximum flight time in minutes.
*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The architecture consists of the following interconnected subsystems:

1. **Power Subsystem (1 mark):** LiPo battery (22.2 V nominal) → supplies power to ESCs, flight controller, payload, and telemetry.
2. **Flight Control Subsystem (1 mark):** Flight controller (PX4/ArduPilot) running on ARM Cortex processor; reads IMU, barometer, magnetometer, and GNSS; outputs PWM signals to ESCs.
3. **Actuation Subsystem (1 mark):** 6 × BLDC motors + ESCs + propellers; produces thrust.
4. **Perception Subsystem (1 mark):** Multispectral camera + RGB camera; captures crop reflectance data.
5. **Payload Subsystem:** Centrifugal spray pump + 4–8 nozzles; dispenses pesticide/fertilizer.
6. **Communication Subsystem:** 2.4 GHz radio for control + 5.8 GHz video + 4G telemetry for cloud upload.
7. **Positioning Subsystem:** GNSS + RTK for cm-level accuracy.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The integration of these subsystems enables autonomous mission execution — takeoff, waypoint navigation, spraying, and return-to-home.

---

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Given:** $m = 22$ kg, $n = 6$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², Safety Factor (SF) = 2.0, $C_{bat} = 16000$ mAh, $V_{bat} = 22.2$ V, $P = 2000$ W, DoD = 0.8.

**(i) Thrust per rotor (3 marks):**

Total thrust at hover:

$$
T_{total} = m \cdot g = 22 \times 9.81 = 215.82 \text{ N}
$$

Thrust per rotor at hover:

$$
T_{rotor} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{n} = \dfrac{215.82}{6} = 35.97 \text{ N}
$$

**Rated thrust per motor** (with safety factor):

$$
T_{rated} = 2 \times 35.97 = 71.94 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Usable battery energy (2 marks):**

$$
C_{Ah} = \dfrac{16000}{1000} = 16 \text{ Ah}
$$

$$
E_{bat} = C_{Ah} \times V_{bat} = 16 \times 22.2 = 355.2 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
E_{usable} = 0.8 \times 355.2 = 284.16 \text{ Wh}
$$

**(iii) Maximum flight time (2 marks):**

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{E_{usable} \times 3600}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{284.16 \times 3600}{2000}
$$

$$
t_{flight} = \dfrac{1022976}{2000} = 511.49 \text{ s} \approx 8.52 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):** $T_{rotor} = 35.97$ N, $E_{usable} = 284.16$ Wh, $t_{flight} \approx 8.52$ min.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast **aerial robots (UAVs)** and **ground robots (UGVs)** for agricultural applications. Your answer must include a comparison table covering **at least six parameters** such as coverage rate, precision, payload, terrain adaptability, energy source, and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A farmer uses a **wheeled UGV** for inter-row weeding in a vegetable field. The UGV travels at a constant speed of **1.2 m/s** and the implement (weeder blade) has a working width of **0.6 m**.
(i) Calculate the **area covered in 30 minutes** in m².
(ii) If the field is rectangular with dimensions **150 m × 80 m**, calculate the **number of complete passes** the UGV must make, assuming the field is traversed along its longer side.
(iii) Find the **total time** to cover the field, neglecting turning time. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

| Parameter | Aerial Robot (UAV) | Ground Robot (UGV) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Medium | Air (above canopy) | Soil (inter/intra-row) |
| Coverage Rate | 4–10 ha/h (multirotor) | 0.5–5 ha/h |
| Precision | High (spray spot) | Very High (cm-level) |
| Payload | 5–25 kg | 50–1000 kg |
| Energy Source | Battery (LiPo) | Battery / Diesel hybrid |
| Terrain Adaptability | Excellent (flies over) | Limited (must traverse) |
| Primary Use | Monitoring, mapping, spraying | Weeding, harvesting, seeding |
| Limitation | Low endurance, weather | Slow, soil compaction |

**Explanation (2 marks):** UAVs are best for **perception and light intervention**; UGVs are best for **physical, contact-based operations**. In modern **smart farming ecosystems**, both work together — UAV surveys first, UGVs perform the action (drone-to-ground handoff model).

---

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Given:** $v = 1.2$ m/s, $w = 0.6$ m, $t = 30$ min, Field: $150$ m × $80$ m.

**(i) Area covered in 30 min (3 marks):**

$$
A = v \cdot w \cdot t = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times (30 \times 60)
$$

$$
A = 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 1800 = 1296 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(ii) Number of passes (2 marks):**

The UGV traverses along the **150 m** side. Total passes = Field width / implement width:

$$
N = \dfrac{80}{0.6} = 133.33 \Rightarrow 134 \text{ passes (rounded up)}
$$

**(iii) Total time (2 marks):**

Time per pass = Length / Speed = $150 / 1.2 = 125$ s.

Total time:

$$
T = 134 \times 125 = 16750 \text{ s} \approx 4.65 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** Area = **1296 m²**; Passes = **134**; Total Time = **4.65 h**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert **mm to µm** in the GSD formula → results off by **1000×**. Always write $f_{\mu m} = f_{mm} \times 1000$.
> 2. **Battery Conversion Mistake:** Forgetting to convert mAh → Ah before multiplying with voltage. The energy is in **Wh**, not mWh.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3600 multiplier** when converting watt-hours to seconds. Energy ($Wh$) × 3600 = Joules.
> 4. **Skipping the Safety Factor:** KTU often explicitly asks for a "rated thrust with safety factor 2.0" — students forget the ×2 and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Rounding passes incorrectly:** Always **round UP**, not down. Partial passes don't count in field operations.
> 6. **Confusing NDVI formula:** NDVI = (NIR − Red)/(NIR + Red) — students sometimes invert numerator. Memorize the form: **NIR first**.
> 7. **Missing Depth of Discharge (DoD):** LiPo batteries cannot be discharged to 0%. Always multiply by **0.8 (80%)** for usable energy.
> 8. **Diagram without labels:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for labeled block diagrams. A diagram without arrow labels and block names = **0 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Aerial Robot** = UAV/drone operating in the air column; best for **perception & spraying**.
- **Ground Robot** = UGV operating in the field; best for **physical intervention**.
- **NDVI** = (NIR − Red)/(NIR + Red); range **[−1, 1]**; healthy vegetation = **0.6 to 0.9**.
- **Flight time formula:** $t = (C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat} \cdot DoD \cdot 3600)/P_{total}$.
- **GSD formula:** $GSD = (H \cdot p)/f_{\mu m}$ (always in **m/pixel** after conversion).
- **Thrust per rotor:** $T_{rotor} = mg/n$ — multiply by **safety factor (usually 2.0)** for rated value.
- **Coverage area:** $A = v \cdot w \cdot t$ — keep units consistent (m/s × m × s = m²).
- **Multirotor types:** Quad (4), Hexa (6), Octo (8) — more rotors = more payload & redundancy.
- **Fixed-wing:** Long endurance, no hovering — used for large-area mapping.
- **Hybrid VTOL:** Combines vertical takeoff with fixed-wing cruise.
- **Wheeled UGV:** Flat fields, fast. **Tracked UGV:** Muddy/uneven (paddy, Kerala terrain).
- **Key sensors:** Multispectral, RGB, LiDAR, Thermal IR, IMU (9-DoF), GNSS-RTK.
- **Positioning accuracy:** RTK GNSS = **±2.5 cm**; standard GNSS = **±2–5 m**.
- **Power:** LiPo 6S = **22.2 V**; energy density **150–250 Wh/kg**.
- **Communication:** **2.4 GHz** (control), **5.8 GHz** (video), **4G/5G** (telemetry).
- **NDVI interpretation:** Water < 0; Bare soil ≈ 0; Sparse vegetation 0.1–0.3; Moderate 0.3–0.6; **Dense healthy > 0.6**.
- **Drone-to-Ground Handoff:** Aerial survey → NDVI map → anomaly detection → coordinate handoff → UGV intervention.
- **Why multirotors lose altitude fast:** Because induced power scales as $P \propto v_{ind}$ and $v_{ind} \propto \sqrt{W/\rho A})$, so heavier payload → more power → shorter flight time.

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 PCRUT602 exam — focus on formulas, units, and labelled diagrams!**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental Monitoring and Disaster Management Robots - Environmental Sensing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Environmental Monitoring and Disaster Management Robots - Environmental Sensing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Environmental Sensing** in field and service robotics refers to the systematic deployment and integration of physical, chemical, biological, and electro-optical transducers onboard autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic platforms, enabling real-time acquisition, quantification, and interpretation of ambient physical-chemical parameters across unstructured, hazardous, or ecologically sensitive environments. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCRUT602, environmental sensing forms the foundational perception layer that transforms a robotic platform from a passive mechanical agent into an intelligent **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** capable of performing quantitative Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), atmospheric monitoring, radiation survey, post-disaster damage assessment, and bioremediation scouting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Environmental sensing is the perception backbone of all monitoring and disaster-response robots. Without robust sensing, autonomy, mapping, decision-making, and actuation become impossible.

### 1.2 Sensor as a Transducer: The Fundamental Principle

At its core, every environmental sensor is a **transducer** — a device that converts one form of energy (chemical, thermal, radiant, mechanical) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency shift). The general input-output relationship is governed by:

$$ y(t) = K \cdot x(t) + b + n(t) $$

where $y(t)$ is the sensor output, $x(t)$ is the physical/chemical measurand, $K$ is the static sensitivity (gain), $b$ is the bias offset, and $n(t)$ is the additive stochastic noise.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Robot Nose & Skin" Analogy**
> Imagine a rescue dog trained to find survivors buried under earthquake rubble. The dog uses its nose to detect trace human scent molecules (chemical sensing), its ears to hear faint cries (acoustic sensing), and its paws to feel ground vibrations (seismic sensing). An **environmental monitoring robot is essentially a robotic rescue dog** — it is fitted with electronic "noses" (Metal-Oxide Semiconductor gas sensors, electrochemical cells), "ears" (microphones, ultrasonic transducers, geophones), and "skin" (temperature, humidity, radiation dosimeters). Just as the dog's brain fuses these multi-modal cues to localize survivors, the robot's onboard computer uses **sensor fusion algorithms** to construct a holistic understanding of the disaster scene.

### 1.4 Why Environmental Sensing is Non-Negotiable in Field Robotics

| Application Domain | Critical Measurands | Failure Consequence if Sensor Absent |
|---|---|---|
| **Volcanic Eruption Monitoring** | SO₂, CO₂ flux, ground temp | Loss of early warning, mass casualties |
| **Nuclear Disaster (e.g., Fukushima)** | Gamma dose rate (Sv/h), Cs-137 | Operator exposure to lethal radiation |
| **Urban Flood Response** | Water level, turbidity, pH | Inaccurate inundation maps |
| **Wildfire Detection** | VOC, CO, particulate matter (PM2.5) | Delayed firefront detection |
| **Agricultural Robotics** | Soil moisture, NPK, leaf wetness | Crop failure, water waste |
| **Marine Pollution Survey** | Dissolved O₂, hydrocarbon, salinity | Ecosystem collapse |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Sensor Specification

- **Sensitivity (K):** Output change per unit input change (V/ppm, mV/°C).
- **Selectivity:** Ability to discriminate the target analyte from interferents.
- **Limit of Detection (LOD):** Lowest reliably distinguishable concentration, typically defined as **3σ** above baseline noise.
- **Response Time (τ₉₀):** Time to reach 90% of final steady-state reading.
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio between maximum and minimum detectable signals, expressed in **decibels (dB)** or decades.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Power ratio of signal to noise, in **dB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** No single sensor is optimal for all criteria. Electrochemical sensors have high selectivity but slow response; Metal-Oxide sensors are fast but cross-sensitive to humidity. This is the central motivation for **multi-modal sensor fusion**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Environmental Sensors (KTU Taxonomy)

Field and service robots integrate sensors that can be taxonomically classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis 1: By Energy Transduction Principle
1. **Electrochemical Sensors** — Redox reaction at working electrode produces current proportional to analyte concentration (e.g., 3-electrode CO sensor).
2. **Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) Sensors** — Tin dioxide (SnO₂) layer changes resistance when target gas adsorbs and reacts with surface oxygen.
3. **Photoionization Detectors (PID)** — UV lamp ionizes VOCs; ion current measured.
4. **Infrared (NDIR) Sensors** — Gases absorb specific IR wavelengths per Beer-Lambert law.
5. **Thermocouple / RTD** — Seebeck effect for temperature.
6. **Geiger-Müller (GM) Tubes** — Avalanche ionization for ionizing radiation.
7. **Scintillation Detectors** — NaI(Tl) crystals + photomultiplier for gamma spectroscopy.
8. **Piezoelectric / MEMS** — Mass loading shifts resonant frequency (Quartz Crystal Microbalance, QCM).
9. **CMOS / CCD / Hyperspectral Imagers** — Photon-to-electron conversion.
10. **LIDAR / Radar** — Time-of-flight (ToF) ranging.

#### Axis 2: By Measurand Type
- **Chemical:** Gases (O₂, CO₂, CO, CH₄, NOₓ, SO₂, H₂S, VOC), pH, dissolved ions, hydrocarbons.
- **Physical:** Temperature, humidity, pressure, flow velocity, particulate concentration.
- **Biological:** Bioaerosols, microbial load, specific antigens (immunosensors).
- **Radiological:** Alpha, beta, gamma, neutron flux and energy spectra.
- **Geophysical:** Seismic acceleration, ground tilt, strain.

#### Axis 3: By Deployment Modality
- **In-situ onboard sensors** (carried by the robot).
- **Remote / standoff sensors** (mounted but sensing at a distance, e.g., LIDAR, TDLAS).
- **Tethered sensor probes** (snake-arm extension into voids).

#### Axis 4: By Active vs. Passive
- **Active sensors** emit energy and measure return (LIDAR pulse, ultrasonic ping).
- **Passive sensors** only receive ambient energy (IR camera, microphone).

### 2.2 The Sensor Response Function (Static Characteristics)

For an ideal linear sensor in steady state:

$$ y = K \cdot x + b $$

The **static sensitivity** $K$ is found experimentally by linear regression of $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs:

$$ K = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} $$

$$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - K \sum x_i}{N} $$

**Real sensors exhibit non-linearities**, often fit to higher-order polynomials:

$$ y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \dots $$

The **non-linearity error** is reported as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO):

$$ \varepsilon_{NL}(\%) = \frac{y_{actual} - y_{ideal}}{y_{FSO}} \times 100\% $$

### 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics and Transient Response

When a step input is applied, a first-order sensor follows:

$$ y(t) = y_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** and $y_{\infty}$ is the steady-state value.

The **rise time** to reach 90% is:

$$ t_{90} = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.1) \approx 2.303 \cdot \tau $$

The **bandwidth** is related to $\tau$ as:

$$ f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Disaster Robotics:** A robot entering a smoke-filled corridor cannot wait minutes for a CO sensor to equilibrate. Fast response time (sub-second $\tau$) is essential for life-safety decisions.

### 2.4 Noise, SNR, and Detection Limit

Sensors are corrupted by **thermal (Johnson) noise**, **shot noise**, and **1/f (flicker) noise**. The total input-referred noise floor defines the **Limit of Detection (LOD)**:

$$ \text{LOD} = 3 \cdot \sigma_{noise} \cdot K^{-1} $$

The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** in decibels:

$$ \text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right) $$

For a **photon-limited sensor** (e.g., LIDAR, hyperspectral imager), the dominant shot noise gives:

$$ \text{SNR} = \frac{N_{signal}}{\sqrt{N_{signal}}} = \sqrt{N_{signal}} $$

This means each doubling of optical power only improves SNR by **3 dB**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y = Kx + b$ | Linear static model | V, ppm, °C | Calibration curve |
| 2 | $\text{LOD} = 3\sigma K^{-1}$ | Detection limit | ppm, mg/m³ | Sensor benchmarking |
| 3 | $y(t) = y_{\infty}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | First-order step response | V, dimensionless | Dynamic response |
| 4 | $f_{-3dB} = (2\pi\tau)^{-1}$ | Sensor bandwidth | Hz | Speed vs. noise trade-off |
| 5 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Communication, imaging |
| 6 | $I(\lambda) = I_0 e^{-\alpha(\lambda) C L}$ | Beer-Lambert absorption | — | NDIR, TDLAS, UV-Vis |
| 7 | $R_{SnO_2} = R_0 e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ | MOS conductivity model | Ω | Gas sensor response |
| 8 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | Bq, s | Radiological sensing |
| 9 | $D = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ | LIDAR / ultrasonic ranging | m | Distance measurement |
| 10 | $\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | RSS uncertainty fusion | various | Multi-sensor fusion |
| 11 | $H = \sum_{i} -p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon / information entropy | bits | Sensor info content |
| 12 | $P_{min} = k_B T \Delta f$ | Johnson-Nyquist noise | W | Thermal noise floor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Equation 6 (Beer-Lambert Law):** This is the physical foundation of NDIR CO₂ sensors, TDLAS (Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy), and UV-Vis spectrometers used on environmental monitoring AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles). Memorize this for KTU problems.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility Mapping

- **Equation 1 (Linear model):** Calibrating soil-NPK electrochemical probes on agri-robots.
- **Equation 6 (Beer-Lambert):** Detecting methane plumes from landfill gas using TDLAS on UAVs.
- **Equation 8 (Decay law):** Estimating remaining activity of a radioactive source during nuclear cleanup robotics.
- **Equation 9 (ToF ranging):** Building 2D/3D occupancy maps in GPS-denied disaster rubble.
- **Equation 10 (RSS fusion):** Combining IMU + LIDAR + GPS for robust localization in disaster zones where GPS is jammed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Static Sensitivity of a Linear Sensor (Least-Squares Fit)
