**Problem Setup:** A field robot carries a metal-oxide gas sensor. Five known CO concentrations (in ppm) are flushed, and the corresponding sensor output voltages are recorded:

| Trial (i) | Concentration $x_i$ (ppm) | Output $y_i$ (V) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 25 | 0.95 |
| 3 | 50 | 1.85 |
| 4 | 75 | 2.78 |
| 5 | 100 | 3.70 |

**Find:** Sensitivity $K$ and bias $b$ using linear regression.

**Step 1 — Compute the required summations:**

$$ N = 5 $$

$$ \sum x_i = 0 + 25 + 50 + 75 + 100 = 250 \text{ ppm} $$

$$ \sum y_i = 0.05 + 0.95 + 1.85 + 2.78 + 3.70 = 9.33 \text{ V} $$

$$ \sum x_i^2 = 0^2 + 25^2 + 50^2 + 75^2 + 100^2 = 0 + 625 + 2500 + 5625 + 10000 = 18750 $$

$$ \sum x_i y_i = (0)(0.05) + (25)(0.95) + (50)(1.85) + (75)(2.78) + (100)(3.70) $$

Computing each product:

$$ (25)(0.95) = 23.75 $$
$$ (50)(1.85) = 92.50 $$
$$ (75)(2.78) = 208.50 $$
$$ (100)(3.70) = 370.00 $$

Adding them:

$$ \sum x_i y_i = 0 + 23.75 + 92.50 + 208.50 + 370.00 = 694.75 \text{ V·ppm} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear regression formula for slope K:**

$$ K = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} $$

Numerator:

$$ N \sum x_i y_i = 5 \times 694.75 = 3473.75 $$

$$ \sum x_i \sum y_i = 250 \times 9.33 = 2332.50 $$

$$ \text{Numerator} = 3473.75 - 2332.50 = 1141.25 $$

Denominator:

$$ N \sum x_i^2 = 5 \times 18750 = 93750 $$

$$ (\sum x_i)^2 = 250^2 = 62500 $$

$$ \text{Denominator} = 93750 - 62500 = 31250 $$

Therefore:

$$ K = \frac{1141.25}{31250} = 0.03652 \text{ V/ppm} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the bias intercept b:**

$$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - K \sum x_i}{N} = \frac{9.33 - (0.03652)(250)}{5} $$

$$ b = \frac{9.33 - 9.13}{5} = \frac{0.20}{5} = 0.04 \text{ V} $$

**Step 4 — Final calibrated model:**

$$ y = 0.03652 \, x + 0.04 $$

with sensitivity $K = 36.52$ mV/ppm and zero-bias offset $b = 0.04$ V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bias (0.04 V) is the residual sensor output at zero CO. This is the "clean-air baseline" that must be subtracted during every measurement cycle — a critical calibration step on autonomous monitoring robots.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Response Time of a First-Order Sensor

**Problem:** A thermistor mounted on a firefighting robot is suddenly plunged from $T_{amb} = 25°\text{C}$ ambient into a smoke plume at $T_{plume} = 425°\text{C}$. The thermistor has a time constant $\tau = 0.8$ s. Find (a) the temperature reading at $t = 2$ s, (b) the time to reach 90% of the final value, and (c) the −3 dB bandwidth.

**Part (a) — Temperature at t = 2 s:**

The step response is:

$$ T(t) = T_{\infty} - (T_{\infty} - T_0) e^{-t/\tau} $$

Substituting $T_{\infty} = 425°\text{C}$, $T_0 = 25°\text{C}$, $t = 2$ s, $\tau = 0.8$ s:

$$ T(2) = 425 - (425 - 25) e^{-2/0.8} $$

$$ T(2) = 425 - 400 \cdot e^{-2.5} $$

Since $e^{-2.5} \approx 0.0821$:

$$ T(2) = 425 - 400(0.0821) = 425 - 32.84 = 392.16°\text{C} $$

**Part (b) — Time to reach 90% of final value:**

By definition, $t_{90} = -\tau \ln(0.1) = 2.303 \tau$:

$$ t_{90} = 2.303 \times 0.8 = 1.842 \text{ s} $$

**Part (c) — Bandwidth:**

$$ f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi(0.8)} = \frac{1}{5.027} = 0.199 \text{ Hz} \approx 0.2 \text{ Hz} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A bandwidth of only 0.2 Hz means this thermistor cannot track rapid temperature fluctuations. For dynamic fire plume sampling, a much faster ($\tau \approx 0.05$ s) bare-wire thermocouple is required, trading ruggedness for speed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: LIDAR Range Equation (Link Budget)

**Problem:** A disaster-response robot uses a pulsed time-of-flight LIDAR. The transmitted pulse energy is $E_t = 10$ nJ, target reflectivity $R = 0.2$ (concrete rubble), beam divergence half-angle $\theta = 1$ mrad, receiver aperture $D_r = 5$ cm, and target range $d = 30$ m. Estimate the received pulse energy $E_r$ using the simplified LIDAR equation:

$$ E_r = \frac{E_t R A_r}{\pi (d \theta)^2} \cdot \eta_{atm}^2 $$

Assume clear air ($\eta_{atm} \approx 1$) and neglect background.

**Step 1 — Compute the receiver collecting area:**

$$ A_r = \pi \left(\frac{D_r}{2}\right)^2 = \pi (0.025)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Compute the beam footprint area at the target:**

$$ A_{spot} = \pi (d \theta)^2 = \pi (30 \times 0.001)^2 = \pi (0.03)^2 = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Plug into the LIDAR range equation:**

$$ E_r = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-9})(0.2)(1.963 \times 10^{-3})}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot (1)^2 $$

Numerator:

$$ (10^{-8})(0.2)(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.927 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} $$

Dividing:

$$ E_r = \frac{3.927 \times 10^{-12}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.389 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J} = 1.389 \text{ nJ } (!!) $$

**Result:** $E_r \approx 1.39$ nJ — actually slightly larger than $E_t$ because we assumed the target fully back-reflects within the receiver FOV (a non-physical simplification). In a fully rigorous equation with $1/d^4$ dependence, the received energy at 30 m would be in the **femtojoule** range, requiring an **avalanche photodiode (APD)** in photon-counting mode.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection for Disaster Scenarios

| Disaster Scenario | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Fusion Strategy | Critical Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Building collapse** | LIDAR (mapping) | IMU (orientation) | EKF SLAM | Dust obscuration |
| **Chemical spill** | Photoionization Detector | MOS array | Hierarchical Bayesian | Sensor poisoning |
| **Nuclear incident** | GM tube + scintillator | Optical camera | Particle filter | Detector saturation |
| **Wildfire front** | IR thermal camera | Gas (CO, VOC) sensor | Decision-level fusion | Thermal blooming |
| **Flooded urban** | Ultrasonic depth | pH + turbidity | Kalman filter | Biofouling |
| **Volcanic plume** | UV spectrometer (SO₂) | TDLAS (CO₂) | Joint inversion | Window contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Environmental Sensing Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ambient Environment] --> B[Physical Measurand]
    A --> C[Chemical Measurand]
    A --> D[Biological Measurand]
    A --> E[Radiological Measurand]

    B --> F1[Temperature / Humidity / Pressure Sensors]
    C --> F2[Electrochemical and MOS Gas Sensors]
    D --> F3[Bioaerosol and Immunosensors]
    E --> F4[GM Tube and Scintillation Detector]

    F1 --> G[Analog Front End - Amplifier and Filter]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    G --> H[16-bit ADC - Simultaneous Sampling]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processor - DSP]

    I --> J{Sensor Fusion Engine}
    J --> K1[Kalman Filter]
    J --> K2[Particle Filter]
    J --> K3[Bayesian Network]

    K1 --> L[Environmental State Estimate]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L

    L --> M[Robot Decision Layer]
    L --> N[Wireless Telemetry to Base Station]
    L --> O[Onboard Data Logger]

    M --> P[Path Planning and Mission Replanning]
    N --> Q[Operator Command Console]
    O --> R[SD Card and SSD Storage]

    P --> S[Robot Actuators]
    Q --> S
    R -.-> S
```

### 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree (Engineering Decision Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Environmental Sensor Selection] --> B{What is the measurand?}
    B -->|Gas or Vapor| C{Gas Class?}
    B -->|Temperature| D[Thermocouple / RTD / Thermistor]
    B -->|Radiation| E[GM Tube / Scintillator / Semiconductor]
    B -->|Distance| F[LIDAR / Ultrasonic / Stereo Camera]
    B -->|Chemical Ion| G[ISFET / Ion Selective Electrode]

    C -->|Toxic Inorganic| H[Electrochemical 3-Electrode]
    C -->|VOC| I[PID 10.6 eV Lamp]
    C -->|Greenhouse| J[NDIR TDLAS]
    C -->|Explosive| K[Catalytic Bead or MOS Pellistor]

    D --> L{Response Speed}
    L -->|< 10 ms| M[Bare Wire Thermocouple K-type]
    L -->|Accept 1 s| N[PT100 RTD]

    E --> O{Spectral Information Needed}
    O -->|Yes| P[NaI Scintillator with MCA]
    O -->|No| Q[GM Pancake Tube]

    F --> R{Range Required}
    R -->|< 10 m| S[Ultrasonic HC-SR04]
    R -->|10 to 100 m| T[Time of Flight LIDAR]
    R -->|> 100 m| U[Scanning LIDAR with APD]
```

### 4.3 Sensor Fusion Data Flow (Centralized vs. Distributed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensor_Node_A
        A1[CO Sensor] --> A2[Local MCU]
        A2 --> A3[CAN Bus TX]
    end

    subgraph Sensor_Node_B
        B1[LIDAR Scan] --> B2[FPGA Pre-processor]
        B2 --> B3[Ethernet TX]
    end

    subgraph Sensor_Node_C
        C1[Geiger Counter] --> C2[ARM Cortex M4]
        C2 --> C3[SPI TX]
    end

    A3 --> D[Centralized Fusion Processor - ROS2 Node]
    B3 --> D
    C3 --> D

    D --> E[Time Synchronization via PTP IEEE 1588]
    D --> F[Extended Kalman Filter]
    D --> G[Occupancy Grid Map Update]
    D --> H[Threat Level Classifier]

    F --> I[Robot State Publisher]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Signal Conditioning Chain (Analog Front End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Sensor Signal mV to uV] --> B[Instrumentation Amplifier - INA128]
    B --> C[Active Low-Pass Filter - 4th Order Butterworth]
    C --> D[Programmable Gain Amplifier]
    D --> E[16-bit SAR ADC - ADS131M04]
    E --> F[Isolation Barrier - Digital Isolator ISO7741]
    F --> G[Microcontroller DMA Transfer]
    G --> H[Digital Calibration Lookup]
    H --> I[Engineering Units Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Students:** Understand the *flow* of data from physical phenomenon → transducer → analog front end → ADC → digital processing. Examiners frequently test this chain because it is the most common failure point in real robotic systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define environmental sensing as applied to field robotics. List any four major classes of environmental sensors used on disaster-management robots.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Environmental sensing is the process of acquiring quantitative information about ambient physical, chemical, biological, and radiological parameters using onboard transducers on a robotic platform, enabling the robot to perceive, localize hazards, and act autonomously in unstructured or hazardous environments.

Four major classes:

1. **Electrochemical gas sensors** (e.g., 3-electrode CO sensor) — convert redox reactions into current.
2. **Metal-Oxide Semiconductor (MOS) sensors** (SnO₂-based) — change resistance on gas adsorption.
3. **LIDAR / ultrasonic ranging sensors** — active time-of-flight for distance and mapping.
4. **Geiger-Müller tubes or scintillation detectors** — measure ionizing radiation flux.

*(Additional acceptable: thermocouples, NDIR spectrometers, photoionization detectors, hyperspectral imagers, ISFET pH probes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Style]**
*What is the Limit of Detection (LOD) of a sensor? Why is the 3σ criterion used in its definition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Limit of Detection (LOD)** is the lowest concentration of an analyte that a sensor can reliably distinguish from a blank (zero-analyte) signal. Mathematically:

$$ \text{LOD} = \frac{3\sigma_{blank}}{K} $$

where $\sigma_{blank}$ is the standard deviation of the baseline noise and $K$ is the sensor sensitivity.

**Why 3σ?** By the properties of a Gaussian (normal) distribution, 99.7% of all baseline noise samples fall within ±3σ of the mean. Setting the detection threshold at 3σ therefore yields a **false-positive probability of only 0.3%** — a practical balance between sensitivity and statistical reliability, accepted by IUPAC and ISO standards.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an environmental sensing module onboard a disaster-management robot. Describe the role of the analog front end, ADC, and sensor fusion engine.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A metal-oxide gas sensor gives the following calibration data:

| Concentration $x$ (ppm) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output $y$ (V) | 0.20 | 0.62 | 1.05 | 1.46 | 1.91 | 2.33 |

Determine (i) the sensitivity and bias, (ii) the expected output at 35 ppm, and (iii) the LOD given that the standard deviation of the zero-gas baseline noise is $\sigma = 0.012$ V.

**Model Solution for (a):**

A complete environmental sensing module consists of **four stages**:

1. **Transducer (Sensor) Stage:** Converts physical/chemical measurands (temperature, CO, radiation, particulates) into electrical signals (mV, nA, or resistance change).
2. **Analog Front End (AFE):** Comprises an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) for low-noise differential amplification, an active low-pass anti-aliasing filter (cut-off at ½ sampling rate), and a programmable gain stage to span the ADC input range.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Samples at ≥ 2× the signal bandwidth (Nyquist), with 16–24 bit resolution for dynamic range > 96 dB. Simultaneous sampling is critical for multi-sensor fusion.
4. **Sensor Fusion Engine:** Runs algorithms (Kalman, Particle, Bayesian) on a DSP/MCU. It produces a unified environmental state estimate with associated uncertainty, which feeds the robot's decision and planning layer.

For *disaster management*, redundancy and hot-swappable sensor bays are added for fault tolerance.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Compute the summations for N = 6:

$$ \sum x_i = 0+10+20+30+40+50 = 150 $$

$$ \sum y_i = 0.20+0.62+1.05+1.46+1.91+2.33 = 7.57 $$

$$ \sum x_i^2 = 0+100+400+900+1600+2500 = 5500 $$

$$ \sum x_i y_i = 0 + 6.2 + 21.0 + 43.8 + 76.4 + 116.5 = 263.9 $$

**(i) Sensitivity K:**

$$ K = \frac{N\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} $$

Numerator: $6(263.9) - (150)(7.57) = 1583.4 - 1135.5 = 447.9$

Denominator: $6(5500) - (150)^2 = 33000 - 22500 = 10500$

$$ K = \frac{447.9}{10500} = 0.04266 \text{ V/ppm} $$

**Bias b:**

$$ b = \frac{\sum y_i - K\sum x_i}{N} = \frac{7.57 - (0.04266)(150)}{6} = \frac{7.57 - 6.399}{6} = \frac{1.171}{6} = 0.1952 \text{ V} $$

So the calibration model is:

$$ y = 0.04266\,x + 0.1952 $$

**(ii) Output at 35 ppm:**

$$ y(35) = 0.04266(35) + 0.1952 = 1.4931 + 0.1952 = 1.688 \text{ V} $$

**(iii) Limit of Detection:**

$$ \text{LOD} = \frac{3\sigma}{K} = \frac{3(0.012)}{0.04266} = \frac{0.036}{0.04266} = 0.844 \text{ ppm} $$

**Final Answers:** (i) $K = 42.66$ mV/ppm, $b = 0.195$ V. (ii) $y(35) = 1.688$ V. (iii) LOD = **0.844 ppm**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the static sensitivity and bias of a linear environmental sensor from a set of $N$ calibration points $(x_i, y_i)$ using the method of least squares. Discuss the importance of the linearity assumption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of an NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) CO₂ sensor based on the **Beer-Lambert law**. State the governing equation and discuss why NDIR is preferred over MOS for environmental CO₂ monitoring.

**Model Solution for (a):**

We assume a linear model $y_i = Kx_i + b + \epsilon_i$, where $\epsilon_i$ is the residual error at point $i$. The least-squares method minimizes the sum of squared residuals:

$$ S(K,b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \epsilon_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - K x_i - b)^2 $$

Take partial derivatives with respect to $K$ and $b$ and set them to zero (necessary conditions for a minimum):

$$ \frac{\partial S}{\partial K} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - K x_i - b) = 0 $$

$$ \frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - K x_i - b) = 0 $$

These yield the **normal equations**:

$$ K \sum x_i^2 + b \sum x_i = \sum x_i y_i $$

$$ K \sum x_i + N b = \sum y_i $$

Solving this $2\times 2$ linear system (e.g., by Cramer's rule) gives the standard regression formulas:

$$ K = \frac{N \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{N \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \quad b = \frac{\sum y_i - K\sum x_i}{N} $$

**Importance of Linearity Assumption:**

- **Computational simplicity** — only two parameters, easy real-time calibration onboard the robot.
- **Invertibility** — concentration is recovered as $x = (y - b)/K$, enabling direct engineering-units output.
- **Traceability** — calibration can be checked with just two reference points (zero and span gas).
- **Limitation:** Real sensors exhibit saturation at high concentrations and non-linear drift with temperature/humidity. A polynomial fit or two-point piecewise linear interpolation is then preferred.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Principle of NDIR CO₂ sensor:**

An NDIR sensor has an infrared source (broadband emitter or heated filament), a gas-filled optical cell (the measurement chamber), a narrow-band optical filter centered at $\lambda = 4.26\ \mu\text{m}$ (a strong CO₂ absorption line), and an IR detector (typically a thermopile or pyroelectric).

CO₂ molecules in the chamber absorb IR photons at 4.26 μm via vibrational-rotational transitions. The transmitted intensity follows the **Beer-Lambert law**:

$$ I(\lambda) = I_0(\lambda) \cdot e^{-\alpha(\lambda) C L} $$

where $I_0(\lambda)$ is the incident intensity, $\alpha(\lambda)$ is the wavelength-dependent absorption coefficient of CO₂, $C$ is the gas concentration (ppm or mol/m³), and $L$ is the optical path length.

The detector measures $I$, and the electronics compute $C$:

$$ C = -\frac{1}{\alpha L} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) $$

A reference channel at a non-absorbing wavelength (e.g., 3.9 μm) corrects for source aging, window contamination, and temperature drift.

**Why NDIR is preferred over MOS for CO₂:**

1. **Selectivity:** NDIR is intrinsically specific to CO₂ at 4.26 μm; MOS would cross-respond to water vapor and many other gases.
2. **Stability:** NDIR does not chemically consume the analyte — no drift due to sensor "burn-in" or poisoning that plagues MOS.
3. **Long lifetime:** Typical NDIR lifespan > 15 years versus 2–5 years for MOS.
4. **Wide dynamic range:** 0–5000 ppm easily, vs. MOS saturation at ~1000 ppm.
5. **Low humidity interference:** NDIR is largely unaffected by ambient humidity; MOS has strong humidity cross-sensitivity.

The trade-off is **higher cost, larger size, and higher power consumption**, which is why NDIR is reserved for fixed environmental monitoring stations and high-end service robots, while MOS is used in cheap, compact mobile platforms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatches in calibration problems:** Always write the units of $K$ (V/ppm) and $b$ (V). Marks deducted for unitless answers.
> 2. **Confusing 3σ (LOD) with 1σ (noise floor):** LOD is **three** standard deviations, not one. A surprising number of students write LOD = σ/K and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Beer-Lambert law: missing the path length L:** Many students write $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C}$ — incorrect. Path length $L$ is non-negotiable in the full form.
> 4. **Mixing up sensitivity with resolution:** Resolution is the smallest *measurable increment* (often ≤ LOD/3), while sensitivity is the slope. They are not the same.
> 5. **In NDIR answers, students often forget the reference channel** — losing 2 marks. A "real" NDIR sensor has at minimum two channels.
> 6. **Sensor fusion answers must list at least two fusion levels** (data-level, feature-level, decision-level). A vague "Kalman filter is used" answer is insufficient for 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Environmental Sensing**

- [ ] **Definition:** Environmental sensing = multi-modal acquisition of physical/chemical/biological/radiological data by a robot's onboard transducers.
- [ ] **Sensor = Transducer** that converts measurand energy → electrical signal.
- [ ] **Four orthogonal classification axes:** Transduction principle, Measurand type, Deployment modality, Active vs. passive.
- [ ] **Static model:** $y = Kx + b$ (linear); $y = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \dots$ (polynomial).
- [ ] **Least-squares regression formulas** for $K$ and $b$ — memorize numerator and denominator structure.
- [ ] **Dynamic model:** First-order step response $y(t) = y_\infty(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; $t_{90} = 2.303\tau$; $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- [ ] **LOD = 3σ/K** (IUPAC definition, 99.7% confidence).
- [ ] **SNR (dB) = 20 log₁₀(V_s / V_n)** for voltages; **10 log₁₀** for power.
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ — foundation of NDIR, TDLAS, UV-Vis, hyperspectral sensing.
- [ ] **MOS gas sensor model:** Resistance change with gas adsorption; sensitive to humidity; cross-sensitive (low selectivity).
- [ ] **NDIR vs MOS for CO₂:** NDIR selective, stable, long-life; MOS cheap, fast, but cross-sensitive.
- [ ] **Electrochemical sensors:** Redox current at working electrode, 3-electrode cell (working, reference, counter); ideal for toxic inorganics (CO, H₂S, NO₂).
- [ ] **Photoionization Detector (PID):** UV lamp 10.6 eV ionizes VOCs; measures ion current; used in hazmat robots.
- [ ] **GM tube / Scintillator:** Geiger counts pulses; NaI(Tl) scintillator gives gamma *energy spectrum* via MCA.
- [ ] **LIDAR range equation:** $E_r \propto E_t R A_r / (d \theta)^2$ — inverse-square on spot, inverse-square on distance to detector, giving effective $1/d^4$ falloff in full derivation.
- [ ] **Time-of-flight ranging:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ (c is signal speed: speed of light for LIDAR, sound for sonar).
- [ ] **Johnson-Nyquist noise:** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — sets the thermal floor of any resistive sensor.
- [ ] **Photon-limited SNR:** $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{N_{photons}}$ — fundamental limit for low-light LIDAR/camera.
- [ ] **Multi-modal sensor fusion:** Combines LIDAR + IMU + gas sensors for robust disaster-scene perception.
- [ ] **Fusion levels:** Data-level (raw signal combine), Feature-level (extracted features), Decision-level (per-sensor decisions).
- [ ] **Common fusion algorithms:** Kalman Filter (linear-Gaussian), Extended Kalman Filter (nonlinear), Particle Filter (multimodal), Bayesian Network (probabilistic reasoning).
- [ ] **Disaster-specific sensor suites:** Earthquake — LIDAR + IMU + acoustic listening; Flood — ultrasonic + pH + turbidity; Nuclear — GM + scintillator + video; Wildfire — IR camera + CO + VOC; Hazmat — PID + electrochemical array.
- [ ] **Signal conditioning chain:** Transducer → INA → Anti-alias LPF → PGA → ADC → Microcontroller → Engineering Units.
- [ ] **Aliasing prevention:** Sample at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist); in practice $f_s \geq 5 f_{max}$ for environmental signals.
- [ ] **Calibration essentials:** Zero-gas baseline (span) check, two-point linear check, periodic multi-point verification with certified reference gas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Field and Service Robotics (PCRUT602)**, a **Sensor** is defined as a transduction device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological quantity from the robot's internal state or external operational environment into a measurable electrical signal. This signal is subsequently processed by the robot's control architecture to enable perception, navigation, decision-making, and intelligent actuation.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** taxonomy, sensors are broadly classified along two orthogonal axes:

| Classification Axis | Category 1 | Category 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of Information** | **Proprioceptive Sensors** (measure internal robot state) | **Exteroceptive Sensors** (measure external environment) |
| **Energy Dependency** | **Active Sensors** (emit energy, then measure response) | **Passive Sensors** (only measure ambient energy) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on understanding sensor characteristics such as **range, resolution, accuracy, precision, repeatability, linearity, bandwidth, and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**. You are expected to map each robot application (field vs. service) to the most appropriate sensing modality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **human blindfolded in a room**:

- The **eyes** correspond to **exteroceptive sensors** (cameras, LiDAR) — they tell you *what is around you*.
- The **muscle spindles and inner ear** (vestibular system) correspond to **proprioceptive sensors** (encoders, IMUs) — they tell you *where your own body is in space*.
- The **eyes actively shining a torch** correspond to **active sensors** (sonar, LiDAR) — they send out a signal and listen for the echo.
- The **eyes simply receiving daylight** correspond to **passive sensors** (regular cameras) — they only listen, they do not emit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** A sensor in robotics acts as the **sensory nervous system** of the robot. Without sensors, a robot is essentially "blind and deaf," and the closed-loop control paradigm (Sense → Plan → Act) collapses into an open-loop, blind actuator.

## 1.3 Fundamental Sensor Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Key Terms)

The following **bold constants/metrics** are universally tested in KTU examinations:

- **Range ($R$)** — The minimum and maximum input values over which the sensor can operate.
- **Resolution ($\delta$)** — The smallest detectable change in the measured quantity.
- **Accuracy** — The closeness of the measured value to the true value (systematic error).
- **Precision / Repeatability** — The closeness of repeated measurements to each other (random error).
- **Linearity** — The degree to which the output is directly proportional to the input.
- **Sensitivity ($S = \Delta y / \Delta x$)** — The ratio of output change to input change.
- **Bandwidth / Dynamic Response** — The frequency range over which the sensor responds faithfully.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — The ratio of meaningful signal power to background noise power.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sensors Used in Field and Service Robotics

### A. Proprioceptive Sensors (Internal State)

These sensors report the **state of the robot itself** — joint angles, wheel rotations, battery voltage, motor current, force at the end-effector.

1. **Position Sensors**
   - **Potentiometer:** Resistive element with a wiper. Output is a voltage proportional to displacement.
   - **Optical Encoders:** Disc with slots; an LED and photodiode count pulses. Two types:
     - **Incremental Encoder:** Produces pulses A and B (quadrature) for relative motion.
     - **Absolute Encoder:** Produces a unique binary (Gray) code for each angular position.
   - **Resolver / Synchro:** Electromagnetic device giving analog sine/cosine outputs proportional to angle.
   - **Hall-Effect Sensors:** Detect magnetic field changes for brushless DC motor commutation.

2. **Velocity Sensors**
   - **Tachogenerator:** A DC generator whose output voltage is proportional to angular velocity.
   - **Differentiation Method:** Velocity is obtained by differentiating the encoder position signal (noisy at low speeds).

3. **Acceleration / Inertial Sensors (IMU)**
   - **Accelerometer:** Measures proper acceleration using a proof mass suspended by springs.
   - **Gyroscope:** Measures angular velocity (MEMS, ring laser, fiber-optic).
   - **Magnetometer:** Measures the Earth's magnetic field for heading (yaw) reference.

4. **Force / Torque Sensors**
   - **Strain Gauge:** Resistance changes with deformation.
   - **Load Cell:** Multi-strain-gauge Wheatstone bridge arrangement.

5. **Current and Voltage Sensors**
   - Hall-effect current transducers, shunt resistors, etc.

### B. Exteroceptive Sensors (External Environment)

These sensors perceive the **surrounding world** — obstacles, terrain, landmarks, human presence, temperature.

1. **Range / Distance Sensors**
   - **Ultrasonic (Sonar) Sensor:** Emits a 40 kHz pulse; measures time-of-flight ($t$). Distance: $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$, where $c \approx 343$ **m/s** is the speed of sound in air.
   - **Infrared (IR) Range Sensor:** Emits IR light; measures reflected intensity (triangulation-based for accuracy).
   - **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging):** Emits laser pulses; uses time-of-flight with $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**. 2D and 3D variants exist.
   - **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera:** Measures phase shift of modulated IR light.

2. **Vision Sensors (2D / 3D)**
   - **CCD / CMOS Cameras:** Passive sensors capturing ambient light on a 2D pixel array.
   - **Stereo Camera:** Two cameras provide disparity for depth estimation.
   - **RGB-D Camera:** Combines RGB with per-pixel depth (e.g., Kinect, RealSense).

3. **Tactile and Proximity Sensors**
   - Used in service robots (e.g., domestic, medical) for safe human-robot interaction.
   - Conductive elastomer, capacitive, piezoelectric, and optical whisker designs.

4. **Environmental Sensors**
   - **GPS (Global Positioning System):** Provides global position (latitude, longitude, altitude) using triangulation from satellites. Accuracy: **±2.5 m** (civilian) to **centimeter-level** (RTK-GPS).
   - Temperature, humidity, gas, and chemical sensors for agricultural and hazardous-environment field robots.

5. **Localization / Mapping Sensors**
   - **Wheel Odometry:** Encoders on wheels estimate pose via dead-reckoning.
   - **Visual Odometry / SLAM:** Cameras combined with algorithms.
   - **UWB (Ultra-Wideband) Beacons:** Indoor positioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A frequent 14-mark question asks you to *select and justify* a sensor suite for a given robot (e.g., autonomous agricultural tractor, hospital service robot, search-and-rescue drone). You must justify using **range, accuracy, environmental robustness, and cost**.

## 2.2 Working Principle Deep-Dive: The Hall-Effect Position Sensor

The **Hall Effect** is the generation of a transverse voltage across a current-carrying conductor when placed in a perpendicular magnetic field.

The Hall voltage is given by:

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $I$ = current through the sensor (A)
- $B$ = magnetic field strength (Tesla)
- $n$ = charge carrier density
- $e$ = electron charge
- $t$ = thickness of the active layer

**Why it matters for robotics:** Hall sensors are the workhorse of brushless DC (BLDC) motor commutation in modern service robots and drones. They are small, contactless, immune to dust, and have a long operational life.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta y_{out}}{\Delta x_{in}}$ | Output unit per input unit |
| 2 | Range of Ultrasonic | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c = 343$ m/s (air, 20°C); $t$ = round-trip time |
| 3 | LiDAR Time-of-Flight | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 4 | Hall Voltage | $V_H = \frac{I B}{n e t}$ | Tesla-based; contactless position sensing |
| 5 | Strain Gauge Output | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor (~2.0); $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| 6 | SNR | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB scale |
| 7 | Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N}$ or $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ rad | $N$ = pulses per revolution |
| 8 | Accelerometer Output | $V_{out} = V_0 + S \cdot a$ | $a$ = acceleration (m/s²) |
| 9 | GPS Pseudo-Range | $\rho = c \cdot (t_{recv} - t_{send}) + c \cdot \delta t_{clock}$ | Position via trilateration |
| 10 | Linearity Error | $e_{lin}(\%) = \frac{y_{actual} - y_{ideal}}{y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $y_{FS}$ = full-scale output |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Agricultural Field Robots (e.g., weeding, harvesting):** GPS + LiDAR + RGB camera + IMU for autonomous navigation in GPS-denied rows.
- **Service Robots (e.g., hospital delivery):** 2D LiDAR + depth camera + wheel encoders for SLAM and obstacle avoidance.
- **Search and Rescue:** Thermal IR cameras + gas sensors + acoustic sensors for victim detection under debris.
- **Domestic Vacuum Robots:** Cliff IR sensors + bumper switches + gyroscope + wheel encoders + top-mounted camera.
- **Underwater AUVs:** Doppler Velocity Log (DVL) + pressure sensor + sonar (since GPS does not work underwater).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

An ultrasonic sensor emits a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically **40 kHz**) and listens for the echo reflected from an object. The time between emission and reception is the **time-of-flight** $t$.

**Given:**
- Speed of sound in air at 20°C: $c = 343$ m/s
- Measured round-trip time: $t$ (seconds)

**Step 1:** During the time $t$, the acoustic wave travels to the object and back. Total distance traveled is $c \cdot t$.

**Step 2:** The one-way distance (sensor to object) is half of the round-trip distance:

$$d_{one-way} = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $c = 343$ m/s:

$$d = \frac{343 \cdot t}{2} = 171.5 \cdot t \text{ (meters)}$$

**Step 4 (Error consideration):** The speed of sound varies with temperature according to:

$$c_{air}(T) \approx 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \;\; \text{(m/s, T in °C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At $T = 40°C$ (a hot agricultural field), $c \approx 355.5$ m/s, producing a 3.6% error if not temperature-compensated. This is a common KTU exam point.

## 3.2 Derivation: Optical Encoder Resolution and Angular Position

An **incremental encoder** disc has $N$ slots uniformly distributed around its circumference. A fixed LED-photodiode pair produces one pulse per slot transition.

**Step 1:** The angular distance per pulse is:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ (radians per pulse)}$$

**Step 2:** In degrees:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{360°}{N}$$

**Step 3:** If the encoder generates $k$ pulses during a rotation, the total angle traversed is:

$$\theta_{total} = k \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

**Step 4 (Quadrature decoding):** Using two channels A and B in phase quadrature, the effective resolution is **quadrupled**:

$$\Delta \theta_{quad} = \frac{2\pi}{4N} = \frac{\pi}{2N}$$

**Numerical example:** For a 500-pulse encoder with quadrature decoding:

$$\Delta \theta_{quad} = \frac{360°}{4 \cdot 500} = 0.18° \text{ per count}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Strain Gauge as Force Sensor

A **strain gauge** is a thin metallic foil whose resistance changes when stretched or compressed.

**Step 1:** Resistance of a wire:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2:** For small deformations, the fractional change in resistance is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A} + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

**Step 3:** Using Poisson's ratio $\nu$ and a piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $G$ is the **gauge factor** (typically $\approx 2.0$ for metallic gauges) and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the strain.

**Step 4:** A **Wheatstone bridge** converts the small resistance change into a measurable voltage:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R / R}{4 + 2(\Delta R / R)} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \text{ for small } \Delta R$$

**Step 5:** Relating strain to force via Hooke's law ($F = k \cdot x$, $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$):

$$F = E \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R \cdot G}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Active vs. Passive Sensors

| Property | Active Sensor (e.g., LiDAR) | Passive Sensor (e.g., Camera) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy emission | Emits its own signal | No emission |
| Works in darkness | **Yes** | No (requires ambient light) |
| Power consumption | Higher (laser/sonar emitter) | Lower |
| Measurand interaction | Round-trip time / phase shift | Reflected / emitted ambient energy |
| Susceptibility to interference | Cross-talk, multipath | Lighting variation, shadows |
| Examples in robotics | LiDAR, sonar, radar, IR triangulation | CCD/CMOS camera, thermal IR, microphone |
| Typical cost | Medium to high | Low (cameras) to high (thermal) |

## 3.5 Sensor Selection Matrix for Common Field/Service Robotics Tasks

| Application | Recommended Sensor Suite | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomous car (field) | LiDAR + stereo camera + radar + GPS + IMU | Need 360° perception at long range, day/night |
| Agricultural weeder | RTK-GPS + RGB camera + IMU + wheel encoders | Centimeter-level row tracking + crop identification |
| Hospital service robot | 2D LiDAR + depth camera + IMU + bumpers | Indoor, structured, human-dense environment |
| Search-and-rescue drone | Thermal camera + RGB camera + LiDAR + gas sensor + GPS | Victim detection through smoke/dust |
| Underwater AUV | DVL + sonar + pressure sensor + IMU | GPS-denied, needs acoustic localization |
| Domestic vacuum | Cliff IR + bumper + gyroscope + encoder + camera | Cost-sensitive, structured indoor environment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop Robotic Sensing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] --> B[Exteroceptive Sensors]
    C[Robot Internal State] --> D[Proprioceptive Sensors]
    B --> E[Signal Conditioning]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Perception and Sensor Fusion Module]
    G --> H[Localization and Mapping SLAM]
    H --> I[Path Planner]
    I --> J[Motion Controller]
    J --> K[Actuators Motors]
    K --> A
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The loop closes through the physical world. Sensors perceive, the planner decides, the actuator acts, and the cycle repeats. The **Signal Conditioning** stage (amplification, filtering) and the **ADC** are essential bridges from the analog physical domain to the digital computational domain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Detailed Internal Signal Path of a Single Sensor Channel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Phenomenon] --> T[Transduction Element]
    T --> AM[Amplifier]
    AM --> FI[Filter LPF or BPF]
    FI --> AD[ADC]
    AD --> MC[Microcontroller or DSP]
    MC --> AL[Algorithm: Calibration, Linearization, Fusion]
    AL --> OUT[Digital Output to Robot Bus CAN, Ethernet, SPI]
```

**Explanation of each block:**

- **Transduction Element:** Converts physical quantity → electrical signal (e.g., piezoelectric crystal in an accelerometer).
- **Amplifier:** Boosts weak signals (e.g., instrumentation amplifier for strain gauges).
- **Filter:** Removes noise and aliasing; **low-pass filter** is most common.
- **ADC:** Converts analog to digital (e.g., 12-bit, 16-bit resolution).
- **Algorithm:** Calibration curves, temperature compensation, sensor fusion (Kalman filter, complementary filter).
- **Output Bus:** Standardized communication — CAN bus in vehicles, SPI/I2C in embedded boards, EtherCAT in industrial robots.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sensor Fusion Architecture (Multi-Sensor SLAM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[2D LiDAR] --> F1[Feature Extraction]
    L2[Stereo Camera] --> F2[Feature Extraction]
    L3[Wheel Encoders] --> F3[Odometry]
    L4[IMU] --> F4[Inertial Integration]
    F1 --> FU[Kalman or Particle Filter Fusion]
    F2 --> FU
    F3 --> FU
    F4 --> FU
    FU --> MAP[Global Map and Robot Pose Estimate]
    MAP --> NAV[Navigation Stack]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked about sensor fusion, explicitly mention that **encoders and IMUs are high-rate (200+ Hz) proprioceptive sources** while **LiDAR and cameras are lower-rate (10–30 Hz) exteroceptive sources**. The Kalman filter optimally fuses them by exploiting their complementary noise characteristics.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Sensor Modalities (Cognitive Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Robot Sensors] --> S1[Proprioceptive]
    S --> S2[Exteroceptive]
    S1 --> S1A[Position Encoders Resolvers Potentiometers]
    S1 --> S1B[Velocity Tachogenerator]
    S1 --> S1C[Inertial IMU Gyro Accel Mag]
    S1 --> S1D[Force Torque Strain Gauges]
    S2 --> S2A[Range LiDAR Sonar IR ToF]
    S2 --> S2B[Vision Mono Stereo RGB D Thermal]
    S2 --> S2C[Localization GPS UWB Beacons]
    S2 --> S2D[Tactile Bumpers Skin Sensors]
    S2 --> S2E[Environmental Temp Humidity Gas]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Measure robot's **internal** state | Measure **external** environment |
| Information | Joint angles, wheel speed, current | Distance to obstacles, images, temperature |
| Example 1 | Optical encoder (joint position) | LiDAR (obstacle distance) |
| Example 2 | IMU (acceleration / angular rate) | CCD camera (visual perception) |

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip time of 6 ms. Calculate the distance to the object. Take $c = 343$ m/s.**

**Model Answer:**

Given $t = 6 \text{ ms} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ s, $c = 343$ m/s.

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}$$

**Distance to object $\approx 1.03$ m.**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **LiDAR sensor**. Derive the distance equation and discuss its advantages and limitations for outdoor field robotics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop sensing architecture** of a mobile service robot. Explain the role of signal conditioning, ADC, and sensor fusion.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle of LiDAR:**

1. **Emission:** A laser diode emits a short pulse of light (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm wavelength) toward the target.
2. **Propagation:** The pulse travels at the speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
3. **Reflection:** A small fraction of the pulse is scattered back by the object.
4. **Detection:** A sensitive photodiode (APD or SPAD) detects the return pulse and timestamps it.
5. **Time Measurement:** The round-trip time $\Delta t$ is measured with picosecond precision.
6. **Distance Calculation:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

7. **Scanning:** A rotating mirror or MEMS mechanism sweeps the beam through 360° (in 2D LiDAR) or a 3D field of view.

**Advantages for Field Robotics:**
- Works day and night
- Range up to 100–200 m
- High accuracy (cm-level)
- Direct geometric mapping (point clouds)

**Limitations:**
- Performance degraded in heavy rain, fog, or dust
- Expensive compared to cameras
- Cannot classify objects (e.g., pedestrian vs. lamp post) without additional AI processing

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Closed-Loop Sensing Architecture:**

The mobile service robot executes a continuous **Sense → Perceive → Plan → Act** loop.

1. **Sensors:** LiDAR, IMU, wheel encoders, and cameras sample the world.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification and anti-aliasing filtering prepare the raw signals.
3. **ADC:** Converts the conditioned analog signals to digital values.
4. **Sensor Fusion:** A Kalman filter or particle filter merges multi-modal data — LiDAR for geometry, IMU for short-term dynamics, encoders for wheel odometry.
5. **Localization & Mapping (SLAM):** Builds a 2D occupancy grid and estimates the robot pose.
6. **Planner:** Computes collision-free paths to the goal.
7. **Controller:** Sends velocity commands to the motor drivers.
8. **Actuators:** Wheels respond, changing the robot state — and the loop closes.

**Role of Each Stage:**
- **Signal Conditioning:** Boosts weak sensor outputs and prevents aliasing.
- **ADC:** Bridges the analog physical world and the digital CPU.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Compensates for individual sensor weaknesses (e.g., IMU drift vs. LiDAR low update rate).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of an **incremental optical encoder** with a neat block diagram. Derive the angular resolution and explain the concept of **quadrature decoding**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital service robot must autonomously navigate a crowded corridor. Propose a suitable **sensor suite** with justification for each choice.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Incremental Optical Encoder:**

1. **Components:** A disc with $N$ transparent slots, an LED source, a photodiode detector, and signal processing electronics.
2. **Operation:** As the disc rotates, the slots alternately transmit and block the LED light. The photodiode produces a pulse train whose frequency is proportional to rotational speed.
3. **Block Diagram:** LED → Slotted Disc → Photodiode → Comparator → Pulse Shaper → Output.
4. **Angular Resolution:**

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{360°}{N}$$

5. **Quadrature Decoding:** A second photodetector is offset by 90° electrical, producing channel B. The phase relationship between A and B reveals direction of rotation, and the four edges (A↑, A↓, B↑, B↓) quadruple the resolution:

$$\Delta \theta_{quad} = \frac{360°}{4N}$$

6. **Index Pulse:** A once-per-revolution pulse provides an absolute reference.

**Numerical example:** For $N = 1000$, $\Delta \theta_{quad} = 0.09°$, more than sufficient for precise wheel odometry in service robots.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Proposed Sensor Suite for a Hospital Service Robot:**

| Sensor | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **2D LiDAR** | Active exteroceptive | 360° real-time obstacle map of corridor; range 25–30 m sufficient for indoor |
| **RGB-D Camera** | Passive exteroceptive | Identifies people, gestures, doors; depth for safe navigation near humans |
| **Wheel Encoders** | Proprioceptive | Dead-reckoning; high-resolution odometry |
| **IMU (9-DOF)** | Proprioceptive | Detects bumps, door thresholds, and orientation through elevators |
| **Bumper / Tactile Sensors** | Passive exteroceptive | Last-resort safety; triggers emergency stop on contact |
| **Ultrasonic (low cost, side-facing)** | Active exteroceptive | Glass-door and narrow gap detection (where LiDAR may fail) |

**Justification Logic:**
- Indoor hospital environments have **structured but dynamic** obstacles (people, wheelchairs, beds).
- LiDAR gives **geometric safety**; cameras add **semantic understanding**.
- IMU and encoders provide **continuity** between LiDAR updates.
- Bumpers are **ethical safety backups** mandated in human-shared spaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** recommend GPS for indoor hospital navigation — it does not work indoors.
> - Do **not** omit the bumper / tactile sensor — KTU examiners specifically check for **safety in human-shared environments**.
> - Always state **quantitative justification** (range, accuracy, update rate) — vague answers lose 2–3 marks.
> - For encoder questions, forgetting to mention **quadrature decoding and direction sensing** is a classic 2-mark loss.
> - For LiDAR/sonar questions, do not forget the **factor of 2** for round-trip time-of-flight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensor** = transducer converting physical quantity to electrical signal.
- **Two-axis classification:** Proprioceptive vs. Exteroceptive × Active vs. Passive.
- **Proprioceptive examples:** Encoders, IMU, strain gauges, tachogenerators.
- **Exteroceptive examples:** LiDAR, sonar, IR, cameras, GPS, tactile sensors.
- **Key formula (Ultrasonic / LiDAR):** $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ — never forget the factor of 2.
- **Speed of sound:** $c = 343$ m/s at 20°C; $c \approx 331.3 + 0.606T$.
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **Encoder resolution:** $\Delta \theta = 360°/N$ (basic), $\Delta \theta = 360°/(4N)$ (quadrature).
- **Hall Voltage:** $V_H = \frac{I B}{n e t}$.
- **Strain gauge:** $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$, with $G \approx 2$.
- **GPS accuracy:** ±2.5 m civilian; cm-level with RTK.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Combines high-rate proprioceptive (IMU, encoders) with low-rate exteroceptive (LiDAR, camera) sources.
- **Selection criteria:** Always justify sensor choice using **range, accuracy, environment, update rate, cost, and safety**.
- **Indoor vs. outdoor:** GPS fails indoors; cameras fail in darkness; choose accordingly.
- **Safety in human-shared service environments:** Always include a **bumper / tactile last-resort sensor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sensor Network** in the context of field and service robotics is a distributed infrastructure comprising spatially deployed autonomous sensing nodes (sensors, processors, communication modules, and power units) that cooperatively monitor physical, environmental, or operational parameters of a robotic system or its surroundings. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, sensor networks form the perceptual backbone of any autonomous robotic platform by enabling **perception, localization, mapping, decision-making, and actuation feedback** through synchronized multi-modal data acquisition and inter-node communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A sensor network is a system of interconnected sensor modules that exchange data via wired or wireless communication protocols to produce a unified, higher-level understanding of the robot's internal state and external environment, surpassing the capability of any individual sensor.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Sensor Node (Mote)** | The fundamental building block housing sensing, processing, communication, and power subsystems. |
| **Sink / Base Station** | A central node that aggregates data from all distributed sensor nodes (e.g., the robot's main controller). |
| **Multi-Modal Sensing** | Combining heterogeneous sensor types (vision, LiDAR, IMU, tactile) for robust perception. |
| **Data Fusion** | The algorithmic combination of data from multiple sensors to reduce uncertainty and improve accuracy. |
| **Network Topology** | The geometric arrangement of nodes (star, bus, mesh, ring, tree, hybrid). |
| **Sensor Resolution** | The smallest detectable change in the measured quantity, often expressed in **bits (n)** for digital systems. |
| **Sampling Rate ($f_s$)** | The number of samples acquired per second, governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Nervous System" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Your Body is a Sensor Network**
> Think of a field robot traversing an unknown terrain. Just as your body has thousands of sensors (skin for touch, eyes for vision, inner ear for balance, proprioceptors in muscles for joint angles), the robot uses a **network of sensors** to perceive the world. Your spinal cord acts as a *local processor* for reflexes, while your brain is the *central sink* fusing all signals. If a single sense is impaired (e.g., closing your eyes in the dark), the brain compensates using touch and hearing — this is precisely what **sensor fusion** achieves in robotics. The communication between your senses and brain happens via nerves (analogous to the **CAN bus, Ethernet, or wireless protocols** in a robot). Hence, a sensor network is nothing but an engineered, electronic replica of a biological nervous system.

## 1.4 Classification of Sensors in Robotic Sensor Networks

Sensors can be classified along multiple orthogonal axes relevant to KTU 2024 examinations:

### A. By Information Source
- **Proprioceptive Sensors:** Measure the robot's *internal* state (wheel encoders, IMU, joint torque sensors, battery voltage).
- **Exteroceptive Sensors:** Measure the *external* environment (cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic, GPS, temperature).

### B. By Energy Usage
- **Passive Sensors:** Do not emit energy; only receive (cameras, microphones, temperature probes). Power = sensing only.
- **Active Sensors:** Emit energy and measure the reflection (LiDAR, radar, sonar, IR distance sensors).

### C. By Output Type
- **Analog Sensors:** Produce continuous voltage/current output (thermistor, potentiometer).
- **Digital Sensors:** Produce discrete binary-coded output (quadrature encoders, I2C IMUs).

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Speed of Light ($c$):** $3 \times 10^8$ m/s — governs LiDAR and ToF camera range calculations.
- **Speed of Sound ($v_{sound}$):** $\approx 343$ m/s at 20 °C — governs ultrasonic sensor timing.
- **Standard ADC Resolution:** **8, 10, 12, 16, 24 bits** for industrial robotic sensor networks.
- **Standard Communication Baud Rates:** CAN — 1 Mbps; SPI — up to 50 MHz; I2C — 100/400 kbps (Standard/Fast mode).
- **Standard Wireless Frequencies:** Wi-Fi 2.4/5 GHz, Bluetooth 2.4 GHz, ZigBee 2.4 GHz, LoRa 433/868/915 MHz.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Robotic Sensor Network

A robotic sensor network operates in a continuous, closed-loop cycle that mirrors the **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA)** paradigm of robotics:

1. **Perception (Sense):** Each sensor node samples the physical world at a predefined $f_s$ and converts the analog signal to a digital value via an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
2. **Local Processing:** The on-board microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M) performs preliminary filtering, calibration, and feature extraction.
3. **Communication (Transmit):** The processed data packet is transmitted over the chosen bus/protocol to a sink or peer node.
4. **Data Fusion (Plan):** The central processor applies fusion algorithms (Kalman filter, particle filter, weighted average) to combine multi-source data.
5. **Actuation (Act):** Control commands are dispatched to motors/actuators based on the fused state estimate.
6. **Feedback:** Actuator states are re-measured by proprioceptive sensors, closing the loop.

## 2.2 Sensor Performance Metrics (Why They Matter)

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measurement to the *true value*. Critical for surgical and field-robotic manipulation.
- **Precision (Repeatability):** Closeness of repeated measurements to *each other*. Governed by sensor noise floor.
- **Resolution ($\Delta$):** The minimum distinguishable increment. For an $n$-bit ADC with range $[V_{min}, V_{max}]$:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^n}$$

- **Dynamic Range (DR):** Ratio between the largest and smallest measurable signal, expressed in dB:

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{max}}{A_{min}}\right)$$

- **Latency ($t_l$):** End-to-end delay from physical event to processed output. Robotic closed-loop control demands $t_l \leq 1$ ms for hard real-time tasks.
- **Bandwidth ($B$):** Maximum data rate the sensor or channel can sustain (Hz for sampling, bps for communication).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE cheat sheet for KTU 2024 ESE and internal evaluations.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^n}$ | ADC quantization resolution for an $n$-bit converter | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | dB |
| 4 | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{max}}{A_{min}}\right)$ | Dynamic range of a sensor or ADC | dB |
| 5 | $D = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$ | Aggregate data rate of $N$ sensors each sampling at $f_s$ with $b$ bits/sample | bps |
| 6 | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{2 R}{c}$ | Round-trip time of flight for a LiDAR pulse to range $R$ | seconds |
| 7 | $t_{ultrasonic} = \dfrac{2 d}{v_{sound}}$ | Ultrasonic time-of-flight for distance $d$ | seconds |
| 8 | $R_{max} = \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2}\scriptstyle 4]{\dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{L \, (4\pi)^3 P_{r,min}}}$ | Radar/LiDAR max-range equation (radar equation form) | meters |
| 9 | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B u_{k-1}$ | Kalman filter state prediction (a-priori estimate) | state units |
| 10 | $P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$ | Kalman filter covariance prediction | covariance units |
| 11 | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain (optimal blending factor) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Kalman filter measurement update (a-posteriori estimate) | state units |
| 13 | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | Kalman filter covariance update (a-posteriori) | covariance units |
| 14 | $\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i^2}\right)^{-1}$ | Variance of fused estimate from $N$ independent Gaussian sensors | variance units |
| 15 | $\hat{x}_{fused} = \sigma_{fused}^2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{x_i}{\sigma_i^2}$ | Fused value (inverse-variance weighted average) | measurement units |

## 2.4 Network Topologies in Robotic Sensor Networks

| Topology | Wiring Complexity | Fault Tolerance | Latency | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Star** | Low (point-to-point) | Low (hub failure = total loss) | Lowest | Centralized robots (e.g., RC servos to flight controller) |
| **Bus (Linear)** | Lowest (single backbone) | Low (single break → total loss) | Medium | CAN bus in automobiles and mobile robots |
| **Ring** | Medium (closed loop) | Medium (dual ring provides redundancy) | Medium | Industrial fieldbus (Token Ring, Fiber-optic rings) |
| **Mesh** | Highest (multi-hop) | Very High (self-healing) | Variable (routing-dependent) | Wireless sensor networks, swarm robots |
| **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Medium | Medium | Variable | Layered mobile robot architectures |

## 2.5 Communication Protocol Summary (Wired)

| Protocol | Speed | Max Nodes | Use in Robotics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **UART** | up to 1 Mbps | Point-to-point (1-to-1) | GPS modules, Bluetooth transceivers |
| **I2C** | 100 kbps / 400 kbps / 3.4 Mbps | 127 (7-bit addr.) | Low-speed IMU, OLED, ToF sensors |
| **SPI** | up to 50 MHz | Limited by CS lines (typically $\leq 10$) | High-speed ADC, SD cards, displays |
| **CAN** | 1 Mbps (CAN 2.0B) | $\approx 2^{29}$ (11/29-bit ID) | Mobile robots, automotive, drones |
| **EtherCAT** | 100 Mbps | $\approx 65{,}535$ | Industrial robotic arms (hard real-time) |
| **Ethernet (TSN)** | 1 Gbps+ | Large | Multi-camera vision pipelines, AMRs |

## 2.6 Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) Considerations

- **Power Budget:** Sensor nodes are typically battery-powered; communication is the largest power drain ($\approx 10\times$ sensing).
- **Duty Cycling:** Nodes sleep most of the time, waking only to sense and transmit.
- **Time Synchronization:** Critical for data fusion; protocols include **NTP, PTP (IEEE 1588), TPSN, FTSP**.
- **MAC Protocols:** CSMA/CA, TDMA, or hybrid — chosen to minimize collisions in dense deployments.
- **Routing:** LEACH, PEGASIS, and directed-diffusion are WSN-specific protocols.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Quantization Resolution

**Problem Setup:** A robotic force-torque sensor uses a 12-bit ADC with input voltage range $[0 \text{ V}, 5 \text{ V}]$. Find the quantization resolution $\Delta$.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation from the formula sheet.

The quantization step is given by $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^n}$.

### Step 2: Substitute the parameters.

We have $V_{max} = 5$ V, $V_{min} = 0$ V, and $n = 12$ bits.

$$\Delta = \frac{5 - 0}{2^{12}}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the denominator.

$$2^{12} = 4096$$

### Step 4: Compute the final value.

$$\Delta = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5: Engineering interpretation.

This means the smallest force change detectable corresponds to a **1.22 mV** voltage step — translating this to Newtons requires the sensor's sensitivity (mV/N) from its calibration certificate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aggregate Data Rate of a Multi-Sensor System

**Problem Setup:** A service robot is equipped with 4 cameras (each 1 MP, 30 fps, 8-bit grayscale), 1 LiDAR (200,000 points/s, 16-bit range), and 1 IMU (1 kHz, 16-bit x 6 axes). Calculate the total data rate.

### Step 1: Compute camera data rate per camera.

$$D_{cam} = 1 \text{ MP} \times 30 \text{ fps} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 240 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 2: Total for 4 cameras.

$$D_{cameras} = 4 \times 240 = 960 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 3: LiDAR data rate.

$$D_{LiDAR} = 200{,}000 \times 16 = 3.2 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 4: IMU data rate.

$$D_{IMU} = 1000 \times 16 \times 6 = 96 \text{ kbps} \approx 0.096 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 5: Sum to find the aggregate data rate.

$$D_{total} = 960 + 3.2 + 0.096 = 963.296 \text{ Mbps}$$

### Step 6: Practical implication.

The robot's internal bus **must** support at least ~1 Gbps. This justifies the use of **Gigabit Ethernet (TSN)** rather than CAN or USB 2.0.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse-Variance Weighted Sensor Fusion

**Problem Setup:** Three independent temperature sensors on a field robot report:
- Sensor 1: $x_1 = 24.1$ °C with $\sigma_1 = 0.5$ °C
- Sensor 2: $x_2 = 24.6$ °C with $\sigma_2 = 0.3$ °C
- Sensor 3: $x_3 = 23.9$ °C with $\sigma_3 = 1.0$ °C

Determine the fused estimate $\hat{x}_{fused}$ and its variance $\sigma_{fused}^2$.

### Step 1: Recall the fusion formulas from the cheat sheet.

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2}\right)^{-1} \quad ; \quad \hat{x}_{fused} = \sigma_{fused}^2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{x_i}{\sigma_i^2}$$

### Step 2: Compute individual precision terms.

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_1^2} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{\sigma_2^2} = \frac{1}{0.09} \approx 11.111 \quad ; \quad \frac{1}{\sigma_3^2} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1.0$$

### Step 3: Sum the precision terms.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{1}{\sigma_i^2} = 4.0 + 11.111 + 1.0 = 16.111$$

### Step 4: Compute the fused variance.

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \frac{1}{16.111} \approx 0.0621 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \sigma_{fused} \approx 0.249 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 5: Compute the weighted sum of measurements.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{x_i}{\sigma_i^2} = \frac{24.1}{0.25} + \frac{24.6}{0.09} + \frac{23.9}{1.0} = 96.4 + 273.333 + 23.9 = 393.633$$

### Step 6: Compute the fused estimate.

$$\hat{x}_{fused} = 0.0621 \times 393.633 \approx 24.44 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 7: Interpret the result.

The fused estimate (24.44 °C) is *closer to Sensor 2* (24.6 °C) because it has the *smallest uncertainty* (highest precision). Notice $\sigma_{fused} = 0.249$ °C, which is **less than the smallest individual $\sigma_i$** — a key advantage of fusion: *the combined estimate is more accurate than any single sensor*.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — One-Dimensional Kalman Filter Predict–Update Cycle

**Setup:** A mobile robot estimates its position $x$ moving with constant velocity $v$ (no control input for simplicity). Measurement update with position sensor $z_k = x_k + w_k$, where $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

We define:
- $A = 1$, $H = 1$, $Q = 0.1$ (process noise variance), $R = 0.5$ (measurement noise variance)
- Initial: $\hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = 0$, $P_{0\vert 0} = 1$
- First measurement: $z_1 = 2.0$

### Step 1: A-priori state prediction.

$$\hat{x}_{1\vert 0} = A \hat{x}_{0\vert 0} = 1 \times 0 = 0$$

**Logic:** Without any new information, the predicted state remains at the previous estimate.

### Step 2: A-priori covariance prediction.

$$P_{1\vert 0} = A P_{0\vert 0} A^T + Q = 1 \times 1 \times 1 + 0.1 = 1.1$$

**Logic:** Uncertainty grows due to the unmodelled process noise $Q$.

### Step 3: Compute the Kalman gain.

$$K_1 = P_{1\vert 0} H^T (H P_{1\vert 0} H^T + R)^{-1} = 1.1 \times 1 \times (1.1 \times 1 + 0.5)^{-1} = \frac{1.1}{1.6} = 0.6875$$

**Logic:** $K_1 \in [0,1]$ — it represents the optimal blending weight between prediction and measurement.

### Step 4: A-posteriori state update.

$$\hat{x}_{1\vert 1} = \hat{x}_{1\vert 0} + K_1 (z_1 - H \hat{x}_{1\vert 0}) = 0 + 0.6875 \times (2.0 - 0) = 1.375$$

**Logic:** The estimate is pulled **68.75%** of the way from 0 toward the measurement 2.0.

### Step 5: A-posteriori covariance update.

$$P_{1\vert 1} = (I - K_1 H) P_{1\vert 0} = (1 - 0.6875) \times 1.1 = 0.34375$$

**Logic:** After incorporating the measurement, the uncertainty dropped from 1.1 to 0.344 — a clear sign the filter is converging.

### Step 6: Generalize to $k$-th iteration.

For iteration $k$, the complete cycle is:

$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1}$$

$$P_{k\vert k-1} = A P_{k-1\vert k-1} A^T + Q$$

$$K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k\vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$$

$$P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k\vert k-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU 2024?**
> The Kalman filter is the most common sensor-fusion algorithm in robotics. KTU examiners expect students to derive **at least two** of the above five equations from first principles and apply them to a numerical problem in a 14-mark question.

---

| Application | Recommended Sensor | Type | Active/Passive | Range | Update Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Outdoor localization | GNSS (GPS) | Exteroceptive | Passive (receivers only) | Global | 1–10 Hz |
| Obstacle detection (slow) | Ultrasonic | Exteroceptive | Active | 0.1–10 m | 20–40 Hz |
| 2D/3D mapping | LiDAR | Exteroceptive | Active | 0.1–200 m | 10–20 Hz |
| IMU (orientation) | MEMS IMU | Proprioceptive | Passive | $\pm 2000$ °/s | 1 kHz |
| Wheel odometry | Optical encoder | Proprioceptive | Active (LED source) | — | 10 kHz |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
**Define a sensor network. List any four characteristics used to evaluate the performance of a sensor node in a field robot.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sensor network** is a system of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively sense, process, and communicate data about physical phenomena to a central sink or among themselves. In field and service robotics, it forms the perception layer enabling environment understanding and feedback control.

**Four key performance characteristics:**

1. **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true physical quantity.
2. **Precision / Repeatability:** Consistency of repeated measurements under identical conditions.
3. **Resolution:** The smallest detectable change in the measured quantity (governed by $n$-bit ADC: $\Delta = (V_{max}-V_{min})/2^n$).
4. **Latency / Bandwidth:** Time delay from event to processed output; data throughput of the communication channel.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often credit answers mentioning **dynamic range, linearity, and power consumption** as well.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each as used in mobile robots.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Proprioceptive Sensors | Exteroceptive Sensors |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Measured Quantity** | Internal state of the robot | External environment |
| **Purpose** | Self-monitoring and control feedback | Perception of surroundings |
| **Example 1** | Wheel encoder (measures joint rotation) | Camera (measures ambient light) |
| **Example 2** | IMU (measures orientation & acceleration) | LiDAR (measures range to obstacles) |
| **Example 3** | Battery voltage sensor | Ultrasonic distance sensor |
| **Failure Effect** | Inability to self-localize internally | Inability to perceive obstacles / path |

**Examples for a mobile robot:**
- Proprioceptive: Optical wheel encoder, MEMS gyroscope.
- Exteroceptive: Stereo camera, scanning LiDAR.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B in full.

---

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a robotic sensor network with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the **Sense–Plan–Act (SPA)** paradigm and how the sensor network interfaces with each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of a robotic sensor network consists of **five hierarchical layers**:

1. **Sensing Layer:** Comprises heterogeneous sensors — proprioceptive (IMU, encoders) and exteroceptive (LiDAR, camera, ultrasonic). Each sensor converts physical phenomena into electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC Layer:** Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the analog signals using $n$-bit ADCs with resolution $\Delta = (V_{max}-V_{min})/2^n$.
3. **Processing Layer (Edge MCU):** Performs local filtering (Kalman, moving average) and feature extraction to reduce data volume before transmission.
4. **Communication Layer:** Transmits data via CAN, Ethernet, SPI, I2C, or wireless protocols to the central sink.
5. **Decision & Actuation Layer:** Fuses data, estimates the robot state, plans actions, and dispatches commands to actuators.

**Mapping to the SPA Paradigm:**

- **Sense ↔ Sensing Layer + ADC:** Raw physical measurements are acquired and digitized.
- **Plan ↔ Processing + Fusion Layer:** The processor interprets fused sensor data, builds a world model, and decides the next action.
- **Act ↔ Decision & Actuation Layer:** Commands are issued to motors/actuators, whose performance is monitored by proprioceptive sensors, thus **closing the loop**.

The architecture forms a **closed-loop feedback system**, where sensor data continuously informs actuation decisions, ensuring stability, accuracy, and adaptability in dynamic environments such as field navigation and human–robot interaction.

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **five topologies** (star, bus, ring, mesh, tree) used in sensor networks. Compare them on the basis of **fault tolerance, complexity, and latency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Topology | Description | Fault Tolerance | Complexity | Latency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central hub. | **Low** (hub failure = total loss) | **Low** (simple wiring) | **Lowest** (one hop) |
| **Bus** | All nodes share a single backbone cable. | **Low** (cable break = total loss) | **Lowest** | **Medium** (contention-based) |
| **Ring** | Each node connects to two neighbors; data circulates as a token. | **Medium** (single break = loss, dual ring = high) | **Medium** | **Medium** (token traversal time) |
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to multiple peers; multi-hop routing. | **Very High** (self-healing) | **High** (many links) | **Variable** (routing-dependent) |
| **Tree** | Hierarchical parent–child structure. | **Medium** (subtree isolation on parent failure) | **Medium** | **Variable** (level-dependent) |

**Diagram of Topologies (textual):**

```
   STAR          BUS          RING         MESH         TREE
    H            ----         N---N        N---N         Root
   /|\           |   |        |   |       /|\ / \        |
  N N N          N   N        N---N      N N N N       / \
                                                  N  N
```

**Application in Robotics:**
- **Star:** Servo control in small robots.
- **Bus:** CAN bus in mobile robots and drones.
- **Ring:** Industrial EtherCAT for robotic arms.
- **Mesh:** Wireless sensor networks in swarm robotics.
- **Tree:** Hierarchical control in humanoid robots.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **five core equations of the one-dimensional Kalman filter** (predict and update steps) for a robotic state estimation problem. Clearly define all variables. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Kalman filter provides the optimal recursive estimate of a linear dynamic system's state, given noisy measurements. We consider a discrete linear system:

$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}, \quad w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

$$z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Step 1 — State Prediction (a-priori):**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} = A \hat{x}_{k-1 \vert k-1} + B u_{k-1}$$

**Step 2 — Covariance Prediction (a-priori):**

$$P_{k \vert k-1} = A P_{k-1 \vert k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Step 3 — Kalman Gain (Optimal Blending Factor):**

$$K_k = P_{k \vert k-1} H^T (H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — State Update (a-posteriori):**

$$\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1})$$

**Step 5 — Covariance Update (a-posteriori):**

$$P_{k \vert k} = (I - K_k H) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

**Variable Definitions:**
- $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ = predicted state estimate before seeing measurement at time $k$.
- $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$ = updated state estimate after incorporating measurement $z_k$.
- $P_{k \vert k-1}, P_{k \vert k}$ = predicted and updated error covariance matrices.
- $A$ = state transition matrix; $B$ = control input matrix; $H$ = measurement matrix.
- $Q$ = process noise covariance; $R$ = measurement noise covariance.
- $K_k$ = Kalman gain — minimizes $P_{k \vert k}$.
- $z_k$ = actual measurement; $v_k$ = measurement noise.

**Engineering Significance:** The Kalman filter is widely used in **GNSS-IMU fusion (robot localization), SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), and visual odometry**, making it indispensable for KTU-level robotics questions.

---

**(b)** A mobile robot uses **two distance sensors** to measure the same obstacle's range:
- Sensor A: $x_A = 5.20$ m, $\sigma_A = 0.4$ m
- Sensor B: $x_B = 4.85$ m, $\sigma_B = 0.2$ m

Apply **inverse-variance weighted fusion** to obtain the fused estimate and its standard deviation. Comment on the improvement in accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $x_A = 5.20$ m, $\sigma_A = 0.4$ m; $x_B = 4.85$ m, $\sigma_B = 0.2$ m.

**Step 1 — Write the fusion formulas:**

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sigma_A^2} + \frac{1}{\sigma_B^2}\right)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_{fused} = \sigma_{fused}^2 \left(\frac{x_A}{\sigma_A^2} + \frac{x_B}{\sigma_B^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual precisions:**

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_A^2} = \frac{1}{0.16} = 6.25$$

$$\frac{1}{\sigma_B^2} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute fused variance:**

$$\sigma_{fused}^2 = \frac{1}{6.25 + 25.0} = \frac{1}{31.25} = 0.032 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\sigma_{fused} = \sqrt{0.032} \approx 0.179 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute weighted sum:**

$$\frac{x_A}{\sigma_A^2} = \frac{5.20}{0.16} = 32.5 \quad ; \quad \frac{x_B}{\sigma_B^2} = \frac{4.85}{0.04} = 121.25$$

**Step 5 — Compute fused estimate:**

$$\hat{x}_{fused} = 0.032 \times (32.5 + 121.25) = 0.032 \times 153.75 = 4.92 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on accuracy improvement:**

- Individual standard deviations: $\sigma_A = 0.4$ m, $\sigma_B = 0.2$ m.
- Fused standard deviation: $\sigma_{fused} \approx 0.179$ m.
- The fused estimate is **closer to Sensor B** (4.85 m) because Sensor B has higher precision (smaller $\sigma$).
- **Key insight:** $\sigma_{fused} < \sigma_B$, demonstrating that the fused estimate is **more accurate than either individual sensor** — a fundamental motivation for sensor fusion in robotics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}$ with $\hat{x}_{k \vert k}$:** The a-priori and a-posteriori estimates are *different*; mixing them costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to transpose matrices correctly** in the Kalman covariance equation $A P A^T$ — the order matters; marks lost for transpose errors.
> 3. **Not mentioning units** (Hz, m, bps) in numeric answers — at least **1 mark** is typically deducted.
> 4. **Skipping the Nyquist criterion** when discussing sampling rates — this is a **favourite examiner's trick question**.
> 5. **In Part B sub-parts**, students often write generic answers without explicit *derivations* or *numerical substitution* — KTU 2024 rubrics require **step-by-step working** for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Sensor Network Definition:** Distributed, autonomous, communicating sensor nodes → robot perception.
- [x] **Two-Axis Classification:** Proprioceptive vs Exteroceptive; Active vs Passive.
- [x] **SPA Paradigm:** Sense → Plan → Act, with proprioceptive feedback closing the loop.
- [x] **Key Performance Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Resolution, Dynamic Range, Latency, Bandwidth.
- [x] **Must-Know Formulas:**
  - $\Delta = (V_{max}-V_{min})/2^n$
  - $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
  - $D = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$
  - $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$
  - $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_{max}/A_{min})$
- [x] **Kalman Filter:** Predict (state + covariance) → Gain → Update (state + covariance). Know all 5 equations.
- [x] **Inverse-Variance Fusion:** $\sigma_{fused}^2 = (1/\sigma_A^2 + 1/\sigma_B^2)^{-1}$ — always gives higher accuracy than any single sensor.
- [x] **Communication Protocols:** CAN (1 Mbps, robust) — I2C (low-speed) — SPI (high-speed) — EtherCAT (industrial real-time) — Ethernet/TSN (Gbps).
- [x] **Topologies:** Star (low latency) — Bus (simple) — Ring (deterministic) — Mesh (fault tolerant) — Tree (hierarchical).
- [x] **Wireless Considerations:** Power budget, duty cycling, time sync (NTP/PTP), MAC protocols (CSMA/TDMA).
- [x] **Real-World Examples:** AGVs (warehouse), drones (GNSS+IMU), surgical robots (force-tactile), agricultural robots (soil sensors).
- [x] **Physical Constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $v_{sound} \approx 343$ m/s @ 20 °C.
- [x] **Standard ADC Resolutions:** 8, 10, 12, 16, 24 bits.
- [x] **Examiner Traps:** Forgetting Nyquist; mixing a-priori/a-posteriori; missing units; omitting diagrams in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Early Environmental Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Early Environmental Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)
