**Early Environmental Robotics** refers to the foundational generation (spanning roughly **1960s–1990s**) of mobile, semi-autonomous, and teleoperated robotic systems engineered to operate in unstructured, outdoor, hazardous, or ecologically sensitive environments. Unlike traditional industrial robots confined to controlled factory cells, these early systems pioneered the integration of **perception**, **navigation**, and **manipulation** in environments characterized by uncertainty, dynamic obstacles, and human absence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Field and Service Robotics, in its embryonic phase, was driven by three imperatives:
> 1. **Hazard Mitigation** – replacing humans in dangerous settings (nuclear, deep-sea, space).
> 2. **Sensory Extension** – gathering data from environments inaccessible to humans.
> 3. **Ecological Stewardship** – monitoring and remediating natural ecosystems.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — A "Pioneer Explorer"

Imagine the difference between a **factory worker** and a **mountain explorer**:
- The factory worker operates in a perfectly lit, temperature-controlled room with predictable tools — this is an **industrial robot**.
- The mountain explorer must make decisions on the fly, deal with unpredictable terrain, harsh weather, and limited visibility — this is an **early environmental robot**.

Just as early polar explorers paved the way for modern Antarctic research stations, early environmental robots laid the groundwork for today's autonomous drones, self-driving cars, and planetary rovers.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Early Environmental Robotics

| Pillar | Description | Representative Early System |
|--------|-------------|----------------------------|
| **Teleoperation** | Human-in-the-loop control for remote manipulation | **ROV** (Remotely Operated Vehicle), **Mobot** (1979) |
| **Semi-Autonomy** | Pre-programmed missions with reactive behaviors | **Shakey the Robot** (Stanford, 1966) |
| **Sensor Fusion Pioneering** | Combining multiple primitive sensors (sonar, cameras, IR) | **Stanford Cart** (1971) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Industrial robots prioritize **repeatability** (accuracy ~**0.01 mm**), while early environmental robots prioritized **robustness** (survival in unstructured terrain) over precision.

## 1.4 Historical Boundary Definition

The **temporal boundary** of "Early" Environmental Robotics is typically defined as the period from the **mid-1960s** (Shakey) to the **late 1990s** (Mars Pathfinder/Sojourner, 1997). This era predates modern SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithms and relied heavily on:
- **Behaviors-based control** (Brooks' Subsumption Architecture, 1986)
- **Rule-based AI** (STRIPS planner in Shakey)
- **Geometric path planning** (Visibility Graphs, Voronoi Diagrams)
- **Acoustic and optical primitive sensors**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evolutionary Trajectory — Step-by-Step Logic

The development of early environmental robotics can be decomposed into **four sequential waves**, each driven by a specific societal or scientific trigger:

### Wave 1 — Academic Genesis (1960s)
- **Trigger:** Cold War-era AI optimism and ARPA funding.
- **Core Logic:** Prove that a machine could *reason* about its environment.
- **Flagship Project:** **Shakey the Robot** (SRI International).
- **Key Innovation:** The **A* search algorithm** was first formalized within Shakey's navigation stack.

### Wave 2 — Hazardous Response (1970s–1980s)
- **Trigger:** **Three Mile Island nuclear accident (1979)** and the rise of offshore oil drilling.
- **Core Logic:** Replace humans in environments with lethal radiation, crushing pressure, or toxic atmospheres.
- **Flagship Projects:** **Mobot** (Caltech/JPL, used at TMI), **Scorpion** (UKAEA), **ROVs (Pioneer-class)**.

### Wave 3 — Ecological Sensing (1980s–1990s)
- **Trigger:** Growing environmental awareness (Chernobyl, 1986; Exxon Valdez, 1989).
- **Core Logic:** Deploy mobile platforms for *measurement* rather than *manipulation*.
- **Flagship Projects:** **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** for agriculture (1990s), **Subsumption-based** robots (MIT AI Lab).

### Wave 4 — Planetary Exploration (1990s)
- **Trigger:** Cheaper computing and renewed space race.
- **Core Logic:** Long-duration autonomous operation in extreme environments.
- **Flagship Project:** **Sojourner Rover** (Mars Pathfinder, July 4, 1997).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Wave

> **Why Wave 1?** Industrial robots were geometric, blind machines. Researchers asked: *Can a robot perceive and plan?*
>
> **How?** Shakey used a TV camera, rangefinder, and bump sensors connected to a **PDP-10 mainframe** via radio link — a 2 GHz of remote computing by today's standards.

> **Why Wave 2?** Industrial accidents revealed human limits — humans cannot enter a **10,000 rad/hour** radiation field.
>
> **How?** Telepresence: human operator "feels" the environment through force feedback joysticks and stereo vision, while the robot absorbs the hazard dose.

> **Why Wave 3?** Environmental disasters demanded *data*, not just cleanup.
>
> **How?** Networks of low-cost sensors with reactive behavioral layers (avoid obstacle → seek goal → explore).

> **Why Wave 4?** Communication delay to Mars (4–24 minutes) made real-time teleoperation impossible.
>
> **How?** Onboard autonomy using waypoint navigation and visual odometry.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead Reckoning Position Update** | $P_{t+1} = P_t + v \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos(\theta_t)$ | Tracks cumulative position error in early odometry |
| **Heading Update** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega \cdot \Delta t$ | Encodes gyroscope-based orientation |
| **Sonar Time-of-Flight** | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$ (speed of sound) |
| **Wheel Encoder Resolution** | $\Delta s = \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{N}$ | $D$ = wheel diameter, $N$ = ticks per revolution |
| **Turning Radius (Differential Drive)** | $R = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{v_R + v_L}{v_R - v_L}$ | $L$ = wheelbase |
| **A* Cost Function** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Heuristic path planning pioneered by Shakey |
| **Subsumption Layer Priority** | $\text{Output}_i = \min(\text{Layer}_i, \text{Output}_{i-1})$ | Brooks' behavior arbitration |
| **Radiation Dose Limit (Human)** | $D_{max} = 5 \text{ rem/year}$ | Justifies robotic intervention at nuclear sites |
| **Mars-Earth Communication Delay** | $t_{delay} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{Mars}}{c_{light}}$ | $c_{light} = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $d_{Mars} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}$ |
| **Field Robot Reliability Metric** | $MTBF = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Mission-critical design parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **odometric resolution** with **positional accuracy**. Resolution is the *smallest measurable* displacement; accuracy is the *deviation from ground truth*. Early environmental robots had resolution of **~1 cm** but accuracy degraded to **>10% of distance traveled** due to wheel slip.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Early environmental robotics birthed the **field robotics industry**, today valued at **>USD 25 billion** globally. Applications span:
- **Nuclear Decommissioning** (Sellafield, Fukushima Daiichi)
- **Deep-Sea Exploration** (Titanic wreck mapping, hydrothermal vent discovery)
- **Planetary Science** (Mars geology, lunar resource prospecting)
- **Precision Agriculture** (autonomous tractors, weed-detecting rovers)
- **Disaster Response** (search-and-rescue at Ground Zero, 9/11)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dead Reckoning Error Accumulation in Early Environmental Robots

One of the defining technical challenges of early environmental robots was **odometric drift**. Below is the rigorous step-by-step derivation.

### Given:
- Wheel diameter $D = 0.30$ m
- Encoder resolution $N = 500$ ticks/rev
- Sampling period $\Delta t = 0.1$ s
- Wheel slip coefficient $\alpha = 0.02$ (2% slip per meter)

### Step 1 — Compute Encoder Resolution

The smallest measurable linear displacement is:

$$\Delta s = \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta s = \pi \cdot 0.30 \cdot \frac{1}{500}$$

$$\Delta s = 0.001885 \text{ m/tick}$$

### Step 2 — Velocity Reconstruction

If the robot records $k$ ticks in interval $\Delta t$:

$$v = \frac{k \cdot \Delta s}{\Delta t}$$

$$v = \frac{k \cdot 0.001885}{0.1} = 0.01885 \cdot k \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — Position Update with Slip Correction

Real position accounting for slip:

$$P_{true}(t) = P_{true}(0) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1 - \alpha) \cdot v_i \cdot \Delta t$$

Estimated position (no slip correction):

$$\hat{P}(t) = \hat{P}(0) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot \Delta t$$

### Step 4 — Cumulative Error

$$\epsilon(t) = \hat{P}(t) - P_{true}(t) = \alpha \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot \Delta t = \alpha \cdot D_{traveled}$$

For a robot traveling $D_{traveled} = 100$ m:

$$\epsilon = 0.02 \cdot 100 = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

This is exactly why early environmental robots like **Shakey** relied on **environmental landmarks** for periodic position correction — a concept now formalized as **SLAM**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Early vs. Modern Environmental Robots

| Parameter | **Early Environmental Robot** (1970s–1990s) | **Modern Field Robot** (2020s) |
|---|---|---|
| **Onboard Compute** | Remote mainframe link / 8-bit μC | Multi-core ARM SoC, GPU accelerators |
| **Processing Power** | $\sim 0.001$ MFLOPS | $\sim 10,000$ MFLOPS |
| **Sensors** | Sonar, IR, bump switches, monochrome CCD | LiDAR, stereo RGB-D, IMU, GNSS-RTK |
| **Positioning Method** | Dead reckoning + landmark beacons | Visual-Inertial SLAM, RTK-GPS |
| **Power Source** | Tethered cable / lead-acid battery | Li-Po, solar-hybrid, fuel cells |
| **Typical Autonomy Duration** | Minutes to ~1 hour | 8–24+ hours |
| **Localization Accuracy** | $\sim 5$–$10\%$ of distance | $\sim 0.01\%$ of distance (cm-level) |
| **Operating Cost (1990 USD)** | $\sim 1$ million (Shakey-class) | $\sim 1,000$–$50,000$ (consumer drones) |
| **Typical Tasks** | Inspection, sample collection | Mapping, intervention, human collaboration |

## 3.3 Derivation: Communication Delay to Mars

For a planetary rover, the one-way light-time to Mars is:

$$t_{delay} = \frac{d_{Mars}}{c_{light}}$$

With $d_{Mars} = 5.5 \times 10^{10}$ m and $c_{light} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$t_{delay} = \frac{5.5 \times 10^{10}}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 183 \text{ s}$$

Round-trip delay:

$$t_{RT} = 2 \cdot t_{delay} \approx 366 \text{ s} \approx 6.1 \text{ min}$$

This is the **physical impossibility** that forced **autonomy** on planetary rovers — the mathematical foundation of why Wave 4 environmental robots had to be self-sufficient.

## 3.4 The Behavioral Arbitration Theorem (Brooks, 1986)

Brooks' Subsumption Architecture replaced centralized planning with layered reactive behaviors. Mathematically:

$$\text{Output} = \min(\text{Layer}_0, \min(\text{Layer}_1, \min(\text{Layer}_2, \ldots)))$$

Or, with proper inhibition signals $I_i \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$\text{Output} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} I_i \cdot \text{Layer}_i$$

This formulation allowed Genghis (MIT, 1989), a six-legged walking robot, to traverse uneven terrain with **zero explicit planning** — a paradigm shift from Shakey's deliberative model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked about *why* early environmental robots often used reactive rather than deliberative control, emphasize **computational frugality** — onboard processors in the 1980s could not run A* in real-time on dense maps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four Waves of Early Environmental Robotics — Timeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wave 1 1960s Academic Genesis] --> B[Wave 2 1970s 80s Hazardous Response]
    B --> C[Wave 3 1980s 90s Ecological Sensing]
    C --> D[Wave 4 1990s Planetary Exploration]
    A --> A1[Shakey 1966]
    A --> A2[Stanford Cart 1971]
    B --> B1[Mobot 1979]
    B --> B2[Three Mile Island Robots]
    B --> B3[Pioneer ROV]
    C --> C1[Subsumption Architecture 1986]
    C --> C2[Genghis 1989]
    D --> D1[Sojourner 1997]
    style A fill:#e6f3ff
    style B fill:#fff0e6
    style C fill:#e6ffe6
    style D fill:#ffe6f3
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic Early Environmental Robot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PerceptionLayer["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        sens1[Sonic Sensor Array]
        sens2[IR Proximity Sensors]
        sens3[Monochrome CCD Camera]
        sens4[Bump Whiskers]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer["CONTROL LAYER"]
        ctrl1[Behavior Arbiter]
        ctrl2[Reactive Controller]
        ctrl3[World Model Memory]
    end
    subgraph ActionLayer["ACTION LAYER"]
        act1[Drive Motor Left]
        act2[Drive Motor Right]
        act3[Manipulator Arm]
        act4[Radio Link to Operator]
    end
    sens1 --> ctrl1
    sens2 --> ctrl1
    sens3 --> ctrl3
    sens4 --> ctrl1
    ctrl3 --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 --> ctrl2
    ctrl2 --> act1
    ctrl2 --> act2
    ctrl2 --> act3
    ctrl3 --> act4
    style PerceptionLayer fill:#f0f8ff
    style ControlLayer fill:#fff5e6
    style ActionLayer fill:#f0fff0
```

## 4.3 Comparative Evolution of Robot Autonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Pure Teleoperation 1960s] --> L2[Teleop with Safeguards 1970s]
    L2 --> L3[Waypoint Navigation 1980s]
    L3 --> L4[Reactive Behaviors 1986]
    L4 --> L5[Hybrid Deliberative Reactive 1990s]
    L5 --> L6[Full Autonomy Modern]
    style L1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style L2 fill:#ffe6cc
    style L3 fill:#ffffcc
    style L4 fill:#e6ffcc
    style L5 fill:#ccffdd
    style L6 fill:#cce6ff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define "Early Environmental Robotics" and distinguish it from traditional industrial robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Early Environmental Robotics refers to the foundational generation (1960s–1990s) of mobile, semi-autonomous, and teleoperated robotic systems designed to operate in **unstructured, hazardous, or ecologically sensitive environments** (1 mark). It differs from industrial robotics in three fundamental aspects: **(i) Operating Environment** — field robots work in outdoor, unpredictable terrains versus controlled factory cells (1 mark); **(ii) Design Priority** — robustness and survival are prioritized over millimeter-level repeatability (0.5 marks); **(iii) Autonomy Level** — early environmental robots relied heavily on teleoperation and reactive behaviors due to limited onboard computing (0.5 marks).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: List any three landmark early environmental robots and their primary applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Shakey the Robot (Stanford, 1966)** — First mobile robot to apply AI-based reasoning for navigation in cluttered indoor environments (1 mark).
2. **Mobot (Caltech/JPL, 1979)** — Deployed at the Three Mile Island nuclear accident site for radiation survey and debris clearance (1 mark).
3. **Sojourner Rover (NASA, 1997)** — First wheeled robotic vehicle on Mars, performing autonomous geological analysis (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the four evolutionary waves of early environmental robotics with representative examples for each wave. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the technical challenges that prevented early environmental robots from achieving full autonomy, with specific reference to computing limitations and sensor technology. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

The development of early environmental robotics unfolded across **four distinct waves**, each catalyzed by a unique socio-technical driver.

**Wave 1 — Academic Genesis (1960s):** Triggered by ARPA funding and AI optimism, this wave sought to prove that machines could perceive and reason. The flagship was **Shakey the Robot** (SRI, 1966), which used a TV camera, rangefinder, and bump sensors to navigate cluttered rooms. Shakey introduced the **A* search algorithm** for path planning. *(2 marks)*

**Wave 2 — Hazardous Response (1970s–1980s):** Triggered by the **Three Mile Island nuclear accident (1979)**, this wave prioritized teleoperated systems to replace humans in lethal environments. Key examples include the **Mobot** (Caltech/JPL) for radiation surveying and **Pioneer-class ROVs** for deep-sea oil rig inspection. *(2 marks)*

**Wave 3 — Ecological Sensing (1980s–1990s):** Triggered by environmental disasters such as **Chernobyl (1986)** and the **Exxon Valdez oil spill (1989)**, this wave emphasized data acquisition. Brooks' **Subsumption Architecture (1986)** enabled behavior-based robots like **Genghis (MIT, 1989)** to traverse rough terrain reactively. *(1.5 marks)*

**Wave 4 — Planetary Exploration (1990s):** Triggered by cheaper computing and renewed space exploration, this wave produced **Sojourner Rover** (Mars Pathfinder, 1997), which operated with up to **~6 minutes** communication delay, necessitating onboard autonomy through waypoint navigation. *(1.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

Full autonomy requires solving three coupled problems: **(i) Perception**, **(ii) Localization**, and **(iii) Planning**. Early environmental robots faced severe constraints in each.

**Computing Limitations:** Early onboard processors operated at **~0.001 MFLOPS** compared to modern **~10,000 MFLOPS**. The **Stanford Cart (1971)** required a remote mainframe connection to process each frame — a single navigation cycle took **~15 minutes**. The **A* algorithm**, while theoretically elegant, was computationally intractable for dense real-world maps in real-time. *(2.5 marks)*

**Sensor Limitations:** Early sensors were primitive and noisy. **Sonar arrays** suffered from specular reflection and crosstalk, providing poor angular resolution (~15°). **Monochrome CCD cameras** had low dynamic range and required controlled lighting. There was no reliable **solid-state LiDAR** until the mid-2000s. Sensor fusion algorithms like Kalman filtering were theoretically known but computationally prohibitive. *(2.5 marks)*

**Power and Reliability:** Early **lead-acid batteries** limited missions to **~1 hour**, and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) was often **<10 hours** in field conditions. Without robust fault tolerance, full autonomy was unsafe. *(2 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a generic early environmental robot. Explain the role of each block in enabling operation in unstructured environments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the cumulative odometric error for a field robot with the following specifications, and explain why landmark-based correction was essential in early systems. **(7 marks)**
- Wheel diameter $D = 0.25$ m
- Encoder resolution $N = 360$ ticks/rev
- Slip coefficient $\alpha = 0.015$
- Total travel distance = $200$ m

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:

A generic early environmental robot comprises **three functional layers** — Perception, Control, and Action — each tightly coupled to enable operation in unstructured environments.

**Perception Layer:** Includes a **sonar array** for obstacle detection (range 0.2–5 m), **IR proximity sensors** for short-range (<0.5 m) blind-spot coverage, a **monochrome CCD camera** for landmark recognition, and **bump whiskers** for last-resort collision detection. *(2 marks)*

**Control Layer:** Houses the **behavior arbiter** that implements subsumption-style priority logic, a **reactive controller** that maps sensor input directly to motor commands (no world model required), and **world model memory** (typically <64 KB RAM) for short-term state tracking. *(2.5 marks)*

**Action Layer:** Contains the **left and right drive motors** (differential drive configuration), an optional **manipulator arm** for sample collection, and a **radio link to the human operator** for supervisory control and emergency override. *(2.5 marks)*

The architecture's strength lies in its **graceful degradation** — even if the camera fails, sonar-based reactive behaviors keep the robot operational.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Compute encoder resolution:**

$$\Delta s = \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{1}{N}$$

$$\Delta s = \pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot \frac{1}{360}$$

$$\Delta s = 2.18 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/tick}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2 — Define cumulative error equation:**

For a robot traveling distance $D_{traveled}$ with uniform slip coefficient $\alpha$, the position estimate $\hat{P}$ deviates from true position $P_{true}$ by:

$$\epsilon = \hat{P} - P_{true} = \alpha \cdot D_{traveled}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\epsilon = 0.015 \cdot 200$$

$$\epsilon = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Express as percentage of distance:**

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{\epsilon}{D_{traveled}} \times 100 = 1.5\%$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Explain necessity of landmark correction:** Early environmental robots experienced **unbounded error growth** with distance traveled. After 200 m, a 3 m error is unacceptable for navigation. To bound this error, systems like **Shakey** and the **Stanford Cart** used **environmental landmarks** (distinctive wall corners, painted markers) for **periodic position resets**. This concept evolved into modern **SLAM** algorithms. *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *encoder resolution* with *positioning accuracy* — the former is hardware, the latter is the cumulative error after slip and drift.
> 2. **Do not** state that early environmental robots were "fully autonomous" — almost all relied on either teleoperation or tethered compute. Specify the level of autonomy.
> 3. **Always** cite a specific year and institution when naming a robot (e.g., "Shakey, SRI, 1966"). Vague references like "an early robot" lose marks.
> 4. **Avoid** the misconception that Shakey was the first robot — it was the first *mobile robot with AI reasoning*. Industrial robots predate it.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Early Environmental Robotics = foundational era (1960s–1990s) of mobile/teleoperated robots for hazardous, outdoor, or ecological applications.
- **Four Waves:** Academic Genesis → Hazardous Response → Ecological Sensing → Planetary Exploration.
- **Flagship Robots:** **Shakey** (1966, SRI), **Stanford Cart** (1971), **Mobot** (1979, TMI), **Genghis** (1989, MIT), **Sojourner** (1997, NASA).
- **Core Challenges:** Limited onboard compute, primitive sensors, unbounded odometric drift, unreliable power systems.
- **Paradigm Shift:** From deliberative AI planning (Shakey) → reactive behavior-based control (Brooks' Subsumption, 1986).
- **Mathematical Anchors:** Dead reckoning $\epsilon = \alpha \cdot D_{traveled}$; Mars delay $t_{delay} \approx 183$ s; A* heuristic $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
- **Sensors:** Sonar (343 m/s), IR, monochrome CCD, bump whiskers — *no* solid-state LiDAR.
- **KTU Buzzwords to Memorize:** Telepresence, Subsumption, Waypoint Navigation, Landmark-based Correction, Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF).
- **Real-World Legacy:** Today's **>USD 25 billion** field robotics industry traces its DNA directly to these early systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy-Efficient Sampling Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy-Efficient Sampling Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-Efficient Sampling Platforms (EESPs)** are specialized field robotic systems engineered to acquire physical, chemical, biological, or environmental data from a target region while minimizing the consumption of onboard energy reserves (battery, fuel, or harvested power). They integrate **energy-aware locomotion**, **duty-cycled sensing**, and **adaptive mission planning** to maximize operational endurance, spatial coverage, and data yield per unit of energy.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, this topic falls under **autonomous field robots** used in unstructured environments such as oceans, forests, agricultural fields, disaster zones, and atmospheric layers. The defining metric for such platforms is the **Energy per Sample (EpS)** — the joules expended to acquire, store, and transmit one valid measurement.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Marathon Blood-Test Doctor"**
>
> Imagine a doctor who must visit 50 houses across a city to collect blood samples, but the doctor only has enough stamina to walk 20 km. A *normal* doctor would walk randomly until tired and quit. An **energy-efficient sampling doctor** would:
> 1. Plan the *shortest route* (energy-aware path planning),
> 2. Combine nearby houses into one trip (batched sampling),
> 3. Carry a small, low-power test kit instead of a heavy full lab (sensor optimization),
> 4. Take the elevator instead of stairs (efficient locomotion),
> 5. Sleep in a taxi between distant zones (hibernate / drift mode).
>
> A **sampling robot** does exactly this — it trades raw speed and power for *intelligent, sustained, low-energy data acquisition*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics govern EESP design:

- **Earth's gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater (reference):** $\rho_w \approx 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of air (sea level, 15 °C):** $\rho_a \approx 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Solar constant (AM1.5):** $G_{sc} \approx 1361 \; \text{W/m}^2$
- **Specific Energy of Li-ion battery:** $\approx 240{-}260 \; \text{Wh/kg}$
- **Standard Metrics:**
  - **Endurance (E):** Mission duration in hours/days.
  - **Range (R):** Total distance traveled in km.
  - **Energy per Sample (EpS):** Joules per measurement.
  - **Sampling Rate (f_s):** Hz or samples/hour.
  - **Duty Cycle (D):** Fraction of time the sensor is active.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 emphasizes the *physical design* of the platform (locomotion mechanism, power source, payload) and the *energy model* that quantifies consumption. Exam questions typically test (a) identification of energy losses, (b) selection of locomotion for a given environment, and (c) calculation of endurance from a power budget.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Energy-Efficient Sampling Platforms

EESPs are broadly classified by the **operating medium**, which dictates the dominant energy-loss mechanism:

| Class | Operating Medium | Dominant Energy Sink | Typical Platform |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aerial Sampling Drones** | Air | Aerodynamic drag | Fixed-wing UAVs, solar gliders |
| **Aquatic / Marine Platforms** | Water | Hydrodynamic drag + buoyancy control | AUVs, gliders, wave-riders |
| **Terrestrial Rovers** | Ground | Rolling/friction resistance, slope work | Wheeled, legged, tracked rovers |
| **Stationary / Hybrid** | Mixed | Sensing + communication | Buoys, sensor pods, hitchhiking bots |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Energy Model of a Sampling Robot

The total energy consumed during a sampling mission, $E_{total}$, is the time-integral of the instantaneous power draw $P(t)$:

$$E_{total} = \int_{0}^{T_{mission}} P(t) \, dt$$

For a discrete mission with $N$ distinct operational states (transit, hover, sample, transmit, hibernate), the energy budget becomes a finite sum:

$$E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i \cdot t_i$$

where:
- $P_i$ = average power drawn during state $i$ (Watts),
- $t_i$ = duration spent in state $i$ (seconds).

### 2.2 The Four Functional Power Subsystems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every EESP, regardless of medium, partitions its energy budget into **four functional blocks**. KTU questions frequently ask students to identify the *largest consumer* in a given platform.

1. **Propulsion / Locomotion Power ($P_{prop}$)** — Energy to overcome drag/friction and accelerate the body.
2. **Sensing & Computation Power ($P_{sense}$)** — Energy for active sensors (sonar, lidar, cameras) and onboard CPUs.
3. **Communication Power ($P_{comm}$)** — Energy for radio/acoustic transmission of data.
4. **Auxiliary / Housekeeping Power ($P_{aux}$)** — Energy for thermal regulation, actuators, payload, and idle electronics.

$$P_{total} = P_{prop} + P_{sense} + P_{comm} + P_{aux}$$

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Optimization

The design philosophy of an EESP follows a five-step "**energy funnel**":

- **Step 1 — Match Locomotion to Medium:** Use buoyancy-driven gliders in water (no propulsion cost), or fixed-wing UAVs in air (low drag coefficient), instead of hovering multirotors.
- **Step 2 — Minimize Propulsive Power:** Select $C_d$-optimized hulls, employ low-Reynolds-number foils, and use vortex generators only when needed.
- **Step 3 — Duty-Cycle the Sensors:** Activate sonar/lidar only when needed; sleep during transit between waypoints.
- **Step 4 — Compress and Store Locally:** Aggregate data on board; transmit in burst mode.
- **Step 5 — Harvest Ambient Energy:** Solar panels (aerial), wave energy (marine), wind (ground).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates the high-yield formulas for EESP problems. Boundary conditions and units are explicitly noted.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{total} = \int P(t) \, dt$ | Total mission energy | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $E_{total} = \sum P_i t_i$ | Discrete-state mission energy | J |
| 3 | $P_{total} = P_{prop} + P_{sense} + P_{comm} + P_{aux}$ | Power budget partition | W |
| 4 | $P_{prop,aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a V^3 S C_D$ | Aerodynamic drag power | W |
| 5 | $P_{prop,hydro} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_w V^3 S C_D$ | Hydrodynamic drag power | W |
| 6 | $P_{hover} = \tfrac{\sqrt{(mg)^3}}{2 \rho_a A}$ | Multirotor hover power | W |
| 7 | $P_{roll} = \mu_r \, m g \, V$ | Rolling resistance power | W |
| 8 | $E_p = P \cdot T_{mission}$ | Endurance from constant power | J |
| 9 | $EpS = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{samples}}$ | Energy per sample | J/sample |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{t_{active}}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$ | Duty cycle | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{total}}$ | Platform energy efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_{solar} = \eta_{pv} \cdot G_{sc} \cdot A_{panel}$ | Harvested solar power | W |
| 13 | $T_{endurance} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{P_{total}}$ | Mission duration from battery | seconds (s) |
| 14 | $R_{mission} = V_{cruise} \cdot T_{endurance}$ | Mission range | meters (m) |

> **Note on notation:** In formulas involving absolute magnitudes, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mathrm{mid}$ — e.g., $\vert V \vert^3$ for speed cubed — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy-efficient sampling platforms are central to:

- **Oceanography:** Long-endurance gliders such as the *Slocum* and *Spray* profile the ocean for months, sampling temperature, salinity, and oxygen at depths down to **1000 m** on a single battery pack.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Solar-powered ground rovers and UAVs map soil moisture and crop health over weeks.
- **Disaster Response:** Snake-like and legged search robots explore rubble with low-energy gaits.
- **Atmospheric Science:** High-altitude pseudo-satellites (HAPS) like the *Google Loon* balloon or *BAE PHASA-35* loiter for months on solar power.
- **Pollution Monitoring:** Drifters and slow AUVs trace oil spills and algal blooms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Endurance Equation from a Power Budget

We derive the **mission endurance** $T_{end}$ of a sampling platform given a fixed battery capacity $C_{batt}$ (in watt-hours) and a constant average power draw $\bar{P}$.

**Step 1 — Convert battery capacity to energy:**
$$C_{batt} \;[\text{Wh}] \;\times \; 3600 \;[\text{s/h}] \;=\; E_{batt} \;[\text{J}]$$

**Step 2 — Define average power draw as the sum of subsystem powers:**
$$\bar{P} = P_{prop} + P_{sense} + P_{comm} + P_{aux}$$

**Step 3 — Endurance is energy divided by power:**
$$T_{end} = \frac{E_{batt}}{\bar{P}} = \frac{C_{batt} \times 3600}{\bar{P}}$$

**Step 4 — If solar/wave harvesting contributes $P_{harv}$, the net power is reduced:**
$$\bar{P}_{net} = \bar{P} - P_{harv}$$

and endurance becomes:

$$T_{end} = \frac{C_{batt} \times 3600}{\bar{P} - P_{harv}}$$

**Step 5 — Energy per sample, given $N_{samples}$ acquired during the mission:**
$$EpS = \frac{E_{batt}}{N_{samples}} = \frac{\bar{P} \cdot T_{end}}{N_{samples}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical insight:** Reducing $\bar{P}$ by 1 W on a platform with a **200 Wh** battery extends endurance by **200 hours divided by the average power**. This is why even small reductions in *hover* or *idle sensing* power produce dramatic mission-life gains.

## 3.2 Derivation — Drag Power for an AUV Hull

A streamlined autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) moving at velocity $V$ through seawater of density $\rho_w$ experiences a drag force $F_D$:

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, V^2 \, S \, C_D$$

where $S$ is the frontal (reference) area and $C_D$ is the drag coefficient. The **propulsive power** required to overcome drag is:

$$P_{prop} = F_D \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho_w \, V^3 \, S \, C_D$$

**Numerical Example (worked out):**
Given: $\rho_w = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $V = 1.5 \; \text{m/s}$, $S = 0.08 \; \text{m}^2$, $C_D = 0.05$.

**Step A — Substitute values:**

$$P_{prop} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times (1.5)^3 \times 0.08 \times 0.05$$

**Step B — Evaluate the velocity cubed:**

$$(1.5)^3 = 3.375 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$

**Step C — Multiply sequentially:**

$$P_{prop} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3.375 \times 0.08 \times 0.05$$

**Step D — Intermediate result:**

$$P_{prop} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.0135$$

**Step E — Final value:**

$$P_{prop} \approx 6.92 \; \text{W}$$

This is the propulsive power of a typical small oceanographic AUV.

## 3.3 Derivation — Solar-Powered Endurance Bound

A solar UAV with battery capacity $C_{batt}$ (Wh), solar panel area $A$ (m²), panel efficiency $\eta_{pv}$, and an average solar irradiance $\bar{G}$ (W/m²) over a daylight period $T_{day}$ harvests:

$$E_{harv} = \eta_{pv} \cdot \bar{G} \cdot A \cdot T_{day}$$

Over a 24-hour cycle, the **net daily energy** is:

$$E_{net,24h} = E_{harv} - \bar{P} \cdot (24 \text{ h})$$

**Steady-state condition (eternal flight):**

$$E_{net,24h} \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{P} \leq \frac{\eta_{pv} \cdot \bar{G} \cdot A \cdot T_{day}}{24 \text{ h}}$$

This is the **solar-feasibility bound** — a key design check in KTU problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Locomotion Modes

| Locomotion | Power vs. Speed | Best Suited Medium | Endurance Class | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hover (multirotor) | $P \propto V^0$ (constant for hover) | Air, short missions | Minutes to ~1 hour | Quadcopter survey drone |
| Fixed-wing cruise | $P \propto V^3$ (cubic, drag-limited) | Air, long range | Hours to days | Solar UAV |
| Buoyancy glider | $P_{prop} \approx 0$ (drift + pump) | Ocean, vertical profiling | Weeks to months | Slocum Glider |
| Wheeled rolling | $P \propto V$ (linear, friction) | Ground, flat terrain | Hours | Mars Rover (Perseverance) |
| Tracked | $P \propto V$ but high $\mu$ | Rough terrain, rescue | Short | EOD robot |
| Legged walking | $P$ variable, gait-dependent | Rough/cluttered terrain | Short | Boston Dynamics Spot |
| Wave-rider | Harvests wave energy | Ocean surface | Indefinite (with maintenance) | Wave Glider (Liquid Robotics) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Energy-Efficient Sampling Platform

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic EESP, showing the energy flow between power source, locomotion, sensing, computation, and communication subsystems. It is drawn as a clean data-flow architecture to avoid physical-drawing pitfalls.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Energy Source: Battery or Fuel Cell] --> B[Power Conditioning Unit: DC-DC Converter]
    H[Energy Harvester: Solar or Wave or Wind] --> B
    B --> C[Propulsion Subsystem: Motors, Thrusters, Wheels]
    B --> D[Sensing Subsystem: Sonar, Lidar, Cameras, Chem-Sensors]
    B --> E[Computation Subsystem: Onboard CPU and Mission Planner]
    B --> F[Communication Subsystem: Radio or Acoustic Modem]
    C --> G[Mission Environment: Air, Water, Ground]
    D --> G
    G --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> C
    F --> I[Offboard Base Station or Satellite]
    C --> J[Telemetry and Status Feedback]
    E --> J
    J --> E

    classDef power fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mecha fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sense fill:#c1ffc1,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comm fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#6a5acd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef env fill:#fafad2,stroke:#8b8b00,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,H power
    class C mecha
    class D,E sense
    class F,I comm
    class G,J env
```

## 4.2 Energy-Flow Sankey-Style Block Diagram

The next diagram maps the **power budget partition** and highlights *where energy is consumed and where it can be reclaimed*. It uses a left-to-right energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Battery Pack] --> pcu[Power Management Unit]
    src2[Solar Panel] --> pcu
    src3[Wave Harvester] --> pcu
    pcu --> prop[Propulsion Power Pprop]
    pcu --> sens[Sensing Power Psense]
    pcu --> comp[Computation Power Pcomp]
    pcu --> comm[Communication Power Pcomm]
    pcu --> aux[Auxiliary Power Paux]
    prop --> drag[Drag and Friction Losses]
    sens --> data[Raw Data Stream]
    comp --> plan[Adaptive Mission Planner]
    comm --> uplink[Data Uplink]
    aux --> therm[Thermal Regulation]
    plan --> prop
    plan --> sens
    plan --> comm
    data --> epS[Energy per Sample EpS]
    uplink --> epS

    classDef inport fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outport fill:#e0ffff,stroke:#008b8b,stroke-width:2px

    class src1,src2,src3,pcu inport
    class prop,sens,comp,comm,aux,plan core
    class drag,data,uplink,therm,epS outport
```

## 4.3 Mission State Machine for Energy-Aware Sampling

The following diagram is the **mission-level state machine** that an EESP traverses. It illustrates how a smart platform alternates between high-power active states and low-power sleep states to conserve energy.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BootUp
    BootUp --> SelfCheck
    SelfCheck --> Transit
    SelfCheck --> FaultHalt: sensor or battery fault
    Transit --> ActiveSampling: arrived at waypoint
    Transit --> Hibernate: low battery
    ActiveSampling --> DataCompression
    DataCompression --> DataTransmit
    DataTransmit --> Transit
    DataTransmit --> Hibernate: low battery
    Hibernate --> Transit: battery recharged
    Hibernate --> SolarCharge: solar platform
    SolarCharge --> Transit
    FaultHalt --> [*]
    Transit --> [*]: mission complete
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each transition (arrow) represents a decision made by the onboard mission planner. The `Hibernate` and `SolarCharge` states are the **energy-recovery states** that distinguish an EESP from a conventional robot. In KTU viva questions, students are often asked to *label the highest-power state* and *justify the duty-cycling strategy*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
**Define an energy-efficient sampling platform. List any two mechanisms by which such platforms conserve energy during long-duration missions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **energy-efficient sampling platform (EESP)** is a field robotic system that acquires environmental data while minimizing onboard energy consumption, typically measured as *energy per sample* or *mission endurance*. (1 mark)

Two energy-conservation mechanisms:
1. **Duty-cycled sensing:** Sensors are activated only at waypoints or when an event of interest is detected, rather than continuously. (1 mark)
2. **Energy-aware locomotion:** Use of low-drag profiles, buoyancy gliders, or solar-assisted flight to reduce propulsive power. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP Set B]`
**What is the "energy per sample" (EpS) metric, and why is it preferred over raw endurance when comparing two sampling platforms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**EpS** is the total mission energy divided by the number of valid samples collected, with units of **J/sample** (or Wh/sample). (1 mark)

It is preferred over raw endurance because endurance alone does not account for **data yield** — a platform that flies for 10 hours but collects no useful data has zero scientific value. (1 mark)

EpS combines *both* energy and information productivity into a single normalized figure, enabling fair comparison between a fast, high-power drone and a slow, low-power glider. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern, each Part-B question offers an internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, describe the **four functional power subsystems** of an energy-efficient sampling platform. For each subsystem, state the typical power range and one method to reduce its consumption.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A solar-powered fixed-wing UAV is to be used for atmospheric sampling. Given:
- Battery capacity: $C_{batt} = 300 \; \text{Wh}$,
- Cruise power: $P_{cruise} = 25 \; \text{W}$ (propulsion only),
- Sensing + comm: $P_{sense+comm} = 5 \; \text{W}$ (duty-cycled, average),
- Auxiliary: $P_{aux} = 2 \; \text{W}$,
- Solar panel area: $A = 0.6 \; \text{m}^2$,
- Panel efficiency: $\eta_{pv} = 0.22$,
- Average daylight irradiance: $\bar{G} = 700 \; \text{W/m}^2$ for $T_{day} = 10 \; \text{h}$.

Compute (i) the steady-state average power draw, (ii) the average harvested solar power, (iii) the net daily energy balance, and (iv) whether the platform can achieve eternal flight. State your assumption clearly.

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**The four power subsystems:**

| Subsystem | Typical Power Range | Energy-Reduction Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Propulsion ($P_{prop}$)** | 50–80% of total | Use drag-optimized hull/airframe; cruise at $V_{L/D,max}$ |
| **Sensing ($P_{sense}$)** | 10–20% | Duty-cycle; use event-triggered wake-up |
| **Communication ($P_{comm}$)** | 5–15% | Compress data; transmit in burst mode |
| **Auxiliary ($P_{aux}$)** | 5–10% | Sleep low-power MCUs; disable unused peripherals |

*(Block diagram already covered in Section 4.1 — full marks for neat block diagram with labels.)* (1 mark for diagram + 1 mark per subsystem × 4 subsystems + 2 marks for methods = **7 marks**)

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Total average power draw:**

$$\bar{P} = P_{cruise} + P_{sense+comm} + P_{aux}$$

$$\bar{P} = 25 + 5 + 2 = 32 \; \text{W}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Average harvested solar power:**

$$P_{harv} = \eta_{pv} \cdot \bar{G} \cdot A$$

$$P_{harv} = 0.22 \times 700 \times 0.6 = 92.4 \; \text{W}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Net daily energy balance:**

**Energy consumed per 24 h:**

$$E_{cons} = \bar{P} \times 24 \; \text{h} = 32 \times 24 = 768 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Energy harvested per 24 h (daylight only):**

$$E_{harv} = P_{harv} \times T_{day} = 92.4 \times 10 = 924 \; \text{Wh}$$

**Net daily energy:**

$$E_{net} = E_{harv} - E_{cons} = 924 - 768 = +156 \; \text{Wh}$$ 
*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Feasibility check:**

Since $E_{net} > 0$, the platform harvests more energy than it consumes daily. **Eternal flight is feasible** under the stated average conditions. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Required assumption:**

The platform must operate only during daylight at the given cruise power, and night-time power must be drawn from the 300 Wh battery. (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy per sample calculation (bonus):** If the platform collects 1 sample every 30 seconds during a 10-hour mission, $N_{samples} = 1200$ and $EpS = 32 \; \text{W} \times 10 \; \text{h} / 1200 = 0.267 \; \text{Wh/sample}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP Set B]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **buoyancy-driven glider** principle of underwater sampling platforms. Compare its energy profile with that of a conventional propeller-driven AUV. Why is the glider preferred for long-endurance oceanographic missions?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A small wheeled rover is used to sample soil moisture over a 1 km² agricultural field. The rover has:
- Mass: $m = 25 \; \text{kg}$,
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $\mu_r = 0.05$,
- Cruise speed: $V = 0.5 \; \text{m/s}$,
- Sensing power: $P_{sense} = 4 \; \text{W}$,
- Computation: $P_{comp} = 3 \; \text{W}$,
- Comm: $P_{comm} = 2 \; \text{W}$ (intermittent, averaged),
- Battery: $C_{batt} = 150 \; \text{Wh}$.

Compute (i) the rolling-friction power, (ii) the total average power, (iii) the mission endurance in hours, and (iv) the total distance the rover can cover on a single charge. State any assumptions.

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Buoyancy-glider principle (3 marks):**
A buoyancy glider (e.g., Slocum, Spray) varies its **net buoyancy** by pumping oil between an internal reservoir and an external bladder. When the glider is *positively buoyant*, it rises; when *negatively buoyant*, it sinks. Wings convert this vertical motion into a sawtooth *horizontal* path. The only energy consumed is for the buoyancy pump — **propulsion power is effectively zero**, replaced by hydrodynamic drag on the wings.

**Comparison with propeller-driven AUV (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Buoyancy Glider | Propeller AUV |
|---|---|---|
| Propulsion power | ~0.1–0.5 W (pump only) | 20–200 W (thruster) |
| Speed | 0.2–0.4 m/s | 1.0–2.0 m/s |
| Endurance | Weeks to months | Hours to days |
| Maneuverability | Limited (sawtooth path) | High (6-DOF) |
| Best for | Large-scale, long-duration profiling | Targeted, fast survey |

**Why preferred for long-endurance (1 mark):**
Because the dominant energy sink — propulsion — is *eliminated* by exploiting gravity and buoyancy. Endurance is limited only by battery capacity for the pump and electronics, enabling months-long missions on a single pack.

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Rolling-friction force:**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m \, g = 0.05 \times 25 \times 9.81 = 12.26 \; \text{N}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Rolling-friction power:**

$$P_{roll} = F_{roll} \cdot V = 12.26 \times 0.5 = 6.13 \; \text{W}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Total average power:**

$$\bar{P} = P_{roll} + P_{sense} + P_{comp} + P_{comm}$$

$$\bar{P} = 6.13 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 15.13 \; \text{W}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Mission endurance:**

$$T_{end} = \frac{C_{batt}}{\bar{P}} = \frac{150}{15.13} \approx 9.91 \; \text{h}$$ 
*(2 marks)*

**Step 5 — Total distance on a single charge:**

$$D = V \cdot T_{end} = 0.5 \times 9.91 \approx 4.96 \; \text{km}$$ 
*(1 mark)*

**Assumption:** Flat terrain (no slope work), constant speed, and stated intermittent comms treated as an average. (1 mark included above)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in EpS:** EpS must be expressed per *sample*, not per mission. Students often forget to divide $E_{total}$ by $N_{samples}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert Wh to J:** When mixing SI units, $1 \; \text{Wh} = 3600 \; \text{J}$. KTU examiners specifically check this conversion.
> 3. **Confusing $P_{hover}$ with $P_{cruise}$:** A multirotor's hover power is *independent* of speed; fixed-wing cruise power scales as $V^3$. Mixing them is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Solar-feasibility check sign error:** Net daily energy $E_{net} = E_{harv} - E_{cons}$ must be **positive** for eternal flight; reversing the sign yields the wrong conclusion.
> 5. **Omitting the rolling-resistance formula** $F_{roll} = \mu_r m g$ in ground-rover problems. A surprising number of students use $P = m g V$ directly without $\mu_r$.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** For any 7-mark numerical, KTU valuators award 1 mark for clear assumptions (e.g., flat terrain, no headwind, daytime only). Omitting them costs a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Energy-Efficient Sampling Platforms**
>
> - **Definition:** EESPs are field robots that acquire data while minimizing energy per unit information.
> - **Four power subsystems:** $P_{prop}$, $P_{sense}$, $P_{comm}$, $P_{aux}$ — *propulsion usually dominates*.
> - **Key energy equations:**
>   - $E_{total} = \sum P_i t_i$
>   - $P_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3 S C_D$ (both air and water)
>   - $P_{hover} = \sqrt{(mg)^3 / (2 \rho_a A)}$
>   - $P_{roll} = \mu_r m g V$
>   - $T_{end} = C_{batt} / \bar{P}$
>   - $EpS = E_{total} / N_{samples}$
> - **Cube-vs-linear law:** Aerodynamic/hydrodynamic drag power scales as $V^3$; rolling resistance scales as $V$. Halving speed reduces drag power by **8×**.
> - **Buoyancy glider:** ~0.1–0.5 W propulsion, months of endurance, sawtooth trajectory.
> - **Solar-feasibility bound:** $\bar{P} \leq \eta_{pv} \bar{G} A T_{day} / 24 \; \text{h}$ for eternal flight.
> - **Duty cycling** is the single most effective sensor-energy technique.
> - **Classify** any sampling platform by its operating medium (air, water, ground) to identify the dominant loss.
> - **Always state units and assumptions** in numerical answers.
> - **Metric of merit:** Energy per Sample (EpS) — not raw endurance.
> - **Energy harvesters** in order of typical power density: solar > wave > wind > vibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Habitat Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Natural Habitat Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Natural Habitat Mapping** is the sub-discipline of field robotics concerned with the autonomous acquisition, geo-referencing, and semantic interpretation of spatial data describing ecologically sensitive or unstructured natural environments (forests, wetlands, grasslands, marine ecosystems, cave systems, and wildlife corridors). It integrates **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)**, **remote sensing**, **multi-modal perception**, and **coverage path planning** to construct geometric, topological, and semantic representations of habitats without significantly disturbing the resident flora and fauna.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602, Module 1):** Natural Habitat Mapping is treated as a flagship application of *mobile robotics in unstructured outdoor environments*. The examiner expects students to demonstrate competence in **(a)** choosing appropriate sensor suites, **(b)** formulating the mapping problem as a probabilistic estimation task, and **(c)** justifying design trade-offs between autonomy, stealth, and map fidelity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silent Forest Surveyor"

Imagine sending a team of highly trained, completely silent forest surveyors into a dense rainforest at night. Each surveyor carries a flashlight, a GPS-like device, a notebook, and a camera. They cannot use bright lights (animals flee), cannot make noise, and have patchy satellite reception under the canopy. Each one walks cautiously, repeatedly noting down: *"I am currently at coordinate (x, y, z), I see a tree trunk 2.3 m away, I see a river 15 m to the east, and the soil beneath me is damp loam."* They whisper to nearby teammates: *"From where you are standing, can you confirm that big fallen log I see to my northwest?"* By fusing all these individual observations into a single shared notebook, the team builds a high-fidelity map of the forest — without ever having had a complete picture at any one moment.

This is precisely what a **natural-habitat-mapping robot swarm** does: each agent is a *robotic surveyor* carrying sensors (LiDAR, hyperspectral camera, acoustic array, IMU); the shared notebook is the *probabilistic map*; the whispered confirmations are *inter-robot loop closures*; and the constraint of "stealth" drives design choices such as **silent electric propulsion, passive sensing, and bio-inspired locomotion**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Field Robotics

Natural habitats are the **canonical GPS-denied, unstructured, dynamic, and ecologically fragile** environments that exercise every capability of a field robot. Studying this application forces students to confront all four major challenges simultaneously:

- **Perception under adversity**: foliage occlusion, low-contrast terrain, sun glare, water reflections.
- **Localization without GNSS**: reliance on Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO), LiDAR SLAM, or landmark-based localization.
- **Autonomous navigation over rough terrain**: legged, aerial, or amphibious platforms instead of wheeled bases.
- **Ethical and ecological constraints**: minimum-disturbance operation, energy efficiency, fail-safe behaviors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Metric to Remember:** A modern habitat-mapping UAV (e.g., for canopy surveys) typically generates **>2 GB of raw sensor data per minute of flight**, of which only **2–5 %** is retained as informative map features after filtering. Choosing *what to store* is therefore as important as choosing *what to sense*.

## 1.4 Categories of Natural Habitats Targeted

| Habitat Type | Representative Robotic Platform | Primary Sensing Modality |
|---|---|---|
| Forest canopy | Quadrotor UAV | LiDAR + hyperspectral |
| Forest floor | Quadruped (e.g., ANYmal-class) | RGB-D + thermal |
| Wetlands / marshes | Amphibious USV–UAV pair | Multispectral + sonar |
| Coral reefs & seabed | Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV) | Side-scan sonar + stereo vision |
| Caves & subterranean | Micro aerial vehicle (MAV) | 3-D LiDAR |
| Grasslands / savannah | Legged or wheeled UGV | Visual SLAM + RTK-GNSS |
| Polar / arctic | Heated UAV | Thermal + radar |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Mapping Problem

Habitat mapping is formulated as the problem of estimating the posterior probability distribution over both the **robot trajectory** $x_{1:t}$ and the **map** $m$, given a stream of **odometry** $u_{1:t}$ and **observations** $z_{1:t}$:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This joint posterior is intractable in its raw form. The robotics community factorizes it in two dominant ways, each producing a different algorithmic family that students **must** know for KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Online SLAM (Most Common for Real-Time Field Use)

The current pose is estimated while marginalizing out the past trajectory:

$$p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This is the assumption underlying the **Extended Kalman Filter SLAM (EKF-SLAM)** and modern **Graph-SLAM front-ends**.

### 2.1.2 Full SLAM (Batch Optimization)

The entire trajectory is estimated jointly, which yields higher accuracy but requires post-processing:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;\propto\; p(x_0)\prod_{i=1}^{t} p(x_i \mid x_{i-1}, u_i)\prod_{j=1}^{t} p(z_j \mid x_j, m)$$

This is the foundation of **pose-graph optimization** tools such as **g2o, GTSAM, and Cartographer**.

## 2.2 Occupancy Grid Mapping — The Workhorse for Habitat Floor Maps

For ground and underwater habitats, the environment is discretized into a 2-D or 3-D voxel grid. Each cell $m_i$ holds the probability that it is **occupied**. To avoid numerical saturation in $[0,1]$, the algorithm stores the **log-odds**:

$$l_i \;=\; \log \frac{p(m_i = \text{occupied} \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i = \text{occupied} \mid z_{1:t})}$$

The inverse relation to recover probability is:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}) \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_i)}$$

The **update rule** when a new range measurement $z_t$ arrives is:

$$l_i^{(t)} \;=\; l_i^{(t-1)} \;+\; \underbrace{l_{\text{inv}} - l_0}_{\text{inverse sensor model}}$$

where $l_0$ is the prior log-odds (typically $0$, i.e. $p=0.5$) and $l_{\text{inv}}$ depends on whether the cell is along the ray (free), at the measured range (occupied), or beyond the range (unknown).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why log-odds?** Probabilistic updates become a *single floating-point addition* per cell. This is why occupancy grids run in real time on embedded habitat-mapping payloads.

## 2.3 EKF-SLAM Core Equations

For habitats where explicit landmark detection is feasible (tree trunks, large rocks, distinctive coral bommies), EKF-SLAM remains a classic exam topic.

**State vector** (pose + landmark positions):

$$X_t \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x_t & y_t & \theta_t & L_1 & L_2 & \dots & L_n \end{bmatrix}^{\top}$$

**Prediction step** using odometry $u_t$:

$$\hat{X}_{t \mid t-1} = f(X_{t-1}, u_t), \qquad P_{t \mid t-1} = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^{\top} + Q_t$$

**Update step** using observation $z_t$ of landmark $j$:

$$K_t \;=\; P_{t \mid t-1} H_j^{\top}\bigl(H_j P_{t \mid t-1} H_j^{\top} + R_t\bigr)^{-1}$$

$$X_t \;=\; \hat{X}_{t \mid t-1} + K_t\bigl(z_t - h(\hat{X}_{t \mid t-1}, L_j)\bigr)$$

$$P_t \;=\; (I - K_t H_j) P_{t \mid t-1}$$

The Jacobian $H_j = \partial h / \partial X$ projects uncertainty from the high-dimensional state into observation space.

## 2.4 Sensor Models Used in Habitat Mapping

| Sensor | Measurement Model | Typical Std. Dev. | Habitat Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2-D LiDAR | $z_t = (r_t, \phi_t)^{\top} = h(x_t, m) + \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ | $\sigma_r \approx 0.02$ m, $\sigma_\phi \approx 0.5^{\circ}$ | Forest understory, cave |
| RGB-D Camera | Pinhole projection + depth: $z = \pi(K\, T\, p)$ | $\sigma_z \approx 0.01$–$0.05$ m | Coral, canopy gaps |
| Hyperspectral | $z = I(\lambda)$ reflectance vector | SNR $> 40$ dB | Vegetation health |
| Side-scan sonar | Range along track + cross-track | $\sigma \approx 0.1$ m | Seafloor / lake-bed |
| Thermal IR | Radiance $\propto \epsilon \sigma T^4$ | NETD $< 50$ mK | Wildlife detection |
| INS / IMU | $\omega, a$ integration | bias drift $< 1^{\circ}$/h | All platforms |

## 2.5 Coverage Path Planning for Complete Habitat Coverage

To guarantee **no region of the habitat is left unmapped**, the robot must solve a Coverage Path Planning (CPP) problem:

$$\min_{p_{1:N}} \; \sum_{k=1}^{N-1} \lVert p_{k+1} - p_k \rVert_2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \bigcup_{k=1}^{N} \text{footprint}(p_k) \supseteq \mathcal{H}_{\text{target}}$$

The classical decomposition-based approach proceeds in three steps:

1. **Decompose** the free habitat region into cells (exact cellular decomposition via boustrophedon or trapezoidal sweep).
2. **Find an optimal coverage order** by computing the minimum spanning tree (MST) over cell adjacency graph.
3. **Smooth the path** within each cell (typically back-and-forth "lawnmower" sweeps).

The lower bound on total path length for a region of area $A$ swept by a sensor footprint of width $w$ is:

$$L_{\min} \;=\; \frac{A}{w} \;+\; \text{(turning overhead)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** Coverage planners designed for forestry UAVs use *boustrophedon* sweeps with adaptive line spacing based on canopy density, reducing flight time by up to **35 %** compared to fixed-spacing grids.

## 2.6 Multi-Robot Habitat Mapping — Distributed Consensus

When a swarm maps a habitat, individual submaps are merged via **pose-graph consensus**. Each agent $i$ maintains a pose estimate $\hat{x}^{(i)}$ and exchanges relative pose measurements $\tilde{x}_{ij}$ with neighbours. The fused estimate minimizes:

$$\hat{x} \;=\; \arg\min_{x} \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} \underbrace{\lVert \tilde{x}_{ij} - (x_j \oplus x_i^{-1}) \rVert_{\Omega_{ij}}^{2}}_{\text{consistency term}}$$

where $\oplus$ is the SE(3) composition operator and $\Omega_{ij}$ is the information matrix of the pairwise measurement. This is the **Distributed Gauss–Newton** formulation used in modern field-robotics toolkits such as **Kimera-Multi** and **OpenMultiTrack**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update

We begin from the definition of the posterior occupancy probability for cell $m_i$ after observing the full history:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Step 2 — Assume measurement independence across cells (standard approximation that makes the grid tractable):**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;\approx\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})\,[\,1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\,]}$$

**Step 3 — Take the odds ratio** $\dfrac{p}{1-p}$ on both sides:

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_t)} \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm** to obtain an **additive** update:

$$\log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_t)} \;=\; \log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \;+\; \log \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the two log-odds terms** and write the compact update:

$$\boxed{\,l_i^{(t)} \;=\; l_i^{(t-1)} \;+\; l_{\text{inv}}(m_i, x_t, z_t)\,}$$

This is the cornerstone equation used in nearly every occupancy-grid-based habitat mapper (e.g., OctoMap, Cartographer's 3-D mode, MRPT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each step matters (KTU examiner's view):** A 14-mark derivation question often tests whether the student understands the *measurement-independence approximation* in Step 2. If you skip justifying it, you lose marks. State explicitly: *"We assume range measurements at distinct cells are conditionally independent given the robot pose."*

## 3.2 Derivation of EKF-SLAM Landmark Update Jacobian

Consider a landmark at world position $(L_{x,j}, L_{y,j})$ observed from robot pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$. The predicted observation in polar form is:

$$\hat{z}_t \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \hat{r}_t \\ \hat{\phi}_t \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{(L_{x,j}-x_t)^2 + (L_{y,j}-y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}\bigl(L_{y,j}-y_t,\; L_{x,j}-x_t\bigr) - \theta_t \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\hat{r}_t$ with respect to the robot pose** $x_t, y_t, \theta_t$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}_t}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}_t}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{r}_t}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{y_t - L_{y,j}}{\hat{r}_t}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{r}_t}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\hat{\phi}_t$ with respect to the robot pose:**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}_t}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{L_{y,j} - y_t}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{\phi}_t}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{\phi}_t}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; -1$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\hat{r}_t$ with respect to the landmark** $(L_{x,j}, L_{y,j})$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}_t}{\partial L_{x,j}} \;=\; \frac{L_{x,j} - x_t}{\hat{r}_t}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{r}_t}{\partial L_{y,j}} \;=\; \frac{L_{y,j} - y_t}{\hat{r}_t}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $\hat{\phi}_t$ with respect to the landmark:**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}_t}{\partial L_{x,j}} \;=\; \frac{y_t - L_{y,j}}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{\phi}_t}{\partial L_{y,j}} \;=\; \frac{L_{x,j} - x_t}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the Jacobian** $H_j = \partial h / \partial X$ (showing only the non-zero columns for brevity):

$$H_j \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}_t} & \dfrac{y_t - L_{y,j}}{\hat{r}_t} & 0 & \dots & \dfrac{L_{x,j} - x_t}{\hat{r}_t} & \dfrac{L_{y,j} - y_t}{\hat{r}_t} & \dots \\[2mm] \dfrac{L_{y,j} - y_t}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}} & \dfrac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}} & -1 & \dots & \dfrac{y_t - L_{y,j}}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}} & \dfrac{L_{x,j} - x_t}{\hat{r}_t^{\,2}} & \dots \end{bmatrix}$$

This matrix is then substituted into the Kalman gain equation. **Forests with widely separated tree trunks** keep $\hat{r}_t$ large, which **shrinks** the off-diagonal landmark–pose coupling terms, improving numerical conditioning.

## 3.3 Coverage Path Lower Bound — Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem statement.** A habitat-mapping UAV must survey a rectangular wetland of dimensions $400 \text{ m} \times 300 \text{ m}$. The on-board hyperspectral sensor produces a swath width of $w = 12 \text{ m}$ on the ground. The UAV flies at a constant ground speed of $v = 8 \text{ m/s}$. Assume each $180^{\circ}$ turn at the end of a sweep consumes $T_{\text{turn}} = 8 \text{ s}$ (no forward motion).

**Step 1 — Compute number of sweeps** along the $300$ m dimension:

$$N_{\text{sweeps}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{300}{12} \right\rceil \;=\; 25$$

**Step 2 — Compute the forward path length per sweep** (one sweep = $400$ m traverse):

$$L_{\text{forward}} \;=\; 25 \times 400 \;=\; 10000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute turning overhead** (one fewer turn than sweep count):

$$L_{\text{turn}} \;=\; (25 - 1) \times v \times T_{\text{turn}} \;=\; 24 \times 8 \times 8 \;=\; 1536 \text{ m (equivalent)}$$

**Step 4 — Total path length:**

$$L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 10000 + 1536 \;=\; 11536 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Total mission time:**

$$T_{\text{mission}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{forward}}}{v} + (N_{\text{sweeps}} - 1)\,T_{\text{turn}} \;=\; \frac{10000}{8} + 24 \times 8 \;=\; 1250 + 192 \;=\; 1442 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 24.0 \text{ min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This number is realistic: real forestry surveys of similar size using multi-rotor UAVs report mission durations of **20–35 minutes**. Battery capacity (typically 25–30 min) is therefore the **binding constraint**, not coverage geometry — a key design insight.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Habitat Mapping Algorithm Selection

| Algorithm | Map Representation | Computational Cost | Best Habitat Type | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Occupancy Grid | Voxel grid | $O(\lvert M \rvert)$ per scan | Forest floor, caves | Memory blow-up in 3-D |
| EKF-SLAM | Landmark list | $O(N^2)$, $N$ landmarks | Sparse tree-trunk maps | Data association errors |
| FastSLAM (particle) | Particles + grids | $O(M \log M)$ per particle | Wetlands, underwater | Particle depletion |
| Pose Graph + iSAM2 | Sparse graph | $O(\lvert \mathcal{E} \rvert)$ incremental | Large canopy surveys | Wrong loop closures |
| Voxel Hashing (e.g., Niessner) | Sparse hashed voxels | $O(1)$ insertion | Dense coral reefs | Hash collisions rare |
| Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) | MLP weights | Heavy GPU | Static cave interiors | Cannot update online |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Functional Architecture of a Habitat-Mapping Robot

The block diagram below traces the complete information flow from raw sensor acquisition to the final semantic habitat map. It is deliberately drawn at the **functional-architecture** level so that it renders reliably in Mermaid without ambiguity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Habitat Scene] --> B[Multi-Modal Sensor Suite]
    B --> B1[3D LiDAR]
    B --> B2[Hyperspectral Camera]
    B --> B3[Thermal IR Array]
    B --> B4[Acoustic / SONAR]
    B --> B5[IMU and GNSS Receiver]
    B1 --> C[Pre-Processing and Calibration]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Front-End Odometry]
    D --> D1[LiDAR Odometry]
    D --> D2[Visual Inertial Odometry]
    D1 --> E[Loop Closure Detector]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Back-End Optimizer]
    F --> F1[Pose Graph SLAM]
    F --> F2[Factor Graph Optimization]
    F1 --> G[Geometric Map Builder]
    F2 --> G
    G --> G1[Occupancy Grid 2D or 3D]
    G --> G2[Sparse Landmark Map]
    G1 --> H[Semantic Segmentation Module]
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1[Tree Trunk Classifier]
    H --> H2[Water Body Detector]
    H --> H3[Wildlife Spotter]
    H1 --> I[Habitat Map Database]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Coverage Path Planner]
    J --> K[Motion Controller]
    K --> L[Robot Actuators]
    L --> A
    I --> M[Field Operator Dashboard]
    M --> N[Ecologist Review Console]
```

## 4.2 Probabilistic Mapping Data-Flow Diagram

The next diagram zooms into the **probabilistic-estimation core** of the mapping stack. It visualizes the dual flow of (i) prediction from the motion model and (ii) correction from observations, which together produce the posterior over the robot trajectory and the map.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POSE[Robot Pose x_t] --> PRED[Prediction Step]
    ODOM[Odometry u_t] --> PRED
    PRED --> STATE[Predicted State x_t_given_t-1]
    STATE --> PREDCOV[Predicted Covariance P_t_given_t-1]
    OBS[Observation z_t] --> INNOV[Innovation z_t minus h_x_t]
    STATE --> INNOV
    INNOV --> GAIN[Kalman Gain K_t]
    PREDCOV --> GAIN
    GAIN --> UPD[Updated State x_t]
    GAIN --> UPDCOV[Updated Covariance P_t]
    UPD --> NEXT[Next Time Step x_t_plus_1]
    UPDCOV --> NEXT
    NEXT --> PRED
    UPD --> MAP[Habitat Map m]
    UPDCOV --> MAP
```

## 4.3 Coverage Planning State Machine

The robot's autonomous surveying mission is best modelled as a finite state machine. This diagram helps students recall the operational sequence expected of an autonomous habitat mapper.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Localize: Acquire initial pose
    Localize --> PlanCoverage: Decompose habitat
    PlanCoverage --> Sweep: Execute boustrophedon line
    Sweep --> TurnAround: End of sweep line reached
    TurnAround --> Sweep: New sweep line acquired
    Sweep --> DetectLoopClosure: Place recognition threshold met
    DetectLoopClosure --> OptimizeGraph: Back-end iSAM2 update
    OptimizeGraph --> Sweep: Resume coverage
    PlanCoverage --> Replan: New obstacle detected
    Replan --> PlanCoverage: Update cellular decomposition
    Sweep --> LowBattery: SOC below 25 percent
    LowBattery --> ReturnToBase: Compute shortest safe path
    ReturnToBase --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently ask *"Draw the block diagram of an autonomous habitat-mapping system."* Use the architecture in **Diagram 4.1**, label every block with one sensor or processing function, and explicitly draw arrows showing **data flow** versus **control flow** to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Define Natural Habitat Mapping in the context of field robotics. List any two distinct ecological environments where it is applied and name the most suitable robotic platform for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> Natural Habitat Mapping is the autonomous acquisition, geo-referencing and semantic interpretation of spatial data describing ecologically sensitive or unstructured outdoor environments using mobile robots equipped with perception, localization and planning subsystems. It overcomes GPS-denied, occluded, and dynamic conditions by combining SLAM, multi-modal sensing and coverage path planning.
>
> **Environment 1 — Dense forest understory:** Suitable platform is a **legged quadruped** (e.g., ANYmal-class), because it can step over roots and fallen logs while carrying a 3-D LiDAR and RGB-D camera for SLAM.
>
> **Environment 2 — Coral reef at 5–30 m depth:** Suitable platform is an **autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV)** carrying side-scan sonar and stereo cameras, because optical visibility is severely limited and GPS is unavailable.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**State and briefly explain the mathematical formulation of the online SLAM posterior. Why is this formulation preferred over the full SLAM posterior in real-time field deployments?**

**Model Answer:**

> The online SLAM posterior is $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — it estimates only the **current** robot pose together with the map, marginalising out the past trajectory. In contrast, full SLAM estimates $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ jointly.
>
> Online SLAM is preferred in real-time field deployments because **(i)** it has constant-size state, enabling fixed computational cost per time step, **(ii)** it produces an immediately usable pose for the motion controller, and **(iii)** it scales gracefully to long-duration surveys of large habitats such as multi-kilometre forest transects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Derive the log-odds update equation for occupancy grid mapping, clearly stating the measurement-independence assumption. **  (7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a habitat-mapping UAV covering a rectangular wetland of dimensions $L_x = 600 \text{ m}$ and $L_y = 400 \text{ m}$ with a sensor swath width $w = 10 \text{ m}$, constant ground speed $v = 10 \text{ m/s}$, and turn-around time $T_{\text{turn}} = 6 \text{ s}$, compute (i) the total path length, (ii) the total mission time, and (iii) the area coverage rate in hectares per hour. **  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1** — Define the posterior occupancy of cell $m_i$ after observing all measurements $z_{1:t}$ from known poses $x_{1:t}$:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})$$

**Step 2** — Apply Bayes' rule and the *Markov* assumption that $z_t$ is conditionally independent of $z_{1:t-1}$ given $x_t$ and $m_i$:

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t})}$$

**Step 3** — Introduce the **measurement-independence assumption**: distinct cells are conditionally independent given the pose, so the denominator simplifies to the marginal:

$$p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t}) \;\approx\; p(z_t \mid x_t) \;=\; \sum_{m_i \in \{0,1\}} p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\, p(m_i)$$

**Step 4** — Take the odds ratio on both sides; the denominator cancels neatly:

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t})} \;=\; \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 5** — Apply the natural logarithm to obtain the **additive** log-odds form:

$$l_i^{(t)} \;=\; l_i^{(t-1)} \;+\; \underbrace{\log \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}}_{l_{\text{inv}}}$$

This derivation is complete and yields the update that any occupancy-grid mapper (OctoMap, Cartographer 3-D) implements in its inner loop.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Sweep count** along the $400$ m dimension:

$$N \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{L_y}{w} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{400}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; 40$$

**Total forward path:**

$$L_{\text{fwd}} \;=\; N \times L_x \;=\; 40 \times 600 \;=\; 24000 \text{ m}$$

**Total turning overhead (in equivalent metres):**

$$L_{\text{turn}} \;=\; (N - 1)\, v\, T_{\text{turn}} \;=\; 39 \times 10 \times 6 \;=\; 2340 \text{ m}$$

**Total path length:**

$$L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 24000 + 2340 \;=\; 26340 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Total mission time:**

$$T_{\text{mission}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{fwd}}}{v} + (N-1)\,T_{\text{turn}} \;=\; \frac{24000}{10} + 39 \times 6 \;=\; 2400 + 234 \;=\; 2634 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 43.9 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Area coverage rate:**

Total area $= 600 \times 400 = 240000 \text{ m}^2 = 24.0 \text{ ha}$.

$$R \;=\; \frac{A}{T_{\text{mission}}} \;=\; \frac{24.0 \text{ ha}}{2634/3600 \text{ h}} \;\approx\; \frac{24.0}{0.7317} \;\approx\; 32.8 \text{ ha/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In coverage-path numerical questions, students routinely forget that the number of turns equals $N-1$ (not $N$). Writing $(N-1) T_{\text{turn}}$ is the single most-checked expression in the answer script — losing it costs you at least **2 marks**.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of an EKF-SLAM system for habitat landmark mapping. Derive the observation Jacobian $H_j$ for a range–bearing sensor observing a single landmark. **  (7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forest-floor robot uses a 2-D LiDAR to detect tree trunks as point landmarks. From its current pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t) = (2.0, 3.0, 0.5) \text{ m, rad}$, it observes landmark $L_j$ at $(L_{x,j}, L_{y,j}) = (7.0, 6.0) \text{ m}$. Compute the predicted range and bearing, and evaluate all six Jacobian entries of $H_j$ corresponding to the robot-pose components. **  (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture description.** EKF-SLAM stores the robot pose and all landmark positions in a single augmented state vector $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^{3+2N}$:

$$X_t \;=\; \bigl[\, x_t,\; y_t,\; \theta_t,\; L_{x,1},\; L_{y,1},\; \dots,\; L_{x,N},\; L_{y,N} \,\bigr]^{\top}$$

It proceeds in two alternating steps: **prediction** using the motion model $f(X_{t-1}, u_t)$ and **update** using the observation model $h(X_t)$. The covariance matrix $P_t$ evolves as $P_{t\mid t-1} = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^{\top} + Q_t$, and the Kalman gain is $K_t = P_{t\mid t-1} H_j^{\top}(H_j P_{t\mid t-1} H_j^{\top} + R_t)^{-1}$.

**Jacobian derivation.** The predicted observation in polar form is:

$$\hat{z} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \hat{r} \\ \hat{\phi} \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} \sqrt{(L_{x,j}-x_t)^2 + (L_{y,j}-y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}\bigl(L_{y,j}-y_t,\; L_{x,j}-x_t\bigr) - \theta_t \end{pmatrix}$$

The Jacobian $H_j = \partial h / \partial X$ has nonzero entries only in the columns belonging to the robot pose and to landmark $j$. The pose-part entries are:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{y_t - L_{y,j}}{\hat{r}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{L_{y,j} - y_t}{\hat{r}^{\,2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{x_t - L_{x,j}}{\hat{r}^{\,2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; -1$$
