The landmark-part entries are the negatives of the corresponding pose-part entries (by symmetry of the range–bearing geometry), and all other columns are zero. This sparsity is exploited by modern EKF-SLAM implementations to reduce computation from $O(N^2)$ toward $O(N)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the predicted range:**

$$\hat{r} \;=\; \sqrt{(7.0 - 2.0)^2 + (6.0 - 3.0)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{25 + 9} \;=\; \sqrt{34} \;\approx\; 5.831 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the predicted bearing:**

$$\hat{\phi} \;=\; \text{atan2}(6.0 - 3.0,\; 7.0 - 2.0) - 0.5 \;=\; \text{atan2}(3.0, 5.0) - 0.5$$

$$\text{atan2}(3.0, 5.0) \;\approx\; 0.5404 \text{ rad} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \hat{\phi} \;\approx\; 0.5404 - 0.5 \;=\; 0.0404 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the six pose-part Jacobian entries** using $\hat{r} \approx 5.831$ m and $\hat{r}^{\,2} \approx 34.0$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{2.0 - 7.0}{5.831} \;\approx\; -0.8575$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{3.0 - 6.0}{5.831} \;\approx\; -0.5145$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{r}}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial x_t} \;=\; \frac{6.0 - 3.0}{34.0} \;\approx\; 0.0882$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial y_t} \;=\; \frac{2.0 - 7.0}{34.0} \;\approx\; -0.1471$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{\phi}}{\partial \theta_t} \;=\; -1$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the $2 \times 3$ pose-block of $H_j$:**

$$H_j^{\text{pose}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -0.8575 & -0.5145 & 0 \\ 0.0882 & -0.1471 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The corresponding **landmark block** is the negation (only for the corresponding $L_{x,j}, L_{y,j}$ columns):

$$H_j^{\text{land}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.8575 & 0.5145 \\ -0.0882 & 0.1471 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Two common errors in Jacobian numerical questions are: **(1)** Sign error in $\partial \hat{\phi} / \partial x_t$ — the correct numerator is $(L_{y,j} - y_t)$, not $(y_t - L_{y,j})$; **(2)** Forgetting that $\hat{\phi}$'s dependence on $\theta_t$ is $-1$, not $+1$. Either error costs the student approximately **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Natural Habitat Mapping** = autonomous geometric + semantic mapping of unstructured outdoor environments using mobile robots.
- The probabilistic foundation is the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$, factorised as **online SLAM** or **full SLAM**.
- **Occupancy grid mapping** uses log-odds updates so that probabilistic fusion becomes a *single floating-point addition* per cell per measurement.
- **EKF-SLAM** is appropriate when landmarks (tree trunks, rocks, coral bommies) are sparse and distinguishable; its bottleneck is the $O(N^2)$ covariance update.
- **Coverage path planning** uses cellular decomposition (e.g., boustrophedon) plus minimum-spanning-tree ordering; turning overhead is $T_{\text{turn}} \times (N-1)$.
- **Sensor suites** are multi-modal by necessity: LiDAR + camera + IMU are the canonical triad; AUVs substitute sonar for LiDAR.
- The **measurement-independence assumption** (cells conditionally independent given the pose) is *the* crucial simplification that makes occupancy grids tractable.
- Key design constraints in habitat mapping: **stealth**, **energy budget**, **GPS-denied operation**, **unstructured terrain traversability**.
- Common exam numbers: swath widths $w \in [5, 15]$ m, UAV speeds $v \in [5, 12]$ m/s, turn times $T_{\text{turn}} \in [4, 10]$ s, coverage rates $20$–$40$ ha/h.
- **Pitfalls to avoid in the exam script:** confusing $N$ turns with $N-1$ turns; sign errors in the EKF Jacobian; forgetting to justify the measurement-independence assumption.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Marine and Atmospheric Plume Detection and Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marine and Atmospheric Plume Detection and Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plume Detection and Localization** is the engineering discipline within field robotics concerned with enabling autonomous robotic platforms (UAVs, ASVs, AUVs, gliders) to perceive, trace, and spatially pinpoint the source of a dispersed physical or chemical anomaly — a *plume* — released into a marine or atmospheric fluid medium.

A **plume** is formally defined as a coherent, advected volume of fluid whose physical or chemical properties (concentration of a tracer gas, suspended oil, heat, dissolved chemicals, biological agents) differ measurably from the ambient background. **Plume localization** is the inverse problem: given sparse, noisy, in-situ measurements collected by a mobile sensor network, the robot must estimate the source location $(x_s, y_s, z_s)$, emission rate $Q$, and instantaneous source strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic lies at the intersection of *Environmental Robotics*, *Multi-Agent Sensing*, and *Stochastic Estimation Theory*. The two canonical application domains are:
> 1. **Atmospheric Plumes** — airborne chemical/biological/radiological releases, volcanic SO₂, stack emissions, urban pollution.
> 2. **Marine Plumes** — subsurface hydrocarbon leaks, oil spills, hydrothermal vents, harmful algal blooms (HABs), sewage outfalls, methane seeps.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Blindfolded Bloodhound:**
> Imagine you are a bloodhound tied to a 50-metre leash, blindfolded, dropped in a vast open field, and asked to find a single piece of dropped meat. You can **smell intermittently** and **walk around**. You do not have a direct camera view of the meat. The wind is unpredictable, and the scent arrives in *whiffs* separated by clean air.
>
> - **Detection** = "I smell something stronger than background." ✓
> - **Tracking** = "I should walk *upwind* of where the whiff came from."
> - **Localization** = "The source is *there* — within 2 m of this point."
>
> A marine/atmospheric plume robot does **exactly this** in 3D, but with electrochemical sensors, e-noses, LIDAR, and Bayesian math instead of a nose and paws.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plume concentration | $C$ | $\mu g/m^3$ (atm) or $\mu g/L$ (marine) | Primary measurement |
| Source emission rate | $Q$ | $g/s$ | Strength of release |
| Wind/current speed | $u$ | $m/s$ | Advection vector |
| Plume standard deviation (lateral) | $\sigma_y$ | $m$ | Cross-wind spread |
| Plume standard deviation (vertical) | $\sigma_z$ | $m$ | Vertical spread |
| Pasquill stability class | A–F | dimensionless | Atmospheric turbulence regime |
| Detection threshold | $C_{th}$ | $\mu g/m^3$ | Minimum signal above noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **detection threshold $C_{th}$** and the **source emission rate $Q$** are the two *most exam-relevant* scalars in KTU questions on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Plumes Encountered in Field Robotics

A KTU answer that scores full marks **must** classify the plume correctly before discussing the robot. Plumes are categorised along three orthogonal axes:

**A. By Medium**
- **Atmospheric plumes** — airborne, dominated by turbulent advection and buoyancy.
- **Marine plumes** — submerged or surface, dominated by stratified flow, tidal currents, and density gradients.

**B. By Buoyancy**
- **Buoyant plumes** (e.g., smokestack gas, hydrothermal vent) — rise vertically with momentum and thermal lift.
- **Neutrally buoyant plumes** (e.g., methane at same temperature) — spread horizontally once emitted.
- **Dense gas plumes** (e.g., LPG, chlorine) — slump downward under gravity.

**C. By Source Type**
- **Continuous / steady-state** ($Q$ ≈ constant).
- **Instantaneous / puff releases** (e.g., explosions, sub-sea well blowouts).
- **Periodic / intermittent** (e.g., ship-stack cycling).

### 2.2 The Gaussian Plume Model (GPM) — The Workhorse Equation

The **Gaussian Plume Model** is the analytical steady-state solution of the advection–diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_x\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + K_y\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

For a continuous point source at height $H$ above ground in a coordinate system where $x$ is downwind, $y$ is crosswind, and $z$ is vertical, the steady-state solution is:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

- The **first exponential** models vertical diffusion from the source.
- The **second exponential** is the **image source term** modelling ground reflection (no flux through $z=0$).
- $\sigma_y(x)$ and $\sigma_z(x)$ are functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class, given by the **Briggs (1973)** or **Pasquill–Gifford** parameterisations.

### 2.3 Why the Gaussian Model? — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> The Gaussian model assumes:
> 1. Steady, uniform mean wind $u$ along $x$.
> 2. Homogeneous, isotropic turbulence (constant $K_y, K_z$).
> 3. Perfect reflection at the ground.
> 4. No chemical reaction or deposition.
>
> These assumptions **fail in real marine/urban environments** — that is precisely why robotic *in-situ* measurements, coupled with Bayesian inference, outperform static Gaussian models.

### 2.4 Plume Detection: Sensing Modalities

| Modality | Sensor | Plume Type | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electrochemical | MQ-3, PID | VOC gas | 1–10 m |
| Metal-oxide (e-nose) | TGS series | Multi-gas | 1–5 m |
| Photoionisation | PID | BTEX, VOCs | 0.5–20 m |
| Fluorometry | CDOM, chlorophyll-a | Marine dye/algae | 0.1–2 m |
| Acoustic backscatter | ADCP, sonar | Suspended particles | 10–100 m |
| LIDAR | DIAL, Raman | Aerosols, SO₂ | 100 m – 10 km |
| Hyperspectral | UAV-mounted | Oil slicks, HABs | 50–500 m |
| Thermal IR | Radiometer | Hot effluents | 1–50 m |

### 2.5 Plume Localization: Algorithmic Families

There are **four canonical algorithmic families** tested in KTU 2024:

1. **Chemotaxis / Gradient Climbing** — robot moves up the local concentration gradient $\nabla C$.
   $$\mathbf{v}_{robot} = k \cdot \nabla C(x,y,z)$$
2. **Anemotaxis / Upwind Searching** — when concentration is detected, robot turns into the wind.
3. **Bayesian Source Estimation** — recursive posterior update of source coordinates:
   $$P(\mathbf{s}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \propto P(\mathbf{z}_k \mid \mathbf{s}_k) \, P(\mathbf{s}_k \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})$$
4. **Infotaxis / Entropy-Driven Search** — maximise information gain, used when gradient is unreliable.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **only reference** a student needs in the exam hall for plume problems. Memorise it.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} e^{-y^2/2\sigma_y^2}\!\left[e^{-(z-H)^2/2\sigma_z^2} + e^{-(z+H)^2/2\sigma_z^2}\right]$ | Steady-state GPM concentration | Atmospheric, marine surface |
| 2 | $\sigma_y = a \cdot x^b,\ \sigma_z = c \cdot x^d$ | Briggs dispersion coefficients (stability class A–F) | Downwind spread |
| 3 | $\nabla C = \left(\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x},\ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial y},\ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}\right)$ | Concentration gradient for chemotaxis | Gradient climbing |
| 4 | $P(s\mid z) = \dfrac{P(z\mid s) P(s)}{P(z)}$ | Bayes update for source posterior | Bayesian localization |
| 5 | $P(z\mid s) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi R}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z - h(s))^2}{2R}\right)$ | Measurement likelihood (linear model) | Kalman/Particle filter |
| 6 | $\dot{x} = k \cdot \nabla C$ | Continuous gradient ascent | Chemotaxis controller |
| 7 | $\mathbf{v} = -k \cdot \mathbf{u}_{wind}$ (after detection) | Upwind search velocity | Anemotaxis |
| 8 | $C_{ground}(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Ground-level centreline conc. | Stack emissions |
| 9 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (C_{obs} - C_{model})^2}{\sum (C_{obs} - \bar{C}_{obs})^2}$ | Goodness of fit | Model validation |
| 10 | $I = -\int p(s) \log p(s) \, ds$ | Information entropy | Infotaxis reward |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Gaussian Plume Model is used by **EPA AERMOD**, **industrial stack designers**, and **emergency response robots** (e.g., QinetiQ Dragonrunner, SRI CentiBOT) to plan waypoints and predict hazard zones during chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) incidents.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gaussian Plume Concentration from the Advection–Diffusion PDE

We begin with the 3D steady-state advection–diffusion equation for a scalar contaminant concentration $C$ released from a point source emitting at rate $Q$ (mass per unit time), located at the origin and at effective release height $H$:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 1 — Make the diffusion coefficients variable.**
Real turbulent diffusion coefficients grow with downwind distance. Define effective standard deviations $\sigma_y(x), \sigma_z(x)$ such that $2 K_y x = u \sigma_y^2$ and $2 K_z x = u \sigma_z^2$. The PDE becomes:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{u \sigma_y^2}{2 x} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + \frac{u \sigma_z^2}{2 x} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Assume separable form.**
Try a Gaussian ansatz $C(x,y,z) = A(x) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$.

**Step 3 — Compute derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} = \left[\frac{y^2}{\sigma_y^4} - \frac{1}{\sigma_y^2}\right] C$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the PDE** and match coefficients of $y^2$ and $z^2$. This yields two ODEs:

$$\frac{d(\ln A)}{dx} = \frac{1}{x}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate** to obtain $A(x) = \dfrac{K}{x}$ for some integration constant $K$.

**Step 6 — Apply the source-conservation boundary condition.** Integrating the concentration over a far-downwind plane must recover the emission rate $Q$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u C(x,y,z)\, dy\, dz = Q$$

This fixes the constant $K = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$.

**Step 7 — Apply the image-source method for ground reflection.** The no-flux condition $\partial C/\partial z = 0$ at $z=0$ is satisfied by adding a mirror source at $z=-H$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

This is the **canonical Gaussian Plume Model** of Section 2.2. ∎

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Question:** A factory stack of effective height $H = 50$ m releases SO₂ at a rate $Q = 100$ g/s. The wind speed at stack height is $u = 4$ m/s, and the Pasquill stability class is **C** (slightly unstable). For a downwind distance of $x = 1$ km, Briggs parameterisation gives $\sigma_y = 47$ m and $\sigma_z = 22$ m. Compute the **ground-level centreline concentration** ($y = 0$, $z = 0$).

**Step 1 — Identify the centreline ground-level formula.** From the KTU Formula Sheet, equation 8:

$$C_{ground}(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} = \frac{100}{\pi \times 4 \times 47 \times 22} = \frac{100}{12{,}989.4} = 7.698 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{g/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the vertical decay exponential.**

$$\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2} = \frac{50^2}{2 \times 22^2} = \frac{2500}{968} = 2.5826$$

$$\exp(-2.5826) = 0.07566$$

**Step 4 — Multiply to obtain the centreline concentration.**

$$C_{ground} = 7.698 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.07566 = 5.825 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{g/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Convert to standard units.**

$$C_{ground} = 5.825 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{g/m}^3 = 582.5 \ \mu g/m^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The ground-level centreline SO₂ concentration at 1 km downwind is **≈ 582.5 µg/m³**. This is the value the KTU examiner expects. Always state units explicitly.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Algorithm

| Scenario | Best Algorithm | Reason | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady wind, smooth plume | Gradient climbing (chemotaxis) | Gradient points to source | Fails in turbulent meander |
| Intermittent detections | Anemotaxis (upwind search) | Wind direction is reliable cue | Requires wind sensor |
| Multi-robot team, sparse data | Bayesian inference | Combines all measurements | Computationally heavy |
| Noisy / patchy plume | Infotaxis | Maximises information | Slower convergence |
| Marine stratified flow | Particle filter + GPM | Handles non-Gaussian tails | Needs good prior |
| Time-varying source | Kalman / Ensemble filter | Tracks moving source | Linearisation errors |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Plume Detection & Localization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Plume Source] --> B[Atmospheric or Marine Medium]
    B --> C[Turbulent Advection and Diffusion]
    C --> D[Sensor Payload on Robot]
    D --> D1[Electrochemical e-nose]
    D --> D2[PID Photoionisation]
    D --> D3[Fluorometer]
    D --> D4[Anemometer or ADCP]
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning and A D Conversion]
    E --> F[Detection Module]
    F --> F1[Threshold Check C above Cth]
    F --> F2[Noise Filtering SNR]
    F1 --> G{Plume Detected}
    F2 --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Localization Engine]
    G -->|No| I[Search Behaviour Planner]
    H --> H1[Bayesian Source Posterior Update]
    H --> H2[Gradient Ascent Module]
    H --> H3[Particle Filter Estimator]
    H1 --> J[Source Estimate xs ys Q]
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    J --> K[Waypoint Generator]
    K --> L[Motion Controller]
    L --> M[Robot Actuator Commands]
    M --> N[Robot Moves]
    N --> D
    I --> L
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Multi-Robot Plume Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[UAV Scout 1] -->|wireless telemetry| S[Central Fusion Server]
    R2[UAV Scout 2] -->|wireless telemetry| S
    R3[ASV Surface Vessel] -->|wireless telemetry| S
    R4[AUV Underwater Glider] -->|acoustic modem| S
    S --> F1[Bayesian Occupancy Grid Map of Plume]
    S --> F2[Estimated Source Location Marker]
    S --> F3[Dispersion Parameter Estimates sigma_y sigma_z]
    F1 --> D[Operator Dashboard]
    F2 --> D
    F3 --> D
    D -->|human or autonomous decision| R1
    D -->|human or autonomous decision| R2
    D -->|human or autonomous decision| R3
    D -->|human or autonomous decision| R4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic Inside the Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Robot Reaches New Waypoint] --> read[Acquire Sensor Reading]
    read --> q1{Is Concentration above Detection Threshold}
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is there recent detection in last 60 s}
    q1 -->|Yes| inside[Plume Flag TRUE]
    q2 -->|No| explore[Execute Lawnmower Search Pattern]
    q2 -->|Yes| upwind[Turn Into Wind by Anemotaxis]
    inside --> grad[Compute Local Gradient via Finite Difference]
    grad --> climb[Move Up the Gradient by Step Size Delta]
    explore --> start
    upwind --> start
    climb --> start
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above three diagrams collectively describe the **functional block architecture** of a real field robotic system. The first handles the *single-robot feedback loop*, the second the *multi-agent fusion*, and the third the *on-board decision tree* — exactly the level of detail KTU expects in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "plume" as used in field robotics. Distinguish between a buoyant plume and a neutrally buoyant plume with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **plume** is a volume of fluid — atmospheric or marine — in which a tracer's concentration, temperature, or particle density is measurably higher than the ambient background, sustained by advection and diffusion from a continuous or instantaneous source (1 mark).
- A **buoyant plume** rises vertically due to density difference (e.g., a hot stack gas, a hydrothermal vent emitting superheated water) (1 mark).
- A **neutrally buoyant plume** has the same density as the surrounding fluid and spreads purely horizontally after emission (e.g., methane released at ambient temperature in seawater) (1 mark).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Gaussian Plume Model equation for ground-level centreline concentration. What does each term physically represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$C_{ground}(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

- $Q$ — source emission rate (g/s) (0.5 mark).
- $u$ — mean wind speed at release height (m/s) (0.5 mark).
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ — lateral and vertical plume spread parameters (m) (1 mark).
- $H$ — effective release height (m) (0.5 mark).
- $x$ — downwind distance, implicit in $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ (0.5 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state Gaussian Plume Model for a continuous elevated point source, listing the assumptions and applying the ground-reflection boundary condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal oil refinery releases H₂S gas at a rate of $Q = 60$ g/s from a stack of effective height $H = 40$ m. The mean wind speed is $u = 3$ m/s under Pasquill class D stability. At downwind distance $x = 500$ m, the dispersion coefficients are $\sigma_y = 25$ m and $\sigma_z = 14$ m. Calculate the **ground-level centreline concentration** and the **concentration at $y = 30$ m, $z = 5$ m** at the same downwind distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
1. State the steady-state 3D advection-diffusion PDE (1 mark).
2. Apply Briggs substitution $2K_y x = u \sigma_y^2$ to obtain variable diffusion form (1 mark).
3. Assume Gaussian separable solution in $y$ and $z$ (1 mark).
4. Substitute and separate to get $A(x) = K/x$ (1 mark).
5. Apply source-conservation integral to fix $K = Q/(2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$ (1 mark).
6. Apply image-source method for ground reflection, yielding the two-term exponential in $z$ (1 mark).
7. List at least 4 of the 6 standard assumptions: steady wind, homogeneous turbulence, perfect reflection, no deposition, no chemistry, point source (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Centreline ground concentration at $y=0, z=0$:**

$$C_{center} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) = \frac{60}{\pi \cdot 3 \cdot 25 \cdot 14} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1600}{2 \cdot 196}\right)$$

$$= \frac{60}{3298.67} \exp(-4.0816) = 0.01819 \times 0.01689 = 3.073 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{g/m}^3$$

$$= 307.3 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad \textbf{(3 marks for centreline)}$$

**Step 2 — Off-centre, off-ground point $y=30$ m, $z=5$ m:**

$$C = \frac{60}{2\pi \cdot 3 \cdot 25 \cdot 14} \exp\!\left(-\frac{30^2}{2 \cdot 25^2}\right) \cdot \left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(5-40)^2}{2 \cdot 14^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\frac{(5+40)^2}{2 \cdot 14^2}\right)\right]$$

Pre-factor:

$$\frac{60}{2\pi \cdot 3 \cdot 25 \cdot 14} = \frac{60}{6597.34} = 9.094 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{g/m}^3$$

Lateral exponential:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{900}{1250}\right) = \exp(-0.72) = 0.4868$$

Vertical term — mirror part 1:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{35^2}{2 \cdot 196}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1225}{392}\right) = \exp(-3.125) = 0.04392$$

Vertical term — mirror part 2:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{45^2}{2 \cdot 196}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2025}{392}\right) = \exp(-5.166) = 5.69 \times 10^{-3}$$

Sum: $0.04392 + 0.00569 = 0.04961$

**Step 3 — Final product:**

$$C = 9.094 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4868 \times 0.04961 = 2.197 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{g/m}^3 = 219.7 \ \mu g/m^3 \quad \textbf{(4 marks for off-centre)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the image source** — students who write only the single-term $z$-exponential in part (a) lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$** — Briggs parameters are NOT interchangeable; crosswind is much larger than vertical for elevated releases.
> 3. **Unit conversion** — write final answer in **µg/m³** explicitly; raw $g/m^3$ values are hard to read.
> 4. **Sign error in the vertical term** — the $(z-H)^2$ in the real-source term vs $(z+H)^2$ in the mirror term is the most common mistake.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the four major algorithmic families used by field robots for plume localization: chemotaxis, anemotaxis, Bayesian inference, and infotaxis. For each, state the physical cue used and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-robot team of 3 ASVs is tracking a submarine methane plume in a coastal estuary. Each robot reports a concentration measurement with Gaussian noise of standard deviation $R = 0.5 \ \mu g/L$. The current prior source location estimate is $\mathbf{s} = (50, 30)$ m with covariance $P = \begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 15 \end{pmatrix}$. Robot 1 measures $z_1 = 8.2 \ \mu g/L$ with predicted concentration $h(\mathbf{s}) = 7.5 \ \mu g/L$. Apply a **single Kalman-filter update** of the source location. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four algorithms (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

1. **Chemotaxis (Gradient Climbing):** Robot estimates the spatial gradient $\nabla C$ from neighbour samples and moves up-gradient (1 mark). Cue = concentration gradient (0.25). Limitation = fails in turbulent meander where gradients flip sign rapidly (0.5).

2. **Anemotaxis (Upwind Search):** Upon detection, robot steers into the prevailing wind, exploiting the advective flow direction (1 mark). Cue = wind vector (0.25). Limitation = useless in still air or when wind direction is ambiguous (0.5).

3. **Bayesian Inference:** Posterior over source location is updated with every measurement using Bayes' rule $P(s\mid z) \propto P(z\mid s) P(s)$ (1 mark). Cue = likelihood of measurement given hypothetical source (0.25). Limitation = requires a likelihood model and significant computation (0.5).

4. **Infotaxis (Information-Drive):** Robot moves to **maximise expected reduction in entropy** of the source posterior, not the gradient itself (1 mark). Cue = expected information gain (0.25). Limitation = slower convergence; computationally expensive integral over possible sources (0.5).

**(b) Kalman update (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Innovation (measurement residual):**

$$\nu = z_1 - h(\mathbf{s}) = 8.2 - 7.5 = 0.7 \ \mu g/L \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Innovation covariance (linear case, $H = 1$ for scalar measurement):**

$$S = H P H^T + R = 1 \times P_{avg} \times 1 + 0.5 = 17.5 + 0.5 = 18.0 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

(Here we take the trace-based scalar equivalent; KTU accepts either scalar or matrix form.)

**Step 3 — Kalman gain:**

$$K = P H^T S^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 20 \\ 15 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{18.0} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.111 \\ 0.833 \end{pmatrix} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Updated state estimate:**

$$\mathbf{s}_{new} = \mathbf{s} + K \nu = \begin{pmatrix} 50 \\ 30 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 1.111 \\ 0.833 \end{pmatrix} \cdot 0.7 = \begin{pmatrix} 50 + 0.778 \\ 30 + 0.583 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 50.78 \\ 30.58 \end{pmatrix} \ \text{m} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Updated covariance (Joseph form simplified):**

$$P_{new} = (I - K H) P = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - 1.111 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 - 0.833 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 15 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 17.78 & 0 \\ 0 & 12.50 \end{pmatrix} \ \text{m}^2 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Kalman questions:**
> 1. **Inconsistent units** — if concentration is in $\mu g/L$ and $R$ is in $(mg/m^3)$, marks are docked. Keep SI throughout.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract** the innovation sign — the robot shifts **toward** the measurement, not away.
> 3. **Neglecting the covariance update** — the posterior covariance is as important as the mean; without it, the next iteration has no uncertainty bound.
> 4. **Treating $H$ as identity** — for non-linear $h(\mathbf{s})$, linearise via Jacobian: $H = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{s}$. KTU gives partial credit for showing the linearisation step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - A **plume** is a continuously-emitted tracer anomaly; localisation = inverse problem.
> - The **Gaussian Plume Model** is the steady-state solution of the advection–diffusion equation with **ground reflection** modelled by an **image source**.
> - **Briggs (1973)** parameterisations: $\sigma_y = a x^b,\ \sigma_z = c x^d$; coefficients depend on Pasquill class **A–F** (A = most unstable, F = most stable).
> - **Centreline ground concentration** formula: $C(0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ — this is the **single most-tested equation**.
> - Four localisation algorithms: **chemotaxis, anemotaxis, Bayesian, infotaxis** — know cue + limitation for each.
> - **Bayes update** for source posterior: $P(s\mid z) \propto P(z\mid s) P(s)$.
> - **Kalman update equations**: innovation $\nu$, gain $K = P H^T S^{-1}$, state update $\mathbf{s}_{new} = \mathbf{s} + K \nu$, covariance update $P_{new} = (I - KH) P$.
> - **Marine vs Atmospheric** difference: marine = density-stratified, tidal; atmospheric = Pasquill stability classes dominate.
> - Common **sensors**: e-nose, PID, fluorometer, ADCP, hyperspectral imager, LIDAR.
> - Multi-robot fusion: **centralised Bayesian filter** in a fusion server, communicated via radio (aerial) or acoustic modem (subsea).
> - **Always state units** — µg/m³ for atmospheric, µg/L or ppb for marine.
> - **Image-source trick** in the GPM gives the doubled $z$-exponential — without it, you lose one mark per question.
> - **Gradient climbing** fails in turbulent meander; **infotaxis** is the modern fix but is computationally heavier.
> - Marine plume robots face **acoustic communication latency** (~1 s/km) and **GPS-denied** operation — KTU loves to test this awareness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Muling WSNs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Muling WSNs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Muling in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)** is a data collection paradigm in which one or more *mobile entities* — referred to as **Data Mules (DMs)** or **Mobile Sinks (MSs)** — traverse a sensor field to physically approach static sensor nodes, establish short-range wireless contact, and harvest buffered sensed data before ferrying it to a base station (sink). This paradigm transforms WSNs into a form of **Delay/Disruption Tolerant Network (DTN)**, where the energy-expensive multi-hop forwarding chain is replaced by **single-hop, proximity-based opportunistic transfers** between the sensor and the mule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 — Module 1):**
> Data Muling is a foundational enabling technique for *field robotics* deployments, where a service robot (UGV / legged platform / UAV) acts as the mule to collect data from sparsely deployed or energy-constrained sensor nodes used in precision agriculture, environmental monitoring, and disaster-zone mapping.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **DM** | Data Mule | Mobile agent that transports data |
| **MS** | Mobile Sink | Synonym for DM in many texts |
| **BS** | Base Station | Final destination / gateway |
| **SoC** | Store-and-Carry | DM buffers data physically between visits |
| **PoI** | Point of Interest | Physical location a sensor monitors |
| **DTN** | Delay/Disruption Tolerant Network | Networking class suited to intermittent links |
| **SoR** | Sphere of Radio | Communication range (~ **10–100 m**) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Walking Postman"

Imagine a village of **20 isolated huts**, each with a mailbox. There are no roads connecting the huts to a central post office. Instead of building telephone lines (multi-hop WSN routing), the village hires a **walking postman** (the *Data Mule*) who:

1. Walks from hut to hut.
2. Collects the letters (sensor data) directly from each mailbox via short-range handshake.
3. Carries the bundle to the central post office (Base Station).
4. Returns the next day to repeat the cycle.

The villagers (sensor nodes) **never** need a long-range transmitter; they just need a short radio to talk to the postman when he stands at their gate. The **postman is the only node paying the long-range communication cost** — exactly the design philosophy of Data Muling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use direct long-range transmission?**
> Because radio energy scales as $d^{2}$ (free-space) or $d^{4}$ (multipath). A 100 m direct transmission consumes the same energy as **10,000 transmissions of 10 m**. The mule converts a *spatial* problem (long distance) into a *temporal* problem (latency), trading delay for huge energy savings — ideal for slow-changing environmental fields.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of a Data-Muling WSN

A canonical data-muling WSN consists of three logical tiers:

1. **Tier 1 — Static Sensor Nodes (SNs):**
   Fixed in the field, equipped with limited battery, sensing unit, short-range radio (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee), and a local **data buffer** of size $B_{\max}$ samples.
   Each node samples its phenomenon at rate $\lambda$ (samples/s) and stores readings until the mule arrives.

2. **Tier 2 — Mobile Data Mule(s):**
   A robot, vehicle, human, animal, or UAV that follows a *deterministic* or *random* mobility trajectory. It carries a larger buffer $M_{\max}$, GPS receiver, and a long-range radio to reach the BS.

3. **Tier 3 — Base Station (BS):**
   Wired infrastructure endpoint that aggregates all data and forwards it to the cloud / control centre.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1 — Sensing & Local Buffering:**
  Each node samples at rate $\lambda_i$ and appends to its buffer. Buffer occupancy grows linearly between mule visits.

- **Step 2 — Mule Approach & Discovery:**
  As the mule enters the communication sphere (radius $R_c$) of node $i$, a *beacon handshake* establishes the link. RSSI / GPS-based ranging can be used.

- **Step 3 — Bulk Transfer:**
  All buffered packets are uploaded to the mule in a single burst using the maximum link capacity $\mathcal{C}$ (bits/s). Transmission time is $T_{tx} = B_i / \mathcal{C}$.

- **Step 4 — Carry & Forward:**
  The mule physically transports the data while moving to the BS (or to the next node).

- **Step 5 — Offload at BS:**
  Upon arrival at the BS, all aggregated data is offloaded via a high-speed wired/wireless link.

## 2.3 Why Data Muling Works — The Core Engineering Rationale

- **Energy Localisation:** Only short-range radios are needed on sensor nodes. The mule pays the long-range transmission cost using its (typically rechargeable or larger) battery.
- **Network Lifetime Extension:** Because $E_{tx} \propto d^{n}$, replacing $d=100$ m multi-hop links with $d=10$ m single-hop links multiplies node lifetime by orders of magnitude.
- **Sparse Deployment Tolerance:** Mules can service nodes that are physically isolated (e.g., across rivers, in pits, on individual trees).
- **Delay Tolerance:** Acceptable for non-realtime applications (soil moisture, weather, structural vibration). Unsuitable for closed-loop control.
- **Self-Healing:** If one mule fails, another can be dispatched; no static routing tables are corrupted.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Tx energy (free-space, $d<d_0$) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d^{2}$ | J/bit | $k$ = packet size (bits) |
| 2 | Tx energy (multipath, $d \ge d_0$) | $E_{tx}(k,d) = E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{mp}\cdot k\cdot d^{4}$ | J/bit | Long-range regime |
| 3 | Rx energy | $E_{rx}(k) = E_{elec}\cdot k$ | J/bit | Reception cost |
| 4 | Crossover distance | $d_{0} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | m | Boundary between models |
| 5 | Buffer growth between visits | $B_i(t+\Delta t) = \min\{B_{\max},\; B_i(t) + \lambda_i\cdot \Delta t\}$ | samples | If no overflow |
| 6 | Transfer time at contact | $T_{tx,i} = \dfrac{k_i \cdot N_i}{\mathcal{C}}$ | s | $N_i$ = buffered packets at $i$ |
| 7 | Average node-to-mule latency | $\bar{L}_i = \dfrac{T_{tour}}{2\cdot N_n}$ | s | $T_{tour}$ = full mule cycle, $N_n$ = nodes |
| 8 | Total tour time | $T_{tour} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_n} T_{tx,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{N_n-1} T_{move,j}$ | s | Includes inter-node motion |
| 9 | Network lifetime (energy-based) | $\mathcal{T}_{life} = \dfrac{E_{init}}{E_{sense} + E_{tx} + E_{idle}}$ | s | Until first node depletes |
| 10 | Buffer overflow probability | $P_{ov} = P\bigl(\lambda_i \cdot \Delta t_{visit} > B_{\max}\bigr)$ | — | Drives design of $B_{\max}$ |
| 11 | Throughput per cycle | $\Theta = \dfrac{\sum_i k_i \cdot N_i}{T_{tour}}$ | bits/s | System-level metric |
| 12 | Mule energy per cycle | $E_{mule} = P_{move}\cdot T_{move} + P_{rx}\cdot\sum_i T_{tx,i}$ | J | Robot battery model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation note:** When writing these equations inline in markdown tables, use `\vert` instead of `|` to delimit absolute-value or conditional expressions, so the table syntax is preserved.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Buffer Occupancy at a Static Node

Consider a sensor node $i$ producing samples at constant rate $\lambda_i$ (samples/s). Between two consecutive mule visits separated by inter-visit interval $\Delta t_i$, the buffer fills monotonically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_i(t+\Delta t) &= B_i(t) + \lambda_i \cdot \Delta t \quad &\text{(linear accumulation)} \\
&\le B_{\max} \quad &\text{(hard cap — physical memory)} \\
\Rightarrow \Delta t_{\max,i} &= \frac{B_{\max}}{\lambda_i} \quad &\text{(maximum tolerable inter-visit gap)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This expression is the **design contract** between the mule mobility planner and the sensor network. If the mule takes longer than $\Delta t_{\max,i}$ to return, packets at node $i$ are dropped — the network's *freshness guarantee* is violated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Total Mule Tour Time

A full mule tour consists of two alternating activities: **moving** between nodes and **transferring** data at each node.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tour} &= \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{N_n-1} T_{move,j}}_{\text{travel between consecutive nodes}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N_n} T_{tx,i}}_{\text{data transfer at each node}} \\
T_{move,j} &= \frac{d_{j,j+1}}{v_{mule}} \quad &\text{(Euclidean transit at velocity }v_{mule}\text{)} \\
T_{tx,i} &= \frac{k_i \cdot B_i}{\mathcal{C}_i} \quad &\text{(packet count }B_i\text{ of size }k_i\text{ at link rate }\mathcal{C}_i\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{tour} = \frac{1}{v_{mule}}\sum_{j=1}^{N_n-1} d_{j,j+1} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_n} \frac{k_i \cdot B_i}{\mathcal{C}_i}\,}
$$

This is the **dominant timing equation** for any data-muling WSN and appears frequently in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Average End-to-End Latency

Each packet generated at node $i$ waits, on average, half of the tour interval before being picked up, then travels with the mule for a fraction of the remaining tour before reaching the BS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L}_i &= \underbrace{\frac{T_{tour}}{2}}_{\text{avg wait before pickup}} + \underbrace{\alpha_i \cdot T_{tour}}_{\text{residual travel after pickup}} \\
&\approx \frac{T_{tour}}{2}\bigl(1 + 2\alpha_i\bigr) \quad &\text{(uniform waiting assumption)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha_i \in [0,1]$ is the fractional position of node $i$ in the tour order. For a *symmetric random tour* through $N_n$ uniformly distributed nodes, averaging over $\alpha_i$:

$$
\bar{L}_{sys} = \frac{T_{tour}}{2}\Bigl(1 + 1\Bigr) = T_{tour}
$$

So **the average system latency equals one full tour period** — a beautifully simple rule-of-thumb.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Network Lifetime Gain from Muling

Compare a static multi-hop WSN where each packet traverses $h$ hops of average length $d_h$, versus a data-muling architecture where each hop has length $d_m \ll d_h$ and the mule does the long-range work.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{multi-hop} &= h \cdot \bigl(E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d_h^{2}\bigr) \\
E_{muling} &= E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d_m^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{E_{multi-hop}}{E_{muling}} &= \frac{h \cdot (E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs}\,d_h^{2})}{E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs}\,d_m^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $d_m \ll d_h$ and typically $d_m \to 0$ in the limit (mule parks *next to* the node), the ratio tends to $h$ — i.e., **muling extends node lifetime by a factor of approximately $h$**, the hop count of the equivalent static network.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Buffer Overflow Probability

Treating inter-visit time $\Delta t$ as a random variable with PDF $f_{\Delta t}(\tau)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ov} &= \Pr\bigl(\lambda_i \cdot \Delta t > B_{\max}\bigr) \\
       &= \Pr\bigl(\Delta t > B_{\max}/\lambda_i\bigr) \\
       &= \int_{B_{\max}/\lambda_i}^{\infty} f_{\Delta t}(\tau)\, d\tau \\
       &= 1 - F_{\Delta t}\!\left(\frac{B_{\max}}{\lambda_i}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering use:** Given a target $P_{ov}^{\text{target}}$, the designer solves for the required $B_{\max}$ or the maximum permissible $\bar{\Delta t}$ — this is a standard KTU 14-mark derivation type.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow Diagram — Mule-Based WSN

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **three-tier functional architecture** of a Data Muling WSN, including the data flow and control handshake between static sensors, the mobile mule, and the base station.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[TIER 1 STATIC SENSOR FIELD]
        S1[nodeA sensor 1 buffer B1]
        S2[nodeB sensor 2 buffer B2]
        S3[nodeC sensor 3 buffer B3]
        S4[nodeD sensor 4 buffer B4]
    end

    subgraph T2[TIER 2 MOBILE DATA MULE ROBOT]
        M1[muleCore onboard processor]
        M2[muleBuf large buffer Mmax]
        M3[muleRadio short range link]
        M4[muleRadio long range link to BS]
    end

    subgraph T3[TIER 3 BASE STATION]
        B1S[bsGateway wired uplink]
        B2S[bsCloud analytics and storage]
    end

    S1 -- short range opportunistic transfer --> M3
    S2 -- short range opportunistic transfer --> M3
    S3 -- short range opportunistic transfer --> M3
    S4 -- short range opportunistic transfer --> M3

    M3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M4
    M4 --> B1S
    B1S --> B2S

    classDef sensor fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000;
    classDef mule fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000;
    classDef base fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000;
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 sensor;
    class M1,M2,M3,M4 mule;
    class B1S,B2S base;
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Handshake Between Node and Mule

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Node as Static Sensor Node
    participant Mule as Mobile Data Mule
    participant BS as Base Station

    loop Every Lambda interval
        Node->>Node: Sample phenomenon and store in buffer
    end

    Mule->>Node: BEACON request to RSSI range R c
    Node-->>Mule: ACK with node ID and buffer size B i
    Mule->>Node: TRANSFER READY grant channel
    Node->>Mule: Bulk packet upload over T tx
    Mule->>Mule: Append to Mmax store and carry
    Mule->>BS: Long range offload at gateway
    BS-->>Mule: ACK offload complete
    Mule->>Node: Next visit after T tour interval
```

## 4.3 State Diagram — Sensor Node Behavioural FSM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SLEEP
    SLEEP --> SENSE: timer tick every 1 over lambda
    SENSE --> BUFFER_APPEND: store sample in B i
    BUFFER_APPEND --> BUFFER_CHECK: is B i at B max
    BUFFER_CHECK --> SENSE: not full continue
    BUFFER_CHECK --> OVERFLOW: full drop oldest sample

    SLEEP --> BEACON_LISTEN: mule in range RSSI above threshold
    BEACON_LISTEN --> HANDSHAKE: valid mule ID received
    HANDSHAKE --> TRANSFER: burst upload of all buffered packets
    TRANSFER --> BUFFER_FLUSH: clear local buffer
    BUFFER_FLUSH --> SLEEP: resume duty cycling

    OVERFLOW --> SLEEP: log drop event
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Define Data Muling in WSNs. List two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Data Muling is a data-gathering strategy where one or more mobile entities (*Data Mules*), such as robots or vehicles, traverse a sensor field to physically approach static nodes and harvest buffered data via short-range wireless links, then ferry it to a base station.

**Advantages (any 2):**
1. Drastically reduces per-node transmission energy because only short-range radio is needed.
2. Extends network lifetime by orders of magnitude in sparse or hostile deployments.

**Limitation (any 1):**
1. Introduces *delay* — packets wait until the mule arrives; unsuitable for hard real-time control loops.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**Why is a Data Muling WSN classified as a Delay Tolerant Network (DTN)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DTN is a network where end-to-end paths may not exist continuously because links are intermittent. In a Data Muling WSN, the sensor node and the base station are **never simultaneously connected** — connectivity only occurs when the mobile mule is within the radio sphere of a node. The mule *stores* data physically in its buffer and *carries* it forward (Store-and-Forward / Store-Carry-Forward). This temporal separation between sensing and delivery is the defining trait of DTNs, hence the classification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Cycle]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with neat block diagram the three-tier architecture of a Data Muling WSN. Discuss the role of each tier.**

**Model Answer:**

A Data Muling WSN is organised into three logical tiers, each performing a distinct role:

- **Tier 1 — Static Sensor Nodes:**
  Deployed at Points of Interest (PoIs). Each node consists of a *sensing unit* (temperature, humidity, soil-moisture, gas, vibration, etc.), a *micro-controller*, a *short-range radio* (typically IEEE 802.15.4, range ~10–100 m), and a *limited data buffer* ($B_{\max}$ samples). Nodes operate on small batteries and are duty-cycled to maximise lifetime.

- **Tier 2 — Mobile Data Mule:**
  The field robot / vehicle / UAV that physically traverses the field. It hosts a *larger buffer*, *GPS*, *on-board computation*, *short-range radio* (to talk to sensors), and a *long-range radio* (Wi-Fi / cellular / LoRa to reach the BS). Its mobility pattern may be deterministic (planned coverage path), random (RWP — Random Waypoint), or guided by a learning policy.

- **Tier 3 — Base Station:**
  A fixed gateway with wired backhaul. It aggregates all mule uploads and forwards them to cloud servers for analytics, visualisation, or actuation triggers.

The **data flow** is: *Sensor → (short range) → Mule buffer → (carried motion) → Mule long-range radio → BS → Cloud*. The **control flow** is the reverse: BS can dispatch mule route updates, sensor sleep schedules, or alert priorities.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for tier description, 2 for flow explanation)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A field has 8 sensor nodes uniformly spaced at 50 m intervals along a straight line. The mule moves at 2 m/s and spends 5 s transferring data at each node. Each node generates 1 sample every 10 s and the buffer cap is 50 samples. Calculate: (i) Total tour time, (ii) Average end-to-end latency, (iii) Whether buffer overflow occurs.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Tour Time $T_{tour}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{move} &= 7 \times \frac{50}{2} = 7 \times 25 = 175 \text{ s} \\
T_{tx} &= 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ s} \\
T_{tour} &= T_{move} + T_{tx} = 175 + 40 = 215 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average End-to-End Latency $\bar{L}$:**

Using the symmetric random tour approximation $\bar{L} \approx T_{tour}$:

$$
\bar{L} \approx 215 \text{ s} \approx 3.58 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Buffer Overflow Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Samples generated per tour} &= \lambda \cdot T_{tour} = \frac{1}{10} \times 215 = 21.5 \text{ samples} \\
B_{\max} &= 50 \text{ samples} \\
\because 21.5 < 50 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{No overflow occurs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The mule tour is *safe* for this sampling rate. The *maximum tolerable tour time* would be:

$$
T_{tour}^{\max} = B_{\max} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} = 50 \times 10 = 500 \text{ s}
$$

So the design has a margin of $500 - 215 = 285$ s before any node would drop packets.

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Cycle]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare static multi-hop WSN data collection with Data Muling architecture. Derive the energy ratio between the two approaches and comment on its implications.**

**Model Answer:**

In a **static multi-hop WSN**, every packet must be relayed across $h$ hops of average length $d_h$ to reach the BS. Each hop costs both transmission and reception energy.

In a **Data Muling WSN**, each sensor transmits only once over a very short distance $d_m$ directly to the mule. The mule does the long-haul.

**Energy Ratio Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{multi-hop} &= h \cdot \bigl(E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d_h^{2}\bigr) \\
E_{muling} &= E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d_m^{2} \\
\eta &= \frac{E_{multi-hop}}{E_{muling}} = \frac{h\bigl(E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs}\,d_h^{2}\bigr)}{E_{elec} + \varepsilon_{fs}\,d_m^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming the radio electronics term $E_{elec}$ is negligible compared to the amplifier term $\varepsilon_{fs}d^{2}$ at the operational range:

$$
\eta \approx h \cdot \frac{d_h^{2}}{d_m^{2}}
$$

**Numerical illustration:** Let $h = 5$, $d_h = 40$ m, $d_m = 5$ m.

$$
\eta \approx 5 \times \frac{40^{2}}{5^{2}} = 5 \times 64 = 320
$$

**Implications:**
1. The muling node consumes ~320× less energy per packet than in a static multi-hop architecture.
2. Network lifetime scales roughly linearly with $\eta$.
3. The mule pays the long-haul cost on its larger, often rechargeable battery — energy burden is *off-loaded* from the resource-starved static nodes.
4. Trade-off: latency increases because data waits for the mule to physically arrive.

*(7 marks: 2 for comparison table/points, 3 for derivation, 2 for implications)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A precision-agriculture field has 10 sensor nodes each producing 1 sample per 5 s. The mule visits each node once per cycle. If each sample is 100 bits, link rate is 50 kbps, and the buffer cap is 30 samples, find: (i) the maximum permissible cycle time, (ii) the transfer time at each node, (iii) the daily data throughput.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum permissible cycle time:**

$$
T_{tour}^{\max} = \frac{B_{\max}}{\lambda} = \frac{30 \text{ samples}}{1/5 \text{ samples/s}} = 30 \times 5 = 150 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Transfer time at each node:**

Number of buffered samples at pickup = $\lambda \cdot T_{tour}$. Assuming a *just-full* buffer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_i &= B_{\max} = 30 \text{ samples} \\
\text{Total bits} &= 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ bits} \\
T_{tx,i} &= \frac{3000}{50{,}000} = 0.06 \text{ s} = 60 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Daily data throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycles per day} &= \frac{86400}{150} = 576 \text{ cycles/day} \\
\text{Samples per day per node} &= 576 \times 30 = 17{,}280 \text{ samples} \\
\text{Samples per day for whole field} &= 10 \times 17{,}280 = 172{,}800 \text{ samples} \\
\Theta_{day} &= 172{,}800 \times 100 \text{ bits} = 1.728 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits/day} \\
&\approx 17.28 \text{ Mbits/day} \approx 2.16 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Data Muling Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the transfer time** to the tour time. Students often compute only the *movement* time and miss the $T_{tx}$ term, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion in throughput problems:** the result of `samples × bits/sample × cycles/day` must be expressed in bits/s (or Mbits/day) explicitly — partial credit is forfeited otherwise.
> 3. **Assuming zero latency:** A common mistake is to treat the mule as a *real-time* conduit. Data Muling is by design **delay-tolerant**; latency is non-zero and equals approximately one tour period.
> 4. **Ignoring buffer overflow:** If a question does not state the cycle time, always verify that $\lambda \cdot T_{tour} \le B_{\max}$ before quoting a valid answer.
> 5. **Conflating $d_0$ regimes:** Use the free-space model ($d^2$) for short mule-to-node links (almost always), not the multipath ($d^4$) model — the latter is reserved for long-range BS uplinks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Muling** = mobile agent physically transports data from static sensors to the base station using short-range opportunistic transfers.
- The architecture has **three tiers**: *Static Sensors → Mobile Mule → Base Station*.
- The mule converts a **spatial** (long-range transmission) problem into a **temporal** (latency) problem.
- Node energy model: $E_{tx} = E_{elec}\cdot k + \varepsilon_{fs}\cdot k\cdot d^{2}$ (free-space, short range).
- **Energy ratio** of static multi-hop vs. muling: $\eta \approx h \cdot d_h^{2}/d_m^{2}$.
- **Buffer constraint:** $\lambda \cdot \Delta t_{\text{visit}} \le B_{\max}$, else overflow.
- **Tour time:** $T_{tour} = \sum T_{move,j} + \sum T_{tx,i}$.
- **Average latency:** $\bar{L} \approx T_{tour}$ for symmetric tours.
- **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ — boundary between $d^2$ and $d^4$ models.
- Data Muling WSNs are a subclass of **Delay/Disruption Tolerant Networks (DTNs)**.
- Ideal for **field robotics** deployments: precision agriculture, forest monitoring, pipeline inspection, disaster-zone telemetry.
- **Not suitable** for hard real-time closed-loop control because of variable latency.
- **Key design contract:** match mule cycle time to node sampling rate and buffer capacity to prevent packet drops.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics in Disaster Management - Surveying and Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotics in Disaster Management - Surveying and Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Field and Service Robotics in Disaster Management

**Disaster Management Robotics** is a specialized branch of *Field and Service Robotics* (FSR) that deals with the design, development, and deployment of unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs), unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), and unmanned underwater/marine vehicles (UUMVs) for **search, survey, mapping, and rescue operations** in post-disaster environments such as earthquake rubble, flood zones, nuclear accident sites, collapsed buildings, and wildfire perimeters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> A disaster management robot is an *autonomous or semi-autonomous mechatronic platform* equipped with multimodal perception, decision-making, and actuation systems that assist or replace human first responders in environments that are *too dangerous, too small, too toxic, or too unstable* for direct human entry.

## 1.2 Surveying vs. Mapping — The Two Pillars

| Term | Formal Definition (KTU) | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Surveying** | The systematic collection of *raw spatial, geometric, and semantic data* about the environment using onboard sensors. | Data acquisition (sensing stage) |
| **Mapping** | The computational transformation and *fusion* of survey data into a coherent spatial representation (2D / 2.5D / 3D) of the environment. | Data interpretation (representation stage) |

> [!NOTE]
> Surveying is to Mapping what *Measurement* is to *Model*. Without surveying there is no data, and without mapping the data is just noise.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robotic Bloodhound"

Imagine sending a **bloodhound** into a collapsed building after an earthquake.
- The bloodhound **sniffs** (sensing / surveying) for survivors, gas leaks, structural voids.
- In its brain, it builds a **mental map** of corridors, weak floors, and scent gradients.
- It **communicates** the map back to rescuers.

A disaster robot behaves identically:
- **Sensors** (LIDAR, camera, IMU, gas sensor) act as the nose and eyes.
- **SLAM algorithms** act as the brain building the mental map.
- **Wireless telemetry** acts as the bark, transmitting the map to human rescuers.

The robot's job is to produce, in real time, a **high-fidelity, geo-referenced map** that allows incident commanders to make life-or-death decisions in **minutes**, not hours.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Disaster Mapping

The following metrics are standardized in FSR literature and frequently tested in KTU examinations:

- **Coverage Ratio (C):** Percentage of the disaster area successfully mapped.
- **Spatial Resolution (R):** Smallest distinguishable feature size, typically **0.05 m to 0.5 m**.
- **Localization Accuracy (ε):** Position error in meters, ideally **< 0.1 m** for indoor rescue.
- **Map Update Rate (f):** Frequency of map refresh in **Hz**, critical for dynamic hazards.
- **Mean Time to First Useful Map (MTTFUM):** Time elapsed from deployment to first map output.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the four core sensor modalities used: **LIDAR, Stereo/RGB-D Camera, IMU, GNSS** — these will appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Surveying Pipeline (Sensing Stage)

Disaster robots follow a layered sensing architecture, often called the **Perception Stack**:

1. **Exteroceptive Sensing Layer** — senses the external world
   - 2D/3D **LIDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging)
   - **RGB / Stereo / RGB-D cameras**
   - **Thermal Infrared (TIR)** imagers for victim detection
   - **Hyperspectral sensors** for chemical/hazard identification
2. **Proprioceptive Sensing Layer** — senses the robot's own state
   - **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** with gyroscope + accelerometer
   - **Wheel Odometry**
   - **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** when outdoor
3. **Exotic / Mission-Specific Layer**
   - **Geiger counters** (nuclear), **CO/CH₄ sensors** (mine rescue), **Doppler radar** (victim heartbeat detection)

## 2.2 The Mapping Pipeline (Representation Stage)

The mapping subsystem must convert raw sensor streams into one of three standard representation classes:

| Map Type | Data Structure | Best Use Case | Example Robot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metric – Occupancy Grid** | 2D/3D grid of probability cells | Indoor rubble mapping | iRobot PackBot |
| **Metric – Point Cloud** | Set of 3D (x, y, z) points | Structural 3D reconstruction | DJI Phantom 4 RTK |
| **Topological** | Graph of nodes + edges | Large-scale outdoor search | Mars Rover-class UGV |
| **Semantic** | Grid/cloud with object labels | Victim/door/fire detection | Spot (Boston Dynamics) |

## 2.3 SLAM — The Mathematical Heart of Surveying + Mapping

**Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM)** is the recursive estimation problem of recovering both the robot's trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the environment map $m$ from a stream of control inputs $u_{1:t}$ and sensor observations $z_{1:t}$.

The SLAM posterior is written as the joint probability:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

This joint posterior is intractable in raw form, so SLAM algorithms factorize it using the Markov assumption:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) \;=\; p(x_0)\prod_{i=1}^{t} p(x_i \mid x_{i-1}, u_i) \cdot p(z_i \mid x_i, m)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two factors are the **Motion Model** (odometry/IMU) and the **Observation Model** (LIDAR/camera), and they form the prediction-update cycle of the **Bayes Filter** that underpins every SLAM variant (EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3).

## 2.4 Why SLAM is Hard in Disasters

Disaster environments break the standard SLAM assumptions:

- **Dynamic objects** (people, fires, moving debris) violate the static-world assumption.
- **Feature-scarce environments** (smoke-filled rooms, white walls) defeat visual SLAM.
- **Wheel slip** on rubble corrupts odometry.
- **GNSS-denied zones** (indoors, urban canyons) eliminate absolute positioning.
- **Sensor degradation** (dust on lenses, water on LIDAR).

The above problems make **sensor fusion** and **robust probabilistic estimation** non-negotiable.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes Filter Predict: $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$ | Robot state prior | Probability density |
| 2 | Bayes Filter Update: $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$ | Posterior | $\eta$ is normalizer |
| 3 | LIDAR Range Model: $z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t = \Vert x_t - x_{lm} \Vert + v_t$ | $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | $v_t$ is Gaussian noise |
| 4 | Occupancy Log-Odds: $\ell_{i,t} = \ell_{i,t-1} + \ell_{\text{inv}} - \ell_0$ | $\ell$ = log-odds of cell $i$ | Computationally efficient |
| 5 | Homogeneous Transform: $T = \begin{bmatrix} R & d \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $R \in SO(3)$, $d \in \mathbb{R}^3$ | World-to-body frame |
| 6 | RMSE Accuracy: $\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\Vert \hat{x}_k - x_k\Vert^2}$ | $\hat{x}$ = estimated pose | Meters |
| 7 | IMU Integration: $x_t = x_{t-1} + v_{t-1}\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}a_{t-1}\Delta t^2$ | Dead reckoning | Drift accumulates |
| 8 | Extended Kalman Linearization: $H = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{\hat{x}_t}$ | Jacobian of observation model | $H$ evaluated at mean |
| 9 | Information Filter Equiv.: $\Omega = H^T R^{-1} H$ | $\Omega$ = information matrix | Sparse in SLAM |
| 10 | Coverage Ratio: $C = \dfrac{N_{\text{visited cells}}}{N_{\text{total cells}}} \times 100\%$ | Map completeness | Percentage |

> [!TIP]
> Equations 1, 2, 3, and 4 are the **most frequently tested** items in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers for this module. Practice drawing the predict-update loop and labeling each term.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update

The **occupancy grid** maintains, for each cell $m_i$ in a discretized 2D/3D map, the probability that the cell is *occupied*. The Bayesian update is intractable if done naively; the standard solution is the **log-odds representation** introduced by Moravec and Elfes (1985) and still used in ROS `costmap_2d`.

**Step 1: Start with the Bayes posterior for cell occupancy.**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)\,p(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}$$

**Step 2: Define the *odds* form by dividing both sides by the complement probability.**

$$\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} \cdot \frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t)}$$

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm of both sides to obtain the *log-odds* form.**

$$\ell_{i,t} \;\triangleq\; \log\frac{p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \log\frac{p(m_i)}{1 - p(m_i)} + \log\frac{p(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{p(z_t \mid \neg m_i, x_t)}$$

**Step 4: Identify the three canonical log-odds terms.**

- $\ell_{i,t}$ — current posterior log-odds of cell $i$
- $\ell_0 = \log\!\left(\dfrac{p(m_i)}{1-p(m_i)}\right)$ — prior log-odds (typically $\ell_0 = 0$ for $p(m_i) = 0.5$)
- $\ell_{\text{inv}} = \log\!\left(\dfrac{p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - p(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}\right)$ — inverse sensor model in log-odds

**Step 5: Recognize the *additive* recursive form.**

$$\ell_{i,t} \;=\; \ell_{i,t-1} \;+\; \ell_{\text{inv}} \;-\; \ell_0$$

**Step 6: Show that addition in log-odds = multiplication in probability.**

$$p(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\ell_{i,t})}$$

**Significance for disaster mapping:** The log-odds form is **additive and bounded** ($\ell \in (-\infty, +\infty)$), so it avoids numerical underflow when integrating thousands of LIDAR scans, and it allows **fast lookup and update** on embedded CPUs used in rescue robots.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the LIDAR Range Measurement Model

For a single LIDAR beam striking a landmark $x_{lm}$, the true range is geometric:

$$z^{\ast} = \sqrt{(x_{lm,x} - x_t)^2 + (x_{lm,y} - y_t)^2}$$

**Step 1: Express the expected range in the body frame via the homogeneous transform.**

$$h(x_t, m) \;=\; \sqrt{(x_{lm,x} - x_t)^2 + (x_{lm,y} - y_t)^2}$$

**Step 2: Add zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise $v_t$.**

$$z_t \;=\; h(x_t, m) + v_t \qquad \text{with} \quad v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, \sigma_r^2)$$

**Step 3: Write the likelihood of a single range measurement.**

$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sigma_r\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma_r^2}\left[z_t - h(x_t, m)\right]^2\right)$$

**Step 4: Compute the Jacobian $H$ needed by the EKF (required for KTU derivations).**

$$H = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x_t - x_{lm,x}}{h(x_t, m)} & \dfrac{y_t - x_{lm,y}}{h(x_t, m)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Form the Kalman gain to fuse this measurement with the motion-model prior.**

$$K_t = \bar{P}_t H^T (H \bar{P}_t H^T + R)^{-1} \quad \text{where} \quad R = \sigma_r^2$$

**Step 6: Update the posterior state estimate.**

$$\hat{x}_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{x}_t, m))$$

**Step 7: Update the posterior covariance.**

$$P_t = (I - K_t H)\,\bar{P}_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Equations in **Step 3 to Step 7** together form the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) update**. In KTU exams, you may be asked to write any three of these steps in order. Memorize the structure: *Predict → Linearize → Kalman Gain → State Update → Covariance Update*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Modalities

| Property | 2D LIDAR | 3D LIDAR | Stereo Camera | RGB-D Camera | Thermal IR | IMU | GNSS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range (m) | 30–100 | 100–200 | 0.5–20 | 0.3–5 | 5–15 | N/A | Global |
| Accuracy | ±2 cm | ±3 cm | ±0.1 px | ±1 cm | ±1 °C | Drift | ±2 m |
| Works in smoke | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Works in dark | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Indoor/Outdoor | Both | Both | Both | Indoor | Both | Both | Outdoor |
| Cost (USD) | 1k–5k | 5k–80k | 0.1k–2k | 0.2k–0.8k | 1k–10k | <0.1k | 0.1k–1k |
| Best for KTU question | Geometric map | 3D scan | Visual SLAM | Dense depth | Victim detect | Pose | Global frame |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** No single sensor suffices. KTU examiners expect you to argue for **multi-sensor fusion** (e.g., LIDAR + IMU + GNSS, or Camera + IMU) as the only robust solution in disaster scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Block Diagram — Autonomous Disaster Mapping Robot

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **functional architecture** of a representative disaster management mapping robot, from sensor ingestion through SLAM processing to map distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[EXTEROCEPTIVE SENSORS] --> B1[Sensor Pre-Processing]
    A2[PROPRIOCEPTIVE SENSORS] --> B1
    A3[MISSION SENSORS] --> B1

    B1 --> C1[Motion Model Predict]
    B1 --> C2[Observation Model Update]

    C1 --> D1[Bayes Filter Engine]
    C2 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Pose Graph Optimizer]
    E1 --> F1[Map Builder Module]
    F1 --> G1[Occupancy Grid 2D]
    F1 --> G2[Point Cloud 3D]
    F1 --> G3[Semantic Map]

    G1 --> H1[Wireless Telemetry]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1

    H1 --> I1[Incident Command Tablet]

    D1 --> J1[Loop Closure Detector]
    J1 --> E1
```

**Reading guide for KTU exams:**

- **Sensors (A1, A2, A3)** — Provide raw data.
- **Pre-Processing (B1)** — Calibration, de-noising, time-synchronization.
- **Bayes Filter (D1)** — Core probabilistic engine; runs *predict* then *update*.
- **Loop Closure (J1)** — Detects "I've been here before" to correct drift.
- **Map Builder (F1)** — Generates the three representation classes.
- **Telemetry (H1)** — Sends map to human rescuers.

---

## 4.2 SLAM Information Flow Diagram

The diagram below shows the **closed-loop data flow** during a typical SLAM run, which is a frequent KTU drawing question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Control Input u_t] --> B[Motion Model]
    B --> C[Pose Prediction x_t_bar]
    C --> D[Predicted Map Region]
    D --> E[Sensor Reading z_t]
    E --> F[Observation Model h x_t m]
    F --> G[Innovation y_t = z_t - h]
    G --> H[Kalman Gain K_t]
    H --> I[Posterior State x_t]
    I --> J[Posterior Map Update]
    J --> K[Loop Closure Test]
    K --> L{Bias Drift High}
    L -- YES --> M[Graph Optimization]
    L -- NO --> N[Continue Scan]
    M --> C
    N --> C
```

---

## 4.3 Multi-Robot Disaster Surveying Collaboration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[UAV Drone Sky Survey] --> X[Central Mapping Server]
    R2[UGV Ground Crawler] --> X
    R3[USV Surface Vehicle] --> X
    R4[Throwbot Tactical] --> X

    X --> Y[Multi-Robot SLAM Fusion]
    Y --> Z[Unified Global Map]
    Z --> W[Rescue Command Center]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark questions:** Always include at least one block diagram (e.g., the SLAM information flow). Examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for a clean, labeled, closed-loop block diagram. Missing labels = lost marks.

---

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion Architecture (Complementary Filter Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMU[IMU High Rate 200 Hz] --> F1[Complementary Filter]
    ODO[Wheel Odometry 50 Hz] --> F1
    LIDAR[LIDAR 10 Hz] --> F2[Pose Correction]
    CAM[Camera 30 Hz] --> F2
    GNSS[GNSS 1 Hz Outdoor] --> F2

    F1 --> OUT[Best Pose Estimate x_t]
    F2 --> OUT
    OUT --> MAP[Updated Map]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The most common mark-losing errors students make on this topic are:
> 1. Confusing *surveying* (data collection) with *mapping* (data representation) — examiners deduct **1 mark** for this.
> 2. Writing only the *predict* step of SLAM and forgetting the *update* step (or vice versa) — both are required for full credit on a 7-mark derivation.
> 3. Using `|` (pipe) inside a markdown table — this **breaks the table rendering** in the digital answer script. Use `\vert` instead.
> 4. Forgetting units in the formula sheet (e.g., writing RMSE without meters).
> 5. Drawing a block diagram without arrows or without labeling the *closed-loop* nature of SLAM.
> 6. Confusing **EKF-SLAM** (single hypothesis) with **FastSLAM** (Rao-Blackwellized particle filter). KTU expects EKF-SLAM by default unless stated.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). Why is SLAM considered a *chicken-and-egg* problem in disaster robotics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SLAM is the recursive estimation problem of computing a robot's pose trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the environment map $m$ simultaneously from a stream of control inputs $u_{1:t}$ and sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$, expressed as the joint posterior $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. **(1 mark)**
It is called a *chicken-and-egg* problem because:
- To localize the robot accurately, you need a good map. **(1 mark)**
- To build a good map, you need accurate robot pose estimates. **(1 mark)**
In disaster scenarios with degraded odometry and no GNSS, both estimates must be refined jointly through probabilistic inference (EKF, particle filter, or graph optimization).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List and briefly explain any three sensors used in surveying disaster environments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **2D/3D LIDAR** — Emits laser pulses; measures time-of-flight to produce accurate range scans unaffected by smoke or darkness. Used for geometric map building.
2. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — Combines gyroscope and accelerometer to provide high-rate pose updates; essential when wheel slip or GNSS denial occurs.
3. **Thermal Infrared Camera** — Detects body heat signatures through darkness and light smoke, enabling *victim localization* during search and rescue.

*(Alternative valid sensors: RGB-D camera, GNSS, hyperspectral sensor, gas sensor, Geiger counter.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an autonomous **disaster surveying and mapping robot**. Describe the role of *exteroceptive*, *proprioceptive*, and *mission-specific* sensors. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **LIDAR range measurement model** and show how it is fused with the motion model via the **Extended Kalman Filter** to obtain the posterior pose estimate. State clearly the Jacobian $H$ and the Kalman gain $K_t$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Sensor Roles (7 marks):**

A disaster surveying robot has a **three-layer sensor architecture**:

- **Exteroceptive layer (2 marks)** — senses the *external* environment:
  - **2D/3D LIDAR** for geometric scans (works in smoke/dark)
  - **RGB / Stereo / RGB-D cameras** for visual features
  - **Thermal IR camera** for victim detection

- **Proprioceptive layer (2 marks)** — senses the *internal* state:
  - **IMU** (gyro + accelerometer) for high-rate pose
  - **Wheel encoders** for odometry
  - **GNSS** for outdoor absolute positioning

- **Mission-specific layer (1 mark):**
  - **Gas / chemical / radiation sensors** for hazard detection (e.g., CO in fire, CH₄ in mines, gamma in Fukushima-style events).

- **Block diagram explanation (2 marks):** Sensors → Pre-processing (calibration, time-sync) → Bayes Filter (predict + update) → Pose Graph Optimizer → Map Builder (occupancy grid + point cloud + semantic) → Telemetry → Incident Command.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a ready-to-draw diagram.)*

---

**(b) Derivation of LIDAR + EKF Fusion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the geometric range model.
$$z_t^{\ast} = h(x_t, m) = \sqrt{(x_{lm,x} - x_t)^2 + (x_{lm,y} - y_t)^2}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Add Gaussian noise $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_r^2)$.
$$z_t = h(x_t, m) + v_t$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Write the Gaussian likelihood.
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = \frac{1}{\sigma_r\sqrt{2\pi}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{[z_t - h(x_t, m)]^2}{2\sigma_r^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the Jacobian $H$.
$$H = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x_t - x_{lm,x}}{h(x_t, m)} & \dfrac{y_t - x_{lm,y}}{h(x_t, m)} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Predict step using motion model.
$$\bar{x}_t = f(x_{t-1}, u_t), \qquad \bar{P}_t = F_t P_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute Kalman gain.
$$K_t = \bar{P}_t H^T \left(H \bar{P}_t H^T + \sigma_r^2\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Posterior update.
$$\hat{x}_t = \bar{x}_t + K_t (z_t - h(\bar{x}_t, m)), \qquad P_t = (I - K_t H)\bar{P}_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the question allows, conclude (bonus 0.5 mark) by noting that in disaster scenarios, multiple LIDAR returns + IMU are fused in an **Iterated EKF-SLAM** to handle non-linearities in cluttered rubble.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**(a)** Explain the **Bayesian formulation of SLAM**. Derive the predict and update equations of the Bayes filter from first principles and identify the *motion model* and *observation model* terms. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **challenges of SLAM in disaster environments** and explain how **multi-sensor fusion** (LIDAR + IMU + Camera + GNSS) mitigates each challenge. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Bayesian Formulation of SLAM (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the SLAM posterior.
$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the Markov assumption (current state depends only on previous state and current input).
$$p(x_t \mid x_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}, z_{1:t-1}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply Bayes' theorem to obtain the *update* equation.
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t, m)\,p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the law of total probability for the *predict* equation.
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)\,p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1})\,dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Identify the **motion model** term.
$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t) \quad \text{(odometry / IMU integration)}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Identify the **observation model** term.
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \quad \text{(LIDAR / camera range/bearing likelihood)}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclude with the predict-update loop diagram or comment on EKF approximation for non-linear models.

---

**(b) Challenges and Sensor Fusion Solutions (7 marks):**

| # | Challenge (1 mark each) | Fusion Solution (with sensor combo) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Wheel slip / odometry drift on rubble** | Fuse **IMU** (200 Hz) with **wheel odometry** using a complementary filter. |
| 2 | **GNSS-denied indoor / urban-canyon zones** | Use **LIDAR scan matching + Visual Odometry** for relative pose, with **loop closure** for global consistency. |
