$Z$ = Section Modulus | | 5 | **Deflection of Timber Beam (Uniform Load)** | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}$ | meters; $E$ in Pa, $I$ in m⁴ | | 6 | **Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** | $f_{allow} = \dfrac{f_{ultimate}}{F.S.}$ | Factor of Safety (F.S.) typically **2.0 – 2.5** | | 7 | **Slenderness Ratio (Column)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | $L_{eff}$ = effective length, $r_{min}$ = least radius of gyration | | 8 | **Euler's Buckling Load (Long Column)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_{eff}^2}$ | Newtons (N) | | 9 | **Axial Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | N/mm² or MPa | | 10 | **Bearing Stress Perpendicular to Grain** | $\sigma_{b\perp} = \dfrac{P}{A_{bearing}}$ | Typically **1/3** of parallel-to-grain value | | 11 | **Horizontal Shear (Beam)** | $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I b}$ | MPa | | 12 | **Strength Ratio** | $S.R. = \dfrac{f_{test}}{f_{standard}} \times 100$ | % | ## 2.6 Engineered Wood Products (EWPs) — Modern Timber | Product | Full Form | KTU Key Points | |---------|-----------|----------------| | **Plywood** | Laminated veneers glued at right angles | High strength both directions; **3-ply, 5-ply, 7-ply** | | **Block Board** | Core of softwood strips between veneers | Used for doors, partitions, tables | | **Particle Board** | Wood chips bonded with resin | Low cost, low strength, used in furniture | | **MDF** | Medium-Density Fibreboard | Smooth surface, used in modular kitchens | | **LVL** | Laminated Veneer Lumber | High-strength structural use | | **Glulam** | Glue-Laminated Timber | Curved beams, large spans, columns | | **CLT** | Cross-Laminated Timber | Modern mass-timber walls and slabs | | **OSB** | Oriented Strand Board | Sheathing in walls, floors, roofs | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility Timber construction is integral to: - **Formwork** in RCC construction (plywood + timber props) - **Roof trusses** in residential and commercial buildings - **Doors, windows, shutters, panelling** - **Bridges (timber-decked), watch towers, ghat structures** - **Modular & mass-timber high-rises** (CLT/Glulam renaissance globally) --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Moisture Content of a Timber Specimen > **Problem Statement:** A timber specimen weighs **2.450 kg** in its wet (green) condition. After oven-drying at **103 ± 2 °C** until constant mass, it weighs **1.750 kg**. Determine the moisture content. ### Given Data - Wet mass, $W_w = 2.450 \text{ kg}$ - Oven-dry mass, $W_d = 1.750 \text{ kg}$ ### Step 1: Recall the Definition By definition, moisture content is the ratio of the mass of water evaporated to the mass of oven-dry wood, expressed as a percentage. $$MC = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \;\; \text{(\%)}$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Mass of Water Evaporated The mass of water lost during drying is: $$M_{water} = W_w - W_d$$ $$M_{water} = 2.450 \text{ kg} - 1.750 \text{ kg}$$ $$M_{water} = 0.700 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute into the Formula $$MC = \frac{0.700}{1.750} \times 100 \;\; \text{(\%)}$$ ### Step 4: Perform the Division $$MC = 0.40 \times 100 \;\; \text{(\%)}$$ $$\boxed{MC = 40.0 \;\; \text{\%}}$$ ### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation > Since **40% > 30%** (the Fibre Saturation Point, FSP), this specimen is in the **green** condition. To use it for construction, the timber must be seasoned to bring MC down to **12 – 20%** to avoid shrinkage, warping, and fungal decay. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Safe Load-Carrying Capacity of a Timber Column (Euler's Formula with Slenderness Check) > **Problem Statement:** A solid timber column of Sal wood has a square cross-section of **150 mm × 150 mm** and a length of **3.0 m**. Both ends are **hinged**. Determine the safe (working) load using a Factor of Safety of **2.5** against buckling. Take $E = 12.0 \text{ GPa}$. ### Given Data - Side of square cross-section, $b = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$ - Length, $L = 3.0 \text{ m}$ - Both ends hinged → Effective length factor, $K = 1.0$ - $E = 12.0 \text{ GPa} = 12.0 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ - Factor of Safety, $F.S. = 2.5$ ### Step 1: Effective Length $$L_{eff} = K \cdot L = 1.0 \times 3.0 \text{ m} = 3.0 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 2: Moment of Inertia and Radius of Gyration (Square Section) For a square of side $b$: $$I_{min} = \frac{b^4}{12}$$ $$I_{min} = \frac{(0.150)^4}{12}$$ $$I_{min} = \frac{5.0625 \times 10^{-4}}{12}$$ $$I_{min} = 4.21875 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m}^4$$ The minimum radius of gyration: $$r_{min} = \frac{b}{\sqrt{12}}$$ $$r_{min} = \frac{0.150}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{0.150}{3.464}$$ $$r_{min} = 0.04330 \;\; \text{m}$$ ### Step 3: Slenderness Ratio $$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$ $$\lambda = \frac{3.0}{0.04330}$$ $$\lambda \approx 69.28$$ Since $\lambda > 60$ (typical transition value for timber), the column is treated as a **long (slender) column**, and **Euler's formula is valid.** ### Step 4: Euler's Critical Buckling Load $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I_{min}}{L_{eff}^2}$$ $$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 12.0 \times 10^9 \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-5}}{(3.0)^2}$$ $$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 12.0 \times 10^9 \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-5}}{9.0}$$ Let us compute the numerator carefully: $$9.8696 \times 12.0 \times 10^9 = 1.18435 \times 10^{11}$$ $$1.18435 \times 10^{11} \times 4.21875 \times 10^{-5} = 4.996 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{N}$$ Now divide by $L_{eff}^2 = 9.0$: $$P_{cr} = \frac{4.996 \times 10^{6}}{9.0}$$ $$P_{cr} \approx 5.551 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{N}$$ $$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 555.1 \;\; \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 5: Apply Factor of Safety to Get Safe Load $$P_{safe} = \frac{P_{cr}}{F.S.}$$ $$P_{safe} = \frac{555.1}{2.5}$$ $$\boxed{P_{safe} \approx 222.04 \;\; \text{kN}}$$ ### Step 6: Check for Crushing Stress Direct compressive stress at safe load: $$\sigma = \frac{P_{safe}}{A}$$ $$A = b^2 = (0.150)^2 = 0.0225 \;\; \text{m}^2$$ $$\sigma = \frac{222.04 \times 10^3}{0.0225}$$ $$\sigma = 9.868 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Pa} = 9.87 \;\; \text{MPa}$$ For Sal wood, $f_{c\parallel} \approx 45$ MPa. Since $9.87 \text{ MPa} \ll 45 \text{ MPa}$, **crushing is not critical** — buckling governs the design. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bending Capacity of a Timber Beam > **Problem:** A simply supported timber beam (Pine) of span **4.0 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **8 kN/m** (excluding self-weight). The cross-section is **200 mm wide × 300 mm deep**. Calculate the maximum bending stress and check if it is safe. Take $f_{b,allow} = 12$ MPa. ### Given Data - Span, $L = 4.0 \text{ m}$ - UDL, $w = 8 \text{ kN/m} = 8000 \text{ N/m}$ - Breadth, $b = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$ - Depth, $d = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$ - $f_{b,allow} = 12 \text{ MPa} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$ ### Step 1: Compute Maximum Bending Moment For a simply supported beam with UDL: $$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = \frac{8 \times (4.0)^2}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = \frac{128}{8} = 16.0 \;\; \text{kN·m}$$ $$M_{max} = 16.0 \times 10^3 \;\; \text{N·m}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Section Modulus For a rectangular section: $$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6}$$ $$Z = \frac{0.200 \times (0.300)^2}{6}$$ $$Z = \frac{0.200 \times 0.0900}{6}$$ $$Z = \frac{0.0180}{6}$$ $$Z = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^3$$ ### Step 3: Maximum Bending Stress $$f_{b,max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z}$$ $$f_{b,max} = \frac{16.0 \times 10^3}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$f_{b,max} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{f_{b,max} = 5.33 \;\; \text{MPa}}$$ ### Step 4: Safety Check $$f_{b,max} = 5.33 \text{ MPa} \; < \; f_{b,allow} = 12.0 \text{ MPa} \;\; \checkmark$$ **Result:** The beam is **SAFE** in bending. ### Step 5: (Bonus) Self-Weight Check Volume per meter length = $0.200 \times 0.300 = 0.060 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$ Assuming pine density $\rho = 500 \text{ kg/m}^3$: $$w_{sw} = 0.060 \times 500 \times 9.81 = 294.3 \text{ N/m} \approx 0.29 \text{ kN/m}$$ Total UDL: $w = 8 + 0.29 = 8.29 \text{ kN/m}$. The self-weight contribution is **negligible** (< 4% of the imposed load). --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart: Timber Procurement to Construction (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the full lifecycle of structural timber from forest to final structural member. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Forest Resource
Felling of Mature Trees] --> B[Conversion in Sawmill
Planks Beams Logs] B --> C[Initial Inspection
Defect Sorting] C --> D1[Seasoning Process
Air Kiln Chemical] C --> D2[Preservation Treatment
CCA Creosote ASCU] D1 --> E[Quality Grading
IS 1331 IS 1700] D2 --> E E --> F[Engineering Design
Section Size Joints] F --> G[Fabrication Workshop
Cutting Shaping Drilling] G --> H[Site Erection
Beam Column Truss] H --> I[Finished Timber Structure] I --> J[Periodic Maintenance
Repair Retrofit] style A fill:#E0F2E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style B fill:#E0F2E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style D1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style D2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100 style E fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style F fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style I fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Tree: Selection of Seasoning Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Newly Converted Timber Stock] --> B{Quantity Required?} B -- Small Scale --> C{Time Available?} B -- Large Scale --> D{Budget Available?} C -- Long Time --> E[Air Seasoning] C -- Short Time --> F[Kiln Seasoning] D -- High --> G[Pressure Preservation
plus Kiln Seasoning] D -- Low --> H[Open Tank
plus Air Seasoning] style A fill:#E1F5FE style E fill:#C8E6C9 style F fill:#FFE0B2 style G fill:#F8BBD0 style H fill:#DCEDC8 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Typical Timber Formwork for an RCC Beam ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vertical Prop
Timber Post 100x100] --> B[Sole Plate
Base 200x40] B --> C[Bearer / Joist
Horizontal 100x80] C --> D[Ledger / Bearer
Second Tier 80x60] D --> E[Plywood Sheathing
12-18 mm Thickness] E --> F[Beam Side Shutter
Timber Planks 40 mm] F --> G[Tie Rods and
Walers for Side Stability] style A fill:#FFF9C4 style E fill:#FFCCBC style G fill:#C5E1A5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Schematic:** The prop transmits the load of the wet concrete **vertically upward** through the **sole plate** (which spreads it over a larger floor area to prevent sinking). The horizontal **bearers** distribute this load laterally to the **plywood sheathing** that actually shapes the concrete beam. **Tie rods and walers** prevent the side shutters from bursting outward under the hydrostatic pressure of fresh concrete. ## 4.4 Concept Map: Major Categories of Timber Defects ```mermaid mindmap root((Timber Defects)) Natural Knots Shakes Wind Cracks Conversion Diagonal Grain Wane Torn Grain Seasoning Bow Cup Twist Check Biological Dry Rot Wet Rot Termites Borers ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define *Fibre Saturation Point* (FSP) of timber. Why is it significant in timber construction? > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]** **Model Answer:** The **Fibre Saturation Point (FSP)** is the moisture content at which all the free water in the cell cavities of timber has evaporated, but the cell walls are still fully saturated with **bound water**. For most species, FSP lies in the range of **25 – 30%** (average ≈ 28%). **Significance:** 1. **Shrinkage begins only below FSP.** Timber does not shrink or swell above FSP; dimensional changes occur only when MC falls below it. 2. **Strength increases below FSP.** As bound water leaves the cell walls, the fibres come closer, dramatically increasing strength (e.g., bending strength roughly doubles from green to oven-dry condition). 3. **FSP is the reference point** for calculating dimensional changes (using shrinkage coefficients) and for quality control of seasoned timber. --- ### **Q2.** Distinguish between **Seasoning** and **Preservation** of timber. Give one example of each process. > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Seasoning | Preservation | |--------|-----------|--------------| | **Purpose** | Removal of moisture to improve stability and strength | Protection against decay, fungi, insects, fire | | **Effect on MC** | **Reduces** moisture content (12–20%) | Does **not** change MC significantly | | **Process** | Drying (natural or artificial) | Chemical treatment with preservatives | | **Example** | **Air seasoning** (stacking in well-ventilated shed) | **Pressure treatment** with Copper-Chrome-Arsenate (CCA) | | **Cost** | Low to moderate | Moderate to high | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choose this OR Question B]** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **defects in timber** classified under five different categories. For each category, describe the cause, appearance, and effect on the strength of the timber. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A timber beam of Teak wood, simply supported over a span of **5.0 m**, has a rectangular cross-section of **120 mm × 240 mm** (width × depth). It carries a uniformly distributed load of **6 kN/m** over the entire span (excluding self-weight). Calculate the **maximum bending stress** developed in the beam. Take the density of Teak as **650 kg/m³** and allowable bending stress $f_{b,allow} = 15$ MPa. Check whether the beam is safe in bending. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Defects in Timber (7 Marks) Timber defects are irregularities that reduce its strength, durability, appearance, or workability. They are classified as follows: **1. Defects due to Natural Forces (during growth):** - **Knots:** Branch bases embedded in the trunk. *Cause:* tree branch died but trunk continued to grow around it. *Effect:* disturb the continuity of fibers, causing **stress concentrations**; knots are classified as live (firmly attached) and dead (loose) — dead knots are more harmful. - **Shakes:** Separations along the grain between annual rings. Sub-types: **Cup shake, Star shake, Ring shake, Heart shake**. *Cause:* uneven growth, frost, or poor felling. *Effect:* reduce shear strength. - **Wind Cracks / Upsets:** Longitudinal separations due to unequal shrinkage or growth stresses. *Effect:* reduce effective cross-section. **2. Defects due to Defective Seasoning:** - **Bow, Cup, Twist:** Distortion of cross-section due to uneven drying. - **Checks:** Surface cracks along the grain. - **Splits:** Through-thickness cracks. - *Effect:* all reduce dimensional accuracy and structural integrity. **3. Defects due to Defective Conversion (sawing):** - **Diagonal Grain:** Wood fibers not parallel to the longitudinal axis. - **Wane:** Bark or lack of wood on one or more edges. - **Torn Grain:** Surface torn by faulty sawing. - *Effect:* reduces effective strength and aesthetic appeal. **4. Defects due to Fungal Attack:** - **Dry Rot:** Fungal decay in dry, well-ventilated timber — extremely destructive. - **Wet Rot:** Decay in damp, poorly ventilated timber. - **Blue Stain:** Discolouration without strength loss (sap-stain fungi). - *Effect:* severe loss of strength, eventual crumbling of wood. **5. Defects due to Insect Attack:** - **Termites:** Subterranean and dry-wood species — destroy timber from inside. - **Borer Beetles:** Powder-post beetles leave 1–2 mm holes with fine dust. - **Marine Borers:** Attack timber in marine environments. - *Effect:* honeycombing and total structural collapse. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute Self-Weight** Volume per meter length: $V = 0.120 \times 0.240 = 0.0288 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$ Self-weight per meter: $w_{sw} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V = 650 \times 9.81 \times 0.0288 = 183.7 \text{ N/m} = 0.184 \text{ kN/m}$ **Step 2: Total UDL** $w_{total} = 6.0 + 0.184 = 6.184 \text{ kN/m}$ **Step 3: Maximum Bending Moment** $M_{max} = \dfrac{w_{total} L^2}{8} = \dfrac{6.184 \times (5.0)^2}{8} = \dfrac{154.6}{8} = 19.325 \text{ kN·m} = 19.325 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$ **Step 4: Section Modulus** $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.120 \times (0.240)^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.120 \times 0.0576}{6} = \dfrac{0.006912}{6} = 1.152 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ **Step 5: Maximum Bending Stress** $f_{b,max} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z} = \dfrac{19.325 \times 10^3}{1.152 \times 10^{-3}} = 16.78 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 16.78 \text{ MPa}$ **Step 6: Safety Check** $f_{b,max} = 16.78 \text{ MPa} \; > \; f_{b,allow} = 15.0 \text{ MPa}$ **Result:** The beam is **NOT SAFE** in bending. The induced stress exceeds the allowable by **~11.9%**. **Remedial Suggestion:** Increase the depth to at least 255 mm, or use a hardwood of higher allowable stress. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative to Question A]** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 2]** **(a)** Describe with neat sketches the **classification of timber joints** commonly used in construction. Explain any three traditional joints with their suitability and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A timber column of Sal wood has a square cross-section of **100 mm × 100 mm** and an unsupported length of **2.5 m**. Both ends are **fixed**. Determine the **safe compressive load** using Euler's formula with a Factor of Safety of **3.0**. Take $E = 12.5 \text{ GPa}$. Check the column for **slenderness** and confirm the validity of Euler's formula. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ##### Part (a) — Timber Joints (7 Marks) Timber joints are classified into: **1. Lengthening Joints** — used to join members end-to-end - **Scarf Joints:** Plain scarf, hooked scarf, table scarf, fished scarf. - **Spliced Joints:** With keys, wedges, or fish plates. **2. Bearing Joints** — used to transfer compressive loads - **Butt Joint, Bevel Joint, Notched Joint.** **3. Widening Joints** — used to join boards side-by-side - **Tongue and Groove, Rebated, Dowelled, Glued.** **4. Framing / Angle Joints** — used at corners - **Mortise & Tenon, Dovetail, Halved, Cross-Lap, Bridle, Housed, Tusk-Tenon.** **5. Modern Joints** - **Nailed, Screwed, Bolted, Glued, Ring-Connectors, Tooth-Plate (Truss) Connectors, Steel Plates (gusset plates).** **Description of Three Traditional Joints:** **(i) Mortise and Tenon Joint:** A rectangular projection (tenon) cut on one piece fits into a corresponding cavity (mortise) on the other. It is secured with a **dowelpin or wedge**. *Suitability:* door/window frames, table legs. *Limitation:* weakens both members (reduces effective section by 30–40%). **(ii) Dovetail Joint:** A fan-shaped tenon interlocks with a matching mortise. *Suitability:* drawer construction, heavy boxes — provides high resistance to **pull-out**. *Limitation:* difficult to machine, requires skilled carpentry. **(iii) Halved Joint (Cross-Lap):** Both members are notched to half their depth and interlocked. *Suitability:* light framing, partition intersections. *Limitation:* reduces effective depth; not suitable for heavy loads. ##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - Square cross-section, $b = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$ - Length, $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$ - Both ends fixed → Effective length factor, $K = 0.5$ - $E = 12.5 \text{ GPa} = 12.5 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ - $F.S. = 3.0$ **Step 1: Effective Length** $L_{eff} = K L = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25 \text{ m}$ **Step 2: Moment of Inertia** $I_{min} = \dfrac{b^4}{12} = \dfrac{(0.100)^4}{12} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 8.333 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$ **Step 3: Minimum Radius of Gyration** $r_{min} = \dfrac{b}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{0.100}{3.464} = 0.02887 \text{ m}$ **Step 4: Slenderness Ratio** $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.02887} = 43.30$ **Step 5: Validity Check** For a timber column, Euler's formula is valid for $\lambda > 60$ (slender column). Since $\lambda = 43.30 < 60$, this is **not** a long column — Euler's formula is **NOT strictly valid** in its basic form. However, applying it as an academic exercise: **Step 6: Euler's Critical Load** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_{eff}^2} = \dfrac{(9.8696) \times (12.5 \times 10^9) \times (8.333 \times 10^{-6})}{(1.25)^2}$ Numerator: $9.8696 \times 12.5 \times 10^9 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0281 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$ Denominator: $1.5625$ $P_{cr} = \dfrac{1.0281 \times 10^6}{1.5625} = 6.580 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 658.0 \text{ kN}$ **Step 7: Safe Load** $P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{F.S.} = \dfrac{658.0}{3.0} = 219.3 \text{ kN}$ **Step 8: Crushing Check** $A = (0.100)^2 = 0.0100 \text{ m}^2$ $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{safe}}{A} = \dfrac{219.3 \times 10^3}{0.0100} = 21.93 \text{ MPa}$ For Sal wood, $f_{c\parallel} \approx 45$ MPa. Since $21.93 \text{ MPa} < 45 \text{ MPa}$, **crushing is safe** even though the slenderness ratio is below the Euler limit. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Highly Penalized Mistake):** > Many students blindly apply Euler's formula **without checking the slenderness ratio**. KTU examiners specifically test whether you verify that the column is **actually slender ($\lambda \geq 60$ for timber)** before applying $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / L_{eff}^2$. If $\lambda < 60$, you must use a **medium column formula** (e.g., Rankine's or IS 883). In this problem, the **correct conclusion is that Euler's formula is not applicable** — and a full-mark answer must explicitly state this and use a Rankine-type approach or the IS 883 empirical formula. The above numerical is shown for **pedagogical demonstration only**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this list — these are the 25 most-asked points on Timber Construction in KTU exams.** - [x] **Timber** = natural, anisotropic, hygroscopic, organic composite. - [x] **Exogenous trees** = outside growing (rings); **Endogenous** = inside (bamboo). - [x] **Softwood (Conifer)** ≠ soft; **Hardwood (Deciduous)** ≠ hard — *botanical classification*. - [x] **Fibre Saturation Point (FSP) ≈ 25–30%** — below this, shrinkage and strength increase occur. - [x] **Moisture Content formula:** $MC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ - [x] **Seasoning = drying.** **Preservation = chemical protection.** - [x] **Air seasoning** = cheapest, slowest. **Kiln seasoning** = fastest, most uniform. - [x] **Creosote** = best oily preservative; **CCA** = best water-soluble salt. - [x] **Pressure processes** = Bethell (full-cell), Rueping (empty-cell), Lowry (modified empty-cell). - [x] **Defects** = 5 categories: Natural, Conversion, Seasoning, Fungal, Insect. - [x] **Dry rot** = most destructive fungus; **Termites** = most destructive insect. - [x] **Allowable stress** = Ultimate stress / Factor of Safety (F.S. typically **2.0 – 2.5**). - [x] **Bending stress:** $f_b = M / Z$; **Section modulus of rectangle:** $Z = b d^2 / 6$. - [x] **Deflection of simply supported beam with UDL:** $\delta_{max} = 5 w L^4 / (384 EI)$. - [x] **Euler's formula valid only for long columns.** For timber, $\lambda \geq 60$. - [x] **Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = L_{eff} / r_{min}$. - [x] **Effective length factor K:** Hinged-hinged = 1.0; Fixed-fixed = 0.5; Fixed-hinged = 0.7; Fixed-free = 2.0. - [x] **Timber is 3–4 times stronger along the grain** than across the grain. - [x] **Bearing stress perpendicular to grain ≈ 1/3 of parallel-to-grain value.** - [x] **Plywood** is made of veneers glued with **alternate grain at 90°** — balanced strength. - [x] **Glulam** = Glue-Laminated Timber; **CLT** = Cross-Laminated Timber; **LVL** = Laminated Veneer Lumber. - [x] **Formwork** = temporary mould for concrete; prop → bearer → sheathing → side shutters. - [x] **Standard modulus of elasticity for Sal/Teak** ≈ 10–15 GPa; **density** ≈ 600–800 kg/m³. - [x] **Indian Standards (BIS) for timber:** IS 399, IS 883, IS 1700, IS 1331. - [x] **Always** check BOTH bending **and** deflection; **and** slenderness for columns — KTU problems test all three.

topics.title: Steel Component Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steel Component Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steel Component Production** within the context of *Field and Service Robotics (PCRUT602)* refers to the integrated, semi- or fully-automated manufacturing of discrete steel parts (beams, plates, pipes, sheet-metal enclosures, structural brackets, forgings, castings, and welded assemblies) using **robotic manipulators, CNC machines, automated guided vehicles (AGVs), vision systems, and sensor-fused process controllers**.

The robotic value chain spans the entire production lifecycle:

1. **Primary material handling** — billet/slab loading into furnaces, roller tables, and presses.
2. **Forming & shaping** — robotic forging, hot/cold rolling, stamping, hydroforming, bending, and spinning.
3. **Joining** — robotic arc welding (GMAW/GTAW), spot welding, stud welding, and laser hybrid welding.
4. **Machining & finishing** — robotic deburring, grinding, polishing, shot-peening, and CNC milling/turning.
5. **Inspection & quality control** — robotic 3-D scanning, ultrasonic testing (UT), dye-penetrant inspection, and dimensional metrology.
6. **Palletizing & dispatch** — robotic stacking, strapping, and AGV-based transport to storage bays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Lens:** In Module 2, the topic is treated as an *application domain* of field and service robotics. The emphasis is on **how robotic systems (manipulators, end-effectors, sensors, and controllers) are configured to operate in harsh, high-temperature, heavy-load steel mill environments**, and the kinematic/dynamic/control considerations unique to such deployments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a giant **orchestra**. Each musician (robot, furnace, conveyor, sensor) plays a different instrument, but they all follow a single **conductor** — the **Manufacturing Execution System (MES)** / **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)**.

| Orchestra Element | Steel Plant Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Conductor (baton in hand) | Central SCADA / PLC controller |
| Violin section | Articulated 6-axis robotic welding arms |
| Percussion (heavy, loud) | Hydraulic forging press & rolling mill |
| Sheet-music readers | Vision systems & laser trackers |
| Stage hands moving pianos | AGVs and overhead cranes |

A **human steelworker** faces extreme heat (~1500 °C), heavy payloads (multi-ton coils), toxic fumes, and dangerous sparks. A **robot** doesn't get tired, doesn't flinch at 60 dB+ noise, and can repeat the same welding bead with **±0.05 mm tolerance** for an 8-hour shift. **That is why robotic automation is not a luxury in modern steel component production — it is a safety and quality necessity.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are referenced throughout steel-component robotic work:

- **Steel melting point: ≈ 1538 °C** (pure iron–carbon eutectoid at 0.77 % C ≈ 727 °C).
- **Density of mild steel: ρ = 7850 kg/m³**.
- **Young's modulus of structural steel: E = 200 GPa**.
- **Yield strength of A36/A283 mild steel: σᵧ ≈ 250 MPa**.
- **Thermal expansion coefficient: α ≈ 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**.
- **Standard payload classes for steel-mill robots: 6 kg – 800 kg** (e.g., ABB IRB 8700, KUKA KR 1000 Titan).
- **Reach envelopes: 1.5 m – 5.0 m** for floor-mounted units; gantry-style units can exceed **15 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to recall **payload, reach, repeatability (typically ±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm for arc-welding robots)**, and the **number of axes (commonly 6, occasionally 7 for complex weld seams on steel brackets)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Robotic Production Stack — Layered Architecture

Steel component production via robotics can be decomposed into **five functional layers**. Each layer communicates with the adjacent ones through standardized industrial protocols (e.g., **OPC-UA, PROFINET, EtherCAT, DeviceNet**).

### Layer 1 — Mechanical & End-Effector Layer
- **Robot arm (articulated / gantry / SCARA / delta)**
- **End-of-arm tooling (EOAT)**: weld torches (gas metal arc, tungsten inert gas), grippers (magnetic, mechanical, vacuum), grinders, sensors.
- **Tool center point (TCP)** calibration is mandatory — error < 1 mm for welding.

### Layer 2 — Drive & Transmission Layer
- Servo motors (typically **AC synchronous**), harmonic-drive gearboxes, RV reducers.
- Peak torque requirements at the wrist can exceed **10,000 N·m** for heavy-payload steel handling.

### Layer 3 — Controller Layer
- Industrial robot controller (e.g., ABB OmniCore, KUKA KR C5, FANUC R-30iB Plus).
- Real-time OS (VxWorks, RTLinux) executing trajectories at **4 ms – 8 ms** cycle time.
- Path planning: joint-interpolated (PTP), linear (LIN), circular (CIRC), spline blending.

### Layer 4 — Sensor & Perception Layer
- **Force/torque sensors** at wrist → adaptive grinding & constant-contact deburring.
- **Arc sensors** in welding torch → seam tracking via current feedback.
- **Laser line scanners / structured-light 3-D cameras** → pick-and-place of randomly piled steel billets.
- **Pyrometers & thermal cameras** → temperature feedback in hot rolling (up to 1200 °C).

### Layer 5 — Plant-Wide Coordination Layer
- **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** for job dispatch.
- **SCADA** for plant-wide visualization.
- **Digital twin** (Siemens Mechatronics Concept Designer, ABB RobotStudio) for offline programming & cycle-time optimization.

---

## 2.2 Kinematic & Static Considerations for Steel-Handling Robots

### 2.2.1 Payload, Reach & Joint Torque

For a 6-DOF articulated robot, the wrist torque requirement is governed by:

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=i}^{n} \left[ \mathbf{r}_{j/i} \times m_j \mathbf{g} + \mathbf{I}_j \boldsymbol{\alpha}_j + \boldsymbol{\omega}_j \times (\mathbf{I}_j \boldsymbol{\omega}_j) \right]$$

where **rⱼ/ᵢ** is the moment arm of link *j* about joint *i*, **mⱼ** is the mass of link *j* plus payload, **Iⱼ** is the inertia tensor, **ωⱼ** and **αⱼ** are the angular velocity and acceleration, and **g** is gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a steel-handling robot carrying a **500 kg billet** at full reach of **3.0 m**, the shoulder joint must produce a holding torque of approximately:
>
> $$\tau \approx m \cdot g \cdot L = 500 \times 9.81 \times 3.0 \approx 14{,}715 \text{ N·m}$$
>
> This is why steel-handling robots are **hydraulically actuated** rather than electrically driven beyond ~1-tonne payloads.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Distortion Compensation

Steel components, especially freshly rolled or forged pieces, radiate heat and cause **thermal expansion** of the steel itself *and* the robot's structural links. The dimensional compensation is:

$$\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T$$

where **α = 12 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**, **L₀** is nominal length, and **ΔT** is temperature rise. For a 10 m steel beam heating from 20 °C to 200 °C:

$$\Delta L = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 180 = 0.0216 \text{ m} = 21.6 \text{ mm}$$

This thermal growth is critical in **robotic trimming/cutting of hot rolled sections** and is corrected in real time using **closed-loop thermal compensation tables** in the controller.

### 2.2.3 Welding Specific Energy & Heat Input

For robotic arc welding of steel, the heat input per unit length is:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

where:
- **η** = arc efficiency (0.6 – 0.85 for GMAW)
- **V** = arc voltage (volts)
- **I** = welding current (amperes)
- **v** = travel speed (mm/s)
- **H** = heat input (J/mm)

Excessive **H** causes **heat-affected zone (HAZ) softening**, distortion, and burn-through — the most common KTU pitfall in robotic welding numericals.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint torque $\tau = m g L \cos\theta$ | m, g, L, θ | N·m, kg, m, rad | Holding torque at a single joint |
| 2 | Payload limit $m_p = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{g \cdot L}$ | τ, g, L | N·m, m/s², m | Compute max payload at full reach |
| 3 | Heat input $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ | η, V, I, v | J/mm, V, A, mm/s | Robotic arc welding parameter |
| 4 | Cooling rate $R = \dfrac{2\pi k (T - T_0)^2}{H}$ | k, T, T₀, H | °C/s, W/m·K, J/mm | Predict HAZ microstructure |
| 5 | Thermal expansion $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | α, L, ΔT | m, /°C, °C | Hot-rolled length compensation |
| 6 | Repeatability $R_p$ | statistical radius | mm | Welding quality benchmark |
| 7 | Cycle time $t_c = t_{motion} + t_{process} + t_{IO}$ | time components | s | Production throughput |
| 8 | Stiffness deflection $\delta = \dfrac{F L^3}{3 E I}$ | F, L, E, I | m, N, m, Pa, m⁴ | Robot link / fixture flexure |
| 9 | Throughput $\Phi = \dfrac{3600}{t_c}$ | t_c | parts/hour | Production line capacity |
| 10 | OEE $= A \times P \times Q$ | Availability, Performance, Quality | % | Plant efficiency metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU 2024 students:** Memorize formulas #2, #3, #5, and #10 — these appear in **>70 %** of past paper numericals on robotic manufacturing.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Robotic Application in Steel Production |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Robotic spot welding of car-body steel panels (a typical car has ~5000 spot welds) |
| **Shipbuilding** | Robotic GMAW of thick steel plates (up to 50 mm) for hull sections |
| **Construction** | Robotic rebar tying and prefabricated steel-beam assembly |
| **Energy (wind)** | Robotic grinding/polishing of large steel hubs and tower flanges |
| **Heavy machinery** | Robotic forging of steel crankshafts, axles, and gear blanks |
| **Pipelines** | Robotic circumferential welding of steel pipe spools |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Robotic Wrist Torque for a Steel-Handling Task

**Problem setup:** A 6-axis articulated robot must transfer a hot steel slab of mass **m = 250 kg** at the end-effector. The slab's center of mass is offset **L = 1.8 m** from the shoulder joint axis. The shoulder joint makes an angle **θ = 45°** with respect to the vertical.

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant torque component.**

At quasi-static holding (no acceleration), the inertial and Coriolis terms vanish. Only the gravitational term survives:

$$\tau_{shoulder} = m \, g \, L \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\tau_{shoulder} = 250 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 \times \cos(45°)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate trigonometric term.**

$$\cos(45°) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$$

**Step 4 — Multiply step by step.**

$$\tau_{shoulder} = 250 \times 9.81 \times 1.8 \times 0.7071$$

$$\tau_{shoulder} = 250 \times 9.81 = 2452.5 \text{ (N·m/m)}$$

$$\tau_{shoulder} = 2452.5 \times 1.8 = 4414.5 \text{ (N·m)}$$

$$\tau_{shoulder} = 4414.5 \times 0.7071 \approx 3121.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Add a safety factor.**

Standard industrial practice applies a **service factor SF = 1.5**:

$$\tau_{design} = SF \times \tau_{shoulder} = 1.5 \times 3121.5 \approx 4682 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Select a robot.**

A robot with a rated shoulder torque **≥ 5000 N·m** is selected. The **KUKA KR 1000 L750 Titan** (1000 kg payload) or **ABB IRB 8700** (800 kg payload) are typical fits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always include the **service factor** in board numericals — failure to do so is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** by KTU examiners.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Robotic Arc Welding Heat Input & Cooling

**Given:** Robotic GMAW of a 6 mm thick mild-steel plate. Voltage V = 24 V, current I = 200 A, travel speed v = 5 mm/s, arc efficiency η = 0.75.

**Step 1 — Compute heat input H.**

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v}$$

$$H = \frac{0.75 \times 24 \times 200}{5}$$

$$H = \frac{3600}{5} = 720 \text{ J/mm}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret against the standard range.**

For 6 mm mild steel, acceptable H is **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. **720 J/mm = 0.72 kJ/mm** is well within range.

**Step 3 — Estimate cooling rate R.**

Thermal conductivity of mild steel k ≈ 45 W/m·K. Difference between peak temperature (≈ 1500 °C) and preheat (≈ 20 °C):

$$R \approx \frac{2 \pi k (T - T_0)^2}{H}$$

$$R = \frac{2 \pi \times 45 \times (1480)^2}{720}$$

$$(1480)^2 = 2{,}190{,}400$$

$$2\pi \times 45 \approx 282.74$$

$$R = \frac{282.74 \times 2{,}190{,}400}{720}$$

$$R = \frac{6.193 \times 10^8}{720}$$

$$R \approx 8.60 \times 10^5 \text{ °C/s}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret microstructural outcome.**

For plain-carbon steel, a cooling rate of ~10⁵ – 10⁶ °C/s at the weld produces **fine-grained ferrite–pearlite** with acceptable toughness. Too slow → coarse grains → brittle HAZ.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Robotic Process Selection for Steel Components

| Steel Component | Primary Process | Recommended Robot Type | Key Sensor | Typical Cycle Time | Common Defect Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Car body panel | Spot welding | 6-axis, 6 – 8 kg payload | Force, current | 1.5 s/weld | Expulsion, weak nugget |
| Pressure vessel | GMAW circumferential seam | 6-axis, heavy-payload, positioner | Arc sensor, seam tracker | 8 – 15 min/circumference | Porosity, undercut |
| Steel billet forging | Material handling | Hydraulic 6-axis, 1 t payload | Force, vision | 30 s/billet | Misplacement, die crash |
| Ship hull plate | Hybrid laser-arc welding | Gantry + 6-axis, multi-torch | Laser seam tracker, IR camera | 1 – 2 m/min | Lack of fusion |
| Rebar cage | Tying | Custom 4-axis portal | Vision | 1 s/tie | Mis-tie, slippage |
| Steel pipe spool | Multi-pass GTAW | 6-axis + rotary positioner | Vision, current | 30 – 60 min/spool | Slag inclusion |
| Forged crankshaft | Robotic deburring & polishing | 6-axis medium payload | Force-compliance | 5 – 10 min/shaft | Surface roughness |
| Hot-rolled coil | Palletizing & strapping | 4-axis high-payload | Vision, barcode | 45 s/coil | Coil damage |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Robotic Steel Component Production Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Central SCADA / MES] --> B[Robot Controller]
    B --> C[Servo Drives]
    C --> D[Articulated 6-axis Robot]
    D --> E[End-of-Arm Tooling]
    E --> F[Steel Component / Workpiece]
    G[Vision System] --> B
    H[Force Torque Sensor] --> B
    I[Welding Power Source] --> E
    J[Safety Light Curtain] --> B
    K[Pyrometer / Thermal Camera] --> B
    L[AGV Conveyor] --> A
    M[Digital Twin Software] --> B
```

**Functional description of each block:**

- **A — Central SCADA / MES:** Plant-level coordinator, dispatches jobs, tracks production.
- **B — Robot Controller:** Real-time path planning, kinematics, I/O handling.
- **C — Servo Drives:** High-bandwidth current/voltage regulation for each joint motor.
- **D — Articulated 6-axis Robot:** Mechanical manipulator with 6 revolute joints.
- **E — End-of-Arm Tooling:** Welding torch / gripper / grinder / sensor bracket.
- **F — Steel Component / Workpiece:** The billet, plate, or assembly being processed.
- **G — Vision System:** 2-D / 3-D camera for part recognition & seam tracking.
- **H — Force/Torque Sensor:** Wrist-mounted for adaptive grinding and assembly.
- **I — Welding Power Source:** Delivers V, I to the welding torch (GMAW/GTAW).
- **J — Safety Light Curtain:** Hard-wired E-stop barrier around the cell.
- **K — Pyrometer / Thermal Camera:** Monitors workpiece temperature for hot rolling.
- **L — AGV Conveyor:** Transports workpieces between cells.
- **M — Digital Twin Software:** Offline simulation (RobotStudio, Process Simulate).

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Robotic Arc-Welding Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Cycle] --> S2[Robot reads Job ID from MES]
    S2 --> S3[Robot moves to Home Position]
    S3 --> S4[Vision System scans Part Position]
    S4 --> S5[Controller computes Seam Path]
    S5 --> S6[Robot approaches with TCP Calibration]
    S6 --> S7[Arc Sensor enables Seam Tracking]
    S7 --> S8[Robot executes Welding Pass 1]
    S8 --> S9{Temperature OK?}
    S9 -- No --> S10[Wait / Apply Interpass Cooling]
    S10 --> S8
    S9 -- Yes --> S11[Robot executes Welding Pass 2]
    S11 --> S12[Robot retracts and Gas Purges]
    S12 --> S13[QC Scan: 3D Profilometer]
    S13 --> S14{QC Pass?}
    S14 -- Yes --> S15[Robot signals Part Complete to MES]
    S14 -- No --> S16[Flag Part for Rework]
    S15 --> S17[End Cycle]
    S16 --> S17
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The loop *S9 → S10 → S8* enforces an **interpass temperature limit** (commonly ≤ 250 °C for mild-steel multi-pass GMAW). This prevents HAZ softening.
- The branch *S14* implements **closed-loop quality control**. In KTU board parlance, this is called a **"feedback-corrective robotic cell."**

---

## 4.3 Concept Map — Robotic Functions in Steel Production

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Robotic Functions in Steel Production]
    R --> H[Material Handling]
    R --> F[Forming and Shaping]
    R --> J[Joining and Welding]
    R --> M[Machining and Finishing]
    R --> I[Inspection and QC]
    R --> P[Palletizing and Logistics]

    H --> H1[Billets / Slabs]
    H --> H2[Coil Handling]
    H --> H3[Press Tending]

    F --> F1[Robotic Forging]
    F --> F2[Roll Forming]
    F --> F3[Hydroforming]
    F --> F4[Spinning]

    J --> J1[GMAW MIG]
    J --> J2[GTAW TIG]
    J --> J3[Spot Welding]
    J --> J4[Stud Welding]
    J --> J5[Laser Hybrid]

    M --> M1[Deburring]
    M --> M2[Grinding]
    M --> M3[Polishing]
    M --> M4[Shot Peening]

    I --> I1[3D Scanning]
    I --> I2[Ultrasonic Testing]
    I --> I3[Dye Penetrant]
    I --> I4[Dimensional Metrology]

    P --> P1[Stacking]
    P --> P2[Strapping]
    P --> P3[AGV Transfer]
    P --> P4[Warehouse Storage]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three robotic processes used in steel component production.**

**Model Answer (target length: 3 – 4 lines per item):**

1. **Robotic Arc Welding (GMAW/GTAW):** A 6-axis articulated robot carries the welding torch along a programmed seam. An arc sensor or laser seam tracker provides real-time feedback so the torch stays centered on the joint despite thermal distortion. This guarantees consistent weld quality on parts such as pressure vessels and ship hull plates.

2. **Robotic Material Handling:** Heavy-payload (1 – 2 tonne) hydraulic robots transfer hot steel billets (≈ 1000 °C) between furnace, press, and roller table. Magnetic or mechanical grippers handle slabs, blooms, and coils, eliminating operator exposure to heat and pinch points.

3. **Robotic Deburring & Grinding:** A force/torque-controlled robot holds a powered abrasive tool against forged or cast steel surfaces. Compliance control maintains constant contact force, ensuring uniform surface finish and removing manual operator fatigue hazards.

> **Examiner's note:** Award 1 mark per correctly identified process with a relevant sensor or application; 0 marks for naming without explanation.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is repeatability a critical specification for a robotic arc-welding cell in steel production?**

**Model Answer:**

Repeatability quantifies the robot's ability to return to a taught pose under identical conditions. For arc welding of steel, the weld pool solidifies in milliseconds and even a 0.5 mm torch offset can cause **porosity, undercut, or lack of fusion** — all of which fail radiographic or ultrasonic inspection.

A typical industrial robotic welder requires repeatability of **±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm** to satisfy standards such as **AWS D1.1 (structural welding code – steel)** and **ISO 3834**. Without this precision, weld seams in critical applications (bridges, pressure vessels, offshore platforms) cannot be certified, making repeatability a non-negotiable specification rather than a "nice-to-have."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the layered architecture of a robotic steel component production cell with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **MES, SCADA, robot controller, and digital twin**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A robotic arc-welding cell is used to weld 8 mm thick mild-steel plates. Process parameters are: V = 26 V, I = 220 A, travel speed = 4 mm/s, arc efficiency = 0.80. Compute: (i) heat input per unit length, (ii) comment on whether this heat input is suitable for the chosen thickness. Standard heat input range for 8 mm mild steel is **0.8 – 2.0 kJ/mm**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Layered Architecture:**

The cell is organized into **five functional layers**:

| Layer | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Mechanical | Robot, EOAT, fixtures, conveyors | ABB IRB 6700 + welding torch |
| 2. Drive & Sensor | Servo drives, force/torque sensor, vision | EtherCAT servo bus |
| 3. Controller | Trajectory planning, kinematics, I/O | ABB OmniCore |
| 4. Supervisory | Job dispatch, line balancing | MES + SCADA |
| 5. Optimization | Offline programming, cycle-time analysis | Digital twin (RobotStudio) |

The **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** issues work orders, tracks each steel component by barcode/RFID, and synchronizes upstream/downstream cells. The **SCADA** layer provides plant-wide visualization, alarms, and historian logging. The **robot controller** executes real-time motion at 4 ms cycles. The **digital twin** allows the engineer to program and validate the cell offline — detecting reach singularities, collisions, and cycle-time violations before physical commissioning.

**(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for MES/SCADA explanation + 2 marks for digital twin)**

**Part (b) — Heat Input Calculation:**

(i) Heat input:

$$H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v} = \frac{0.80 \times 26 \times 220}{4}$$

$$H = \frac{4576}{4} = 1144 \text{ J/mm} = 1.144 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

(ii) **Comparison:** The acceptable range for 8 mm mild steel is **0.8 – 2.0 kJ/mm**. Computed H = **1.144 kJ/mm** lies comfortably inside this range. Therefore the parameter set is **acceptable**, producing a balance between penetration (sufficient H to fuse the joint) and HAZ integrity (not so much H as to cause excessive grain growth or distortion).

**(3 marks for substitution & arithmetic, 2 marks for unit conversion, 2 marks for comparison & comment)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Describe with neat sketches the role of **robotic material handling** in a steel mill. Discuss the type of grippers used and the safety considerations specific to hot steel handling. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 6-axis robot arm must lift a hot steel slab of mass **m = 800 kg**. The slab's center of mass is offset **L = 2.5 m** from the shoulder joint, and the shoulder joint is at an angle **θ = 60°** with the vertical. Compute the static shoulder torque required. Apply a service factor of 1.5 and recommend a robot from the KUKA heavy-payload family. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Robotic Material Handling in a Steel Mill:**

A typical steel mill features **continuous material flow** between furnace, rolling stand, cooling bed, and finishing line. Robots and AGVs replace manual labor in the most hazardous zones:

- **Hot billet loading:** Hydraulic 6-axis robots with **electromagnetic grippers** transfer red-hot (≈ 950 °C) billets from the reheating furnace onto the entry roller table of the rolling mill.
- **Coil handling:** Magnetic or C-hook grippers handle coils of weight **10 – 30 tonnes** at the coil bay.
- **Slab/bloom handling:** Mechanical clamp grippers transport semi-finished slabs (each several tonnes) between process stations.

**Gripper types:**

| Gripper | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Electromagnetic | Energized coil produces magnetic flux | Ferromagnetic billets, slabs, scrap |
| Mechanical clamp | Pneumatic/hydraulic jaws | Blooms, ingots, structural shapes |
| Vacuum | Suction cups on sealed plate | Sheet steel, thin plates |
| C-hook | Swivel hook | Coils, eye-bar stock |

**Safety considerations:**

- **Heat shielding** at the wrist and cabling to survive ambient temperatures up to **80 °C**.
- **Redundant joint brakes** to lock the arm on loss of hydraulic pressure.
- **Light curtains + safety-rated PLC** meeting **ISO 10218-1 / ISO 13849-1 PL d** category 3.
- **Thermal imaging** to confirm gripper contact without manual intervention.
- **Drop-zone exclusion** beneath any lifted load — operators prohibited.

**(2 marks for gripper types, 2 marks for safety, 3 marks for description with sketch)**

**Part (b) — Shoulder Torque Computation:**

Static holding torque:

$$\tau = m \, g \, L \cos\theta$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = 800 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times \cos(60°)$$

$$\cos(60°) = 0.5$$

$$\tau = 800 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 0.5$$

$$\tau = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \text{ (N·m/m)}$$

$$\tau = 7848 \times 2.5 = 19{,}620 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\tau = 19{,}620 \times 0.5 = 9810 \text{ N·m}$$

Apply service factor:

$$\tau_{design} = 1.5 \times 9810 = 14{,}715 \text{ N·m}$$

**Recommended robot:** The **KUKA KR 1000 L750 Titan** (1000 kg payload, 3.6 m reach, wrist torque ≈ 60,000 N·m) comfortably exceeds the design torque. Alternative: **ABB IRB 8700** (800 kg payload).

**(3 marks formula, 2 marks substitution & arithmetic, 1 mark service factor, 1 mark robot recommendation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes in KTU 2024 valuation — Steel Component Production questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the service factor** in torque calculations → loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing units** (e.g., mm/s vs m/min in heat-input formula) → loses **1 mark** and propagates error.
> 3. **Naming a robot without justification** — KTU examiners require the student to *recommend* a specific model **with reason** (payload, reach, axis count).
> 4. **Ignoring thermal expansion** in hot-steel applications — frequently asked as a 3-mark question; answer must cite the formula **ΔL = α L₀ ΔT** and a numerical example.
> 5. **Confusing accuracy vs repeatability** — repeatability is the relevant metric in robotic welding.
> 6. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** → minimum **1-mark deduction** under KTU 2024 marking scheme.
> 7. **Forgetting the digital-twin / Industry 4.0 dimension** — KTU 2024 scheme strongly emphasizes IoT, digital twins, and cyber-physical systems; expect a question on this every semester.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robotic steel production** integrates manipulators, EOAT, sensors, controllers, MES/SCADA, and digital twins.
- **Process families:** Material handling → Forming → Joining → Machining → Inspection → Palletizing.
- **Payload classes** range from 6 kg (light spot welding) to 1000+ kg (heavy billet handling).
- **Repeatability ±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm** is the gold standard for robotic arc welding.
- **Heat-input formula** $H = \dfrac{\eta V I}{v}$ governs weld quality.
- **Thermal expansion** $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ must be compensated in hot-rolled steel handling.
- **Static torque** $\tau = m g L \cos\theta$ with **service factor 1.5** governs robot selection.
- **Cooling rate** dictates HAZ microstructure — fast cooling → fine grains → tougher weld.
- **Safety standards:** ISO 10218-1 (robots), ISO 13849-1 (safety-rated control), ISO 3834 (welding quality).
- **Communication protocols:** PROFINET, EtherCAT, OPC-UA.
- **Industry 4.0 linkage:** Digital twins, IoT sensor fusion, predictive maintenance.
- **Memorize the OEE formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ — frequently tested as a 3-mark question.
- **Common robot models in board answers:** ABB IRB 6700 / 8700, KUKA KR 16 / KR 1000 Titan, FANUC R-2000iC, Yaskawa Motoman MH50.
- **Always pair a process with its dominant sensor** in board answers (welding → arc/laser sensor, grinding → force/torque, picking → vision).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Robot Systems and Humanoid Construction Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Service Robot Systems and Humanoid Construction Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Service Robots — The Formal Definition

According to **ISO 8373:2021** (the international standard governing robot vocabulary), a **service robot** is formally defined as:

> A robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, **excluding** industrial automation applications in manufacturing (i.e., excluding robots governed by ISO 10218).

The **Industrial robot**, by contrast, is defined as an *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes*, used exclusively in industrial automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 Module 2):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly differentiates **Field Robots** (outdoor, unstructured environments) from **Service Robots** (human-centric, semi-structured indoor/domestic environments). The construction-robotics sub-topic sits at the intersection of both domains.

### Classification of Service Robots (ISO 8373 — Per KTU Reference)

| Category | Description | Representative Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Domestic/Household** | Vacuuming, lawn mowing, pool cleaning | iRobot Roomba, Husqvarna Automower |
| **Personal Service** | Assist elderly/disabled, rehabilitation | Care-O-bot, HOBBIT |
| **Professional Service** | Logistics, agriculture, medical, defense | Da Vinci Surgical System, Kiva (Amazon) |
| **Field Service** | Mining, forestry, search & rescue, agriculture | Spot (Boston Dynamics), PackBot |
| **Construction** | Demolition, brick-laying, concrete pouring, surveying | Brokk, Hadrian X, SAM |

## 1.2 Humanoid Robots — Definition and Intuition

A **humanoid robot** is a bipedal anthropomorphic robot whose body plan resembles the human form: a torso, a head, two arms, and two legs. Per the broader KTU taxonomy, it falls under the **legged locomotion** class of field/service robots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a service robot as a *Swiss Army knife on wheels* — a generic tool designed for structured, repetitive tasks. A humanoid, by contrast, is like a *human apprentice* — built to navigate the world exactly as we do: walk up stairs, open doors, climb ladders, pick up an oddly shaped tool, climb into a manhole, or work in a human-built construction site. The humanoid is essentially a **robot designed to inherit the human world** instead of forcing the world to be redesigned for the robot.

The flagship modern humanoids — **ASIMO (Honda, retired 2018)**, **Atlas (Boston Dynamics)**, **Optimus (Tesla)**, **Digit (Agility Robotics)**, **H1 (Unitree)** — all converge on roughly **23–34 degrees of freedom (DoF)** for the full body.

## 1.3 Construction Robots — Definition

A **construction robot** is a service/field robot deployed at building sites to automate or augment tasks such as surveying, earth-moving, demolition, structural assembly, finishing, and inspection. Per the International Association for Automation and Robotics in Construction (**IAARC**), construction robots are categorized by *task* rather than by *morphology*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics used throughout KTU valuation:**
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** Number of independent actuated joints.
> - **Payload:** Maximum mass the robot can manipulate, typically **0.5–50 kg** for service humanoids and **up to 1500 kg** for demolition arms.
> - **Workspace:** Reachable volume, often expressed in m³.
> - **Autonomy Level (ALFUS):** Per NIST — varies from **Level 1 (Remote Control)** to **Level 10 (Fully Autonomous)**.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** A reliability metric, typically **≥ 1000 hours** for professional-grade service robots.

## 1.4 Why Humanoid Construction Robots?

The motivation for humanoid morphology in construction is **ergonomic and infrastructural compatibility**. Buildings are designed for human dimensions: door widths (~**900 mm**), step heights (~**180 mm**), ceiling heights (~**2.7 m**), and tool ergonomics. A humanoid can, in principle, walk into a half-built site and *use the same tools humans use* — drills, saws, hammers, and BIM tablets — without redesigning the construction site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** **Figure 01 (Figure AI)** is a 1.7 m, 60 kg humanoid explicitly targeted at warehouse and light construction tasks. **Apptronik Apollo** and **Tesla Optimus Gen 2** are similar. The economic thesis: human labor in construction is dangerous, scarce, and costly, while the *built environment remains human-shaped*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Service Robot System Architecture (Block-Level Decomposition)

A service robot system is decomposed into **five interacting subsystems** — this is the standard KTU Module 2 structure:

1. **Perception Subsystem** — Sensors (LiDAR, RGB-D cameras, IMU, force/torque).
2. **Decision / Cognition Subsystem** — SLAM, task planner, behavior tree.
3. **Actuation Subsystem** — Motors, gearheads, hydraulic actuators, drivers.
4. **Communication / HRI Subsystem** — Speech, gestures, ROS 2 topics, teleop links.
5. **Energy Subsystem** — Battery, BMS, supercapacitor hybrid, tether.

### Why these five?
- The robot must **sense** (perception).
- It must **think** (cognition).
- It must **move** (actuation).
- It must **collaborate** (HRI / comms).
- It must **survive** (energy).

Removing any one collapses the system into a non-functional device.

## 2.2 Humanoid Robot Anatomy — Degrees of Freedom Budget

A typical 28-DoF humanoid budget (used as the KTU reference model):

| Body Segment | Joints | DoF | Typical Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head (Pan + Tilt) | 2 | 2 | Servo |
| Each Arm (Shoulder 3 + Elbow 1 + Forearm 1 + Wrist 2) | 7 × 2 = 14 | 14 | BLDC + harmonic drive |
| Torso (Yaw + Pitch) | 2 | 2 | Servo |
| Each Leg (Hip 3 + Knee 1 + Ankle 2) | 6 × 2 = 12 | 12 | BLDC + cycloidal reducer |
| **Total** | | **30** | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 28–30 DoF?** A full human has ~244 DoF (over-constrained, under-actuated). Engineers choose a **functionally sufficient** subset: enough for walking, grasping, and torso stabilization, but minimal enough to keep control tractable.

## 2.3 The Bipedal Stability Problem — ZMP

A humanoid standing on two feet is inherently **unstable** — it is an inverted pendulum on a tiny base of support. The standard control criterion is the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)**, introduced by Miomir Vukobratović (1968, Mihailo Pupin Institute).

> **ZMP Definition (KTU textbook standard):**
> The point on the ground (or floor) where the **net moment of the ground reaction force** has **zero horizontal components** — equivalently, where the inertial and gravitational moments about that point cancel.

For stable static/dynamic walking, **ZMP must lie strictly inside the support polygon** (the convex hull of contact feet). This is the **Vukobratović stability criterion**.

## 2.4 Construction Robot Categorization (KTU Module 2 reference)

| Sub-class | Primary Task | Example Platform | Mobility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surveying/Mapping** | Scan-to-BIM, progress monitoring | Leica BLK ARC, Boston Dynamics Spot + LiDAR | Quadruped |
| **Demolition** | Concrete breaking in confined spaces | Brokk 200, Husqvarna DXR | Tracked |
| **Earth-moving** | Excavation, grading | Built Robotics Cat 336 retrofit | Wheeled/tracked |
| **Structural Assembly** | Steel/wood framing, brick-laying | Hadrian X (FBR), SAM (Construction Robotics) | Wheeled/rail |
| **Concrete/Rebar** | Rebar tying, concrete finishing | TyBot, Concrete-X | Rail/gantry |
| **Inspection** | Weld QC, crack detection, thermal | ANYmal C, ANYbotics | Quadruped |
| **Humanoid / Multi-purpose** | Generic site labor | Apollo, Figure 01, Optimus | Bipedal |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equations a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 2 valuation:**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \bigl(x_i(\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{x}_i\bigr)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | ZMP abscissa (sagittal plane) | m |
| 2 | $y_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \bigl(y_i(\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{y}_i\bigr)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)}$ | ZMP ordinate (frontal plane) | m |
| 3 | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Full-body dynamics (Lagrangian) | N·m |
| 4 | $T_i^{i-1} = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) transform | homogeneous 4×4 |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{1}{2}\bigl(m_h \dot{x}^2 + I_h \dot{\theta}^2\bigr) + m_h g x \sin\theta$ | Energy of inverted pendulum (LIPM) | J |
| 6 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{z_c}}$ | Natural frequency of Linear Inverted Pendulum Model | rad/s |
| 7 | $T_{step} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{z_c}{g}}$ | Step period for LIPM | s |
| 8 | $P_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{task}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot DOD \cdot C_{bat}}$ | Battery sizing for service mission | Wh |
| 9 | $SSM = \min_i \bigl(d_{ZMP,COP,i}\bigr)$ | Static Stability Margin | m |
| 10 | $\dot{x} = A x + B u,\ y = C x + D u$ | LTI state-space for EKF-based sensor fusion | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Formula 4:** The pipe symbol `|` (absolute-value) is avoided per KTU markdown rendering rules. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead if a modulus appears in derivations.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **ZMP-based control** powers every modern humanoid: ASIMO, Atlas, Digit, Optimus.
- **DH parameters** underpin CAD/CAM chains from 6-axis arms to 7-DoF cobot arms.
- **LIPM step period** lets a robot pre-plan footstep placement at 1.0–1.2 m/s walking speed — critical for humanoid construction traversal.
- **Battery sizing (Eq. 8)** is the dominant engineering bottleneck for *all* field service robots; current Li-ion delivers ~**250 Wh/kg**, hence the 2–4 hour operational envelope of most humanoids.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Zero-Moment Point (ZMP) — Sagittal Plane

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a humanoid modeled as an articulated multi-rigid-body system. Let the $i$-th link have:
- Mass $m_i$ (kg)
- Position of CoM $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in world frame (m)
- Acceleration $(\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{y}_i, \ddot{z}_i)$ (m/s²)

The net ground reaction force **must produce zero moment** about the ZMP. This is the Vukobratović equilibrium condition.

### 3.1.2 Moment Balance About a Point $(p_x, p_y, 0)$

Sum of moments from gravity + inertial forces about $(p_x, p_y, 0)$ must vanish.

Taking moments about the $y$-axis through ZMP:

$$
\sum_i \bigl[ (x_i - p_x) \cdot m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - (z_i - 0) \cdot m_i \ddot{x}_i \bigr] = 0
$$

> Row 1: The force at the $i$-th CoM has a vertical component $m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)$ acting at horizontal lever arm $(x_i - p_x)$.
> Row 2: The horizontal inertial force $m_i \ddot{x}_i$ acts at vertical lever arm $z_i$.

Expanding:

$$
\sum_i (x_i - p_x) \, m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - \sum_i z_i \, m_i \ddot{x}_i = 0
$$

$$
\sum_i x_i \, m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - p_x \sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - \sum_i z_i \, m_i \ddot{x}_i = 0
$$

Solving for $p_x$:

$$
p_x = \dfrac{\sum_i x_i \, m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - \sum_i z_i \, m_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum_i m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}
$$

> Row 3: Divide by the denominator — the *normal* ground reaction force summed across the system.

Renaming $p_x \equiv x_{ZMP}$:

$$
\boxed{\,x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \bigl[x_i(\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{x}_i\bigr]}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)}\,}
$$

By identical argument about the $x$-axis:

$$
\boxed{\,y_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \bigl[y_i(\ddot{z}_i + g) - z_i \ddot{y}_i\bigr]}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i(\ddot{z}_i + g)}\,}
$$

### 3.1.3 Quasi-Static Limit ($\ddot{x}_i, \ddot{y}_i, \ddot{z}_i \to 0$)

In slow walking, all accelerations vanish, and the ZMP collapses to the **Center of Pressure (CoP)**:

$$
x_{ZMP} \to x_{CoP} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i g \, x_i}{\sum_i m_i g} = \dfrac{\sum_i m_i x_i}{\sum_i m_i} = x_{CoM}
$$

> **Critical Insight:** In quasi-static motion, **ZMP = CoM projection = CoP**. This is why a slow-moving humanoid behaves like a simple inverted pendulum whose pivot is the foot.

### 3.1.4 Stability Margin

Define the **Stability Margin (SM)** as the minimum Euclidean distance from the ZMP to the boundary of the support polygon $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
SM = \min_{(x_b, y_b) \in \partial \mathcal{S}} \sqrt{(x_{ZMP} - x_b)^2 + (y_{ZMP} - y_b)^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Key Point:** A positive SM guarantees quasi-static stability. A negative or zero SM means **the robot is in a falling state** and a **recovery step** must be triggered within the single-support phase duration.

## 3.2 Linear Inverted Pendulum Model (LIPM) — Step Time Derivation

### 3.2.1 LIPM Assumptions
- All mass concentrated at the robot's CoM at height $z_c$.
- Pendulum leg is massless, variable length.
- CoM constrained to a horizontal plane at constant $z_c$.

### 3.2.2 Energy Conservation

At the apex of the swing, the leg is vertical: $\theta = 0$, $\dot{\theta} = 0$.
At the swing mid-point, $\theta = \theta_0$, $\dot{\theta} = \dot{\theta}_0$, horizontal CoM displacement $x$.

Kinetic energy at mid-swing:

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{2} m_h \dot{x}^2 + \dfrac{1}{2} I_h \dot{\theta}^2
$$

Potential energy gained by raising the CoM as it rotates through $\theta$:

$$
V = m_h g \, z_c (1 - \cos\theta)
$$

> Row 1: Total energy = T + V; conserved.

For a point mass ($I_h = 0$), $V$ at the lowest point of the arc (over the foot) is zero, so:

$$
T_{bottom} = \dfrac{1}{2} m_h \dot{x}^2_{max}
$$

Linearizing the pendulum about the vertical gives the effective spring constant $k = m_h g / z_c$. The undamped natural frequency is therefore:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{z_c}}
$$

The half-period — which corresponds to the time for a full step — is:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{step} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{z_c}{g}}\,}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

Take a humanoid with CoM height $z_c = 0.9$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
T_{step} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{0.9}{9.81}} = \pi \cdot 0.3029 = 0.951 \text{ s}
$$

For a step length of $L = 0.5$ m:

$$
v_{walk} = \dfrac{2L}{T_{step}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.5}{0.951} = 1.052 \text{ m/s}
$$

> This matches the typical walking speed of ASIMO and Digit (~**1.0–1.2 m/s**). The model shows that **raising the CoM slows the step but increases stability** — a critical design trade-off in humanoid construction robots that must reach high shelves.

## 3.3 DH Transformation for a 7-DoF Humanoid Arm

### 3.3.1 Joint Layout (S-R-R-R-R-R-R, Shoulder-3 + Elbow + 3-Wrist)

| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ (m) | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (shoulder yaw) | $\theta_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 2 (shoulder pitch) | $\theta_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 3 (shoulder roll) | $\theta_3$ | 0.15 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 4 (elbow) | $\theta_4$ | 0 | 0.40 | 0 |
| 5 (forearm roll) | $\theta_5$ | 0 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 6 (wrist pitch) | $\theta_6$ | 0 | 0 | $\pi/2$ |
| 7 (wrist roll) | $\theta_7$ | 0.10 | 0 | 0 |

### 3.3.2 Sample End-Effector Pose

For configuration $\theta = [0, -\pi/2, 0, -\pi/2, 0, 0, 0]$:

The forward kinematics chain $T_0^7 = T_0^1 \cdot T_1^2 \cdot T_2^3 \cdots T_6^7$ yields the end-effector pose. The base of the arm sits at the shoulder; with the elbow bent at 90°, the hand lies at:

$$
x_{EE} = 0.15, \quad y_{EE} = 0, \quad z_{EE} = -0.40
$$

> This is the **arm-extended-forward** canonical pose used to evaluate the humanoid's reach envelope.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Service vs. Humanoid vs. Construction

| Attribute | Wheeled Service (e.g., Care-O-bot) | Humanoid (e.g., Optimus) | Construction (e.g., Brokk) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Locomotion DoF** | 2–3 (Differential/omnidirectional) | 12–14 (per two legs) | 0 (stationary) or 4 (tracked base) |
| **Max Speed** | 1.5 m/s | 1.2 m/s walking, 2.5 m/s run | 2.0 km/h tracked |
| **Payload** | 3–10 kg | 20–25 kg arm payload | 200–1500 kg demolition head |
| **Battery** | 8–12 h | 2–4 h | Tethered (electric/hydraulic) |
| **Terrain** | Flat indoor | Stairs, rubble, ladders | Confined, vertical, debris |
| **Cost (USD)** | 30 k – 150 k | 100 k – 250 k (early production) | 80 k – 350 k |
| **Autonomy Level** | 5–7 | 4–6 | 2–4 (teleop-dominant) |
| **Dominant Failure Mode** | Wheel slip on rugs | Falling over | Hydraulic hose rupture |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Robot System Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph perception["PERCEPTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        sensor1["LiDAR 2D/3D"]
        sensor2["RGB-D Camera"]
        sensor3["IMU 6-Axis"]
        sensor4["Force Torque Sensor"]
        sensor5["Ultrasonic Array"]
    end

    subgraph cognition["COGNITION SUBSYSTEM"]
        slam["SLAM Module"]
        planner["Task Planner"]
        behavior["Behavior Tree Engine"]
        perception_fusion["Sensor Fusion EKF"]
    end

    subgraph actuation["ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        motor1["BLDC Motor Drivers"]
        motor2["Harmonic Reducers"]
        motor3["Hydraulic Actuators"]
    end

    subgraph hri["HMI / COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        speech["Speech I/O"]
        gesture["Gesture Recognition"]
        ros["ROS 2 Network"]
        teleop["Teleop Link"]
    end

    subgraph energy["ENERGY SUBSYSTEM"]
        battery["Li-ion Pack"]
        bms["Battery Management System"]
        tether["Tethered Hybrid"]
    end

    sensor1 --> perception_fusion
    sensor2 --> perception_fusion
    sensor3 --> perception_fusion
    sensor4 --> perception_fusion
    sensor5 --> perception_fusion

    perception_fusion --> slam
    perception_fusion --> planner
    slam --> behavior
    planner --> behavior

    behavior --> motor1
    behavior --> motor2
    behavior --> motor3

    behavior --> ros
    ros --> speech
    ros --> gesture
    ros --> teleop

    motor1 --> battery
    motor2 --> battery
    motor3 --> tether
    battery --> bms
```

## 4.2 Humanoid Robot ZMP Control Hierarchy

```mermaid

```
