- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** A MEMS chip combining a **3-axis gyroscope, 3-axis accelerometer, and 3-axis magnetometer**. Outputs roll, pitch, yaw.
- **Joint Torque Sensors:** Strain-gauge based, used in compliant manipulators.
- **Battery Management Sensors:** Voltage, current, and temperature transducers.

### 2.1.2 Exteroceptive Sensors (External World)

These measure the **external environment**.

- **2D / 3D LiDAR:** Time-of-Flight laser scanner. Range typically **0.1 m to 100 m**, accuracy **±2 cm**.
- **Stereo / RGB-D Cameras:** Passive (stereo) or active (structured light, ToF) depth cameras. Range **0.3 m to 10 m**.
- **GPS / GNSS Receivers:** Absolute positioning. Accuracy **±2.5 m** (civilian L1) or **±0.02 m** (RTK-GPS).
- **Ultrasonic / IR Range Finders:** Short-range proximity. Range **0.02 m to 5 m**.
- **Tactile / Force-Skin Arrays:** Used in service robots handling fragile objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Selection Heuristic (KTU Examiner Tip):** For **outdoor field robotics**, prefer **LiDAR + GPS + IMU**. For **indoor service robotics**, prefer **RGB-D cameras + wheel odometry + IMU**. This contrast is a frequent 3-mark short-answer topic.

## 2.2 Family 2 — The Actuator (Action) Family

Actuators convert stored energy into controlled mechanical work.

### 2.2.1 Electromagnetic Actuators
- **DC Brushed Motors:** Simple, cheap, but brush wear limits lifetime to **~1000 hours**.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors:** High efficiency (**>90%**), long life, used in drone propulsion and AGV wheels.
- **Servo Motors:** Geared BLDC with integrated encoder and PID controller. Standard in robot arms.
- **Stepper Motors:** Open-loop position control, used in 3D printers and low-cost CNC.

### 2.2.2 Fluid-Power Actuators
- **Hydraulic Actuators:** Force density up to **20 kN per kg of actuator mass**. Used in heavy field robots (e.g., Boston Dynamics' earlier prototypes, mining robots).
- **Pneumatic Actuators (Pneumatic Artificial Muscles / PAMs):** Compliant, safe for human interaction. Used in rehabilitation exoskeletons.

### 2.2.3 Smart Materials
- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA):** Nitinol wires that contract when heated. Used in micro-grippers.
- **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA):** A spring intentionally placed between motor and load. Provides force control and shock tolerance — used in MIT's Cheetah and rehabilitation robots.

## 2.3 Family 3 — The Power (Energy) Family

| Energy Source | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid Battery | 30 – 50 | Legacy AGVs, budget robots |
| NiMH Battery | 60 – 120 | Older medical robots |
| **Li-Ion Battery** | **150 – 250** | **Modern mobile robots (default)** |
| LiPo Battery | 130 – 200 | Drones, lightweight UGVs |
| Hydrogen Fuel Cell | 400 – 1000 | Long-endurance field robots |
| Diesel Engine + Generator | 3000 – 6000 (fuel-level) | Heavy field robots |
| Solar PV | 150 W/m² (incident) | Agricultural & planetary rovers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Li-Ion dominates service robotics:** Best trade-off between **specific energy (Wh/kg)**, **cycle life (500–2000 cycles)**, and **safety**. LiPo offers higher discharge rate (C-rating) but is more volatile.

## 2.4 Family 4 — The Mechanical (Structural) Family

- **Chassis / Frame:** Aluminum 6061-T6, carbon-fiber composites, or 3D-printed PLA/PETG.
- **Joints:** Revolute (R), Prismatic (P), and Spherical (S). A 6-DOF arm typically uses **RRRRRR** configuration.
- **Locomotion Subsystems:**
  - **Wheels:** Differential-drive (2 wheels), Ackermann (car-like), omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish).
  - **Tracked:** High traction in mud/sand; used in search-and-rescue robots.
  - **Legged:** 2, 4, or 6 legs; trade-off between stability and complexity.
  - **Aerial / Aquatic:** Propellers, thrusters, fins.
- **Transmissions:** Gears (planetary, harmonic/RV reducers), belts, chains, cable drives.

## 2.5 Family 5 — The Control & Computation (Brain) Family

- **Microcontroller (MCU):** STM32, Arduino, ESP32 — for low-level real-time control (motor PWM, encoder reading).
- **Single-Board Computer (SBC):** Raspberry Pi 4, NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano — for high-level perception (computer vision, SLAM).
- **Motor Drivers / ESCs:** VESC, ODrive, RoboClaw.
- **Communication Buses:**
  - **CAN Bus:** Automotive-grade, multi-drop, noise-immune. Standard in mobile robotics.
  - **EtherCAT:** High-speed real-time bus for industrial arms.
  - **RS-485 / UART:** Legacy low-cost sensor networks.
  - **WiFi / Bluetooth / 4G/5G:** Teleoperation and cloud telemetry.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Component-Level Key Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **high-yield equations** that frequently appear in KTU University Exam Part-B questions. Pay special attention to units — the examiner deducts marks for missing SI units.

| # | Equation | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{P}$ | Motor speed (RPM) from supply frequency $f$ (Hz) and pole pairs $P$ | BLDC / Stepper |
| 2 | $\tau = k_t \cdot I$ | Motor torque proportional to current; $k_t$ in N·m/A | All DC motors |
| 3 | $V = k_e \cdot \omega + I \cdot R$ | Back-EMF equation; $k_e$ in V·s/rad | DC/BLDC |
| 4 | $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy stored in moving mass | Mobile robot |
| 5 | $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output of actuator | All actuators |
| 6 | $R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | LiDAR max range from speed of light $c$ and ToF $\Delta t$ | LiDAR |
| 7 | $f = \frac{c}{2 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\theta/2)}$ | Angular resolution of LiDAR scan | LiDAR |
| 8 | $E_{specific} = \frac{E_{bat}}{m_{bat}}$ | Specific energy in Wh/kg | Battery |
| 9 | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | Wheel linear velocity from radius $r$ and angular velocity $\omega$ | Wheeled robot |
| 10 | $\rho = \frac{\text{signal power}}{\text{noise power}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (linear); SNR(dB) $= 10 \log_{10} \rho$ | All sensors |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Motor Torque-Speed Characteristic

This is the most frequently tested derivation in the Actuator family.

**Given:**

A permanent-magnet DC motor has armature resistance $R_a$ and back-EMF constant $k_e$ (V·s/rad). The supply voltage is $V_s$.

**To Derive:** The mechanical output torque $\tau$ as a function of angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 1 — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on the armature circuit:**

$$V_s = I_a R_a + V_{back\text{-}EMF}$$

$$V_s = I_a R_a + k_e \omega$$

**Step 2 — Solve for armature current $I_a$:**

$$I_a = \frac{V_s - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the motor torque constant relationship $\tau = k_t I_a$:**

$$\tau = k_t \cdot \frac{V_s - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the standard **torque-speed straight-line** form:**

$$\tau = \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \left( \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \right) \omega$$

**Step 5 — Identify the two boundary points:**

- **Stall torque** ($\omega = 0$): $\tau_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t V_s}{R_a}$
- **No-load speed** ($\tau = 0$): $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \dfrac{V_s}{k_e}$

**Step 6 — Maximum mechanical power point** occurs at $\tau = \tfrac{1}{2} \tau_{stall}$ and $\omega = \tfrac{1}{2} \omega_{no\text{-}load}$:

$$P_{max} = \tau \cdot \omega = \frac{1}{4} \tau_{stall} \cdot \omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{k_t V_s^{2}}{4 R_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mechanical time-constant of the motor is $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{k_t k_e}$, where $J$ is the rotor inertia. This governs how fast the motor can accelerate the robot's wheels — a critical parameter in differential-drive field robots negotiating uneven terrain.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LiDAR Maximum Range

A 2D LiDAR emits a laser pulse and measures the time-of-flight. Derive the maximum unambiguous range.

**Step 1:** The pulse travels to the target and back, so total distance = $2R$.

$$\Delta t = \frac{2R}{c}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $R$:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Inserting $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and a typical $\Delta t = 667$ ns for a 100 m round trip:

$$R = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 667 \times 10^{-9}}{2} = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** For a rotating LiDAR with angular step $\theta_{step}$ (e.g., 0.25°), the point density at distance $R$ is:

$$d_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{step} = R \cdot \frac{\pi}{180 \cdot N}$$

where $N$ is the number of points per scan (e.g., $N = 1440$ for 0.25° resolution).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Differential-Drive Wheel Kinematics (Preview of Module 3)

A 2-wheeled mobile robot has wheel radius $r$, wheel base $L$, left and right wheel angular velocities $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

**Step 1:** Linear velocity of each wheel:

$$v_L = r \omega_L, \quad v_R = r \omega_R$$

**Step 2:** Robot's instantaneous linear and angular velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Instantaneous Center of Curvature (ICC) radius:

$$R_{ICC} = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_R + \omega_L}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation bridges **Family 2 (Actuators)** and **Family 4 (Mechanical)**. It will be extended in Module 3 for full path planning.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Family Selection for Common FSR Scenarios

| Application | Sensor Family | Actuator Family | Power | Mech. Structure | Brain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Agricultural Tractor** | GPS-RTK, LiDAR, IMU, RGB cam | Diesel + hydraulic steering | Diesel fuel | 4-wheel Ackermann | Jetson + CAN bus |
| **Indoor Service Robot (Hotel)** | 2D LiDAR, RGB-D, IMU | BLDC hub motors | Li-Ion | Differential drive | Raspberry Pi |
| **Search & Rescue UGV** | Thermal cam, 3D LiDAR, gas sensor | BLDC + tracked | LiPo (hot-swap) | Tracked, articulated | Jetson + 4G |
| **Surgical Robot Arm** | Force/torque, encoder, vision | Servo + harmonic drive | Mains / UPS | RR + cable | Real-time MCU + EtherCAT |
| **Aerial Inspection Drone** | IMU, GPS, ToF cam | BLDC + propeller | LiPo (high C) | Quad-frame | Pixhawk + companion PC |
| **Rehabilitation Exoskeleton** | EMG, joint encoders, IMU | SEA / pneumatic | Li-Ion | Exo-frame | Distributed MCU + CAN |
| **Planetary Rover (NASA-class)** | Stereo cam, LiDAR, IMU | BLDC + harmonic | Solar + Li-Ion | 6-wheel rocker-bogie | Radiation-hardened SBC |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five-Family Architecture of a Field/Service Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Source - Energy Family] --> B[Power Distribution Board]
    B --> C[Motor Drivers - Action Family]
    B --> D[Embedded Computer - Brain Family]
    B --> E[Sensor Array - Perception Family]
    D --> C
    E --> D
    C --> F[Mechanical Chassis - Skeleton Family]
    F --> G[End Effector - Action Family]
    D --> H[Communication Module - Brain Family]
    H --> I[Cloud / Operator]
    E --> F
    F --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Energy flows upward from the Power Source. Arrows from the Embedded Computer represent **control signals**; arrows from the Sensor Array represent **feedback signals**. The loop `E -> F -> D` is the classic **perception–action–perception feedback loop** of robotics.

## 4.2 Hierarchy of the Five Families

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Families of Components] --> S[Sensor Family - Perception]
    ROOT --> AC[Actuator Family - Action]
    ROOT --> P[Power Family - Energy]
    ROOT --> M[Mechanical Family - Structure]
    ROOT --> B[Control and Computation Family - Brain]
    S --> S1[Proprioceptive]
    S --> S2[Exteroceptive]
    AC --> AC1[Electromagnetic]
    AC --> AC2[Fluid Power]
    AC --> AC3[Smart Materials]
    P --> P1[Battery]
    P --> P2[Fuel Cell]
    P --> P3[Engine Generator]
    M --> M1[Joints and Links]
    M --> M2[Locomotion Wheels Tracks Legs]
    M --> M3[End Effectors]
    B --> B1[Microcontroller]
    B --> B2[Single Board Computer]
    B --> B3[Communication Bus]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Signal Chain in a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|Light / Sound / RF| SENS[Sensors - Family 1]
    SENS -->|Analog / Digital| MCU[Microcontroller - Family 5]
    MCU -->|CAN / UART| SBC[Single Board Computer - Family 5]
    SBC -->|Decision: PWM setpoints| MD[Motor Driver - Family 5]
    PWR[Battery - Family 3] -->|12V / 24V / 48V| MD
    MD -->|High current| MOT[BLDC Motor - Family 2]
    MOT -->|Torque| GEAR[Gearbox - Family 4]
    GEAR -->|Reduced RPM| WHEEL[Wheel - Family 4]
    WHEEL -->|Displacement| GROUND[Ground]
    GROUND -->|Slip / Vibration| SENS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The two **arrows labeled "Feedback"** (from Wheel to SENS, from Ground to SENS) form the **closed-loop control system**. Without them, the robot operates open-loop and cannot correct for wheel slip or terrain irregularities — a guaranteed mark deduction in design questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Families of Components" in the context of field and service robotics. List the five primary families. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The term **"Families of Components"** in field and service robotics refers to the systematic classification of hardware building blocks that enable a robot to perceive, decide, act, and sustain itself in real-world environments. Components are grouped by their **functional role** rather than by their underlying technology, allowing modular design and interchangeability.

The five primary families are:

1. **Sensor (Perception) Family** — Encoders, IMU, LiDAR, GPS, cameras.
2. **Actuator (Action) Family** — DC/BLDC motors, servos, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders, SEAs.
3. **Power (Energy) Family** — Li-Ion batteries, fuel cells, solar PV, diesel generators.
4. **Mechanical (Structural) Family** — Chassis, joints, wheels/tracks/legs, transmissions, end effectors.
5. **Control & Computation (Brain) Family** — MCUs, SBCs, motor drivers, CAN/EtherCAT communication buses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if the student lists **all five families** and clearly states the **classification criterion is functional role, not technology**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give one example of each used in a mobile robot. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Proprioceptive Sensor | Exteroceptive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures the **internal state** of the robot (its own pose, velocity, joint angles, battery state). | Measures the **external environment** (obstacles, landmarks, terrain). |
| **Example 1** | **Wheel encoder** — measures wheel rotation to estimate odometry. | **2D LiDAR** — measures distance to surrounding obstacles. |
| **Example 2** | **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** — measures robot's roll, pitch, yaw. | **GPS receiver** — measures robot's absolute geographic position. |
| **Role in SLAM** | Provides **prediction** (motion model). | Provides **correction** (observation model). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is giving **two exteroceptive examples** for the proprioceptive slot. Award only 1.5 marks for partial correctness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A:  [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]**

**A (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **five families of components** used in a typical field/service robot. For each family, list two representative components and state their primary function. **[7 Marks]**

**A (b)** A differential-drive service robot uses two BLDC motors with the following specifications:
- Wheel radius $r = 0.10$ m
- Wheel base $L = 0.40$ m
- Motor torque constant $k_t = 0.05$ N·m/A
- Motor back-EMF constant $k_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad
- Armature resistance $R_a = 1.0$ Ω
- Supply voltage $V_s = 24$ V

**Calculate:**
(i) The stall torque of each motor.
(ii) The no-load speed of each motor.
(iii) The forward linear velocity and turning angular velocity when both wheels run at the no-load speed. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to A (a):**

Refer to **Section 4.1 (Block Diagram)** of these notes for the diagram. The five families are:

1. **Sensor (Perception) Family:** Provides data about the robot and its environment. Examples: (i) 2D LiDAR — measures obstacle distance via time-of-flight; (ii) IMU — measures orientation and angular velocity.

2. **Actuator (Action) Family:** Produces physical motion. Examples: (i) BLDC motor — high-efficiency rotational drive for wheels/joints; (ii) Servo motor — closed-loop position control for robot arm joints.

3. **Power (Energy) Family:** Stores and supplies electrical energy. Examples: (i) Li-Ion battery pack — high specific energy for mobile operation; (ii) DC-DC converter — regulates voltage rails.

4. **Mechanical (Structural) Family:** Provides physical form and motion transmission. Examples: (i) Harmonic drive gearbox — high-ratio torque transmission for robot joints; (ii) Mecanum wheel — omnidirectional locomotion.

5. **Control & Computation (Brain) Family:** Executes decision algorithms and interfaces with hardware. Examples: (i) NVIDIA Jetson SBC — runs SLAM and vision pipelines; (ii) CAN bus — deterministic communication between MCUs and motor drivers.

The **closed-loop interaction** is: sensors feed data to the brain; the brain issues commands to actuators; actuators drive the mechanical structure; the structure moves the robot; the sensors observe the new state. The power family energizes the entire loop.

---

**Model Solution to A (b):**

**(i) Stall Torque**

At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $V_{back\text{-}EMF} = 0$ and all voltage drops across $R_a$.

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_s}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1.0} = 24 \text{ A}$$

$$\tau_{stall} = k_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \times 24 = \mathbf{1.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) No-Load Speed**

At no-load, $\tau = 0$, so $I_a \approx 0$ and $V_s = k_e \omega_{no\text{-}load}$.

$$\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_s}{k_e} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{480 \times 60}{2\pi} = \mathbf{4583 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) Linear and Angular Velocities of Robot**

When both wheels run at the same no-load speed, $\omega_L = \omega_R = 480$ rad/s.

Linear velocity:
$$v = \frac{r (\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.10 \times (480 + 480)}{2} = \mathbf{48 \text{ m/s}}$$

Angular velocity (turning rate):
$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.10 \times (480 - 480)}{0.40} = \mathbf{0 \text{ rad/s}}$$

The robot moves **straight forward at 48 m/s** (= 172.8 km/h), which is unrealistically high for a service robot — illustrating the importance of gearing down motor speed to match application requirements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert $\omega$ from rad/s to RPM in (ii). **Always show both units** to earn full marks.
> 2. In (iii), a common error is to use $v = r\omega$ for a **single wheel** rather than the **average** of both wheels. The differential-drive formula must be used.
> 3. Always specify the **SI unit** in the boxed final answer. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per part.

---

### Question B: [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**B (a)** Explain the **Sensor (Perception) Family** in detail. Classify it into proprioceptive and exteroceptive categories, giving two examples of each with their operating principles. **[7 Marks]**

**B (b)** A field robot uses a **2D LiDAR** with the following specifications:
- Angular resolution: $\theta_{step} = 0.5°$
- Maximum time-of-flight measurement: $\Delta t_{max} = 1.33$ μs
- Pulse repetition rate: $f_{rep} = 20$ kHz

**Calculate:**
(i) The maximum unambiguous range of the LiDAR.
(ii) The number of points per full 360° scan.
(iii) The arc-length distance between two adjacent points at a target located 30 m away. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to B (a):**

The **Sensor Family** enables the robot to perceive both its **internal state** (proprioception) and its **external environment** (exteroception).

**Proprioceptive Sensors:**

1. **Optical Encoder:** A disc with alternating transparent/opaque slots rotates between an LED and a photodetector. Each slot crossing produces a pulse. Counting pulses gives angular displacement; pulse frequency gives angular velocity. Used for wheel odometry and joint angle feedback.

2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU):** A MEMS chip integrating a 3-axis gyroscope (measures angular velocity via Coriolis effect), 3-axis accelerometer (measures linear acceleration via proof-mass displacement), and 3-axis magnetometer (measures Earth's magnetic field for heading). Sensor fusion yields roll, pitch, and yaw of the robot.

**Exteroceptive Sensors:**

1. **2D LiDAR:** Emits infrared laser pulses (typically 905 nm) and measures the round-trip time-of-flight $\Delta t$. Distance is computed as $R = c\Delta t/2$. A rotating mirror produces a 360° scan. Range up to 100 m, accuracy ±2 cm.

2. **RGB-D Camera (e.g., Intel RealSense):** Combines an RGB sensor with an IR structured-light projector. The IR pattern projected on the scene is captured by an IR camera; triangulation against a known pattern produces per-pixel depth. Effective range 0.3 – 3 m. Used for indoor 3D obstacle detection.

---

**Model Solution to B (b):**

**(i) Maximum Unambiguous Range**

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_{max}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 1.33 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$R_{max} = \frac{399}{2} = \mathbf{199.5 \text{ m} \approx 200 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Number of Points per Full Scan**

Angular step in radians:

$$\theta_{step} = 0.5° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.00873 \text{ rad}$$

Number of points:

$$N = \frac{2\pi}{\theta_{step}} = \frac{360°}{0.5°} = \mathbf{720 \text{ points/scan}}$$

**(iii) Arc-Length Between Adjacent Points at 30 m**

$$d_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{step} = 30 \times 0.00873 = \mathbf{0.262 \text{ m} \approx 26.2 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In (i), the most common mistake is **forgetting the factor of 2** in the denominator (because light travels to the target AND back). This leads to a doubled answer of 399 m — instant loss of 2 marks.
> 2. In (ii), students often compute $N = 360 / \theta_{step}$ **in degrees correctly** but then write the numerical answer without units ("points"). Always write "720 points per scan."
> 3. In (iii), if you use degrees instead of radians, you'll get an answer 57.3× too large. **Always convert to radians** for the formula $d = R\theta$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Families of Components**

- [ ] **Definition:** Classification of robot hardware by **functional role**, not by technology.
- [ ] **Five Families:** Sensor (Perception) | Actuator (Action) | Power (Energy) | Mechanical (Structure) | Control & Computation (Brain).
- [ ] **Proprioceptive vs Exteroceptive:** Internal state vs External world. **Examples: Encoder/IMU vs LiDAR/Camera/GPS.**
- [ ] **Actuator Sub-Families:** Electromagnetic (DC/BLDC/Servo/Stepper), Fluid Power (Hydraulic/Pneumatic), Smart Materials (SMA/SEA).
- [ ] **Power Hierarchy by Specific Energy:** Lead-Acid < NiMH < Li-Ion < LiPo < Fuel Cell < Diesel.
- [ ] **Locomotion Types:** Wheeled (Diff/Ackermann/Mecanum) | Tracked | Legged (2/4/6) | Aerial | Aquatic.
- [ ] **Key Motor Equation:** $\tau = \dfrac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \dfrac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega$ — the **straight-line torque-speed curve**.
- [ ] **Stall Torque:** $\tau_{stall} = k_t V_s / R_a$. **No-load speed:** $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = V_s / k_e$.
- [ ] **LiDAR Range:** $R_{max} = c \Delta t / 2$ — remember the **factor of 2**.
- [ ] **LiDAR Point Spacing:** $d_{arc} = R \theta_{step}$ — convert $\theta$ to **radians**.
- [ ] **Differential Drive Kinematics:** $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$, $\omega_{robot} = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.
- [ ] **Communication Buses:** CAN (mobile, 1 Mbps), EtherCAT (industrial, 100 Mbps), RS-485/UART (low-cost sensors).
- [ ] **Design Heuristic:** Outdoor → LiDAR + GPS + IMU | Indoor → RGB-D + Odom + IMU.
- [ ] **Closed-Loop Rule:** Every robot must have a **sensor feedback path** from the mechanical structure back to the brain. Open-loop robots are penalized in KTU design questions.
- [ ] **Units Matter:** Always write SI units (N·m, rad/s, m/s, W/kg) in final answers. Missing unit = 0.5 mark deduction.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — How the Five Families Interact

A field or service robot is never designed family-by-family in isolation. The **operational concept** is that all five families participate in a single **perception–cognition–action** cycle running in real time. The breakdown of the operational theorem (the **FSR Component Integration Theorem**) into structured logic steps is as follows:

1. **Energy Priming (Family 3 → All):** The power source (typically a Li-Ion pack) is brought online first. Voltage regulators (DC-DC converters) derive stable rails (5 V, 12 V, 24 V) for low-power electronics and high-power actuators.
2. **Boot and Self-Test (Family 5):** The MCU and SBC power up, run a **Built-In Self-Test (BIST)**, and initialize communication buses (CAN, UART, I²C, SPI). Sensors are queried for presence and sanity.
3. **Perception Sweep (Family 1 → Family 5):** Encoders, IMU, LiDAR, and cameras stream data into the SBC. The perception pipeline (filtering, feature extraction, SLAM) updates the robot's belief of its own pose and the world map.
4. **Decision (Family 5 internal):** A planner (A*, D*, RRT*) computes a trajectory; a controller (PID, MPC) computes the **velocity setpoints** for each wheel/joint.
5. **Action Dispatch (Family 5 → Family 2):** Setpoints are dispatched as PWM signals or CAN frames to motor drivers, which energize the actuators.
6. **Mechanical Transformation (Family 2 → Family 4):** Actuators produce torque; gearboxes reduce speed and multiply torque; wheels/tracks/legs convert this into ground-relative motion.
7. **Feedback (Family 4 → Family 1):** Wheel encoders, IMU, and any contact/tactile sensors report the **actual** motion back to the SBC, closing the loop.
8. **Iteration:** The cycle repeats at **50–200 Hz** for control loops and **1–30 Hz** for high-level planning.

> The "Why" behind each step is rooted in the **principle of feedback control** (Wiener, 1948) — without a feedback path, the system is open-loop and cannot correct for disturbances such as wheel slip on a wet floor or wind gust on a drone.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative formula list** for any numerical problem on this topic in the KTU University Exam. Note the **units** column — examiners specifically test unit awareness.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = I_a R_a + k_e \omega$ | Armature KVL for a DC/BLDC motor | $V_s$: V; $I_a$: A; $R_a$: Ω; $k_e$: V·s/rad; $\omega$: rad/s |
| 2 | $\tau = k_t I_a$ | Motor torque from current | $\tau$: N·m; $k_t$: N·m/A |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \dfrac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega$ | Linear torque-speed curve | Same as above |
| 4 | $\tau_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t V_s}{R_a}$ | Maximum torque at zero speed | N·m |
| 5 | $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \dfrac{V_s}{k_e}$ | Maximum speed at zero torque | rad/s |
| 6 | $N_{RPM} = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$ | Convert rad/s to RPM | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = \tau \omega$ | Mechanical power output | W |
| 8 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{k_t V_s^{2}}{4 R_a}$ | Max mechanical power at $\tfrac{1}{2}\tau_{stall}$ | W |
| 9 | $R_{LiDAR} = \dfrac{c \Delta t}{2}$ | LiDAR range from time-of-flight | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $R$: m; $\Delta t$: s |
| 10 | $d_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{step}$ | Arc-length between LiDAR points | $R$: m; $\theta$: rad |
| 11 | $v = r \omega$ | Wheel linear velocity | $v$: m/s; $r$: m; $\omega$: rad/s |
| 12 | $v_{robot} = \dfrac{r (\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | Diff-drive forward velocity | m/s |
| 13 | $\omega_{robot} = \dfrac{r (\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L}$ | Diff-drive turning rate | rad/s |
| 14 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of moving mass | J |
| 15 | $E_{spec} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{m_{bat}}$ | Battery specific energy | Wh/kg |
| 16 | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of a spring-mass actuator (SEA) | Hz |
| 17 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{\tau \omega}{V_s I_a}$ | Motor electrical-to-mechanical efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 18 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | Sensor signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 19 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{k_t k_e}$ | Motor mechanical time constant | seconds |
| 20 | $n = \dfrac{60 f}{P}$ | Synchronous motor speed (BLDC, stepper, induction) | RPM |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equation 3 (torque-speed curve):** Used by every **mobile robot drivetrain designer** to size motors against payload + slope. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to plot this line and identify the **operating point** at a given load.
- **Equation 9 (LiDAR range):** Critical for **autonomous vehicle perception** (e.g., Velodyne VLP-32, Hesai Pandar). Determines how far ahead a vehicle can "see."
- **Equation 15 (specific energy):** Governs the **mission endurance** of a UAV or UGV. A 10 kg agricultural drone with a 5 kg LiPo pack (200 Wh/kg) can fly for ~20 minutes at 1.5 kW hover power: $t = 5 \times 200 / 1500 = 0.667$ h = 40 min theoretical, ~25 min practical.
- **Equation 16 (SEA natural frequency):** Designers of **rehabilitation exoskeletons** tune the spring stiffness $k$ so the SEA bandwidth is **above the human neuromuscular bandwidth (5–10 Hz)** to ensure transparency.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BLDC Motor Torque-Speed Characteristic (Full Algebraic Walk-Through)

**Problem Context.** A robot wheel is driven by a BLDC motor. We wish to find the relationship between the **output torque** $\tau$ delivered to the wheel and the **angular velocity** $\omega$ of the motor shaft.

**Given Constants.**
- Armature (phase) resistance: $R_a$ (Ω)
- Back-EMF constant: $k_e$ (V·s/rad)
- Torque constant: $k_t$ (N·m/A)
- Supply voltage: $V_s$ (V)

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop.**

The supply voltage $V_s$ must overcome the resistive drop $I_a R_a$ and the back-EMF $k_e \omega$ that opposes the supply.

$$V_s = I_a R_a + k_e \omega$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the armature current $I_a$.**

This is the current drawn by the motor at any operating point $(\tau, \omega)$.

$$I_a = \frac{V_s - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the electromechanical coupling $\tau = k_t I_a$.**

Substitute the expression for $I_a$:

$$\tau = k_t \cdot \frac{V_s - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 4 — Distribute $k_t$ to obtain the linear form.**

$$\tau = \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the boundary points.**

- **Stall** ($\omega = 0$): $\tau_{stall} = \dfrac{k_t V_s}{R_a}$
- **No-load** ($\tau = 0$): $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \dfrac{V_s}{k_e}$
- **Slope** of the line: $-\dfrac{k_t k_e}{R_a}$ (negative because torque decreases as speed increases)

**Step 6 — Identify the maximum power transfer point.**

Mechanical power output:

$$P = \tau \omega = \left( \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega \right) \omega = \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} \omega - \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega^{2}$$

Differentiate with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{d\omega} = \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \frac{2 k_t k_e}{R_a} \omega = 0$$

Solve:

$$\omega^{*} = \frac{V_s}{2 k_e} = \frac{\omega_{no\text{-}load}}{2}$$

Substitute back:

$$\tau^{*} = \frac{k_t V_s}{R_a} - \frac{k_t k_e}{R_a} \cdot \frac{V_s}{2 k_e} = \frac{k_t V_s}{2 R_a} = \frac{\tau_{stall}}{2}$$

Maximum mechanical power:

$$P_{max} = \tau^{*} \omega^{*} = \frac{k_t V_s}{2 R_a} \cdot \frac{V_s}{2 k_e} = \frac{k_t V_s^{2}}{4 R_a k_e}$$

If $k_t = k_e$ (in SI units this holds exactly), then:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_s^{2}}{4 R_a}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the mechanical time constant.**

When the motor is energized from rest with no external load, the dynamics are governed by:

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = \tau = k_t I_a = \frac{k_t (V_s - k_e \omega)}{R_a}$$

Rearrange into standard first-order form:

$$\frac{R_a J}{k_t k_e} \frac{d\omega}{dt} + \omega = \frac{V_s}{k_e} = \omega_{no\text{-}load}$$

Therefore the **mechanical time constant** is:

$$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{k_t k_e}$$

**Engineering Interpretation.** $\tau_m$ tells the designer how quickly the motor can reach its no-load speed. A small $\tau_m$ (small $R_a$, small $J$, large $k_t k_e$) gives snappy response — essential for legged robots that must reposition limbs in milliseconds.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Mobile Robot Drivetrain Sizing

**Problem.** A four-wheeled field robot must climb a 15° slope at 0.5 m/s. Total robot mass $m = 50$ kg. Wheel radius $r = 0.15$ m. Assume rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.02$ and drivetrain efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Determine:
(i) The required tractive force.
(ii) The required torque per wheel.
(iii) The required motor power per wheel.
(iv) Whether a motor with $\tau_{stall} = 4$ N·m, $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = 300$ rad/s is adequate at half of its no-load speed.

**Step (i) — Tractive force.**

The robot must overcome gravity along the slope and rolling resistance. On a slope of angle $\alpha$:

$$F_{trac} = m g \sin \alpha + \mu_r m g \cos \alpha$$

With $g = 9.81$ m/s², $m = 50$ kg, $\alpha = 15°$, $\mu_r = 0.02$:

$$F_{trac} = 50 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin 15° + 0.02 \cdot 50 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos 15°$$

$$= 50 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2588 + 0.02 \cdot 50 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9659$$

$$= 126.95 + 9.48 = \mathbf{136.4 \text{ N}}$$

**Step (ii) — Torque per wheel (4 wheels share the load equally).**

$$\tau_{wheel} = \frac{F_{trac} \cdot r}{4 \cdot \eta} = \frac{136.4 \times 0.15}{4 \times 0.85} = \frac{20.46}{3.4} = \mathbf{6.02 \text{ N·m per wheel}}$$

**Step (iii) — Motor power per wheel.**

Wheel angular velocity: $\omega_{wheel} = v/r = 0.5/0.15 = 3.33$ rad/s.

Motor speed (assuming 30:1 gearbox): $\omega_{motor} = 3.33 \times 30 = 100$ rad/s.

$$P_{motor} = \tau_{wheel} \cdot \omega_{wheel} / \eta = 6.02 \times 3.33 / 0.85 = \mathbf{23.6 \text{ W per wheel}}$$

**Step (iv) — Adequacy check.**

At $\omega = 0.5 \cdot \omega_{no\text{-}load} = 150$ rad/s, the torque from the linear curve is:

$$\tau = \frac{\tau_{stall}}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ N·m}$$

But the required motor torque (after gearbox ratio 30) is:

$$\tau_{motor,req} = \frac{\tau_{wheel}}{30 \cdot \eta} = \frac{6.02}{30 \times 0.85} = 0.236 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $0.236 < 2.0$, the motor is **comfortably adequate** with a 8.5× safety margin. A smaller, cheaper motor could be selected.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Family-by-Family Trade-off Summary

| Family | Sub-Category | Strength | Weakness | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor | 2D LiDAR | Long range, 360°, accurate | Heavy, power-hungry, expensive | Autonomous car, warehouse AGV |
| Sensor | RGB-D Camera | Rich 3D data, low cost | Short range (~3 m), sunlight-sensitive | Indoor service robot |
| Sensor | IMU | High-rate (1 kHz), no external dep. | Drifts over time | Drone flight controller |
| Sensor | GPS-RTK | Absolute position, cm accuracy | Outdoor only, multi-path errors | Agricultural tractor |
| Actuator | DC Brushed | Cheap, simple control | Brush wear, lower efficiency | Hobby robot, toy |
| Actuator | BLDC | High efficiency, long life | Needs ESC, more complex | Drone, AGV, exoskeleton |
| Actuator | Hydraulic | Highest force density | Needs pump, leaks, noisy | Mining robot, heavy arm |
| Actuator | Pneumatic (PAM) | Compliant, safe | Needs compressor, slow | Rehabilitation robot |
| Power | Li-Ion | Best all-round | Needs BMS, thermal management | Default for service robots |
| Power | LiPo | High discharge rate | Volatile, swelling risk | Drone |
| Power | Fuel Cell | Long endurance | Hydrogen infrastructure | Long-range UGV |
| Mechanical | Differential Drive | Simple, cheap | Cannot move sideways | Indoor service robot |
| Mechanical | Mecanum Wheel | Omnidirectional | Roller wear, lower efficiency | Warehouse robot |
| Mechanical | Tracked | High traction, rough terrain | Slow, track wear | Search-and-rescue UGV |
| Mechanical | Quadruped | Steps over obstacles | Complex gait control | Spot, ANYmal |
| Control | STM32 MCU | Real-time deterministic | Limited compute | Motor control loop |
| Control | Jetson SBC | GPU-accelerated AI | Not real-time, power-hungry | Vision/SLAM pipeline |
| Control | CAN bus | Robust, multi-node | 1 Mbps ceiling | Automotive robotics |
| Control | EtherCAT | Sub-μs deterministic | Expensive, short cable | Industrial arm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Five-Family Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[Power Source - Family 3] -->|24V DC| PDU[Power Distribution Unit]
    PWR -->|12V DC| SBC[Single Board Computer - Family 5]
    PWR -->|5V DC| MCU[Microcontroller - Family 5]
    PDU -->|High Current| MD[Motor Drivers - Family 5]
    SENS[Sensor Array - Family 1] -->|USB / Ethernet| SBC
    SENS -->|SPI / I2C / UART| MCU
    MCU -->|CAN bus| MD
    SBC -->|Decision Commands| MCU
    MD -->|3-phase PWM| MOT[BLDC Motors - Family 2]
    MOT -->|Torque| GBX[Gearbox - Family 4]
    GBX -->|Reduced RPM| WHL[Wheels / Legs - Family 4]
    WHL -->|Motion| GROUND[Ground Environment]
    WHL -->|Encoder Feedback| SENS
    GROUND -->|Slip and Terrain| SENS
    SBC -->|WiFi / 4G| OP[Operator / Cloud]
```

> **How to read this diagram.** Energy flows downward from the Power Source. Control signals flow **downward** (SBC → MCU → MD → MOT → WHL). Feedback signals flow **upward** (WHL → SENS → SBC). The four ground-level arrows close the perception–action loop.

### 4.2 Hierarchy Diagram of the Five Families

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Families of Components in FSR] --> A1[Family 1: Sensors - Perception]
    ROOT --> A2[Family 2: Actuators - Action]
    ROOT --> A3[Family 3: Power Systems - Energy]
    ROOT --> A4[Family 4: Mechanical Structure - Skeleton]
    ROOT --> A5[Family 5: Control and Computation - Brain]
    A1 --> A1A[Proprioceptive]
    A1 --> A1B[Exteroceptive]
    A1A --> A1A1[Encoders]
    A1A --> A1A2[IMU]
    A1A --> A1A3[Joint Torque Sensors]
    A1B --> A1B1[2D and 3D LiDAR]
    A1B --> A1B2[Stereo and RGB-D Cameras]
    A1B --> A1B3[GPS and GNSS]
    A1B --> A1B4[Ultrasonic and IR]
    A2 --> A2A[Electromagnetic]
    A2 --> A2B[Fluid Power]
    A2 --> A2C[Smart Materials]
    A2A --> A2A1[DC Brushed]
    A2A --> A2A2[BLDC]
    A2A --> A2A3[Servo]
    A2A --> A2A4[Stepper]
    A2B --> A2B1[Hydraulic Cylinders]
    A2B --> A2B2[Pneumatic Artificial Muscles]
    A2C --> A2C1[Shape Memory Alloys]
    A2C --> A2C2[Series Elastic Actuators]
    A3 --> A3A[Electrochemical]
    A3 --> A3B[Chemical to Mechanical]
    A3 --> A3C[Photovoltaic]
    A3A --> A3A1[Li-Ion]
    A3A --> A3A2[LiPo]
    A3A --> A3A3[Lead Acid]
    A3B --> A3B1[Hydrogen Fuel Cell]
    A3B --> A3B2[Diesel Engine]
    A3C --> A3C1[Solar Panels]
    A4 --> A4A[Joints and Links]
    A4 --> A4B[Locomotion]
    A4 --> A4C[Transmissions]
    A4 --> A4D[End Effectors]
    A4A --> A4A1[Revolute]
    A4A --> A4A2[Prismatic]
    A4A --> A4A3[Spherical]
    A4B --> A4B1[Wheels]
    A4B --> A4B2[Tracked]
    A4B --> A4B3[Legged]
    A4B --> A4B4[Aerial Propellers]
    A4C --> A4C1[Planetary Gear]
    A4C --> A4C2[Harmonic Drive]
    A4C --> A4C3[Belt and Chain]
    A5 --> A5A[Low Level - MCU]
    A5 --> A5B[High Level - SBC]
    A5 --> A5C[Communication Buses]
    A5A --> A5A1[STM32]
    A5A --> A5A2[Arduino]
    A5A --> A5A3[ESP32]
    A5B --> A5B1[Raspberry Pi]
    A5B --> A5B2[NVIDIA Jetson]
    A5C --> A5C1[CAN Bus]
    A5C --> A5C2[EtherCAT]
    A5C --> A5C3[UART and RS485]
```

### 4.3 Sensor Sub-Family Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensor Family] --> PROP[Proprioceptive]
    SENS --> EXT[Exteroceptive]
    PROP --> ENC[Encoders - Wheel Odometry]
    PROP --> IMU[IMU - 6-DOF Pose]
    PROP --> JTS[Joint Torque Sensors]
    PROP --> BAT[Battery Sensors - V I T]
    EXT --> LID[LiDAR - 2D and 3D]
    EXT --> CAM[Stereo and RGB-D Cameras]
    EXT --> GPS[GPS and GNSS]
    EXT --> ULT[Ultrasonic and IR]
    EXT --> TAC[Tactile and Force-Skin]
```

### 4.4 Power and Energy Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Energy Source] --> STO[Storage]
    SRC --> GEN[Generator]
    STO --> REG[Voltage Regulators]
    GEN --> REG
    REG --> RAIL5[5V Rail]
    REG --> RAIL12[12V Rail]
    REG --> RAIL24[24V Rail]
    RAIL5 --> LOGIC[Logic and Sensors]
    RAIL12 --> COMP[Single Board Computer]
    RAIL24 --> PWR[Power Stage]
    PWR --> MOT[BLDC Motors]
    REG --> LOSS[Thermal Losses]
```

> **Why this matters in KTU questions:** When asked to "justify the choice of power source for a 4-hour mission," you must show the **energy flow** from source to load, including **losses** at the regulator and motor stages. A common student error is to ignore the **DC-DC converter efficiency (90–95%)** and the **motor efficiency (80–95%)**, which together reduce delivered energy by **15–25%**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1.** [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Families of Components"** in the context of field and service robotics. Enumerate the five primary families with one example component for each.

**Model Answer.**

The term **"Families of Components"** refers to the systematic classification of all hardware building blocks of a field or service robot into groups based on the **functional role** they perform in the perception–decision–action loop, rather than on the underlying physical technology.

The five primary families are:

1. **Sensor (Perception) Family** — e.g., 2D LiDAR for obstacle detection.
2. **Actuator (Action) Family** — e.g., BLDC motor for wheel drive.
3. **Power (Energy) Family** — e.g., Li-Ion battery pack.
4. **Mechanical (Structure) Family** — e.g., harmonic-drive gearbox.
5. **Control & Computation (Brain) Family** — e.g., NVIDIA Jetson SBC running ROS 2.

> **Valuation Tip:** Full 3 marks require **all five families** and **one example each**. Vague lists ("motors, sensors, batteries") without grouping by family lose 1.5 marks.

---

**Q2.** [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **proprioceptive** and **exteroceptive** sensors. Give two examples of each as deployed in a mobile robot.

**Model Answer.**

| Property | Proprioceptive Sensor | Exteroceptive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | Internal robot state (pose, joint angles, battery) | External world (obstacles, landmarks) |
| **Energy source** | Robot's own motion / battery | External stimuli (light, sound, RF) |
| **Examples** | (i) Wheel optical encoder (odometry); (ii) MEMS IMU (roll/pitch/yaw) | (i) 2D LiDAR (obstacle map); (ii) GPS receiver (absolute position) |

> **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is to list **LiDAR and Camera under proprioception**. Both are exteroceptive. The examiner deducts 1 mark per wrong classification.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A.** [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]**

**A (a).** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, describe the **five families of components** of a typical mobile service robot. For each family, state two representative components and their primary function. **[7 Marks]**

**A (b).** A differential-drive mobile robot is driven by two identical BLDC motors with the following specifications:
- Wheel radius $r = 0.10$ m
- Wheel base $L = 0.40$ m
- Torque constant $k_t = 0.05$ N·m/A
- Back-EMF constant $k_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad
- Armature resistance $R_a = 1.0$ Ω
- Supply voltage $V_s = 24$ V

Compute: (i) the **stall torque** of each motor; (ii) the **no-load speed** in RPM; (iii) the **linear velocity** and **angular velocity** of the robot when both wheels are driven at half the no-load speed. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Answer to A (a).**

Refer to the **Master Block Diagram (Section 4.1)** for the figure.

1. **Sensor (Perception) Family** — collects data about the robot and its environment.
   - 2D LiDAR: measures obstacle distances via time-of-flight, builds a 360° scan.
   - IMU: provides 6-DOF inertial state (acceleration + angular velocity).

2. **Actuator (Action) Family** — converts electrical energy to mechanical motion.
   - BLDC motor: high-efficiency rotational drive for wheels.
   - Servo motor: closed-loop position control for robot arm joints.

3. **Power (Energy) Family** — stores and distributes electrical energy.
   - Li-Ion battery pack: high specific energy source for mobile operation.
   - DC-DC converter: regulates bus voltage for downstream electronics.

4. **Mechanical (Structure) Family** — provides physical form and transmits motion.
   - Harmonic drive reducer: high-ratio (1:50 to 1:160) torque multiplier.
   - Mecanum wheel: omnidirectional locomotion via angled rollers.

5. **Control & Computation (Brain) Family** — processes data and issues commands.
   - NVIDIA Jetson: high-level AI / SLAM computation.
   - CAN bus: deterministic multi-node communication between MCUs and motor drivers.

The five families are linked in a **closed perception–action loop**: sensors feed the brain; the brain commands actuators; actuators drive the structure; the structure moves the robot; sensors observe the new state.

---

**Model Answer to A (b).**

**(i) Stall Torque.**

At $\omega = 0$, all supply voltage drops across $R_a$:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_s}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1.0} = 24 \text{ A}$$

$$\tau_{stall} = k_t I_{stall} = 0.05 \times 24 = \mathbf{1.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(ii) No-Load Speed (RPM).**

At no load, $V_s = k_e \omega_{nl}$:

$$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_s}{k_e} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{nl} = \frac{60 \omega_{nl}}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 480}{2\pi} = \mathbf{4583 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) Robot Velocities at Half No-Load Speed.**

Half no-load speed: $\omega_{motor} = 240$ rad/s. With direct drive (gear ratio 1:1), wheel speed is also 240 rad/s.

Linear velocity:

$$v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2} = \frac{0.10 \times (240 + 240)}{2} = \mathbf{24 \text{ m/s}}$$

Angular velocity (robot turning rate):

$$\omega_{robot} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{L} = \frac{0.10 \times (240 - 240)}{0.40} = \mathbf{0 \text{ rad/s}}$$

The robot moves **straight ahead at 24 m/s (= 86.4 km/h)**, which is far too fast for an indoor service robot — illustrating the necessity of a **reduction gearbox** (typically 30:1 to 100:1) to bring the operating speed into a practical range (0.3 – 1.5 m/s).

---

**Question B.** [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**B (a).** Classify the **Sensor (Perception) Family** into proprioceptive and exteroceptive categories. For each category, explain the operating principle of **two representative sensors** used in field and service robots. **[7 Marks]**

**B (b).** A 2D LiDAR used on an autonomous car has the following specifications:
- Pulse repetition rate: $f_{rep} = 20$ kHz
- Angular resolution: $\theta_{step} = 0.25°$
- Maximum time-of-flight window: $\Delta t_{max} = 1.5$ μs

Calculate: (i) the **maximum unambiguous range**; (ii) the **number of points per full 360° scan**; (iii) the **arc-length between two consecutive points** at a target located 25 m away. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Answer to B (a).**

The Sensor Family is divided by **what is being sensed**:

**Proprioceptive Sensors (Internal State):**

1. **Optical Encoder.** A disc with $N$ alternating transparent and opaque slots rotates between an LED and a photodiode. Each slot edge produces an electrical pulse. The pulse count over a sampling interval $\Delta t$ gives angular displacement $\theta = (2\pi / N) \cdot \text{count}$, and the pulse frequency gives angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f / N$. Encoders are used for **wheel odometry** and **joint position feedback** in robot arms.

2. **MEMS Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU).** Combines three MEMS devices: (a) a 3-axis gyroscope exploiting the **Coriolis effect** on a vibrating proof mass; (b) a 3-axis accelerometer exploiting **proof-mass displacement** against a capacitive sensing element; (c) a 3-axis magnetometer using the **Hall effect** or **magnetoresistance** to sense Earth's magnetic field. Sensor fusion (e.g., Madgwick or Mahony filter) yields roll, pitch, and yaw at 1 kHz.

**Exteroceptive Sensors (External Environment):**

1. **2D LiDAR.** A laser diode (typically 905 nm, eye-safe class 1) emits short pulses; a rotating mirror sweeps the beam through 360°. The receiver detects the reflected pulse and a Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$. Range is computed as $R = c \Delta t / 2$. The scan produces a 2D point cloud used for **obstacle detection and SLAM**.

2. **Stereo Camera.** Two cameras separated by a baseline $b$ image the same scene from slightly different viewpoints. For a feature observed at pixel disparity $d$, depth is computed via triangulation:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

where $f$ is the focal length in pixels. Stereo cameras provide **dense 3D perception** at close range (1 – 20 m) without active illumination.

---

**Model Answer to B (b).**

**(i) Maximum Unambiguous Range.**

The pulse travels to the target and back, so total path = $2R$:

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_{max}}{2} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{450}{2} = \mathbf{225 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Number of Points per 360° Scan.**

$$\theta_{step} = 0.25° \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{360°}{0.25°} = \mathbf{1440 \text{ points/scan}}$$

**(iii) Arc-Length Between Adjacent Points at 25 m.**

Convert $\theta$ to radians:

$$\theta_{step} = 0.25 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 4.363 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

Arc-length:

$$d_{arc} = R \cdot \theta_{step} = 25 \times 4.363 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{0.109 \text{ m} \approx 10.9 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (B (b)):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in (i) — answer 450 m is **wrong**; you must divide by 2 because light makes a round trip.
> 2. **Using degrees instead of radians** in (iii) — the formula $d = R\theta$ requires $\theta$ in radians. Using degrees yields an answer 57.3× too large.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit on $N$** in (ii) — write "1440 points per scan," not just "1440."
> 4. **Sanity check:** 0.25° at 25 m gives ~11 cm spacing — a reasonable value for a moving car (objects smaller than 11 cm may be missed, which is acceptable for highway driving).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Families of Components**

**Foundational Concepts**
- [ ] Five families: **Sensor | Actuator | Power | Mechanical | Control**.
- [ ] Classification criterion is **functional role**, not technology.
- [ ] All five families participate in a **closed perception–action loop** at 50–200 Hz.

**Sensor Family**
- [ ] Proprioceptive = internal state (encoder, IMU, joint torque, battery sensors).
- [ ] Exteroceptive = external world (LiDAR, camera, GPS, ultrasonic, tactile).
- [ ] LiDAR range: $R = c \Delta t / 2$ (round trip — divide by 2!).
- [ ] LiDAR point spacing: $d = R \theta_{step}$ (radians!).
- [ ] SNR in dB: $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$.

**Actuator Family**
- [ ] Torque-speed line: $\tau = \tau_{stall} - (k_t k_e / R_a) \omega$.
- [ ] Stall torque: $\tau_{stall} = k_t V_s / R_a$.
- [ ] No-load speed: $\omega_{nl} = V_s / k_e$.
- [ ] Max power at $\tfrac{1}{2} \tau_{stall}$ and $\tfrac{1}{2} \omega_{nl}$.
- [ ] BLDC: high efficiency, long life. Stepper: open-loop positioning. Servo: closed-loop. Hydraulic: high force. Pneumatic: compliant. SEA: force-controlled.

**Power Family**
- [ ] Li-Ion is the default for service robots (150–250 Wh/kg).
- [ ] LiPo for drones (high C-rate, more volatile).
- [ ] Fuel cell for long endurance.
- [ ] Solar for planetary rovers.
- [ ] Specific energy $E_{spec} = E_{bat} / m_{bat}$ in Wh/kg.

**Mechanical Family**
- [ ] Joints: R (revolute), P (prismatic), S (spherical).
- [ ] Locomotion: wheels (diff / Ackermann / Mecanum), tracks, legs.
- [ ] Harmonic drive: high ratio (1:50–1:160), zero backlash.
- [ ] Differential-drive kinematics: $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$, $\omega_{robot} = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/L$.

**Control & Computation Family**
- [ ] MCU for real-time low-level (PWM, encoder).
- [ ] SBC for high-level perception (SLAM, vision).
- [ ] CAN bus: 1 Mbps, automotive, robust. EtherCAT: sub-μs, industrial.
- [ ] Always specify **communication protocol** in design answers.

**Exam Hygiene**
- [ ] Always carry the **factor of 2** for LiDAR range.
- [ ] Always convert **angles to radians** in arc-length formulas.
- [ ] Always write **SI units** in the boxed final answer.
- [ ] Always specify **both the family and the function** of any component you list.
- [ ] When a design question asks for a "choice," justify with **at least one quantitative metric** (range, accuracy, specific energy, torque, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Component Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Component Interfaces** in Field and Service Robotics refer to the **standardized mechanical, electrical, electronic, communication, and software boundaries** that enable heterogeneous hardware and software modules (sensors, actuators, controllers, power systems, and computing units) to be physically coupled, exchange data, and operate as a coherent robotic system without ambiguity in signal, power, or protocol semantics.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT602)**, Component Interfaces constitute the **abstraction layer** between the *physical embodiment* of the robot and the *computational/control logic*. They ensure **interoperability, modularity, scalability, and fault isolation** — the four cardinal pillars demanded by service and field-grade robotic platforms operating in unstructured environments.

The interfaces are broadly classified under five pillars:

| S.No | Interface Domain | Role in Robot |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mechanical Interface** | Physical mounting, kinematic coupling, force transmission |
| 2 | **Electrical / Power Interface** | Voltage rails, current delivery, grounding |
| 3 | **Communication Interface** | Data exchange between distributed modules |
| 4 | **Sensor-Actuator Interface** | Signal conditioning, ADC/DAC, driver electronics |
| 5 | **Software / Middleware Interface** | ROS nodes, message passing, HAL, drivers |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Human Body**
> Imagine a humanoid robot as a **human body**. The *bones* (mechanical interfaces) hold the *muscles* (actuators) together. The *nervous system* (communication interfaces like CAN, Ethernet) carries signals between the *brain* (CPU/controller) and the *eyes, ears, skin* (sensors). The *bloodstream* (power interfaces) supplies energy. The *synapses* (software interfaces like ROS topics) define how one organ talks to another.
> If any of these interfaces is missing or non-standard, the body fails — even if each organ is perfect in isolation.

**Engineering Intuition:** Component interfaces are essentially the **"contracts"** between modules. Just as a USB Type-C connector guarantees that *any* USB-C device will plug into *any* USB-C port with predictable voltage (5 V / 20 V max), data rate (up to 10 Gbps in USB 3.1), and protocol semantics — well-defined robotic interfaces guarantee that a new LiDAR can replace an old one with minimal re-engineering.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** govern the design of component interfaces in field/service robotics:

- **Operating Voltage Rails:** $\mathbf{3.3\ V,\ 5\ V,\ 12\ V,\ 24\ V,\ 48\ V}$
- **Communication Baud Rates:** UART up to $\mathbf{1\ Mbps}$; SPI up to $\mathbf{50\ Mbps}$; CAN up to $\mathbf{1\ Mbps}$; Ethernet up to $\mathbf{1\ Gbps}$; USB up to $\mathbf{10\ Gbps}$
- **Standardized Mechanical Pitch:** $\mathbf{25.4\ mm}$ (1 inch) hole-pattern for modular mounts
- **ROS Message Frequency:** typically $\mathbf{10–100\ Hz}$ for control loops, $\mathbf{30\ Hz}$ for vision
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** $\mathbf{SIL\ 1\ to\ SIL\ 3}$ per IEC 61508 for service robots
- **EMC Compliance:** $\mathbf{EN\ 55032}$ / $\mathbf{FCC\ Part\ 15}$

---

## 1.4 Why Component Interfaces Matter in Field Robotics

Field robots operate in **unstructured, harsh, and remote environments** (agriculture, mining, underwater, space, disaster zones). The **modular design philosophy** — enabled by clean interfaces — allows:
1. **Hot-swap replacement** of damaged modules in the field.
2. **Plug-and-play addition** of new sensors without firmware rewrite.
3. **Cross-vendor interoperability** (e.g., mixing Velodyne LiDAR with Hokuyo lasers via standardized drivers).
4. **Fault isolation** so a sensor failure does not cascade into total system shutdown.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes that *every* robotic component must be evaluated via three interface questions — **(1) How does it mount? (2) How does it draw power? (3) How does it communicate?** Master this triad and you master the topic.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Interface Architecture

A field/service robot's interfaces are organized into a **hierarchical five-layer model**, conceptually similar to the OSI model used in networking:

### Layer 1 — Mechanical Interface
Responsible for the *physical coupling* between components.
- **Mounting Standards:** VESA patterns, NEMA stepper mounts, GoPro-style fingers, ROS-Industrial mounting conventions.
- **Load Considerations:** Static + dynamic loads must be supported; vibration damping is critical.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** Defined by the kinematic coupling (e.g., revolute, prismatic, spherical).

### Layer 2 — Power Interface
Defines how electrical energy is delivered.
- **Voltage regulation** (LDO, buck, boost converters).
- **Current budgeting** — total system current is the sum of all component draws.
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS)** with SOC (State of Charge) and SOH (State of Health) monitoring.
- **Ground isolation** to prevent ground loops and noise injection.

### Layer 3 — Signal / Sensor-Actuator Interface
The analog/digital boundary.
- **ADC Resolution:** Governed by the formula in §3.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, isolation.
- **PWM Frequency Selection:** Critical to avoid audible noise and motor cogging.
- **Encoder Interfaces:** Quadrature, SSI, BiSS for absolute position feedback.

### Layer 4 — Communication Interface
The *data transport backbone*.
- **Synchronous Protocols:** SPI, I²C (clock line shared).
- **Asynchronous Protocols:** UART, RS-232, RS-485.
- **Fieldbus Protocols:** CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay.
- **High-Speed Protocols:** Ethernet (TCP/UDP), EtherCAT, PROFINET, USB 3.0.
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ac/ax), Bluetooth 5.0, LoRa, Zigbee.

### Layer 5 — Software / Middleware Interface
The *semantic* layer.
- **Robot Operating System (ROS/ROS 2):** Topic-based pub/sub, services, actions.
- **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Vendor-neutral APIs.
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service):** Underlying middleware of ROS 2.
- **Message Standards:** sensor_msgs, geometry_msgs, nav_msgs.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **exam-relevant equations** for the Component Interfaces topic. Master these — they appear in derivations, numerical problems, and design questions.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Sensor interface lower bound |
| 2 | ADC Quantization Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} - 1}$ | Volts | $N$ = ADC bit-width |
| 3 | Quantization SNR | $\mathrm{SNR}_Q = 6.02 N + 1.76$ | dB | For ideal ADC |
| 4 | Signal Power (avg) | $P = \dfrac{V_{rms}^{2}}{R}$ | Watts | $R$ = load impedance |
| 5 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq D \leq 1$ |
| 6 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Volts | For ideal DC motor drive |
| 7 | CAN Bus Bit Time | $t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$ | seconds | Sum of four segments |
| 8 | Throughput (Baud) | $R_{bps} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}}$ | bits/s | Inverse of bit time |
| 9 | UART Frame Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + N_{start} + N_{stop} + N_{parity}}$ | ratio | Typically 80% for 8N1 |
| 10 | I²C Pull-up Resistance | $R_{pull} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | Ohms | Min/Max from datasheet |
| 11 | Encoder Resolution | $P_{counts} = P_{lines} \times 4$ | counts/rev | Quadrature decoding |
| 12 | DC Motor Back-EMF | $V_b = K_e \omega$ | Volts | $K_e$ in V·s/rad |
| 13 | Total System Current | $I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i$ | Amperes | Sum of all loads |
| 14 | Power Dissipation (regulator) | $P_{diss} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{load}$ | Watts | Linear regulator |
| 15 | Latency (Pipeline) | $L = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_i$ | seconds | Sum of interface delays |
| 16 | Maximum Cable Length (RS-485) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{10^{7}}{R_{baud}}$ | meters | Rule of thumb at 24 AWG |
| 17 | ROS Topic Bandwidth | $B = S \cdot f \cdot W$ | bytes/s | $S$=msg size, $f$=freq, $W$=width |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Domain | Interface Used | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Agricultural Robots** | CAN bus + ROS 2 + GPS | Robust to EMI from tractor alternators |
| **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)** | EtherCAT + SIL-3 hardware | Deterministic latency under 1 ms |
| **Mars Rovers (Perseverance)** | SpaceWire + rad-hardened CAN | Radiation tolerance, fault isolation |
| **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva)** | Wi-Fi 6 + ROS 2 + DDS | Multi-robot coordination at scale |
| **Underwater AUVs** | RS-485 + acoustic modem | Long-distance, low-bandwidth telemetry |
| **Disaster Response Robots** | Hot-swap modular mounts + USB-C PD | Field-replaceable in < 5 minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of interface is not academic — it is **mission-critical**. A wrong baud rate in CAN can stall a 2-ton agricultural robot. A missing pull-up resistor on I²C can lock up an entire sensor array. Always refer to the **datasheet timing diagrams** before integration.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** for the most exam-relevant formulas. Every algebraic transition is justified.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Quantization Resolution ($V_{LSB}$)

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC operating over a full-scale voltage range of $V_{FS} = 3.3\ \text{V}$ is interfaced to a LiDAR analog front-end. Determine the Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage.

**Step 1 — Definition of Full-Scale Range**
The ADC maps a continuous analog input $V_{in} \in [0, V_{FS}]$ to a discrete digital code $D \in [0, 2^N - 1]$, where $N$ is the bit-width.

**Step 2 — Number of Discrete Levels**
The total number of quantization levels is:

$$
L = 2^N - 1
$$

For $N = 12$:

$$
L = 2^{12} - 1 = 4096 - 1 = 4095
$$

**Step 3 — Define the LSB Voltage**
The LSB voltage is the analog step corresponding to a 1-bit change in the digital code. By definition:

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{L} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{3.3\ \text{V}}{4095}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate**

$$
V_{LSB} = 8.06 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 0.806\ \text{mV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 12-bit ADC on a 3.3 V rail resolves analog signals in steps of **0.806 mV**. Any signal variation smaller than this is *invisible* to the ADC — a fundamental interface limitation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quantization SNR for an Ideal ADC

**Step 1 — Define Quantization Noise Power**
For a uniform quantizer with step size $V_{LSB}$, the quantization error $e$ lies uniformly in $[-V_{LSB}/2, +V_{LSB}/2]$. The mean-square error (noise power) is:

$$
\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e^{2}\, de
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \dfrac{e^{3}}{3}\Big|_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}\left[\left(\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^{3}\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 2 \cdot \dfrac{V_{LSB}^{3}}{8}
$$

$$
\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}^{2}}{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume Full-Scale Sinusoidal Signal**
The signal power for a sine wave with peak amplitude $V_p = V_{FS}/2$ is:

$$
P_{signal} = \dfrac{V_{p}^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{V_{FS}^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_{FS} = V_{LSB}(2^N - 1) \approx V_{LSB} \cdot 2^N$**

$$
P_{signal} = \dfrac{(V_{LSB} \cdot 2^N)^{2}}{8} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}^{2} \cdot 2^{2N}}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Form the SNR Ratio**

$$
\mathrm{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}^{2} \cdot 2^{2N} / 8}{V_{LSB}^{2}/12} = \dfrac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8}
$$

$$
\mathrm{SNR} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Decibels**

$$
\mathrm{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20N \log_{10}(2)
$$

$$
\mathrm{SNR}_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02\,N
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{SNR}_{Q} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\ \text{dB}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PWM Average Voltage & Power Delivery to DC Motor

**Step 1 — Define Duty Cycle**
A PWM signal with period $T$ has an "on" duration of $T_{on}$ and "off" duration of $T_{off}$. The duty cycle is:

$$
D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Time-Averaged Output Voltage**
For an ideal switching element (zero rise/fall time), the average voltage delivered to the motor terminals is:

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt = \dfrac{1}{T}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\, dt\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral**

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T}\left[V_s \cdot T_{on}\right] = V_s \cdot \dfrac{T_{on}}{T}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Power Delivered to a Resistive Load $R_m$**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{avg}^{2}}{R_m} = \dfrac{(D \cdot V_s)^{2}}{R_m} = D^{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^{2}}{R_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Power scales with $D^{2}$, not $D$. A 50% duty cycle delivers only **25%** of full power — a frequent conceptual error in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CAN Bus Bit Time & Maximum Achievable Baud Rate

**Step 1 — Bit Time Composition**
Per ISO 11898-1, a CAN bit is partitioned into four non-overlapping time segments:

$$
t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}
$$

All segments are integer multiples of the *Time Quantum* $t_q$, set by the CAN controller's baud-rate prescaler:

$$
t_q = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{PRESDIV} + 1}{f_{CAN\_CLK}}
$$

where $f_{CAN\_CLK}$ is the peripheral clock of the microcontroller (e.g., 42 MHz on STM32).

**Step 2 — Define the Total Time Quanta per Bit**

$$
N_{bit} = 1 + \text{PROP} + \text{PHASE1} + \text{PHASE2}
$$

For a typical 1 Mbps CAN-FD configuration: $N_{bit} = 1 + 3 + 7 + 7 = 18$ time quanta.

**Step 3 — Solve for the Maximum Bit Rate**

$$
t_{bit} = N_{bit} \cdot t_q
$$

$$
R_{bps} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}} = \dfrac{f_{CAN\_CLK}}{N_{bit}(2 \cdot \text{PRESDIV} + 1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**
Assume $f_{CAN\_CLK} = 42\ \text{MHz}$, $\text{PRESDIV} = 1$, $N_{bit} = 18$:

$$
R_{bps} = \dfrac{42 \times 10^{6}}{18 \times 3} = \dfrac{42 \times 10^{6}}{54} = 7.78 \times 10^{5}\ \text{bps}
$$

$$
R_{bps} \approx 778\ \text{kbps}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — ROS Topic Bandwidth Requirement

**Step 1 — Define Parameters**
- Message size per publish: $S$ bytes
- Publish frequency: $f$ Hz
- Subscribers count: $W$

**Step 2 — Bandwidth Formula**
Each subscriber consumes bandwidth $S \cdot f$. With $W$ subscribers:

$$
B = W \cdot S \cdot f
$$

**Step 3 — Example: Velodyne VLP-16 Point Cloud**
- $S = 1200$ bytes/msg (compressed point cloud packet)
- $f = 10$ Hz
- $W = 3$ subscribers (localization, mapping, obstacle detection)

$$
B = 3 \cdot 1200 \cdot 10 = 36{,}000\ \text{bytes/s} = 36\ \text{KB/s} = 288\ \text{kbps}
$$

This fits comfortably within a 1 Gbps Ethernet interface.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Interface to Choose?

| Scenario | Recommended Interface | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Short-distance, multi-drop, sensor network | **I²C** | 2-wire simplicity, addressable nodes |
| High-speed SPI sensor (e.g., IMU) | **SPI** | Up to 50 Mbps, full-duplex |
| Vehicle chassis wiring (> 1 m, noisy) | **CAN** | Differential, CRC, robust to EMI |
| Real-time multi-axis motor control | **EtherCAT** | Sub-microsecond synchronization |
| Multi-robot fleet coordination | **Wi-Fi + ROS 2 DDS** | High bandwidth, scalable discovery |
| Long-distance telemetry (field, 1 km+) | **LoRa / RS-485** | Low power, differential noise immunity |
| Surgical / safety-critical | **EtherCAT + SIL-3** | Deterministic + certified safety |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define "Component Interface" in the context of field robotics. List any four types of interfaces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Component Interface* is a standardized boundary — mechanical, electrical, communication, or software — that defines how one robotic module physically couples, exchanges power, and communicates with another module without ambiguity in semantics.

Four types:
1. **Mechanical Interface** — physical mounting and kinematic coupling (1 mark)
2. **Electrical / Power Interface** — voltage rails, current delivery (½ mark)
3. **Communication Interface** — CAN, SPI, I²C, Ethernet (1 mark)
4. **Software / Middleware Interface** — ROS topics, services, HAL (½ mark)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]`
**State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Why is it critical in sensor interfaces?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (2 marks).

**Importance in sensor interfaces:** If the ADC of a LiDAR or IMU interface samples below this rate, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency noise masquerades as low-frequency signal, causing the robot to misinterpret obstacles, drift in localization, or destabilize control loops (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main Paper]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain the **five-layer interface architecture** of a field/service robot with neat block diagrams. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A **12-bit ADC** is interfaced to an analog front-end of a robotic sensor operating between **0 V and 5 V**. Compute: (i) the LSB voltage, (ii) the theoretical quantization SNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Five-Layer Interface Architecture

1. **Mechanical Layer (1.5 marks)** — Physical mounting: VESA, NEMA patterns; defines kinematic coupling (revolute/prismatic joints), vibration isolation, and load-bearing capacity.

2. **Power Layer (1 mark)** — Distributes energy from battery/power supply through DC-DC converters to all modules. Includes BMS, fuses, ground isolation.

3. **Signal / Sensor-Actuator Layer (1.5 marks)** — Analog-to-digital conversion, PWM generation, encoder decoding, signal conditioning (amplification, filtering).

4. **Communication Layer (1.5 marks)** — Bus protocols (CAN, SPI, I²C, Ethernet). Manages data integrity via CRC, arbitration, addressing.

5. **Software Middleware Layer (1.5 marks)** — ROS 2 / DDS / HAL. Provides publish-subscribe semantics, hardware abstraction, and message standards (sensor_msgs, geometry_msgs).

**Sketch:** Draw a vertical stack of 5 boxes labeled Mechanical → Power → Signal → Communication → Software, with arrows showing data flow from physical world upward to computation, and control commands downward.

---

#### (b) Numerical Computation

**Given:** $N = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 5\ \text{V}$

**(i) LSB Voltage (3 marks)**

Apply:

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} - 1} = \dfrac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{5}{4095}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} = 1.221 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 1.221\ \text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Quantization SNR (4 marks)**

Apply:

$$
\mathrm{SNR}_Q = 6.02\,N + 1.76
$$

$$
\mathrm{SNR}_Q = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\ \text{dB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{LSB} = 1.221\ \text{mV}$, $\mathrm{SNR}_Q = 74\ \text{dB}$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Compare **CAN, SPI, and I²C** communication interfaces with respect to speed, wiring, arbitration, and typical robotic application. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A PWM signal driving a **24 V DC motor** has a duty cycle of **$D = 0.4$**. Compute: (i) the average voltage across the motor, (ii) the average power if the motor winding resistance is **$R = 6\ \Omega$**, (iii) comment on why PWM is preferred over a linear rheostat control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | **CAN** | **SPI** | **I²C** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | 1 Mbps (CAN), 5 Mbps (CAN-FD) | Up to 50 Mbps | Up to 5 Mbps (High-speed mode) |
| **Wiring** | 2 wires (CAN-H, CAN-L) differential | 4 wires (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, CS) | 2 wires (SDA, SCL) |
| **Arbitration** | Non-destructive CSMA/CA — priority by ID | Chip-select based, master-driven | Addressed (7/10-bit), also master-driven |
| **Topology** | Multi-drop, up to 1 Mbps | Point-to-point (or daisy via CS) | Multi-master, multi-slave |
| **Robotic Application** | Vehicle chassis, motor controllers | IMU, high-speed ADC | Low-speed sensors, EEPROM config |

(Each correct row: 1 mark × 5 rows ≈ 5 marks + 2 marks for any valid robotic example.)

---

#### (b) PWM Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$, $D = 0.4$, $R = 6\ \Omega$

**(i) Average Voltage (2 marks)**

$$
V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Average Power (3 marks)**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{V_{avg}^{2}}{R} = \dfrac{(9.6)^{2}}{6} = \dfrac{92.16}{6} = 15.36\ \text{W}
$$

**(iii) Why PWM over Linear Rheostat (2 marks)**

PWM drives the transistor in **cut-off** (zero current, zero dissipation) or **saturation** (full current, small $V_{CE}$ drop) — yielding efficiencies of **90–98%**. A linear rheostat dissipates $(V_s - V_m) \cdot I$ as heat, giving efficiencies often below 50%, which is unacceptable for battery-powered field robots. PWM also enables precise digital speed control via duty-cycle modulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Component Interface Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{LSB}$ formula denominator:** Some students write $2^N$ instead of $2^N - 1$. Always use $\mathbf{2^{N} - 1}$ unless the question explicitly says "approximately."
> 2. **PWM Power scaling:** Power scales with $\mathbf{D^{2}}$, not $D$. Writing $P = D \cdot V^2 / R$ loses 2 marks.
> 3. **CAN vs CAN-FD:** Examiners distinguish strictly. CAN-FD = flexible data-rate, up to 5 Mbps; classic CAN = 1 Mbps max. Mixing these up costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always carry units through derivations. A correct number without unit loses ½ mark in numericals.
> 5. **ROS 1 vs ROS 2:** ROS 1 uses XMLRPC + TCPROS; ROS 2 uses **DDS**. If the question says "modern middleware," write ROS 2.
> 6. **Sampling theorem inequality direction:** Some students write $f_s \leq 2 f_{max}$. The correct form is $\mathbf{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}$. Read the inequality arrow carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Component Interface = Contract** between hardware/software modules.
- [ ] **5 layers:** Mechanical → Power → Signal → Communication → Software.
- [ ] **3 master questions for any new component:** How does it mount? How is it powered? How does it communicate?
- [ ] **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- [ ] **ADC:** $V_{LSB} = V_{FS} / (2^N - 1)$; $\mathrm{SNR}_Q = 6.02N + 1.76\ \text{dB}$.
- [ ] **PWM:** $V_{avg} = D V_s$; $P \propto D^{2}$.
- [ ] **CAN:** differential, 1 Mbps classic / 5 Mbps FD, non-destructive arbitration.
- [ ] **SPI:** 4-wire, fast (50 Mbps), point-to-point.
- [ ] **I²C:** 2-wire, addressable, needs pull-ups.
- [ ] **ROS 2 = DDS-based**, not XMLRPC like ROS 1.
- [ ] **Encoder:** Quadrature gives $4 \times$ line count.
- [ ] **Standard rails:** 3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V.
- [ ] **EMC:** Always check EMI compliance for field robots.
- [ ] **Modular philosophy:** Enable hot-swap, plug-and-play, fault isolation.
- [ ] **Safety critical (surgical/military):** Use EtherCAT + SIL-3 hardware.
- [ ] **Field robots:** Prefer CAN/Ethernet wired + Wi-Fi/LoRa wireless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integral Product Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integral Product Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Field and Service Robotics**, an **Integral Product Structure** (often referred to as the **Product of Exponentials (PoE)** formulation in modern robotics literature) is a compact, geometrically meaningful mathematical representation used to describe the forward kinematics of a serial-chain robot manipulator.

It expresses the end-effector's rigid-body configuration $T(\boldsymbol{q}) \in SE(3)$ as the ordered product of matrix exponentials, where each factor represents the cumulative effect of a single revolute or prismatic joint's screw motion acting along the kinematic chain.

$$
T(\boldsymbol{q}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{\mathcal{S}_i q_i} \cdot M
$$

where:
- $\boldsymbol{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ is the vector of active joint variables
- $\mathcal{S}_i$ is the **twist** (screw axis) associated with the $i$-th joint, expressed in the **base (space) frame**
- $M \in SE(3)$ is the end-effector's configuration when the robot is at its **home (zero) pose**
- $e^{\mathcal{S}_i q_i}$ is the matrix exponential mapping a single screw displacement $q_i$ into a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Integral Product Structures form the mathematical backbone of **modern geometric robotics**.
