> **(b)** Compute the on-site mission flight time (excluding takeoff, landing, and turnaround overheads). Discuss the safety considerations and DGCA regulatory requirements that must be followed for such an inspection in an urban Indian airspace. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — GSD, Image Area, and Number of Images (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute GSD** (2 marks)

Given: $H = 40$ m, $p = 3.76 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $f = 28 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$
GSD = \frac{H \cdot p}{f} = \frac{40 \times 3.76 \times 10^{-6}}{0.028}
$$

$$
GSD = \frac{150.4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.028} = 5.371 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/px} \approx 5.37\ \text{mm/px}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the area covered by a single image** (2 marks)

Ground width per image:

$$
W_{img} = GSD \times I_w = 5.371 \times 10^{-3} \times 6000 = 32.23\ \text{m}
$$

Ground height per image:

$$
H_{img} = GSD \times I_h = 5.371 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000 = 21.48\ \text{m}
$$

Area per image:

$$
A_{img} = 32.23 \times 21.48 = 692.4\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the number of images required** (3 marks)

Effective area contributed per image (after overlap):

$$
A_{eff} = A_{img} \times (1 - o_l)(1 - o_s) = 692.4 \times 0.20 \times 0.30 = 41.54\ \text{m}^2
$$

Number of images:

$$
N = \frac{A_{site}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{4800}{41.54} = 115.55 \rightarrow \text{round up to } \mathbf{116\ images}
$$

### Part (b) — Mission Time and Safety/Regulatory Compliance (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute mission flight time** (3 marks)

The product of ground speed and image footprint (in the direction of travel) gives the time to cover one image, but a cleaner approach is to use the effective swath width:

Effective swath (side overlap considered):

$$
W_{eff} = W_{img} \times (1 - o_s) = 32.23 \times 0.30 = 9.67\ \text{m}
$$

Effective along-track distance per image:

$$
L_{eff} = H_{img} \times (1 - o_l) = 21.48 \times 0.20 = 4.30\ \text{m}
$$

Time per image:

$$
t_{img} = \frac{L_{eff}}{v} = \frac{4.30}{4} = 1.075\ \text{s}
$$

Total flight time:

$$
t_{flight} = N \times t_{img} = 116 \times 1.075 = 124.7\ \text{s} \approx \mathbf{2.08\ minutes}
$$

*(Or, using the formula $t = A_{site}/(v \cdot d_s) = 4800/(4 \times 9.67) = 124\ \text{s}$ — same result.)*

**Step 2: Safety considerations** (2 marks)
- Maintain Visual Line of Sight (VLOS) with a visual observer.
- Geofencing to prevent fly-aways into the building.
- Propeller guards when operating near people.
- Emergency parachute or ballistic recovery system.
- Avoid flight near pedestrians, vehicles, and other aircraft.

**Step 3: DGCA regulatory requirements in India (Digital Sky Platform)** (2 marks)
- **Drone type:** Inspection UAV is typically classified as a **Small or Medium UAV** (≤ 25 kg MTOM).
- **Registration:** Obtain a **UAS Operator Permit (UAoP)** and register the UAV on the Digital Sky portal.
- **Pilot licence:** **Remote Pilot Certificate (RPC)** from an authorised training organisation.
- **Insurance:** Third-party liability insurance is mandatory.
- **Airspace zones:** The Digital Sky platform classifies airspace into **Red (no-flight)**, **Yellow (controlled, ATC approval needed)**, and **Green (automatic approval)**. Urban building inspection is usually **Yellow zone**, requiring ATC permission.
- **Operational restrictions:** Maximum altitude **120 m AGL**, day-time operation, **VLOS** unless BVLOS waiver obtained.

---

### **QUESTION B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

> **(a)** Describe the **architecture of a typical infrastructure-inspection UAV**, clearly explaining the role of each subsystem. Discuss the advantages of using a **multirotor over a fixed-wing UAV** for building façade inspection. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **bridge inspection mission** uses a UAV with a **6S 22.2 V 10000 mAh LiPo battery**. The **total mass at takeoff is 4.5 kg**, the **propeller diameter is 0.28 m**, and there are **four propellers**. The **overall system efficiency is 80%**. Compute the **hover power** and the **theoretical flight endurance** of this UAV. Comment on how **weather conditions** (wind, rain, temperature) affect the endurance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Architecture and Multirotor vs Fixed-Wing (7 marks)

**Architecture (4 marks):**

| Subsystem | Components | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Airframe & Propulsion** | Carbon-fiber frame, BLDC motors, ESCs, propellers, LiPo battery | Generate thrust and provide structural support |
| **Flight Control** | Flight controller (PX4/ArduPilot), IMU, barometer | Maintain stable flight via PID control loops |
| **Navigation** | GNSS (with RTK), magnetometer, optical flow | Provide absolute position and heading |
| **Perception/Payload** | RGB camera, thermal camera, LiDAR | Capture inspection data |
| **Communication** | C2 radio, video transmitter, 4G/5G link | Bidirectional control and data downlink |
| **Ground Control** | Mission planner software, GCS hardware, video receiver | Plan mission, monitor flight, record data |

**Multirotor vs Fixed-Wing for façade inspection (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Multirotor | Fixed-Wing |
|---|---|---|
| **Hover capability** | **Yes** — can stay at a fixed point to image a crack | **No** — must keep moving forward |
| **Close-range inspection** | **Excellent** — can fly within 1–2 m of a wall | **Poor** — needs continuous forward motion |
| **Vertical façade scanning** | **Yes** — can fly top-to-bottom along a wall | **Difficult** — usually requires multirotor |
| **Endurance** | Low (20–35 min) | High (60–180 min) |
| **Wind tolerance** | Lower | Higher |
| **Verdict for façade** | **Preferred** | Not suitable |

### Part (b) — Hover Power, Endurance, and Weather Effects (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute total propeller disk area** (1 mark)

$$
A_{prop} = 4 \times \pi \times \left(\frac{0.28}{2}\right)^2 = 4 \times \pi \times 0.0196 = 0.2463\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute hover power** (2 marks)

$$
P_{hover} = \frac{(mg)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{2 \rho A_{prop}}} = \frac{(4.5 \times 9.81)^{1.5}}{\sqrt{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.2463}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(44.145)^{1.5} = 44.145 \times \sqrt{44.145} = 44.145 \times 6.644 = 293.3\ \text{W}^{1.5}\text{·s}^{1.5}
$$

Denominator:

$$
\sqrt{0.6032} = 0.7767\ \text{(dimensionless check: }\sqrt{\text{kg/m} \cdot \text{m}^2} = \text{kg}^{0.5}\text{m}^{0.5}\text{)}
$$

Actually, let us carefully check units. The formula is in SI with $P$ in watts when inputs are in kg, m, s. The result:

$$
P_{hover} = \frac{293.3}{0.7767} \approx \mathbf{377.6\ W}
$$

*(Alternative direct computation gives the same value within rounding.)*

**Step 3: Compute battery energy and endurance** (2 marks)

Battery energy:

$$
E_{bat} = 22.2 \times 10 \times 3600 = 799\,200\ \text{J}
$$

Useful power with efficiency:

$$
P_{useful} = \frac{P_{hover}}{\eta_{system}} = \frac{377.6}{0.80} = 472.0\ \text{W}
$$

Endurance:

$$
t_{end} = \frac{799\,200}{472.0} = 1693\ \text{s} \approx \mathbf{28.2\ minutes}
$$

**Step 4: Effect of weather on endurance** (2 marks)

- **Wind:** Headwind component increases the power required to maintain ground speed, reducing endurance by **15 – 30%**.
- **Rain:** Adds drag, increases motor load, and may trigger failsafe landing; **not recommended**.
- **Temperature:** Cold weather (≤ 0 °C) **reduces LiPo capacity by 20 – 30%** (chemical kinetics slow down). Hot weather (≥ 40 °C) increases motor-controller thermal stress and **reduces available lift** (air density drops from $1.225$ to $1.11$ kg/m³ at 40 °C), increasing required hover power by $\approx 7\%$.
- **Altitude:** Higher altitude → lower $\rho$ → more power → less endurance (per Equation 8 in the cheat sheet).
- **Humidity:** Slightly reduces air density, marginally increasing hover power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing GSD with image resolution:** GSD is a *ground* measurement (m or mm), image resolution is a *pixel count* (e.g., 6000 × 4000). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1 - o_l)(1 - o_s)$ factor:** A common mistake is to divide $A_{site}$ by $A_{img}$ without accounting for overlap, leading to a number 3–5 times too small.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in GSD:** Forgetting to convert mm to m for focal length, or µm to m for pixel pitch. *Always convert to SI units first.*
> 4. **Ignoring the $g^{3/2}$ term in hover power:** A common student error is to write $P = mgv$ for hover, which is dimensionally wrong (force × velocity gives power, but $v = 0$ in hover). The correct momentum-theory formula is **derived from actuator disk theory** as shown.
> 5. **Not mentioning DGCA / Digital Sky / RPC in Indian regulatory questions:** Examiners expect students to mention **Digital Sky Platform**, **UAS Operator Permit (UAoP)**, **Remote Pilot Certificate (RPC)**, and **Red/Yellow/Green zone classification**. A vague *"get a licence"* answer loses 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Choosing the wrong UAV type:** For façade inspection, a fixed-wing UAV is a **wrong answer** in a "which UAV would you use and why" question. Always match platform to mission profile.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — UAVs for Infrastructure Inspection**

**Core Definitions (must be exact for full marks):**
- ☐ UAV = unmanned aircraft; UAS = UAV + GCS + C2 link; RPAS = regulatory term
- ☐ GSD = ground-projected size of one pixel: $GSD = H \cdot p / f$
- ☐ Photogrammetry = deriving 3D geometry from overlapping 2D images
- ☐ RTK = Real-Time Kinematic GNSS giving cm-level positioning
- ☐ PPK = Post-Processed Kinematic (similar accuracy, post-flight correction)
- ☐ VLOS vs BVLOS = Visual Line of Sight vs Beyond Visual Line of Sight
- ☐ AGL = Above Ground Level; AMSL = Above Mean Sea Level
- ☐ DGCA = Directorate General of Civil Aviation (India's civil aviation regulator)

**Architectural Subsystems (remember all six):**
- ☐ Airframe & Propulsion | Flight Control | Navigation | Payload | Communication | GCS

**Key Formulas (must memorise):**
- ☐ $GSD = H \cdot p / f$
- ☐ $A_{img} = GSD_x \cdot I_w \cdot GSD_y \cdot I_h$
- ☐ $N = A_{site} / [A_{img}(1-o_l)(1-o_s)]$
- ☐ $P_{hover} = (mg)^{3/2} / \sqrt{2 \rho A_{prop}}$
- ☐ $t_{end} = V \cdot C \cdot 3600 / (P \cdot 1/\eta)$

**UAV Selection Rules:**
- ☐ **Close-range, vertical, indoor** → Multirotor or indoor micro-UAV
- ☐ **Large area, linear asset, mapping** → Fixed-wing
- ☐ **Mixed (hover + endurance)** → Hybrid VTOL
- ☐ **Persistent single-spot monitoring** → Tethered multirotor

**Payloads (mention what each detects):**
- ☐ **RGB** → cracks, spalling, geometry, orthomosaics
- ☐ **Thermal IR** → moisture ingress, heat loss, hot spots, delamination
- ☐ **LiDAR** → 3D point cloud, deformation, vegetation encroachment
- ☐ **Multispectral** → vegetation health near assets
- ☐ **Gas sensor** → pipeline leak detection (specialised)

**Sensors (mention fusion):**
- ☐ **IMU** (high rate) + **GNSS** (low rate, absolute) → **EKF** for state estimation
- ☐ **Barometer** for altitude, **magnetometer** for heading
- ☐ **Optical flow / visual-inertial odometry** for GPS-denied environments

**Process Workflow (sequence matters):**
- ☐ Plan → Approve → Pre-flight → Fly → Capture → Offload → Georeference → Process → Annotate → Report → Update BIM

**Indian Regulatory Knowledge (high-frequency KTU topic):**
- ☐ Digital Sky Platform | Red/Yellow/Green zones | UAoP | RPC | Insurance
- ☐ Max altitude 120 m AGL | Day operation | VLOS default
- ☐ No flight over people, vehicles, airports, military areas without waiver

**Challenges (be ready to discuss):**
- ☐ Battery endurance (multirotor) | GPS-denied environments (urban canyon, indoor)
- ☐ Wind & weather | Privacy concerns | Data security | Airspace congestion
- ☐ Sensor calibration drift | Photogrammetric reconstruction failures on featureless surfaces
- ☐ Regulatory variability across countries | BVLOS restrictions

**Cross-Cutting KTU Themes (combine with other modules):**
- ☐ SLAM (Module 1 + 2 link) — for GPS-denied indoor inspection
- ☐ Sensor fusion — links to Module 1 perception
- ☐ Path planning — waypoint navigation is a planning algorithm
- ☐ Human-robot interaction — teleoperation of inspection UAVs

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam-Day Mantra:** *"A UAV is not a flying camera — it is a *reconfigurable sensor platform* whose value comes from the synergy of *platform, payload, planning, and processing*."* This framing wins full marks in any 14-mark design question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGV)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGV)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV):** A *mechanized, computer-controlled ground platform* that operates on the surface of the earth (terrestrial environment) without an onboard human operator, capable of *sensing its environment*, *navigating autonomously or via tele-operation*, and *executing a defined mission* involving mobility, manipulation, or surveillance.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT602 – Module 2)** syllabus, a UGV is formally classified as a *Field Robot* that satisfies the following three foundational criteria simultaneously:

1. **Platform Mobility** – the ability to traverse unstructured, semi-structured, or structured terrain through wheels, tracks, legs, or hybrid locomotion.
2. **Sensing & Perception** – onboard sensors (LIDAR, IMU, cameras, GPS, encoders) that produce a state estimate of the robot and its environment.
3. **Autonomy / Decision Layer** – a control architecture (reactive, deliberative, or hybrid) that translates perception into motion commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard UGV Mass Class Metric:** UGVs are commonly classified by payload. The widely accepted operational taxonomy in field robotics literature uses the following categories — *Micro (< 10 kg)*, *Mini (10–50 kg)*, *Small (50–500 kg)*, *Medium (500 kg–2 t)*, *Large (2–10 t)*, and *Heavy (> 10 t)*. The *autonomy level* is graded on the **SAE J3016-style 0–5 scale**, with **Level 5** denoting *full mission autonomy without human intervention*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a *delivery robot* rolling down the sidewalk of a busy city street. It has no driver, no steering wheel, and no remote control held by a human — yet it senses pedestrians, stops at crossings, and plans its route to the next apartment block. That compact, wheeled platform is a UGV.

Now, scale the same idea up: replace the wheels with tank-like tracks, drop the robot into a *collapsed mine tunnel* after an earthquake, give it a robotic arm to search for survivors, and you have a *rescue UGV*. The *family resemblance* across all these machines is that they are **mobile ground platforms capable of executing meaningful work without a human body inside them**.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple mental model:** *UGV = (Locomotion System) + (Sensor Suite) + (Brain/Controller) + (Mission Payload)*. If any one of these four pillars is missing, the system ceases to qualify as a true UGV under the KTU 2024 scheme definition.

### 1.3 Distinguishing UGVs from Related Field Robotic Platforms

| Property | UGV | UAV (Aerial) | USV/ASV (Surface) | UUV/ROV (Underwater) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Medium** | Terrestrial | Air | Water surface | Sub-surface |
| **Dominant Locomotion** | Wheels / Tracks / Legs | Rotors / Fixed wings | Propellers / Waterjets | Thrusters |
| **Primary Constraint** | Terrain contact friction | Aerodynamics | Buoyancy & waves | Hydrostatic pressure |
| **Typical Sensor Set** | LIDAR, IMU, GPS, Wheel odometry, RGB-D | IMU, GPS, Barometer, Cameras | Radar, GPS, IMU, Sonar | Sonar, DVL, Pressure sensor |
| **Example Mission** | Bomb disposal, agriculture | Surveillance, delivery | Hydrography, search | Pipeline inspection |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer UGV Architecture

A UGV, regardless of its mission profile, is decomposed into **three logical layers** in the KTU-recommended functional architecture:

#### Layer 1 — *Perception Layer*
- **Proprioceptive sensors** (measure the robot's internal state): wheel encoders, IMU, gyroscope, joint torque sensors, battery monitors.
- **Exteroceptive sensors** (measure the environment): 2D/3D LIDAR, stereo/RGB cameras, ultrasonic rangefinders, GPS receivers, millimeter-wave radar, time-of-flight (ToF) sensors.
- **Function:** convert physical phenomena into numerical state vectors $\mathbf{x}(t)$.

#### Layer 2 — *Localization & Mapping Layer*
- **Odometry:** integration of wheel encoder data to estimate incremental displacement.
- **Sensor fusion:** typically *Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)*, *Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)*, or *Particle Filter* combining IMU + GPS + LIDAR scan-matching.
- **Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM):** incrementally builds a map $\mathcal{M}$ while estimating pose $\mathbf{p}_k$ at time step $k$.

#### Layer 3 — *Decision & Control Layer*
- **Global planner:** generates a topological or metric route from start to goal (A*, Dijkstra, RRT*).
- **Local planner / Behaviour layer:** reactive obstacle avoidance (Dynamic Window Approach, Vector Field Histogram, Potential Fields).
- **Motion controller:** converts the planned trajectory into actuator commands — typically a *PID*, *state-feedback*, or *model-predictive controller (MPC)*.

### 2.2 Why Each Component Matters

- **Perception without localization** is blind — the robot sees the world but does not know where *it* is.
- **Localization without perception** is impossible — the robot needs landmarks to localize against.
- **Planning without control** is academic — the plan must be executable by the actuators under real-world dynamic constraints.
- **Control without planning** is reactive only — the robot cannot pursue a long-horizon mission.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every equation, parameter, and unit that is **high-yield** for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE on this topic.

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$ | Angular velocity $\omega$, linear velocity $v$, wheel radius $r$ | $\omega$ rad/s, $v$ m/s, $r$ m | Wheel speed → motor RPM |
| 2 | $v = r\,\omega$ | Forward velocity of a driven wheel | m/s | Drive-train sizing |
| 3 | $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta,\ \dot{y} = v\sin\theta,\ \dot{\theta} = \omega$ | Non-holonomic unicycle in world frame | m/s, rad/s | 2D planar UGV pose update |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | Ackermann turning radius $R$, wheelbase $L$, steer angle $\delta$ | m, rad | Steering geometry |
| 5 | $\delta_{\text{inner}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R - t/2}\right)$ | Inner wheel steer angle, $t$ = track width | rad | Ackermann linkage |
| 6 | $\delta_{\text{outer}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{R + t/2}\right)$ | Outer wheel steer angle | rad | Ackermann linkage |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{v_R - v_L}{b}$ | Differential drive angular vel., wheel base $b$ | rad/s | Skid-steer kinematics |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{v_R + v_L}{2}$ | Differential drive linear velocity | m/s | Skid-steer kinematics |
| 9 | $\text{ICC} = \left(0,\ \dfrac{b(v_R+v_L)}{2(v_R - v_L)}\right)$ | Instantaneous Centre of Curvature | m | Differential drive geometry |
| 10 | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta \theta_{\text{enc}} \cdot \pi \cdot d}{N \cdot C}$ | Odometric displacement, $d$ wheel dia, $N$ ticks/rev, $C$ gear ratio | m | Wheel-odometry update |
| 11 | $P(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k}) \propto P(\mathbf{z}_k \vert \mathbf{x}_k) P(\mathbf{x}_k \vert \mathbf{z}_{1:k-1})$ | Bayesian filter recursion | — | EKF/Particle SLAM |
| 12 | $f(n) = O(b^d)$ | A* search branching factor $b$, depth $d$ | — | Path-planner complexity |
| 13 | $\tau_m - \tau_L = J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta}$ | DC motor torque balance, $J$ inertia, $b$ damping | Nm, kg·m², Nms/rad | Motor sizing |
| 14 | $V_{\text{bat}} = I R_{\text{int}} + \text{EMF}$ | Battery equivalent circuit | V, A, Ω | Power budget |
| 15 | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p + K_i/s + K_d s}{...}$ | PID controller transfer function | — | Motion control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For differential-drive UGVs, equations (7), (8) and (9) are the *most frequently tested* in KTU ESE numericals. Memorize them along with the sign convention: $v_R > v_L$ → left turn (positive $\omega$); $v_R < v_L$ → right turn.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search & Rescue (SAR) UGVs** use SLAM and thermal cameras to locate victims in collapsed structures.
- **Agricultural UGVs** use RTK-GPS + vision to perform row-crop weeding with sub-centimetre accuracy.
- **Military / EOD UGVs** use skid-steer differential drives and manipulator arms for bomb disposal.
- **Planetary Rovers** (e.g., NASA *Perseverance*, *Curiosity*) use rocker-bogie suspensions and 6-wheel Ackermann variants on extraterrestrial terrain.
- **Warehouse AMRs** (Autonomous Mobile Robots) use 2D LIDAR SLAM for goods transport in e-commerce fulfilment centres.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Non-Holonomic Unicycle Kinematic Model (Differential Drive)

The *differential-drive UGV* is the canonical platform tested across all KTU robotics papers. We derive its kinematic model from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the configuration (pose) vector.**
Let the UGV's pose in the world (inertial) frame be:

$$\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x, y)$ is the location of the centre of the wheel axle and $\theta$ is the heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the $X$-axis of the world frame.

**Step 2 — Define the actuator inputs.**
The two independently driven wheels — one on the left and one on the right — generate linear velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$ at the contact patch. The wheel separation (track length) is $b$.

**Step 3 — Compute the forward velocity of the robot's centre.**
The instantaneous centre of curvature (ICC) lies along the line perpendicular to the wheel axle. The forward velocity of the robot's centre is the *mean* of the two wheel velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angular velocity of the robot.**
The angular velocity is the rate of change of heading, equal to the difference of the wheel velocities divided by the wheelbase:

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$$

**Step 5 — Project the planar velocities onto the world frame.**
Since the robot is constrained to move in the direction it is currently facing (a *non-holonomic* constraint, because $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$), the world-frame velocities are:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the kinematic state equation.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the *standard unicycle model* used universally for differential-drive UGV motion planning and control.

**Step 7 — Forward-Euler discrete-time update (used in simulation and odometry).**
For a control period $\Delta t$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \,\Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \,\Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \,\Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The above is the *starting point* for almost every Part-B numerical involving UGV trajectory tracking, controller design, or pose prediction. Examiners expect you to write the state equation *and* the discrete update, then either (a) trace a trajectory given constant $(v, \omega)$, or (b) design a feedback law to drive $\mathbf{q} \to \mathbf{q}_d$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Ackermann Steering Geometry

A car-like UGV (Ackermann-steered) has a *steerable front axle* and a *fixed rear axle*. The objective of Ackermann geometry is to ensure that, during a turn, *all four wheels rotate about a common instantaneous centre of curvature (ICC)* — preventing tire scrub.

**Step 1 — Define geometric parameters.**
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles).
- $t$ = track width (distance between left and right wheels).
- $R$ = turning radius of the *centre* of the rear axle.
- $\delta$ = average front-wheel steer angle (steering wheel command).

**Step 2 — Use the rear-axle pivot property.**
The rear-axle centre traces a circle of radius $R$ around the ICC. From the right triangle formed by the rear-axle centre, the front-axle midpoint, and the ICC:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$$

This is the *fundamental Ackermann relation* — given a desired $R$, the required steering angle is:

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inner and outer wheel steer angles.**
Because the inner and outer wheels are at different distances from the ICC, their required steer angles differ:

$$\delta_{\text{inner}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R - t/2}\right)$$
$$\delta_{\text{outer}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R + t/2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Ackermann condition.**
The *Ackermann percentage* is a measure of how close the actual steering linkage comes to the ideal:

$$\text{Ackermann \%} = 100\% \cdot \frac{\delta_{\text{inner,actual}} - \delta_{\text{outer,actual}}}{\delta_{\text{inner,ideal}} - \delta_{\text{outer,ideal}}}$$

A value of 100 % means the geometry is perfect; values above 100 % are called *over-Ackermann*, below 100 % are *under-Ackermann*.

> [!TIP]
> **Numerical worked example (typical KTU sub-part):** $L = 2.5$ m, $t = 1.5$ m, $R = 10$ m. Then $\delta = \arctan(2.5/10) = 14.04°$. Inner-wheel angle $= \arctan(2.5/9.25) = 15.10°$. Outer-wheel angle $= \arctan(2.5/10.75) = 13.09°$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Wheel Odometry Update

Wheel odometry is the *dead-reckoning* technique by which a UGV estimates its pose from incremental wheel rotations.

**Step 1 — Encoder measurement.**
Each wheel encoder produces $N$ ticks per revolution. Over a sampling interval $\Delta t$, the right encoder reports $\Delta n_R$ ticks and the left encoder reports $\Delta n_L$ ticks.

**Step 2 — Convert ticks to wheel rotations.**

$$\Delta \phi_R = \frac{2\pi \,\Delta n_R}{N}, \quad \Delta \phi_L = \frac{2\pi \,\Delta n_L}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Convert rotations to linear displacement at the wheel.**
With wheel diameter $d$:

$$\Delta s_R = \frac{\Delta \phi_R \cdot d}{2}, \quad \Delta s_L = \frac{\Delta \phi_L \cdot d}{2}$$

(Equivalently $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta \phi \cdot \pi d}{2}$ since $2\pi r = \pi d$.)

**Step 4 — Compute incremental translation and rotation of the robot's centre.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$$
$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{b}$$

**Step 5 — Pose update.**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta \theta$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Using $\cos\theta_k$ instead of $\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)$ in the odometry update. The midpoint correction significantly reduces drift in long trajectories — examiners *do* check this.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Trajectory of a Constant-Input Differential Drive

A common KTU sub-question: *Given constant $(v, \omega)$, derive the path traced by the UGV.*

Starting from the unicycle model:

$$\dot{x} = v\cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v\sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$

With initial condition $\mathbf{q}(0) = (x_0, y_0, \theta_0)$, integrate:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \omega t$$

$$x(t) = x_0 + \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\sin(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \sin\theta_0\bigr]$$

$$y(t) = y_0 - \frac{v}{\omega}\bigl[\cos(\theta_0 + \omega t) - \cos\theta_0\bigr]$$

These are the parametric equations of a **circle of radius $R = v/\omega$** centred at:

$$C = \left( x_0 - \dfrac{v}{\omega}\sin\theta_0,\ y_0 + \dfrac{v}{\omega}\cos\theta_0 \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case $\omega \to 0$:** A pure straight-line trajectory. Use L'Hôpital's rule on $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ to recover $x(t) = x_0 + v t \cos\theta_0$ and $y(t) = y_0 + v t \sin\theta_0$. KTU examiners *love* testing this limit.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Locomotion Modes

| Criterion | Differential Drive (Skid-Steer) | Ackermann (Car-Like) | Tracked | Quadruped / Hexapod |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low | High (steering linkage) | Medium | Very high (many actuators) |
| **Terrain adaptability** | Medium (indoor / smooth) | Low (roads only) | High (rough) | Very high (stairs, rubble) |
| **Energy efficiency** | High | High | Low (track slip) | Low |
| **Control complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | Very high (whole-body) |
| **Typical payload** | Light–Medium | Medium | Medium–Heavy | Light |
| **Example** | TurtleBot, Husky | Autonomous car, AGV | PackBot, TALON | ANYmal, Spot, BigDog |
| **Maneuverability in place** | Yes (zero-radius turn) | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Holonomic option** | No (non-holonomic) | No | No | Sometimes (omni legs) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

The following diagrams comply with the *Node Identifier Alpha Rule* (all IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters) and the *Label Formatting Restriction* (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

### 4.2 UGV Three-Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Mission Goal] --> B1[Global Planner]
    B1 --> C1[Local Planner and Behaviour Layer]
    C1 --> D1[Motion Controller]
    D1 --> E1[Actuator Drivers]
    E1 --> F1[Wheel Motors and Steering]
    F1 --> G1[UGV Chassis]

    G1 --> H1[Wheel Encoders]
    G1 --> I1[IMU and Gyroscope]
    G1 --> J1[LIDAR Scanner]
    G1 --> K1[GPS Receiver]
    G1 --> L1[RGB and Depth Camera]

    H1 --> M1[Sensor Fusion and EKF SLAM]
    I1 --> M1
    J1 --> M1
    K1 --> M1
    L1 --> M1

    M1 --> N1[Pose Estimate qx qy theta]
    N1 --> B1
    N1 --> C1
    N1 --> D1
    N1 --> O1[Operator HMI and Tele-op]
    O1 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Differential-Drive Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2[Robot Centre C] --> B2[Right Wheel]
    A2 --> C2[Left Wheel]
    A2 --> D2[Wheelbase b]
    B2 --> E2[Linear Velocity vR]
    C2 --> F2[Linear Velocity vL]
    A2 --> G2[Heading Angle theta]
    A2 --> H2[Forward Velocity v equals vR plus vL over 2]
    A2 --> I2[Angular Velocity omega equals vR minus vL over b]
    H2 --> J2[World Frame Xdot equals v cos theta]
    H2 --> K2[World Frame Ydot equals v sin theta]
    I2 --> L2[Heading Rate Thetadot equals omega]
```

### 4.4 Ackermann Steering Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A3[Rear Axle Centre] --> B3[Wheelbase L]
    B3 --> C3[Front Axle Centre]
    C3 --> D3[Inner Front Wheel]
    C3 --> E3[Outer Front Wheel]
    A3 --> F3[Track Width t]
    D3 --> G3[Steer Angle delta inner]
    E3 --> H3[Steer Angle delta outer]
    A3 --> I3[ICC at distance R]
    I3 --> D3
    I3 --> E3
    I3 --> A3
    I3 --> J3[All wheels rotate about ICC]
```

### 4.5 SLAM and Navigation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Raw Sensor Stream] --> Q1[Preprocessing and Feature Extraction]
    Q1 --> R1[Scan Matching or Visual Odometry]
    R1 --> S1[Pose Hypothesis]
    P1 --> T1[Loop Closure Detection]
    T1 --> U1[Graph Optimization]
    S1 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Global Map and Corrected Trajectory]
    V1 --> W1[Path Planner A star or RRT star]
    W1 --> X1[Trajectory Tracking Controller]
    X1 --> Y1[Motor Commands]
    Y1 --> Z1[UGV Motion]
    Z1 --> P1
```

### 4.6 UGV Mission Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Operator Defines Mission] --> L2[Pre-Mission Check]
    L2 --> L3[Localization Initialization]
    L3 --> L4[Global Map Upload or Build]
    L4 --> L5[Goal Pose Specified]
    L5 --> L6[Global Path Planning]
    L6 --> L7[Local Path Execution]
    L7 --> L8{Mission Complete or Obstacle Detected}
    L8 -->|Complete| L9[Return to Home]
    L8 -->|Obstacle| L10[Replan Local Path]
    L10 --> L7
    L9 --> L11[Shutdown and Telemetry Upload]
    L11 --> L12[Post-Mission Analysis]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Define an Unmanned Ground Vehicle. List the **four essential functional subsystems** that a UGV must possess.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV)* is a computer-controlled, ground-based mobile platform that operates without an onboard human operator, capable of sensing its environment, navigating autonomously or under tele-operation, and executing a defined mission.

The four essential subsystems are:

1. **Locomotion system** (wheels, tracks, legs).
2. **Sensing / Perception system** (LIDAR, IMU, cameras, GPS).
3. **Decision and control system** (onboard computer, planning, control laws).
4. **Mission payload** (manipulator, sensors, cargo, weapons system — application-specific).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 for listing subsystems = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Differentiate between *Ackermann steering* and *skid-steer* locomotion in UGVs. Mention **one advantage** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ackermann Steering | Skid-Steer (Differential Drive) |
|---|---|---|
| Steering mechanism | Steered front wheels, fixed rear | Both sides driven at different speeds |
| Turning radius | Non-zero (cannot turn in place) | Zero (can rotate about its own centre) |
| Terrain | Smooth, structured | Rough, semi-structured |
| **Advantage** | High energy efficiency, low tire wear on roads | High maneuverability, mechanically simple, can pivot in place |

*(2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for one advantage each = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **forward kinematics equations of a differential-drive UGV**. Clearly state the non-holonomic constraint. (7 marks)

**(b)** A differential-drive UGV has wheelbase $b = 0.5$ m and wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s. Compute the forward velocity, angular velocity, and radius of curvature of the path. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the derivation in *Section 3.1* of these notes.

The pose vector is $\mathbf{q} = [x,\ y,\ \theta]^T$. With wheel velocities $v_L$ and $v_R$:

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2}, \quad \omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b}$$

The kinematic state equation in the world frame is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

The *non-holonomic constraint* (the UGV cannot move sideways) is:

$$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$

Discrete update for control period $\Delta t$:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \,\Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \,\Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \,\Delta t$$

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for derivation, 1 for constraint, 1 for discrete update.)*

**(b)** Given:
- $b = 0.5$ m
- $r = 0.1$ m
- $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s
- $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s

**Step 1 — Linear velocities of the wheels.**

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.1 \times 12 = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Forward velocity.**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{1.2 + 1.0}{2} = 1.1 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} = \frac{1.2 - 1.0}{0.5} = 0.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of curvature.**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{1.1}{0.4} = 2.75 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** $v = 1.1$ m/s, $\omega = 0.4$ rad/s, $R = 2.75$ m. Since $v_R > v_L$, the UGV turns **left** (counter-clockwise). *(7 marks: 1 each for $v_L, v_R$; 1 for $v$; 1 for $\omega$; 1 for $R$; 1 for direction comment; 1 for units/sign convention.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **Ackermann steering geometry** for a car-like UGV. Derive the relationship between wheelbase, steer angle, and turning radius. Why is the Ackermann condition important? (7 marks)

**(b)** A car-like UGV has wheelbase $L = 2.8$ m and track width $t = 1.6$ m. The vehicle is to follow a circular path of radius $R = 15$ m. Compute (i) the average steering angle, (ii) the inner-wheel steer angle, and (iii) the outer-wheel steer angle. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the derivation in *Section 3.2* of these notes.

In Ackermann geometry, all four wheels must rotate about a *common instantaneous centre of curvature (ICC)* to avoid tire scrub during turning. The rear-axle centre traces a circle of radius $R$ about the ICC. From the right triangle formed by the rear-axle centre, the front-axle midpoint, and the ICC, the geometric relation is:

$$R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R}\right)$$

The inner and outer wheel angles are:

$$\delta_{\text{inner}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R - t/2}\right), \quad \delta_{\text{outer}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R + t/2}\right)$$

**Why Ackermann is important:** In an *ideal* Ackermann linkage, the steering trapezoid ensures that during a turn, every wheel's perpendicular axis passes through the ICC. This *eliminates tire scrub*, *reduces rolling resistance*, *minimizes tire wear*, and *improves cornering stability*. Real linkages are characterized by an *Ackermann percentage* (100 % = ideal, < 100 % = under-Ackermann, > 100 % = over-Ackermann).

*(7 marks: 2 for explanation with sketch, 2 for derivation, 1 for inner/outer formulae, 2 for importance.)*

**(b)** Given: $L = 2.8$ m, $t = 1.6$ m, $R = 15$ m.

**(i) Average steering angle.**

$$\delta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{L}{R}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2.8}{15}\right) = \arctan(0.1867) \approx 10.58°$$

**(ii) Inner-wheel steer angle.** (Inner wheel is closer to ICC, so $R_{\text{inner}} = R - t/2 = 15 - 0.8 = 14.2$ m)

$$\delta_{\text{inner}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2.8}{14.2}\right) = \arctan(0.1972) \approx 11.16°$$

**(iii) Outer-wheel steer angle.** (Outer wheel, $R_{\text{outer}} = R + t/2 = 15.8$ m)

$$\delta_{\text{outer}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2.8}{15.8}\right) = \arctan(0.1772) \approx 10.06°$$

**Result:** $\delta = 10.58°$, $\delta_{\text{inner}} = 11.16°$, $\delta_{\text{outer}} = 10.06°$. Note that $\delta_{\text{inner}} > \delta > \delta_{\text{outer}}$, as geometrically required. *(7 marks: 2 each for sub-parts (i)–(iii), 1 for verifying the inner > outer inequality, 1 for final answer with units.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on UGV questions — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the non-holonomic constraint.** In every differential-drive derivation, you *must* state $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. Omitting it costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Sign convention errors.** $v_R > v_L$ → *left* turn (positive $\omega$). Many students write "right" because the right wheel is faster. Wrong.
> 3. **Confusing wheelbase with track width.** Wheelbase $L$ = front-to-rear distance. Track width $t$ = left-to-right distance. Swapping them in the Ackermann formula is the #1 numerical error.
> 4. **Using the wrong midpoint in odometry.** $x_{k+1}$ uses $\cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2)$, *not* $\cos\theta_k$. Examiners check this — losing **1 mark** is common.
> 5. **Not mentioning units.** In numerical answers, every numerical value must carry its unit (m, m/s, rad/s, °). Missing units can cost **½ mark each**.
> 6. **Forgetting the limit case $\omega \to 0$.** When asked to derive the constant-input trajectory, students forget that for *pure straight-line motion* the equations reduce to L'Hôpital form. Examiners specifically test this — losing **2 marks** is typical.
> 7. **No sketch / no block diagram.** In Part-B derivations, a labelled diagram is worth **at least 1 mark** even if your algebra is slightly off.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A UGV is a ground-mobile, computer-controlled platform operating without an onboard human, with perception, planning, and motion subsystems.
- **Three-layer architecture:** Perception → Localization/Mapping → Decision/Control.
- **Standard kinematic model (unicycle):** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$.
- **Differential-drive formulas:** $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$, $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/b$, $R = v/\omega$.
- **Ackermann relation:** $R = L/\tan\delta$; inner and outer wheel angles differ by the track-width correction.
- **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — the UGV *cannot* move sideways.
- **Wheel odometry:** integrates encoder ticks; discrete update uses the *midpoint* $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$.
- **Trajectory under constant input:** a circle of radius $v/\omega$, degenerating to a straight line as $\omega \to 0$.
- **Locomotion modes:** differential (skid-steer), Ackermann, tracked, legged — each with a distinct trade-off in maneuverability, terrain adaptability, and control complexity.
- **Sensor suite:** proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) + exteroceptive (LIDAR, cameras, GPS); fused via EKF/UKF/Particle filters.
- **SLAM:** simultaneously estimates pose and builds the map; foundational to autonomy.
- **Planning hierarchy:** global planner (A*, RRT*) + local planner (DWA, VFH) + motion controller (PID, MPC).
- **Standard KTU marks split:** 3-mark questions test definitions and lists; 14-mark questions test derivations and numericals on kinematics.
- **Examiner's hot topics:** differential-drive kinematics, Ackermann geometry, odometry update, trajectory equations, locomotion-mode comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wall-Climbing Robots
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Wall-Climbing Robots — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wall-Climbing Robots

---

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wall-Climbing Robot (WCR)** is a specialised class of service robot designed to traverse, inspect, or perform tasks on **vertical, inclined, or inverted surfaces** (walls, ceilings, pipes, ship hulls, building façades) using an active or passive adhesion mechanism that overcomes gravitational loading. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCRUT602, WCRs belong to the broader category of *field and service robots* and are characterised by three integrated subsystems: **(i) a locomotion mechanism** (wheels, legs, tracks, or hybrid), **(ii) an adhesion system** (suction, magnetic, gripping, or bio-inspired), and **(iii) a payload/control module** (sensors, cameras, manipulators, communication).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A wall-climbing robot is fundamentally distinguished from a mobile ground robot by its **adhesion-generation module**, which is the core engineering challenge of this class. Without sustained adhesion, no locomotion strategy can succeed.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Gecko on Glass

Imagine a **house gecko (Hemidactylus)** walking effortlessly upside-down on your ceiling. It does not use glue, suction, or magnets. It uses millions of microscopic hair-like structures called **setae** on its toes, which generate **van der Waals forces** with the surface. The gecko's "design" tells us three universal truths about wall-climbing:

1. **Adhesion must be controllable and reversible** — the robot must stick on command and release on command.
2. **The adhesion force must exceed the gravitational pull** acting on the robot's total mass, plus any dynamic inertial loading.
3. **The locomotion mechanism must be decoupled from the adhesion system** so that the robot can move one limb/contact point while the others remain adhered.

A wall-climbing robot is essentially an **engineered gecko** — a mechatronic system that replicates biological adhesion principles using engineered materials (elastomers, electromagnets, vacuum pumps, micro-spines).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Adhesion Principles (Foundation Overview)

Wall-climbing robots are classified in KTU 2024 syllabus primarily by their **adhesion mechanism**. A high-level preview is given below; detailed treatment follows in Sections 2 and 3.

| Adhesion Principle | Working Principle | Typical Surface |
|---|---|---|
| **Vacuum / Suction** | Negative pressure between cup and wall | Smooth, non-porous |
| **Magnetic** | Electromagnetic or permanent-magnet attraction | Ferromagnetic (steel) |
| **Mechanical Gripping** | Claws, spines, or clamping jaws | Rough, porous, brick |
| **Bio-inspired (Dry Adhesion)** | Micro-fibre/elastomer mimicking gecko setae | Smooth, clean |
| **Propulsion-based (Thrust)** | Downward propeller thrust pushes robot to wall | Any vertical surface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants frequently used in WCR analysis:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 101 325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Coefficient of dry static friction (rubber–steel): **μ ≈ 0.6 to 0.9**
> - Coefficient of friction (rubber–concrete): **μ ≈ 0.6 to 0.8**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Engineering Problems

Every wall-climbing robot must solve **two simultaneous problems**:

### Problem 1 — Adhesion Generation
Produce a force vector directed **normal (perpendicular) to the climbing surface** that is at least equal to the weight component acting perpendicular to the surface (or the full weight, for a vertical wall).

### Problem 2 — Stable Locomotion
Translate the robot along the surface while **maintaining continuous adhesion** at one or more contact points, without inducing pitch/roll instabilities or excessive moment loading.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Concept — Generic WCR

**Step 1 — Surface Contact and Seal Formation**
The adhesion module makes conformal contact with the surface. For suction cups, this requires a smooth, non-porous substrate. For magnetic wheels, ferromagnetic coupling. For grippers, mechanical interlock with surface asperities.

**Step 2 — Activation of Adhesion Mechanism**
A pump evacuates air (suction), a coil is energised (electromagnet), or microfibre arrays engage the surface (bio-inspired). The adhesion force ramps up to its steady-state value over a finite time constant.

**Step 3 — Force Equilibrium Verification**
The robot's controller verifies that the normal adhesion force exceeds the destabilising force (weight × cos θ, where θ is the wall inclination from vertical).

**Step 4 — Locomotion Cycle**
One or more actuators (wheels, legs, tracks) propel the robot along the wall while the adhesion module maintains the hold. In **legged WCRs**, the gait follows a static-stable sequence: detach one foot → swing forward → re-attach.

**Step 5 — Payload Operation**
On-board sensors (cameras, ultrasonic, LiDAR, gas detectors) gather data while the robot is adhered. Manipulators can be deployed for cleaning, welding, or NDT.

**Step 6 — Controlled Release**
At the mission end, the adhesion is disengaged (vacuum released, electromagnet de-energised, fingers unclamped), and the robot returns to the ground via cable, parachute, or stair traversal.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship hull inspection and cleaning** — replaces human "blasters" working at dangerous heights.
- **Nuclear plant decontamination** — operates in high-radiation zones where humans cannot.
- **Building façade maintenance** — window cleaning of skyscrapers (e.g., Skywash systems in Singapore).
- **Wind turbine blade inspection** — reduces rope-access costs.
- **Bridge cable inspection** — climbs cylindrical stay-cables.
- **Anti-terrorism / EOD** — surveillance of building exteriors.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **board-exam essential**. Master the variable definitions and the algebraic form.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_a \ge W \cos\theta$ | Adhesion force required for static hold on inclined wall (θ from horizontal) | N |
| 2 | $F_a \ge W$ (for θ = 90°) | Required adhesion on a vertical wall equals robot weight | N |
| 3 | $F_a = \Delta P \cdot A_c$ | Suction adhesion force (ΔP = pressure differential, A_c = cup area) | N, Pa, m² |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{min} = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$ | Minimum pressure differential required for vertical hold | Pa |
| 5 | $F_f \le \mu N$ | Limiting static friction force (μ = coefficient, N = normal reaction) | N |
| 6 | $F_m = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Magnetic adhesion force (B = flux density, A = pole area, μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ H/m) | N |
| 7 | $T_{adhesion} = \dfrac{V_{pump}}{A_c} \cdot \eta$ | Time to evacuate suction cup (V = pump flow rate, η = seal quality) | s |
| 8 | $\mu_{min} = \tan\theta$ | Minimum friction coefficient to prevent sliding down an incline | dimensionless |
| 9 | $SF = \dfrac{F_a}{W \cos\theta}$ | Safety factor for adhesion design (typically SF ≥ 2 to 3) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma_{wall} = \dfrac{F_a}{A_{contact}}$ | Contact pressure on wall surface | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used in the KTU formula sheet:** Inclination angle θ is measured **from the horizontal ground up to the wall**. Therefore a perfectly vertical wall corresponds to θ = 90°, and a ceiling corresponds to θ = 180°. The component of weight pressing the robot *into* the wall is **W cos θ**; the component pulling the robot *down the wall* is **W sin θ**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Suction Pressure for a Vertical Wall

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE for wall-climbing robots. It establishes the fundamental link between robot mass, cup area, and required pump performance.

### Given
- Robot total mass: **m** (kg)
- Single suction cup contact area: **A_c** (m²)
- Surface: smooth, vertical, non-porous
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Cup seal: perfect (no leakage)

### Required
Minimum gauge pressure differential ΔP_min across the suction cup for the robot to remain statically adhered.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the weight of the robot acting vertically downward.**

$$
W = m \cdot g
$$

where **W** is the weight in newtons, **m** is the mass in kilograms, and **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

**Step 2 — For a vertical wall (θ = 90°), the component of weight acting perpendicular to the wall (i.e., pulling the robot away from the wall) equals the full weight.**

For a vertical surface, the wall-normal direction is horizontal. Gravity acts vertically downward. Therefore the *entire weight* W is the load that the adhesion force must counteract.

$$
F_{load} = W = m g
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the adhesion force provided by the suction cup to the required load.**

The suction adhesion force is generated by the pressure difference between atmosphere (P_atm, acting on the outside of the cup pushing the robot onto the wall) and the reduced pressure inside the cup (P_internal). The net normal force is:

$$
F_a = (P_{atm} - P_{internal}) \cdot A_c = \Delta P \cdot A_c
$$

where ΔP is the **gauge pressure differential** and A_c is the cup's effective sealing area.

**Step 4 — Apply static equilibrium for the limiting (minimum) case.**

At the minimum design pressure, the adhesion force just equals the weight:

$$
\Delta P_{min} \cdot A_c = m \cdot g
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for ΔP_min.**

$$
\Delta P_{min} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the safety factor.**

For engineering design (and consistent with KTU valuation expectations), a **safety factor SF** is applied:

$$
\Delta P_{design} = SF \cdot \frac{m \cdot g}{A_c}
$$

For WCRs, **SF ≥ 2** is the typical board-exam recommendation.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> A wall-climbing robot has a total mass of **8 kg** and uses **two identical suction cups**, each of effective area **A_c = 30 cm² = 30 × 10⁻⁴ m²**. Determine (i) the minimum gauge pressure differential needed in each cup, and (ii) the design pressure with a safety factor of **2.5**.

**Solution:**

Total cup area (both cups together):

$$
A_{total} = 2 \cdot A_c = 2 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-4} = 60 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Weight of robot:

$$
W = m g = 8 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \text{ N}
$$

(i) Minimum pressure differential:

$$
\Delta P_{min} = \frac{W}{A_{total}} = \frac{78.48}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 13\,080 \text{ Pa} \approx 13.08 \text{ kPa}
$$

(ii) Design pressure with safety factor:

$$
\Delta P_{design} = 2.5 \cdot 13\,080 = 32\,700 \text{ Pa} \approx 32.7 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that **13 kPa is only ~13 % of atmospheric pressure**, which is well within the capability of a small electric vacuum pump. This is why suction-based WCRs are practical and dominant in industry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Constraint on an Inclined Surface

When the wall is **not vertical**, the robot experiences a tangential gravity component **W sin θ** that tends to slide it down. The adhesion force (normal to wall) generates friction **F_f = μ N** along the wall direction. We need to find the **minimum coefficient of friction** that the wheel/track material must possess.

### Given
- Wall inclination θ from horizontal
- Robot weight W
- Coefficient of static friction μ between wheel and wall

### Required
The minimum μ for static equilibrium (no slipping down the wall).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Resolve weight into components perpendicular and parallel to the wall surface.**

The wall-normal direction makes angle (90° − θ) with the vertical gravity vector. Therefore:

- Component **perpendicular to wall** (pressing robot *into* wall): 

$$
N = W \cos\theta
$$

- Component **parallel to wall** (pulling robot *down* the slope): 

$$
T = W \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Write the adhesion-balance condition perpendicular to the wall.**

The suction cup must generate a normal force that at least cancels the inward component (and provides safety margin):

$$
F_a \ge W \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Write the friction-balance condition along the wall.**

For the robot not to slide down:

$$
F_f = \mu \cdot N_{contact} \ge W \sin\theta
$$

If the cup supplies the normal reaction:

$$
\mu \cdot F_a \ge W \sin\theta
$$

Substituting the minimum adhesion $F_a = W \cos\theta$ (from Step 2):

$$
\mu \cdot W \cos\theta \ge W \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel W and solve for μ_min.**

$$
\mu_{min} \cdot \cos\theta = \sin\theta
$$

$$
\mu_{min} = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta
$$

### Interpretation

- For a **vertical wall** (θ = 90°), the required μ → ∞. This is **physically impossible** for friction alone; vertical-wall support **must come from the adhesion force**, not friction. Friction on a vertical wall only resists lateral disturbances.
- For **θ = 45°**, μ_min = 1.0 — a high-friction elastomer tyre is needed.
- For **θ = 30°**, μ_min ≈ 0.577 — achievable with rubber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-exam pitfall:** Many students confuse the direction of the friction force. On a vertical wall, friction acts *horizontally* (resisting sideways loads from the locomotion actuators), not vertically. The vertical support on a vertical wall comes **purely from adhesion**, not friction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnetic Adhesion for a Ferromagnetic Wall

For WCRs designed to climb **steel structures** (ship hulls, storage tanks, bridges), permanent magnets or electromagnets are used.

### Given
- Pole-face area: A (m²)
- Magnetic flux density at pole: B (T)
- Vacuum permeability: μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m

### Required
Theoretical maximum attractive force between a flat-faced magnet and a thick ferromagnetic surface.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the magnetic field of the gap.**

For an air gap of length g, the field energy per unit volume is:

$$
u = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Total energy in the gap volume (area × gap):**

$$
U = u \cdot A \cdot g = \frac{B^2 A g}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**Step 3 — The force is the negative gradient of energy with respect to gap length (Maxwell stress method):**

$$
F_m = -\frac{dU}{dg} = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values to estimate.**

For B = 0.5 T, A = 25 cm² = 25 × 10⁻⁴ m²:

$$
F_m = \frac{(0.5)^2 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \cdot 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}
$$

Computing the denominator first:

$$
2 \mu_0 = 8\pi \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.513 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Numerator:

$$
0.25 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-4} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_m = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 248.7 \text{ N}
$$

This single 25 cm² pole face can hold about **25 kg** of robot — illustrating why permanent-magnet WCRs are powerful but limited to ferromagnetic surfaces.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Adhesion Mechanism Trade-Offs

| Mechanism | Adhesion Force / Area | Best Surface | Key Limitation | Typical Payload | Energy Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Suction cup | ~20–80 kPa | Smooth glass, tile | Surface porosity | 5–50 kg | High (pump) |
| Electromagnet | ~100–200 kPa | Steel only | Power-off drop risk | 10–100 kg | High (coil) |
| Permanent magnet | ~100–200 kPa | Steel only | Hard to release | 10–50 kg | Zero |
| Bio-inspired dry adhesion | ~5–20 kPa | Smooth, clean | Wear, contamination | 1–5 kg | Zero |
| Mechanical gripper (claws) | Variable | Rough brick, concrete | Complex control | 5–30 kg | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wall-Climbing Robot

The following Mermaid diagram presents the integrated functional architecture of a generic WCR. Every node is purely alphanumeric and labelled with raw uppercase text (no embedded markdown formatting) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[POWER MODULE BATTERY PACK DC BUS] --> nodeB[ADHESION SUBSYSTEM]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[VACUUM PUMP OR MAGNETIC COIL OR GRIPPER ACTUATOR]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[PRESSURE SENSOR OR FORCE SENSOR]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB3[ADHESION CONTACT INTERFACE TO WALL SURFACE]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC[CONTROL UNIT MCU OR DSP]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[LOCOMOTION SUBSYSTEM]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[DRIVE MOTORS DC OR BLDC]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[WHEELS OR LEGS OR TRACKS]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeE[PAYLOAD MODULE]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[CAMERA AND VISION SENSOR]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[ULTRASONIC OR LIDAR SENSOR]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[MANIPULATOR ARM OR END EFFECTOR]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[WIRELESS COMMUNICATION LINK]
    nodeC --> nodeF[TELEOPERATION OR AUTONOMY LAYER]
    nodeF --> nodeG[GROUND CONTROL STATION OR OPERATOR HMI]
    nodeB2 --> nodeF
    nodeE1 --> nodeF
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE4 --> nodeG
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **nodeA → nodeB**: The battery supplies energy to the adhesion subsystem (vacuum pump, electromagnet coil, or gripper actuator).
- **nodeB1 / nodeB2 → nodeB3 → nodeC**: The adhesion interface generates the holding force; sensors feed back pressure/force to the controller.
- **nodeC → nodeD**: Once adhesion is verified, the controller commands the drive motors.
- **nodeD1 → nodeD2**: Motor torque is delivered to wheels/legs/tracks.
- **nodeD2 → nodeE**: Locomotion carries the payload module along the wall.
- **nodeE → nodeF → nodeG**: Sensor data is sent up to either an on-board autonomy layer or a remote ground station.

---

## 4.2 Adhesion-Mechanism Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[SELECT WALL SURFACE MATERIAL] --> q1{IS SURFACE FERROMAGNETIC}
    q1 -- YES --> m1[USE MAGNETIC ADHESION]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{IS SURFACE SMOOTH AND NON POROUS}
    q2 -- YES --> m2[USE VACUUM OR SUCTION ADHESION]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{IS SURFACE ROUGH AND POROUS}
    q3 -- YES --> m3[USE MECHANICAL GRIPPER OR CLAW]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{IS SURFACE CLEAN AND SMOOTH AND NON MAGNETIC}
    q4 -- YES --> m4[USE BIO INSPIRED DRY ADHESION]
    q4 -- NO --> m5[USE HYBRID OR THRUST BASED APPROACH]
    m1 --> out[DEPLOY WALL CLIMBING ROBOT]
    m2 --> out
    m3 --> out
    m4 --> out
    m5 --> out
```

### Interpretation

This decision flow is directly aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus outcome of **designing an adhesion strategy given the operating environment**. It is also a frequent exam question — students are expected to be able to justify the choice of mechanism based on surface properties.

---

## 4.3 Force Diagram — Robot on a Vertical Wall (Free-Body Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wall[VERTICAL WALL SURFACE LEFT SIDE] --> contact[CONTACT POINT SUCTION CUP OR MAGNET]
    contact --> fa[ADHESION FORCE FA PUSHING ROBOT INTO WALL HORIZONTAL]
    contact --> ff[FRICTION FORCE FF RESISTING SLIDE VERTICAL OR HORIZONTAL]
    robot[ROBOT BODY CENTRE OF MASS] --> w[WEIGHT W ACTING DOWNWARD]
    robot --> fa2[REACTION OF FA HORIZONTAL RIGHTWARD]
    robot --> w
```

### Force Balance Summary (Textual, Board-Exam Ready)

For a robot held against a **vertical wall**:

- **Horizontal direction (perpendicular to wall):** $F_a$ (leftward, into wall) balances the reaction force (rightward, away from wall). Sum = 0.
- **Vertical direction (parallel to wall):** $F_f$ (friction, upward) balances weight $W$ (downward) only **if** $F_f \ge W$. Otherwise the robot slides.

The crucial realisation is that **on a perfectly vertical wall, the friction cannot exceed $\mu \cdot F_a$**, which sets a coupled design constraint:

$$
F_a \ge \frac{W}{\mu}
$$

Therefore, designing the suction pressure requires considering the **friction-limited vertical load**, not just the weight component.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers should be concise — typically 3 to 5 sentences or a short bulleted list. The expected word count is 50–80 words per answer.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Define a wall-climbing robot. List any two adhesion mechanisms commonly used in wall-climbing robots.**

**Model Answer:**

A wall-climbing robot is a specialised service robot designed to traverse vertical, inclined, or inverted surfaces using an active adhesion mechanism that counteracts gravity. Two common adhesion mechanisms are:

1. **Vacuum/suction adhesion** — uses negative pressure between a cup and the surface.
2. **Magnetic adhesion** — uses permanent magnets or electromagnets for ferromagnetic surfaces.

Other valid mechanisms include mechanical gripping and bio-inspired dry adhesion.

**[Mark Distribution: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**State the function of the adhesion subsystem and the locomotion subsystem in a wall-climbing robot.**

**Model Answer:**

The **adhesion subsystem** generates the normal force that holds the robot against the climbing surface, counteracting the weight component perpendicular to the surface (e.g., suction cups, electromagnets, grippers).

The **locomotion subsystem** propels the robot along the surface using wheels, legs, or tracks while the adhesion subsystem maintains static or quasi-static contact. The two are decoupled — adhesion provides the hold, locomotion provides translation.

**[Mark Distribution: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Part B questions test **Apply**, **Analyse**, and **Evaluate** levels. The expected answer length is 350–500 words with at least one numerical derivation or diagram. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the minimum gauge pressure differential required to hold a wall-climbing robot of mass m against a smooth vertical wall using a single suction cup of area A_c. Assume perfect sealing. Include a safety factor in your final design equation.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A wall-climbing robot weighs 12 kg and uses four identical suction cups, each of effective sealing area 25 cm². Calculate (i) the minimum pressure differential per cup for static hold on a vertical wall, and (ii) the design pressure differential with a safety factor of 3. Assume g = 9.81 m/s² and neglect friction effects.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

Let the robot mass be **m** and total weight be **W = mg**.

For a vertical wall, the weight acts perpendicular to the wall surface. Hence the adhesion force $F_a$ must equal **W** at minimum:

$$
\Delta P \cdot A_c = m g
$$

Solving for ΔP:

$$
\Delta P_{min} = \frac{m g}{A_c}
$$

With safety factor SF:

$$
\Delta P_{design} = SF \cdot \frac{m g}{A_c}
$$

This is the minimum gauge pressure differential the suction pump must achieve across the cup.

**[7 marks breakdown: 1 (free-body) + 2 (equilibrium equation) + 2 (solving for ΔP) + 2 (safety factor inclusion)]**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- m = 12 kg
- 4 cups, each A_c = 25 cm² = 25 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- SF = 3, g = 9.81 m/s²

Total cup area:

$$
A_{total} = 4 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-4} = 100 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

Weight:

$$
W = 12 \cdot 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N}
$$

(i) Minimum pressure differential:

$$
\Delta P_{min} = \frac{117.72}{10^{-2}} = 11\,772 \text{ Pa} \approx 11.77 \text{ kPa}
$$

(ii) Design pressure differential with SF = 3:

$$
\Delta P_{design} = 3 \cdot 11\,772 = 35\,316 \text{ Pa} \approx 35.32 \text{ kPa}
$$

Per-cup differential (since cups are in parallel, each sees the same ΔP):

$$
\Delta P_{cup} = 35.32 \text{ kPa}
$$

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 (data extraction) + 2 (weight) + 2 (pressure calculation) + 1 (design pressure with SF)]**

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a wall-climbing robot. Identify and describe any three major subsystems.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Compare suction-based, magnetic, and bio-inspired (gecko-type) adhesion mechanisms for wall-climbing robots in terms of working principle, surface compatibility, advantages, and limitations.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

A wall-climbing robot consists of **four major subsystems**:

1. **Adhesion Subsystem** — generates the normal holding force. Includes suction pump + cup (or magnet + coil, or gripper actuator) plus pressure/force sensors for closed-loop control.

2. **Locomotion Subsystem** — provides translational motion. Includes DC/BLDC drive motors coupled to wheels, legs, or tracks. Wheel-based systems are simpler; leg-based systems handle obstacles better.

3. **Control Unit** — microcontroller or DSP that reads sensor inputs (pressure, IMU, encoders) and issues actuation commands. Implements the state machine: *search → seal → evacuate → verify → drive → release*.

4. **Payload Module** — application-specific sensors and tools. May include vision cameras, ultrasonic transducers, gas detectors, manipulator arms, and wireless communication.

A **power module** (Li-Po battery with DC-DC converters) supplies all subsystems. A **telemetry link** transmits sensor data to a ground station.

**[7 marks breakdown: 2 (diagram) + 1.5 × 3 (subsystem descriptions) + 0.5 (power/telemetry)]**

**(b) Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Suction Adhesion | Magnetic Adhesion | Bio-inspired (Gecko) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Negative pressure between cup and surface | Electromagnetic or permanent-magnet attraction | Van der Waals forces via micro-fibre arrays |
| **Best Surface** | Smooth, non-porous (glass, tile) | Ferromagnetic (steel) | Smooth, clean, dry |
| **Force per Unit Area** | 20–80 kPa | 100–200 kPa | 5–20 kPa |
| **Advantage** | Simple, low cost, works on most smooth walls | Very strong, no pump needed (permanent magnet) | Works on any smooth surface; no power needed |
| **Limitation** | Useless on porous/rough surfaces | Limited to steel; release is hard | Low payload; sensitive to contamination and wear |
| **Typical Robot Mass** | 5–50 kg | 10–100 kg | 1–5 kg |

Conclusion: **Selection depends on surface type, payload, and energy budget**. Suction is the most versatile industrial choice; magnetic dominates steel-structure inspection; bio-inspired is emerging for lightweight inspection.

**[7 marks breakdown: 1.5 (working principle × 3) + 1 (table) + 2 (advantages/limitations) + 1 (conclusion)]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in WCR questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — Examiners explicitly ask for the *design* pressure (with SF), not the *minimum* pressure. Not including SF = lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong angle convention** — Many students define θ from the vertical instead of the horizontal. The KTU convention is θ from horizontal. Mixing conventions leads to wrong formulas ($W \sin\theta$ vs $W \cos\theta$).
> 3. **Confusing normal and tangential force components** — On a vertical wall, weight is the *entire normal load*. Students often incorrectly decompose it as $W \sin 90° = W$ for the tangential component and forget the normal load equals W.
> 4. **Ignoring friction on vertical walls** — Friction does NOT support the weight on a vertical wall; it only resists lateral loads. Stating "friction holds the robot up" = lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing unit conversion** — Cup area is often given in cm²; students forget to convert to m², leading to pressures off by a factor of 10⁴.
> 6. **Not drawing a free-body diagram** — Even when the question does not explicitly ask for one, including a clear FBD earns 1–2 bonus marks and shows examiner-friendly working.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Wall-Climbing Robots:**

- [ ] A **wall-climbing robot** has three subsystems: **adhesion**, **locomotion**, **payload/control**.
- [ ] The **adhesion force** on a vertical wall must equal **W = mg** (full weight, not a component).
- [ ] The minimum suction pressure formula is $\Delta P_{min} = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$; design pressure uses **SF ≥ 2 to 3**.
- [ ] Inclination angle **θ** is measured **from the horizontal**, so vertical wall = θ = 90°.
- [ ] On a vertical wall, **friction does not support weight** — only the adhesion normal force does. Friction resists lateral disturbances only.
- [ ] On an inclined wall, the robot needs both adhesion AND friction; the minimum friction coefficient is $\mu_{min} = \tan\theta$.
- [ ] **Magnetic adhesion** works only on ferromagnetic surfaces; permanent magnets have zero energy cost but are hard to release.
- [ ] **Suction adhesion** requires smooth, non-porous surfaces; it is the most common industrial mechanism.
- [ ] **Bio-inspired (gecko) adhesion** uses dry, van der Waals force via microfibre arrays; lightweight, no power, but contamination-sensitive.
- [ ] **Mechanical grippers/claws** work on rough, porous surfaces (brick, concrete) by interlock with surface asperities.
- [ ] Standard values to memorise: $g = 9.81$ m/s², $P_{atm} \approx 101.3$ kPa, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- [ ] Application domains: ship hull inspection, nuclear decontamination, building façade cleaning, bridge cable inspection, wind turbine blade inspection, EOD surveillance.
- [ ] Design flow for choosing mechanism: **Surface material → Adhesion type → Locomotion type → Payload → Control**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Wall-Climbing Robots**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCRUT602 — Field and Service Robotics.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cable-Crawling Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cable-Crawling Robots # 🐍 Cable-Crawling Robots – Fundamentals ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cable-Crawling Robot (CCR):** A specialized class of **field/service robot** designed to traverse, inspect, and perform maintenance operations along flexible linear infrastructure such as **power transmission lines, telecommunication cables, optical fibre bundles, suspension bridge cables, and stay-cables** — without disrupting the operational function of the host cable. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, cable-crawling robots fall under the broader classification of **infrastructure inspection mobile robots** (sub-class of *climbing robots*). The robot must satisfy the following operational constraints simultaneously: | Constraint | Operational Implication | |------------|--------------------------| | **Self-weight support** | Must generate enough adhesion/normal force to resist gravity on inclined or vertical spans | | **Cable non-damage** | Contact forces must remain below the **elastic yield threshold** of the cable sheath | | **Obstacle negotiation** | Must traverse **suspension clamps, splices, spacers, and tower junctions** | | **Energy autonomy** | On-board battery or **inductive power harvesting** for long-distance missions (typical spans: 300–1500 m) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metric:** *Continuous Span Coverage Rate (CSCR)* — measured in **metres of cable inspected per hour (m/hr)**. State-of-the-art industrial CCRs achieve **CSCR ≈ 200–500 m/hr** on bundled 220 kV lines. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **slow loris** moving along a thin tree branch in a dark forest. The loris uses a *grip-release-grip* sequence: it wraps its hands tightly (locking phase), pulls its body forward (propulsion phase), releases its rear grip, and re-grips ahead. A **cable-crawling robot does exactly the same thing** — but on a steel conductor suspended hundreds of metres above the ground, where a single mistake means a catastrophic fall. The "branch" in our case is a **live high-voltage conductor** (often energized at **66 kV / 110 kV / 220 kV / 400 kV**), and the loris is replaced by: - A pair of **motorized clamp-wheels** (analogous to the hands), and - A **central drive body** containing batteries, processors, and sensors (analogous to the torso). ## 1.3 Why Cable-Crawling Robots Matter in Field Robotics > [!NOTE] > Manual inspection of transmission cables involves **helicopter patrols** (cost ≈ ₹50,000/hour), **line-walkers** (high fatality risk), or **outage-induced shutdowns** (revenue loss in crores). CCRs reduce human risk, eliminate outages, and provide **higher-fidelity data** (visual, thermal, partial-discharge). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Cable-Crawling Robots Cable-crawling robots are classified along **three orthogonal axes**: ### Axis 1 — Locomotion Principle 1. **Wheel/Roller-Based CCRs** – Continuous rolling motion using motorized wheels; highest speed; difficulty with obstacles. 2. **Legged (Walking) CCRs** – Discrete gait cycles (tripod, wave, inchworm); excellent obstacle negotiation; slower. 3. **Inchworm/Bellows CCRs** – Pneumatic or shape-memory-alloy (SMA) actuated extension-contraction; simple structure. 4. **Hybrid CCRs** – Wheel + leg combination (e.g., **Expliner** by Hibot, **LineScout** by Hydro-Québec). ### Axis 2 — Cable Cross-Section Compatibility - **Single-Conductor CCRs** – Designed for one cable (e.g., 220 kV phase conductor). - **Bundle-Conductor CCRs** – Navigate across **multiple parallel sub-conductors** spaced **400–600 mm** apart. - **Earth-Wire (OPGW) CCRs** – Smaller, lighter; traverse the **Optical Ground Wire** at tower tops. ### Axis 3 — Adhesion Mechanism - **Passive mechanical grip** (spring-loaded jaws) – Low complexity. - **Active mechanical clamp** (servo-driven jaws) – Adaptable to varying cable diameters. - **Magnetic adhesion** – Only for ferromagnetic cables (rare in HV lines). - **Electrostatic adhesion** – Experimental; uses **electroadhesion (ELAD)**. ## 2.2 Operational Phases of a Cable-Crawling Mission A typical cable-crawling mission unfolds in **five sequential phases**: 1. **Launch Phase** — Robot is hoisted onto the cable using a **lineman's hot-stick or drone deployment**. 2. **Traverse Phase** — Robot crawls along the cable at the **rated CSCR**. 3. **Inspection Phase** — On-board sensors acquire data: **visual (RGB/thermal cameras), LiDAR, partial-discharge (PD) detectors, corrosion sensors**. 4. **Obstacle-Crossing Phase** — Robot must navigate past **spacer dampers, suspension clamps, tower attachment hardware**. 5. **Recovery Phase** — Robot is retrieved at the next tower or returns to launch point. ## 2.3 Kinematic & Static Force Analysis The fundamental challenge in CCR design is **force balance on an inclined cable**. Consider a CCR of mass $m$ resting on a cable inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. ### Static Force Balance The robot must apply a **clamping normal force** $N$ between its top and bottom jaws. The total friction force available is: $$ F_{\text{friction}} = 2 \mu N $$ where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of static friction** between the robot's gripping pads and the cable sheath. To prevent slipping on an incline: $$ F_{\text{friction}} \geq W_{\parallel} = mg \sin\theta $$ Substituting: $$ 2 \mu N \geq mg \sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \geq \frac{mg \sin\theta}{2\mu} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** On a **vertical span** ($\theta = 90^\circ$), the required clamping force becomes $N \geq \dfrac{mg}{2\mu}$. With $\mu \approx 0.4$ (rubber on aluminium conductor), the jaws must clamp at **≈ 1.25 × the robot's weight** — which is why most CCRs use **pneumatically preloaded jaws** for redundancy. ### Driving Force Requirement For locomotion at velocity $v$ up the incline, the robot's drive motor must overcome: $$ F_{\text{drive}} = mg(\sin\theta + \mu_r \cos\theta) $$ where $\mu_r$ is the **rolling/sliding friction coefficient** at the drive wheel. The corresponding motor power: $$ P_{\text{drive}} = F_{\text{drive}} \cdot v = mg\,v\,(\sin\theta + \mu_r \cos\theta) $$ ### Tipping / Overturning Moment The robot can overturn about its lower jaw if the **centre of gravity (CoG)** is too far from the cable.
