The stability condition on an incline: $$ d \leq \frac{b}{2}\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\theta}{2}\right) $$ where: - $d$ = horizontal offset of the CoG from the cable axis - $b$ = vertical distance between the upper and lower jaws (jaw separation) ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Values / Units | |---|---------|------------------|------------------------| | 1 | $N \geq \dfrac{mg\sin\theta}{2\mu}$ | Minimum clamping force on inclined cable | $N$ in **N (Newton)** | | 2 | $F_{\text{drive}} = mg(\sin\theta + \mu_r\cos\theta)$ | Drive force on inclined cable | Force in **N** | | 3 | $P_{\text{drive}} = mg\,v\,(\sin\theta + \mu_r\cos\theta)$ | Motor power required for climbing | Power in **W (Watt)** | | 4 | $E_{\text{mission}} = P_{\text{drive}} \cdot t_{\text{mission}}$ | Total energy per mission | **Joules (J)** or **Wh** | | 5 | $t_{\text{mission}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{span}}}{v_{\text{CSCR}}}$ | Mission duration for span $L$ | **seconds** or **hours** | | 6 | $\text{CSCR} = \dfrac{L_{\text{span}}}{t_{\text{mission}}}$ | Continuous Span Coverage Rate | **m/hr** | | 7 | $\mu_{\text{req}} \geq \dfrac{mg\sin\theta}{2N}$ | Required friction coefficient | Dimensionless | | 8 | $\tau_{\text{clamp}} = N \cdot r_{\text{cam}}$ | Clamp actuator torque (cam-driven jaws) | **N·m** | | 9 | $\omega_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{v}{r_{\text{wheel}}}$ | Motor angular speed | **rad/s** | | 10 | $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{gear}}^n \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{wheel}}$ | Drivetrain efficiency (cascaded stages) | Dimensionless (0–1) | | 11 | $P_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{drive}}}{\eta_{\text{total}}}$ | Motor-rated power (after losses) | **W** | | 12 | $\text{SF}_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{restore}}}{M_{\text{tip}}}$ | Safety factor against tipping (≥ 2.0) | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Industry:** > - Coefficient of friction (rubber pad on aluminium) ≈ **0.35 – 0.45** > - Coefficient of friction (polyurethane on galvanized steel) ≈ **0.30 – 0.50** > - Typical CCR self-weight: **8 – 25 kg** > - Typical crawl speed: **0.3 – 1.0 m/s** --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Clamping Force on a Vertical Cable **Problem Setup:** A cable-crawling robot of total mass $m = 15$ kg must ascend a vertical conductor ($\theta = 90^\circ$). The jaws use rubber pads with static friction coefficient $\mu = 0.40$. The robot designer wants a **safety factor of 2.0** against slip. Determine the required clamping force per jaw. ### Step 1 — Identify the Forces The robot on a vertical cable experiences: - **Gravity:** $W = mg$, acting downward. - **Normal forces** from upper and lower jaws: $N_1$ (upper) and $N_2$ (lower). - **Friction forces** $f_1 = \mu N_1$ and $f_2 = \mu N_2$ acting upward (to oppose slip). ### Step 2 — Force Equilibrium (Static) Vertical equilibrium (no slip): $$ f_1 + f_2 \geq mg $$ Substituting $f_i = \mu N_i$: $$ \mu N_1 + \mu N_2 \geq mg $$ For a **symmetric jaw design** ($N_1 = N_2 = N$): $$ 2\mu N \geq mg \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \geq \frac{mg}{2\mu} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values $$ N \geq \frac{(15\,\text{kg})(9.81\,\text{m/s}^2)}{2 \times 0.40} $$ Evaluating the numerator: $$ mg = 15 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N} $$ Evaluating the denominator: $$ 2\mu = 2 \times 0.40 = 0.80 $$ Therefore: $$ N \geq \frac{147.15}{0.80} = 183.94 \text{ N} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Safety Factor $$ N_{\text{design}} = \text{SF} \times N_{\text{min}} = 2.0 \times 183.94 = 367.87 \text{ N} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert to Clamping Actuator Specification If a **lead-screw actuator** of pitch $p = 4$ mm is used, the required axial force translates to torque: $$ \tau = \frac{N_{\text{design}} \cdot p}{2\pi \eta_{\text{screw}}} $$ Assuming screw efficiency $\eta_{\text{screw}} = 0.30$ (typical for ACME threads): $$ \tau = \frac{(367.87)(0.004)}{2\pi (0.30)} = \frac{1.4715}{1.8850} = 0.7807 \text{ N·m} $$ ### Step 6 — Final Answer $$ \boxed{N_{\text{design}} \approx 368 \text{ N per jaw},\quad \tau_{\text{actuator}} \approx 0.78 \text{ N·m}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Drive Power on an Inclined Span **Problem:** The same robot ($m = 15$ kg) must crawl at $v = 0.5$ m/s up an inclined cable at $\theta = 30^\circ$. Rolling friction coefficient at the drive wheel $\mu_r = 0.05$. Total drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{total}} = 0.45$. Calculate the **rated motor power**. ### Step 1 — Compute Drive Force $$ F_{\text{drive}} = mg(\sin\theta + \mu_r \cos\theta) $$ Computing $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5000$ and $\cos 30^\circ = 0.8660$: $$ F_{\text{drive}} = 147.15 \times (0.5000 + 0.05 \times 0.8660) $$ $$ F_{\text{drive}} = 147.15 \times (0.5000 + 0.04330) $$ $$ F_{\text{drive}} = 147.15 \times 0.5433 = 79.94 \text{ N} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Ideal Power $$ P_{\text{ideal}} = F_{\text{drive}} \cdot v = 79.94 \times 0.5 = 39.97 \text{ W} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Drivetrain Efficiency $$ P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{\eta_{\text{total}}} = \frac{39.97}{0.45} = 88.82 \text{ W} $$ ### Step 4 — Select Motor with Derating Applying a motor derating factor of 1.5× for thermal safety: $$ P_{\text{motor,rating}} = 1.5 \times 88.82 = 133.23 \text{ W} $$ ### Step 5 — Final Answer $$ \boxed{P_{\text{motor,rating}} \approx 135 \text{ W (rounded up to commercial 150 W BLDC)}} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of CCR Architectures | Architecture | Locomotion Principle | Clamping Method | Max Slope | Typical Speed | Obstacle Handling | Complexity | |--------------|----------------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|------------| | **Hibot Expliner** | Wheel-on-rail | Motorized roller clamps | 30° (continuous) | 200 m/hr | Active arm for spacers | High | | **Hydro-Québec LineScout** | Wheeled + arm | Pneumatic jaws | 35° | 300 m/hr | Articulated arm | High | | **CSIRO/TowerBot** | Tracked wheels | Spring-loaded tracks | 60° (transient) | 100 m/hr | Tracks conform to obstacles | Medium | | **Inchworm-type (academic)** | Pneumatic bellows | SMA/cylinder clamping | 90° | 30 m/hr | Body articulation | Low | | **Magnetic-wheel CCR** | Wheels with permanent magnets | Magnetic adhesion (no jaws) | 90° | 80 m/hr | Limited to ferromagnetic cables | Low | | **Drone-deployed CCR** | Quadrotor + cable grabber | Pneumatic suction + clamp | 90° (post-landing) | Limited by drone | Drops payload, returns | Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Matrix:** Notice the **inverse correlation** between *speed* and *obstacle-handling capability*. Wheel-based CCRs are fast but struggle at spacer-dampers; legged/inchworm CCRs handle obstacles gracefully but at **5–10× lower speeds**. This is a classic **Pareto trade-off** in field robotics design. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Cable-Crawling Robot The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **internal functional architecture** of a typical CCR, mapping how subsystems interact during a cable-inspection mission. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["Power Subsystem"] BAT["Li-ion Battery Pack
22.2V 6Ah"] BMS["Battery Management System"] DC["DC-DC Converters"] BAT --> BMS --> DC end subgraph DRIVE["Drive Subsystem"] BLDC["BLDC Drive Motor x2"] GEAR["Planetary Gearbox"] WHEEL["Drive Wheel Pair"] BLDC --> GEAR --> WHEEL end subgraph CLAMP["Clamping Subsystem"] ACT["Lead-screw Actuator"] JAWU["Upper Jaw Assembly"] JAWL["Lower Jaw Assembly"] FSR["Force Sensors FSR"] ACT --> JAWU ACT --> JAWL JAWU --> FSR JAWL --> FSR end subgraph SENS["Sensing Subsystem"] CAM["HD Visual Camera"] IR["Thermal IR Camera"] LID["LiDAR Scanner"] IMU["9-DOF IMU"] PD["Partial Discharge Sensor"] CAM --> SENS IR --> SENS LID --> SENS IMU --> SENS PD --> SENS end subgraph CTRL["Control Subsystem"] MCU["Onboard SBC
NVIDIA Jetson"] FOG["Edge AI Accelerator"] MCU --> FOG end subgraph COMM["Communication Subsystem"] RF["5.8 GHz Radio Link"] GIM["Stabilized Gimbal Antenna"] RF --> GIM end DC --> BLDC DC --> ACT DC --> MCU DC --> RF FSR --> MCU IMU --> MCU CAM --> MCU IR --> MCU LID --> MCU PD --> MCU MCU --> BLDC MCU --> ACT MCU --> RF ``` ## 4.2 Locomotion Phase State Machine The following Mermaid state diagram captures the **discrete operational states** a CCR transitions through during a typical mission. This is the kind of **finite-state controller (FSC)** logic implemented in the on-board MCU. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Clamping: Start Command Clamping --> Locked: FSR = N_design Locked --> DriveFwd: Motor PWM ON DriveFwd --> Obstacle: IMU detects disturbance
or LiDAR shows obstacle DriveFwd --> Pause: Operator Pause / Low Battery Pause --> DriveFwd: Resume Command DriveFwd --> StopReached: Tower Junction Detected Obstacle --> CrossArm: CrossArm Routine CrossArm --> DriveFwd: Obstacle Cleared StopReached --> Unclamp: End of Mission Pause --> Unclamp: Emergency Release Unclamp --> [*] note right of Clamping Actuator engages jaws until FSR = N_design end note note right of CrossArm Active arm rotates over spacer-damper or clamp end note ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram (Textual Schematic) While physical FBDs cannot be rendered in Mermaid, here is a **labeled ASCII schematic** of a CCR on an inclined cable — this style of diagram is **frequently asked in KTU exams**: ``` ↑ | N (upper jaw) ┌────┴────┐ │ │ │ CCR │ ← CoG (offset d) │ Body │ │ │ └────┬────┘ | N (lower jaw) ↓ ══════════════════════════════════════════ Inclined Cable ──────────────────→ θ to horizontal ══════════════════════════════════════════ Weight components on incline: W_par = m·g·sin θ (along cable, downward) W_perp = m·g·cos θ (perpendicular to cable) Friction forces at jaws: f_upper = μ·N_upper (along cable, upward) f_lower = μ·N_lower (along cable, upward) ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B, Q1(a)]** **Q1. Define a cable-crawling robot. Mention any two of its industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** A cable-crawling robot is a specialized mobile robot designed to traverse and inspect linear flexible infrastructure such as power transmission lines, telecommunication cables, and suspension bridge cables without interrupting their service. Industrial applications: 1. **Visual and thermal inspection of high-voltage transmission conductors** (66 kV – 765 kV) to detect broken strands, hot-spots, and corrosion. 2. **Inspection of Optical Ground Wires (OPGW)** for fibre-optic splice integrity and lightning-strike damage. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1(c)]** **Q2. List the three major locomotion principles used in cable-crawling robots and give one advantage of each.** **Model Answer:** | Locomotion | Advantage | |------------|-----------| | Wheel-based | High speed (200–500 m/hr CSCR) | | Legged/walking | Excellent obstacle negotiation at spacer-dampers | | Inchworm/bellows | Simple mechanical structure; robust on vertical cables | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 2, Q4]** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **working principle of a wheel-based cable-crawling robot** used for power-line inspection. Discuss how the robot **crosses a spacer-damper obstacle**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cable-crawling robot of mass **m = 20 kg** is required to climb a vertical conductor. The jaw pads have a **static friction coefficient μ = 0.35** with the conductor sheath. Determine the **minimum clamping force per jaw** and the **motor power required** to climb at **v = 0.4 m/s**, given the drivetrain efficiency **η = 0.40**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Working Principle of Wheel-Based CCR:** A wheel-based CCR uses **two (or more) pairs of motorized wheels** that press against the cable from opposite sides. The clamping force $N$ is generated by a **spring-preloaded or motor-driven mechanism**. The wheels are driven by a **BLDC motor through a planetary gearbox** for high torque at low RPM. The robot advances as the wheels rotate. **Components:** - Upper and lower wheel pairs (drive wheels) - Spring-loaded clamping frame - BLDC motor + planetary gearbox - Onboard battery and control electronics - Sensor payload (cameras, LiDAR) **Spacer-Damper Crossing Algorithm:** 1. The LiDAR or vision sensor detects the spacer-damper ahead. 2. The CCR decelerates and halts ~200 mm before the obstacle. 3. The **active manipulator arm** (a small articulated arm on the robot) reaches forward, grips the cable beyond the spacer, and transfers the robot's weight temporarily. 4. The main body **slides forward** past the spacer using the auxiliary arm as an anchor. 5. The auxiliary arm releases and the main drive wheels re-engage. 6. The robot resumes normal crawling. **Sketch:** (Refer to the ASCII schematic in §4.3 — student should draw labeled figure showing wheels, jaws, motor, and obstacle-crossing arm.) #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Minimum Clamping Force on Vertical Cable ($\theta = 90^\circ$):** $$ N \geq \frac{mg}{2\mu} = \frac{20 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.35} = \frac{196.2}{0.70} = 280.29 \text{ N} $$ Applying safety factor of 2.0: $$ N_{\text{design}} = 2.0 \times 280.29 = 560.57 \text{ N per jaw} $$ **Step 2 — Drive Power on Vertical Cable:** For $\theta = 90^\circ$, $\sin\theta = 1$ and $\cos\theta = 0$, so: $$ F_{\text{drive}} = mg(\sin\theta + \mu_r\cos\theta) \approx mg = 196.2 \text{ N} $$ (neglecting rolling friction on vertical cable) $$ P_{\text{ideal}} = F_{\text{drive}} \times v = 196.2 \times 0.4 = 78.48 \text{ W} $$ $$ P_{\text{actual}} = \frac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{\eta} = \frac{78.48}{0.40} = 196.2 \text{ W} $$ **Final Answers:** $$ \boxed{N_{\text{design}} \approx 561 \text{ N per jaw},\quad P_{\text{actual}} \approx 196 \text{ W}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):** > Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by: > 1. **Forgetting to apply the safety factor** on $N$. Always apply $\text{SF} \geq 2.0$ in field-robot designs since cables vibrate in wind (Aeolian vibration). > 2. **Not explicitly stating $\sin 90^\circ = 1$ and $\cos 90^\circ = 0$** in the inclined-cable formula — the examiner expects this simplification step shown. > 3. **Confusing "drive power" with "battery power"** — they differ by the drivetrain efficiency. The battery must supply $P_{\text{actual}}$, but the mechanical output is $P_{\text{ideal}}$. --- ### ❓ Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe the **classification of cable-crawling robots** based on locomotion principle and adhesion mechanism. Compare **wheel-based vs. inchworm-based** CCRs in a tabular form (5 comparison points). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **expression for minimum clamping force** $N$ for a CCR on an **inclined cable** of slope angle $\theta$, in terms of robot mass $m$ and friction coefficient $\mu$. Hence, evaluate $N$ for **m = 12 kg**, **θ = 45°**, **μ = 0.45**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Classification (3 points × 1 mark + 2 marks for table):** **By Locomotion Principle:** 1. Wheel-based (rolling) 2. Legged (walking gait) 3. Inchworm (extension-contraction) 4. Hybrid (wheel + leg) **By Adhesion Mechanism:** 1. Mechanical clamping (passive springs, active jaws) 2. Magnetic adhesion 3. Electrostatic / electroadhesion (ELAD) 4. Suction / vacuum (rare for cables) **Comparison Table (any 5 valid points):** | Parameter | Wheel-Based CCR | Inchworm CCR | |-----------|-----------------|--------------| | Locomotion | Continuous rolling | Discrete step-wise | | Speed | 200–500 m/hr | 20–50 m/hr | | Obstacle handling | Requires active arm | Body itself negotiates | | Mechanical complexity | Moderate | Low | | Energy efficiency | Higher | Lower | | Control complexity | Easy (PWM speed) | Moderate (gait timing) | #### Model Solution to (b): **Derivation (5 marks):** For a CCR of mass $m$ on an inclined cable of slope $\theta$: - Weight component along cable: $W_{\parallel} = mg\sin\theta$ - Friction force from two jaws: $F_f = 2\mu N$ (assuming symmetric jaws with equal normal force $N$) For static equilibrium without slip: $$ 2\mu N \geq mg\sin\theta $$ $$ \boxed{N_{\min} = \frac{mg\sin\theta}{2\mu}} $$ **Numerical Evaluation (2 marks):** $$ N_{\min} = \frac{12 \times 9.81 \times \sin 45^\circ}{2 \times 0.45} $$ Computing $\sin 45^\circ = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$: $$ N_{\min} = \frac{12 \times 9.81 \times 0.7071}{0.90} = \frac{83.23}{0.90} = 92.48 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{N_{\min} \approx 92.5 \text{ N}} $$ With safety factor of 2.0: $$ N_{\text{design}} = 2.0 \times 92.48 = 184.96 \text{ N} \approx 185 \text{ N} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):** > 1. **Writing $N$ instead of $N_{\min}$** in the final box — the examiner specifically wants the *minimum* threshold, not the design value (unless explicitly asked). > 2. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always write "N" (Newtons) after the numerical value. > 3. **Not showing the substitution of $\sin 45^\circ = 1/\sqrt{2}$** as an explicit step — write it out, don't skip. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cable-Crawling Robots** ### ✅ Core Definitions - Cable-crawling robot = field robot that traverses linear flexible infrastructure (cables, wires, ropes) - **CSCR** (Continuous Span Coverage Rate) is the key performance metric in **m/hr** - Three operational phases: **Traverse → Inspect → Obstacle-Cross** ### ✅ Must-Know Formulas (Equation Sheet) - $N_{\min} = \dfrac{mg\sin\theta}{2\mu}$ — minimum clamping force - $F_{\text{drive}} = mg(\sin\theta + \mu_r\cos\theta)$ — drive force - $P_{\text{drive}} = \dfrac{mg\,v\,(\sin\theta + \mu_r\cos\theta)}{\eta_{\text{total}}}$ — motor power - Tipping safety factor ≥ **2.0** for field deployment ### ✅ Architecture Categories (Remember All 4 × 3 × 3) - **4 locomotion types:** Wheel, Leg, Inchworm, Hybrid - **3 adhesion types:** Mechanical, Magnetic, Electrostatic - **3 cable types:** Single-conductor, Bundle, OPGW ### ✅ Typical Numerical Values (Memorize) | Parameter | Typical Value | |-----------|---------------| | Robot mass | 8 – 25 kg | | Crawl speed | 0.3 – 1.0 m/s | | Friction coefficient (rubber/aluminium) | 0.35 – 0.45 | | CSCR (industrial) | 200 – 500 m/hr | | Safety factor (clamp force) | ≥ 2.0 | | Drivetrain efficiency | 30 – 50 % | ### ✅ Industrial Examples (Name at Least 3) - **Hibot Expliner** (Japan) — wheel-based, bundle-conductor - **Hydro-Québec LineScout** (Canada) — wheel + arm hybrid - **CSIRO Tower Climbing Robot** (Australia) — tracked wheels - **ICCAS CableBot** (Germany) — academic prototype ### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps 1. Confusing **drive power** with **battery power** (efficiency correction required). 2. Forgetting to apply the **safety factor** to $N$. 3. Mixing up the **friction coefficient $\mu$ (jaw-cable)** with **$\mu_r$ (drive-wheel rolling)**. 4. Writing $mg\cos\theta$ instead of $mg\sin\theta$ for the **along-cable** weight component. ### ✅ Sensor Suite (Frequently Asked in 3-mark Questions) - HD visual + thermal IR cameras - LiDAR for obstacle detection - IMU for vibration / tilt sensing - Partial-discharge (PD) ultrasonic detector - Corrosion / paint-thickness sensor ---

topics.title: Marine Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Marine Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌊 Marine Robots: Foundational Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Marine Robots** are autonomous or remotely operated electro-mechanical systems designed to operate in maritime environments — including surface waters, the water column, and the seafloor — to perform tasks that are dangerous, repetitive, or inaccessible to humans. They are broadly classified as **Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs)**, **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs)**, **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs)**, and **Underwater Gliders**.

Marine robots are essential in modern ocean engineering because the ocean covers over **70 %** of Earth's surface, yet more than **80 %** remains unmapped and unexplored. The aquatic medium is one of the most hostile engineering environments: high pressure, corrosive saltwater, no GPS signals, no electromagnetic propagation beyond optical ranges, and limited bandwidth for communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — AUV vs ROV (Board-Favourite Question):**
> - **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** Self-powered, intelligent, operates without a tether. Carries its own decision-making computer and energy.
> - **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** Connected to a surface ship via a tether/umbilical cable that supplies power and real-time command/data links.

## 🌊 The Marine Environment — The "Why" of Unique Design

| Environmental Factor | Value / Range | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³** | ~800× denser than air → high drag, buoyancy matters |
| Speed of sound in water | **c ≈ 1500 m/s** | ~4.5× faster than in air → acoustic communication needed |
| Light attenuation | < 200 m in clear water | Optical sensors useless below 200 m |
| Hydrostatic pressure | **P = ρgh** (≈ 1 atm / 10 m) | Hulls must withstand crushing pressures |
| GPS availability | Surface only | Underwater vehicles need acoustic/INS navigation |
| Salinity | ~35 ppt | Corrosion-resistant alloys mandatory |

## 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Submarine Postman"

Imagine trying to deliver a letter across a **murky, 4 km deep ocean canyon** with **no torch, no radio, no GPS, and a battery that must last months**. That is the design challenge of marine robotics. Like a blind postman in a tunnel, the robot must:
- *Feel* its way using sound echoes (SONAR),
- *Sense* its orientation using inertial measurement (IMU/INS),
- *Count* its depth using pressure (pressure sensor),
- *Talk* to the surface using slow acoustic modems,
- *Survive* high pressure in a watertight hull.

That is marine robotics in a nutshell.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧭 1. Classification of Marine Robots

Marine robots are categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

### (a) By Operating Medium
- **USV (Unmanned Surface Vehicle):** Operates at the air–water interface. Has GPS, radio, and visual access.
- **UUV (Unmanned Underwater Vehicle):** Sub-surface operation. The umbrella term.
  - **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** Untethered, free-swimming, intelligent.
  - **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** Tethered, pilot-controlled.
  - **Hybrid ROV/AUV:** Can switch modes.
  - **Glider:** Buoyancy-driven, low-speed, long-endurance.

### (b) By Hull Form
- **Open-frame:** Skeleton structure with exposed components (typical ROVs).
- **Torpedo-shaped:** Streamlined, low drag (typical AUVs like REMUS).
- **Laminar-flow body:** Myring hull form for efficient gliding.
- **Cylindrical/Modular:** For deep-sea work.

### (c) By Mission Profile
- Observation, mapping, intervention, inspection, surveillance.

## ⚙️ 2. Functional Architecture of a Marine Robot

A typical AUV/ROV system has the following subsystems:

1. **Mechanical Hull (Pressure Vessel):** Titanium/AL-6061 sphere or cylinder. Hydrostatic pressure rating determines depth rating.
2. **Propulsion System:** Thrusters (ducted, Kort-nozzle, brushless DC) or buoyancy engines (gliders).
3. **Energy System:** Li-ion battery packs, sometimes fuel cells.
4. **Navigation & Guidance Suite:** INS (IMU + DVL), GPS (surface), depth sensor, USBL/LBL.
5. **Payload/Sensors:** Multibeam SONAR, side-scan SONAR, sub-bottom profiler, magnetometer, CTD.
6. **Communication Suite:** Acoustic modem (underwater), radio/Wi-Fi (surface).
7. **Onboard Computer:** Mission planner, control loops, fault management.
8. **Surface Support (for ROVs):** Tether management system (TMS), winch, control van.

## 📡 3. Underwater Acoustics — The Language of the Deep

Because electromagnetic waves attenuate in microseconds underwater, **acoustic waves are the only practical means of long-range underwater communication and sensing**. The speed of sound in seawater is a function of temperature (T), salinity (S), and depth (z), described empirically by the **Chen-Millero** or **UNESCO** equation:

$$c = 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^{2} + 2.374 \times 10^{-4}T^{3} + 1.340(S - 35) + 1.630 \times 10^{-2}D + 1.675 \times 10^{-7}D^{2}$$

where *T* is in °C, *S* in ppt, *D* is depth in m, and *c* in m/s. The **Mackenzie approximation** is a compact form:

$$c \approx 1448.96 + 4.591T - 0.05304T^{2} + 1.340(S - 35) + 0.01625D$$

For most engineering purposes, **c ≈ 1500 m/s** is the working constant.

### Snell's Law of Acoustics in Water
Sound rays bend due to sound speed gradients (a form of underwater refraction):

$$\frac{\sin\theta_{1}}{c_{1}} = \frac{\sin\theta_{2}}{c_{2}}$$

This causes the famous **SOFAR channel** at ~1000 m depth where sound travels thousands of kilometres.

## 📍 4. Underwater Positioning Systems (Localization)

This is the heart of marine robotics — the student **must** know the three flavors of acoustic positioning:

| System | Abbreviation | Range | Accuracy | Principle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Long Base-Line | **LBL** | 1–10 km | ± 0.1–1 m | Transponders fixed on seabed; baseline > ship length |
| Short Base-Line | **SBL** | 100 m | ± 1 m | Transducers on the hull of surface ship |
| Ultra-Short Base-Line | **USBL** | 10 km | ± 0.1–10 m | Single transducer head measures angle + range |

The fundamental ranging equation for one-way acoustic travel time is:

$$R = c \cdot t_{travel}$$

For a two-way (round-trip) ping:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_{2-way}}{2}$$

## 🌀 5. Hydrodynamics & Equations of Motion (6-DOF)

A marine robot is a **6-DOF rigid body** with translation (surge, sway, heave) and rotation (roll, pitch, yaw). The forces include hydrostatic, hydrodynamic (added mass, drag, lift), and propulsion.

The hydrodynamic drag force on the vehicle is:

$$F_{D} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,A\,v^{2}$$

where:
- **ρ** = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- **C_D** = drag coefficient (0.1–0.4 for torpedo hulls)
- **A** = frontal cross-sectional area (m²)
- **v** = relative velocity (m/s)

Added mass (Fossen formulation) arises because the vehicle must also accelerate the surrounding water:

$$F_{added} = -M_{A}\dot{\nu} - C_{A}(\nu)\nu$$

The full 6-DOF equation of motion (Fossen, 1994 — standard marine robotics textbook):

$$M\dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau + \tau_{disturbance}$$

where:
- **M** = rigid-body + added-mass inertia matrix
- **C(ν)** = Coriolis-centripetal matrix
- **D(ν)** = damping matrix
- **g(η)** = restoring forces (gravity + buoyancy)
- **τ** = control forces and moments from thrusters
- **ν** = body-fixed velocity vector, **η** = position/orientation in NED frame

## 🧠 6. Navigation Sensors (Sensor Stack)

| Sensor | Quantity Measured | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** | Velocity w.r.t. seafloor | Dead-reckoning integration |
| **IMU (Fiber-Optic/Ring Laser Gyro)** | Angular rate, acceleration | INS core |
| **Pressure Sensor** | Depth (via P = ρgh) | Heave control |
| **USBL / LBL Transponder** | Range to known point | Absolute position fixes |
| **Magnetometer** | Earth's magnetic field | Heading reference |
| **GPS (surface only)** | Position, velocity | Surface fixes |
| **CTD** | Conductivity, T, Depth | Sound-speed profile |

## 🎯 7. KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Depth from pressure |
| 2 | $c \approx 1500$ m/s | Speed of sound in water | Travel-time to range |
| 3 | $R = c \cdot t$ | Acoustic range | One-way travel time |
| 4 | $R = \tfrac{c t_{2w}}{2}$ | Two-way range | SONAR altitude/range |
| 5 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^{2}$ | Hydrodynamic drag | Energy budget |
| 6 | $B = \rho V g$ | Buoyancy force | Static stability check |
| 7 | $f_D = \tfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ | Doppler shift | DVL speed measurement |
| 8 | $\eta_{prop} = \tfrac{F_{thrust}\cdot v}{P_{elec}}$ | Propulsive efficiency | Endurance estimation |
| 9 | $t_{mission} = \tfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}}$ | Battery life | Mission planning |
| 10 | $RL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{P_t}{P_r}\right) + \alpha R$ | Transmission loss | Acoustic link budget |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in the Real World:**
> These equations are used by **Defence (mine countermeasures), ONGC (pipeline inspection), ISRO (MOM/mangalyaan analog), and NIO (deep-sea biodiversity mapping).** Kerala's coast makes marine robotics a high-value industry — Kochi's *SAGAR* initiative and CSIR-NIO recruit engineers trained in this field.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 Derivation 1: From Hydrostatic Pressure to Depth Measurement

> **Problem:** A pressure sensor reports *P = 11.30 × 10⁵ Pa* on a marine robot. The atmospheric pressure is *P₀ = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa*, density of seawater *ρ = 1025 kg/m³*, and *g = 9.81 m/s²*. Determine the operating depth.

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental hydrostatic relation.**

The absolute pressure at depth *h* is the sum of atmospheric pressure and the fluid column weight:

$$P = P_{0} + \rho g h$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for *h*.**

$$h = \frac{P - P_{0}}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$h = \frac{11.30 \times 10^{5} \; - \; 1.013 \times 10^{5}}{1025 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$11.30 \times 10^{5} - 1.013 \times 10^{5} = 10.287 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**

$$1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ N/m}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$h = \frac{10.287 \times 10^{5}}{10055.25} \approx 10.23 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: The robot is at a depth of ~10.23 m below the surface.**

---

## 🔬 Derivation 2: SONAR Range From Two-Way Travel Time

> **Problem:** A multibeam SONAR ping travels down to the seafloor, reflects, and returns. The recorded two-way time is *t₂w = 0.046 s*. Using *c = 1500 m/s*, find the altitude of the AUV.

**Step 1 — Geometric principle.**

Acoustic energy travels from transducer to target and back. The two-way distance is *c · t₂w*. The actual altitude is half of this.

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_{2w}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$R = \frac{1500 \times 0.046}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

$$1500 \times 0.046 = 69 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Halve.**

$$R = \frac{69}{2} = 34.5 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: The AUV is flying 34.5 m above the seafloor.**

---

## 🔬 Derivation 3: Drag Force and Energy Budget for a Glider

> **Problem:** A torpedo-shaped AUV has frontal area *A = 0.05 m²*, drag coefficient *C_D = 0.21*, and cruises at *v = 1.5 m/s*. Compute drag force and the energy consumed over a *5 km* mission.

**Step 1 — Drag force formula.**

$$F_{D} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_{D} A v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$F_{D} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.21 \times 0.05 \times (1.5)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute *v²*.**

$$v^{2} = 2.25 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply step by step.**

$$F_{D} = 0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$$

$$512.5 \times 0.21 = 107.625$$

$$107.625 \times 0.05 = 5.381$$

$$5.381 \times 2.25 = 12.108 \text{ N}$$

> ✅ **Drag force ≈ 12.11 N**

**Step 5 — Energy required = Force × distance.**

$$W = F_D \cdot d = 12.108 \times 5000 = 60540 \text{ J} \approx 60.54 \text{ kJ}$$

> ✅ **Energy for 5 km mission ≈ 60.5 kJ**

**Step 6 — Time of mission.**

$$t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{5000}{1.5} = 3333.3 \text{ s} \approx 55.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 7 — Average power requirement.**

$$P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{60540}{3333.3} \approx 18.16 \text{ W}$$

> ✅ **Required power ≈ 18.2 W**

---

## 🔬 Derivation 4: Doppler Velocity Log (DVL) Speed Calculation

> **Problem:** A DVL transmits at *f₀ = 600 kHz*. The return from the seafloor is shifted by *Δf = +80 Hz* (vehicle moving away from floor along beam axis). The speed of sound is *c = 1500 m/s*. Find the vehicle's vertical speed.

**Step 1 — Doppler shift equation (for a moving observer and stationary scatterer, simplified).**

$$v = \frac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_{0}}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for round-trip Doppler shift.

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$v = \frac{80 \times 1500}{2 \times 600000}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$80 \times 1500 = 120000$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$2 \times 600000 = 1200000$$

**Step 5 — Division.**

$$v = \frac{120000}{1200000} = 0.1 \text{ m/s}$$

> ✅ **Result: AUV is moving at 0.1 m/s away from the seafloor.**

---

## 🔬 Derivation 5: USBL Trilateration Position

> **Problem:** A USBL head on a ship reports the slant range *R = 2500 m*, bearing *θ_az = 045°*, and elevation *θ_el = 30°* (below horizontal) to an AUV. Find the AUV's NED coordinates relative to the ship.

**Step 1 — Decompose slant range into North, East, Down components.**

$$N = R \cos(\theta_{el}) \cos(\theta_{az})$$

$$E = R \cos(\theta_{el}) \sin(\theta_{az})$$

$$D = R \sin(\theta_{el})$$

**Step 2 — Compute *cos(30°) = √3/2 ≈ 0.8660* and *sin(30°) = 0.5*.**

**Step 3 — Compute horizontal projection.**

$$R_{horiz} = R \cos(\theta_{el}) = 2500 \times 0.8660 = 2165 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute North component.**

$$N = 2165 \times \cos(45°) = 2165 \times 0.7071 = 1530.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute East component.**

$$E = 2165 \times \sin(45°) = 2165 \times 0.7071 = 1530.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Down component.**

$$D = 2500 \times 0.5 = 1250 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: AUV is at N = 1530.9 m, E = 1530.9 m, D = 1250 m relative to the ship.**

---

## 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AUV vs ROV vs Glider

| Parameter | **AUV** | **ROV** | **Underwater Glider** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power | Onboard battery | Via tether | Onboard battery |
| Communication | Acoustic modem (slow) | Tether (real-time) | Acoustic modem (low rate) |
| Endurance | Hours – days | Unlimited (mains via ship) | Weeks – months |
| Max Depth | 6000 m+ | 6500 m+ | 1000 m (typical) |
| Speed | 1–3 m/s | 0.5–1.5 m/s | 0.25 m/s |
| Intelligence | High (onboard AI) | Low (teleop) | Medium (mission-driven) |
| Cost | Medium | High (with ship) | Low |
| Example | REMUS, HUGIN | Hercules, Jason | Slocum, Spray |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📐 Diagram 1: Master Classification of Marine Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MR[Marine Robots] --> UMV[Unmanned Marine Vehicles]
    MR --> HOV[Manned Submersibles]

    UMV --> USV[Unmanned Surface Vehicle USV]
    UMV --> UUV[Unmanned Underwater Vehicle UUV]

    UUV --> AUV[Autonomous Underwater Vehicle AUV]
    UUV --> ROV[Remotely Operated Vehicle ROV]
    UUV --> GL[Underwater Glider]
    UUV --> HYB[Hybrid ROV-AUV]

    AUV --> OBS1[Observation class AUV REMUS]
    AUV --> OBS2[Deep diving AUV HUGIN]
    GL --> GL1[Slocum Glider]
    GL --> GL2[Spray Glider]

    USV --> USV1[Wave Glider]
    USV --> USV2[Sail Drone]

    ROV --> ROV1[Observation ROV]
    ROV --> ROV2[Work class ROV]
    ROV --> ROV3[Heavy work class ROV]
```

## 📐 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AUV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Power Source Li-ion battery] --> DC[DC Bus]
    DC --> MCU[Main Control Unit]
    DC --> PRS[Propulsion Drivers]
    DC --> SNS[Sensor Power Conditioning]

    MCU --> PRS
    PRS --> THR[Thrusters BLDC]
    THR --> HULL[Hull and Hydrodynamics]

    SNS --> IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit]
    SNS --> DVL[Doppler Velocity Log]
    SNS --> PRS2[Pressure Sensor]
    SNS --> CTD[CTD Probe]
    SNS --> CAM[Camera and Lights]
    SNS --> SONAR[Multibeam SONAR]

    IMU --> NAV[Navigation Filter EKF]
    DVL --> NAV
    PRS2 --> NAV
    NAV --> MCU
    NAV --> ACOM[Acoustic Modem]
    ACOM --> SURF[Surface Ship or Buoy]
```

## 📐 Diagram 3: Underwater Acoustic Positioning Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Absolute Position] --> B{Operating Range}
    B -->|1 to 10 km| C[LBL Long BaseLine]
    B -->|100 m| D[SBL Short BaseLine]
    B -->|up to 10 km| E[USBL Ultra Short BaseLine]

    C --> C1[Seabed Transponders]
    C --> C2[High Accuracy Survey]

    D --> D1[Hull Mounted Transducers]
    D --> D2[Ship Relative]

    E --> E1[Single Transducer Head]
    E --> E2[Angle and Range Measurement]
    E --> E3[Most Common for AUV Tracking]
```

## 📐 Diagram 4: 6-DOF Marine Robot Reference Frames

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Fixed NED Frame] -->|Transform J1 eta| B[Body Frame]
    A -->|Transform J2| C[Velocity in Body Frame]
    B --> D[Thruster Allocation]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Forces and Moments]
    E --> F[Equation of Motion Fossen]
    F -->|Update| A
```

## 📐 Diagram 5: Sensor-Fusion Data-Flow for AUV Navigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IMU[IMU Accel and Gyro] --> EKF[Extended Kalman Filter]
    DVL[DVL Velocity] --> EKF
    GPS[GPS Surface] --> EKF
    USBL[USBL Fix] --> EKF
    PRS[Pressure Depth] --> EKF
    MAG[Magnetometer] --> EKF

    EKF --> STATE[Best Estimate Position Velocity Attitude]
    STATE --> GUID[Guidance Controller]
    GUID --> CTRL[Low Level Control]
    CTRL --> THR[Thrusters]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of an AUV," examiners expect a figure that contains: **(i) Power block**, **(ii) Computer/control block**, **(iii) Sensor block**, **(iv) Propulsion block**, **(v) Communication block**, and **(vi) Feedback arrows**. The Mermaid architecture above is the gold standard for full marks.
