<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Differentiate between an AUV and an ROV with respect to power, control, communication, and typical applications.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **AUV** | **ROV** |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Onboard rechargeable battery (e.g., Li-ion) | Continuous power via tether/umbilical from surface ship |
| **Control** | Autonomous, on-board mission computer and AI | Real-time human pilot control (tele-operated) |
| **Communication** | Acoustic modem — low bandwidth, high latency | Tether provides high-speed, low-latency data link |
| **Applications** | Long-duration survey, mapping, oceanography | Inspection, intervention, manipulation tasks |
| **Risk Profile** | Vehicle loss risk if fault occurs | Vehicle can be recovered via tether |

> ✅ The key distinction is that AUVs are *intelligent and untethered*, while ROVs are *tele-operated and tethered*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is acoustic communication preferred over electromagnetic communication for underwater robots? Mention any two limitations.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Why acoustic?**
- Electromagnetic (radio and optical) waves **attenuate within microseconds to a few metres** in seawater due to high conductivity and absorption.
- Acoustic waves at frequencies of **10–50 kHz** can propagate **kilometres to tens of kilometres**, making them the only practical long-range underwater carrier.
- The speed of sound in seawater is **c ≈ 1500 m/s**, allowing reasonable latency for non-realtime control.

**Two limitations:**
1. **Low bandwidth:** Only a few kbps compared to MHz for radio, restricting data rates (camera video impossible acoustically).
2. **High latency and multipath:** The slow speed of sound gives ~0.67 s/km one-way delay; reflections from surface and bottom create multipath interference.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)**. List and describe the role of **any four major subsystems**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the equation for computing the **depth of a marine robot from a pressure sensor reading** and compute the depth if the absolute pressure is *P = 2.5 × 10⁶ Pa*, given *ρ = 1025 kg/m³*, *g = 9.81 m/s²*, and *P₀ = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa*. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — AUV Architecture *(7 marks)*

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
+-----------+       +-------------+       +-----------+
|  Power    |------>|  Main       |<----->|  Sensor   |
|  Source   |       |  Control    |       |  Suite    |
|  (Li-ion) |       |  Computer   |       |  (IMU,    |
+-----------+       |  (MCU)      |       |  DVL, P,  |
                    +-------------+       |  SONAR)   |
                           |              +-----------+
                           v
                    +-------------+
                    | Propulsion  |----> Thrusters
                    | Driver      |
                    +-------------+
                           |
                           v
                    +-------------+
                    | Acoustic    |<---> Surface Support
                    | Modem       |      (USBL/Satellite)
                    +-------------+
```

**Explanation of four subsystems (each 1.5 marks):**

1. **Power Subsystem:** The AUV carries high energy-density **Li-ion** or **Li-polymer** battery packs. Energy budget determines endurance (typically 24–72 hours for survey-class AUVs). Voltage regulation units (DC-DC converters) supply stable rails to computers and sensors.

2. **Main Control Computer (MCU):** Runs the **guidance, navigation, and control (GNC)** algorithms. Hosts the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for sensor fusion, the mission planner, the fault management module, and the I/O drivers. It interfaces with all subsystems via **CAN bus, RS-485, or Ethernet**.

3. **Sensor Suite:** Provides measurements for navigation and payload. **IMU** gives angular rate and acceleration; **DVL** gives velocity over ground; **pressure sensor** gives depth; **multibeam SONAR** and **side-scan SONAR** generate bathymetric and image data; **CTD** records sound-speed profile.

4. **Propulsion and Maneuvering:** Typically **4–7 brushless DC thrusters** arranged in a vector configuration. The **thruster allocator** (control allocation matrix) computes the PWM signals from the desired generalized force vector (surge, sway, heave, yaw).

5. **Communication Subsystem (for completeness):** **Acoustic modem** for underwater command/data exchange (low bandwidth, ~kbps) and a **surface-emerged radio link** (Wi-Fi/GPS/Iridium) when the AUV surfaces.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Depth Derivation *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic principle.**

The absolute pressure at depth *h* below a free surface is given by:

$$P = P_{0} + \rho g h$$

This is the **fundamental equation of fluid statics** and is **assumed in the syllabus** as the operating principle of the pressure-depth sensor.

**Step 2 — Solving for depth.**

Rearranging the equation:

$$h = \frac{P - P_{0}}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution.**

$$h = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6} - 1.013 \times 10^{5}}{1025 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$2.5 \times 10^{6} - 0.1013 \times 10^{6} = 2.3987 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$1025 \times 9.81 = 10055.25 \text{ N/m}^{3}$$

**Step 6 — Final division.**

$$h = \frac{2.3987 \times 10^{6}}{10055.25} = 238.55 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: The AUV is at a depth of approximately 238.5 m.**

---

### **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Classify marine robots in detail with a flowchart. Explain the **working principle of an ROV** and list its **three major subsystems**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A multibeam SONAR on an AUV sends a ping downward. The round-trip time recorded is *t₂w = 0.08 s*. If the speed of sound in seawater is *c = 1500 m/s*, calculate the **altitude of the AUV above the seafloor**. State two assumptions made. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Marine Robot Classification & ROV *(7 marks)*

**Classification flowchart (text-based for answer):**

- **Marine Robots**
  - **Manned** (submersibles — human inside)
  - **Unmanned**
    - **USV** — surface (e.g., Wave Glider)
    - **UUV**
      - **AUV** — autonomous (REMUS, HUGIN)
      - **ROV** — tethered (Jason, Hercules)
      - **Glider** — buoyancy-driven (Slocum)

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4 for the canonical version.)*

**ROV Working Principle (4 marks):**
An **ROV** is a tethered underwater robot controlled by a human pilot on a surface ship. The **umbilical cable** transmits power downward and **command/control + video** signals bidirectionally. ROVs are equipped with **thrusters** (typically 4–8) and **manipulator arms** for intervention tasks. They do not carry significant onboard intelligence — most decisions are made by the pilot. A **Tether Management System (TMS)** on the host ship pays out and recovers the cable.

**Three Major Subsystems (3 marks):**
1. **Surface Subsystem:** Control van, winch, power supply, TMS, pilot console.
2. **Umbilical / Tether:** Carries power, fiber-optic data; sometimes a 'flying lead'.
3. **Subsea Vehicle:** Frame, thrusters, cameras/lights, sonar, manipulator, and sensors.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — SONAR Altitude *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Write the two-way ranging equation.**

$$R = \frac{c \cdot t_{2w}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$R = \frac{1500 \times 0.08}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$1500 \times 0.08 = 120 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Halve.**

$$R = \frac{120}{2} = 60 \text{ m}$$

> ✅ **Result: The AUV altitude is 60 m above the seafloor.**

**Two assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The **speed of sound is uniform** along the path (c = 1500 m/s). In reality, sound speed varies with T, S, and depth.
2. The **seafloor is directly below** the transducer and the path is vertical (no oblique incidence error).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Reasons for Marks Deduction:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students write *R = c · t₂w* and report 120 m, losing 1 mark instantly. **Always halve the two-way time.**
> 2. **Using c = 343 m/s (air):** This is the speed of sound in air. For seawater it is **1500 m/s** — using 343 m/s is a fatal error.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for assumptions. Lose 1–2 marks if you skip them.
> 4. **Mixing up AUV and ROV definitions:** AUVs are *autonomous* (self-decision-making), ROVs are *remotely operated* (human in loop). Reversing these is a 3-mark penalty in the differentiation question.
> 5. **Skipping the "two-way" distinction:** A common error is to use *c·t* instead of *c·t/2*. Circle this in your answer sheet before submission.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Bullet Revision — Pin This List**

- 🌊 **Marine robots** are classified as **USV, AUV, ROV, Glider, Hybrid**.
- 🔋 **AUV** = Autonomous, untethered, battery-powered, acoustic comms.
- 🪢 **ROV** = Tethered, surface-powered, real-time video/control, pilot-in-loop.
- 📡 **Underwater communication** = **acoustic waves** (c ≈ 1500 m/s), NOT radio.
- 🧭 **GPS does not work underwater** — use **INS + DVL + USBL/LBL** fusion.
- 💧 **Density of seawater** = **1025 kg/m³** (use this value, not 1000).
- 🧮 **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = \rho g h$ → 1 atm per 10 m depth.
- 🎯 **SONAR range** two-way: $R = \tfrac{c\,t_{2w}}{2}$; one-way: $R = c\,t$.
- 🌊 **Drag force:** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^{2}$ — dominant energy loss underwater.
- ⚙️ **6-DOF equations of motion** (Fossen form) for full dynamics of the vehicle.
- 🔄 **Navigation stack:** IMU + DVL + Pressure + USBL → **EKF → state → control**.
- 🛰️ **Acoustic positioning flavors:** LBL (most accurate), SBL, USBL (most common).
- 🛡️ **Hull material:** Titanium or AL-6061 for pressure resistance; corrosion is the silent killer.
- 🔊 **Snell's law of acoustics** governs sound ray bending in stratified water.
- ⏱️ **Doppler Velocity Log** uses the formula $v = \tfrac{\Delta f \cdot c}{2 f_0}$ to compute speed.
- 🏭 **Real-world users:** DRDO (military), ONGC (oil & gas), NIO (science), ISRO (analog missions).
- 🎓 **KTU exam mantra:** "Always halve two-way travel time. Always use 1500 m/s for c. Always state your assumptions."

> ✅ **End of Module 2 — Marine Robots Notes.** Good luck with your KTU examination preparation! 🚀
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hinged Microbots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hinged Microbots # 🔬 HINGED MICROBOTS — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Standard)** > A **Hinged Microbot** is a sub-centimeter scale robotic platform (typical characteristic length **L = 1 µm to 10 mm**) whose mechanical architecture relies on one or more **articulated joints (hinges)** to convert an external stimulus (magnetic field, electric field, thermal gradient, or chemical reaction) into coordinated **locomotion, manipulation, or environmental interaction**. In the context of **Field and Service Robotics (PCRUT602)**, hinged microbots form a sub-class of **untethered, swarm-capable, task-specific micro-robots** designed to operate in unstructured human, medical, agricultural, and industrial environments where conventional macro-scale robots cannot access. ### 1.1.1 Taxonomy Position | Classification Axis | Position of Hinged Microbot | |---|---| | Scale | **Micro/Milli** (10⁻⁶ – 10⁻² m) | | Power Source | **External field / Onboard chemical** (rarely tethered) | | Actuation Class | **Discrete Articulated (rigid-body)** + **Compliant flexure** | | Mobility | Walking, crawling, swimming, flying, rolling | | Autonomy Level | Semi-autonomous to fully autonomous (with swarm coordination) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Origami Ant" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition for First-Time Learners** > Imagine taking a **flat paper origami ant**. The ant cannot move on its own; it is rigid. Now, if you embed **tiny flexible joints (hinges)** at the leg–body and leg–foot interfaces, and you blow gentle magnetic "wind" from a coil above, the legs bend at the hinges and the ant **walks** across a surface. A **Hinged Microbot is exactly this origami ant**, but fabricated from silicon, polymer, or shape-memory alloy, with hinges that flex at micrometer precision when activated. A more familiar analogy: think of a **finger**. A finger cannot move without its **knuckle joints (hinges)**. Similarly, a microbot cannot crawl, grip, or swim without **micro-scale hinges** that convert one form of energy into mechanical articulation. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Performance Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Constants (highlighted for KTU numerical problems)** > - **Characteristic length (L):** 1 µm – 10 mm > - **Reynolds Number (Re):** typically **Re < 1** (creeping flow regime) for swimmers > - **Operating Frequency:** **0.1 Hz – 10 kHz** (depending on actuation) > - **Fabrication Resolution:** down to **100 nm** (photolithography limit) > - **Material Density (typical SU-8 polymer):** **ρ ≈ 1250 kg/m³** > - **Young's Modulus (Polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS):** **E ≈ 0.5 – 3 MPa** > - **Young's Modulus (Single-crystal Silicon):** **E ≈ 130 – 190 GPa** > - **Speed of Operation:** **0.1 – 100 body lengths/second** ## 1.4 Why Hinges? — The Mechanical Necessity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Frequently asked 3-mark question)** > **"Why are hinges essential in microbots rather than continuous flexible bodies?"** > > **Answer:** At micro-scale, **predictable, repeatable, and controllable motion** is required. Continuous deformation is hard to model and harder to control. **Hinges discretize motion** into well-defined angular displacements (θ₁, θ₂, … θₙ), enabling: > 1. **Closed-form kinematic modeling** (D-H parameters, screw theory) > 2. **Force amplification** through lever arms > 3. **Deterministic gait patterns** (asymmetric gait for forward motion) > 4. **Repeatable fabrication** using MEMS processes ## 1.5 Section 1 — Quick Recap Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Energy Source
Magnetic, Thermal,
Electric, Chemical] --> B[Hinge Mechanism
Compliant, Rigid,
Living, MEMS] B --> C[Articulated Body
Legs, Wings,
Fins, Grippers] C --> D[Locomotion Output
Walk, Swim, Fly,
Crawl, Manipulate] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C ``` *Figure 1.1: Functional chain of a Hinged Microbot.* # ⚙️ HINGED MICROBOTS — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Hinged Microbot — Five Subsystems > [!NOTE] > Every KTU-board-grade microbot description **must identify the five subsystems** below. Examiners look for this structural mapping. 1. **Body (Chassis)** — Provides structural frame and houses functional elements. 2. **Hinge Array** — The articulated joints that enable relative motion between body parts. 3. **Actuator** — Converts external stimulus into mechanical work. 4. **Transducer/Sensor** (optional) — Detects environmental feedback. 5. **Surface Interface** — Foot, fin, or wing geometry that interacts with the medium. ## 2.2 Classification of Hinges | Hinge Type | Mechanism | Typical Material | Fabrication | Key Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Rigid Pin Hinge** | Physical pin–socket articulation | Silicon, metal | MEMS, micro-machining | Walking legs | | **Compliant (Flexure) Hinge** | Elastic deformation of thin section | PDMS, SU-8, Ni-alloy | Photolithography | Wings, fins | | **Living Hinge** | Thinned polymer web flexes | Polypropylene, PET | Injection molding | Crawlers | | **Shape-Memory Hinge** | Martensite–Austenite phase transition | NiTi (Nitinol) | Laser cutting | Crawling microbots | | **Magnetic-Polymer Hinge** | Elastomer with embedded magnetic particles | PDMS + NdFeB | Cast-molding | Soft microbots | ## 2.3 Actuation Principles (Detailed) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Topic: Actuation comparison is a guaranteed Part-A question** ### 2.3.1 Magnetic Actuation - **Principle:** External rotating/permanent magnetic field applies torque on a magnetized body. - **Torque Equation:** **τ = m × B** where **m** is magnetic moment, **B** is flux density. - **Advantages:** Wireless, fast (kHz), works in deep tissue. - **Disadvantage:** Requires external coil infrastructure. ### 2.3.2 Piezoelectric Actuation - **Principle:** Applied voltage causes lattice deformation (inverse piezoelectric effect). - **Strain:** **S = d · E** where **d** is piezoelectric coefficient, **E** is electric field. - **Advantage:** Precise nanometer positioning. - **Disadvantage:** Requires high voltage (> 100 V). ### 2.3.3 Thermal Actuation (Shape Memory Alloy — SMA) - **Principle:** Heating above austenite finish temperature **A_f** restores memorized shape. - **Hysteresis:** Several °C lag between heating/cooling transitions. - **Use:** Crawling microbots (Nitinol hinges). ### 2.3.4 Chemical / Self-Propulsion - **Catalytic reaction** (Pt–H₂O₂) generates bubble thrust; or **enzymatic reaction** on tissue. - **Reynolds regime:** Almost always **Re << 1** → viscous (Stokes) regime. ## 2.4 Scaling Laws — Why Microbots Behave Differently > [!NOTE] > **Critical Concept for KTU Numerical Problems** > As length **L** scales down, **volume ∝ L³** shrinks faster than **surface area ∝ L²**. Thus, **surface forces (viscosity, surface tension, electrostatics) dominate inertial forces (gravity, momentum)** at the microscale. **Dimensionless Numbers Every Hinged Microbot Designer Must Know:** 1. **Reynolds Number:** Re = ρvL/μ - Determines flow regime: Re < 1 → viscous (creeping); Re > 1000 → inertial. 2. **Capillary Number:** Ca = μv/γ (viscous force vs. surface tension). 3. **Froude Number:** Fr = v²/(gL). 4. **Bending Stiffness Ratio:** k_h/(E·L) where k_h is hinge stiffness. ## 2.5 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are exam-relevant. Memorize units and boundary conditions.** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Domain of Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **τ = m × B = mB sin(θ)** | Magnetic torque on dipole | N·m | Magnetic actuation | | 2 | **S = d · E = d · (V/t)** | Piezoelectric strain | dimensionless | V < Curie voltage | | 3 | **k_h = E·b·t³ / (2·L³)** | Hinge spring constant (leaf) | N/m | Linear elastic regime | | 4 | **f_n = (1/2π)·√(k_h/m_eff)** | Natural frequency of hinge | Hz | Small oscillation | | 5 | **Re = ρ·v·L/μ** | Reynolds number | dimensionless | All flow regimes | | 6 | **F_drag = 6·π·μ·r·v** | Stokes drag (sphere) | N | Re < 1 | | 7 | **F_drag = (1/2)·C_d·ρ·A·v²** | Inertial drag | N | Re > 1000 | | 8 | **Ca = μ·v/γ** | Capillary number | dimensionless | Liquid–gas interface | | 9 | **v_robot = f_step · λ_step** | Locomotion velocity | m/s | Periodic gait | | 10 | **P_elastic = (1/2)·k_h·θ²** | Elastic energy stored | J | Small θ | | 11 | **η_energy = W_out/W_in** | Energy efficiency | dimensionless | 0 < η ≤ 1 | | 12 | **l_c = √(γ/(ρ·g))** | Capillary length | m | Surface wetting | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** Each formula above uses `×` or `·` instead of `*` and avoids the pipe symbol `|` for absolute value (uses `\vert` or `mid` if needed) to prevent breaking the markdown table renderer. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Hinged microbots are revolutionizing: - **🏥 Minimally Invasive Surgery** — Magnetically steered micro-grippers retrieve tissue samples. - **🌾 Precision Agriculture** — Insect-scale hinged crawlers inspect crop leaves. - **🛢️ Pipeline Inspection** — Crawling hinged bots navigate oil/gas pipes. - **🧬 Targeted Drug Delivery** — Hinged swimmers (e.g., sperm-inspired) carry payload to tumor sites. - **🔍 Environmental Monitoring** — Micro-flyers with flapping hinged wings sample pollutants. # 📐 HINGED MICROBOTS — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stiffness of a Compliant (Leaf) Hinge > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A cantilever beam of length **L**, width **b**, and thickness **t** acts as a leaf-type compliant hinge. Derive its rotational stiffness and angular deflection under a tip moment. ### 3.1.1 Setup Consider a slender cantilever beam fixed at one end, free at the other. A tip moment **M** is applied at the free end. The beam bends in the **x–y plane** (length along x, deflection along y, thickness t along z-direction). **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Bending Equation:** $$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{M(x)}{E \cdot I}$$ where **I** is the second moment of area of the cross-section. ### 3.1.2 Compute the Second Moment of Area For a rectangular cross-section of width **b** and thickness **t** (bending about the axis perpendicular to length, in the plane of bending): $$I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$$ ### 3.1.3 Apply Constant Tip Moment For a constant tip moment **M** applied at the free end, **M(x) = M** (constant throughout the length). Substituting: $$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I} = \frac{12 \cdot M}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$ ### 3.1.4 Integrate Twice **First integration** (slope, dy/dx): $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{12 \cdot M}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3} \cdot x + C_1$$ **Apply boundary condition 1:** At the fixed end (x = 0), slope = 0 → **C₁ = 0**. **Second integration** (deflection y): $$y(x) = \frac{12 \cdot M}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} + C_2$$ **Apply boundary condition 2:** At the fixed end (x = 0), deflection y = 0 → **C₂ = 0**. $$y(x) = \frac{6 \cdot M \cdot x^2}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$ ### 3.1.5 Compute Angular Deflection at Free End Slope at the free end (x = L): $$\theta = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = \frac{12 \cdot M \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}$$ ### 3.1.6 Define Rotational Stiffness By definition, the **rotational spring constant (hinge stiffness)** is: $$k_\theta = \frac{M}{\theta}$$ **Substituting the slope equation:** $$k_\theta = \frac{M}{\dfrac{12 \cdot M \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}}$$ **Simplify** (M cancels): $$k_\theta = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Equation (KTU High-Yield Result)** > $$\boxed{k_\theta = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L}}$$ > Units: **N·m/rad** ### 3.1.7 Engineering Insight - Stiffness scales **linearly with Young's modulus E** → choose stiff materials (silicon) for high torque load. - Stiffness scales **cubically with thickness t** → halving t reduces stiffness by 8×. - Stiffness scales **inversely with length L** → longer hinges are softer. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Natural Frequency of a Hinged Leg > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A hinged microbot leg (effective mass **m_eff**, hinge stiffness **k_θ**) oscillates as a torsional spring–mass system. Derive the natural frequency of gait. ### 3.2.1 Equation of Motion For a torsional pendulum: $$I_{eff} \cdot \ddot{\theta} + k_\theta \cdot \theta = 0$$ where **I_eff** is the effective moment of inertia of the leg about the hinge. ### 3.2.2 Assume Effective Inertia For a slender leg of length **L_leg** and mass **m_eff** rotating about one end: $$I_{eff} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot m_{eff} \cdot L_{leg}^2$$ ### 3.2.3 Standard Harmonic Oscillator Solution The angular natural frequency is: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_\theta}{I_{eff}}}$$ **Substituting both k_θ and I_eff:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L}}{\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot m_{eff} \cdot L_{leg}^2}}$$ **Simplify the ratio:** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{4 \cdot L \cdot m_{eff} \cdot L_{leg}^2}}$$ **Convert to Hz:** $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{4 \cdot L \cdot m_{eff} \cdot L_{leg}^2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Equation (KTU Result)** > $$\boxed{f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{4 \cdot L \cdot m_{eff} \cdot L_{leg}^2}}}$$ > Units: **Hz** --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Locomotion Velocity from Asymmetric Gait > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context:** A walking microbot uses a four-bar hinged leg with **stride length λ** and **step frequency f**. Derive forward velocity. The robot moves through a periodic gait. In one cycle, the foot: 1. Lifts, swings forward by **λ/2** (return stroke, no ground contact). 2. Plants, drags backward by **λ/2** (power stroke, ground contact). The **effective forward displacement per cycle** is **λ/2** (the difference between forward swing and backward push), assuming equal magnitudes. **Velocity:** $$v = f \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Equation (KTU Result)** > $$\boxed{v = f \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}}$$ > Units: **m/s** **Step length λ** is related to hinge angle sweep: $$\lambda = 2 \cdot L_{leg} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\theta_{max}}{2}\right)$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Magnetic Actuation Torque on a Hinged Limb A permanent magnet (moment **m**) embedded in a hinged limb experiences torque in an external field **B**. $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$$ **Magnitude:** $$\tau = m \cdot B \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ where **α** is the angle between **m** and **B**. **Maximum torque** occurs when α = 90°: $$\tau_{max} = m \cdot B$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Equation** > $$\boxed{\tau = m \cdot B \cdot \sin(\alpha)}$$ **This torque must overcome hinge elastic restoring torque** for motion to occur: $$m \cdot B \cdot \sin(\alpha) \geq k_\theta \cdot \theta$$ --- ## 3.5 KTU Numerical-Style Worked Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)** **Question:** A compliant leaf hinge in a microbot has the following parameters: - Material: Polysilicon, **E = 160 GPa** - Dimensions: **L = 200 µm, b = 50 µm, t = 5 µm** - Effective leg inertia: **I_eff = 1.2 × 10⁻¹⁵ kg·m²** **Calculate: (a) Hinge stiffness k_θ, (b) Natural frequency f_n.** ### Solution **Part (a): Hinge Stiffness** Convert all to SI: - E = 160 × 10⁹ Pa - L = 200 × 10⁻⁶ m = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m - b = 50 × 10⁻⁶ m = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m - t = 5 × 10⁻⁶ m $$k_\theta = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L}$$ $$k_\theta = \frac{(160 \times 10^9)(5 \times 10^{-5})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})}$$ **Compute numerator step:** - t³ = (5 × 10⁻⁶)³ = 125 × 10⁻¹⁸ m³ - b · t³ = (5 × 10⁻⁵) × (125 × 10⁻¹⁸) = 625 × 10⁻²³ = 6.25 × 10⁻²¹ - E · b · t³ = (160 × 10⁹) × (6.25 × 10⁻²¹) = 1.0 × 10⁻⁹ **Denominator:** 12 × 2 × 10⁻⁴ = 24 × 10⁻⁴ = 2.4 × 10⁻³ $$k_\theta = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.17 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N·m/rad}$$ **Part (b): Natural Frequency** $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_\theta}{I_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.17 \times 10^{-7}}{1.2 \times 10^{-15}}}$$ $$= \sqrt{3.475 \times 10^{8}} = 1.864 \times 10^{4} \text{ rad/s}$$ $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1.864 \times 10^{4}}{6.2832} \approx 2967 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.97 \text{ kHz}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Always show **unit conversion** and **separate numerator/denominator computation**. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for unit consistency. # 🗺️ HINGED MICROBOTS — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hinged Microbot ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ENERGY[Energy Source Subsystem] E1[External Magnetic Field Coil] E2[Power Supply / Battery] E3[Chemical Fuel Tank] E1 --> CTRL E2 --> CTRL E3 --> CTRL end subgraph CONTROL[Control Subsystem] CTRL[Controller MCU or External Field Driver] SENSE[Feedback Sensor Temperature Position Force] CTRL <--> SENSE end subgraph ACTUATE[Actuation Subsystem] ACT[Magnetic Torquer / Piezo Stack / SMA Wire] end subgraph HINGE[Hinge Subsystem] H1[Compliant Flexure Hinge] H2[Rigid Pin Hinge] H3[Living Hinge] H4[Shape Memory Hinge] end subgraph LINK[Articulated Linkages] L1[Rigid Links] L2[Soft Elastomer Links] end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Locomotion Subsystem] OUT1[Walking Gait on Surface] OUT2[Swimming in Fluid] OUT3[Flapping Flight] OUT4[Grasping and Manipulation] end CTRL --> ACT ACT --> H1 ACT --> H2 ACT --> H3 ACT --> H4 H1 --> L1 H2 --> L1 H3 --> L2 H4 --> L1 L1 --> OUT1 L1 --> OUT2 L1 --> OUT3 L2 --> OUT1 L2 --> OUT4 style ENERGY fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520 style CONTROL fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#9370DB style ACTUATE fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#CD5C5C style HINGE fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style LINK fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22 style OUTPUT fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#FF8C00 ``` *Figure 4.1: Block-level functional architecture of a generic Hinged Microbot platform.* ## 4.2 Hinge Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Hinge Mechanisms in Microbots] --> B[Discrete Articulated Hinges] A --> C[Continuous Compliant Hinges] B --> B1[Pin Joint Hinge] B --> B2[Revolute MEMS Hinge] B --> B3[Ball and Socket Hinge] C --> C1[Leaf Flexure Hinge] C --> C2[Notched Hinge] C --> C3[Living Hinge] C --> C4[SMA Hinge] C1 --> C1A[Single Axis] C1 --> C1B[Multi Axis Crossed] C2 --> C2A[Corner Filleted] C2 --> C2B[Elliptical] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22 style B1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style B2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style B3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B style C2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B style C3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B style C4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B ``` *Figure 4.2: Hierarchical classification of hinge mechanisms used in microbots.* ## 4.3 Actuation Principle — Magnetic Hinge Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Coil Generates B] --> B[Rotating Magnetic Field B] B --> C[Magnetic Dipole m in Hinge Limb] C --> D[Torque tau = m x B] D --> E[Compliant Hinge Twists by theta] E --> F[Angular Motion of Leg or Wing] F --> G[Locomotion or Manipulation] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style D fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22 style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22 style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C ``` *Figure 4.3: Functional chain of magnetically-actuated hinged microbot motion.* ## 4.4 Locomotion Gait Cycle (Walking Hinged Microbot) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1: Foot Planted
Power Stroke] --> S2[Stage 2: Body Translates Forward] S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Foot Lifts
Return Stroke] S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Leg Swings Forward
Hinge Rotates Reversed] S4 --> S1 style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B style S2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4 style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style S4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22 ``` *Figure 4.4: Asymmetric gait cycle of a hinged walking microbot (one full locomotion period).* # 📝 HINGED MICROBOTS — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Hinged Microbot and State its Five Subsystems. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]** **Model Answer:** A **Hinged Microbot** is a sub-centimeter scale robotic device (characteristic length 1 µm – 10 mm) that employs one or more **articulated joints (hinges)** to transform an external energy input into mechanical motion for locomotion or manipulation. **The five subsystems are:** 1. **Body (Chassis)** — Structural frame. 2. **Hinge Array** — Articulated joints. 3. **Actuator** — Energy conversion unit. 4. **Transducer/Sensor** — Feedback mechanism. 5. **Surface Interface** — Foot, fin, wing. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate Between Compliant and Rigid Hinges in Microbots. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Compliant Hinge | Rigid Pin Hinge | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Elastic deformation of thin section | Physical pin–socket articulation | | **Material** | Polymers (PDMS, SU-8) | Silicon, metal alloys | | **Friction/Wear** | Zero (no sliding contact) | High (sliding surfaces) | | **Lifetime** | Indefinite | Limited (wear-out failure) | | **Fabrication** | Photolithography (single-step) | Multi-step MEMS assembly | | **Range of Motion** | Limited (~30°) | Large (~360°) | | **Application** | Flapping wings, soft grippers | Walking legs, manipulators | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory. ### ⭐ QUESTION A — Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper Pattern)]** **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a magnetically-actuated hinged microbot**. Discuss the role of the **external magnetic field** and the **embedded magnetic dipole** in generating hinge rotation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A hinged microbot has a polysilicon compliant hinge with dimensions **L = 300 µm, b = 60 µm, t = 4 µm**. The polysilicon Young's modulus is **E = 165 GPa**. Compute: - (i) Hinge rotational stiffness k_θ - (ii) Maximum allowable angular deflection for **M = 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m** applied moment - (iii) Elastic strain energy stored at this deflection **Model Solution:** **Part (a) Answer:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 diagram for block architecture.)* A magnetically-actuated hinged microbot consists of the following key elements: 1. **External Helmholtz Coil Pair** — generates uniform, rotating magnetic field **B** (typically 10–100 mT). 2. **Embedded Permanent Magnet (NdFeB)** — polarized with magnetic moment **m** (typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ A·m²). 3. **Compliant Hinge** — converts the magnetic torque into controlled angular rotation. 4. **Rigid Linkage** — transmits hinge motion to leg/wing tip. 5. **Foot or Wing Surface** — interacts with the environment to produce locomotion. **Operating Principle:** The external rotating field **B** exerts a torque on the embedded dipole **m** given by: $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{m} \times \vec{B} = m B \sin(\alpha)$$ This torque overcomes the **elastic restoring torque of the hinge**: $$k_\theta \cdot \theta = m B \sin(\alpha)$$ The hinge rotates by angle **θ** until equilibrium. By modulating **B(t)**, the field-programmed gait produces **walking, swimming, or rolling motion**. **Role of Hinge:** - Converts magnetic torque to angular displacement. - Stores elastic energy for recovery stroke. - Provides deterministic, repeatable motion. **Part (b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - E = 165 × 10⁹ Pa - L = 300 × 10⁻⁶ m = 3 × 10⁻⁴ m - b = 60 × 10⁻⁶ m = 6 × 10⁻⁵ m - t = 4 × 10⁻⁶ m - M = 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m **(i) Hinge Stiffness:** $$k_\theta = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot L}$$ Compute t³: $$t^3 = (4 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 64 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^3$$ Compute numerator: $$E \cdot b \cdot t^3 = (165 \times 10^9)(6 \times 10^{-5})(64 \times 10^{-18})$$ $$= 165 \times 6 \times 64 \times 10^{9-5-18} = 63360 \times 10^{-14}$$ $$= 6.336 \times 10^{-10} \text{ N·m}^2$$ Compute denominator: $$12 \cdot L = 12 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$k_\theta = \frac{6.336 \times 10^{-10}}{3.6 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.76 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N·m/rad}$$ **(ii) Maximum Angular Deflection:** $$\theta_{max} = \frac{M}{k_\theta} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.76 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.52 \text{ rad}$$ Wait — this exceeds physical limits. **Re-check:** For small deflection theory to hold, θ should be < 0.5 rad. The applied moment is too large, indicating the hinge will fail or saturate. **Corrected interpretation:** The hinge is designed for much smaller moments (micro-Newton range). For the given parameters, the moment must be limited to: $$M_{safe} = k_\theta \cdot \theta_{safe} = 1.76 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.5 = 8.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N·m}$$ **(iii) Strain Energy at θ = 0.5 rad (safe limit):** $$P_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} k_\theta \cdot \theta^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.76 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.25$$ $$P_{elastic} = 2.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ J} = 22 \text{ nJ}$$ --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2025 (Model Paper Pattern)]** **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **scaling laws and dimensionless numbers** governing the design of hinged microbots. Discuss why **Reynolds number Re < 1** is critical for swimming microbots and how it affects hinge gait design. **(b) [7 Marks]** A swimming hinged microbot has **four articulated fins**, each producing **thrust F_T = 5 µN** during the power stroke and **zero** during the recovery stroke. The fin frequency is **f = 8 Hz**, density of water **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**, viscosity **μ = 10⁻³ Pa·s**, body length **L = 3 mm**, and swimming speed **v = 1 cm/s**. Compute: - (i) Reynolds number Re - (ii) Average forward thrust force - (iii) Drag force at the given velocity (assuming Stokes regime, with body cross-section diameter d = 1 mm) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) Theory Answer:** **Scaling Laws in Microbots:** As characteristic length **L** decreases, two key effects emerge: 1. **Volume ∝ L³** scales faster than **surface area ∝ L²**. 2. Therefore, **surface forces (viscous, capillary, electrostatic) dominate volume forces (inertial, gravitational)**. **Key Dimensionless Numbers:** | Number | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Microbot Value | |---|---|---|---| | Reynolds | Re = ρvL/μ | Inertial vs. viscous force | **Re ≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻²** | | Capillary | Ca = μv/γ | Viscous vs. surface tension | **Ca ≈ 10⁻³ to 1** | | Froude | Fr = v²/(gL) | Inertial vs. gravity | **Fr << 1** | | Weber | We = ρv²L/γ | Inertial vs. surface tension | **We << 1** | **Why Re < 1 matters for swimming:** At **Re < 1**, the flow is **time-reversible** (scallop theorem by Purcell, 1977). This means: - A **symmetric reciprocal gait** (open–close) produces **zero net displacement**. - Therefore, swimmers must use **asymmetric, non-reciprocal gaits** (e.g., rotating corkscrew flagella, two-hinge flapping). - The hinge **must be designed for asymmetric motion**: e.g., fast power stroke, slow recovery stroke. **Implications for hinge design:** - Power stroke duration ≠ Recovery stroke duration. - Hinge angular velocity profile must be tailored. - Multiple hinges are needed (one-hinge swimmers are ineffective at low Re). **Part (b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** - F_T (per fin) = 5 × 10⁻⁶ N, 4 fins → total peak thrust = 20 µN - f = 8 Hz, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 10⁻³ Pa·s - L = 3 × 10⁻³ m, v = 10⁻² m/s - d = 1 × 10⁻³ m **(i) Reynolds Number:** $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-2} \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}$$ $$Re = \frac{1000 \times 3 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-3}} = 30$$ *(This is in the **transitional regime** — not fully Stokes, but viscous effects still significant.)* **(ii) Average Forward Thrust:** Power stroke duration per cycle: 50% of T → T/2, where T = 1/f = 0.125 s. Power stroke time = 0.0625 s. Recovery stroke time = 0.0625 s (zero thrust). Time-averaged thrust over one cycle: $$F_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T F(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \cdot F_{total} \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$ $$F_{avg} = \frac{F_{total}}{2} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N} = 10 \, \mu N$$ **(iii) Stokes Drag Force:** At Re = 30, Stokes approximation is **borderline valid**. For a sphere of diameter d: $$F_{drag} = 6 \pi \mu r v = 6 \pi \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 10^{-2}$$ $$= 6 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}$$ $$= 6 \pi \times 0.5 \times 10^{-8} = 3 \pi \times 10^{-8} \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N}$$ $$F_{drag} \approx 0.094 \, \mu N$$ **Comparison:** F_avg = 10 µN >> F_drag = 0.094 µN → net forward motion is ensured with large force margin. The microbot is **over-powered** at this velocity; a 1:100 thrust-to-drag ratio is excellent for stability. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > > **1. Confusing 'hinge' with 'joint' type:** Examiners strictly differentiate between **discrete pin hinge** and **compliant flexure hinge**. Using the words interchangeably costs 1 mark. > > **2. Skipping unit conversion in numericals:** A typical 7-mark problem has **1 mark reserved for unit consistency**. Always show SI conversion of µm, mm, GPa to base units. > > **3. Forgetting the scallop theorem:** In swimming microbot questions, students often propose a single-hinge oscillating fin. The examiner expects you to **explicitly state** that this fails at Re < 1 (Purcell's scallop theorem) and propose an asymmetric non-reciprocal gait. > > **4. Wrong sign convention for torque:** Magnetic torque **τ = mB sin(α)** has **α = 0 → no torque, α = 90° → maximum**. Many students invert this. > > **5. Using the wrong formula for drag:** Stokes drag is for **Re < 1 only**. Using it for Re = 30 is technically incorrect. Either justify why it's a reasonable approximation OR use the drag coefficient chart. > > **6. Missing the diagram in Part-B (a):** Any 7-mark "explain" question **must** include a labelled diagram. Skipping it loses 2 marks. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Hinged Microbots** ### ✅ Core Concepts - [x] Hinged microbots = sub-cm robots using articulated joints for motion. - [x] Five subsystems: Body, Hinge, Actuator, Sensor, Surface Interface. - [x] Hinges are **discretizers of motion** — they convert continuous deformation into predictable angular motion. ### ✅ Hinge Types (Memorize) - [x] **Rigid Pin Hinge** — physical articulation. - [x] **Compliant Flexure Hinge** — elastic deformation, zero wear. - [x] **Living Hinge** — thinned polymer web. - [x] **SMA Hinge** — shape-memory alloy (Nitinol). ### ✅ Actuation Methods - [x] **Magnetic**: τ = mB sin(α) — wireless, fast. - [x] **Piezoelectric**: S = dE — high precision. - [x] **Thermal (SMA)**: phase transition-based. - [x] **Chemical**: catalytic propulsion (Pt + H₂O₂). ### ✅ Critical Formulas (Top 5) - [x] Hinge stiffness: **k_θ = E·b·t³ / (12·L)** - [x] Magnetic torque: **τ = mB sin(α)** - [x] Reynolds number: **Re = ρvL/μ** - [x] Stokes drag: **F_drag = 6πμrv** - [x] Robot velocity: **v = f · (λ/2)** ### ✅ Physical Phenomena - [x] **Scallop Theorem (Purcell, 1977)**: Re < 1 demands non-reciprocal gaits. - [x] Surface forces dominate at microscale (volume ∝ L³ vs. surface ∝ L²). - [x] Elastic energy storage in hinges: **P = ½ k_θ θ²**. ### ✅ Applications (Always mention at least one in long answers) - [x] Minimally invasive surgery. - [x] Targeted drug delivery. - [x] Pipeline inspection. - [x] Precision agriculture. - [x] Environmental monitoring. ### ✅ Numerical Problem Template - [x] Always state given data. - [x] Convert all units to SI. - [x] Identify which formula to use. - [x] Show step-by-step substitution. - [x] Mention physical interpretation of the answer. ### ✅ Key Numbers to Memorize - [x] **E (Silicon)** ≈ 130–190 GPa - [x] **E (PDMS)** ≈ 0.5–3 MPa - [x] **E (Nitinol)** ≈ 30–80 GPa (Austenite) - [x] **Water viscosity** μ ≈ 10⁻³ Pa·s - [x] **Air viscosity** μ ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s - [x] **Curie temperature of typical piezo** ≈ 150–350°C ### ✅ High-Yield Keywords for Exam - [x] "Compliant mechanism" (always use this term, not "flexible joint") - [x] "Non-reciprocal gait" (for low-Re swimmers) - [x] "Hinge discretization" (the core design principle) - [x] "Field-programmed motion" (for magnetic actuation) - [x] "Power stroke vs. recovery stroke" (asymmetric gait) - [x] "Viscous-dominated regime" (Re < 1)

topics.title: Legged Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Legged Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **legged robot** is a class of mobile robot that achieves locomotion by means of a discrete number of articulated mechanical limbs (legs), each terminating in a **foot** (also called an *effector tip* or *contact point*). Unlike wheeled or tracked platforms, legged robots establish a series of *intermittent*, *discrete* ground-contact points. The robot is supported at any instant by a finite subset of feet in **stance phase**, while the remaining legs are lifted and moved forward in **swing phase**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A legged robot is a multi-body kinematic chain that uses $n \ge 1$ articulated limbs, each with a finite number of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, to generate propulsion through a coordinated, periodic sequence of ground contacts known as a **gait**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to cross a rocky stream. If you were on **roller skates** (wheeled), the moment you hit a pebble larger than your wheel radius, you would stumble and stop — wheels *demand* a continuous, prepared surface. Now imagine crossing the same stream on **stilts** versus on your **own two feet**. Your two feet *sample* the ground at discrete points; if one foot lands on a wobbly rock, the other three (well, your other foot plus your brain's reflexes) keep you balanced.

> A legged robot operates on exactly this principle. Each leg is essentially a **discrete, repositionable, articulated pillar**. By lifting one leg at a time and placing it on a *chosen* spot, the robot can climb stairs, traverse rubble, and step over obstacles that would be impassable to a wheel of comparable size.

- A **wheel** provides continuous, low-energy rolling but is **terrain-blind**.
- A **leg** is energy-expensive but **terrain-adaptive** — it picks where it wants to push.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Legged Robotics (Bold Highlights)

| Metric | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Legs ($n$)** | **1, 2, 4, 6, 8** | Determines stability and gait complexity. |
| **DOF per Leg** | **2 to 6** | More DOF $\Rightarrow$ greater terrain adaptability. |
| **Stride Length ($S$)** | **0.1 m to 1.0 m** | Distance traveled per complete gait cycle. |
| **Step Height ($h_s$)** | **0.05 m to 0.3 m** | Maximum obstacle that can be cleared. |
| **Static Stability Margin ($SSM$)** | **0 (unstable) to 0.5 m (very stable)** | Distance from CoG projection to support polygon edge. |
| **Froude Number ($Fr$)** | **0.01 to 10** | Dimensionless gait-speed indicator. |
| **Body–Leg Mass Ratio** | **5:1 to 20:1** | Critical for leg-swing energy efficiency. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module-2 expects students to clearly distinguish between **statically stable** walking (used by 4, 6, 8-legged robots) and **dynamically stable** walking (used by bipeds and hoppers).

### 1.4 Why Use Legs? (The Engineering Motivation)

- **Discrete Footing:** Legs can place force vectors on isolated ground patches — essential for *gapped*, *stepped*, or *uneven* terrain.
- **Active Suspension:** The body can be kept level (and even stationary) while the legs absorb terrain bumps — a *wheeled* robot simply rides up and down.
- **Omnidirectional Capability:** With appropriate leg kinematics, a legged robot can strafe sideways, turn in place, and crab-walk.
- **Damage Tolerance:** Loss of one leg out of six (hexapod) is often survivable; loss of one wheel out of four on a car is catastrophic.
- **Obstacle Negotiation:** Step height $h_s$ can be comparable to the leg length, whereas a wheel's effective obstacle clearance is limited to roughly its radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** All of the above comes at a **mechanical complexity**, **control complexity**, and **energy cost** that is typically 10× to 100× higher than a wheeled counterpart. The choice of a legged platform is therefore always *terrain-driven*.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Legged Robots by Number of Legs

The most fundamental classification axis in legged robotics is the **number of legs $n$**. Each value of $n$ dictates the *stability regime*, the *gait vocabulary*, and the *mechanical design philosophy*.

#### 2.1.1 One-Legged Robots (Hoppers / Mono-pods)

- **Examples:** Raibert's 3D Hopper (MIT, 1983), Salto-1P (UC Berkeley).
- **Mechanism:** A single springy, prismatic/inertial leg.
- **Stability Regime:** **Inherently dynamically stable only** — the robot *must* be in continuous hopping motion; it cannot stand still.
- **Why it works:** The leg acts as a **spring-loaded inverted pendulum (SLIP)**, and energy stored in the spring during stance is returned during the aerial phase.
- **Use Case:** Research platforms for biomechanics and running dynamics.

#### 2.1.2 Two-Legged Robots (Bipeds / Humanoids)

- **Examples:** ASIMO (Honda), Atlas (Boston Dynamics), Nao (SoftBank), Digit (Agility Robotics), HRP series (AIST).
- **Stability Regime:** **Dynamic stability required.** A biped with $n=2$ feet can never be statically stable (a 2-point support polygon has zero area).
- **Critical Insight:** Bipedal walking is essentially *controlled falling* — the robot tips forward onto the swing foot before the support polygon collapses.
- **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP):** The standard KTU/Kane-Donelan stability criterion; the ZMP must remain inside the support polygon at all times.

#### 2.1.3 Four-Legged Robots (Quadrupeds)

- **Examples:** BigDog, Spot, Spot Mini (Boston Dynamics), ANYmal (ETH Zürich), Laikago (Unitree), MIT Mini-Cheetah.
- **Stability Regime:** **Statically stable** in many configurations (3 feet always on ground) and **dynamically stable** during fast gaits (trotting, galloping, bounding).
- **Gaits:** Walk, Amble, Trot, Pace, Bound, Gallop.
- **Why 4 is the Sweet Spot:** Minimum number of legs for *quasi-static* locomotion with reasonable leg density; widely adopted in industry.

#### 2.1.4 Six-Legged Robots (Hexapods)

- **Examples:** LAURON (FZI Karlsruhe), Messor, WeB-1, RHex (with only 1 DOF per leg but very robust).
- **Stability Regime:** **Inherently statically stable** — at least 3 feet always on the ground in any tripod/wave gait.
- **Gaits:** Tripod (alternating triplets), Wave (one leg at a time), Ripple.
- **Why 6 is the "Gold Standard" for Rough Terrain:** A hexapod is *never* required to balance dynamically for *basic* walking; this is why insects dominate Earth's terrain.

#### 2.1.5 Eight-Legged Robots (Octopods)

- **Examples:** Most experimental spider-inspired robots.
- **Stability Regime:** Massively over-determined statically; rarely built because 6 is already mechanically sufficient and additional legs add weight and coordination cost.

### 2.2 Number of Legs vs. Stability & Control Complexity

| $n$ (Legs) | Min. Legs in Stance for Stability | Statically Stable? | Gait Examples | Typical DOF per Leg |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | 1 | **No** (always dynamic) | Continuous hop | 2–3 |
| **2** | 1 | **No** (always dynamic) | Walk, Run | 5–6 |
| **3** | 2 | Borderline (line support) | Static not robust | 3–4 |
| **4** | 3 | **Yes** (triangular support) | Walk, Trot, Pace, Bound | 3 |
| **6** | 3 | **Yes** (always) | Tripod, Wave, Ripple | 3 |
| **8** | 3 | **Yes** (redundant) | Wave | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Magic Number is 3.** A polygon formed by 3 points has a non-zero area; this is why **statically stable walking requires $n \ge 3$ legs with at least 3 feet on the ground at all times.**

### 2.3 Joint Types Used in Legged Robot Legs

| Joint Type | Symbol | Typical DOF Contribution | Common Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | $\theta$ | 1 DOF | Knee, ankle, hip (most common) |
| **Prismatic (P)** | $d$ | 1 DOF | Linear actuator leg (Hoppers) |
| **Spherical (S)** | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | 3 DOF | Hip (compact 3-axis joint) |
| **Universal (U)** | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ | 2 DOF | Some hip assemblies |
| **Cylindrical (C)** | $d, \theta$ | 2 DOF | Rare in legged robots |

A typical 3-DOF insect-style leg is the **RRR configuration** (3 revolute joints: hip yaw, hip pitch, knee pitch).

### 2.4 Gait Fundamentals

A **gait** is a *periodic*, *coordinated* sequence of leg-lift and leg-place events. Mathematically, a gait is described by a **gait diagram** — a 2D plot of time vs. leg index, with each leg's *swing phase* shaded.

- **Duty Factor $\beta$:** The fraction of the gait cycle that a foot is in stance. $0 < \beta \le 1$.
  - $\beta = 0.5$: equal stance and swing (typical of a fast trot).
  - $\beta > 0.5$: stance dominates (typical of slow, stable walking).
  - $\beta = 1.0$: all feet always on the ground (creep / static pose).
- **Stride $S$:** The distance the body advances per gait cycle.
- **Step Length $L$:** $S = 2L$ for symmetric gaits.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU Module-2 derivations and numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_s = n - k$ | Number of legs in stance = total legs $n$ minus legs in swing $k$. |
| 2 | $n_s \ge 3$ | **Static stability condition** — at least 3 feet must remain on ground. |
| 3 | $SSM = \min_i \, d_i$ | Static Stability Margin = minimum distance from CoG projection to support-polygon edge. |
| 4 | $S = 2 L$ | Stride = 2 × step length (symmetric gait). |
| 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{t_{stance}}{t_{cycle}}$ | Duty factor. |
| 6 | $Fr = \dfrac{v^2}{g \, \ell}$ | Froude number — gait similarity across sizes. |
| 7 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{v}{L}$ | Step frequency. |
| 8 | $P_{poly} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$ | Area of support polygon (shoelace formula). |
| 9 | $DOF_{leg} = 3 \, n - 6$ (ground mobility) | Kutzbach criterion for planar ground mechanism. |
| 10 | $M_{stability} = \dfrac{A_{support}}{A_{CoG footprint}}$ | Geometric stability index. |

**Note on the `|...|` in formula 8:** This is the only place an absolute value appears. The shoelace area formula must be implemented via the **signed area** approach; the absolute value is taken *after* summation. When this appears in a KTU exam, you may write $\left\vert \sum \ldots \right\vert$ inline; the **vertical pipe character `\|` must never appear inside any table row** — instead, in tables, write `\lvert \ldots \rvert`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Quadrupeds like Spot penetrate collapsed buildings where wheeled UGVs cannot roll over rubble.
- **Planetary Exploration:** NASA's Athlete (All-Terrain Hex-Legged Extra-Terrestrial Explorer) is a 6-legged concept for lunar crater walls.
- **Industrial Inspection:** ANYmal performs autonomous inspection of oil & gas and power-plant sites.
- **Agriculture & Forestry:** Legged platforms can navigate soft soil, fallen logs, and uneven crop rows.
- **Defense & Logistics:** BigDog-class robots carry payloads over broken terrain for soldiers.
- **Humanitarian Demining:** Hexapods step carefully between anti-personnel mines.

> [!NOTE]
> The unifying engineering theme: **legs trade energy efficiency for terrain versatility.** A 4-legged robot consumes roughly 10–20× more energy per unit distance than a 4-wheeled robot on flat ground, but it can go where no wheel can.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Number of Legs for Static Stability

> **Statement:** Prove that a legged robot with $n$ legs can be statically stable *during walking* if and only if $n \ge 4$ and at least 3 legs are always in stance.

**Step 1 — Setting up the count.** Let:
- $n$ = total number of legs.
- $k$ = number of legs simultaneously in swing (lifted) phase.
- $n_s = n - k$ = number of legs remaining in stance.

**Step 2 — Static-stability requirement.** For the robot's body to be supported by a polygon with non-zero area, we require the stance feet to form a polygon of at least 3 non-collinear points:

$$n_s \ge 3$$

**Step 3 — Walking requirement.** To actually *move*, at least one leg must be lifted at a time (otherwise the robot is just standing). Thus:

$$k \ge 1$$

**Step 4 — Combining the two constraints.** Substituting $k \ge 1$ into $n_s = n - k \ge 3$:

$$n - 1 \ge n_s \ge 3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n \ge 4$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

- $n = 1$: $n_s = 0$ in swing $\Rightarrow$ the robot is in the air $\Rightarrow$ **must hop dynamically**.
- $n = 2$: $n_s = 1$ in swing $\Rightarrow$ a single contact point cannot form a polygon $\Rightarrow$ **must walk dynamically** (ZMP-controlled biped).
- $n = 3$: $n_s = 2$ in swing $\Rightarrow$ a line segment has zero polygon area $\Rightarrow$ **cannot walk with full static stability** in the strict sense.
- $n = 4, 5, 6, 8$: $n_s = 3$ or more in swing $\Rightarrow$ **statically stable walking possible**.

> **Conclusion:** The minimum number of legs for statically stable *walking* is **4**, with **3 legs always in stance**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Area of the Support Polygon (Shoelace Formula)

> **Statement:** For a legged robot with $m$ feet in stance at ground-plane coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$, derive the area of the support polygon $P$.

**Step 1 — Polygon description.** Let the stance feet be ordered cyclically around the convex hull, indexed $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$ with $(x_{m+1}, y_{m+1}) \equiv (x_1, y_1)$.

**Step 2 — Decomposition into triangles.** Pick a reference point (the polygon centroid, or simply the origin). The polygon area equals the sum of signed triangle areas:

$$A = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{2} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)$$

**Step 3 — The cross-product identity.** Each term $\frac{1}{2}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)$ is the signed area of the triangle formed by the origin and the edge from $(x_i, y_i)$ to $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$.

**Step 4 — Taking the absolute value for the unsigned area:**

$$A_{poly} = \frac{1}{2} \left\lvert \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\rvert$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** Consider a 3-legged tripod with feet at $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(0.5, \sqrt{3}/2)$ m.

Applying the shoelace formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{poly} &= \frac{1}{2} \left\lvert x_1 y_2 - x_2 y_1 + x_2 y_3 - x_3 y_2 + x_3 y_1 - x_1 y_3 \right\rvert \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \left\lvert (0)(0) - (1)(0) + (1)(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) - (0.5)(0) + (0.5)(0) - (0)(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) \right\rvert \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \left\lvert 0 + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + 0 \right\rvert \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} \;\text{m}^2 \;\approx\; 0.433 \;\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the well-known area of an **equilateral triangle** of side **1 m**, confirming the formula.

> **Conclusion:** The signed-area approach reduces the support-polygon area calculation to a single sum of $m$ cross-product terms, executable in $O(m)$ time on a real-time controller. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Stability Margin (SSM) for a Tripod-Stance Hexapod

> **Statement:** A hexapod is in tripod stance with feet $A=(0,0)$, $B=(0.6, 0)$, $C=(0.3, 0.52)$ forming the support triangle. The robot's Center of Gravity (CoG) projects onto the ground at $G=(0.3, 0.18)$. Compute the **Static Stability Margin**.

**Step 1 — Identify the three edges of the support triangle.** Edges: $AB$, $BC$, $CA$.

**Step 2 — Distance from a point to a line.** The perpendicular distance from point $(x_0, y_0)$ to the line through $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is:

$$d = \frac{\left\lvert (y_2 - y_1) x_0 - (x_2 - x_1) y_0 + x_2 y_1 - y_2 x_1 \right\rvert}{\sqrt{(y_2 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - x_1)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance to edge $AB$** (line $y=0$):

$$d_{AB} = \frac{\left\lvert 0 \cdot x_0 - 1 \cdot y_0 + 0 - 0 \right\rvert}{\sqrt{0^2 + 1^2}} = \left\lvert y_0 \right\rvert = 0.18 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute distance to edge $AC$** (line from $(0,0)$ to $(0.3, 0.52)$):

The line equation is $0.52 x - 0.3 y = 0$, i.e. $52 x - 30 y = 0$.

$$d_{AC} = \frac{\left\lvert 52 (0.3) - 30 (0.18) \right\rvert}{\sqrt{52^2 + 30^2}} = \frac{\left\lvert 15.6 - 5.4 \right\rvert}{\sqrt{2704 + 900}} = \frac{10.2}{\sqrt{3604}} = \frac{10.2}{60.03} \approx 0.170 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute distance to edge $BC$** (line from $(0.6, 0)$ to $(0.3, 0.52)$):

The line equation is $0.52(x - 0.6) - (-0.3)(y - 0) = 0$, i.e. $52 x + 30 y - 31.2 = 0$.

$$d_{BC} = \frac{\left\lvert 52 (0.3) + 30 (0.18) - 31.2 \right\rvert}{\sqrt{52^2 + 30^2}} = \frac{\left\lvert 15.6 + 5.4 - 31.2 \right\rvert}{60.03} = \frac{\left\lvert -10.2 \right\rvert}{60.03} \approx 0.170 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Static Stability Margin.** The SSM is the *minimum* of these three distances:

$$SSM = \min \{ d_{AB}, d_{AC}, d_{BC} \} = \min \{ 0.180, 0.170, 0.170 \} = 0.170 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.** A positive SSM of **0.170 m** means the CoG projection is at least 17 cm away from tipping over any edge of the support triangle. A larger SSM means a more stable robot; a negative or zero SSM means the robot will tip. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Froude Number for Gait Scaling

> **Statement:** A quadruped robot has leg length $\ell = 0.5$ m and walks at $v = 1.0$ m/s on Earth ($g = 9.81$ m/s²). Compute the Froude number and predict the gait transition.

**Step 1 — Definition.** The Froude number relates inertial to gravitational forces in a gait:

$$Fr = \frac{v^2}{g \, \ell}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$Fr = \frac{(1.0)^2}{9.81 \times 0.5} = \frac{1.0}{4.905} \approx 0.204$$

**Step 3 — Gait prediction.** Empirically, quadrupeds transition from *walk* to *trot* near $Fr \approx 0.5$ and from *trot* to *gallop* near $Fr \approx 2.5$ to $3.0$. Since $0.204 < 0.5$, the robot is in the **walk** regime. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Legged Platform

| Application | Recommended $n$ | Stance Required | Key Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor service robot | 2 (biped) | 1 | Human-like environment (stairs, doors) |
| Rough outdoor SAR | 4 or 6 | 3 | Static stability + obstacle stepping |
| Pipe / confined inspection | 4 (small) | 3–4 | Compactness + redundancy |
| Research on running dynamics | 1 (hopper) | 1 | Dynamic stability studies |
| Lunar crater exploration | 6 | 3 | Extreme terrain, no recovery if tipped |
| Last-mile delivery | 4 | 3 | Payload + legged mobility |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Legged Robot Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High-Level Planner] --> B[Gait Scheduler]
    B --> C[Foot Trajectory Generator]
    C --> D[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    D --> E[Joint Space Controller]
    E --> F[Servo Drivers]
    F --> G[Leg Actuators]
    G --> H[Foot Ground Contact]
    H --> I[Force Torque Sensors]
    I --> J[State Estimator]
    J --> K[Balance and ZMP Controller]
    K --> B
    A --> K
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Planning flows top-down; sensing flows bottom-up. The gait scheduler receives corrections from the balance controller (ZMP/SSM feedback), forming a closed-loop hierarchy. This is the standard architecture used in ASIMO, Atlas, and ANYmal.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Legged Robots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Hopper n=1] --> M0[Dynamic SLIP Model]
    L1[Biped n=2] --> M1[ZMP and Capture Point]
    L2[Quadruped n=4] --> M2{Trot or Walk}
    L3[Hexapod n=6] --> M3[Tripod or Wave Gait]
    L4[Octopod n=8] --> M4[Highly Stable Redundant]

    M0 --> N[Stability Regime]
    M1 --> N
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
    M4 --> N
    N --> O[Dynamic Stability Only]
    N --> P[Quasi Static Stable]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Leg Anatomy (3-DOF Insect-Style Leg)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Body Attachment Point] --> B[Hip Yaw Joint theta1]
    B --> C[Hip Pitch Joint theta2]
    C --> D[Knee Pitch Joint theta3]
    D --> E[Foot Contact Point]
```

> **Functional interpretation:** The **hip-yaw** swings the leg forward/backward in the body's horizontal plane (swing vs. stance). The **hip-pitch** raises/lowers the leg vertically. The **knee** extends/reaches the foot to a target ground point. This is the canonical RR leg used in 90% of research hexapods and quadrupeds.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Gait Cycle State Machine (One Leg)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stance
    Stance --> LiftOff : Trigger at duty factor
    LiftOff --> Swing : Leg clears ground
    Swing --> TouchDown : Foot reaches next foothold
    TouchDown --> Stance : Weight transferred
    Stance --> [*]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single leg cycles through *Stance* (foot on ground, supports body) → *Lift-off* (initiates swing) → *Swing* (foot in air, repositioned) → *Touch-down* (foot contacts new ground point) → *Stance* (cycle repeats). The duty factor $\beta$ determines the fraction of the cycle spent in the Stance state.
