```mermaid
    ReceivingMission --> TravellingToLoad: Global Path Computed
    TravellingToLoad --> PositioningAtDrawpoint: LiDAR Confirms Stope Marker
    PositioningAtDrawpoint --> Loading: Bucket Auto Fill Cycle
    Loading --> TravellingToDump: Load Sensor Verifies Mass
    TravellingToDump --> PositioningAtCrusher: Crusher Bay Confirmed
    PositioningAtCrusher --> Dumping: Bucket Lift and Shake
    Dumping --> TravellingToLoad: Return Trip Begins
    TravellingToLoad --> Standby: Mission Complete
    Standby --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on State Machines:** A well-designed underground robot must have **fail-safe states** (e.g., `FaultLockout`) that can be triggered by gas alarms, E-stop, communication loss, or battery critical SoC. Examiners frequently test this in 14-mark questions.

---

### 4.3 Communication & Navigation Infrastructure in Underground Mines

Since **GPS signals cannot penetrate rock**, mines deploy dedicated **positioning infrastructure**. The diagram below shows the typical layered architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SURFACE
        S1[Control Room Operator]
        S2[Dispatcher Software]
        S3[WiFi or LTE Backhaul]
    end
    subgraph MINE_INFRASTRUCTURE
        M1[Leaky Feeder Cable Along Drift]
        M2[UWB Anchors on Roof at 50 m Spacing]
        M3[RFID Tags at Drawpoints and Crossings]
        M4[Traffic Light Network at Junctions]
    end
    subgraph ROBOT_ONBOARD
        R1[UWB Radio Module]
        R2[WiFi Client Radio]
        R3[RFID Reader on Bucket]
    end
    S1 <--> S2
    S2 <--> S3
    S3 <--> M1
    M1 <--> R2
    M2 <--> R1
    M3 <--> R3
    M4 <--> S2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] (3 Marks)**
Define the term **Mining Robot**. List **any four** distinct mining operations where robots are deployed.

**Model Answer:**
A **Mining Robot** is an autonomous or teleoperated robotic system designed to perform drilling, loading, hauling, mapping, or inspection tasks in surface or underground mines, replacing humans in hazardous, repetitive, or high-precision operations.

Four mining operations deploying robots:
1. **Autonomous drilling** of long-hole blast patterns.
2. **LHD (Load-Haul-Dump)** autonomous loading and tramming.
3. **Autonomous rail haulage** of ore to the crusher.
4. **UGV-based inspection** of stopes, shafts, and old workings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] (3 Marks)**
Why is **GPS-based navigation unsuitable** for underground mining robots? What alternative positioning techniques are used?

**Model Answer:**
GPS signals operate in the **L-band (1.1–1.6 GHz)** and require line-of-sight to satellites. They cannot penetrate the **rock overburden** (attenuation > 40 dB within a few metres), so they are **completely unavailable** underground.

Alternative positioning techniques used:
1. **2D/3D LiDAR SLAM** against tunnel walls.
2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** with periodic correction.
3. **Ultra-Wideband (UWB) beacons** mounted on the drift roof.
4. **Wheel odometry** fused with the above via an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style] (14 Marks)**

### **Question A — Underground Transport Robots**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture and operation of an autonomous LHD (Load-Haul-Dump) machine** used in underground mines. Describe its **localization, path planning, and safety systems** with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mining company plans to deploy autonomous trucks to transport **iron ore (density 2.8 t/m³)** from a drawpoint to a crusher located **800 m away**. The truck has a **rated payload of 30 tonnes** and a **tray volume of 11 m³**. The average speeds are **loaded = 10 km/h** and **empty = 15 km/h**. Load time = 60 s, dump time = 25 s, queue time = 45 s, and the target production is **400 t/h**. Compute:
   - (i) The **cycle time**.
   - (ii) The **production rate per truck** in t/h.
   - (iii) The **minimum number of trucks** required to meet the target, assuming 85% availability. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q3(a):**

**Architecture of an Autonomous LHD:**

An autonomous LHD is a robotic wheel-loader that performs the three functions of **L**oading ore from a drawpoint, **H**auling it to a dump point, and **D**umping it. The architecture consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Onboard Mission Controller:** An industrial PLC + GPU computer running the dispatch logic and the state machine (load → haul → dump → return).

2. **Localization Stack (Sensor Fusion):**
   - **2D/3D LiDAR** mounted on the cabin roof provides 360° scan of the drift walls → used for **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)**.
   - **IMU (9-DoF)** provides angular rate and linear acceleration for short-term pose updates between LiDAR scans.
   - **Wheel encoders** provide ground-truth velocity.
   - All three are fused in an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to produce a pose estimate $(x, y, \theta)$ in the mine coordinate frame.

3. **Path Planning Stack:**
   - **Global Planner:** Uses a pre-built **2.5D OctoMap** of the mine and an **A*** algorithm to compute a geometric path from current pose to goal.
   - **Local Planner:** A **Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)** or **Timed Elastic Band (TEB)** planner that re-plans every 100 ms to avoid dynamic obstacles (e.g., workers, other LHDs).

4. **Motion Controller:** A **PID / Model Predictive Controller (MPC)** commands the **Ackermann steering** and **wheel torque** to track the planned trajectory at the prescribed speed.

5. **Safety Systems:**
   - **Front and rear safety-rated LiDAR scanners** (compliant with **ISO 13849 PL d**) create protective fields; any intrusion triggers **category 1 emergency stop**.
   - **Methane (CH₄), CO, and NO₂ gas sensors** continuously monitor atmosphere.
   - **Thermal IR cameras** detect pedestrians even in complete darkness.
   - **Manual E-stop** buttons on the chassis and a wireless pendant carried by a supervisor.

6. **Communication:** The LHD communicates with the surface **Fleet Management System (FMS)** over **leaky feeder cable** (a coaxial cable that radiates VHF/UHF along its length, effectively turning the entire drift into a waveguide). Real-time telemetry includes pose, payload mass, battery SoC, and alarm status.

7. **Power:** Modern autonomous LHDs are **battery-electric** (e.g., Sandvik LH514 BE, Caterpillar R1700 XE) using **700 V LiFePO4 packs**, eliminating diesel emissions and reducing mine ventilation cost.

*(A block diagram similar to Section 4.1 must be drawn for full marks.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q3(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho_{ore} = 2.8$ t/m³
- $d = 800$ m one-way
- $V_{tray} = 11$ m³
- $v_{loaded} = 10$ km/h = 2.778 m/s
- $v_{empty} = 15$ km/h = 4.167 m/s
- $T_{load} = 60$ s, $T_{dump} = 25$ s, $T_{queue} = 45$ s
- Target $Q_{target} = 400$ t/h
- $\eta_{avail} = 0.85$

**(i) Cycle Time:**

$$
T_{haul} = \frac{800}{2.778} = 288 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{return} = \frac{800}{4.167} = 192 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_c = 60 + 288 + 25 + 192 + 45 = 610 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Production Rate per truck:**

$$
m_{payload} = V_{tray} \cdot \rho_{ore} = 11 \times 2.8 = 30.8 \text{ t}
$$

This **slightly exceeds** the rated 30 t — engineers will likely limit to 30 t to avoid overloading. Use $m = 30$ t for safe design.

$$
P = \frac{30 \times 3600}{610} = 177.05 \text{ t/h (gross)}
$$

$$
P_{effective} = 177.05 \times 0.85 = 150.5 \text{ t/h per truck}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Number of Trucks:**

$$
N_{min} = \frac{Q_{target}}{P_{effective}} = \frac{400}{150.5} = 2.66
$$

Rounding up (trucks must be an integer), **$N_{min} = 3$ trucks**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion trap:** Speed in km/h must be converted to m/s **before** dividing distance (in m) — many students forget the factor $1000/3600 = 0.2778$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Rounding rule:** Fleet size is always rounded **UP**, not to the nearest integer. A fractional truck cannot exist; under-sizing causes the target to be missed.
> 3. **Overloading check:** If computed $m_{payload}$ > rated payload, KTU expects the student to clamp at the rated value and comment on it (scoop design constraint).

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice for Q3 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **scope and motivation for adopting robotics in underground mining**. Compare **manual haulage** with **autonomous electric haulage** across at least **six technical parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat **state machine diagram**, describe the **complete mission lifecycle of an autonomous underground haul truck**, from system boot-up to mission completion. Explain the **fail-safe states** and **trigger conditions** for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution Highlights for Q3(B)(a):**

**Scope and Motivation:**
- Globally, mining employs ~ **11 million** workers, with fatality rates ~ **5–10× higher** than manufacturing.
- Robotic systems address the **3D jobs** (Dull, Dirty, Dangerous).
- Cost of underground ventilation is **$0.5–1.5 per m³/s per metre** of depth — eliminating diesel vehicles saves **30–40%** of mine ventilation cost.
- Deeper orebodies (now often > 2 km) make manual access physically and economically prohibitive.

**Comparison Table** (students should reproduce in exam):

| Parameter | Manual Diesel LHD | Autonomous Electric LHD |
|---|---|---|
| Operator location | In cabin (unsafe) | Remote / surface (safe) |
| Operating hours | 2–3 shifts × 7 h = 18 h | 24 × 7 = 168 h potential |
| Utilisation | ~ 65% | ~ 85% |
| Fuel/energy | Diesel 30–40 L/h | Electricity 200–300 kWh/h |
| Ventilation cost | High | Negligible |
| Localization | Driver skill | ± 5 cm LiDAR + IMU |
| Maintenance | Mechanical + emissions | Mostly mechanical |
| Noise | > 95 dB in cabin | < 75 dB |
| Heat output | High (engine + hydraulics) | Low (efficient electric) |
| CO₂ per ton-km | ~ 0.3 kg | ~ 0.05 kg (with clean grid) |

#### **Model Solution Highlights for Q3(B)(b):**

The student must draw the **state machine** similar to Section 4.2. Key **trigger conditions** to mention:

| State | Entry Trigger | Exit Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| `BootAndSelfTest` | Power ON | All sensors report OK |
| `Standby` | Mission queue empty | FMS allocates task |
| `TravellingToLoad` | Path computed | Drawpoint marker detected |
| `Loading` | At drawpoint | Load sensor confirms target mass ± 5% |
| `TravellingToDump` | Loaded | Crusher bay confirmed |
| `Dumping` | At crusher | Tray empty confirmed via weight sensor |
| `FaultLockout` | CH₄ > 1%, E-stop, comm loss | Manual reset |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Mining robots** are field robots designed for harsh, GPS-denied, dust-laden mining environments.
- [x] The **5 key drivers** for mining robotics: **Safety, Productivity, Precision, Data, Ventilation Cost**.
- [x] **Underground transport** accounts for **30–50%** of underground mine OPEX and is the prime target for automation.
- [x] The **3 modes** of underground transport: **Track (rail)**, **Trackless (LHD/truck)**, **Continuous (conveyor)**.
- [x] **GPS is unusable underground** — alternative is **LiDAR SLAM + IMU + UWB + wheel odometry** fusion.
- [x] **Key formula:** $P = \dfrac{V_{bucket} \cdot \rho_{ore} \cdot 3600}{T_c}$ — remember the **3600** conversion factor.
- [x] **Key formula:** $F_{te} = m \cdot g \left[ \sin(\alpha) + C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha) + \dfrac{a}{g} \right]$ — for motor sizing.
- [x] **Always round fleet size UP** in KTU numericals; a fractional truck does not exist.
- [x] **Methane (CH₄)** and **CO/NO₂** sensors are mandatory for mine safety certification (ISO 13849 PL d).
- [x] **Leaky feeder cable** is the standard underground communication backbone.
- [x] **Battery-electric LHDs** are the modern trend to reduce ventilation OPEX and emissions.
- [x] **Haulage cycle phases:** Load → Haul → Dump → Return → Queue — total of **5 phases**.
- [x] **Real-world benchmark:** Rio Tinto's **AutoHaul** runs 130+ autonomous trains in the Pilbara, Australia.
- [x] **Major vendors to remember:** Sandvik (AutoMine), Caterpillar (MineStar), Komatsu (FrontRunner), Hitachi, Epiroc.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark answers, always **draw a labelled block diagram or state machine** — it carries 2–3 marks by itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Drilling # Module 2 — Drilling: Section 1 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Robotic Drilling** is a field-and-service robotic application in which an industrial multi-axis manipulator (typically a 6-DOF articulated robot arm) is integrated with a high-speed rotating spindle, a feed actuator, a force/torque sensor, and a closed-loop controller to perform **automated hole-making operations** on workpieces such as aircraft fuselage panels, automotive body parts, and large composite structures. The process encompasses automated **normal approach, perpendicular alignment, hole formation, and quality verification** without continuous human intervention. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, drilling is treated as a representative **contact-based, force-controlled robotic process** that bridges mechanical manufacturing knowledge with intelligent robotic execution. The system must dynamically compensate for workpiece tolerances, fixture errors, panel deformation, and tool deflection — tasks impossible to perform with a stiff CNC machine but well-suited to a compliance-aware robot. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Drilling* topic in Module 2 covers (a) process parameters, (b) drilling mechanics (forces, torque, power, MRR), (c) robotic drilling cell architecture, (d) alignment strategies (NDT, vision, force feedback), and (e) common defects (delamination, burr, over-/under-cut, peel-up) in composite and metallic stacks. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of robotic drilling like **a careful eye-doctor performing LASIK surgery**, but on a giant airplane wing. A surgeon cannot rely on brute force — they must feel the resistance of the cornea, align the laser perpendicular to a curved surface, and stop at exactly the right depth. Similarly: - The **robot arm** is the surgeon's hand — dexterous but compliant (flexible). - The **spindle + drill bit** is the surgical tool — high speed, very specific geometry. - The **force/torque sensor at the wrist** is the surgeon's fingertip — it "feels" the contact. - The **workpiece (e.g., aluminum + CFRP stack)** is the curved, layered cornea — it is neither perfectly flat nor perfectly rigid. - The **controller** is the surgeon's training + reflexes — it converts sensation into action in milliseconds. Without these feedback loops, the drill would wander, chatter, break, or ruin the workpiece. *This is why drilling is one of the hardest robotic tasks to automate, and one of the most commercially valuable.* ### 1.3 Standard Process Metrics and Physical Constants The following quantities are universally used in KTU-evaluated drilling problems: - **Spindle speed (N): revolutions per minute (rpm)** — typically **5,000 to 30,000 rpm** in robotic drilling, much higher than conventional drilling (≤ 1,500 rpm). - **Feed rate (v_f): mm/min** — typically **50 to 500 mm/min** in robotic cells. - **Thrust force (F_z): N** — critical force, must be controlled within ±5% of nominal. - **Cutting torque (M_c): N·m** — main indicator of tool condition. - **Power (P): W or kW** — bounded by spindle motor rating. - **Material Removal Rate (MRR): mm³/s** — productivity metric. - **Specific cutting energy (e): J/mm³** — material property (Al ≈ 0.4 J/mm³, CFRP ≈ 0.8 J/mm³, Ti ≈ 1.6 J/mm³). - **Hole diameter tolerance:** typically **H8–H10** (≈ ±0.03 mm) for aerospace assembly holes. > [!NOTE] > **Why use a robot instead of a CNC gantry for drilling?** Cost, footprint, dexterity, and ability to drill **on a 3-D curved assembly** without reclamping. A CNC cannot reach inside a fuselage barrel; a 6-axis robot can. --- # Module 2 — Drilling: Section 2 ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Drilling Process — Operational Breakdown A complete robotic drilling cycle is composed of **five sequential phases**, each governed by distinct physical phenomena and control laws: 1. **Approach & Localization Phase** - The end-effector is commanded to a *taught* or *vision-derived* nominal pose above the hole. - A search strategy (spiral scan, force gradient, or visual servoing) corrects pose errors caused by fixture and panel-stack-up tolerances. - *Outcome:* the tool tip is positioned within **±0.5 mm** laterally and **±2°** of surface normal. 2. **Contact & Normal-Alignment Phase** - The spindle advances slowly (≤ 50 mm/min) until a small **contact force (≈ 5–10 N)** is detected by the wrist F/T sensor. - The controller then performs *compliant normal alignment* — the robot slides the tool along the surface (tangential compliance) while regulating the normal contact force to a constant setpoint. - *Outcome:* the drill axis is aligned within **±0.1°** of the surface normal. 3. **Pilot Hole & Pecking Phase** - A short pilot hole is drilled at **low feed (≈ 30 mm/min) and moderate speed (≈ 8,000 rpm)**. - For deep holes, *pecking* (retraction to clear chips) is applied to avoid chip jamming and heat build-up. - *Outcome:* the tool is stably engaged in the workpiece. 4. **Full-Depth Drilling Phase** - Feed and speed are ramped to their nominal operating values. - Closed-loop **feed-rate or thrust-force control** is active to maintain constant chip load. - *Outcome:* a clean, dimensionally accurate hole with acceptable surface finish. 5. **Hole Quality Verification Phase** - The drill retracts, and a vision or laser probe inspects diameter, roundness, and burr. - AI-based classifiers may flag defective holes in real time. - *Outcome:* pass/fail decision relayed to the manufacturing execution system (MES). > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind force-controlled drilling:** Composite laminates (CFRP, GFRP) are **anisotropic and delamination-prone**. If the drill is not perpendicular to the surface, lateral forces rise, causing **peel-up delamination at entry** and **push-down delamination at exit**. Force control keeps these lateral components bounded. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | $v_c$ | Cutting speed (peripheral) | $v_c = \pi \, D \, N \, / \, 60$ | m/min | $D$ = drill diameter (m), $N$ = rpm | | $N$ | Spindle speed | $N = 60 \, v_c \, / \, (\pi \, D)$ | rpm | Inverse of $v_c$ formula | | $f_r$ | Feed rate (linear) | $f_r = N \cdot f$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev) | | $f_z$ | Feed per tooth | $f_z = f \, / \, Z$ | mm/tooth | $Z$ = number of effective cutting edges (drill: 2) | | MRR | Material removal rate | $\text{MRR} = (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | For a solid drill (full diameter) | | $F_z$ | Thrust (axial) force | $F_z = K_{fc} \cdot (\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot f$ | N | $K_{fc}$ = specific thrust force (N/mm²) | | $M_c$ | Cutting torque | $M_c = K_{mc} \cdot D^3 \cdot f^{0.8}$ | N·m | Empirical, Kienzle-style exponent | | $P_c$ | Cutting power | $P_c = M_c \cdot \omega = (2\pi N / 60) \cdot M_c$ | W | Must be ≤ spindle rated power | | $e_s$ | Specific cutting energy | $e_s = P_c \, / \, \text{MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Material property indicator | | $T$ | Tool life (Taylor) | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | min | $V = v_c$ in m/min, $C$ & $n$ from machinability data | | $k$ | Chip load (per tooth) | $k = f_z$ | mm/tooth | Critical for surface finish & delamination | | $\eta$ | Spindle-to-robot stiffness ratio | $\eta = k_{spindle} / k_{robot}$ | dimensionless | Typical $\eta \approx 100$–500; affects chatter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Absolute value is written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) **never** with vertical pipes `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table. ### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Drilling System - **Aerospace assembly (Airbus A350, Boeing 787):** Each fuselage barrel has **~50,000 to 100,000** fastener holes; **80%+** are drilled by robots (e.g., KUKA KR 1000 L750 titan with Electroimpact/Flex Track end-effectors). - **Automotive body-in-white:** Robotic drilling of spot-weld and bolt holes; reduction of cycle time from 90 s (manual) to 25 s (robotic). - **Wind-turbine blade manufacture:** Drilling root-bolt holes in 80 m composite blades; positional tolerances of ±0.1 mm across multi-meter lengths. - **Shipbuilding and rail:** Large-scale 6-axis drilling of thick steel sections (5–50 mm) where workpieces are too large to move to a CNC. --- # Module 2 — Drilling: Section 3 ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed **Given:** Required peripheral cutting speed $v_c = 80$ m/min, drill diameter $D = 8$ mm = $0.008$ m. **Find:** Spindle speed $N$ in rpm. $$ \begin{aligned} v_c &= \frac{\pi \, D \, N}{60} \\[4pt] \text{Step 1 — Rearrange for } N: \quad N &= \frac{60 \, v_c}{\pi \, D} \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:} \quad N &= \frac{60 \times 80}{\pi \times 0.008} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator:} \quad N &= \frac{4800}{0.025133} \\[4pt] \text{Step 4 — Final value:} \quad N &\approx 1.91 \times 10^{5} \;\text{rpm} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** This extremely high theoretical rpm is reduced in practice by using **high-helix / micro-grain carbide drills** and **HF spindlers** (e.g., **30,000–40,000 rpm**). The derivation confirms that $N$ scales **inversely with $D$** — small holes demand impractically high rpms, so we cap $N$ and accept a lower $v_c$ in CFRP. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) **Given:** $D = 10$ mm, feed per revolution $f = 0.10$ mm/rev, $N = 3000$ rpm. **Find:** MRR in mm³/min. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area of the drill:} \quad A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \;\text{mm}^2 \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 — Compute the feed rate:} \quad f_r &= N \cdot f = 3000 \times 0.10 = 300 \;\text{mm/min} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 — Compute MRR (volume swept per minute):} \quad \text{MRR} &= A \cdot f_r = 78.54 \times 300 \\[4pt] \text{Step 4 — Final value:} \quad \text{MRR} &= 23{,}562 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 23.6 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** This is the *bulk* removal; in a stack-up (e.g., 4 mm Al + 4 mm CFRP) the effective MRR changes layer-by-layer because cutting forces and chip morphology differ. Robotic force controllers exploit this to *detect layer transitions* in real time. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Force and Torque (Kienzle-style Model) **Given:** Drilling Al 7075-T6, $D = 6$ mm, $f = 0.08$ mm/rev. **Known material constants (from machinability handbook):** $k_{c1.1} = 750$ N/mm², exponent $1-m_c = 0.25$ (so $m_c = 0.75$). **Step 1 — Compute uncut chip thickness $h$:** $$ \begin{aligned} h &= \frac{f}{2} = \frac{0.08}{2} = 0.04 \;\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute uncut chip width $b$ (drill lip half-width, 2 flutes):** $$ \begin{aligned} b &= \frac{D}{2 \cdot \sin(\kappa)} \approx \frac{D}{2} = 3 \;\text{mm} \quad \text{(with rake angle correction later)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Kienzle's specific cutting force at the actual chip thickness:** $$ \begin{aligned} k_c &= k_{c1.1} \cdot h^{-m_c} = 750 \cdot (0.04)^{-0.75} \\[4pt] &= 750 \cdot (0.04)^{-0.75} = 750 \cdot 9.45 \\[4pt] &\approx 7087.5 \;\text{N/mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute cutting force per flute:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{c,\text{per flute}} &= k_c \cdot b \cdot h = 7087.5 \times 3 \times 0.04 = 850.5 \;\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Total tangential force (sum of 2 flutes):** $$ \begin{aligned} F_c &= 2 \cdot F_{c,\text{per flute}} = 2 \times 850.5 = 1701 \;\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Compute torque:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_c &= F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 1701 \times 0.003 = 5.10 \;\text{N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** A 5 N·m torque is well within the rated capacity of a typical 12 kW robotic spindle. If CFRP were drilled instead, $k_c$ would be roughly **40% higher** in the tangential direction but **delamination-sensitive** in the thrust direction — motivating the need for force control in composite stacks. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tool Life via Taylor's Equation **Given:** Drill HSS-Co, $v_{c1} = 30$ m/min at $T_1 = 60$ min, $v_{c2} = 50$ m/min at $T_2 = 20$ min. **Find:** Taylor exponent $n$ and constant $C$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1 — Take logarithms of } v_c \cdot T^n = C: \quad \ln v_c + n \ln T &= \ln C \\[4pt] \text{Step 2 — Subtract the two operating points:} \quad \ln v_{c1} - \ln v_{c2} &= -n \cdot (\ln T_1 - \ln T_2) \\[4pt] \text{Step 3 — Evaluate logs numerically:} \quad \ln 30 - \ln 50 &= -n (\ln 60 - \ln 20) \\[4pt] 3.401 - 3.912 &= -n (4.094 - 2.996) \\[4pt] -0.511 &= -n \times 1.098 \\[4pt] n &= 0.466 \\[4pt] \text{Step 4 — Solve for } C: \quad C &= v_{c1} \cdot T_1^n = 30 \cdot 60^{0.466} = 30 \cdot 7.10 = 213.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify with second point:** $v_{c2} \cdot T_2^n = 50 \times 20^{0.466} = 50 \times 4.04 = 202$ ≈ 213 ✓ (within rounding). **Engineering interpretation:** $n \approx 0.47$ is typical for HSS drilling of steel. **Lower $n$ → tool life is more sensitive to cutting speed** (dangerous for uncontrolled robotic drilling). Carbide drills exhibit $n \approx 0.25$–$0.35$ (more forgiving). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defect vs. Root Cause vs. Mitigation | **Defect** | **Physical Root Cause** | **Detection Sensor** | **Robotic Mitigation Strategy** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Peel-up delamination (CFRP entry) | Excessive thrust at entry, blunt tool | Acoustic emission, F/T sensor | Reduce entry feed; use pilot hole; "ramp-in" trajectory | | Push-down delamination (CFRP exit) | Loss of backing support at exit | Thrust-force drop, vision | Add sacrificial backing plate; use force-controlled exit | | Hole oversize | Tool deflection, runout > 0.02 mm | Laser micrometer, CMM | Use one-shot or reamed drills; reduce feed; improve holder | | Burr at exit | Ductile material + dull cutting edge | Vision / tactile | Replace tool; chamfer; deburring pass | | Layered hole ovality | Stack-up with differing $k_c$ | Optical comparator | Adaptive feed per layer; helical interpolation | | Thermal damage (CFRP) | Heat accumulation in chips | IR camera, embedded thermocouple | Peck cycle, MQL, high helix geometry | --- # Module 2 — Drilling: Section 4 ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Robotic Drilling Cell — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Workpiece Stack
Al + CFRP Panel] --> B[Fixture
Vacuum Clamp Table] B --> C[Localization Subsystem
Vision / IR Scanner] C --> D[Robot Controller
KUKA KR C5 / ABB OmniCore] D --> E[6-DOF Articulated Robot Arm
Payload 750 kg] E --> F[Wrist F or T Sensor
ATI Delta IP65] F --> G[End-Effector] G --> H[Spindle Module
30 krpm HF Spindle] H --> I[Drill Bit + Tool Changer] I --> J[Hole Quality Probe
Vision + Laser] J --> K[Quality Database and MES] D --> L[Adaptive Force Controller
PID + Fuzzy + MRAC] F --> L L --> D K --> D ``` **Reading guide:** This Mermaid chart shows the **closed-loop data flow**. Notice the **two feedback arrows** returning to the controller — one from the F/T sensor (real-time force control) and one from the quality database (run-to-run learning). This is the architectural signature of an *intelligent* drilling cell, as opposed to a "blind" CNC gantry. --- ### 4.2 Control Hierarchy of the Drilling Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph L1[Level 1 - Trajectory Planning] T1[Teach Pendant Input] T2[CAD-derived Hole Map] T3[Collision-free Path Planner] end subgraph L2[Level 2 - Real-time Servo Control] S1[Joint Position Controllers] S2[Cartesian Impedance Controller] end subgraph L3[Level 3 - Process Control] P1[Thrust Force Regulator] P2[Torque Limiter] P3[Adaptive Feed Override] end subgraph L4[Level 4 - Quality and Diagnostics] Q1[In-process Monitoring] Q2[Post-process Inspection] Q3[Tool Wear Estimator] end T1 --> S1 T2 --> T3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> P1 S2 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P3 --> S2 P2 --> Q3 Q3 --> P3 Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> T3 ``` **Reading guide:** The four levels mirror the **ISA-95 automation pyramid** adapted for robotic machining. Level 3 (Process Control) is the *unique* layer of robotic drilling — CNC gantries typically lack the impedance/admittance control needed at Level 2 to honor Level 3's force setpoints. --- ### 4.3 Drilling State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Approaching : Hole selected from queue Approaching --> Searching : Vision error greater than 0.5 mm Searching --> Approaching : Visual lock acquired Approaching --> ContactDetected : F greater than 5 N ContactDetected --> Aligning : Normal alignment engaged Aligning --> Piloting : Tilt less than 0.1 deg Piloting --> Drilling : Stable engagement confirmed Drilling --> Pecking : Depth greater than 3 times D Pecking --> Drilling : Retract complete Drilling --> Retracting : Target depth reached Retracting --> Inspecting : Spindle at safe height Inspecting --> Pass : Diameter and roundness within tolerance Inspecting --> Fail : Out of tolerance flagged Pass --> Idle : Cycle complete Fail --> Rework : MES records defect Rework --> Idle : Operator notified ``` **Reading guide:** Each transition has a measurable *trigger* (force threshold, angle threshold, depth threshold). This explicitness is what makes robotic drilling **auditable under AS9100D / ISO 9001** aerospace quality standards. --- # Module 2 — Drilling: Section 5 ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]** *Define "specific cutting energy" in the context of robotic drilling. Mention its typical range for aluminum alloys and CFRP composites.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) *Definition:* Specific cutting energy $e_s$ is the energy required to remove a unit volume of material. It is computed as $e_s = P_c \, / \, \text{MMR}$, where $P_c$ is the cutting power (W or J/s) and MRR is the material removal rate (mm³/s or cm³/s). Units: **J/mm³**. - (1 mark) *Aluminum alloys:* $e_s \approx 0.4$–$0.7$ J/mm³. - (1 mark) *CFRP composites:* $e_s \approx 0.6$–$1.2$ J/mm³, with high variability due to fiber orientation. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List any three advantages of using a 6-DOF articulated robot for drilling aerospace structures over a gantry-type CNC drilling machine.* **Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark each):** 1. **Workspace dexterity:** A 6-axis robot can drill on curved, non-prismatic surfaces (e.g., a fuselage barrel) without reclamping; a gantry cannot reach such geometries. 2. **Lower capital cost & footprint:** A robotic cell costs ~40–60% of an equivalent 5-axis gantry for the same work envelope. 3. **Reconfigurability:** The same robot can be re-tasked for riveting, trimming, NDT, or assembly by swapping the end-effector — gantries are single-purpose. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) **Q. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With neat functional block diagrams, explain the architecture of a robotic drilling cell used in aircraft fuselage assembly. Identify the role of the wrist force/torque sensor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A robotic drill of diameter $D = 6$ mm operates at $N = 12{,}000$ rpm with feed per revolution $f = 0.06$ mm/rev in an Al 7075 panel. Compute (i) cutting speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) cutting power, assuming $M_c = 4.5$ N·m. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q(a) — 7 marks - **(1 mark) Cell components:** 6-DOF robot arm (e.g., KUKA KR 500), HF spindle, F/T sensor, end-effector with auto tool-change, workpiece fixture (vacuum or clamp), vision system, MES link. - **(2 marks) Architecture description:** A *cell-level controller* (robot OEM) generates motion commands. The *spindle controller* maintains rpm. The *F/T sensor* streams 6-axis wrench data at 1 kHz to the *impedance/admittance controller* in the robot. The *vision system* performs hole localization (template matching, fringe projection). The *MES* records cycle data and accepts part programs (e.g., NC code, APT, or proprietary format). - **(1.5 marks) Normal-alignment procedure:** Approach → low-force contact → lateral search to align with surface normal (force gradient method) → drill proceeds. - **(1 mark) Role of F/T sensor:** Provides real-time measurement of (a) thrust force for force control, (b) lateral forces for normal-alignment, (c) torque for chip-load regulation and tool-wear detection, and (d) impact detection for safety (immediate spindle stop on abnormal loading). - **(1 mark) Advantages of force control:** Compensates for panel stack-up errors, prevents delamination in CFRP, suppresses chatter, and enables *one-shot drilling* with high positional tolerance. - **(0.5 mark) Conclusion:** The architecture represents a *cyber-physical* integration of mechanical machining knowledge and real-time robotic intelligence. #### Model Solution to Q(b) — 7 marks **Given:** $D = 6$ mm = 0.006 m, $N = 12{,}000$ rpm, $f = 0.06$ mm/rev, $M_c = 4.5$ N·m. **(i) Cutting speed (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} v_c &= \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.006 \times 12{,}000}{60} \\[4pt] &= \frac{226.19}{60} = 3.77 \;\text{m/s} \approx 226 \;\text{m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Feed rate (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} f_r &= N \times f = 12{,}000 \times 0.06 = 720 \;\text{mm/min} = 0.012 \;\text{m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) MRR (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MRR} &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times f_r = \frac{\pi (6)^2}{4} \times 720 \\[4pt] &= 28.27 \times 720 = 20{,}358 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 339.3 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Cutting power (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_c &= M_c \times \omega = M_c \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} = 4.5 \times \frac{2\pi \times 12{,}000}{60} \\[4pt] &= 4.5 \times 1256.6 = 5655 \;\text{W} \approx 5.66 \;\text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q(b)):** > - Do *not* confuse the **peripheral cutting speed** (m/s or m/min) with the **feed rate** (mm/min). They are *two different* velocities. > - The MRR formula **for a solid drill** is $\pi D^2/4 \cdot f_r$, *not* $\pi D \cdot f_r$ (that would be a wrong "swept volume"). > - Power must be reported in **consistent SI units**; mixing $M_c$ in N·mm with $\omega$ in rad/s is a common unit-conversion error costing 1–2 marks. --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **Q. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative]** **(a)** Discuss the various defects encountered in robotic drilling of composite (CFRP) stacks. For each defect, mention one sensing technique used for its detection and one control strategy used for its mitigation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using Taylor's tool-life equation with $n = 0.30$ and $C = 350$ (m/min·minⁿ), determine the operating cutting speed for a desired tool life of $T = 90$ minutes. If the operator wants to double the tool life, by what percentage must the cutting speed be reduced? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks | **Defect (1.5 marks each)** | **Sensor (0.5)** | **Control Strategy (0.5)** | |:---|:---|:---| | **Peel-up delamination (CFRP entry):** top-ply separation caused by insufficient chip-thickness support at entry. | Acoustic emission (AE) sensor at tool holder. | Pilot-hole step + reduced entry feed (50% of nominal). | | **Push-down delamination (CFRP exit):** bottom-ply separation due to loss of back support. | Thrust-force drop detector (rapid decline signals exit). | Sacrificial backing plate + force-controlled exit (ramp down to 30% feed). | | **Fiber pull-out / uncut fibers:** at hole wall; caused by dull tool or wrong geometry. | Vision-based hole-wall inspection. | Replace drill on schedule; use diamond-coated or PCD tool. | | **Hole oversize / ovality:** caused by tool deflection and runout. | Laser micrometer in spindle housing. | Helical interpolation path; reduce radial engagement; use one-shot drill. | | **Burr at exit (metal stack):** ductile flow on exit surface. | Tactile / capacitive sensor. | Chamfering cycle; deburring pass with rotary brush end-effector. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (B(a)):** Do not list defects without pairing them with a sensor and a control strategy. Marks are awarded **per (defect, sensor, mitigation) triplet**. Vague statements like "use force control" without specifying *how* lose 0.5 mark per defect. #### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks **Given:** $n = 0.30$, $C = 350$, $T = 90$ min. **(i) Cutting speed for $T = 90$ min (3.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} v_c \cdot T^n &= C \\[4pt] v_c &= \frac{C}{T^n} = \frac{350}{90^{0.30}} = \frac{350}{4.041} = 86.61 \;\text{m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Cutting speed for $T' = 180$ min (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} v_c' &= \frac{C}{T'^{0.30}} = \frac{350}{180^{0.30}} = \frac{350}{4.948} = 70.74 \;\text{m/min} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Percentage reduction in cutting speed (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{reduction} &= \frac{v_c - v_c'}{v_c} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{86.61 - 70.74}{86.61} \times 100 = \frac{15.87}{86.61} \times 100 \approx 18.3\% \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering interpretation (extra for high marks):** A 2× increase in tool life requires only an **18% reduction in cutting speed**, not 50%. This non-linear trade-off is the central insight of Taylor's equation and is exploited in production scheduling for robotic drilling cells. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Error (B(b)):** Students often mistakenly treat Taylor's equation linearly — i.e., halving $T$ halves $v_c$. This is **wrong**; the relationship is a **power law**. Marks are deducted heavily for linear interpolation. --- ## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ Robotic drilling is a **force-controlled contact task** — stiffness control and impedance control are essential. - ✅ The five process phases: **Approach → Search → Align → Pilot → Drill → Inspect**. - ✅ Cutting speed $v_c = \pi D N / 60$ (m/min if $D$ in m, $N$ in rpm). - ✅ Feed rate $f_r = N \times f$ (mm/min). - ✅ MRR (solid drill) $= \pi D^2/4 \cdot f_r$. - ✅ Torque $M_c = K_{mc} \cdot D^3 \cdot f^{0.8}$ — scales as $D^3$ and $f^{0.8}$. - ✅ Specific cutting energy $e_s = P_c / \text{MRR}$; lower for ductile metals, higher for composites. - ✅ Taylor tool life: $V \cdot T^n = C$; remember the **power law**, not linear. - ✅ CFRP defects: **peel-up** (entry), **push-down** (exit), **fiber pull-out** — each needs specific sensing and control. - ✅ Wrist F/T sensor enables: normal alignment, force control, chatter suppression, collision detection. - ✅ Robotic drilling has lower static stiffness than CNC (~1 N/µm vs ~50 N/µm) — this *motivates* force control rather than being a disadvantage. - ✅ Peck-drilling is required for depth $> 3D$ to clear chips and reduce heat. - ✅ Cutting speed is **inversely** proportional to drill diameter — small holes need impractically high rpm, so cutting speed is reduced in CFRP. - ✅ **Kienzle's specific cutting force $k_c$** increases as chip thickness decreases — small feeds amplify the force density. - ✅ Hole-quality inspection should be **in-cycle** (laser/vision) to enable 100% in-process verification — a regulatory requirement in AS9100D aerospace manufacturing. - ✅ **Always carry units through every step** of numerical derivation; KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing or inconsistent unit. - ✅ **Two KTU-favorite pitfalls:** (1) confusing cutting speed with feed rate; (2) treating Taylor's equation as linear. ---

topics.title: Explosive Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosive Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Explosive Placement** in the context of Field and Service Robotics refers to the specialized application of **EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal) robots** or **bomb disposal manipulators** to safely transport, position, and deposit explosive devices, charges, or counter-charges in hazardous, inaccessible, or contaminated environments without direct human intervention.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the broader umbrella of **Hazardous Environment Robotics** and **Remote Manipulation**, where the primary objective is to execute **precision placement tasks** on objects classified as energetic materials (explosives, propellants, pyrotechnics) while maintaining a **safe standoff distance** between the operator and the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 - Module 2):**
> Explosive Placement is a sub-topic under *Service Robot Applications in Hazardous Environments*. The focus is on **end-effector design**, **teleoperated manipulation**, **safe-standoff kinematics**, and **task-space trajectory generation** for placing charges or disruptive tools onto suspicious objects.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chess player controlling a robotic hand from another room** to carefully pick up a delicate, dangerous object and place it exactly on a marked spot on a chessboard. The player cannot see the board directly — only through a camera feed. The robotic hand must be:

1. **Gentle enough** not to trigger the object's sensors.
2. **Precise enough** to place the object within a few millimeters.
3. **Strong enough** to lift the weight of the object against gravity.
4. **Compliant enough** to absorb unexpected contacts without crushing the object.

This is exactly what an **Explosive Placement Robot** does — but instead of a chess piece, it's placing a **shaped charge, disruptor, or EOD tool** onto a **suspicious package, IED (Improvised Explosive Device), or unexploded ordnance (UXO)**.

**Another Analogy:** Think of a **surgeon's robotic arm (like the Da Vinci system)** performing a delicate operation. Replace the patient with a **live explosive**, and the surgeon with a **bomb technician** operating from a safe distance. The principles of precision, force-feedback, and safety scaling remain identical.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Standoff Distance (d)** | **300 m to 1000 m** | Minimum safe operating distance for human controllers |
| **Manipulator Reach (L)** | **2 m to 4 m** | Typical EOD arm length |
| **Payload Capacity** | **10 kg to 75 kg** | Maximum explosive mass the gripper can handle |
| **Placement Accuracy** | **±2 mm to ±5 mm** | Required positioning tolerance for charge alignment |
| **Joint Resolution** | **0.1°** | Angular precision of arm joints |
| **Maximum Tool Speed** | **0.5 m/s** | Speed cap to prevent accidental triggering |
| **Reaction Time (Operator)** | **200 ms to 500 ms** | Human-in-the-loop delay in teleoperation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:**
> Explosive Placement = *Teleoperated or autonomous positioning of energetic materials using a robotic manipulator, governed by safe-standoff kinematics, compliant end-effectors, and force-limited control strategies.*

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Field Robotics

- **Human Safety:** Eliminates direct human exposure to blast, fragmentation, and toxic fumes.
- **Precision:** Robots achieve sub-millimeter placement accuracy impossible with manual tools under stress.
- **Repeatability:** Standardized placement procedures for demining and demolition.
- **Remote Reach:** Access to confined spaces (vehicle undercarriages, pipe interiors, aircraft holds).
- **Force Control:** Maintains grip force within strict bounds to prevent accidental detonation via impact, friction, or electrostatic discharge (ESD).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Explosive Placement

The explosive placement task is decomposed into **five sequential operational phases**, each governed by distinct kinematic and control constraints:

### Phase 1: Reconnaissance & Localization
- The robot approaches the target zone (suspected IED or placement site).
- **Sensors active:** Stereo cameras, LIDAR, thermal imaging, X-ray backscatter.
- **Output:** A **3D point cloud map** of the target object and surrounding terrain.
- **Constraint:** Maintain a **preliminary standoff distance** ($d_{\text{prelim}} \geq 50$ m) until the threat level is classified.

### Phase 2: Approach & Alignment
- The manipulator base or mobile platform navigates to the **deployment position**.
- Alignment uses **visual servoing**: the image-plane error $e = s^{*} - s$ is minimized, where $s^{*}$ is the desired feature position and $s$ is the current feature position in the camera frame.
- **Constraint:** Approach velocity $v_{\text{approach}} \leq 0.1$ m/s.

### Phase 3: Grasping / Tool Engagement
- The end-effector (gripper, disruptor holder, or shaped-charge cradle) engages the explosive or counter-charge.
- **Force-feedback control** ensures the grip force $F_{\text{grip}}$ remains within the safe window:
$$F_{\text{min}} \leq F_{\text{grip}} \leq F_{\text{max}}$$
where $F_{\text{min}}$ prevents slip and $F_{\text{max}}$ prevents crushing or shock initiation.

### Phase 4: Transport & Placement
- The arm moves the charge along a **pre-planned trajectory** in task space.
- Trajectory uses **trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profiles** to minimize jerk (rate of change of acceleration), reducing inertial forces on the payload.
- **Constraint:** Maximum acceleration $a_{\text{max}} \leq 0.5$ m/s² to prevent shock initiation of sensitive explosives.

### Phase 5: Release & Retraction
- The gripper opens, the charge is placed, and the arm retracts along a **collision-free path**.
- **Constraint:** Retraction speed $v_{\text{retract}} \geq 2 \times v_{\text{approach}}$ to minimize time in the hazard zone.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Phase

| Phase | Primary 'Why' | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Reconnaissance | **Information gathering** | You cannot manipulate what you cannot model. |
| Approach | **Minimize exposure time** | Every second near the device increases risk. |
| Grasping | **Secure coupling** | Slip = uncontrolled drop = potential detonation. |
| Transport | **Controlled dynamics** | Jerk creates shock loads on the charge. |
| Release | **Clean disengagement** | Residual contact can trigger friction-sensitive explosives. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{\text{safe}} = K \cdot \sqrt[3]{W}$ | **Cube-root standoff law** (Hopkinson's law simplified) | m, kg |
| 2 | $F_{\text{grip}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot S F$ | Minimum grip force to prevent slip | N |
| 3 | $F_{\text{shock}} = m_{\text{payload}} \cdot a_{\text{jerk}}$ | Inertial force during acceleration | N |
| 4 | $\tau = J(q)^T \cdot F_{\text{task}}$ | **Jacobian transpose** for joint torques | N·m, N |
| 5 | $q = IK(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ | Inverse kinematics from task space to joint space | rad |
| 6 | $v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot a_{\text{max}} \cdot d_{\text{brake}}}$ | Maximum velocity for given braking distance | m/s |
| 7 | $t_{\text{retract}} = \frac{d_{\text{retract}}}{v_{\text{retract}}}$ | Time to clear the blast radius | s |
| 8 | $E_{\text{blast}} \propto \frac{W}{d^3}$ | Blast overpressure decay with distance | Pa, m, kg |
| 9 | $\text{PEFR} = K_2 \cdot \left(\frac{W^{1/3}}{d}\right)$ | Peak free-field overpressure (Kingery-Bulmash) | bar |
| 10 | $R = e^{J \theta}$ | Exponential map for SO(3) rotation | — |
| 11 | $M(q) \ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau + J^T F$ | Full manipulator dynamics equation | N·m |
| 12 | $K_p, K_d$: PID gains for joint position control | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All distances in **meters (m)**, masses in **kilograms (kg)**, forces in **Newtons (N)**, angles in **radians (rad)**, and time in **seconds (s)**. KTU examiners are strict about SI unit notation.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Military EOD:** Remotely placing shaped charges on IEDs for controlled detonation.
- **Demining:** Deploying disruptors (water jets, shotgun shells) onto landmines.
- **Bomb Squad (Police):** Moving suspicious packages to containment vessels.
- **Space Robotics:** Placing pyrotechnic separation bolts on satellites (NASA's Robonaut legacy).
- **Nuclear Industry:** Handling radioactive explosive actuators in decommissioning.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safe Standoff Distance Using the Cube-Root Law

**Problem Context:** An EOD technician must determine the minimum safe distance to position the control unit given a suspected explosive of equivalent mass $W = 5$ kg of TNT.

### Step 1: State the Cube-Root Scaling Law

The Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root law states that **similar blast effects occur at distances that scale with the cube root of the charge energy**. This is based on the dimensional analysis of the blast wave:

$$d_{\text{safe}} = K \cdot W^{1/3}$$

where:
- $d_{\text{safe}}$ = safe standoff distance (m)
- $W$ = equivalent TNT mass (kg)
- $K$ = safety constant (depends on overpressure threshold)

### Step 2: Determine the Safety Constant $K$

For a peak overpressure threshold of $\Delta P = 70$ kPa (the threshold for eardrum rupture and minor structural damage, the standard for EOD control units), the empirically derived constant is:

$$K = 7.5 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $K = 7.5$?** This value is derived from the **Kingery-Bulmash equations** for free-air blast, calibrated against field tests conducted by the U.S. Army and NATO EOD working groups. Different values of $K$ correspond to different overpressure thresholds (e.g., $K = 3.5$ for 280 kPa — lung injury threshold).

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Values

We have $W = 5$ kg. Substituting:

$$d_{\text{safe}} = 7.5 \cdot (5)^{1/3}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Cube Root

$$(5)^{1/3} = 5^{0.3333} = 1.7100$$

### Step 5: Compute the Final Distance

$$d_{\text{safe}} = 7.5 \times 1.7100 = 12.825 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6: Apply the Engineering Safety Factor

In practice, EOD protocols mandate a **3× safety multiplier**:

$$d_{\text{operational}} = 3 \times d_{\text{safe}} = 3 \times 12.825 = 38.475 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** The control unit must be placed at least **38.5 m** from a 5 kg TNT-equivalent device.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Kinematics for a 3-DOF Explosive Placement Arm

**Problem Context:** A planar 3-DOF arm (Revolute-Revolute-Revolute) must place a shaped charge at task-space coordinates $(x, y, z) = (0.6, 0.2, 0.0)$ m, with link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, $L_3 = 0.1$ m.

### Step 1: Define the Forward Kinematics

For a 3R planar arm in the $xy$-plane:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) \\
y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Reduce to a 2R Problem (Wrist at Target)

The wrist center must be positioned at:
$$x_w = x - L_3 \cos(\phi), \quad y_w = y - L_3 \sin(\phi)$$

where $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3$ is the end-effector orientation.

Assuming the charge must be placed vertically downward: $\phi = -\pi/2$ (i.e., $-90°$).

$$x_w = 0.6 - 0.1 \cdot \cos(-90°) = 0.6 - 0 = 0.6 \text{ m}$$
$$y_w = 0.2 - 0.1 \cdot \sin(-90°) = 0.2 - (-0.1) = 0.3 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Apply the 2R Inverse Kinematics Solution

For a 2R planar arm reaching $(x_w, y_w)$:

$$\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x_w^2 + y_w^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$$

Substituting:
$$\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{(0.6)^2 + (0.3)^2 - (0.4)^2 - (0.3)^2}{2 \times 0.4 \times 0.3}$$

$$\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{0.36 + 0.09 - 0.16 - 0.09}{0.24} = \frac{0.20}{0.24} = 0.8333$$

$$\theta_2 = \pm \arccos(0.8333) = \pm 33.56°$$

**Choosing the "elbow-up" configuration** (standard for overhead placement): $\theta_2 = -33.56°$ (negative for elbow above the line).

### Step 4: Solve for $\theta_1$

$$\theta_1 = \arctan2(y_w, x_w) - \arctan2(L_2 \sin(\theta_2), L_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2))$$

$$\theta_1 = \arctan2(0.3, 0.6) - \arctan2(0.3 \times \sin(-33.56°), 0.4 + 0.3 \times \cos(-33.56°))$$

$$\theta_1 = 26.57° - \arctan2(-0.1658, 0.4 + 0.2500)$$

$$\theta_1 = 26.57° - \arctan2(-0.1658, 0.6500)$$

$$\theta_1 = 26.57° - (-14.31°) = 40.88°$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\theta_3$

$$\theta_3 = \phi - \theta_1 - \theta_2 = -90° - 40.88° - (-33.56°) = -97.32°$$

### Final Joint Angles

$$\boxed{\theta_1 = 40.88°, \quad \theta_2 = -33.56°, \quad \theta_3 = -97.32°}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Grip Force Calculation

**Problem:** A gripper must hold a cylindrical charge of mass $m = 3$ kg, with a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$ between the rubber gripper pads and the charge casing. The charge is subject to a maximum acceleration of $a = 0.5$ m/s² during transport. Find the minimum grip force.

### Step 1: Identify the Forces

The effective weight during transport (including inertial load):
$$F_{\text{eff}} = m \cdot (g + a) = 3 \times (9.81 + 0.5) = 3 \times 10.31 = 30.93 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Friction Model

For a two-finger gripper, each finger provides a normal force $F_N$. The total friction force must exceed $F_{\text{eff}}$:

$$2 \cdot \mu \cdot F_N \geq F_{\text{eff}}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Normal Force

$$F_N \geq \frac{F_{\text{eff}}}{2\mu} = \frac{30.93}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{30.93}{0.8} = 38.66 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Safety Factor (SF = 2.0)

$$F_{N,\text{design}} = 2.0 \times 38.66 = 77.33 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** Each finger of the gripper must exert at least **77.3 N** of normal force.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the inertial term $m \cdot a$ and only use $m \cdot g$. This leads to a grip force that is **insufficient during acceleration phases**, causing the charge to slip and potentially detonate upon impact. **Always include dynamic effects!**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Force Budget for Explosive Placement

| Force Type | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value (3 kg charge) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational | $F_g$ | $m \cdot g$ | 29.43 N |
| Inertial (max accel) | $F_i$ | $m \cdot a_{\text{max}}$ | 1.50 N |
| Centripetal (if rotating) | $F_c$ | $m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Variable |
| Wind/Drag | $F_d$ | $0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | ~0.1 N (indoor) |
| Grip Friction Required | $F_{\text{fric}}$ | $\frac{m(g+a_{\text{max}})}{2\mu}$ | 38.66 N (each finger) |
| Shock Threshold (PETN) | $F_{\text{shock,max}}$ | Empirical | ~5 N (drop height dependent) |
