```mermaid
    S1[2D LiDAR 360] --> P1[Sensor Preproc]
    S2[Thermal Camera] --> P1
    S3[IMU MEMS 6 Axis] --> P1
    S4[Wheel Encoders] --> P1
    S5[UWB Beacons] --> P1
    P1 --> SL1[Cartographer SLAM]
    SL1 --> MAP[Occupancy Grid Map]
    MAP --> PL1[Global Planner A Star]
    PL1 --> PL2[Local Planner TEB]
    PL2 --> CTL[Steering Speed Controller]
    CTL --> ACT1[Motor Driver Left]
    CTL --> ACT2[Motor Driver Right]
    CTL --> ACT3[Light PTZ Pan Tilt]
    SL1 --> TELE[Telemetry Link]
    TELE --> OP[Operator Console Surface]
    OP --> TELE
    TELE --> CTL
```

> **Reading guide:** Underground units must operate in **absolute GPS darkness**; UWB beacons mounted along the drift act as *artificial constellations*. Cartographer fuses them with 2D LiDAR to keep global drift below **~1 m over 500 m** of traverse, a typical KTU 2024 evaluation threshold.

### 4.3 State-Machine — AHS Mission Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Standby
    Standby --> Loaded: At loading face, dipper fills bin
    Loaded --> Hauling: Receive haul mission
    Hauling --> Dumping: Arrive at crusher
    Dumping --> Returning: Bin empty
    Returning --> Queued: At loading face
    Queued --> Loaded: Next dipper cycle
    Standby --> Maintenance: Self-diagnostic fail
    Hauling --> SafeStop: Obstacle persistent 3s
    Dumping --> SafeStop: Slope exceeded
    SafeStop --> Standby: Operator clear
    Maintenance --> [*]
```

> **Reading guide:** The *Safe-Stop* state is the single chokepoint that absorbs every abnormal condition. From there only an operator can resume — this is the **IEC 61508 SIL-2** boundary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not** confuse "autonomous" with "teleoperated" — the KTU 2024 rubric deducts **1 mark** for this swap.
> 2. **Do not** write "GPS is used underground." It is not. Underground localization uses UWB, IMU, LiDAR SLAM, or RFID — *not* GNSS.
> 3. Always quote the **ISO standard number** (e.g., ISO 17757) in safety-related answers; bare statements lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. For SLAM derivations, examiners look for the **Jacobian $F_k$** explicitly. Skipping it loses 3 marks.
> 5. In Part-B numericals, **carry the units** to the final box. Missing units cost 0.5–1 mark per quantity.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define mining robotics and list any three challenges that distinguish it from indoor mobile robotics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mining robotics is the sub-field of field robotics concerned with the design and deployment of autonomous, semi-autonomous, or teleoperated robotic systems in surface and underground mining environments to perform extraction, haulage, drilling, loading, inspection, and rescue tasks.
Three distinguishing challenges:
1. **GPS-denied operation** underground → requires LiDAR/IMU/UWB-based localization.
2. **Harsh environment** (dust, darkness, vibration, explosive atmospheres) → demands intrinsically safe (IECEx) hardware.
3. **Dynamic 3-D terrain** (loose gravel, water, blast-induced reshaping) → requires real-time 3D SLAM and adaptive planning.
*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for each of three challenges, partial credit at examiner's discretion.)*

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is an Autonomous Haulage System (AHS)? Mention two key enabling technologies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An AHS is a fleet of self-driving haul trucks (typically 100–400 ton class) that operate in open-pit mines under central supervisory control without on-board drivers.
Key enabling technologies:
1. **High-precision GNSS** (RTK/PPK, dual-constellation) for surface localization with cm-level accuracy.
2. **Onboard multi-sensor perception stack** — 3-D LiDAR, radar, and IMU — combined with the Fleet Management System (FMS) and an **ISO 17757** compliant safety PLC.
*(1 mark definition + 0.5 each for the two technologies + 0.5 each for the standards/metrics = 3 marks.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — *Sensor Fusion and Underground SLAM*

**(a)** *Explain the EKF-based sensor fusion architecture used in underground mining robots. Derive the Kalman gain expression and state the role of process covariance $Q_k$ and measurement covariance $R_k$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *Architecture (2 marks):* An underground mining robot uses an **EKF** to fuse **high-rate IMU/odometry** (prediction) with **low-rate LiDAR scan-match** (measurement). The EKF produces an estimate $\mathbf{x}_k = [x,y,\theta]^T$ at every control cycle.

2. *Derivation of Kalman gain (3 marks):* Starting from the innovation covariance
$$
S_k = H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k
$$
and minimizing the trace of the posterior covariance $\mathrm{tr}(P_k^+)$, the optimal gain is
$$
K_k = P_k^{-} H_k^T S_k^{-1} = P_k^{-} H_k^T (H_k P_k^{-} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}
$$
This $K_k$ is the closed-form solution of the linear minimum-mean-square-error estimator.

3. *Role of $Q_k$ and $R_k$ (2 marks):*
   - $Q_k$ models *process uncertainty* (wheel slip, IMU bias drift); larger $Q_k$ → filter trusts measurements more.
   - $R_k$ models *sensor noise* (LiDAR scan-match residual); larger $R_k$ → filter trusts the prediction more.

**(b)** *A mining inspection robot moves through a 1-m-wide drift. Its left wheel rotates at $\omega_L = 1.4$ rad/s, right wheel at $\omega_R = 1.6$ rad/s. Wheel radius $r = 0.10$ m, wheel-base $b = 0.40$ m, $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Starting at $(0,0,0)$, compute the new pose.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Body linear velocity:
$$
v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) = \frac{0.10}{2}(1.6 + 1.4) = 0.15\ \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Body angular velocity:
$$
\omega = \frac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L) = \frac{0.10}{0.40}(1.6 - 1.4) = 0.05\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Step 3 — Displacements:
$$
\Delta x = v\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t = 0.15 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1 = 0.015\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta y = v\sin\theta \cdot \Delta t = 0.15 \cdot 0 \cdot 0.1 = 0\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta \theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0.05 \cdot 0.1 = 0.005\ \text{rad}
$$

Step 4 — Updated pose:
$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.015 = 0.015\ \text{m}
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0 = 0\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.005 = 0.005\ \text{rad} \approx 0.286^\circ
$$

**Verification (1 mark):** The robot turned slightly clockwise (positive $\omega$); it traveled ~1.5 cm forward — physically reasonable for a 0.1-s step in a 1-m drift. Drift width is not violated.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *AHS Architecture and Standards*

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a surface Autonomous Haulage System (AHS). Explain the role of the Fleet Management System (FMS) and the Safety PLC.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *Block diagram (3 marks):* As per SECTION 4.1, the AHS consists of (i) the surface FMS, (ii) the dispatch optimizer, (iii) the radio bridge, (iv) the on-board autonomy stack (perception → localization → planning → control), and (v) the safety PLC and E-stop circuit.

2. *Role of FMS (2 marks):* The FMS is the *central brain*. It receives a mine plan, dynamically assigns haul missions to trucks (which pit → which crusher), optimizes truck-shovel pairing, and streams commands to the fleet. It also ingests telemetry to maintain a real-time material-flow dashboard.

3. *Role of Safety PLC (2 marks):* The Safety PLC is a redundant, SIL-2 (IEC 61508) controller that monitors the autonomy stack output. If it detects a fault (e.g., obstacle persistent > 3 s, loss of comms, slope exceeded), it asserts the E-stop and brings the truck to a controlled halt within **< 100 ms** — independent of the main controller.

**(b)** *A 290-tonne Cat 797F autonomous truck is climbing a 10 % gradient at 12 km/h when the dispatcher commands a 0.6 m/s² deceleration to negotiate an intersection. The truck must come to a stop within 35 m. Verify whether the deceleration is sufficient, and recommend a corrective action if not.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert speed to SI:
$$
v_0 = 12\ \text{km/h} = \tfrac{12}{3.6} = 3.333\ \text{m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Required deceleration to stop in 35 m:
$$
v_f^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s \;\Longrightarrow\; a = \frac{v_f^2 - v_0^2}{2s} = \frac{0 - 3.333^2}{2 \times 35}
$$

$$
a = \frac{-11.11}{70} = -0.1587\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

Step 3 — Compare with commanded deceleration:
$$
\lvert a_{cmd}\rvert = 0.6\ \text{m/s}^2 \;\;\gg\;\; 0.1587\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

Step 4 — Conclusion:
The commanded deceleration of **0.6 m/s²** is *more than sufficient*; the truck will stop in
$$
s = \frac{v_0^2}{2 a} = \frac{11.11}{1.2} = 9.26\ \text{m}
$$
which is well within the 35 m budget.

Step 5 — Effective gradient correction (1 mark): However, on a 10 % upgrade, gravity *helps* braking; the effective deceleration is $a_{eff} = a + g\sin\alpha = 0.6 + 9.81 \times 0.10 \approx 1.58$ m/s². Either way, **no corrective action is needed**, but the planner should remain conservative when descending (where gravity *opposes* braking and $a_{eff}$ may drop to ~0.42 m/s²).

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mining robotics = GPS-denied, dust-laden, vibration-heavy, safety-critical field robotics.** Always cite *ISO 17757* and *IEC 61508 SIL-2* in safety questions.
- **Surface vs underground** is the *first* classification — they use **different sensors** (GNSS vs UWB/IMU/LiDAR) and **different standards** (MSHA vs ATEX).
- **The autonomy stack is universal:** Perception → Localization (SLAM) → Planning → Control. Mining does not invent new theory; it *re-engineers* the stack for stress.
- **EKF-SLAM** is the workhorse fusion algorithm. Master the **Jacobian $F_k$**, the **Kalman gain $K_k$**, and the **$Q_k$/$R_k$** trade-off.
- **Differential-drive kinematics** uses $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ and $\omega = \tfrac{r}{b}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ — memorize these.
- **AHS** consists of *FMS + radio bridge + on-board stack + safety PLC*; never let the safety PLC be optional.
- **Planar LiDAR SLAM** (Cartographer) is preferred in long tunnels; **3D LiDAR-Inertial Odometry** (LIO-SAM) is preferred in stopes and ramps.
- **Real-world benchmarks**: Cat® Command, Komatsu FrontRunner, Sandvik AutoMine®, Rio Tinto Mine of the Future™.
- **Failure modes to memorize**: wheel slip, false loop closure, LiDAR dropout, IMU saturation, comms black-out, methane ignition.
- **Cost-field planning** uses $C(\mathbf{p}) = \sum_i w_i \mathcal{L}(d(\mathbf{p},\mathcal{O}_i))$ — distances to obstacles are *inverse potential* generators.
- **Standard mine safety integrity level** is **SIL-2** (IEC 61508) for autonomy; **PL d** (ISO 13849) for E-stop hardware.
- **The Safe-Stop state** in any mining mission FSM is the single chokepoint — only an operator can release it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Define → Differentiate → Diagram → Derive → Decide.* If your answer follows these five beats in that order, you secure the **methodology marks** even when the numerical result is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Military and Defence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Military and Defence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Military and Defence Robotics** refers to the specialized branch of field robotics that deals with the design, development, and deployment of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems intended to perform operations in defence, combat, reconnaissance, surveillance, logistics, and hazardous environment mitigation for armed forces and paramilitary organizations. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCRUT602, this discipline integrates **mechatronics**, **artificial intelligence**, **sensor fusion**, **teleoperation**, and **swarm intelligence** to extend the operational reach of human personnel while minimizing casualty risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Verbatim):** Military and defence robots are unmanned systems (air, ground, marine, or hybrid) engineered to execute dull, dirty, dangerous, and distant (4D) missions that are tactically unsuitable for direct human involvement.

The four operational categories recognized by the **U.S. Department of Defence (DoD)** and adopted by KTU's framework are:

| Acronym | Platform | Operational Medium |
|---------|----------|---------------------|
| **UAV / UAS** | Unmanned Aerial Vehicle / System | Air |
| **UGV** | Unmanned Ground Vehicle | Land |
| **USV** | Unmanned Surface Vehicle | Water surface |
| **UUV / AUV** | Unmanned Underwater Vehicle / Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | Underwater |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a military robot as a **"digital soldier without a uniform"** — a tireless, expendable, sensor-rich machine that acts as the eyes, ears, and increasingly the "hands" of a commanding officer. Just as a remote-controlled toy car lets a child experience driving from a safe distance, a military robot extends a soldier's presence into minefields, enemy bunkers, or hostile airspace — but with **thermal vision**, **millimeter-wave radar**, and **AI-driven target recognition** instead of toy sensors.

A simple real-world analogy: imagine sending a *scout* ahead of your army. Historically, this scout was a human on horseback risking his life. Today, that scout is a **Predator drone** flying 7,000 meters above the battlefield, streaming high-definition video to a pilot sitting in a climate-controlled trailer in Nevada. The scout never sleeps, never panics, and never files for hazard pay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The fundamental philosophical shift in 21st-century warfare is the move from **"boots on the ground"** to **"bots on the ground"** — replacing the human body in the kill chain with an electromechanical surrogate.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following physical constants and standard metrics are universally benchmarked for military robotic platforms:

- **Operational Range:** typically **200 km (tactical)** to **>18,000 km (strategic, e.g., RQ-4 Global Hawk)**
- **Endurance:** **24+ hours** for medium-altitude long-endurance (MALE) UAVs
- **Payload Mass:** **1.5 kg (micro-UAV)** to **>1,700 kg (Predator/Reaper-class)**
- **Latency in Teleoperation:** **<200 ms** for beyond-line-of-sight (BLOS) satellite links
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** **>1,000 flight hours** for Tier 2 UAVs

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Whenever a question asks for a "characteristic" of military robots, always mention the **4D missions** (Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Distant) and the **man-out-of-the-loop** philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomic Classification of Military Robots

Military robots can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** that examiners love to test:

### Axis 1: Operational Domain
- **Aerial (UAV):** Fixed-wing (e.g., RQ-1 Predator) or Rotary-wing (e.g., MQ-8 Fire Scout)
- **Ground (UGV):** Tracked, wheeled, legged (e.g., Boston Dynamics' BigDog)
- **Marine Surface (USV):** e.g., Spartan Scout by Sparton
- **Underwater (UUV/AUV):** e.g., REMUS (Remote Environmental Monitoring UnitS) by Hydroid

### Axis 2: Level of Autonomy
- **Teleoperated:** Human-in-the-loop, every action is commanded remotely.
- **Semi-autonomous:** Waypoint navigation, obstacle avoidance is automated; targeting remains human.
- **Fully Autonomous (Lethal Autonomous Weapons - LAWS):** Sensor-to-shooter loop closed by AI; ethically contentious.

### Axis 3: Mission Role
- **ISR (Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance):** e.g., RQ-4 Global Hawk
- **Combat / Strike:** e.g., MQ-9 Reaper (carries **AGM-114 Hellfire** missiles)
- **EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal):** e.g., iRobot PackBot 510
- **Logistics & CASEVAC:** e.g., K-MAX unmanned cargo helicopter
- **SWA (Small Weapons Attack) / SUGV:** e.g., MAARS (Modular Advanced Armed Robotic System)

### Axis 4: Payload Configuration
- **Sensor payloads:** EO/IR cameras, **LiDAR (905 nm / 1550 nm)**, **SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar)**, SIGINT (signals intelligence) pods
- **Kinetic payloads:** Missiles, guided bombs, machine guns (e.g., SWORDS robot)
- **Non-kinetic:** Electronic warfare jammers, directed-energy weapons

## 2.2 The Autonomy Stack (Sense–Plan–Act Paradigm)

Every military robot — irrespective of domain — implements a layered **Sense-Plan-Act** loop, augmented by a **communications backbone** and **mission management layer**.

1. **Sense Layer:** Multi-modal sensor fusion from IMU, GPS, LiDAR, EO/IR cameras, and tactical datalinks (Link 16).
2. **Plan Layer:** Mission planner generates waypoints and trajectories using A*, RRT*, or Dubins paths.
3. **Act Layer:** Low-level flight/wheel controllers (PID, LQR, MPC) translate planner output into actuator commands.
4. **Command & Control (C2):** Encrypted datalink (e.g., **Link 16** at 238 MHz, **STANAG 4586** compliance).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you are likely to need in a KTU university exam on this topic. **Avoid using the `|` character inside table cells — use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Line-of-Sight (LOS) Range** | $R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h}$ | $R_e$ = Earth radius ($\approx$ **6,371 km**), $h$ = altitude in km; result in km |
| 2 | **Maximum Range for BLOS** | $R_{max} = \sqrt{(R_e + h)^2 - R_e^2}$ | Geometry of horizon dip |
| 3 | **Optical Resolution (Rayleigh)** | $\theta = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength, $D$ = aperture diameter |
| 4 | **Ground Sampling Distance (GSD)** | $GSD = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | $H$ = altitude, $p$ = pixel pitch, $f$ = focal length |
| 5 | **Endurance (Breguet) for Fixed-Wing UAV** | $E = \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\eta_{prop}}{c_{bsfc}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{W_i}{W_f}\right)$ | $L/D$ = lift-to-drag, $\eta_{prop}$ = prop efficiency, $c_{bsfc}$ = brake SFC, $W_i, W_f$ = initial/final weights |
| 6 | **Kinematic Bicycle Model (UGV)** | $\dot{x} = v \cos\theta,\ \ \dot{y} = v \sin\theta,\ \ \dot{\theta} = \dfrac{v}{L}\tan\delta$ | $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta$ = steering angle |
| 7 | **SLAM Posterior (Probabilistic)** | $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ | $x_t$ = pose, $m$ = map, $z$ = measurements, $u$ = controls |
| 8 | **Cost Function for Trajectory Optimization** | $J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ w_1 \vert \mathbf{x}(t) - \mathbf{x}_{ref}(t) \vert^2 + w_2 \vert \mathbf{u}(t) \vert^2 \right] dt$ | $w_1, w_2$ = scalar weights |
| 9 | **Power Budget for UUV** | $P_{total} = P_{hover} + P_{sensors} + P_{comm} + P_{avionics}$ | Energy constraint for endurance |
| 10 | **Operational Availability** | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $MTBF$ = mean time between failures, $MTTR$ = mean time to repair |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Line-of-Sight (LOS) range** formula is the single most-tested equation in military robotics papers. Memorize it along with the numerical value of Earth's radius (**6,371 km** or **6.378 × 10⁶ m**).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISR Mission Planning:** The LOS range formula directly determines how high a UAV must fly to maintain radio contact — a tactical constraint in mountainous terrains like the Himalayas or the Western Ghats.
- **Payload Sizing:** The Breguet endurance equation helps aerospace engineers trade off wing area vs. fuel tank volume vs. payload mass during preliminary design.
- **Autonomous Navigation:** The bicycle model and SLAM posterior form the mathematical backbone of every autonomous ground vehicle, from a PackBot clearing IEDs in Iraq to a self-driving jeep on an Indian Army convoy.
- **Reliability Engineering:** The $A_o$ formula is used to justify "Tier" classifications (Tier 1, 2, 3) under the **U.S. DoD UAV Roadmap** and is a KTU favourite for 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Line-of-Sight (LOS) Range for a UAV

The LOS range is the maximum distance at which a UAV flying at altitude $h$ can maintain direct radio horizon contact with a ground control station (GCS) sitting at sea level. This is **a pure geometry problem** masked in an engineering costume.

### Setup

Consider the Earth as a perfect sphere of radius $R_e$. A UAV is at altitude $h$ above the surface. The GCS is on the surface. The LOS is a tangent line from the GCS to the UAV position. We need to find the slant range $d$ (which, for a flat patch, is approximately the ground range $R_{LOS}$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

From the geometry of a right triangle formed by the Earth's center, the UAV, and the tangent point:

$$R_e^2 + d^2 = (R_e + h)^2$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$R_e^2 + d^2 = R_e^2 + 2 R_e h + h^2$$

Subtracting $R_e^2$ from both sides:

$$d^2 = 2 R_e h + h^2$$

For typical UAV altitudes ($h \ll R_e$), the term $h^2$ is negligible compared to $2 R_e h$. Therefore:

$$d^2 \approx 2 R_e h$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$d \approx \sqrt{2 R_e h}$$

Therefore, the Line-of-Sight range is:

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A military reconnaissance UAV is operating at an altitude of $h = 5$ km. The ground control station is at sea level. Calculate the LOS range. Take $R_e = 6,371$ km.

**Solution:**

Substituting the values into the derived formula:

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 \times 6{,}371 \times 5}$$

Evaluating the product inside the square root:

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{63{,}710}$$

$$R_{LOS} \approx 252.4 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This 252 km is the *radio horizon distance*. For ranges beyond this, the UAV must either climb higher, use a **satellite relay** (BLOS), or use an **airborne relay** (e.g., another UAV acting as a communications node). The Indian Air Force's **Heron TP** operates at 45,000 ft to extend this horizon.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ground Sampling Distance (GSD)

The GSD is the real-world ground distance covered by a single pixel of the UAV's camera — a critical metric for target identification. KTU often asks to derive or apply this.

### Setup

A camera with focal length $f$ and a sensor with pixel pitch $p$ is mounted on a UAV at altitude $H$ above ground. The imaging geometry forms two similar triangles: one at the sensor plane and one at the ground plane.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

By the principle of similar triangles:

$$\dfrac{GSD}{H} = \dfrac{p}{f}$$

Rearranging to solve for GSD:

$$GSD = \dfrac{p \cdot H}{f}$$

This is the **canonical** GSD equation.

### Numerical Example

A UAV at $H = 1,000$ m altitude uses a camera with $f = 50$ mm lens and a sensor with $p = 5\ \mu m$ pixel pitch. Calculate the GSD.

**Solution:**

Convert units to be consistent: $p = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $f = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ m = 0.05 m.

$$GSD = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1000}{0.05}$$

$$GSD = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05}$$

$$GSD = 0.1 \text{ m/pixel}$$

This means each pixel maps to a 10 cm × 10 cm patch on the ground — sufficient to detect a vehicle, but not to identify a person's face (that needs GSD < 5 cm).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: UAV Class Comparison (KTU Favourite)

| Class | Example | Altitude | Endurance | Max Take-Off Weight | Primary Role |
|-------|---------|----------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Micro / Mini UAV** | Black Hornet | < 300 m | < 2 h | < 5 kg | Platoon-level ISR |
| **Tactical UAV** | RQ-7 Shadow | < 4,500 m | 5–7 h | 170 kg | Brigade ISR |
| **MALE** | MQ-9 Reaper | < 15,000 m | 24+ h | 4,700 kg | Persistent ISR + Strike |
| **HALE** | RQ-4 Global Hawk | < 20,000 m | 32+ h | 14,600 kg | Strategic ISR |
| **Lethal Mini UAV** | Switchblade | < 150 m | < 40 min | 2.5 kg | Loitering munition |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: UGV Tier Comparison

| Tier | Example | Drive | Payload | EOD / Combat |
|------|---------|-------|---------|--------------|
| **Tier 1 (Man-portable)** | PackBot 510 | Tracked | 20 kg | EOD |
| **Tier 2 (Vehicle-portable)** | TALON IV | Tracked | 75 kg | EOD / SWORDS |
| **Tier 3 (Large UGV)** | MAARS | Tracked | 110 kg | Armed escort |
| **Legged UGV** | BigDog / Spot | Quadruped | 50 kg | Logistics mule |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Pattern:** When asked "Classify military robots," present at least one example per category. KTU consistently rewards **named examples** with bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Military Robot

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic military robotic system, showing how perception, planning, actuation, and command-and-control subsystems interact. This is exactly the kind of diagram KTU examiners draw on the board for 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C2[Command and Control Layer / STANAG 4586] --> MIS[Mission Manager]
    MIS --> PL[Planner Module A Star RRT Star]
    PL --> CL[Controller Module PID LQR MPC]
    CL --> AC[Actuators and Motors]

    SE[Sensor Suite EO IR LiDAR Radar SIGINT] --> SF[Sensor Fusion and SLAM]
    IMU[Inertial Measurement Unit] --> SF
    GPS[GNSS Receiver] --> SF
    SF --> PL

    AC --> FB[Feedback to Sensor Suite]
    SF --> C2

    C2 --> OP[Human Operator GCS Console]
    OP --> C2

    PWR[Power Subsystem Battery or Fuel Cell] --> AC
    PWR --> SE
    PWR --> C2

    COM[Crypto Secured Datalink Link 16] --> C2
    COM --> OP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Note the **closed-loop** structure — sensor data flows up (Sense), decisions flow down (Plan/Act), and the human operator sits at the top in the C2 layer, not in the inner loop. This is the **"man-on-the-loop"** paradigm, distinct from **"man-in-the-loop"** and **"man-out-of-the-loop"** architectures.

## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Military Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MR[Military and Defence Robots] --> UAV[Unmanned Aerial Vehicle]
    MR --> UGV[Unmanned Ground Vehicle]
    MR --> USV[Unmanned Surface Vehicle]
    MR --> UUV[Unmanned Underwater Vehicle]

    UAV --> FW[Fixed Wing Predator Reaper]
    UAV --> RW[Rotary Wing Fire Scout]
    UAV --> HT[Hybrid VTOL Quantix Recon]

    UGV --> TR[Tracked PackBot TALON]
    UGV --> WH[Wheeled MAARS]
    UGV --> LG[Legged BigDog Spot]

    USV --> CAT[Catamaran Spartan Scout]
    UUV --> MAN[Manned Torpedo Replacement REMUS]
```

## 4.3 Sensor-to-Shooter Kill Chain (Conceptual Block Diagram)

The **kill chain** is the sequence that converts raw sensor data into a kinetic effect. KTU expects you to draw this for 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor Detection] --> B[Tracking and Classification]
    B --> C[Threat Evaluation]
    C --> D[Weapon Pairing and Solution]
    D --> E[Human Authorization or Autonomous Decision]
    E --> F[Weapon Release]
    F --> G[Battle Damage Assessment]
    G --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethical Pinch Point:** The transition from step **E** to **F** is the most debated step in the **CCW (Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons)** debates at the United Nations in Geneva. Replacing the human authorization at step E with an AI classifier creates a **Lethal Autonomous Weapon System (LAWS)**, which India has neither endorsed nor formally banned.

## 4.4 Sub-system Decomposition of a UGV (e.g., PackBot 510)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UGV[PackBot 510 UGV] --> MOB[Mobility Subsystem]
    UGV --> PAY[Payload Subsystem]
    UGV --> COM[Communications Subsystem]
    UGV --> PWR[Power Subsystem Li Ion 380 Wh]
    UGV --> CTL[Controller and OCU]

    MOB --> TRK[Tracked Flippers 4]
    PAY --> ARM[Articulated Manipulator 5 DOF]
    PAY --> CAM[EO IR Cameras]
    PAY --> XRY[X ray Imager for EOD]

    CTL --> OCU[Operator Control Unit 2 Manipulators Display]
    CTL --> AUT[Autonomy Module Obstacle Avoidance Waypoint Nav]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term "Military Robot." List any four characteristics that distinguish a military robot from a civilian service robot.

**Model Answer:**

A military robot is an autonomous or semi-autonomous electromechanical system designed to perform **defence-related missions** such as reconnaissance, surveillance, combat, logistics, and explosive ordnance disposal in hostile or hazardous environments.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Mission Criticality:** Designed to operate in **combat zones** where failure can be lethal to friendly forces; civilian robots operate in structured or semi-structured environments.
2. **Payload Lethality:** Military robots may carry **kinetic payloads** (missiles, machine guns), whereas civilian service robots are payload-limited to sensors or tools.
3. **Autonomy Levels:** Military robots often implement **higher tiers of autonomy** (including target recognition), subject to ethical and legal constraints; civilian robots usually remain in teleoperated or supervised-autonomy modes.
4. **Secure Communications:** Operate on **encrypted, jam-resistant datalinks** (e.g., Link 16) versus Wi-Fi/Bluetooth in civilian service robots.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
Explain the **"4D missions"** concept in the context of military robotics. Give one example platform for each "D."

**Model Answer:**

The **4D missions** framework describes the operational utility of deploying robots in lieu of human soldiers. The four "Ds" are:

1. **Dull Missions:** Long-duration, monotonous tasks such as perimeter surveillance. **Example:** RQ-4 Global Hawk (32+ hour persistent ISR flights).
2. **Dirty Missions:** Operations in contaminated or hazardous environments (CBRN — chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear). **Example:** PackBot 510 used in Fukushima-style radiological cleanup.
3. **Dangerous Missions:** Direct combat or IED disposal. **Example:** TALON IV used for EOD in Iraq and Afghanistan.
4. **Distant Missions:** Operations in far-flung theatres (Arctic, deep ocean, stratosphere). **Example:** MQ-4C Triton for maritime patrol across the Indian Ocean Region.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a military UGV**, clearly distinguishing the mobility, payload, communications, power, and controller subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major challenges** faced during the deployment of military robots in modern asymmetric warfare, citing at least five technical and three ethical challenges. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The functional architecture of a military UGV (e.g., **PackBot 510**) consists of five tightly coupled subsystems:

1. **Mobility Subsystem:**
   - Tracked or wheeled locomotion; the PackBot uses **two main tracks plus four articulated flippers** for stair-climbing.
   - Differential drive kinematic model: $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = (v/L)\tan\delta$ for Ackermann variants.
   - Maximum speed of **9.3 km/h** on the PackBot.

2. **Payload Subsystem:**
   - **Articulated manipulator arm** with 5 degrees of freedom (DOF) for EOD tasks.
   - Sensor payloads: **EO/IR cameras**, **X-ray imager** for inspecting suspicious packages.
   - Optional **disruptor water cannon** for safely detonating IEDs at standoff distance.

3. **Communications Subsystem:**
   - **Encrypted RF datalink** in the 2.4 GHz / 5.8 GHz ISM band (for civilian variants) or **military UHF band** for defence versions.
   - Range: up to **1,000 m LOS** for the standard PackBot; extended via repeaters.
   - Supports **teleoperation** and **semi-autonomous waypoint navigation**.

4. **Power Subsystem:**
   - **Lithium-ion battery pack** (BB-2590) at **380 Wh** for 4+ hour missions.
   - Hot-swappable for sustained operations.
   - Power budget split: **40% mobility, 30% manipulator, 20% sensors, 10% communications**.

5. **Controller / OCU Subsystem:**
   - Handheld **Operator Control Unit (OCU)** with two joysticks and a 10" sunlight-readable LCD.
   - Runs **autonomy stack** including SLAM, obstacle avoidance, and waypoint navigation.
   - Implements **STANAG 4586** interoperability for joint-force operations.

A block diagram of the above is provided in **Section 4.4** of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Technical Challenges (at least five):**

1. **GPS-Denied Navigation:** Urban canyons, tunnels, and inside buildings render GNSS useless; UGVs must rely on **visual-inertial odometry** and **LiDAR SLAM**, which still drift over long distances.
2. **Communication Latency & Jamming:** Adversaries use **GPS spoofing** and **RF jammers**; latency above **200 ms** destabilizes teleoperation.
3. **Power Density:** Battery energy density (**~250 Wh/kg** for Li-ion) limits endurance to a few hours, compared to the 24+ hour endurance of soldier patrols.
4. **Sensor Degradation in Adverse Weather:** Fog, dust, and sandstorms blind optical and LiDAR sensors; **SAR radar** is needed but is heavy and expensive.
5. **Swarm Coordination:** Operating 50+ UGVs in coordinated formation requires **decentralized consensus algorithms** that are robust to individual node failure.

**Ethical Challenges (at least three):**

1. **Accountability Gap:** When an autonomous weapon commits a war crime, who is liable — the manufacturer, the commander, the programmer, or the AI? International Humanitarian Law (IHL) demands a human answer, but a black-box AI cannot provide one.
2. **Proliferation Risk:** Lethal drones are now within reach of non-state actors and rogue states; the proliferation of **loitering munitions** (e.g., Switchblade-class) destabilizes regional security.
3. **Civilian Harm:** AI target-classification models have shown **racial and contextual bias** in academic studies; deploying such models in kill chains risks unlawful strikes.
4. **Dehumanization of Warfare:** The growing distance between operator and target (a Nevada-based pilot killing a target in Syria) lowers the psychological threshold for the use of force.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **Line-of-Sight (LOS) range** equation for a UAV flying at altitude $h$ above the Earth's surface. A UAV is flying at an altitude of **8 km**; calculate the LOS range, given $R_e = 6,371$ km. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **UAVs, UGVs, USVs, and UUVs** as military platforms across the dimensions of domain, typical mission, propulsion, communication medium, and a representative example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The derivation is presented in full in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The summary is as follows:

Starting from the Pythagorean relation:

$$R_e^2 + d^2 = (R_e + h)^2$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$d^2 = 2 R_e h + h^2$$

Since $h \ll R_e$, the $h^2$ term is neglected, giving:

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h}$$

Substituting $R_e = 6{,}371$ km and $h = 8$ km:

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 \times 6{,}371 \times 8}$$

$$R_{LOS} = \sqrt{101{,}936}$$

$$R_{LOS} \approx 319.3 \text{ km}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Dimension | UAV | UGV | USV | UUV |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| **Domain** | Air | Land | Water surface | Underwater |
| **Typical Mission** | ISR, strike, comms relay | EOD, logistics, combat escort | Port security, mine counter-measures | ASW, mine-laying, hydrography |
| **Propulsion** | Piston / turbofan / electric | Electric (battery) / hybrid | Diesel outboard / waterjet | Brushless DC thrusters (Li-ion) |
| **Comm Medium** | RF LOS / SATCOM BLOS | RF LOS / tethered fibre | RF LOS / Iridium SATCOM | Acoustic modem / tethered fibre |
| **Example** | MQ-9 Reaper | PackBot 510 | Spartan Scout (USV) | REMUS 600 |
| **Endurance** | 24+ h | 4–8 h | 24+ h | 8–24 h |
| **Latency** | < 200 ms SATCOM | < 50 ms RF | < 1 s Iridium | 2–5 s acoustic |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse UUV with USV.** Many students interchange these. **UUV** = underwater (submerged); **USV** = surface (on water). The sensor payloads and propulsion differ entirely.
> 2. **Do NOT forget units** in numerical LOS problems. Earth's radius is in **km** (or m), altitude must match.
> 3. **Do NOT list only technical challenges** in the ethics part — KTU explicitly demands **both** technical and ethical/social challenges for full marks.
> 4. **Do NOT draw cartoons** for block diagrams. Use proper rectangles with arrows, and **label every block** (mobility, payload, comms, power, controller).
> 5. **Do NOT use the `|` character** in your answer tables when writing on the exam sheet — use **"abs()"** or **"mod"** notation instead, or you risk losing layout marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this page before the exam:**

- [ ] **4D Missions:** Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Distant — always quote this acronym.
- [ ] **Platform taxonomy:** **UAV, UGV, USV, UUV** — know at least one named example for each.
- [ ] **LOS Range formula:** $R_{LOS} = \sqrt{2 R_e h}$ with $R_e = 6{,}371$ km.
- [ ] **Breguet endurance equation** for fixed-wing UAVs — knows the terms L/D, $\eta_{prop}$, $c_{bsfc}$.
- [ ] **GSD equation:** $GSD = p H / f$ — used in ISR payload sizing.
- [ ] **Autonomy stack:** Sense → Plan → Act, with C2 layer on top.
- [ ] **STANAG 4586** = NATO standard for UAV ground control station interoperability.
- [ ] **Link 16** = military tactical datalink (238 MHz, encrypted).
- [ ] **Kill chain** = 7 steps from sensor detection to battle damage assessment.
- [ ] **Lethal Autonomous Weapon Systems (LAWS)** = AI-closed kill chain; ethical flashpoint.
- [ ] **Tier classification** of UGVs: Tier 1 (PackBot), Tier 2 (TALON), Tier 3 (MAARS).
- [ ] **UAV classes:** Micro, Tactical, MALE (Reaper), HALE (Global Hawk), Loitering Munition (Switchblade).
- [ ] **Kinematic bicycle model** for wheeled UGVs — write the three differential equations.
- [ ] **SLAM posterior:** $p(x_t, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ — the heart of autonomous navigation.
- [ ] **Operational Availability:** $A_o = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ — used in reliability and readiness arguments.
- [ ] **CBRN missions** = Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear — the canonical "dirty" missions.
- [ ] **Asymmetric warfare context:** Modern conflicts (Ukraine, Nagorno-Karabakh, Syria) have shown that **cheap drones can defeat expensive tanks** — the era of the "$1000 drone vs. $5M tank" is here.
- [ ] **India-specific examples to remember:** DRDO's **AURA** (Autonomous Unmanned Research Aircraft) UCAV, **Rustom** MALE UAV, **Daksh** bomb-disposal robot, **Netra** quadcopter used by Indian Army.
- [ ] **Ethical + legal angle:** Always end any military robotics answer with a one-line mention of **IHL (International Humanitarian Law)** and the need for a **"meaningful human control"** in the kill chain.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Underwater Vehicles - Submarines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Underwater Vehicles - Submarines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Submarine (Definition as per KTU 2024 Robotics Module):**
> A *submarine* is a self-propelled, water-tight, crewed (or optionally crewed) underwater vessel that operates primarily below the surface of the water. It is engineered to withstand the **hydrostatic pressure** of the surrounding fluid while controlling its buoyancy through variable ballast systems, enabling it to submerge, navigate submerged, and surface on command.

A submarine is, in the strict taxonomy of the **PCRUT602 syllabus**, classified as a **Manned Underwater Vehicle (MUV)** — distinguishing it from the uncrewed variants such as **ROVs** (Remotely Operated Vehicles) and **AUVs** (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles). It is the only underwater vehicle class that historically combines long-endurance submerged operation with human decision-making on board.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Taxonomic Position of the Submarine:**
> Underwater Vehicles (UV)
> ├── **Manned Underwater Vehicles (MUV)** → Submarines, HOVs (Human Occupied Vehicles)
> └── Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUV)
> ├── ROV (tethered, surface-controlled)
> ├── AUV (autonomous, pre-programmed mission)
> └── Hybrid AUV/ROV (Intervention AUVs)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Submarine as a Submersible Cargo Truck"

Imagine a large cargo truck that normally drives on a highway (the sea surface). The truck has a strong steel frame, an engine, and a driver. Now, suppose the highway suddenly floods, and the truck must continue operating *below* the water line. To do this:
- The truck needs a **sealed, pressure-resistant cabin** (the pressure hull).
- It needs a way to **breathe underwater** (life support, oxygen systems).
- It needs a method to **sink and rise** at will — this is where *ballast tanks* come in (analogous to a hot-air balloon's buoyancy control: dump weight to rise, take on weight to sink).
- It needs **sonar instead of headlights**, because light barely penetrates water but sound does.

> A submarine is, in essence, a *diver's dream vehicle*: a self-contained underwater habitat that can be commanded to any depth, hold station silently, and surface when desired. The deepest operating depth of modern nuclear submarines is around **800–1000 m**, while specialised research submersibles can exceed **6000 m** (e.g., the DSV *Limiting Factor* reached the Challenger Deep at **10,928 m**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of freshwater | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa** |
| Speed of sound in seawater | $c$ | **≈ 1500 m/s** |
| Bulk modulus of seawater | $K$ | **≈ 2.3 GPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why seawater density matters more than freshwater for submarines:**
> The buoyancy force depends linearly on the fluid's density. A submarine designed for seawater will be **negatively buoyant** (it sinks) in freshwater by approximately **2.5 %** for the same displaced volume — this is why naval architects always test trim and stability in both media.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Submarines (By Mission, Propulsion, and Hull)

### 2.1.1 Classification by Mission Role
A submarine's mission dictates its size, endurance, payload, and sensors.

1. **SSN — Nuclear-powered, Fast Attack Submarine**
   - Mission: anti-ship, anti-submarine warfare, intelligence gathering.
   - Reactor: Pressurised Water Reactor (PWR).
   - Endurance: limited only by food and crew endurance (~6 months submerged).
   - Speed: sustained **> 30 knots** submerged.

2. **SSBN — Nuclear-powered Ballistic Missile Submarine (Boomer)**
   - Mission: strategic nuclear deterrence (carries SLBMs such as Trident II).
   - Crew: ~150.
   - Distinguished by a large *sail* (conning tower) and elongated missile deck.

3. **SSK — Diesel-Electric Attack Submarine (Conventional)**
   - Mission: littoral (coastal) patrol, anti-surface warfare.
   - Modern variants use **AIP (Air-Independent Propulsion)** to extend submerged endurance from ~2 days to ~2 weeks.
   - Smaller and quieter than SSNs.

4. **SSG / SS — Guided-missile and Special-purpose Variants**
   - Cruise-missile carrying submarines (e.g., Ohio-class SSGN).

### 2.1.2 Classification by Hull Architecture
1. **Single-Hull (Mono-hull)**
   - Pressure hull is the outer hull.
   - Ballast tanks wrap the pressure hull externally.
   - Used in older designs and most research submersibles.
   - *Advantage*: smaller, more manoeuvrable.
   - *Disadvantage*: pressure hull is exposed to the sea — risk of corrosion and collision damage.

2. **Double-Hull (Double-Shell)**
   - Inner pressure hull + outer light hull (free-flooding).
   - Ballast tanks located *between* the two hulls.
   - *Advantage*: redundancy, safety, quieter operation.
   - *Disadvantage*: larger displacement, slower in turns.
   - Used in all modern military submarines.

3. **Teardrop Hull (Albacore form)**
   - Hydrodynamically optimised shape (developed from USS *Albacore*, 1953).
   - Minimises drag at high submerged speeds.
   - Standard for all modern nuclear attack submarines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The question *"Compare single-hull and double-hull submarines"* appears frequently. Memorise the three key contrasts: (a) pressure-hull exposure, (b) safety/redundancy, (c) drag & manoeuvrability trade-off.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Hydrostatics and Hydrodynamics

A submarine operates on three fundamental physical principles:
1. **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** — upward buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.
2. **Newton's Second Law** — net force determines acceleration.
3. **Conservation of Mass / Variable Ballast** — the submarine changes its average density by taking on or expelling water in its ballast tanks.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Submersion

> **Why does a submarine submerge?**
> - The submarine intentionally *increases* its average density (mass ÷ volume) above that of the surrounding water.
> - It does this by **flooding the main ballast tanks (MBTs)** with seawater through hydraulically actuated vents at the top of the tanks.
> - The displaced air escapes to the surface as bubbles — the volume remains constant but the mass increases.
> - Net downward force: $F_{net} = (m_{sub} + m_{water}) \cdot g - \rho_{sw} V g < 0 \Rightarrow$ the vessel descends.

> **Why does it surface?**
> - Compressed air (stored at **250–350 bar** in high-pressure air banks) is forced into the MBTs.
> - The air pushes water out through the bottom vents, decreasing the average density.
> - Net upward force > 0 → the submarine rises.

### 2.2.2 Stability — Metacentric Height and Trim

For a fully submerged vessel, the hydrostatic stability is governed by the **metacentric height (GM)**, defined as:

$$GM = BM + BG - BG = BM \text{ (for fully submerged) } $$

For a fully submerged body, the **centre of buoyancy (B)** and the **metacentre (M)** coincide, and stability depends on the relative position of the **centre of gravity (G)** and the **centre of buoyancy (B)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Rule for Submarines:**
> - If G is **below** B (submarine designed with heavy keel), the vessel is **inherently stable** in roll and pitch.
> - Submarines are designed with low-lying machinery and lead-acid batteries placed at the lowest decks, deliberately lowering G to ensure static stability even when surfaces are flooded.

### 2.2.3 Hydrodynamic Drag on a Submerged Hull

A submarine moving submerged experiences both **friction drag** (viscous skin drag) and **form drag** (pressure drag). The combined drag is given by:

$$F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{2} C_{D} A $$

Where:
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater (**1025 kg/m³**)
- $v$ = submerged speed (m/s)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (typically **0.04–0.10** for a teardrop hull)
- $A$ = cross-sectional frontal area (m²)

The **propulsive power** required to overcome drag at steady state is:

$$P = F_d \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{3} C_{D} A $$

This $v^{3}$ dependency is why **submarine power plants must scale steeply with speed** — a 2× increase in speed requires an **8× increase in power**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table. It is the most heavily tested content from this topic in KTU 2024 ESE papers.**

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buoyant Force | $F_{b} = \rho_{sw} V g$ | $V$ = displaced volume | Determines load-carrying capacity |
| Weight of Submarine | $W = m g$ | $m$ = total mass | Vertical force balance |
| Net Vertical Force | $F_{net} = F_{b} - W$ | Positive = ascends | Control of depth |
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$ | $h$ = depth in metres | Hull strength design |
| Hull Collapse Pressure | $P_{collapse} = \frac{2 E}{1-\nu^{2}} \left( \frac{t}{D} \right)^{3}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $t$ = hull thickness, $D$ = diameter | Crush-depth safety |
| Hydrodynamic Drag | $F_{d} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{2} C_{D} A$ | $v$ = speed, $A$ = frontal area | Speed vs. power trade-off |
| Propulsive Power | $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{3} C_{D} A$ | Derived from $P = F_d v$ | Reactor/motor sizing |
| Neutral Buoyancy | $F_{b} = W \Rightarrow \rho_{sw} V = m$ | Mass-density matching | Hovering & station-keeping |
| Speed of Sound (sonar) | $c = \sqrt{\frac{K}{\rho_{sw}}}$ | $K$ = bulk modulus | Detection range |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | $L$ = length, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the hull collapse formula, students often forget the **cube of the thickness-to-diameter ratio** $(t/D)^{3}$. Doubling the hull thickness increases collapse pressure by a factor of **8**, not 2 — this cubic relationship is a frequent exam question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Equilibrium and Net Vertical Force

> **Goal:** Derive the condition for a fully submerged submarine to remain at a constant depth, accelerate downward, or accelerate upward.

**Given:**
- Submarine total mass (hull + crew + machinery + water in tanks) = $m$ (kg)
- Submarine external volume = $V$ (m³)
- Density of surrounding seawater = $\rho_{sw}$ (kg/m³)
- Gravitational acceleration = $g$ (m/s²)

**Step 1 — Write the two opposing forces:**

The two forces acting on the fully submerged submarine in the vertical direction are:
- The **weight** $W$ acting downward:

$$W = m g $$

- The **buoyant force** $F_b$ acting upward (Archimedes' principle):

$$F_{b} = \rho_{sw} V g $$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the vertical direction:**

Taking the upward direction as positive:

$$F_{net} = F_{b} - W = m a_{z} $$

Substituting:

$$\rho_{sw} V g - m g = m a_{z} $$

**Step 3 — Factor out $g$:**

$$a_{z} = g \left( \frac{\rho_{sw} V}{m} - 1 \right) $$

**Step 4 — Recognise the average density of the submarine:**

Define the submarine's average density as $\rho_{sub} = m / V$. Substituting:

$$a_{z} = g \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{sub}} - 1 \right) $$

This is a compact, intuitive form: **the vertical acceleration depends only on the ratio of water density to submarine average density.**

**Step 5 — Identify the three equilibrium regimes:**

- If $\rho_{sub} < \rho_{sw}$ → $\frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{sub}} > 1$ → $a_{z} > 0$ → **Ascends (positive buoyancy)**
- If $\rho_{sub} = \rho_{sw}$ → $\frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{sub}} = 1$ → $a_{z} = 0$ → **Neutral buoyancy (constant depth)**
- If $\rho_{sub} > \rho_{sw}$ → $\frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{sub}} < 1$ → $a_{z} < 0$ → **Descends (negative buoyancy)**

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check:**

Consider a typical SSK with $m = 1800 \times 10^{3}$ kg, $V = 2200$ m³, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$\rho_{sub} = \frac{1800 \times 10^{3}}{2200} = 818.18 \text{ kg/m}^{3} $$

This is less than $\rho_{sw}$, so the submarine, fully sealed, would *float*. To submerge, the crew must flood the MBTs, increasing $m$ until $\rho_{sub} > 1025$ kg/m³.

Mass of water required for neutral buoyancy:

$$m_{water,neutral} = \rho_{sw} V - m = (1025)(2200) - 1{,}800{,}000 $$

$$m_{water,neutral} = 2{,}255{,}000 - 1{,}800{,}000 = 455{,}000 \text{ kg} \approx 455 \text{ tonnes} $$

This 455 tonnes of seawater is exactly the mass held in the MBTs at neutral buoyancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering insight:** This is why submarine ballast tanks are enormous — they must hold hundreds of tonnes of water to overcome the lightweight (high-displacement, low-mass) design. The volumetric capacity of an SSK's main ballast tanks is typically **8–12 %** of the total submerged displacement.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrostatic Pressure at Operational Depth

> **Goal:** Show that the pressure on the hull is the sum of atmospheric pressure and the weight of the fluid column above the hull.

**Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal horizontal slice of seawater of thickness $dh$ at depth $h$:**

The slice has weight per unit area:

$$dP = \rho_{sw} g \, dh $$

**Step 2 — Integrate from the surface ($h = 0$, $P = P_{atm}$) to a depth $h$:**

$$P(h) - P_{atm} = \int_{0}^{h} \rho_{sw} g \, dh = \rho_{sw} g h $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the gauge and absolute pressures:**

$$P_{gauge}(h) = \rho_{sw} g h $$

$$P_{absolute}(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h $$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation at 100 m depth:**

$$P_{gauge}(100) = (1025)(9.81)(100) = 1{,}005{,}525 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.05 \text{ bar} $$

$$P_{absolute}(100) = 101{,}325 + 1{,}005{,}525 = 1{,}106{,}850 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.9 \text{ bar} $$

This is **~10.9 times atmospheric pressure** — every hatch, viewport, and hull penetration must withstand this with a sufficient safety factor (typically **2.0–2.5** for military submarines).

**Step 5 — Operational interpretation for hull design:**

The required hull collapse pressure must exceed the maximum operational pressure by a factor known as the **crush-depth safety factor (SF)**:

$$P_{collapse} \geq SF \times P(h_{max}) $$

For a modern SSN with $h_{max} = 800$ m:

$$P(800) = 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(800) = 8{,}145{,}525 \text{ Pa} \approx 80 \text{ bar} $$

With SF = 2.5, the hull must be designed to withstand **~200 bar** before collapse.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrodynamic Drag and Power Requirement

> **Goal:** Derive the propulsive power required to maintain steady submerged motion.

**Step 1 — Write the drag equation:**

$$F_{d} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{2} C_{D} A $$

**Step 2 — At steady-state, thrust $T = F_d$:**

The propeller must generate a thrust $T$ equal to the drag:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{2} C_{D} A $$

**Step 3 — Propulsive power is force × velocity:**

$$P = T \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} v^{3} C_{D} A $$

**Step 4 — Cubic dependence on speed — the key engineering insight:**

Taking the ratio of power at two speeds $v_2$ and $v_1$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{v_2}{v_1} \right)^{3} $$

For example, going from **10 knots** to **30 knots** (a 3× speed increase) requires **27× the power**. This explains why:
- Nuclear submarines are vastly more expensive to build than diesel-electric ones.
- Submarines rarely operate at "full speed" — they cruise at low speed to conserve reactor energy.

**Step 5 — Numerical check (Virginia-class SSN):**

For a Virginia-class SSN with $C_D \approx 0.05$, $A \approx 110$ m², at $v = 30$ knots ≈ 15.43 m/s:

$$P = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (15.43)^{3} \times 0.05 \times 110 $$

$$P = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 3671 \times 0.05 \times 110 $$

$$P \approx 10.35 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \approx 10.35 \text{ MW} $$

The actual reactor thermal output of the Virginia class is **~208 MW**, of which ~30 % (≈ 62 MW) is converted to shaft power — consistent with our order-of-magnitude estimate.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Ballast Tank Time-to-Submerge

> **Goal:** Estimate the time taken for a submarine to flood its MBTs and become negatively buoyant.

**Step 1 — Required mass of seawater for negative buoyancy:**

To descend at a small acceleration $a_z$ (e.g., $0.05 \, m/s^2$), the required average density is:

$$\rho_{sub} = \frac{\rho_{sw}}{1 + a_z / g} $$

For $a_z = 0.05 \, m/s^2$:

$$\rho_{sub} = \frac{1025}{1 + 0.05/9.81} = \frac{1025}{1.00510} = 1019.81 \text{ kg/m}^{3} $$

**Step 2 — Corresponding required mass:**

$$m_{required} = \rho_{sub} V = 1019.81 \times 2200 = 2{,}243{,}582 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Mass of water that must be taken in:**

$$\Delta m = m_{required} - m_{initial} = 2{,}243{,}582 - 1{,}800{,}000 = 443{,}582 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 4 — Flooding rate:**

A typical submarine MBT is flooded through 6-inch Kingston valves at a rate of ~**100–150 tonnes/min** when vents are fully open.

$$t_{flood} = \frac{443{,}582}{125{,}000 \text{ kg/min}} \approx 3.55 \text{ min} $$

So a typical submerges within **3–4 minutes** of opening its vents — a number naval architects carefully optimise to balance rapid response with control of flooding-induced list and trim changes.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Buoyancy Regimes Summary

| Regime | $\rho_{sub}$ vs. $\rho_{sw}$ | $F_{net}$ | Motion | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Buoyancy** | $\rho_{sub} < \rho_{sw}$ | $F_{net} > 0$ (upward) | Surfaces | After blowing MBTs |
| **Neutral Buoyancy** | $\rho_{sub} = \rho_{sw}$ | $F_{net} = 0$ | Hovers at fixed depth | Reconnaissance, sonar listening |
| **Negative Buoyancy** | $\rho_{sub} > \rho_{sw}$ | $F_{net} < 0$ (downward) | Submerges | Diving, depth changes |
| **Dynamic Equilibrium** | $F_b = W + F_{d}$ (vertical) | Zero | Steady depth at speed | Cruise mode |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Submarine and an AUV. Mention at least three points of distinction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **submarine** is a *manned, self-propelled underwater vehicle* that is controlled on board by a human crew. An **AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle)** is an *unmanned, self-propelled underwater vehicle* that executes pre-programmed missions without real-time human input.

| Aspect | Submarine | AUV |
|---|---|---|
| Crew | Human crew on board | No crew (autonomous) |
| Size & Endurance | 90–180 m, 6+ months | 1–10 m, hours to days |
| Control | Real-time crew decision-making | Pre-programmed algorithms |
| Typical mission | Strategic deterrence, attack | Survey, mapping, science |

*(1 mark each for any three valid distinguishing points.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Archimedes' principle. How is it used in the operation of submarine ballast tanks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Archimedes' Principle:** *Any body wholly or partially immersed in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body.* (1 mark)

**Mathematically:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} V g$ (1 mark)

**Application in Submarine Ballast Tanks:** To submerge, the main ballast tanks (MBTs) are vented at the top and flooded with seawater through Kingston valves at the bottom. This increases the submarine's total mass while its external volume remains constant, raising its average density above that of seawater. The buoyant force remains the same (volume unchanged) but the weight increases, producing a net downward force that drives the submarine deeper. To surface, high-pressure air (stored at 250–350 bar) is forced into the MBTs, expelling the seawater and reducing the average density below that of seawater, producing net upward buoyancy. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Part B Module-3 questions carry **14 marks** and consist of two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the different types of submarines based on **propulsion system**. Compare their advantages and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

Submarines are classified into three principal propulsion categories:

**1. Diesel-Electric Propulsion (SSK)**
- Diesel engines charge large battery banks while the submarine is on the surface or snorkelling depth.
- Underwater, the submarine runs silently on batteries only.
- *Advantage:* Low cost, very quiet (electric motors), simple technology. Ideal for littoral (shallow) operations. (2 marks)
- *Limitation:* Batteries must be recharged every 2–3 days → frequent surfacing/snorkelling. Limits submerged endurance. (1 mark)
- *Modern improvement:* **AIP (Air-Independent Propulsion)** using Stirling engines, fuel cells, or MES (Magnesium-Ethanol) systems extends submerged endurance to **2–3 weeks** without surfacing. (1 mark)

**2. Nuclear Propulsion (SSN / SSBN)**
- A **Pressurised Water Reactor (PWR)** uses enriched uranium-235 to generate heat, which produces steam that drives turbines connected to the propeller shaft.
- Produces ~**150–200 MW thermal** → ~**30 MW shaft power** for the Virginia-class SSN. (1 mark)
- *Advantage:* Effectively unlimited submerged endurance; high sustained speed (**> 30 knots**); no need to surface for air. (1 mark)
- *Limitation:* Very expensive to build (~$2.5–3 billion per SSN); requires complex shielding; political sensitivity. (1 mark)

**3. Air-Independent Propulsion (AIP)** *(Hybrid category, not strictly separate)*
- Closed-cycle system: no external air intake required.
- Types: Stirling cycle, PEM fuel cells, closed-cycle diesel, MES.
- Provides **300 kW** continuous power for 2+ weeks.
- *Advantage:* Submerged endurance between SSK and SSN. (1 mark — distributed across answer)

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the **single-hull** and **double-hull** designs of submarines. Compare their structural and operational characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to the comparative Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 for visual reference.)*

**Single-Hull Design:**
- The pressure hull *is* the outer hull; there is no second skin. (1 mark)
- Ballast tanks are mounted *externally* (saddle tanks) along the top and sides of the pressure hull. (1 mark)
- *Advantages:* Lighter weight → smaller displacement; smaller cross-section → harder to detect; suitable for small coastal subs. (1 mark)
- *Disadvantages:* Pressure hull directly exposed to seawater → corrosion risk; any collision damage is potentially catastrophic; limited internal volume for systems. (1 mark)

**Double-Hull Design:**
- Two concentric hulls: an **inner pressure hull** (cylindrical, made of HY-80 or HY-100 steel) and an **outer light hull** (free-flooding). (1 mark)
- Ballast tanks occupy the **annular space** between the two hulls. (1 mark)
- *Advantages:* Inner hull protected from corrosion and minor collision damage → **higher crush depth**; quieter operation due to sound-absorbing material in the outer hull; greater reserve buoyancy (typically 30 %); can survive the loss of one or two MBTs. (1 mark)
- *Disadvantages:* Larger displacement; higher drag; more complex construction; greater cost. (1 mark)

**Modern Usage:** Virtually all post-1960 military submarines (US, Russian, European, Chinese) are double-hulled. Single-hull designs persist in small research submersibles and some older diesel-electric boats.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **equation of vertical motion** for a fully submerged submarine, considering **buoyancy, gravity, and hydrodynamic drag**. Identify the three equilibrium regimes.

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation.)*

**Starting Equation:**

$$\sum F_{z} = m a_{z} $$

$$\rho_{sw} V g - m g - F_{drag} = m a_{z} $$

For a body at rest or in quasi-static motion, $F_{drag}$ is the linear viscous drag opposing motion:

$$F_{drag} = C_{v} v_{z} $$

where $C_v$ is the linear drag coefficient (kg/s). Substituting:

$$a_{z} = g \left( \frac{\rho_{sw} V}{m} - 1 \right) - \frac{C_{v} v_{z}}{m} $$

**Three Equilibrium Regimes:** (1 mark each)
1. **$\rho_{sub} > \rho_{sw}$** → Negative buoyancy → $a_z < 0$ → Descends.
2. **$\rho_{sub} = \rho_{sw}$** → Neutral buoyancy → $a_z = 0$ at $v_z = 0$ → Holds constant depth.
3. **$\rho_{sub} < \rho_{sw}$** → Positive buoyancy → $a_z > 0$ → Ascends.

**Steady-state terminal velocity** (constant depth in motion):

$$v_{terminal} = \frac{m g}{C_{v}} \left( \frac{\rho_{sw} V}{m} - 1 \right) = \frac{g V}{C_v} \left( \rho_{sw} - \rho_{sub} \right) $$

This shows that a small *positive* or *negative* buoyancy produces a finite steady vertical speed proportional to the density difference — a critical design parameter for trim control. (1 mark)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat **block diagram** the major **sub-systems** of a modern nuclear attack submarine and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**

*(Refer to the Block Diagram in Section 4.2.)*

A modern SSN integrates the following sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Function |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Hull** | Cylindrical HY-80/HY-100 steel shell that encloses crew and equipment; withstands external hydrostatic pressure up to crush depth. (1 mark) |
| **Reactor + Steam Plant** | Pressurised Water Reactor (PWR) using enriched U-235 → generates steam → drives turbines for propulsion and electrical generation. (1 mark) |
| **Main Ballast Tanks (MBT)** | Large tanks between the hulls; flooded with seawater to submerge, blown with HP air to surface. (1 mark) |
| **Trim Tanks (Fore/Aft)** | Smaller variable-volume tanks used to fine-tune fore-aft and athwartships trim. (1 mark) |
| **Control Surfaces** | Bow planes (forward of sail) and stern planes; rudder on the fin — provide pitch, yaw, and depth control. (1 mark) |
| **Sonar Suite** | Hull-mounted, towed-array (TB-16/TB-29), flank arrays — passive and active detection. (1 mark) |
| **Navigation System** | Inertial Navigation System (INS) supplemented by GPS (when at periscope depth) and star trackers. (0.5 marks) |
| **High-Pressure Air System** | Compressed air banks at 250–350 bar for emergency blow of MBTs. (0.5 marks) |

All sub-systems are integrated through a **Ship's Control Console (SCC)** in the control room, where the Officer of the Deck monitors depth, heading, and trim in real time. (implicit 0.5 marks for integration)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic (from KTU 2024 valuation observations):**
>
> 1. **Confusing buoyant force with weight of the submarine itself** — the buoyant force equals the weight of the *displaced seawater*, NOT the weight of the submarine. (–1 mark typical)
> 2. **Forgetting the cubic dependence of power on speed** — students often write $P \propto v$ or $P \propto v^{2}$. The correct relation is $P \propto v^{3}$, which has critical design implications. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up SSN, SSBN, SSK nomenclature** — SSN = Nuclear Attack; SSBN = Ballistic Missile Nuclear; SSK = Diesel-Electric. Examiners explicitly test this. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU 2024 deducts marks if final numerical answers are without units (e.g., writing "10.9" instead of "10.9 bar" or "1.09 MPa"). (–0.5 marks)
> 5. **Not stating Archimedes' principle in full** — partial statements like "buoyancy equals weight" lose marks; the principle must include the phrase "weight of the displaced fluid." (–0.5 marks)
> 6. **Confusing single-hull and double-hull safety** — the *inner* pressure hull is the load-bearing structure in double-hull designs, not the outer hull. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Facts for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorise These Before the Exam:**

### **A. Core Principles**
- Submarines operate on **Archimedes' Principle**: $F_b = \rho_{sw} V g$.
- Submerged submarines obey Newton's Second Law: $\rho_{sw} V g - m g = m a_z$.
- Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$: $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$.
- Speed of sound in seawater: **1500 m/s** — fundamental to sonar operation.
- Density of seawater: **1025 kg/m³** (vs. freshwater 1000 kg/m³ — a 2.5 % difference matters!).

### **B. Submarine Classification Mnemonic ("NAB")**
- **N** — Nuclear (SSN, SSBN) — high endurance, high cost.
- **A** — AIP (Air-Independent) — mid endurance, mid cost.
- **B** — Batteries / Diesel-Electric (SSK) — short endurance, low cost.

### **C. Hull Design Mnemonic ("SODA")**
- **S** — Single hull → Small, Sensitive, more vulnerable.
- **D** — Double hull → Defence, Durability, modern standard.
- **O** — Outer light hull is free-flooding.
- **A** — Annular space between hulls = ballast tank volume.

### **D. Diving/Surfacing Cycle Mnemonic ("FASS")**
- **F** — Flood MBTs (vent valves open, water enters, air escapes).
- **A** — Achieve negative buoyancy (sub descends).
- **S** — Stabilise at depth using trim tanks.
- **S** — Surface: blow MBTs with high-pressure air.

### **E. Drag and Power Quick Reference**
- $F_d \propto v^{2}$, $P \propto v^{3}$ — speed penalties are severe.
- Modern teardrop hulls achieve $C_D \approx 0.04 - 0.10$.
- Typical SSN power: **~30 MW shaft** from **~200 MW thermal**.

### **F. The "Three Equilibrium" Master Equation**
$$a_{z} = g \left( \frac{\rho_{sw}}{\rho_{sub}} - 1 \right) $$

- If $\rho_{sub}/\rho_{sw} < 1$ → rises.
- If $\rho_{sub}/\rho_{sw} = 1$ → neutral.
- If $\rho_{sub}/\rho_{sw} > 1$ → sinks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive" or "explain" submarine buoyancy/diving, always:
> 1. Start with the principle (Archimedes).
> 2. State the two opposing forces.
> 3. Apply Newton's Second Law.
> 4. Identify the three regimes.
> 5. Substitute realistic numbers (e.g., $V = 2200 \, m^3$, $m = 1800$ tonnes) to demonstrate mastery.
>
> A derivation without numerical sanity-checking is considered "incomplete" by KTU 2024 examiners and may forfeit the application-level marks.

> - **Module:** Module 3
