<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Submersible (Underwater Vehicle):** A submersible is a specialized robotic or manned vehicle engineered to operate beneath the surface of water, capable of withstanding elevated hydrostatic pressure while performing tasks such as observation, manipulation, surveying, and intervention. In the context of Field and Service Robotics, submersibles are broadly classified into three categories: **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs)**, **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs)**, and **Human-Occupied Vehicles (HOVs)**.

The term *submersible* is technically distinct from a *submarine*: a submersible is a vehicle that is launched from a surface vessel and operates primarily underwater, often with limited independent surface capability, while a submarine is a fully self-sufficient warship with both surface and underwater operational envelopes.

### 1.2 KTU-Syllabus Classification of Submersibles

| Class | Full Form | Power Source | Control Link | Typical Depth |
|-------|-----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| ROV | Remotely Operated Vehicle | Surface (via tether) | Tethered, real-time | Up to **6,000 m** |
| AUV | Autonomous Underwater Vehicle | Onboard battery | Pre-programmed / autonomous | Up to **11,000 m** |
| HOV | Human-Occupied Vehicle | Onboard battery | Manned piloting | Up to **10,928 m** (Trieste) |
| Hybrid ROV/AUV | — | Hybrid (battery + tether) | Tethered or autonomous | Up to **4,500 m** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Scuba Diver with Cameras" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a scuba diver holding a high-definition camera underwater. The diver experiences water pressure on their body, must control their movement precisely against currents, and uses fins and a buoyancy control device (BCD) to stay afloat or sink. A submersible is essentially a "mechanical scuba diver": it has a **pressure hull** (the BCD/dry suit), **thrusters** (the fins), **sensors** (the eyes and ears), and a **buoyancy system** (the weights and floats). Where a diver thinks and reacts, the submersible's onboard computer or remote operator takes over the decision-making.

### 1.4 Why Submersibles Matter in Field Robotics

Submersibles are deployed in environments that are:
- **Inaccessible to humans** (deep ocean trenches like the Mariana Trench at ~10,994 m)
- **Hazardous** (nuclear reactor cooling pools, oil pipeline interiors)
- **Time-critical** (submarine rescue, hull inspection)
- **Scientifically valuable** (coral reefs, hydrothermal vents, shipwrecks)

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard reference values used throughout submersible engineering:
> - Density of seawater (average): **ρ_sw ≈ 1,025 kg/m³**
> - Density of fresh water: **ρ_fw ≈ 1,000 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
> - Atmospheric pressure at sea level: **P_atm ≈ 101,325 Pa ≈ 1 bar**
> - Hydrostatic pressure gradient: **≈ 1 bar per 10 m of seawater depth**
> - Speed of sound in seawater: **c ≈ 1,500 m/s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

A submersible must simultaneously satisfy four competing engineering constraints, often visualized as the **"Submersible Design Diamond"**:

1. **Hydrostatic Equilibrium** — neutral buoyancy to avoid sinking/floating uncontrollably.
2. **Hydrodynamic Maneuverability** — controllable thrust in all 6 degrees of freedom (DOF).
3. **Pressure Integrity** — a hull that survives crushing pressure.
4. **Mission Endurance** — sufficient battery or tether capacity.

### 2.2 Hydrostatic Analysis — Archimedes' Principle Applied

> [!NOTE]
> **Archimedes' Principle:** Any object fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object.

The vertical force balance on a stationary, neutrally buoyant submersible is:

$$F_{buoyancy} = W_{submersible}$$

where $F_b = \rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$ and $W = m \cdot g$.

For **trim** (pitch balance) and **stability**, the **metacenter** of the vehicle must lie above the **center of gravity (CG)**.

### 2.3 Hydrodynamic Forces and the 6-DOF Equation of Motion

A submersible moving in water experiences six independent forces and moments. The standard formulation uses the **SNAME (Society of Naval Architects and Marine Engineers) notation**:

$$M \dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau + \tau_{disturbance}$$

where each term represents a physical effect:

- $M$ — Rigid-body + **added mass** inertia matrix
- $C(\nu)$ — Coriolis and centripetal matrix
- $D(\nu)$ — Hydrodynamic damping matrix
- $g(\eta)$ — Restoring forces (gravity + buoyancy)
- $\tau$ — Control input from thrusters
- $\nu$ — Body-fixed linear/angular velocity vector $\{u, v, w, p, q, r\}$
- $\eta$ — Earth-fixed position/orientation $\{x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi\}$

### 2.4 Thruster Configurations

Most ROVs and AUVs use a **4-thruster vectored** or **5-thruster** arrangement:

- 4 thrusters at 45° angles in the horizontal plane → surge, sway, yaw control
- 1 vertical thruster → heave control
- Optional lateral thrusters → station keeping

### 2.5 Added Mass — The "Invisible Drag" of Acceleration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a body accelerates underwater, it must also accelerate the surrounding water column. This produces an *apparent* additional inertia called **added mass** ($m_a$). For a sphere of radius $r$, $m_a = \frac{1}{2} \rho \pi r^3$. This term can **exceed** the vehicle's own mass and is critical to controller design.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for Submersibles — Must Memorize**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | Pa | Hull strength design |
| 2 | $F_b = \rho V g$ | Buoyant force | N | Buoyancy balance |
| 3 | $W = m g$ | Weight of vehicle | N | Equilibrium check |
| 4 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ | Drag force (quadratic) | N | Forward resistance |
| 5 | $F_D = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes drag (laminar, small sphere) | N | Low-speed flow |
| 6 | $m_{eff} = m + m_a$ | Effective mass (with added mass) | kg | Dynamics |
| 7 | $GZ = \overline{GM} \sin\phi$ | Righting arm (stability) | m | Roll/pitch stability |
| 8 | $\overline{GM} = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}$ | Metacentric height | m | Stability criterion |
| 9 | $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | Metacentric radius | m | Transverse stability |
| 10 | $v_{max} = \dfrac{P_{thr}}{\sqrt{2 \rho F_D / A}}$ | Theoretical max velocity | m/s | Performance |
| 11 | $t_{mission} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}}$ | Mission endurance | s | AUV planning |
| 12 | $f_{sonar} = \dfrac{c}{4 R \tan(\theta/2)}$ | Maximum unambiguous range (sonar) | m | Sonar design |

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

These equations drive every stage of submersible design:
- **Hull thickness** is sized by hydrostatic pressure (Formula 1).
- **Ballast tanks** are sized using buoyancy/weight balance (Formulas 2, 3).
- **Energy budgets** for AUVs use Formula 11 to predict mission duration.
- **Stability analysis** (Formulas 7–9) ensures the vehicle does not capsize.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth

**Goal:** Show that pressure increases linearly with depth.

**Step 1 — Start with a fluid element of thickness $dh$ and cross-section $A$ at depth $h$.**
The element is in static equilibrium: forces above push down, pressure below pushes up, gravity pulls down.

**Step 2 — Force balance:**
$$P(h) \cdot A - P(h+dh) \cdot A - \rho g \cdot A \cdot dh = 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplify by dividing by $A$ and rearranging:**
$$dP = -\rho g \, dh$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from surface (where $P = P_{atm}$, $h = 0$) to depth $h$:**
$$\int_{P_{atm}}^{P(h)} dP = -\rho g \int_{0}^{h} dh$$

**Step 5 — Result:**
$$\boxed{P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-style):** Find pressure at the average ocean depth of the Mariana Trench challenger deep (10,994 m) in seawater.

$$P = 101{,}325 + (1{,}025)(9.81)(10{,}994)$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 110{,}530{,}768.5$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.105 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 1{,}091 \text{ bar}}$$

This is why submersibles use **titanium** or **syntactic foam** pressure hulls.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Condition for Neutral Buoyancy

**Goal:** Find the displaced volume required for a submersible of given mass to achieve neutral buoyancy.

**Step 1 — Definition of neutral buoyancy:** Vehicle neither rises nor sinks → vertical net force is zero.

$$F_{buoyancy} - W = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute buoyant force and weight:**
$$\rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g - m \cdot g = 0$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $g$ and solve for $V_{displaced}$:**
$$\boxed{V_{displaced} = \frac{m}{\rho_{fluid}}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** A 500 kg ROV operates in seawater ($\rho = 1{,}025$ kg/m³). Find required displaced volume.

$$V = \frac{500}{1{,}025} = 0.4878 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:** The hull and any fixed buoyancy foam together must displace at least **0.4878 m³** of seawater. If the actual displacement is less, the vehicle sinks; if more, it floats.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drag Force Using Dimensional Analysis

**Goal:** Show that hydrodynamic drag on a bluff body at moderate Reynolds number scales as $v^2$.

**Step 1 — Identify relevant physical quantities:**
Drag $F_D$ depends on fluid density $\rho$, velocity $v$, and a characteristic area $A$.

**Step 2 — Form a dimensionally consistent expression (Buckingham Pi):**
$$F_D \propto \rho^a v^b A^c$$

**Step 3 — Equate dimensions $[MLT^{-2}]$:**
$$[M L T^{-2}] = [M L^{-3}]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [L^2]^c$$

This yields the system:
- Mass: $1 = a$
- Length: $1 = -3a + b + 2c$
- Time: $-2 = -b$

**Step 4 — Solve:** From time equation, $b = 2$. From mass equation, $a = 1$. From length equation: $c = (1 - 2 + 3)/2 = 1$.

**Step 5 — Final functional form:**
$$\boxed{F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_d\, \rho\, A\, v^2}$$

where $C_d$ is the dimensionless drag coefficient (typically 0.6–1.2 for AUV-shaped bodies).

**Step 6 — Numerical example:** A 100 kg AUV with frontal area 0.1 m², $C_d = 0.8$, cruising at 1.5 m/s in seawater.

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 1{,}025 \times 0.1 \times (1.5)^2$$

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 0.8 \times 1{,}025 \times 0.1 \times 2.25$$

$$\boxed{F_D \approx 92.25 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 7 — Required thrust to maintain cruise:**
$$F_{thrust} = F_D \Rightarrow P_{thrust} = F_{thrust} \times v = 92.25 \times 1.5 = 138.4 \text{ W}$$

This is the cruise power demand — used to size batteries in AUVs.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stability Criterion via Metacentric Height

**Goal:** Derive the metacentric height $\overline{GM}$ and show that $\overline{GM} > 0$ ensures stability.

**Step 1 — Define key points (referenced to the keel K):**
- $B$ — center of buoyancy
- $G$ — center of gravity
- $M$ — metacenter (intersection of vertical through tilted $B$ and original centerline)
- $\overline{KB}$, $\overline{KG}$, $\overline{BM}$ — vertical distances

**Step 2 — For a small tilt $\phi$, the new center of buoyancy shifts horizontally. The horizontal shift equals $\overline{BM} \sin\phi$, where $\overline{BM}$ is given by:**
$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I_{waterplane}}{V_{displaced}}$$

**Step 3 — The righting arm is:**
$$GZ = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} \cdot \sin\phi - \overline{KG} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \tan\phi$$

**Step 4 — For small angles ($\sin\phi \approx \phi$, $\cos\phi \approx 1$, $\tan\phi \approx \phi$):**
$$GZ \approx (\overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}) \sin\phi = \overline{GM} \sin\phi$$

**Step 5 — Stability criterion:**
$$\boxed{\overline{GM} > 0 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \overline{KG} < \overline{KB} + \overline{BM}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical meaning:** If the metacenter $M$ lies above the center of gravity $G$, the buoyancy and gravity forces form a **restoring couple** that returns the vehicle to upright. If $G$ is above $M$, the vehicle capsizes.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps each derived equation to its governing physics, typical numerical magnitude, and KTU relevance.

| Equation | Governing Physics | Order of Magnitude | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Fluid statics | 1 bar / 10 m depth | Hull design, sensor rating |
| $V_{displaced} = m/\rho$ | Archimedes' principle | 0.5 m³ for 500 kg ROV | Buoyancy module sizing |
| $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$ | Momentum transfer | ~100 N at 1.5 m/s | Thruster sizing |
| $\overline{GM} > 0$ | Rigid-body rotational dynamics | Typically 5–20 cm for AUVs | Trim and stability analysis |
| $t_{mission} = E_{batt}/P_{avg}$ | Energy conservation | Hours for AUVs | Mission planning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Submersible System Architecture (Block-Level)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define (i) ROV and (ii) AUV. State two key differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** An underwater robotic vehicle that is tethered to a surface vessel, receives power and control commands through the tether, and is operated in real time by a human pilot.
- **(ii) AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle):** A self-powered underwater vehicle that carries its own energy source, executes pre-programmed missions, and operates without a physical tether.

**Two Key Differences:**

| Aspect | ROV | AUV |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Power | Supplied via tether from surface | Onboard battery |
| Control | Real-time human pilot | Autonomous / pre-programmed |
| Endurance | Unlimited (surface power) | Limited by battery capacity |
| Bandwidth | High (fiber optic tether) | Low (acoustic modem) |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`
**State Archimedes' principle. A 750 kg ROV operates in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³). Compute the minimum displaced volume for neutral buoyancy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Archimedes' Principle:** *Any body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.*

For neutral buoyancy: $F_b = W$

$$\rho V g = m g$$

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{750}{1025} = 0.7317 \text{ m}^3$$

The ROV must displace at least **0.7317 m³** of seawater.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure formula $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h$. A submersible is rated for **3,000 m** depth in seawater. Compute the pressure at this depth and discuss the implications for hull material selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the 6 degrees of freedom (6-DOF) of a submersible using the SNAME notation. For each DOF, name the corresponding linear or angular velocity symbol and identify which thruster configuration would control it. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Derivation:**
Consider a fluid element of cross-section $A$ and thickness $dh$ at depth $h$, in static equilibrium.
$$P(h) A - P(h+dh) A - \rho g A dh = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{dP}{dh} = -\rho g$$
Integrating from $h = 0$ (where $P = P_{atm}$) to $h$:
$$P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

**Numerical Evaluation:**

$$P = 101{,}325 + (1{,}025)(9.81)(3{,}000)$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 30{,}165{,}750$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 3.027 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 299 \text{ bar}}$$

**Hull Material Implications:**
At 300 bar, aluminum alloy hulls (yield ~300 MPa) would require prohibitively thick walls. Instead, designers use **titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V)** with yield strength ~880 MPa, allowing thin-walled spherical hulls. Alternative: **ceramic spheres** or **syntactic foam** for buoyancy modules.

#### Model Solution (b):

| DOF | Symbol | Linear/Angular | Description | Controlling Thruster |
|-----|--------|----------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Surge | $u$ | Linear | Forward/backward along X | Aft pair at 45° |
| Sway | $v$ | Linear | Lateral port/starboard along Y | Forward pair at 45° |
| Heave | $w$ | Linear | Vertical up/down along Z | Vertical thruster |
| Roll | $p$ | Angular | Rotation about X | Lateral thruster pair |
| Pitch | $q$ | Angular | Rotation about Y | Bow + stern vertical |
| Yaw | $r$ | Angular | Rotation about Z | Differential port/stbd thrust |

**Standard 5-thruster layout:** 4 vectored thrusters (45° in horizontal plane) for surge/sway/yaw + 1 vertical thruster for heave. Pitch and roll are typically controlled by **moving the center of gravity** using internal ballast shifters rather than dedicated thrusters, to save power.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** A 1,200 kg AUV with frontal area $A = 0.25$ m² and drag coefficient $C_d = 0.9$ operates at a constant cruise speed of 2.0 m/s in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³). Compute: **(i)** the drag force, **(ii)** the required thrust power, and **(iii)** the mission endurance if the onboard battery stores 5 kWh of usable energy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, explain the **metacentric height** concept. State and prove the stability criterion $\overline{GM} > 0$ for a submersible, and discuss what happens if $\overline{GM} < 0$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**(i) Drag Force:**
$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2 = 0.5 \times 0.9 \times 1{,}025 \times 0.25 \times (2.0)^2$$
$$F_D = 0.5 \times 0.9 \times 1{,}025 \times 0.25 \times 4.0$$
$$\boxed{F_D = 461.25 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Thrust Power:**
$$P_{thrust} = F_D \cdot v = 461.25 \times 2.0$$
$$\boxed{P_{thrust} = 922.5 \text{ W} \approx 0.92 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Mission Endurance:**
$$t = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{5{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{922.5 \text{ W}}$$
$$\boxed{t \approx 5.42 \text{ hours}}$$

> At this speed, the AUV covers a distance of $2.0 \times 5.42 = 10.84$ km in a single mission.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Metacentric Height Diagram (conceptual description):**

```
        M  ← Metacenter (above G)
        |
        | GZ = GM sin φ
        G  ← Center of Gravity
        |
        B  ← Center of Buoyancy (tilted position)
       /|
      / |
     /  | KB = vertical distance from keel to buoyancy center
    /   |
   / φ  |
  K───── ← Keel (reference)
```

**Derivation:** When the vehicle tilts by a small angle $\phi$, the buoyancy center $B$ shifts laterally. The new buoyancy vector passes through $B$, while the gravity vector continues to act through $G$. The horizontal separation $GZ$ is the **righting arm**:

$$GZ = \overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \tan\phi$$

For small $\phi$ ($\sin\phi \approx \phi$, $\cos\phi \approx 1$):

$$GZ \approx (\overline{KB} + \overline{BM} - \overline{KG}) \sin\phi = \overline{GM} \sin\phi$$

The restoring moment is $M_{restore} = W \cdot GZ = W \cdot \overline{GM} \sin\phi$.

**Stability Criterion:**
- If $\overline{GM} > 0$: restoring moment acts opposite to tilt → **stable** (vehicle returns upright).
- If $\overline{GM} = 0$: **neutral** (no restoring moment; vehicle stays at tilted angle).
- If $\overline{GM} < 0$: **unstable**; the buoyancy force creates an **overturning moment** that increases the tilt → vehicle capsizes.

**Physical Consequence of $\overline{GM} < 0$:** The vehicle may roll uncontrollably, actuators saturate, and mission abort is forced. Designers ensure $\overline{GM} \approx 5$–$20$ cm for typical AUVs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $P_{atm}$** when computing absolute pressure at depth. Many students write $P = \rho g h$ alone — this costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in drag formula** — students often use $C_d = 0.5$ for a streamlined body when the question specifies a bluff one. Read carefully.
> 3. **Confusing surge/sway with heave** in 6-DOF diagrams. Surge = forward/back; sway = side; heave = vertical. Do NOT mix up.
> 4. **Stability derivation sign error** — the negative sign in $\overline{KG}$ is critical. Skip it and lose the entire 4-mark derivation.
> 5. **Battery units** — express energy in Wh (not Joules) for AUV endurance; examiners expect kWh or Wh.
> 6. **No diagram in (b)** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks for missing metacentric height sketches.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Submersibles**

- ☐ Three classes of submersibles: **ROV, AUV, HOV** — know power, control, and depth for each.
- ☐ Hydrostatic pressure: $P(h) = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ — pressure increases by **~1 bar every 10 m** in seawater.
- ☐ Buoyancy / weight balance: $\rho V g = m g$ for neutral buoyancy.
- ☐ Drag force (quadratic regime): $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} C_d \rho A v^2$ — used for thruster sizing.
- ☐ **Added mass** is critical — accelerating underwater is harder than in air because of fluid inertia.
- ☐ **6-DOF SNAME notation**: $(u, v, w, p, q, r)$ for body velocities; $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ for Earth-fixed.
- ☐ **Metacentric height** $\overline{GM} > 0$ ensures rotational stability.
- ☐ Standard seawater density to memorize: **1025 kg/m³**; g = 9.81 m/s².
- ☐ AUV endurance: $t = E_{batt}/P_{avg}$ — drag power grows with $v^3$.
- ☐ Hull materials for deep submersibles: **titanium alloy, ceramic spheres, syntactic foam** (NOT aluminum below ~1000 m).
- ☐ ROVs are tethered and human-controlled; AUVs are autonomous and battery-limited.
- ☐ Sonar equation key variable: speed of sound in water ≈ 1500 m/s.
- ☐ Common applications: oil & gas pipeline inspection, shipwreck archaeology, mine countermeasures, oceanographic survey, hull cleaning.
- ☐ KTU favourite numerical: **pressure at depth** — practice at least 3 numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle):** An ROV is a tethered, unoccupied, highly maneuverable underwater mobile robot operated by a human at the surface who controls its motion and onboard systems through an umbilical cable (tether) that transmits power, command signals, and sensor data between the surface control station and the submerged vehicle.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT602 – Field and Service Robotics)** syllabus, an ROV is classified under the broader family of **Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUVs)** and is distinguished from Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) by the presence of a **real-time human-in-the-loop control link**. The standard operational depth ranges from **300 m (Work-Class ROVs)** to **6,000 m (Deep-Sea ROVs)**, with the deepest operational ROVs reaching the Challenger Deep at approximately **10,935 m**.

| Classification | Depth Rating | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Observation-Class ROV** | < 300 m | Inspection, aquaculture, search & recovery |
| **Work-Class ROV** | 300 m – 3,000 m | Oil & gas intervention, heavy lifting |
| **Deep-Sea ROV** | > 3,000 m | Scientific exploration, trench surveys |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why the tether matters:** Unlike AUVs which are free-swimming and battery-limited, ROVs receive **continuous electrical power** through the umbilical. This eliminates dive-time limits and provides **near-infinite endurance**, making them the preferred platform for industrial subsea intervention.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are scuba diving, but instead of carrying tanks, you sit comfortably in a boat at the surface. You wear a virtual reality headset that lets you *see* through the eyes of a small underwater drone. In your hands, you hold a joystick — push it forward, and the drone moves forward; twist it, and the drone rolls. A long "life-support cable" runs from the boat down to the drone, carrying electricity down and live video up. That drone, with that cable, controlled by *you* — that is an ROV.

**The analogy maps cleanly to engineering:**
- The boat = **Surface Control Unit (SCU)**
- The cable = **Umbilical / Tether**
- The headset + joystick = **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**
- The drone = **ROV Body (Subsea Vehicle)**

## 1.3 Core Metrics Highlighted

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key performance metrics every KTU student must memorize:**
> - **Operating Depth:** typically **300 m – 6,000 m**
> - **Payload Capacity:** **5 kg (observation)** to **1,500 kg (work-class)**
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** typically **4 to 7 DOF**
> - **Tether Diameter:** **10 mm – 50 mm**
> - **Power Transmission:** **440 VAC / 3-phase / up to 25 kW** (industrial ROVs)
> - **Latency:** **< 50 ms** for stable teleoperation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture Decomposition

An ROV system is not a single robot — it is a **distributed mechatronic system** with three major subsystems:

### A. Surface Subsystem
- Control console (pilot's HMI)
- Power supply unit (PSU)
- Tether management system (TMS) — a winch and cable drum
- Data acquisition and recording computers
- Video overlay and sonar processing units

### B. Umbilical (Tether)
- Carries **electrical power** (high voltage AC)
- Carries **fiber-optic data lines** (control + telemetry)
- Provides **mechanical load-bearing** (supports vehicle weight)
- Acts as a **safety retrieval line**

### C. Subsea Subsystem (The ROV Itself)
- **Frame** (buoyant syntactic foam + structural chassis)
- **Thrusters** (typically 4 to 8, vectored configuration)
- **Sensors** (cameras, sonar, depth, altimeter, IMU, DVL)
- **Manipulator arm(s)** (for intervention tasks)
- **Onboard electronics** (pressure housings, micro-controllers)

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom Analysis

A standard work-class ROV has the following DOF:

| DOF | Motion | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surge (forward/backward) | 2 horizontal thrusters |
| 2 | Sway (lateral left/right) | 1–2 horizontal thrusters |
| 3 | Heave (up/down) | 2–4 vertical thrusters |
| 4 | Yaw (rotation about vertical axis) | Differential thrust or dedicated thruster |
| 5 (optional) | Roll (rotation about longitudinal axis) | Dedicated roll thruster |
| 6 (optional) | Pitch (rotation about lateral axis) | Manipulator or active ballast |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield Formula Table for ROV Module — Memorize for ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{\text{thrust}} = \rho \cdot Q \cdot (V_{\text{exit}} - V_{\text{inlet}})$ | Momentum-based thrust of a thruster | N |
| 2 | $F_{\text{drag}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | Hydrodynamic drag force | N |
| 3 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha$ | Torque on rotating body (yaw equation) | N·m |
| 4 | $P_{\text{cable}} = I^{2} \cdot R_{\text{cable}}$ | Power loss in umbilical (I²R heating) | W |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 6 | $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ | Density of seawater | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $F_{\text{buoy}} = \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot V_{\text{disp}}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes) | N |
| 8 | $W_{\text{ROV}} = m \cdot g$ | Weight of ROV in air | N |
| 9 | $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{buoy}} - W_{\text{ROV}}$ | Net vertical force | N |
| 10 | $v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F_{\text{thrust}}}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A}}$ | Maximum velocity under thrust balance | m/s |
| 11 | $\omega_{\text{cable}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | Cable wave velocity (tether dynamics) | m/s |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$ | Thruster efficiency | % |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Boundary conditions for KTU problems:**
> - Seawater density: $\rho_{\text{sw}} = \mathbf{1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (not 1,000)
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$
> - Drag coefficient $C_d$ for bluff bodies: $C_d \approx \mathbf{0.8 - 1.2}$
> - Drag coefficient for streamlined bodies: $C_d \approx \mathbf{0.04 - 0.1}$

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Equations 1–2** determine whether an ROV can hover stably in a current (thrust-to-drag ratio).
- **Equation 4** is critical for deep ROVs — a 6 km tether can lose **40 % of surface power** as heat.
- **Equation 5** governs pressure-housing design: every **10 m of depth adds ~1 atm (101 kPa)**.
- **Equation 11** is essential for tether management — if surface commands arrive faster than $\omega_{\text{cable}}$, shockwaves (snap loads) damage the vehicle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Velocity of an ROV (Thrust-Drag Equilibrium)

**Problem Setup:** A streamlined observation-class ROV has frontal area $A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.1$, and total thrust capability $F_{\text{thrust}} = 50 \text{ N}$. Find the maximum forward velocity in seawater.

**Step 1 — Identify the physical principle.**

At maximum velocity, the ROV is in steady-state (no acceleration). Newton's Second Law demands:

$$
\sum F = m \cdot a = 0 \quad \text{(steady state)}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the force balance.**

The only two horizontal forces are thrust (forward) and drag (backward):

$$
F_{\text{thrust}} - F_{\text{drag}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the drag equation.**

$$
F_{\text{thrust}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for velocity.**

$$
v^{2} = \dfrac{2 \cdot F_{\text{thrust}}}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot F_{\text{thrust}}}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A}}
$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 50}{1{,}025 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.05}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{5.125}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{19.512} \approx 4.42 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to knots (engineering convention).**

$$
v_{\text{knots}} = \dfrac{4.42}{0.5144} \approx 8.59 \text{ knots}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is consistent with the **2–4 m/s** operational speed of typical observation ROVs. Work-class ROVs trade speed (1–1.5 m/s) for higher payload capacity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrostatic Pressure on an ROV Housing

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the pressure acting on a spherical pressure housing at **3,000 m depth** in the ocean.

**Step 1 — State the hydrostatic equation.**

$$
\Delta P = \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 2 — Identify atmospheric contribution.**

Total absolute pressure = hydrostatic + atmospheric:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{sw}} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the constants.**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 101{,}325 + 1{,}025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3{,}000
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 101{,}325 + 30{,}165{,}750
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 30{,}267{,}075 \text{ Pa} \approx 30.27 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to atmospheres.**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{30{,}267{,}075}{101{,}325} \approx 298.7 \text{ atm}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is why deep-sea ROV housings use **titanium or aluminum syntactic foam** rated to **450+ atm**, with safety factors of **1.5–2.0**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cable Power Loss in a Deep-Tether ROV

**Problem Setup:** A 6,000 m umbilical with resistance $R_{\text{cable}} = 60 \text{ }\Omega$ carries a current of $I = 25 \text{ A}$. Compute power dissipated as heat.

**Step 1 — Apply Joule's First Law.**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = I^{2} \cdot R
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = (25)^{2} \cdot 60 = 625 \cdot 60 = 37{,}500 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as fraction of typical surface supply.**

If surface supplies $P_{\text{surface}} = 100{,}000 \text{ W}$:

$$
\text{Loss fraction} = \dfrac{37{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100\% = 37.5\%
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is why modern deep-sea ROVs use **high-voltage AC transmission (3,000–5,000 VAC)** to reduce current and hence $I^{2}R$ losses.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thruster Vectoring for Yaw Control

For a 4-thruster vectored configuration (two pairs at $\pm 45°$ to the longitudinal axis), the yaw torque is:

$$
\tau_{\text{yaw}} = r \cdot (F_1 - F_2 + F_3 - F_4) \cdot \cos(45°)
$$

where $r$ is the moment arm. Differential thrust on opposite pairs produces pure yaw rotation without translation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between an ROV and an AUV.**

**Model Answer:**
An ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle) is connected to the surface via a tether (umbilical cable) that provides continuous power and two-way communication, allowing a human pilot to control it in real-time. An AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) is tetherless and operates independently using onboard batteries and pre-programmed mission logic. ROVs offer unlimited endurance but limited mobility due to the cable; AUVs offer high mobility but limited mission duration due to battery constraints.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three sensors commonly used in an ROV.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Imaging Sonar** — used for obstacle detection and navigation in low-visibility water where cameras fail.
2. **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** — measures the ROV's velocity relative to the seafloor for precise positioning.
3. **Pressure-depth sensor** — measures hydrostatic pressure to determine the vehicle's current depth with high accuracy.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical ROV system, clearly identifying the surface, umbilical, and subsea subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation for the maximum forward velocity of an ROV under steady-state conditions. Given: $F_{\text{thrust}} = 200 \text{ N}$, $C_d = 0.08$, $A = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Compute the numerical answer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):
The ROV architecture consists of three coupled subsystems:

1. **Surface Subsystem:** Houses the pilot console, power supply, tether winch, and data recorders. The pilot receives live video and sonar feeds and issues joystick commands.
2. **Umbilical:** A reinforced cable carrying high-voltage AC power and fiber-optic data lines. It also provides mechanical support and a retrieval safety line.
3. **Subsea Vehicle:** Contains the buoyant frame, thruster array, pressure-housed electronics, manipulator arm, and sensor suite (cameras, sonar, IMU, depth sensor).

Signal flow: Pilot commands $\rightarrow$ Surface encoder $\rightarrow$ Tether $\rightarrow$ Onboard MCU $\rightarrow$ Thruster drivers $\rightarrow$ Vehicle motion. Sensor data returns via the reverse path.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):
**Step 1:** At steady state, $\sum F = 0$, so:

$$
F_{\text{thrust}} = F_{\text{drag}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $F_{\text{drag}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2}$:

$$
F_{\text{thrust}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $v$:

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F_{\text{thrust}}}{\rho C_d A}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values:

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 200}{1{,}025 \times 0.08 \times 0.08}}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400}{6.56}}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \sqrt{60.98} \approx 7.81 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of 2 in the numerator of the drag equation. Writing $F_{\text{drag}} = \rho C_d A v^{2}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2}$ will cost you **2 marks** immediately. Also, use $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — not 1,000 — unless the problem explicitly states fresh water.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the function of the umbilical (tether) in an ROV system. Discuss three engineering challenges associated with long umbilicals in deep-sea ROVs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ROV operates at a depth of 2,500 m. The pressure housing has an outer diameter of 0.4 m and is a sphere. The housing material is aluminum alloy with yield strength $250 \text{ MPa}$. Using a safety factor of 2.0, check whether the housing is safe. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):
**Functions of the umbilical:**
1. Transmits electrical power from the surface PSU to the subsea vehicle.
2. Carries bidirectional data (commands down, telemetry up) via fiber-optic lines.
3. Acts as a mechanical support and safety retrieval line for the vehicle.

**Engineering challenges for long umbilicals:**
1. **Power Loss ($I^2 R$ heating):** A 6 km cable can dissipate 30–40 % of transmitted power as heat, reducing efficiency. Mitigation: use high-voltage AC transmission.
2. **Cable Dynamics and Snap Loads:** The tether acts like a spring; sudden pilot commands create tension shockwaves. Mitigation: use Tether Management Systems (TMS) and dampeners.
3. **Cable Weight and Drag:** The tether's own weight and hydrodynamic drag can overload the vehicle. Mitigation: use buoyant cables with syntactic foam.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):
**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 2,500 m:**

$$
P_{\text{hydro}} = \rho g h = 1{,}025 \times 9.81 \times 2{,}500 = 25{,}138{,}125 \text{ Pa} \approx 25.14 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Add atmospheric pressure:**

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 25.14 + 0.101 \approx 25.24 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Hoop stress in a thin spherical shell:**

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 t}
$$

Assuming wall thickness $t = 0.02 \text{ m}$, radius $r = 0.2 \text{ m}$:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{25.24 \times 10^{6} \times 0.2}{2 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{5.048 \times 10^{6}}{0.04} = 126.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety factor:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\text{SF}} = \dfrac{250}{2.0} = 125 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

Since $\sigma_{\text{actual}} = 126.2 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{\text{allow}} = 125 \text{ MPa}$, the housing **fails marginally** by **1.0 %**. The design should either increase wall thickness to $t \geq 0.0202 \text{ m}$ or use a higher-grade alloy.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark Loss:** Many students forget to add **atmospheric pressure** to hydrostatic pressure when computing total collapse load. Also, always state the **safety factor explicitly** in your answer; KTU examiners award **1 mark** for naming SF and **1 mark** for correct application.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROV = tethered, human-controlled underwater robot** — the tether is its defining feature.
- **Three subsystems:** Surface Control Unit, Umbilical, Subsea Vehicle.
- **Seawater density $\rho = 1{,}025 \text{ kg/m}^3$** — never use 1,000 in ocean problems.
- **Drag equation:** $F_{\text{drag}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2}$ — memorize the factor of 2.
- **Hydrostatic pressure rule:** Every **10 m of depth ≈ 1 atm**.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** Observation ROVs have **4 DOF**; work-class have **5–7 DOF**.
- **Umbilical carries power + data + mechanical support** — three-in-one.
- **$I^2 R$ losses** dominate long-tether design — high-voltage AC is the industry solution.
- **Buoyancy balance:** $F_{\text{buoy}} \approx W_{\text{ROV}}$ for a near-neutral vehicle (slightly positive for safety).
- **Sensors you must know:** Camera, Imaging Sonar, DVL, IMU, Depth Sensor, Altimeter.
- **Actuators:** Thrusters (vectored) + Manipulator arms.
- **Pressure housing material:** Titanium (deep) or aluminum alloy (shallow), safety factor ≥ 1.5.
- **Thruster efficiency $\eta$** is typically **50–70 %** for electric underwater thrusters.
- **Tether Management System (TMS)** is mandatory for depths > 300 m to prevent snap loads.
- **Real-world examples:** Jason/ROV (WHOI), Hercules (IFE), CURV (US Navy), Deep Discoverer (NOAA).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AUVs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AUVs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)** is a self-propelled, untethered, crewless underwater robotic system that operates without real-time human input from the surface. It executes pre-programmed mission profiles using onboard computational intelligence, sensor fusion, and navigation algorithms. AUVs are a primary sub-class of **Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUVs)**, distinctly differentiated from Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) by the absence of a physical tether/cable connection to a surface host vessel.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCRUT602)**, AUVs are categorized under field robotics as a critical application domain for unstructured, hazardous, and GPS-denied environments where terrestrial autonomous navigation fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** AUVs are differentiated from ROVs primarily by three attributes: **(1) Untethered Operation**, **(2) Onboard Power Storage**, and **(3) Autonomous Decision-Making Capability** without continuous surface supervision.

### 1.2 Classification by Operational Depth

AUVs are classified by the engineering community using NATO and academic conventions based on the maximum operational depth rating:

| Depth Class | Operational Range | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Shallow-Water AUV** | 0 – **100 m** | Coastal survey, harbor inspection, mine counter-measures |
| **Medium-Water AUV** | 100 – **1500 m** | Continental shelf exploration, pipeline inspection |
| **Deep-Water AUV** | 1500 – **6000 m** | Oceanographic research, oil & gas survey |
| **Full-Ocean-Depth AUV** | > **6000 m** | Hadal zone exploration, abyssal mapping |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Submarine Drone"**
> Imagine a self-driving car that navigates city traffic using GPS and cameras. Now strip away the GPS (because radio waves cannot penetrate water efficiently) and replace cameras with sonar. The vehicle must now decide its path using only sound echoes, inertial measurements, and pre-loaded maps. That is exactly what an AUV is — **a self-driving car in a dark, GPS-denied, high-pressure environment** where the only reliable senses are acoustic (sound-based).

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants are **universally referenced** in AUV design and analysis (per **UNESCO Technical Papers in Marine Science**):

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Seawater Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Speed of Sound in Seawater | $c$ | **~1500 m/s** |
| Dynamic Viscosity of Seawater | $\mu$ | **1.08 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | **1.05 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked about acoustic communication limitations in AUVs, always cite **c ≈ 1500 m/s** in seawater and contrast it with the speed of light (~3×10⁸ m/s) in air. This 5-order-of-magnitude difference is the **root cause** of all underwater communication latency problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Operating Principles

An AUV operates on the synthesis of four fundamental physical principles:

**A. Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)**
- The upward buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid.
- For static equilibrium: $F_b = W$ (Weight of vehicle).
- The vehicle is **positively buoyant** (rises), **neutrally buoyant** (hovers), or **negatively buoyant** (sinks).

**B. Hydrodynamic Drag**
- The resistance offered by the fluid against the vehicle's motion.
- Function of velocity, frontal area, drag coefficient, and fluid density.
- Governs **propulsive power budget**.

**C. Propulsion Mechanics**
- Thrust generated by propellers, water jets, or biomimetic fins must overcome drag.
- Efficiency is governed by the **advance coefficient** $J = \dfrac{v}{nD}$.

**D. Sensor-Based Navigation & Control**
- Inertial, acoustic, and pressure sensors feed state estimates to the controller.
- Controller commands actuators (thrusters, control surfaces, ballast).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **exam-essential** mathematical toolkit for AUV-related problems. Memorize and practice each entry.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Archimedes' Buoyancy | $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g$ | $F_b$: Buoyant force (N), $V$: Displaced volume (m³) | Determines static stability & ballast sizing |
| 2 | Hydrodynamic Drag | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $C_d$: Drag coeff., $A$: Frontal area, $v$: Velocity | Propulsion sizing, energy budget |
| 3 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu}$ | $L$: Characteristic length (m) | Determines flow regime (laminar vs turbulent) |
| 4 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Compares inertial to gravitational forces | Wave-resistance and surface-running analysis |
| 5 | Propulsive Power | $P = F_d \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3$ | $P$: Power in Watts | Battery sizing, mission endurance estimation |
| 6 | Advance Coefficient | $J = \dfrac{v}{nD}$ | $n$: Rotational speed (rps), $D$: Propeller diameter | Propeller open-water efficiency |
| 7 | Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{m_{AUV}}$ | $E_{batt}$: Battery energy (J), $m$: Mass | Endurance per unit mass |
| 8 | Vehicle Drag Coeff. | $C_d = \dfrac{2F_d}{\rho A v^2}$ | Dimensionless form of drag | Used in CFD validation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power ∝ v³ — The Cube Law Trap:** Note carefully in formula (5) that power scales with the **cube** of velocity. Doubling the speed requires **8× more power**. This is the single most important engineering trade-off in AUV mission planning — speed selection directly determines mission endurance.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts above govern the design of nearly every modern AUV:
- **HUGIN AUV (Norway)** — Used for deep-sea pipeline inspection; uses $Re \approx 10^7$ turbulent drag regime.
- **REMUS 600 (USA)** — Shallow-water AUV using low-drag torpedo hull; optimized for low-power survey.
- **Autosub Long Range (NPL, UK)** — Operates under the cube-law power constraint for months-long polar missions.

> [!TIP]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Questions on AUVs in the ESE typically test whether the student can identify which formula applies to a given scenario (e.g., depth-related pressure vs. buoyancy vs. drag). Understanding the *governing physical principle* behind each formula is what separates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrodynamic Drag Force on an AUV

**Problem Setup:** A torpedo-shaped AUV of length $L = 2.0$ m and frontal diameter $D = 0.21$ m travels horizontally through seawater at a forward velocity $v = 1.5$ m/s. Compute the total drag force and the required propulsive power. Assume streamlined-body drag coefficient $C_d = 0.12$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Reynolds Number to identify the flow regime:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho_{sw} \cdot v \cdot L}{\mu}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{1025 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2.0}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{3075}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re \approx 2.85 \times 10^6
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $Re > 5 \times 10^5$, the flow regime is fully **turbulent**, validating the use of the quadratic drag law $F_d \propto v^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the frontal area of the cylindrical hull:**

$$
A = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2
$$

$$
A = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{0.21}{2}\right)^2
$$

$$
A = \pi \cdot (0.105)^2
$$

$$
A \approx 0.0346 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the drag formula $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$:**

$$
F_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.0346 \cdot (1.5)^2
$$

$$
F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.0346 \cdot 2.25
$$

$$
F_d \approx 4.79 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the propulsive power required:**

$$
P = F_d \cdot v
$$

$$
P = 4.79 \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
P \approx 7.19 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Validate using the cube-law form $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3$:**

$$
P = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.0346 \cdot (1.5)^3
$$

$$
P = 2.13 \cdot 3.375
$$

$$
P \approx 7.19 \text{ W} \quad \text{(consistent)} \checkmark
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Buoyancy & Static Stability

**Problem Setup:** An AUV has a total mass $m = 50$ kg and is to operate in seawater ($\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³). Determine the displaced volume for **neutral buoyancy**, and the additional ballast mass required if the hull volume is only $V_h = 0.040$ m³.

**Step 1 — Neutral Buoyancy Condition:**
For hovering, $F_b = W \Rightarrow \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = m \cdot g$.

$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{50}{1025}
$$

$$
V \approx 0.0488 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with available hull volume:**

Since $V_h = 0.040 \text{ m}^3 < V = 0.0488 \text{ m}^3$, the hull alone displaces insufficient water. **The AUV will sink** without additional buoyancy.

**Step 3 — Compute volume deficit:**

$$
\Delta V = V - V_h = 0.0488 - 0.040 = 0.0088 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute additional buoyancy mass required (foam syntactic, density $\rho_f \approx 600$ kg/m³):**

$$
m_f = \rho_f \cdot \Delta V = 600 \cdot 0.0088
$$

$$
m_f \approx 5.28 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
This lightweight syntactic foam provides 8.8 liters of displaced seawater volume, contributing **~90 N** of buoyant force at zero added weight penalty — a classic engineering trick to achieve near-neutral buoyancy.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix (for Exam Answer Structuring)

| Analytical Step | Governing Principle | Justification Phrasing for Answer |
|---|---|---|
| Identify mission depth | Hydrostatic Pressure | Use $P = \rho g h$ for hull pressure rating |
| Compute static buoyancy | Archimedes' Principle | Match $\rho_{sw} V$ to vehicle weight |
| Compute drag force | Fluid Dynamics | Use $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ in turbulent regime |
| Compute propulsion power | Energy Budget | Use $P = F_d v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^3$ (cube law) |
| Estimate endurance | Battery Specific Energy | Use $E_s = E_{batt}/m$ and mission time $t = E/P$ |
| Select navigation suite | Sensor Fusion | INS + DVL + LBL for deep missions |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to score full marks:** Every AUV numerical problem must close with an **engineering interpretation sentence** (e.g., "This power requirement necessitates a 5.5 V × 2.6 Ah Li-ion battery pack for a 4-hour mission"). Examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for interpretation, not just calculation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Typical AUV

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** mapping the interaction between the major subsystems of an AUV. This is the canonical answer diagram expected in KTU Part B 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Mission Planner Unit]:::planner --> node2[Navigation Filter EKF]:::control
    node2 --> node3[Guidance Controller]:::control
    node3 --> node4[Thruster Driver]:::actuator
    node4 --> node5[Propulsion Module]:::actuator
    node5 --> node6[Hull Hydrodynamics]:::env
    node6 --> node5

    node7[INS Inertial Measurement Unit]:::sensor --> node2
    node8[DVL Doppler Velocity Log]:::sensor --> node2
    node9[Pressure Sensor Depth]:::sensor --> node2
    node10[Side Scan Sonar Payload]:::payload --> node11[Onboard Storage]:::data
    node11 --> node12[Acoustic Modem Surface Link]:::comm

    node13[Main Battery Pack Lithium]:::power --> node14[Power Distribution Board]:::power
    node14 --> node1
    node14 --> node2
    node14 --> node3
    node14 --> node4
    node14 --> node7
    node14 --> node8
    node14 --> node9

    classDef planner fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef payload fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef env fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Navigation Subsystem Flow (INS + DVL + Acoustic LBL)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[GPS Surface Fix Pre-Dive] --> nodeB[Initial INS Alignment]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Kalman Filter State Estimator]
    nodeD[DVL Bottom Track Velocity] --> nodeC
    nodeE[LBL Acoustic Beacons Range Data] --> nodeC
    nodeF[Depth Pressure Sensor] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeG[Position Velocity Attitude Estimate]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Guidance Law Pure Pursuit]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Waypoint Tracker]
```

### 4.3 AUV Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SurfacePreLaunch
    SurfacePreLaunch --> Diving : Mission Start Command
    Diving --> Transit : Reach Survey Depth
    Transit --> Survey : Approach Target Area
    Survey --> AltitudeHold : Maintain Constant Depth
    AltitudeHold --> Survey : Track Complete
    Survey --> EmergencySurface : Low Battery Fault
    EmergencySurface --> Recovery : Buoy Release
    Survey --> Transiting : Mission Complete
    Transiting --> Surfacing : Surface Command
    Surfacing --> Recovery : GPS Lock and Recovery
    Recovery --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram Interpretation Notes

- **Mission Planner** (yellow): Pre-loaded mission file containing waypoints, speeds, sensor schedules.
- **Navigation Filter (EKF)** (light blue): Extended Kalman Filter fuses INS, DVL, LBL, and depth data.
- **Thrusters** (light coral): Brushless DC motors driving propellers through sealed magnetic couplings.
- **Sensors** (light green): Provide raw measurements to the estimator.
- **Power Bus** (light gray): Single 24–48 V DC distribution backbone.
- **Acoustic Modem** (light steel blue): Sole means of low-bandwidth communication with surface vessel during dive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Architecture Insight:** The "tightest" 14-mark answer always includes a feedback arrow from the hull dynamics back to the navigation filter (drift compensation) AND from the thrusters back to the battery (energy monitoring). If you forget the energy monitoring feedback, you lose 1 mark per KTU valuation patterns.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between an AUV and an ROV. Mention any two advantages of AUVs over ROVs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | AUV | ROV |
|---|---|---|
| Tether | **Untethered** | **Tethered** via umbilical |
| Power Source | Onboard battery | Supplied via tether |
| Operation | Autonomous, pre-programmed | Real-time remote-controlled |

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Greater operational range** — no tether drag or length limitation (1.5 marks).
2. **Reduced risk to support vessel & personnel** — operates in hazardous zones without manned presence (1.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**List any three sensors typically used in AUV navigation and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **INS (Inertial Navigation System)** — Provides attitude, heading, and acceleration estimates. *(1 mark)*
2. **DVL (Doppler Velocity Log)** — Measures bottom-relative velocity using acoustic Doppler shift. *(1 mark)*
3. **Pressure Sensor** — Provides depth measurement through hydrostatic pressure conversion. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Module Question**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Define an Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV). Explain the major subsystems of a typical AUV with a neat functional block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the hydrodynamic drag force equation for an AUV moving at constant velocity. A torpedo-shaped AUV of length **3 m** and frontal diameter **0.25 m** travels at **2 m/s** through seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Take the drag coefficient $C_d = 0.10$. Compute: (i) Reynolds number, (ii) Drag force, and (iii) Required propulsive power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** An AUV is a self-propelled, untethered underwater robotic vehicle capable of executing pre-programmed missions using onboard sensors, actuators, and computational intelligence, without real-time human intervention.

**Subsystems (5 marks):**
1. **Hull & Mechanical Structure** — Pressure-rated enclosure.
2. **Propulsion System** — Thrusters with brushless motors.
3. **Power System** — Lithium battery pack with BMS.
4. **Navigation Suite** — INS, DVL, depth sensor, LBL.
5. **Guidance & Control** — Onboard computer running EKF and controllers.
6. **Communication** — Acoustic modem.
7. **Payload** — Mission-specific sensors (sonar, cameras).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (2 marks):** The drag on a streamlined body moving through fluid arises from pressure and viscous shear stresses. By dimensional analysis and experimental verification (for $Re > 10^5$), drag is proportional to $\rho$, $v^2$, and $A$:

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} C_d A v^2$$

**Computation (5 marks):**

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{1025 \cdot 2 \cdot 3}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 5.69 \times 10^6
$$

Since $Re > 5 \times 10^5$, flow is **turbulent**.

**(ii) Frontal Area:**

$$
A = \pi \left(\frac{0.25}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \cdot 0.0156 \approx 0.0491 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(iii) Drag Force:**

$$
F_d = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.0491 \cdot (2)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.0491 \cdot 4 \approx 10.06 \text{ N}
$$

**(iv) Propulsive Power:**

$$
P = F_d \cdot v = 10.06 \cdot 2 \approx 20.1 \text{ W}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** This power demand corresponds to a small AUV like REMUS 100 class. At 20 W, with a 1.5 kWh Li-ion battery, theoretical endurance is ~75 hours, subject to sensor and computation overheads.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the different navigation techniques used in AUVs. Compare INS-based dead reckoning with acoustic-based long baseline (LBL) navigation in terms of accuracy, drift, and operational depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small AUV has a mass of **40 kg** and operates in seawater. The hull internal volume is **0.030 m³**. Calculate: (i) the displaced volume required for neutral buoyancy, (ii) the volume of syntactic foam ($\rho_f = 600$ kg/m³) needed to achieve neutral buoyancy, and (iii) the buoyant force contributed by the foam. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Navigation Techniques (4 marks):**
1. **Inertial Navigation (INS):** Uses gyroscopes and accelerometers; self-contained but suffers drift (~0.1% of distance traveled).
2. **Acoustic Beacons (LBL/SBL/USBL):** External reference; uses range measurements from acoustic transponders.
3. **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL):** Bottom-track velocity measurement; high short-term accuracy.
4. **Geophysical Navigation:** Matches terrain or magnetic field to stored maps.
5. **GPS Surface Fixes:** Only available at surface; used for initial alignment and final fix.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | INS Dead Reckoning | LBL Acoustic |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Degrades with time | High (~1 m) |
| Drift | Unbounded | Bounded |
| Operational Depth | All depths | Limited by beacon deployment |
| Infrastructure | None | Pre-deployed transponders |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Required displacement volume:**

$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{40}{1025} \approx 0.0390 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(ii) Volume deficit:**

$$
\Delta V = 0.0390 - 0.030 = 0.0090 \text{ m}^3
$$

This is the foam volume needed.

**(iii) Mass of foam and buoyant force:**

$$
m_f = \rho_f \cdot \Delta V = 600 \cdot 0.0090 = 5.4 \text{ kg}
$$

Buoyant force contributed by foam:

$$
F_{b,foam} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \Delta V \cdot g = 1025 \cdot 0.0090 \cdot 9.81 \approx 90.5 \text{ N}
$$

The net effect: foam displaces 9 L of seawater (~90 N upward force) while adding only ~53 N of downward weight, contributing **~37 N net buoyant lift**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit of dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ — must be in Pa·s (= kg/m·s), not cP.
> 2. **Using freshwater density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** — loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the engineering interpretation sentence** at the end — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up AUV vs ROV characteristics** in comparison questions — common in Part A.
> 5. **Not stating the flow regime** after computing Reynolds number — examiners explicitly test this link.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before entering the exam hall, ensure you can confidently state the following:**

- [ ] **AUV Definition** — Self-propelled, untethered, autonomous underwater robot.
- [ ] **Three Differentiators from ROVs** — No tether, onboard power, autonomous.
- [ ] **Depth Classifications** — Shallow (<100 m), Medium (<1500 m), Deep (<6000 m), Hadal (>6000 m).
- [ ] **Archimedes' Principle** — $F_b = \rho V g$ — defines static buoyancy.
- [ ] **Drag Formula** — $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ — quadratic in velocity.
- [ ] **Power Cube Law** — $P \propto v^3$ — explains speed-endurance trade-off.
- [ ] **Reynolds Number** — $Re = \rho v L / \mu$ — determines flow regime (>10⁵ → turbulent).
- [ ] **Froude Number** — $Fr = v / \sqrt{gL}$ — used for surface-piercing AUVs.
- [ ] **Propeller Advance Coefficient** — $J = v/(nD)$ — governs propeller efficiency.
- [ ] **Navigation Sensors** — INS, DVL, LBL, depth, GPS (surface only).
- [ ] **Communication Bottleneck** — Acoustic only (c ≈ 1500 m/s), low bandwidth.
- [ ] **Syntactic Foam Trick** — Lightweight filler providing net positive buoyancy.
- [ ] **Typical AUVs** — REMUS, HUGIN, Autosub, Bluefin.
- [ ] **Cube-Law Implication** — Doubling speed needs 8× power.
- [ ] **Specific Energy** — $E_s = E_{batt}/m$ for endurance calculation.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Mnemonic for the Four AUV Subsystems:**
> **"P-N-C-P" → Power, Navigation, Control, Propulsion.** Memorize this loop for instant subsystem recall during the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Underwater Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Underwater Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🌊 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid Underwater Vehicle (HUV):** A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle is a specialized class of unmanned marine robotic system that integrates the operational capabilities of two or more distinct underwater vehicle archetypes — most commonly the **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV)** and the **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** — within a single reconfigurable platform. The vehicle can dynamically switch its mode of operation (tethered/remote-controlled vs. untethered/autonomous) to leverage the advantages of both paradigms for deep-sea intervention, surveying, and scientific exploration tasks.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT602)**, the term *Hybrid* specifically refers to a system that can function either as a **free-swimming AUV** (untethered, propeller-driven, mission-programmed) or as a **tethered ROV** (surface-controlled, real-time tele-operated) — often referred to in literature as an **H-AUV (Hybrid AUV)** or **Intervention AUV (I-AUV)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Swiss Army Knife of the Deep Sea 🇨🇭🔪**
> Imagine a regular pocket knife. It has a blade (for cutting) and a screwdriver (for screwing). Each tool is useful, but a **Swiss Army Knife** cleverly combines them into one device. A **Hybrid Underwater Vehicle is the "Swiss Army Knife" of the ocean.**
> - When the **knife blade** is extended → it acts like an **AUV**, swimming freely, navigating itself, and mapping the seafloor.
> - When the **screwdriver** is flipped out → it transforms into an **ROV**, connected to a ship by a cable, and a human pilot drives it in real-time to grab a sample or repair a pipeline.
> - The genius is that **one vehicle can do both jobs** — without needing a second robot.

## 1.3 Why Hybrid? — The Underlying Need

Traditional underwater vehicles face a fundamental **design trade-off**:

| Vehicle Type | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **AUV** | Untethered freedom, long range, no cable drag | Cannot perform heavy intervention (no real-time link) |
| **ROV** | Real-time control, high power, dexterous manipulation | Tether restricts range, cable management is complex |

A **Hybrid Underwater Vehicle breaks this trade-off** by offering a single platform that can operate *autonomously* over large survey areas and *remotely* in localized intervention zones.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Standard)

- **Operating Depth:** typically **6000 m** to **11,000 m** (full ocean depth)
- **AUV Speed:** **1.5–4 knots** (≈ 0.77–2.06 m/s)
- **ROV Speed:** **0.5–2 knots** (slower, precision-oriented)
- **Tether Diameter:** **5–25 mm** depending on power/data requirements
- **Endurance (AUV mode):** **20–60 hours** (battery-limited)
- **Power Source:** **Lithium-ion battery packs** (typically **3–15 kWh**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hybrid Underwater Vehicles

HUVs can be classified based on **which two paradigms are fused**:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Primary Classification**

1. **H-AUV (AUV + ROV):** Most common. Vehicle operates autonomously, then docks/connects to a tether for intervention.
2. **Glider-AUV Hybrid:** Combines buoyancy-driven gliding with propeller-driven cruising.
3. **USV-AUV Hybrid (Unmanned Surface Vehicle + AUV):** A surface mothership deploys and recovers an AUV.
4. **HOV-AUV Hybrid:** Manned submersible carries an AUV for survey work.
5. **Aerial-Diving Hybrid:** Drone that flies, then dives underwater (e.g., AquaMAV).

## 2.2 Operational Modes — Step-by-Step Logic

The HUV operates in **three distinct modes**, transitioned via an onboard mechanism:

### **Mode 1 — Free-Swimming AUV Mode**
- Tether is **stowed** in a tether management system (TMS) or spooled inside the vehicle.
- Navigation is via **DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) + INS (Inertial Navigation System)** dead reckoning.
- Mission is pre-programmed: waypoint-based survey, lawn-mower pattern, transect lines.
- Power consumption is low → **long endurance**.

### **Mode 2 — Transition Phase (Docking / Tether Release)**
- The vehicle approaches a docking station on the seafloor **OR** ascends to a surface vessel.
- A mechanical **latching mechanism** engages, and a fiber-optic/power tether is connected.
- Alternatively, a lightweight **micro-tether** is paid out from the vehicle itself.

### **Mode 3 — Tethered ROV Mode**
- Tether transmits **high-bandwidth data, video, and pilot commands**.
- Hydraulic or electric **manipulator arm** is activated for intervention.
- Thrusters respond in real-time to joystick inputs.
- Power can be supplemented from the surface ship → **unlimited endurance**.

## 2.3 Design Architecture Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle typically comprises the following subsystems:

- **Hull:** Pressure-rated titanium or syntactic foam hull (rated for target depth).
- **Thruster Array:** Typically **5–7 thrusters** (4 vectored + 1–3 vertical/lateral) for 5–6 DOF control.
- **Tether Management System (TMS):** Either onboard spool or surface-drum configuration.
- **Hybrid Power Module:** Rechargeable **Li-ion battery bank** + tether-fed external power.
- **Navigation Suite:** DVL, INS, USBL/LBL acoustic positioning, depth sensor, altimeter.
- **Manipulator Arm:** Typically a **7-function hydraulic manipulator** (e.g., Kraft Teleoperator or custom).
- **Sonar Payload:** Multibeam echosounder, side-scan sonar, sub-bottom profiler.
- **Docking Interface:** Mechanical cone-and-rod funnel for autonomous rendezvous.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Hybrid Underwater Vehicle Analysis**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Hydrodynamic drag force on the hull | N |
| 2 | $P_{thrust} = F_{thrust} \cdot v$ | Mechanical power required to overcome drag at speed $v$ | W |
| 3 | $E_{mission} = P_{thrust} \cdot t_{mission}$ | Energy consumed in AUV mode (battery sizing) | J or Wh |
| 4 | $C_{b} = \dfrac{V_{displaced}}{L \cdot B \cdot D}$ | Block coefficient (hull fullness, typically 0.5–0.65) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $t_{dive} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{g}}$ | Time to descend to depth $h$ under free-fall (glider mode) | s |
| 6 | $v_{glide} = \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta}{F_{drag}}$ | Steady glide velocity at angle of attack $\theta$ | m/s |
| 7 | $R_{acoustic} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_{source}}{I_{reference}}\right)$ | Acoustic source level for communication/sonar | dB re 1 μPa |
| 8 | $SNR = SL - TL - (NL - DI)$ | Sonar equation: signal-to-noise ratio | dB |
| 9 | $\tau_{thruster} = K_t \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5$ | Thruster torque output | N·m |
| 10 | $C_{tether} = \dfrac{R_{tether}}{V_{supply}}$ | Power loss in tether (cable resistance matters for ROV mode) | W |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **hydrodynamic drag equation** ($F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$) and the **sonar equation** — these appear in nearly every numerical problem in Module 3.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Oil & Gas:** Pipeline inspection + intervention in deep waters (e.g., Equinor, Shell deployments).
- **Marine Archaeology:** Survey large wrecks (AUV mode) then collect artifacts (ROV mode).
- **Mine Countermeasures (MCM):** Autonomous mine hunting, then neutralization via manipulator.
- **Cabled Ocean Observatories:** HUVs service junction boxes on seafloor networks (e.g., Ocean Networks Canada).
- **Search & Recovery:** Locating black boxes, then physical recovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drag Force and Endurance Trade-off in HUV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle has a frontal area $A = 0.4\ m^2$ and a drag coefficient $C_d = 0.6$. The seawater density is $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$. Calculate the drag force at survey speed $v = 1.5\ m/s$, and then determine the **battery energy required** for a 10-hour AUV-mode mission assuming the propulsion system is 40% efficient.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The hydrodynamic drag force is given by the standard fluid-resistance equation:

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, v^2$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 \times (1.5)^2$$

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 2.25$$

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 0.54$$

$$F_{drag} = 276.75\ N$$

### Step 3 — Compute propulsion (thrust) power

At steady state, thrust equals drag. The mechanical power needed:

$$P_{thrust} = F_{drag} \times v$$

$$P_{thrust} = 276.75 \times 1.5 = 415.125\ W$$

### Step 4 — Account for propulsion efficiency

Propellers and motor systems are not 100% efficient. With efficiency $\eta = 0.4$:

$$P_{electrical} = \dfrac{P_{thrust}}{\eta} = \dfrac{415.125}{0.4} = 1037.8\ W \approx 1.04\ kW$$

### Step 5 — Compute mission energy

For a mission duration $t = 10\ hours$:

$$E_{mission} = P_{electrical} \times t$$

$$E_{mission} = 1037.8 \times 10 = 10{,}378\ Wh \approx 10.4\ kWh$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The HUV's battery pack must be sized to at least **10.4 kWh** (with safety margin → ~12 kWh). This justifies why Hybrid Vehicles are designed with **dual power modes**: when docked or tethered, the cable supplies power, **eliminating the endurance limitation**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Glider Velocity in Buoyancy-Driven Mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle switches to glider mode. The vehicle mass is $m = 50\ kg$, glide angle $\theta = 26.6°$ ($\sin\theta = 0.447$), and the net buoyancy force along the glide path is $F_B = 220\ N$. If the drag force is given by $F_{drag} = 12.5\, v^2$, derive the steady glide velocity.

### Step 1 — Force balance along the glide direction

The component of weight + buoyancy pulling the vehicle down-slope must equal drag at steady state:

$$F_{B}\sin\theta = F_{drag}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute drag expression

$$F_{B}\sin\theta = k \cdot v^2\quad \text{where}\ k = 12.5$$

### Step 3 — Solve for glide velocity

$$v_{glide} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_{B}\sin\theta}{k}}$$

### Step 4 — Plug in values

$$v_{glide} = \sqrt{\dfrac{220 \times 0.447}{12.5}}$$

$$v_{glide} = \sqrt{\dfrac{98.34}{12.5}} = \sqrt{7.867}$$

$$v_{glide} = 2.805\ m/s$$

### Step 5 — Comment

> [!NOTE]
> The steady glide speed is **2.8 m/s** (~5.5 knots), which is a reasonable speed for an underwater glider. This glider-mode speed is much lower than the AUV's propeller mode, but the **energy cost is near-zero** (only buoyancy changes, no propeller action) — giving weeks of endurance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AUV vs. ROV vs. HUV

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Reference: The following matrix is high-yield for 14-mark essay questions.**

| Parameter | AUV | ROV | HUV (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tether** | None | Mandatory | Optional (stowable) |
| **Endurance** | 20–60 hours (battery) | Unlimited (surface power) | Unlimited (tether) / 60 hr (free) |
| **Operating Range** | 10–200 km | 100 m – 5 km (tether limit) | 200+ km (hybrid) |
| **Real-time Control** | No | Yes | Yes (in ROV mode) |
| **Manipulation** | No | Yes (heavy) | Yes (light to medium) |
| **Survey Speed** | 1.5–4 knots | 0.5–1.5 knots | 1.5–4 knots (AUV) / 0.5–1.5 (ROV) |
| **Cable Management Complexity** | None | Very High | Medium |
| **Deployment Cost** | Medium | High (ship + TMS) | Medium-High |
| **Mission Flexibility** | Survey only | Intervention only | Both |
| **Risk of Loss** | Medium (if fault) | Low (tethered) | Low in ROV / Medium in AUV |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Tether Power Loss (ROV Mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A Hybrid vehicle operates in ROV mode via a copper tether of length $L = 1000\ m$, cross-section $A_c = 4\ mm^2$, and resistivity $\rho_{cu} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot m$. The vehicle draws current $I = 10\ A$ at $V = 500\ V$. Calculate the percentage power loss in the tether.

### Step 1 — Tether resistance

$$R_{tether} = \dfrac{\rho_{cu}\cdot L}{A_c}$$

$$R_{tether} = \dfrac{1.68 \times 10^{-8} \times 1000}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{tether} = \dfrac{1.68 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.2\ \Omega$$

### Step 2 — Power loss in tether

$$P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{tether} = (10)^2 \times 4.2 = 420\ W$$

### Step 3 — Total power drawn

$$P_{total} = V \cdot I = 500 \times 10 = 5000\ W$$

### Step 4 — Percentage loss

$$\% \text{ loss} = \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{total}} \times 100 = \dfrac{420}{5000} \times 100 = 8.4\%$$

### Step 5 — Insight

> [!NOTE]
> An **8.4% power loss** is significant. This is why high-power ROVs use **3-phase AC** or **high-voltage DC** tethers, and why Hybrid vehicles use **optical fiber for data + thin power cores**, drastically reducing cable diameter and drag.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HUV System Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of a Hybrid Underwater Vehicle, showing the interaction between AUV subsystems, ROV subsystems, and the shared core platform. This diagram maps directly to Section 2.3 of these notes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CorePlatform["CORE PLATFORM OF HUV"]
        hullA["TITANIUM PRESSURE HULL"]
        ctrlCore["CENTRAL CONTROL COMPUTER CPU FPGA"]
        navCore["NAVIGATION FUSION UNIT INS DVL GPS"]
    end

    subgraph AUVmode["AUV MODE SUBSYSTEMS"]
        batA["LI-ION BATTERY BANK 12 KWH"]
        missA["MISSION PLANNER AUTONOMY STACK"]

        spdA["PROPULSION THRUSTERS 4X VECTORED"]
    end

    subgraph ROVmode["ROV MODE SUBSYSTEMS"]

    end

    subgraph SharedActuators["SHARED ACTUATORS AND SENSORS"]

    subgraph DockingModule["DOCKING AND TRANSITION"]

    ctrlCore --> missA
    ctrlCore --> joyA
    ctrlCore --> transA
    batA --> thrA
    surfaceA --> tmsA
    tmsA --> ctrlCore
    navCore --> ctrlCore
    thrA --> ctrlCore
    usblA --> ctrlCore
    altA --> ctrlCore
    snaA --> ctrlCore
    vidA --> ctrlCore
    manA --> ctrlCore

```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Operational Mode Transition Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AUV_Mode
    AUV_Mode --> TransitionPhase : REACHES INTERVENTION ZONE
    TransitionPhase --> ROV_Mode : TETHER CONNECTED
    TransitionPhase --> Docked_Mode : DOCKS AT SEAFLOOR STATION

```

    HUV_BLOCK["HUV: RECONFIGURABLE PLATFORM AUV ROV"] -->|Long Range + Intervention| MISSION3["SURVEY + INTERVENTION HYBRID MISSION"]
