<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 3 marks, direct/conceptual
> - **Part B:** 14 marks, internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Hybrid Underwater Vehicle (HUV). How does it differ from a conventional AUV and ROV?**

**Model Answer:**

A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle is a reconfigurable unmanned underwater platform that combines the operating modes of both an AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) and an ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle). Unlike a pure **AUV**, which is untethered and operates fully autonomously using onboard batteries, a Hybrid vehicle can switch to a tethered mode where it receives real-time commands from a surface operator. Unlike a pure **ROV**, which is permanently tethered and limited by cable length, a Hybrid vehicle can detach from the tether and operate freely over long distances. Thus, it eliminates the **endurance vs. intervention** trade-off faced by both traditional classes. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of Hybrid Underwater Vehicles in deep-sea applications.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Extended operational range:** In AUV mode, the HUV can survey large areas (10–200 km) untethered, far exceeding the range of a tethered ROV.
2. **Real-time intervention capability:** When switched to ROV mode with a tether, the vehicle can perform manipulation tasks such as valve operation, sampling, or connector mating.
3. **Reduced ship-time and cost:** A single Hybrid vehicle replaces the need to deploy two separate vehicles (one AUV + one ROV), saving deployment logistics, ship days, and operating cost. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the three operational modes of a Hybrid Underwater Vehicle with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Tether Management System (TMS)** in mode transition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Hybrid Underwater Vehicle is designed to operate at **4500 m depth**. The hull is a titanium cylinder of inner diameter 600 mm and wall thickness 35 mm. The seawater density is $1025\ kg/m^3$ and the yield strength of titanium is $880\ MPa$. Using the **thin-wall pressure vessel equation**, verify whether the hull can withstand the hydrostatic pressure at this depth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

The three operational modes of an HUV are:

1. **Free-Swimming AUV Mode:** The vehicle navigates autonomously using INS/DVL dead reckoning, with the tether stowed in an onboard spool. The mission is pre-programmed, and the vehicle surveys large areas using multibeam sonar and side-scan payloads. Battery-powered thrusters provide propulsion.

2. **Transition Phase:** When the vehicle reaches an intervention zone, it ascends to the support vessel (or descends to a seafloor docking station). A mechanical latching interface engages, and the fiber-optic/power tether is connected. The mode transition controller arbitrates sensor and command routing.

3. **Tethered ROV Mode:** Once tethered, the surface operator takes control via joystick. The vehicle uses real-time video, manipulator arms, and high-power hydraulic systems to perform intervention tasks. Power is supplemented from the surface, removing endurance limits.

> **Role of TMS:** The Tether Management System **stows, pays out, and retrieves the tether** while controlling cable tension. In AUV mode, it keeps the tether coiled without snagging. In ROV mode, it dispenses the right length to maintain maneuverability without dragging. The TMS is critical because loose tether creates **cable snag hazards**, and over-tension can damage the connector. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Depth: $h = 4500\ m$
- Inner diameter: $D = 600\ mm = 0.6\ m$
- Wall thickness: $t = 35\ mm = 0.035\ m$
- Seawater density: $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$
- Yield strength: $\sigma_y = 880\ MPa$
- $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 4500 m depth**

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$P = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4500$$

$$P = 45{,}256{,}125\ Pa \approx 45.26\ MPa$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stress using thin-wall pressure vessel equation**

For a cylindrical hull, hoop stress is:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$$

where $r = D/2 = 0.3\ m$.

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{45.26 \times 10^6 \times 0.3}{0.035}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{13.578 \times 10^6}{0.035}$$

$$\sigma_{hoop} = 387.94\ MPa$$

**Step 3 — Compare with yield strength (with safety factor)**

With a typical safety factor of **1.5**, allowable stress:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{SF} = \dfrac{880}{1.5} \approx 586.7\ MPa$$

**Step 4 — Verification**

Since $\sigma_{hoop} = 387.94\ MPa < \sigma_{allow} = 586.7\ MPa$, the hull **satisfactorily withstands** the hydrostatic pressure at 4500 m depth with a comfortable margin of safety.

> Therefore, the design is **SAFE**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a comparative table, explain the differences between AUV, ROV, and HUV in terms of tether requirement, endurance, real-time control, and manipulation capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An HUV operating in AUV mode has frontal area $A = 0.5\ m^2$ and drag coefficient $C_d = 0.5$. The seawater density is $1025\ kg/m^3$. Compute the **(i)** drag force at $v = 2\ m/s$, **(ii)** thrust power, and **(iii)** total mission energy in kWh for an 8-hour mission, assuming propulsion efficiency is 45%. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

| Parameter | AUV | ROV | HUV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tether** | None | Mandatory | Optional (deployable) |
| **Endurance** | 20–60 hr (battery) | Unlimited (surface power) | 60 hr (free) / Unlimited (tethered) |
| **Real-time Control** | No | Yes | Yes (in ROV mode) |
| **Manipulation** | Not possible | Heavy hydraulic arm | Light to medium arm |
| **Range** | 10–200 km | 0.1–5 km (cable) | 200+ km |
| **Cost** | Medium | High | High (but two-in-one) |
| **Best Use** | Wide-area survey | Precision intervention | Both survey and intervention |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 0.5\ m^2$, $C_d = 0.5$, $\rho = 1025\ kg/m^3$, $v = 2\ m/s$, $t = 8\ hr$, $\eta = 0.45$

**Step (i) — Drag force**

$$F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$$

$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times (2)^2$$

$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 4$$

$$F_{drag} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.0$$

$$F_{drag} = 512.5\ N$$

**Step (ii) — Thrust power**

$$P_{thrust} = F_{drag} \times v = 512.5 \times 2 = 1025\ W = 1.025\ kW$$

**Step (iii) — Mission energy**

$$P_{electrical} = \dfrac{P_{thrust}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1025}{0.45} = 2277.8\ W \approx 2.28\ kW$$

$$E_{mission} = P_{electrical} \times t = 2.28 \times 8 = 18.22\ kWh$$

> The HUV battery must be sized to **at least 18.22 kWh** (plus a 20% safety margin → ~22 kWh). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often write $C_d A v^2$ without the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor and lose **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by efficiency:** Many answers compute mechanical power but forget the propulsion efficiency step (1 mark lost).
> 3. **Confusing hoop and longitudinal stress:** In Part (b) of Question A, some students use $\sigma = Pr/t$ for a sphere, but the question explicitly says **cylindrical** → hoop stress is $Pr/t$, longitudinal is $Pr/(2t)$. Mixing these loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **In HUV diagrams, students forget to draw the TMS** — examiners specifically look for this in the 7-mark block diagram question.
> 5. **Do NOT mix AUV mode and ROV mode energy/power** in the same calculation. State the mode clearly first.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for HUV (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** HUV = AUV + ROV modes in one reconfigurable platform.
- ✅ **Three operational modes:** Free-swimming AUV → Transition → Tethered ROV.
- ✅ **AUV mode:** Untethered, autonomous, low power, high endurance, no real-time control.
- ✅ **ROV mode:** Tethered, surface-controlled, high power, real-time manipulation.
- ✅ **HUV mode advantage:** Combines long-range survey + real-time intervention.
- ✅ **Tether Management System (TMS):** Stows/pays tether, prevents snag, controls tension.
- ✅ **Key drag equation:** $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Power required:** $P = F_{drag}\cdot v / \eta$ (divide by propulsion efficiency).
- ✅ **Hull design:** Thin-wall cylinder hoop stress = $\sigma = Pr/t$ — verify against yield/SF.
- ✅ **Hull material:** Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) for depths >3000 m; syntactic foam for shallower.
- ✅ **Navigation:** DVL + INS (dead reckoning); USBL/LBL acoustic positioning for absolute fix.
- ✅ **Glider mode:** Buoyancy-driven, near-zero energy, very low speed (0.25 m/s), weeks of endurance.
- ✅ **Sensors:** Multibeam sonar (bathymetry), side-scan sonar (imagery), sub-bottom profiler (sediment).
- ✅ **Manipulator:** 7-function hydraulic arm typical; Kraft Predator or Schilling Titan.
- ✅ **Real-world HUVs:** HUGIN (Kongsberg), Bluefin (Blue Robotics), Sentinel (Saab Seaeye), H-ROV.
- ✅ **Docking:** Cone-and-rod funnel with USBL homing for autonomous rendezvous.
- ✅ **Applications:** Oil & gas, archaeology, MCM (mine countermeasures), cabled observatories, search & recovery.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite formulas:** Drag force, thrust power, mission energy, hoop stress, sonar equation, glide velocity.
- ✅ **Don't confuse:** AUV ≠ ROV ≠ HUV — the comparison table is a guaranteed 7-mark question.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision strategy:** Draw the **HUV block diagram from memory** (TMS + thrusters + batteries + manipulator + nav + sonar). Then re-derive the **drag and energy equations** in your notebook. This single exercise covers ~70% of possible exam questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Moving a Vehicle Through Water - Newton's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Moving a Vehicle Through Water - Newton's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aquatic Vehicle Dynamics (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The study of moving a vehicle through water is the branch of marine robotics and hydrodynamics that deals with the analysis, modelling, and control of the motion of **autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs)**, **remotely operated vehicles (ROVs)**, and surface marine vessels. The motion is governed fundamentally by **Sir Isaac Newton's Three Laws of Motion**, expressed in six degrees of freedom (6-DOF) under the influence of hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, and propulsion forces.

A vehicle moving through water experiences a complex interplay of forces. Unlike motion in air, water is a **dense, viscous, and nearly incompressible fluid** (density $\rho \approx \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$ at $4^{\circ}C$). This makes Newton's laws manifest differently than they do in terrestrial or aerial robotics.

## 1.2 The Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF) of an Aquatic Vehicle

A free-moving body in a fluid has six independent motions:

| Linear (Surge, Sway, Heave) | Rotational (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Surge** — forward/back translation (x-axis) | **Roll** — rotation about x-axis |
| **Sway** — sideways translation (y-axis) | **Pitch** — rotation about y-axis |
| **Heave** — vertical translation (z-axis) | **Yaw** — rotation about z-axis |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Floating Paddle":**
> Imagine you are standing chest-deep in a swimming pool and try to push a beach ball underwater. You immediately feel two things:
> 1. A strong **upward push** from the water (buoyancy — Newton's Third Law at work — water is pushed down, ball is pushed up).
> 2. A **resistance** to your motion as you try to drag the ball through water quickly (drag — Newton's First Law of inertia, water resists changes in motion).
>
> An underwater robot (AUV) feels exactly these forces continuously, plus the **thrust** from its propellers and the **weight** of its own structure. To move predictably, all these forces must be balanced according to **Newton's Second Law: $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$**.

The **fundamental physical constant** governing water-borne motion is the **gravitational acceleration $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$** and the **density of water $\rho = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$** (for fresh water) and $\rho = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ (for sea water).

## 1.4 Why Newton's Laws Are the Foundation

Newton's three laws form the bedrock of marine vehicle dynamics because:
- The **fluid itself does not "decide" to push the vehicle** — every force on the vehicle is the **reaction** of water molecules against the vehicle's surface.
- The water's resistance is purely a manifestation of **inertia** (the fluid's own $m\vec{a}$ resisting change).
- Every propulsion mechanism — propellers, water jets, thrusters — ultimately works by **ejecting water backwards** to push the vehicle forward, an exact application of Newton's Third Law.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 of *Field and Service Robotics* emphasizes that before you can design a controller, plan a path, or even build a thruster system, you **must** be able to write down the force and moment balance on the vehicle. This starts and ends with Newton's Laws.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Three Laws in the Context of Water-Borne Vehicles

### 2.1.1 Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) — Applied to AUVs

> **Statement:** A vehicle moving at constant velocity in still water will continue to do so unless acted upon by an external force (e.g., thrust, drag, current).

* **Why it matters:** When an AUV's propellers stop, the vehicle does **not** stop immediately. It **coasts** for a distance because of inertia, and is gradually decelerated by hydrodynamic drag.
* **Engineering Implication:** Designers must plan a **coasting distance** and a **braking maneuver** before reaching an obstacle.

### 2.1.2 Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$) — The Core Equation of Motion

For a rigid body moving in 3D space, Newton's second law in vector form is:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = m \cdot \vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$$

where $\vec{F}_{\text{ext}}$ is the sum of all external forces, $m$ is the vehicle mass, and $\vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$ is the acceleration of the centre of gravity.

For an **AUV in 6-DOF**, this expands into six scalar equations:

$$X = m(\dot{u} - vr + wq) + \text{hydrodynamic terms}$$

$$Y = m(\dot{v} - wp + ur) + \text{hydrodynamic terms}$$

$$Z = m(\dot{w} - uq + vp) + \text{hydrodynamic terms}$$

where $u, v, w$ are linear velocities and $p, q, r$ are angular velocities in the body frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** The terms like $vr$, $wq$, $wp$ are **Coriolis-centripetal terms** — they arise purely from rotation of the body frame and are a direct consequence of Newton's second law written in a moving reference frame.

### 2.1.3 Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction) — The Working Principle of Propulsion

> **Statement:** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

* **How a propeller works:** The propeller blade pushes water **backwards** (action). The water pushes the propeller, and hence the AUV, **forwards** (reaction).
* **Engineering corollary:** To move **downward**, the vehicle must eject water **upward** through downward-firing thrusters.

## 2.2 The Four Fundamental Forces on an Aquatic Vehicle

The total external force on an AUV is the vector sum of four components:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{total}} = \vec{F}_{\text{gravity}} + \vec{F}_{\text{buoyancy}} + \vec{F}_{\text{drag}} + \vec{F}_{\text{thrust}}$$

### (a) Gravity Force (Weight)

Acts at the **centre of gravity (CG)**, always pointing down:

$$\vec{W} = m \cdot g \quad (\text{downward, magnitude} = mg)$$

### (b) Buoyancy Force (Archimedes' Principle, a consequence of Newton's Third Law)

Acts at the **centre of buoyancy (CB)**, always pointing up:

$$\vec{B} = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot V_{\text{displaced}} \cdot g$$

For a **neutrally buoyant** AUV (designed to hover at constant depth):

$$B = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot V_{\text{displaced}} = m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Rule:** If the **CB is below** the **CG**, the vehicle is **statically stable** (like a pendulum). If CB is above CG, the vehicle is **unstable** and will tend to flip. This is why submarines carry ballast tanks low.

### (c) Hydrodynamic Drag Force

Water resists the motion of any body moving through it. For low-to-moderate speeds, the drag is:

$$F_{\text{drag}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot C_D \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

where:
- $C_D$ = **drag coefficient** (depends on shape; streamlined ≈ 0.04, blunt ≈ 1.0)
- $A$ = **reference (frontal) area** in $\text{m}^2$
- $v$ = velocity in m/s
- $\rho$ = water density in $\text{kg/m}^3$

For very low speeds (Re $\leq 1$), drag is **linear** in velocity (viscous/Stokes drag):

$$F_{\text{viscous}} = 6\pi \mu r v$$

where $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity and $r$ is the equivalent body radius.

### (d) Thrust Force

Produced by propellers or water jets. For an ideal propeller:

$$T = \dot{m}_{\text{water}} \cdot (v_{\text{jet}} - v_{\text{vehicle}})$$

This is **pure Newton's Third Law**: the rate of change of water momentum equals the thrust.

## 2.3 Added Mass — A Subtler Effect

When an AUV accelerates, it must also **accelerate the surrounding water**. This is modelled as an **added mass** $m_a$:

$$F_{\text{inertial}} = (m + m_a) \cdot \dot{v}$$

For a sphere, $m_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V$, meaning a sphere's effective inertia in water is **1.5× its dry mass**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | Newton's 2nd Law (vector) | N, kg, m/s² |
| 2 | $\sum M = I\dot{\omega} + \omega \times (I\omega)$ | Rotational Newton's 2nd Law | N·m, kg·m², rad/s |
| 3 | $W = mg$ | Weight (gravity force) | N |
| 4 | $B = \rho g V$ | Buoyancy force | N |
| 5 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ | Quadratic (turbulent) drag | N |
| 6 | $F_D = 6\pi\mu r v$ | Stokes (viscous) drag | N |
| 7 | $F_T = \dot{m}(v_j - v)$ | Propeller thrust | N |
| 8 | $m_{\text{eff}} = m + m_a$ | Effective (added) mass | kg |
| 9 | $P_{\text{drag}} = F_D \cdot v$ | Power to overcome drag | W |
| 10 | $v_{\text{terminal}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(mg - B)}{\rho C_D A}}$ | Terminal velocity of a sinking body | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** In the table above, all `|` characters inside the LaTeX expressions have been replaced with `\vert` or removed to prevent breaking the table syntax. Be careful in your own answer scripts.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These equations are used in:
* **AUV/ROV design** (Bluefin, REMUS, Sentry classes) — to predict mission endurance.
* **Submarine manoeuvring** — for dive-plane and rudder sizing.
* **Underwater cable/pipeline inspection robots** — to maintain station-keeping against currents.
* **Biomimetic swimming robots** — to model fish-fin propulsion, again using $\sum F = ma$.
* **Search and Rescue (SAR) underwater drones** — to balance thrust vs. drag in turbulent river conditions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equation of Motion from Newton's Second Law

We start with Newton's second law applied to the centre of gravity of the AUV.

**Step 1 — Write the vector form**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_i = m \vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the four force contributions**

$$\vec{F}_{\text{gravity}} + \vec{F}_{\text{buoyancy}} + \vec{F}_{\text{drag}} + \vec{F}_{\text{thrust}} = m \vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$$

**Step 3 — Decompose into body-fixed x, y, z components (surge, sway, heave)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{thrust},x} - F_{\text{drag},x} + F_{\text{buoy},x} + F_{\text{grav},x} &= m(\dot{u} + wq - vr) \\
F_{\text{thrust},y} - F_{\text{drag},y} + F_{\text{buoy},y} + F_{\text{grav},y} &= m(\dot{v} + ur - wp) \\
F_{\text{thrust},z} - F_{\text{drag},z} + F_{\text{buoy},z} + F_{\text{grav},z} &= m(\dot{w} + vp - uq)
\end{aligned}
$$

The terms $wq, vr, ur, wp, vp, uq$ are the **Coriolis and centripetal accelerations** that appear because the body frame is rotating.

**Step 4 — Add the moment equation (Newton-Euler)**

For rotation about the centre of gravity:

$$\sum \vec{M}_{\text{CG}} = I_{\text{CG}} \vec{\dot{\omega}} + \vec{\omega} \times (I_{\text{CG}} \vec{\omega})$$

where $I_{\text{CG}}$ is the inertia tensor and $\vec{\omega} = [p, q, r]^T$ is the angular velocity vector.

**Step 5 — Combine into the 6-DOF compact form**

This can be written in the standard Fossen / KTU textbook matrix form:

$$
M\ \dot{\nu} + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $M$ | Mass + added-mass matrix (inertia tensor including fluid effects) |
| $C(\nu)$ | Coriolis and centripetal matrix |
| $D(\nu)$ | Hydrodynamic damping matrix (drag-related) |
| $g(\eta)$ | Gravitational/buoyancy restoring vector |
| $\nu$ | Body-fixed velocity vector $[u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ |
| $\tau$ | Control forces and moments (thrust inputs) |
| $\eta$ | Earth-fixed position/orientation $[x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important equation in marine robotics.** A KTU 14-mark question will often ask you to write this equation and identify each term. Memorize it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Terminal Velocity of a Sinking Body

Consider a small submersible (or a sensor package) released from the surface and allowed to sink. It is acted upon by gravity downward, buoyancy upward, and quadratic drag upward.

**Step 1 — Force balance at terminal velocity**

At terminal velocity, acceleration $\dot{v} = 0$, so net force = 0:

$$W - B - F_D = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the formulas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
mg - \rho g V - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v_t^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $v_t^2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v_t^2 &= mg - \rho g V \\
v_t^2 &= \frac{2(mg - \rho g V)}{\rho C_D A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form expression**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2(mg - \rho g V)}{\rho C_D A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> * If $mg > \rho g V$ (denser than water), the body sinks and $v_t > 0$.
> * If $mg < \rho g V$ (lighter than water), the body rises — the equation gives an imaginary $v_t$, indicating motion in the opposite direction.
> * If $mg = \rho g V$, the body is **neutrally buoyant** and $v_t = 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Newton's Third Law Applied to a Propeller

A propeller of diameter $D$ accelerates water from rest to jet velocity $v_j$ relative to the AUV. The mass flow rate through the disc is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A_{\text{disc}} (v_j + v_{\text{vehicle}}) = \rho \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot (v_j + v_{\text{vehicle}})
$$

(The water enters from the front at $v_{\text{vehicle}}$ and leaves from the back faster, so the relative speed-up is $v_j$.)

By Newton's second law applied to the water (rate of change of momentum = force on water):

$$
F_{\text{on water}} = \dot{m} \cdot v_j
$$

By Newton's third law, the force on the AUV is equal in magnitude and opposite:

$$
F_{\text{thrust}} = \dot{m} \cdot v_j = \rho \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot (v_j + v_{\text{vehicle}}) \cdot v_j
$$

This is the **momentum-theory** (or actuator-disc) derivation of thrust, directly traceable to $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ and $\vec{F}_{12} = -\vec{F}_{21}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Forces by Newton's Law Origin

| Force / Effect | Newton's Law of Origin | Direction | Depends on |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inertia (resistance to $\dot v$) | 1st & 2nd | Opposes $\dot v$ | mass $m$ + added mass $m_a$ |
| Weight | 2nd (gravity) | Down | $m, g$ |
| Buoyancy | 3rd (reaction to displaced water) | Up | $\rho, V_{\text{disp}}, g$ |
| Drag | 1st (water's inertia) | Opposes $v$ | $\rho, C_D, A, v^2$ |
| Propeller thrust | 2nd + 3rd (momentum exchange) | Along thrust axis | $\dot m, v_j$ |
| Lift on control fins | 3rd (pressure reaction) | Perpendicular to flow | $\rho, C_L, A_{\text{fin}}, v^2$ |
| Coriolis terms ($wq, vr$, etc.) | 2nd in rotating frame | Cross-coupled | $\omega$, $v$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force Diagram — Free Body of an AUV in Still Water

Below is a Mermaid block-level schematic showing all forces acting on a free-moving underwater vehicle. Note that node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[AUV Hull]:::vehicle
    A2[Weight mg - acts at CG]:::force
    A3[Buoyancy B - acts at CB]:::force
    A4[Drag F_D - opposes motion]:::force
    A5[Thrust T - from propeller]:::force
    A6[Lift on fins F_L]:::force
    A7[Net Force = m times a_CG]:::result

    A2 -->|downward| A1
    A3 -->|upward| A1
    A4 -->|opposes velocity| A1
    A5 -->|along propeller axis| A1
    A6 -->|perpendicular to flow| A1
    A1 --> A7

    classDef vehicle fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d6efd
    classDef force fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px,color:#856404
    classDef result fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f5132
```

## 4.2 6-DOF Equation of Motion — Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the standard Fossen-form equation $M\dot\nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau$ as a functional block diagram, showing how each physical effect enters the equation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[nu - body velocity vector]:::input
    B2[M - mass plus added mass matrix]:::block
    B3[d/dt operator]:::op
    B4[nu-dot - acceleration]:::signal
    B5[C of nu - Coriolis matrix]:::block
    B6[nu]:::input
    B7[D of nu - Damping matrix]:::block
    B8[g of eta - Restoring forces]:::block
    B9[eta - position and orientation]:::input
    B10[tau - Thrust and control inputs]:::input
    B11[Sum of forces and moments]:::sum
    B12[Equation of motion output]:::result

    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B11
    B2 --> B11
    B1 --> B5
    B5 --> B11
    B1 --> B7
    B7 --> B11
    B1 --> B6
    B6 --> B7
    B9 --> B8
    B8 --> B11
    B10 --> B11
    B11 --> B12

    classDef input fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:1px
    classDef block fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1px
    classDef op fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,stroke-width:1px
    classDef signal fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,stroke-width:1px
    classDef sum fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,stroke-width:2px
    classDef result fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Newton's Law Application Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Newton First Law - Inertia]:::law
    L2[Vehicle continues at constant v in still water]:::desc
    L3[Drag decelerates a coasting AUV]:::example

    L4[Newton Second Law - F equals m times a]:::law
    L5[Sum of all forces equals m times a_CG]:::desc
    L6[Used to size thrusters]:::example

    L7[Newton Third Law - Action Reaction]:::law
    L8[Propeller pushes water back, vehicle goes forward]:::desc
    L9[Buoyancy is reaction to displaced water]:::example

    L1 --> L2 --> L3
    L4 --> L5 --> L6
    L7 --> L8 --> L9

    classDef law fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#0a3622
    classDef desc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:1px
    classDef example fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams (not physical sketches)?** AUV dynamics is a *systems* topic — the engineering value lies in the **flow of forces and equations**, not in pretty pictures of submarines. The block diagrams above directly map to the 14-mark question pattern KTU examiners use.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**State and explain Newton's Third Law of Motion in the context of how an underwater vehicle propels itself.**

**Model Answer:**

Newton's Third Law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. In an AUV, the rotating propeller blade exerts a force on the surrounding water, accelerating water mass backwards (action). By Newton's Third Law, the water exerts an equal and opposite force on the propeller and hence the AUV hull, pushing it forward (reaction). The thrust magnitude is given by $T = \dot{m}(v_j - v)$, where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of water accelerated by the propeller. Buoyancy is also a direct consequence of this law — the vehicle displaces water downward (action), and the water pushes the vehicle upward (reaction).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Define buoyancy force and write its mathematical expression. What is meant by "neutral buoyancy"?**

**Model Answer:**

Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid on a body immersed in it, arising from the pressure difference between the top and bottom surfaces of the body (a consequence of Newton's Third Law applied to displaced fluid).

$$
F_B = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot V_{\text{displaced}} \cdot g
$$

**Neutral buoyancy** is the condition in which the buoyancy force exactly equals the weight of the vehicle ($F_B = W$). Under this condition, the AUV neither sinks nor rises — it hovers at a constant depth without using any vertical thrust, conserving energy. This is the most desirable operational state for hovering AUVs performing inspection tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the 6-DOF equation of motion of an underwater vehicle in matrix form, starting from Newton's Second Law. Clearly identify each term in the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small spherical sensor probe of mass $m = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $r = 0.1\ \text{m}$ is released in sea water ($\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$). Assuming Stokes flow (low Reynolds number), calculate:
  (i) the terminal sinking velocity, and
  (ii) the time taken to reach 95% of terminal velocity from rest.
  Take $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Newton's second law in vector form:
$\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$.

**Step 2:** Resolving into 6 components (3 forces + 3 moments) in the body-fixed frame and adding hydrodynamic terms (added mass, Coriolis, damping, restoring forces), the standard marine vehicle equation of motion is:

$$
M\ \dot\nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau
$$

**Step 3 — Term identification (each correctly identified term gets 1 mark):**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $M$ | System inertia matrix (rigid-body mass + added mass of the fluid) |
| $C(\nu)\nu$ | Coriolis and centripetal forces (arise from body rotation) |
| $D(\nu)\nu$ | Hydrodynamic damping (viscous + quadratic drag) |
| $g(\eta)$ | Gravitational/buoyancy restoring forces and moments |
| $\tau$ | Vector of control forces and moments from thrusters |
| $\nu = [u, v, w, p, q, r]^T$ | Body-fixed linear and angular velocities |
| $\eta = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ | Earth-fixed position and orientation |

**Step 4:** Note that for statics ($\dot\nu = 0, \nu = 0$), the equation reduces to $g(\eta) = \tau$, which is the condition for a hovering vehicle.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (i) — Terminal velocity under Stokes drag:**

For Stokes flow, the drag force is:
$F_D = 6\pi \mu r v$.

At terminal velocity, weight = buoyancy + drag:

$$
mg = \rho g V + 6\pi\mu r v_t
$$

**Compute the volume of the sphere:**
$V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi (0.1)^3 = 4.189 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$.

**Compute weight:**
$W = mg = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05\ \text{N}$.

**Compute buoyancy:**
$B = \rho g V = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.189 \times 10^{-3} = 42.10\ \text{N}$.

**Net downward force (weight − buoyancy):**
$F_{\text{net}} = 49.05 - 42.10 = 6.95\ \text{N}$.

**Set equal to Stokes drag and solve for $v_t$:**

$$
v_t = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{6\pi\mu r} = \frac{6.95}{6\pi \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1} = \frac{6.95}{1.885 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 3686\ \text{m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistake:** Students often forget the buoyancy term and write $v_t = mg/(6\pi\mu r)$, giving an answer of about $2600\ \text{m/s}$ — about 30% too high. **Always include $B$ in the force balance.** Also check Reynolds number: $Re = \rho v_t d / \mu \approx 1025 \times 3686 \times 0.2 / 10^{-3} \approx 7.5 \times 10^8$. This is *very far* from Stokes flow, so in practice one must use quadratic drag. KTU will still accept Stokes answer because the problem explicitly says "assuming Stokes flow."

**Part (ii) — Time to reach 95% of terminal velocity:**

The equation of motion in Stokes flow is a first-order ODE:
$m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F_{\text{net}} - 6\pi\mu r v$

Define the time constant:
$\tau_c = \dfrac{m}{6\pi\mu r} = \dfrac{5}{1.885 \times 10^{-3}} = 2652\ \text{s}$

The solution with $v(0) = 0$ is:
$v(t) = v_t (1 - e^{-t/\tau_c})$

Set $v(t_{95}) = 0.95 v_t$:

$$
0.95 = 1 - e^{-t_{95}/\tau_c} \;\Rightarrow\; e^{-t_{95}/\tau_c} = 0.05 \;\Rightarrow\; t_{95} = \tau_c \ln(20)
$$

$$
t_{95} = 2652 \times 2.996 \approx 7944\ \text{s} \approx 2.21\ \text{hours}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram, explain the four fundamental forces acting on an AUV moving with constant velocity through still water. Show how Newton's Second Law is applied to obtain a steady-state balance equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A torpedo-shaped AUV of length $L = 2\ \text{m}$ and frontal area $A = 0.05\ \text{m}^2$ has a drag coefficient $C_D = 0.08$ and mass $m = 150\ \text{kg}$. Its propeller can deliver a maximum thrust of $T = 400\ \text{N}$.
  (i) Calculate the **maximum steady (terminal) speed** of the AUV in fresh water ($\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$).
  (ii) Calculate the **steady-state propulsive power** required at this speed.
  (iii) If the AUV is to maintain a speed of $5\ \text{m/s}$, what thrust is required? What percentage of full thrust is this? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Free-Body Diagram (described in text since examiner expects a labelled sketch):**

| Force | Symbol | Direction | Origin (Newton's Law) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Weight | $W = mg$ | Downward | 2nd Law (gravity) |
| Buoyancy | $B = \rho g V$ | Upward | 3rd Law (displaced water reacts) |
| Thrust | $T$ | Forward (along propeller axis) | 2nd & 3rd Laws (momentum exchange) |
| Drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ | Backward (opposes motion) | 1st & 2nd Laws (fluid inertia) |

**Newton's Second Law in steady state** ($\dot v = 0$):

For **surge** (forward) motion, summing forces along the body x-axis:
$T - F_D + (\text{component of } W \text{ along } x) + (\text{component of } B \text{ along } x) = 0$

For a vehicle moving **horizontally** at constant depth, $W$ and $B$ are vertical and have **no component along the surge axis**, so:

$$
T = F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2
$$

This is the **steady-state balance equation** of an AUV in horizontal motion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (i) — Maximum steady speed:**

At maximum speed, $T = F_D$:

$$
400 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 \times v^2
$$

$$
v^2 = \frac{400}{2.0} = 200 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{\max} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Part (ii) — Propulsive power at $v_{\max}$:**

$$
P = T \cdot v = 400 \times 14.14 \approx 5657\ \text{W} \approx 5.66\ \text{kW}
$$

**Part (iii) — Thrust required for $v = 5\ \text{m/s}$:**

$$
T_{5} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 0.08 \times 0.05 \times 5^2 = 2.0 \times 25 = 50\ \text{N}
$$

**Percentage of full thrust:**

$$
\frac{50}{400} \times 100\% = 12.5\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$** in the drag formula — loses 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Units!** $A$ in $\text{m}^2$, $\rho$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$, $v$ in m/s. If you mix units you will get a wrong answer with no method marks.
> 3. **Not stating Newton's law** when listing each force — many students describe the forces but forget to *attribute* them to Newton's law, losing 1–2 marks in part (a).
> 4. **Conflating steady-state and accelerating motion.** In steady state $\sum F = 0$ (1st law in disguise); during acceleration $\sum F = m\dot v$ (2nd law). Examiners *do* check this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 3**

* ☐ **Newton's 1st Law** explains why a coasting AUV decelerates gradually (inertia of the AUV *and* of the displaced water).
* ☐ **Newton's 2nd Law** is the master equation: $\sum \vec F = m\vec a$ → expanded to 6-DOF: $M\dot\nu + C(\nu)\nu + D(\nu)\nu + g(\eta) = \tau$.
* ☐ **Newton's 3rd Law** is the basis of *every* propulsion system (propeller, water-jet, biomimetic fin) and of *buoyancy*.
* ☐ **Four fundamental forces:** Weight, Buoyancy, Drag, Thrust — always draw the free-body diagram first.
* ☐ **Drag formula:** $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A v^2$ (quadratic, high Re) OR $F_D = 6\pi\mu r v$ (Stokes, low Re).
* ☐ **Buoyancy:** $B = \rho g V$. Neutral buoyancy when $B = W$.
* ☐ **Added mass** $m_a$: the AUV must accelerate surrounding water too; $m_{\text{eff}} = m + m_a$.
* ☐ **Coriolis terms** ($wq, vr$, etc.) appear automatically in 6-DOF; do not drop them.
* ☐ **Terminal velocity** derivation: set $\sum F = 0$ at $v = v_t$ and solve.
* ☐ **Propeller thrust** is a momentum-flux calculation: $T = \dot m (v_j - v)$.
* ☐ **Power to overcome drag:** $P = F_D \cdot v$.
* ☐ For a 14-mark question: always (1) state the law, (2) draw the FBD, (3) write the equation, (4) substitute numbers with units, (5) box the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces on Underwater Vehicles
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forces on Underwater Vehicles

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Underwater Vehicle (UV)** is a submersible robotic platform designed to operate beneath the water surface for missions such as oceanographic survey, pipeline inspection, mine counter-measures, and bathymetric mapping. The two principal classes studied under the KTU 2024 scheme (PCRUT602) are:

- **Autonomous Underwater Vehicle (AUV):** Self-propelled, free-swimming, untethered vehicle executing pre-programmed trajectories.
- **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV):** Tethered vehicle whose motion and actuators are commanded by a surface operator.

The dynamic behaviour of such vehicles is governed by a six degree-of-freedom (6-DOF) rigid-body formulation in which the **net force** acting on the hull must equal the rate of change of its **linear and angular momentum**. These forces fall into four canonical families:

| # | Force Family | Physical Origin |
|---|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Hydrostatic** | Gravity and Buoyancy |
| 2 | **Hydrodynamic** | Pressure distribution & viscous shear on the wetted hull |
| 3 | **Propulsive** | Thrust from propellers / thrusters |
| 4 | **Environmental** | Ocean currents, waves, wind-induced surface drift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Forces on underwater vehicles are the resultant hydrodynamic, hydrostatic, propulsive and environmental force-moments that govern the 6-DOF motion of an AUV/ROV in a fluid medium of density $\rho$."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a **watermelon underwater in a swimming pool** with one hand. You instantly feel four things:

1. The **weight of the melon pulling it down** → that is the **gravitational force (W)**.
2. The pool water **pushing it up** with an equal but opposite force when you let go → that is the **buoyancy force (B)**.
3. As you drag the melon horizontally, you feel a **resistance** that grows with speed (faster = harder to push) → that is the **hydrodynamic drag (F_D)**.
4. The moment you suddenly *accelerate* the melon sideways, the water in front seems to "pile up" and resist you more than when moving at constant speed → that is the **added-mass effect (F_A)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does water behave this way?**
> Unlike air, water is **~800 times denser** ($\rho_{water} \approx \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ for seawater vs. $\mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$ for air). Hence, any motion through water demands far greater thrust, and **inertial coupling** between the vehicle and the surrounding fluid becomes non-negligible.

---

## 1.3 Nomenclature Used Throughout the Notes

- $\rho$ → Mass density of the surrounding fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ → Submerged volume of the vehicle (m³)
- $m$ → Mass of the vehicle (kg)
- $g$ → Gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m/s²**
- $v$ → Relative velocity between vehicle and fluid (m/s)
- $U$ → Cruise speed of the vehicle (m/s)
- $C_D$, $C_L$ → Drag and Lift coefficients (dimensionless)
- $S$ → Characteristic reference area (m²)
- $L$ → Characteristic length (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Quick reference constants for KTU numericals:**
> - Seawater density: $\rho = \mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$
> - Freshwater density: $\rho = \mathbf{1000\ kg/m^3}$
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater at 20°C: $\nu = \mathbf{1.05 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrostatic Forces (Restoring Forces)

### 2.1.1 Gravitational Force (Weight)

Acts at the **centre of gravity (CoG)** and is always directed downward along the global $z$-axis.

$$W \;=\; m\,g \quad \text{[N]}$$

**Why?** Newton's second law for a body in a uniform gravitational field. The line of action passes through the CoG because gravity is a *body force* proportional to the local mass element.

### 2.1.2 Buoyancy Force

Equal in magnitude to the weight of the fluid **displaced** by the submerged hull, acting vertically upward through the **centre of buoyancy (CoB)**.

$$B \;=\; \rho\,V\,g \quad \text{[N]}$$

- If $B > W$ → vehicle is **positively buoyant** (rises).
- If $B < W$ → vehicle is **negatively buoyant** (sinks).
- If $B = W$ → vehicle is **neutrally buoyant** (preferred for hovering ROVs and AUVs on mission).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A neutrally buoyant vehicle is *statically* stable only when the CoB lies **above** the CoG. Otherwise, even a small tilt produces a destabilising moment (think of a floating pen balanced on its tip).

---

## 2.2 Hydrodynamic Forces (Moving Fluid Effects)

Hydrodynamic forces are split into two physically distinct categories:

### 2.2.1 Inertial (Acceleration-Dependent) Forces

These arise because accelerating a body requires accelerating the **surrounding fluid mass** as well. The fluid mass effectively added to the system is called **added mass** ($m_a$).

$$F_{A} \;=\; -m_{a}\,\dfrac{du}{dt} \quad \text{[N]}$$

The added mass depends on the body shape and the **direction of motion**. For an ellipsoidal hull of semi-axes $a, b, c$:

$$m_{a,\,x} \;=\; k_{x}\,\rho\,V \quad ; \quad k_{x} \in [0.05,\, 0.20] \text{ for prolate spheroids}$$

A second inertial force, the **Froude–Krylov force**, accounts for the pressure field created by undisturbed wave motion on the hull:

$$F_{FK} \;=\; C_{FK}\,\rho\,V\,\dot{U}_{\infty}$$

### 2.2.2 Dissipative (Velocity-Dependent) Forces

These dissipate mechanical energy as heat and are proportional to the velocity (linear damping) or its square (quadratic / form drag).

**(a) Viscous (Skin-Friction) Drag** — dominates at low Reynolds numbers.

$$F_{D,\,visc} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{f}\,S\,U^{2} \quad ; \quad C_{f} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}}$$

**(b) Pressure (Form) Drag** — dominant for bluff bodies.

$$F_{D,\,form} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,S\,U^{2}$$

**(c) Total Drag** — sum of viscous and pressure components.

$$F_{D} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}(\!Re)\,S\,U^{2} \quad \text{[N]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite numerical:** At cruise speed of $U = 2$ m/s, with $C_D = 0.6$, $S = 0.5$ m², the drag on a small AUV in seawater is:
> $$F_D = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times (2)^2 \approx 615\ \text{N}$$
> This is the order of thrust a typical 1-kW thruster must deliver.

---

## 2.3 Lift and Side Forces

When the vehicle moves at a small **angle of attack** $\alpha$, an asymmetric pressure distribution produces a **lift force** perpendicular to the relative flow:

$$F_{L} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{L}(\alpha)\,S\,U^{2} \quad ; \quad C_{L} \;\approx\; C_{L\alpha}\,\alpha$$

A typical AUV control fin produces a lift coefficient slope $C_{L\alpha} \approx 2\pi$ per radian (thin-airfoil analogy adapted to low Re).

---

## 2.4 Propulsive Force (Thrust)

Generated by a propeller or ducted thruster, with the standard KTU propeller-curve relation:

$$T \;=\; \rho\,n^{2}\,D^{4}\,K_{T}(J) \quad ; \quad J \;=\; \dfrac{U}{nD}$$

Where $n$ = rotational speed (rev/s) and $D$ = propeller diameter. The advance ratio $J$ dictates whether the propeller is in bollard (zero-speed) or free-running condition.

---

## 2.5 Environmental Disturbance Forces

Ocean currents of velocity $\vec{v}_{c}$ create an **apparent** velocity on the hull:

$$\vec{v}_{rel} \;=\; \vec{v}_{vehicle} - \vec{v}_{current}$$

Hence, **all hydrodynamic forces and moments must be computed using $\vec{v}_{rel}$** rather than the absolute speed. A 0.3 m/s cross-current is enough to push a 50-kg AUV several metres off-track in 100 s if uncorrected.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) for PCRUT602.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Weight | $W = m g$ | N | Acts at CoG, downward |
| 2 | Buoyancy | $B = \rho V g$ | N | Acts at CoB, upward |
| 3 | Total Drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D S U^2$ | N | $U$ = relative speed |
| 4 | Lift | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_L S U^2$ | N | $\perp$ to flow |
| 5 | Added-mass force | $F_A = m_a \dot{u}$ | N | $\parallel$ to acceleration |
| 6 | Froude–Krylov | $F_{FK} = C_{FK}\rho V \dot{U}_\infty$ | N | Disturbance pressure |
| 7 | Skin-friction $C_f$ | $0.075/(\log_{10}Re-2)^2$ | — | ITTC 1978 line |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = UL/\nu$ | — | Determines flow regime |
| 9 | Thrust (Bollard) | $T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_{T0}$ | N | $J = 0$ |
| 10 | Thrust (general) | $T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T(J)$ | N | $J = U/(nD)$ |
| 11 | Equilibrium trim | $B = W$ | N | For neutral buoyancy |
| 12 | Righting moment | $M = (W-CoB)\,g\sin\phi$ | N·m | For longitudinal stability |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations form the foundation of CFD-based hull design, mission-energy estimation, control-law synthesis (sliding-mode, MPC), and dynamic positioning (DP) of work-class ROVs used by the oil & gas industry in the Kerala offshore zone and Arabian Sea.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Generalised 6-DOF Equation of Motion

The KTU 2024 scheme expects the student to be able to **derive and interpret** the standard Fossen-form equation of motion for an underwater vehicle.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Newton's Second Law in a Fluid

For a rigid body immersed in a fluid, the total force is the sum of the rate of change of linear momentum of the body **plus** that of the fluid it entrains (added mass). The total external force $F_{ext}$ is:

$$F_{ext} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left( m\,v \;+\; m_{a}\,v_{rel} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the derivative using the product rule.

$$F_{ext} \;=\; m\,\dot{v} \;+\; m_{a}\,\dot{v}_{rel} \;+\; \dot{m}_{a}\,v_{rel}$$

**Step 2:** For a constant-geometry vehicle, $\dot{m}_{a} \approx 0$ (added mass is *not* time-dependent for a rigid hull moving steadily).

$$F_{ext} \;=\; m\,\dot{v} \;+\; m_{a}\,\dot{v}_{rel}$$

**Step 3:** Restate the apparent (relative) velocity. The vehicle velocity in the body frame is $v$, and the current in the body frame is $v_c$, so:

$$v_{rel} \;=\; v \;-\; v_{c} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \dot{v}_{rel} \;=\; \dot{v} \;-\; \dot{v}_{c}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back.

$$F_{ext} \;=\; (m + m_{a})\,\dot{v} \;-\; m_{a}\,\dot{v}_{c}$$

**Step 5:** Now write the *balance* between external force and the other force components. The external force decomposes into hydrostatic, dissipative, and propulsive parts:

$$F_{hydrostatic} \;+\; F_{dissipative} \;+\; F_{thrust} \;=\; (m + m_{a})\,\dot{v} \;-\; m_{a}\,\dot{v}_{c}$$

**Step 6:** Move all terms to the standard Fossen form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(m + m_{a})\,\dot{v} \;+\; C_{RB}(v)\,v \;+\; C_{A}(v_{rel})\,v_{rel} \;+\; D(v_{rel})\,v_{rel} \;+\; g(\eta) \;=\; \tau \;+\; \tau_{dist}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where:
> - $C_{RB}(v)$ — **Rigid-body Coriolis–centripetal matrix** (depends on body-frame angular velocity $\omega$)
> - $C_A(v_{rel})$ — **Added-mass Coriolis matrix**
> - $D(v_{rel})$ — **Hydrodynamic damping matrix** (linear $D_l$ + quadratic $D_q \cdot \vert v_{rel}\vert$)
> - $g(\eta)$ — **Restoring-force vector** combining gravity and buoyancy in the earth-fixed frame
> - $\tau$ — Control force/moment vector from thrusters
> - $\tau_{dist}$ — Disturbance force/moment from currents and waves

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Quadratic Damping Term

KTU frequently asks to **show** that the non-linear damping can be expressed as $D(v) v$ with $D(v) = D_l + D_q \cdot \mathrm{diag}(\vert v_{rel}\vert)$.

### Starting Point

The general expression for drag along the surge direction:

$$F_{D,x} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D,x}(\!Re)\,S_{x}\,u_{rel}^{2}$$

**Step 1:** Sign the drag so that it opposes the relative velocity.

$$F_{D,x} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D,x}\,S_{x}\,u_{rel}\,\vert u_{rel}\vert$$

**Step 2:** Group the constant coefficient into $X_{\vert uu\vert}$.

$$X_{\vert uu\vert} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D,x}\,S_{x} \quad \Longrightarrow\quad F_{D,x} \;=\; -X_{\vert uu\vert}\,u_{rel}\,\vert u_{rel}\vert$$

**Step 3:** Identify the quadratic damping coefficient.

$$D_{q,\,x}(u_{rel}) \;=\; X_{\vert uu\vert}\,\vert u_{rel}\vert$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the damping matrix element that multiplies $u_{rel}$ in the equation of motion is:

$$D_{x}(u_{rel}) \;=\; D_{l,x} \;+\; X_{\vert uu\vert}\,\vert u_{rel}\vert$$

**Step 5:** Reassemble for all 6 DOF in compact vector form.

$$D(v_{rel})\,v_{rel} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} X_{u} + X_{\vert uu\vert}\vert u_{rel}\vert & 0 & \cdots \\ 0 & Y_{v} + Y_{\vert vv\vert}\vert v_{rel}\vert & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u_{rel} \\ v_{rel} \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Buoyancy vs Drag Trade-off

> **KTU-style 7-mark problem:** A small AUV of mass $m = 80$ kg, displaced volume $V = 0.078$ m³, frontal area $S = 0.25$ m², and drag coefficient $C_D = 0.55$ operates in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Find: (a) the net vertical force at rest, (b) the steady surge thrust required to cruise at $U = 1.5$ m/s, (c) the required propeller diameter for a bollard-thrust coefficient $K_{T0} = 0.4$ at $n = 12$ rev/s.

### Part (a) — Net Vertical Force at Rest

**Step 1:** Compute the weight.

$$W \;=\; m\,g \;=\; 80 \times 9.81 \;=\; 784.8\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the buoyancy.

$$B \;=\; \rho\,V\,g \;=\; 1025 \times 0.078 \times 9.81$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\;=\; 1025 \times 0.078 \times 9.81 \\
&\;=\; 79.95 \times 9.81 \\
&\;\approx\; 784.3\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Net force = Buoyancy − Weight.

$$F_{net} \;=\; B - W \;=\; 784.3 - 784.8 \;=\; -0.5\ \text{N}$$

The vehicle is **slightly negatively buoyant** by 0.5 N — a fine-tuned AUV design.

### Part (b) — Steady Cruise Thrust

**Step 1:** Plug into the drag equation.

$$F_{D} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}\,S\,U^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D} &\;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.55 \times 0.25 \times (1.5)^2 \\
&\;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.55 \times 0.25 \times 2.25 \\
&\;=\; 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.309375 \\
&\;\approx\; 158.5\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For steady cruise, thrust equals drag.

$$T_{cruise} \;\approx\; 158.5\ \text{N}$$

### Part (c) — Propeller Diameter for Bollard Condition

**Step 1:** At bollard, $K_T = K_{T0} = 0.4$ and $J = 0$.

$$T_{bollard} \;=\; \rho\,n^{2}\,D^{4}\,K_{T0}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $D^4$.

$$D^{4} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{bollard}}{\rho\,n^{2}\,K_{T0}} \;=\; \dfrac{158.5}{1025 \times (12)^2 \times 0.4}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{4} &\;=\; \dfrac{158.5}{1025 \times 144 \times 0.4} \\
&\;=\; \dfrac{158.5}{59040} \\
&\;\approx\; 2.685 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the fourth root.

$$D \;=\; (2.685 \times 10^{-3})^{0.25} \;\approx\; 0.228\ \text{m}$$

A propeller of diameter $\approx 23$ cm is needed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Each Force Dominates

| Speed Regime | Reynolds Number | Dominant Force | Governing Equation | Physical Picture |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Creep / hovering | $Re < 10^3$ | Viscous drag | $F_D \propto U$ | Laminar boundary layer |
| Low-speed survey | $10^3 < Re < 10^5$ | Quadratic drag | $F_D = 0.5 \rho C_D S U^2$ | Turbulent, pressure-dominated |
| High-speed transit | $Re > 10^5$ | Drag + added mass | $F = m_a \dot{u} + 0.5 \rho C_D S U^2$ | Inertial & viscous together |
| Surface / wave zone | — | Froude–Krylov | $F_{FK} = C_{FK}\rho V \dot U_\infty$ | Wave-particle motion forces hull |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Forces on an AUV

The figure below maps every physical force family to its mathematical surrogate inside the Fossen 6-DOF equation of motion, as required for KTU 2024 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Mission Profile and Environment] --> B[Earth Fixed Frame eta]
    B --> C[Coordinate Transformation J eta to body frame nu]
    C --> D[Rigid Body Dynamics M RB nu dot]
    D --> E[Coriolis Centripetal Matrix C RB times nu]
    D --> F[Added Mass Matrix M A]
    D --> G[Hydrodynamic Damping D nu]
    D --> H[Restoring Forces g of eta]
    H --> H1[Weight m g at CoG]
    H --> H2[Buoyancy rho V g at CoB]
    G --> G1[Linear Damping Dl]
    G --> G2[Quadratic Damping Dq absolute nu]
    E --> E1[Coupling Between Surge Sway Heave]
    F --> F1[Added Mass In Surge Sway Heave]
    F --> F2[Added Mass In Roll Pitch Yaw]
    D --> I[Right Hand Side of Equation]
    I --> I1[Control Thrust tau from propellers]
    I --> I2[Disturbance tau dist from currents and waves]
    D --> J[Output 6 DOF Acceleration]
    J --> K[Integrate Once to get body velocity nu]
    K --> L[Integrate Again to get position eta]
    L --> M[Publish Vehicle State to Mission Planner]
```

## 4.2 Force Decomposition on a Submerged Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Submerged Vehicle Hull] --> W[Weight W = m g acting downward at CoG]
    V --> B[Buoyancy B = rho V g acting upward at CoB]
    V --> FD[Drag F D = 0 point 5 rho C D S U squared opposing motion]
    V --> FL[Lift F L = 0 point 5 rho C L S U squared perpendicular to flow]
    V --> FA[Added Mass F A = m a du dt opposing acceleration]
    V --> FFK[Froude Krylov F FK from wave pressure field]
    V --> T[Thrust T from propeller along body x axis]
    W --> Net[Resultant Force Vector]
    B --> Net
    FD --> Net
    FL --> Net
    FA --> Net
    FFK --> Net
    T --> Net
    Net --> Eq[Newton Second Law in Body Frame]
    Eq --> DOF[6 DOF Equations of Motion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the diagrams:**
> - Top-level flowchart (4.1) shows the **computational flow** of the simulation.
> - Bottom diagram (4.2) shows the **physical force decomposition** on the hull.
> - Together they answer the two halves of any KTU 14-mark question: (a) *draw the force diagram* and (b) *write the equations of motion*.
