# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between positive, negative, and neutral buoyancy in an underwater vehicle. How is neutral buoyancy achieved in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

Buoyancy $B$ is the upward force exerted by the displaced fluid on the submerged hull, equal to $B = \rho V g$, while weight $W = mg$ acts downward.

- **Positive buoyancy:** $B > W$ → vehicle rises. Typical of a flooded foam collar on a rescue submersible.
- **Negative buoyancy:** $B < W$ → vehicle sinks. Used for seabed-resting AUVs.
- **Neutral buoyancy:** $B = W$ → no net vertical force, ideal for hovering and low-energy cruising.

**In practice**, neutral buoyancy is achieved by attaching *syntactic foam blocks* (low-density, pressure-tolerant) and *trim weights* (lead or tungsten) to the hull, then fine-tuned using an onboard buoyancy-adjustment bladder or by displacing oil between an internal reservoir and an external bladder.

*(3 marks: 1 for definitions, 2 for practical method)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define added mass. Why is the added-mass effect more significant in water than in air?**

**Model Answer:**

**Added mass** $m_a$ is the virtual mass of the fluid that must be accelerated along with the body when it changes its velocity. It produces a force $F_A = -m_a \dot{u}$ opposing acceleration.

It is more significant in water because:

1. Water is **~830 times denser** than air, so a given hull geometry entrains a proportionally larger fluid mass.
2. The **Reynolds number** in water is smaller for the same $U$ and $L$, so the boundary layer is thinner and the pressure field couples more strongly to the hull acceleration.
3. The ratio of inertial to gravitational forces is higher, so the **virtual-mass coefficient** $k = m_a / (\rho V)$ becomes a dominant parameter in dynamic models.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for comparison with air)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks + Part (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **one** of the two choices.

---

### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Derive the general 6-DOF equation of motion of an underwater vehicle in the Fossen form. Clearly identify each term and state its physical meaning.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Start with Newton's second law in the body frame for a rigid body immersed in fluid.
2. Include the added-mass tensor $M_A$ to account for the entrained fluid momentum.
3. Add the rigid-body Coriolis–centripetal matrix $C_{RB}(\nu)$ and added-mass Coriolis matrix $C_A(\nu_{rel})$ to capture coupling between translational and rotational DOF.
4. Introduce the damping matrix $D(\nu_{rel})$ with both linear $D_l$ and quadratic $D_q$ components.
5. Define the restoring-force vector $g(\eta)$ that combines gravity and buoyancy expressed in the earth frame.
6. Equate to control input $\tau$ and disturbance $\tau_{dist}$.

**Final equation (to be written on the answer sheet):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\underbrace{M_{RB}\,\dot{\nu}}_{\text{inertia}} \;+\; \underbrace{C_{RB}(\nu)\,\nu}_{\text{Coriolis}} \;+\; \underbrace{M_{A}\,\dot{\nu}_{rel}}_{\text{added mass}} \;+\; \underbrace{C_{A}(\nu_{rel})\,\nu_{rel}}_{\text{added-mass Coriolis}} \;+\; \underbrace{D(\nu_{rel})\,\nu_{rel}}_{\text{damping}} \;+\; \underbrace{g(\eta)}_{\text{restoring}} \;=\; \underbrace{\tau}_{\text{control}} \;+\; \underbrace{\tau_{dist}}_{\text{disturbance}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $M_{RB}$ | Rigid-body mass/inertia matrix (m, $I$) |
| $C_{RB}(\nu)$ | Centripetal/coriolis torques from body rotation |
| $M_A$ | Added-mass and added-inertia matrix |
| $C_A$ | Coriolis-like terms from $M_A$ |
| $D(\nu_{rel})$ | Linear + quadratic drag in surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw |
| $g(\eta)$ | Gravity–buoyancy restoring moment based on CoB–CoG offset |
| $\tau$ | Forces/moments from propellers and control surfaces |
| $\tau_{dist}$ | Currents, waves, tether forces |

*(Full marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for term identification, 1 for diagram)*

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**A torpedo-shaped AUV of mass $m = 250$ kg, displaced volume $V = 0.26$ m³, and frontal area $S = 0.32$ m² cruises at $U = 2.5$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). The drag coefficient is $C_D = 0.42$. Compute: (i) net buoyant force, (ii) total drag, (iii) thrust power required at the propeller, and (iv) Reynolds number for $L = 1.8$ m and $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Net buoyant force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= m g = 250 \times 9.81 = 2452.5\ \text{N} \\
B &= \rho V g = 1025 \times 0.26 \times 9.81 \approx 2614.4\ \text{N} \\
F_{net} &= B - W = 2614.4 - 2452.5 = 161.9\ \text{N (upward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The vehicle is **positively buoyant** by 161.9 N. To maintain depth, the control system must command a small downward elevator deflection or a vertical thruster.

**(ii) Total drag:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{D} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D S U^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.42 \times 0.32 \times (2.5)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.42 \times 0.32 \times 6.25 \\
&\approx 430.5\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Propulsive power (assuming 100% propulsive efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F_{D} \times U \\
&= 430.5 \times 2.5 \\
&= 1076.3\ \text{W} \;\approx\; 1.08\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \dfrac{U L}{\nu} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 1.8}{1.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 4.29 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is firmly in the **turbulent regime**, justifying the use of the quadratic drag formula.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for steps i, ii, iii, iv)*

---

### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**With a neat sketch, explain the different drag components acting on an underwater vehicle. Derive the expression for total drag force.**

**Model Solution:**

**Components of drag:**

1. **Frictional (viscous) drag** $F_{Df}$ — caused by tangential shear in the boundary layer.
2. **Form (pressure) drag** $F_{Dp}$ — caused by pressure difference between the leading and trailing edges of a bluff body.
3. **Wave drag** $F_{Dw}$ — significant only near the free surface; for fully submerged vehicles at depth $> 0.5L$ it is negligible.
4. **Induced drag** $F_{Di}$ — associated with lift generation, e.g., on control fins.

**Total drag (sub-merged, deep-depth operation):**

$$F_{D,\,total} \;=\; F_{Df} + F_{Dp} + F_{Di}$$

**Derivation of frictional drag using the ITTC 1978 line:**

**Step 1:** Define Reynolds number based on wetted length $L_{wl}$.

$$Re \;=\; \dfrac{U L_{wl}}{\nu}$$

**Step 2:** ITTC correlation for a flat plate.

$$C_{f} \;=\; \dfrac{0.075}{(\log_{10} Re - 2)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Skin-friction drag.

$$F_{Df} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{f}\,S_{wet}\,U^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Pressure drag from experiments / CFD.

$$F_{Dp} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{Dp}\,S_{front}\,U^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Combine to form the **effective drag coefficient** $C_D = C_{Df}(S_{wet}/S) + C_{Dp}$, then:

$$F_{D,\,total} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{D}(\!Re)\,S_{ref}\,U^{2}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 3 for drag types, 2 for derivation)*

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**An ROV hovers in a 0.4 m/s cross-current. Its drag coefficient in the lateral direction is $C_{Dy} = 1.1$, with a projected area $S_y = 0.18$ m². If the ROV is to remain stationary relative to the seabed, calculate (i) the lateral thruster force required and (ii) the steady-state power draw, assuming 70% thruster efficiency. Use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Lateral thruster force:**

The ROV is stationary in the **earth frame**, but the current produces a relative velocity of $v_{rel} = 0.4$ m/s across the hull. The hydrodynamic drag is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{y} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{Dy} S_y v_{rel}^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.1 \times 0.18 \times (0.4)^{2} \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.1 \times 0.18 \times 0.16 \\
&\approx 16.2\ \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

The thruster must produce an **equal and opposite** force of $\approx 16.2$ N to hold station.

**(ii) Steady-state power draw:**

Useful mechanical power:

$$P_{mech} \;=\; F_{y} \times v_{rel} \;=\; 16.2 \times 0.4 \;=\; 6.48\ \text{W}$$

Electrical power at 70% efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{elec} &= \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta} = \dfrac{6.48}{0.70} \\
&\approx 9.26\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 + 4 for sub-parts i and ii respectively)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign of the relative velocity:** Many students use $U$ (vehicle speed) instead of $U_{rel} = U - U_{current}$ in the drag equation. Even a 0.3 m/s current can change the answer by 25–40%. **Always state clearly which velocity you are using.**
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch on $g$:** A few students omit $g$ when computing buoyancy ($B = \rho V$ instead of $B = \rho V g$). Buoyancy is a *force*, in Newtons, not a mass.
>
> 3. **Confusing added mass with actual mass:** Added mass is **only invoked during acceleration**, not at constant velocity. Markers deduct marks for writing $F = (m + m_a) g$ in a static problem.
>
> 4. **Neglecting Coriolis terms:** When the vehicle moves in a curve, the rotation creates cross-coupling forces (surge-sway, sway-yaw, etc.). A complete 6-DOF answer must include the $C_{RB}(\nu)\nu$ and $C_A(\nu_{rel})\nu_{rel}$ terms.
>
> 5. **Drawing a "weight-balance" diagram without a CoB label:** Always mark both the Centre of Gravity (CoG) and Centre of Buoyancy (CoB). Their relative vertical position determines static stability.
>
> 6. **Forgetting $\rho$ in propeller thrust:** Marine propellers scale with $\rho$, not just $n$ and $D$. Forgetting $\rho$ in the bollard-thrust equation is a 1-mark penalty.
>
> 7. **Mixing SI and CGS:** Seawater density is **1025 kg/m³**, not 1.025 g/cm³, when SI units are used throughout the calculation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ☐ Buoyancy: $B = \rho V g$, acts at CoB.
- ☐ Weight: $W = m g$, acts at CoG.
- ☐ Neutral buoyancy: $B = W$ → *no* net vertical force.
- ☐ Total drag: $F_D = 0.5 \rho C_D S U^2$ (use $U_{rel}$).
- ☐ Lift: $F_L = 0.5 \rho C_L S U^2$ with $C_L \approx 2\pi \alpha$ (small $\alpha$).
- ☐ Added-mass force: $F_A = m_a \dot{u}$, only during acceleration.
- ☐ Froude–Krylov force: wave-induced pressure on the hull.
- ☐ Skin-friction $C_f = 0.075/(\log_{10}Re - 2)^2$ (ITTC 1978).
- ☐ Reynolds number: $Re = UL/\nu$. Re > $5 \times 10^5$ → turbulent; use quadratic drag.
- ☐ Propeller thrust: $T = \rho n^2 D^4 K_T(J)$, with $J = U/(nD)$.
- ☐ 6-DOF Fossen equation is the **master framework** — every KTU Part-B answer must be cast in this form.
- ☐ Apparent velocity $v_{rel} = v_{vehicle} - v_{current}$ governs *all* hydrodynamic forces.
- ☐ Static stability requires CoB **above** CoG; otherwise, a destabilising moment develops.
- ☐ Seawater standard: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\nu = 1.05 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ☐ Power required = $F_D \times U$; efficiency correction for electric thrusters.
- ☐ Always **label** the CoG and CoB on every free-body diagram; markers specifically look for this.
- ☐ In the equation of motion, distinguish carefully between $\nu$ (body velocity), $\nu_{rel}$ (apparent velocity), and $\dot{\nu}_{rel}$ (apparent acceleration).
- ☐ Environmental disturbances ($\tau_{dist}$) are non-zero even for a hovering vehicle and must appear on the **right-hand side** of the Fossen equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation - Specifying Position or Location - Landmarks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigation - Specifying Position or Location - Landmarks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation** in field and service robotics is defined as the process of determining and controlling the course of a mobile robot from one location to another. It encompasses three fundamental sub-problems: **(i) Localization** (Where am I?), **(ii) Path Planning** (Where do I go?), and **(iii) Map Building / SLAM** (What does the world look like?).

Within this framework, **Specifying Position or Location** is the foundational step of *localization* — the act of mathematically representing the robot's pose (position + orientation) with respect to a chosen reference frame.

A **Landmark** is formally defined as a **distinctive, reliably detectable, and spatially stable feature** in the robot's environment whose position (and often geometric signature) is known either a priori or can be estimated relative to the robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** A landmark is any feature in the environment that can be sensed (using onboard sensors such as cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic transducers, or RF receivers) and distinguished from other features, and whose location relative to a global reference frame is known or can be computed.

Formally, a landmark $\ell_i$ is characterized by the tuple:

$$
\ell_i \;\equiv\; \big\langle \mathbf{x}_i,\; \mathbf{s}_i,\; \mathcal{D}_i \big\rangle
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$) is the **landmark position** in the global (world) frame
- $\mathbf{s}_i$ is the **landmark signature/descriptor** (visual, geometric, or coded)
- $\mathcal{D}_i$ is the **detectability region** — the locus of robot poses from which the landmark can be sensed with sufficient confidence

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Treasure Map"** 🎯
>
> Imagine you wake up blindfolded in an unfamiliar forest. You cannot see the sun, you have no GPS, and you cannot count your footsteps reliably. But you remember your grandfather's old treasure map, on which three special features are drawn: a *giant oak tree*, a *river bend*, and a *stone lighthouse*. You do not need to know your own coordinates; you only need to:
> 1. **Detect** these features,
> 2. **Measure** their bearing (direction) and/or range (distance) from where you stand, and
> 3. **Intersect** these geometric observations on the map.
>
> The instant you can "see" two or more landmarks, you can geometrically pinpoint yourself. **This is precisely how a robot uses landmarks for localization.**

The three essential properties that make a feature qualify as a *landmark* for a mobile robot are:

| Property | Robot-Side Meaning | Real-World Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Distinctiveness** | The sensor can tell this feature apart from background clutter | Recognising a *specific* oak among many trees |
| **Stability** | The feature does not move and does not change appearance over short horizons | A permanent lighthouse, not a parked car |
| **Observability** | It is detectable from a meaningful portion of the robot's workspace | Visible from a hill, hidden in a valley |

## 1.3 Why Landmarks Matter in Field and Service Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** Whenever a question begins with *"Explain how a robot determines its position in an unknown environment"*, the expected answer MUST begin with a discussion of landmarks and observation models. Dead-reckoning alone is **inadequate** because of unbounded odometry drift; landmarks provide **absolute reference** to reset and bound this drift.

Field robots (agricultural, mining, planetary, search-and-rescue) and service robots (hospital, domestic, warehouse) operate in semi-structured or unstructured environments where global infrastructure (GPS, magnetic tape) is either **unavailable**, **denied**, or **insufficient**. Landmarks are the only practical substitute for absolute positioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3:**
> The following bullets constitute the *exact* syllabus outcome expected to be assessed:
> 1. Classify the types of landmarks used in robot navigation.
> 2. Explain the geometric observation models (bearing-only, range-only, bearing-range).
> 3. Describe triangulation and trilateration as position-specifying techniques.
> 4. Distinguish natural landmarks from artificial landmarks, and active beacons from passive landmarks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Landmarks

Landmarks are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in modern robotics literature. Understanding the classification is critical for both theory and KTU viva questions.

### Axis 1 — Origin of the Landmark

- **Natural Landmarks** — pre-existing features of the environment. Examples: tree trunks, building corners, door frames, rock formations, ceiling lights.
  - *Pros:* Zero infrastructure cost; abundant.
  - *Cons:* Detection is noisy; signature is data-driven (SIFT, SURF, deep features); environmental sensitivity (lighting, occlusion).
- **Artificial Landmarks** — deliberately placed markers. Examples: AprilTags, AR markers, retroreflective tape, QR codes, colored barcodes.
  - *Pros:* Highly distinctive, fast detection, sub-pixel accuracy.
  - *Cons:* Require installation and maintenance; not always feasible in public spaces.

### Axis 2 — Active vs Passive

- **Passive Landmarks** — do not emit energy; the robot's sensor *illuminates* them (e.g., camera imaging a printed tag, LiDAR hitting a retroreflector).
- **Active Beacons** — emit their own signal (RF, IR, ultrasonic, magnetic) which the robot's receiver detects. Active beacons allow **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** measurement, giving direct range.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Passive = needs a flashlight; Active = self-glowing."*

### Axis 3 — Information Type

- **Range-only Landmarks** (e.g., UWB beacons, GPS satellites) — provide $r_i = \Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{x}_i \Vert$.
- **Bearing-only Landmarks** (e.g., visual features in a monocular camera, compass-aimed RF antennas) — provide $\theta_i = \mathrm{atan2}\,(y_i - y,\; x_i - x)$.
- **Range-Bearing Landmarks** (e.g., LiDAR returns with intensity, stereo camera) — provide both $r_i$ and $\theta_i$.

## 2.2 The Position-Specification Problem (Mathematical Setup)

Let the robot's true pose in the global (world) frame $\mathcal{W}$ be:

$$
\mathbf{p} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \phi \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times \mathcal{S}^1
$$

where $(x, y)$ is the planar position and $\phi$ is the heading (yaw). For 3D ground/field robots, $z$ and pitch/roll may be appended.

A **single observation** of landmark $\ell_i$ whose known global position is $\mathbf{x}_i = [x_i,\; y_i]^T$ yields an observation vector $\mathbf{z}_i$ through the **observation model**:

$$
\mathbf{z}_i \;=\; h(\mathbf{p},\; \mathbf{x}_i) \;+\; \mathbf{v}_i
$$

where:
- $h(\cdot)$ is the **nonlinear measurement function** (geometry-dependent).
- $\mathbf{v}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; \mathbf{R}_i)$ is the **measurement noise** with covariance $\mathbf{R}_i$.

The **range** and **bearing** components are:

$$
r_i \;=\; \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} \;+\; v_{r,i}
$$

$$
\theta_i \;=\; \mathrm{atan2}\!\big(y_i - y,\; x_i - x\big) \;-\; \phi \;+\; v_{\theta,i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bearing is measured in the *robot frame*, hence the subtraction of the heading $\phi$.** This single detail is responsible for the majority of KTU exam mistakes — students often forget to subtract $\phi$ and produce nonsensical geometry.

## 2.3 Position Specification Techniques

### A. Triangulation (Bearing-Only)

Given bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ to two known landmarks $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$, the robot lies at the **intersection of two rays**.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\tan\theta_1 & -1 \\
\tan\theta_2 & -1
\end{bmatrix}^{\!-1}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \tan\theta_1 - y_1 \\
x_2 \tan\theta_2 - y_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the classical 2D closed-form, derivable from the two line equations.

### B. Trilateration (Range-Only)

Given ranges $r_1, r_2, r_3$ to three known landmarks, the robot is the **intersection of three circles**, with closed-form solution:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 \;=\; r_i^2,\qquad i = 1,2,3
$$

### C. Combined Range-Bearing

The most information-rich and robust. Uses a single landmark to estimate both position and heading (with $r, \theta$), and two landmarks to fully over-constrain the problem for noise rejection.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for the KTU End Semester Examination on this topic. Memorize the equations, the assumptions, and the units.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Conditions | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Range to landmark** $r_i$ | $r_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$ | metres (m) | Direct distance; basis of all ToF beacons (UWB, ultrasonic) |
| 2 | **Bearing to landmark** $\theta_i$ | $\theta_i = \mathrm{atan2}(y_i - y,\; x_i - x) - \phi$ | radians (rad) | Heading-invariant; camera & radar use this |
| 3 | **Triangulation (2 bearings)** | $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ where $A$ is the 2×2 Jacobian above | global frame (m) | Bearing-only cameras, sun/star sensors |
| 4 | **Trilateration (3 ranges)** | Three circle-intersection equations | requires 3 non-collinear beacons | UWB, GPS pseudoranges, GNSS |
| 5 | **Observation Jacobian** $H_i = \partial h / \partial \mathbf{p}$ | $H_i = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{x - x_i}{r} & \frac{y - y_i}{r} & 0 \\ -\frac{y - y_i}{r^2} & \frac{x - x_i}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ | row 1 = range, row 2 = bearing | Linearization for EKF localization |
| 6 | **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** (position variance) | $\Sigma_{\mathbf{p}} \geq \big(\sum_i H_i^T R_i^{-1} H_i\big)^{-1}$ | m$^2$ | Quantifies "best possible" accuracy from landmark geometry |
| 7 | **Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)** | $\mathrm{GDOP} = \sqrt{\mathrm{tr}\big(\Sigma_{\mathbf{p}}\big)}$ | dimensionless | Quality of landmark constellation |
| 8 | **Distinctiveness criterion** | $\vert \mathbf{s}_i - \mathbf{s}_j \vert \;>\; 3\sqrt{\sigma_s^2 + \sigma_n^2}$ | dimensionless | Probability of false association $< 0.1\%$ |
| 9 | **Time-of-Flight range** | $r = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | m; $c$ = signal speed | Active beacons, UWB, sonar |
| 10 | **Field of View (FoV) constraint** | $\theta_{\min} \leq \theta_i \leq \theta_{\max}$ | sensor-dependent | Determines detectability region $\mathcal{D}_i$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Forbidden Markdown Symbol:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\big( \cdots \big)` instead of the raw pipe `|` character in the formulas above. Using a raw pipe inside a markdown table would **break the table rendering** and cost you presentation marks. KTU examiners have started docking marks for poorly rendered answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS / GNSS** is a *global-scale active beacon system* with pseudorange trilateration.
- **AprilTag-based SLAM** is the de-facto standard in UAV labs, surgical robotics, and AR; uses planar AR markers + bearing-range fusion.
- **UWB indoor positioning** in warehouses (e.g., Decawave / Qorvo) provides 10-cm accuracy via trilateration from 4–8 anchors.
- **Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity)** use *visual hazard detection landmarks* (rocks, craters) and *hazcam-based triangulation* because there is no GPS on Mars.
- **Autonomous cars (Waymo, Tesla)** extract landmark-style features from HD maps (curb edges, lane markings, pole bases) for high-definition localization.
- **Agricultural robots** use tree-trunk landmarks and plant rows for row-following navigation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bearing-Only Triangulation (2D Closed Form)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most likely 7-mark analytical question** on a KTU Module 3 paper. Practice it until the algebraic transitions are reflexive.

**Given:**
- Two known landmarks $\ell_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $\ell_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ in the world frame.
- Measured bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ in the robot's local frame (or world frame if the robot's heading is already known).
- Robot pose to be determined: $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Write the geometric constraint (line equation).**
A bearing of $\theta_i$ from the robot means the robot lies on the line passing through landmark $\ell_i$ with slope $\tan\theta_i$. In slope-intercept form, the line passing through $(x_i, y_i)$ is:

$$
y - y_i \;=\; \tan\theta_i\,(x - x_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange into standard linear form.**

$$
\tan\theta_i \cdot x \;-\; y \;=\; \tan\theta_i \cdot x_i \;-\; y_i
$$

**Step 3 — Stack the two equations into matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\tan\theta_1 & -1 \\
\tan\theta_2 & -1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \tan\theta_1 - y_1 \\
x_2 \tan\theta_2 - y_2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve by matrix inversion (Cramer's rule or direct inversion).**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\;=\;
A^{-1} \mathbf{b},
\quad
A = \begin{bmatrix} \tan\theta_1 & -1 \\ \tan\theta_2 & -1 \end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \tan\theta_1 - y_1 \\ x_2 \tan\theta_2 - y_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — The determinant condition for a unique solution.**

$$
\det(A) \;=\; -\tan\theta_1 \;+\; \tan\theta_2 \;=\; \tan\theta_2 - \tan\theta_1
$$

The system has a **unique solution iff** $\tan\theta_1 \neq \tan\theta_2$, i.e., the two bearings are not parallel. Physically, the two landmarks must not be **collinear with the robot**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The determinant condition $\tan\theta_1 \neq \tan\theta_2$ is equivalent to demanding that the **angle subtended by the two landmarks at the robot's position is non-zero**. This angle is called the **baseline angle** or **parallax**, and it is the single most important geometric quantity in triangulation. **A small parallax → large position uncertainty.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Range-Only Trilateration (2D Closed Form)

**Given:** Three known landmarks $(x_i, y_i)$, $i = 1, 2, 3$ and three measured ranges $r_i$.

**Step 1 — Write the three circle equations.**

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 \;=\; r_i^2, \qquad i = 1, 2, 3
$$

**Step 2 — Expand each equation.**

$$
x^2 - 2x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2y y_i + y_i^2 \;=\; r_i^2
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the equation for $i = 1$ from those for $i = 2$ and $i = 3$** to eliminate the quadratic $x^2 + y^2$.

For $i = 2$:

$$
-2x x_2 + 2x x_1 - 2y y_2 + 2y y_1 + (x_2^2 - x_1^2) + (y_2^2 - y_1^2) \;=\; r_2^2 - r_1^2
$$

Simplify:

$$
2x(x_1 - x_2) + 2y(y_1 - y_2) \;=\; r_2^2 - r_1^2 - (x_2^2 - x_1^2) - (y_2^2 - y_1^2)
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for $i = 3$:**

$$
2x(x_1 - x_3) + 2y(y_1 - y_3) \;=\; r_3^2 - r_1^2 - (x_3^2 - x_1^2) - (y_3^2 - y_1^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Stack into a 2×2 linear system in $(x, y)$.**

$$
2\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 - x_2 & y_1 - y_2 \\
x_1 - x_3 & y_1 - y_3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
r_2^2 - r_1^2 - (x_2^2 - x_1^2) - (y_2^2 - y_1^2) \\
r_3^2 - r_1^2 - (x_3^2 - x_1^2) - (y_3^2 - y_1^2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve via matrix inversion.**

$$
\mathbf{p} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} M^{-1} \mathbf{c}
$$

**Step 7 — Solvability condition.**

$$
\det(M) \;=\; 2 \big[(x_1 - x_2)(y_1 - y_3) - (x_1 - x_3)(y_1 - y_2)\big] \;\neq\; 0
$$

This requires that the **three landmarks are non-collinear** — the same physical condition that the **circumscribed circle is well-defined**.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check (KTU Numerical):** When the robot lies **exactly on the circumcircle** of the three landmarks, the triangulation/trilateration becomes *degenerate* and the position is ill-defined. This is the same singularity that breaks GPS in the equatorial belt when only three satellites are visible.

## 3.3 Derivation: Range-Bearing Observation Jacobian (for EKF Localization)

The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) linearizes the observation model about the current pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{p}}$. The Jacobian $H_i = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{p}}$ at landmark $\ell_i$ is:

**Step 1 — Define the relative displacement vector $\Delta \mathbf{x} = [x_i - x,\; y_i - y]^T$.**

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives of range $r$:**

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} \;=\; -\frac{x - x_i}{r} \;=\; \frac{x_i - x}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} \;=\; -\frac{y - y_i}{r} \;=\; \frac{y_i - y}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial \phi} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute partial derivatives of bearing $\theta$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} \;=\; \frac{y - y_i}{r^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial y} \;=\; \frac{x_i - x}{r^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \phi} \;=\; -1
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the 2×3 Jacobian $H_i$:**

$$
H_i \;=\;
\begin{bmatrix}
\dfrac{x_i - x}{r} & \dfrac{y_i - y}{r} & 0 \\[8pt]
\dfrac{y - y_i}{r^2} & \dfrac{x_i - x}{r^2} & -1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This Jacobian is the **cornerstone of landmark-based EKF SLAM**. The EKF update equation uses $K = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ where $P$ is the pose covariance and $R$ is the measurement noise. KTU exam questions frequently ask for this Jacobian row-by-row.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Landmarks for Robust Localization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix gives the **practical engineering criteria** for selecting landmarks in a real deployment. Use it as a checklist for the 7-mark "compare" question.

| Criterion | Ideal Choice | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric Diversity (GDOP)** | Wide angular spread, large baseline | Reduces GDOP; bounds position error |
| **Sensor-Compatibility** | Landmark size ≈ 5–10× sensor resolution | Avoids aliasing, sub-pixel issues |
| **Distinctiveness** | High information content (e.g., encoded IDs) | Prevents data-association failures |
| **Stability Over Time** | Permanent, weather-resistant | Bounds map drift |
| **Detectability Range** | $\mathcal{D}_i$ covers the operational area | Eliminates dead zones |
| **Cost & Maintainability** | Low infrastructure, low calibration | Total-cost-of-ownership |
| **Robustness to Occlusion** | Multiple redundant landmarks per cell | Graceful degradation |

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> [!TIP]
> **Question:** A mobile robot observes landmark $\ell_1$ at $(0, 0)$ with bearing $\theta_1 = 45^\circ$ and landmark $\ell_2$ at $(10, 0)$ with bearing $\theta_2 = 135^\circ$, both in the world frame. Determine the robot's position.

**Solution using the triangulation derivation of §3.1:**

Step 1 — Compute $\tan\theta_1 = \tan 45^\circ = 1$, $\tan\theta_2 = \tan 135^\circ = -1$.

Step 2 — Form matrix $A$ and vector $\mathbf{b}$:

$$
A \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{b} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \cdot 1 - 0 \\ 10 \cdot (-1) - 0 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Compute $A^{-1}$. Since $\det(A) = (1)(-1) - (-1)(-1) = -2$:

$$
A^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{-2}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 — Multiply:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \;=\; A^{-1} \mathbf{b} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -10 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Answer:** Robot is at $(x, y) = (5, 5)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The robot is equidistant from both landmarks (distance $= \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ m). The angle subtended at the robot is $135^\circ - 45^\circ = 90^\circ$, and the two rays intersect at exactly the correct point. The baseline distance is 10 m, and the geometry is symmetric, so the result is consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Landmark-Based Localization Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below renders the **complete functional architecture** of a landmark-based localization system, from raw sensor ingestion through to pose update. This is the answer-expectation for a typical 7-mark *"Explain the block diagram of a landmark-based localization system"* question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot World Frame p] --> B[Motion Model u_t]
    B --> C[Pose Predictor p_hat_t]
    C --> D{Sensor Scan}
    D --> E[Raw Measurement z_t]
    E --> F[Feature Extractor]
    F --> G[Landmark Candidates]
    G --> H[Data Association Block]
    H --> I[Matched Landmarks]
    I --> J[Observation Model h p x_i]
    J --> K[Innovation nu = z - h]
    K --> L[Kalman Gain K = P H trans S inv]
    L --> M[Pose Update p = p_hat + K nu]
    M --> N[Updated Belief p_t]
    H --> O[Unmatched Landmarks]
    O --> P[Map Update SLAM Branch]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style P fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```
