- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (used in laser triangulation timing, not in geometric triangulation).
- Standard stereo baseline for outdoor robots: $B = \mathbf{0.1 \text{ to } 1.0}$ m.
- Typical camera focal length: $f = \mathbf{3 \text{ to } 12}$ mm.
- Acceptable angular resolution for bearing triangulation: $\sigma_\theta \approx \mathbf{0.1^\circ \text{ to } 1^\circ}$.

> [!NOTE]
> Triangulation accuracy scales **linearly with baseline length $B$** and **focal length $f$**, and **inversely with the square of the depth $Z$** when disparity noise is constant — this is the central trade-off engineers must design around.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Geometric Principle

The foundational idea is the **Triangle Congruence / Similarity Theorems**:

- **SSS** (Side-Side-Side): If all three sides of a triangle are known, the triangle is uniquely determined.
- **SAS** (Side-Angle-Side): If two sides and the included angle are known, the triangle is unique.
- **ASA / AAS**: If two angles and one side are known, the triangle is unique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A robot performing triangulation always attempts to satisfy **ASA**, **SAS**, or **SAA** using measurements it can physically make. Visual triangulation uses **SAS** (baseline $B$ + two ray angles through pixels). Bearing-based localization uses **ASA** (known landmark separation + two observed bearings).

### 2.2 Logical Pipeline of Triangulation (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Reference Establishment:** Identify or place at least two known reference points (landmarks, beacons, camera pair, or camera + laser).

**Step 2 — Baseline Calibration:** Measure the geometric relationship between the references (baseline $B$, baseline orientation $\alpha$, intrinsic camera matrix $K$).

**Step 3 — Measurement Acquisition:** Sense angular or pixel-disparity information from each reference toward the target.

**Step 4 — Geometric Solve:** Apply the relevant triangulation equation to recover the unknown coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ or the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$.

**Step 5 — Uncertainty Quantification:** Propagate measurement noise through the equations using the Jacobian $J$ to obtain $\sigma_{X}, \sigma_{Y}, \sigma_{Z}$.

**Step 6 — Fusion (Advanced):** In modern service robots, the triangulated pose is fused with wheel odometry, IMU, and GPS in an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the minimum formula set the examiner expects you to reproduce for any triangulation problem in the ESE. Memorize the symbols and the noise-propagation rules.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $f$: focal length, $B$: baseline, $d$: disparity | Stereo depth recovery |
| F2 | $d = x_L - x_R$ | Pixel disparity in image plane | Stereo preprocessing |
| F3 | $x = \dfrac{d \cdot \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2}$ | Bearing from landmark 1 | 2D robot localization |
| F4 | $y = \dfrac{d \cdot \tan\theta_1 \cdot \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2}$ | Perpendicular distance to baseline | 2D robot localization |
| F5 | $Z = \dfrac{B \cdot f}{x_L - x_c}$ | $x_L$: laser pixel, $x_c$: camera center pixel | Active laser triangulation |
| F6 | $\sigma_Z = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \, \sigma_d$ | Depth uncertainty from disparity noise | Error analysis |
| F7 | $\sigma_X \approx \dfrac{Z \cdot \sigma_f}{f}$ | Lateral uncertainty from focal-length noise | Calibration analysis |
| F8 | $\Sigma_p = J \, \Sigma_m \, J^{T}$ | General nonlinear covariance propagation | EKF style fusion |
| F9 | $\dfrac{r}{\sin R} = \dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B}$ | Law of Sines | Generic bearing solve |
| F10 | $c^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2} - 2ab\cos C$ | Law of Cosines | Generic range solve |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Agricultural Robots:** Triangulate crop rows using stereo cameras for precise weeding.
- **Hospital Service Robots:** Localize the charging dock using two IR beacons triangulated by onboard camera.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Use overhead fiducial tags triangulated by LiDAR-camera fusion for cm-level localization.
- **Disaster-Response Robots:** Active laser triangulation to map partially collapsed rooms where GPS is denied.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Triangulate the docking platform using acoustic beacons when light is unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stereo Depth from Disparity (Equation F1)

**Setup:** Two cameras with identical focal length $f$ are separated by baseline $B$. They observe the same 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ in front of them. The optical axes are parallel to the $Z$-axis.

**Step 1 — Pinhole projection onto left image plane:**

Using the pinhole model, the projection of $P$ on the left camera's image plane gives pixel coordinate:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Pinhole projection onto right image plane:**

The right camera is shifted by $-B$ along the $X$-axis. The world $X$-coordinate relative to the right camera is $(X - B)$:

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Define disparity $d$:**

The disparity is the horizontal pixel separation between the two matched image points:

$$d = x_L - x_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$d = f \cdot \frac{X - X + B}{Z} = f \cdot \frac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to obtain depth $Z$:**

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inverse relationship $Z \propto 1/d$ explains why **far objects** (small disparity) have **large depth uncertainty** — a critical observation frequently tested in KTU problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — 2D Position from Two Bearings (Equations F3 & F4)

**Setup:** Two landmarks $L_1 = (0, 0)$ and $L_2 = (d, 0)$ lie on the X-axis. The robot is at unknown position $R = (x, y)$ with $y > 0$. It measures bearings $\theta_1$ to $L_1$ and $\theta_2$ to $L_2$ (measured from its own heading).

**Step 1 — Equation of the ray from $L_1$ through $R$:**

The slope of the line from $(0,0)$ to $(x,y)$ is $\tan\theta_1$:

$$\tan\theta_1 = \frac{y - 0}{x - 0} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = x \tan\theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Equation of the ray from $L_2$ through $R$:**

The slope of the line from $(d,0)$ to $(x,y)$ is $\tan\theta_2$:

$$\tan\theta_2 = \frac{y - 0}{x - d} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y = (x - d)\tan\theta_2$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $y$:**

$$x \tan\theta_1 = (x - d)\tan\theta_2 = x \tan\theta_2 - d \tan\theta_2$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms containing $x$:**

$$x \tan\theta_1 - x \tan\theta_2 = - d \tan\theta_2$$

$$x (\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2) = -d \tan\theta_2$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x$:**

$$x = \frac{-d \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2} = \frac{d \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_2 - \tan\theta_1}$$

By rearranging the sign convention (bearing measured inward):

$$\boxed{x = \frac{d \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into $y = x \tan\theta_1$:**

$$y = \frac{d \tan\theta_1 \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2}$$

$$\boxed{y = \frac{d \tan\theta_1 \tan\theta_2}{\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uncertainty Propagation in Depth (Equation F6)

**Setup:** The depth equation is $Z = fB/d$. The focal length $f$ and baseline $B$ are calibrated and treated as constants. The only random variable is the measured disparity $d$, with Gaussian noise $\sigma_d$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $Z$ with respect to $d$:**

$$\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} = \frac{\partial}{\partial d}\left(\frac{fB}{d}\right) = -\frac{fB}{d^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply first-order error propagation:**

$$\sigma_Z = \left|\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d}\right| \sigma_d = \frac{fB}{d^{2}} \sigma_d$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $fB = Z \cdot d$ from F1:**

$$\sigma_Z = \frac{Z \cdot d}{d^{2}} \sigma_d = \frac{Z}{d} \sigma_d$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of $Z$ itself using $d = fB/Z$:**

$$\sigma_Z = \frac{Z}{fB / Z} \sigma_d = \frac{Z^{2}}{fB} \sigma_d$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_Z = \frac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \, \sigma_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **quadratic growth law of depth uncertainty**: doubling the range makes the depth error **four times worse** for a fixed disparity noise. KTU examiners often test this scaling behaviour.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Active Laser Triangulation (Equation F5)

**Setup:** A camera with focal length $f$ and a laser emitter are mounted on a robot with a baseline $B$ between them. The laser hits the target at depth $Z$, and the reflected spot lands on the image plane at pixel $x_L$. The camera's principal axis intersects the image plane at $x_c$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the reflection ray:**

Using similar triangles, the laser ray from the emitter passes through the target at depth $Z$ with horizontal offset equal to the image-plane projection:

$$x_L - x_c = f \cdot \frac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $Z$:**

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{B \cdot f}{x_L - x_c}}$$

This is mathematically identical to stereo, except that the "disparity" $x_L - x_c$ is now measured between a **synthesized feature** (laser spot) and the **optical centre** of the camera. This is the dominant technique used in **Kinect v1 (structured light)**, industrial line-scan profilers, and 3D scanners on service robots.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix during revision — examiners expect students to compare and contrast methods.

| Aspect | Passive Stereo | Active Laser | Bearing-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| Light source | Ambient | Laser / structured | Beacons / RF |
| Texture dependency | High (needs features) | None | None |
| Range | Short–medium | Short–medium | Long (RF) |
| Outdoor use | Limited (sun glare) | Limited (sun noise) | Excellent |
| Hardware cost | Low–medium | Medium–high | Low (just receiver) |
| Accuracy | mm–cm | sub-mm | m-level (GPS-like) |
| Typical KTU application | Indoor service robots | Profilers, Bin picking | Outdoor field robots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Active Laser Triangulation Sensor Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Power Supply Unit] --> nodeB[Microcontroller FPGA]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Laser Diode Driver]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Laser Line Projector]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Target Object Surface]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Reflected Laser Spot]
    nodeF --> nodeG[CMOS Camera Sensor]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Image Preprocessing Filter]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Spot Centroid Extraction]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Triangulation Solver Module]
    nodeB --> nodeK[Camera Trigger and Sync]
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Depth Map Z x y]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Uncertainty Estimator]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Pose Update to EKF]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stereo Vision Triangulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subLeftCam[Left Camera Subsystem]
        nodeLA[Lens Left] --> nodeLB[Sensor Left]
        nodeLB --> nodeLC[Rectification]
    end
    subgraph subRightCam[Right Camera Subsystem]
        nodeRA[Lens Right] --> nodeRB[Sensor Right]
        nodeRB --> nodeRC[Rectification]
    end
    subLeftCam --> nodeMATCH[Feature Matching Block SAD or ORB]
    subRightCam --> nodeMATCH
    nodeMATCH --> nodeDISP[Disparity Map Generator]
    nodeDISP --> nodeTRI[Triangulation Module f B lookup]
    nodeTRI --> nodeCLOUD[Point Cloud Output X Y Z]
    nodeCLOUD --> nodeFUSE[Sensor Fusion EKF Node]
    nodeFUSE --> nodeODOM[Robot Odometry Update]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Bearing-Based Robot Localization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Initialize Robot Pose] --> nodeS2[Scan Environment for Landmarks]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[Detect Landmark L1]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4[Detect Landmark L2]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS5[Compute Bearing Theta1]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS6[Compute Bearing Theta2]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS7[Apply Closed Form Bearing Equation]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[Estimate Pose x y theta]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[Compute Covariance via Jacobian]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS10[Update Global Map and Pose]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11[Trigger Next Sensor Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Geometric Schematic — The Triangulation Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph subGeom[2D Triangulation Geometry]
        nodeA[Landmark L1 at 0 comma 0] --- nodeB[Robot at x comma y]
        nodeB --- nodeC[Landmark L2 at d comma 0]
        nodeA --- nodeC
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The triangle $L_1$–Robot–$L_2$ has known side $L_1L_2 = d$ and known angles $\theta_1$ at $L_1$ and $\theta_2$ at $L_2$. The remaining sides and the position of the apex (Robot) are recovered by ASA congruence.

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Uncertainty Propagation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeU1[Measured Inputs d Theta1 Theta2] --> nodeU2[Linearisation via Jacobian J]
    nodeU2 --> nodeU3[Input Covariance Sigma input]
    nodeU3 --> nodeU4[Output Covariance J Sigma J transpose]
    nodeU4 --> nodeU5[Final Uncertainty Bounds Sigma x Sigma y Sigma Z]
    nodeU5 --> nodeU6[Confidence Ellipse Plot]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
*Define triangulation as used in field robotics. Differentiate between active and passive triangulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Triangulation is a localization technique in which the position of a robot or a target point is determined by measuring angles (and/or ranges) from two or more known reference points and solving the resulting triangle geometrically. (1 mark)

- **Active Triangulation:** The robot projects its own energy (laser, IR, structured light) onto the target and observes the reflected signal from a known baseline. Example: line-laser + camera 3D scanner. (1 mark)
- **Passive Triangulation:** The robot only observes existing features or beacons in the environment using two viewpoints or two sensors. Example: stereo camera pair. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*Write the depth equation for stereo vision triangulation and state how depth uncertainty scales with range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The depth equation is

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

where $f$ is the focal length, $B$ is the baseline, and $d$ is the disparity. (2 marks)

Depth uncertainty scales **quadratically with range** for a fixed disparity noise $\sigma_d$:

$$\sigma_Z = \frac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \, \sigma_d$$

Thus, doubling the range causes depth uncertainty to increase by a factor of four. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Module in the ESE carries an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Choose exactly ONE of the two options.

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — PCRUT602, Model ESE Question A]**

**(a) Derive the depth equation $Z = fB/d$ for a stereo camera pair. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an active laser triangulation system used in service robots.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** Use the pinhole projection model. Place the two cameras parallel with baseline $B$ and identical focal length $f$.

For a 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$:
- Left camera pixel: $x_L = fX/Z$
- Right camera pixel: $x_R = f(X-B)/Z$
- Disparity: $d = x_L - x_R = fB/Z$
- Inverting: $Z = fB/d$. The relationship is inverse-linear with disparity.

**Active Laser System (3 marks):** The system consists of a laser line projector, a CMOS camera, and a baseline of known length $B$ between them. The laser projects a line onto the target, the camera captures the bright spot, and a centroid-extraction algorithm computes the pixel offset $x_L - x_c$. The depth equation $Z = Bf/(x_L - x_c)$ is then evaluated for every pixel along the line to produce a height profile. This is used in bin-picking robots, floor-cleaning robots with obstacle profiling, and agricultural robots for plant-height estimation.

**(b) A stereo camera on a service robot has baseline $B = 0.20$ m and focal length $f = 800$ pixels. For an observed disparity $d = 16$ pixels, compute (i) the depth, (ii) the depth uncertainty if $\sigma_d = 0.5$ pixels, and (iii) the new disparity if the same point is observed at half the depth using a 0.40 m baseline.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Depth (2 marks):**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.20}{16} = \frac{160}{16} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Depth uncertainty (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_Z = \frac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \sigma_d = \frac{(10)^{2}}{800 \times 0.20} \times 0.5 = \frac{100}{160} \times 0.5 = 0.3125 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) New disparity at half the depth with $B' = 0.40$ m (2 marks):**

Half depth: $Z' = 5$ m.

$$d' = \frac{f \cdot B'}{Z'} = \frac{800 \times 0.40}{5} = 64 \text{ pixels}$$

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — PCRUT602, Model ESE Question B]**

**(a) Two landmarks $L_1$ and $L_2$ are placed 30 m apart on a straight road. A field robot observes $L_1$ at bearing $60^\circ$ and $L_2$ at bearing $120^\circ$ from its current heading. Using the bearing-triangulation equations, find the position of the robot.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Given $d = 30$ m, $\theta_1 = 60^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 120^\circ$.

Compute the tangents:

$$\tan 60^\circ = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$
$$\tan 120^\circ = -\sqrt{3} \approx -1.732$$

Sum of tangents: $\tan\theta_1 + \tan\theta_2 = 1.732 - 1.732 = 0$. **(1 mark — recognition of degenerate case)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A naive substitution gives $x = 0/0$, which is undefined. The student must recognize that bearings $60^\circ$ and $120^\circ$ place the robot on the **perpendicular bisector** of the baseline (an isoceles configuration).

**Geometric reasoning (3 marks):** Since the triangle $L_1 R L_2$ is isoceles with apex angle $60^\circ$ (the difference of the bearings as seen from the robot), the perpendicular distance $y$ to the baseline is:

$$y = \frac{d}{2} \tan\theta_{\text{apex}} = \frac{30}{2} \tan 60^\circ = 15 \sqrt{3} \approx 25.98 \text{ m}$$

**Alternative algebraic route (3 marks):** Apply a $90^\circ$ rotation in the bearing frame. If the two bearings are symmetric about the perpendicular bisector, the robot lies on $x = d/2 = 15$ m. Then:

$$y = x \tan\theta_1 = 15 \tan 60^\circ = 15\sqrt{3} \approx 25.98 \text{ m}$$

**Final position: $(x, y) = (15 \text{ m}, \, 25.98 \text{ m})$.**

---

**(b) List and briefly explain five major sources of error in triangulation-based sensing for outdoor field robots. How is each mitigated in practice?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| # | Error Source | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pixel/disparity noise** | Finite sensor resolution and photon shot noise | Use larger baseline, sensor with smaller pixel pitch, multi-frame averaging |
| 2 | **Lens distortion** | Radial and tangential distortion of camera lens | Calibrate with checkerboard and apply Brown–Conrady model |
| 3 | **Synchronization error** | Camera and laser (or two cameras) not sampled simultaneously | Hardware trigger line, GPS-PPS timestamping |
| 4 | **Baseline uncertainty** | Manufacturing tolerance, vibration, thermal expansion | Mechanical rigidity, temperature compensation, periodic recalibration |
| 5 | **Feature matching error** | Wrong correspondence in passive stereo | Use robust descriptors (SIFT, ORB), RANSAC outlier rejection |
| 6 | **Specular / transparent surfaces** | Active triangulation fails on glass or mirrors | Polarization filters, multimodal fusion with LiDAR |
| 7 | **Sun glare / ambient IR** | Washes out laser spot outdoors | Narrow-band optical filter, modulated laser with lock-in detection |

**(Award 1 mark per correctly explained error + mitigation, capped at 7 marks.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Triangulation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inverse-square nature of $\sigma_Z$:** Many students write $\sigma_Z = Z\sigma_d/(fB)$ — this is dimensionally wrong. The correct form is $\sigma_Z = Z^{2}\sigma_d/(fB)$.
> 2. **Mixing up triangulation with trilateration:** Triangulation uses **angles/bearings**; trilateration uses **distances only**. Examiners deduct 2 marks if the distinction is not made when explicitly asked.
> 3. **Sign errors in bearing equations:** The two bearings $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ must be measured in a **consistent frame** (e.g., both from the robot's heading, or both from the baseline). Mixing conventions loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Always state $Z$ in metres, $\sigma_Z$ in metres, $d$ in pixels.
> 5. **No diagram on a 7-mark question:** A labelled sketch of the geometry is worth at least 1 mark and often differentiates a 6 from a 7.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Triangulation = **determining position from angles + a known baseline** (SAS or ASA triangle).
- ✅ Stereo depth equation: $Z = fB/d$ — derived from the pinhole model.
- ✅ Bearing 2D localization equations: $x = d\tan\theta_2/(\tan\theta_1+\tan\theta_2)$ and $y = d\tan\theta_1\tan\theta_2/(\tan\theta_1+\tan\theta_2)$.
- ✅ Active triangulation = **robot emits + receives** (laser, IR, structured light).
- ✅ Passive triangulation = **robot only receives** (stereo camera, beacons).
- ✅ Depth uncertainty grows as **$Z^{2}$** for fixed disparity noise — quadratic growth law.
- ✅ Triangulation accuracy **increases linearly with baseline** $B$ and focal length $f$.
- ✅ Main error sources: pixel/disparity noise, lens distortion, sync error, baseline drift, feature mismatches, specular surfaces, ambient light.
- ✅ Applications: indoor service robots, agricultural robots, warehouse AMRs, ROV docking, profilers.
- ✅ Exam keywords to use: **disparity, baseline, ASA/SAS, Jacobian, covariance propagation, EKF fusion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigational Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Navigational Instruments # Navigational Instruments in Field and Service Robotics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Navigational Instruments** are a class of onboard sensors and electromechanical devices mounted on a field or service robotic platform that measure, compute, and provide real-time estimates of the robot's **position, velocity, orientation (pose), and heading** relative to a chosen reference frame. They form the perceptual backbone of any autonomous navigation stack, enabling **localization, path planning, mapping, and control**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCRUT602**, navigational instruments are broadly classified as: 1. **Inertial Sensors** — Gyroscopes, Accelerometers, and their fused form, the **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)**. 2. **Magnetic Heading Sensors** — Magnetometers and fluxgate compasses. 3. **Satellite-Based Sensors** — **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite Systems)** such as GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and NavIC. 4. **Dead-Reckoning Encoders** — Wheel/optical shaft encoders providing odometry. 5. **Altitude/Range Sensors** — Barometric altimeters, ultrasonic, LiDAR. 6. **Vision-Based Sensors** — Cameras (visual odometry, VSLAM). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — A Hiker in the Forest** > > Imagine you are a hiker deep inside a dense forest with no cell signal. How would you find your way home? You would use: > - A **compass** (magnetometer) to know which direction is North. > - A **pedometer + step length** (wheel odometry) to estimate distance walked. > - Your **inner-ear balance** (gyroscope + accelerometer) to know if you turned left or climbed a slope. > - Occasionally, a **GPS lock** when you reach a clearing (GNSS). > > A field robot navigates exactly the same way! It **fuses** all these "senses" because no single one is perfect. The compass drifts slowly, the pedometer accumulates error, GPS drops under tree canopy, and the inner ear accumulates bias. Together, they yield robust navigation — this is called **sensor fusion** (often implemented via a Kalman Filter). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metrics in Navigation:** > - **Accuracy** — measured in **meters** (position) or **degrees** (heading). > - **Update Rate** — measured in **Hz** (e.g., IMU: 100–1000 Hz; GPS: 1–10 Hz). > - **Drift Rate** — measured in **meters/second** or **degrees/hour** (for gyros). > - **WGS-84 Ellipsoid** — standard reference for global GPS coordinates. > - **Earth's Rotation Rate** $\Omega_E$ ≈ **15.041°/hour** (or 7.2921 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s). > - **Standard Gravity** $g$ ≈ **9.80665 m/s²**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Gyroscope — Measuring Angular Rate A gyroscope measures **angular velocity** $\omega$ about one or more axes. Modern MEMS gyroscopes use the **Coriolis Effect** — a vibrating mass is deflected proportional to the rotation rate. - **Operating Principle:** $\vec{F}_c = 2m (\vec{v} \times \vec{\omega})$ - **Output:** Angular velocity in **rad/s** or **°/s**. - **Integration:** To get orientation (roll, pitch, yaw), integrate angular rate over time: $\theta(t) = \int \omega(t)\, dt$. - **Limitation:** Integration causes **drift** — even tiny bias errors accumulate unboundedly. > [!NOTE] > **Types of Gyroscopes (in increasing accuracy):** > 1. **MEMS Gyro** — cheap, small, high drift (~°/s bias). > 2. **Fiber Optic Gyro (FOG)** — uses Sagnac effect; medium accuracy. > 3. **Ring Laser Gyro (RLG)** — used in aircraft; very low drift. > 4. **Hemispherical Resonator Gyro (HRG)** — ultra-precise; used in space. ### 2.2 Accelerometer — Measuring Specific Force An accelerometer measures **specific force** $\vec{f}$ — the non-gravitational acceleration acting on a proof mass. - **Output:** Acceleration in **m/s²** (or in units of $g$). - **Tilt Sensing:** When stationary, the accelerometer reads the gravity vector, allowing **roll and pitch** estimation: $$\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right), \qquad \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right)$$ - **Limitation:** Cannot distinguish gravity from linear acceleration — a major reason why **IMU fusion** is essential. ### 2.3 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) and INS > [!IMPORTANT] > An **IMU** is a 6-DOF sensor combining a **3-axis gyroscope** + **3-axis accelerometer** (often + a 3-axis magnetometer → 9-DOF "MARGsensor"). When integrated with computational algorithms over time, it forms an **Inertial Navigation System (INS)** that provides position, velocity, and attitude. **Strapdown INS Mechanization (Core Equations):** $$ \dot{\vec{\omega}}_{nb}^b = \vec{\omega}_{ib}^b - C_n^b\, \vec{\omega}_{ie}^n $$ $$ \dot{\vec{v}}^n = C_b^n\, \vec{f}^b - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n $$ $$ \dot{\vec{r}}^n = \vec{v}^n $$ where $C_b^n$ is the Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM) from body to navigation frame. ### 2.4 Magnetometer (Compass) Measures the Earth's magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ (~25–65 µT). Provides **absolute heading** (yaw) referenced to **Magnetic North**. - **Heading Calculation:** $$\psi = \arctan2\!\left(-B_y,\, B_x\right)$$ - **Disturbances:** Soft-iron and hard-iron distortions from motors, steel structures, and electronics require **magnetic calibration** (ellipsoid fitting). ### 2.5 GNSS / GPS > [!IMPORTANT] > **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** provides absolute position by trilateration using timing signals from satellites. The standard reference frame is **WGS-84**. - **Position Fix Accuracy:** ~**2–5 m** (civilian L1 C/A), ~**cm-level** with RTK/PPK corrections. - **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** quantifies satellite geometry quality. ### 2.6 Wheel Odometry / Encoders Optical or magnetic encoders mounted on drive wheels provide tick counts proportional to wheel rotation. Position is obtained by integrating the wheel rotation: $$ \Delta s = r \cdot \Delta\phi, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l}{b} $$ where $r$ is wheel radius, $b$ is wheelbase. **Limitation:** Wheel slip and uneven terrain cause unbounded position drift. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Notes / Boundary Condition** | |---|---|---|---| | Coriolis Force | $F_c = 2m(v \times \omega)$ | N | Basis of MEMS gyroscope | | Roll from Accel. | $\phi = \arctan(a_y / a_z)$ | rad | Stationary only | | Pitch from Accel. | $\theta = \arcsin(a_x / g)$ | rad | Stationary only | | Yaw from Magnetometer | $\psi = \arctan2(-B_y, B_x)$ | rad | After hard-iron calibration | | Wheel displacement | $\Delta s = r \cdot \Delta\phi$ | m | Assumes no slip | | Heading change | $\Delta\theta = (\Delta\phi_r - \Delta\phi_l)/b$ | rad | Differential drive | | Position update (INS) | $\dot{r}^n = v^n$ | m/s | Integrated in nav-frame | | Velocity update (INS) | $\dot{v}^n = C_b^n f^b - (2\omega_{ie} + \omega_{en}) \times v^n + g^n$ | m/s² | Coriolis-corrected | | Earth rotation rate | $\omega_{ie} = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ | rad/s | Used in Schuler effect | | Schuler Period | $T_s = 2\pi\sqrt{R_e/g} \approx 84.4$ | min | Period of undamped oscillation | | GPS Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | m | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** INS is used in **aircraft autopilots, submarine navigation, missile guidance, and Mars rovers (NASA's Curiosity uses an IMU + visual odometry fusion)**. GNSS-INS fusion dominates commercial UAVs (DJI drones) and autonomous cars (Waymo). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Tilt Angles from a Stationary Accelerometer When a robot is **stationary** (or moving at constant velocity), the accelerometer measures only the reaction to gravity. We want roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$. **Step 1 — Define the gravity vector in body frame.** For a body rotated by roll $\phi$ (about $x$-axis) and pitch $\theta$ (about $y$-axis), the gravity vector in the body frame is: $$ \vec{g}^b = R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi)\, \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -g \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Expand the rotation matrices.** $$ R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Multiply to obtain the gravity components.** After multiplication, the measured specific force components (opposite to gravity) are: $$ a_x = g \sin\theta $$ $$ a_y = -g \sin\phi \cos\theta $$ $$ a_z = -g \cos\phi \cos\theta $$ **Step 4 — Invert to extract tilt.** From the first equation: $$ \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) $$ From the ratio of the second to the third: $$ \frac{a_y}{a_z} = \frac{-g \sin\phi \cos\theta}{-g \cos\phi \cos\theta} = \tan\phi $$ $$ \phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic recap:** Roll comes from the ratio of lateral to vertical acceleration; pitch comes from the arcsine of the forward component normalized by gravity. This derivation assumes no linear acceleration — a critical limitation that motivates IMU fusion with gyroscopes. ### 3.2 Derivation of Wheel-Odometry Update (Differential Drive) **Setup:** Two wheels of radius $r$, separated by wheelbase $b$. Encoders count $N_r$ and $N_l$ ticks over a sampling interval $\Delta T$. Encoder resolution: $C$ ticks per revolution. **Step 1 — Convert ticks to angular displacement.** $$ \Delta\phi_r = \frac{2\pi N_r}{C}, \qquad \Delta\phi_l = \frac{2\pi N_l}{C} $$ **Step 2 — Compute arc lengths traversed by each wheel.** $$ \Delta s_r = r \cdot \Delta\phi_r, \qquad \Delta s_l = r \cdot \Delta\phi_l $$ **Step 3 — Compute linear displacement and rotation of the robot center.** $$ \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2} $$ $$ \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} $$ **Step 4 — Update pose in 2D plane.** $$ x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2) $$ $$ y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2) $$ $$ \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic recap:** The midpoint approximation $\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2$ is used for the heading during the arc to avoid the inconsistency of integrating a constant heading over a turning arc. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Characteristics Comparison | **Sensor** | **Measures** | **Absolute / Relative** | **Drift?** | **Update Rate (Hz)** | **Typical Accuracy** | **Susceptibility to Disturbance** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Gyroscope | Angular rate | Relative | **Yes** (bias) | 100 – 1000 | 0.01 – 1 °/s bias | Vibration sensitive | | Accelerometer | Specific force | Both (gravity) | Low (gravity) | 100 – 1000 | 0.001 – 0.01 g | Linear acceleration disturbs tilt | | Magnetometer | Magnetic field | Absolute (heading) | No | 10 – 100 | 1 – 2° | High (motors, steel) | | GNSS | Position | Absolute | Bounded | 1 – 10 | 2 – 5 m (L1) | Multipath, jamming, canopy | | Wheel Encoder | Wheel rotation | Relative | **Yes** (slip) | 100 – 1000 | < 1% distance | Wheel slip, deflation | | Barometer | Pressure → altitude | Relative | Low | 10 – 50 | ~0.5 m | Weather, wind | | LiDAR / Camera | Range/feature | Both (loop closure) | Bounded | 10 – 30 | cm-level | Lighting, dust, rain | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Example (KTU-style):** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.1$ m, wheelbase $b = 0.5$ m. Over 1 s, the right encoder gives 5000 ticks and left gives 4900 ticks. Encoder resolution $C = 10000$ ticks/rev. Starting pose $(x, y, \theta) = (0, 0, 0)$. Find the new pose. **Step 1 — Angular displacements:** $$ \Delta\phi_r = \frac{2\pi \times 5000}{10000} = \pi \text{ rad} \approx 3.1416 \text{ rad} $$ $$ \Delta\phi_l = \frac{2\pi \times 4900}{10000} = 0.98\pi \text{ rad} \approx 3.079 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 2 — Arc lengths:** $$ \Delta s_r = 0.1 \times \pi \approx 0.3142 \text{ m} $$ $$ \Delta s_l = 0.1 \times 0.98\pi \approx 0.3079 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Linear and angular displacements:** $$ \Delta s = \frac{0.3142 + 0.3079}{2} \approx 0.3110 \text{ m} $$ $$ \Delta\theta = \frac{0.3142 - 0.3079}{0.5} \approx 0.0126 \text{ rad} \approx 0.72° $$ **Step 4 — Updated pose:** $$ x_1 = 0 + 0.3110 \cdot \cos(0 + 0.0063) \approx 0.3110 \text{ m} $$ $$ y_1 = 0 + 0.3110 \cdot \sin(0.0063) \approx 0.00196 \text{ m} $$ $$ \theta_1 = 0 + 0.0126 \approx 0.0126 \text{ rad} $$ **Result:** New pose ≈ $(0.311,\ 0.002,\ 0.013)$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robot Navigation Stack The following Mermaid block represents the **block-level functional architecture flow** of a navigation system, mapping interactions between instruments, processors, and the controller. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SATELLITE CONSTELLATION
GNSS Signal Source] --> B[GNSS RECEIVER
L1 L5 RTK Module] C[EARTH MAGNETIC FIELD] --> D[MAGNETOMETER
3 Axis Fluxgate] E[ROBOT MOTION
Rotation Translation] --> F[IMU SENSOR
3 Axis Gyro plus 3 Axis Accel] G[WHEEL ROTATION] --> H[OPTICAL ENCODER
Tick Counter] I[ENVIRONMENT FEATURES
Landmarks Walls Trees] --> J[VISION OR LIDAR
Camera or Laser Scanner] B --> K[SENSOR FUSION ENGINE
Extended Kalman Filter EKF] D --> K F --> K H --> K J --> K K --> L[POSE ESTIMATOR
Position Velocity Attitude] L --> M[PATH PLANNER
Global and Local] M --> N[MOTION CONTROLLER
PID or MPC] N --> O[ROBOT ACTUATORS
Wheels Steer Motors] K --> P[STATE FEEDBACK LOOP
Correction Update] P --> K style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800 style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800 style E fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800 style G fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800 style I fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800 style K fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style M fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style N fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style O fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 ``` ### 4.2 Sensor Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NAVIGATIONAL INSTRUMENTS] --> B[INERTIAL] A --> C[NON INERTIAL] A --> D[EXTEROCEPTIVE] B --> B1[GYROSCOPE
Angular Rate] B --> B2[ACCELEROMETER
Specific Force] B --> B3[IMU MODULE
Combined] C --> C1[MAGNETOMETER
Heading] C --> C2[ENCODER
Wheel Odometry] D --> D1[GNSS RECEIVER
Absolute Position] D --> D2[BAROMETER
Altitude] D --> D3[VISION SENSOR
Visual Odometry] D --> D4[LIDAR
Scan Matching] B3 --> E[SENSOR FUSION
EKF or UKF] C1 --> E C2 --> E D1 --> E D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[NAVIGATION OUTPUT
Pose Velocity Attitude] ``` ### 4.3 Error Drift Behaviour — Conceptual Mermaid Plot (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[TIME T EQUALS 0] --> B[INITIAL ERROR ZERO] B --> C[GYROSCOPE ONLY] B --> D[WHEEL ODOMETRY ONLY] B --> E[GNSS ONLY] B --> F[IMU PLUS GNSS FUSED] C --> G[ERROR GROWS QUADRATIC
INS DOUBLE INTEGRATION] D --> H[ERROR GROWS LINEAR
SLIP ACCUMULATION] E --> I[ERROR BOUNDED BY SATELLITE GEOMETRY] F --> J[ERROR BOUNDED AND LOW
FUSION ADVANTAGE] style G fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Pure inertial navigation diverges quadratically over time (because position = double integral of acceleration), while GNSS-only has bounded but noisy errors. **Fusion gives the best of both worlds** — bounded error with smoothness. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). List its main components and state one advantage and one disadvantage.* **Model Answer:** An **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** is an electronic device that measures and reports a body's **specific force (acceleration)**, **angular rate**, and sometimes **magnetic field orientation**, using a combination of accelerometers, gyroscopes, and (optionally) magnetometers. - **Main Components:** 3-axis gyroscope, 3-axis accelerometer (and optionally 3-axis magnetometer). - **Advantage:** Provides high-rate (100–1000 Hz) self-contained navigation data with no external dependency. - **Disadvantage:** Inertial-only position estimates **drift unboundedly** over time due to integration of sensor bias. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a gyroscope and an accelerometer in terms of the physical quantity measured and the type of output (absolute vs relative).* **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **Gyroscope** | **Accelerometer** | |---|---|---| | Measures | Angular velocity $\omega$ (°/s) | Specific force $f$ (m/s²) | | Output type | **Relative** (integrate to get orientation) | **Mixed** — absolute when stationary (gravity), relative under motion | | Provides | Roll, Pitch, Yaw rate | Tilt (roll, pitch) at rest; linear acceleration under motion | | Drift | Yes — unbounded angular error | Low for tilt, but linear acceleration disturbs tilt reading | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model]** **(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working of a **strapdown Inertial Navigation System (INS)**. Derive the **velocity update equation** in the navigation frame, clearly indicating all terms. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working of Strapdown INS:** A strapdown INS mounts sensors directly on the vehicle body (no gimbal). Gyros measure body angular rates, accelerometers measure specific force, and a computer maintains a real-time **Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)** $C_b^n$ from body to navigation frame. The mechanization equations are integrated every cycle. **Functional Diagram:** ``` Gyro → ω_ib^b ─┐ ├─►[Strapdown Computer]─► DCM C_b^n ─►[Transformed accel f^n]─►[Velocity Update]─►[Position Update] Accel → f^b ─┘ ─►[Attitude Update] ``` **Derivation of Velocity Update Equation:** The fundamental equation of inertial navigation in the inertial frame is: $$ \dot{\vec{v}}^i = \vec{a}^i = \vec{f}^i + \vec{g}^i $$ where $\vec{f}$ is specific force and $\vec{g}$ is gravitational acceleration. We need this in the **navigation (Earth-fixed rotating) frame**. Differentiating in a rotating frame introduces Coriolis and centripetal terms: $$ \dot{\vec{v}}^n = \vec{f}^n - (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n + \vec{g}^n $$ **Step 1 — Convert specific force from body to navigation frame:** $$ \vec{f}^n = C_b^n\, \vec{f}^b $$ **Step 2 — Coriolis acceleration** (due to Earth rotation $\omega_{ie}$): $$ \vec{a}_{\text{Coriolis}} = 2\, \vec{\omega}_{ie}^n \times \vec{v}^n $$ **Step 3 — Transport rate** (due to navigation frame rotation $\omega_{en}$): $$ \vec{a}_{\text{transport}} = \vec{\omega}_{en}^n \times \vec{v}^n $$ **Step 4 — Combine into the final velocity update equation:** $$ \boxed{\;\dot{\vec{v}}^n = C_b^n\, \vec{f}^b \;-\; (2\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n + \vec{\omega}_{en}^n) \times \vec{v}^n \;+\; \vec{g}^n\;} $$ where: - $C_b^n$ = DCM from body to nav frame, - $\vec{\omega}_{ie}^n$ = Earth rate vector in nav frame, - $\vec{\omega}_{en}^n$ = transport rate, - $\vec{v}^n$ = velocity in nav frame, - $\vec{g}^n$ = local gravity vector. --- **(b)** Explain the **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** based fusion of **GNSS and IMU** for robot navigation. Why is sensor fusion necessary? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Why Sensor Fusion is Necessary:** Neither GNSS nor IMU alone is sufficient: - **GNSS-only:** Low update rate (1–10 Hz), noisy (~2–5 m), blocked indoors/under canopy. - **IMU-only:** High rate (100–1000 Hz), smooth, but **drifts unboundedly** due to integrated bias. **Fusion Concept (EKF):** The EKF maintains an estimate of the state vector: $$ \vec{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \vec{p}^n \\ \vec{v}^n \\ \vec{q}_b^n \\ \vec{b}_a \\ \vec{b}_g \end{bmatrix} $$ containing position, velocity, attitude quaternion, accelerometer bias, and gyro bias. **EKF Two-Step Recursion:** **Prediction (using IMU at high rate):** $$ \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1} = f(\hat{\vec{x}}_{k-1},\, \vec{u}_k) + \text{noise} $$ $$ P_{k|k-1} = F_k\, P_{k-1}\, F_k^T + Q_k $$ **Update (using GNSS at low rate):** $$ K_k = P_{k|k-1}\, H_k^T\,(H_k\, P_{k|k-1}\, H_k^T + R_k)^{-1} $$ $$ \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k} = \hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1} + K_k\,(\vec{z}_k - h(\hat{\vec{x}}_{k|k-1})) $$ $$ P_{k|k} = (I - K_k H_k)\, P_{k|k-1} $$ where $K_k$ is the **Kalman gain**, $R_k$ is GNSS measurement noise covariance, $H_k$ is the measurement Jacobian. **Net Effect:** - IMU provides smooth, high-rate estimates. - GNSS periodically **resets drift** in position. - Biases $\vec{b}_a, \vec{b}_g$ are estimated online. - Output is a robust, drift-bounded, smooth pose at high rate. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 model] — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Derive the equations for computing **roll and pitch from a 3-axis accelerometer** under stationary conditions. Why does this method fail during linear motion? Suggest a remedy. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** [Refer to derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes] When stationary, accelerometer reads: $$ a_x = g \sin\theta, \quad a_y = -g\sin\phi\cos\theta, \quad a_z = -g\cos\phi\cos\theta $$ Hence: $$ \phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{a_y}{a_z}\right), \quad \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) $$ **Why does it fail during linear motion?** During linear motion, the accelerometer measures **gravity + linear acceleration**: $$ \vec{a}_{\text{meas}} = \vec{g} + \vec{a}_{\text{linear}} $$ Since the accelerometer cannot distinguish these, the tilt estimate becomes **corrupted** by any forward, lateral, or vertical acceleration. A robot accelerating at $1\, g$ sideways would estimate a roll angle of $90°$ — clearly wrong. **Remedy:** Fuse with a gyroscope using a **complementary filter** or **EKF**. The gyroscope provides short-term accurate orientation changes ($\theta = \int \omega\, dt$), while the accelerometer provides long-term absolute reference. The fused tilt is: $$ \hat{\theta}_k = \alpha (\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \Delta t) + (1-\alpha)\,\theta_{\text{accel},k} $$ with $\alpha \approx 0.98$, giving the gyroscope dominant weight at high frequencies and the accelerometer anchoring at low frequencies. --- **(b)** Explain the working of a **magnetometer** for robot heading estimation. List the major sources of error and the calibration procedure to mitigate them. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle:** A magnetometer measures the **3 components of Earth's magnetic field** ($\sim 25\text{–}65\,\mu\text{T}$). The horizontal projection defines **Magnetic North**. **Heading Calculation:** $$ \psi = \arctan2(-B_y,\, B_x) $$ (assuming $B_x$ points forward and $B_y$ points left in the body frame). **Major Sources of Error:** 1. **Hard-Iron Distortion:** Permanent magnets, DC currents, and magnetized steel add a constant offset $\vec{b}$ to the field. 2. **Soft-Iron Distortion:** Ferromagnetic materials distort the field direction with a matrix $A$, so measured $\vec{B}_m = A \vec{B}_e + \vec{b}$. 3. **Magnetic Declination:** Difference between Magnetic North and True North (~ −23° to +23° depending on location). 4. **Tilt Error:** A tilted magnetometer needs compensation using roll/pitch from the IMU before computing heading. **Calibration Procedure (Ellipsoid Fitting):** 1. Rotate the robot slowly through all orientations. 2. Collect $(B_x, B_y, B_z)$ samples — they form a tilted ellipsoid. 3. Fit an ellipsoid model: $$ (\vec{B}_m - \vec{b})^T A^{-1} (\vec{B}_m - \vec{b}) = 1 $$ 4. Compute corrected field $\vec{B}_c = A^{-1}(\vec{B}_m - \vec{b})$. 5. Apply declination correction: $\psi_{\text{true}} = \psi_{\text{mag}} + \delta_{\text{declination}}$. 6. Apply tilt compensation using IMU-derived $\phi, \theta$. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to state units** — Always include rad/s, m/s², degrees, etc. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > 2. **Confusing angular rate ($\omega$) with angle ($\theta$)** — Gyro gives $\omega$, must integrate to get $\theta$. > 3. **Confusing specific force with acceleration** — Accelerometer reads $\vec{f} = \vec{a} - \vec{g}$. This trips up many students in derivations. > 4. **Sign errors in rotation matrices** — KTU strictly checks signs. Always define positive rotation directions in your answer. > 5. **Not explaining the "why"** — Pure formulae without justification get only partial marks (max 60% credit). Always state the **principle** before the math. > 6. **Using `GPS` and `GNSS` interchangeably without clarification** — GNSS is the generic term; GPS is the US-specific system. Be precise. > 7. **Skipping the assumption "robot is stationary"** in accelerometer tilt problems — this is a mandatory preamble. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Navigational Instruments** - **Gyroscope:** Measures angular velocity $\omega$; integrates to give orientation; suffers **bias drift**. - **Accelerometer:** Measures specific force $f$; gives **tilt (roll/pitch) when stationary**; useless for tilt during acceleration. - **IMU:** 6-DOF (gyro + accel) or 9-DOF (+ magnetometer). Foundation of every inertial system. - **INS:** IMU + mechanization equations → position, velocity, attitude. **Drifts over time** (quadratic in position). - **Magnetometer:** Measures Earth's magnetic field; gives **absolute heading** (yaw). Needs hard-iron calibration. - **GNSS:** Absolute position via satellites; **bounded error** but noisy and intermittent. Reference frame: **WGS-84**. - **Wheel Encoder:** Cheap dead reckoning; **drifts due to slip**; works only on flat, firm terrain. - **Sensor Fusion (EKF/UKF):** Combines complementary sensors → best estimate. **Always necessary** for robust navigation. - **Schuler Period:** ~**84.4 min** — characteristic oscillation of an undamped INS (Schuler tuning keeps errors bounded). - **Earth Rate:** $\omega_{ie} \approx 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s — required in all INS equations for Coriolis correction. - **Coordinate Frames to remember:** Body (b), Navigation/local-tangent (n), Earth-Centered-Earth-Fixed (ECEF), Inertial (i). - **GNSS-INS Fusion** is the de-facto standard in modern field robotics (UAVs, AGVs, autonomous cars, Mars rovers). - **Differential Drive Odometry Formulae** $\Delta s = (\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)/2$, $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)/b$ are **frequently asked numericals**. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Navigational Instruments**

topics.title: Case Study: A Detailed Look at a Work Class ROV - Mechanical Subsystems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: A Detailed Look at a Work Class ROV - Mechanical Subsystems

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Work Class Remotely Operated Vehicle (Work Class ROV)** is a tethered, unmanned, free-swimming subsea robotic system rated for depths typically between **3,000 m and 6,000 m**, capable of performing heavy intervention tasks such as drilling support, pipeline inspection, valve manipulation, and subsea construction. The **mechanical subsystem** of a Work Class ROV constitutes the structural, hydrodynamic, actuation, buoyancy, and pressure-tolerant hardware that physically enables the vehicle to operate, manipulate payloads, and survive the deep-sea environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of *PCRUT602 - Field and Service Robotics* requires students to identify and functionally describe the principal mechanical subsystems of a Work Class ROV, including the frame, thruster array, buoyancy modules, tether, TMS (Tether Management System), skid, manipulator, and pressure housings.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The ROV as a "Deep-Sea Astronaut"

Imagine a scuba-diving **astronaut** who cannot breathe air directly and must be connected to the surface by an **umbilical cord (tether)** supplying power, communication, and life support. The astronaut wears:

- A **rigid exoskeleton (frame)** to carry heavy tools.
- **Jet boots (thrusters)** to hover and move in 3D.
- A **toolbelt and mechanical arm (manipulator + skid)** to perform tasks.
- A **foam vest (buoyancy module)** to stay neutrally afloat.
- A **pressure suit (pressure housings & oil-filled compartments)** to survive crushing depths.
- A **winch and cable reel (TMS)** on the surface ship that pays out and retrieves the cord smoothly.

That "astronaut" is your Work Class ROV. The mechanical subsystems are the bones, muscles, skin, and life-support rolled into one underwater machine.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Standard)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Depth Rating | **3,000 – 6,000 m** | Defines pressure housing design pressure |
| Payload Capacity | **100 – 350 kg** (in air) | Dictates skid & frame sizing |
| Thrust Force | **50 – 800 kgf** total | Determines thruster count and sizing |
| Weight in Air | **1,500 – 5,000 kg** | Buoyancy to provide ~5–10% net positive reserve |
| Power | **75 – 250 hp (hydraulic)** | Drives HPU on the vehicle |

> [!NOTE]
> The metric **kgf (kilogram-force)** is the standard unit for ROV thrust; 1 kgf ≈ **9.81 N**.

## 1.4 Overview of Mechanical Subsystems

A Work Class ROV mechanical architecture comprises the following **interlocking subsystems**:

1. **Main Frame (Chassis)** – The load-bearing skeleton.
2. **Buoyancy Module** – Syntactic foam providing net buoyancy.
3. **Thruster Array** – Hydraulic/proportional thrusters for 6-DOF motion.
4. **Pressure Housings** – Sealed vessels for electronics.
5. **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** – Pumps, valves, reservoir, filter.
6. **Manipulator Arm(s)** – Usually 7-function master–slave arms.
7. **Tether / Umbilical** – Power, communication, hydraulic supply lines.
8. **Tether Management System (TMS)** – Surface or subsea winch & sheave.
9. **Skid / Tooling Base Plate** – Interface for intervention tools.
10. **Cable / Hose Routing & Connectors** – Wet-mateable connectors.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love a **labelled block diagram** of the ROV. Memorise the names and the function of each block.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem 1 — The Main Frame (Chassis)

The frame is a **welded steel (often API 2H Grade 50) or marine-grade aluminium** open-lattice structure designed to:
- Carry payload inertia during descent/lift.
- Provide mounting points for thrusters, buoyancy, skids.
- Resist hydrodynamic drag and current-induced bending moments.
- Allow access for maintenance and tooling.

### Design Philosophy
- **Modular bolted assembly** so field technicians can swap out thrusters, sonar, or arms without returning to shore.
- **Open architecture** to allow free water flow-through, reducing added-mass and drag.
- **Cathodic protection (zinc anodes)** to combat galvanic corrosion in seawater.

> [!NOTE]
> The frame must withstand **1.5× the operational weight in air** as a safety factor per DNV-OS-E101 / API 17D standards.

## 2.2 Subsystem 2 — Buoyancy Module

### Material: **Syntactic Foam**
A composite of **hollow glass microspheres (microballoons)** dispersed in an epoxy matrix. The microspheres (typically 20–200 µm diameter) trap gas, giving extremely low density.

### Density Equation

$$\rho_{foam} = \rho_{matrix} \cdot (1 - V_f) + \rho_{gas} \cdot V_f$$

Where:
- $\rho_{matrix}$ = density of epoxy resin (~1,100 kg/m³)
- $\rho_{gas}$ = density of trapped air (~1.2 kg/m³)
- $V_f$ = volume fraction of microballoons (typically 0.55–0.65)

> For typical Work Class ROV foam: $\rho_{foam} \approx$ **480 – 560 kg/m³**

## 2.3 Subsystem 3 — Thruster Array

### Types
- **Hydraulic thrusters** (most common in Work Class) using a **fixed-pitch propeller** with variable-speed reversible hydraulic motor.
- **Electric thrusters** (Brushless DC) used in smaller Observation Class ROVs.

### Configuration: **4 + 2 Vectored**
A standard Work Class ROV uses **4 corner (lateral) thrusters** for surge/sway/yaw and **2 vertical thrusters** for heave and roll control. Some heavy ROVs add 2 more vertical thrusters (4+4).

### Thrust Equation (Ideal Open-Water Propeller)

$$T = \rho_{sw} \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \cdot K_T$$

Where:
- $T$ = thrust (N)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density (**1,025 kg/m³** for saltwater, **1,000 kg/m³** for fresh)
- $n$ = propeller rotational speed (rev/s)
- $D$ = propeller diameter (m)
- $K_T$ = thrust coefficient (function of advance ratio $J$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **advance ratio** $J = \dfrac{V_a}{nD}$, where $V_a$ is the advance velocity. For a bollard (zero-velocity) condition, $J=0$ and $K_T$ is maximum.

## 2.4 Subsystem 4 — Pressure Housings

Subsea pressure at depth $h$:

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$$

At **3,000 m**:
$$P \approx 101,325 + (1025)(9.81)(3000) \approx 30.27 \text{ MPa}$$

The pressure housing is a **spherical or cylindrical** (with hemispherical end caps) vessel of **aluminium 6061-T6 or titanium Grade 5**. The wall thickness for a **cylinder** is given by:

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{\sigma_{allow} - 0.6 \cdot P}$$

For a **sphere**:

$$t = \dfrac{P \cdot r_i}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} - 0.2 \cdot P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Spherical housings are 2× more efficient (less material for the same pressure rating) but harder to manufacture — that's why **cylindrical** housings are common.

## 2.5 Subsystem 5 — Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)

Components:
- **Electric motor** (driven by surface via tether) — typically 75–250 hp.
- **Hydraulic pump** (axial piston, variable displacement).
- **Reservoir** (compensated to ambient pressure via bladder).
- **Filters, accumulators, valves, manifolds**.
- **Compensator bladder** — keeps internal oil pressure ≥ ambient seawater pressure (preventing implosion of reservoir and ingress of seawater).

Working pressure: typically **207 bar (3,000 psi)** or **300 bar (4,350 psi)**.

## 2.6 Subsystem 6 — Manipulator Arm

A Work Class ROV typically carries **two arms**:
1. **7-function master–slave arm** (e.g., Schilling Titan, Kraft Predator) — 3 shoulder, 1 elbow, 3 wrist, plus gripper.
2. **5-function** auxiliary arm for simpler tasks.

Each joint is driven by a **hydraulic actuator** commanded by the pilot's master controller via bilateral force-feedback telemetry.

## 2.7 Subsystem 7 — Tether / Umbilical

A composite cable carrying:
- **Power conductors** (3-phase AC, 3× AWG #6 or larger).
- **Signal cores** (twisted pairs, fibre optics for high bandwidth video).
- **Hydraulic supply/return hoses** (2× high-pressure, 1× low-pressure).

Typical diameter: **20 – 30 mm**. Weight in water: ~1–2 kg/m.

## 2.8 Subsystem 8 — Tether Management System (TMS)

A **garage / cage** mounted on top of the ROV. Functions:
- **Storage** of 100–300 m of tether during transit (decouples surface heave).
- **Snubber / sheave** for deploying the tether smoothly.
- **Docking** the ROV to launch & recover via a frame (LARS).

> [!TIP]
> A "**garage TMS**" travels with the ROV; a "**Top-hat TMS**" stays on the surface vessel.

## 2.9 Subsystem 9 — Skid / Base Plate

Removable platform beneath the ROV carrying:
- **Tooling** (jet pumps, torque tools, cutter).
- **Auxiliary sensors** (CP probe, UT thickness gauge).
- **Sample baskets** for recovered items.

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$ | Pa | $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | Cylindrical Housing Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P r_i}{\sigma - 0.6 P}$ | m | Barlow's modified formula |
| 3 | Spherical Housing Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P r_i}{2\sigma - 0.2 P}$ | m | More material-efficient |
| 4 | Open-Water Thrust | $T = \rho_{sw} n^2 D^4 K_T$ | N | Function of $J$ |
| 5 | Advance Ratio | $J = V_a / (nD)$ | – | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Syntactic Foam Density | $\rho_{foam} = \rho_{mat}(1-V_f) + \rho_{gas} V_f$ | kg/m³ | $V_f \in [0.55, 0.65]$ |
| 7 | Hydrodynamic Drag | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} C_D A V^2$ | N | $C_D$ depends on shape |
| 8 | Added Mass (Sphere) | $m_{a} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V$ | kg | Potential-flow theory |
| 9 | Buoyant Force | $F_b = \rho_{sw} g V_{disp}$ | N | Archimedes' principle |
| 10 | Net Reserve Buoyancy | $\Delta F = F_b - W_{sub}$ | N | Should be **+5–10%** of $W_{sub}$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Net Reserve Buoyancy for a Work Class ROV

The ROV must be **slightly positively buoyant** so that, in case of power loss, it can be retrieved to the surface without active thrust. Let us derive the required syntactic foam volume.

### Given (Representative Work Class ROV)
- Weight of frame + HPU + electronics in air, $W_{air}$ = **3,000 kgf**
- Weight of manipulators + skid, $W_{tools}$ = **500 kgf**
- Density of syntactic foam, $\rho_{foam}$ = **500 kg/m³**
- Density of seawater, $\rho_{sw}$ = **1,025 kg/m³**
- Required net reserve buoyancy = **+8%** of submerged weight.

### Step 1 — Compute Submerged Weight (without foam)

$$W_{sub,\,no\,foam} = (W_{air} + W_{tools}) \cdot \frac{g}{g} = 3{,}500 \text{ kgf}$$

We are working in kgf for convenience; the net is a weight ratio.

### Step 2 — Apply Reserve Buoyancy Requirement

Total desired buoyant force:

$$F_{b,\,req} = W_{sub,\,no\,foam} \times 1.08 = 3{,}500 \times 1.08 = 3{,}780 \text{ kgf}$$

### Step 3 — Volume Displacement Required

Using $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$:

$$V_{disp} = \frac{F_{b,\,req}}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{3{,}780}{1{,}025} = 3.688 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Subtract Existing Displacement (Steel + Electronics)

Assume the metal/electronics displace $V_{metal} \approx 0.40 \text{ m}^3$ (from their mass and average density ~8,750 kg/m³).

> Let us be precise:

$$V_{metal} = \frac{3{,}500}{8{,}750} \approx 0.40 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5 — Volume of Syntactic Foam Needed

$$V_{foam} = V_{disp} - V_{metal} = 3.688 - 0.40 = 3.288 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 6 — Mass of Syntactic Foam

$$m_{foam} = \rho_{foam} \cdot V_{foam} = 500 \times 3.288 = 1{,}644 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 7 — Final Submerged Weight (with foam)

$$W_{sub,\,final} = W_{air} + W_{tools} + m_{foam} \cdot 1\text{(weight ratio)} = 3{,}500 + 1{,}644 = 5{,}144 \text{ kgf}$$

Buoyant force at $V_{disp} = 3.688$ m³:

$$F_b = 1{,}025 \times 3.688 \times 9.81 / 9.81 = 3{,}780 \text{ kgf}$$

### Step 8 — Verify Net Reserve

$$\Delta F = F_b - W_{sub,\,final} = 3{,}780 - 5{,}144 = -1{,}364 \text{ kgf}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Error Pitfall:** If $\Delta F < 0$, the ROV sinks. We must add MORE foam. The iteration: required foam volume is found by solving simultaneously for $V_{foam}$ such that $\Delta F = +0.08 \times W_{sub,\,final}$.

### Step 9 — Re-iterate Correctly

Let the foam volume be $V_f$. Then:
- Foam mass $= 500 V_f$
- Submerged weight $= 3{,}500 + 500 V_f$ (kgf)
- Total displaced volume $\approx 0.40 + V_f$ (assuming foam is fully flooded, only metals displace — but syntactic foam is *sealed* and contributes its own volume to displacement).

Re-evaluate: foam displaces seawater at its outer volume. So:
- $V_{disp} = V_{metal} + V_{foam} = 0.40 + V_f$
- $F_b = 1{,}025 (0.40 + V_f)$
- $W_{sub} = 3{,}500 + 500 V_f$

Set $F_b = 1.08 \, W_{sub}$:

$$1{,}025(0.40 + V_f) = 1.08(3{,}500 + 500 V_f)$$

$$410 + 1{,}025 V_f = 3{,}780 + 540 V_f$$

$$485 V_f = 3{,}370$$

$$V_f = 6.95 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 10 — Final Mass of Foam

$$m_{foam} = 500 \times 6.95 = 3{,}475 \text{ kg}$$

> **Analytical Insight:** A 3-tonne ROV typically requires **6–8 m³** of syntactic foam — a substantial fraction of its overall size! This is why the buoyancy module is one of the largest mechanical subsystems visually.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Pressure Housing Wall Thickness

### Given
- Design depth $h$ = 3,000 m
- Housing internal radius $r_i$ = 0.10 m (200 mm OD cylinder)
- Material: Aluminium 6061-T6, yield $\sigma_y$ = 240 MPa
- Safety factor $SF$ = 1.5 → $\sigma_{allow} = 160$ MPa

### Step 1 — Compute External Pressure

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h = 101{,}325 + (1{,}025)(9.81)(3{,}000)$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 30{,}165{,}750 = 30.27 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Cylindrical Formula (Hoop Stress Dominant)

$$t = \frac{P \cdot r_i}{\sigma_{allow} - 0.6 P}$$

$$t = \frac{30.27 \times 10^6 \times 0.10}{160 \times 10^6 - 0.6 \times 30.27 \times 10^6}$$

$$t = \frac{3.027 \times 10^6}{(160 - 18.16) \times 10^6} = \frac{3.027}{141.84}$$

$$t = 0.0213 \text{ m} = 21.3 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Check Buckling

For a long cylinder under external pressure, **elastic buckling** (Bresse-Bryan formula) often governs:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 E \, t^{2.5}}{L \cdot r^{1.5} \cdot (1 - \nu^2)^{0.75}}$$

With $E = 70$ GPa, $L = 1$ m, $\nu = 0.33$, $t = 0.0213$ m, $r = 0.10$ m:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times (0.0213)^{2.5}}{1 \times (0.10)^{1.5} \times (1 - 0.109)^{0.75}}$$

$$P_{cr} \approx \frac{2.6 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 6.60 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0316 \times 0.926}$$

$$P_{cr} \approx \frac{1.20 \times 10^7}{0.0293} \approx 410 \text{ MPa}$$

> Since $P_{cr} = 410$ MPa $\gg P = 30.27$ MPa, the housing is safe against buckling by a factor of **~13.5×**. A thicker wall is unnecessary.

### Step 4 — Compare with Spherical Housing

For a sphere of same $r_i$:

$$t_{sphere} = \frac{P r_i}{2 \sigma_{allow} - 0.2 P} = \frac{30.27 \times 0.10}{320 - 6.05} \times 10^6 / 10^6$$

$$t_{sphere} = \frac{3.027}{313.95} = 9.64 \text{ mm}$$

> The sphere needs **~half** the wall thickness — confirming its material efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Subsystem vs. Design Trade-off

| Subsystem | Primary Function | Material | Critical Design Constraint | Typical Failure Mode |
|-----------|------------------|----------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| Frame | Load-bearing structure | API 2H steel / Al 6082 | Yield stress vs. payload | Buckling, weld fatigue |
| Buoyancy | Net positive lift | Syntactic foam (glass-epoxy) | Crush depth of microspheres | Foam collapse at depth |
| Thruster | 6-DOF propulsion | Ni-Al-Bronze propeller | Cavitation, bollard thrust | Seal failure, blade erosion |
| Pressure Housing | Protect electronics | Al 6061-T6 / Ti Gr-5 | External collapse | Buckling, O-ring breach |
| HPU | Hydraulic power | Stainless manifolds | Internal cleanliness | Pump cavitation, hose rupture |
| Manipulator | Subsea intervention | Hard-anodised Al, Ti gears | Joint torque vs. stall | Seal failure, gripper stall |
| Tether | Power + comms + hyd | Polyethylene jacket | Tensile + bending fatigue | Conductor break, kink |
| TMS | Tether management | Al sheaves, steel cable | Sheave wear, slip | Cable birdcaging |
| Skid | Tool mounting | Al / SS plate | Bolt shear | Bolt loosening, corrosion |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Work Class ROV Mechanical Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    surface[Surface Vessel LARS] --> tms[Tether Management System]
    tms -->|umbilical cable| tether[Tether and Umbilical]
    tether -->|power, signal, hydraulic| rovmain[ROV Main Body]
    
    rovmain --> frame[Open Lattice Frame]
    rovmain --> buoy[Buoyancy Module Syntactic Foam]
    rovmain --> hpu[Hydraulic Power Unit HPU]
    rovmain --> housings[Pressure Housings Electronics Pods]
    
    frame --> thrusters[Thruster Array 4 and 2 Vectored]
    frame --> arm1[7-Function Manipulator Arm]
    frame --> arm2[5-Function Auxiliary Arm]
    frame --> skid[Skid and Base Plate]
    
    hpu -->|hydraulic fluid| thrusters
    hpu -->|hydraulic fluid| arm1
    hpu -->|hydraulic fluid| arm2
    hpu -->|hydraulic fluid| skidtools[Skid Mounted Tools]
    
    housings -->|sensor data| tether
    thrusters -->|thrust vector| water[Surrounding Seawater]
    buoy -->|net lift| water
    arm1 -->|manipulation| water
    skid -->|tooling| water
    
    tms2[Subsea Garage TMS Optional] -.-> tether
```

## 4.2 Thruster Configuration Schematic (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopView[ROV Top View]
        FL[Front-Left Thruster Surge Sway] 
        FR[Front-Right Thruster Surge Sway]
        RL[Rear-Left Thruster Surge Sway]
        RR[Rear-Right Thruster Surge Sway]
        VL[Vertical Thruster Left Heave Roll]
        VR[Vertical Thruster Right Heave Roll]
    end
    FL --- FR
    RL --- RR
    FL --- RL
    FR --- RR
    VL ~~~ VR
```

> [!NOTE]
> Four corner thrusters provide **surge, sway, and yaw**. The differential speed of diagonal pairs gives yaw control. Two vertical thrusters give heave and roll.

## 4.3 Pressure Housing Geometry Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cyl[Cylindrical Pressure Housing]
    cyl --> ec1[Hemispherical End Cap Front]
    cyl --> ec2[Hemispherical End Cap Rear]
    ec1 --> conn1[Wet-Mateable Connector Port]
    ec2 --> conn2[Wet-Mateable Connector Port]
    cyl --> seal[O-Ring Seals Between Sections]
    cyl --> int[Internal Electronics Payload]
```

## 4.4 Syntactic Foam Microstructure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    foam[Syntactic Foam Block] --> matrix[Epoxy Matrix]
    foam --> spheres[Hollow Glass Microballoons]
    spheres --> g1[Gas-Filled Sphere 50 micron]
    spheres --> g2[Gas-Filled Sphere 100 micron]
    spheres --> g3[Gas-Filled Sphere 200 micron]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List any three functions of a Tether Management System (TMS) on a Work Class ROV.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Storage** of a length of the tether (100–300 m) so that the ROV can be decoupled from surface heave motions and operated in "garage" mode.
2. **Heave compensation** by acting as a snubber/sheave that pays out and retrieves tether smoothly, reducing dynamic loading.
3. **Launch & Recovery (LARS)** aid — docks the ROV into a protective frame during deployment and recovery in rough seas.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]
**Why is syntactic foam preferred over solid plastic for ROV buoyancy modules?**

**Model Answer:**
- Syntactic foam has a **much lower density** (480–560 kg/m³) than solid plastic (~900–1,200 kg/m³) because its hollow glass microballoons trap gas.
- It is **compressive-strength resistant** at depth (microspheres resist hydrostatic collapse up to their rated crush depth, often 4,000–6,000 m).
- It provides high buoyancy with **low water absorption** when correctly sealed, giving long-term stable net reserve buoyancy.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **mechanical subsystems of a Work Class ROV** and state the function of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Work Class ROV is to operate at a depth of **4,000 m**. Its cylindrical aluminium 6061-T6 pressure housing has an internal radius of **120 mm**, allowable stress of **160 MPa**, and safety factor already included. Calculate the required wall thickness. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{sw} = 1,025$ kg/m³, $P_{atm} = 101$ kPa.

#### Model Solution (a):
A labelled block diagram is required (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard layout). Each subsystem's function:

| Subsystem | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Frame | Carries payload, mounting points |
| Buoyancy Module | Provides net positive lift |
| Thruster Array | 6-DOF propulsion |
| Pressure Housing | Protects electronics from sea pressure |
| HPU | Generates & distributes hydraulic power |
| Manipulator | Performs intervention tasks |
| Tether | Supplies power, signals, hydraulics |
| TMS | Manages tether, aids LARS |
| Skid | Mounts tools, sensors |
| Connectors | Allow modular hot-swap |

#### Model Solution (b):
**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure**

$$P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h = 101{,}325 + (1{,}025)(9.81)(4{,}000)$$

$$P = 101{,}325 + 40{,}221{,}000 = 40.32 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Wall Thickness (Cylinder)**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot r_i}{\sigma_{allow} - 0.6 P} = \frac{40.32 \times 10^6 \times 0.120}{160 \times 10^6 - 0.6 \times 40.32 \times 10^6}$$

$$t = \frac{4.838 \times 10^6}{160 \times 10^6 - 24.19 \times 10^6} = \frac{4.838}{135.81} \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10^3$$

Wait — let me recompute clearly:

$$t = \frac{40.32 \times 0.120}{160 - 24.19} \text{ (in m, with pressures in MPa)}$$

$$t = \frac{4.838}{135.81} = 0.03563 \text{ m} \approx 35.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** $t \approx$ **35.6 mm** of aluminium wall thickness is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **4 + 2 vectored thruster configuration** of a Work Class ROV. Show how surge, sway, heave, and yaw are produced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Work Class ROV has **mass in air = 4,000 kg** (including all metal and electronics) and uses syntactic foam of density **520 kg/m³**. The required net reserve buoyancy is **+7%** of the submerged weight. Seawater density is 1,025 kg/m³. Calculate the volume of foam required. (Assume all metal displaces water equal to its mass / 8,000 kg/m³.)

#### Model Solution (a):
The **4 + 2 vectored** configuration is the industry standard for Work Class ROVs. It provides control in 4 of the 6 DOF (surge, sway, yaw, heave) with roll passively stabilised by metacentric height and pitch passively stabilised by fore-aft symmetry.

| Motion | Thrusters Used | Mechanism |
|--------|----------------|-----------|
| **Surge (forward/back)** | All 4 corner thrusters at same direction | Equal vector sum along x-axis |
| **Sway (lateral)** | All 4 corner thrusters at right angles | Vector sum along y-axis |
| **Yaw (rotating)** | Diagonal pairs in opposite directions | Differential thrust on diagonals |
| **Heave (vertical)** | Both vertical thrusters | Down/up thrust equal & opposite to gravity |
| **Roll** | Differential vertical thruster speeds | Right vs. left vertical |
| **Pitch** | Passively stable | Fore-aft symmetric design |

Some advanced ROVs add **2 more vertical thrusters (4+4)** to improve roll control and provide redundancy for heavy payloads.

#### Model Solution (b):
**Step 1 — Existing Displacement of Metals**

$$V_{metal} = \frac{4{,}000}{8{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Submerged Weight Before Foam**

$$W_{sub,0} = 4{,}000 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 3 — Buoyancy Force Equation**

Let $V_f$ = foam volume. Foam mass = $520 V_f$ kgf.

- $W_{sub} = 4{,}000 + 520 V_f$
- $V_{disp} = 0.50 + V_f$
- $F_b = 1{,}025 (0.50 + V_f)$

**Step 4 — Set $F_b = 1.07 \, W_{sub}$**

$$1{,}025 (0.50 + V_f) = 1.07 (4{,}000 + 520 V_f)$$

$$512.5 + 1{,}025 V_f = 4{,}280 + 556.4 V_f$$

$$468.6 V_f = 3{,}767.5$$

$$V_f = 8.04 \text{ m}^3$$

**Answer:** Approximately **8.04 m³** of syntactic foam is required. The foam mass = 520 × 8.04 = **4,181 kg**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Units Mix-up:** Using N and kgf interchangeably. Always state the unit; 1 kgf = 9.81 N. Examiners deduct **½ mark** per wrong unit.
> 2. **Wall-thickness formula confusion:** Cylinder vs. sphere formula must be applied correctly. **½–1 mark** lost on misapplication.
> 3. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure:** Some students compute $P = \rho g h$ only. Include $P_{atm}$.
> 4. **Reserve buoyancy iteration:** Students often forget to **iterate** to make the foam's own mass self-consistent — losing **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **No diagram in Q.(a) of Part B:** A **neat, labelled block diagram is compulsory** for full marks (typically 2 marks allotted to the diagram alone).
> 6. **Mixing up TMS types:** "Top-hat" (surface) vs. "Garage" (subsea) — be precise.
> 7. **Ignoring depth-related foam crush:** Syntactic foam has a depth rating; using foam rated for 1,000 m at 3,000 m causes collapse.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recap — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Work Class ROV** = tethered, hydraulic, 3,000–6,000 m depth rated, 100–350 kg payload.
- ☐ **Mechanical subsystems = 10**: Frame, Buoyancy, Thrusters, Pressure Housings, HPU, Manipulator, Tether, TMS, Skid, Connectors.
- ☐ **Syntactic foam density** ≈ 500 kg/m³, made of glass microballoons in epoxy.
- ☐ **Thruster configuration** = 4 (corner) + 2 (vertical) for 6-DOF (or 4+4 for heavy ROVs).
- ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure** $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$ — never forget $P_{atm}$!
- ☐ **Cylindrical housing thickness** $t = \dfrac{P r_i}{\sigma - 0.6 P}$.
- ☐ **Spherical housing thickness** $t = \dfrac{P r_i}{2\sigma - 0.2 P}$ (more material-efficient).
- ☐ **Reserve buoyancy** target = +5% to +10% of submerged weight.
- ☐ **HPU pressure** = 207 bar (3,000 psi) standard; uses pressure-compensated reservoir with bladder.
- ☐ **Tether** carries power, signals, and hydraulic hoses — typically 20–30 mm diameter.
- ☐ **TMS** decouples surface heave, stores tether, and aids Launch & Recovery.
- ☐ **Pressure housings** use Al 6061-T6 (shallow) or Ti Grade 5 (deep).
- ☐ **Frame** uses API 2H steel or marine Al, with cathodic (zinc) anodes.
- ☐ **Manipulator** is typically 7-function master–slave hydraulic (e.g., Schilling Titan).
- ☐ **Skid** is a removable base plate carrying tools and sensors.
- ☐ **Wet-mateable connectors** enable subsea hot-swap of components.
- ☐ **Failure modes**: foam collapse, housing buckling, O-ring breach, thruster seal failure, tether fatigue, galvanic corrosion.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions on this topic, expect either a *subsystem description with diagram* OR a *design calculation (housing/buoyancy/thruster)*. Practise both question types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Power Source ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power Source (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *power source* in a field or service robot is the onboard energy supply subsystem that converts stored chemical, electrical, mechanical, or radiant energy into regulated electrical energy required to actuate the robot's locomotion, manipulation, sensing, computation, and communication subsystems. In the context of field and service robotics, the power source is **not** merely a battery — it is a complete **energy management ecosystem** consisting of: - **Primary energy reservoir** (battery, fuel tank, hydrogen cylinder, solar panel) - **Energy conversion element** (DC-DC converter, fuel cell stack, motor-generator) - **Energy storage element** (ultracapacitor, flywheel, secondary battery) - **Power Management & Distribution Unit (PMDU)** - **Battery Management System (BMS)** ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Human Body:** > Think of a robot's power source exactly like the *human cardiovascular system*. The **battery/fuel cell is the heart**, **wires and bus bars are the arteries**, **DC-DC converters are the lungs** (regulating oxygen = voltage), **ultracapacitors are the fast-twitch muscles** (delivering bursts of energy), and the **BMS is the brain stem** (autonomously monitoring heart-rate, temperature, and oxygen levels). Without any one of these, the robot cannot function in the field. ### 1.3 Why the Power Source is the Most Critical Subsystem | Parameter | Impact on Robot | |---|---| | **Energy Density (Wh/kg)** | Directly determines mission duration | | **Power Density (W/kg)** | Determines acceleration & peak payload handling | | **Round-trip Efficiency (%)** | Affects recharging/refueling frequency | | **Cycle Life (cycles)** | Determines Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) | | **Operating Temperature Range** | Determines deployment environments (desert, arctic) | ### 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification of Robotic Power Sources 1. **Chemical Energy Sources** - Primary batteries (non-rechargeable: Alkaline, Lithium primary) - Secondary batteries (rechargeable: Li-ion, Li-Po, NiMH, Lead-acid) 2. **Hydrogen / Fuel Cell Systems** (PEMFC, SOFC) 3. **Solar / Photovoltaic Systems** 4. **Internal Combustion Engines (ICE)** + Generator 5. **Hybrid Power Systems** (Battery + Ultracapacitor, Fuel Cell + Battery) 6. **Pneumatic / Hydraulic** (compressed gas, hydraulic fluid) 7. **Ambient Energy Harvesting** (vibration, RF, thermal) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 Module 3):** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **mobile field robots** (agricultural, inspection, search-and-rescue) where power autonomy is the primary design constraint. Wired/tethered robots are *out of scope* for this module. ## 2.
