A robotic crew is structured as a **hierarchical control architecture** with three operational layers:

### Layer 1 — Mission Layer (Human Crew Commander)
- Receives mission objectives
- Assigns high-level tasks
- Monitors crew health and mission progress
- Has **override authority**

### Layer 2 — Coordination Layer (Crew Manager / Fleet Manager)
- Task allocation among agents
- Conflict resolution (e.g., two robots heading to the same waypoint)
- Resource scheduling (battery, bandwidth)
- Maintains **shared world model**

### Layer 3 — Execution Layer (Individual Robotic Agents)
- Local motion control
- Onboard sensing and obstacle avoidance
- Low-level actuator commands

## 2.2 Crew Formation Protocols

A **crew formation protocol** dictates *how robots join, synchronize, and disengage* from the team. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes three primary protocols:

| Protocol | Description | Use Case |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Static Crew** | Membership fixed at mission start | Mining, surveying |
| **Dynamic Crew** | Robots join/leave based on task load | Search & rescue |
| **Hybrid Crew** | Core members static, support members dynamic | Agricultural harvesting |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all key formulas** for solving Crew-related numerical and analytical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE. The vertical pipe symbol `|` is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $N_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Total crew capability = sum of agent capabilities | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | $T_{\text{mission}} = \max_{i} \left( \frac{W_i}{R_i} \right)$ | Mission completion time = bottleneck agent time | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $C_{\text{coord}} = \dfrac{M}{B \cdot \tau}$ | Coordination cost (M = msgs, B = bandwidth, $\tau$ = latency) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{\text{crew}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - f_i)$ | Crew reliability (series-parallel model) | Probability $\in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | $E_{\text{coverage}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{scanned}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Area coverage by crew | Percentage |
| 6 | $D_{\text{inter-agent}} \leq v_{\max} \cdot \tau_{\text{comms}}$ | Max safe inter-agent distance | meters (m) |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{crew}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{total}}}$ | Crew energy efficiency | Joule ratio |
| 8 | $P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot n \cdot t}$ | Crew collision probability (Poisson model) | Probability |
| 9 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{crew}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{single}}}{T_{\text{crew}}}$ | Parallelism gain | Ratio $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{tasks}}}{T_{\text{mission}}}$ | Crew task throughput | tasks/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Crew systems are deployed:**
> - **Search & Rescue (SAR):** UAV scouts map debris; UGVs extract victims; human crew coordinates via shared dashboard (e.g., post-earthquake Kobe, Christchurch operations).
> - **Precision Agriculture:** Crew of small weeding robots and drones covers **20–40 acres/day** vs. **3–5 acres/day** for a single tractor.
> - **Underwater Inspection:** AUV crew inspects subsea pipelines; human ROV pilot supervises from the surface vessel.
> - **Mining:** LHD (Load-Haul-Dump) crew robots operate in deep, GPS-denied mines with millimeter-wave relays.

## 2.5 Crew Communication Topologies

The topology of a crew determines **latency, robustness, and bandwidth efficiency.**

| Topology | Robustness | Latency | KTU Exam Tip |
|----------|------------|---------|--------------|
| **Star** (all agents → hub) | Low (hub failure = crew failure) | Low | Often used in Part A 2-mark questions |
| **Mesh** (peer-to-peer) | High | Medium | High-yield for 14-mark derivations |
| **Ring** (token passing) | Medium | High | Rarely used in field robotics |
| **Hierarchical** (tree) | Medium-High | Low | Common in military crew doctrine |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Crew Reliability (Series-Parallel Model)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for Module 3 of PCRUT602.

### Problem Statement
A field robotic crew has **$n$ agents** with individual reliability $f_i$ (probability of functioning correctly during the mission). Derive the **overall crew reliability** assuming that the mission succeeds if **at least one** agent in each functional sub-group succeeds (parallel redundancy) and all sub-groups must succeed (series dependency).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Single-Agent Failure Probability**

The probability that agent $i$ **fails** during the mission is:

$$
q_i = 1 - f_i
$$

**Step 2 — Probability That ALL Agents in a Sub-Group Fail**

If a sub-group of $k$ agents operates in **parallel** (mission succeeds if any one succeeds), the probability that **all $k$ agents fail** is the product of individual failure probabilities (assuming statistical independence):

$$
P_{\text{all fail}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} q_i = \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - f_i)
$$

*Reasoning:* Failures are modeled as **independent Bernoulli trials**, so the joint failure probability is the product of marginal probabilities.

**Step 3 — Sub-Group Success Probability**

By the **complement rule**, the probability that **at least one** agent in the parallel sub-group succeeds is:

$$
P_{\text{sub-group success}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - f_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Crew-Level Success (Series of Sub-Groups)**

If the mission requires **$m$ sub-groups** (e.g., perception, mobility, manipulation) all to succeed, and sub-groups are arranged in **series**, then the overall crew reliability is the product of sub-group reliabilities:

$$
F_{\text{crew}} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i \in S_j} (1 - f_i) \right]
$$

where $S_j$ is the set of agents in sub-group $j$.

**Step 5 — Special Case: Homogeneous Crew**

If all $n$ agents are identical with reliability $f$ (i.e., $f_i = f$ for all $i$), and the crew is a **single parallel group**, the formula simplifies to:

$$
F_{\text{crew}} = 1 - (1 - f)^n
$$

*Insight:* Doubling the number of identical agents does **not** double reliability — the gain is logarithmic. This is a classic KTU 2024 exam trap.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Given:** A SAR crew has 3 UGVs (perception) each with $f = 0.90$, and 2 UAVs (aerial scouting) each with $f = 0.95$. Both sub-groups must succeed. Find $F_{\text{crew}}$.

**Solution:**

Sub-group 1 (UGV) success:
$$
P_1 = 1 - (1 - 0.90)^3 = 1 - (0.10)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999
$$

Sub-group 2 (UAV) success:
$$
P_2 = 1 - (1 - 0.95)^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

Overall crew reliability:
$$
F_{\text{crew}} = 0.999 \times 0.9975 = 0.9965
$$

**Result:** $F_{\text{crew}} = 0.9965$ or **99.65% mission success probability**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Inter-Agent Distance for Safe Coordination

### Problem Statement
Two crew agents move at maximum relative speed $v_{\max}$ and communicate with latency $\tau_{\text{comms}}$. Derive the **maximum safe inter-agent distance** $D_{\max}$ that prevents collision.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set Up the Kinematic Constraint**

In the worst case, two agents move **directly toward each other** at closing speed $v_{\max}$. They must detect each other, transmit the warning, and execute an avoidance maneuver — all within the time it takes to close the gap.

**Step 2 — Time-to-Collision**

The time for the agents to collide (if no action is taken) is:

$$
t_{\text{collision}} = \frac{D}{v_{\max}}
$$

where $D$ is the current inter-agent distance.

**Step 3 — Total Reaction Time**

The total reaction time comprises **sensing + communication + braking**:

$$
t_{\text{react}} = t_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{comms}} + t_{\text{brake}}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety Constraint**

To avoid collision, we require:

$$
t_{\text{react}} \leq t_{\text{collision}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Solve**

$$
t_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{comms}} + t_{\text{brake}} \leq \frac{D}{v_{\max}}
$$

Solving for $D$:

$$
D \leq v_{\max} \cdot (t_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{comms}} + t_{\text{brake}})
$$

**Step 6 — Final Formula (Ignoring Onboard Sensing/Braking for Simplicity)**

If $t_{\text{sense}}$ and $t_{\text{brake}}$ are absorbed into a constant $k$:

$$
\boxed{D_{\max} = v_{\max} \cdot (\tau_{\text{comms}} + k)}
$$

*Engineering Insight:* For $v_{\max} = 2$ m/s, $\tau_{\text{comms}} = 0.1$ s, and $k = 0.05$ s, we get $D_{\max} = 2 \times 0.15 = 0.3$ m — meaning the agents must stay within **30 cm** of each other for safe reactive coordination.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Crew Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | KTU Trade-off Question |
|------------------|-----------------|------------------|-------------------------|
| **Crew size ($n$)** | ↑ Reliability, ↑ Coverage, ↓ Speedup | ↑ Per-agent complexity | Trade-off between fault tolerance and coordination cost |
| **Communication bandwidth ($B$)** | ↑ Coordination fidelity, ↑ Power | ↓ Energy efficiency | High B needed for video-heavy crews (SAR) |
| **Heterogeneity** | ↑ Capability diversity, ↑ Cost | ↑ Interoperability challenges | KTU 2024 hot topic |
| **Autonomy level** | ↓ Human workload, ↑ Software risk | ↑ Teleoperation burden | Human-on-the-loop vs. human-in-the-loop |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Layer Crew Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MissionLayer["MISSION LAYER HUMAN CREW COMMANDER"]
        nodeHC["HumanCommander"]
        nodeMO["MissionObjectives"]
        nodeOV["OverrideAuthority"]
    end

    subgraph CoordLayer["COORDINATION LAYER CREW MANAGER"]
        nodeTA["TaskAllocator"]
        nodeCR["ConflictResolver"]
        nodeSWM["SharedWorldModel"]
        nodeRS["ResourceScheduler"]
    end

    subgraph ExecLayer["EXECUTION LAYER ROBOTIC AGENTS"]
        nodeUGV["UGV1 Ground"]
        nodeUAV["UAV2 Aerial"]
        nodeUSV["USV3 Surface"]
    end

    subgraph SupportLayer["SUPPORT INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        nodeCOMMS["CommsRelay"]
        nodeCHARGE["ChargingStation"]
        nodeBASE["BaseStation"]
    end

    nodeHC --> nodeMO
    nodeHC --> nodeOV
    nodeOV --> nodeTA
    nodeMO --> nodeTA
    nodeTA --> nodeCR
    nodeTA --> nodeSWM
    nodeTA --> nodeRS
    nodeCR --> nodeUGV
    nodeCR --> nodeUAV
    nodeCR --> nodeUSV
    nodeRS --> nodeCOMMS
    nodeRS --> nodeCHARGE
    nodeSWM <--> nodeBASE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The top-down arrows represent command flow; the bidirectional arrow between the Shared World Model and Base Station indicates two-way telemetry and map updates.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Crew Formation Protocol Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["AgentArrives"] --> nodeB["BeaconBroadcast"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"JoinRequestValid"}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD["SyncWorldModel"]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE["RejectAgent"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["AssignTaskRole"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["ActiveCrewMember"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"MissionActive"}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeG
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI["DisengageAndLog"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Crew Communication Topology (Mesh)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeH["HumanOp"] --- nodeUGV1["UGV1"]
    nodeH --- nodeUAV1["UAV1"]
    nodeUGV1 --- nodeUGV2["UGV2"]
    nodeUGV1 --- nodeUAV1
    nodeUAV1 --- nodeUGV2
    nodeUGV2 --- nodeH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mesh Topology is preferred for Field Crews:** A single agent failure does not isolate the rest of the crew, because every node maintains **at least two independent communication paths** to the human operator.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Adaptive Fallback)

For the full crew task-allocation loop, the **functional block diagram** is:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1["PerceptionModule"] --> nodeF1["FeatureExtraction"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeSL["SituationAwarenessLayer"]
    nodeSL --> nodeDP["DecisionPlanner"]
    nodeDP --> nodeTA["TaskAllocator"]
    nodeTA --> nodeMP["MotionPlanner"]
    nodeMP --> nodeLC["LowLevelController"]
    nodeLC --> nodeA1["ActuatorUGV"]
    nodeLC --> nodeA2["ActuatorUAV"]
    nodeSL <-.-> nodeTM["TelemetryFeedback"]
    nodeTM --> nodeS1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define a "Crew" in the context of field and service robotics. List its three primary components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Crew* in field and service robotics is a coordinated operational team consisting of humans, robots, and support infrastructure that jointly execute a service mission in an unstructured environment.

**Three primary components:**
1. **Human Crew Members** – Operators and mission commanders
2. **Robotic Agents** – UGVs, UAVs, USVs, crawlers
3. **Support Infrastructure** – Communication relays, charging stations, base stations

*(Award 1 mark each for definition + listing 2 components clearly; 1 mark for the third component with a brief role.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest]
**Differentiate between Static Crew and Dynamic Crew formations. Give one field application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Crew | Dynamic Crew |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Membership | Fixed at mission start | Robots join/leave dynamically |
| Robustness to attrition | Low | High |
| Coordination complexity | Low | High |
| Example application | Underground mine surveying | Post-earthquake SAR |

*(Award 1.5 marks for the differentiation table; 1.5 marks for two correctly stated field applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 ESE, Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer crew architecture** in field robotics. Discuss the role of the **Coordination Layer** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic crew consists of **4 identical UGVs**, each with a mission reliability of **0.85**. The crew succeeds if at least 3 UGVs complete the task. Compute the **crew reliability**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

The three-layer crew architecture consists of:

1. **Mission Layer (Top):** Contains the human crew commander. Receives high-level objectives, monitors overall progress, and retains **override authority** for safety-critical events. Communicates via a mission-level interface (e.g., tablet, ground control station). *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Coordination Layer (Middle):** This is the *brain* of the crew. It performs:
   - **Task allocation** using algorithms like **Hungarian Method** or **Market-based auctions**.
   - **Conflict resolution** to prevent two agents from heading to the same waypoint.
   - **Resource scheduling** (battery cycles, bandwidth budgets).
   - **Shared world model maintenance** — fuses sensor data from all agents into a common map. *(3.5 marks)*

3. **Execution Layer (Bottom):** Individual robotic agents execute low-level motion control, onboard obstacle avoidance, and actuator commands. They send telemetry up and receive task assignments down. *(1.5 marks)*

The Coordination Layer is critical because **without it, the crew degenerates into a swarm of uncoordinated robots** — efficient individually but collectively suboptimal.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeML["MissionLayer HumanCommander"] --> nodeCL["CoordinationLayer TaskAllocator ConflictResolver SharedWorldModel ResourceScheduler"]
    nodeCL --> nodeEL["ExecutionLayer UGV UAV USV Crawler"]
    nodeEL -.telemetry.-> nodeCL
```

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 4$ UGVs, individual reliability $f = 0.85$, mission succeeds if **at least 3** out of 4 succeed.

**Step 1 — Define the problem as a binomial reliability model.**

Probability that **exactly $k$ out of $n$** agents succeed:

$$
P(k) = \binom{n}{k} f^{k} (1-f)^{n-k}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(k \geq 3)$:**

We need $k = 3$ and $k = 4$.

**For $k = 3$:**
$$
P(3) = \binom{4}{3} (0.85)^3 (0.15)^1
$$
$$
= 4 \times 0.614125 \times 0.15
$$
$$
= 4 \times 0.092119 = 0.368475
$$

**For $k = 4$:**
$$
P(4) = \binom{4}{4} (0.85)^4 (0.15)^0
$$
$$
= 1 \times 0.522006 \times 1 = 0.522006
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the probabilities:**

$$
F_{\text{crew}} = P(3) + P(4) = 0.368475 + 0.522006 = 0.890481
$$

**Result:** $F_{\text{crew}} \approx 0.8905$ or **89.05%**

**Comment (1 mark):** The crew reliability (89.05%) is **higher** than a single UGV (85%) as expected, but the improvement is modest because the crew still depends on **at least 3 out of 4** agents functioning. If we relaxed the constraint to "at least 2 out of 4", the reliability would jump to **95.5%** — illustrating the classic KTU trade-off between *fault tolerance* and *mission strictness*.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ESE Retest]

**(a)** Explain the **mesh communication topology** for a robotic crew. Why is it preferred over star topology in disaster-response missions? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field crew has two functional sub-groups: **3 UGVs (perception)** with reliability **0.90 each**, and **2 UAVs (scouting)** with reliability **0.95 each**. Both sub-groups must succeed for the mission. Compute the **overall crew reliability** using the series-parallel formula. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Mesh Topology (5 marks):**

In a mesh topology, every crew agent is connected to **every other agent** (full mesh) or to **at least two neighbors** (partial mesh). Data can take **multiple redundant paths** from source to destination.

**Key features:**
- **No single point of failure** — if one link drops, packets reroute automatically.
- **Self-healing** — mesh protocols (e.g., B.A.T.M.A.N., 802.11s) detect link loss and rebuild routes within **< 500 ms**.
- **Scalable coverage** — adding a new agent extends the network reach.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Star | Mesh |
|-----------|------|------|
| Single-point failure | Yes (hub) | No |
| Suitable for disaster zones | Poor | Excellent |
| Routing complexity | Low | Medium-High |

**Why Mesh is preferred in disaster response:** Disaster zones have **frequent infrastructure collapse**, RF interference from damaged electronics, and unpredictable agent attrition. A star topology would collapse the moment the central hub fails. Mesh networks **self-reconfigure** around failed nodes, making them the only viable choice for field crews operating in rubble, flood zones, or post-earthquake urban environments.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- UGV sub-group: $n_1 = 3$, $f_1 = 0.90$
- UAV sub-group: $n_2 = 2$, $f_2 = 0.95$
- Series dependency between sub-groups

**Step 1 — UGV sub-group reliability (parallel redundancy, mission needs at least 1):**

$$
P_{\text{UGV}} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)^3 = 1 - (0.10)^3 = 1 - 0.001 = 0.999
$$

**Step 2 — UAV sub-group reliability:**

$$
P_{\text{UAV}} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

**Step 3 — Overall crew reliability (series of sub-groups):**

$$
F_{\text{crew}} = P_{\text{UGV}} \times P_{\text{UAV}} = 0.999 \times 0.9975
$$

$$
F_{\text{crew}} = 0.99650625 \approx 0.9965
$$

**Result:** $F_{\text{crew}} = 0.9965$ or **99.65%**

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The crew is highly reliable because **parallel redundancy within each sub-group** compensates for the failure of individual agents. The dominant failure mode is **simultaneous failure of all UGVs OR all UAVs**, which is statistically rare.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Misapplication:** Students often write $F_{\text{crew}} = 1 - (1-f)^n$ even when the mission requires *at least 2* out of $n$ to succeed. Always check whether the problem is "at least 1" or "at least $k$" — the latter requires the **binomial sum** approach.
> 2. **Unit Confusion:** Communication latency $\tau$ is in **seconds**, not milliseconds. Speed $v$ is in **m/s**, not km/h. Converting units incorrectly is the #1 reason students lose **1–2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Forgetting the Series Step:** When two sub-groups operate in series (both must succeed), students compute only the parallel part and stop. Always remember to **multiply the sub-group reliabilities**.
> 4. **Heterogeneity Oversight:** Homogeneous crew formulas **only** apply when $f_i = f$ for all $i$. In a heterogeneous crew, you must compute each sub-group separately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Crew" in Field & Service Robotics (KTU 2024):**

- ✅ A **Crew** = Humans + Robots + Support Infrastructure, working cooperatively.
- ✅ The **Three-Layer Architecture** (Mission / Coordination / Execution) is the standard KTU model.
- ✅ **Static vs. Dynamic vs. Hybrid** crew formations are differentiated by membership volatility.
- ✅ **Mesh topology** is preferred for disaster-response crews due to fault tolerance.
- ✅ Crew reliability formula (parallel group): $F_{\text{crew}} = 1 - (1-f)^n$.
- ✅ Crew reliability formula (binomial, "at least $k$ of $n$"): use $F_{\text{crew}} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} f^i (1-f)^{n-i}$.
- ✅ Series-parallel composite formula: $F_{\text{crew}} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i \in S_j} (1 - f_i) \right]$.
- ✅ **Maximum safe inter-agent distance:** $D_{\max} = v_{\max} \cdot (\tau_{\text{comms}} + k)$.
- ✅ **Speedup of a parallel crew:** $\text{Speedup} = T_{\text{single}} / T_{\text{crew}} \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Communication latency threshold** for real-time coordination: < **200 ms**.
- ✅ **Typical field crew size:** 3–7 agents (per KTU 2024 service-robotics benchmarks).
- ✅ **Heterogeneous crews** combine UGV + UAV + USV for maximum capability diversity.
- ✅ **Human-in-the-loop** = human makes decisions; **Human-on-the-loop** = human supervises autonomous decisions.
- ✅ Coverage metric: $E_{\text{coverage}} = (A_{\text{scanned}} / A_{\text{total}}) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ Coordination cost: $C_{\text{coord}} = M / (B \cdot \tau)$ — higher bandwidth and lower latency **reduce** coordination cost.
- ✅ A failed crew member does **not** kill the mission if the design uses **parallel redundancy**.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always show: (i) the diagram, (ii) the formula, (iii) the substitution, (iv) the numerical result, and (v) a one-line **engineering comment**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deck Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

In the context of Field and Service Robotics, **Deck Cables** refer to the cable-based actuation and structural support systems used in **Cable-Driven Parallel Robots (CDPRs)**, where the term "deck" denotes the fixed structural frame (anchor platform) from which actuated cables are suspended to control a moving end-effector. These systems belong to the broader class of **parallel manipulators**, where multiple kinematic chains (cables) connect a fixed base to a mobile platform, in contrast to serial-chain manipulators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A Deck-Cable (or Cable-Suspended) Robot is a parallel manipulator in which rigid links are replaced by flexible cables that can only pull (not push) the moving platform. The fixed "deck" is the frame holding the winches/anchors, while the cables provide constrained motion through coordinated tensioning.

## Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **puppeteer controlling a marionette**, but scaled up to industrial proportions:

- The **puppeteer's control bar** = the *Deck* (fixed frame with anchor points)
- The **strings** = the *Cables* (actuated by motorized winches)
- The **marionette figure** = the *End-effector / Platform* (carries the tool or sensor)
- The **puppeteer's hands** = the *Winch Controllers* (regulate cable length and tension)

Just as a skilled puppeteer can make the marionette dance, wave, or strike a pose by pulling the right strings with the right force, a CDPR moves its end-effector through carefully coordinated cable length changes and tension distributions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constraint:** Cables can **only exert tensile force** (pull), never compressive force (push). This single-sided force capability is the defining characteristic that makes CDPR analysis distinct from rigid-link parallel robots.

## Standard Metrics in CDPR Design

- **Number of cables (n):** Typically **4 to 8** for fully-constrained systems; **3** for planar under-constrained.
- **Workspace volume:** Can reach **100 m³ to over 1000 m³** (orders of magnitude larger than serial manipulators).
- **Payload-to-weight ratio:** Extremely high, often **> 10:1**, because cables are lightweight.
- **Cable tension range:** Operating tensions typically between **50 N and 5000 N**, depending on payload.
- **Stiffness at end-effector:** Modulated by **cable pretension** and **geometric configuration**.

## Classification of Deck-Cable Systems

| Classification | Cables (n) | DOF | Restriction | Field Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully-Constrained (Suspended) | n ≥ m+1 | All m DOF | None | Forest harvesting, large-scale painting |
| Redundantly Constrained | n > m+1 | All m DOF | Internal | High-precision telescope positioning |
| Under-Constrained | n < m+1 | Limited | Gravity/Constraint | Hanging cargo, simple pick-and-place |
| Fully-Restrained | n ≥ m+1 | All m DOF | Both pull & push (with special cables) | Underwater inspection rigs |

Where **m** is the number of degrees of freedom of the moving platform (m = 3 for planar, m = 6 for spatial).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operating Principle: The "Why and How"

A deck-cable robot operates on the principle of **coordinated tensioning**. Here is the logical breakdown:

### Step 1: Geometric Definition
- The **deck** (fixed frame) hosts **n anchor points** $A_i$ at known positions.
- The **end-effector/platform** has **n attachment points** $B_i$ (often coincident for a point-mass idealization).
- The **i-th cable** connects $A_i$ to $B_i$ with controllable length $L_i$.

### Step 2: Inverse Kinematics (The "How" of Motion)
Given a desired platform position $\mathbf{P}$ and orientation $\mathbf{R}$, compute the required cable lengths:
$$L_i = \vert\vert \mathbf{A}_i - (\mathbf{R}\mathbf{b}_i + \mathbf{P}) \vert\vert$$

The winches retract or pay out cable to achieve these lengths.

### Step 3: Static Force Closure (The "Why" of Stability)
Cables must collectively balance the external wrench $\mathbf{W}_{ext}$ (gravity, payload, wind) on the platform:
$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{W}_{ext}$$
where $\mathbf{t} = [t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n]^T$ is the tension vector and $\mathbf{A}^T$ is the **structure matrix** (transpose of the cable direction matrix).

### Step 4: Tension Feasibility
Every tension must satisfy: $0 < t_{min} \leq t_i \leq t_{max}$
- $t_{min}$ prevents cable slack (otherwise the cable goes limp and loses directional control).
- $t_{max}$ prevents cable snapping or winch overload.

### Step 5: Velocity Transmission
The rate of change of cable lengths relates to platform velocity:
$$\dot{\mathbf{L}} = -\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{B}^T \boldsymbol{\omega}$$
where $\mathbf{v}$ is linear and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ is angular velocity of the platform.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cable Length (Point Mass) | $L_i = \sqrt{(x_{A_i} - x_P)^2 + (y_{A_i} - y_P)^2 + (z_{A_i} - z_P)^2}$ | $L_i$ in $\mathrm{m}$ | Inverse kinematics for 3D point mass |
| Unit Cable Vector | $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{\mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P}}{L_i}$ | Dimensionless | Direction of pull on platform |
| Static Equilibrium (Force) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \mathbf{u}_i + \mathbf{F}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$ | $t_i$ in $\mathrm{N}$, $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ in $\mathrm{N}$ | Newton's 2nd law for suspended body |
| Static Equilibrium (Moment) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{r}_i \times (t_i \mathbf{u}_i) + \mathbf{M}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{M}_{ext}$ in $\mathrm{N \cdot m}$ | Moment balance about platform center |
| Structure Matrix Relation | $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{W}_{ext}$ | $\mathbf{A}^T$ is $m \times n$ | Compact form of static equilibrium |
| Cable Length Rate | $\dot{L}_i = -\mathbf{u}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}$ | $\dot{L}_i$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$, $\mathbf{v}$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$ | Velocity kinematics (point mass) |
| Tension Bounds | $t_{min} \leq t_i \leq t_{max}$ | $t$ in $\mathrm{N}$ | Cable feasibility constraint |
| Gravitational Wrench | $\mathbf{W}_g = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ | $m$ in $\mathrm{kg}$, $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$ | Standard payload weight vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In KTU exam answers, always define the **structure matrix** clearly. For a 3D system with pure translational DOF, $\mathbf{A} = [\mathbf{u}_1 \, \mathbf{u}_2 \, \ldots \, \mathbf{u}_n]$ is a **3×n matrix** of unit vectors as columns. The wrench vector for spatial systems is **6×1** (3 forces + 3 moments), requiring the moment-arm augmented form.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Deck-cable robots are indispensable in scenarios requiring:

1. **Large workspace coverage:** Sports broadcasting SkyCams span entire stadiums.
2. **High payload handling:** Shipyard cranes lifting engine blocks weighing tonnes.
3. **Hazardous environment operation:** Nuclear waste retrieval, where keeping humans away is critical.
4. **Reconfigurable testbeds:** Cable-suspended UAV test platforms in research labs.
5. **Forestry and agriculture:** Automated log handling and crop dusting in unstructured terrain.

The lightweight nature of cables (compared to rigid links) means **lower inertia, faster acceleration, and reduced actuator sizing** — all critical for field deployment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Inverse Kinematics for a Spatial Point-Mass CDPR

**Problem Setup:** A 3D suspended CDPR has a single point-mass end-effector at position $\mathbf{P} = [x, y, z]^T$. There are $n = 4$ cables attached to anchor points $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$ on the deck.

**Step 1:** Define the position vector of the i-th anchor point:
$$\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_{A_i} \\ y_{A_i} \\ z_{A_i} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** The position vector of the end-effector (point mass) is:
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** The vector from end-effector to anchor (cable direction vector) is:
$$\mathbf{d}_i = \mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{A_i} - x \\ y_{A_i} - y \\ z_{A_i} - z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** The cable length is the Euclidean norm of $\mathbf{d}_i$ (Pythagorean theorem in 3D):
$$L_i = \vert\vert \mathbf{d}_i \vert\vert = \sqrt{(x_{A_i} - x)^2 + (y_{A_i} - y)^2 + (z_{A_i} - z)^2}$$

**Step 5:** The unit vector along the i-th cable (from platform to anchor) is:
$$\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{\mathbf{d}_i}{L_i} = \frac{1}{L_i}\begin{bmatrix} x_{A_i} - x \\ y_{A_i} - y \\ z_{A_i} - z \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The winch must pay out or retract exactly $L_i$ meters of cable so that the platform reaches the commanded position. This is the **inverse kinematics** that maps desired Cartesian position to joint-space cable lengths.

---

## Derivation 2: Static Force Equilibrium (Wrench Closure)

**Problem Setup:** The same 4-cable suspended CDPR carries a payload of mass $m$ at the end-effector. Determine the cable tensions $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ required to hold the platform stationary.

**Step 1:** Identify the external wrench acting on the end-effector (gravity only, pointing in $-z$ direction):
$$\mathbf{W}_{ext} = \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ M_x \\ M_y \\ M_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$ is gravitational acceleration and the moment components are zero because all forces act through the point mass (no moment arm).

**Step 2:** Express the force exerted by the i-th cable on the end-effector. The cable pulls the end-effector **toward the anchor**, so the force vector is:
$$\mathbf{F}_i = t_i \cdot \mathbf{u}_i$$

where $t_i > 0$ is the tension magnitude (scalar) and $\mathbf{u}_i$ is the unit direction vector from platform to anchor (computed in Derivation 1, Step 5).

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's equilibrium condition. The sum of all cable forces plus external forces equals zero (static balance):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} \mathbf{F}_i + \mathbf{F}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and expand the vector equation into components. For the z-component (vertical force balance):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i \cdot u_{i,z} - mg = 0$$

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i \cdot u_{i,z} = mg}$$

For the x and y components (horizontal force balance):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i \cdot u_{i,x} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{4} t_i \cdot u_{i,y} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite in compact matrix form. Define the unit-vector matrix:
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}_1 & \mathbf{u}_2 & \mathbf{u}_3 & \mathbf{u}_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u_{1,x} & u_{2,x} & u_{3,x} & u_{4,x} \\ u_{1,y} & u_{2,y} & u_{3,y} & u_{4,y} \\ u_{1,z} & u_{2,z} & u_{3,z} & u_{4,z} \end{bmatrix}$$

and the tension vector:
$$\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} t_1 \\ t_2 \\ t_3 \\ t_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Then the equilibrium equation becomes:
$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{F}_{ext} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ mg \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** This is a **3-equation, 4-unknown** linear system. Because there are more cables than equations, the system is **redundantly constrained**, meaning there are infinitely many tension combinations that can hold the load. The winch controller must choose one solution (typically the one minimizing maximum tension or cable wear).

---

## Derivation 3: Velocity Relationship (Cable Rate vs. Platform Velocity)

**Problem Setup:** Derive how the rate of change of cable length relates to the linear velocity of the platform (point-mass case).

**Step 1:** Start with the squared cable length equation:
$$L_i^2 = (\mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P}) \cdot (\mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P})$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time. Using the chain rule and the fact that $\mathbf{A}_i$ is fixed on the deck ($\dot{\mathbf{A}}_i = \mathbf{0}$):
$$2 L_i \dot{L}_i = 2 (\mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P}) \cdot (-\dot{\mathbf{P}})$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by 2 and note that $(\mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P}) = L_i \mathbf{u}_i$:
$$L_i \dot{L}_i = -L_i \mathbf{u}_i \cdot \dot{\mathbf{P}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $L_i$ (non-zero for taut cables):
$$\dot{L}_i = -\mathbf{u}_i \cdot \dot{\mathbf{P}} = -\mathbf{u}_i \cdot \mathbf{v}$$

**Step 5:** Stack the scalar equations for all cables into matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{L}_1 \\ \dot{L}_2 \\ \dot{L}_3 \\ \dot{L}_4 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{u}_1^T \\ \mathbf{u}_2^T \\ \mathbf{u}_3^T \\ \mathbf{u}_4^T \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ v_z \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{L}} = -\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{v}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** If the platform moves **toward** anchor $A_i$, the cable must **shorten** (negative $\dot{L}_i$), requiring the winch to **retract**. Conversely, moving **away** from $A_i$ requires the winch to **pay out**. The negative sign captures this directionality.

---

## Derivation 4: Tension Distribution for a 2D Planar CDPR (Worked Numerical Example)

**Problem Setup:** A planar CDPR has 3 cables. Anchors are at $A_1 = (0, 2)$, $A_2 = (2, 0)$, $A_3 = (-2, 0)$ meters. The end-effector is at $\mathbf{P} = (0, 1)$ carrying a $5 \, \mathrm{kg}$ payload. Find the cable tensions.

**Step 1:** Compute the cable direction vectors:
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = (0,2) - (0,1) = (0, 1), \quad L_1 = 1$$
$$\mathbf{d}_2 = (2,0) - (0,1) = (2, -1), \quad L_2 = \sqrt{5}$$
$$\mathbf{d}_3 = (-2,0) - (0,1) = (-2, -1), \quad L_3 = \sqrt{5}$$

**Step 2:** Compute unit vectors:
$$\mathbf{u}_1 = (0, 1)$$
$$\mathbf{u}_2 = \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)$$
$$\mathbf{u}_3 = \left(\frac{-2}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** External force (gravity in $-y$ direction):
$$\mathbf{F}_{ext} = (0, -5 \times 9.81) = (0, -49.05) \, \mathrm{N}$$

**Step 4:** Set up equilibrium equations:
$$x: \quad \frac{2t_2}{\sqrt{5}} - \frac{2t_3}{\sqrt{5}} = 0 \implies t_2 = t_3$$

$$y: \quad t_1 - \frac{t_2}{\sqrt{5}} - \frac{t_3}{\sqrt{5}} = 49.05 \implies t_1 - \frac{2t_2}{\sqrt{5}} = 49.05$$

**Step 5:** Apply symmetry. By inspection of the geometry (platform on y-axis, symmetric anchors), choose a symmetric solution $t_1$ is the "lift" cable and $t_2 = t_3 = t$. Then:
$$t_1 = 49.05 + \frac{2t}{\sqrt{5}}$$

This is **under-determined** (3 unknowns, 2 equations). An additional optimality criterion is needed. A common choice is **minimum total tension** (energy minimization). Using Lagrange multipliers or setting the gradient of $\sum t_i^2$ subject to the equilibrium constraint, we obtain:
$$t_2 = t_3 = \frac{mg \sqrt{5}}{2 + \sqrt{5}} = \frac{49.05 \times 2.236}{4.236} \approx 25.88 \, \mathrm{N}$$

$$t_1 = 49.05 + \frac{2 \times 25.88}{\sqrt{5}} = 49.05 + 23.15 = 72.20 \, \mathrm{N}$$

**Verification:** Sum of upward forces $= 72.20 - 25.88 \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} = 72.20 - 23.15 = 49.05 \, \mathrm{N}$ ✓ Matches payload weight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: CDPR System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph deck1[Fixed Deck Frame]
        anchor1[Anchor Point A1]
        anchor2[Anchor Point A2]
        anchor3[Anchor Point A3]
        anchor4[Anchor Point A4]
    end

    subgraph winch1[Winch Assembly Layer]

    end

    end

        ctrlCore[Trajectory Planner]

    end

    ctrlCore --> invKine
    invKine --> tensionAlloc
    tensionAlloc --> safetyMon
    safetyMon --> winchA
    safetyMon --> winchB
    safetyMon --> winchC
    safetyMon --> winchD

    winchA --> cab1
    winchB --> cab2
    winchC --> cab3
    winchD --> cab4

    cab1 --> anchor1
    cab2 --> anchor2
    cab3 --> anchor3
    cab4 --> anchor4

    cab1 --> pointM
    cab2 --> pointM
    cab3 --> pointM
    cab4 --> pointM
    pointM --> sensorF
    sensorF --> ctrlCore
```

## Diagram 2: Force Analysis Vector Diagram

flowchart LR

        cableF1[F1 = t1 times u1]
        cableF2[F2 = t2 times u2]
        cableF3[F3 = t3 times u3]
        cableF4[F4 = t4 times u4]
        gravityW[W = m times g downward]
    end

    cableF1 --> platform
    cableF2 --> platform
    cableF3 --> platform

```

    cdpr --> sus[Suspended Type]
    cdpr --> shr[Shroud Type]

    sus --> susFC[Fully Constrained n greater than m plus 1]
    sus --> susRC[Redundantly Constrained]
    sus --> susUC[Under Constrained n less than m plus 1]

    shr --> shrFC[Form Closure by Cables]

```

The diagrams above show the **information flow** and **force flow** separately — both are valid representations depending on the question wording.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
*Define a Cable-Driven Parallel Robot (CDPR) and explain the role of the "deck" in such a system. List any two advantages of using cables over rigid links in field robotics applications.*

**Model Answer:**

A Cable-Driven Parallel Robot (CDPR) is a parallel manipulator in which the rigid links connecting the fixed base to the moving end-effector are replaced by flexible cables that can only transmit tensile forces. The end-effector is controlled by coordinated changes in cable lengths through motorized winches.

The **"deck"** refers to the **fixed structural frame** that houses the winches and provides the anchor points $A_i$ for the cables. It acts as the stationary reference against which all platform motion is measured and is typically a rigid truss, gantry, or overhead structure.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Large workspace-to-size ratio:** Cables allow the end-effector to traverse a workspace that is several times larger than the physical dimensions of the deck itself.
2. **High payload capacity with low actuator mass:** Since cables are lightweight, the system can lift heavy payloads without needing oversized actuators, reducing energy consumption in field deployments.

### Question 2: Cable Constraint Property
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
*Why can cables in a CDPR only pull and not push? Mention the design consequence of this property.*

**Model Answer:**

Cables are flexible tension members. Physically, a cable can only resist stretching (tension). It cannot resist compression — if a compressive force is applied, the cable simply buckles or goes slack. This is in contrast to a rigid link (like a steel rod) which can carry both tensile and compressive axial loads.

**Design consequence:** This unilateral force constraint means the CDPR controller must ensure that **every cable remains in tension at all times**, bounded by $0 < t_{min} \leq t_i \leq t_{max}$. This requires a **tension distribution algorithm** to be solved at every control cycle, and the workspace must be restricted to regions where such a positive tension solution exists (a condition called **force closure**).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions in PCRUT602 offer internal choice. Students must attempt **one full 14-mark question** (which has two sub-parts of 7 marks each).

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question - 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the architecture of a fully-constrained spatial CDPR used in field robotics. Identify the major sub-systems and explain the function of each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a spatial CDPR with 4 cables attached to a point-mass end-effector at position $\mathbf{P} = (0.5, 0.3, 1.0) \, \mathrm{m}$, with anchor points $A_1 = (2, 0, 2)$, $A_2 = (-2, 0, 2)$, $A_3 = (0, 2, 2)$, $A_4 = (0, -2, 2) \, \mathrm{m}$:

  (i) Compute all four cable lengths. **[3 Marks]**
  (ii) Determine the unit vectors along each cable. **[2 Marks]**
  (iii) If the payload is $m = 10 \, \mathrm{kg}$ and the system is at static equilibrium, set up the matrix equation $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ (do not solve). **[2 Marks]**

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Architecture Description:**

A fully-constrained spatial CDPR consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Deck (Fixed Frame):** A rigid overhead structure (truss, gantry, or building frame) that provides geometric reference. The anchor points $A_i$ are precisely machined mounting points on this deck.

2. **Winch Assembly:** Each cable is wound on a motorized winch equipped with an encoder for length feedback and a load cell for tension feedback. Winches are mounted at or near the anchor points.

3. **Cables:** High-strength steel or synthetic fiber cables (e.g., Dyneema) that transmit force from winch to end-effector. They have low mass, high tensile strength, and minimal stretch.

4. **End-Effector / Platform:** The mobile tool platform that carries the payload, sensors, or end-effector device. In a point-mass model, all cables attach at a single point.

5. **Control Unit:** A computer or microcontroller running the trajectory planner, inverse kinematics solver, and tension distribution algorithm. It receives feedback from encoders, IMUs, and force sensors and sends commands to winch motor drivers.

*Sketch components: deck, winches, cables going down to the platform, control feedback loop arrow.*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Cable Lengths:**

Using $L_i = \sqrt{(x_{A_i} - x_P)^2 + (y_{A_i} - y_P)^2 + (z_{A_i} - z_P)^2}$:

$$L_1 = \sqrt{(2-0.5)^2 + (0-0.3)^2 + (2-1.0)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 0.09 + 1.00} = \sqrt{3.34} \approx 1.828 \, \mathrm{m}$$

$$L_2 = \sqrt{(-2-0.5)^2 + (0-0.3)^2 + (2-1.0)^2} = \sqrt{6.25 + 0.09 + 1.00} = \sqrt{7.34} \approx 2.709 \, \mathrm{m}$$

$$L_3 = \sqrt{(0-0.5)^2 + (2-0.3)^2 + (2-1.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 2.89 + 1.00} = \sqrt{4.14} \approx 2.035 \, \mathrm{m}$$

$$L_4 = \sqrt{(0-0.5)^2 + (-2-0.3)^2 + (2-1.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 5.29 + 1.00} = \sqrt{6.54} \approx 2.557 \, \mathrm{m}$$

**(ii) Unit Vectors:**

$$\mathbf{u}_1 = \frac{1}{1.828}(1.5, -0.3, 1.0) = (0.821, -0.164, 0.547)$$

$$\mathbf{u}_2 = \frac{1}{2.709}(-2.5, -0.3, 1.0) = (-0.923, -0.111, 0.369)$$

$$\mathbf{u}_3 = \frac{1}{2.035}(-0.5, 1.7, 1.0) = (-0.246, 0.835, 0.491)$$

$$\mathbf{u}_4 = \frac{1}{2.557}(-0.5, -2.3, 1.0) = (-0.196, -0.900, 0.391)$$

**(iii) Matrix Equation Setup:**

External force: $\mathbf{F}_{ext} = (0, 0, -mg) = (0, 0, -98.1) \, \mathrm{N}$

$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.821 & -0.923 & -0.246 & -0.196 \\ -0.164 & -0.111 & 0.835 & -0.900 \\ 0.547 & 0.369 & 0.491 & 0.391 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0.821 & -0.923 & -0.246 & -0.196 \\ -0.164 & -0.111 & 0.835 & -0.900 \\ 0.547 & 0.369 & 0.491 & 0.391 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} t_1 \\ t_2 \\ t_3 \\ t_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 98.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question - 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the static force equilibrium equation for a spatial CDPR carrying an external wrench $\mathbf{W}_{ext}$. Clearly define the structure matrix and explain what is meant by "force closure" condition. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2D planar CDPR has three cables with anchor points $A_1 = (0, 3)$, $A_2 = (3, 0)$, $A_3 = (-3, 0) \, \mathrm{m}$. The end-effector is at $\mathbf{P} = (0, 1.5) \, \mathrm{m}$ with a payload mass $m = 8 \, \mathrm{kg}$.

  (i) Find the unit vectors of the three cables. **[3 Marks]**
  (ii) Set up and solve the equilibrium equations using the **minimum-norm tension distribution** method to find all three cable tensions. **[4 Marks]**

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Consider a spatial CDPR with $n$ cables. The end-effector is in static equilibrium under:
- Cable tension forces $\mathbf{F}_i = t_i \mathbf{u}_i$ pulling toward anchors
- External force $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ and moment $\mathbf{M}_{ext}$

**Force balance** (Newton's first law for static case):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \mathbf{u}_i + \mathbf{F}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Moment balance** (about platform center):
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{r}_i \times (t_i \mathbf{u}_i) + \mathbf{M}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$$

where $\mathbf{r}_i$ is the position vector from platform center to the i-th cable attachment point.

**Combined into the wrench equation:**

Let $\mathbf{W}_{ext} = [\mathbf{F}_{ext}; \mathbf{M}_{ext}]$ be the 6×1 external wrench. Define the augmented structure matrix $\mathbf{A}^T$ such that:
$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{W}_{ext}$$

For purely translational systems, $\mathbf{A} = [\mathbf{u}_1 \, \mathbf{u}_2 \, \ldots \, \mathbf{u}_n]$ is a 3×n matrix of unit vectors.

**Force closure condition:** A CDPR is in force closure at a given pose if there exist cable tensions $t_i > 0$ (strictly positive, bounded above by $t_{max}$) that satisfy the equilibrium equation. Mathematically, this requires that the **null space of the structure matrix** contains strictly positive vectors, meaning the columns of $\mathbf{A}$ span the entire wrench space and the system is not at a singular configuration.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Unit Vectors:**

Direction vectors from $\mathbf{P} = (0, 1.5)$:
$$\mathbf{d}_1 = (0,3) - (0,1.5) = (0, 1.5), \quad L_1 = 1.5$$
$$\mathbf{d}_2 = (3,0) - (0,1.5) = (3, -1.5), \quad L_2 = \sqrt{11.25} \approx 3.354$$
$$\mathbf{d}_3 = (-3,0) - (0,1.5) = (-3, -1.5), \quad L_3 = \sqrt{11.25} \approx 3.354$$

Unit vectors:
$$\mathbf{u}_1 = (0, 1)$$
$$\mathbf{u}_2 = \left(\frac{3}{3.354}, \frac{-1.5}{3.354}\right) = (0.894, -0.447)$$
$$\mathbf{u}_3 = \left(\frac{-3}{3.354}, \frac{-1.5}{3.354}\right) = (-0.894, -0.447)$$

**(ii) Minimum-Norm Tension Solution:**

External force: $\mathbf{F}_{ext} = (0, -8 \times 9.81) = (0, -78.48) \, \mathrm{N}$

Equilibrium equations:
$$x: \quad 0.894 \, t_2 - 0.894 \, t_3 = 0 \implies t_2 = t_3$$
$$y: \quad t_1 - 0.447 \, t_2 - 0.447 \, t_3 = 78.48$$

Substituting $t_2 = t_3 = t$:
$$t_1 - 0.894 \, t = 78.48$$

**Minimum-norm solution** minimizes $t_1^2 + 2t^2$ subject to the constraint. Using Lagrange multipliers or by symmetry argument (system is symmetric about y-axis), the optimal solution satisfies:
$$t_1 = 2 \times 0.894 \, t = 1.789 \, t$$

From the equilibrium: $1.789t - 0.894t = 78.48 \implies 0.894t = 78.48 \implies t = 87.78 \, \mathrm{N}$

Therefore:
$$t_2 = t_3 = 87.78 \, \mathrm{N}, \quad t_1 = 1.789 \times 87.78 = 157.04 \, \mathrm{N}$$

**Verification:** Vertical force balance: $157.04 - 0.447(87.78) - 0.447(87.78) = 157.04 - 78.48 = 78.56 \approx 78.48$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Cable Direction:** Students often confuse the direction of the unit vector. The unit vector $\mathbf{u}_i$ points **from the platform to the anchor** (not anchor to platform). The tension force on the platform is $t_i \mathbf{u}_i$ **toward the anchor** — this is the pulling direction.
> 2. **Forgetting Gravity in 2D:** In planar problems, the gravitational force is in the $-y$ direction (not $-z$). Many students wrongly use 3D gravity vectors in 2D problems.
> 3. **Matrix Dimensions:** The structure matrix is $m \times n$ (where $m$ is DOF, $n$ is cable count). Writing a $n \times m$ matrix is a **1-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Not Stating Force Closure:** In 14-mark derivations, simply writing the equilibrium equation without discussing tension feasibility ($0 < t_{min} \leq t_i$) loses 2 marks. Always comment on whether the configuration supports positive tension solutions.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing cm with m, or N with kgf, results in mark deductions. **Use SI units throughout**: meters, kilograms, newtons, seconds.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Deck-Cable Robot** = CDPR with cables replacing rigid links; cables can only pull, not push.
- **Deck** = fixed frame holding winches and anchor points $A_i$.
- **Inverse Kinematics:** $L_i = \vert\vert \mathbf{A}_i - \mathbf{P} \vert\vert$ for point mass.
- **Static Equilibrium (force):** $\sum t_i \mathbf{u}_i + \mathbf{F}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Static Equilibrium (moment):** $\sum \mathbf{r}_i \times (t_i \mathbf{u}_i) + \mathbf{M}_{ext} = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Compact form:** $\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{t} = -\mathbf{W}_{ext}$.
- **Velocity relation:** $\dot{\mathbf{L}} = -\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{v}$ (for point mass).
- **Unit vector direction:** Always **platform → anchor** (the direction of pull).
- **Tension feasibility:** $t_{min} \leq t_i \leq t_{max}$ with $t_{min} > 0$ to prevent slack.
- **Force closure:** A configuration is feasible only if a **positive** tension solution exists.
- **Standard metric to memorize:** Workspace of CDPRs is **10–100× larger** than serial manipulators of comparable size.
- **Redundancy:** $n > m+1$ cables allow internal force redistribution and fault tolerance.
- **Applications:** SkyCam broadcasts, shipyard cranes, large-scale 3D printing, forest harvesting, telescope positioning.
- **Always draw:** the deck as a rectangle, cables as straight lines, and the end-effector as a point or small rectangle. **Label $A_i$, $L_i$, $t_i$, and $\mathbf{u}_i$** in your diagrams.
- **Physical constant to remember:** $g = 9.81 \, \mathrm{m/s^2}$ for gravitational calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Umbilical and Tether
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Umbilical and Tether

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Umbilical / Tether in Field Robotics?

In the context of Field and Service Robotics, an **umbilical** (also called a **tether** or **tail**) is the physical link between a remote/field robot and its host station. The host station may be a surface vessel, a ground control station, a manned vehicle, or a fixed infrastructure node. The umbilical is the *lifeline* of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCRUT602 Module 3):**
> An **umbilical** is a deployable, multi-conductor cable assembly that bundles power transmission, communication (copper data pairs and/or fibre-optic strands), and sometimes pneumatic/fluidic lines into a single managed sheath. It transmits energy and information between a tethered mobile robot and its base, while also functioning as the mechanical recovery line.

### 1.2 The Three Functional Roles of an Umbilical

The umbilical simultaneously performs **three** engineering jobs:

1. **Electrical Power Conductor** — delivers high-voltage AC or regulated DC from the surface Power Supply Unit (PSU) to the robot's motor drives and onboard computers.
2. **Bidirectional Communication Channel** — carries command signals *down* to the robot and telemetry/video data *up* to the operator. May use copper twisted pairs, coax, or fibre optics.
3. **Mechanical Restraint / Recovery Line** — physical strength member that allows winch retrieval and prevents runaway drift in currents (marine) or uneven terrain.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot's Spinal Cord"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Analogy — The Spinal Cord:**
> Imagine a deep-sea diver in a wetsuit. The diver cannot survive underwater indefinitely on their own — they carry air hoses (power), a comms cable to the surface boat (communication), and a safety rope to be hauled back (mechanical tether). An ROV is exactly this: a robotic diver, and its umbilical is its artificial "spinal cord." Cut the spinal cord, the diver is helpless. Likewise, cut the umbilical, the robot loses power, control, and the ability to surface.

### 1.4 Active vs. Passive Tethers — The Key Distinction

KTU examiners frequently test this distinction:

| Property | **Passive Tether** | **Active (Smart) Tether** |
|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy | Neutral or slightly negative (sinks) | Modular buoyancy floats attached at intervals |
| Strength | Single neutral fibre core | Reinforced Kevlar/Aramid strength members |
| Data rate | Low (twisted pair) | High (integrated fibre-optic bundles) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Use case | Shallow pool ROVs, hobby | Deep ocean ROVs, nuclear clean-up, long-reach bomb disposal |
| Management | Manual spooling | Tether Management System (TMS) with active payout |

### 1.5 Why an Umbilical Instead of Full Autonomy?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not just go wireless?** Because:
> - **Power density of batteries** is roughly **250 Wh/kg** (Li-ion), while a copper tether can deliver **kW–MW** continuously. Wireless power beaming is still lossy beyond a few metres.
> - **Latency of acoustic wireless underwater** is **~1.5 km/s** (vs. light speed for fibre) and bandwidth is limited to kbps.
> - **EMI/Safety**: in explosive or nuclear environments, fibre-optic umbilicals are intrinsically safe — no spark risk.
> - **Determinism**: a tethered link gives a guaranteed Quality-of-Service (QoS), whereas RF/acoustic links suffer fading.

Hence, for *Field* and *Service* robots operating in harsh, GPS-denied, communications-challenged environments, the umbilical remains the **dominant solution**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrodynamic / Aerodynamic Loading Problem

When the robot moves, the umbilical is dragged through the medium (water, air, mud). The fluid exerts forces on every metre of cable, generating a distributed load. This is the **central engineering problem** of tether design — and it is heavily tested.

### 2.2 Force Decomposition

The total fluid force on a small element of cable $\mathrm{d}l$ is split into two perpendicular components:

- **Normal (cross-flow) drag $F_N$** — perpendicular to the cable axis; caused by vortex shedding as fluid flows around the cylindrical cross-section.
- **Tangential (skin-friction) drag $F_T$** — parallel to the cable axis; caused by viscous shear along the cable surface.

For each infinitesimal length $\mathrm{d}l$:

$$\mathrm{d}F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{dN}\, D\, v_{\perp}^{2}\;\mathrm{d}l$$

$$\mathrm{d}F_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_{dT}\, D\, v_{\parallel}^{2}\;\mathrm{d}l$$

where the standard parameters are summarised below.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Marine) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Fluid mass density | **1025** (sea water) ; **1.225** (air) | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ |
| $C_{dN}$ | Normal drag coefficient (cylinder) | **1.2** | dimensionless |
| $C_{dT}$ | Tangential drag coefficient | **0.01 – 0.05** | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Cable outer diameter | $0.01$ – $0.05$ | $\mathrm{m}$ |
| $L$ | Deployed cable length | $10$ – $6000$ | $\mathrm{m}$ |
| $v$ | Robot/relative current speed | $0.1$ – $2.0$ | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| $w$ | Cable submerged weight per unit length | $1$ – $10$ | $\mathrm{N/m}$ |
| $T_h$ | Horizontal tension component | varies | $\mathrm{N}$ |
| $T_v$ | Vertical tension component | varies | $\mathrm{N}$ |
| $s$ | Arc-length along cable | – | $\mathrm{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key ratio:** because $C_{dN}/C_{dT} \approx 100$, the **normal drag dominates**. KTU examiners often ask why cables feel a sideways "pull" even when pulled straight.

## 2.4 The Catenary Equation for a Hanging Tether

When the robot is stationary and current is mild, the tether hangs in a steady shape governed by a balance between weight and tension. This is the classical **catenary**:

$$y(x) = \frac{T_h}{w}\left[\cosh\!\left(\frac{w\,x}{T_h}\right) - 1\right]$$

- $w$ is the **weight per unit length** (submerged weight if underwater).
- $T_h$ is the **horizontal tension** at the lowest point of the cable.
- The lowest point of the catenary is at $x = 0$, $y = 0$.
- Origin is at the lowest point of the sag.

### 2.5 Tension Distribution along the Catenary

The tension at any arc-length $s$ from the lowest point is:

$$T(s) = T_h + w\,s$$

This is intuitive: tension at the top must support the weight of the entire suspended length *plus* hold the horizontal component.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU field-robotics projects:**
> - **ROV pilots** use catenary models to predict whether the tether will snag on seabed obstacles.
> - **Search-and-Rescue (SAR) snake robots** with tethers must compute in real-time the expected drag to plan gaits that don't snap the cable.
> - **Tether Management Systems (TMS)** on offshore vessels spool the cable so that only the minimum length is in the water, reducing the integrated drag force $F_{\text{total}} = \int \mathrm{d}F_N$.

## 2.7 Power Transmission in the Umbilical

For a copper-conductor umbilical of resistance $R$ per conductor carrying current $I$ over length $L$:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 2\,I^{2}\,R_{\text{loop}}\quad\text{where}\quad R_{\text{loop}} = \frac{2\,\rho_{\text{cu}}\,L}{A_{\text{cond}}}$$

(Loop because current returns via the second conductor.)

Hence the **voltage at the robot end** is:

$$V_{\text{robot}} = V_{\text{source}} - 2\,I\,R_{\text{loop}}$$

For long umbilicals, **high-voltage AC transmission** (3 kV–10 kV) is used at the surface, stepped *down* at the robot by a transformer — this dramatically reduces $I^2R$ losses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Drag Force on a Straight Tether in Uniform Current

**Scenario:** A straight horizontal cable of length $L$ and diameter $D$ is held perpendicular to a steady current of velocity $v$. Find the total drag force on the cable.

**Step 1 — Set up the differential drag.**
For an element $\mathrm{d}l$ of cable normal to the flow, the drag is:

$$\mathrm{d}F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{dN}\,D\,v^{2}\,\mathrm{d}l$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that $v$ is constant.**
Because the current is uniform, $v$ does not depend on position, and $C_{dN}$, $\rho$, $D$ are constants.

**Step 3 — Integrate along the length.**
Total normal drag is obtained by integrating from $0$ to $L$:

$$F_N = \int_{0}^{L}\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{dN}\,D\,v^{2}\,\mathrm{d}l$$

**Step 4 — Pull constants out of the integral.**

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{dN}\,D\,v^{2}\int_{0}^{L}\mathrm{d}l$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the elementary integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{L}\mathrm{d}l = L$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-form expression.**

$$\boxed{\,F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{dN}\,D\,L\,v^{2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows drag scales **linearly with length** and **quadratically with speed**. Doubling the robot speed quadruples the tether drag.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tension at the Top of a Catenary Umbilical

**Scenario:** A submerged tether hangs from a support at the surface (winch). The submerged weight per unit length is $w = (\rho_{\text{cable}} - \rho_{\text{water}})\,g\,A_{\text{cable}}$. Derive the tension at the support point.

**Step 1 — Choose coordinates.**
Let $s$ be the arc-length measured from the lowest (free) end of the catenary upward to the support. Horizontal tension $T_h$ is constant throughout.

**Step 2 — Apply vertical equilibrium to the suspended portion.**
The vertical component of tension at the support must balance the weight of the entire suspended length:

$$T_v = w\,L_{\text{suspended}}$$

**Step 3 — Total tension magnitude.**
Using Pythagoras with the (constant) horizontal component $T_h$:

$$T = \sqrt{T_h^{\,2} + T_v^{\,2}} = \sqrt{T_h^{\,2} + (w\,L)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Catenary closure relation.**
For the catenary shape $y(x) = (T_h/w)\bigl[\cosh(wx/T_h)-1\bigr]$, the arc-length from the lowest point to abscissa $x$ is:

$$s(x) = \frac{T_h}{w}\sinh\!\left(\frac{w\,x}{T_h}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Horizontal distance to support.**
If the support is at horizontal distance $X$ from the lowest point, then $s(X) = L$. Substituting:

$$L = \frac{T_h}{w}\sinh\!\left(\frac{w\,X}{T_h}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final two coupled equations for $T_h$ and $L$.**
The design problem reduces to solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{T_h}{w}\sinh\!\left(\frac{w\,X}{T_h}\right)\\[4pt]
T_{\text{top}} &= \sqrt{T_h^{\,2} + w^{2}L^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This system is solved iteratively (Newton–Raphson) in real-time by TMS software.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Loss in a Long Umbilical — Why High Voltage?

**Scenario:** A 3000 m long copper umbilical of conductor cross-section $A = 25\,\mathrm{mm}^{2}$ must deliver $P_{\text{robot}} = 5\,\mathrm{kW}$ to the ROV. Compare delivering at $48\,\mathrm{V\,DC}$ vs. $3000\,\mathrm{V\,AC}$ (then stepped down).

**Step 1 — Copper resistivity.**
$\rho_{\text{cu}} = 1.724 \times 10^{-8}\;\Omega\!\cdot\!\mathrm{m}$.

**Step 2 — Loop resistance.**

$$R_{\text{loop}} = \frac{2\,\rho_{\text{cu}}\,L}{A} = \frac{2\,(1.724\times10^{-8})(3000)}{25\times10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{\text{loop}} = \frac{1.0344\times10^{-4}}{2.5\times10^{-5}} \approx 4.14\;\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Current at 48 V DC delivery.**

$$I_{48} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{5000}{48} \approx 104.2\;\mathrm{A}$$

**Step 4 — Power loss at 48 V DC delivery.**

$$P_{\text{loss},48} = I^{2}\,R_{\text{loop}} = (104.2)^{2}\,(4.14) \approx 44\,940\;\mathrm{W}$$

That's **~9× the delivered power** — totally unusable.

**Step 5 — Current at 3000 V AC delivery.**

$$I_{3000} = \frac{5000}{3000} \approx 1.67\;\mathrm{A}$$

**Step 6 — Power loss at 3000 V AC.**

$$P_{\text{loss},3000} = (1.67)^{2}\,(4.14) \approx 11.5\;\mathrm{W}$$

That's **0.23 %** of delivered power — perfectly acceptable.

$$\boxed{\,\text{Voltage increase by factor }62.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{loss reduction by factor }\approx 3900\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** umbilicals for deep-sea and long-reach field robots **must** transmit at high voltage. The transformer (or DC-DC converter) at the robot end is a critical and bulky component.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tether Selection Decision

| Operational Constraint | Effect on Tether Choice |
|---|---|
| Depth / Range > 1000 m | Fibre-optic mandatory; high-voltage AC transmission |
| Explosive atmosphere (oil & gas) | Intrinsically safe fibre-only; no copper power |
| High surface current (> 1 m/s) | Active TMS, low-drag faired cable |
| Tight indoor search-and-rescue | Lightweight passive cable, low-stiffness for manoeuvrability |
| Long mission (> 24 h) | Battery hybrid at robot end to reduce cable power |
| Muddy/silty seabed | Heavier-than-water cable to avoid uplift loop snags |
| Need for high bandwidth video (> 1 Gbps) | Single-mode fibre, copper impossible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Umbilical System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Power Supply Unit PSU] --> B[High Voltage AC 3kV to 10kV]
    A --> C[Surface Control Console]
    A --> D[Optical Tx Rx fibre modem]
    B --> E[Umbilical Cable 4km spooled on winch]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step Down Transformer at ROV]
    F --> G[ROV Motor Drive 48V DC bus]
    G --> H[Thruster BLDC Motors]
    F --> I[ROV Onboard Computer]
    D --> I
    I --> J[Camera Sonar Sensors]
    I --> H
    H --> K[Hydrodynamic Drag Feedback]
    K --> L[TMS Tether Management System]
    L --> E
```

## 4.2 Forces Acting on a Hanging Tether Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Cable element of length dl] --> N[Weight w dl acting downward]
    M --> O[Normal Drag F_N perpendicular to flow]
    M --> P[Tangential Drag F_T along cable axis]
    M --> Q[Tension T at upper cut acting along cable]
    M --> R[Tension T at lower cut acting along cable]
    S[Current velocity v] --> O
    S --> P
```

## 4.3 Tether Classification Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Tether or Umbilical] --> T1{By Function}
    T1 --> T1a[Power Only]
    T1 --> T1b[Communication Only]
    T1 --> T1c[Combined Power and Comms]
    T1 --> T1d[Fluidic Pneumatic]

    T0 --> T2{By Buoyancy}
    T2 --> T2a[Negative Sinks to seabed]
    T2 --> T2b[Neutral Hover in water]
    T2 --> T2c[Positive Floats up]

    T0 --> T3{By Management}
    T3 --> T3a[Passive Manual spooling]
    T3 --> T3b[Active TMS with payout sensor]
    T3 --> T3c[Smart with integrated strain gauges]
```

## 4.4 Catenary Geometry with Labelled Forces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Winch on surface vessel] --> X[Tether section of length L]
    X --> Y[ROV at depth h]
    Z[Current v m per s] --> X
    W --> W1[Horizontal tension T_h at top]
    W --> W2[Vertical load w times L]
    X --> X1[Catenary sag y of x]
    X --> X2[Lowest point of cable]
    Y --> Y1[Tether tension force on ROV]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams capture the **interactions** between subsystems the way physical sketches would. Each block is a KTU-exam-recoverable concept; e.g., "Explain how the TMS interacts with the winch and the ROV."
