<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "umbilical" as used in field and service robotics. List any two functions it serves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An umbilical is a multi-conductor cable assembly that physically links a remote field robot to its host station. It bundles together power transmission lines, communication conductors (copper and/or fibre-optic), and a strength member for mechanical retrieval.
**Functions (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. Transmits electrical power from surface to robot.
2. Carries bidirectional data — commands down, telemetry up.
3. Acts as a mechanical restraint enabling winch recovery.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between an active (smart) tether and a passive tether. Give one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **passive tether** has fixed buoyancy, low data rate (typically twisted-pair copper), and is manually spooled; it is suited to **shallow-water inspection ROVs**.
An **active (smart) tether** incorporates distributed buoyancy modules, fibre-optic bundles for high-bandwidth communication, and is dynamically managed by a TMS; it is used in **deep-sea oil-and-gas ROVs operating beyond 3000 m**.
*(2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for application)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**A subsea ROV is connected to its host vessel by a 1500 m long umbilical of diameter 0.04 m. The seawater density is 1025 kg/m³ and the normal drag coefficient is 1.2. The ROV is moving at 1.5 m/s perpendicular to a current of 0.8 m/s. Compute the total normal drag force on the umbilical and the expected power loss in kW. Assume relative velocity is vector sum of ROV speed and current speed opposing the motion.**

#### (a) Compute the total normal drag force on the umbilical. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify relative velocity.**
The ROV moves at 1.5 m/s and current opposes at 0.8 m/s. Effective relative speed (assuming collinear worst case):

$$v_{\text{rel}} = 1.5 + 0.8 = 2.3\;\mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Identify parameters.**
$\rho = 1025\;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $C_{dN}=1.2$, $D = 0.04\;\mathrm{m}$, $L = 1500\;\mathrm{m}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the drag formula.**

$$F_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_{dN}\,D\,L\,v_{\text{rel}}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$F_N = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 0.04 \times 1500 \times (2.3)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate in stages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(2.3)^{2} &= 5.29\\
1025 \times 1.2 &= 1230\\
1230 \times 0.04 &= 49.2\\
49.2 \times 1500 &= 73\,800\\
73\,800 \times 5.29 &= 390\,372\\
0.5 \times 390\,372 &= 195\,186\;\mathrm{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$\boxed{F_N \approx 195.2\;\mathrm{kN}}$$

*(Full marks awarded for correct formula and substitution. 1 mark for listing the assumptions.)*

#### (b) Compute the expected power loss due to this drag, and comment on the operational implications. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Drag acts opposite to ROV motion, so power required to overcome tether drag.**

$$P_{\text{drag}} = F_N \times v_{\text{ROV}} = 195\,186 \times 1.5$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{drag}} \approx 292.8\;\mathrm{kW}}$$

**Step 2 — Operational implications (4 marks of qualitative discussion):**
- The ROV's thrusters must continuously expend ~293 kW just to haul the tether through water.
- This is *comparable* to the useful work output of small ROVs — tether drag dominates energy budget.
- Practical mitigation: **Tether Management System (TMS)** that holds the cable in a cage, paying out only the required length — this changes $L$ from 1500 m to perhaps 50–100 m, reducing drag by a factor of ~20.
- Use of **low-drag faired cables** with $C_{dN} \approx 0.4$ (instead of 1.2) reduces drag by ~3×.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**A tethered ground-search robot explores a collapsed building. The tether is 30 m long, of diameter 6 mm, mass per unit length 0.08 kg/m. The host station exerts a horizontal pulling force of 12 N at the tether entry point. The robot is stationary while the tether drapes into a U-shape under gravity. Using the catenary model, derive the sag and the maximum tension in the tether.**

#### (a) Derive the catenary sag equation and compute the sag $y_{\max}$ at the midpoint. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Weight per unit length.**

$$w = m\,g = 0.08 \times 9.81 = 0.785\;\mathrm{N/m}$$

**Step 2 — Catenary equation.**
With the origin at the lowest point of the sag and horizontal tension $T_h$:

$$y(x) = \frac{T_h}{w}\left[\cosh\!\left(\frac{w\,x}{T_h}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Use the boundary condition: at entry point $x = X$, the horizontal tension equals the applied pull.**
The problem states the host applies a horizontal pull of $T_h = 12\;\mathrm{N}$ at the entry point.

**Step 4 — Half-span.** Total length is $L = 30\;\mathrm{m}$. By symmetry, half-span $X = 15\;\mathrm{m}$ measured horizontally from the lowest point.

**Step 5 — Solve for the catenary parameter.**
Half-length (arc) equals $L/2 = 15\;\mathrm{m}$. Use arc-length relation:

$$s = \frac{T_h}{w}\sinh\!\left(\frac{w\,X}{T_h}\right)$$

$$15 = \frac{12}{0.785}\sinh\!\left(\frac{0.785 \times 15}{12}\right) = 15.29\,\sinh(0.981)$$

$$\sinh(0.981) = \frac{e^{0.981}-e^{-0.981}}{2} = \frac{2.667 - 0.375}{2} \approx 1.146$$

$$15.29 \times 1.146 = 17.52$$

So $s_{\text{from eqn}} = 17.52\;\mathrm{m}$, but we need $15\;\mathrm{m}$. The tension must therefore be *slightly* higher than 12 N. Iterating, $T_h \approx 13.5\;\mathrm{N}$ gives convergence. *(Examiner accepts iterative method.)*

**Step 6 — Compute sag at midpoint** ($x = X = 15\;\mathrm{m}$, using $T_h \approx 13.5\;\mathrm{N}$):

$$\frac{wX}{T_h} = \frac{0.785 \times 15}{13.5} = 0.872$$

$$\cosh(0.872) = \frac{e^{0.872}+e^{-0.872}}{2} = \frac{2.392 + 0.418}{2} \approx 1.405$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{13.5}{0.785}\,(1.405 - 1) = 17.20 \times 0.405$$

$$\boxed{y_{\max} \approx 6.97\;\mathrm{m}}$$

#### (b) Compute the maximum tension in the tether and discuss failure-mode considerations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum tension occurs at the highest point (entry/exit).**

$$T_{\max} = \sqrt{T_h^{\,2} + (w L_{\text{suspended}})^{2}}$$

With the entire half $L/2 = 15\;\mathrm{m}$ suspending:

$$T_v = w\,s = 0.785 \times 15 = 11.78\;\mathrm{N}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude.**

$$T_{\max} = \sqrt{(13.5)^{2} + (11.78)^{2}} = \sqrt{182.25 + 138.77} = \sqrt{321.02}$$

$$\boxed{T_{\max} \approx 17.9\;\mathrm{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Failure-mode discussion.**
- For a typical aramid-strength-member tether of breaking strength ~1500 N, the safety factor is **1500/17.9 ≈ 84** — ample for SAR use.
- Real-world failure risks: **snag on rebar**, **sharp edge abrasion** (not modelled here but critical), and **connector pull-out** at the host station.
- The catenary model assumes uniform weight and no snagging; in rubble-filled SAR environments the cable will form multi-point contacts, drastically increasing local tension — design must include a 5–10× safety factor over the catenary-predicted tension.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting relative velocity sign** in the drag formula (using only ROV speed and ignoring current). Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up the arc-length $s$ and horizontal distance $X$** in the catenary equation. The arc-length is *greater* than the horizontal span. Loss: 2 marks.
> 3. **Not including the loop factor of 2** in the power-transmission derivation. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing catenary without stating the assumption** of uniform weight per unit length and static equilibrium. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final boxed answer — instant ½ mark deduction in KTU.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Umbilical = power + communication + mechanical restraint**, all in one managed cable.
- ✅ **Normal drag dominates** tangential drag by ~100× for typical cables — cable design focuses on cross-section.
- ✅ **Drag scales as $v^{2}$** and linearly with length; **TMS shortens the in-water length**.
- ✅ **Catenary shape**: $y = (T_h/w)[\cosh(wx/T_h)-1]$; tension at any point: $T = T_h + ws$.
- ✅ **Power transmission**: $P_{\text{loss}} = I^{2}R_{\text{loop}}$; high-voltage AC at surface, step-down at robot.
- ✅ **Fibre-optic umbilicals** are mandatory for > 1 Gbps video and for explosive atmospheres.
- ✅ **Active vs. passive tethers**: active has distributed buoyancy and TMS; passive does not.
- ✅ **Buoyancy types**: negative (sinks, avoids surface snags), neutral (stable drape), positive (floats up).
- ✅ **Safety factor** for tether tension: typically 5–10× for SAR, 3–5× for offshore ROV.
- ✅ **KTU must-mention assumptions** when deriving: uniform $\rho$, steady $v$, rigid cylindrical cable, $C_{dN}=1.2$ for smooth cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LARS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LARS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of LARS

**LARS (Laser Radar Systems)**, commonly known in modern literature as **LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)**, is a fundamental active remote-sensing technology that uses pulsed laser beams (typically in the near-infrared, visible, or ultraviolet spectrum) to measure distances to a target by analyzing the reflected light. In the specific domain of **Field and Service Robotics**, LARS forms the perceptual backbone of autonomous navigation, enabling a robot to perceive its surroundings with high spatial resolution and centimeter-level accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition Snapshot:** LARS refers to **Laser Radar Systems** — an active ranging sensor that emits laser pulses and measures the Time-of-Flight (ToF) of the returned echo to construct a dense, geo-referenced 3D point cloud of the environment. It is also referred to as **LADAR** (Laser Detection and Ranging) in defense robotics literature.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark cave with a powerful flashlight and an extremely precise stopwatch. You shine the flashlight at the cave wall. The moment the light leaves you, you start the stopwatch. When the reflection hits your eye, you stop it. If you know the speed of light (a constant), you can calculate the distance to the wall.

Now imagine doing this **millions of times per second**, sweeping the beam in **every direction (360°)** like a lighthouse. Each distance measurement becomes a single dot. Stack millions of these dots together, and you get a **3D model of the entire cave** — this is precisely how LARS works inside a robot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Operational Insight:** LARS does **not depend on ambient light** (unlike cameras). It is an **active sensor**, meaning it provides its own illumination (the laser pulse). This is why autonomous cars can "see" perfectly at night, in tunnels, or in foggy conditions where vision-based systems fail.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally used in LARS calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8}$ | m/s |
| Refractive index of air (approx.) | $n$ | $\mathbf{1.00027}$ | dimensionless |
| Speed of light in air | $c_{air} = c/n$ | $\mathbf{2.997 \times 10^8}$ | m/s |
| Typical LARS wavelength ($\lambda$) | — | **905 nm** or **1550 nm** | nm |
| Standard measurement range | $R$ | **0.1 to 250** | m |
| Pulse repetition frequency | PRF | **5 kHz to 1 MHz** | Hz |
| Field of view (2D scanner) | $\theta$ | **360°** (azimuth), **±15° to ±40°** (elevation) | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two wavelengths?** **905 nm** is cheaper and uses silicon detectors (used in automotive LARS). **1550 nm** is eye-safe at higher powers and is preferred for long-range military and surveying LARS — a frequent 3-mark question in KTU exams.

### 1.4 Position of LARS in Field & Service Robotics

LARS is the **primary exteroceptive sensor** for:

- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs)** — self-driving cars (e.g., Waymo, Velodyne roof-mounted units)
- **Aerial Robots (UAVs/MAVs)** — for obstacle avoidance and terrain mapping
- **Search and Rescue (SAR) Robots** — operating in GPS-denied, smoke-filled environments
- **Agricultural Robots** — crop canopy profiling and yield estimation
- **Marine/Underwater Robots** — using Blue-Green LARS (532 nm) for seafloor mapping
- **Domestic Service Robots** — vacuum cleaners (e.g., Neato, Roborock) for SLAM

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three Pillars of LARS

LARS technology is built upon three distinct ranging methodologies. A KTU examiner expects you to identify the principle used by a specific LARS class.

#### Pillar 1: Time-of-Flight (ToF) — The Pulsed Method

This is the **most common principle** used in field robotics.

**Logic Steps:**
1. The laser diode emits a **short, high-peak-power optical pulse** (typically 5–20 ns wide).
2. The pulse travels to the target surface and part of it is back-scattered (diffuse reflection).
3. A highly sensitive **avalanche photodiode (APD)** detects the returning echo.
4. An **extremely precise timer** (with picosecond resolution) measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$.
5. The range $R$ is computed as:
$$R = \frac{c_{air} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the **round-trip** (down to target, and back to sensor).

#### Pillar 2: Phase-Shift Detection (Amplitude Modulated Continuous Wave — AMCW)

Used in **medium-range, high-precision** LARS (e.g., surveying instruments like Leica RTC360).

**Logic Steps:**
1. The laser emits a **continuously modulated sinusoid** at frequency $f_{mod}$.
2. The emitted and reflected waves are compared in phase.
3. The phase difference $\Delta \phi$ is measured.
4. Range is computed as:
$$R = \frac{c_{air} \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why divided by 4π?** The phase shift happens over the round-trip (factor 2) and is converted to distance via one full wavelength $\lambda_{mod} = c_{air}/f_{mod}$ equating to $2\pi$ radians (another factor 2).

#### Pillar 3: Triangulation (Geometric Method)

Used in **short-range, high-accuracy** industrial LARS (e.g., SICK, Keyence).

**Logic Steps:**
1. A laser projects a point on the target.
2. A CMOS/CCD sensor captures the image of the spot from a **baseline distance** $B$ away.
3. The angle $\alpha$ between the emitted beam and the imaging axis is measured.
4. Range is computed using the **similar triangles principle**:
$$R = \frac{B}{\tan(\alpha)}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable LARS Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | ToF range equation | meters | Pulsed LARS |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | Phase-shift range | meters | AMCW LARS |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{B}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | Triangulation range | meters | Triangulation LARS |
| 4 | $\sigma_R = \dfrac{c}{2} \cdot \sigma_{\Delta t}$ | Range uncertainty (ToF) | meters | Pulsed LARS |
| 5 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \cdot \rho \cdot A_r \cdot \eta}{R^4 \cdot \Omega_t}$ | LARS Radar Equation | Watts | All LARS |
| 6 | $\theta_{div} = \dfrac{\lambda}{D_{aperture}}$ | Beam divergence angle | radians | All LARS |
| 7 | $N_{points} = \text{PRF} \cdot N_{rotations}$ | Points per scan | dimensionless | Rotating LARS |
| 8 | $\delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$ | Range resolution | meters | Pulsed LARS |
| 9 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_r \cdot f_0}{c}$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz | Doppler LARS |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_r}{P_{noise}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dimensionless | All LARS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $R = c \cdot \Delta t$ **without the factor of 2**. The light travels to the object AND back. The $\frac{1}{2}$ factor is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if forgotten.

### 2.3 The LARS Radar Equation (Power Link Budget)

This is the **single most important equation** that governs whether a LARS system can detect a target at a given range. It is derived from the standard radar range equation adapted for optical wavelengths:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2 \cdot \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \cdot R^4}$$

Where:

- $P_r$ = received optical power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted peak power (W)
- $G_t$ = transmit antenna gain (function of aperture diameter and wavelength)
- $G_r$ = receive antenna gain (typically $G_r = G_t$ for monostatic LARS)
- $\lambda$ = laser wavelength (m)
- $\sigma$ = LARS cross-section of the target (m²) — depends on reflectivity $\rho$
- $R$ = slant range to target (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineers use this equation during the design of autonomous vehicle LARS to determine whether the laser power is sufficient for highway-speed obstacle detection (typically 200 m range) versus low-speed warehouse robot operation (typically 30 m range). The $R^4$ dependence explains why doubling the range requires **16× more laser power** — a fundamental engineering constraint.

### 2.4 Components of a LARS System

A complete LARS unit consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Laser Transmitter** — Pulsed laser diode (905 nm Si) or fiber laser (1550 nm InGaAs)
2. **Scanning Mechanism** — Rotating prism, spinning mirror, MEMS mirror, or Risley prism
3. **Receiver Optics** — Telescope/lens system to gather returning photons
4. **Photodetector** — Avalanche Photodiode (APD) or Single-Photon Avalanche Diode (SPAD)
5. **Timing Electronics** — TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) with picosecond precision
6. **Signal Processor** — Extracts ToF, filters noise, builds point cloud
7. **Mechanical Housing** — Vibration-isolated, IP67-rated for field deployment

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-of-Flight Range Equation (From First Principles)

**Starting Premise:** Light travels at a constant velocity $c$ in vacuum (and very nearly $c$ in air).

**Step 1:** Let $\Delta t$ be the total time measured by the timer from pulse emission to echo reception.

Since the light travels from the sensor → target → sensor, the **total path length** is:

$$\text{Total Path} = 2R$$

where $R$ is the one-way distance (range) to the target.

**Step 2:** Applying the fundamental kinematic relation $d = v \cdot t$:

$$2R = c_{air} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{c_{air} \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $c_{air} = c/n$ where $n \approx 1.00027$ for air at sea level:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2n}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A LARS measures a round-trip time of $\Delta t = 667$ ns for a wall. Calculate the range.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{(3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}) \cdot (667 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s})}{2} \\
R &= \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (6.67 \times 10^{-7})}{2} \\
R &= \frac{200.1}{2} \\
R &= 100.05 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Unambiguous Range

**Concept:** The laser cannot fire a new pulse until the previous pulse's echo has returned. Otherwise, echoes will be **ambiguously assigned** to the wrong pulse.

**Step 1:** Let $T_{PRF}$ be the time between consecutive pulses, given by $T_{PRF} = 1/PRF$.

**Step 2:** The maximum range is reached when the echo arrives exactly at the moment the next pulse is emitted:

$$R_{max} = \frac{c \cdot T_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$$

**Numerical Example:** For a LARS operating at $PRF = 100 \text{ kHz}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 100 \times 10^3} \\
R_{max} &= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^5} \\
R_{max} &= 1500 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Range Resolution

**Concept:** The shortest separation between two targets along the same line of sight that the LARS can distinguish.

**Step 1:** The receiver can only distinguish two echoes if they are separated by at least the **pulse width** $\tau$.

**Step 2:** Two targets separated by distance $\delta R$ produce echoes separated by:

$$\Delta t_{sep} = \frac{2 \cdot \delta R}{c}$$

**Step 3:** Setting $\Delta t_{sep} = \tau$ and solving for $\delta R$:

$$\delta R = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting bandwidth $BW = 1/\tau$ (for a rectangular pulse approximation):

$$\delta R = \frac{c}{2 \cdot BW}$$

**Numerical Example:** A LARS uses 1 ns pulses. Calculate range resolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-9})}{2} \\
\delta R &= \frac{0.3}{2} \\
\delta R &= 0.15 \text{ meters} = 15 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: 3D Point Cloud Reconstruction (Spherical → Cartesian)

A scanning LARS measures three quantities for each laser shot:

- $R$ — range (distance)
- $\theta$ — azimuth angle (horizontal rotation)
- $\phi$ — elevation angle (vertical tilt of mirror)

**Step 1:** Starting from the **spherical coordinate representation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= R \cos(\phi) \cos(\theta) \\
y &= R \cos(\phi) \sin(\theta) \\
z &= R \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Each tuple $(x, y, z)$ represents a single point in 3D space. A full rotation produces millions of such points → the **point cloud**.

**Step 3:** Including the robot's ego-motion (pose $\xi_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$), the global coordinates become:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{global} &= x_t + x \cos(\theta_t) - y \sin(\theta_t) \\
Y_{global} &= y_t + x \sin(\theta_t) + y \cos(\theta_t) \\
Z_{global} &= z_t + z
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LARS vs Other Field Sensors

| Property | LARS (LiDAR) | Camera (Stereo) | Radar | Ultrasonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range | **0.1 – 250 m** | 0.5 – 80 m | 0.1 – 250 m | 0.05 – 10 m |
| Accuracy | **±2 cm** | ±10 cm at 10 m | ±50 cm | ±5 cm |
| Works in dark | **Yes** | No | Yes | Yes |
| Works in fog | **Degraded** | Poor | Excellent | Poor |
| Angular resolution | **0.1°–0.4°** | < 0.1° (with effort) | ~1° | ~15° |
| Cost | **Medium–High** | Low | High | Very Low |
| Data rate | **1–10 M pts/sec** | 30–90 fps | 20 Hz | 20 Hz |
| Color info | **No** | Yes (RGB) | No | No |
| Typical use | Self-driving cars | Object recognition | Automotive ACC | Robot vacuums |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Pulsed LARS System

The following block diagram maps the complete signal and data flow inside a typical ToF LARS unit, from the laser pulse emission to the final 3D point cloud output:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller Trigger] --> B[Pulse Generator]
    B --> C[Laser Diode 905nm]
    C --> D[Beam Expander Optics]
    D --> E[Rotating Scanning Mirror Assembly]
    E --> F[Target Object in Environment]
    F --> G[Return Photons Captured]
    G --> H[Receiver Telescope]
    H --> I[Narrowband Optical Filter 905nm]
    I --> J[Avalanche Photodiode APD]
    J --> K[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    K --> L[Constant Fraction Discriminator CFD]
    L --> M[Time to Digital Converter TDC]
    M --> N[Range Calculation Processor]
    A --> M
    N --> O[Point Cloud Buffer]
    O --> P[Robot SLAM and Navigation Module]
    P --> Q[Obstacle Avoidance Decision]
    Q --> R[Motor Controller Command Output]
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

- **A → B → C:** The microcontroller fires a trigger. The pulse generator drives the laser diode to emit a nanosecond-wide optical pulse.
- **D:** The beam expander widens and collimates the laser pulse.
- **E:** The scanning mirror deflects the beam at a precise angle $\theta$ (azimuth) and $\phi$ (elevation), enabling 360° coverage.
- **F:** The pulse hits an object. Diffuse reflection (Lambertian scattering) sends a tiny fraction back.
- **G → H:** The receiver telescope captures these weak returning photons.
- **I:** The narrowband filter rejects background sunlight (critical for daytime operation).
- **J → K:** The APD converts photons to electrons; the amplifier boosts the tiny current.
- **L:** The CFD produces a clean timing marker independent of pulse amplitude (avoids "walk error").
- **M:** The TDC measures $\Delta t$ with picosecond precision.
- **N:** Range $R$ is computed using $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.
- **O → P:** The point cloud is fed into the robot's SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) algorithm.
- **Q → R:** Navigation decisions (turn left, brake, slow down) are issued to the motor controller.

### 4.2 LARS Classification Tree (Field Robotics Taxonomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LARS Laser Radar Systems] --> B[1D Line Scanners]
    A --> C[2D Planar Scanners]
    A --> D[3D Multi Layer Scanners]
    A --> E[Solid State LARS]
    B --> B1[Safety Scanners SICK microScan3]
    C --> C1[Rotating Drum Hokuyo]
    C --> C2[Rotating Mirror Velodyne VLP 16]
    D --> D1[32 Layer Velodyne HDL 32E]
    D --> D2[64 Layer Velodyne HDL 64E]
    D --> D3[128 Layer Hesai Pandar 128]
    E --> E1[MEMS Mirrors Innoviz One]
    E --> E2[Optical Phased Array Quanergy S3]
    E --> E3[Flash LARS Ouster ES2]
```

**Use-Case Mapping:**

- **B1 (1D Safety Scanners):** Industrial AGVs, robotic arms in collaborative workspaces (detect human entry into safety zone).
- **C1, C2 (2D Scanners):** Indoor service robots (vacuum cleaners, hotel delivery robots).
- **D1–D3 (Multi-Layer 3D):** Autonomous vehicles, outdoor field robots.
- **E1–E3 (Solid-State):** Future of automotive LARS — no moving parts, mass-producible, automotive-grade reliability.

### 4.3 Scanning Mechanism Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotating Polygon Mirror] -->|High Speed 600 RPM| B[Automotive LARS]
    C[Spinning Top Assembly] -->|360 degrees| D[Velodyne Classic]
    E[MEMS Micromirror] -->|Low Cost| F[Consumer Robots]
    G[Electronic Beam Steering] -->|No Moving Parts| H[Solid State LARS]
    I[Flash LARS] -->|Single Pulse 3D| J[Automotive Solid State]
```

### 4.4 LARS Measurement Sequence (Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ctrl as Microcontroller
    participant Laser as Laser Diode
    participant Tgt as Target Object
    participant APD as Avalanche Photodiode
    participant TDC as Time to Digital Converter
    Ctrl->>Laser: Trigger pulse t = 0 ns
    Laser->>Tgt: Optical pulse emitted
    Tgt->>APD: Echo received at t = 667 ns
    APD->>TDC: Electrical pulse generated
    TDC->>Ctrl: Range R = 100 m computed
    Ctrl->>Laser: Trigger next pulse
```

**Key Timing Insights:**

- The 667 ns in this example corresponds to a 100 m range (matches our earlier numerical example).
- The TDC must resolve sub-nanosecond timing — this is why LARS timing electronics are more expensive than the laser itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define LARS. List any three applications of LARS in field robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** LARS, or Laser Radar System (also known as LiDAR — Light Detection and Ranging), is an active remote-sensing technology that determines the distance to a target by emitting laser pulses and measuring the time-of-flight (ToF) of the backscattered echo. The range is computed using $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.

**Applications (2 marks — any three):**

1. **Autonomous Ground Vehicles:** LARS mounted on the roof provides 360° 3D perception for self-driving cars (e.g., Waymo, Cruise).
2. **Aerial Mapping (UAV LiDAR):** Used in drone-based surveying for terrain modeling and forestry.
3. **Search and Rescue Robots:** Operates in smoke-filled, GPS-denied environments where vision sensors fail.
4. **Agricultural Robotics:** Mounted on tractors for crop health profiling and terrain leveling.
5. **Domestic Service Robots:** Robot vacuum cleaners use low-cost 2D LARS for SLAM-based room mapping.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Time-of-Flight (ToF) and Phase-Shift methods of LARS ranging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Time-of-Flight (ToF) | Phase-Shift (AMCW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Emission type** | Short pulsed laser | Continuous modulated wave |
| **Measurement** | Direct time $\Delta t$ | Phase difference $\Delta \phi$ |
| **Equation** | $R = c \Delta t / 2$ | $R = c \Delta \phi / 4\pi f_{mod}$ |
| **Typical range** | Long (up to 250 m) | Medium (up to 100 m) |
| **Accuracy** | ±2 cm | Sub-millimeter |
| **Use case** | Automotive, UAVs | Surveying, metrology |

**(1 mark)** for clear definition of each; **(1 mark)** for the table differentiating key parameters; **(1 mark)** for appropriate examples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-level Part B questions carry **14 marks total**, typically divided into **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Either Question A OR Question B must be answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of a Time-of-Flight Laser Radar System (LARS) with a neat block diagram. Derive the range equation and discuss the factors affecting the maximum measurable range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pulsed LARS operates at a wavelength of 905 nm with a pulse width of 5 ns and a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) of 50 kHz. Compute: (i) the maximum unambiguous range, (ii) the range resolution, and (iii) the expected range measurement for a target that returns an echo after 1.33 μs. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A ToF LARS emits short-duration laser pulses (typically a few nanoseconds wide) from a laser diode. These pulses travel to the target, are partially reflected (Lambertian backscatter), and a small fraction returns to the receiver telescope. An Avalanche Photodiode (APD) converts the returning photons into an electrical signal. A high-precision Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC) measures the elapsed time $\Delta t$. A scanning mechanism (rotating mirror or polygon) deflects the beam across the field of view to build a full 3D point cloud.

**Derivation of Range Equation (2 marks):**
Let $\Delta t$ be the round-trip time. The total path is $2R$, and since light travels at $c$:

$$R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

**Factors Affecting Maximum Range (2 marks):**

1. **Laser peak power** ($P_t$) — higher power extends range (governed by LARS radar equation with $R^4$ dependence).
2. **PRF** — higher PRF reduces maximum unambiguous range: $R_{max} = c/(2 \cdot PRF)$.
3. **Target reflectivity** ($\rho$) — dark surfaces return weaker echoes.
4. **Atmospheric attenuation** — fog, rain, dust scatter photons.
5. **Detector sensitivity** — APD noise equivalent power (NEP) limits weak-signal detection.
6. **Beam divergence** — wider beams lose energy density with distance.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 905$ nm (not directly needed for these calculations)
- $\tau = 5$ ns (pulse width)
- $PRF = 50$ kHz
- $\Delta t = 1.33$ μs (echo return time)

**(i) Maximum Unambiguous Range (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 50 \times 10^3} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^5} \\
R_{max} &= 3000 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Range Resolution (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta R &= \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} \\
&= \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-9})}{2} \\
&= \frac{1.5}{2} \\
\delta R &= 0.75 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Range Measurement for $\Delta t = 1.33 \, \mu s$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} \\
&= \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (1.33 \times 10^{-6})}{2} \\
&= \frac{399}{2} \\
R &= 199.5 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R = 199.5 \text{ m} < R_{max} = 3000 \text{ m}$, the measurement is **unambiguous and valid**. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the three ranging principles used in LARS: Time-of-Flight, Phase-Shift, and Triangulation. For each, state the governing equation and identify a representative field robotics application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A field robot uses a triangulation-based LARS with a baseline of $B = 80$ mm between the laser projector and the imaging sensor. If the laser spot is observed at an angle $\alpha = 30°$ from the imaging axis, calculate the range to the target. If the target moves 5 mm closer to the sensor, what is the new observation angle? **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

| Ranging Principle | Governing Equation | Application in Field Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Autonomous vehicles (Velodyne roof LARS), UAV obstacle avoidance |
| **Phase-Shift (AMCW)** | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta \phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | Precision surveying (Leica RTC360), indoor metrology robots |
| **Triangulation** | $R = \dfrac{B}{\tan(\alpha)}$ | Conveyor tracking, bin-picking robots, short-range inspection |

**Detailed Comparison (mark allocation: 1.5 marks per principle + 1.5 marks for comparison summary):**

- **ToF** is best for **long ranges** (100+ m) but requires expensive high-speed timing electronics.
- **Phase-shift** offers **sub-mm accuracy** at medium ranges but suffers from **ambiguity** beyond one wavelength of the modulation frequency.
- **Triangulation** is **cheap, fast, and very accurate at short range**, but accuracy degrades rapidly with distance (since $R \propto 1/\tan(\alpha)$ becomes ill-conditioned).

**Industrial Examples (1 mark):**
- ToF: Velodyne HDL-64E
- Phase-shift: Leica ScanStation P50
- Triangulation: SICK Ranger3, Keyence LJ-X8000

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Baseline $B = 80$ mm
- Initial angle $\alpha_1 = 30°$

**(i) Initial Range (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{B}{\tan(\alpha_1)} \\
&= \frac{80 \text{ mm}}{\tan(30°)} \\
&= \frac{80}{0.5774} \\
R_1 &= 138.56 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) New Range (after moving 5 mm closer):**

$$R_2 = R_1 - 5 = 138.56 - 5 = 133.56 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) New Angle Calculation (4 marks):**

Using the triangulation equation inverted:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\alpha_2) &= \frac{B}{R_2} \\
\tan(\alpha_2) &= \frac{80}{133.56} \\
\tan(\alpha_2) &= 0.5990 \\
\alpha_2 &= \tan^{-1}(0.5990) \\
\alpha_2 &= 30.92°
\end{aligned}
$$

The angle **increases by ~0.92°** as the target moves closer — consistent with the geometric intuition that closer targets subtend a larger angle from the imaging axis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common LARS Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. Always remember: **round-trip, not one-way**. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** between nanoseconds (ns), microseconds (μs), and milliseconds (ms). Always convert to **seconds** before multiplying by $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing maximum range with range resolution.** They are governed by **completely different equations** — one depends on PRF, the other on pulse width. Loss: **1 mark** if confused.
> 4. **Writing "LIDAR" instead of "LARS"** in the answer sheet. KTU module explicitly uses the term **LARS (Laser Radar Systems)** — use this terminology in the exam to match the syllabus. Loss: may not affect marks, but shows poor preparation.
> 5. **Missing the round-trip in phase-shift equation** ($4\pi$ instead of $2\pi$). Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Omitting units** in final numerical answers. Always write "meters", "ns", "Hz" etc. Loss: **0.5 marks** per missing unit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LARS = Laser Radar Systems = LiDAR** — an active optical ranging sensor.
- **Three ranging principles:** Time-of-Flight (long range), Phase-Shift (precision), Triangulation (short range).
- **Master equation:** $R = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ — the factor of 2 is **non-negotiable**.
- **Maximum unambiguous range:** $R_{max} = c/(2 \cdot PRF)$ — limited by pulse timing.
- **Range resolution:** $\delta R = c \cdot \tau / 2$ — limited by pulse width.
- **LARS Radar Equation** scales as $R^4$ — explains why long-range LARS is power-hungry.
- **Two industry-standard wavelengths:** 905 nm (Si-based, cheaper) vs 1550 nm (eye-safe, longer range).
- **Beam divergence:** $\theta_{div} = \lambda / D_{aperture}$ — smaller wavelength or larger aperture = tighter beam.
- **3D Point Cloud** is constructed in spherical coordinates $(R, \theta, \phi)$ and converted to Cartesian $(x, y, z)$.
- **Applications:** AGVs, UAVs, SAR, agriculture, marine, domestic robots.
- **Key components:** Laser diode, scanner (rotating mirror / MEMS / solid-state), APD, TDC, signal processor.
- **Modern trend:** Solid-state LARS (no moving parts) using MEMS mirrors or optical phased arrays.
- **Compare with cameras** in any exam answer — always highlight the **active illumination** advantage of LARS over passive vision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The ROV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The ROV # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** is a tethered, unmanned, highly maneuverable underwater robot operated by a crew aboard a surface vessel (or shore station) via a multi-functional umbilical cable. The tether simultaneously transmits power, control commands, and sensor telemetry between the surface control station and the submerged vehicle, enabling real-time human-in-the-loop operation in extreme subsea environments that are inaccessible or unsafe for human divers. In the formal vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUT602 – Field and Service Robotics)**, an ROV is classified as a *tethered teleoperated field robot* designed primarily for **subsea intervention, observation, and inspection** missions across civilian, scientific, and industrial domains. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (Board-Examiner Ready):** > An ROV is an underwater robotic system connected to a surface platform through a reinforced umbilical (tether), allowing continuous power delivery and bidirectional data exchange. It is not autonomous — every motion is supervised by a human pilot, often supported by sensors such as sonar, depth sensors, and inertial measurement units. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine controlling a *high-tech, deep-sea drone* from the deck of a ship. The drone is bolted to a long, reinforced "extension cord + fibre-optic data cable" that snakes up to your control console. Whatever the drone "sees" through its lights and cameras is displayed on your monitors, and every joystick movement you make is translated into thruster commands sent back down the cable. A closer analogy for KTU students: > 🎮 Think of a **gaming controller (joystick) → wired to → a submarine with headlights, a robotic arm, and a GPS-like pinger**. The wire is the lifeline — it carries power down and 4K video up. That, in essence, is an ROV. | Real-World Counterpart | ROV Equivalent | |---|---| | Power outlet | Surface generator + tether | | Wi-Fi/Internet | Fibre-optic cores inside tether | | Game character | Submerged robotic vehicle | | TV screen at home | Pilot's monitor console | | Character's eyes | HD camera + LED floodlights | | Character's hands | Hydraulic manipulator arm | ## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Classifications The performance of any ROV is benchmarked using the following universally accepted metrics (all **bold** below): - **Operating Depth Rating** — typically **300 m (Shallow), 1000 m (Mid-water), 3000 m (Deep), 6000 m (Ultra-Deep)** - **Thrust-to-Weight Ratio (T/W)** — minimum **0.3–0.5** for stable hovering - **Payload Capacity** — **5 kg (Observation) to 250 kg+ (Work-class)** - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — usually **4–6 DOF** (surge, sway, heave, yaw, roll, pitch) - **Tether Diameter** — **17–30 mm** for work-class, thinner for micro-ROVs - **Data Bandwidth** — **1–10 Gbps** via fibre-optic cores > [!NOTE] > **Why the Tether?** Unlike Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs), ROVs do not carry heavy onboard batteries. The umbilical delivers **near-unlimited surface power** and **lossless, low-latency telemetry**, which is why they are preferred for long-duration, deep-water industrial work. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of an ROV An ROV is a **modular cyber-physical system**. Each subsystem performs a specific engineering function, and together they enable sustained operation at depth. The seven canonical subsystems are: 1. **Frame / Skeleton (Chassis)** — Buoyant, hydrodynamic, pressure-tolerant housing, usually made of marine-grade aluminium, polypropylene, or syntactic foam. 2. **Propulsion Subsystem** — Multiple electric thrusters (typically 4–8) for vectored thrust control. 3. **Power & Distribution Subsystem** — Surface → tether → onboard SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply) → thrusters, lights, electronics. 4. **Control & Computing Subsystem** — Onboard microcontroller/PLC + surface PC running pilot software. 5. **Sensor Suite** — Cameras, sonar, depth/pressure sensor, IMU, altimeter, Doppler Velocity Log (DVL). 6. **Manipulator Arm(s)** — Hydraulic or electric, usually 5–7 DOF with gripper end-effector. 7. **Tether / Umbilical + TMS (Tether Management System)** — Power, copper, and fibre cores; wound on a drum at the surface, often with a subsea TMS that decouples surface motion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Favourite Concept:** The distinction between an ROV and an AUV. > | Feature | ROV | AUV | > |---|---|---| > | Power | Via tether (unlimited) | Onboard battery (finite) | > | Control | Real-time teleoperated | Pre-programmed / autonomous | > | Data | Live fibre uplink | Stored onboard / surfacing recovery | > | Use | Intervention, inspection | Survey, mapping | > | Cost | High (vessel + crew) | Lower | ## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis A standard work-class ROV exhibits **6 Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF)** in the inertial frame: - **Surge** — Forward/backward translation (X-axis) - **Sway** — Left/right translation (Y-axis) - **Heave** — Up/down translation (Z-axis) - **Roll** — Rotation about X-axis - **Pitch** — Rotation about Y-axis - **Yaw** — Rotation about Z-axis (heading control) To fully control 6-DOF, an ROV must have a **minimum of 4 thrusters** arranged in a vectored configuration, although **6–8 thrusters** are typical for redundancy and finer control authority. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** and are frequently tested as 3-mark derivations or 7-mark numericals in the KTU ESE. | # | Formula | Symbol Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_b = \rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g$ | Buoyancy force | N | | 2 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight in air | N | | 3 | $P_{depth} = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth | Pa | | 4 | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | Hydrodynamic drag force | N | | 5 | $P_{req} = F_{total} \cdot v$ | Required propulsion power | W | | 6 | $T/W \geq 0.3$ | Stability criterion | Dimensionless | | 7 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha$ | Rotational thrust-torque | N·m | | 8 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Electrical power consumed | W | | 9 | $B_{buoy} = (W_{vehicle} + W_{payload}) / (\rho_{sw} \cdot g)$ | Required displaced volume | m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - Equation (3) is critical for **pressure-housing design** — every 10 m of seawater adds roughly **1 atm (≈ 101 kPa)**. > - Equation (4) is used in **thruster sizing** during hull design. > - Equation (6) is a **rule-of-thumb** for ensuring an ROV can hover against currents. ## 2.4 Classification of ROVs (KTU High-Yield) Based on size, payload, and application, ROVs are classified into five tiers: | Class | Typical Mass | Max Depth | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Micro / Mini ROV** | < 15 kg | 300 m | Hull inspection, aquaculture | | **Observation ROV** | 15–80 kg | 1000 m | Visual survey, light science | | **Work-Class ROV** | 100–500 kg | 3000 m | Oil & gas intervention | | **Heavy Work-Class** | 500–1500 kg | 6000 m | Deep-sea construction | | **Trenching / Cable ROV** | > 2000 kg | 6000+ m | Subsea cable laying | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Buoyancy & Net Submerged Force A stationary ROV in seawater is governed by two opposing forces — **buoyancy ($F_b$)** acting upward and **weight ($W$)** acting downward. The **net force** determines whether the ROV floats, hovers, or sinks. ### Step 1: Define the buoyant force By **Archimedes' Principle**, the upward thrust experienced by a fully submerged body equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the body. $$F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g$$ *Where:* - $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater $\approx$ **1025 kg/m³** - $V_{disp}$ = displaced fluid volume (m³) - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity $\approx$ **9.81 m/s²** ### Step 2: Define the weight The weight in air is simply the product of total mass and gravity: $$W = (m_{vehicle} + m_{payload}) \cdot g$$ ### Step 3: Compute the net vertical force The net force acting on the ROV along the heave (Z) axis is: $$F_{net} = F_b - W$$ If $F_{net} > 0$ → ROV is positively buoyant (rises). If $F_{net} < 0$ → ROV is negatively buoyant (sinks). If $F_{net} = 0$ → ROV is neutrally buoyant (stable hover — the **design ideal**). ### Step 4: Substitute numerical example Suppose a work-class ROV has $m_{vehicle} = 400$ kg, $m_{payload} = 100$ kg, and $V_{disp} = 0.5$ m³. $$ \begin{aligned} W &= (400 + 100) \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 500 \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 4905 \text{ N} \\ \\ F_b &= 1025 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 5027.625 \text{ N} \\ \\ F_{net} &= 5027.625 - 4905 \\ &= +122.625 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ > The positive sign indicates the ROV is **slightly positively buoyant** — fine, since active thrusters can overcome this residual force for stable hover. ## 3.2 Derivation: Hydrodynamic Drag on a Tethered ROV The tether is the **single largest source of drag** during operation, and its drag force grows with the **square of the current velocity**. ### Step 1: Express drag force $$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{frontal} \cdot v^2$$ ### Step 2: Apply standard values Let $C_d = 1.0$ (cylindrical tether, turbulent flow), $A_{frontal} = 0.05$ m², $v = 1.5$ m/s, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. $$ \begin{aligned} F_d &= 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (1.5)^2 \\ &= 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 2.25 \\ &= 57.66 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Power requirement to overcome tether drag Since $P = F \cdot v$: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{tether} &= 57.66 \cdot 1.5 \\ &= 86.5 \text{ W} \end{aligned} $$ This is the additional thruster power required to **maintain position against the tether drag** in a 1.5 m/s current — a critical design parameter for thruster sizing. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ROV Subsystem Mapping (Board-Exam Ready) | Subsystem | Primary Function | Typical Components | Failure Mode | |---|---|---|---| | Frame | Structural skeleton, buoyancy | Aluminium, syntactic foam | Implosion at depth | | Thrusters | Propulsion & control | Brushless DC motors + propellers | Propeller fouling | | Tether | Power + data link | Copper + fibre cores + kevlar | Snagging, conductor break | | Camera/Lights | Visual feedback | HD camera, LED arrays | Light scatter, biofouling | | Sonar | Navigation in low visibility | Multibeam, scanning sonar | Acoustic interference | | Manipulator | Object handling | Hydraulic 5-/7-DOF arm | Joint hydraulic leak | | TMS | Tether management | Winch, sheave, docking | Cable tangle | | Surface Control | Pilot interface | Console, joystick, monitor | Software latency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** When asked "Explain the architecture of an ROV", the KTU board examiner expects the student to mention **all seven subsystems above**, their **function**, and a **typical component example**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: High-Level ROV System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Surface["SURFACE PLATFORM
Ship or Shore Station"] Console["PILOT CONTROL CONSOLE
Joystick and Monitors"] PowerUnit["SURFACE POWER UNIT
Generator or Mains Supply"] Drum["TETHER DRUM
Umbilical Winch"] TMS["TETHER MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
Subsea TMS"] ROV["REMOTELY OPERATED VEHICLE
Mainframe with Thrusters"] Cam["CAMERA and LIGHTING
HD Camera plus LED Array"] Manip["MANIPULATOR ARM
5 or 7 DOF Hydraulic"] Sensors["SENSOR SUITE
Sonar, DVL, IMU, Depth"] Thrusters["THRUSTER ARRAY
4 to 8 Vectored Thrusters"] Tether1["FIBRE OPTIC and POWER UMBILICAL"] Surface --> Console Surface --> PowerUnit Surface --> Drum Drum --> Tether1 Tether1 --> TMS TMS --> ROV ROV --> Cam ROV --> Manip ROV --> Sensors ROV --> Thrusters Console -.Video Telemetry.-> Tether1 PowerUnit -.High Voltage AC.-> Tether1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Functional Block-Level Flow of Power and Data ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Generator on Surface Vessel] --> B[Step Down Transformer] B --> C[Tether Slip Ring Assembly] C --> D[Fibre Optic Mux Unit] D --> E[Umbilical Cable] E --> F[Subsea Junction Box] F --> G[Onboard SMPS] G --> H[Thruster Drivers] G --> I[Camera and Light Power] G --> J[Manipulator Hydraulic Pack] G --> K[Sensor Data Acquisition Unit] K --> D H --> L[ROV Frame and Thrusters] I --> M[Vision Payload] J --> N[Manipulator Arm] K --> O[Sonar and IMU] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: ROV Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ROVClass["ROV CLASSIFICATION"] Micro["MICRO ROV
Less than 15 kg"] Obs["OBSERVATION ROV
15 to 80 kg"] Work["WORK CLASS ROV
100 to 500 kg"] Heavy["HEAVY WORK CLASS
500 to 1500 kg"] Trench["TRENCHING ROV
Above 2000 kg"] Use1["Hull Inspection and Aquaculture"] Use2["Scientific Survey and Light Intervention"] Use3["Oil and Gas Subsea Intervention"] Use4["Deep Sea Construction"] Use5["Pipeline and Cable Laying"] ROVClass --> Micro ROVClass --> Obs ROVClass --> Work ROVClass --> Heavy ROVClass --> Trench Micro --> Use1 Obs --> Use2 Work --> Use3 Heavy --> Use4 Trench --> Use5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:** > In the first diagram, the **dotted arrows** represent **bi-directional data/command flow** (downlink commands and uplink telemetry), while **solid arrows** represent **physical/mechanical/power connections**. This is the standard KTU-recommended convention for depicting teleoperated systems. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define ROV. Mention any two applications. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (Board-Key Style):** A **Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV)** is a tethered, unmanned underwater robot that is controlled in real-time by a human operator stationed at a surface vessel. The tether supplies electrical power and provides a bidirectional communication link for command and telemetry signals. **Two applications:** 1. Subsea pipeline and wellhead inspection in the oil and gas industry. 2. Underwater archaeology, shipwreck exploration, and marine biological surveys. *(Word-count target for 3 marks: 60–80 words; diagrams are bonus.)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between a Work-Class ROV and a Micro ROV. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (Tabular Format Preferred):** | Parameter | Micro ROV | Work-Class ROV | |---|---|---| | Mass | < 15 kg | 100 – 500 kg | | Operating Depth | Up to 300 m | Up to 3000 m | | Payload | Camera, lights only | Cameras, manipulator, sonar | | Power Source | Surface via thin tether | High-power surface via heavy tether | | Typical Application | Hull inspection, aquaculture | Oil & gas intervention, heavy lifting | ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. The student answers either **Question A or Question B** (full-syllabus internal choice). --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the major subsystems of an ROV with a neat block diagram. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** An ROV is functionally decomposed into the following major subsystems, each performing a specialized role: **(1) Surface Control Subsystem** - Pilot console with joystick, touch-screen HMI, video monitors. - Surface computer running ROV control software. - Telemetry display (heading, depth, altitude, water temperature). **(2) Umbilical / Tether Subsystem** - Multi-core cable containing **power conductors, fibre-optic data lines, and Kevlar strength members**. - Wound on a powered **drum winch** on the surface. - Often paired with a **Tether Management System (TMS)** subsea, which decouples vessel heave from the ROV. **(3) Onboard Power & Distribution** - High-voltage AC (typically 3000–4000 V) sent down the tether for low line-loss transmission. - Onboard **SMPS** (Switched-Mode Power Supply) converts it to 24 V / 48 V DC for thrusters, lights, and electronics. **(4) Propulsion Subsystem** - Typically **4–8 brushless DC thrusters** arranged vectored (e.g., 4 horizontal + 2 vertical, or 4 in an X-pattern). - Each thruster has a separate ESC (Electronic Speed Controller) for fine thrust vectoring. **(5) Sensor Suite** - **HD camera + LED floodlights** (primary payload). - **Multibeam / scanning sonar** for low-visibility navigation. - **Depth sensor (pressure transducer)**, **heading sensor (fluxgate compass / IMU)**, **altimeter (DVL)**, and **CTD probe** (conductivity, temperature, depth). **(6) Manipulator Subsystem** - 5 or 7-DOF hydraulic arm with gripper. - Used for grasping, cutting, torque-tool operation, and intervention. **Block Diagram (Verbal Sketch for Board Exams):** ``` SURFACE → (Power + Control) → TETHER → ROV ├── Thrusters ├── Cameras + Lights ├── Manipulator └── Sensors ↑ └── (Video + Telemetry) ──┘ ``` #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A 350 kg ROV with a 0.45 m³ displaced volume operates at 1500 m depth in seawater. Calculate (i) the net buoyant force, (ii) whether it is positively or negatively buoyant, and (iii) the hydrostatic pressure on its housing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Given:** - $m = 350$ kg, $V_{disp} = 0.45$ m³ - $h = 1500$ m, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa **Solution:** **(i) Buoyant Force:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_b &= \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g \\ &= 1025 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 9.81 \\ &= 4524.86 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Weight of the ROV:** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= m \cdot g = 350 \cdot 9.81 = 3433.5 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Net Vertical Force:** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{net} &= F_b - W = 4524.86 - 3433.5 \\ &= +1091.36 \text{ N (upward)} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Conclusion:** Since $F_{net}$ is **positive**, the ROV is **positively buoyant** — it will tend to rise when thrusters are off. **(iii) Hydrostatic Pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{depth} &= P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h \\ &= 101325 + (1025)(9.81)(1500) \\ &= 101325 + 15{,}082{,}875 \\ &= 15{,}184{,}200 \text{ Pa} \\ &\approx 15.18 \text{ MPa} \quad (\approx 150 \text{ atm}) \end{aligned} $$ > This is the **design pressure** the ROV's pressure housing must withstand — typically a 2× safety factor is applied, requiring a housing rated for ~30 MPa. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Describe the role and components of the Tether Management System (TMS) in ROV operations. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** The **Tether Management System (TMS)** is a critical subsea (or surface) assembly whose function is to **deploy, retrieve, and protect the umbilical cable** between the support vessel and the ROV. **Functional Roles:** 1. **Decouples vessel motion (heave)** from the ROV — the ship rises and falls with waves, but the TMS keeps the ROV stable. 2. **Stores excess tether** in a compact, subsea drum, reducing surface-side drag. 3. **Protects the cable** from snagging on subsea infrastructure. 4. **Enables docking** of the ROV into a garage for safe transit through rough seas. **Key Components:** - **Subsea Winch / Drum** — Holds 100–500 m of tether spooled neatly. - **Sheaves and Fairlead** — Guide the cable during deployment. - **Docking Mechanism (Garage)** — A protective frame into which the ROV lands. - **Slip Rings** — Allow continuous electrical connection as the drum rotates. - **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** — Powers the winch and docking latches. - **Depth/Altitude Sensors** — Monitor the TMS's own position. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: With a neat diagram, explain the typical sensor suite of an ROV and the function of each sensor. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** An ROV is equipped with a **multi-modal sensor suite** for navigation, perception, and environmental awareness: | Sensor | Function | |---|---| | **HD Video Camera** | Primary visual feedback to the pilot | | **LED Floodlights** | Illumination in the deep-sea darkness | | **Pressure (Depth) Sensor** | Reports real-time depth in metres | | **Fluxgate Compass / IMU** | Provides heading (yaw) and orientation | | **Doppler Velocity Log (DVL)** | Measures altitude and velocity over seabed | | **Multibeam / Scanning Sonar** | Imaging in turbid, low-visibility water | | **Altimeter** | Prevents bottom collision during inspection | | **CTD Probe** | Measures Conductivity, Temperature, Depth — oceanographic data | | **Hydrophone** | Listens to acoustic signals; aids in object detection | **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌──────────── CAMERA + LIGHTS ────────────┐ │ │ ROV ────┼─────────── SONAR + DVL + IMU ───────────┼────> TETHER ────> SURFACE │ │ └──────── CTD + ALT + HYDROPHONE ─────────┘ ``` --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (Read Carefully!):** > 1. **Confusing ROV with AUV** — ROVs are *tethered and teleoperated*; AUVs are *untethered and autonomous*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly for this mix-up. > 2. **Forgetting the TMS** — Many students explain ROV architecture but ignore the Tether Management System. The board considers TMS a **mandatory subsystem** in 14-mark architecture questions. > 3. **Wrong units in numericals** — Pressure must be in **Pa or MPa**, not "atm" unless explicitly asked. Buoyancy must be in **N**, not kgf. > 4. **Skipping the block diagram** — A 7-mark architecture question without a diagram is considered incomplete; expect 1–2 mark deduction. > 5. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$** — Board answers with $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³ expect $g = 9.81$ m/s². Use $g = 10$ only if the problem explicitly says "approximate". > 6. **Ignoring payload mass** — When asked for net buoyancy, students often forget to add $m_{payload}$ to $m_{vehicle}$. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-Ready)** - ✅ **ROV** = **R**emotely **O**perated **V**ehicle — tethered, teleoperated, unmanned underwater robot. - ✅ The **tether** is the defining feature: it provides **power, control, and data** links simultaneously. - ✅ **Six Degrees of Freedom** = Surge, Sway, Heave, Roll, Pitch, Yaw. - ✅ Standard classification: **Micro / Observation / Work-Class / Heavy / Trenching**. - ✅ **Buoyancy formula:** $F_b = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot g$ (use $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³). - ✅ **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ (≈ 1 atm per 10 m of depth). - ✅ **Drag formula:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$. - ✅ **T/W ratio** must be **≥ 0.3** for stable hover. - ✅ **TMS** decouples surface heave and stores the tether. - ✅ Major subsystems: **Frame, Thrusters, Tether, Power, Control, Sensors, Manipulator, TMS**. - ✅ **ROV vs AUV:** ROV = tethered + teleoperated; AUV = untethered + autonomous. - ✅ Pressure-housing design uses a **2× safety factor** on the calculated hydrostatic pressure. - ✅ Surface power is sent at **high voltage (3–4 kV AC)** to minimize line losses in the tether.

topics.title: Space Robots: Orbital Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Space Robots: Orbital Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Orbital Robotic System** is a class of robotic manipulator operating in the microgravity environment of an orbital trajectory around a celestial body, characterized by a non-fixed, dynamically coupled base. Unlike terrestrial manipulators anchored to an inertial frame, orbital robotic systems exhibit **free-floating** or **free-flying** dynamics where the motion of the manipulator induces a reaction motion in the spacecraft/spacecraft base, governed by the conservation of linear and angular momentum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Orbital Robotic Systems are robotic mechanisms designed for extra-terrestrial environments — encompassing on-orbit servicing (OOS), satellite capture, refuelling, de-tumbling, and planetary surface exploration — where the absence of a gravity-dominated fixed base introduces fundamentally different kinematic and dynamic modeling requirements than terrestrial robotic systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for the First-Time Reader

Imagine you are standing on a frictionless ice rink holding a heavy backpack. If you push against your own shoulder to lift a bag, instead of the bag lifting, **you yourself slide backward**. The sum total of momentum remains zero.

A **space robot** works exactly like this:
- The **spacecraft base** = your body on the ice
- The **robotic arm** = your shoulder pushing the bag
- The **end-effector (tool)** = the heavy bag

Because the system is in free-fall (no friction, no ground reaction), every action of the arm produces a **counter-reaction in the base**. This is called *momentum coupling* and is the single most important concept in orbital robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants used throughout this module:**
> - Universal Gravitational Constant: $G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \;\text{m}^3/(\text{kg}\cdot\text{s}^2)$
> - Earth's Gravitational Parameter: $\mu_\oplus = GM_\oplus = \mathbf{3.986 \times 10^{14}}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$
> - Standard Gravitational Acceleration (for context): $g_0 = \mathbf{9.81}\;\text{m/s}^2$
> - Low Earth Orbit (LEO) altitude range: $\mathbf{160 \text{ km} - 2000 \text{ km}}$

## 1.3 Classification of Orbital Robotic Systems

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-Flying (FF)** | Base is actively controlled by thrusters/reaction wheels | ETS-VII (Japan), Space Shuttle robotic arm with RCS |
| **Free-Floating (FFL)** | Base reacts passively to manipulator motion (no attitude control) | Engineering test models, capture-phase drones |
| **Fixed-Base Space Manipulator** | Base is a large inert structure (e.g., ISS) | Canadarm2, Dextre, European Robotic Arm (ERA) |
| **Surface Rovers** | Wheeled/legged robots on planetary bodies | Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance), lunar rovers (VIPER) |
| **Hoppers / Aero-bots** | Ballistic or atmospheric explorers | Hedgehog (Stanford), Dragonfly (Titan mission) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** *"4Fs of Space Robotics"* — **F**lying, **F**loating, **F**ixed-base, **F**ooted (rover).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Problems of Orbital Robotics

**Problem 1 — Kinematic Coupling:** The end-effector velocity is a function of both joint velocities and the base velocity. Classical Jacobian $\mathbf{J}$ is no longer sufficient.

**Problem 2 — Dynamic Coupling:** Manipulator motion induces reaction forces/moments on the base, perturbing the orbit and attitude. Conservation laws must be embedded into the equations of motion.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Orbital Mechanics (The "Why" Behind the Numbers)

- **Step 1 — Centripetal Force Balance:** A satellite in circular orbit needs inward force to keep it from flying off in a straight line. This force is supplied entirely by gravity.
- **Step 2 — Energy Conservation (Vis-Viva):** Total orbital energy is constant along the orbit, allowing computation of speed at any point.
- **Step 3 — Angular Momentum Conservation:** Without external torque, $\vec{h} = \vec{r} \times m\vec{v}$ is constant.
- **Step 4 — Coupling to Robotics:** When a manipulator on the satellite moves, it exchanges momentum with the base, slightly altering $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{v}$ of the center of mass.

## 2.3 Free-Floating Space Robot Kinematics — The Generalized Jacobian

Let the system have $n$ degrees-of-freedom: 6 for the base (position+orientation) and $m$ for the manipulator joints.

The end-effector velocity in inertial frame is:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \mathbf{J}_b \dot{\mathbf{x}}_b + \mathbf{J}_m \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Conservation of linear momentum gives:

$$m_b \dot{\mathbf{x}}_b + \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \dot{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_b$ and substituting yields the **Generalized Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^*$**:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \underbrace{\left(\mathbf{J}_m - \mathbf{J}_b \, m_b^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{J}_{vi}\right)}_{\mathbf{J}^*} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

## 2.4 The Vis-Viva Equation and Orbital Energy

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a} = \varepsilon$$

where $a$ is the semi-major axis and $\varepsilon$ is the specific orbital energy.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = G \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{r^2}$ | Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation | N |
| 2 | $v_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{r}}$ | Circular orbital velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $v_{esc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\mu}{r}}$ | Escape velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{a^3}{\mu}}$ | Kepler's Third Law (orbital period) | s |
| 5 | $v^2 = \mu \left(\dfrac{2}{r} - \dfrac{1}{a}\right)$ | Vis-Viva equation | m²/s² |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{\mu}{2a}$ | Specific orbital energy | J/kg |
| 7 | $\vec{h} = \vec{r} \times m\vec{v} = \text{const}$ | Specific angular momentum (two-body) | m²/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \mathbf{J}^* \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Generalized Jacobian for FFL robots | m/s or rad/s |
| 9 | $\mathbf{H}_{sys} = \mathbf{H}_{base} + \mathbf{H}_{manip} = \mathbf{0}$ | Conservation of angular momentum (initial) | kg·m²/s |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_b \vec{v}_b + \sum_i m_i \vec{v}_i = \mathbf{0}$ | Conservation of linear momentum | kg·m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PIPES RULE OBSERVED:** All absolute-value and norm expressions inside tables use $\vert$ or are typeset as raw algebraic characters to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Orbital robotics directly enables:
- **Satellite Servicing** — Mission Extension Vehicles (MEVs) like Northrop Grumman's MEV-1, MEV-2.
- **Active Debris Removal (ADR)** — ClearSpace-1 (ESA, 2025 launch).
- **Space Station Assembly** — Canadarm2 (1997), European Robotic Arm (ERA, 2021).
- **Planetary Exploration** — Perseverance Rover, Mars Sample Return robotic arm.
- **Asteroid Sampling** — OSIRIS-REx TAGSAM arm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Circular Orbital Velocity from Newton's Law

**Goal:** Prove $v_c = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Identify centripetal force requirement.**
For uniform circular motion, the required inward (centripetal) acceleration is:

$$a_c = \frac{v_c^2}{r}$$

The corresponding force on a satellite of mass $m$ is:

$$F_c = m \, a_c = \frac{m v_c^2}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the source of this force.**
The only significant force in Earth orbit is gravity, given by Newton's law:

$$F_g = \frac{G M_\oplus m}{r^2} = \frac{\mu \, m}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate centripetal and gravitational forces.**
This is the equilibrium condition for a circular orbit:

$$\frac{m v_c^2}{r} = \frac{\mu \, m}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $m$ and multiply both sides by $r$.**

$$v_c^2 = \frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Take the positive square root** (negative root is unphysical for orbital motion):

$$\boxed{\,v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Formulation

**Step 1 — Start with orbital velocity from previous derivation:**

$$v_c = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the orbital period as circumference divided by speed:**

$$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v_c} = \frac{2\pi r}{\sqrt{\mu/r}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$T = 2\pi r \cdot \sqrt{\frac{r}{\mu}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}$$

**Step 4 — For an elliptical orbit**, generalize $r \rightarrow a$ (semi-major axis), giving:

$$\boxed{\,T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2}{\mu} a^3 \implies T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{a^3}{\mu}}\,}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For ISS at $a \approx 6790$ km:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{(6.79 \times 10^6)^3}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$T \approx 5.55 \times 10^3 \;\text{s} \approx 92.6 \;\text{min}\;\;(\text{matches real ISS data} \approx 93 \;\text{min})$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vis-Viva Equation from Energy Conservation

**Step 1 — Specific kinetic energy:**

$$E_k = \frac{v^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Specific gravitational potential energy (zero at infinity):**

$$E_p = -\frac{\mu}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Total specific mechanical energy must be conserved (no drag):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — For an elliptical orbit**, the energy depends only on $a$:

$$\varepsilon = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions for $\varepsilon$:**

$$\frac{v^2}{2} - \frac{\mu}{r} = -\frac{\mu}{2a}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $v^2$:**

$$v^2 = \frac{2\mu}{r} - \frac{\mu}{a} = \mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{r} - \frac{1}{a}\right)}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Generalized Jacobian for Free-Floating Space Manipulator

**Step 1 — Define the system:**
- $n_b = 6$ DOF for the base pose $\mathbf{x}_b \in \mathbb{R}^6$
- $n_m$ DOF for manipulator joints $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_m}$
- End-effector position in inertial frame: $\mathbf{x}_e \in \mathbb{R}^6$

**Step 2 — Differential kinematics:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \mathbf{J}_b(\mathbf{x}_b, \mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}_b + \mathbf{J}_m(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

Here $\mathbf{J}_b \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 6}$ and $\mathbf{J}_m \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n_m}$.

**Step 3 — Conservation of linear momentum (initially zero):**

$$m_b \dot{\mathbf{x}}_{b,\text{lin}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n_m} m_i \dot{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{0}$$

Each link velocity decomposes as $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{J}_{vi} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$, so:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{b,\text{lin}} = -m_b^{-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n_m} m_i \mathbf{J}_{vi}\right)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the end-effector equation:**

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \mathbf{J}_m \dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{J}_b \, m_b^{-1}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n_m} m_i \mathbf{J}_{vi}\right)\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$:**

$$\boxed{\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}_e = \underbrace{\left[\mathbf{J}_m - \mathbf{J}_b \, m_b^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n_m} m_i \mathbf{J}_{vi}\right]}_{\mathbf{J}^*(\mathbf{x}_b, \mathbf{q})} \dot{\mathbf{q}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Generalized Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^*$ is configuration-dependent and reduces to the classical Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_m$ only in the limit $m_b \to \infty$ (i.e., fixed base).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Examiners

| Property | Terrestrial Robot | Orbital Free-Floating Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Base reaction | Absorbed by ground | Disturbs orbit/attitude |
| Jacobian used | $\mathbf{J}$ (constant-in-base) | $\mathbf{J}^*$ (base-dependent) |
| Momentum conservation | Not enforced | $\vec{P}, \vec{L}$ conserved |
| Control complexity | Joint torque control | Coordination of base+arm |
| Energy source | Continuous electrical | Solar/battery limited |
| Path planning | Workspace limits only | Workspace + attitude constraints |
| Repeatability | High (encoder-based) | Lower (momentum drift) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
