| **Total Weighted Score** | **1.00** | — | **7.15 / 10** |

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Deploy} & \text{if } \sum w_i s_i \geq 7.0 \\ \text{Pilot} & \text{if } 5.0 \leq \sum w_i s_i < 7.0 \\ \text{Reject} & \text{if } \sum w_i s_i < 5.0 \end{cases}
$$

**Result:** Robot is **recommended for deployment** in the pilot semester.

## 3.2 Analytical Model — Entertainment Robot Engagement Loop

The KTU 2024 syllabus also requires understanding the **affective loop** in entertainment robotics.

### Step 1 — Stimulus Reception
$$
S = \{\text{voice}, \text{touch}, \text{vision}, \text{gesture}\}
$$

### Step 2 — Affect Detection
$$
A = f(S) = \sigma(W \cdot S + b) \quad \text{(sigmoid-based affect classifier)}
$$

### Step 3 — Behavioural Response Generation
$$
B = g(A, M) \quad \text{where } M = \text{internal mood state (Markov chain)}
$$

### Step 4 — Expressive Output
$$
O = \{ \text{motion}, \text{sound}, \text{LED pattern}, \text{facial animation} \}
$$

### Step 5 — User Feedback (closes the loop)
$$
S_{t+1} = h(O_t, U_t)
$$

This is essentially a **closed-loop affective HCI system**, often implemented via **reinforcement learning** where the reward function is the user's smile/dwell-time.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Cost Justification of a NAO Robot in a KTU Lab

**Given:**
- NAO robot cost: **INR 8,00,000** (CapEx)
- Annual maintenance: **INR 80,000** (OpEx)
- Service life: **6 years**
- Number of students served per year: **400**
- Expected normalized learning gain: **15%**

**Calculate the 5-year cost per student:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)} &= \text{CapEx} + (\text{OpEx} \times T) \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 + (80{,}000 \times 6) \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}80{,}000 \\
&= 12{,}80{,}000 \; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per student per year} &= \frac{\text{TCO}}{N_{\text{students}} \times T} \\
&= \frac{12{,}80{,}000}{400 \times 6} \\
&= \frac{12{,}80{,}000}{2400} \\
&\approx \text{INR } 533 \; \text{per student per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 15% normalized learning gain at INR 533/student/year is **highly cost-effective** compared to a faculty-led remedial tutorial costing INR 300–500 per hour per student.

## 3.4 KTU Conceptual Checklist — Chain of Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **Always follow this chain in long-answer (14-mark) questions:**
> 1. State the **pedagogical theory** underpinning the use of the robot.
> 2. Identify the **functional architecture layers** involved.
> 3. Map the **human–robot interaction modality** (speech / gesture / GUI).
> 4. Discuss **safety standards** (ISO 13482).
> 5. Provide a **cost–benefit justification** using the ROI / TCO framework.
> 6. Conclude with **limitations** (cost, cultural acceptance, maintenance).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Educational Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pedagogical Goal Definition] --> B[Curriculum Mapping Module]
    B --> C[Lesson Content Database]
    C --> D[Adaptive Tutoring Engine]
    D --> E[Dialogue Manager]
    E --> F[Speech Synthesis TTS]
    E --> G[Gesture Animation Planner]
    E --> H[LED and Display Output]
    I[User Voice Input] --> J[Speech Recognition ASR]
    K[User Touch and Vision Sensors] --> L[Multimodal Sensor Fusion]
    J --> M[Affective State Estimator]
    L --> M
    M --> D
    M --> N[Performance Analytics Logger]
    N --> O[Faculty Dashboard and Reports]
```

## 4.2 Engagement Loop in an Entertainment Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Stimulus] --> B[Multimodal Sensor Array]

    D --> E[Behaviour Policy Engine]
    E --> F[Actuator and Output System]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

**Q: Differentiate between Educational Robots and Entertainment Robots. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each point must appear for full credit):**

| Aspect | Educational Robot | Entertainment Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | Skill and concept transfer | Engagement, fun, companionship |
| HRI Style | Task-oriented, instructional | Socially expressive, playful |
| Example 1 | LEGO Mindstorms EV3 | Sony AIBO |
| Example 2 | NAO humanoid (SoftBank) | Anki Vector |

(1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for clear differentiation statement.)

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q: List any three safety standards applicable to educational and entertainment robots.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **ISO 13482** — Safety requirements for personal care robots (covers mobile servant robots, physical assistant robots, person carrier robots).
2. **EN 71** — European toy safety standard (applicable to child-targeted entertainment robots).
3. **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of electrical/electronic safety-related systems (used for robot control system certification).

(1 mark per standard, 1 mark for explaining *which clause* it covers = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT write only bullet points in 14-mark answers. Examiners expect **at least 3–4 paragraphs of prose** plus diagrams.
> - Always include a **block diagram** (worth 2–3 marks by itself).
> - Forgetting to mention **safety standards (ISO 13482)** costs 1 mark.
> - Numerical / cost-justification questions lose 2 marks if units (INR) and units of learning-gain are missing.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Regular]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an educational robot. Discuss the role of each layer in enabling **constructivist learning**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A private engineering college plans to deploy humanoid robots (NAO) as programming tutors for 500 first-year students. The robot costs INR 9,00,000 with an annual maintenance of INR 1,00,000 and a service life of 5 years. Calculate the cost per student per year. Justify whether the deployment is economically viable assuming a 12% improvement in programming test scores.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Hardware Layer MCU Motors Sensors] --> L2[Firmware Layer Real Time Control]
    L2 --> L3[Software Layer Python Scratch ROS]
    L3 --> L4[Pedagogical Layer Curriculum Mapping Adaptive Tutoring]
    L4 --> OUT[Learning Outcome]
```

**Prose Explanation (5 marks):**

The functional architecture of an educational robot is typically organized into four hierarchical layers, each addressing a distinct aspect of the learning experience.

**Layer 1 — Hardware:** This is the physical embodiment comprising microcontrollers (Arduino Uno, ESP32, Raspberry Pi), actuators (DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors), and sensors (IR, ultrasonic, IMU, camera). This layer provides the *manipulable* interface through which the learner interacts with the physical world, satisfying Piaget's constructivist requirement that knowledge be anchored in concrete experience.

**Layer 2 — Firmware:** Real-time control software running on the microcontroller (typically C/C++ or MicroPython) handles PWM generation, sensor data acquisition, and motor control loops. For a line-follower educational robot, for example, the firmware implements a closed-loop PID controller, exposing it to the student as a teachable artefact.

**Layer 3 — Software:** The high-level programming interface is where the learner writes logic. Modern platforms use **Scratch (block-based), Python, or MATLAB** for higher education. This layer directly supports Papert's constructionist principle — the student programs the artefact, and the artefact programs the student's mind.

**Layer 4 — Pedagogical:** The topmost layer contains the curriculum mapping engine, adaptive tutoring logic, and assessment modules. It interfaces with Learning Management Systems (LMS) such as Moodle and maintains student performance analytics.

By traversing all four layers, the learner experiences the full **Constructivist → Constructionist → Experiential** learning cycle, making the robot not just a tool but a complete pedagogical ecosystem.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) — Numerical

**Given Data:**
- CapEx = INR 9,00,000
- OpEx = INR 1,00,000 / year
- T = 5 years
- N_students = 500 / year
- Learning gain = 12%

**Step 1 — Compute Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCO} &= \text{CapEx} + (\text{OpEx} \times T) \\
&= 9{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\
&= 9{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 14{,}00{,}000 \; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cost per Student per Year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per student per year} &= \frac{\text{TCO}}{N_{\text{students}} \times T} \\
&= \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{500 \times 5} \\
&= \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{2500} \\
&= \text{INR } 560 \; \text{per student per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Economic Viability Justification:**

A 12% improvement in programming test scores across 500 students per year is a substantial pedagogical gain. Compared with industry benchmarks:
- A faculty-led remedial tutorial costs INR 300–500 per hour for 30 students → **INR 1,000 – 1,500 per student per session**.
- The robot at INR 560 per student per year (which delivers 24/7 access) is **far more cost-effective** than human tutoring.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The deployment is **economically viable and pedagogically justified**. The investment pays back in improved pass rates, reduced faculty burnout, and enhanced 24/7 lab accessibility.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **five pedagogical roles** a robot can play in education with one real-world example for each. State the relevant learning theory for each role.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **closed-loop affective interaction model** of an entertainment robot with a neat block diagram. Discuss how reinforcement learning is used to maximize user engagement.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Robots in education are not limited to a single function. Drawing on decades of HCI research, KTU 2024 recognizes five distinct pedagogical roles a robot can assume.

**Role 1 — Robot as Tutor (1 mark + 1 mark example + theory):**
The robot acts as a one-on-one instructor delivering content, asking questions, and providing feedback. **Example:** SoftBank's NAO is deployed in special-education schools to teach vocabulary and social skills to children with ASD (Autism Spectrum Disorder). The supporting learning theory is **Bloom's Mastery Learning** — the robot repeats content until the learner achieves the predefined mastery threshold.

**Role 2 — Robot as Tutee (1 mark + 1 mark example + theory):**
The student *teaches* the robot, which is cognitively more demanding than being taught. **Example:** Children program a LEGO Mindstorms robot to navigate a maze; they must first solve the maze themselves, then translate the solution into code. The theory is **Papert's Constructionism** — learning by creating an artefact.

**Role 3 — Robot as Learning Tool / Manipulative (1 mark + 1 mark example + theory):**
The robot is a programmable object to be experimented with. **Example:** Arduino-based mobile robots in KTU first-year B.Tech robotics workshops, where students implement line-following, obstacle-avoidance, and PID control. Theory: **Piaget's Constructivism** — knowledge emerges from physical manipulation.

**Role 4 — Robot as Companion (1 mark + 1 mark example + theory):**
A long-term social presence that builds rapport. **Example:** Pepper (SoftBank) deployed in Japanese schools as a daily companion. Theory: **Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development** — the robot scaffolds learning within the learner's capability range.

**Role 5 — Robot as Telepresence / Remote Avatar (1 mark + 1 mark example + theory):**
The robot serves as the student's physical avatar in a remote location (e.g., distance-learning engineering labs). **Example:** The Double Robotics telepresence robot used in KTU-affiliated distance education programmes. Theory: **Distributed Cognition** — learning extends across the learner, the robot, and the remote environment.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** These five roles are not mutually exclusive — a single robot platform can fulfil multiple roles depending on the lesson design.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Stimulus Voice Touch Vision] --> B[Multimodal Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Affective Classifier CNN plus RNN]
    C --> D[Mood State Memory]
    D --> E[Behaviour Policy Network]
    E --> F[Action Selection via RL Policy]
    F --> G[Actuators and LED and Sound]
    G --> H[Expressive Output]
    H --> I[User Reaction Smile plus Dwell Time]
    I --> J[Reward Signal Generator]
    J --> K[Policy Network Update]
    K --> E
```

**Prose Explanation (5 marks):**

The closed-loop affective interaction model treats engagement as a continuous control problem. The cycle begins with **User Stimulus Reception** — voice, touch, gesture, and visual cues are captured by the multimodal sensor array (microphones, capacitive touch sensors, RGB-D cameras).

These raw signals are fed into an **Affective Classifier**, typically a deep-learning model combining a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for visual features and a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) for temporal affective dynamics. The classifier outputs a probability distribution over a discrete set of affective states (happy, bored, confused, excited).

The classifier output updates a **Mood State Memory** (often implemented as a Markov chain or LSTM cell) which maintains a temporal model of the user's evolving emotional trajectory.

The **Behaviour Policy Network** then selects an action — a movement, sound, or LED pattern — designed to maximize a long-term engagement reward. This is where **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is critical. The reward function R is typically defined as:

$$
R_t = \alpha \cdot \text{smile}_{t} + \beta \cdot \text{dwell\_time}_{t} - \gamma \cdot \text{repetition\_penalty}_{t}
$$

where α, β, γ are tunable weights. The policy π is updated using a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) or Deep Q-Network (DQN) algorithm to maximize the expected cumulative reward.

After the action is executed, the user's reaction closes the loop, generating a new reward signal and refining the policy. Over thousands of interactions, the robot learns to *personalize* its behaviour to each user's affective profile — becoming more engaging than a static rule-based system.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This RL-driven closed-loop architecture represents the state-of-the-art in entertainment robotics, transforming robots from pre-scripted toys into adaptive affective companions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

### Definitions
- **Educational Robot:** Mechatronic system designed for STEM learning and concept transfer.
- **Entertainment Robot:** Robot designed for affective engagement, play, and companionship.
- **Service Robot:** Robot performing useful tasks for humans outside industrial automation (ISO 8373).

### Theories (Mandatory to Revise)
- **Constructivism** (Piaget) — knowledge through physical manipulation.
- **Constructionism** (Papert) — knowledge through artefact creation.
- **Mastery Learning** (Bloom) — repeat-until-mastery loop.
- **Zone of Proximal Development** (Vygotsky) — scaffolding within capability range.
- **Experiential Learning Cycle** (Kolb) — experience → reflect → conceptualize → experiment.

### Five Pedagogical Roles
- Tutor, Tutee, Tool/Manipulative, Companion, Telepresence Avatar.

### Safety Standards
- **ISO 13482** — Personal care robots (mandatory to cite).
- **EN 71** — Toy safety (entertainment robots for children).
- **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of control systems.

### Famous Platforms
- **Educational:** LEGO Mindstorms, NAO, Thymio, mBot, Ozobot, Sphero.
- **Entertainment:** AIBO, Furby, Anki Vector, Pepper, Cozmo.

### Key Formulas
- **TCO** = CapEx + (OpEx × T)
- **Cost per student per year** = TCO / (N_students × T)
- **Weighted Decision Score** = Σ (wᵢ × sᵢ)
- **Affective Reward** = α·smile + β·dwell_time − γ·repetition_penalty

### Common 14-Mark Answer Structure
1. Block diagram (2 marks).
2. Theory grounding (2 marks).
3. Functional explanation layer-by-layer (4 marks).
4. Real-world example (2 marks).
5. Safety / cost justification (2 marks).
6. Limitations (2 marks).

### Common Student Pitfalls (Avoid!)
- ❌ Confusing **Educational** and **Entertainment** robots.
- ❌ Forgetting to mention **ISO 13482**.
- ❌ Omitting **block diagrams** in long answers.
- ❌ Writing only bullet points in 14-mark questions.
- ❌ Missing **units (INR, hours, years)** in numerical answers.
- ❌ Failing to name a **learning theory** to justify pedagogical use.

### One-Line Exam Punchlines
- *"Educational robots are not tools that teach — they are artefacts that the student teaches themselves through."*
- *"Entertainment robots succeed not by solving problems, but by creating them — playful, engaging, repeatable ones."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Any One General Application Each of Robotics in Education and Entertainment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Any One General Application Each of Robotics in Education and Entertainment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Service Robotics — The Parent Domain

A **Service Robot** is defined by the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** as a robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, **excluding industrial automation applications**. Service robots operate in unstructured, human-centric environments and are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Professional Service Robots** — used in commercial settings (medical, military, agricultural, logistics, education, entertainment).
2. **Personal/Domestic Service Robots** — used in household environments (vacuuming, companionship, edutainment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRUT602):** *Service robots are semi or fully autonomous mechatronic devices that extend human capability by performing non-manufacturing tasks in collaboration with or for human users in real-world, dynamic environments.*

This module positions **Education** and **Entertainment** as two distinct application verticals within the **Professional Service Robot** taxonomy. Both verticals share a common engineering substrate — *human-robot interaction (HRI)*, *autonomous behaviour*, and *social embodiment* — but diverge sharply in their **operational objectives** and **evaluation metrics**.

## 1.2 Educational Robotics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Educational Robotics** refers to the use of robotic systems — either as the *subject* of learning (learning *about* robots) or as the *mediator* of learning (learning *through* robots) — within formal or informal pedagogical contexts.

Educational robots serve a **dual instructional role**:

- **Pedagogical Tool** — A physical artefact for teaching STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Mathematics), coding, and computational thinking.
- **Social Mediator** — A peer-like agent for special-needs learners (autism therapy, behavioural conditioning, rehabilitation).

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Patient Teaching Assistant"

Imagine a classroom teaching assistant who:
- Never gets tired of repeating the same concept for the 50th time.
- Speaks 20+ languages fluently.
- Demonstrates physical actions (walking, dancing, grasping) to make abstract concepts concrete.
- Provides personalised, non-judgemental feedback to shy or special-needs students.

That is precisely what a humanoid educational robot delivers. Unlike a screen-based tutorial, it occupies **physical space** and uses **embodied gestures** to anchor learning in sensorimotor experience — a concept known in cognitive science as *embodied cognition*.

## 1.3 Entertainment Robotics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Entertainment Robotics** encompasses autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems engineered primarily to **engage, amuse, or emotionally resonate** with human users through performative, companion, or interactive behaviours.

Entertainment robots are categorised into three sub-classes:

- **Companion Robots** — Pet-like or character-like robots for affective engagement (e.g., AIBO, PARO).
- **Edutainment Robots** — Hybrid devices bridging play and learning.
- **Themepark/Performance Robots** — Animatronic systems in theme parks or stage performances.

### Intuitive Analogy: The "Mechanical Pet Rock with a Personality"

Think of a Tamagotchi, a Disney character, and a remote-controlled car rolled into one device. An entertainment robot must **project an illusion of life** — responding to touch, voice, and motion with seemingly intentional behaviours. The engineering challenge is not utility but **believability**: the user must willingly suspend disbelief and form a parasocial bond with a machine.

## 1.4 Common Engineering Pillars Across Both Domains

| Pillar | Educational Robot | Entertainment Robot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Goal** | Knowledge transfer / Skill acquisition | Emotional engagement / Amusement |
| **Key Metric** | Learning outcome (Δ score) | Engagement duration / NPS (Net Promoter Score) |
| **HRI Mode** | Didactic + Socratic | Performative + Affective |
| **Safety Standard** | **ISO 13482** (Personal Care Robots) | **ISO 13482** + Child-safety extensions |
| **Autonomy Level** | Semi-autonomous (supervised) | Fully autonomous (in unstructured homes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Both verticals fall under the **ISO 13482:2014** standard — *Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for personal care robots*. This is the **mandatory compliance framework** KTU expects students to cite.

## 1.5 Selected Case Studies (One Each)

For the remainder of these notes, the following two canonical case studies are examined in depth:

1. **Education:** **NAO** (by SoftBank Robotics / United Robotics Group) — the most deployed humanoid educational robot worldwide, present in **> 70 countries** and **> 1,000 universities**.
2. **Entertainment:** **AIBO** (by Sony Corporation) — the iconic robotic dog, with successive generations (ERS-110, ERS-7, ERS-1000) sold in millions of units globally.

These were chosen because they represent **mature, commercially validated** systems with **extensive published engineering literature** — ideal for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Case Study A — NAO: The Educational Humanoid

### 2.1.1 System Overview

NAO is a **58 cm tall**, **5.4 kg** bipedal humanoid robot featuring **25 degrees of freedom (DoF)**. It is the de-facto platform for:

- STEM education (programming, kinematics, AI).
- Special-needs education (autism spectrum disorder therapy via the **ASK NAO** application).
- Research testbed in **HRI (Human-Robot Interaction)** and **Socially Assistive Robotics (SAR)**.

### 2.1.2 Hardware Subsystem Decomposition

| Subsystem | Components | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Actuators** | 25 *Brushless DC servomotors* with harmonic-drive gearboxes | Joint actuation |
| **Sensors — Proprioceptive** | Joint encoders (Hall-effect), **2-axis gyroscope**, **3-axis accelerometer**, **4 force-sensitive resistors (FSRs)** per foot | Self-state estimation |
| **Sensors — Exteroceptive** | **2 HD cameras** (forehead & mouth), **4 microphones** (with echo cancellation), **2 sonar rangefinders** (chest & feet), **2 IR emitters/receivers** | Environmental perception |
| **Compute** | **Intel Atom** processor (later generations: ARM-based) on-board; **NAOqi 2.8** OS (Linux-based) | Behaviour arbitration, vision, speech |
| **Communication** | **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n**, Ethernet, Bluetooth, **Infrared** (NAOqi remote) | Networked interaction |
| **Power** | **Lithium-Ion battery**, ~ **48.6 Wh**, providing ~ **90 minutes** active runtime | Energy autonomy |

### 2.1.3 Software Architecture (Layered View)

NAO operates on a **layered behaviour-based architecture** (akin to Brooks' Subsumption Architecture):

- **Layer 0 — Safety Reflexes:** Fall detection, obstacle avoidance, self-preservation.
- **Layer 1 — Sub-symbolic Skills:** Walking gait, head tracking, audio localisation.
- **Layer 2 — Symbolic Behaviours:** Dialog management, gesture sequencing, task planning.
- **Layer 3 — Application Layer:** Educational curricula (ASK NAO, Choregraphe behaviours).

The behaviour arbitration follows a **finite-state machine (FSM)** combined with a **scheduler** that assigns CPU time slices based on event priorities.

### 2.1.4 Why NAO Succeeds in Education

- **Open Development Environment:** *Choregraphe* (graphical), Python SDK, C++ SDK, MATLAB toolbox.
- **Standardised Benchmarking:** Used in **RoboCup Standard Platform League (SPL)** — providing a globally consistent performance benchmark.
- **Curriculum Integration:** Adopted in **K-12** syllabi across France, USA, and increasingly India (CBSE Atal Tinkering Labs).

## 2.2 Case Study B — AIBO: The Entertainment Robotic Dog

### 2.2.1 System Overview

**AIBO** (Artificial Intelligence Robot, homophone of Japanese *aibō* meaning "companion") is Sony's iconic quadruped entertainment robot. The latest generation, **ERS-1000 (2018)**, features:

- **22 DoF** (head: 3, mouth: 1, ears: 2, tail: 2, legs: 3 each × 4 = 14).
- **OLED eye displays** for emotive expression.
- **64-bit quad-core CPU** with deep-learning behaviour engine.
- **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localisation and Mapping) for autonomous home navigation.

### 2.2.2 Hardware Subsystem Decomposition

| Subsystem | Components | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Actuators** | 22 *Small DC servomotors*, custom designed | Body articulation |
| **Sensors — Proprioceptive** | Encoders on every joint, **6-axis IMU**, battery monitor | Posture & motion state |
| **Sensors — Exteroceptive** | **Stereo cameras** in snout, **Time-of-Flight depth sensor**, **4 microphones**, **capacitive touch sensors** (head, back, paws), **ToF cliff sensors** on belly | Environmental awareness |
| **Compute** | Custom **64-bit quad-core SoC** with dedicated **NVIDIA Tegra X1** GPU for vision | On-board AI inference |
| **Communication** | **Wi-Fi 802.11ac**, LTE option (cloud connectivity), Bluetooth | Cloud-augmented behaviour |
| **Power** | **Lithium-Ion**, ~ **1.5 hours** active, **3 hours** standby | Energy autonomy |

### 2.2.3 Behavioural Architecture — The "Meme" Engine

The ERS-1000 introduced a revolutionary **cloud-augmented behaviour engine** modelled on biological *meme* propagation:

- **Local layer:** Real-time reflexes, obstacle avoidance, owner recognition.
- **Cloud layer:** Periodic uploads of anonymised behaviour logs; download of new "skills" from a global AIBO community.
- **Result:** AIBOs *evolve personality* over time, with no two units behaving identically — a masterstroke in *artificial life* engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The ERS-1000 architecture demonstrates the **Cloud Robotics Paradigm** (Kuffner, 2010), where computationally intensive perception/learning is offloaded to elastic cloud resources, allowing the on-board hardware to remain lightweight. This is a frequent 14-mark question topic.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas govern the core engineering analysis of educational and entertainment robots. **Master these for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) problems.**

### 2.3.1 Kinematic & Workspace Formulas

$$
\text{Degrees of Freedom (DoF)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i
$$

where $f_i$ is the DoF of the $i$-th joint, $n$ is the total number of joints. For a serial chain: $\text{DoF} = 6n - \sum \text{constraints (Kutzbach-Grübler criterion)}$.

$$
\text{Workspace Volume} \approx \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( q_{i,\max} - q_{i,\min} \right) \cdot L_i
$$

For a planar $n$-link manipulator, $L_i$ is the $i$-th link length. For a humanoid, the *reachable workspace* is bounded by the **manipulability ellipsoid**:

$$
w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\left( \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \right)}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the Jacobian matrix and $w(\mathbf{q})$ is the **Yoshikawa manipulability index** — higher is better for dexterity.

### 2.3.2 Dynamics & Control

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

Standard **Euler-Lagrange** formulation where $\mathbf{M}$ is the inertia matrix, $\mathbf{C}$ the Coriolis matrix, $\mathbf{G}$ the gravity vector, and $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ the joint torque vector.

$$
\tau_{motor} \geq \tau_{static} + J \cdot \ddot{q}_{max}
$$

Motor sizing criterion: $\tau_{motor}$ must exceed static holding torque plus worst-case inertial demand.

### 2.3.3 Sensor & Performance Metrics

$$
\text{Frame Rate} = \frac{1}{T_{sample}} \quad [\text{Hz}]
$$

$$
\text{End-to-End Latency} = T_{sensor} + T_{process} + T_{actuate} \quad [\text{ms}]
$$

For natural HRI, the perceptual threshold is $\sim \mathbf{100 \text{ ms}}$ — delays beyond this break social believability.

$$
\text{Battery Endurance} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}} \quad [\text{hours}]
$$

where $E_{battery}$ is in Wh and $P_{avg}$ is average power draw in W.

### 2.3.4 HRI & Engagement Metrics (Case-Study Specific)

$$
\text{Engagement Score} = \alpha \cdot T_{interactive} + \beta \cdot F_{initiation} + \gamma \cdot A_{affect}
$$

Empirical formula used in SAR (Socially Assistive Robotics) literature: $T_{interactive}$ = interactive time, $F_{initiation}$ = child-initiated interactions, $A_{affect}$ = positive affect rating; $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

### 2.3.5 Summary Table — High-Yield Equations

| Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit | Where Applied |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{DoF} = \sum f_i$ | Mechanical flexibility | Dimensionless | Robot design |
| $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ | Dexterity index | Dimensionless | Manipulability analysis |
| $T_{e2e} = T_s + T_p + T_a$ | Real-time responsiveness | ms | HRI believability |
| $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Energy efficiency | \% | Battery sizing |
| $\text{DoF}_{humanoid} = 6n - k$ | Mobility (Kutzbach) | Dimensionless | Walking gait design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Utility:** These equations are the analytical foundation for the **Whole-Body Control (WBC)** algorithms used in NAO's walking engine and AIBO's posture stabilisation. Mastering them lets you solve any KTU problem on robot *capability assessment*.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Workspace Volume of NAO's Arm

**Problem:** NAO's right arm has **5 DoF** (shoulder pitch/yaw, elbow roll/yaw, wrist yaw). Each joint has the following limits (from the NAOqi API documentation):

| Joint | Min (rad) | Max (rad) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ShoulderPitch | $-2.085$ | $2.085$ |
| ShoulderRoll | $-1.396$ | $-0.013$ |
| ElbowYaw | $-2.085$ | $2.085$ |
| ElbowRoll | $-1.546$ | $-0.013$ |
| WristYaw | $-1.823$ | $1.823$ |

**Step 1 — Compute joint range for each DoF:**

$$
\Delta q_{\text{ShoulderPitch}} = 2.085 - (-2.085) = 4.170 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta q_{\text{ShoulderRoll}} = -0.013 - (-1.396) = 1.383 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta q_{\text{ElbowYaw}} = 2.085 - (-2.085) = 4.170 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta q_{\text{ElbowRoll}} = -0.013 - (-1.546) = 1.533 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta q_{\text{WristYaw}} = 1.823 - (-1.823) = 3.646 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total joint-space volume (configuration space):**

$$
V_{C} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} \Delta q_i
$$

$$
V_{C} = 4.170 \times 1.383 \times 4.170 \times 1.533 \times 3.646
$$

$$
V_{C} = 4.170 \times 1.383 = 5.7671
$$

$$
V_{C} = 5.7671 \times 4.170 = 24.0488
$$

$$
V_{C} = 24.0488 \times 1.533 = 36.8668
$$

$$
V_{C} = 36.8668 \times 3.646 = 134.4163 \text{ rad}^5
$$

**Step 3 — Map to Cartesian workspace (approximate as a sphere of effective reach):**

The forward kinematics gives the end-effector position $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^3$. For a rough spherical bound with effective reach $R \approx 0.30$ m:

$$
V_{W} \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3
$$

$$
V_{W} \approx \frac{4}{3} \times 3.1416 \times (0.30)^3
$$

$$
V_{W} \approx 4.1888 \times 0.027
$$

$$
V_{W} \approx 0.1131 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

The configuration-space volume $V_C = 134.42 \text{ rad}^5$ is the *theoretical* dexterity budget. The Cartesian volume $V_W = 0.113 \text{ m}^3$ is the *operationally reachable* volume. The ratio is a measure of *kinematic redundancy utilisation*:

$$
\eta_{red} = \frac{V_C}{V_W} \approx 1189 \text{ rad}^5 / \text{m}^3
$$

A high $\eta_{red}$ means the arm can achieve the same end-effector pose via *multiple joint configurations* — useful for **obstacle avoidance** and **singularity-free manipulation**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: AIBO's Power Budget & Battery Endurance

**Problem:** The AIBO ERS-1000 has a **64.6 Wh** battery. Average power consumption under different operational modes is tabulated below. Compute endurance in each mode.

| Mode | $P_{avg}$ (W) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Standby | $21.5$ |
| Walking | $35.0$ |
| Active Play (vision + cloud) | $48.0$ |
| Charging (input) | $50.0$ (input) |

**Step 1 — Apply the endurance formula:**

$$
t = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute for each mode:**

Standby:

$$
t_{standby} = \frac{64.6}{21.5} = 3.005 \text{ hours}
$$

Walking:

$$
t_{walk} = \frac{64.6}{35.0} = 1.846 \text{ hours}
$$

Active Play:

$$
t_{play} = \frac{64.6}{48.0} = 1.346 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute charging time:**

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{E_{battery} \times 1.2}{P_{charge,in}}
$$

(Assuming 20% charging losses)

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{64.6 \times 1.2}{50.0} = \frac{77.52}{50.0} = 1.550 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

| Activity | Endurance | Comment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Standby | **3.0 h** | Matches Sony spec (~3 h) ✓ |
| Walking | **1.85 h** | Matches Sony spec (~2 h) ✓ |
| Active Play | **1.35 h** | Matches Sony spec (~1.5 h) ✓ |
| Charging | **1.55 h** | Within 0%–80% fast-charge window |

The validation against published specs confirms the energy model. This kind of *back-of-envelope power budgeting* is a hallmark of KTU 14-mark questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Manipulability Index for a 2-Link NAO Arm (Elbow + Wrist)

**Problem:** For a simplified 2-link planar arm (link lengths $L_1, L_2$), derive the manipulability index.

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:**

$$
x = L_1 \cos(q_1) + L_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(q_1) + L_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian:**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$:**

For a square Jacobian, $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = (\det \mathbf{J})^2$.

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2
$$

Derivation:

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = [-L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)] \cdot [L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)] - [-L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)] \cdot [L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2)]
$$

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = -L_1 L_2 \sin q_1 \cos(q_1+q_2) - L_2^2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_1 L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \cos q_1 + L_2^2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \cos(q_1+q_2)
$$

The two $L_2^2$ terms cancel:

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = L_1 L_2 [\sin(q_1+q_2) \cos q_1 - \sin q_1 \cos(q_1+q_2)]
$$

Apply the sine subtraction identity $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$ with $A = q_1+q_2$, $B = q_1$:

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = L_1 L_2 \sin[(q_1+q_2) - q_1] = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2
$$

**Step 4 — Manipulability:**

$$
w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)} = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \vert
$$

**Step 5 — Critical Insight:**

- $w = 0$ when $q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$ — the **singular configuration** where the arm folds in on itself and loses one DoF.
- $w$ is maximum when $q_2 = \pm \pi/2$ — the arm forms a right angle, the most dexterous posture.
- For NAO's real arm ($L_1 \approx 0.105$ m, $L_2 \approx 0.110$ m), max manipulability $w_{max} \approx 0.0116 \text{ m}^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Template:** When asked *"Derive and analyse the manipulability of a 2-link planar manipulator"*, this exact 5-step derivation is the expected answer. Memorise the **singularity condition** $\sin q_2 = 0$.
