# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

### Q1. Differentiate between an Educational Robot and an Entertainment Robot with one example each. List the safety standard applicable to both categories. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

An **Educational Robot** is a service robot designed to facilitate *learning outcomes* — either as a subject of study (e.g., teaching coding) or as a mediator (e.g., autism therapy). Example: **NAO** by SoftBank Robotics.

An **Entertainment Robot** is a service robot designed to *engage, amuse, or emotionally bond* with users, with no explicit learning outcome. Example: **AIBO** by Sony Corporation.

The common safety standard is **ISO 13482:2014** — *Safety requirements for personal care robots*, which both robots must comply with.

*Key differentiator:* Educational robots optimise for *didactic clarity* and *measurable learning gain*; entertainment robots optimise for *believability* and *affective engagement*.

---

### Q2. Define "Embodied Cognition" in the context of service robots and explain its relevance to educational robotics. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Embodied Cognition** is the cognitive science theory that *physical body and sensorimotor experience* are essential to higher learning — not just abstract symbols. In educational robotics, this means a **physical, gestural robot** (like NAO) anchors abstract concepts in bodily experience (e.g., demonstrating a triangle by *walking* its vertices), leading to deeper retention than screen-based equivalents.

**Relevance:**
- Enhances engagement through multimodal (visual + auditory + gestural) feedback.
- Enables *enactive learning* — students learn by *doing* alongside the robot.
- Proven effective in special-needs education, particularly **Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)** therapy, where physical predictability aids social-skill acquisition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandatory under KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **subsystem architecture** of the NAO humanoid robot. List its actuators, sensors, and the role of the **NAOqi operating system** in behaviour arbitration. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m. Compute and analyse its **manipulability index** $w(\mathbf{q})$ as a function of $q_2$. Identify the singular configuration and the configuration of maximum dexterity. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**NAO Subsystem Architecture:**

The NAO robot is organised into **four primary subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical Subsystem** — 25 DoF distributed as:
   - Head: 2 DoF (yaw + pitch)
   - Arms: 2 × 5 DoF (shoulder pitch/roll, elbow yaw/roll, wrist yaw)
   - Pelvis: 1 DoF (hip yaw)
   - Legs: 2 × 6 DoF (hip yaw/roll/pitch, knee pitch, ankle pitch/roll)
2. **Actuation Subsystem** — 25 *Brushless DC servo motors* with harmonic-drive gearboxes, each with a **Hall-effect encoder** for closed-loop position control.
3. **Sensory Subsystem:**
   - *Proprioceptive:* Joint encoders, **3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer** (combined IMU), **4 force-sensitive resistors (FSRs)** per foot.
   - *Exteroceptive:* **2 CMOS cameras** (forehead 1280×960, mouth 640×480), **4 microphones** (with echo cancellation), **2 ultrasonic sonar** rangefinders, **IR** sensors.
4. **Computational Subsystem** — Intel Atom (or ARM) processor running **NAOqi 2.8** on a Linux base.

**Role of NAOqi:**

NAOqi is a **distributed, modular robotic operating system** that:
- Manages **modular behaviours** (ALModules — e.g., `ALMotion`, `ALTextToSpeech`, `ALFaceDetection`).
- Implements a **finite-state machine (FSM) scheduler** with **priority-based arbitration** — behaviours with higher priority can *subsume* lower ones (Brooks-style Subsumption).
- Exposes a **uniform API** across C++, Python, and the **Choregraphe** graphical IDE.
- Handles **inter-process communication** between modules over a **publish-subscribe broker** running on port 9559.

**Block Diagram (described):**

```
[Sensors] → [Perception Modules] → [Behaviour Scheduler (NAOqi)] → [Motion Controller] → [Actuators]
       ↑                                ↓
       └────── [User App / Choregraphe] ┘
```

*(Full block diagram was provided in SECTION_4, Fig 4.1 — students may reproduce it for full marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m.

From the derivation in Section 3.3, the manipulability index for a 2-link planar arm is:

$$
w(\mathbf{q}) = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \vert = \vert 0.4 \times 0.3 \times \sin q_2 \vert = \vert 0.12 \sin q_2 \vert
$$

**Singular Configuration:**

$w = 0$ when $\sin q_2 = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0 \text{ or } q_2 = \pi \text{ (or } 180°\text{)}$

At $q_2 = 0$: the arm is **fully stretched** (singular).
At $q_2 = \pi$: the arm is **fully folded back on itself** (singular).

In both cases, $\det \mathbf{J} = 0$, meaning the Jacobian loses rank and the arm cannot generate velocity in one Cartesian direction — a **kinematic singularity**.

**Maximum Dexterity:**

$w$ is maximum when $\vert \sin q_2 \vert = 1 \Rightarrow q_2 = \pm \pi/2 \text{ (i.e., } \pm 90°\text{)}$

$$
w_{max} = 0.12 \text{ m}^2
$$

At $q_2 = \pm 90°$, the arm forms a **right angle** — the most dexterous posture, where the end-effector can move equally well in all planar directions.

**Numerical Table (for clarity):**

| $q_2$ (deg) | $\sin q_2$ | $w(\mathbf{q})$ (m²) | Interpretation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | Singular — fully extended |
| 30 | 0.500 | 0.060 | Moderate dexterity |
| 60 | 0.866 | 0.104 | High dexterity |
| 90 | 1.000 | **0.120** | **Maximum dexterity** |
| 120 | 0.866 | 0.104 | High dexterity |
| 150 | 0.500 | 0.060 | Moderate dexterity |
| 180 | 0.000 | 0.000 | Singular — fully folded |

**Conclusion:** For optimal manipulation planning, NAO's elbow joint should be commanded away from the singular configurations ($0°$ and $180°$) and ideally operate near $q_2 = \pm 90°$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Describe the **system architecture** of Sony's AIBO ERS-1000 entertainment robot. Explain the concept of **cloud-augmented behaviour** and how it enables *personality evolution*. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** AIBO's battery is rated **64.6 Wh**. If the average power consumption during active play is **48 W** and during standby is **21.5 W**, calculate the **endurance** in each mode. If the robot plays actively for 1 hour and is on standby for 2 hours, what is the **total energy consumed**? How long does it take to recharge the battery fully, assuming a **50 W charger with 20% losses**? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**AIBO ERS-1000 System Architecture:**

The ERS-1000 architecture is **two-tiered**: an on-board tier for real-time reflexes, and a cloud tier for personality learning and skill acquisition.

**On-board Tier:**

- **Sensor Stack:** Stereo cameras in snout, ToF depth sensor, 4-microphone array, capacitive touch sensors (head, back, paws), 6-axis IMU, ToF cliff sensors on belly.
- **Perception Module:** Real-time SLAM (using the depth sensor), face/owner recognition (via on-device CNN), voice activity detection.
- **Behaviour Engine:** A *personality matrix* — a vector of behavioural weights that determines which pre-trained behaviour primitive is selected in response to a stimulus. Each AIBO has a *unique* initial personality vector.
- **Motor Controller:** 22 servo drivers executing joint trajectories at 100 Hz.

**Cloud Tier (Sony AI Cloud Platform):**

- **Behaviour Aggregation Service:** Receives anonymised behaviour logs (stimulus → response → outcome) from the global AIBO fleet.
- **Deep Learning Personality Trainer:** Periodically retrains the personality matrix using federated learning — *no personal data leaves the home; only model gradients are aggregated.*
- **Global Skill Marketplace:** Distributes newly "discovered" skills back to the fleet.
- **Owner Dashboard:** Mobile app for owners to view engagement statistics, set preferences, and push custom behaviours.

**Concept of Cloud-Augmented Behaviour:**

Cloud-augmented behaviour is a paradigm where the robot's *learning* is offloaded to elastic cloud resources, while real-time *reaction* remains on-device. This separation is critical because:
- On-board compute is constrained by weight, power, and thermal limits.
- Behavioural learning requires **large-scale data** (millions of stimulus-response pairs) that no single household can generate.
- The cloud provides a *population-level learning signal* that no individual robot could access alone.

**Personality Evolution:**

The personality vector $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ evolves as:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{t+1} = \mathbf{p}_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{p}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{p}; \mathcal{D}_{cloud})
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate, $\mathcal{L}$ is the engagement-maximisation loss, and $\mathcal{D}_{cloud}$ is the cloud-aggregated dataset. Because the initial $\mathbf{p}_0$ is randomised per unit, and the cloud gradient varies with each unit's interaction history, **no two AIBOs develop identical personalities** — an intentional design choice that enhances believability and user attachment.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Endurance in Each Mode:**

$$
t_{active} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{active}} = \frac{64.6}{48} = 1.346 \text{ h} \approx 80.7 \text{ min}
$$

$$
t_{standby} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{standby}} = \frac{64.6}{21.5} = 3.005 \text{ h} \approx 180.3 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy Consumed in Mixed Use:**

Energy in active mode (1 h):

$$
E_{active} = P_{active} \times t_{active} = 48 \times 1 = 48 \text{ Wh}
$$

Energy in standby mode (2 h):

$$
E_{standby} = P_{standby} \times t_{standby} = 21.5 \times 2 = 43 \text{ Wh}
$$

Total energy:

$$
E_{total} = E_{active} + E_{standby} = 48 + 43 = 91 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — State of Charge (SoC) After Use:**

$$
\Delta \text{SoC} = \frac{E_{total}}{E_{battery}} = \frac{91}{64.6} = 1.409 = 140.9\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The SoC exceeds 100% — physically impossible! This indicates the battery is **depleted before the 3 hours elapse**. The correct interpretation is: AIBO will run out of charge *during* the standby period. Compute the *time-to-empty*:
>
> $t_{empty} = E_{battery} / P_{standby} = 3.005$ h from a full charge.
> After 1 h of active play, energy remaining = $64.6 - 48 = 16.6$ Wh.
> Time remaining in standby = $16.6 / 21.5 = 0.772$ h ≈ 46.3 min.
> So AIBO dies after **1 h 46 min total** — a key engineering insight.
>
> **Many students will mark themselves wrong by blindly adding the energies without checking for SoC > 100%.** Always validate your result against physical constraints!

**Step 4 — Recharge Time:**

Effective charging power (with 20% loss):

$$
P_{eff} = P_{charger} \times (1 - 0.20) = 50 \times 0.80 = 40 \text{ W}
$$

Recharge time:

$$
t_{charge} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{eff}} = \frac{64.6}{40} = 1.615 \text{ h} \approx 1 \text{ h } 37 \text{ min}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Endurance (active play) | **1.35 h** (80.7 min) |
| Endurance (standby) | **3.0 h** (180.3 min) |
| Energy in 1 h active | 48 Wh |
| Energy in 2 h standby | 43 Wh (only 0.77 h sustainable) |
| Time to empty (mixed use) | **1 h 46 min** |
| Recharge time (full) | **1.62 h** (97 min) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Degrees of Freedom with Number of Motors.** NAO has **25 motors, 25 DoF** — but a *human arm* has 7 DoF with 7 motors. DoF is a *kinematic* concept; motors are an *actuation* count. They may differ in under-actuated systems.
> 2. **Skipping the Singular Configuration Analysis in Manipulability Problems.** The 2-mark "singularity" sub-question is mandatory. Skipping it forfeits 2 of 7 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Power Budget Problems.** A 14-mark question that concludes "endurance = 1.35" without "hours" loses **1 mark** for incomplete engineering communication.
> 4. **Not Validating SoC.** As shown in Q.B(b), an SoC > 100% must be flagged as physically infeasible. Blind arithmetic loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Citing "ISO 13482" without specifying the year.** Always write "ISO 13482:2014".
> 6. **Confusing Educational vs Entertainment Robots with Industrial Robots.** KTU penalises factual errors in classification.
> 7. **Omitting the Subsumption / FSM Diagram in Architecture Questions.** A block diagram is worth 2 marks alone. Always include one.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Education & Entertainment Case Studies):**

- [x] A **Service Robot** (IFR definition) excludes industrial automation. Both educational and entertainment robots fall under *Professional Service Robots*.
- [x] The **mandatory safety standard** is **ISO 13482:2014** — *Personal Care Robots*.
- [x] **NAO** (Education) — 25 DoF humanoid, 5.4 kg, 58 cm, NAOqi 2.8 OS, deployed in > 70 countries.
- [x] **AIBO ERS-1000** (Entertainment) — 22 DoF quadruped, cloud-augmented behaviour, federated personality learning.
- [x] **Embodied Cognition** underpins why physical robots outperform screen-based learning tools.
- [x] **Manipulability Index** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)} = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \vert$ for a 2-link planar arm.
- [x] **Singularities** occur at $q_2 = 0, \pi$ — Jacobian rank drops; arm loses 1 DoF.
- [x] **Maximum dexterity** at $q_2 = \pm 90°$ — arm forms a right angle.
- [x] **Battery Endurance** $t = E / P$ — always validate SoC ≤ 100%.
- [x] **End-to-end HRI latency** should be < **100 ms** for natural interaction.
- [x] **Cloud Robotics Paradigm** — separates real-time reflexes (on-board) from learning (cloud), enabling *population-level* personality evolution.
- [x] **Subsumption Architecture** (Brooks, 1986) is the foundational layered model used by both NAO and AIBO.
- [x] **Engagement Score** in SAR = weighted sum of interactive time, child-initiated interactions, and positive affect.
- [x] **Choregraphe** is NAO's visual behaviour-authoring IDE; **ASK NAO** is the autism therapy application.
- [x] **Federated Learning** preserves privacy in AIBO's cloud training — only model gradients, not raw data, are uploaded.
- [x] **Always include a labelled block diagram** in any 7-mark architecture sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Localization, Mapping & SLAM" in Field Robotics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** Module 4 of *PCRUT602 – Field and Service Robotics* introduces the **perception and cognition layer** of autonomous mobile robots. Before a field robot can perform agriculture spraying, mine inspection, or planetary exploration, it must answer three fundamental questions: *"Where am I? What does the world look like? How do I build both representations at once?"* — this is the heart of **Localization, Mapping, and SLAM**.

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Localization** is the computational process of estimating the **pose** (position + orientation) of a mobile robot relative to a given reference frame, using on-board sensor measurements and prior knowledge of the robot's motion.

$$ \text{Localization} : \mathbf{x}_t = f(\mathbf{u}_{1:t}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m}) $$

where:
- $\mathbf{x}_t$ = robot pose at time $t$
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ = sequence of control inputs (odometry) from start to time $t$
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ = sequence of sensor measurements from start to time $t$
- $\mathbf{m}$ = known environment map

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping** is the process of constructing a **spatial representation** (occupancy grid, feature map, or topological graph) of the environment from a sequence of sensor measurements collected by the robot at known poses.

$$ \text{Mapping} : \mathbf{m} = g(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is the *chicken-and-egg* problem where the robot must **simultaneously** estimate both its pose $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$ **and** the map $\mathbf{m}$ of an *unknown* environment, using only its on-board sensors and control inputs.

$$ \text{SLAM} : p(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) $$

This is the famous *posterior* probability over the joint state of poses and map.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 🍕 The Pizza Delivery Robot Analogy

Imagine a **delivery robot** in a city it has never visited before, carrying only a **GPS**, a **lidar sensor**, and a **wheel-encoder odometer**.

| Situation | Robot's Real Problem | Robotics Term |
|---|---|---|
| Robot knows the *map* of the city but doesn't know where on that map it is | *"Where am I on this known map?"* | **Localization** (with known map) |
| Robot knows its *exact position* at every step but has no map | *"What does the world around me look like?"* | **Mapping** (with known poses) |
| Robot knows **neither** the map nor its exact position | *"I must build the map AND figure out my position at the same time"* | **SLAM** |

> [!TIP]
> Think of SLAM as a **blindfolded hiker** trying to draw a map of a forest while walking through it. Every step has uncertainty, every measurement has noise, and yet the map must converge to a globally consistent representation.

### 1.2.1 The Three Canonical Problems of Robotics

In any autonomous navigation system, we encounter three sub-problems, in increasing difficulty:

1. **Position Tracking** — Robot starts with known pose; small, bounded error to correct.
2. **Global Localization** — Robot starts *anywhere*; must find its pose from scratch (the "kidnapped robot problem").
3. **SLAM** — Both pose and map are unknown and must be built together.

> [!NOTE]
> The **kidnapped robot problem** is the hardest localization variant: the robot is teleported to an unknown location and must re-localize without assuming continuity of motion. It is a KTU favourite conceptual question.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary — KTU Must-Know Terms

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Pose** | The complete 6-DOF state of a robot: $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ — 3 translations + 3 rotations |
| **Landmark** | A distinguishable, re-observable feature in the environment (corner, pillar, tree) |
| **Odometry** | Estimation of pose change from **proprioceptive** sensors (wheel encoders, IMU) |
| **Observation** | Measurement of a landmark's position relative to the robot (**exteroceptive** sensor) |
| **Sensor Noise** | Stochastic uncertainty $\epsilon$ corrupting every measurement |
| **Data Association** | Matching observed features to previously stored map landmarks |
| **Loop Closure** | Recognizing that the robot has returned to a previously visited location |
| **Drift** | Cumulative error in odometry leading to systematic deviation from ground truth |
| **Covariance Matrix** | $\Sigma$ — symmetric matrix quantifying uncertainty in a multi-variate estimate |
| **Belief** | The robot's internal probability distribution $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ over its current pose |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Metric:** A field robot's localization accuracy is typically measured in **meters (m)** for position and **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** for orientation. State-of-the-art outdoor SLAM systems achieve **decimeter-level (0.1 m)** accuracy with RTK-GPS fusion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the SLAM Problem

The SLAM posterior can be factored using the **Markov assumption** (the future depends only on the present, not the entire history):

$$ p(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) = p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \cdot p(\mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{z}_{1:t}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> This factorization is the foundation of the **Recursive Bayesian Filter** — the mathematical engine behind every SLAM algorithm (EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM3).

---

## 2.2 The Two Probabilistic Models of SLAM

### 2.2.1 Motion Model (Prediction Step)

The motion model describes how the robot's pose evolves given the control input:

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{(m)} $$

- $g(\cdot)$ = deterministic kinematic function
- $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{(m)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_t)$ = zero-mean Gaussian motion noise with covariance $R_t$

### 2.2.2 Observation Model (Update Step)

The observation model describes how a sensor perceives a landmark:

$$ \mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j) + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{(o)} $$

- $h(\cdot)$ = sensor measurement function
- $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t^{(o)} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_t)$ = zero-mean Gaussian measurement noise with covariance $Q_t$

### 2.2.3 Bayes Filter Recursion

The **Bayes filter** alternates between prediction and update:

$$ \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \cdot bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1} $$

$$ bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \cdot \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) $$

- $\eta$ = normalization constant ensuring the belief integrates to 1
- $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ = predicted belief (before seeing $\mathbf{z}_t$)
- $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ = posterior belief (after incorporating $\mathbf{z}_t$)

---

## 2.3 Why is SLAM Hard? — The "Chicken-and-Egg" Loop

> [!TIP]
> **The Coupling Problem:** To localize, you need a map. To build a map, you need accurate poses. This mutual dependency is what makes SLAM *simultaneously* a localization and mapping problem.

- **Without a good map** → observations are useless for localization.
- **Without good localization** → map features end up in wrong positions.
- **Small pose errors accumulate** → map is globally inconsistent ("banana-shaped" maps).

---

## 2.4 Coordinate Frames and 2D Pose Representation

In a 2D ground robot, the pose is:

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \times S^1 $$

- $(x_t, y_t)$ = position in the **world frame** (meters)
- $\theta_t$ = heading angle in the **world frame** (radians)

The 2D rigid-body transformation that takes a point in the **robot frame** to the **world frame**:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} x^w \\ y^w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & x_t \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & y_t \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x^r \\ y^r \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

This $3 \times 3$ matrix is denoted $T(\mathbf{x}_t) \in SE(2)$ — the **Special Euclidean Group in 2D**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.** All KTU numericals on Module 4 are based on these equations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Robot Pose (2D) | $\mathbf{x}_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t]^T$ | $x, y$ in **m**, $\theta$ in **rad** | Localization |
| 2 | Motion Model | $\mathbf{x}_t = g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) + \epsilon_t$ | $\mathbf{u}_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^T$ (m/s, rad/s) | Prediction step |
| 3 | Observation Model | $\mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j) + \delta_t$ | Range **m**, bearing **rad** | Update step |
| 4 | Range-Bearing Observation | $r_t = \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y_t)^2}$ | $r$ in **m** | Lidar, sonar |
| 5 | Bearing Observation | $\phi_t = \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y_t, \, m_{j,x} - x_t) - \theta_t$ | $\phi$ in **rad** | Camera, lidar |
| 6 | 2D Transformation | $T(\mathbf{x}) \in SE(2)$ | $3 \times 3$ matrix | Frame conversion |
| 7 | Bayes Filter Prediction | $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ | Probability density | SLAM |
| 8 | Bayes Filter Update | $bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ | $\eta$ normalizer | SLAM |
| 9 | Odometry Update (Diff-Drive) | $x_t = x_{t-1} + v \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$ | **m** | Wheel odometry |
| 10 | Odometry Update (Diff-Drive) | $y_t = y_{t-1} + v \Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1}$ | **m** | Wheel odometry |
| 11 | Odometry Update (Diff-Drive) | $\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega \Delta t$ | **rad** | Wheel odometry |
| 12 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{z} - \hat{\mathbf{z}})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{z} - \hat{\mathbf{z}})}$ | Scalar | Data association |
| 13 | Multivariate Gaussian | $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}; \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert\Sigma\vert}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\mu)\right)$ | $\vert\Sigma\vert$ = determinant | Noise modeling |
| 14 | EKF Linearization | $F = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\vert_{\mu_{t-1}}$ | Jacobian | Extended Kalman Filter |
| 15 | EKF Linearization | $H = \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\big\vert_{\mu_t$ | Jacobian | Extended Kalman Filter |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the backbone of:
> - **Autonomous tractors** (John Deere, Mahindra) — use EKF-SLAM for row navigation.
> - **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, MiR) — use particle-filter localization against stored maps.
> - **Mars rovers** (NASA Perseverance, Curiosity) — use Graph-SLAM with stereo vision.
> - **Autonomous cars** (Waymo, Tesla) — use pose-graph optimization with lidar + GPS + IMU fusion.

---

## 2.6 Probabilistic vs. Deterministic Approaches

| Approach | Philosophy | Example Algorithm | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Probabilistic** | Explicitly models uncertainty | EKF-SLAM, FastSLAM, Particle Filter | Robust to noise, principled | Computationally expensive |
| **Deterministic** | Treats robot state as a fixed point | Scan matching, ICP | Fast, simple | Fails under uncertainty |
| **Hybrid** | Optimization on graphs of poses | Cartographer, ORB-SLAM3 | Globally consistent | Needs good front-end |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Odometry-Based Motion Model for Differential-Drive Robot

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a **differential-drive mobile robot** moving on a 2D plane. At time $t-1$, the pose is $\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = [x_{t-1}, y_{t-1}, \theta_{t-1}]^T$. The robot receives a control input $\mathbf{u}_t = [v_t, \omega_t]^T$ (linear and angular velocities) over a time interval $\Delta t$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the deterministic kinematic equation.**

In continuous time, the differential-drive kinematic model is:

$$ \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v \cos\theta \\ v \sin\theta \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order Euler integration over $\Delta t$.**

Treating $v$ and $\omega$ as approximately constant over the small interval $\Delta t$:

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} v_t \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ v_t \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix} \Delta t $$

**Step 3 — Write out the components explicitly.**

$$ x_t = x_{t-1} + v_t \cdot \Delta t \cdot \cos\theta_{t-1} $$

$$ y_t = y_{t-1} + v_t \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sin\theta_{t-1} $$

$$ \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \cdot \Delta t $$

**Step 4 — Add the motion noise term.**

In a real system, $v$ and $\omega$ have errors. We add zero-mean Gaussian noise $\epsilon_v, \epsilon_\omega$:

$$ x_t = x_{t-1} + (v_t + \epsilon_v) \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1} $$

$$ y_t = y_{t-1} + (v_t + \epsilon_v) \Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1} $$

$$ \theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + (\omega_t + \epsilon_\omega) \Delta t $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This is the canonical motion model asked in ESE questions. You may be given $v_t$, $\omega_t$, $\Delta t$, and $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$, and asked to predict $\mathbf{x}_t$.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = [2.0 \text{ m}, 3.0 \text{ m}, 0.785 \text{ rad}]^T$ (i.e., 45° heading)
- $v_t = 0.5$ m/s, $\omega_t = 0.1$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 1.0$ s
- Assume no noise.

**Step 1 — Compute $x_t$:**

$$ x_t = 2.0 + 0.5 \times 1.0 \times \cos(0.785) $$

$$ x_t = 2.0 + 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 2.0 + 0.3536 = 2.3536 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $y_t$:**

$$ y_t = 3.0 + 0.5 \times 1.0 \times \sin(0.785) $$

$$ y_t = 3.0 + 0.5 \times 0.7071 = 3.0 + 0.3536 = 3.3536 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\theta_t$:**

$$ \theta_t = 0.785 + 0.1 \times 1.0 = 0.885 \text{ rad} $$

**Step 4 — Normalize the angle to $[-\pi, \pi]$:**

$$ \theta_t = 0.885 \text{ rad} \approx 50.7° \quad \text{(no wrap needed)} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = [2.3536 \text{ m}, \; 3.3536 \text{ m}, \; 0.885 \text{ rad}]^T $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Range-Bearing Observation Model

### 3.2.1 Setup

The robot at $\mathbf{x}_t = [x_t, y_t, \theta_t]^T$ observes a landmark at known position $\mathbf{m}_j = [m_{j,x}, m_{j,y}]^T$ using a 2D lidar.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the relative position in the world frame.**

$$ \Delta x = m_{j,x} - x_t, \quad \Delta y = m_{j,y} - y_t $$

**Step 2 — Compute the Euclidean distance (range).**

$$ r_t = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the bearing (angle in the robot's frame).**

$$ \phi_t = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta_t $$

**Step 4 — Construct the full observation vector with noise.**

$$ \mathbf{z}_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_r \\ \epsilon_\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{where} \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, Q_t) $$

**Step 5 — Express as a vector function $h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j)$:**

$$ h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y_t)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y_t, \, m_{j,x} - x_t) - \theta_t \end{bmatrix} $$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Robot pose: $\mathbf{x}_t = [0, 0, 0]^T$ (at origin, facing +X)
- Landmark: $\mathbf{m}_j = [3, 4]^T$

**Step 1 — Range:**

$$ r_t = \sqrt{(3 - 0)^2 + (4 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Bearing:**

$$ \phi_t = \text{atan2}(4, 3) - 0 = 0.9273 \text{ rad} \approx 53.13° $$

**Step 3 — Add sensor noise** (say, $\sigma_r = 0.1$ m, $\sigma_\phi = 0.05$ rad):

$$ \mathbf{z}_t = [5.0, \; 0.9273]^T + \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \, \text{diag}(0.01, 0.0025)) $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Concept-to-Equation Mapping

| KTU Concept | Mathematical Anchor | Real Robot Action | Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robot pose state | $\mathbf{x}_t \in SE(2)$ | Read encoder + IMU | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Motion prediction | $\mathbf{x}_t = g(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ | Dead-reckoning step | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Range measurement | $r_t = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Lidar returns distance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Bearing measurement | $\phi_t = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta_t$ | Camera/lidar angle | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Belief update | $bel \propto p(\mathbf{z} \mid \mathbf{x}) \cdot \overline{bel}$ | Bayesian fusion | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Loop closure | $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{current}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{visited}} \Vert < \delta$ | Recognize revisit | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Uncertainty growth | $\Sigma_t = F \Sigma_{t-1} F^T + R_t$ | Covariance propagation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — SLAM Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Environment m] --> B[Exteroceptive Sensors Lidar Camera GPS]
    C[Robot Actuators Motors] --> D[Proprioceptive Sensors Wheel Encoders IMU]
    B --> E[Sensor Pre-processing]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Prediction Step Motion Model g xt ut]
    E --> G[Update Step Observation Model h xt mj]
    F --> H[Bayesian Filter Core bel xt]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Pose Estimate x hat t]
    H --> J[Map Estimate m hat]
    I --> K[Path Planner Field Robot]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Actuator Commands u t]
    L --> C
    H --> M[Loop Closure Detection]
    M --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show data flow. The Bayesian filter is the *heart* of SLAM — it fuses motion predictions (from encoders/IMU) with sensor observations (from lidar/camera) to produce joint estimates of pose and map. The loop closure block feeds back into the filter to correct accumulated drift.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Bayes Filter Recursion (Predict ↔ Update)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Previous Belief bel xt-1] --> B[Prediction Motion Model p xt xt-1 ut]
    B --> C[Predicted Belief bar bel xt]
    C --> D[Multiply by Observation Likelihood p zt xt m]
    D --> E[Normalization eta]
    E --> F[Posterior Belief bel xt]
    F --> G[Output Pose Estimate x hat t]
    F --> A
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — SLAM Problem Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Navigation Problem] --> B{Is the map known?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Localization]
    C --> D{Starting position known?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Position Tracking]
    D -- No --> F[Global Localization]
    D -- Teleported --> G[Kidnapped Robot Problem]
    B -- No --> H{Is the pose known?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Mapping Only]
    H -- No --> J[SLAM]
    J --> K[EKF-SLAM]
    J --> L[FastSLAM]
    J --> M[Graph SLAM]
    J --> N[Particle Filter SLAM]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Sensor Taxonomy for Field Robotics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Sensors] --> B[Proprioceptive]
    A --> C[Exteroceptive]
    B --> B1[Wheel Encoders]
    B --> B2[IMU]
    B --> B3[Joint Encoders]
    C --> C1[2D 3D Lidar]
    C --> C2[Stereo Monocular Camera]
    C --> C3[Ultrasonic Sonar]
    C --> C4[RTK-GPS]
    C --> C5[UWB Beacons]
    B1 --> D[Used in Motion Model]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> E[Used in Observation Model]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> F[Used for Global Correction]
    C5 --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a typical field robot, the **proprioceptive sensors** (encoders, IMU) feed the **prediction step**, while **exteroceptive sensors** (lidar, camera) feed the **update step**. **GPS/UWB** are *absolute* sensors and can be used as global corrections whenever available.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the SLAM problem. Why is it considered "simultaneous" in nature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** is defined as the computational problem of constructing a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating the robot's pose within that evolving map, using only the robot's on-board sensors and control inputs.
>
> Mathematically:
> $$ p(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t}) $$
>
> It is called *simultaneous* because **neither the pose $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$ nor the map $\mathbf{m}$ is known a priori** — the two estimates are *mutually dependent*: a good map requires accurate pose estimates, and accurate pose estimates require a good map. This creates a chicken-and-egg problem that must be solved jointly, hence "simultaneous".
>
> **(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the joint posterior, 1 mark for explaining the chicken-and-egg coupling.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between position tracking, global localization, and the kidnapped robot problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> | Problem | Initial Knowledge | Difficulty | Use Case |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Position Tracking** | Robot's starting pose is known with small uncertainty | Easiest — bounded error correction | Wheel-odometry drift correction |
> | **Global Localization** | Robot's starting pose is completely unknown | Harder — must converge from arbitrary initial belief | Recovery after system reboot |
> | **Kidnapped Robot** | Robot is suddenly teleported to an unknown location *while running* | Hardest — must detect discontinuity in pose | Fault detection, robustness testing |
>
> The kidnapped problem is the most demanding because the robot's belief can become **multi-modal** (multiple plausible poses) and it must detect the *discontinuity* in motion, not just refine a single hypothesis.
>
> **(3 marks)** — full marks for a clean three-row table with examples.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Module 4 Definitions:**
> - Students often **omit the probabilistic formulation** ($p(\cdot \mid \cdot)$) of SLAM. Always write the posterior explicitly — it carries 2 marks.
> - Students frequently **confuse localization and SLAM**. If the map is given, it is **localization**, not SLAM.
> - In motion-model derivations, students forget to **add the noise term** $\epsilon_t$, losing 1 mark.
> - Units are mandatory: state **meters (m)** for $x, y, r$ and **radians (rad)** for $\theta, \phi, \omega$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the **pose** of a mobile robot in 2D. Derive the **dead-reckoning motion model** for a differential-drive robot using Euler integration, clearly listing all variables and units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot is at pose $\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = [1.0 \text{ m}, 2.0 \text{ m}, \pi/4 \text{ rad}]^T$. It receives control input $v_t = 0.8$ m/s and $\omega_t = 0.2$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 2.0$ s. Assuming noise-free motion, compute the new pose $\mathbf{x}_t$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Definition of pose (2 marks):**

The **pose** of a 2D mobile robot is the triplet:

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix} $$

- $x_t$ = X-coordinate in the world frame (**m**)
- $y_t$ = Y-coordinate in the world frame (**m**)
- $\theta_t$ = heading angle measured counter-clockwise from world +X axis (**rad**)

**Step 2 — Continuous-time kinematics (2 marks):**

For a differential-drive robot with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$:

$$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}(t) \\ \dot{y}(t) \\ \dot{\theta}(t) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v(t) \cos\theta(t) \\ v(t) \sin\theta(t) \\ \omega(t) \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 3 — Euler integration (2 marks):**

Assuming $v$ and $\omega$ are constant over the interval $\Delta t$:

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \begin{bmatrix} v_t \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ v_t \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \omega_t \end{bmatrix} \Delta t $$

**Step 4 — With noise (1 mark):**

$$ \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_{t-1} + (v_t + \epsilon_v) \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1} \\ y_{t-1} + (v_t + \epsilon_v) \Delta t \sin\theta_{t-1} \\ \theta_{t-1} + (\omega_t + \epsilon_\omega) \Delta t \end{bmatrix} $$

where $\epsilon_v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_v^2)$ and $\epsilon_\omega \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\omega^2)$.

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = [1.0, 2.0, \pi/4]^T = [1.0, 2.0, 0.7854]^T$
- $v_t = 0.8$ m/s, $\omega_t = 0.2$ rad/s, $\Delta t = 2.0$ s

**Step 1 — Compute $x_t$ (2 marks):**

$$ x_t = 1.0 + 0.8 \times 2.0 \times \cos(0.7854) $$

$$ x_t = 1.0 + 1.6 \times 0.7071 = 1.0 + 1.1314 = 2.1314 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $y_t$ (2 marks):**

$$ y_t = 2.0 + 0.8 \times 2.0 \times \sin(0.7854) $$

$$ y_t = 2.0 + 1.6 \times 0.7071 = 2.0 + 1.1314 = 3.1314 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $\theta_t$ (2 marks):**

$$ \theta_t = 0.7854 + 0.2 \times 2.0 = 0.7854 + 0.4 = 1.1854 \text{ rad} $$

**Step 4 — Normalize the angle to $[-\pi, \pi]$ (1 mark):**

$$ 1.1854 \text{ rad} \approx 67.9° \quad \text{(within range, no wrap needed)} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ \boxed{\mathbf{x}_t = [2.1314 \text{ m}, \; 3.1314 \text{ m}, \; 1.1854 \text{ rad}]^T} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the **observation model** $h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j)$ for a 2D range-bearing sensor. A robot at $\mathbf{x}_t = [2, 3, \pi/6]^T$ observes a landmark at $\mathbf{m}_j = [7, 11]^T$. Compute the expected measurement $\mathbf{z}_t$ (range and bearing, in **m** and **rad**). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write down the **Bayes filter** equations for the SLAM posterior, clearly defining the prediction and update steps. Explain, in your own words, why the observation likelihood $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m})$ acts as a *correction* term on the predicted belief. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Definition of observation model (2 marks):**

The **range-bearing observation model** for a 2D sensor is:

$$ \mathbf{z}_t = h(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}_j) + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_r \\ \epsilon_\phi \end{bmatrix} $$

$$ r_t = \sqrt{(m_{j,x} - x_t)^2 + (m_{j,y} - y_t)^2} $$

$$ \phi_t = \text{atan2}(m_{j,y} - y_t, \, m_{j,x} - x_t) - \theta_t $$

**Step 2 — Compute range (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta x = 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta y = 11 - 3 = 8 \text{ m} $$

$$ r_t = \sqrt{5^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{25 + 64} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.434 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute bearing (2 marks):**

$$ \phi_t = \text{atan2}(8, 5) - \pi/6 $$

$$ \text{atan2}(8, 5) = 1.0122 \text{ rad} \approx 58.0° $$

$$ \phi_t = 1.0122 - 0.5236 = 0.4886 \text{ rad} \approx 28.0° $$

**Step 4 — Final measurement vector (1 mark):**

$$ \boxed{\mathbf{z}_t \approx \begin{bmatrix} 9.434 \text{ m} \\ 0.4886 \text{ rad} \end{bmatrix}} $$

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Bayes filter equations (4 marks):**

**Prediction step:**

$$ \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \cdot bel(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1} $$

**Update step:**

$$ bel(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \cdot \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) $$

where:
- $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ = **motion model** (Chapman–Kolmogorov prediction)
- $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m})$ = **observation likelihood**
- $\eta$ = normalization constant = $\left[ \int p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m}) \overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) d\mathbf{x}_t \right]^{-1}$
- $bel(\mathbf{x}_t)$ = posterior belief over pose

**Step 2 — Why the observation likelihood is a correction (3 marks):**

> [!TIP]
> The **prediction** $\overline{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is computed *before* the new sensor reading $\mathbf{z}_t$ arrives — it only uses the **motion model** $p(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ and the prior belief. As time progresses, the prediction alone **spreads out** the belief (uncertainty grows) because of accumulated motion noise.
>
> When the sensor returns $\mathbf{z}_t$, the term $p(\mathbf{z}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{m})$ is **large** for pose hypotheses $\mathbf{x}_t$ that are *consistent* with the measurement, and **small** for inconsistent ones. Multiplying by this likelihood **sharpens** the belief back, reducing uncertainty.
>
> In essence: the prediction *extrapolates* the prior belief forward in time, while the observation likelihood *re-anchors* it to the current sensor reality. This is the Bayesian principle of **prior × likelihood = posterior**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 4 Definitions**

- ✅ **Localization** = estimate pose $\mathbf{x}_t$ given known map $\mathbf{m}$.
- ✅ **Mapping** = estimate map $\mathbf{m}$ given known poses $\mathbf{x}_{1:t}$.
- ✅ **SLAM** = estimate BOTH jointly — chicken-and-egg problem.
- ✅ **SLAM posterior** = $p(\mathbf{x}_{1:t}, \mathbf{m} \mid \mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})$.
- ✅ **Three sub-problems** = position tracking < global localization < kidnapped robot.
- ✅ **2D pose** = $(x, y, \theta)$ in **m, m, rad**.
- ✅ **Motion model** (differential-drive, Euler) = $x_t = x_{t-1} + v \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$ and similar.
- ✅ **Range observation** = $r = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ in **m**.
- ✅ **Bearing observation** = $\phi = \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) - \theta$ in **rad**.
- ✅ **Bayes filter** = predict (motion) → update (observation likelihood × normalize).
- ✅ **Sensor types** = proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) + exteroceptive (lidar, camera, GPS).
- ✅ **Key challenge** = uncertainty propagation; mitigated by loop closure.
- ✅ **Loop closure** = detecting a revisit to correct accumulated drift.
- ✅ **Always include units** — m for distances, rad for angles, s for time.
- ✅ **Always add noise term** $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ in motion and observation models.
- ✅ **Transformations** = $T(\mathbf{x}) \in SE(2)$ for 2D rigid body motion.

---

> [!TIP]
> **End of Module 4 — Definition Notes.** For the next topics (EKF-SLAM, Particle Filter SLAM, Graph SLAM), this foundational vocabulary and the Bayes filter equations will be reused extensively. Master these definitions first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping and Navigation for Service Robots: Map Building and Localization - Localization with Wi-Fi and Other Networked Radio Communications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping and Navigation for Service Robots: Map Building and Localization - Localization with Wi-Fi and Other Networked Radio Communications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network-based radio localization** is the process of estimating the spatial coordinates (pose) of a service robot within a known or partially known environment by exploiting the electromagnetic signal characteristics exchanged between the robot and a fixed or mobile network of wireless infrastructure nodes (such as Wi-Fi access points, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) beacons, Ultra-Wideband (UWB) anchors, RFID readers, or ZigBee coordinators). The robot uses measurable signal parameters — **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)**, **Time of Arrival (ToA)**, **Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)**, **Angle of Arrival (AoA)**, or **Channel State Information (CSI)** — to compute its absolute or relative position without relying exclusively on wheel odometry or laser scan-matching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 / Module 4):** The course requires students to articulate the differences between *map building* (SLAM — Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and *network-based localization*, identify the radio-frequency (RF) modalities suitable for indoor service robotics, and justify the selection of a particular RF technique based on accuracy, latency, cost, and infrastructure density.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Robot Finds Itself Using Wi-Fi"

Imagine you are dropped blindfolded inside a large shopping mall, and you are given a smartphone that can hear Wi-Fi routers at known fixed locations. You did **not** build a mental map; the mall has Wi-Fi routers installed at known coordinates. By measuring how *loud* the signal is from each router, you can roughly guess how far you are from each one. With three or more such routers, you can pinpoint your location through geometric intersection — the same way a bat uses echoes or a sailor uses three lighthouses.

A service robot inside a hospital, warehouse, or hotel does exactly this. It "listens" to multiple Wi-Fi access points (APs), reads the **RSSI** from each, and converts signal strength into a distance estimate. Combining distance estimates from several APs using **trilateration** gives the robot an approximate position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction:** Wheel-odometry errors accumulate without bound (drift), but Wi-Fi localization provides an *absolute* global reference that bounds this drift — analogous to how a sailor uses stars to recalibrate a faulty compass.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8\ m/s}$ | Upper bound on RF propagation |
| Wi-Fi frequency (2.4 GHz) | $f$ | $\mathbf{2.4\ GHz}$ | Wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.125$ m |
| Wi-Fi frequency (5 GHz) | $f$ | $\mathbf{5\ GHz}$ | Wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.06$ m |
| Path-loss exponent (free space) | $n$ | $\mathbf{2.0}$ | Used in log-distance model |
| Path-loss exponent (indoor) | $n$ | $\mathbf{1.6 - 3.0}$ | Higher values = more obstruction |
| Reference distance | $d_0$ | $\mathbf{1\ m}$ | Calibration distance for RSSI |
| UWB bandwidth | $BW$ | $\mathbf{500 - 1000\ MHz}$ | Yields cm-level ranging accuracy |
| BLE transmit power | $P_{tx}$ | $\mathbf{-12\ to\ +4\ dBm}$ | Limits effective range to 5–50 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why networked radio and not only cameras/LiDAR?** Cameras and LiDAR fail in low-light, smoky, repetitive (e.g., long corridor), or featureless environments. Radio signals **penetrate walls, smoke, and darkness**, making them indispensable for 24×7 service robotics in hospitals, airports, and industrial sites.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localization Pipeline — Five Logical Phases

A networked radio localization system is decomposed into the following sequential stages:

1. **Signal Acquisition Phase:** The robot's radio transceiver (Wi-Fi/BLE/UWB) measures one or more signal observables $\mathbf{z}_k = [z_1, z_2, \dots, z_n]$ from $n$ fixed anchors at time $k$.
2. **Feature Extraction Phase:** Raw RSSI samples are filtered (Kalman, moving average, or median filter) to suppress multipath-induced outliers.
3. **Range/Angle Conversion Phase:** Each filtered observable is mapped to either a distance $d_i$, a bearing $\theta_i$, or a time-difference $\Delta t_i$ using a propagation model.
4. **Position Estimation Phase:** A geometric or probabilistic algorithm fuses all converted measurements to produce a pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{p}}_k = (\hat{x}, \hat{y}, \hat{\theta})$.
5. **Uncertainty Quantification Phase:** A covariance matrix $\mathbf{P}_k$ or particle cloud represents confidence in $\hat{\mathbf{p}}_k$.

## 2.2 The Log-Distance Path Loss (RSSI) Model — The "Why" and "How"

**Why it matters:** RSSI is the cheapest observable because every commercial Wi-Fi chipset reports it natively (no time-synchronization hardware required). The relationship between received power $P_r$ and distance $d$ is modelled empirically.

**How it works:** The classical log-distance path loss equation expresses the received power in dBm as:

$$P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$$

where $P_r(d_0)$ is the received power at the reference distance $d_0 = 1$ m, $n$ is the path-loss exponent, and $X_{\sigma} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable modelling shadow-fading noise.

## 2.3 Conversion from RSSI to Distance

Rearranging the path loss equation to solve for $d$ yields:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,\frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10\,n}}$$

This is the **fundamental ranging equation** for RSSI-based Wi-Fi localization and is guaranteed to appear in KTU derivations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Log-distance path loss | $P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$ | $P_r$ in dBm, $d$ in m, $n$ dimensionless | RSSI fingerprinting & ranging |
| 2 | RSSI-to-distance | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(P_r(d_0) - P_r)/(10\,n)}$ | $d$ in m | Trilateration input |
| 3 | Free-space Friis path loss | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)$ | $P_r, P_t$ in dBm, $\lambda$ in m | Theoretical baseline |
| 4 | Trilateration circle | $(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2$ | $x, y$ robot position; $(x_i, y_i)$ AP coordinate | 2-D positioning |
| 5 | Linearized trilateration | $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ with $\mathbf{x} = [x, y]^T$ | $\mathbf{A}$ is $2 \times 2$ Jacobian | Closed-form solution |
| 6 | Time of Flight (ToF) | $d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\Delta t$ round-trip | UWB ranging |
| 7 | Time Difference of Arrival | $\Delta d_{ij} = c \cdot (t_i - t_j)$ | $\Delta d$ in m | TDoA hyperbolic fix |
| 8 | AoA geometry | $\theta_i = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}\right)$ | $\theta$ in rad | Bearing-based fix |
| 9 | Bayes filter (posterior) | $\text{bel}(x_t) = \eta \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})\, \text{bel}(x_{t-1})\, dx_{t-1}$ | $x_t$ pose, $z_t$ measurement, $u_t$ control | Probabilistic localization |
| 10 | Particle filter weight | $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | $w$ weight, $i$ particle index | Monte-Carlo localization |
| 11 | RSSI Gaussian likelihood | $p(z \mid x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z - \mu(x))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $\mu(x)$ expected RSSI at $x$ | Fingerprint matching |
| 12 | Cramér–Rao lower bound (ToA) | $\sigma_d^2 \geq \frac{c^2}}{8\pi^2 \cdot \text{SNR} \cdot \beta^2}$ | $\beta$ effective bandwidth | Theoretical accuracy limit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** RSSI-to-distance conversion (Equation 2) is at the heart of every indoor positioning SDK (e.g., Google Indoor Maps, Apple Indoor Maps, Cisco DNA Spaces). For hospital service robots delivering medicines in multi-floor buildings, RSSI-based Wi-Fi localization provides **3–5 m accuracy** with zero additional infrastructure beyond the existing enterprise WLAN.

## 2.5 Comparison of Networked Radio Modalities for Service Robots

| Modality | Observable | Typical Accuracy | Range | Cost | Power | KTU-Relevant Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (RSSI)** | Signal strength | 3–5 m | 30–50 m | Low (reuses existing APs) | Medium | Hospital, mall, airport service robots |
| **Wi-Fi (CSI / FTM)** | Channel state / round-trip time | 1–2 m | 30 m | Medium (newer chipsets) | Medium | Warehouse AGVs |
| **BLE Beacons** | RSSI | 2–4 m | 10–30 m | Very low | Very low | Retail, museums, hotels |
| **UWB** | ToF / TDoA | 5–30 cm | 10–100 m | High | Low | Precision docking, multi-robot fleets |
| **RFID** | Proximity / RSSI | 0.5–2 m (with antenna arrays) | 1–5 m | Very low | Passive tags possible | Inventory, asset tracking |
| **ZigBee / Thread** | RSSI | 3–5 m | 10–30 m | Low | Very low | Smart home service robots |
| **5G NR positioning** | RTT, AoA, multi-RTT | Sub-meter (cm-level with carrier-phase) | Wide area | High | High (for outdoor) | Outdoor autonomous delivery |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Path-Loss to Distance

**Starting premise:** The received power at a reference distance $d_0$ is $P_r(d_0)$ in dBm. We wish to find the distance $d$ given a current measurement $P_r(d)$.

**Step 1 — Write the log-distance model:**

$$P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Neglect the shadow-fading noise term (deterministic baseline) by taking the expectation $\mathbb{E}[X_\sigma] = 0$:**

$$P_r(d) = P_r(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Move the path-loss term to the left-hand side:**

$$P_r(d) - P_r(d_0) = -10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $-10n$ to isolate the logarithm:**

$$\frac{P_r(d_0) - P_r(d)}{10\,n} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the exponential base-10 to both sides:**

$$\frac{d}{d_0} = 10^{\,(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/(10\,n)}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by $d_0$ to obtain the closed-form range:**

$$\boxed{\,d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/(10\,n)}\,}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The exponent term $(P_r(d_0) - P_r(d))/(10n)$ shows that if the path-loss exponent is large (e.g., $n = 3$ in a cluttered indoor environment), the same RSSI drop corresponds to a *shorter* physical distance than in free space. This is why the same RSSI value can map to vastly different distances across rooms, motivating the use of **fingerprinting** instead of pure trilateration in heterogeneous indoor environments.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form 2-D Trilateration from Three Wi-Fi APs

**Starting premise:** Three APs at known positions $A_1 = (x_1, y_1)$, $A_2 = (x_2, y_2)$, $A_3 = (x_3, y_3)$ provide range estimates $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to the unknown robot position $(x, y)$.

**Step 1 — Write the three circle equations:**

$$(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 = d_1^2$$
$$(x - x_2)^2 + (y - y_2)^2 = d_2^2$$
$$(x - x_3)^2 + (y - y_3)^2 = d_3^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the first two squares and subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2 to eliminate $x^2 + y^2$:**

$$x^2 - 2x x_2 + x_2^2 + y^2 - 2y y_2 + y_2^2 - \bigl(x^2 - 2x x_1 + x_1^2 + y^2 - 2y y_1 + y_1^2\bigr) = d_2^2 - d_1^2$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $x^2$ and $y^2$; collect linear terms:**

$$-2x x_2 + 2x x_1 - 2y y_2 + 2y y_1 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 = d_2^2 - d_1^2$$

**Step 4 — Group into standard linear form $a_1 x + b_1 y = c_1$:**

$$2(x_1 - x_2)\, x + 2(y_1 - y_2)\, y = d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for Equation 3 minus Equation 1 to get a second linear equation:**

$$2(x_1 - x_3)\, x + 2(y_1 - y_3)\, y = d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the matrix form $\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - x_2) & 2(y_1 - y_2) \\ 2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(y_1 - y_3) \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} &=
\begin{bmatrix} d_2^2 - d_1^2 - x_2^2 + x_1^2 - y_2^2 + y_1^2 \\ d_3^2 - d_1^2 - x_3^2 + x_1^2 - y_3^2 + y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Invert the $2 \times 2$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$ to obtain the closed-form position:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

with $\mathbf{A}^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det(\mathbf{A})}
\begin{bmatrix} 2(y_1 - y_3) & -2(y_1 - y_2) \\ -2(x_1 - x_3) & 2(x_1 - x_2) \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 8 — Determinant condition (geometric sanity check):** A unique solution exists only if $\det(\mathbf{A}) \neq 0$, i.e., the three APs are **non-collinear** (they span a 2-D triangle of positive area). This is a *classic KTU board question* — "State the condition for a unique trilateration solution."

**Engineering interpretation:** Although the math is closed-form, in practice the RSSI-derived distances $d_i$ are noisy, so the *intersection point* of the three circles is not exact. Real systems therefore use **least-squares** minimization:

$$\min_{x,y} \sum_{i=1}^{3} \left(\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2} - d_i\right)^2$$

which is solved iteratively via Gauss–Newton or Levenberg–Marquardt.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayesian Posterior for RSSI Fingerprint Localization

**Starting premise:** The environment is divided into a grid of cells $x_i$, each with a stored RSSI *fingerprint* $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = [\mu_{i,1}, \mu_{i,2}, \dots, \mu_{i,n}]$ (mean RSSI from $n$ APs). The robot observes a new RSSI vector $\mathbf{z} = [z_1, \dots, z_n]$.

**Step 1 — Likelihood (assuming Gaussian per-AP RSSI):**

$$p(z_j \mid x_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_j^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_j - \mu_{i,j})^2}{2\sigma_j^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Joint likelihood (conditional independence across APs):**

$$p(\mathbf{z} \mid x_i) = \prod_{j=1}^{n} p(z_j \mid x_i)$$

**Step 3 — Bayes' theorem for the posterior probability of being at $x_i$:**

$$p(x_i \mid \mathbf{z}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z} \mid x_i)\, p(x_i)}{p(\mathbf{z})}$$

where $p(x_i)$ is the prior (often uniform across grid cells in absence of odometry) and $p(\mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k} p(\mathbf{z} \mid x_k)\, p(x_k)$ is a normalizing constant.

**Step 4 — Final position estimate (maximum a posteriori, MAP):**

$$\hat{x}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{x_i}\, p(x_i \mid \mathbf{z})$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the **probabilistic backbone of Wi-Fi fingerprinting systems** such as those deployed at Changi Airport, Samsung Hospital Seoul, and many large warehouses. The grid resolution (typically 1 m × 1 m) directly trades memory for accuracy.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End Wi-Fi Localization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Anchor AP 1 at x1 y1] -->|RSSI r1| B1[Radio Receiver on Robot]
    A2[Anchor AP 2 at x2 y2] -->|RSSI r2| B1
    A3[Anchor AP 3 at x3 y3] -->|RSSI r3| B1
    A4[BLE Beacon] -->|RSSI rb| B1
    A5[UWB Anchor] -->|ToF t uwb| B1

    B1 --> C1[Median Filter to Remove Outliers]
    C1 --> C2[Convert RSSI to Distance via Log Distance Model]
    C2 --> C3{Trilateration or Fingerprint}
    C3 -->|Analytic| D1[Linear Solver A x equals b]
    C3 -->|Probabilistic| D2[Bayesian MAP over Grid]
    D1 --> E1[Position Estimate x hat y hat]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Fuse with Wheel Odometry in EKF]
    F1 --> G1[Final Pose Published to Navigation Stack]
    G1 --> H1[Service Robot Executes Delivery or Patrol Task]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Probabilistic Markov Localization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State at t-1: belief over grid] -->|Motion Model u t| S2[Predicted Belief at t]
    S2 -->|Measurement z t| S3[Update with Likelihood p of z t given x]
    S3 --> S4[Posterior Belief at t]
    S4 --> S1
    S4 --> O1[Output: x hat equals argmax belief]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Fingerprint Database Construction (Offline Phase) vs Online Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O1[Offline Survey Phase] --> O2[Robot Walks Grid with Ground Truth Position]
    O2 --> O3[Collect RSSI Samples r1 r2 r3 from APs at Each Cell]
    O3 --> O4[Compute Mean and Std per Cell]
    O4 --> O5[Store Fingerprint Database mu i sigma i]

    L1[Online Localization Phase] --> L2[Robot Measures Live RSSI Vector z]
    L2 --> L3[Compute Likelihood p of z given mu i sigma i for All Cells]
    L3 --> L4[Select Cell with Maximum Likelihood]
    L4 --> L5[Publish Estimated Position]
```
