## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Beam Model and the Likelihood Field Model for laser range finders. List two advantages of the latter.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Beam Model** | **Likelihood Field Model** |
|---|---|
| Computes per-beam likelihood by ray-casting from the robot pose through the map. | Uses a precomputed Euclidean distance transform of the map. |
| Produces a spiky, non-smooth likelihood function. | Produces a smooth, Gaussian-shaped likelihood. |
| Computational cost is $O(K \cdot N_{ray})$ — slow. | Per-beam cost is $O(1)$ — fast. |
| Handles short readings (unexpected objects) via $p_{short}$. | Treats unexpected objects as random noise. |

**Two advantages of likelihood field:**
1. **Speed:** Two orders of magnitude faster at runtime due to lookup table.
2. **Smoothness:** Continuous, differentiable likelihood surface → better convergence for EKF, gradient-based optimization, and scan matching (ICP, CSM).

*(3 marks: 1 for the comparison, 2 for the advantages)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the principle of Time-of-Flight (ToF) used in a 2D laser range finder. State the formula and the physical constant involved.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A laser range finder emits a short, focused pulse of infrared light (typically $\lambda = 905$ nm) from a rotating mirror. The pulse travels to a target, reflects off the surface, and a portion of the reflected energy returns to a sensitive photodetector (an Avalanche Photodiode, APD). The electronics measure the **round-trip time** $t_f$ between emission and detection.

**Formula:**

$$r = \frac{c \cdot t_f}{2}$$

where $r$ is the range to the obstacle, $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s is the **speed of light in vacuum**, and the factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip path.

**Key constant:** For 30 mm range accuracy, the timing must resolve $t_f$ to $\Delta t \approx 200$ picoseconds — requiring TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) chips.

*(3 marks: 1 for the principle, 1 for the formula, 1 for the constant/accuracy discussion)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) Derive the per-beam likelihood $p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m)$ for the Beam Model of a laser range finder, clearly stating the four mixture components and their physical justifications.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the working of Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) for a 2D LIDAR-equipped mobile robot. Show the importance weight update equation and discuss the problem of particle depletion and how the Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization (AMCL) algorithm addresses it.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

The Beam Model assumes that each beam's measurement $z_t^k$ is generated by one of four mutually exclusive physical processes, with prior probabilities $z_{hit}$, $z_{short}$, $z_{max}$, $z_{rand}$ (such that they sum to 1).

**Step 1 — The expected range $z_t^{k*}$ via ray-casting:** From pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$, cast a ray through sensor angle $\theta_{k,sensor}$ into the map $m$ and find the first occupied cell.

**Step 2 — The Hit component:** When the beam correctly detects a known obstacle, the range is normally distributed around the true range:

$$p_{hit}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \eta \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{hit}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(z_t^k - z_t^{k*})^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right) & 0 \leq z_t^k \leq z_{max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — The Short component:** When an unexpected object (e.g., a person, a chair) intercepts the beam before the expected obstacle:

$$p_{short}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \eta \cdot \lambda_{short} e^{-\lambda_{short} z_t^k} & 0 \leq z_t^k \leq z_t^{k*} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The exponential form models decreasing probability as the unexpected hit gets farther from the sensor.

**Step 4 — The Max component:** When the beam misses everything (e.g., hits a mirror at grazing angle) and the sensor saturates at the maximum range:

$$p_{max}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} 1 & z_t^k = z_{max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — The Random component:** To model completely unexplained measurements:

$$p_{rand}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{z_{max}} & 0 \leq z_t^k \leq z_{max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — The mixture:**

$$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = z_{hit} \cdot p_{hit} + z_{short} \cdot p_{short} + z_{max} \cdot p_{max} + z_{rand} \cdot p_{rand}$$

The local normalizer $\eta$ ensures each component is properly normalized over the support of $z_t^k$.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** is a particle filter that approximates the posterior belief $\text{bel}(x_t)$ over robot poses using a set of $M$ weighted samples.

**Algorithm steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Sample $M$ particles $\mathcal{X}_0 = \{x_0^{[m]}\}_{m=1}^{M}$ from the prior belief $p(x_0)$.

2. **Prediction (Sampling):** Propagate each particle using the motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$ where $u_t$ is the odometry reading.
$$x_t^{[m]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[m]})$$

3. **Correction (Weighting):** Set the importance weight of each particle proportional to the scan likelihood:
$$w_t^{[m]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]}, m)$$

4. **Normalization:** $w_t^{[m]} \leftarrow w_t^{[m]} / \sum_{i=1}^M w_t^{[i]}$

5. **Resampling:** Draw $M$ new particles with replacement, with probability $w_t^{[m]}$.

6. **Pose Estimate:** $\hat{x}_t = \sum_{m=1}^{M} w_t^{[m]} x_t^{[m]}$ (or the MAP estimate).

**Importance weight update derivation (from Bayes' rule):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bel}(x_t) &= \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, \text{bel}(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1} \\
\text{bel}(x_t) &\approx \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]}, m) \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[m]})}_{\text{particle approximation of } \text{bel}(x_{t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Hence the weight $w_t^{[m]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]}, m) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[m]}$. After resampling, $w_{t-1}^{[m]} = 1/M$ (uniform), so $w_t^{[m]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]}, m)$.

**Particle Depletion Problem:** When all but a few particles carry negligible weight, resampling concentrates probability mass on a handful of samples, reducing the diversity needed to recover from future localization failures (e.g., the "kidnapped robot" problem).

**AMCL (Adaptive MCL) Solutions:**
- **KLD Sampling:** Adaptively chooses the number of particles $M$ based on the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the true and sampled distribution, keeping $M$ small when the robot is well-localized and large when it is uncertain.
- **Random Particle Injection:** Periodically injects a small fraction of particles sampled uniformly over the free space, allowing the filter to recover from global localization failures.
- **Augmented MCL:** Triggers recovery only when the average scan likelihood falls below a threshold.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) Explain the Likelihood Field Model for a 2D LIDAR scan. Derive the expression for the per-beam likelihood and show how the endpoint coordinates are computed from the robot pose and beam angle.** (7 marks)

**(b) Describe the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm for laser scan matching. State the objective function, the two main steps, and explain why ICP is sensitive to initial alignment — and how this is mitigated in practice.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

The **Likelihood Field Model** (also called *Proximity Model* or *Endpoint Model with Precomputed Distance Field*) replaces the expensive ray-casting operation in the Beam Model with a precomputed table of Euclidean distances to the nearest obstacle for every map cell.

**Step 1 — Precompute the distance field:** Once the map is loaded, compute for every free cell $(i, j)$:

$$\text{dist}(i, j) = \min_{(i', j') \in \text{occupied}} \sqrt{(i-i')^2 + (j-j')^2}$$

This is computed via the **Two-Pass Distance Transform (Felzenszwalb & Huttenlocher, 2012)** in $O(N)$ time.

**Step 2 — Project the beam endpoint to world coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{z_t^k} &= x + z_t^k \cos(\theta + \theta_{k,sensor}) \\
y_{z_t^k} &= y + z_t^k \sin(\theta + \theta_{k,sensor})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up the distance:**

$$d_k = \text{dist}(\text{round}(x_{z_t^k}), \text{round}(y_{z_t^k}))$$

**Step 4 — Compute the likelihood** (Gaussian on $d_k$, truncated to $d_{threshold}$):

$$
p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{cases} z_{hit} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_{hit}^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{d_k^2}{2\sigma_{hit}^2}\right) + z_{rand} \cdot \frac{1}{z_{max}} & \text{if } d_k < d_{threshold} \\ z_{max} \cdot \frac{1}{z_{max}} + z_{rand} \cdot \frac{1}{z_{max}} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Advantages:** $O(1)$ per beam, smooth likelihood, robust to small map errors, ideal for EKF/gradient-based scan matching.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** is the standard algorithm for aligning two laser scans (or a scan with a reference scan) by estimating the relative rigid-body transform.

**Inputs:** Reference scan $\{p_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$, current scan $\{q_j\}_{j=1}^{M}$, initial guess $T_0 = (R_0, t_0)$.

**Output:** Optimal rigid transform $T^* = (R^*, t^*)$ minimizing:

$$\min_{R, t} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert\vert R p_i + t - q_{c(i)} \vert\vert^2$$

where $c(i) = \arg\min_j \vert\vert R p_i + t - q_j \vert\vert$ is the **closest-point correspondence**.

**Two main steps per iteration:**

1. **Correspondence Step:** For each point in the reference scan, find the closest point in the current scan (using a KD-Tree for $O(\log M)$ lookup).
$$c^{(k)}(i) = \arg\min_{j} \vert\vert R^{(k-1)} p_i + t^{(k-1)} - q_j \vert\vert$$

2. **Minimization Step:** Given fixed correspondences, solve the orthogonal Procrustes problem for $(R^{(k)}, t^{(k)})$ in closed form using SVD of the cross-covariance matrix:
$$
H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R^{(k-1)} p_i - \bar{p})(q_{c^{(k)}(i)} - \bar{q})^T = U S V^T \quad \Rightarrow \quad R^{(k)} = V U^T, \quad t^{(k)} = \bar{q} - R^{(k)} \bar{p}
$$

3. **Convergence Check:** Stop when $\vert\vert T^{(k)} - T^{(k-1)} \vert\vert < \epsilon$ or after $K_{max}$ iterations.

**Sensitivity to initial alignment:** ICP's objective is **non-convex** with many local minima. If the initial guess is too far from the true alignment, ICP converges to the nearest local minimum — which is usually wrong.

**Mitigation strategies:**
- **Coarse-to-fine pyramid:** Downsample scans at multiple resolutions; align coarse first, refine at finer scales.
- **IMU/odometry prior:** Use $T_0$ from wheel odometry (good for short-term motion) or IMU (good orientation prior).
- **Random restart / Multi-hypothesis:** Run ICP from $N$ different initializations, pick the one with lowest residual.
- **Feature-based pre-alignment:** Extract line/point features (e.g., using Hough transform on the scan) and align them first.
- **Branch-and-bound (Go-ICP, KISS-ICP):** Guaranteed global optimality at the cost of higher runtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MCL with EKF:** Students often write EKF equations inside an MCL question. Always check the question stem.
> 2. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in the beam model — costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using range $r = c \cdot t_f$** (one-way) instead of $r = c \cdot t_f / 2$ (round-trip) — costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not explaining the physical reason** for $p_{short}$ (unexpected obstacles) — examiners expect justification, not just the formula.
> 5. **Writing $p(z_t \mid x_t, m) = p(z_t \mid x_t) p(m)$** — incorrect; the map $m$ is a *conditioning variable*, not independent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Time-of-Flight:** $r = c t_f / 2$; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; accuracy demands **picosecond timing resolution**.
- **Two dominant measurement models:** Beam Model (slow, handles failures explicitly) vs. **Likelihood Field** (fast, smooth, default in ROS `amcl`).
- **Beam Model has 4 mixture components:** $z_{hit}$ (Gaussian on ray-cast range), $z_{short}$ (exponential), $z_{max}$ (uniform at $z_{max}$), $z_{rand}$ (uniform in $[0, z_{max}]$).
- **Likelihood Field precomputes** a Euclidean distance transform — query is $O(1)$ per beam.
- **Endpoint world coordinates:** $x_{z} = x + z \cos(\theta + \theta_{k,s})$; $y_{z} = y + z \sin(\theta + \theta_{k,s})$.
- **MCL is a particle filter** with 4 steps: Initialize → Predict (motion model) → Weight (measurement likelihood) → Resample.
- **Importance weight:** $w_t^{[m]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[m]}, m) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[m]}$.
- **Particle depletion** is the central failure mode; **AMCL** fixes it with **KLD sampling + random particle injection**.
- **ICP objective:** minimize sum of squared distances between corresponding points; sensitive to initialization; mitigated by multi-resolution, IMU priors, or branch-and-bound.
- **Standard sensors:** Hokuyo URG-04LX (indoor, 5.6 m, 240°), SICK LMS-511 (outdoor, 80 m, 190°), Velodyne VLP-16 (3D, 100 m, 360°).
- **Default ROS package:** `amcl` (Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization) uses the **Likelihood Field Model** by default.
- **Number of particles** in MCL typically: 100 (well-localized) to 5000 (global localization / kidnapped robot).
- **Coordinate frames to remember:** *Sensor frame* → *Robot frame* → *World frame*. Always transform beam endpoints to world frame before querying the map.
- **Failure modes of LIDAR:** specular reflection (glass), mixed pixels (object edges), sun glare (outdoor), rain/fog attenuation, transparent obstacles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camera-Based Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Camera-Based Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📷 Camera-Based Localization — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Camera-Based Localization** is the process of estimating the **pose** (position + orientation) of a mobile robot relative to a known map, a global reference frame, or its own previous pose, using **passive visual sensors** (monocular, stereo, or RGB-D cameras) as the primary exteroceptive measurement source.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic sits at the intersection of **robot perception**, **computer vision**, and **state estimation**. The robot must answer two fundamental questions continuously:

1. **Where am I?** → Localization (pose estimation: $x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi$)
2. **Where have I been?** → Odometry (incremental motion estimation)

The discipline spans three hierarchical methods, in order of complexity:

| Hierarchy Level | Method | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Level 1** | Visual Odometry (VO) | Frame-to-frame incremental motion |
| **Level 2** | Visual SLAM (VSLAM) | VO + global map building & loop closure |
| **Level 3** | Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) | VO + IMU fusion for robust estimation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Walking Human"

Imagine you are blindfolded and pushed into an unfamiliar room. You cannot use GPS or a laser range finder, but you have a **single camera** (your eye). How do you know where you are?

- You observe **distinctive landmarks** (a red chair, a door frame) — these are **features**.
- You remember how these landmarks appeared **moments ago** and how they have **shifted or rotated** in your current view — this is **feature tracking**.
- By knowing that a closer object moves "faster" across your field of view than a far one (motion parallax), you estimate **depth** — this is **triangulation / Structure from Motion (SfM)**.
- As you walk, you stitch your estimates together to track your **trajectory** — this is **visual odometry**.
- If you ever return to a previously seen room, you recognize it and **correct accumulated drift** — this is **loop closure** in SLAM.

This is *exactly* what a camera-based localization system does for a robot, only with mathematical precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cameras?** Cameras are **passive, low-power, lightweight, and information-rich** sensors. They capture dense texture, color, and semantic cues unavailable to LiDAR or sonar — making them indispensable for **service robots** operating in human-centric, GPS-denied indoor environments (hospitals, warehouses, homes).

## 1.3 Camera Modalities in Field Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Primary Camera Configurations Used in KTU-aligned Robotics Curriculum:**

1. **Monocular Camera** — A single moving lens. Cheapest, smallest, but suffers from **scale ambiguity** (cannot determine absolute size of scene).
2. **Stereo Camera** — Two cameras with a known baseline $b$. Mimics human binocular vision; can compute metric depth directly via **disparity**.
3. **RGB-D Camera** — Combines a regular RGB sensor with an active depth sensor (e.g., structured light or Time-of-Flight). Provides per-pixel depth in real-time; exemplified by **Microsoft Kinect, Intel RealSense, Azure Kinect DK**.

A fourth, increasingly common variant is the **event camera** (Dynamic Vision Sensor), which reports per-pixel brightness *changes* asynchronously with microsecond latency — ideal for high-speed and high-dynamic-range field robotics.

## 1.4 The Pinhole Camera Model — Foundation of All Visual Localization

Every visual localization algorithm is rooted in the **pinhole projection model**. A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = [X, Y, Z, 1]^T$ (homogeneous) projects to a 2D image point $\mathbf{x} = [u, v, 1]^T$ via:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \, \mathbf{X}$$

where $\mathbf{K}$ is the **intrinsic matrix**, and $[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$ is the **extrinsic matrix** encoding camera pose.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** "**KRT**" — **K**alibration (intrinsics), **R**otation, **T**ranslation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visual Odometry (VO) Pipeline — Stepwise Logic

Visual odometry is the **core engine** of camera-based localization. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand the full pipeline:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Stages of the Visual Odometry Pipeline:**
> 1. **Image Acquisition & Pre-processing** — Capture frame $I_t$, $I_{t+1}$; apply undistortion, grayscale conversion, and (optionally) illumination normalization.
> 2. **Feature Detection** — Identify salient, repeatable keypoints (corners, blobs). Common detectors: **Harris, FAST, Shi-Tomasi, SIFT, SURF, ORB**.
> 3. **Feature Description & Matching** — Compute a descriptor vector for each keypoint; match descriptors between frames using **Brute-Force** or **FLANN** matchers; filter via **Lowe's ratio test** and **RANSAC**.
> 4. **Motion Estimation** — Use 2D-2D (epipolar geometry) or 3D-2D (PnP) correspondences to recover the **Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E}$ or **Homography** $\mathbf{H}$, then decompose into $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$.
> 5. **Scale Recovery & Pose Refinement** — For monocular VO, fuse with IMU, stereo baseline, or known object height to recover metric scale; refine using **bundle adjustment** or a **non-linear least squares** optimizer.

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The 'Why' Behind the Pipeline

- **Why feature detection?** Raw pixels are noisy and dense. Features are *invariant* to illumination, scale, and rotation (in the case of SIFT/SURF), making matching reliable.
- **Why RANSAC?** Outlier matches are inevitable. RANSAC (Random Sample Consensus) robustly estimates a geometric model (e.g., $\mathbf{E}$) from a set of inliers and outliers.
- **Why bundle adjustment?** Sequential motion estimation accumulates drift. Bundle adjustment globally optimizes camera poses and 3D landmark positions by minimizing **reprojection error**.

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU numerical/analytical questions on this topic.**

| # | Equation | Description | Boundary / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}$ | Pinhole projection (world to image) | $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ homogeneous |
| 2 | $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic matrix | $f_x, f_y$: focal lengths in pixels; $c_x, c_y$: principal point; $s$: skew (≈0) |
| 3 | $\mathbf{x}'^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{x} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (calibrated) | $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$, rank 2, 5 DOF |
| 4 | $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ | Essential matrix from pose | $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$: skew-symmetric of $\mathbf{t}$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}'^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x} = 0$ | Epipolar constraint (uncalibrated) | $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$, rank 2, 7 DOF |
| 6 | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K} (\mathbf{R} - \frac{\mathbf{t} \mathbf{n}^T}{d}) \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ | Homography (planar scene) | $\mathbf{n}$: plane normal, $d$: distance to plane |
| 7 | $Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$ | Stereo depth from disparity | $b$: baseline, $d = u_L - u_R$: disparity |
| 8 | $s = \frac{\text{observed parallax}}{\text{expected parallax}}$ | Monocular scale recovery factor | Often fused with IMU height prior |
| 9 | $\mathbf{T} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{T}} \sum_i \rho\left( \left\| \pi(\mathbf{T}, \mathbf{X}_i) - \mathbf{x}_i \right\|^2 \right)$ | Bundle adjustment (reprojection) | $\pi(\cdot)$: projection function, $\rho$: robust kernel (Huber) |
| 10 | $\mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i$ | Reprojection error vector | Minimized in BA; drives VSLAM accuracy |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - Equations 1–6 underpin **all** structure-from-motion pipelines in autonomous driving (e.g., Tesla Vision, Mobileye).
> - Equation 7 is the workhorse of **stereo service robots** in warehouses (Fetch, Locus Robotics).
> - Equation 9 is the gold-standard refinement used in **AR/VR** (ARKit, ARCore) and **Mars rover** navigation (NASA's MER/Curiosity/Perseverance visual odometry).

## 2.4 Coordinate Frames in Visual Localization

A KTU exam often requires identifying the relevant frames:

- **World Frame** $\mathcal{W}$ — Fixed global reference.
- **Camera Frame** $\mathcal{C}$ — Origin at optical center; $Z$-axis points along the optical axis.
- **Robot/Body Frame** $\mathcal{B}$ — Attached to robot chassis.
- **IMU Frame** $\mathcal{I}$ — Co-located with the inertial measurement unit.

The full state vector for a 6-DOF monocular VO system is:

$$\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{T}_{k}^{\mathcal{W}} \\ \mathbf{v}_k^{\mathcal{W}} \\ \mathbf{b}_k^a \\ \mathbf{b}_k^g \end{bmatrix}$$

where $\mathbf{T}_k^{\mathcal{W}}$ is the camera-to-world transform, $\mathbf{v}_k^{\mathcal{W}}$ is velocity, and $\mathbf{b}_k^a, \mathbf{b}_k^g$ are accelerometer and gyroscope biases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Exhaustive Derivations & Analytical Reasoning

## 3.1 Derivation: The Pinhole Projection Equation

**Starting point:** A 3D point $\mathbf{X}_c = [X_c, Y_c, Z_c]^T$ in the camera coordinate frame. By similar triangles of the pinhole geometry, the projection onto the image plane (at focal distance $f$) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} \\
y &= f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Conversion to pixel coordinates:** The image sensor has pixel dimensions $d_x, d_y$ and a principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= f \cdot d_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x = f_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \\
v &= f \cdot d_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y = f_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Express in homogeneous form** by appending a 1 and stacking the equations:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z_c} \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Transform from world to camera frame** using extrinsic $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X}$$

This is the **fundamental pinhole equation** governing all camera-based localization.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Essential Matrix and Epipolar Geometry

**Setup:** Consider two camera views, $\mathcal{C}_1$ (reference) and $\mathcal{C}_2$ (current), related by a rigid motion $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$. A 3D point $\mathbf{P}$ projects to $\mathbf{p}_1$ in view 1 and $\mathbf{p}_2$ in view 2 (in normalized image coordinates, i.e., $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$ applied).

**Step 1 — Geometry in camera 1's frame:** The point is at $\mathbf{P}_1 = [X_1, Y_1, Z_1]^T$ in $\mathcal{C}_1$. Then in $\mathcal{C}_2$:

$$\mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{t}$$

**Step 2 — Coplanarity constraint:** The vectors $\mathbf{P}_1$, $\mathbf{P}_2$, and $\mathbf{t}$ are **coplanar** (they all lie in the epipolar plane). Hence the scalar triple product is zero:

$$\mathbf{P}_2 \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\mathbf{P}_2$:**

$$(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{t}) \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand and use the skew-symmetric matrix** $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$, defined such that $[\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{x}$:

$$(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1)^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{t}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{P}_1 = 0$$

**Step 5 — The second term vanishes** because $\mathbf{t}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times = \mathbf{t}^T (\mathbf{t} \times \cdot) = 0$ (a vector is orthogonal to its own cross product). Therefore:

$$\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{R}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{P}_1 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Define the Essential Matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$:

$$\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{E}^T \mathbf{P}_1 = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{p}_1^T \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{p}_2 = 0$$

since $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{P} / Z$ (normalized). The **epipolar constraint** is:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{p}_1^T \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{p}_2 = 0}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Decomposition of $\mathbf{E}$ into $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$

Given $\mathbf{E}$, perform SVD: $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{U} \, \mathbf{\Sigma} \, \mathbf{V}^T$. The four possible decompositions are:

$$\begin{aligned}
[\mathbf{t}]_\times^{(1)} &= \mathbf{U} \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{U}^T, \quad \mathbf{R}^{(1)} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{V}^T \\
[\mathbf{t}]_\times^{(2)} &= -\mathbf{U} \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{U}^T, \quad \mathbf{R}^{(2)} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W} \mathbf{V}^T \\
[\mathbf{t}]_\times^{(3)} &= \mathbf{U} \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{U}^T, \quad \mathbf{R}^{(3)} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{V}^T \\
[\mathbf{t}]_\times^{(4)} &= -\mathbf{U} \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{U}^T, \quad \mathbf{R}^{(4)} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{V}^T
\end{aligned}$$

where:

$$\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{Z} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**The cheirality check:** Of the four solutions, only one places the 3D points in front of *both* cameras (positive depth). This is selected by verifying that $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_1$ and $\mathbf{p}_2$ have consistent signs of their $Z$ components.

## 3.4 Derivation: Linear Triangulation (Direct Linear Transform)

**Problem:** Given two projection matrices $\mathbf{P}_1 = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}]$, $\mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{K}[\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$, and corresponding homogeneous image points $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^3$, recover the 3D point $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^4$.

**Step 1 — Projective relation:** For each view, the 3D point projects to the image as $\lambda_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{P}_i \mathbf{X}$ for some depth $\lambda_i$.

**Step 2 — Cross-product formulation:** The cross product $\mathbf{x}_i \times (\mathbf{P}_i \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{0}$ eliminates the scale factor:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}_1 \times (\mathbf{P}_1 \mathbf{X}) &= \mathbf{0} \\
\mathbf{x}_2 \times (\mathbf{P}_2 \mathbf{X}) &= \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Stack into a linear system:** Expanding $\mathbf{x}_i \times (\mathbf{P}_i \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{0}$ yields 3 equations, but only 2 are linearly independent. Thus we obtain 4 linear equations in 4 unknowns:

$$\mathbf{A} \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$$

**Step 4 — Solve via SVD:** Compute the SVD $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{U} \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^T$. The solution $\mathbf{X}$ is the right singular vector corresponding to the **smallest singular value** of $\mathbf{A}$ (the unit vector that minimizes $\|\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\|$).

**Step 5 — Convert from homogeneous to Euclidean:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{\text{Euclidean}} = \frac{1}{X_4} \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \\ X_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric interpretation:** Triangulation finds the **3D point closest to both optical rays**. The DLT method above gives the algebraic minimum; for higher accuracy, a **non-linear refinement** minimizing geometric reprojection error is applied iteratively (Gauss-Newton or Levenberg-Marquardt).

## 3.5 Derivation: Perspective-n-Point (PnP) Problem

**Problem:** Given $n$ known 3D points $\mathbf{X}_i$ and their 2D image observations $\mathbf{x}_i$, recover the camera pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$.

**P3P (Three-Point) Case:** With three points forming a triangle with sides $a, b, c$, we use the law of cosines in the camera frame. Let the distances from the optical center to the 3D points be $r_1, r_2, r_3$. By the cosine rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_2^2 + r_3^2 - 2 r_2 r_3 \cos(\alpha) &= a^2 \\
r_1^2 + r_3^2 - 2 r_1 r_3 \cos(\beta) &= b^2 \\
r_1^2 + r_2^2 - 2 r_1 r_2 \cos(\gamma) &= c^2
\end{aligned}$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the angles subtended at the optical center (obtained from image observations). This system yields **up to 4 algebraic solutions** for the depths; a fourth point is used as a cheirality disambiguator.

**EPnP (Efficient PnP) for $n \geq 4$ Points:** Express each 3D point as a weighted combination of 4 **virtual control points** $\mathbf{C}_j$:

$$\mathbf{X}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{4} \alpha_{ij} \mathbf{C}_j, \quad \sum_j \alpha_{ij} = 1$$

In the camera frame, the same weights apply to the projected control points $\mathbf{c}_j = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{C}_j + \mathbf{t}$:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{K} \sum_{j=1}^{4} \alpha_{ij} \mathbf{c}_j$$

This yields a linear system in the 12 unknowns (the 4 control points in camera frame), solvable by SVD. The full pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ is then extracted by aligning world and camera control points via the **Arun et al. (1987) SVD-based rigid alignment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The weights $\alpha_{ij}$ are computed in the world frame** using the **homogeneous barycentric coordinates** of each $\mathbf{X}_i$ with respect to the four control points. The four control points are typically chosen as the centroid of all points plus three principal directions of the point cloud (from PCA).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Typical 7-Mark Sub-Part):** A robot moves between two positions. Camera intrinsics are $f_x = 800$, $f_y = 800$, $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$. A 3D world point at $\mathbf{X} = [1.0, 0.5, 5.0]^T$ m is observed at image coordinates $(560, 320)$ px. If the robot moves by $\mathbf{t} = [0.1, 0, 0]^T$ m and rotates by $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$, find the projected coordinates in the second view.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} 800 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 800 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2: Apply extrinsic $\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\mathbf{X}_c^{(1)} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 5.0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \\ 5.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3: Project to image:

$$\begin{aligned}
u^{(1)} &= 800 \cdot \frac{1.0}{5.0} + 320 = 160 + 320 = 480 \\
v^{(1)} &= 800 \cdot \frac{0.5}{5.0} + 240 = 80 + 240 = 320
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4: After motion, $\mathbf{X}_c^{(2)} = \mathbf{X}_c^{(1)} - \mathbf{t} = [0.9, 0.5, 5.0]^T$ (camera moves by $+\mathbf{t}$ → world appears shifted by $-\mathbf{t}$).

Step 5: Project to second view:

$$\begin{aligned}
u^{(2)} &= 800 \cdot \frac{0.9}{5.0} + 320 = 144 + 320 = 464 \\
v^{(2)} &= 800 \cdot \frac{0.5}{5.0} + 240 = 80 + 240 = 320
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The point shifted left by $\Delta u = 16$ pixels — the **parallax** induced by the 0.1 m translation. Estimating this parallax is exactly what enables depth recovery and, conversely, motion recovery.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & System Architectures

## 4.1 Visual Odometry Pipeline — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Camera Image Frame T] --> B[Image Preprocessing Undistort and Grayscale]
    B --> C[Feature Detection Harris OR FAST OR ORB]
    C --> D[Feature Description Descriptor Vector Computation]
    D --> E[Feature Matching FLANN OR Brute Force]
    E --> F{RANSAC Outlier Rejection}
    F -->|Inliers| G[Essential Matrix Estimation]
    F -->|Inliers| H[Homography Estimation]
    G --> I[SVD Decomposition of E]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Pose Recovery R and t via Cheirality Check]
    J --> K[Scale Recovery IMU OR Stereo OR Known Object]
    K --> L[Bundle Adjustment Nonlinear Refinement]
    L --> M[Robot Pose Update in World Frame]
    M --> N[Output 6 DOF Pose and Trajectory]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style N fill:#d4edda
    style L fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.2 Visual SLAM Architecture — Frontend vs Backend

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF[SLAM Frontend Sensing and Front End Processing]
        F1[Camera Input Frames] --> F2[Feature Extraction]
        F2 --> F3[Data Association and Matching]
        F3 --> F4[Frame to Frame Motion Estimation]
    end
    subgraph SB[SLAM Backend Optimization and Global Consistency]
        B1[Pose Graph Construction]
        B2[Loop Closure Detection Bag of Words]
        B3[Bundle Adjustment Global Optimization]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B1
    end
    subgraph SM[Mapping Module]
        M1[Landmark Triangulation]
        M2[Sparse OR Dense OR Semi Dense Map]
    end
    F4 --> B1
    F4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> F3
    B3 --> N[Final Globally Consistent Trajectory and Map]
    style SF fill:#cfe2ff
    style SB fill:#f8d7da
    style SM fill:#d1e7dd
    style N fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.3 Pinhole Camera Geometry — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[3D World Point X Y Z] -->|Rigid Transform R t| C[Camera Frame Xc Yc Zc]
    C -->|Perspective Division x fXc per Zc| P[Normalized Image Plane x y]
    P -->|Pixel Conversion u fxx per Zc plus cx| I[Image Pixel Coordinates u v]
    I -->|Distortion Correction| U[Undistorted Pixel Coordinates]
    U --> O[Output for Feature Matching]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q -->|Metric Depth Required| S[Stereo Camera Baseline b]

    M --> M1[Advantage Low Cost Compact]
    M --> M2[Disadvantage Scale Ambiguity]
```
