# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Visual Odometry and list its key stages.**

**Model Answer:**
Visual Odometry (VO) is the process of estimating the ego-motion of a camera (or robot carrying a camera) by analyzing the changes in image features between consecutive frames. Its key stages are: (1) image acquisition and preprocessing, (2) feature detection, (3) feature description and matching, (4) motion estimation via Essential Matrix or Homography decomposition, and (5) pose refinement via bundle adjustment or filtering. VO is the foundational step in Visual SLAM and provides incremental, real-time pose updates without requiring GPS.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the scale ambiguity problem in monocular visual odometry? How is it resolved?**

**Model Answer:**
A single camera cannot distinguish between a small scene close-up and a large scene far-away because both produce identical image projections. This is called **scale ambiguity**. Formally, for a true translation $\mathbf{t}$, the monocular system can only recover $\mathbf{t}$ up to a positive scalar $s$. It is resolved by: (1) using a **stereo camera** with known baseline $b$, (2) fusing with **IMU** measurements (gravity and acceleration provide metric scale), (3) using **depth sensors** (RGB-D), or (4) recognizing a **known object of known size** in the scene (e.g., a fiducial marker or AruCo tag).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **any one** full question (with sub-parts) from the choice provided.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the Essential Matrix from the epipolar geometry of two camera views. Starting from the coplanarity condition of the vectors, show all algebraic steps leading to $\mathbf{p}_1^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{p}_2 = 0$.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Let $\mathbf{P}$ be a 3D point. In the first camera frame $\mathcal{C}_1$, its coordinates are $\mathbf{P}_1$. The second camera $\mathcal{C}_2$ is related to $\mathcal{C}_1$ by rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{t}$$

The vectors $\mathbf{t}$, $\mathbf{P}_1$, and $\mathbf{P}_2$ all lie in the **epipolar plane**. The coplanarity condition is the scalar triple product equal to zero:

$$(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1 + \mathbf{t}) \cdot (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) = 0$$

Expand:

$$(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{P}_1)^T (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) + \mathbf{t}^T (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) = 0$$

The second term vanishes since $\mathbf{t}^T (\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1) = 0$. Using the skew-symmetric identity $\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{P}_1 = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{P}_1$:

$$\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{R}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{P}_1 = 0$$

Define the Essential Matrix:

$$\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{E}^T = \mathbf{R}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times^T = -\mathbf{R}^T [\mathbf{t}]_\times$$

Substituting and using $\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{E}^T \mathbf{P}_1 = (\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{E}^T \mathbf{P}_1)^T = \mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{P}_1$ (since it is a scalar), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{P}_1^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{P}_1 = 0}$$

Since image coordinates are $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{P} / Z$ (normalized), and the scalar $Z$ cancels:

$$\mathbf{p}_1^T \mathbf{E} \, \mathbf{p}_2 = 0$$

**(b) Explain the Perspective-n-Point (PnP) problem. Solve the P3P case to show it yields four algebraic solutions and describe how a fourth point disambiguates them.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The PnP problem: given $n$ known 3D world points $\mathbf{X}_i$ and their 2D image projections $\mathbf{x}_i$, recover the camera pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$. For the **P3P** case with three points $\mathbf{X}_1, \mathbf{X}_2, \mathbf{X}_3$ and image observations forming angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ at the optical center, let the unknown distances be $r_1, r_2, r_3$. By the law of cosines in triangles formed at the optical center:

$$\begin{aligned}
r_2^2 + r_3^2 - 2 r_2 r_3 \cos\alpha &= a^2 \\
r_1^2 + r_3^2 - 2 r_1 r_3 \cos\beta &= b^2 \\
r_1^2 + r_2^2 - 2 r_1 r_2 \cos\gamma &= c^2
\end{aligned}$$

where $a, b, c$ are the known distances between the 3D world points. This non-linear system yields **up to 4 algebraic solutions** for $(r_1, r_2, r_3)$, each corresponding to a geometrically valid camera pose. The four solutions differ in the orientation of the triangle relative to the camera (the "twisted" solutions). A **fourth 3D-2D correspondence** acts as a disambiguator: the solution whose reprojection of the fourth point best matches the observation (minimum reprojection error) is selected. This is the standard approach in **OpenCV's `solvePnP`** function.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Visual SLAM (VSLAM) architecture. Differentiate between the frontend and backend, and state the role of loop closure.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

VSLAM consists of three primary modules: **Frontend**, **Backend**, and **Mapping**.

**Frontend** (Sensor Processing Layer):
- Performs image acquisition, feature extraction (ORB, SIFT), and feature matching.
- Estimates frame-to-frame motion using Essential Matrix or Optical Flow.
- Performs initial pose estimation and data association (linking observations to map landmarks).

**Backend** (Optimization Layer):
- Maintains a **pose graph** of robot poses and landmark positions.
- Performs **Bundle Adjustment (BA)** to minimize the global reprojection error.
- Executes **loop closure detection** using techniques like **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** (DBoW2, ORB-SLAM's DBoW2 module) to recognize previously visited locations.
- Once a loop is detected, a **relative pose constraint** is added between non-consecutive keyframes, and the entire pose graph is re-optimized — eliminating accumulated drift.

**Mapping Module**:
- Maintains the global map of 3D landmarks (sparse, semi-dense, or dense).
- Adds new landmarks via **triangulation** and refines them during BA.

**Role of Loop Closure:** Sequential pose estimation accumulates drift (small errors compound over time). Loop closure detects when the robot revisits a known location and enforces a global consistency constraint, allowing the backend to redistribute errors across the trajectory and **eliminate drift**. This is what makes SLAM "globally consistent" — distinguishing it from pure odometry.

**(b) Derive the linear triangulation (DLT) method to recover a 3D point from two camera views. State all assumptions.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption:** The camera is calibrated (intrinsics $\mathbf{K}$ known) and the relative pose $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ between the two views is known. Image points are in homogeneous coordinates.

**Setup:** Let the two projection matrices be:

$$\mathbf{P}_1 = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{0}], \quad \mathbf{P}_2 = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}]$$

Let the observed image points (homogeneous) be $\mathbf{x}_1 = [u_1, v_1, 1]^T$ and $\mathbf{x}_2 = [u_2, v_2, 1]^T$. We seek $\mathbf{X} = [X, Y, Z, 1]^T$ in world coordinates.

**Step 1 — Projection relation:** The 3D point projects to each view as $\lambda_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{P}_i \mathbf{X}$ for some scalar depth $\lambda_i > 0$.

**Step 2 — Eliminate depth via cross product:** $\mathbf{x}_i \times (\mathbf{P}_i \mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{0}$. Expanding for $i = 1, 2$ gives 3 equations each, but only 2 are linearly independent (the third is a linear combination). So we have **4 linear equations** in 4 unknowns:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
u_1 \mathbf{P}_1^{3T} - \mathbf{P}_1^{1T} \\
v_1 \mathbf{P}_1^{3T} - \mathbf{P}_1^{2T} \\
u_2 \mathbf{P}_2^{3T} - \mathbf{P}_2^{1T} \\
v_2 \mathbf{P}_2^{3T} - \mathbf{P}_2^{2T}
\end{bmatrix} \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}$$

where $\mathbf{P}_i^{jT}$ denotes the $j$-th row of $\mathbf{P}_i$.

**Step 3 — Solve via SVD:** Compute the SVD of the $4 \times 4$ matrix $\mathbf{A}$. The solution $\mathbf{X}$ is the right singular vector corresponding to the **smallest singular value** of $\mathbf{A}$.

**Step 4 — Convert from homogeneous to Euclidean coordinates:**

$$\mathbf{X}_{\text{Euclidean}} = \frac{1}{X_4} \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \\ X_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X}_{\text{homogeneous}} = \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \\ X_3 \\ X_4 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students often forget to state the **calibrated camera assumption** and lose 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing $\mathbf{F}$ (Fundamental Matrix, 7 DOF, uncalibrated) with $\mathbf{E}$ (Essential Matrix, 5 DOF, calibrated) is a **fatal error** in 14-mark derivations.
> 3. In PnP, students frequently forget the **cheirality check** (depth must be positive in front of camera) — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. In the DLT triangulation, the 4×4 system is **homogeneous** ($\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}$), not $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{b}$. Using a non-homogeneous solver (like Gaussian elimination without SVD) is incorrect.

---

## 5.3 🚀 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Camera-Based Localization — Key Takeaways (Last-Minute KTU Revision):**

- **Core Idea:** Cameras provide rich, passive observations for robot localization via feature tracking, geometric reasoning, and non-linear optimization.
- **Three Modalities:** Monocular (cheap, scale ambiguous), Stereo (metric depth via disparity), RGB-D (dense real-time depth, range-limited).
- **Pinhole Model:** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K} [\mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t}] \mathbf{X}$ — memorize the KRT structure.
- **Essential Matrix:** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$, satisfies $\mathbf{p}_1^T \mathbf{E} \mathbf{p}_2 = 0$, recovered via the **8-point algorithm** (or 5-point for calibrated case), decomposed via SVD into 4 possible $(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t})$ pairs; cheirality selects the correct one.
- **Fundamental Matrix:** $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{K}^{-T} \mathbf{E} \mathbf{K}^{-1}$ — used when camera is uncalibrated.
- **Homography:** Used for purely **planar scenes**; $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{K}(\mathbf{R} - \frac{\mathbf{t} \mathbf{n}^T}{d}) \mathbf{K}^{-1}$.
- **Triangulation (DLT):** 4×4 homogeneous system solved by SVD; smallest singular value gives $\mathbf{X}$.
- **PnP Problem:** Recover pose from $n \geq 3$ 3D-2D correspondences. **P3P** → up to 4 algebraic solutions; **EPnP** → linear, suitable for $n \geq 4$.
- **Bundle Adjustment (BA):** Global non-linear refinement of poses and landmarks; minimizes reprojection error $\sum_i \|\mathbf{x}_i - \pi(\mathbf{X}_i)\|^2$.
- **Scale Recovery (Monocular):** Fused with IMU, stereo baseline, or known object size.
- **VSLAM vs VO:** VO is incremental and drifts; VSLAM adds loop closure and BA for global consistency.
- **Real-World Systems:** ORB-SLAM2/3, VINS-Mono, OKVIS, MSCKF (all open-source, used in research and industry).
- **Service Robotics Applications:** Hospital robots (TUG), warehouse AMRs (Fetch, MiR), domestic vacuum cleaners (iRobot with VSLAM), agricultural robots (row-following with stereo VO), and Mars rovers (NASA Perseverance uses VIO).
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always state the **assumption** (calibrated/uncalibrated, planar/non-planar, scale known/unknown) before any derivation. This single habit can earn you 1–2 free marks per question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-of-Flight Camera Localization
topics.content:
# Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera Localization ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Camera Localization** is the process of determining the **pose** (position $(x, y, z)$ and orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$) of a mobile robot within a known or partially known environment by using a Time-of-Flight depth sensor that emits amplitude-modulated or pulsed near-infrared light and measures the **round-trip phase shift** or **time delay** of the reflected signal to produce a dense, per-pixel **3D point cloud**, which is subsequently matched against a reference map using registration algorithms. A ToF camera is an **active range imaging sensor** that delivers, for every pixel $(u,v)$, a tuple: $$\mathbf{m}(u, v) = \{d(u, v),\; A(u, v),\; I(u, v)\}$$ where $d$ is depth, $A$ is the reflected signal amplitude, and $I$ is the active illumination intensity. This **per-pixel metric depth** is the foundational observable that feeds the localization pipeline. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Echolocation Bat" 🦇 Imagine you are standing in a dark, unfamiliar room holding a **flashlight** that pulses at a fixed rhythm. You flash the light at a wall and wait for the reflection to return. The **time** the light took to bounce back tells you exactly **how far** the wall is. If you could do this **one million times per second** in every direction simultaneously — like a *bat's echolocation* — you would build a **3D map** of the room in milliseconds. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this revolutionary for robotics?** > Unlike stereo cameras (which fail on plain walls with no texture) or LiDAR (which is expensive and mechanical), a ToF camera provides: > - Depth **per pixel** without mechanical scanning. > - Robustness in **low-light** and even complete darkness. > - Operation on **textureless surfaces** (plain walls, whiteboards). > - Compact, **solid-state** form factor ideal for service robots. The fundamental physical observable is the **speed of light**, a universal constant: $$c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\; \text{m/s}$$ Because $c$ is so large, the round-trip time for typical indoor ranges (1 m – 10 m) lies in the **nanosecond regime**, which is why ToF cameras use clever modulation tricks (continuous-wave phase detection) rather than direct stopwatch timing. --- ## 1.3 Why "Localization" Specifically? Localization is the problem of answering: *"Where am I?"* given a sensor stream. For a ToF-equipped robot, the localization pipeline is: | Stage | Operation | Output | |------|-----------|--------| | 1. Depth Acquisition | Emit IR, measure phase | Range image $D(u,v)$ | | 2. Point Cloud Construction | Back-project pixels using intrinsics $(f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y)$ | 3D points $\mathbf{P}_i = (X_i, Y_i, Z_i)$ | | 3. Feature Extraction | Detect planes, corners, edges, SURF/SHOT descriptors | Compact descriptors | | 4. Registration | Match against a-priori map (ICP, NDT, feature-based) | 6-DoF pose $\mathbf{T}_{robot \to world}$ | | 5. Filtering | Fuse with odometry in EKF/Particle Filter | Smoothed pose estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_t$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Field & Service Robotics syllabus, ToF camera localization is treated as a **range-sensor-based pose estimation module**, often compared and contrasted with 2D-LiDAR SLAM and visual (RGB-D) SLAM. The emphasis is on **sensor model**, **error characterization**, and **fusion with wheel odometry / IMU**. --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principles of a ToF Camera ### 2.1.1 Continuous-Wave Modulation (iToF — Indirect ToF) The sensor emits a sinusoidally modulated IR wave of frequency $f_m$ and observes the returning wave. The **phase shift** $\varphi$ between emitted and received signal is measured at every pixel: $$\boxed{\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Q}{I}\right)}$$ where $I$ and $Q$ are the **in-phase** and **quadrature** components obtained by correlating the received signal with four phase-shifted reference signals (the "four-bucket" or "four-tap" method). ### 2.1.2 Pulsed Modulation (dToF — Direct ToF) A short light pulse is emitted and the time-of-arrival $\Delta t$ is measured directly (often via SPAD — Single-Photon Avalanche Diodes). The distance is: $$d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$ --- ## 2.2 Depth Reconstruction (Phase-Based iToF) The **primary equation** of an iToF camera that every KTU student must know: $$\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \varphi}{4\pi f_m}}$$ - $d$ — radial distance from the camera focal point to the scene point (m) - $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** — speed of light - $\varphi$ — measured phase shift (radians), $\varphi \in [0, 2\pi)$ - $f_m$ — modulation frequency (Hz), typically **10 MHz – 100 MHz** > [!NOTE] > **Why the factor of 4?** The light travels the distance **twice** (out + back), and the phase detector produces the phase difference in **one full cycle per round-trip**. For a $2\pi$ radian phase ambiguity, the **maximum unambiguous range** is: > $$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 f_m}$$ > For $f_m = 20$ MHz, $d_{\max} = 7.5$ m — perfect for indoor service robots. ### 2.2.1 Multi-Frequency Extension To resolve the phase-wrapping ambiguity beyond $d_{\max}$, **two modulation frequencies** $f_1, f_2$ are used. The synthetic wavelength is: $$\Lambda = \frac{c}{2 \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert}$$ giving an extended unambiguous range, with phase-unwrapping algorithms applied to recover the true depth. --- ## 2.3 Error Sources in ToF Cameras (High-Yield for KTU) | Error Source | Description | Effect on Depth | |-------------|-------------|-----------------| | **Phase Wrapping** | True $\varphi > 2\pi$ wraps to $[0,2\pi)$ | Catastrophic — wrong distance | | **Multi-Path Interference (MPI)** | Light reflects off multiple surfaces | Depth bias towards closer wall | | **Flying Pixels / Mixed Pixels** | Pixel straddles depth discontinuity | Ghost points at edges | | **Motion Artifacts** | Scene moves during integration time | Sinusoidal distortion | | **Ambient IR** | Sunlight or other IR sources | Reduced SNR, biased amplitude | | **Temperature Drift** | Sensor temperature changes LED delay | Systematic depth offset | | **Amplitude Decay** | $A \propto 1/d^2$ | Lower SNR at longer range | The **standard depth variance** model (used in localization filters) is: $$\sigma_d^{2} = \sigma_{d,\text{sys}}^{2} + \frac{\sigma_{d,\text{noise}}^{2}}{A^{2}(d)}$$ where $\sigma_{d,\text{sys}}$ is a systematic floor and the noise term grows as $1/A^{2}$ — i.e. quadratically with distance due to the $1/r^{2}$ intensity fall-off. --- ## 2.4 Pinhole Back-Projection: Pixel → 3D Point For a pixel with image coordinates $(u,v)$ and depth $d$ from a ToF camera with intrinsics $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$ (pixels), the back-projected 3D point in the camera frame is: $$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \cdot d$$ $$Y_c = \frac{(v - c_y)}{f_y} \cdot d$$ $$Z_c = d$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $Z = d$?** By convention, in pinhole optics the **optical axis** points along $Z$, and the ToF depth is measured **along this axis**. This is a common point of confusion — the value $d$ reported by the sensor is *not* the radial distance $\sqrt{X^2 + Y^2 + Z^2}$ but the **axial** component. The radial correction is: > $$d_{\text{radial}} = \frac{d}{\cos\alpha}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{X_c^{2} + Y_c^{2}}}{Z_c}\right)$$ --- ## 2.5 The ToF Localization Sensor Model (KTU Formula Sheet) The **measurement equation** of a ToF pixel, from the perspective of a localization filter, is: $$\tilde{d}(u,v) = d^{\star}(u,v) + b_{\text{ToF}} + n_{\text{ToF}}(u,v)$$ - $d^{\star}(u,v)$ — true depth, function of robot pose $\mathbf{x}_t = (x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ and map - $b_{\text{ToF}}$ — systematic bias (often 1 – 3 cm) - $n_{\text{ToF}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_d^{2})$ — zero-mean Gaussian noise ### 2.5.1 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Markdown Table) | # | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Engineering Use | |---|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | 1 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \varphi}{4\pi f_m}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\varphi$ = phase (rad), $f_m$ = modulation Hz | ToF depth from phase | | 2 | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 f_m}$ | Max unambiguous range | Choose $f_m$ for room size | | 3 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $\Delta t$ = round-trip time | Pulsed (dToF) | | 4 | $\Lambda = \dfrac{c}{2 \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert}$ | Synthetic wavelength | Multi-freq ambiguity resolution | | 5 | $X_c = \dfrac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} d,\; Y_c = \dfrac{(v - c_y)}{f_y} d,\; Z_c = d$ | Pixel → 3D point | Point cloud generation | | 6 | $\varphi = \arctan(Q/I)$ | $I,Q$ = correlations | iToF demodulation | | 7 | $A = \dfrac{\sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}}}{2}$ | Reflected amplitude | Confidence / quality | | 8 | $\sigma_d^{2} = \sigma_{d,\text{sys}}^{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_{d,\text{noise}}^{2}}{A^{2}}$ | Depth noise model | Filter covariance | | 9 | $A \propto \dfrac{\rho \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | $\rho$ = albedo, $\theta$ = incidence | Signal-strength model | | 10 | $d_{\text{radial}} = d / \cos\alpha$ | Correction for off-axis pixels | Geometric consistency | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |--------|-------------| | **Warehouse AGVs** | Floor-to-shelf obstacle mapping, pallet pocket detection | | **Hospital Service Robots** | Patient/bed detection in cluttered wards | | **Domestic Vacuum Cleaners** | Cliff detection, room shape estimation | | **Industrial Pick-and-Place** | Bin-picking on textureless, glossy parts | | **Human-Robot Interaction** | Gesture / proximity detection (ToF arrays on robot torso) | | **Autonomous Wheelchairs** | Corridor following, doorway detection | --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth from Phase Shift (Phase-Based iToF) **Starting point.** Let the emitted light be sinusoidally modulated: $$s_{\text{emit}}(t) = A_0 \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$ This wave travels a distance $2d$ (out and back) before returning to the sensor. The received signal is: $$s_{\text{rec}}(t) = A \cos\!\big(2\pi f_m (t - \tau)\big)$$ where $\tau = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ is the round-trip delay. Substituting: $$s_{\text{rec}}(t) = A \cos\!\left(2\pi f_m t - 2\pi f_m \cdot \frac{2d}{c}\right)$$ $$s_{\text{rec}}(t) = A \cos\!\left(2\pi f_m t - \frac{4\pi f_m d}{c}\right)$$ The **phase shift** introduced by the propagation is therefore: $$\varphi = \frac{4\pi f_m d}{c}$$ Solving algebraically for $d$: $$d = \frac{c \, \varphi}{4 \pi f_m}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **master ToF equation**. Every other depth formulation in this module is a corollary of it. **Numerical example (typical indoor ToF).** Given: $f_m = 20$ MHz, $\varphi = \pi/2$ rad, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. $$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (\pi/2)}{4\pi \cdot (20 \times 10^{6})}$$ $$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8})}{8 \cdot (20 \times 10^{6})}$$ $$d = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.6 \times 10^{8}}$$ $$d = 1.875\; \text{m}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Unambiguous Range The phase detector outputs a value in $\varphi \in [0, 2\pi)$. A phase of $2\pi$ corresponds to one full wavelength of round-trip, i.e. a path length of one wavelength. Set $\varphi = 2\pi$ in the master equation: $$d_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot (2\pi)}{4\pi f_m} = \frac{c}{2 f_m}$$ **Numerical example.** For $f_m = 20$ MHz: $$d_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \cdot 20 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{7}} = 7.5\; \text{m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design rule:** For a room of length $L$, choose $f_m \geq \dfrac{c}{2L}$. For a 10 m hall, $f_m \geq 15$ MHz. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Four-Bucket Phase Extraction (iToF Demodulation) The received signal $s_{\text{rec}}(t) = A \cos(\omega t - \varphi) + B$ is correlated with four reference signals shifted by $0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$: $$C_0 = \int_{0}^{T} s_{\text{rec}}(t) \cdot \cos(\omega t)\, dt = A \cos\varphi + B$$ $$C_1 = \int_{0}^{T} s_{\text{rec}}(t) \cdot \cos(\omega t - \pi/2)\, dt = A \sin\varphi$$ $$C_2 = \int_{0}^{T} s_{\text{rec}}(t) \cdot \cos(\omega t - \pi)\, dt = -A \cos\varphi + B$$ $$C_3 = \int_{0}^{T} s_{\text{rec}}(t) \cdot \cos(\omega t - 3\pi/2)\, dt = -A \sin\varphi$$ Form the difference pairs to eliminate the ambient offset $B$ and DC: $$I = C_0 - C_2 = 2A \cos\varphi$$ $$Q = C_1 - C_3 = 2A \sin\varphi$$ Recover the phase: $$\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Q}{I}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{C_1 - C_3}{C_0 - C_2}\right)$$ And the amplitude (used for confidence weighting): $$A = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(C_0 - C_2)^{2} + (C_1 - C_3)^{2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why four taps?** Using more taps averages out noise, but the **minimum** required to solve for both $A$ and $\varphi$ is **two** ($I$ and $Q$). Four is industry-standard because it cancels DC / ambient light. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Multi-Frequency Phase Unwrapping Let $f_1 = 20$ MHz, $f_2 = 19$ MHz. Compute the unambiguous ranges: $$d_{1,\max} = \frac{c}{2 f_1} = 7.5\; \text{m}, \qquad d_{2,\max} = \frac{c}{2 f_2} \approx 7.89\; \text{m}$$ The **difference frequency** is $\Delta f = 1$ MHz, giving a synthetic range: $$\Lambda = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 150\; \text{m}$$ This synthetic range covers any practical indoor distance. The two phase measurements, after appropriate integer-ambiguity resolution, are combined to recover the **absolute** depth beyond $d_{1,\max}$. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: ToF vs LiDAR vs Stereo | Property | **ToF Camera** | **2D/3D LiDAR** | **Stereo Camera** | |----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------| | Depth per pixel | ✅ Yes | ❌ (sparse lines/points) | ❌ (dense but matching-dependent) | | Mechanical parts | ❌ None | ✅ Rotating mirror | ❌ None | | Range (indoor) | 0.1 – 10 m | 0.1 – 100 m | 0.5 – 20 m | | Works on textureless surfaces | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Fails | | Works in darkness | ✅ Yes (active) | ✅ Yes | ❌ Needs light | | Outdoor sunlight | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good | | Multi-path sensitivity | ⚠️ High | ⚠️ Low | ⚠️ Low | | Cost (2024) | Low (€) | High (€€€) | Low (€) | | Output rate | 30 – 60 fps point cloud | 10 – 20 Hz scan | 30 fps disparity | | Best for | Indoor service robots | Outdoor / large spaces | Textured indoor scenes | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ToF Camera Operating Principle — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Modulation Unit
20 MHz Sinusoidal Drive"] nodeB["IR LED Array
850 nm or 940 nm"] nodeC["Scene with
Objects and Walls"] nodeD["Receiving Lens
Pinhole Optics"] nodeE["Image Sensor
CMOS Demodulation Pixels"] nodeF["Correlator Array
4 Bucket Taps"] nodeG["DSP Processor
Phase and Amplitude Calc"] nodeH["Depth Image
d u v per pixel"] nodeI["Point Cloud
X Y Z per pixel"] nodeJ["Localization Filter
EKF or Particle"] nodeK["Robot Pose
x y z roll pitch yaw"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB -->|"Emitted IR
modulated wave"| nodeC nodeC -->|"Reflected IR
phase shifted"| nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG -->|"Phase phi and Amplitude A"| nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagram is rendered as a **functional architecture flow** rather than a physical sketch, per the protocol for complex physical topics. Each block corresponds to a distinct stage in the ToF camera localization chain. --- ## 4.2 Phase Demodulation Pipeline (4-Bucket Method) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Received Signal
A cos wt minus phi plus B"] nodeB["Tap 0
cos wt"] nodeC["Tap 1
cos wt minus pi over 2"] nodeD["Tap 2
cos wt minus pi"] nodeE["Tap 3
cos wt minus 3pi over 2"] nodeF["C0 C1 C2 C3
Correlations"] nodeG["I equals C0 minus C2
Q equals C1 minus C3"] nodeH["phi equals arctan Q over I"] nodeI["A equals half sqrt I squared plus Q squared"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI ``` --- ## 4.3 ToF-Based Robot Localization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Robot Pose x hat t minus 1"] nodeB["Predict Step
Wheel Odometry plus IMU"] nodeC["Predicted Pose x bar t"] nodeD["ToF Camera Capture
Depth Image"] nodeE["Point Cloud Back Projection
Pinhole Model"] nodeF["Feature Extraction
Planes Corners SHOT"] nodeG["Map Matching
ICP or NDT"] nodeH["Innovation Delta z
Measured minus Predicted"] nodeI["Kalman Gain K t
From ToF Covariance"] nodeJ["Update Step
x hat t equals x bar t plus K Delta z"] nodeK["Output Pose x hat t"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeC --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeA ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The **predict** path (left) uses dead-reckoning from wheel encoders + IMU. The **measurement** path (right) produces a point cloud, extracts features, and matches them against a stored map. The **Kalman filter** fuses both. The ToF covariance matrix $\mathbf{R}_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_d^2)$ directly controls how much weight the filter places on the camera measurement. --- ## 4.4 Multi-Frequency Phase Unwrapping Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Raw Phase phi1 at f1
20 MHz"] nodeB["Raw Phase phi2 at f2
19 MHz"] nodeC["Phase Difference
phi12 equals phi1 minus phi2"] nodeD["Synthetic Wavelength
Lambda equals c over 2 delta f"] nodeE["Coarse Depth dcoarse
From Lambda"] nodeF["Fine Depth dfine
From f1"] nodeG["Unwrapped Absolute Depth
d equals dfine plus n Lambda"] nodeH["Confidence Check
Amp and SNR"] nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **State and explain the principle of operation of a Time-of-Flight (ToF) camera. Mention two advantages over stereo vision for indoor robot localization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A ToF camera emits an amplitude-modulated near-infrared light wave of frequency $f_m$ (typically 10 – 100 MHz) and measures the **phase shift** $\varphi$ between the emitted and reflected wave at every pixel using correlation (four-bucket method). The depth is recovered as $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \varphi}{4\pi f_m}$. **Advantages over stereo (any two):** 1. Generates **per-pixel depth** without solving the correspondence problem → works on **textureless** walls and floors. 2. **Active illumination** → operates in complete darkness, ideal for night-shift service robots. 3. **Solid-state** with no moving parts → lower maintenance and higher MTBF than mechanical scanning units. *(1 mark: principle, 1 mark: phase/depth equation, 1 mark: any two valid advantages.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]** **What is the maximum unambiguous range of a ToF camera with modulation frequency $f_m = 25$ MHz? If the true object distance is 9 m, what is the phenomenon observed and how is it mitigated?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 f_m} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 25 \times 10^{6}} = 6\; \text{m}$$ Since the true distance (9 m) exceeds $d_{\max}$, the phenomenon is **phase wrapping** (also called *phase ambiguity*). The measured phase wraps around to a value in $[0, 2\pi)$, producing an **incorrect** range (here, the camera would report $\approx 3$ m). **Mitigation:** Use **multi-frequency modulation**. Two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ produce a synthetic wavelength $\Lambda = \dfrac{c}{2 \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert}$ large enough to cover the true range; phase-unwrapping algorithms then recover the absolute depth. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, two 14-mark questions are provided; the student answers **one**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the operating principle of a **continuous-wave indirect Time-of-Flight (iToF) camera**. Derive the expression for depth $d$ in terms of the modulation frequency $f_m$ and the measured phase $\varphi$. Compute the depth for $f_m = 20$ MHz, $\varphi = \pi/3$ rad. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the major **systematic and random error sources** in ToF camera measurements. How does each affect depth accuracy? What is the typical depth noise model used when integrating a ToF sensor into an EKF-based localization filter for a service robot? --- ### Model Answer to Question A #### Part (a) — Operating Principle and Depth Derivation **Block Diagram** (see Section 4.1, functional architecture flow). **Principle:** The ToF camera consists of (i) a modulation unit generating a sinusoidal drive at $f_m$, (ii) an IR LED emitter array (typically 850 nm or 940 nm), (iii) a receiving lens, (iv) a CMOS image sensor with **per-pixel demodulation** pixels, (v) a correlator performing the four-bucket correlation, and (vi) a DSP computing depth, amplitude, and intensity per pixel. **Derivation** (see Section 3.1 of this module for the full derivation): $$\varphi = \frac{4 \pi f_m d}{c} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d = \frac{c \, \varphi}{4 \pi f_m}$$ **Numerical computation:** For $f_m = 20$ MHz, $\varphi = \pi/3$ rad: $$d = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (\pi/3)}{4\pi \cdot (20 \times 10^{6})} = \frac{10^{8}}{80 \times 10^{6}} = 1.25\; \text{m}$$ #### Part (b) — Error Sources and EKF Noise Model | Error Source | Type | Effect on Depth | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | Phase wrapping | Systematic | Catastrophic (jumps of $d_{\max}$) | Multi-frequency | | Multi-Path Interference | Systematic | Bias towards closer surface | Coding / averaging | | Mixed/flying pixels | Systematic | Ghost points at depth edges | Edge-aware filtering | | Ambient IR (sun) | Random | Increased variance | Narrow-band filter | | Motion during exposure | Random | Sinusoidal distortion | Short integration / global shutter | | Temperature drift | Systematic | Constant offset $b_{\text{ToF}}$ | Calibration per temperature | | Photon shot noise | Random | $\sigma \propto 1/A$ | Larger integration time | **EKF Noise Model.** The ToF range measurement along the optical axis is modeled as: $$\tilde{d}(u,v) = d^{\star}(u,v) + b_{\text{ToF}} + n_{\text{ToF}}$$ with the variance: $$\sigma_{d}^{2}(u,v) = \sigma_{d,\text{sys}}^{2} + \frac{\sigma_{d,\text{noise}}^{2}}{A^{2}(u,v)}$$ In the EKF measurement update, each pixel contributes an innovation $$\nu_i = \tilde{d}_i - \hat{d}_i$$ with the Jacobian $\mathbf{H}_i = \dfrac{\partial d_i}{\partial \mathbf{x}_t}$ computed from the back-projection geometry. The Kalman gain is: $$\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_t^{-} \mathbf{H}^{\top} \left(\mathbf{H}\mathbf{P}_t^{-} \mathbf{H}^{\top} + \mathbf{R}_t\right)^{-1}$$ where $\mathbf{R}_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{d,i}^{2})$ aggregates the per-pixel variances, weighted by the amplitude $A$ (low-amplitude pixels are trusted less). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **four-bucket correlation method** used in iToF cameras to extract the phase $\varphi$ and amplitude $A$ from the reflected signal. Derive the in-phase $I$ and quadrature $Q$ components and show how $\varphi = \arctan(Q/I)$ is obtained. **(b) [7 marks]** A mobile service robot uses a ToF camera to localize against a-priori 3D point cloud map. Describe the **point cloud back-projection** step from depth image to 3D world coordinates, the **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** registration, and the way the ToF amplitude is used to weight residuals in the EKF measurement update. --- ### Model Answer to Question B (Highlights) #### Part (a) — Four-Bucket Method The received signal is correlated with four reference sinusoids at phase shifts $0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$. The four correlations $C_0, C_1, C_2, C_3$ are: $$\begin{aligned} C_0 &= A \cos\varphi + B \\ C_1 &= A \sin\varphi \\ C_2 &= -A \cos\varphi + B \\ C_3 &= -A \sin\varphi \end{aligned}$$ Form the differences: $$I = C_0 - C_2 = 2A \cos\varphi, \qquad Q = C_1 - C_3 = 2A \sin\varphi$$ Then: $$\varphi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{Q}{I}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{C_1 - C_3}{C_0 - C_2}\right)$$ $$A = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}}$$ The ambient offset $B$ is cancelled by the subtraction; the phase is therefore robust to direct-current ambient light. #### Part (b) — Back-Projection, ICP, and Amplitude Weighting **Back-projection** of pixel $(u,v)$ with depth $d$: $$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x) d}{f_x},\quad Y_c = \frac{(v - c_y) d}{f_y},\quad Z_c = d$$ Transform to world frame: $\mathbf{P}_w = \mathbf{T}_{\text{robot}\to\text{world}} \, \mathbf{P}_c$. **ICP registration** iterates: 1. For each point in the measured cloud, find the closest point in the map. 2. Compute a 6-DoF rigid transform $\mathbf{T}$ minimizing: $$\mathbf{T}^{\star} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{T}} \sum_{i} w_i \,\Vert \mathbf{T}\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{q}_i \Vert^{2}$$ 3. Apply $\mathbf{T}$ to the measured cloud, re-iterate until convergence. **Amplitude weighting.** The residual weight is: $$w_i = \frac{A_i^{2}}{\sum_j A_j^{2}}$$ Low-amplitude (low-confidence) pixels contribute little to the transform estimate, preventing spurious matches near distant or low-reflectivity surfaces from corrupting the localization. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake 1:** Students often write $d = c \varphi / (2 \pi f_m)$ instead of $d = c \varphi / (4 \pi f_m)$. The factor of **4** comes from round-trip + demodulation. Lose 1 mark. > - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **back-projection sign convention** — the camera $Z$ axis points **forward**, so the depth is along $+Z$. Don't write $Z = -d$. > - **Mistake 3:** Conflating the **radial** distance with the **axial** depth; use $d_{\text{radial}} = d / \cos\alpha$ for off-axis pixels. > - **Mistake 4:** Omitting units in numerical answers. KTU examiners **strictly deduct ½ mark** for missing units in Part A. > - **Mistake 5:** In ICP derivations, students forget the **closed-form** SVD step (Arun's method). Examiner expects the optimization equation even if not solved fully. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅ - **Master equation:** $d = \dfrac{c \varphi}{4 \pi f_m}$ — the **single most important formula** in this module. - **Maximum unambiguous range:** $d_{\max} = c / (2 f_m)$ — choose $f_m$ based on room size. - **Four-bucket method:** $I = C_0 - C_2$, $Q = C_1 - C_3$, $\varphi = \arctan(Q/I)$, $A = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}}$. - **Pulsed (dToF):** $d = c \Delta t / 2$. - **Multi-frequency:** synthetic wavelength $\Lambda = c / (2 \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert)$ resolves ambiguity. - **Back-projection:** $X_c = (u - c_x) d / f_x$, $Y_c = (v - c_y) d / f_y$, $Z_c = d$. - **Depth noise:** $\sigma_d^{2} = \sigma_{d,\text{sys}}^{2} + \sigma_{d,\text{noise}}^{2} / A^{2}$ — amplitude-weighted. - **Localization pipeline:** Depth → Point cloud → Features → ICP/NDT → Pose in EKF. - **Amplitude is confidence:** use $A^2$ as ICP weight. - **Major errors to know:** phase wrapping, multi-path interference, mixed pixels, motion artifacts, ambient IR, temperature drift. - **Physical constant to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (use $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ for high-precision numericals). - **ToF vs stereo:** ToF wins on textureless surfaces and dark environments. - **ToF vs LiDAR:** ToF is solid-state, lower cost, shorter range; LiDAR is mechanical, higher cost, longer range. - **Typical ToF specs (2024 service robots):** 20 MHz modulation, 7.5 m max range, 320×240 resolution, 30 fps, ±1 cm accuracy at 1 m. - **Engineering tip:** When fusing ToF with wheel odometry, the **systematic bias** $b_{\text{ToF}}$ should be estimated **online** (e.g., as part of the EKF state) — otherwise localization drifts on long corridors. - **Examiner hot keywords to use in answers:** "active illumination," "phase demodulation," "four-bucket correlation," "amplitude-weighted residual," "point cloud registration," "EKF measurement update." > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"P-F-I-A-C"** for the ToF localization chain: > **P**hase measurement → **F**our-bucket correlation → **I**nverse pinhole back-projection → **A**mplitude-weighted ICP → **C**losed-loop EKF pose.

topics.title: Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Navigation in Field and Service Robotics** is defined as the autonomous computational process by which a mobile robot determines its current pose (position and orientation) within an operational environment, plans a collision-free trajectory from a start configuration to a goal configuration, and executes motion commands while continuously updating its state estimate using onboard sensors and environmental representations.

Navigation is a **multi-layered cognitive capability** that integrates three tightly coupled sub-problems:

| Sub-Problem | Formal Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Perception** | Environmental Sensing | Acquiring data from exteroceptive and proprioceptive sensors |
| **2. Localization** | Pose Estimation | Determining the robot's pose relative to a global or local frame |
| **3. Path Planning** | Motion Decision | Generating an optimal, collision-free trajectory to the goal |

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4 emphasizes the following architecture for navigation:

$$\text{Navigation Pipeline} = \underbrace{f_{localize}(\mathbf{z}_{1:t}, \mathbf{u}_{1:t})}_{\text{Localization}} \rightarrow \underbrace{f_{plan}(\mathcal{M}, \mathbf{x}_{cur}, \mathbf{x}_{goal})}_{\text{Planning}} \rightarrow \underbrace{f_{control}(\mathbf{x}_{cur}, \mathbf{x}_{ref})}_{\text{Control}}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{z}_{1:t}$ is the sensor measurement history up to time $t$
- $\mathbf{u}_{1:t}$ is the control input history
- $\mathcal{M}$ is the environment map
- $\mathbf{x}_{cur}$ and $\mathbf{x}_{goal}$ are the current and goal robot states

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The navigation module specifically focuses on *outdoor* and *service* environments (unstructured, dynamic, GPS-denied or partially-denied). Students must distinguish between **structured indoor** (warehouse AGV) and **unstructured outdoor** (agricultural, search-rescue) navigation paradigms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Navigating an Unfamiliar Mall"**
> 
> Imagine walking into a massive shopping mall you have never visited. You want to reach a specific store (the *goal*). Here's what happens in your brain:
> 
> 1. **"Where am I?"** — You glance at the mall directory, your phone GPS, or landmarks. → *This is **Localization***
> 2. **"How do I get there?"** — You trace a route in your mind, avoiding escalators, walls, and crowd clusters. → *This is **Path Planning***
> 3. **"Watch out for that toddler!"** — As you walk, you reactively dodge obstacles in real time. → *This is **Obstacle Avoidance / Reactive Control***
> 4. **"Did I just pass the food court?"** — You constantly recheck landmarks to know your progress. → *This is **State Estimation with Sensor Fusion***
> 
> A **field robot navigating a vineyard, a hospital service robot, or a planetary rover** does *exactly* the same — but with LiDAR, IMUs, encoders, and algorithms like A*, RRT, and Extended Kalman Filters (EKF).

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Physical Constants for Navigation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Navigation Metrics (used in KTU 2024 Scheme lab evaluations and ESE paper questions):**
> 
> - **Path Length ($L_{path}$)** — measured in **meters (m)**
> - **Time-to-Goal ($T_g$)** — measured in **seconds (s)**
> - **Clearance ($d_{clear}$)** — minimum distance to any obstacle, **meters (m)**
> - **Smoothness ($S$)** — integral of squared jerk, $\int_0^T \dddot{\mathbf{x}}^2 \, dt$, units $\mathbf{m^2/s^6}$
> - **Localization Error ($\sigma_{pose}$)** — standard deviation of pose estimate, **meters (m)** and **radians (rad)**
> - **Computational Cost ($C_{comp}$)** — execution time per cycle, **milliseconds (ms)**

### 1.4 Classification of Navigation (KTU Board Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes four canonical navigation paradigms:**

**1.4.1 Reactive (Behavior-Based) Navigation**
- Direct sensor-to-action mapping (e.g., Braitenberg vehicles, Subsumption Architecture)
- **No map required**; purely stimulus-response
- Fast response, but myopic (no look-ahead)

**1.4.2 Deliberative (Map-Based) Navigation**
- Full environment model (occupancy grid, topological, semantic)
- Global path planner + local trajectory generator
- Computationally expensive but globally optimal

**1.4.3 Hybrid (Three-Layer) Navigation**
- Combines **deliberative** (top) + **executive** (middle) + **reactive** (bottom) layers
- Industry standard for service robots

**1.4.4 Learning-Based Navigation**
- End-to-end deep reinforcement learning (DRL)
- Imitation learning from human demonstrations
- Emerging paradigm; KTU 2024 syllabus expects awareness, not derivation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Navigation Stack — Layered Architecture

The **KTU 2024 Module 4** curriculum describes a navigation stack as a hierarchy of competence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 0 (Hardware Interface):** Driver layer interfacing with wheels, motors, IMU, encoders
> **Layer 1 (Perception):** Sensor data acquisition, filtering, feature extraction
> **Layer 2 (Localization):** Pose estimation relative to map
> **Layer 3 (Mapping):** World model maintenance
> **Layer 4 (Global Planning):** Coarse path from start to goal
> **Layer 5 (Local Planning):** Velocity commands avoiding immediate obstacles
> **Layer 6 (Behavior Coordination):** Arbitration between competing behaviors

## 2.2 Map Representations (KTU High-Yield Topic)

A mobile robot's environmental representation is a critical academic focus. KTU 2024 expects students to articulate the **trade-offs** of each representation:

### 2.2.1 Metric (Grid) Maps
The environment is discretized into uniform cells of size $\Delta r$:

$$\mathcal{M} = \{m_{i,j} \;|\; i \in [1, N_x], \; j \in [1, N_y]\}, \quad m_{i,j} \in [0, 1]$$

- $m_{i,j} = 1$ → **occupied**
- $m_{i,j} = 0$ → **free**
- $0 < m_{i,j} < 1$ → **unknown** (probabilistic)

### 2.2.2 Topological Maps
The environment is represented as a **graph** $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$:
- **Vertices** ($V$): distinctive places (rooms, intersections)
- **Edges** ($E$): traversable connections

### 2.2.3 Semantic Maps
Objects in the environment are labeled with **high-level categories** (e.g., "kitchen", "door", "table"). Built using deep neural networks (YOLO, Mask R-CNN) fused with geometric maps.

### 2.2.4 Hybrid Metric-Topological Maps
Used in large-scale service robotics (museums, hospitals). Combine local metric grids with global topological structure for efficient hierarchical planning.

## 2.3 Localization — The Foundation of Navigation

### 2.3.1 Dead Reckoning
The simplest localization approach. The pose is updated by integrating control inputs:

$$x_t = x_{t-1} + v_t \cos(\theta_{t-1}) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$y_t = y_{t-1} + v_t \sin(\theta_{t-1}) \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\theta_t = \theta_{t-1} + \omega_t \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Drift Accumulation:** Dead reckoning error grows **unbounded** with distance. Standard deviation of position error scales as $\sigma_{drift} \propto \sigma_v \cdot t$. For long missions, sensor fusion is mandatory.

### 2.3.2 Probabilistic Localization — Bayes Filter
The general recursive Bayesian formulation:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_t) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \cdot \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

Where:
- $\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t)$ = posterior belief of state $\mathbf{x}_t$
- $p(\mathbf{z}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_t)$ = measurement model (likelihood)
- $p(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t)$ = motion model
- $\eta$ = normalization constant

### 2.3.3 Kalman Filter (KF) for Linear Systems
Under linear-Gaussian assumptions, the KF provides the **optimal estimator**:

**Prediction Step:**
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t\vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1 \vert t-1} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{P}_{t-1 \vert t-1} \mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$$

**Update Step:**
$$\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})$$

$$\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1}$$

### 2.3.4 Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Non-Linear Systems
For non-linear motion/measurement models, the EKF linearizes via Jacobian:

$$\mathbf{F}_t = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t}, \quad \mathbf{H}_t = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \right|_{\mathbf{x}_t}$$

The Jacobians replace the matrices $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{H}$ in the KF equations above.

## 2.4 Path Planning Algorithms (The Most-Tested Topic in KTU 2024)

### 2.4.1 Graph Search Planners

**A* Algorithm** — The workhorse of mobile robot navigation:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

Where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to node $n$
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimated cost from $n$ to goal
- $f(n)$ = total estimated cost through $n$

**Admissibility Condition:**
$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \text{(heuristic never overestimates)}$$

**Consistency Condition:**
$$h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n') \quad \text{(triangle inequality)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Heuristics in KTU Board Exam Questions:**
> - **Manhattan distance:** $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{goal} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{goal} \vert$ (4-connected grid)
> - **Euclidean distance:** $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{goal})^2 + (y_n - y_{goal})^2}$ (8-connected grid)
> - **Octile distance:** $h(n) = (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min(\Delta x, \Delta y) + \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ (8-connected with diagonals)

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** — A* with $h(n) = 0$ for all $n$. Guarantees optimality, but explores more nodes.

### 2.4.2 Sampling-Based Planners

**Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT):**
1. Sample a random configuration $\mathbf{x}_{rand}$ in free space
2. Find nearest node $\mathbf{x}_{near}$ in the tree
3. Extend toward $\mathbf{x}_{rand}$ by step size $\epsilon$
4. Add new node $\mathbf{x}_{new}$ to the tree
5. Repeat until goal reached or budget exhausted

**RRT* (Asymptotically Optimal):** Rewires the tree to minimize path cost. Convergence: $\lim_{n \to \infty} c_n = c^*$.

### 2.4.3 Artificial Potential Field (APF) Method

The robot moves in a combined potential field:

$$U_{total}(\mathbf{x}) = U_{att}(\mathbf{x}) + U_{rep}(\mathbf{x})$$

**Attractive Potential** (pulls robot to goal):
$$U_{att}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \vert^2$$

**Repulsive Potential** (pushes robot from obstacles):
$$U_{rep}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left(\frac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{x})} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2 & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{x}) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{if } \rho(\mathbf{x}) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Force (negative gradient of potential):**
$$\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = -\nabla U_{total}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{x})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation of APF — Local Minima:** The robot can get trapped at points where $\mathbf{F}_{att} + \mathbf{F}_{rep} = \mathbf{0}$ but the goal is not reached. KTU 2024 board exam questions frequently test this pitfall.

## 2.5 Reactive Obstacle Avoidance

### 2.5.1 Vector Field Histogram (VFH)
A polar histogram of obstacle density is constructed in the robot's local frame. The robot selects the sector with the lowest density along the direction closest to the goal.

### 2.5.2 Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)
Samples admissible velocities $(v, \omega)$ and scores them:

$$\text{score}(v, \omega) = \alpha \cdot \text{heading}(v, \omega) + \beta \cdot \text{dist}(v, \omega) + \gamma \cdot \text{velocity}(v, \omega)$$

### 2.5.3 Time Elastic Band (TEB)
Optimizes trajectory timing as an elastic band deformed by obstacle and kinematic constraints. Solved as a non-linear optimization via g2o or similar.

## 2.6 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table before any KTU ESE — questions often directly substitute values into these equations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dead Reckoning Update | $x_t = x_{t-1} + v \Delta t \cos\theta_{t-1}$ | Position in **m**, $\theta$ in **rad** |
| 2 | Bayes Filter Belief | $\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_t) = \eta \cdot p(\mathbf{z}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_t) \int p(\mathbf{x}_t \vert \mathbf{x}_{t-1}, \mathbf{u}_t) \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ | Probabilistic, dimensionless |
| 3 | Kalman Prediction | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t$ | Linear system, $n$-dim state |
| 4 | Kalman Covariance Update | $\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{P}_{t-1} \mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$ | $n \times n$ matrix, units $\mathbf{m^2}$ |
| 5 | Kalman Gain | $\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$ | Balances process vs. measurement noise |
| 6 | A* Cost Function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | $g$ actual, $h$ heuristic, $f$ total |
| 7 | Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Guarantees optimality |
| 8 | Manhattan Heuristic | $h(n) = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | For 4-connected grid |
| 9 | Euclidean Heuristic | $h(n) = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | Admissible for 8-connected grid |
| 10 | Attractive Potential | $U_{att} = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \rho_{goal}^2$ | Conic or parabolic |
| 11 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} (\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0})^2$ | Within $\rho_0$ of obstacle |
| 12 | Total Force | $\mathbf{F} = -\nabla U_{total}$ | Vector in **N** (newtons) |
| 13 | DWA Score | $\text{score} = \alpha \cdot \text{heading} + \beta \cdot \text{dist} + \gamma \cdot \text{vel}$ | Weighted linear combination |
| 14 | Drift Error Growth | $\sigma_{drift} \propto \sigma_v \cdot t$ | Dead reckoning, unbounded |
| 15 | Path Length | $L_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert \mathbf{x}_{i+1} - \mathbf{x}_i \vert$ | Sum of segment lengths, **m** |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications of Robot Navigation (KTU 2024 Case Studies):**
> 
> - **Agricultural Field Robotics:** Autonomous tractors (John Deere, AGCO) use RTK-GPS + IMU + LiDAR to navigate rows of crops with **centimeter-level accuracy**
> - **Warehouse Automation:** Amazon Kiva robots navigate via 2D barcode floor markers + on-board cameras. A* + traffic-aware replanning
> - **Hospital Service Robots:** TUG robots deliver medications; navigate via SLAM with pre-mapped floor plans
> - **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs):** Navigate via DVL (Doppler Velocity Log) + USBL + terrain-aided localization
> - **Planetary Rovers (NASA Mars rovers):** Visual SLAM with stereo cameras; path planning considers slippage, terrain roughness, and energy budget
> - **Disaster Response Robots:** Snake-arm and tracked robots navigate collapsed buildings via thermal cameras + LiDAR SLAM in GPS-denied rubble

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Filter → Kalman Filter

This derivation is a **favourite KTU ESE question** (typically 7-mark sub-part).

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Assumption:** The state transition and measurement models are **linear** and **Gaussian**:
> 
> $$\mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{Q})$$
> 
> $$\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{H} \mathbf{x}_t + \boldsymbol{\delta}_t, \quad \boldsymbol{\delta}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R})$$

**Step 1: Initialize the belief with a Gaussian prior**

The belief at time $t-1$ is a multivariate Gaussian:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}, \mathbf{P}_{t-1})$$

This means:

$$\text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^n \vert \mathbf{P}_{t-1} \vert}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1})^T \mathbf{P}_{t-1}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_{t-1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1})\right)$$

**Step 2: Apply the motion model (prediction)**

Given the linear motion model, the predicted state is also Gaussian. The new mean is:

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \int (\mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t) \cdot \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$$

$$= \mathbf{A} \underbrace{\int \mathbf{x}_{t-1} \cdot \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t \underbrace{\int \text{bel}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) \, d\mathbf{x}_{t-1}}_{1}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1} + \mathbf{B} \mathbf{u}_t$$

**Step 3: Compute the predicted covariance**

$$\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} = E[(\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})(\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})^T]$$

$$\mathbf{x}_t - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t-1}) + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_t$$

Substituting and using the fact that process noise is independent of state:

$$\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{P}_{t-1} \mathbf{A}^T + \mathbf{Q}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Kalman Gain**

The Kalman gain is derived by minimizing the trace of posterior covariance $\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t}$. Setting $\partial \text{tr}(\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t}) / \partial \mathbf{K}_t = 0$:

$$\mathbf{K}_t = \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1} \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Measurement update**

$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1} + \mathbf{K}_t (\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})$$

The term $(\mathbf{z}_t - \mathbf{H} \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t \vert t-1})$ is called the **innovation** or **measurement residual**.

**Step 6: Posterior covariance update**

$$\mathbf{P}_{t \vert t} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_t \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}_{t \vert t-1}$$

**Conclusion:** The Kalman filter is **the optimal linear estimator** in the minimum mean squared error (MMSE) sense for linear-Gaussian systems. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example: A* on a 4×4 Grid (Board Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-typical problem:** A 4×4 grid with obstacles. Find the optimal path from **S(0,0)** to **G(3,3)** using A* with Manhattan heuristic. **8-connected** motion allowed, with **diagonal cost = $\sqrt{2}$**.

**Given:**
- Start: $S = (0, 0)$
- Goal: $G = (3, 3)$
- Obstacles: cells $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$
- Heuristic: $h(n) = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$

**Step 1: Initialize the open set with $S$**
- $g(S) = 0$
- $h(S) = \vert 3 - 0 \vert + \vert 3 - 0 \vert = 6$
- $f(S) = g + h = 0 + 6 = 6$

**Step 2: Expand $S(0,0)$** — Possible neighbors: $(1,0)$, $(0,1)$, $(1,1)$ (obstacle, skip)

For $(1, 0)$:
- $g(1,0) = 1$ (cardinal move)
- $h(1,0) = \vert 3-1 \vert + \vert 3-0 \vert = 5$
- $f(1,0) = 1 + 5 = 6$

For $(0, 1)$:
- $g(0,1) = 1$
- $h(0,1) = \vert 3-0 \vert + \vert 3-1 \vert = 5$
- $f(0,1) = 1 + 5 = 6$

**Step 3: Expand node with lowest $f$ → tie between $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$, pick $(1,0)$**

Expand $(1, 0)$ — Neighbors: $(2, 0)$, $(1, 1)$ (obstacle), $(0, 0)$ (parent), $(2, 1)$

For $(2, 0)$:
- $g(2,0) = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $h(2,0) = 4$
- $f(2,0) = 6$

For $(2, 1)$:
- $g(2,1) = 1 + \sqrt{2} \approx 2.414$ (diagonal)
- $h(2,1) = \vert 3-2 \vert + \vert 3-1 \vert = 3$
- $f(2,1) = 2.414 + 3 = 5.414$ ← **best so far**

**Step 4: Continue the expansion systematically**

Eventually, the algorithm extracts $G(3,3)$ from the open set.

**Final optimal path:**
$$S(0,0) \rightarrow (1,0) \rightarrow (2,1) \rightarrow (3,2) \rightarrow G(3,3)$$

**Total path cost:**
$$L_{path} = 1 + \sqrt{2} + \sqrt{2} + 1 = 2 + 2\sqrt{2} \approx 4.828 \text{ m}$$

This example is **canonical** for KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Path Planning Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dijkstra** | Complete | Optimal | $O(V^2)$ or $O(E \log V)$ | $O(V)$ | Known graph, small map |
| **A*** | Complete (with admissibility) | Optimal | $O(b^d)$ worst case | $O(b^d)$ | Static grid, known heuristic |
| **BFS** | Complete | Optimal (uniform cost) | $O(V + E)$ | $O(V)$ | Unweighted graph |
| **RRT** | Probabilistically complete | Sub-optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | High-dimensional, holonomic |
| **RRT*** | Asymptotically complete | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Long-horizon planning |
| **D* Lite** | Complete | Optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Unknown / dynamic env |
| **APF** | Not complete | Local optimum | $O(k \cdot n)$ | $O(n)$ | Real-time local avoidance |
| **DWA** | Complete (in velocity space) | Local optimum | $O(v \cdot \omega)$ samples | Constant | Local reactive avoidance |

## 3.4 Derivation: Attractive + Repulsive Force in APF

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Derivation for 7-Mark Board Question**

**Setup:** A point robot at $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)^T$ must navigate to $\mathbf{x}_{goal} = (x_g, y_g)^T$ while avoiding a circular obstacle centered at $\mathbf{x}_{obs} = (x_o, y_o)^T$ with radius $R$.

**Attractive potential** (conic or parabolic):

$$U_{att}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \cdot \rho_{goal}^2(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\rho_{goal}(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(x - x_g)^2 + (y - y_g)^2}$.

**Attractive force** (negative gradient):

$$\mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{x}) = -\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att} \cdot \rho_{goal}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}_{goal}$$

where $\hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}_{goal} = \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{goal}}{\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{goal} \vert}$ is the unit vector toward the goal.

**Repulsive potential:**

Let $\rho_{obs} = \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{obs} \vert$ and influence distance $\rho_0$:

$$U_{rep}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{obs}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \rho_{obs} \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho_{obs} > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Repulsive force:**

Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{x}) = -\nabla U_{rep} = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{obs}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho_{obs}^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho_{obs}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}$$

Since $\frac{\partial \rho_{obs}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{obs}}{\rho_{obs}} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}_{obs}$:

$$\mathbf{F}_{rep}(\mathbf{x}) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho_{obs}} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho_{obs}^2} \cdot \hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}}_{obs}$$

**Total force on robot:**

$$\mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{F}_{att}(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{N_{obs}} \mathbf{F}_{rep,i}(\mathbf{x})$$

**Robot velocity command** (proportional control):

$$\mathbf{v}_{cmd} = k_v \cdot \mathbf{F}_{total}(\mathbf{x})$$

**Conclusion:** The robot's motion is governed by the vector sum of goal-pulling and obstacle-pushing forces. $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Overall Navigation Stack — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors Layer<br/>LiDAR, Camera, IMU, GPS, Encoders] --> B[Perception Module<br/>Feature Extraction, Filtering]
    B --> C[Localization Module<br/>EKF, Particle Filter, AMCL]
    B --> D[Mapping Module<br/>Occupancy Grid, SLAM]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Global Planner<br/>A Star, Dijkstra, RRT]
    E --> F[Local Planner<br/>DWA, TEB, APF]
    F --> G[Motion Controller<br/>PID, MPC, Pure Pursuit]
    G --> H[Motor Drivers<br/>Wheel Actuators]
    H --> I[Robot Motion in Environment]
    I --> A
    C --> J[Behavior Coordinator<br/>Subsumption, Arbitration]
    J --> F
```

## 4.2 A* Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init[Initialize OPEN with start node S<br/>Set gS = 0, fS = hS<br/>Set CLOSED empty]
    Init --> CheckOpen{OPEN empty?}
    CheckOpen -- Yes --> Fail([Failure: No path])
    CheckOpen -- No --> Pop[Pop node n with lowest f value]
    Pop --> GoalCheck{n is goal?}
    GoalCheck -- Yes --> Success([Return path by backtracking pointers])
    GoalCheck -- No --> MoveClosed[Move n to CLOSED]
    MoveClosed --> Expand[Generate all valid neighbors of n]
    Expand --> Loop[For each neighbor m]
    Loop --> CalcG[Compute tentative gM = gN plus cost of edge]
    CalcG --> CheckG{gM less than gM previous?}
    CheckG -- Yes --> Update[Set parent of m = n<br/>Update gM and fM = gM + hM<br/>Add m to OPEN]
    CheckG -- No --> Skip[Skip m]
    Skip --> More{More neighbors?}
    Update --> More
    More -- Yes --> Loop
    More -- No --> CheckOpen
```

## 4.3 Artificial Potential Field — Force Interaction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Goal((Goal Xg)) -->|Attractive Force Fatt pulls robot| Robot((Robot X))
    Obs1((Obstacle 1)) -->|Repulsive Force Frep1 pushes| Robot
    Obs2((Obstacle 2)) -->|Repulsive Force Frep2 pushes| Robot
    Obs3((Obstacle 3)) -->|Repulsive Force Frep3 pushes| Robot
    Robot -->|Net Force Ftotal drives motion| NextPos((Next Position Xnext))
    NextPos -. updates robot state .-> Robot
    NextPos -->|Check distance to obstacles| NewForces{Within influence radius?}
    NewForces -- Yes --> UpdateForces[Recompute Ftotal]
    NewForces -- No --> GoalReached{At goal?}
    GoalReached -- No --> NextPos
    GoalReached -- Yes --> Done([Navigation Complete])
    UpdateForces --> NextPos
```

## 4.4 Bayes Filter / EKF Localization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0([Time t minus 1]) --> Motion[Apply Motion Model<br/>xPred = f xt-1, ut plus process noise]
    Motion --> PredState[Predicted State x t slash t-1<br/>Covariance P t slash t-1]
    PredState --> PredictDist[Predicted Measurement<br/>zPred = h xPred]
    PredictDist --> Compare[Compare with Actual Sensor Reading zt<br/>Innovation nu = zt - zPred]
    Compare --> ComputeGain[Compute Kalman Gain Kt]
    ComputeGain --> Update[Update State<br/>xt slash t = xPred + Kt times nu]
    Update --> UpdateCov[Update Covariance<br/>Pt slash t = I - Kt H Pt slash t-1]
    UpdateCov --> Output[Output: Posterior bel xt]
    Output --> T1([Time t])
    T1 --> T0
    Output --> Decision{Navigation Decision Required?}
    Decision -- Yes --> PathPlan[Call Path Planner]
    Decision -- No --> T1
```

## 4.5 Hybrid Three-Layer Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopLayer["DELIBERATIVE LAYER (Slow, Global)"]
        Top1[Global Path Planner] --> Top2[Generate coarse path A to B]
        Top2 --> Top3[Update waypoint sequence]
    end
    subgraph MidLayer["EXECUTIVE LAYER (Medium, Coordination)"]
        Mid1[Sequence behaviors] --> Mid2[Resolve conflicts]
        Mid2 --> Mid3[Pass commands to reactive layer]
    end
    subgraph BotLayer["REACTIVE LAYER (Fast, Local)"]
        Bot1[Obstacle avoidance] --> Bot2[Wall following]
        Bot2 --> Bot3[Goal seeking]
    end
    Top3 --> Mid1
    Mid3 --> Bot1
    Bot3 --> Motors[Motor Velocity Commands]
    Motors --> RobotBody[Robot Body]
    RobotBody --> Sensors[Sensor Feedback]
    Sensors --> Bot1
    Sensors --> Top1
```
