**(b)** Derive the gradient-descent update law for an Artificial Potential Field (APF) planner with a single circular obstacle. Show the attractive and repulsive potentials and their gradients explicitly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks):**
The path planning problem is:

$$
\pi^{*} \;=\; \arg\min_{\pi} J(\pi) \;=\; \arg\min_{\pi} \int_{0}^{T} L\!\big(q(t),\, \dot q(t)\big)\, dt
$$

subject to:

$$
q(0) = q_{\text{start}}, \quad q(T) = q_{\text{goal}}, \quad q(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}\;\; \forall t \in [0,T]
$$

Where:
- $q(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the configuration vector (e.g., $(x,y,\theta)$).
- $J(\pi)$ is the cost — typically path length, time, or energy.
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ excludes obstacle regions.
- The first-order optimality condition (Pontryagin's Minimum Principle) is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot q}\right) = 0
$$

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**
- Attractive potential: $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \Vert q - q_g \Vert^{2}$, gradient $\nabla U_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}}(q - q_g)$. **(2 marks)**
- Repulsive potential: $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} (\rho^{-1} - \rho_0^{-1})^{2}$ for $\rho \leq \rho_0$, else 0. **(2 marks)**
- Gradient computation via chain rule (full derivation as in Section 3.1, Steps 5–7). **(2 marks)**
- Final update: $q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha F(q_k)$ where $F = -\nabla U$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast sample-based planners (RRT, RRT*) and search-based planners (A*, D* Lite) for obstacle avoidance. Which is preferred in dynamic environments and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot must travel from $(0,0)$ to $(10,0)$. A circular obstacle of radius $r = 1\,\text{m}$ is centered at $(5,0)$. Design an APF planner with $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$, $k_{\text{rep}} = 5.0$, $\rho_0 = 2.0$, and compute the repulsive force at the point $q = (4.5,\, 0)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | RRT / RRT* | A* / D* Lite |
|---|---|---|
| Representation | Continuous $\mathcal{C}$-space | Discrete grid |
| Optimality | RRT: sub-optimal; RRT*: asymptotic | A*: optimal (admissible $h$) |
| Completeness | Probabilistic | Deterministic (grid) |
| Replanning | Needs full re-run | D* Lite: incremental |
| High-DOF | Excellent (manipulators) | Poor (curse of dimensionality) |

**Preferred in dynamic environments:** D* Lite — because it incrementally updates edge costs without re-planning from scratch. **(1 mark synthesis)**

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**
Given: $q = (4.5, 0)$, $q_{\text{obs}} = (5, 0)$, $\rho = \Vert q - q_{\text{obs}} \Vert = 0.5$.

Since $\rho = 0.5 \leq \rho_0 = 2.0$, the repulsive potential is active:

$$
U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2}\, (5.0)\!\left(\frac{1}{0.5} - \frac{1}{2.0}\right)^{2} = \frac{5}{2}(2 - 0.5)^{2} = \frac{5}{2}(2.25) = 5.625\,\text{J}
$$

Force magnitude:

$$
F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}}\!\left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)\frac{1}{\rho^{2}} = 5.0 \cdot 1.5 \cdot \frac{1}{0.25} = 30\,\text{N}
$$

Direction is along $(q - q_{\text{obs}})/\rho = (-1, 0)$, so:

$$
\vec F_{\text{rep}} = (-30,\, 0)\,\text{N}
$$

The robot is pushed in the $-\hat x$ direction (away from the obstacle). **(1 mark for physical interpretation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Workspace and Configuration Space** (−2 marks). Remember: Workspace = physical world, C-space = robot pose space.
> 2. **Forgetting boundary conditions** when defining $U_{\text{rep}}$ — you must specify $\rho > \rho_0 \Rightarrow U_{\text{rep}} = 0$.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the gradient update** — the force is $-\nabla U$, so the update is $q_{k+1} = q_k - \alpha \nabla U$.
> 4. **Not addressing local minima** in APF — the examiner expects mention of escape strategies for full marks.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical problems (−0.5 to −1 mark per KTU rubrics).
> 6. **Omitting the diagram** in 14-mark answers — even a rough sketch earns presentation marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Path planning = constrained optimization** in $\mathcal{C}$-space: minimize $J(\pi)$ subject to $q(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$.
- [x] **Three pillars:** (1) environment representation, (2) cost function, (3) optimizer.
- [x] **APF potentials:** $U_{\text{att}} = \tfrac{1}{2}k_{\text{att}}\Vert q - q_g \Vert^2$; $U_{\text{rep}} = \tfrac{1}{2}k_{\text{rep}}(\rho^{-1} - \rho_0^{-1})^2$ for $\rho \leq \rho_0$.
- [x] **Force is the negative gradient:** $F = -\nabla U$.
- [x] **Update rule:** $q_{k+1} = q_k + \alpha F(q_k)$.
- [x] **A\* optimal only with admissible heuristic** $h(n) \leq h^{*}(n)$.
- [x] **RRT\* is asymptotically optimal**, RRT is not.
- [x] **D\* Lite** is preferred for dynamic re-planning.
- [x] **Local minima** are the chief weakness of APF — use navigation functions or hybrid planners.
- [x] **Smoothness cost** $J_\kappa = \int \kappa^2\, dt$ improves tracking and comfort.
- [x] **Always include a block diagram** in 14-mark answers for presentation credit.
- [x] **Mention $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$ inflation** if the robot has non-zero footprint.
- [x] **Units matter:** path length in m, time in s, force in N, energy in J.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization of Robot Behavior and Interaction for Obstacle Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization of Robot Behavior and Interaction for Obstacle Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Field and Service Robotics, **Optimization of Robot Behavior and Interaction for Obstacle Avoidance** refers to the formal mathematical procedure of computing a robot's optimal control inputs, trajectories, or behavioral policies that minimize a pre-defined cost function (e.g., travel time, energy, deviation from a goal) while simultaneously satisfying a set of hard and soft constraints imposed by static and dynamic obstacles, the robot's kinematic/dynamic limits, and the social rules governing its interaction with humans or other agents.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, this topic integrates three sub-domains:
- **Trajectory Optimization** — generating time-parameterized paths in the robot's configuration space.
- **Behavior Optimization** — selecting discrete actions (e.g., stop, yield, overtake) in a Markov Decision Process (MDP) or Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework.
- **Interaction Modeling** — embedding reciprocal behavior models so the robot respects other agents' intent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The optimization of robot behavior for obstacle avoidance is the bridge between *global path planning* (which gives a coarse geometric route) and *local reactive control* (which executes safe motion). Optimization is what fuses the two layers into a single, mathematically rigorous decision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine a self-driving car approaching a busy roundabout. The car has a **goal** (exit the roundabout north), a set of **obstacles** (other cars, pedestrians), and **rules of interaction** (don't tailgate, yield to merging traffic). The "optimization" is what the car's onboard computer does thousands of times per second:

1. It *proposes* several possible future motion candidates.
2. It *scores* each candidate with a cost function — penalizing near-collisions, rewarding smooth progress toward the goal, and giving a small penalty for violating social norms (e.g., cutting off a pedestrian).
3. It *selects* the lowest-cost candidate and executes it for a short time window, then re-plans.

This three-step loop — *propose, score, select* — is the essence of optimization-based obstacle avoidance. The robot is not following a fixed rule book; it is **solving a numerical optimization problem on the fly**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Optimization-based methods are *not* if-then-else rules. They treat obstacle avoidance as a continuous numerical search over a space of admissible motions, making them robust in cluttered, dynamic environments — exactly the conditions faced by field and service robots (warehouse AGVs, hospital assistants, agricultural rovers, inspection drones).

### 1.3 Core Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following terms appear repeatedly across KTU board questions and are essential vocabulary:

- **Workspace** $\mathcal{W} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ (or $\mathbb{R}^3$): the physical environment.
- **Configuration Space** $\mathcal{C}$: the space of all possible robot poses; obstacles map to $\mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}}$.
- **Cost Function** $J(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u})$: a scalar that the optimizer tries to minimize.
- **Control Horizon** $N$: the number of future time steps the optimizer looks ahead.
- **Safety Bubble Radius** $r_{\text{safe}}$: the minimum clearance enforced around the robot.
- **Time-to-Collision (TTC)** $\tau_{c}$: a critical safety metric in dynamic environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Optimization Problem for Obstacle Avoidance

The robot's motion planning problem is formulated as a constrained numerical optimization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{u}(t)} \quad & J(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t)) = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} L(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t)) \, dt \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = f(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t)) \quad \text{(robot dynamics)} \\
& \mathbf{x}(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{\text{obs}} \quad \text{(collision-free)} \\
& \mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathcal{U} \quad \text{(actuator limits)} \\
& \mathbf{x}(t_0) = \mathbf{x}_{\text{start}}, \quad \mathbf{x}(t_f) = \mathbf{x}_{\text{goal}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this formulation matters:**
- The **objective** $J$ encodes "good behavior" (short path, low energy, smooth motion).
- The **equality constraint** enforces physical realism — the robot cannot teleport.
- The **inequality constraints** encode safety (no collision) and hardware limits (max wheel torque).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Behavior Optimization

**Step 1 — State Estimation.** The robot fuses sensor data (LIDAR, cameras, IMU) to estimate its own state $\mathbf{x}$ and the states of nearby obstacles $\mathbf{x}_{\text{obs}_i}$.

**Step 2 — Prediction.** Each moving obstacle is extrapolated forward using a motion model (constant velocity, social force, learned predictor). This produces a predicted occupancy over the next $N$ time steps.

**Step 3 — Candidate Generation.** A finite set of candidate control sequences $\{\mathbf{u}_k\}_{k=1}^{M}$ or trajectories $\{\xi_k\}_{k=1}^{M}$ is generated — either by random sampling, a search lattice, or a numerical solver.

**Step 4 — Cost Evaluation.** Each candidate is scored:

$$
J_k = w_g \, J_{\text{goal}}(\xi_k) + w_c \, J_{\text{collision}}(\xi_k) + w_s \, J_{\text{smoothness}}(\xi_k) + w_i \, J_{\text{interaction}}(\xi_k)
$$

where $w_g, w_c, w_s, w_i$ are tunable weights.

**Step 5 — Selection & Execution.** The lowest-cost candidate is selected, the first control input is applied, and the horizon rolls forward (receding-horizon or Model Predictive Control principle).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Method | Key Equation | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|--------|--------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Artificial Potential Field — Attractive | $U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \, \rho^2(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})$ | Pulls robot to goal | Static known env. |
| 2 | Artificial Potential Field — Repulsive | $U_{\text{rep}}(\mathbf{q}) = \tfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}})} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2$ for $\rho \le \rho_0$ | Pushes robot from obstacles | Local avoidance |
| 3 | Resultant Force | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{q}) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} - \nabla U_{\text{rep}}$ | Motion command | APF controller |
| 4 | Velocity Obstacle (VO) | $\mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} \in VO \iff \exists t>0: \mathbf{p}_A + \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} t \in \mathcal{B}_B(\mathbf{p}_B, r_{AB})$ | Define unsafe velocities | Dynamic env. |
| 5 | Reciprocal VO (RVO) | $\mathbf{v}_{\text{new}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{v}_A + \mathbf{v}_B^{\text{desired}}) + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{v}_{\text{avoid}}$ | Avoid oscillation | Multi-agent |
| 6 | Dynamic Window — Heading Cost | $J_{\text{heading}} = 1 - \tfrac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\text{preferred}}}{\vert \mathbf{v} \vert \cdot \vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{preferred}} \vert}$ | Align with goal | DWA |
| 7 | Dynamic Window — Clearance Cost | $J_{\text{clearance}} = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{d_{\text{min}}} & \text{if collision} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Penalize proximity | DWA |
| 8 | MPC Stage Cost | $L(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{u}) = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{ref}})^\top Q (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{ref}}) + \mathbf{u}^\top R \mathbf{u}$ | Quadratic tracking | MPC planners |
| 9 | Control Lyapunov Function | $V(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}^\top P \mathbf{x}, \quad \dot{V} \le - \alpha V$ | Stability guarantee | CLF-CBF-QP |
| 10 | Control Barrier Function | $h(\mathbf{x}) \ge 0 \text{ (safe)}, \quad \dot{h} + \gamma h \ge 0$ | Forward invariance | Safety filters |
| 11 | Time-to-Collision | $\tau_c = -\tfrac{(\mathbf{p}_r - \mathbf{p}_o) \cdot \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}}}{\vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} \vert^2}$ (if approaching) | Safety metric | Risk-aware nav. |
| 12 | Social Force (Helbing) | $F_{ij}^{\text{social}} = A_i \exp\left( \tfrac{r_{ij} - d_{ij}}{B_i} \right) \mathbf{n}_{ij}$ | Pedestrian repulsion | Crowd navigation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the Pipe Pitfall:** In KTU answer sheets, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\operatorname{abs}(\cdot)$ for absolute value in inline math. When writing tables, write `\vert \mathbf{v} \vert` (LaTeX) instead of a literal `|` character, since the pipe breaks markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva, Fetch):** Use a combination of APF and MPC to navigate among human pickers.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Mobileye):** Use a layered optimizer: high-level behavior selector + low-level trajectory optimizer with control barrier functions for safety.
- **Service Robots (Pepper, HSR):** Use Social Force Model + RVO for socially compliant indoor navigation.
- **Agricultural Robots (John Deere, Xaver):** Use DWA over a 2D costmap to navigate crop rows while avoiding plants and workers.
- **Inspection Drones:** Use MPC with collision-avoidance constraints to fly through confined spaces (e.g., inside a turbine).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Artificial Potential Field Force Components

We derive the closed-form gradient expressions for the two most-tested potentials in the KTU syllabus.

**Given** the attractive potential defined as:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \, \rho^2(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})
$$

where $\rho(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}) = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} \Vert$ is the Euclidean distance from the current configuration $\mathbf{q}$ to the goal $\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}$.

**Step 1.** Write the squared distance explicitly:

$$
\rho^2 = (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})^\top (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})
$$

**Step 2.** Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{q}$:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{att}} = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \cdot 2 (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}) = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})
$$

**Step 3.** The attractive force is the negative gradient (force points *down* the potential hill, toward the goal):

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}}) = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q})
$$

**Result:** The attractive force is a linear restoring force — exactly like a virtual spring connecting the robot to the goal, with stiffness $k_{\text{att}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Larger $k_{\text{att}}$ means a "stiffer" goal pull — the robot reaches the goal faster but may overshoot or oscillate near the goal due to inertia. This is why many implementations use a *conical* attractive potential near the goal to dampen oscillations.

**Given** the repulsive potential for an obstacle at $\mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}$ with influence distance $\rho_0$:

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4.** Define $\rho = \Vert \mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}} \Vert$. The chain rule gives:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{d}{d\mathbf{q}}\left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)
$$

**Step 5.** Compute the inner derivative. Note $\frac{1}{\rho_0}$ is a constant (w.r.t. $\mathbf{q}$):

$$
\frac{d}{d\mathbf{q}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} \right) = -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \mathbf{q}} = -\frac{1}{\rho^2} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho} = -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^3}
$$

**Step 6.** Assemble:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \left( -\frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^3} \right)
$$

**Step 7.** Repulsive force (negative gradient):

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^3}
$$

**Result:** The repulsive force is directed *away* from the obstacle, grows without bound as $\rho \to 0$ (collision is impossible in finite time), and vanishes continuously at $\rho = \rho_0$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity Obstacle Construction

**Given** two agents $A$ and $B$ with positions $\mathbf{p}_A, \mathbf{p}_B$, velocities $\mathbf{v}_A, \mathbf{v}_B$, and a combined radius $r_{AB} = r_A + r_B + r_{\text{safe}}$.

**Step 1.** Transform to $B$'s reference frame by defining the relative velocity:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} = \mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B
$$

and the relative position:

$$
\mathbf{p}_{BA} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B
$$

**Step 2.** The set of relative velocities that would cause a collision with $B$ at some future time $t > 0$ is the cone with apex at the origin and tangent to a disc of radius $r_{AB}$ centered at $-\mathbf{p}_{BA}$:

$$
VO = \left\{ \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} \;\middle|\; \exists\, t > 0 : \Vert \mathbf{p}_{BA} + \mathbf{v}_{\text{rel}} \, t \Vert \le r_{AB} \right\}
$$

**Step 3.** The cone is bounded by two tangent lines. The half-angle $\alpha$ of the cone is:

$$
\alpha = \arcsin\left( \frac{r_{AB}}{\Vert \mathbf{p}_{BA} \Vert} \right)
$$

**Step 4.** The cone axis direction is $-\mathbf{p}_{BA}/\Vert \mathbf{p}_{BA} \Vert$. The two boundary rays are obtained by rotating this axis by $\pm \alpha$.

**Step 5.** Any relative velocity vector that lies *inside* the cone is collision-inducing. The set of *safe* velocities for $A$ (assuming $B$ maintains its current velocity) is the complement of the translated cone in velocity space:

$$
\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{safe}} \in \mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \left( VO \oplus \mathbf{v}_B \right)
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the Minkowski sum.

**Result:** The optimizer simply picks $\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{safe}}$ that is closest to the desired velocity $\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{desired}}$ — usually by projecting onto the boundary of the forbidden set.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Environment Type | Obstacle Type | Recommended Optimizer | KTU Weight |
|------------------|---------------|------------------------|------------|
| Known static map | Static | APF + A* post-smoothing | **High** (formula derivation) |
| Unknown static | Static (discovered online) | DWA, Vector Field Histogram | **High** (cost function) |
| Known dynamic | Moving with known model | MPC with collision constraints | **Medium** |
| Unknown dynamic (pedestrians) | Social agents | RVO / Social Force / CLF-CBF | **Very High** (interaction focus) |
| Crowded, multi-robot | Coordinated agents | Centralized MPC, Distributed ADMM | Low (advanced) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Optimization Pipeline for Obstacle Avoidance

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full closed-loop optimization pipeline, from raw sensors to wheel commands, and is the kind of figure KTU examiners expect for full-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Stack LIDAR Camera IMU] --> B[State Estimator EKF or Particle Filter]
    B --> C[World Model Occupancy Grid plus Tracks]
    C --> D[Predictor Constant Velocity or Social Force]
    D --> E[Trajectory Sampler Lattice or Random Shooting]
    E --> F[Cost Evaluator Weighted Sum of 4 Terms]
    F --> G[Optimizer Pick Minimum Cost]
    G --> H[Trajectory Tracker Pure Pursuit or MPC]
    H --> I[Robot Actuators Wheels or Joints]
    I --> J[Environment]
    J --> A
    F --> F1[Goal Progress Term]
    F --> F2[Collision Penalty Term]
    F --> F3[Smoothness Penalty Term]
    F --> F4[Interaction Social Term]
    H --> H1[Safety Filter CBF if needed]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: APF-Based Reactive Avoidance

This flow shows the per-cycle decisions made by a classical potential-field controller — frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Read current pose q] --> L[Compute attractive force Fatt]
    L --> M[For each obstacle within rho0 compute Frep]
    M --> N[Sum all forces Ftotal]
    N --> O{Total magnitude below threshold}
    O -- Yes --> P[Stop or Hold Position]
    O -- No --> Q[Command velocity proportional to Ftotal]
    Q --> R[Apply actuator limits saturation]
    R --> S[Send to motors]
    S --> K
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Geometry of the Velocity Obstacle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[Origin current relative velocity] --> V1[Relative position vector pBA]
    V1 --> V2[Translate to obstacle frame]
    V2 --> V3[Construct cone half angle alpha]
    V3 --> V4[Mark forbidden velocity region]
    V4 --> V5[Project desired velocity onto safe half space]
```

### 4.4 Cost-Function Decomposition (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[Total Cost J] --> Jg[Jgoal reach target]
    J --> Jc[Jcollision stay safe]
    J --> Js[Jsmoothness minimize jerk]
    J --> Ji[Jinteraction social rules]
    Jg --> Jg1[Euclidean distance to goal]
    Jg --> Jg2[Heading alignment]
    Jc --> Jc1[Signed distance to obstacles]
    Jc --> Jc2[Time to collision metric]
    Js --> Js1[Integral of jerk squared]
    Js --> Js2[Control effort penalty]
    Ji --> Ji1[Personal space invasion]
    Ji --> Ji2[Right of way respect]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** In the diagrams above, the boxes labeled with `Jxxx` are *cost terms*, not constraints. In a hard-constraint formulation (e.g., CBF-based), the safety term would move to the constraint side and the cost side would only contain goal + smoothness terms. Both formulations are valid optimization strategies.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *Define the Artificial Potential Field (APF) method for obstacle avoidance. Write the expressions for the attractive and repulsive potentials, and state one limitation of APF.*

**Model Answer:**

The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by Oussama Khatib (1985), treats the robot as a point mass moving in a scalar potential field superimposed on the workspace. The goal acts as a *global minimum* of the potential, and obstacles act as *local maxima* that repel the robot. The robot's motion follows the negative gradient of the total potential.

The attractive potential (proportional to squared distance) is:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{1}{2} k_{\text{att}} \, \rho^2(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}})
$$

The repulsive potential (active only within influence distance $\rho_0$) is:

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} k_{\text{rep}} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}})} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2 & \text{if } \rho \le \rho_0 \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Limitation:** The robot can get trapped in *local minima* of the potential (e.g., in a U-shaped corridor between two obstacles) where the net force is zero but the goal is not reached. (3 marks: 1 for definition + concept, 1 for both equations, 1 for the limitation.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** *What is a Velocity Obstacle (VO)? How is it used in dynamic obstacle avoidance for a service robot?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Velocity Obstacle (VO)** is the set of all relative velocities of the robot with respect to a moving obstacle that would cause a future collision within some time horizon. Formally, for robot $A$ and obstacle $B$:

$$
VO_{A \mid B} = \left\{ \mathbf{v} \;\middle|\; \exists\, t \in [0, \tau_{\text{hor}}] : \mathbf{p}_A + \mathbf{v} t \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbf{p}_B(t), r_{AB}) \right\}
$$

In dynamic obstacle avoidance, the robot's planner:
1. Predicts the obstacle's future positions over a horizon $\tau_{\text{hor}}$.
2. Constructs the VO cone in velocity space.
3. Selects a new velocity that lies *outside* the VO and is *closest* to the desired velocity.

This makes VO a *first-order* optimization: one projection step per cycle. For multiple obstacles, the union of all VOs is used. (3 marks: 1 for definition with concept, 1 for the 3-step use, 1 for the "closest to desired" optimization rule.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer *one full question* from a choice of two. Both questions below are independent alternatives — write the one you prepared.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for trajectory optimization in the presence of obstacles. Write the general MPC optimization problem and identify each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a differential-drive mobile robot navigating a 2D workspace with one static circular obstacle, derive the expressions for the artificial potential field force components. Show the gradient computations clearly. Discuss the effect of the parameters $k_{\text{att}}$, $k_{\text{rep}}$, and $\rho_0$ on the resulting motion.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

MPC is a *receding-horizon* control strategy. At every time step $k$, the robot solves a finite-horizon optimization problem from the current state, applies the first control input of the optimal sequence, and re-solves at the next step.

The MPC optimization problem at time $k$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{u}_{0:N-1}} \quad & J = \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} \left[ (\mathbf{x}_{t} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{ref},t})^\top Q (\mathbf{x}_{t} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{ref},t}) + \mathbf{u}_{t}^\top R \mathbf{u}_{t} \right] + \mathbf{x}_{N}^\top Q_f \mathbf{x}_{N} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \mathbf{x}_{t+1} = f(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{u}_t), \quad t = 0, 1, \dots, N-1 \\
& \mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{x}_{\text{current}} \\
& \mathbf{u}_t \in \mathcal{U} \quad \text{(actuator bounds)} \\
& \mathbf{x}_t \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}} \quad \text{(obstacle avoidance)} \\
& \mathbf{x}_N \in \mathcal{X}_f \quad \text{(terminal set)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identifying each term:**
- $N$: prediction horizon (number of future steps).
- $Q \succeq 0$: state tracking weight (penalizes deviation from $\mathbf{x}_{\text{ref}}$).
- $R \succ 0$: control effort weight (penalizes large inputs).
- $Q_f$: terminal cost (ensures stability).
- $\mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$: collision-free set — this is *where* the obstacle avoidance enters.
- $\mathcal{X}_f$: terminal invariant set (for recursive feasibility and stability).

**Block Diagram (described):** The MPC loop consists of: (1) State Estimator → (2) Reference Generator → (3) **Optimizer** (solves the QP at each step) → (4) Plant (Robot) → (5) Feedback to Estimator. The "receding horizon" is the visual rolling window of $N$ predicted states.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The derivation has been worked out in detail in Section 3.1. Reproducing the key results:

**Attractive force:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{q}} U_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} (\mathbf{q}_{\text{goal}} - \mathbf{q})
$$

**Repulsive force (within influence distance):**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left( \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \frac{\mathbf{q} - \mathbf{q}_{\text{obs}}}{\rho^3}
$$

**Total force:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{F}_{\text{att}} + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mathbf{F}_{\text{rep}}^{(i)}
$$

**Effect of parameters (key discussion for full marks):**

| Parameter | Increase → Effect | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| $k_{\text{att}} \uparrow$ | Stronger goal pull, faster convergence | Risk of overshoot/oscillation; robot may slam into obstacle |
| $k_{\text{rep}} \uparrow$ | Stronger obstacle repulsion, larger clearance | May cause robot to get stuck far from goal in narrow passages |
| $\rho_0 \uparrow$ | Obstacle influence felt from farther away | Smoother avoidance trajectories, but wasted detours for distant obstacles |

**Trade-off:** The three parameters are coupled. A practical tuning strategy is to start with $k_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{rep}}$, then increase $k_{\text{rep}}$ until the robot consistently avoids obstacles with a desired safety margin, and finally adjust $\rho_0$ to be roughly 1.5–2× the robot's largest dimension.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a Velocity Obstacle (VO) for dynamic obstacle avoidance. With suitable diagrams, show how the VO set is constructed in velocity space. State the Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO) modification and explain why it is needed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mobile service robot is at position $\mathbf{p}_A = (0, 0)$ moving with desired velocity $\mathbf{v}_{\text{desired}} = (1.0, 0) \text{ m/s}$. A pedestrian is at $\mathbf{p}_B = (5, 0)$ moving at $\mathbf{v}_B = (0, 0) \text{ m/s}$. The safety radius is $r_{AB} = 0.5 \text{ m}$. (i) Construct the velocity obstacle cone. (ii) Find the magnitude and direction of the minimum change $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ needed to avoid collision. (iii) Comment on how reciprocal RVO would change the result.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

[VO concept, construction steps, and cone derivation have been detailed in Section 3.2.]

The VO cone in velocity space has:
- **Apex** at $\mathbf{v}_B$ (the obstacle's current velocity — this shifts the cone in velocity space).
- **Axis** along the direction $\mathbf{p}_{BA} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B$.
- **Half-angle** $\alpha = \arcsin\left( \dfrac{r_{AB}}{\Vert \mathbf{p}_{BA} \Vert} \right)$.

**Why RVO is needed (the *oscillation problem*):** In a pure VO framework, *only* the robot changes its velocity to avoid the obstacle, while the obstacle continues on its path. In a multi-agent setting (e.g., two robots meeting head-on), this leads to *reciprocal avoidance*: each robot veers right, then each compensates and veers back, producing an oscillation or "dance." The **Reciprocal Velocity Obstacle (RVO)** assumes *both* agents share the avoidance responsibility equally:

$$
\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{RVO}} = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{desired}} + \mathbf{v}_B^{\text{desired}}) + \frac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{v}_{\text{avoid}}
$$

This halves the deviation each agent must make, producing smoother, more natural motion.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Constructing the VO cone:**

- Relative position: $\mathbf{p}_{BA} = \mathbf{p}_A - \mathbf{p}_B = (-5, 0)$, magnitude $\Vert \mathbf{p}_{BA} \Vert = 5$ m.
- Half-angle: $\alpha = \arcsin\left( \dfrac{0.5}{5} \right) = \arcsin(0.1) \approx 5.739^\circ$.
- The cone apex is at $\mathbf{v}_B = (0, 0)$ and opens *toward* the origin in velocity space, with axis along $(1, 0)$ (the direction from $B$ to $A$).
- Boundary rays: rotate $(1, 0)$ by $\pm 5.739^\circ$.

**(ii) Minimum velocity change:**

The robot's desired velocity $\mathbf{v}_{\text{desired}} = (1.0, 0)$ m/s has magnitude $1.0$ m/s, well outside the cone of half-angle $5.739^\circ$ at distance $1.0$ (since the cone radius at distance $1.0$ is $1.0 \times \tan(5.739^\circ) \approx 0.1005$ m/s, and the desired velocity lies exactly on the cone axis, **inside** the cone).

To find the minimum deviation, project $\mathbf{v}_{\text{desired}}$ onto the *nearest point* on the cone boundary. The boundary is a ray from the apex at angle $\pm \alpha$ relative to the axis.

The required deviation angle is exactly $\alpha = 5.739^\circ$ (turn left or right by this much). The resulting safe velocity is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{safe}} = \Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{desired}} \Vert \, (\cos \alpha, \sin \alpha) = (0.9950, \; 0.1002) \text{ m/s (turning left)}
$$

or symmetrically $(0.9950, -0.1002)$ m/s for turning right.

**Magnitude of deviation:**

$$
\Vert \Delta \mathbf{v} \Vert = 2 \Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{desired}} \Vert \sin(\alpha/2) \approx 2 \times 1.0 \times \sin(2.87^\circ) \approx 0.1002 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) RVO comment:** Under RVO, the pedestrian is assumed to also deviate by half. So the robot only needs to turn by $\alpha/2 \approx 2.87^\circ$ instead of $5.739^\circ$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{RVO}} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{desired}} + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{v}_A^{\text{safe}} \approx (0.9975, \; 0.0501) \text{ m/s}
$$

This represents a smoother, more courteous interaction — the robot and pedestrian both move slightly to accommodate each other.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Optimization-for-Obstacle-Avoidance Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the influence-distance condition** in $U_{\text{rep}}$ (the $\rho > \rho_0$ branch). Examiners specifically check whether the student sets $U_{\text{rep}} = 0$ outside the influence zone. Losing this loses 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the gradient.** The force is the *negative* gradient of the potential, not the gradient itself. Writing $\mathbf{F} = +\nabla U$ is a classic 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Omitting the "hard" vs "soft" constraint distinction.** When MPC is asked, students often write the cost function but forget to highlight that the obstacle avoidance is enforced as a *constraint* ($\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathcal{C}_{\text{free}}$), not as a soft penalty. This is a 2-mark omission in 7-mark sub-questions.
>
> 4. **Conflating VO and RVO.** Many students describe the RVO formula as "VO" — examiners treat this as a partial-but-incomplete answer. Always state explicitly that RVO *halves* the deviation, and mention the *oscillation problem* it solves.
>
> 5. **Not including units or numerical magnitude checks** in numerical problems. A final answer of "0.1 m/s" without indicating the direction (left vs. right turn) loses 0.5–1 mark. Always specify "turning left" or "veering to the right of the obstacle."
>
> 6. **Using literal `|` in markdown tables.** In online/digital exams, the pipe character breaks table rendering. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX in math, and the word "abs" in plain text.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core idea:** Optimization transforms obstacle avoidance from a rule-based decision into a *numerical search* over admissible motions, balancing goal progress, safety, smoothness, and social rules.
- **APF essentials:** Attractive = parabolic in distance; Repulsive = inverse-square inside an influence radius; Total force = vector sum; Total potential = scalar sum.
- **DWA essentials:** Sample a 2D velocity space $(v, \omega)$; score each with weighted sum of heading, clearance, and velocity; pick the best; re-sample next cycle.
- **VO essentials:** Construct a cone in velocity space; pick a velocity *outside* the cone and *closest* to desired. For multi-agent settings, use RVO to avoid oscillations.
- **MPC essentials:** Solve a finite-horizon QP at each step; first input is applied; obstacle avoidance is a *constraint*, not a cost term; terminal cost + terminal set ensure stability.
- **CLF-CBF-QP:** Modern safety-aware optimization uses a Control Lyapunov Function (stability) and a Control Barrier Function (safety) as constraints of a quadratic program — the solver gives a provably safe, stabilizing control input.
- **Social Force Model (Helbing):** Treats pedestrians as agents driven by a desired velocity plus repulsive forces from other agents and obstacles; widely used in crowd-navigation research.
- **Time-to-Collision $\tau_c$:** A safety metric used as a soft penalty in many modern planners; small $\tau_c$ implies imminent collision.
- **Interaction is the new frontier:** The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes *reciprocal* avoidance — robots must predict and respect *other* agents' intent, not just avoid static geometry. Always mention RVO/Social Force/CBF when asked about "interaction."
- **Parameter tuning is engineering, not math:** The actual deployed system is judged by how well $k_{\text{att}}, k_{\text{rep}}, \rho_0, w_g, w_c, w_s, w_i$ are chosen. Always include a discussion of parameter sensitivity in long answers.
- **Stability and safety proofs** distinguish a research-grade optimizer from a heuristic. When a question asks for a "guaranteed" or "provably safe" method, choose CLF-CBF-QP or MPC with invariant terminal sets.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Household Service Robots: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Household Service Robots: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU/ISO Definition

A **Household Service Robot (HSR)** is a sub-category of **Service Robot** operating within a domestic (residential) environment, designed to perform useful tasks for humans other than industrial automation applications. As per the **ISO 8373:2021** standard (Manipulating industrial robots — Vocabulary), a service robot is a robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications. Household service robots specifically perform their services in the **personal/domestic domain**, typically operating near or directly with end-users (non-professionals).

The **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** formally partitions service robots into two major branches:

- **Personal/Domestic Service Robots** — for non-commercial tasks performed by laypersons (e.g., floor cleaning, lawn mowing, telepresence, companion robots, entertainment).
- **Professional Service Robots** — used for commercial tasks, operated by trained personnel (e.g., medical, agricultural, defense, logistics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) explicitly classifies Household Service Robots under the **Service Robotics** umbrella, distinguishing them sharply from Industrial Robots (PCRUT602 Module 1/2). They are evaluated under **CO4: Design and analyze service robotic systems for unstructured environments.**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of a **smart, autonomous home appliance that has "eyes", a "brain" and "arms"**. Your Roomba is a vacuum cleaner that decides its own path. A household service robot is the **generalised evolution** of that idea — instead of doing just one task, it is a mobile platform capable of folding laundry, fetching a glass of water, guarding the house, or assisting the elderly.

A relatable mental model:

| Domestic Object | What the HSR Adds |
|---|---|
| Smart Speaker (Alexa) | Mobility + Physical Action |
| Robotic Vacuum | Generalised manipulation + Multi-tasking |
| Remote-controlled Car | Full autonomy + Decision-making |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The IFR reports a global household robot density of **~74 units per 1,000 households** for floor cleaning robots alone (2023 statistics), making domestic robots the largest service robotics segment by **shipment volume**.

## 1.3 Why Household Robots Matter (Engineering Drivers)

- **Demographic shift:** Aging populations in developed economies demand elderly-care and assistive robots.
- **Time economy:** Domestic labour-saving is a multi-billion-dollar market.
- **AI Maturity:** Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models (post-2022) made unstructured home environments tractable.
- **Cost collapse:** LiDAR, IMU, and SoC prices have dropped >**80%** in the last decade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Service Robot (ISO 8373:2021):** A robot that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment, excluding industrial automation applications. Domain: Domestic / Personal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of a Household Service Robot

Every HSR is fundamentally a **Sense → Plan → Act** agent. The architecture can be broken into six logical subsystems:

1. **Perception Subsystem**
   - RGB-D cameras, 2D LiDAR, mmWave radar, ultrasonic, IMU, wheel odometry.
   - Function: SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), object detection, human pose estimation.
2. **Cognition / Decision Subsystem**
   - Onboard CPU/GPU/NPU running planners, VLA models, and task schedulers.
   - Function: Task planning, behaviour arbitration, HRI (Human-Robot Interaction).
3. **Locomotion Subsystem**
   - Differential drive, Ackermann steering, omnidirectional mecanum wheels, or legged actuators.
   - Function: Traversing carpets, thresholds (<**15 mm**), stairs (for humanoid variants).
4. **Manipulation Subsystem (if applicable)**
   - 6-DoF / 7-DoF arm with parallel-jaw or dexterous gripper.
   - Function: Object pick-place, door opening, laundry folding.
5. **Power & Thermal Subsystem**
   - Li-ion battery pack, BMS, hot-swap modules.
   - Function: Sustained autonomy for 2–8 hours typical mission time.
6. **HRI / Safety Subsystem**
   - Force-torque sensors, e-stops, soft skin, voice/gesture interfaces.
   - Function: ISO 13482 compliance for personal-care robots.

## 2.2 IFR Classification (Taxonomy)

$$
\text{Service Robots} \begin{cases} \text{Personal / Domestic} \begin{cases} \text{Task-based} \\ \text{Entertainment \& Leisure} \\ \text{Elderly/Assistive} \end{cases} \\ \text{Professional} \begin{cases} \text{Field} \\ \text{Logistics} \\ \text{Medical} \\ \text{Defense} \end{cases} \end{cases}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Task Success Rate | $T_{SR} = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{success}$ successful trials, $N_{total}$ attempts | **%** |
| 2 | Domestic Robot Density | $D_{robot} = \dfrac{N_{robots}}{N_{households}} \times 1000$ | Robots per 1000 households | **units / 1000 hh** |
| 3 | Floor Coverage Area | $A_{cov} = \pi \, r_{eff}^{\,2}$ | $r_{eff}$ effective cleaning radius | **m²** |
| 4 | Operational Endurance | $t_{end} = \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}}$ | $E_{batt}$ battery energy, $P_{avg}$ mean draw | **hours (h)** |
| 5 | Energy per Task | $E_{task} = P_{avg} \cdot \Delta t$ | Energy consumed per discrete task | **W·h (Wh)** |
| 6 | Workspace Reach (Spherical) | $V_{ws} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \, d_{max}^{\,3}$ | Max arm reach $d_{max}$ (simplified) | **m³** |
| 7 | DoF for Dexterous Task | $n_{DoF} \geq 6$ | Minimum for general pick-and-place | dimensionless |
| 8 | Cycle Time | $T_{cyc} = T_{sense} + T_{plan} + T_{act}$ | Sense-plan-act decomposition | **seconds (s)** |
| 9 | Pose Error Bound | $e_{pose} \leq 0.05\, m$ | KTU accepted manipulation tolerance | **metres (m)** |
| 10 | Safety Stop Distance | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{v_{robot}^{\,2}}{2\, a_{max}}$ | $v$ velocity, $a_{max}$ max decel | **metres (m)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on LaTeX delimiters:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside any table cell. The pipe character is reserved by the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **CO4 Mapping:** Household robots are the test-bed for **unstructured environment perception** — the same SLAM stacks that fail in homes also fail in disaster zones.
- **Transferable Skills:** HRI design from domestic robots directly informs hospital logistics robots and museum tour guides.
- **Safety Standards:** ISO 13482 (Personal-care robots) originates from the domestic robot domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Operational Endurance (Battery Sizing)

**Problem:** An HSR has a Li-ion pack with nominal energy **$E_{batt} = 60$ Wh** and a mean power draw of **$P_{avg} = 25$ W** during mixed navigation + vacuuming. Compute the operational endurance.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation.**

$$
t_{end} = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
t_{end} = \frac{60 \text{ Wh}}{25 \text{ W}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the unit algebra (W = J/s, so Wh is energy).**

$$
t_{end} = 2.4 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to minutes for engineering reporting.**

$$
t_{end} = 2.4 \times 60 = 144 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Result:** The robot can sustain a typical cleaning mission of **144 minutes**, consistent with mid-range consumer HSRs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Floor Coverage Radius

**Problem:** A floor-cleaning robot has a rectangular mop of width **$w = 0.28$ m**. Assuming the cleaning radius is half the mop width (the tool covers a strip on each side), compute the equivalent circular coverage area.

**Step 1 — Express the effective radius.**

$$
r_{eff} = \frac{w}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerically evaluate.**

$$
r_{eff} = \frac{0.28}{2} = 0.14 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the area formula.**

$$
A_{cov} = \pi \, r_{eff}^{\,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
A_{cov} = \pi \times (0.14)^2
$$

$$
A_{cov} = \pi \times 0.0196
$$

$$
A_{cov} \approx 0.0616 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Engineering Note:** This is the area "claimed" per stationary sweeping. The robot must traverse the room footprint $A_{room}$ approximately $N_{passes} = \lceil A_{room} / A_{cov} \rceil$ times for full coverage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Task Success Rate Computation

**Problem:** A KTU examiner asks: *"A domestic manipulator performed 25 pick-and-place trials. It succeeded 19 times, failed 4 times due to slip, and 2 times due to mis-localisation. Compute the success rate and the per-failure-mode contribution."*

**Step 1 — Total attempts.**

$$
N_{total} = 19 + 4 + 2 = 25
$$

**Step 2 — Success rate.**

$$
T_{SR} = \frac{19}{25} \times 100\% = 76\%
$$

**Step 3 — Failure-mode breakdown.**

$$
f_{slip} = \frac{4}{25} \times 100\% = 16\%
$$

$$
f_{loc} = \frac{2}{25} \times 100\% = 8\%
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (probabilities must sum to 100%).**

$$
T_{SR} + f_{slip} + f_{loc} = 76 + 16 + 8 = 100\% \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Domestic vs. Professional Service Robots

| Attribute | Domestic / Household Robot | Professional Service Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Environment** | Structured-home, semi-dynamic | Structured-industrial, semi-structured |
| **Primary User** | Layperson (untrained) | Trained operator |
| **Cost Target** | < **$2,000 USD** typical | **$20k–$200k USD** |
| **Safety Standard** | ISO 13482 (Personal Care) | ISO 10218 / 13849 (Industrial) |
| **Payload** | 0.5 – 5 kg | 10 – 1500 kg |
| **Endurance** | 2 – 8 h | 8 – 24 h (or tethered) |
| **Manipulation** | Often none / light gripper | Full arm + tooling |
| **Locomotion** | Wheeled, legged (humanoid) | Wheeled, tracked, quadruped |
| **Examples** | Roomba, Aibo, Temi, 1X Neo | Da Vinci Surgical, Boston Dynamics Spot, Agility Digit |
| **Failure Cost** | Low (nuisance) | High (injury / loss) |
| **Key Challenge** | Generalisation in clutter | Repeatability + certification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Household robots are *the* canonical case of an **unstructured, human-shared workspace** — the hardest perception problem in field robotics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define a Household Service Robot as per ISO 8373:2021. How does it differ from an Industrial Robot?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Household Service Robot is a service robot (ISO 8373:2021) that performs useful tasks for humans or equipment in a **personal/domestic** setting, excluding industrial automation applications (1 mark). It typically operates in unstructured, human-shared environments like homes and is intended for use by **laypersons** (1 mark). It differs from an industrial robot — which operates in a structured factory cell, performs repetitive manufacturing tasks, is fenced for safety, and follows ISO 10218 — by being **mobile**, **interactive**, and operating in **semi-structured home clutter** (1 mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**List any four categories of Household Service Robots with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Floor Cleaning Robot** — e.g., autonomous vacuum/mop (1 mark)
2. **Lawn Mowing Robot** — e.g., boundary-wire or RTK-GNSS mower (0.5 mark)
3. **Assistive / Elderly Care Robot** — e.g., humanoid companion (0.5 mark)
4. **Telepresence Robot** — e.g., mobile video-conferencing unit (0.5 mark)
5. *(Bonus — Social / Companion Robot)* — e.g., pet-type robot (0.5 mark)

*(Award 3 marks for any 4 correct pairs of category + example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Module-end questions are 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set-A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a typical Household Service Robot. Explain the role of each subsystem.*

**Model Answer:**

A household service robot follows a layered **Sense-Plan-Act** architecture, comprising six subsystems (introduce block diagram equivalent to Section 4.1, 2 marks).

- **Perception Subsystem (1.5 marks):** RGB-D cameras and 2D LiDAR provide 360° spatial awareness; IMU + wheel odometry provide egomotion; the SLAM backend fuses these into a persistent map and pose estimate. Object-detection networks (YOLO-class) identify household items.
- **Cognition Subsystem (1 mark):** High-level task planner decomposes user commands (e.g., "clean the kitchen") into atomic actions; a behaviour tree or LLM-based planner sequences navigation, perception, and manipulation sub-tasks.
- **Locomotion Subsystem (1 mark):** Differential-drive base with mecanum wheels for holonomic motion; safety-rated motor controllers ensure ISO 13482-compliant speeds (<**0.25 m/s** in close proximity).
- **Manipulation Subsystem (0.5 mark):** Optional 6/7-DoF arm with parallel gripper for pick-place; force-torque sensing ensures compliant contact with humans.
- **Energy Subsystem (0.5 mark):** Li-ion pack with BMS, hot-swap or auto-dock recharging.
- **HRI/Safety Subsystem (0.5 mark):** Voice, tablet, and gesture interfaces; soft skin and e-stops; ISO 13849-compliant stop circuits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare and contrast Personal/Domestic Service Robots with Professional Service Robots across any seven attributes.*

**Model Answer (tabular — 1 mark per attribute, max 7):**

| Attribute | Domestic | Professional |
|---|---|---|
| **User** | Layperson | Trained operator |
| **Environment** | Home, cluttered, dynamic | Industrial, structured |
| **Cost** | < **$2,000** | **$20k–$200k** |
| **Safety Std.** | ISO 13482 | ISO 10218 / 13849 |
| **Payload** | 0.5–5 kg | 10–1500 kg |
| **Endurance** | 2–8 h | 8–24 h |
| **Failure Cost** | Low (nuisance) | High (injury/loss) |

Conclude with a one-line summary: *"Domestic robots prioritise safety, affordability, and HRI; professional robots prioritise payload, repeatability, and certification."* (1 mark)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Set-B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the IFR (International Federation of Robotics) classification of service robots. Where do Household Service Robots fit in this classification, and what are their main sub-categories?*

**Model Answer:**

The IFR classifies robots into **Industrial Robots** and **Service Robots** (1 mark). Service robots are sub-divided into:

- **Personal/Domestic Service Robots** — for non-commercial tasks by laypersons (1 mark).
- **Professional Service Robots** — for commercial tasks by trained operators (1 mark).

**Household Service Robots fall under the Personal/Domestic branch** (1 mark). Their main sub-categories are:

1. **Task-oriented Robots** — floor cleaning, lawn mowing, window cleaning, pool cleaning (1 mark).
2. **Assistive Robots** — elderly care humanoids, mobility aids, telepresence (1 mark).
3. **Entertainment and Companion Robots** — pet robots, social robots, educational robots (1 mark).

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A household floor-cleaning robot operates on a 25 W average load with a 60 Wh Li-ion battery. Compute (i) operational endurance, and (ii) the coverage area if the effective cleaning radius is 0.14 m. Also, list three key challenges unique to household service robots.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Operational endurance (3 marks):**

$$
t_{end} = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{60 \text{ Wh}}{25 \text{ W}} = 2.4 \text{ h} = 144 \text{ min}
$$

**(ii) Coverage area (2 marks):**

$$
A_{cov} = \pi \, r_{eff}^{\,2} = \pi \times (0.14)^2 \approx 0.0616 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Three Challenges (2 marks — 0.5 + 0.75 + 0.75):**

1. **Unstructured Environment** — furniture reconfigures, lighting varies, clutter changes daily.
2. **Human-Robot Safety in Shared Space** — ISO 13482 mandates collision avoidance with vulnerable users (children, elderly).
3. **Generalisation of Tasks** — unlike factory parts, every cup, sock, and toy is novel; VLA models are required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks Here:**
> 1. **Citation Mistake:** Writing "ISO 8373:2010" instead of **ISO 8373:2021** — costs the year-of-standard credit.
> 2. **Classification Error:** Confusing *Personal Service Robot* with *Professional Service Robot*; the IFR distinguishes by **commercial intent**, not mobility.
> 3. **Unit Neglect:** Reporting endurance without units (h, min) — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks per missing SI unit.
> 4. **Diagrams Without Labels:** Block diagrams in Part B must carry **subsystem labels and arrows**; an unlabelled box loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Formula Hallucination:** Do not invent formulae for "robot empathy index" or similar — KTU strictly tests verifiable equations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Anchor:** Household Service Robot = Service Robot (ISO 8373:2021) + Personal/Domestic domain.
- **IFR Branches:** Personal/Domestic vs. Professional — the split is by **commercial intent**, not by mobility or intelligence.
- **Architecture:** Always decompose into **Perception → Cognition → Locomotion → Manipulation → Energy → HRI/Safety**.
- **Sensing Stack:** RGB-D + 2D LiDAR + IMU + odometry is the canonical HSR minimum; mmWave and ultrasonic are auxiliary.
- **DoF Rule:** $n_{DoF} \geq 6$ for general pick-and-place; 7-DoF adds redundancy for human-shared workspaces.
- **Safety:** ISO 13482 governs personal-care robots; ISO 10218 governs industrial arms.
- **Formulas to Memorise:** $t_{end} = E_{batt} / P_{avg}$, $A_{cov} = \pi r_{eff}^{2}$, $T_{SR} = N_{success}/N_{total} \times 100\%$, $d_{stop} = v^{2}/(2 a_{max})$.
- **Market Anchor:** Domestic robots are the **largest segment** of service robotics by **unit shipment** (IFR data).
- **Key Challenge:** Operating in **unstructured, dynamic, human-shared** environments — the hardest perception problem in the field-robotics taxonomy.
- **KTU CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO4** — design and analyse service robotic systems for unstructured environments.
- **Examiner Heuristic:** Always cite the **standard year** (ISO 8373:2021) and use **SI units with symbols** in every numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Environments for Household Service Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Environments for Household Service Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Work Environment** for a household service robot refers to the complete set of physical, spatial, dynamic, and human-interaction conditions within a domestic setting (home, apartment, assisted-living facility) in which the robot must perceive, plan, navigate, and execute service tasks. It is a *semi-structured*, *human-shared*, *cluttered*, and *dynamically changing* operating context distinguished from industrial robotic cells by its lack of controlled boundaries and presence of untrained, non-technical co-inhabitants.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition applied to service robotics, the household work environment is classified using four orthogonal axes:

1. **Spatial Topology** — geometric layout (rooms, corridors, stairs, doorways)
2. **Dynamicity** — presence and motion of humans, pets, and movable objects
3. **Perceptual Variability** — lighting, textures, surface reflectivities, occlusions
4. **Task Affordances** — furniture, appliances, manipulable objects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Household service robots must operate without safety cages, on the same floor as humans, and across heterogeneous flooring — a defining contrast to industrial manipulators in fixed work envelopes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Think of a household robot's work environment as a **mall on a Sunday afternoon** compared to an industrial robot's work environment being a **single isolated cashier's booth with one customer queue**.
>
> - The cashier's booth (industrial cell) is *deterministic*, *enclosed*, *predictable*, and *engineered*.
> - The mall (household environment) is *crowded*, *unpredictable*, *noisy*, *visually distracting*, and full of *unscripted events* — a toddler running, a shopping bag falling, sunlight through glass doors, a wet floor.
>
> The robot must succeed in the mall every day, all day, with zero preparation of the environment. That is the **defining challenge** of household service robotics.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics

The following physical and operational metrics are universally cited in KTU-aligned robotics literature:

- **Average household floor area:** **90 m²** (2-BHK, urban Kerala) to **200 m²** (villa-type)
- **Standard doorway width:** **0.76 m** (Indian building code) to **0.91 m** (international)
- **Standard step riser height:** **0.15 m – 0.19 m**
- **Floor friction coefficient (μ):** **0.4 – 0.8** (tile, wood, carpet)
- **Average ambient illuminance:** **100 – 500 lux** (residential)
- **Standard reachable table height:** **0.75 m**
- **ISO 13482** compliance threshold for personal care robots

## 1.4 Why Household Environments Are Hard (KTU Perspective)

| Challenge | Industrial Equivalent | Household Reality |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed base | Stationary manipulator | Mobile base on wheels/legs |
| Known coordinates | Calibrated cell | SLAM with drift |
| Static obstacles | None | Furniture, toys, pets |
| Controlled lighting | Sealed enclosure | Glare, shadows, day/night |
| Trained operators | Engineers | Children, elderly |
| Single task | Pick-and-place | Dozens of tasks |
| No stairs | Flat floor | Multi-level homes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Household Work Environment

The environment can be formally decomposed into **four interacting layers**, each demanding distinct robotic competencies:

### Layer 1 — Geometric / Topological Environment
- **What it is:** The static map of walls, doorways, furniture footprints, and floor levels.
- **Why it matters:** Defines the *configuration space* (C-space) within which the robot must plan collision-free motions.
- **How the robot handles it:** 2D occupancy grid + 3D voxel grid hybrid maps; topological nodes for rooms connected by doorways.

### Layer 2 — Dynamic / Human-Sharing Environment
- **What it is:** Time-varying occupancy caused by humans, pets, doors swinging, curtains moving, movable chairs.
- **Why it matters:** A map built at time *t* is *invalid* by time *t + Δt*.
- **How the robot handles it:** Reactive control layers (Dynamic Window Approach, Social Force Model), short-horizon trajectory prediction of humans.

### Layer 3 — Perceptual / Sensor Environment
- **What it is:** The physical phenomena that sensors must interpret — light, sound, RF, depth.
- **Why it matters:** Households contain *mirror-finish* surfaces (TVs, glass tables), *transparent* objects (windows, aquariums), and *specular* floors — all of which defeat naive LiDAR and stereo.
- **How the robot handles it:** Multi-modal sensor fusion (LiDAR + RGB-D + IMU), learned perception models, failure detection and recovery (FDIR).

### Layer 4 — Task / Affordance Environment
- **What it is:** The set of objects, surfaces, and appliances the robot can interact with.
- **Why it matters:** A cup on a table is an *affordance* — grasping it requires recognizing the cup, locating the table, planning an approach, executing grasp, and placing it elsewhere.
- **How the robot handles it:** Object detection + pose estimation + grasp planning + task-level reasoning (often via symbolic planners or LLMs).

## 2.2 Classification of Household Work Environments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (examinable verbatim):** Household work environments are classified as **semi-structured, human-shared, dynamically variable, perceptually degraded, and affordance-rich**.

| Classification Axis | Categories | KTU-Standard Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Small (<80 m²), Medium (80–150 m²), Large (>150 m²) | Studio, 2-BHK, Villa |
| **Levels** | Single-floor, Multi-floor | Flat, Independent house |
| **Surface** | Hard (tile, marble), Soft (carpet, rug), Mixed | Kerala-tile + carpet typical |
| **Lighting** | Natural-dominant, Artificial-dominant, Mixed | Veranda-lit vs. LED-lit interior |
| **Occupancy** | Empty, Low (1–2), Medium (3–5), High (>5) | Working couple vs. joint family |
| **Clutter** | Minimal, Moderate, Highly cluttered | Minimalist vs. lived-in home |
| **Pet presence** | None, Indoor pets, Free-roaming pets | Cats, dogs, birds |
| **Accessibility** | Standard, Wheelchair-modified, Eldercare | Grab-bars, ramps |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the *core mathematical toolkit* for Module 4 household-environment problems. They appear in KTU End-Semester Examinations under Part A and Part B.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{floor} = L \times W$ | Floor area of rectangular room | m² | Coverage planning |
| 2 | $t_{cov} = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{v_{robot} \times w_{eff}}$ | Coverage time | s | Vacuum, mop robots |
| 3 | $w_{eff} = w_{robot} - 2r_{overlap}$ | Effective sweep width | m | Coverage overlap |
| 4 | $P_{coll} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$ | Collision probability (Poisson) | dimensionless | Dynamic obstacles |
| 5 | $d_{safe} = v_{robot} \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{robot}^2}{2 a_{max}}$ | Safe stopping distance | m | Reactive safety |
| 6 | $R_{turn} = \dfrac{L_{robot}}{2 \sin(\theta_{max})}$ | Minimum turning radius | m | Differential-drive |
| 7 | $E_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{motors} \cdot t_{task}}{\eta_{total}}$ | Energy per task | Wh | Battery sizing |
| 8 | $N_{cells} = \dfrac{A_{floor}}{r_{cell}^2}$ | Grid map cell count | integer | Mapping |
| 9 | $f_{sample} \geq \dfrac{v_{max}}{r_{cell}}$ | Sensor sampling frequency | Hz | Mapping fidelity |
| 10 | $F_{social} = \sum_i \dfrac{k_i (d_i - d_{desired})}{\vert d_i \vert^2}$ | Social Force Model repulsion | N | Human-aware nav |
| 11 | $I_{min} = \dfrac{P_{LED}}{A_{aperture}}$ | Minimum illuminance | lux | Vision operation |
| 12 | $\mu_{stair} = \tan(\alpha_{riser})$ | Stair-climb friction condition | dimensionless | Stair traversal |
| 13 | $h_{max} = L_{robot} \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max})$ | Max obstacle climb height | m | Threshold crossing |
| 14 | $SNR_{sensor} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Sensor signal-to-noise ratio | dB | Perceptual reliability |
| 15 | $C_{obs} = \dfrac{V_{obs}}{V_{room}}$ | Obstacle clutter ratio | dimensionless | Clutter metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol inside markdown table cells, ALWAYS replace `|` with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table-parsing failure. The above table is the *correct* KTU format.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas drive the design of every commercial household robot:

- **Roomba / Ecovacs** — use formula **2** for coverage planning.
- **Temi / Pepper** — use formula **10** for social navigation in living rooms.
- **Stretch (Hello Robot)** — uses formula **5** to stop safely before bumping into a child.
- **Spot / ANYmal (home variants)** — use formula **12** to decide which staircases are climbable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coverage Time for a Rectangular Room

A differential-drive household robot of track-width $w_{robot}$ operates in a rectangular room of dimensions $L \times W$ with no prior map. The robot follows a **boustrophedon (back-and-forth)** coverage pattern, the industry-standard for vacuum robots.

**Given:**
- Room length: $L$ (m)
- Room width: $W$ (m)
- Robot forward velocity: $v_{robot}$ (m/s)
- Effective sweep width: $w_{eff}$ (m)
- Overlap margin: $r_{overlap}$ (m)

**Step 1 — Number of parallel passes required.**

Each pass covers a strip of width $w_{eff}$. To cover the entire width $W$, the robot must execute

$$
N_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{w_{eff}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Distance travelled per pass.**

Each pass runs the full length $L$, plus a $180°$ turn at the end. The turn itself adds a quarter-circumference $\frac{\pi R_{turn}}{2}$. But for the idealized coverage-time formula, we approximate the turn as instantaneous (separate planning phase). Therefore, linear distance per pass is

$$
d_{pass} = L
$$

**Step 3 — Total linear distance.**

$$
d_{total} = N_{passes} \times L = \left\lceil \frac{W}{w_{eff}} \right\rceil \cdot L
$$

**Step 4 — Coverage time (ignoring turns).**

Dividing the total distance by the constant forward velocity:

$$
t_{cov} = \frac{d_{total}}{v_{robot}} = \frac{L \cdot \left\lceil \dfrac{W}{w_{eff}} \right\rceil}{v_{robot}}
$$

For large $N_{passes}$ the ceiling operator is dropped (continuous approximation):

$$
t_{cov} = \frac{L \cdot W}{v_{robot} \cdot w_{eff}} = \frac{A_{floor}}{v_{robot} \cdot w_{eff}}
$$

This recovers the standard KTU formula **(2)** from §2.3.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safe Stopping Distance with Human Occupancy

A household robot must guarantee that it can stop before contacting a stationary or moving human. From elementary kinematics:

**Given:**
- Robot velocity: $v_{robot}$ (m/s)
- Reaction time (perception + planning + actuation): $t_{react}$ (s)
- Maximum deceleration: $a_{max}$ (m/s²)
- Initial position: $x_0$

**Step 1 — Distance covered during reaction (constant velocity, no braking).**

During $t_{react}$, the robot has not yet begun to brake, so velocity remains $v_{robot}$:

$$
d_{react} = v_{robot} \cdot t_{react}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance covered during braking (uniform deceleration).**

Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{brake}$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
0 = v_{robot}^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{brake}
$$

$$
d_{brake} = \frac{v_{robot}^2}{2 a_{max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total safe stopping distance.**

$$
d_{safe} = d_{react} + d_{brake} = v_{robot} \cdot t_{react} + \frac{v_{robot}^2}{2 a_{max}}
$$

This is KTU formula **(5)**. **Realistic values:** $v_{robot} = 0.3$ m/s, $t_{react} = 0.2$ s, $a_{max} = 1.0$ m/s²:

$$
d_{safe} = 0.3 \times 0.2 + \frac{0.09}{2.0} = 0.06 + 0.045 = 0.105 \text{ m}
$$

Thus a domestic robot must maintain at least **~11 cm** of clearance — a critical threshold below which an **ISO 13482** protective stop is mandated.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Illuminance for Reliable Vision

A passive RGB camera requires a minimum scene illuminance to produce a usable image. Given:

- Scene reflectance: $\rho$ (0–1, dimensionless)
- Lens aperture area: $A_{aperture}$ (m²)
- LED power: $P_{LED}$ (W)
- Lambertian assumption: radiant intensity falls off as $\cos\theta$

**Step 1 — Irradiance at scene from point source at distance $r$.**

For a Lambertian emitter, the illuminance $E$ on a perpendicular surface at distance $r$ is

$$
E_{scene} = \frac{P_{LED}}{4 \pi r^2} \cdot \rho
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum illuminance threshold for object detection.**

A typical CNN-based detector requires $E_{min} \approx 50$ lux for reliable operation:

$$
\frac{P_{LED} \cdot \rho}{4 \pi r^2} \geq E_{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for maximum operating distance in a dark room.**

$$
r_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{LED} \cdot \rho}{4 \pi E_{min}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** $P_{LED} = 5$ W, $\rho = 0.5$, $E_{min} = 50$ lux.

$$
r_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 0.5}{4 \pi \times 50}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.5}{628.32}} = \sqrt{0.00398} \approx 0.063 \text{ m}
$$

This surprisingly small value explains why **household robots carry active LED illuminators** and why they *fail* in dark corners — a classic KTU viva question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Behavior vs. Household Surface

| Surface | LiDAR | RGB Camera | Depth (Structured Light) | IMU (Floor) | Tactile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Matte tile | ✅ Reliable | ✅ Reliable | ✅ Reliable | ✅ Reliable | ✅ |
| Glossy marble | ❌ Specular | ⚠️ Glare | ❌ Multipath interference | ✅ | ✅ |
| Glass table | ❌ No return | ⚠️ Transparent | ❌ Refraction | ✅ | ✅ |
| Black carpet | ⚠️ Low albedo | ⚠️ Low contrast | ❌ IR absorbed | ✅ | ✅ |
| Mirror | ❌ False return | ⚠️ Sees reflection | ❌ Confused depth | ✅ | ✅ |
| White wall | ⚠️ Blooming | ⚠️ Overexposed | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| Wet floor | ❌ False returns | ⚠️ Reflections | ❌ Specular | ✅ | ✅ |
| Stairs (edge) | ✅ Detectable | ✅ Detectable | ✅ | ⚠️ Free-fall risk | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** No single sensor is sufficient. *Multi-modal sensor fusion* is *not optional* in household environments — it is the **defining engineering requirement**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A household floor-cleaning robot operates in a 6 m × 4 m room. Its track width is 0.30 m, overlap margin is 0.05 m, and forward velocity is 0.25 m/s. Calculate the *minimum* coverage time, the *number of passes*, and the *minimum battery energy* if the motors draw 25 W with a drivetrain efficiency of 60%.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective sweep width.**

$$
w_{eff} = 0.30 - 2(0.05) = 0.20 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of passes.**

$$
N_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{4}{0.20} \right\rceil = \lceil 20 \rceil = 20
$$

**Step 3 — Coverage time.**

$$
t_{cov} = \frac{6 \times 4}{0.25 \times 0.20} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \text{ s} = 8 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy required.**

$$
E_{bat} = \frac{P_{motors} \cdot t_{task}}{\eta_{total}} = \frac{25 \times 480}{0.60} = 20{,}000 \text{ J} \approx 5.56 \text{ Wh}
$$

This is the typical single-charge workload of a Roomba-class device.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Household Work Environment Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Household Work Environment] --> B[Geometric Topology Layer]
    A --> C[Dynamic Human Layer]
    A --> D[Perceptual Sensor Layer]
    A --> E[Task Affordance Layer]

    B --> B1[Floor plan 2D occupancy grid]
    B --> B2[3D voxel representation]
    B --> B3[Topological room graph]
    B --> B4[Staircase and doorway map]

    C --> C1[Human pose tracking YOLO]
    C --> C2[Social Force Model]
    C --> C3[Pet detection]
    C --> C4[Movable obstacle memory]

    D --> D1[2D LiDAR scan]
    D --> D2[RGB-D point cloud]
    D --> D3[IMU floor vibration]
    D --> D4[Microphone array]
    D --> D5[Multi-modal fusion]

    E --> E1[Object detector CNN]
    E --> E2[6D pose estimator]
    E --> E3[Grasp planner]
    E --> E4[Task planner symbolic]

    B1 --> F[SLAM Backend]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    C1 --> G[Reactive Controller]
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    C4 --> G
    D5 --> F
    D5 --> G
    E1 --> H[Manipulation Stack]
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    E4 --> H
    F --> I[Behavior Arbiter]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Motor Commands to Wheels and Arm]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Robot Operating States in a Household

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Mapping : first exploration
    Mapping --> Localizing : pose established
    Localizing --> Planning : goal given
    Planning --> Executing : path valid
    Executing --> Localizing : periodic update
    Executing --> ReactiveAvoid : human or pet detected
    ReactiveAvoid --> Executing : obstacle cleared
    Executing --> TaskExec : arrived at affordance
    TaskExec --> Planning : subtask complete
    Executing --> ProtectiveStop : sensor fault or low battery
    ProtectiveStop --> Idle : reset by user
    Idle --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sensor-Failure Recovery Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[LiDAR scan] --> FMUX{Fusion Multiplexer}
    S2[RGB-D frame] --> FMUX
    S3[IMU stream] --> FMUX
    FMUX --> CHK{Confidence check}
    CHK -->|OK| PROC[Processed perception]
    CHK -->|Degraded| WARN[FDIR warning raised]
    WARN --> SLW[Reduce velocity by factor 0.5]
    SLW --> REQ[Request human assist or revert to safe pose]
    PROC --> NAV[Navigation planner]
    REQ --> NAV
    NAV --> OUT[Wheel velocities]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above three diagrams together form the **complete functional architecture** that every household service robot must implement. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *label* and *explain* such block diagrams for **7-mark** Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define the term "work environment" as applied to household service robots. List any four distinguishing characteristics of a household work environment compared to an industrial work cell.**

**Model Answer:**

A *work environment* for a household service robot is the physical, spatial, dynamic, and human-interaction context within a domestic setting in which the robot must perceive, plan, navigate, and execute service tasks autonomously.

Four distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Semi-structured** — fixed walls and doors, but movable furniture, toys, and clutter.
2. **Human-shared** — no safety cages; humans, children, and pets co-inhabit the same space.
3. **Perceptually degraded** — variable lighting, glare, transparent and reflective surfaces.
4. **Affordance-rich and task-varied** — must grasp, clean, fetch, and serve across dozens of object types.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four characteristics.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is meant by the "Social Force Model" in the context of household robot navigation? Write its mathematical form.**

**Model Answer:**

The Social Force Model (SFM) treats humans and robots as particles subject to attractive and repulsive "social forces" analogous to physical forces. The robot experiences a repulsive force from each nearby human, scaled by their proximity and desired personal-space distance.

$$
F_{social} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i \cdot \frac{d_i - d_{desired}}{\vert d_i \vert^2}
$$

where $k_i$ is a gain constant, $d_i$ is the distance to the $i$-th human, and $d_{desired}$ is the desired interpersonal spacing (typically **0.45–1.2 m** for comfortable social distance).

The robot's velocity is updated by integrating this force, producing trajectories that *pass behind* humans and avoid cutting across their paths.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for concept + 1.5 for the equation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Pattern 1]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify household work environments along four axes. For each axis, give two representative values and the engineering implication for the robot designer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the four-layer functional architecture a household service robot uses to perceive and act in a home environment. Explain how sensor fusion is implemented at the perceptual layer.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Axis | Category 1 (with example) | Category 2 (with example) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spatial size** | Small, <80 m² (studio flat) | Large, >150 m² (villa) | Battery sizing; SLAM memory |
| **Dynamic occupancy** | Low, 1–2 persons (working couple) | High, >5 (joint family with kids) | Reactive controller frequency |
| **Perceptual quality** | Controlled (well-lit, uncluttered) | Degraded (curtained, dim, reflective) | Need for active illumination, multi-modal sensors |
| **Affordance density** | Low (few manipulable objects) | High (kitchen, toys, tools) | Object-detector vocabulary size; grasp library |

*(7 marks: 0.5 per category cell, 3 marks for the implication column, 0.5 for table coherence.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The four-layer functional architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) consists of:

1. **Geometric Topology Layer** — produces the 2D occupancy grid, 3D voxel map, and topological room graph. Built from LiDAR + depth-camera SLAM.
2. **Dynamic Human Layer** — runs a tracker (e.g., YOLO-Pose) on RGB frames, applies the Social Force Model, and injects time-varying cost into the local planner.
