# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of a Field/Service Robot

Below is the **complete, exam-ready** block diagram. Study the **arrow directions** carefully — the forward path carries commands *downstream*, while the feedback path carries sensed information *upstream*.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUP["SUPERVISORY LAYER Mission Computer"]
        HMI["HMI / Teleop Interface"]
        PLAN["Task Planner A-star or RRT-star"]
        DEC["Decision Module Behavior Tree"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER RTOS - ROS 2 / FreeRTOS"]
        PER["Perception and SLAM EKF / Graph SLAM"]
        LOC["Localization GPS + IMU Fusion"]
        PID["Motion Controller PID / MPC"]
        SAF["Safety Monitor E-Stop Logic"]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER Sensors Actuators Power"]
        SENS["Sensor Array LiDAR Camera IMU GPS Ultrasonic Encoder"]
        DAQ["DAQ Block Amplify Filter ADC"]
        ACT["Actuator Block DC Motor / Servo / Hydraulic"]
        PLANT["Plant Chassis Wheels Manipulator"]
        FB["Feedback Sensor Encoder IMU Force Torque"]
        PWR["Power Subsystem Li-Po Battery 22.2V BMS"]
        COMM["Comm Bus CAN EtherCAT Wi-Fi 5G"]
    end

    HMI --> PLAN
    PLAN --> DEC
    DEC --> PID
    PID --> ACT
    ACT --> PLANT
    PLANT --> FB
    FB --> PID
    SENS --> DAQ
    DAQ --> PER
    PER --> LOC
    LOC --> DEC
    SAF -.->|Interrupt| PID
    PWR -.->|Energy| ACT
    PWR -.->|Energy| SENS
    PWR -.->|Energy| DAQ
    COMM -.->|Data| PER
    COMM -.->|Data| PID
    COMM -.->|Data| HMI
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (KTU Examiner's Tip):**
- **Solid arrows** = forward path (command signals flowing from brain → muscles).
- **Dashed arrows** = energy / monitoring / communication (supporting infrastructure).
- The loop **PID → ACT → PLANT → FB → PID** is the **mandatory closed loop** — never omit this in your KTU answer script.

---

## 4.2 Simplified Control-Theoretic Block Diagram (For Part B Answers)

For control-theory questions in the exam, the KTU answer script expects a **transfer-function level** block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["R(s) Reference Input"]:::input
    SUM["Summing Junction + -"]:::sum
    C["C(s) PID Controller"]:::ctrl
    G["G(s) Plant - Motor + Robot"]:::plant
    Y["Y(s) Output - Joint Angle"]:::output
    H["H(s) Feedback Sensor - Encoder"]:::fb

    R --> SUM
    SUM -->|E(s) Error| C
    C --> G
    G --> Y
    Y --> H
    H -->|B(s) Feedback Signal| SUM

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph Equivalence (Advanced KTU)

The block diagram above is mathematically equivalent to **Mason's Gain Formula**:

$$T = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

Where:
- $P_k$ = forward path gain
- $\Delta$ = $1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \dots$ (loop gains)
- $\Delta_k$ = cofactor (the $\Delta$ with the $k$-th forward path removed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Mason's formula is *essential* for KTU questions where multiple feedback loops exist (e.g., inner current loop + outer velocity loop + outermost position loop in a CNC-style service robot).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**List and briefly explain any four functional blocks in the block diagram of a field/service robotic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — bullet points + diagrams):**

1. **Sensor Block (S):** Converts physical quantities (distance, light, force) into electrical signals. Examples: LiDAR for 2D/3D mapping, IMU for orientation, GPS for global position, RGB-D camera for object recognition.

2. **Perception/Localization Block (P):** Processes raw sensor data using algorithms like **EKF-SLAM** or **particle filter** to estimate robot pose $(x, y, \theta)$ and build an environmental map.

3. **Controller Block (C):** Computes actuator commands to minimize tracking error. Implements **PID, state-feedback, or MPC** algorithms running at 100–1000 Hz on real-time hardware.

4. **Actuator Block (A):** Executes physical motion via DC motors, BLDC motors, stepper motors, or hydraulic actuators. Convert electrical $\rightarrow$ mechanical energy.

5. *(Optional 5th)* **Power Block:** Li-Po battery pack (22.2 V nominal, 6S configuration) with BMS delivering regulated 5 V, 12 V, and 24 V rails to subsystems.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Key):** 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks (each block = 0.75 marks for name + function).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is a closed-loop control system? Why is closed-loop control essential in field/service robotics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **closed-loop control system** is one in which the actual output is continuously measured by a sensor and fed back to a comparator, where it is subtracted from the reference input to produce an **error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. This error drives the controller to reduce the discrepancy.

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**Why essential in field/service robotics (any 3 reasons, 1 mark each):**

1. **Disturbance Rejection:** Outdoor environments have wind, uneven terrain, and payload variations — closed loop actively compensates.
2. **Model Uncertainty:** Robot dynamics (mass, friction) change with soil conditions; closed loop adapts via feedback.
3. **Safety:** If a sensor detects an imminent collision, the feedback loop overrides the planner within milliseconds.
4. **Accuracy:** Open-loop robots drift due to wheel slip; closed loop using encoders + IMU maintains heading to within $\pm 1°$.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Part B Module questions carry an **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Attempting both will lead to valuation of the **first answered only**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the complete block diagram of a field/service robotic system. Label all functional blocks and indicate the forward path, feedback path, and the type of signal carried (analog/digital/power).

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a robotic manipulator joint, the open-loop transfer function is:

$$G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

A PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ is added with unity feedback. Determine the values of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ using the **Ziegler-Nichols tuning method** (using the ultimate gain $K_u$ and ultimate period $T_u$). Assume the system becomes marginally stable at $K_u = 30$ with oscillation period $T_u = 2$ seconds.

---

### **Model Answer to A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["SUPERVISORY LAYER"]
        A1["High-Level Task Planner"]
    end
    subgraph L2["CONTROL LAYER"]
        A2["Perception Module - SLAM"]
        A3["Motion Controller - PID"]
        A4["Safety Supervisor - E-Stop"]
    end
    subgraph L3["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        A5["Sensors - LiDAR Camera IMU"]
        A6["Actuators - DC Motor Servo"]
        A7["Robot Chassis and Manipulator"]
        A8["Encoder Feedback"]
        A9["Battery - 24V Li-Po"]
    end

    A1 -->|Digital - Trajectory| A3
    A5 -->|Analog - Raw Voltage| A2
    A2 -->|Digital - Pose Estimate| A3
    A3 -->|PWM Signal - Digital| A6
    A6 -->|Torque - Mechanical| A7
    A7 -->|Position - Mechanical| A8
    A8 -->|Digital Counts| A3
    A9 -.->|Power - 24V DC| A6
    A9 -.->|Power - 5V| A5
    A4 -.->|Interrupt - Digital| A3
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**

- **Forward path (Planner → Controller → Actuator → Plant):** Carries high-level trajectory → motor PWM → torque → motion.
- **Feedback path (Encoder → Controller):** Real-time position/velocity feedback in digital form.
- **Support channels (Battery, Safety, Sensors):** Power rails and parallel safety monitoring.

---

### **Model Answer to A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify ultimate gain and period (1 mark):**

Given: $K_u = 30$, $T_u = 2$ s.

**Step 2 — Apply Ziegler-Nichols PID formula (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 30 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_i &= 0.5 \, T_u = 0.5 \times 2 = 1 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= 0.125 \, T_u = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $T_i$ and $T_d$ to $K_i$ and $K_d$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_i &= \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{18}{1} = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_d &= K_p \times T_d = 18 \times 0.25 = 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the final PID controller (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{C(s) = 18 + \frac{18}{s} + 4.5 \, s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification check (1 mark):** All gains are positive → necessary condition for stability satisfied. Apply Routh-Hurwitz on the closed-loop characteristic equation for sufficiency.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** control architectures for field robots. Give **two real-world examples** of each from agricultural or service robotics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A differential-drive field robot uses the following velocity-tracking loop. The plant is modeled as $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s(s+4)}$ and the controller is a PI controller $C(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$. Find the **steady-state error** for (i) a unit step input, (ii) a unit ramp input, when $K_p = 8$ and $K_i = 12$.

---

### **Model Answer to B(a) — 7 Marks**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (sensors to controller) | 1 |
| **Accuracy** | Low (model-dependent) | High (error-driven) | 1 |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None | Excellent | 1 |
| **Stability** | Always stable (if plant is) | Can become unstable | 1 |
| **Complexity & Cost** | Low | High (sensors, processors) | 1 |
| **Examples — Agricultural** | Fixed-rate fertilizer spreader; irrigation timer | GPS-guided auto-steer tractor; variable-rate sprayer with weed-detection camera | 2 |

---

### **Model Answer to B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Form the open-loop transfer function (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(s) &= C(s) \cdot G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{5}{s(s+4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(s) &= \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot \frac{5}{s(s+4)} = \frac{5(K_p s + K_i)}{s^2(s+4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $K_p = 8$, $K_i = 12$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(s) &= \frac{5(8s + 12)}{s^2(s+4)} = \frac{40s + 60}{s^2(s+4)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify system type (1 mark):** Two integrators → **Type 2 system**.

**Step 3 — Compute static error constants (2 marks):**

**Position constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \lim_{s \to 0} L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{40s + 60}{s^2(s+4)} = \frac{60}{0 \cdot 4} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Velocity constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_v &= \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{40s + 60}{s(s+4)} = \frac{60}{4} = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Acceleration constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_a &= \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot L(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{40s + 60}{s+4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute steady-state errors (2 marks):**

**For unit step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**For unit ramp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{ss} &= \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{15} \approx 0.0667 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot reaches any commanded position exactly and has only **6.67 cm** steady-state lag when tracking a 1 m/s velocity ramp.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Block Diagram Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback path** — A block diagram *without* feedback arrow is worth **0 marks** for the control-theory part. Always draw the encoder/IMU line returning to the summing junction.
>
> 2. **Wrong arrow direction** — Feedback goes **from output back to input**, not the reverse. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this.
>
> 3. **Mixing signal types without labeling** — Always write "Analog", "Digital", "PWM", or "Mechanical" on each arrow. Marks are reserved for this.
>
> 4. **Using `s` for the integrator instead of $\dfrac{1}{s}$** — A common sign-error that flips the entire transfer function.
>
> 5. **Confusing $K_p$ (sensor gain) with $K_p$ (proportional gain)** — In Part B questions, $K_p$ almost always refers to **proportional gain** in PID.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units in the Formula Sheet** — Even if the formula is correct, the answer is considered incomplete without SI units (rad/s, Nm, V).
>
> 7. **Not drawing the Safety / E-Stop block** — KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists this in Module 4 CO mapping; missing it loses **1–2 marks**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of the System**
>
> ✅ **Architecture has 3 layers:** Supervisory (mission), Control (real-time), Physical (hardware).
>
> ✅ **10 mandatory functional blocks:** Sensor → DAQ → Perception → Planner → Controller → Actuator → Plant → Feedback → Power → Communication.
>
> ✅ **Closed-loop is non-negotiable:** Every autonomous field robot must have a feedback path.
>
> ✅ **PID formula:** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ — memorize verbatim.
>
> ✅ **Closed-loop TF:** $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ — draws 2 marks by itself.
>
> ✅ **System type = number of pure integrators** in $L(s)$ — determines $e_{ss}$ behavior.
>
> ✅ **Ziegler-Nichols PID values:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
>
> ✅ **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — critical for sensor selection.
>
> ✅ **Barkhausen criterion:** $|L(j\omega)| = 1$ and $\angle L(j\omega) = -180°$ → marginal stability.
>
> ✅ **Safety block is mandatory** in any KTU 2024 answer — worth **1–2 marks**.
>
> ✅ **Always label signal types** on arrows — Analog / Digital / PWM / Mechanical / Power.
>
> ✅ **Battery specific energy = 150–250 Wh/kg** for modern Li-Po — used in autonomy calculations.
>
> ✅ **Differential-drive kinematics:** $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$, $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ — must be able to write from memory.
>
> ✅ **Real-world analogy:** Robot = Human Body (senses → brain → muscles → feedback).
>
> ✅ **KTU standard:** Reference ISO 13482 (service robots) and ISO 10218 (industrial safety) wherever applicable.
>
> ✅ **Stability check:** Apply **Routh-Hurwitz** to the characteristic polynomial — necessary and sufficient condition for KTU.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**System Initialization** in the context of Field and Service Robotics refers to the deterministic boot-up and calibration procedure executed by a robotic platform at power-on (or on cold-start) to bring all subsystems—including sensors, actuators, estimators, and communication buses—into a known, synchronized, and well-conditioned operating state prior to mission execution. For a robot equipped with a microphone array, initialization also includes **acoustic sensor self-calibration**, **clock synchronization** across data acquisition channels, and the seeding of any recursive Bayesian filter (EKF, UKF, or Particle Filter) used for source tracking.

**Passive Acoustic Localization (PAL)** is the process of estimating the spatial coordinates (range, bearing, or full 3-D position) of a sound-emitting target **without emitting any probing signal**. The robot relies solely on the acoustic wavefronts arriving at its on-board microphone array. The dominant physical observables are the **Time Difference of Arrival (TDOA)** across microphone pairs and the **inter-channel phase difference**, which are processed through algorithms such as **Generalized Cross-Correlation with Phase Transform (GCC-PHAT)** or **Steered Response Power with PHAT (SRP-PHAT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 / 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps directly to **CO4 — *Design and integrate perception pipelines for unstructured environments***. The defining distinction of "passive" operation is that the robot remains electromagnetically and acoustically silent, preserving stealth, reducing power budget, and avoiding interference with other agents—an essential property for **search-and-rescue (SAR)**, **surveillance**, and **human–robot interaction (HRI)** service scenarios.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a large open field and someone claps once. You cannot *see* the clapper, but with **two ears** you can roughly tell *which direction* the sound came from—because the clap reached your left ear a fraction of a millisecond before (or after) your right ear. Your brain performs this **TDOA computation** subconsciously.

Now imagine you grow a **third ear** placed strategically. With three non-collinear "ears" and the same clap, you can triangulate not only direction but also an approximate **distance**. A robotic microphone array does exactly this, only with mathematical rigor:

- The **microphones** are the "ears."
- The **TDOA** is the measured time offset between arrivals.
- The **speed of sound** in air (≈ **343 m/s** at **20 °C**) acts as the "yardstick" converting time into distance.
- The **robot's onboard computer** plays the role of the brain, solving hyperbolic equations to recover the source position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Speed of sound in air at **20 °C**: $c \approx \mathbf{343 \; m/s}$
> - Speed of sound at **0 °C**: $c \approx \mathbf{331 \; m/s}$
> - Temperature correction: $c(T) \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \; (m/s)$, with $T$ in °C.
> - Human audible band: **20 Hz – 20 kHz** (sets sampling requirement of $\geq$ 44.1 kHz per Nyquist).

### 1.3 Why "Passive" Matters in Service Robotics

| Property | Active Sonar / RF | Passive Acoustic |
|---|---|---|
| Emits energy? | Yes | **No** |
| Power consumption | High | Low |
| Detectability by target | Easily detected | **Covert** |
| Multi-robot interference | Severe | Minimal |
| Regulatory constraint | Frequency licensing | **None** (audible band) |
| Typical KTU application | Underwater ROVs | SAR drones, hospital service bots |

The trade-off is **reduced range and accuracy** (the robot cannot control the source's signature), but the gain in stealth and energy efficiency is decisive for **field robotics** deployments in disaster zones or sensitive human environments.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Initialization — Operational Pipeline

A robust initialization sequence for an acoustic-aware service robot proceeds through **six deterministic stages**:

1. **Power-On Self-Test (POST):** Each microphone channel is excited by a known reference tone (e.g., 1 kHz sine burst from an on-board reference emitter) to verify the analog front-end, ADC, and digital gain chain. The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** per channel must exceed a threshold (typically $\geq$ **30 dB**) for the channel to be marked *healthy*.
2. **Clock & Sample-Rate Synchronization:** All ADCs must share a common time base (achieved via hardware triggering or IEEE-1588 PTP). Drift between channels introduces a *systematic TDOA bias* proportional to the drift rate.
3. **Array Geometry Calibration:** The relative positions $\mathbf{m}_i = [x_i, y_i, z_i]^T$ of the microphones are measured (or estimated via an acoustic self-calibration routine using a loudspeaker at known positions). Errors in $\mathbf{m}_i$ propagate directly into localization error.
4. **Reference Frame Registration:** The acoustic frame (defined by the array geometry) is registered to the robot's body frame (from IMU/odometry) via an extrinsic calibration $\mathbf{T}_{ac \to body} \in SE(3)$.
5. **Filter Seeding:** The state vector of the EKF/UKF/PF—typically $[\mathbf{p}_s, \dot{\mathbf{p}}_s]$ where $\mathbf{p}_s$ is the source position—is initialized. Common choices: $\mathbf{p}_s^{(0)} = $ first geometric estimate from TDOAs; covariance $\mathbf{P}^{(0)} = \sigma_0^2 \mathbf{I}$ with $\sigma_0 \approx$ 0.5 m.
6. **Health Monitoring Loop:** Continuous watchdog checks SNR, clipping, and packet-loss; channels failing are masked without re-initializing the entire stack.

### 2.2 Passive Acoustic Localization — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The localization pipeline converts a *physical acoustic event* into a *spatial hypothesis* through a chain of signal-processing and geometric reasoning steps:

- **Wavefront capture:** A point source emits a spherical wave; at sufficient range (Fraunhofer distance $d \geq \frac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$, where $D$ is the array aperture and $\lambda$ the wavelength), the wavefront can be locally approximated as **planar**.
- **Time-stamping at each microphone:** Each channel samples the pressure waveform $x_i(t)$ at rate $f_s$.
- **Inter-channel delay estimation:** The Generalized Cross-Correlation (GCC) computes $R_{x_i x_j}(\tau)$ to find the lag $\tau_{ij}$ that maximizes correlation. **PHAT weighting** whitens the spectrum before the inverse FFT, suppressing the dominance of low-frequency formant energy and improving robustness under reverberation.
- **Geometric inversion:** Each $\tau_{ij}$ defines a **hyperboloid** of constant range-difference; the intersection of $N-1$ such hyperboloids (for $N$ microphones) yields the source position.
- **Filtering & tracking:** The point estimate from the geometric solver feeds a recursive filter that fuses consecutive estimates with the robot's ego-motion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert x\vert` in tables. Vertical pipes inside math break markdown.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed of sound (air) | $c(T) = 331 + 0.6\,T$ | $T$ in °C; $c$ in m/s | At 20 °C: **343 m/s** |
| 2 | TDOA definition | $\tau_{ij} = t_j - t_i$ | seconds | Geometric constraint |
| 3 | Range-difference | $d_{ij} = c \cdot \tau_{ij}$ | meters | Linearised if far-field |
| 4 | TDOA hyperbola (2-D) | $\sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2} - \sqrt{(x-x_j)^2+(y-y_j)^2} = d_{ij}$ | positions in m | Two such equations ⇒ unique 2-D fix |
| 5 | Standard Cross-Correlation | $R_{x_1 x_2}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(t)\,x_2(t+\tau)\,dt$ | samples | Sensitive to noise & reverberation |
| 6 | GCC-PHAT weight | $\Psi_{PHAT}(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{\vert X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)\vert}$ | spectral magnitude | Whitens spectrum |
| 7 | GCC-PHAT impulse | $R_{PHAT}(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\Psi_{PHAT}(\omega)\,X_1(\omega)X_2^{*}(\omega)\right\}$ | inverse FFT | Peak $\Rightarrow \tau_{ij}$ |
| 8 | SRP-PHAT steering | $P(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} R_{PHAT,\,ij}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{q}-\mathbf{m}_i\vert - \vert \mathbf{q}-\mathbf{m}_j\vert}{c}\right)$ | scans candidate grid $\mathbf{q}$ | Robust in reverberant rooms |
| 9 | Far-field DOA (linear pair) | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{c\,\tau_{ij}}{d_{ij}}$ | $\theta$ is angle from broadside | Valid when $r \gg D^2/\lambda$ |
| 10 | Fraunhofer distance | $d_F = \dfrac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$ | meters | Beyond this, planar wave approx. holds |
| 11 | EKF measurement model | $\mathbf{z}_k = h(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{v}_k$ | $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\mathbf{R}_k)$ | $\mathbf{R}_k$ derived from $\sigma_\tau$ |
| 12 | TDOA noise → range noise | $\sigma_d = c \cdot \sigma_\tau$ | meters | Direct scaling |
| 13 | CRLB on TDOA | $\sigma_\tau^2 \geq \dfrac{1}{8\pi^2\,\text{SNR}\,\beta^2}$ | $\beta$ = RMS bandwidth (Hz) | Lower bound for unbiased estimator |
| 14 | Cramér-Rao for DOA | $\sigma_\theta^2 \geq \dfrac{3 c^2}{2 \pi^2 \text{SNR}\, N^3 d^2 \cos^2\theta}$ | $N$ mics, spacing $d$ | Wider apertures lower the bound |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** Locating trapped victims by their cries/calls; a microphone-array drone can fly above rubble passively.
- **Service robots in hospitals:** Tracking a patient who has fallen and called for help.
- **Industrial inspection:** Localizing gas leaks, electrical discharges (corona/partial discharge), or bearing faults by their acoustic signature.
- **Smart environments:** Robot vacuums localizing a human who spoke to them; **HRI** speech enhancement via beamforming toward the talker.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compare GCC vs GCC-PHAT, emphasize **two things**: (i) PHAT weighting suppresses the spectral colouration bias that makes GCC peak at the strongest formant, not the true direct-path delay; (ii) GCC-PHAT degrades under low SNR because the denominator normalizes *noise* too—mention this trade-off.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the TDOA Hyperbolic Localization Equation

We start from first principles. Consider a point sound source at unknown position $\mathbf{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ and two microphones at known positions $\mathbf{m}_i = [x_i, y_i, z_i]^T$ and $\mathbf{m}_j = [x_j, y_j, z_j]^T$.

**Step 1 — Acoustic propagation time from source to each microphone:**

The acoustic wave propagates isotropically at speed $c$. The time taken to reach microphone $i$ is

$$t_i = \frac{\|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_i\|}{c} = \frac{1}{c}\sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2}.$$

Similarly, the arrival time at microphone $j$ is

$$t_j = \frac{1}{c}\sqrt{(x-x_j)^2 + (y-y_j)^2 + (z-z_j)^2}.$$

**Step 2 — Form the time difference of arrival (TDOA):**

$$\tau_{ij} = t_j - t_i = \frac{1}{c}\left(\|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_j\| - \|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_i\|\right).$$

Multiplying through by $c$ yields the *range difference* $d_{ij} = c\,\tau_{ij}$:

$$\|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_j\| - \|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_i\| = d_{ij}.$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the geometric locus (2-D case):**

The locus of points satisfying a constant range-difference to two foci is a **hyperbola** (in 2-D) or a **hyperboloid** (in 3-D). Writing the 2-D form explicitly:

$$\sqrt{(x-x_j)^2 + (y-y_j)^2} - \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2} = d_{ij}.$$

**Step 4 — Solve with a second independent hyperbola:**

A single hyperbola does not localise uniquely. Adding a third microphone $k$ gives

$$\|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_k\| - \|\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{m}_i\| = d_{ik}.$$

Squaring both equations and subtracting eliminates the square-root nonlinearity; after algebraic rearrangement the system reduces to a **linear** equation in $(x,y)$. A third independent equation (or a prior on $z$ for 3-D) over-determines the system, which is solved in the **least-squares** sense:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{p} &= \mathbf{b}, \\
\mathbf{p}^{*} &= \left(\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{b},
\end{aligned}$$

where the rows of $\mathbf{A}$ and entries of $\mathbf{b}$ are built from the microphone positions and measured range-differences.

### 3.2 Derivation of the GCC-PHAT Estimator

**Step 1 — Standard cross-correlation in time domain:**

$$R_{x_1 x_2}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(t)\,x_2(t+\tau)\,dt.$$

The lag $\tau$ that maximises $R_{x_1 x_2}$ is the *raw* TDOA estimate $\hat{\tau}_{12}$.

**Step 2 — Express in the frequency domain using Parseval's theorem:**

$$R_{x_1 x_2}(\tau) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)\right\},$$

where $X_2^{*}(\omega)$ is the complex conjugate of $X_2(\omega)$.

**Step 3 — Identify the bias problem in standard GCC:**

The cross-power spectrum $X_1(\omega)X_2^{*}(\omega)$ is dominated by frequency bins with high energy (e.g., the fundamental and first formant of voiced speech, the tonal hum of machinery). The IFFT therefore peaks not at the true direct-path delay but at a *spectrally-weighted* delay biased toward these strong components.

**Step 4 — Apply PHAT normalisation:**

We define the *phase transform* weight

$$\Psi_{PHAT}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\vert X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)\vert}.$$

This weight divides each frequency bin by its magnitude, leaving only the **phase** information—which carries the pure geometric delay.

**Step 5 — Compute the GCC-PHAT cross-correlation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{PHAT}(\tau) &= \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\Psi_{PHAT}(\omega)\,X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)\right\} \\
&= \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)}{\vert X_1(\omega)\,X_2^{*}(\omega)\vert}\right\}.
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Estimate the TDOA as the arg-max:**

$$\hat{\tau}_{12} = \arg\max_{\tau} R_{PHAT}(\tau).$$

**Step 7 — Convert time to range-difference:**

$$\hat{d}_{12} = c \cdot \hat{\tau}_{12},$$

and feed $\hat{d}_{12}$ into the geometric hyperbolic solver of §3.1.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison for KTU

| Algorithm | Computational Cost | Robustness to Noise | Robustness to Reverberation | Best Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plain Cross-Correlation | $O(N \log N)$ | Poor | **Very poor** | Idealised simulations |
| GCC (Maximum Likelihood / ML) | $O(N \log N)$ | Optimal at high SNR | Poor | Anechoic chambers |
| **GCC-PHAT** | $O(N \log N)$ | Moderate (degrades at low SNR) | **Good** | Real rooms, speech |
| GCC-ML with SCOT | $O(N \log N)$ | Good | Moderate | Industrial noise |
| **SRP-PHAT** | $O(N^2 \cdot G)$ where $G$ = grid size | Excellent | **Excellent** | Real-time robot localization |
| MUSIC / ESPRIT (subspace) | $O(N^3)$ | High SNR only | Poor | Narrowband, high-resolution |
| Beamforming (delay-and-sum) | $O(N \cdot G)$ | Moderate | Poor | Initial coarse scan |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A robot carries a 3-microphone linear array along its $x$-axis at positions $\mathbf{m}_1 = (0,0,0)$, $\mathbf{m}_2 = (0.20,0,0)$, $\mathbf{m}_3 = (0.40,0,0)$ (all in metres). The GCC-PHAT processor reports TDOAs $\tau_{12} = 0.30$ ms (mic 2 lags mic 1) and $\tau_{13} = 0.60$ ms (mic 3 lags mic 1). Assume $c = 343$ m/s and a far-field source. Find the source bearing $\theta$ from broadside.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert TDOA to range-difference:**

$$d_{12} = c\,\tau_{12} = 343 \times 0.30 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1029 \; \text{m}.$$

$$d_{13} = c\,\tau_{13} = 343 \times 0.60 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2058 \; \text{m}.$$

**Step 2 — Use far-field DOA formula:**

For a uniform linear array (ULA) in the far-field, the wavefront is planar and the projected aperture between two mics separated by $d_{ij}$ is $d_{ij}\sin\theta$. Therefore

$$\sin\theta = \frac{d_{12}}{|\mathbf{m}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1|} = \frac{0.1029}{0.20} = 0.5145.$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bearing:**

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.5145) \approx 30.96^{\circ}.$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with second pair:**

$$\sin\theta' = \frac{d_{13}}{|\mathbf{m}_3 - \mathbf{m}_1|} = \frac{0.2058}{0.40} = 0.5145, \quad \theta' = 30.96^{\circ}.$$

The two measurements agree, confirming the far-field assumption and the consistency of the GCC-PHAT estimator.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of System Initialization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POWER ON] --> B[SENSOR SELF TEST]
    B --> C{POST PASS}
    C -- NO --> Z[FAULT LED AND HALT]
    C -- YES --> D[CLOCK SYNC VIA PTP]
    D --> E[ADC PHASE ALIGN]
    E --> F[ARRAY GEOMETRY CALIBRATION]
    F --> G[ACOUSTIC TO BODY FRAME REGISTRATION]
    G --> H[FILTER STATE SEEDING]
    H --> I[HEALTH MONITOR START]
    I --> J[READY STATE ENTER MISSION LOOP]
```

### 4.2 Passive Acoustic Localization Signal-Flow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[ACOUSTIC SOURCE] --> WAVE[SPHERICAL OR PLANAR WAVEFRONT]
    WAVE --> MIC1[MICROPHONE 1]
    WAVE --> MIC2[MICROPHONE 2]
    WAVE --> MIC3[MICROPHONE 3]
    MIC1 --> PRE[PREAMP AND ANTI ALIAS FILTER]
    MIC2 --> PRE
    MIC3 --> PRE
    PRE --> ADC[SYNCHRONIZED ADC]
    ADC --> WIN[FRAME WINDOWING FFT]
    WIN --> GCC[GCC PHAT CROSS CORRELATION]
    GCC --> TDOA[TDOA ESTIMATE VECTOR]
    TDOA --> GEO[HYPERBOLIC GEOMETRIC SOLVER]
    GEO --> POS[INITIAL SOURCE POSITION]
    POS --> EKF[EKF OR PARTICLE FILTER]
    EKF --> TRK[REFINED SOURCE TRACK]
    EKF --> FB[FEEDBACK TO ROBOT NAVIGATION]
```

### 4.3 State-Transition Diagram of the Initialization FSM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BOOT
    BOOT --> SELFTEST: POWER ON
    SELFTEST --> FAULT: SENSOR FAIL
    FAULT --> [*]
    SELFTEST --> CALIB: POST PASS
    CALIB --> SYNC: ARRAY GEOMETRY FIXED
    SYNC --> SEED: CLOCKS LOCKED
    SEED --> READY: FILTER INITIALIZED
    READY --> READY: PERIODIC HEALTH CHECK
    READY --> MISSION: TASK START CMD
    MISSION --> READY: TASK PAUSE
    READY --> CALIB: RECALIB REQUEST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram and Not a Physical Sketch?** The microphone array's *physical* geometry (linear, circular, spherical) is parameterised by the array element positions $\mathbf{m}_i$, which are entered as calibration data at initialization. The above **functional architecture flow** captures the *information flow*—which is what KTU examiners test under OBE—the physical drawing adds no exam value once the block structure is understood.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1.** Define *passive acoustic localization*. List any **two advantages** it offers over active sonar-based localization for a field service robot operating in a disaster zone.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Passive acoustic localization is the process of estimating the spatial coordinates of a sound-emitting target using only the acoustic signals received by a robot's on-board microphone array, **without the robot emitting any probing energy**. (1.5 marks)

Two advantages:
1. **Covertness / Stealth** — the robot remains acoustically and electromagnetically silent, so neither survivors nor hostile agents can detect it. (0.75 marks)
2. **Low power consumption & regulatory freedom** — no high-power transmitter or licensed band is required, enabling longer mission endurance and operation in restricted RF environments such as hospitals or underground mines. (0.75 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q2.** What is the **Time Difference of Arrival (TDOA)** between two microphones? Why is the speed of sound $c$ an *essential* parameter, and how does temperature $T$ affect its value?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TDOA is the time offset $\tau_{ij} = t_j - t_i$ between the arrival of the same acoustic wavefront at microphones $i$ and $j$. (1 mark)

The speed of sound $c$ converts this measured time into a **geometric range difference** $d_{ij} = c\,\tau_{ij}$ that defines a hyperbolic locus of candidate source positions. (1 mark)

Temperature affects $c$ via $c(T) = 331 + 0.6\,T$ m/s. For example, at $T=0°\text{C}$ we get $c=331$ m/s while at $T=30°\text{C}$ we get $c=349$ m/s — a $\sim 5\%$ variation that, if uncorrected, propagates directly into a $\sim 5\%$ range bias. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Acoustic Algorithm Depth*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the **TDOA equation** for a pair of microphones and explain how **three non-collinear microphones** uniquely determine the source position in 2-D. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Block diagram:*
```
SOURCE p(x,y)  ---> sound waves  ---> [MIC i] --> t_i = |p - m_i|/c
                                  --> [MIC j] --> t_j = |p - m_j|/c
                                  --> [MIC k] --> t_k = |p - m_k|/c
                                              |
                                              v
                              τ_ij = t_j - t_i   --> HYPERBOLA 1
                              τ_ik = t_k - t_i   --> HYPERBOLA 2
                                              |
                                              v
                                  LEAST-SQUARES p*
```

*Derivation (4 marks):*

The wave reaches microphone $i$ at time

$$t_i = \frac{1}{c}\sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2}.$$

The TDOA between microphones $i$ and $j$ is

$$\tau_{ij} = \frac{1}{c}\left[\sqrt{(x-x_j)^2+(y-y_j)^2} - \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2}\right].$$

Multiplying by $c$ gives the **range-difference hyperbola**

$$\sqrt{(x-x_j)^2+(y-y_j)^2} - \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2} = d_{ij} = c\,\tau_{ij}.$$

*Uniqueness (3 marks):*

A single hyperbola has two branches and is infinite. Adding a **second independent hyperbola** from a third microphone $k$ intersects the first at typically **two points**; the third, non-collinear microphone removes this ambiguity (or chooses the physically-real branch). The over-determined system of $N-1$ hyperbolae for $N$ microphones is solved as

$$\mathbf{p}^{*} = \left(\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{A}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{b}$$

in the least-squares sense, yielding a unique 2-D position estimate.

---

**(b)** Describe the **GCC-PHAT algorithm** step-by-step with a block diagram. Explain why **PHAT weighting** is used and discuss its trade-off under low SNR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Block diagram (2 marks):*

```
x1(t) --> [FFT] --> X1(w) -----+
                                x  --> X1(w) X2*(w) --> [|/...|] --> PHAT WEIGHT --> [IFFT] --> R_PHAT(tau) --> argmax --> tau_hat
x2(t) --> [FFT] --> X2(w) -----+                                                          |
                                                                                          v
                                                                                PHAT WEIGHT = 1/|X1 X2*|
```

*Step-by-step (3 marks):*

1. Segment the microphone signals into overlapping frames (e.g., 20 ms, 50% overlap).
2. Compute FFT $X_1(\omega)$, $X_2(\omega)$ of each frame.
3. Form the cross-power spectrum $X_1(\omega)X_2^{*}(\omega)$.
4. Apply the PHAT weight $\Psi_{PHAT} = \dfrac{1}{\vert X_1 X_2^{*}\vert}$ — this **divides out** the spectral magnitudes, retaining only phase.
5. Compute the inverse FFT to obtain $R_{PHAT}(\tau)$.
6. The TDOA estimate is $\hat{\tau}_{12} = \arg\max_{\tau} R_{PHAT}(\tau)$.

*Why PHAT and trade-off (2 marks):*

- **Why:** Plain cross-correlation in the time domain is dominated by frequency bins with the highest energy (e.g., formants of voiced speech). PHAT normalises each bin to unit magnitude, ensuring all frequencies contribute equally to the peak search. This makes the estimator robust to **reverberation** and to the *colouration* of the source.
- **Trade-off:** At **low SNR**, the denominator $\vert X_1 X_2^{*}\vert$ in the PHAT weight amplifies **noise bins** disproportionately (because their magnitude is small but unreliable), causing spurious peaks. Hence GCC-PHAT is preferred in moderately reverberant, high-SNR conditions; **GCC-ML** (smoothed coherence) is preferred at low SNR.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *System Initialization & Far-Field Computation*

**(a)** Discuss the **complete system initialization procedure** for a mobile service robot equipped with a microphone array for passive acoustic localization. Cover at least **six sequential stages** with their engineering purpose. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A robust initialization proceeds in six stages (1 mark each + 1 mark for cohesive diagram/flow):

1. **Power-On Self-Test (POST):** Each microphone channel is excited by an internal reference tone; the analog front-end, gain chain, and ADC are validated. Any failed channel is masked.
2. **Clock & Sample-Rate Synchronization:** A shared time base (IEEE-1588 PTP or hardware trigger) is established across all ADCs to eliminate inter-channel drift bias.
3. **Array Geometry Calibration:** The 3-D positions $\mathbf{m}_i$ of the microphones are measured; if not directly known, an acoustic self-calibration using a loudspeaker emitting at known grid positions is performed.
4. **Reference-Frame Registration:** The acoustic frame is registered to the robot's body frame via an extrinsic transform $\mathbf{T}_{ac \to body} \in SE(3)$, so source positions can be reported in the robot's navigation frame.
5. **Filter State Seeding:** The EKF/UKF/PF state vector $[\hat{\mathbf{p}}_s, \hat{\dot{\mathbf{p}}}_s]$ and covariance $\mathbf{P}_0$ are initialised from the first geometric TDOA fix.
6. **Health-Monitoring Watchdog:** A continuous check on SNR, clipping, and packet-loss is enabled; degraded channels are dynamically masked without halting the robot.

*(1 mark awarded for a labelled block diagram linking the six stages.)*

---

**(b)** A service robot carries a **4-microphone square array** with microphones at $(0,0)$, $(0.30, 0)$, $(0.30, 0.30)$ and $(0, 0.30)$ metres. The GCC-PHAT processor reports $\tau_{12} = 0.6$ ms (mic 2 lags mic 1) and $\tau_{14} = -0.4$ ms (mic 1 lags mic 4). Compute the **direction of arrival** of the source assuming far-field conditions and $c = 343$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the microphone pairs and their spacings (1 mark):**

- Pair (1, 2): along $x$-axis, baseline $d_{12} = 0.30$ m.
- Pair (1, 4): along $y$-axis, baseline $d_{14} = 0.30$ m.

**Step 2 — Convert TDOAs to range-differences (1 mark):**

$$d_{12} = c\,\tau_{12} = 343 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.2058 \; \text{m}.$$

$$d_{14} = c\,\tau_{14} = 343 \times (-0.4) \times 10^{-3} = -0.1372 \; \text{m}.$$

**Step 3 — Apply far-field DOA formula for each pair (3 marks):**

For the $x$-aligned pair (broadside = $y$-axis, $\theta_x$ measured from $y$-axis toward $x$):

$$\sin\theta_x = \frac{d_{12}}{d_{12,baseline}} = \frac{0.2058}{0.30} = 0.686 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_x = \arcsin(0.686) \approx 43.3^{\circ}.$$

For the $y$-aligned pair (broadside = $x$-axis, $\theta_y$ measured from $x$-axis toward $y$):

$$\sin\theta_y = \frac{d_{14}}{d_{14,baseline}} = \frac{-0.1372}{0.30} = -0.457 \;\Rightarrow\; \theta_y = \arcsin(-0.457) \approx -27.2^{\circ}.$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a 2-D azimuth (2 marks):**

The source azimuth in the robot's frame is

$$\phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\sin\theta_x}{\sin\theta_y}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.686}{-0.457}\right) \approx \arctan(-1.501) \approx -56.3^{\circ},$$

with a sign correction by quadrant inspection placing the source in the second quadrant of the array: roughly **(−0.687, 0.319)** unit-direction, i.e., bearing ≈ **155°** measured counter-clockwise from the array's $+x$-axis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the speed-of-sound temperature correction** — a 10 °C ambient swing changes $c$ by ~1.7%. Examiners WILL dock a mark if you hard-code 343 m/s without comment. (Loss: up to 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Sign confusion in TDOA** — $\tau_{ij} = t_j - t_i$, so a positive $\tau_{ij}$ means mic $j$ hears the source *later*. Inverting the sign reverses the bearing direction. (Loss: 1–2 marks in DOA problems.)
> 3. **Ignoring the CRLB / SNR dependency** — when asked "what limits TDOA accuracy?", examiners expect bandwidth $\beta$ and SNR to appear; students who only answer "noise" lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing GCC block diagrams without the PHAT weight box** — full 2-mark credit for the diagram requires the normalisation block to be explicit.
> 5. **Confusing active and passive localization** — students who mention the robot emitting a chirp in a passive localization question lose the algorithmic-definitions credit entirely.
> 6. **Skipping the filter-seed step in system initialization** — full marks require *all six* initialization stages including filter covariance $\mathbf{P}_0$ initialisation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Speed of sound**: $c = 331 + 0.6\,T$ m/s; **343 m/s at 20 °C**.
- [ ] **TDOA** = arrival-time difference between two mics; defines a **hyperbola** of possible source positions.
- [ ] **Three non-collinear microphones** ⇒ unique 2-D position via hyperbolic intersection.
- [ ] **GCC** = cross-correlation in time / cross-power spectrum in frequency.
- [ ] **PHAT weighting** = divide cross-power spectrum by its magnitude → only phase survives → robust to reverberation.
- [ ] **GCC-PHAT** is preferred in real rooms; **GCC-ML** preferred at low SNR.
- [ ] **SRP-PHAT** scans a candidate grid; best robustness, highest cost $O(N^2 G)$.
- [ ] **Far-field DOA**: $\sin\theta = c\,\tau / d_{ij}$ — valid when $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Fraunhofer distance** $d_F = 2D^2/\lambda$ — beyond this, planar wave approximation is valid.
- [ ] **CRLB on TDOA**: $\sigma_\tau^2 \geq \dfrac{1}{8\pi^2\,\text{SNR}\,\beta^2}$ — wider bandwidth ⇒ lower bound.
- [ ] **System Initialization** has six stages: POST → Clock Sync → Array Calib → Frame Reg → Filter Seed → Health Watchdog.
- [ ] **Passive ≠ Active** — passive localization emits **no** probing energy.
- [ ] **Typical KTU applications**: SAR drones, hospital service bots, gas-leak detection, HRI.
- [ ] **Filter coupling**: acoustic fix seeds EKF state $[\mathbf{p}_s, \dot{\mathbf{p}}_s]$ with $\mathbf{P}_0 = \sigma_0^2 \mathbf{I}$.
- [ ] **Spatial aliasing** (grating lobes) appears when microphone spacing exceeds $\lambda/2$ of the highest frequency of interest.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Target Detection Using the Frame Differencing Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Target Detection Using the Frame Differencing Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Target Detection Using the Frame Differencing Method** is a fundamental **motion-based segmentation** technique used in computer vision and field robotics to identify moving objects (targets) in a video sequence by computing the **pixel-wise intensity difference** between consecutive frames (or groups of frames) captured by a robot's onboard vision sensor.

Mathematically, if $I_t(x,y)$ represents the intensity value at pixel coordinate $(x,y)$ in frame captured at time $t$, the frame difference image is computed as the absolute difference between two temporally adjacent frames:

$$D_t(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$$

A pixel is classified as belonging to a moving target if its difference magnitude exceeds a predefined threshold $\tau$. This forms a **binary motion mask** $M_t(x,y)$ where:

$$M_t(x,y) = \begin{cases} 255 \text{ (or 1)} & \text{if } D_t(x,y) > \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The clusters of "1" pixels in $M_t$ constitute the **Region of Interest (ROI)** corresponding to the detected target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Frame differencing is classified under *Passive Visual Sensing* techniques for field robots, distinguished from active sensors (LiDAR, Radar) by its reliance on ambient illumination and a monocular or stereo camera.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spot the Difference" Puzzle

Imagine you are a surveillance guard watching a **security camera feed** displaying the same garden every second. If nothing moves, every frame looks identical. The instant a stray cat enters the scene, the pixels representing the cat change from one second to the next — the grass, trees, and fence remain identical.

> **You, the guard, perform "frame differencing" in your brain:** *"What looks different between the previous picture and this one? That's the cat!"*

The **Frame Differencing Algorithm** automates exactly this mental process for a robot. The robot:
1. **Stores** the previous frame as a "reference" picture (analogous to your short-term memory).
2. **Subtracts** the current frame pixel-by-pixel to find the "disagreement" map.
3. **Highlights** pixels where the disagreement is significant (above a threshold) as the **target**.

In essence, the algorithm operates on a beautifully simple assumption: **"If a pixel's value changed significantly between two time instants, something physical must have moved there."**

### 1.3 Classification of Frame Differencing Approaches

In KTU-aligned curricula, frame differencing methods are typically categorized into three families:

| Approach | Frames Compared | Best Use Case |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Single Frame (Consecutive) Differencing** | $t$ vs $t-1$ | Real-time, low-latency detection |
| **Multi-Frame (Double / Triple) Differencing** | $t-1$, $t$, $t+1$ | Suppression of noise and partial occlusions |
| **Accumulative (Running Average) Differencing** | $t$ vs statistical model of $N$ past frames | Slow-moving targets, illumination robustness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** For grayscale images, pixel intensities range in $[0, 255]$; for RGB, each channel is processed independently and combined. Threshold $\tau$ is typically tuned in the range $\tau \in [10, 50]$ for outdoor field conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Operational Principles

The frame differencing pipeline, when deployed on a field robot, executes the following logical sequence:

- **Frame Acquisition:** The vision sensor (CMOS/CCD camera, stereo rig, or event camera) captures an image $I_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ at discrete time $t$, where $H$ is height, $W$ is width, and $C$ is the number of color channels.
- **Pre-processing:** Converting color frames to grayscale ($I_t^{gray} = 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B$) reduces computational load by factor $C$, since motion detection rarely requires color information.
- **Buffering:** The previous frame $I_{t-1}$ is held in a memory buffer for synchronous comparison.
- **Absolute Difference Computation:** A pixel-wise absolute difference is calculated to eliminate sign information — only the magnitude of change matters.
- **Thresholding:** A global or adaptive threshold $\tau$ converts the continuous-valued difference image into a binary mask.
- **Post-processing:** Morphological operations (erosion, dilation) and connected-component analysis isolate the target blob from salt-and-pepper noise.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why absolute difference?** Because we do not know whether the target became brighter or darker as it moved (e.g., entering shadow vs. spotlight). Sign cancels out; magnitude persists.
- **Why a threshold?** Sensor noise (electronic shot noise, quantization) causes random intensity fluctuations of $\pm 2$ to $\pm 5$ even for a stationary scene. A threshold filters out these spurious changes.
- **Why morphological filtering?** A single moving edge can produce fragmented masks. Dilation bridges small gaps; erosion removes isolated noise pixels.
- **Why accumulate frames?** Static objects (parked vehicles, fallen debris) are not targets — accumulating helps suppress "ghosting" artifacts from objects that briefly moved and then stopped.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** In the table below, the symbol $\mid \cdot \mid$ denotes absolute value. We use $\vert \cdot \vert$ instead of the pipe character `$\mid$` to preserve table formatting.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Values |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $I_t^{gray}(x,y) = 0.299 R_t + 0.587 G_t + 0.114 B_t$ | RGB-to-grayscale conversion (luminance) | Intensity range $[0, 255]$ |
| 2 | $D_t(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$ | Single-frame difference image | Intensity range $[0, 255]$ |
| 3 | $M_t(x,y) = \mathbb{1}[D_t(x,y) > \tau]$ | Binary thresholding (1 if true, else 0) | Logical $\{0, 1\}$ |
| 4 | $D_t^{(2)}(x,y) = D_t^{(a)}(x,y) \cap D_t^{(b)}(x,y)$ | Two-frame (double) difference — intersection | Logical $\{0, 1\}$ |
| 5 | $B_t(x,y) = \alpha \cdot I_t(x,y) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot B_{t-1}(x,y)$ | Running (adaptive) background model | $\alpha \in [0.01, 0.1]$ (learning rate) |
| 6 | $D_t^{acc}(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - B_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$ | Background-subtracted difference | Intensity range $[0, 255]$ |
| 7 | $\text{FPS} = \frac{1}{\Delta t}$ | Frame rate of acquisition | Hz (typical 30–60 fps) |
| 8 | $R_{\text{recall}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Detection recall (true positives rate) | Dimensionless $[0,1]$ |
| 9 | $P_{\text{precision}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Detection precision (positive predictive value) | Dimensionless $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | $\text{F1} = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | Dimensionless $[0,1]$ |

Where:
- $\alpha$ = learning rate (background update factor)
- $B_t$ = background model at time $t$
- $TP, FP, FN$ = true positive, false positive, false negative detections
- $\Delta t$ = inter-frame time interval

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **field and service robotics**, frame differencing is the workhorse behind:

- **Agricultural Robots:** Detecting livestock, wild animals, or unauthorized intruders in orchards.
- **Autonomous Ground Vehicles (AGVs):** Real-time obstacle detection in low-speed warehouse environments.
- **Surveillance & Security Robots:** Identifying loitering persons or abandoned objects.
- **Search and Rescue (SAR) Robots:** Locating moving survivors in disaster zones (e.g., earthquake rubble).
- **Domestic Service Robots:** Tracking pets or humans for follow-me behavior in cleaning and assistant robots.

The technique's **computational efficiency** (single subtraction + comparison per pixel) makes it ideal for embedded processors (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Raspberry Pi) deployed on battery-powered field robots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Single-Frame (Consecutive) Differencing

We begin by establishing the underlying physical model that motivates the algorithm.

**Step 1 — Image Formation Model:**

A digital camera captures an image where each pixel records the integrated photon flux over its exposure time $\Delta t$. For a static scene under constant illumination, the intensity $I(x,y)$ at pixel $(x,y)$ is time-invariant. However, when a moving object occupies pixel $(x,y)$ at time $t$, the intensity shifts to a new value $I_t(x,y) \neq I_{t-1}(x,y)$.

Let us define a binary occupancy indicator:

$$O_t(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if pixel } (x,y) \text{ is covered by moving target at time } t \\ 0 & \text{otherwise (background)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Pixel Intensity Decomposition:**

For each pixel, we model the observed intensity as:

$$I_t(x,y) = O_t(x,y) \cdot I^{fg}(x,y) + [1 - O_t(x,y)] \cdot I^{bg}(x,y)$$

where $I^{fg}$ is foreground (target) intensity and $I^{bg}$ is background intensity.

**Step 3 — Apply to Two Consecutive Frames:**

For frame $t$:
$$I_t(x,y) = O_t \cdot I^{fg} + (1 - O_t) \cdot I^{bg}$$

For frame $t-1$:
$$I_{t-1}(x,y) = O_{t-1} \cdot I^{fg} + (1 - O_{t-1}) \cdot I^{bg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Absolute Difference:**

The pixel-wise difference is:
$$I_t - I_{t-1} = (O_t - O_{t-1}) \cdot I^{fg} + (O_{t-1} - O_t) \cdot I^{bg}$$

Factoring:
$$I_t - I_{t-1} = (O_t - O_{t-1})(I^{fg} - I^{bg})$$

Taking absolute value:
$$\vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert = \vert O_t - O_{t-1} \vert \cdot \vert I^{fg} - I^{bg} \vert$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of Result:**

Since $O_t \in \{0, 1\}$, we have $\vert O_t - O_{t-1} \vert = 1$ if and only if the occupancy state changed (target entered or left the pixel between $t-1$ and $t$); otherwise it equals 0. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_t(x,y) = \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert = \begin{cases} \vert I^{fg} - I^{bg} \vert & \text{if occupancy changed} \\ 0 & \text{if both background or both foreground} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Thresholding Decision Rule:**

The final binary mask is obtained by thresholding:

$$M_t(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } D_t(x,y) = \vert I^{fg} - I^{bg} \vert > \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation Exposed:** This derivation reveals a critical flaw of single-frame differencing — if a target occupies the pixel in *both* $t-1$ and $t$ (i.e., slow movement), then $\vert O_t - O_{t-1} \vert = 0$ and the target is **missed** entirely. This motivates the multi-frame variant.

### 3.2 Derivation: Two-Frame (Double) Differencing for Slow Targets

To overcome the slow-target miss, double differencing uses three consecutive frames $I_{t-1}, I_t, I_{t+1}$:

**Step 7 — Define Two Difference Images:**

$$D_t^{(a)}(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$$
$$D_t^{(b)}(x,y) = \vert I_{t+1}(x,y) - I_t(x,y) \vert$$

**Step 8 — Logical AND Operation:**

The final mask is the intersection (logical AND) of both thresholded differences:

$$M_t^{(2)}(x,y) = \mathbb{1}[D_t^{(a)} > \tau] \cap \mathbb{1}[D_t^{(b)} > \tau]$$

**Step 9 — Why This Catches Slow Targets:**

A slow-moving object will change the pixel value at $t-1 \to t$ AND at $t \to t+1$, both transitions yielding large differences. The logical AND therefore captures the *interior* of the moving object — a "filled-in" mask rather than a hollow silhouette.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight:** Double differencing introduces a one-frame delay (needs $t+1$ to decide on $t$), reducing responsiveness by $\Delta t$. This is a fundamental **latency vs. robustness trade-off** frequently examined in KTU problems.

### 3.3 Derivation: Accumulative (Adaptive Background) Differencing

For illumination robustness, a running average background $B_t$ is maintained:

$$B_t(x,y) = \alpha \cdot I_t(x,y) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot B_{t-1}(x,y)$$

**Step 10 — Expand the Recursion:**

Unrolling the recurrence for $N$ past frames:

$$B_t(x,y) = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} (1-\alpha)^i I_{t-i}(x,y) + (1-\alpha)^N B_{t-N}(x,y)$$

**Step 11 — Steady-State Limiting Behavior:**

As $N \to \infty$, $(1-\alpha)^N \to 0$ (since $0 < \alpha < 1$), so:

$$B_\infty(x,y) = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^i I_{t-i}(x,y)$$

This is a geometric series with sum $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^i = \frac{1}{\alpha}$, confirming:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i = 1$$

Hence $B_\infty$ is a **normalized exponentially weighted average** of past frames — older frames contribute exponentially less weight.

**Step 12 — Detection on the Background Model:**

$$D_t^{acc}(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - B_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$$
$$M_t^{acc}(x,y) = \mathbb{1}[D_t^{acc}(x,y) > \tau]$$

The learning rate $\alpha$ controls adaptability: small $\alpha$ → slow background adaptation (good for slow targets), large $\alpha$ → fast adaptation (good for changing illumination, but risks "absorbing" slow targets into the background).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Frame Differencing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture as deployed on a field robot's onboard vision processor:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CamIn: Image Sensor Capture I_t HW Frame"] --> B["P1: Grayscale Conversion via Luma Weighting"]
    B --> C["P2: Memory Buffer Store I_t_minus_1 in RAM"]
    C --> D["P3: Pixel-wise Absolute Difference D_t Equals mod I_t minus I_t_minus_1"]
    D --> E["P4: Threshold Decision against Tau"]
    E --> F["P5: Morphological Dilation Closing Gaps"]
    F --> G["P6: Morphological Erosion Remove Noise"]
    G --> H["P7: Connected Component Labeling Blob Extraction"]
    H --> I["P8: Bounding Box Computation ROI"]
    I --> J["P9: Target Tracker Initialization or Update"]
    J --> K["P10: Field Robot Action Planner Obstacle Avoid or Follow"]

    L["Adaptive Background Model B_t running avg"] -.-> D
    M["Illumination Estimator"] -.-> L

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style K fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **main forward path (solid arrows)** shows the per-frame processing chain from sensor capture to robot actuation.
- The **dashed arrows** represent the parallel "background learning" loop that maintains the adaptive model $B_t$.
- The **orange-shaded nodes** ($D$ and $E$) form the **mathematical core** of frame differencing — the heart of the algorithm.
- The **green node** ($J$) marks the handoff to the **target tracker**, which converts per-frame detections into persistent object trajectories.
- The **yellow node** ($K$) marks the interface with the robot's **behavioral planner**.

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture of Differencing Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD["Strategy 1: Single Frame Differencing"]
        SD1["Frame at t"] --> SD2["Subtract Frame at t-1"]
        SD2 --> SD3["Threshold tau"]
        SD3 --> SD4["Binary Mask"]
    end

    subgraph DD["Strategy 2: Double Frame Differencing"]
        DD1["Frame at t-1"] --> DD2["Subtract Frame at t"]
        DD2 --> DD3["Threshold tau"]
        DD3 --> DD4["AND with Frame at t+1 minus t Result"]
        DD4 --> DD5["Binary Mask"]
    end

    subgraph AD["Strategy 3: Accumulative Background Differencing"]
        AD1["Running Average B_t_minus_1"] --> AD2["Subtract from Frame at t"]
        AD2 --> AD3["Threshold tau"]
        AD3 --> AD4["Update Background with Learning Rate alpha"]
        AD4 --> AD5["Binary Mask"]
    end

    SD4 --> OUT["Decision Output: Target ROIs"]
    DD5 --> OUT
    AD5 --> OUT

    style SD fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style DD fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style AD fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

### 4.3 Noise Propagation and Detection Quality Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Sensor Noise sigma_n squared"] --> Q2["Accumulated in Difference Image"]
    Q3["Threshold tau too low"] --> Q4["High False Positives FP"]
    Q5["Threshold tau too high"] --> Q6["High False Negatives FN Missed Targets"]
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q4 --> Q7["Precision drops but Recall high"]
    Q6 --> Q8["Recall drops but Precision high"]
    Q7 --> Q9["Optimal ROC curve selection required"]
    Q8 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q10["Tune tau via ground-truth validation set"]

    style Q4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style Q6 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style Q10 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

This schematic teaches an important KTU-exam-relevant concept: the threshold $\tau$ acts as the **operating point** on an ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve, and selecting it improperly degrades either precision or recall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**  
Define the Frame Differencing method for target detection in field robotics. State its two main limitations.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Frame differencing is a motion-based target detection technique in which the absolute pixel-wise difference between two consecutive frames — $D_t(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$ — is thresholded against a value $\tau$ to produce a binary mask $M_t(x,y)$ identifying pixels that have changed between frames.

**Limitation 1 (1 mark):** It produces "hollow" masks — only the leading and trailing edges of a moving target generate non-zero differences, leaving the interior undetected.

**Limitation 2 (1 mark):** It is highly sensitive to camera ego-motion and illumination changes; even a stationary camera vibrating causes widespread spurious detections across the entire frame.

**Definition (1 mark):** Clearly stating the pixel-wise absolute difference and threshold operation.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**  
Why is a **running average background model** preferred over consecutive frame differencing for detecting slowly moving targets in agricultural field robots?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Consecutive frame differencing detects only *changes* between two frames, so a target that barely moves between $t-1$ and $t$ produces near-zero difference and is **missed (False Negative)**.

A running average background model $B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$ maintains a slowly evolving "memory" of the static scene over many frames (1 mark). When the target is essentially stationary for several frames, it is *not yet absorbed* into the background (because $\alpha$ is small), so the target-vs-background difference $\vert I_t - B_{t-1} \vert$ remains large and consistently detectable (1 mark).

The learning rate $\alpha \in [0.01, 0.1]$ (1 mark) must be tuned carefully — too large a value causes the background to absorb the target; too small a value prevents adaptation to genuine scene changes (e.g., setting sun, swaying trees).

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical formulation of the **Single-Frame Differencing** algorithm starting from the pixel occupancy model $O_t(x,y)$. Show explicitly how the binary mask $M_t(x,y)$ is obtained. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Two-Frame (Double) Differencing** method with proper equations. Compare its detection performance against single-frame differencing in terms of (i) hollow-mask problem, (ii) noise sensitivity, and (iii) computational latency. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the occupancy indicator:
$$O_t(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if moving target covers pixel } (x,y) \text{ at time } t \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express observed intensity as a mixture:
$$I_t(x,y) = O_t \cdot I^{fg}(x,y) + (1 - O_t) \cdot I^{bg}(x,y)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the difference between frames $t$ and $t-1$:
$$I_t - I_{t-1} = (O_t - O_{t-1}) I^{fg} + (O_{t-1} - O_t) I^{bg} = (O_t - O_{t-1})(I^{fg} - I^{bg})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Take absolute value:
$$\vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert = \vert O_t - O_{t-1} \vert \cdot \vert I^{fg} - I^{bg} \vert$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the threshold:
$$M_t(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert > \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Interpretation — Since $O_t \in \{0,1\}$, the term $\vert O_t - O_{t-1} \vert$ equals 1 only when occupancy flips (target enters or leaves the pixel) and 0 otherwise. Thus the mask captures the *edges* of the moving target only, producing the characteristic "hollow" silhouette.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Final algorithm summary in pseudocode-style: at each new frame, compute $D_t$, threshold against $\tau$, output binary mask.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Equations (3 marks):** Define the two difference images:

$$D_t^{(a)}(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$$
$$D_t^{(b)}(x,y) = \vert I_{t+1}(x,y) - I_t(x,y) \vert$$

Apply thresholds to obtain two binary masks, then take their intersection:

$$M_t^{(2)}(x,y) = \mathbb{1}[D_t^{(a)} > \tau] \cap \mathbb{1}[D_t^{(b)} > \tau]$$

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Single-Frame | Double-Frame |
|:---|:---|:---|
| (i) Hollow mask | Produces only edges (hollow interior) | Interior pixels also detected if motion continuous across 3 frames → filled mask |
| (ii) Noise sensitivity | Highly noise-sensitive (single subtraction) | Less sensitive (AND operation filters spurious single-frame noise) |
| (iii) Latency | Zero-frame delay (output at $t$) | One-frame delay (needs $t+1$ to compute output for $t$) — not real-time in strict sense |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Double differencing trades one frame of latency for substantially more reliable detection — a worthwhile trade for slow-moving field-robot scenarios.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake on Q.A(a):**
> Many students write $D_t = I_t - I_{t-1}$ *without* the absolute value. This loses half the signal (target entering vs. leaving cannot be distinguished) and earns **−1 mark**. Always include $\vert \cdot \vert$ unless explicitly told otherwise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete pipeline for **target detection using the accumulative (running average) background differencing** method. Derive the update equation for the background model and explain the role of the learning rate $\alpha$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A field robot captures grayscale frames of size $640 \times 480$ at 30 fps. The sensor noise variance is $\sigma_n^2 = 9$ (intensity units squared). Using the **Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR)** approach, derive an appropriate threshold $\tau$ assuming Gaussian noise such that the false alarm probability per pixel is $P_{FA} = 10^{-4}$. Standard normal table: $Q(3.72) \approx 10^{-4}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** The pipeline consists of:

1. **Image Capture Module** → grabs $I_t(x,y)$ at 30 fps.
2. **Background Model Unit** → maintains $B_{t-1}(x,y)$ from past $N$ frames.
3. **Difference Computation Unit** → computes $D_t = \vert I_t - B_{t-1} \vert$.
4. **Thresholding Unit** → generates binary mask $M_t$.
5. **Background Update Unit** → updates $B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$.
6. **Morphological Filter** → cleans noise.
7. **Connected Component Analyzer** → extracts target bounding box.

**Derivation of Update Equation (2 marks):**

For exponential weighting of past frames:

$$B_t(x,y) = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} (1-\alpha)^i I_{t-i}(x,y)$$

Unrolling once:
$$B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$$

As $N \to \infty$, $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha (1-\alpha)^i = 1$, so the background is a normalized weighted average.

**Role of $\alpha$ (2 marks):**

- **Small $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.01):** Background updates slowly → robust against temporary disturbances, but cannot adapt to genuine illumination changes. Slow-moving targets remain visible longer.
- **Large $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.3):** Background updates rapidly → adapts quickly to scene changes, but risks absorbing stationary or slow targets into the background model, causing missed detections.

**Practical Tuning (1 mark):** For outdoor field robots with variable illumination, $\alpha \in [0.005, 0.05]$ is typical, with periodic re-initialization during twilight hours.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify noise model (1 mark):**

For a stationary pixel under additive Gaussian sensor noise:

$$I_t(x,y) = I^{bg}(x,y) + n_t(x,y), \quad n_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2)$$

**Step 2 — Distribution of the difference (2 marks):**

The difference $D_t = \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert$ for two i.i.d. Gaussian noise samples has:

$$I_t - I_{t-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 2\sigma_n^2)$$

The standard deviation of the difference is:
$$\sigma_D = \sqrt{2} \sigma_n = \sqrt{2} \times 3 \approx 4.243$$

**Step 3 — CFAR Threshold Equation (2 marks):**

For a target detection threshold under Gaussian noise with false alarm probability $P_{FA}$:

$$P_{FA} = Q\left(\frac{\tau}{\sigma_D}\right) = 10^{-4}$$

Using the inverse Q-function:
$$\frac{\tau}{\sigma_D} = Q^{-1}(10^{-4}) \approx 3.72$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau$ (2 marks):**

$$\tau = 3.72 \times \sigma_D = 3.72 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9}$$
$$\tau = 3.72 \times \sqrt{18} = 3.72 \times 4.2426$$
$$\tau \approx 15.78 \approx 16 \text{ intensity units}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake on Q.B(b):**
> A frequent error is to write $\sigma_D = \sigma_n = 3$ instead of $\sigma_D = \sqrt{2} \sigma_n$. Forgetting the factor $\sqrt{2}$ (because differencing two independent noises adds variances) yields $\tau = 3.72 \times 3 = 11.16$, which is **incorrect** and loses **1 full mark**. Always remember that *difference of independent Gaussians doubles the variance*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frame Differencing for Target Detection**

- **Core Equation:** $D_t(x,y) = \vert I_t(x,y) - I_{t-1}(x,y) \vert$; threshold against $\tau$ to obtain mask $M_t$. *(Memorize verbatim.)*

- **Three Variants:** (1) Single-frame — fast but hollow masks; (2) Double-frame — slower but filled masks; (3) Accumulative — robust to illumination, requires $\alpha$ tuning.

- **Grayscale Conversion Formula:** $I^{gray} = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ — the luma-weighted ITU-R BT.601 coefficients.

- **Threshold Range:** For outdoor field conditions with $\sigma_n \approx 3$–$5$, threshold $\tau \approx 15$–$30$ is typical. Use **CFAR** approach for principled selection.

- **Difference of Two Gaussian Noises:** $\sigma_D = \sqrt{2}\sigma_n$ — a high-yield KTU derivational fact.

- **Running Average Background:** $B_t = \alpha I_t + (1-\alpha) B_{t-1}$; weights sum to unity over infinite horizon.

- **Double Differencing AND:** $M_t^{(2)} = M_t^{(a)} \cap M_t^{(b)}$ — eliminates single-frame noise spikes.

- **Common Failures to Remember:** (i) Hollow masks in single-frame; (ii) target absorption at high $\alpha$; (iii) ego-motion of robot masquerading as target motion; (iv) illumination flicker (e.g., shadows from clouds) triggering false positives.

- **Computational Cost:** $O(H \cdot W)$ per frame for single-frame differencing — highly suitable for embedded field robotics platforms.

- **Performance Metrics:** Always quote **Precision, Recall, and F1-score** when discussing detection quality in exam answers; numerical justification earns full marks.

- **Sensor Specs to Memorize:** Typical frame sizes $640 \times 480$ (VGA), $1280 \times 720$ (HD), $1920 \times 1080$ (Full HD); typical frame rates 30 fps (standard), 60 fps (high speed).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature Selection and Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feature Selection and Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feature Selection** in field and service robotics refers to the systematic process of identifying, extracting, and selecting the most informative, distinctive, and computationally tractable regions or keypoints from sensor data (cameras, LiDAR, depth sensors) for tasks such as visual SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), object recognition, place recognition, and visual odometry. A *feature* is a locally significant image pattern—such as a corner, blob, edge, or texture patch—whose descriptors remain invariant under rotation, scale, illumination, and viewpoint changes.

**Image Segmentation** is the pixel-level partitioning of a robot's perceived scene into multiple semantically meaningful regions or objects. The goal is to assign a class label (semantic segmentation) or instance ID (instance segmentation) to every pixel/point, enabling a mobile robot to distinguish traversable terrain from obstacles, graspable objects from background clutter, and human collaborators from inanimate fixtures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 4 of PCRUT602 directly links perception algorithms to mobile-manipulation outcomes. Feature selection acts as the **"perceptual filter"** that compresses raw sensor data into compact, discriminative descriptors, while segmentation acts as the **"perceptual labeler"** that converts dense sensor arrays into actionable scene understanding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a tourist in an unfamiliar city trying to recognize landmarks for a GPS-style SLAM problem.

- **Feature Selection** is like identifying the **most recognizable corners of buildings**—the sharp roofline, the unique window arch, the carved doorframe—that remain identifiable from any distance, angle, or lighting. The tourist ignores flat painted walls (low-information regions) and focuses only on these distinctive corners (corners, blobs, keypoints).
- **Segmentation** is like **outlining every building, road, and pedestrian** on a paper map, giving each one a distinct color so the tourist can immediately see "this is a road I can walk on" versus "this is a building I must avoid." It converts a confusing photograph into a structured, navigable scene.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Keypoint / Interest Point** | A pixel location with well-defined local image structure (e.g., a corner). |
| **Descriptor** | A high-dimensional vector that summarizes the local image appearance around a keypoint. |
| **Invariance** | Property of a feature to remain stable under specific transformations (rotation, scale). |
| **Covariance** | Property of a feature to detect the change under a transformation. |
| **Thresholding** | Binarization of pixel intensities using a cutoff value $T$. |
| **Cluster** | A group of pixels/points with similar attributes. |
| **Superpixel** | A perceptually meaningful atomic region produced by over-segmentation. |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU-mandated evaluation metrics for features include **Repeatability Rate** (≥ 0.5 desirable), **Matching Score**, and **Distinctiveness Index**. For segmentation, the **Intersection-over-Union (IoU)** and **Dice Coefficient** are the canonical KTU-accepted quality measures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Feature Selection

The feature selection pipeline in a robot's perception stack follows a strict four-stage protocol:

1. **Detection Stage** — Locate candidate keypoints in the image. A robust detector must be repeatable (the same physical point must be detected under different viewpoints).
2. **Localization Stage** — Refine keypoint positions to sub-pixel accuracy using Taylor expansion of the scale-space function.
3. **Description Stage** — Compute a descriptor vector that encodes the local neighborhood (e.g., 128-D SIFT, 256-B ORB).
4. **Matching Stage** — Use a similarity metric (Euclidean, Hamming) to find correspondences between frames or against a map.

### 2.2 Why Feature Selection Matters in Field Robotics

- **Computational Efficiency**: A 1080p image has ≈ 2 million pixels. A field robot may only have an embedded GPU. Selecting 500–2000 features reduces the data payload by **99.9 %**.
- **Robustness to Dynamic Environments**: Outdoor lighting, weather, and motion blur corrupt raw pixels. Features aggregate information over local neighborhoods, providing statistical resilience.
- **Loop Closure Detection**: In SLAM, identifying a previously visited place relies on matching feature descriptors to a bag-of-words index.

### 2.3 The Harris Corner Detector — Operational Logic

The Harris detector measures intensity changes in two orthogonal directions. A flat region produces no change, an edge produces change in one direction, and a corner produces change in **both directions**.

1. Compute image gradients $I_x, I_y$ using Sobel operators.
2. Construct the **second-moment (structure) matrix** $M$ over a window $W$.
3. Compute the Harris response $R$.
4. Threshold $R$ and apply non-maximum suppression.

### 2.4 SIFT — Scale-Invariant Feature Transform
