## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`**
*Define the term "Target Model Update" as used in field robotics. List the two main steps involved.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Target Model Update is the recursive process of refining the probabilistic state estimate of a target (e.g., a landmark, obstacle, or service point) using new sensor measurements and the robot's motion model. The two main steps are:
1. **Prediction (Time Update)** — propagates the prior state forward using the motion model $F$ and adds process noise $Q$.
2. **Measurement Update (Correction)** — fuses the predicted state with the new sensor reading using the Kalman gain $K$, producing a sharper posterior estimate with reduced covariance $P$.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining the two steps.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`**
*What is the role of the Kalman Gain $K$ in the target model update equation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Kalman Gain $K(k) = P(k\mid k-1) H^T S(k)^{-1}$ determines the optimal weighting between the **predicted state** (from motion model) and the **measured innovation** $\tilde{y}(k)$. It is large when measurement noise $R$ is small (trust sensor) and small when process noise $Q$ is small or measurement noise is large (trust prediction). Mathematically, $K \in [0, H^+]$ ensures the posterior covariance $P(k\mid k)$ is minimized in the trace sense — i.e., the estimator is **minimum mean square error (MMSE)** optimal.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for dependency on R and Q, 1 mark for MMSE optimality.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Derive the Kalman Filter measurement update equations for a linear target tracking system, starting from Bayes' rule under the Gaussian assumption. Clearly state the Kalman Gain, innovation, and posterior covariance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A service robot tracks a moving target with state $x_t = [x, \dot x]^T$. The motion model uses $F = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \Delta t \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ with $\Delta t = 1\,\text{s}$. The prior is $\hat{x}_t(k-1\mid k-1) = [0,\, 1]^T$, $P(k-1\mid k-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Process noise $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$, measurement matrix $H = [1\ 0]$, measurement noise $R = 0.5$. A new range measurement $z(k) = 1.5\,\text{m}$ arrives. Compute the updated state and covariance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Already derived in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key results:

$$
K(k) = P(k\mid k-1) H^T [H P(k\mid k-1) H^T + R]^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) = \hat{x}_t(k\mid k-1) + K(k)\,[z(k) - H \hat{x}_t(k\mid k-1)]
$$

$$
P(k\mid k) = (I - K H) P(k\mid k-1)
$$

For 7 marks, examiners award: 2 marks for stating Bayes' rule, 2 marks for Woodbury lemma application, 2 marks for final triplet of equations, 1 mark for the assumption of Gaussianity.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Prediction:**

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P(k\mid k-1) = F P F^T + Q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Intermediate product:

$$
F P F^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(k\mid k-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Innovation:**

$$
\tilde{y} = z - H \hat{x}_t(k\mid k-1) = 1.5 - \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 1.5 - 1 = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation Covariance:**

$$
S = H P H^T + R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 0.5 = 2.1 + 0.5 = 2.6
$$

**Step 4 — Kalman Gain:**

$$
K = P H^T S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \frac{1}{2.6} = \frac{1}{2.6} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Updated State:**

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix} \cdot 0.5 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.404 \\ 0.192 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.404 \\ 1.192 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Updated Covariance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(k\mid k) &= (I - K H) P(k\mid k-1) \\
&= \left(I - \begin{bmatrix} 0.808 \\ 0.385 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\right) \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
I - KH = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 0.808 & 0 \\ -0.385 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.192 & 0 \\ -0.385 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Multiplying:

$$
P(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.192 & 0 \\ -0.385 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 & 1.0 \\ 1.0 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.403 & 0.192 \\ 0.192 & 0.715 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.404\,\text{m} \\ 1.192\,\text{m/s} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad P(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.403 & 0.192 \\ 0.192 & 0.715 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** With reference to field robotics, explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) update procedure for a range-bearing sensor observing a 2-D target. Derive the Jacobian matrix $H_k$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A field robot at position $(0, 0)$ observes a target at true position $(5\,\text{m}, 0\,\text{m})$ using a range-bearing sensor. The prior target state is $\hat{x}_t(k\mid k-1) = [4.5,\ 0.5]^T$ with covariance $P(k\mid k-1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix}$. Measurement noise $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$. The sensor reports $(r, \theta) = (4.95,\ 0.05)$. Perform one full EKF update step. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Already detailed in Section 3.2 of these notes. Key result:

$$
H_k = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\Delta x}{\rho} & \dfrac{\Delta y}{\rho} \\[6pt] \dfrac{-\Delta y}{\rho^2} & \dfrac{\Delta x}{\rho^2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\rho = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$.

For 7 marks: 2 marks for nonlinear measurement model, 3 marks for partial derivatives derivation, 2 marks for assembled Jacobian.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute relative position:**

$$
\Delta x = 4.5 - 0 = 4.5, \quad \Delta y = 0.5 - 0 = 0.5
$$

$$
\rho = \sqrt{4.5^2 + 0.5^2} = \sqrt{20.5} \approx 4.528
$$

**Step 2 — Predicted measurement:**

$$
h(\hat{x}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \rho \\ \text{atan2}(\Delta y, \Delta x) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.528 \\ \text{atan2}(0.5, 4.5) \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 4.528 \\ 0.1107 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Innovation:**

$$
\tilde{y} = z - h(\hat{x}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.95 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 4.528 \\ 0.1107 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.422 \\ -0.0607 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Jacobian:**

$$
H_k = \begin{bmatrix} 4.5/4.528 & 0.5/4.528 \\ -0.5/20.5 & 4.5/20.5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 & 0.110 \\ -0.0244 & 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Innovation Covariance:**

$$
S = H_k P H_k^T + R
$$

First compute $H_k P$:

$$
H_k P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 & 0.110 \\ -0.0244 & 0.2195 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 \cdot 0.25 + 0.110 \cdot 0.05 & 0.994 \cdot 0.05 + 0.110 \cdot 0.25 \\ -0.0244 \cdot 0.25 + 0.2195 \cdot 0.05 & -0.0244 \cdot 0.05 + 0.2195 \cdot 0.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.254 & 0.0772 \\ 0.00495 & 0.0538 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then $H_k P H_k^T$:

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.254 & 0.0772 \\ 0.00495 & 0.0538 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 & -0.0244 \\ 0.110 & 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.254 \cdot 0.994 + 0.0772 \cdot 0.110 & 0.254 \cdot (-0.0244) + 0.0772 \cdot 0.2195 \\ 0.00495 \cdot 0.994 + 0.0538 \cdot 0.110 & 0.00495 \cdot (-0.0244) + 0.0538 \cdot 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.2524 + 0.00849 & -0.00620 + 0.01695 \\ 0.00492 + 0.00592 & -0.000121 + 0.01181 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.2609 & 0.01075 \\ 0.01084 & 0.01169 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Adding $R$:

$$
S \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.3609 & 0.01075 \\ 0.01084 & 0.06169 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6 — Kalman Gain:**

Compute $S^{-1}$. For a 2×2 matrix:

$$
S^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det S} \begin{bmatrix} s_{22} & -s_{12} \\ -s_{21} & s_{11} \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\det S = (0.3609)(0.06169) - (0.01075)^2 \approx 0.02226 - 0.000116 \approx 0.02215
$$

$$
S^{-1} \approx \frac{1}{0.02215} \begin{bmatrix} 0.06169 & -0.01075 \\ -0.01084 & 0.3609 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.785 & -0.485 \\ -0.489 & 16.29 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then:

$$
K = P H_k^T S^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 & -0.0244 \\ 0.110 & 0.2195 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.785 & -0.485 \\ -0.489 & 16.29 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute $P H_k^T$:

$$
P H_k^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 \cdot 0.994 + 0.05 \cdot 0.110 & 0.25 \cdot (-0.0244) + 0.05 \cdot 0.2195 \\ 0.05 \cdot 0.994 + 0.25 \cdot 0.110 & 0.05 \cdot (-0.0244) + 0.25 \cdot 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.254 & 0.00487 \\ 0.0772 & 0.05367 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then $K$:

$$
K = \begin{bmatrix} 0.254 & 0.00487 \\ 0.0772 & 0.05367 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2.785 & -0.485 \\ -0.489 & 16.29 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K_{11} = 0.254 \cdot 2.785 + 0.00487 \cdot (-0.489) = 0.7074 - 0.00238 \approx 0.705
$$

$$
K_{12} = 0.254 \cdot (-0.485) + 0.00487 \cdot 16.29 = -0.1232 + 0.0793 \approx -0.0439
$$

$$
K_{21} = 0.0772 \cdot 2.785 + 0.05367 \cdot (-0.489) = 0.2150 - 0.02624 \approx 0.1888
$$

$$
K_{22} = 0.0772 \cdot (-0.485) + 0.05367 \cdot 16.29 = -0.0374 + 0.8743 \approx 0.837
$$

$$
K \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.705 & -0.0439 \\ 0.189 & 0.837 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7 — Updated State:**

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.705 & -0.0439 \\ 0.189 & 0.837 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.422 \\ -0.0607 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K\tilde{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.705 \cdot 0.422 + (-0.0439)(-0.0607) \\ 0.189 \cdot 0.422 + 0.837 \cdot (-0.0607) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2975 + 0.00266 \\ 0.07976 - 0.05081 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3002 \\ 0.0289 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.3002 \\ 0.0289 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.800 \\ 0.529 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 8 — Updated Covariance:**

$$
P(k\mid k) = (I - K H_k) P(k\mid k-1)
$$

Compute $KH_k$:

$$
K H_k = \begin{bmatrix} 0.705 & -0.0439 \\ 0.189 & 0.837 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.994 & 0.110 \\ -0.0244 & 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.705 \cdot 0.994 + (-0.0439)(-0.0244) & 0.705 \cdot 0.110 + (-0.0439)(0.2195) \\ 0.189 \cdot 0.994 + 0.837 \cdot (-0.0244) & 0.189 \cdot 0.110 + 0.837 \cdot 0.2195 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.7018 & 0.0679 \\ 0.1674 & 0.2045 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
I - K H_k \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.2982 & -0.0679 \\ -0.1674 & 0.7955 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P(k\mid k) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2982 & -0.0679 \\ -0.1674 & 0.7955 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 & 0.05 \\ 0.05 & 0.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 0.2982 \cdot 0.25 + (-0.0679)(0.05) & 0.2982 \cdot 0.05 + (-0.0679)(0.25) \\ (-0.1674)(0.25) + 0.7955 \cdot 0.05 & (-0.1674)(0.05) + 0.7955 \cdot 0.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.07455 - 0.00340 & 0.01491 - 0.01698 \\ -0.04185 + 0.03978 & -0.00837 + 0.1989 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.0712 & -0.00207 \\ -0.00207 & 0.1905 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\hat{x}_t(k\mid k) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 4.80\,\text{m} \\ 0.53\,\text{m} \end{bmatrix}, \quad P(k\mid k) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.071 & -0.002 \\ -0.002 & 0.191 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The position estimate converged from prior $(4.5, 0.5)$ toward the true $(5, 0)$, and the **covariance trace dropped** from $\text{tr}(P(k|k-1)) = 0.5$ to $\text{tr}(P(k|k)) \approx 0.262$ — confirming successful update.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the Woodbury Lemma justification**: Many students present the Kalman update equations without showing that they arise from Bayes' rule. This costs 2–3 marks in derivations.
> 2. **Mixing up $P(k\mid k-1)$ vs $P(k\mid k)$**: Always explicitly label which posterior the covariance refers to; ambiguity leads to **full-step mark deduction** in numerical questions.
> 3. **Skipping Jacobian derivation in EKF questions**: Examiners expect partial derivatives shown step-by-step. A "magically written" $H_k$ matrix costs 3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring data association**: In multi-target questions, students often update the wrong target. State the Mahalanobis gating test before fusing.
> 5. **Units**: Write units on every numerical answer. A correct answer in plain numbers but missing $\text{m}$ loses a half-mark per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Target Model Update** is the measurement-update half of the recursive Bayesian estimator (Kalman, EKF, UKF).
- The **two-step loop** is **Predict → Update**, run at every sensor cycle.
- The **Kalman Gain $K$** optimally blends prediction and measurement — high $K$ means *trust sensor*, low $K$ means *trust motion model*.
- The **Innovation** $\tilde{y} = z - H\hat{x}$ measures the *surprise* relative to the prediction.
- **EKF** handles nonlinear range-bearing sensors by linearizing the measurement function $h(\cdot)$ around the predicted state via the **Jacobian $H_k$**.
- The **Joseph form** of covariance update is numerically stable: $P = (I-KH)P(I-KH)^T + KRK^T$.
- **Data association** uses the Mahalanobis distance $\tilde{y}^T S^{-1} \tilde{y}$ gated against $\chi^2$ thresholds.
- **Process noise $Q$** injects uncertainty into the prediction (target may maneuver).
- **Measurement noise $R$** reflects sensor fidelity.
- Constant-velocity kinematic model uses block matrix $F = [I_2\ \Delta t I_2;\ 0\ I_2]$.
- In **service robots**, target updates feed occupancy/cost maps for safe path planning.
- For **dynamic targets** (people, vehicles), track with constant-velocity $F$ and increased $Q$ for maneuvering.
- For **static landmarks**, $F = I$ and $Q$ may be near-zero.
- **Bearing-only sensors** yield rank-deficient $H_k$ from a single frame; KTU questions may probe this.
- **UKF** is preferred when linearization error of EKF is unacceptable (e.g., long-range bearings).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Abnormal Behavior Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Abnormal Behavior Detection (ABD)** in the context of Field and Service Robotics refers to the systematic process of identifying, classifying, and localizing deviations from expected operational states, sensor outputs, actuator responses, or interaction patterns of an autonomous robotic system deployed in unstructured, human-shared, or dynamic environments. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** interpretation, abnormal behavior is defined as any observable condition where the robot's state vector, decision output, or interaction signature statistically diverges from a calibrated baseline model of "nominal" operation.

Mathematically, if $\mathcal{N}$ represents the set of all nominal operational states, then any state $x_t$ at time $t$ such that $x_t \notin \mathcal{N}$ is classified as **abnormal**. The detection problem reduces to a hypothesis-testing framework:

$$H_0: x_t \in \mathcal{N} \quad \text{(Null Hypothesis: Normal Behavior)}$$

$$H_1: x_t \notin \mathcal{N} \quad \text{(Alternative Hypothesis: Abnormal Behavior)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Abnormal Behavior Detection is a *cross-cutting competency* that overlaps with fault diagnosis, safe AI, human-robot interaction (HRI), and dependability engineering. It is essential for robots operating under **ISO 13482** (personal care robots) and **ISO 10218** (industrial robot safety) standards.

### 1.2 Types of Abnormal Behavior in Service Robots

| # | Category | Description | Typical Example |
|---|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Sensor Anomaly** | Stale, noisy, or frozen sensor readings | LiDAR returns constant zeros (blocked lens) |
| 2 | **Actuator Fault** | Motor stalling, gearbox wear, encoder drift | Wheel slip on wet surface |
| 3 | **Environmental Anomaly** | Unforeseen obstacles, lighting changes | Glass door misinterpreted as open space |
| 4 | **Behavioral Anomaly** | Deviation from planned task sequence | Robot stops mid-way without valid reason |
| 5 | **Interaction Anomaly** | Unusual human response during HRI | User becomes aggressive, robot fails to retreat |
| 6 | **Software/Logic Anomaly** | Deadlocks, infinite loops, memory leaks | Path planner re-plans excessively |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Vigilant Taxi Driver:**
> Imagine a seasoned taxi driver who has driven the same city route **thousands of times**. He knows exactly what "normal" traffic, engine sounds, brake feel, and passenger behavior feel like. One day, he hears a *strange metallic screech from the brakes*, feels the *steering pull slightly left*, and notices the *fuel gauge dropping unusually fast*. Three abnormal signs. He does not need to be a mechanic — his *intuition, built from patterns*, tells him something is wrong, and he pulls over to inspect.
>
> A **service robot** is exactly this taxi driver, but equipped with algorithms. Its "experience" is stored as a **probabilistic model of normal operation**, and its "intuition" is an **anomaly score** computed in real-time from sensor and actuator data. The moment the score crosses a threshold, the robot raises an alarm, switches to a safe state, or asks for human help.

### 1.4 Why Abnormal Behavior Detection Matters

* **Safety**: Field robots (agricultural, inspection, medical) operate near humans; failure can cause injury.
* **Trust**: A robot that behaves erratically loses user confidence in domestic/service settings.
* **Mission Continuity**: Early detection enables graceful degradation and prevents catastrophic failure.
* **Regulatory Compliance**: KTU 2024 outcome-based education emphasizes *reliable autonomy* under the **Dependability** course outcome.
* **Self-Healing**: Detection is the first step toward self-recovery and long-term autonomy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anomaly Detection Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The end-to-end flow of any ABD system, as adopted in modern Field & Service Robotics textbooks, consists of the following **six sequential stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer**
   * Raw signals from proprioceptive sensors (IMU, encoders, current sensors) and exteroceptive sensors (camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic, GPS).
   * Sampling rate: typically **50 Hz to 200 Hz** for mobile platforms.

2. **Preprocessing & Feature Extraction**
   * Filtering (Kalman, low-pass, median).
   * Time-domain features: mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$, RMS, peak-to-peak.
   * Frequency-domain features: FFT magnitudes, spectral entropy.
   * Domain-specific: SLAM residuals, control effort $u_t$, battery state-of-charge (SoC).

3. **Nominal Model Construction (Training Phase)**
   * A statistical or ML model $\mathcal{M}$ is fit on data known to be *normal*.
   * The model implicitly defines the boundary $\partial \mathcal{N}$ of the nominal region.

4. **Anomaly Scoring (Inference Phase)**
   * At runtime, compute a scalar or vector score $s_t = f(x_t, \mathcal{M})$ indicating how "far" $x_t$ lies from the nominal distribution.

5. **Thresholding & Decision**
   * Compare $s_t$ to threshold $\tau$. If $s_t > \tau$, flag as abnormal.
   * Optionally apply a debouncing window of length $W$ to suppress spurious alarms.

6. **Response & Recovery Action**
   * Safe-stop, replan trajectory, notify operator, switch to backup sensor, or initiate self-diagnosis.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

* *Why filter?* — Sensors in field environments are noisy; raw data inflates false alarms.
* *Why features?* — Raw high-dimensional streams are computationally expensive; features compress the information.
* *Why a nominal model?* — In real deployments, abnormal data is **rare and unlabeled**; one-class / unsupervised learning becomes the practical choice.
* *Why thresholding?* — Converts a continuous score into a binary decision suitable for actuators and alarms.
* *Why debounce?* — A single abnormal sample is often a transient artifact; aggregating over a window improves robustness.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **most frequently tested equations** for Abnormal Behavior Detection. Memorize the symbols, units, and the **direction of deviation** (one-tailed vs two-tailed).

| # | Method | Core Equation | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|--------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Z-Score (Univariate)** | $Z_t = \dfrac{x_t - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $x_t$ — sample, $\mu$ — mean, $\sigma$ — std. dev. (same units) | Wheel speed, current, voltage drift |
| 2 | **Mahalanobis Distance (Multivariate)** | $D_M = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | $x, \mu \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\Sigma$ — covariance matrix | Multi-sensor joint anomaly |
| 3 | **Modified Z-Score (Robust)** | $M_i = \dfrac{0.6745 \cdot (x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}$ | $\tilde{x}$ — median, MAD — median absolute deviation | Outlier-resistant detection |
| 4 | **EWMA Control Chart Statistic** | $z_t = \lambda x_t + (1-\lambda) z_{t-1}$ | $\lambda \in (0,1]$ — smoothing constant | Slow-drift fault detection |
| 5 | **Control Chart Limits (3-sigma)** | $\text{UCL} = \mu + L\sigma,\ \text{LCL} = \mu - L\sigma$ | $L = 3$ typical (Shewhart) | Process monitoring |
| 6 | **Bayes Posterior Odds** | $\dfrac{P(H_1 \mid x)}{P(H_0 \mid x)} = \dfrac{P(x \mid H_1)}{P(x \mid H_0)} \cdot \dfrac{P(H_1)}{P(H_0)}$ | Likelihood ratio $\Lambda(x)$ | Decision-theoretic detection |
| 7 | **Reconstruction Error (Autoencoder)** | $e_t = \lVert x_t - \hat{x}_t \rVert_2^2$ | $\hat{x}_t$ — reconstructed output | Deep anomaly detection |
| 8 | **Isolation Forest Path Length** | $s(x, n) = 2^{-\mathbb{E}[h(x)] / c(n)}$ | $h(x)$ — avg. path length, $c(n)$ — normalization | Tree-based outlier scoring |
| 9 | **One-Class SVM Decision** | $f(x) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_i \alpha_i K(x_i, x) - \rho \right)$ | $K$ — kernel, $\rho$ — offset | Nonlinear boundary |
| 10 | **Precision-Recall-F1** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $P = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \tfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Performance evaluation |
| 11 | **ROC-AUC** | $\text{AUC} = \displaystyle\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$ | Scalar in $[0,1]$ | Threshold-free metric |
| 12 | **Chi-Square Statistic (Multivariate)** | $\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $O_i$ — observed, $E_i$ — expected | Sensor cross-check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** In all table entries, scalar absolute values and norms are written with $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table grid. Do not write raw `|x|` in a table cell.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Agricultural Robots** (e.g., weeding robots): detect wheel slip on wet soil via encoder–IMU discrepancy → $D_M$ spikes.
* **Hospital Service Robots**: detect stalled wheel via motor current $Z$-score crossing $\tau = 3$.
* **Inspection Drones**: detect motor overheating via EWMA of temperature sensor.
* **Domestic Vacuum Robots**: detect cliff sensor failure via Mahalanobis test on multi-sensor array.
* **Autonomous Wheelchairs**: detect user-intent anomaly via HRI deviation from learned user profile.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Optimal Decision Threshold Under Gaussian Assumption

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):** A mobile robot's wheel encoder produces a velocity reading $v_t$ in m/s. Under normal operation, $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \sigma_0^2)$ with $\mu_0 = 1.0$ m/s, $\sigma_0 = 0.1$ m/s. When the wheel slips (abnormal), $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ with $\mu_1 = 1.5$ m/s, $\sigma_1 = 0.15$ m/s. Prior probabilities: $P(H_0) = 0.98$, $P(H_1) = 0.02$. Cost of miss: $C_{10} = 10$; cost of false alarm: $C_{01} = 1$. Determine the optimal threshold $\tau^*$.

**Step 1 — Formulate Bayes Risk.**

The expected risk of deciding $H_1$ when $H_0$ is true (false alarm) and deciding $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true (miss) is minimized at the threshold $\tau^*$ where:

$$P(x \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1) \cdot C_{10} = P(x \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0) \cdot C_{01}$$

This is the **likelihood ratio test (LRT)** decision boundary.

**Step 2 — Substitute Gaussian Likelihoods.**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_1} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right) \cdot P(H_1) \cdot C_{10} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_0} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right) \cdot P(H_0) \cdot C_{01}$$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm (log-likelihood form).**

$$-\ln(\sigma_1) - \frac{(x-\mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} + \ln P(H_1) + \ln C_{10} = -\ln(\sigma_0) - \frac{(x-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} + \ln P(H_0) + \ln C_{01}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Isolate the Quadratic in $x$.**

$$\frac{(x-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} - \frac{(x-\mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} = \ln\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_0} + \ln\frac{P(H_0)\, C_{01}}{P(H_1)\, C_{10}}$$

**Step 5 — Plug Numerical Values.**

$$\mu_0 = 1.0,\ \sigma_0 = 0.1,\ \mu_1 = 1.5,\ \sigma_1 = 0.15,\ P(H_0)=0.98,\ P(H_1)=0.02,\ C_{10}=10,\ C_{01}=1$$

Compute the constant term on the right-hand side:

$$\ln\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_0} = \ln\frac{0.15}{0.10} = \ln(1.5) \approx 0.4055$$

$$\ln\frac{P(H_0)\, C_{01}}{P(H_1)\, C_{10}} = \ln\frac{0.98 \times 1}{0.02 \times 10} = \ln(4.9) \approx 1.5892$$

Right-hand side total:

$$K = 0.4055 + 1.5892 = 1.9947$$

**Step 6 — Expand the Quadratic Terms.**

$$\frac{(x-1.0)^2}{2(0.1)^2} - \frac{(x-1.5)^2}{2(0.15)^2} = 1.9947$$

$$\frac{(x-1.0)^2}{0.02} - \frac{(x-1.5)^2}{0.045} = 1.9947$$

Multiply through by $\text{lcm}(0.02, 0.045) = 0.09$:

$$4.5(x-1.0)^2 - 2.0(x-1.5)^2 = 0.17952$$

**Step 7 — Expand and Collect Terms.**

$$4.5(x^2 - 2x + 1) - 2.0(x^2 - 3x + 2.25) = 0.17952$$

$$4.5x^2 - 9x + 4.5 - 2.0x^2 + 6x - 4.5 = 0.17952$$

$$2.5x^2 - 3x + 0 = 0.17952$$

$$2.5x^2 - 3x - 0.17952 = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve the Quadratic Equation.**

Using the quadratic formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a=2.5$, $b=-3$, $c=-0.17952$:

$$x = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9 + 4(2.5)(0.17952)}}{5} = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9 + 1.7952}}{5} = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{10.7952}}{5}$$

$$\sqrt{10.7952} \approx 3.2856$$

$$x_1 = \frac{3 + 3.2856}{5} = 1.2571\ \text{m/s}, \quad x_2 = \frac{3 - 3.2856}{5} = -0.0571\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 9 — Select the Physically Meaningful Root.**

Since the robot's velocity is in the range $\sim 1$ m/s, the relevant threshold is:

$$\boxed{\tau^* \approx 1.257\ \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 10 — Interpretation for KTU Exam Answer.**

Any encoder reading above **1.257 m/s** triggers the abnormal-behavior alarm (probable wheel slip). The threshold sits closer to $\mu_1$ because the prior probability of slip is low ($P(H_1)=0.02$) and the cost of miss is high ($C_{10}=10$), which biases the test toward *sensitivity* (catching slip) over *specificity* (avoiding false alarms). This is a classic *asymmetric cost* LRT — a frequent 14-mark problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Mahalanobis Distance for Multi-Sensor Anomaly

**Problem Setup:** A robot's two-sensor vector $\mathbf{x} = [v, \omega]^T$ (linear and angular velocity) under nominal operation has mean $\boldsymbol{\mu} = [1.0, 0.5]^T$ and covariance:

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.01 \\ 0.01 & 0.09 \end{bmatrix}$$

A live reading is $\mathbf{x}_{obs} = [1.3, 0.9]^T$. Determine whether this is abnormal at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (for 2-D data, $\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.991$).

**Step 1 — Compute the Deviation Vector.**

$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{x}_{obs} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.3 - 1.0 \\ 0.9 - 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Inverse Covariance.**

$$\det(\Sigma) = (0.04)(0.09) - (0.01)^2 = 0.0036 - 0.0001 = 0.0035$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.0035}\begin{bmatrix} 0.09 & -0.01 \\ -0.01 & 0.04 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 25.714 & -2.857 \\ -2.857 & 11.429 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Squared Mahalanobis Distance.**

$$D_M^2 = \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{d} = [0.3\ \ 0.4] \begin{bmatrix} 25.714 & -2.857 \\ -2.857 & 11.429 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the intermediate vector:

$$\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{d} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.714 \times 0.3 + (-2.857) \times 0.4 \\ -2.857 \times 0.3 + 11.429 \times 0.4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.714 - 1.143 \\ -0.857 + 4.572 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6.571 \\ 3.715 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now the dot product:

$$D_M^2 = 0.3 \times 6.571 + 0.4 \times 3.715 = 1.971 + 1.486 = 3.457$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the Chi-Square Critical Value.**

$$D_M^2 = 3.457 < \chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.991$$

**Step 5 — Decision.**

Since the squared Mahalanobis distance is **less than** the critical value, we **fail to reject $H_0$**. The reading is *statistically normal*, even though both components individually deviate from the mean — because the *joint correlation structure* (positive covariance) is consistent with the reading.

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: Normal Behavior (No alarm at } \alpha = 0.05\text{)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** This example demonstrates *why* multivariate methods (Mahalanobis) outperform univariate methods (Z-score) for field robotics — they respect sensor correlation and avoid false alarms caused by correlated normal fluctuations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Robot Scenario | Data Type | Recommended Method | Reason |
|----------------|-----------|--------------------|--------|
| Single encoder drift | Univariate scalar | Z-Score / Modified Z-Score | Simple, fast, low compute |
| Wheel slip on rough terrain | Multivariate (v, ω, I) | Mahalanobis Distance | Correlated sensors |
| Slow battery degradation | Time series, gradual | EWMA Control Chart | Sensitive to small mean shifts |
| Catastrophic motor stall | High-magnitude abrupt | 3-σ Shewhart + CUSUM | Quick detection of step changes |
| Unknown fault types | Rich multivariate logs | One-Class SVM / Isolation Forest | Learns nonlinear nominal boundary |
| Visual scene anomaly | Image patches | Autoencoder reconstruction error | Captures high-dimensional patterns |
| HRI behavioral drift | Sequential, labeled | LSTM + threshold | Models temporal context |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ABD System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Proprioceptive Sensors] --> A1[Data Acquisition Unit]
    S2[Exteroceptive Sensors] --> A1
    S3[Human Interaction Logs] --> A1
    A1 --> B1[Preprocessing Filter]
    B1 --> B2[Feature Extraction Module]
    B2 --> C1{Nominal Model M}
    C1 --> D1[Anomaly Score Engine]
    D1 --> D2[Threshold Comparator]
    D2 --> E1{Decision Module}
    E1 -->|Normal| F1[Continue Operation]
    E1 -->|Abnormal| F2[Safety Handler]
    F2 --> G1[Alarm and Notify]
    F2 --> G2[Graceful Degradation]
    F2 --> G3[Self Diagnosis Trigger]
    G3 --> H1[Recovery Action Planner]
    H1 --> A1
```

**Figure 4.1 — Functional architecture of a closed-loop ABD system in a service robot.** Sensor data flows upward through preprocessing, scoring, and thresholding, then branches into a binary decision: continue operation or trigger safety handling. The recovery planner feeds back to the acquisition unit, enabling *resilient* long-term autonomy.

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Abnormal Behavior (Hierarchical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Abnormal Behavior] --> B0[Hardware Level]
    A0 --> C0[Software Level]
    A0 --> D0[Environmental Level]
    A0 --> E0[Interaction Level]
    B0 --> B1[Sensor Fault]
    B0 --> B2[Actuator Fault]
    B0 --> B3[Power Supply Fault]
    C0 --> C1[Planning Anomaly]
    C0 --> C2[Control Anomaly]
    C0 --> C3[Perception Anomaly]
    D0 --> D1[Dynamic Obstacle]
    D0 --> D2[Lighting Change]
    D0 --> D3[Terrain Change]
    E0 --> E1[Unexpected User Action]
    E0 --> E2[Verbal Conflict]
    E0 --> E3[Safety Violation]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Hierarchical taxonomy of abnormal behavior in field/service robots.** This 4-tier decomposition is widely used in KTU-endorsed robotics curricula for fault-mode analysis (FMEA) and is a frequent 7-mark question.

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Threshold-Based Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Sample xt] --> Q1[Compute Score st]
    Q1 --> Q2{Compare st vs tau}
    Q2 -->|st less than tau| R1[Increment Normal Counter]
    Q2 -->|st greater than tau| R2[Increment Alarm Counter]
    R1 --> S1{Counter greater than W}
    R2 --> S1
    S1 -->|No| T1[Wait Next Sample]
    S1 -->|Yes| U1[Raise Anomaly Flag]
    U1 --> V1[Trigger Recovery]
```

**Figure 4.3 — Debounced threshold logic.** A sliding window of length $W$ suppresses transient spikes, reducing false alarms in noisy field environments — a critical KTU high-yield concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define Abnormal Behavior Detection in the context of a service robot. List any four categories of abnormal behavior with one example each.**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

Abnormal Behavior Detection (ABD) is the process of identifying deviations from a robot's expected operational state, sensory input, or interaction pattern, using statistical or machine-learning models trained on nominal behavior.

Four categories:

1. **Sensor Anomaly** — e.g., LiDAR returns all zeros due to a blocked lens.
2. **Actuator Anomaly** — e.g., a stalled wheel motor drawing abnormal current.
3. **Environmental Anomaly** — e.g., a transparent glass door perceived as free space.
4. **Interaction Anomaly** — e.g., a user grabbing the robot's arm, triggering an unexpected force-torque reading.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners award only **1 mark** for the definition if examples are missing or only one category is listed. Always pair definition with **classification + example** to secure full marks.

---

**Q2. What is the Mahalanobis distance? How is it superior to the standard Z-score for multivariate robot sensor data?**

*Model Answer (3 marks):*

The Mahalanobis distance is a multivariate statistical measure that quantifies the distance of a data point from the mean of a distribution, *accounting for correlations between variables*:

$$D_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$$

**Superiority over Z-score:**
* The Z-score treats each variable independently and assumes zero correlation. In robotic sensor arrays (IMU + encoder + LiDAR), variables are correlated.
* The Mahalanobis distance uses the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ to "whiten" the data, producing a *unitless, correlation-aware* anomaly score.
* A Z-score may flag correlated normal fluctuations as abnormal, but Mahalanobis correctly accepts them.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Adapted Style]**

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an Abnormal Behavior Detection system in a service robot. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A mobile robot's wheel encoder produces a velocity signal $v$ (m/s) that is normally distributed with mean $\mu_0 = 0.8$ m/s and standard deviation $\sigma_0 = 0.05$ m/s. Under an abnormal (wheel-slip) condition, the distribution shifts to $\mu_1 = 1.0$ m/s with $\sigma_1 = 0.08$ m/s. Using the Bayes Minimum Risk criterion with $P(H_0) = 0.95$, $P(H_1) = 0.05$, $C_{10} = 8$, $C_{01} = 1$, determine the optimal decision threshold $\tau^*$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.A(a)

The functional architecture consists of the following sequential blocks (refer to **Figure 4.1**):

1. **Data Acquisition Unit (1 mark):** Collects raw signals from proprioceptive sensors (encoders, IMU, current sensors) and exteroceptive sensors (camera, LiDAR, ultrasonic). Sampling at 50–200 Hz.

2. **Preprocessing Filter (1 mark):** Applies low-pass, median, or Kalman filters to suppress noise. Removes outliers from raw data.

3. **Feature Extraction Module (1 mark):** Computes time-domain features (mean, variance, RMS, peak-to-peak) and frequency-domain features (FFT magnitudes, spectral entropy). Optionally extracts SLAM residuals or control effort signals.

4. **Nominal Model (1 mark):** A statistical model (Gaussian, multivariate Gaussian) or ML model (One-Class SVM, Autoencoder) trained on confirmed-normal data, defining the region $\mathcal{N}$ of nominal behavior.

5. **Anomaly Score Engine (1 mark):** At runtime, computes the score $s_t$ — e.g., Z-score, Mahalanobis distance, or reconstruction error.

6. **Threshold Comparator (1 mark):** Compares $s_t$ with the calibrated threshold $\tau$. Applies a debouncing window of length $W$ to suppress transient spikes.

7. **Safety Handler (1 mark):** On detecting an anomaly, executes a recovery action — alarm, safe-stop, replan trajectory, or switch to backup sensor.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.A(b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Apply Bayes Minimum Risk Criterion.**

The optimal threshold $\tau^*$ satisfies:

$$P(\tau^* \mid H_1) \cdot P(H_1) \cdot C_{10} = P(\tau^* \mid H_0) \cdot P(H_0) \cdot C_{01}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Gaussian PDFs.**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\tau^* - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right) P(H_1) C_{10} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma_0}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(\tau^* - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right) P(H_0) C_{01}$$

**Step 3 — Take Log and Rearrange.**

$$\frac{(\tau^* - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2} - \frac{(\tau^* - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2} = \ln\!\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_0}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{P(H_0) C_{01}}{P(H_1) C_{10}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Values.**

$$\mu_0 = 0.8,\ \sigma_0 = 0.05,\ \mu_1 = 1.0,\ \sigma_1 = 0.08$$

Right-hand side:

$$\ln(0.08/0.05) = \ln(1.6) = 0.4700$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.95 \times 1}{0.05 \times 8}\right) = \ln(2.375) = 0.8650$$

$$K = 0.4700 + 0.8650 = 1.3350$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Left Side.**

$$\frac{(\tau^* - 0.8)^2}{0.005} - \frac{(\tau^* - 1.0)^2}{0.0128} = 1.3350$$

Multiply through by $0.0128$:

$$2.56(\tau^* - 0.8)^2 - (\tau^* - 1.0)^2 = 0.01709$$

Expand:

$$2.56(\tau^{*2} - 1.6\tau^* + 0.64) - (\tau^{*2} - 2\tau^* + 1.0) = 0.01709$$

$$2.56\tau^{*2} - 4.096\tau^* + 1.6384 - \tau^{*2} + 2\tau^* - 1.0 = 0.01709$$

$$1.56\tau^{*2} - 2.096\tau^* + 0.6384 = 0.01709$$

$$1.56\tau^{*2} - 2.096\tau^* + 0.6213 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve Quadratic.**

$$a = 1.56,\ b = -2.096,\ c = 0.6213$$

$$\tau^* = \frac{2.096 \pm \sqrt{(2.096)^2 - 4(1.56)(0.6213)}}{2(1.56)}$$

Discriminant:

$$4.3932 - 3.8769 = 0.5163,\quad \sqrt{0.5163} \approx 0.7185$$

$$\tau^*_1 = \frac{2.096 + 0.7185}{3.12} = 0.902\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\tau^*_2 = \frac{2.096 - 0.7185}{3.12} = 0.442\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Select Physically Meaningful Root.**

The robot's nominal velocity is 0.8 m/s, so the relevant threshold lies between $\mu_0$ and $\mu_1$:

$$\boxed{\tau^* \approx 0.902\ \text{m/s}}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation (1 mark).**

Any encoder reading exceeding **0.902 m/s** triggers a wheel-slip alarm. The threshold is closer to $\mu_0$ because the cost of missing a slip ($C_{10} = 8$) is much higher than the cost of a false alarm ($C_{01} = 1$), biasing the system toward *sensitivity*.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q.B (a)** Compare statistical and machine-learning approaches to abnormal behavior detection in field robots. Discuss at least three methods from each category with their merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** The pose estimate of a 2-wheeled mobile robot from its odometry and IMU is represented by the vector $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, \theta]^T$. Under nominal conditions, the mean residual vector is $\boldsymbol{\mu} = [0, 0, 0]^T$ and the covariance is:

$$\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix} 0.04 & 0.005 & 0.002 \\ 0.005 & 0.09 & 0.003 \\ 0.002 & 0.003 & 0.01 \end{bmatrix}$$

A live residual reading is $\mathbf{x}_{obs} = [0.3, -0.4, 0.2]^T$. Using the Mahalanobis test at $\alpha = 0.05$ ($\chi^2_{3, 0.05} = 7.815$), determine whether the reading indicates abnormal behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.B(a) — Comparison

**Statistical Methods:**

1. **Z-Score Test (1 mark)** — Simple, low compute, univariate. *Limitation:* ignores correlations.
2. **Mahalanobis Distance (1 mark)** — Multivariate, correlation-aware. *Limitation:* assumes Gaussian distribution.
3. **EWMA / CUSUM Control Charts (1 mark)** — Excellent for small mean shifts over time. *Limitation:* requires sequential data; slow for abrupt faults.

**Machine Learning Methods:**

1. **One-Class SVM (1 mark)** — Learns nonlinear nominal boundary. *Limitation:* sensitive to hyperparameters; needs representative training data.
2. **Isolation Forest (1 mark)** — Fast, scalable, no density assumption. *Limitation:* may misclassify sparse-but-normal regions.
3. **Autoencoder (1 mark)** — Captures complex high-dimensional patterns (vision, audio). *Limitation:* requires large training data; reconstruction threshold is empirical.

**Comparative Remarks (1 mark):** Statistical methods are preferred for *interpretable, low-latency* embedded applications; ML methods dominate when *rich multimodal sensor data* and *nonlinear nominal regions* are involved, as in modern HRI scenarios.

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q.B(b) — Step-by-Step Mahalanobis Test

**Step 1 — Compute Deviation Vector.**

$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{x}_{obs} - \boldsymbol{\mu} = [0.3,\ -0.4,\ 0.2]^T$$

**Step 2 — Compute Determinant of $\Sigma$.**

$$\det(\Sigma) = 0.04(0.09 \times 0.01 - 0.003^2) - 0.005(0.005 \times 0.01 - 0.003 \times 0.002) + 0.002(0.005 \times 0.003 - 0.09 \times 0.002)$$

$$= 0.04(0.0009 - 0.000009) - 0.005(0.00005 - 0.000006) + 0.002(0.000015 - 0.00018)$$

$$= 0.04(0.000891) - 0.005(0.000044) + 0.002(-0.000165)$$

$$= 0.00003564 - 0.00000022 - 0.00000033 = 0.00003509$$

$$\det(\Sigma) \approx 3.509 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cofactor Matrix.**

Using cofactor expansion:

$$C_{11} = (0.09)(0.01) - (0.003)^2 = 0.000891$$
$$C_{12} = -[(0.005)(0.01) - (0.003)(0.002)] = -(0.000044) = -0.000044$$
$$C_{13} = (0.005)(0.003) - (0.09)(0.002) = 0.000015 - 0.00018 = -0.000165$$
$$C_{21} = -[(0.005)(0.01) - (0.002)(0.003)] = -(0.000044) = -0.000044$$
$$C_{22} = (0.04)(0.01) - (0.002)^2 = 0.0004 - 0.000004 = 0.000396$$
$$C_{23} = -[(0.04)(0.003) - (0.005)(0.002)] = -(0.00012 - 0.00001) = -0.00011$$
$$C_{31} = (0.005)(0.003) - (0.002)(0.09) = 0.000015 - 0.00018 = -0.000165$$
$$C_{32} = -[(0.04)(0.003) - (0.005)(0.002)] = -(0.00012 - 0.00001) = -0.00011$$
$$C_{33} = (0.04)(0.09) - (0.005)^2 = 0.0036 - 0.000025 = 0.003575$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$.**

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det(\Sigma)} \cdot \text{adj}(\Sigma) = \frac{1}{3.509 \times 10^{-5}} \begin{bmatrix} 0.000891 & -0.000044 & -0.000165 \\ -0.000044 & 0.000396 & -0.00011 \\ -0.000165 & -0.00011 & 0.003575 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 25.393 & -1.254 & -4.703 \\ -1.254 & 11.286 & -3.135 \\ -4.703 & -3.135 & 101.880 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{d}$.**

$$\Sigma^{-1}\mathbf{d} = \begin{bmatrix} 25.393(0.3) + (-1.254)(-0.4) + (-4.703)(0.2) \\ -1.254(0.3) + 11.286(-0.4) + (-3.135)(0.2) \\ -4.703(0.3) + (-3.135)(-0.4) + 101.880(0.2) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 7.618 + 0.502 - 0.941 \\ -0.376 - 4.514 - 0.627 \\ -1.411 + 1.254 + 20.376 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.179 \\ -5.517 \\ 20.219 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Squared Mahalanobis Distance.**

$$D_M^2 = \mathbf{d}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{d} = (0.3)(7.179) + (-0.4)(-5.517) + (0.2)(20.219)$$

$$= 2.154 + 2.207 + 4.044 = 8.405$$

**Step 7 — Decision.**

$$D_M^2 = 8.405 > \chi^2_{3, 0.05} = 7.815$$

Since the squared Mahalanobis distance **exceeds** the critical value, we **reject $H_0$**.

$$\boxed{\text{Decision: Abnormal Behavior Detected — Trigger alarm / safe-stop.}}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation (1 mark).**

The robot's pose residual vector lies in a region of the state space that is statistically very unlikely under nominal operation. The strong deviation in the heading component ($\theta = 0.2$ rad, scaled by the high $\Sigma^{-1}_{33}$ weight of **101.88**) drives most of the anomaly score. A likely root cause is IMU bias drift or wheel odometry slip.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the null and alternative hypotheses explicitly** before testing (–1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing the threshold direction** — students often compare $D_M^2$ with $D_M$ or with the wrong percentile (–1 mark).
> 3. **Skipping the physical interpretation** — even if the math is right, omitting the *engineering meaning* of the result forfeits the final 1 mark.
> 4. **Arithmetic slip in computing $\Sigma^{-1}$** — the cofactor signs (especially $(-1)^{i+j}$) are a common mistake; double-check using a calculator in the exam.
> 5. **Failing to round final answers to 3 significant figures** — KTU examiners penalize unrounded or over-rounded numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [x] **Definition First:** Always define ABD as *deviation detection from a nominal model*; mention the hypothesis test $H_0$ vs $H_1$.
- [x] **Six-Block Pipeline:** Acquisition → Preprocessing → Features → Nominal Model → Scoring → Threshold → Recovery. Draw this in 30 seconds for full marks.
- [x] **Z-Score Threshold:** $\lvert Z \rvert > 3$ is the conventional 3-sigma rule; corresponds to ~0.27% false alarm rate under Gaussian assumption.
- [x] **Mahalanobis > Z-Score** when sensors are correlated — *always mention correlation* in your answer.
- [x] **EWMA** is best for slow drifts; **CUSUM** is best for small step changes; **Shewhart** is best for large abrupt shifts.
- [x] **Bayes Risk Formulation:** $P(\tau^* \mid H_1) P(H_1) C_{10} = P(\tau^* \mid H_0) P(H_0) C_{01}$ — memorize this LRT condition.
- [x] **Chi-Square Critical Values** to remember: $\chi^2_{1, 0.05} = 3.841$, $\chi^2_{2, 0.05} = 5.991$, $\chi^2_{3, 0.05} = 7.815$, $\chi^2_{4, 0.05} = 9.488$.
- [x] **Asymmetric Cost Principle:** Higher $C_{10}$ (miss cost) shifts threshold *toward* $\mu_0$ to catch more true positives.
- [x] **False Alarm Mitigation:** Use debouncing window of length $W$ (typically 3–10 samples) to suppress transient spikes.
- [x] **Performance Metrics:** Always report **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and ROC-AUC** in comparative answers.
- [x] **ML Methods** (One-Class SVM, Isolation Forest, Autoencoder) outperform statistics for *nonlinear, high-dimensional* nominal regions.
- [x] **Real-world Anchors:** Agricultural weeding robots, hospital delivery robots, domestic vacuum cleaners, inspection drones — quote at least one in your answer to demonstrate contextual awareness.
- [x] **Safety Standards:** Reference **ISO 13482** (personal care robots) and **ISO 10218** (industrial robots) when discussing the *why*.
- [x] **Closing Statement Template:** "Hence, the abnormal behavior has been detected with [method], and the robot must execute [recovery action] to maintain ISO-compliant safe operation."

---

> **End of Module 4 — Abnormal Behavior Detection Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme · Course Outcomes: CO4 (Dependability), CO5 (Safe Autonomy)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MFCC Feature Extraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MFCC Feature Extraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients)** is a feature extraction technique that converts a time-domain audio signal into a compact set of coefficients representing its short-term power spectrum on a perceptually-relevant (Mel) scale. It is derived by applying the **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** to the logarithm of the **Mel-scaled filterbank energies** of the signal's magnitude spectrum.

In the context of **Field and Service Robotics (PCRUT602)**, MFCC is the de-facto front-end for audio-based perception modules such as:
- Voice command recognition for Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)
- Acoustic event detection (glass break, alarms, screams)
- Fault diagnosis of robotic actuators via sound signatures
- Environmental sound classification in service robots

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ear Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine the human ear is a translator that does not treat all frequencies equally. The ear is *highly sensitive* to low frequencies (e.g., the rumble of a vacuum-cleaner robot) and *less sensitive* to high frequencies (e.g., a high-pitched ultrasonic ping). The **Mel scale** is the mathematical representation of this non-linear human auditory perception. MFCC essentially says: *"Don't measure pitch the way a physicist would (linear Hz). Measure it the way your ear would (Mel)."*

A real-world analogy in robotics:
- A **service robot in a hospital** hears a sound at 1000 Hz and another at 2000 Hz. To a linear microphone these are "1× and 2× frequencies." To a human nurse (and the robot mimicking her perception via MFCC), the *perceived* difference is much smaller than the difference between, say, 100 Hz and 1100 Hz. MFCC captures this perceptual weighting.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)** | Number of samples per second in the digital audio signal | **16 kHz** (speech), **44.1 kHz** (audio) |
| **Frame Length** | Duration of one analysis window | **20 – 40 ms** |
| **Frame Shift (Hop)** | Step between successive frames | **10 ms** (50% overlap) |
| **FFT Size ($N$)** | Number of points in the Fast Fourier Transform | **512** or **1024** |
| **Number of Mel Filters ($M$)** | Triangular filters spanning the Mel scale | **20 – 40** |
| **Number of Cepstral Coefficients ($C$)** | DCT output features retained | **12 – 13** |
| **Pre-emphasis Coefficient ($\alpha$)** | High-pass filter factor to boost high frequencies | **0.95 – 0.97** |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to *derive* the Mel scale formula, *sketch* the filterbank, and *explain* the role of DCT in decorrelating log-Mel energies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete MFCC Pipeline — Structured Logic

The MFCC extraction algorithm performs the following sequence on a raw audio waveform $x[n]$:

1. **Pre-emphasis Filtering** — Boost the high-frequency energy that is naturally attenuated in speech/sound.
2. **Frame Blocking (Framing)** — Slice the signal into short, quasi-stationary frames of 20–40 ms.
3. **Windowing** — Multiply each frame by a smooth window (Hamming/Hanning) to reduce spectral leakage.
4. **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** — Convert the time-domain frame into the frequency domain.
5. **Magnitude Spectrum** — Compute $\vert X(k) \vert$ by taking the absolute value of the complex FFT output.
6. **Mel Filterbank** — Pass the magnitude spectrum through a bank of triangular filters spaced on the Mel scale.
7. **Logarithm** — Take the natural log of the filterbank energies (mimics human loudness perception).
8. **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)** — Decorrelate the log-Mel energies and produce the cepstral coefficients.
9. **Dynamic Features (Deltas)** — Compute $\Delta$ and $\Delta\Delta$ to capture temporal evolution.
10. **Energy Normalization** — Mean and variance normalization for robustness.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" each step?**
> - Pre-emphasis → counteracts the $-6$ dB/octave roll-off of the glottal source.
> - Framing → audio is *non-stationary* globally but *quasi-stationary* over short windows.
> - Windowing → abrupt frame boundaries cause Gibbs phenomenon (spectral leakage).
> - Mel filterbank → matches the non-linear frequency resolution of the cochlea.
> - Log → converts multiplicative variations (e.g., vocal-tract filtering) into additive ones, enabling cepstral analysis.
> - DCT → real-valued, decorrelated features; only the lower-order coefficients carry the *spectral envelope* (vocal-tract info).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \, x[n-1]$ | Pre-emphasis filter (typical $\alpha = 0.97$) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{mel}(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$ | Hertz → Mel (logarithmic) conversion | **mel** |
| 3 | $f = 700 \left(e^{\text{mel}/1127} - 1\right)$ | Mel → Hertz inverse conversion | **Hz** |
| 4 | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Hamming window | dimensionless |
| 5 | $X[k] = \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_w[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | $N$-point FFT | complex |
| 6 | $E_m = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^{2}\, H_m[k]$ | Energy at the $m$-th Mel filter | **Joules** (relative) |
| 7 | $S_m = \ln(E_m)$ | Log filterbank energy | **log-units** |
| 8 | $c_n = \displaystyle\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{M}} \sum_{m=1}^{M} S_m \cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi n}{M}(m - \tfrac{1}{2})\right]$ | DCT of log-Mel energies → MFCC | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta c_n[t] = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} k (c_n[t+k] - c_n[t-k])}{2 \sum_{k=1}^{K} k^{2}}$ | First-order time derivative (delta) | coefficient/rate |
| 10 | $\Delta\Delta c_n[t] = \Delta(\Delta c_n[t])$ | Second-order derivative (acceleration) | coefficient/rate² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Validity Conditions:**
> - The Mel scale is valid for $f \geq 0$ Hz and is **monotonically increasing**.
> - The DCT formula above is valid for $n = 1, 2, \dots, C$ (typically $C = 12$).
> - $\alpha$ in pre-emphasis: $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$, with $0.97$ as the KTU-typical value.
> - The $H_m[k]$ triangular filter is **non-zero** only between its lower and upper cutoffs.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Field Robotics**, MFCC features are used in:

- **Autonomous Vacuum Robots (e.g., Roomba-class):** Detect acoustic signatures of dirt type, surface (carpet vs. tile), or stall conditions from motor current-induced vibrations.
- **Search-and-Rescue Robots:** Identify human voice, screams, or knocking sounds in collapsed structures.
- **Agricultural Robots:** Distinguish crop-ripening sounds, pest activity, or machinery malfunctions.
- **Wearable Service Robots:** Hands-free voice command interfaces for elderly or disabled users.
- **Industrial Fault Diagnosis:** Acoustic emission monitoring of bearings and gears.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mel Scale from Psychoacoustic Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Justify the closed-form conversion between linear frequency (Hz) and the perceptually-motivated Mel scale.

The human ear's *critical bandwidth* $BW_c$ (in Hz) is approximately linear below 1 kHz and logarithmic above. Stevens, Volkmann, and Newman (1937) empirically proposed the relationship:

$$BW_c(f) \;\approx\; 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4\,f^{2}\right)^{0.69}$$

**Step 1 — Define Mel as the integral of the inverse critical bandwidth:**

$$\text{mel}(f) \;=\; k \int_{0}^{f} \dfrac{df'}{BW_c(f')}$$

For the simplified model where $BW_c$ is taken as constant ($25$ Hz) plus a logarithmic term, this integral yields:

$$\text{mel}(f) = k \int_{0}^{f} \dfrac{df'}{a + b \ln(1 + f'/c)}$$

**Step 2 — Approximate for high frequencies (where $\ln$ dominates):**

For $f \gg 1$, the integral simplifies to:

$$\text{mel}(f) \;\approx\; 1127 \,\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Verify boundary conditions:**

- At $f = 0$ Hz: $\text{mel}(0) = 1127 \ln(1) = 0$ mel ✓
- At $f = 1000$ Hz: $\text{mel}(1000) = 1127 \ln(1 + 1000/700) = 1127 \ln(2.4286) \approx 1000$ mel ✓

> This confirms the anchor: **1 kHz ≈ 1000 mel**, a property the linear scale cannot satisfy.

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain Hz from Mel:**

Starting from $M = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{M}{1127} &= \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right) \\[4pt]
e^{M/1127} &= 1 + \dfrac{f}{700} \\[4pt]
f &= 700 \left(e^{M/1127} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This inverse is essential for placing the triangular filter cutoffs in Hz before mapping them to FFT bin indices.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Triangular Mel Filterbank Weights

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Express the weight $H_m[k]$ of the $m$-th triangular filter evaluated at FFT bin $k$.

**Step 1 — Convert filter edges from Mel to Hz:**

For $M$ filters spanning $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mel}_{\min} &= 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f_{\min}}{700}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{mel}_{\max} &= 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f_{\max}}{700}\right) \\[4pt]
\Delta \text{mel} &= \dfrac{\text{mel}_{\max} - \text{mel}_{\min}}{M + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the $m$-th filter's lower, center, and upper frequencies in Mel:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mel}_{\text{low}}(m) &= \text{mel}_{\min} + m \cdot \Delta \text{mel} \\[4pt]
\text{mel}_{\text{center}}(m) &= \text{mel}_{\min} + (m+1) \cdot \Delta \text{mel} \\[4pt]
\text{mel}_{\text{high}}(m) &= \text{mel}_{\min} + (m+2) \cdot \Delta \text{mel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to Hz and then to FFT bin indices:**

$$
f_{*} = 700 \left(e^{\text{mel}_{*}/1127} - 1\right), \qquad k_{*} = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{N \cdot f_{*}}{f_s}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Write the piecewise linear weight $H_m[k]$:**

$$
H_m[k] = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{k - k_{\text{low}}(m)}{k_{\text{center}}(m) - k_{\text{low}}(m)}, & k_{\text{low}}(m) \leq k \leq k_{\text{center}}(m) \\[6pt]
\dfrac{k_{\text{high}}(m) - k}{k_{\text{high}}(m) - k_{\text{center}}(m)}, & k_{\text{center}}(m) \leq k \leq k_{\text{high}}(m) \\[6pt]
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This ensures the filterbank mimics the **critical-band grouping** of the cochlea.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Cepstrum via DCT

**Step 1 — Realize that the speech production model is convolutional:**

The speech signal $s[n]$ is the convolution of an *excitation* $e[n]$ (glottal pulses or noise) and a *vocal-tract filter* $h[n]$:

$$S[k] = E[k] \cdot H[k]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the logarithm (complex cepstrum):**

$$\ln S[k] = \ln E[k] + \ln H[k]$$

The logarithm converts the multiplicative interaction into an *additive* one.

**Step 3 — Take the inverse DFT to obtain the cepstrum:**

$$c[n] = \text{IDFT}\{\ln S[k]\}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the resulting cepstrum has two distinct regions:**

- **Low quefrencies** ($n$ small): contain the *vocal-tract envelope* $\rightarrow$ *kept* as MFCCs.
- **High quefrencies** ($n$ large): contain the *excitation harmonics* $\rightarrow$ *discarded* by truncating to $C = 12$ coefficients.

**Step 5 — Use the DCT (Type-II) instead of the IDFT for real-valued, decorrelated output:**

$$
c_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{M}} \sum_{m=1}^{M} S_m \cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi n}{M}\!\left(m - \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\right], \quad n = 1, 2, \dots, C
$$

where $S_m = \ln(E_m)$ is the log-energy of the $m$-th Mel filter.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The DCT is preferred over the DFT because:
> (a) it produces *real-valued* coefficients (DFT is generally complex),
> (b) it has *better energy compaction* (most info in first few coefficients),
> (c) it decorrelates the log-Mel energies, which is essential for Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) and Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) used later in speech/robotic recognition.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A service robot's microphone records a tonal alarm at $f = 2000$ Hz with $f_s = 16$ kHz, $N = 512$ FFT points, and $M = 20$ Mel filters. Compute the corresponding Mel value and the FFT bin index.

**Step 1 — Mel value:**

$$\text{mel}(2000) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2000}{700}\right) = 1127 \ln(3.857) \approx 1127 \times 1.3499 \approx 1521.4 \text{ mel}$$

**Step 2 — FFT bin index:**

$$k = \dfrac{N \cdot f}{f_s} = \dfrac{512 \times 2000}{16000} = 64$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** A 2 kHz alarm tone sits in Mel-band ~1521, with most of its spectral energy concentrated near bin 64. The triangular filterbank will smooth this bin into the appropriate Mel channel for downstream classification (alarm vs. non-alarm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MFCC Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Audio Signal x of n] --> B[Pre Emphasis Filter alpha 0.97]
    B --> C[Frame Blocking 25 ms frames 10 ms hop]
    C --> D[Windowing Hamming w of n]
    D --> E[FFT N point N equals 512]
    E --> F[Magnitude Spectrum X of k magnitude squared]
    F --> G[Mel Filterbank M 20 to 40 triangular filters]
    G --> H[Logarithm natural log of energies]
    H --> I[DCT Type II Decorrelation]
    I --> J[MFCC Vector c1 to c12]
    J --> K[Delta and Delta Delta Features]
    K --> L[Energy Normalization CMVN]
    L --> M[Feature Vector for Classifier SVM HMM DNN]
    M --> N[Robotic Decision Voice Command Event Detection Fault Class]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:** Follow the arrow flow from raw audio (left) to the final feature vector (right). Each block performs a *signal-processing* or *feature-transformation* step. The **Mel filterbank** is the *only* perceptually-motivated stage — all others are standard DSP operations.

## 4.2 Mel Filterbank Geometry (Conceptual Sketch via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Filter 0\nlow freq] --> F1[Filter 1] --> F2[Filter 2] --> F3[Filter 3] --> F4[Filter 4\nhigh freq]
    AX["Frequency Axis in Hz\nNarrow spacing at low f\nWide spacing at high f"] ~~~ F0
    AX ~~~ F1
    AX ~~~ F2
    AX ~~~ F3
    AX ~~~ F4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Note:** In a real plot, the triangular filters are *narrow* at low frequencies (mimicking fine frequency discrimination in the cochlear base) and *wide* at high frequencies (mimicking coarser discrimination at the cochlear apex). This Mel-warped resolution is the *core* contribution of MFCC over raw FFT magnitudes.

## 4.3 Information Flow inside the Cepstral Domain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Spectrum Log S of k] --> S2[Inverse DFT or DCT]
    S2 --> S3[Cepstrum c of n]
    S3 --> S4[Low Quefrency n 1 to 12\nVocal Tract Envelope]
    S3 --> S5[High Quefrency n 13 to M\nExcitation Harmonics]
    S4 --> S6[Retained as MFCCs]
    S5 --> S7[Discarded]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Point:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why do we discard the higher-order cepstral coefficients?"* The answer lies in this diagram — the higher quefrencies encode *source* characteristics (pitch, harmonics), which vary with the speaker or specific actuator and are not robust features for *content recognition* in robotic audio perception.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Mel scale. Why is it preferred over the linear Hz scale in audio feature extraction for robotic perception?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mel scale** is a psychoacoustic frequency scale in which pitches are perceived as equally spaced by human listeners. It is defined as:

$$\text{mel}(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$$

It is preferred because:
1. **It mirrors human auditory perception**, where frequency resolution is high at low frequencies and degrades at high frequencies.
2. It **improves classification accuracy** in robotic audio recognition tasks (voice commands, acoustic events) by emphasizing perceptually salient regions.
3. It **reduces feature dimensionality** without losing perceptually important information, easing real-time computation on resource-constrained robot processors.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) in the MFCC pipeline?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DCT is applied to the **log-Mel filterbank energies** to produce the final cepstral coefficients. Its roles are:

1. **Decorrelation:** It transforms the correlated log-Mel energies into a set of approximately independent coefficients, which is beneficial for downstream classifiers like GMM/HMM.
2. **Energy Compaction:** It concentrates most of the signal's information into the lower-order coefficients, enabling truncation to 12–13 features.
3. **Real-valued Output:** Unlike the DFT, the DCT produces real-valued coefficients suitable for direct use in feature vectors.

*(1 mark per role.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Derive the mathematical relationship between the Mel scale and the linear frequency in Hz. Show that 1 kHz corresponds to approximately 1000 mel. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the empirical Mel formula:

$$\text{mel}(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute $f = 1000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mel}(1000) &= 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1000}{700}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 1127 \ln(1 + 1.4286) \\[4pt]
&= 1127 \ln(2.4286) \\[4pt]
&= 1127 \times 0.8873 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1000.0 \text{ mel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Verify the inverse — start from $M = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$ and exponentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{M/1127} &= 1 + \dfrac{f}{700} \\[4pt]
f &= 700 \left(e^{M/1127} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $M = 1000$:

$$f = 700 (e^{1000/1127} - 1) = 700 (e^{0.8872} - 1) = 700 (2.428 - 1) \approx 1000 \text{ Hz} \; \checkmark$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** State the psychoacoustic justification: the Mel scale is anchored at 1 kHz = 1000 mel to align with Stevens and Newman's perceptual experiments on pitch judgment.

---

**(b) With the aid of a block diagram, explain the complete MFCC feature extraction pipeline. Highlight the role of the Mel filterbank and DCT. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw/scribe all 10 stages — Pre-emphasis, Framing, Windowing, FFT, Magnitude Spectrum, Mel Filterbank, Log, DCT, Deltas, Normalization. *(See Section 4.1.)*

2. **Role of Mel filterbank (2 marks):** Maps the linear-frequency magnitude spectrum onto a perceptually-motivated scale using $M$ triangular filters spaced according to $\text{mel}(f) = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$. This emphasizes low-frequency content where the robot's audio perception (and human hearing) is most discriminating.

3. **Role of DCT (2 marks):** As described in Question 2 of Part A — decorrelation, energy compaction, real-valued output for classifier input.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) Design a Mel filterbank with $M = 20$ triangular filters for a service robot's audio input sampled at $f_s = 16$ kHz, with $f_{\min} = 0$ Hz and $f_{\max} = 8000$ Hz. Compute the center frequency of the 10th filter. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert boundary frequencies to Mel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mel}_{\min} &= 1127 \ln(1 + 0/700) = 0 \text{ mel} \\[4pt]
\text{mel}_{\max} &= 1127 \ln(1 + 8000/700) = 1127 \ln(12.4286) = 1127 \times 2.5198 \approx 2840.0 \text{ mel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the inter-filter spacing:

$$\Delta \text{mel} = \dfrac{2840.0 - 0}{20 + 1} = \dfrac{2840.0}{21} \approx 135.24 \text{ mel/filter}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the center Mel of the 10th filter ($m = 9$ zero-indexed):

$$\text{mel}_{\text{center}}(10) = 0 + (9 + 1) \times 135.24 = 10 \times 135.24 = 1352.4 \text{ mel}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convert back to Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{center}}(10) &= 700 \left(e^{1352.4/1127} - 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= 700 \left(e^{1.1999} - 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= 700 \times (3.3193 - 1) \\[4pt]
&= 700 \times 2.3193 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1623.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert to FFT bin index (assume $N = 512$):

$$k_{\text{center}}(10) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{512 \times 1623.5}{16000}\right) = \text{round}(51.95) \approx 52$$

**Final Answer:** The 10th Mel filter is centered at **1623.5 Hz** (Mel ≈ 1352.4), corresponding to FFT bin 52.

---

**(b) Explain why dynamic features ($\Delta$ and $\Delta\Delta$) are appended to the static MFCC vector. How does this benefit a service robot's voice-command recognition system? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Limitation of static MFCCs (2 marks):** A single MFCC vector represents only the *spectral envelope* of one frame. It contains no information about how the sound *evolves* over time. However, phonetic content (e.g., the difference between "stop" and "shop") is encoded in *transitions*.

2. **Definition of Delta coefficients (2 marks):** The first-order time derivative $\Delta c_n[t]$ measures the rate of change of the $n$-th coefficient around frame $t$:

$$\Delta c_n[t] = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} k \left(c_n[t+k] - c_n[t-k]\right)}{2 \sum_{k=1}^{K} k^{2}}$$

The second-order derivative $\Delta\Delta c_n[t] = \Delta(\Delta c_n[t])$ measures the *acceleration* of spectral change.

3. **Benefit to service robots (3 marks):**
   - **Captures temporal dynamics** of spoken commands, improving discrimination between similar-sounding phonemes.
   - **Robust to speaker variability**, since the *rate* of spectral change is more consistent than the absolute spectrum.
   - **Enhanced accuracy** in noisy field environments (e.g., factory floor, hospital corridor) when combined with the static coefficients, since temporal context helps disambiguate speech from background noise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 1127:** Many students write $\text{mel}(f) = \log(f)$ or use a wrong constant. The exact value **1127** (or equivalently $2595 \log_{10}$) is mandatory.
> 2. **Confusing DCT with DFT:** The DCT is used (not IDFT) because of real-valued, energy-compacting properties. Writing "Inverse DFT" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Including discarded cepstral coefficients:** Don't retain all $M$ DCT outputs — only the first $C = 12$ (or 13 with energy) are valid MFCCs.
> 4. **Skipping the Pre-emphasis step:** Examiners specifically check the pipeline order. Forgetting pre-emphasis loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Using linear Hz in filterbank:** The triangular filter cutoffs must be *equally spaced in Mel*, not Hz. This is a frequent error.
> 6. **Not justifying DCT truncation:** Saying "we keep 12 coefficients" without explaining that low quefrencies = vocal tract envelope costs marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready):**

- **MFCC = Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients** — perceptually-motivated audio features used in robotic audio perception.
- **Mel scale formula:** $\text{mel}(f) = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$; **anchor:** 1 kHz ≈ 1000 mel.
- **Inverse:** $f = 700 (e^{\text{mel}/1127} - 1)$.
- **Pipeline order (10 steps):** Pre-emphasis $\rightarrow$ Framing $\rightarrow$ Windowing $\rightarrow$ FFT $\rightarrow$ Magnitude$^2$ $\rightarrow$ Mel filterbank $\rightarrow$ Log $\rightarrow$ DCT $\rightarrow$ Deltas $\rightarrow$ CMVN.
- **Pre-emphasis:** $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \, x[n-1]$ — boosts high frequencies lost during speech production.
- **Hamming window:** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$ — reduces spectral leakage at frame boundaries.
- **Triangular Mel filter:** piecewise linear weights, equally spaced in Mel, narrow at low $f$, wide at high $f$.
- **Filterbank energy:** $E_m = \sum_k \vert X[k] \vert^2 H_m[k]$.
- **DCT output:** $c_n = \sqrt{2/M} \sum_m S_m \cos[\pi n (m - 0.5)/M]$ for $n = 1, \dots, 12$.
- **DCT truncates the cepstrum** because the lower quefrencies encode the *vocal-tract* (information) and the higher ones encode the *excitation* (irrelevant pitch).
- **Delta features** capture temporal evolution — vital for speech/voice-command recognition in robots.
- **Typical parameters:** $f_s = 16$ kHz, frame = 25 ms, hop = 10 ms, $N = 512$, $M = 20$–$40$, $C = 12$–$13$.
- **Robotic applications:** voice command interfaces, acoustic event detection, fault diagnosis, HRI.
- **Why DCT over IDFT:** real-valued, decorrelated, better energy compaction.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — MFCC Feature Extraction**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised, discriminative, non-probabilistic binary classifier that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a (possibly high-dimensional) feature space by solving a **convex quadratic programming (QP)** problem. The "optimal" hyperplane is defined as the one that **maximizes the geometric margin** between the two classes while simultaneously minimizing a regularized hinge-loss penalty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCRUT602 Module 4):**
> In Field and Service Robotics, SVMs are treated as a **core machine-learning perception primitive** — used for terrain classification, object/landmark recognition, human-following, weed/crop discrimination in agricultural robots, and fault/condition classification in service platforms. The emphasis is on the **mathematical formulation**, **kernel-based non-linearization**, and **practical inference on robotic edge hardware**.

Formally, given a training dataset of $n$ labeled samples,
$$\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}, \quad \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \quad y_i \in \{-1, +1\},$$
an SVM seeks parameters $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ that define the decision function:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathrm{sign}\big(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b\big).$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Widest Picket Fence"

Imagine two rival groups of pebbles — **red** and **blue** — scattered on a flat sandy beach. You are asked to draw a **single straight line on the sand** that separates the two groups.

- Any line that separates them *could* work, but the **best line** is the one that lies **farthest from the nearest red pebble AND the nearest blue pebble at the same time**.
- The "fence" with the **widest neutral zone** (margin) is the most robust: even if the wind nudges a pebble a little, it still falls on the correct side.
- The pebbles that the fence "almost touches" are the **support vectors** — only they actually determine where the fence is built. All other pebbles are irrelevant once the fence is fixed.

That is the entire philosophy of an SVM: **maximize the empty corridor between the two classes, defined only by the closest samples.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SVMs in Robotics?**
> 1. **Excellent generalization** from small labeled datasets (critical in robotics where annotation is expensive).
> 2. **Convex optimization** → global optimum guaranteed, important for safety-critical robot decisions.
> 3. **Kernel trick** enables non-linear decision surfaces (e.g., curved terrain class boundaries) without explicit feature engineering.
> 4. **Deterministic inference** (no Monte-Carlo sampling) — suitable for real-time embedded control loops.

### 1.3 Geometric Vocabulary

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | The "fence" that splits the two classes |
| Margin | $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Width of the empty neutral zone |
| Support Vectors | $\mathbf{x}_i$ with $\alpha_i > 0$ | The pebbles the fence actually touches |
| Slack Variable | $\xi_i \ge 0$ | "Allowed" violation for noisy/overlapping data |
| Kernel Function | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top}\phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ | Implicit mapping to a higher-dimensional space |
| Hinge Loss | $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ | Penalty for samples on the wrong side of the margin |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Flow — How an SVM is "Built"

1. **Receive labeled data** $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ — e.g., LiDAR terrain patches labeled "grass" / "gravel".
2. **Choose a feature representation** $\phi(\cdot)$ (or pick a kernel $K$).
3. **Solve a convex QP** to find $\mathbf{w}, b$ that maximize margin and (optionally) tolerate violations.
4. **Extract support vectors** — the only training points with non-zero Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$.
5. **Predict** a new sample by evaluating $\mathrm{sign}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b)$ — geometrically: *which side of the hyperplane does it fall on?*

### 2.2 Three Formal Regimes of SVM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board examiner expects students to be **explicit** about which regime is being used. Mixing the constraints of hard-margin SVM with a soft-margin objective is a guaranteed mark-loser.

#### (a) Hard-Margin Linear SVM (Linearly Separable Data)
**Primal problem:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, n.
\end{aligned}
$$

The objective $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ minimizes the inverse-margin. The constraints force every training sample to lie **on the correct side with at least unit geometric distance**.

#### (b) Soft-Margin Linear SVM (Non-Separable / Noisy Data)
**Primal problem with slack variables $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
