\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

- $C > 0$ is the **regularization hyperparameter**.
  - **Large $C$** → hard margin, low bias / high variance, sensitive to outliers.
  - **Small $C$** → wide margin, high bias / low variance, robust to noise.
- In robotics this $C$ is frequently **cross-validated** using leave-one-out-style protocols on mission data.

#### (c) Dual Formulation (Gateway to Kernels)
Using **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \ge 0$ (and $\mu_i \ge 0$ for slack constraints in the soft case), the dual is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice the data appears **only via inner products** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ — the entry point for the **kernel trick**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Geometric separator in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| 2 | Signed Distance of a Point | $\dfrac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Distance in feature-space units |
| 3 | Geometric Margin | $\gamma = \dfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Width of the empty corridor; $\gamma \in (0, \infty)$ |
| 4 | Hard-Margin Objective | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ | Equivalent to margin maximization |
| 5 | Soft-Margin Objective | $\min \tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C\sum \xi_i$ | Regularized hinge-loss |
| 6 | Hinge Loss | $\ell_i = \max(0,\; 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b))$ | Piecewise-linear surrogate of 0–1 loss |
| 7 | Dual Objective | $\max \sum \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j K_{ij}$ | QP in $\alpha$; enables kernels |
| 8 | Box Constraint | $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ | KKT complementary slackness boundary |
| 9 | Equality Constraint | $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Ensures bias balance |
| 10 | Bias Recovery | $b = y_i - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i$ for any $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | Computed from a margin support vector |
| 11 | Decision Function (Kernel) | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in \text{SV}} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$ | Only support vectors contribute |
| 12 | Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ | Identity mapping $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ |
| 13 | Polynomial Kernel (degree $p$) | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + c)^{p}$ | Captures polynomial feature interactions |
| 14 | RBF (Gaussian) Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\big(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}\big)$ | $\gamma > 0$ — implicit infinite-dim space |
| 15 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\kappa \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + \theta)$ | MLP-like; not always PSD — caveat |
| 16 | Representer Theorem | $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Solution lies in span of training data |
| 17 | Support Vectors | $\{i \mid \alpha_i > 0\}$ | Memory & compute footprint at inference |
| 18 | VC Dimension (margin $\gamma$) | $\le \min\!\big(\lceil \tfrac{R^{2}}{\gamma^{2}} \rceil, d\big) + 1$ | Statistical complexity bound |
| 19 | Platt Scaling | $P(y{=}1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{1+\exp(A f(\mathbf{x}) + B)}$ | Converts SVM scores to probabilities |
| 20 | One-vs-Rest Extension | $\arg\max_{k} f_{k}(\mathbf{x})$ | Multi-class via $K$ binary SVMs |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for KTU Exam Scripts:** When writing $\vert \mathbf{w} \vert$ (the norm of $\mathbf{w}$) in a tabular answer, **always use $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** — KTU valuation scripts often strip the wrong pipe character and the answer is misread. Also remember to enclose every free-standing display equation with a single blank line above and below.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Field & Service Robotics

| Robotic Sub-system | Typical SVM Use | Input Features $\mathbf{x}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Agricultural Field Robot** | Weed vs. crop discrimination | Multispectral pixel vectors, GLCM texture |
| **Autonomous UGV (Unmanned Ground Vehicle)** | Traversable vs. non-traversable terrain | LiDAR intensity + slope + roughness |
| **Service / Assistive Robot** | Human vs. non-human follower target | HOG / LBP histograms of detected blobs |
| **Warehouse AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** | Pallet / box / obstacle recognition | 2D scan + depth patches |
| **Aerial Drone (UAV)** | Building façade defect classification | SIFT / SURF descriptors |
| **Underwater ROV (Remotely Operated Vehicle)** | Coral / sand / rubble mapping | Acoustic backscatter + color |
| **Robot Fault Diagnosis** | Healthy vs. faulty joint | Vibration PSD features (FFT bins) |
| **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)** | Gesture / intent recognition | Skeleton joint coordinates over time |

> [!TIP]
> The **RBF (Gaussian) kernel** is by far the most common default in field robotics because sensor noise is well-modeled as Gaussian and the resulting classifier is robust to outliers in cluttered environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No "by similar reasoning" or "it can be shown" shortcuts. This is the format that KTU board examiners reward with full marks in 14-mark analytical questions.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Margin from Signed Distance

**Goal:** Prove that the geometric (perpendicular) distance from any point $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ equals $\dfrac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 1 — Project a point onto the normal direction.**
The unit normal to the hyperplane is $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. Pick an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$ on the hyperplane, so $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_0 + b = 0$.

**Step 2 — Construct the perpendicular vector from $\mathbf{x}_0$ to $\mathbf{x}_i$.**
The displacement is $\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_0$. The component of this displacement along the normal is the **signed distance** $d_i$:

$$
d_i = (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_0)^{\top}\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_0)^{\top}\frac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}.
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and cancel $\mathbf{x}_0$.**
Since $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_0 = -b$, we have:

$$
d_i = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}.
$$

**Step 4 — Take absolute value for unsigned distance.**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{dist}(\mathbf{x}_i, \text{hyperplane}) = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply class label $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ for "functional margin".**
Because $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, multiplying the signed distance by $y_i$ gives a **positive quantity when the point is correctly classified**:

$$
\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) > 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{correct classification}.
$$

**Step 6 — Define the geometric margin of the dataset.**
The minimum geometric distance among all training points is:

$$
\gamma = \min_{i=1,\dots,n} \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}.
$$

For separable data, we **rescale** $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ so that the closest point lies at distance exactly $\tfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$, which yields the canonical form $\;y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$ for all $i$.

**Step 7 — Margin width between the two bounding hyperplanes.**
The two canonical boundaries are $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + b = -1$. Their distance is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Margin} = \frac{(\,1\,) - (\,-1\,)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}\;}
$$

Hence maximizing margin is mathematically identical to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hard-Margin Dual via Lagrangian Duality

**Goal:** Derive the dual QP of the hard-margin SVM and show the **Representer Theorem** $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$.

**Step 1 — Write the primal problem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \le 0, \quad \forall i.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian with multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \big[\,1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)\,\big].
$$

**Step 3 — Set partial derivatives to zero (stationarity).**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}^{\star} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i\;}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0.
$$

These are the **Representer Theorem** and the **bias-balance equality constraint** of the dual.

**Step 4 — Back-substitute $\mathbf{w}^{\star}$ into $\mathcal{L}$ to eliminate $\mathbf{w}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) &= \frac{1}{2}\Big(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i\Big)^{\top}\Big(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\Big) + \sum_i \alpha_i - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{x}_i - b\sum_i \alpha_i y_i \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + \sum_i \alpha_i - \sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j - 0 \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the final dual problem.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \alpha_i \ge 0,\quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0.
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 6 — State the KKT complementary slackness conditions.**

$$
\alpha_i \big[\,1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b^{\star})\,\big] = 0, \quad \forall i.
$$

This means:
- If $\alpha_i = 0$ → point is **not** a support vector; lies strictly outside the margin.
- If $\alpha_i > 0$ → point is a **support vector**; lies exactly on the margin ($y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\star\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b^{\star}) = 1$).

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Soft-Margin Dual with Box Constraints

**Goal:** Extend to soft-margin and show the appearance of the box constraint $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$.

**Step 1 — Primal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Lagrangian (introduce $\alpha_i$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i$ for $\xi_i \ge 0$).**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C\sum_i \xi_i + \sum_i \alpha_i \big[1 - \xi_i - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)\big] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i.
$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity w.r.t. $\xi_i$.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha_i = C - \mu_i.
$$

Since $\mu_i \ge 0$, this gives the **upper bound** $\alpha_i \le C$. Combined with $\alpha_i \ge 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;0 \le \alpha_i \le C\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Stationarity w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ — same as hard case.**

$$
\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i, \qquad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0.
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to get the same dual QP, only the feasible set has changed.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C,\quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0.
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation of the three classes of training points.**

| Condition on $\alpha_i$ | Position of $\mathbf{x}_i$ | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha_i = 0$ | Outside the margin or correct side | Non-support vector |
| $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | **On** the margin hyperplane | "Margin support vector" |
| $\alpha_i = C$ | Inside the margin or misclassified | "Non-margin support vector" (bounded by $C$) |

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Kernel Trick via Inner-Product Replacement

**Goal:** Show how replacing $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ implicitly maps data into a higher (possibly infinite) dimensional space.

**Step 1 — Mercer's Theorem (Kernel Qualification).**
A symmetric function $K : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a valid kernel **iff** for all finite sets $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$, the **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ with $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**.

**Step 2 — Implicit feature map.**
If $K$ is a valid kernel, there exists a (possibly infinite-dimensional) Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$ and a feature map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{H}$ such that:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}}.
$$

**Step 3 — Substitution into the dual objective.**

$$
\boxed{\;
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}\; \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j \, \boxed{K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)}\;
}
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting non-linear decision function.**

$$
\boxed{\;
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i \in \text{SV}} \alpha_i y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b
\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked example with polynomial kernel.**
Take $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j)^2$ for $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$, $\mathbf{x} = (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)})$. Then:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \big(x_i^{(1)}x_j^{(1)} + x_i^{(2)}x_j^{(2)}\big)^{2} = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle,
$$

with $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \big((x^{(1)})^{2},\; \sqrt{2}\,x^{(1)}x^{(2)},\; (x^{(2)})^{2}\big) \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$.

So a **degree-2 polynomial boundary in 2-D** corresponds to a **linear hyperplane in 3-D** — without ever computing $\phi(\cdot)$ explicitly. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (End-to-End Toy Problem)

**Problem.** 4 points in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$: $(1,1), (2,2), (2,0), (0,1)$ with labels $+1, +1, -1, -1$ respectively. Solve the hard-margin SVM.

**Step 1 — Primal.**
Find $\mathbf{w}, b$ minimizing $\tfrac{1}{2}(w_1^{2} + w_2^{2})$ such that:
- $+1\cdot(w_1 + w_2 + b) \ge 1$
- $+1\cdot(2w_1 + 2w_2 + b) \ge 1$
- $-1\cdot(2w_1 + 0\cdot w_2 + b) \ge 1 \Rightarrow -2w_1 - b \ge 1$
- $-1\cdot(0 + w_2 + b) \ge 1 \Rightarrow -w_2 - b \ge 1$

**Step 2 — By symmetry of the geometry, expect $\mathbf{w} = (w, w)$.** Substitute:

- $w + w + b \ge 1 \Rightarrow 2w + b \ge 1$ … (i)
- $4w + b \ge 1$ … (ii)
- $-2w - b \ge 1$ … (iii)
- $-w - b \ge 1$ … (iv)

**Step 3 — Active constraints at the optimum.** Combine (i) and (iv): $(2w + b) + (-w - b) = w = 2 \Rightarrow w = 2$. Wait — this over-constrains. The active pair is the **closest pair from each class**: $(1,1)$ (class $+1$) and $(2,0)$ (class $-1$).

Set (i) and (iii) active:
- $2w + b = 1$  (from $(1,1)$)
- $-2w - b = 1$  (from $(2,0)$)

Adding: $0 = 2$ — contradiction. So the **active pair** is $(1,1)$ and $(0,1)$ (both? No, different classes: $(1,1)$ is $+1$ and $(0,1)$ is $-1$).

Set (i) and (iv) active:
- $2w + b = 1$
- $-w - b = 1$

Adding: $w = 2$, then $b = 1 - 2w = 1 - 4 = -3$.

**Step 4 — Verify with (ii) and (iii).**
- (ii): $4(2) + (-3) = 5 \ge 1$ ✓
- (iii): $-2(2) - (-3) = -1 \ge 1$? **No, $-1 \not\ge 1$** — violates (iii).

**Step 5 — The constraint (iii) is also active.** Active set: (i), (iii), (iv).

From (i) and (iii): $2w + b = 1$, $-2w - b = 1 \Rightarrow 0 = 2$. **Inconsistent.**

Re-examine geometry: the two class centroids are at $(1.5, 1.5)$ and $(1, 0.5)$. The separating line has slope $1$ by symmetry. Solve again with active constraints being the **two support vectors**: $(1,1)$ at $+1$ and $(2,0)$ at $-1$.

- $w_1 + w_2 + b = 1$ … (A)
- $-(2w_1 + b) = 1 \Rightarrow 2w_1 + b = -1$ … (B)

Subtract (A) from (B): $w_1 - w_2 = -2 \Rightarrow w_2 = w_1 + 2$.

Plug into (A): $w_1 + (w_1 + 2) + b = 1 \Rightarrow 2w_1 + b = -1$ ✓ (same as B).

Minimize $\tfrac{1}{2}(w_1^{2} + w_2^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(w_1^{2} + (w_1+2)^{2})$.

$\dfrac{d}{dw_1}\big[w_1^{2} + (w_1+2)^{2}\big] = 2w_1 + 2(w_1+2) = 4w_1 + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow w_1 = -1$.

So $w_2 = -1 + 2 = 1$, and $b = -1 - 2w_1 = -1 + 2 = 1$.

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{w} = (-1, 1), \quad b = 1, \quad \text{hyperplane: } -x_1 + x_2 + 1 = 0, \quad \text{margin} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify all four points.**
- $(1,1): -1 + 1 + 1 = 1 \ge 1$ ✓
- $(2,2): -2 + 2 + 1 = 1 \ge 1$ ✓
- $(2,0): -2 + 0 + 1 = -1 \le -1$ ✓
- $(0,1): 0 + 1 + 1 = 2 \ge 1$ ✓

**Step 8 — Support vectors:** $(1,1)$ and $(2,0)$ (the two active constraints). $\blacksquare$

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Feature Extraction HOG LBP GLCM FFT]
    C --> D[Kernel Mapping K x_i x]
    D --> E[Dot Products with Support Vectors]
    E --> F[Weighted Sum Sum alpha_i y_i K]
    F --> G[Add Bias b]
    G --> H{Sign Test}
    H -- f x greater than 0 --> I[Class A e.g. Traversable]
    H -- f x less than 0 --> J[Class B e.g. Obstacle]

    J --> K
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "support vector" in the context of a Support Vector Machine. Why is it called a "support" vector?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A support vector is a training sample $\mathbf{x}_i$ whose associated **Lagrange multiplier** satisfies $\alpha_i > 0$ in the dual SVM solution. Geometrically, it is a point that **lies exactly on the margin hyperplane** (i.e., $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$) or **violates the margin** (i.e., inside the margin or misclassified, with $\alpha_i = C$ in the soft-margin case).
It is called a "support" vector because it **supports (holds up) the separating hyperplane** in space — moving any other (non-support) training point does not change the decision boundary, but moving even a single support vector does. Mathematically, this follows from the **Representer Theorem** $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$, which shows that only points with $\alpha_i \ne 0$ contribute to $\mathbf{w}$. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State the kernel trick. Write the RBF kernel and state the role of the parameter $\gamma$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **kernel trick** is a technique in which the inner product $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ appearing in the dual SVM formulation is replaced by a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle$, allowing the classifier to learn **non-linear decision boundaries** in the original input space while still solving a linear problem in a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space — without ever computing $\phi(\cdot)$ explicitly. **(1 mark)**
The **Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian kernel** is:
$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\big(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2}\big), \quad \gamma > 0.$$
The parameter $\gamma > 0$ controls the **width of the kernel's influence**:
- **Large $\gamma$** → narrow kernel → high model complexity, risk of overfitting.
- **Small $\gamma$** → wide kernel → smoother decision boundary, risk of underfitting.
In field robotics, $\gamma$ is often tuned via **grid search with k-fold cross-validation** on mission data. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> KTU 2024 ESE convention: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one** of the two alternative questions.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** Formulate the **soft-margin linear SVM** primal optimization problem. Clearly define every variable and constraint, and explain the role of the hyperparameter $C$. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Derive the corresponding **dual problem** using Lagrange multipliers. Show that the dual feasible set becomes $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Primal Formulation (7 marks)**

For a dataset $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ with $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the soft-margin linear SVM primal is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} \;+\; C \sum_{i=1}^{n} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad i = 1, \dots, n \\
& \xi_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, n.
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Role |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Normal vector to the hyperplane |
| $b \in \mathbb{R}$ | Bias / offset |
| $\xi_i \ge 0$ | Slack variable — measures how much sample $i$ violates the margin |
| $C > 0$ | Regularization constant (penalty for slack) |

**Role of $C$:**
- **Large $C$** → heavy penalty on margin violations → harder margin, narrower corridor, lower bias / higher variance. Suitable when the data is **believed to be near-separable** (e.g., a calibrated lab environment).
- **Small $C$** → soft penalty → wider margin, higher bias / lower variance. Suitable for **noisy field environments** (slippery floors, dusty sensors, mislabeled samples).
- In practice, $C$ is selected by **k-fold cross-validation** on a held-out mission log.

The objective balances two competing goals: (i) keep the margin large (small $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$), and (ii) keep the cumulative margin-violation small (small $\sum \xi_i$). *(7 marks)*

---

**(b) Dual Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Lagrangian.** Introduce multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \ge 0$ for the non-negativity of $\xi_i$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i + \sum_i \alpha_i \big[1 - \xi_i - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b)\big].
$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity w.r.t. the primal variables.**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i. \quad \text{(3 marks: w, b, ξ)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0.
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha_i = C - \mu_i \le C \;\;(\text{since } \mu_i \ge 0).
$$

**Step 3 — Box constraint deduced.** Since $\alpha_i \ge 0$ and $\alpha_i = C - \mu_i \le C$:

$$
\boxed{\;0 \le \alpha_i \le C\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\mathbf{w}, b, \xi$ from the Lagrangian.** Substituting back (as in the hard-margin derivation in Section 3.2):

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0.
\end{aligned}}
$$

**Step 5 — Geometric interpretation of the box.**
- $\alpha_i = 0$ → point outside margin (non-support).
- $0 < \alpha_i < C$ → point on the margin (canonical support vector).
- $\alpha_i = C$ → point inside margin or misclassified (bounded by $C$).

The "box" $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ is precisely what soft-margin SVM adds over hard-margin SVM. *(7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **kernel trick** in SVM. Show, with a clear worked example, that the kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j)^{2}$ corresponds to a specific explicit feature map. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe a **terrain classification pipeline for an agricultural field robot** that uses an SVM. Justify the choice of features, kernel, and hyperparameters. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kernel Trick + Worked Example (7 marks)**

**Definition.** The kernel trick is a method to learn a non-linear SVM in the original input space $\mathcal{X}$ by replacing every inner product $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j$ in the dual formulation with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$, where

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle_{\mathcal{H}},
$$

for some (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature map $\phi : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{H}$. Computing $K$ directly is **far cheaper** than computing $\phi$ and then taking the inner product. **(2 marks)**

**Worked example.** Let $\mathbf{x} = (x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ and consider $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j)^{2}$.

Expanding:

$$
K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \big(x_i^{(1)}x_j^{(1)} + x_i^{(2)}x_j^{(2)}\big)^{2} = (x_i^{(1)})^{2}(x_j^{(1)})^{2} + 2x_i^{(1)}x_i^{(2)}x_j^{(1)}x_j^{(2)} + (x_i^{(2)})^{2}(x_j^{(2)})^{2}.
$$

This is exactly the inner product in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ of the vectors:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \big((x^{(1)})^{2},\; \sqrt{2}\,x^{(1)}x^{(2)},\; (x^{(2)})^{2}\big) \in \mathbb{R}^{3}.
$$

Verification:

$$
\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle = (x_i^{(1)})^{2}(x_j^{(1)})^{2} + 2x_i^{(1)}x_i^{(2)}x_j^{(1)}x_j^{(2)} + (x_i^{(2)})^{2}(x_j^{(2)})^{2} = (\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j)^{2} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j). \;\checkmark
$$

So a **degree-2 polynomial boundary in 2-D input space** is realized as a **linear hyperplane in 3-D feature space** — without ever materializing $\phi$. **(5 marks)**

---

**(b) Terrain Classification Pipeline for an Agricultural Field Robot (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (annotated as text — equivalent to the Mermaid block in Section 4.1):**

`Camera/LiDAR → Preprocessing (denoise, color-balance) → Patch Extraction → Feature Computation (HOG + LBP + NDVI) → Kernel Mapping (RBF) → SVM Decision Function → Class Label (Weed / Crop / Soil) → Action (Spray / Skip / Continue)`

| Stage | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Multi-spectral camera + 2D LiDAR; chosen for ground-level discrimination of weed vs. crop. |
| **Features** | (i) HOG (shape/edge) — distinguishes leaf shapes; (ii) LBP (texture) — distinguishes soil vs. vegetation; (iii) NDVI from NIR band — chlorophyll index. Concatenated $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. |
| **Kernel** | **RBF** — robust to Gaussian sensor noise in outdoor lighting; supports complex non-linear crop-row boundaries. |
| **Hyperparameters** | $C$ and $\gamma$ tuned by **5-fold CV** on a labeled field walk. Typical ranges: $C \in [1, 100]$, $\gamma \in [10^{-3}, 10]$. |
| **Output** | Binary "weed / not-weed" per patch; multi-class via **one-vs-rest** for {weed, crop, soil}. |
| **Action** | Selective herbicide spray only on weed patches → **40–60% chemical savings**, a real-world service-robot KPI. |

**Why SVM and not, say, a deep CNN?**
- The field robot is **edge-deployed** (e.g., on a solar-powered UGV) — SVM inference is **2–3 orders of magnitude cheaper** than a CNN.
- Training data is **scarce and expert-labeled** (an agronomist must annotate weed patches) — SVM generalizes well from small samples.
- Inference is **deterministic and latency-bounded** (no GPU required), critical for real-time sprayer actuation. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing the primal with the dual.** The primal minimizes $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$ over $(\mathbf{w}, b)$. The dual maximizes $\sum \alpha_i - \tfrac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K_{ij}$ over $\alpha$. Examiners check this swap carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the box constraint.** Writing the soft-margin dual as $0 \le \alpha_i$ (hard margin) instead of $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ costs full 7 marks in a derivation question.
> 3. **Wrong sign in the representer theorem.** It is $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ — **not** $\sum \alpha_i \mathbf{x}_i$ and **not** $\sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i^{\top}$.
> 4. **Mis-stating the margin.** Margin is $\tfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$, **not** $\tfrac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ and **not** $\tfrac{2}{\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w}}$.
> 5. **Mixing up the kernel parameter $\gamma$ with the margin symbol $\gamma$.** In RBF, the kernel bandwidth is conventionally $\gamma$ (or sometimes $\tfrac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$). In the margin formula, $\gamma$ is the geometric margin. Use distinct symbols (e.g., $\gamma_{\text{kernel}}$ and $\gamma_{\text{geom}}$) to be safe.
> 6. **Skipping steps in derivations.** KTU board examiners explicitly allocate step-wise partial marks. Always show the Lagrangian, the stationarity conditions, and the substitution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density bullets for last-minute reading):**

- ✅ SVM is a **maximum-margin, discriminative, binary classifier** based on convex QP.
- ✅ **Hard-margin SVM** assumes linear separability; **soft-margin SVM** introduces slack $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$.
- ✅ **Margin width** $= \tfrac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$; **maximizing** margin $\equiv$ **minimizing** $\tfrac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2}$.
- ✅ **Primal** minimizes over $(\mathbf{w}, b)$; **Dual** maximizes over $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Representer Theorem**: $\mathbf{w}^{\star} = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ — only support vectors matter.
- ✅ **Support vectors** = training samples with $\alpha_i > 0$.
- ✅ **Soft-margin dual box**: $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ (hard-margin = $C = \infty$).
- ✅ **Bias-balance equality constraint**: $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- ✅ **Bias recovery**: $b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_k$ for any $k$ with $0 < \alpha_k < C$.
- ✅ **Kernel trick** replaces inner products with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ → non-linear boundaries.
- ✅ **Mercer's condition**: Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ must be **positive semi-definite** for a valid kernel.
- ✅ **Common kernels**: Linear, Polynomial $(\mathbf{x}_i^{\top}\mathbf{x}_j + c)^{p}$, RBF $\exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^{2})$, Sigmoid.
- ✅ **RBF $\gamma$**: large → narrow → overfit; small → wide → underfit.
- ✅ **Multi-class SVM**: One-vs-Rest (OvR) or One-vs-One (OvO); rarely direct.
- ✅ **Inference** cost depends only on the number of support vectors — sparse solutions are fast.
- ✅ **Platt scaling** converts SVM scores to calibrated probabilities for downstream probabilistic planning.
- ✅ **In robotics**: SVM is the workhorse for **small-data, edge-deployed perception** — terrain, gesture, fault, weed/crop classification.
- ✅ **Hyperparameter selection**: $k$-fold cross-validation, grid search, or Bayesian optimization over $(C, \gamma)$.
- ✅ **Convexity** guarantees a global optimum — no random restarts required.
- ✅ **Hinge loss** is the convex surrogate of 0–1 loss used in the soft-margin objective.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Robot for Elders - Robot System - System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Service Robot for Elders - Robot System - System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Service Robot for Elders — Robot System & System Architecture

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A *Service Robot for Elders* is a category of **personal care robot** (as classified under **ISO 13482:2014**) that performs useful tasks for the well-being, healthcare monitoring, mobility assistance, and social companionship of aged human users in domestic, assisted-living, or healthcare environments. The *Robot System* encompasses the integrated hardware, software, sensors, actuators, and human-robot interaction (HRI) modules, while the *System Architecture* defines the **structured, layered organization** of these components specifying how perception, cognition, decision-making, and actuation interact to deliver autonomous service.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Caregiver" Intuition

Imagine a **devoted butler** who lives in the house of an elderly person. This butler has:

- **Eyes & Ears** (cameras, microphones, depth sensors) → constantly observes the environment and the elder
- **Brain** (on-board computer, AI processor) → reasons, plans, and decides
- **Hands & Legs** (manipulator arm, mobile base with wheels/legs) → physically interacts and moves
- **Nervous System** (communication buses — CAN, EtherCAT, Wi-Fi) → routes data and commands between subsystems
- **Memory & Diary** (cloud server, local database) → stores health logs, preferences, maps
- **Mouth & Ears for Conversation** (speech synthesis and recognition modules) → communicates naturally with the elder

Just as a butler must coordinate all these faculties **simultaneously and safely**, a service robot's architecture organizes its electronic and mechanical faculties into **well-defined layers** that communicate with each other through standardized interfaces. Without this organized architecture, the robot would behave chaotically — like a butler with amnesia, doing one task at a time while ignoring emergencies.

### Key System Parameters for Elder Care Robots (Standard Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics define the design envelope of a service robot for elders. Every KTU question on this topic will reference at least one of these:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload Capacity** | **2 – 5 kg** | For fetching objects, holding a tray, opening doors |
| **Operating Speed** | **0.25 – 0.6 m/s** | Safe indoor walking-pace for elderly environments |
| **Battery Autonomy** | **8 – 12 hours** | Full-day unsupervised operation |
| **Response Latency (HRI)** | **< 500 ms** | Perceived as natural conversation |
| **Fall Detection Time** | **< 3 seconds** | Critical safety metric |
| **Degrees of Freedom (Manipulator)** | **6 – 7 DOF** | Reaches human-scale objects on shelves/floor |
| **Operating Temperature** | **10 °C – 35 °C** | Indoor domestic environment range |

### Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Syllabus

The *System Architecture* of an elder-care robot is the **backbone of every other module** in PCRUT602 — it ties together SLAM, perception, manipulation, navigation, and HRI. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to:
- Identify the major subsystems and their roles
- Justify the choice of architectural paradigm (reactive vs. deliberative vs. hybrid)
- Apply safety standards (**ISO 13482**) in architectural design
- Analyze trade-offs (centralized vs. distributed, cloud vs. edge)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Layered Decomposition of the System Architecture

A modern elder-care service robot architecture is best understood as a **five-layer stack**, where each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer below it. This is the *Sense → Think → Act* paradigm, formalized for service robotics.

### Layer 1 — Hardware / Physical Layer (The "Body")

This is the physical embodiment of the robot.

**Subsystems:**

- **Mobile Base** — Differential drive, omnidirectional (Mecanum/Omni wheels), or legged (bipedal/quadruped). For elders, **differential or omni** are preferred for stability and simplicity.
- **Manipulator Arm** — Typically 6–7 DOF anthropomorphic arm with a gripper. Used to fetch medicine bottles, glasses, books.
- **Sensor Suite:**
  - **RGB-D Cameras** (e.g., Intel RealSense, Orbbec) for 3D perception
  - **2D/3D LiDAR** for SLAM and obstacle detection
  - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** for balance and orientation
  - **Force/Torque Sensors** at the wrist for safe physical interaction
  - **Microphone Array** for far-field speech capture
  - **Tactile / Capacitive Skin** on the arm for safe contact
  - **Physiological Sensors** (wristband integration): heart rate, SpO₂, body temperature
- **On-board Computer** — GPU-accelerated edge AI (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Orin)
- **Power System** — Li-Ion battery pack with Battery Management System (**BMS**)
- **Safety Hardware** — Emergency stop buttons, bumper sensors, cliff sensors

### Layer 2 — Perception Layer (The "Senses")

Transforms raw sensor streams into semantic understanding.

**Core Functional Modules:**

- **Sensor Fusion** — Combines LiDAR + Camera + IMU using **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF)** for robust state estimation.
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization & Mapping)** — Builds a map of the home and tracks the robot within it (algorithms: **Hector SLAM, Cartographer, ORB-SLAM3**).
- **Object Detection & Recognition** — Deep learning models (YOLO, EfficientDet) trained to recognize household objects, medicine bottles, eyeglasses, walking aids.
- **Face Recognition & Identification** — Distinguishes the elder from visitors and family members.
- **Person Detection & Pose Estimation** — Detects falls (lying posture on floor) and abnormal gaits.
- **Speech Recognition (ASR)** — Converts spoken commands to text (Whisper, Google STT, Kaldi).
- **Emotion/Affective Computing** — Classifies vocal tone and facial expression to detect distress, pain, or loneliness.

### Layer 3 — Cognition / Decision Layer (The "Brain")

Implements the "deliberative" reasoning of the robot.

**Core Functional Modules:**

- **Task Planner** — High-level symbolic planner (PDDL-based or hierarchical task network) that decomposes goals (e.g., "Bring water to grandfather") into subtasks.
- **Behavior Coordinator** — Manages concurrent behaviors (navigation, monitoring, conversation) and arbitrates conflicts via priority logic.
- **Knowledge Base / Ontology** — Stores user preferences, daily routines, medication schedule, room semantics.
- **Natural Language Understanding (NLU)** — Extracts intent, entities, and context from recognized text.
- **Anomaly Detection Engine** — Monitors vital signs and activity patterns; triggers alerts on deviation.

### Layer 4 — Motion Planning & Control Layer (The "Muscle Coordination")

Translates high-level tasks into low-level actuator commands.

**Core Functional Modules:**

- **Global Path Planner** — A* or Dijkstra on the occupancy grid for room-to-room navigation.
- **Local Planner / Obstacle Avoidance** — Dynamic Window Approach (DWA) or TEB (Timed Elastic Band).
- **Motion Controller** — PID, MPC (Model Predictive Control), or computed-torque control for the arm.
- **Behavior-based Reflexes** — Bounded-speed emergency stop, force-limited contact, compliant arm control.

### Layer 5 — Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) & Application Layer (The "Personality")

The user-facing surface of the robot.

**Core Functional Modules:**

- **Multimodal HRI** — Speech + touch screen + LED facial display + gesture
- **Dialogue Manager** — Maintains conversational context across turns
- **Notification System** — Medication reminders, hydration prompts, appointment alerts
- **Telepresence Interface** — Video calling with caregivers/doctors
- **Cloud Sync** — Health data uploaded to family/caregiver dashboard

---

## 3. Architectural Paradigms — Comparative Analysis

A KTU examiner expects students to **compare and justify** the architectural style. The three classical paradigms are:

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Fit For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deliberative (Hierarchical)** | Sense → Plan → Act sequence | Logical, explainable | Slow, brittle if world changes | Structured task execution |
| **Reactive (Behavior-Based)** | Many parallel reflexes triggered by stimuli | Fast, robust, fault-tolerant | No global reasoning, no learning | Emergency response, obstacle avoidance |
| **Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive** | Slow deliberative layer + fast reactive layer | Combines reasoning + reflex speed | Complex integration, possible layer conflicts | **Elder-care robots (industry standard)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **Hybrid Architecture** is the de facto choice for elder-care robots. Examples include the *LAAS Architecture* (France) and the popular *ROS 2 + Behavior Trees* stack. The slow layer handles planning, the fast layer handles safety reflexes.

---

## 4. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations appear frequently in KTU derivations and numerical questions on service robot architecture:

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Budget** | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | $P_i$ in Watts | Sizing the battery and power distribution |
| 2 | **Battery Runtime** | $t_{run} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{P_{total}}$ | $C_{bat}$ in Wh, $t_{run}$ in hours | 8–12 hour autonomy requirement |
| 3 | **State Update Rate (EKF)** | $f_{update} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t}$ | $\Delta t$ in seconds | Sensor fusion timing |
| 4 | **Safe Stopping Distance** | $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v^2}{2a_{max}}$ | $v$ = m/s, $a_{max}$ = m/s², $d$ = meters | Indoor safety at 0.25–0.6 m/s |
| 5 | **Round-Trip HRI Latency** | $\tau_{HRI} = \tau_{ASR} + \tau_{NLU} + \tau_{plan} + \tau_{TTS}$ | All in ms | Must be **< 500 ms** for natural feel |
| 6 | **Sensor Coverage (FOV)** | $\Omega_{cover} = 2\pi \left(1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\theta_{FOV}}{2}\right)\right)$ | $\theta_{FOV}$ in radians, $\Omega$ in steradians | Multi-camera placement |
| 7 | **Reactive Layer Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} \le T_{safety\,threshold}$ | $T$ in ms | Typically 10–50 ms |
| 8 | **Force-Limited Contact (ISO 13482)** | $F_{contact} \le F_{max,\,quasi-static}$ | Quasi-static contact limits from **Table 1 of ISO/TS 15066** | Safe arm–human contact |
| 9 | **Localization Uncertainty (EKF Covariance)** | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | $P$ = covariance matrix, $K$ = Kalman gain | Bound on pose error |
| 10 | **Communication Bandwidth Requirement** | $B = f_s \cdot N_c \cdot N_b$ | $f_s$ = Hz, $N_c$ = channels, $N_b$ = bits/sample | Bus selection (CAN, Ethernet) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use the vertical pipe symbol `|x|` in your exam answer tables. Use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX. This is a common valuation deduction.

---

## 5. Safety Architecture — ISO 13482 Compliance

A KTU 2024 question will almost certainly test **safety**. The architecture must implement:

- **Safety-rated monitored stop (SRMS)** when an obstacle enters the protective field.
- **Power & force limiting (PFL)** on the manipulator to prevent injury.
- **Risk assessment per ISO 12100** for every intended contact scenario.
- **Redundancy** in critical sensors (two independent obstacle detectors).
- **Fail-safe defaults** — if any perception module fails, the robot halts in a safe state.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 6. Exhaustive Derivation — Battery Sizing for 12-Hour Autonomy

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **typical 7-mark KTU numerical**. Walk through it slowly.

### Problem Statement

A service robot for elders has the following power-consuming subsystems:

| Subsystem | Power $P_i$ (W) |
|---|---|
| Mobile Base Motors | 60 |
| Manipulator Arm Motors | 40 |
| On-board Computer (GPU + CPU) | 35 |
| Sensors (LiDAR + Cameras + IMU) | 15 |
| Communication (Wi-Fi + Bluetooth) | 5 |
| HRI Module (Speakers + Display) | 10 |

The robot must operate for **12 hours** continuously. Determine the **minimum battery capacity** required (in Watt-hours) and verify the **nominal pack sizing** if the system uses a **25.2 V nominal Li-Ion pack** with 80% depth-of-discharge (DoD) allowance.

### Step 1 — Compute the Total Power Consumption

$$
P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} P_i = 60 + 40 + 35 + 15 + 5 + 10
$$

$$
P_{total} = 165 \text{ W}
$$

**Explanation:** We sum the worst-case (peak) power of all six subsystems. This is a standard worst-case power budget assumption used in KTU numericals.

### Step 2 — Compute the Theoretical Energy Required

$$
E_{theoretical} = P_{total} \times t_{run} = 165 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ h}
$$

$$
E_{theoretical} = 1980 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Explanation:** Energy is the product of power and time. We need 1980 Watt-hours of energy delivered to the load.

### Step 3 — Apply the Depth-of-Discharge Derating

A Li-Ion battery should not be discharged below 20% state-of-charge to preserve cycle life. So only **80%** of the rated capacity is usable:

$$
E_{battery} = \dfrac{E_{theoretical}}{\eta_{DoD}} = \dfrac{1980}{0.80}
$$

$$
E_{battery} = 2475 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Explanation:** The battery must store 2475 Wh *more* than the energy delivered, so that the 20% reserve is preserved.

### Step 4 — Convert to Battery Pack Capacity in Ampere-Hours

For a 25.2 V nominal pack:

$$
C_{pack} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{V_{pack}} = \dfrac{2475}{25.2}
$$

$$
C_{pack} = 98.21 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Explanation:** Since battery capacity is sold in Ampere-hours, we convert by dividing by nominal pack voltage.

### Step 5 — Practical Pack Sizing

Use a **6S Li-Ion pack** (6 cells in series, each 4.2 V fully charged, 3.6 V nominal). Nominal = $6 \times 3.6 = 21.6$ V — slightly low. Use a **7S configuration** (7 × 3.6 V = 25.2 V nominal). Each 18650 cell is typically 2.5–3.5 Ah. To get 98.21 Ah, use a **10P** (10 cells in parallel) configuration with 3.0 Ah cells:

$$
C_{parallel} = 10 \times 3.0 = 30 \text{ Ah} \quad \text{(per string)}
$$

We need 98.21 Ah, so we require **3.27 parallel strings**, rounded to **4 parallel strings** for margin:

$$
C_{final} = 4 \times 30 = 120 \text{ Ah}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{C_{battery} = 120 \text{ Ah at } 25.2\text{ V nominal, equivalent to } 3024 \text{ Wh}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** A full-solution must include: (1) the power sum, (2) the energy product, (3) the DoD derating, (4) the Wh-to-Ah conversion, AND (5) the practical cell-count statement. Missing step (3) is the most common 2-mark deduction.

---

## 7. Analytical Matrix — Mapping Functional Requirements to Subsystems

This matrix is a **favorite KTU question type** ("Map the following functional requirements to the appropriate architectural layer"). Memorize it.

| Functional Requirement | Layer | Specific Module |
|---|---|---|
| Detect that the elder has fallen | Perception | Person Pose Estimation + Anomaly Detection |
| Remind elder to take medicine at 9 AM | Application | Notification System + Knowledge Base |
| Avoid the pet cat while navigating | Motion Control | Local Planner (DWA/TEB) |
| Understand "I am feeling cold" | Cognition | ASR + NLU + Dialogue Manager |
| Physically hand over a glass of water | Motion Control | Manipulator Planner + Force Controller |
| Stop immediately if a child runs in front | Motion Control + Safety | Reactive Reflex + Bumper Sensor |
| Upload daily activity report to the family | Application + Communication | Cloud Sync + Notification |
| Recognize the elder's face vs. a stranger | Perception | Face Recognition Module |
| Decide whether to call ambulance | Cognition | Anomaly Detection + Task Planner |
| Speak in the elder's mother tongue | Application + Cognition | TTS + Language Selection Module |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 8. Master System Architecture Diagram (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagrams render the full five-layer architecture of an elder-care service robot, including the safety and cloud sub-systems. These are valid KTU schematic answers.

### 8.1 Five-Layer Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 - HARDWARE BODY"]
        A1[Mobile Base]
        A2[Manipulator Arm 6DOF]
        A3[Sensor Suite LiDAR Camera IMU Mic]
        A4[Onboard Computer Jetson]
        A5[Battery Pack LiIon with BMS]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 - PERCEPTION SENSES"]
        B1[Sensor Fusion EKF]
        B2[SLAM Cartographer]
        B3[Object Detection YOLO]
        B4[Face Recognition]
        B5[Speech Recognition ASR]
        B6[Fall Detection PoseNet]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 - COGNITION BRAIN"]
        C1[Task Planner PDDL]
        C2[Behavior Coordinator]
        C3[Knowledge Base Ontology]
        C4[Natural Language Understanding]
        C5[Anomaly Detection Engine]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 - MOTION PLANNING MUSCLE"]
        D1[Global Path Planner A star]
        D2[Local Planner DWA]
        D3[Motion Controller PID MPC]
        D4[Force Compliant Controller]
        D5[Reactive Safety Reflex]
    end

    subgraph L5["LAYER 5 - HRI APPLICATION"]
        E1[Multimodal Dialog Manager]
        E2[Speech Synthesis TTS]
        E3[Touch Screen LED Face]
        E4[Cloud Sync Family Portal]
        E5[Telepresence Video Call]
    end

    subgraph L6["CROSS CUTTING - SAFETY ISO 13482"]
        F1[Emergency Stop Hardware]
        F2[Power Force Limiting PFL]
        F3[Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    A3 --> B5
    A3 --> B6
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> D1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C5
    B5 --> C4
    B6 --> C5
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> D1
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> C1
    C4 --> C1
    C5 --> E1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> A1
    D3 --> A2
    D4 --> A2
    C2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
    C1 --> E4
    C1 --> E5
    F1 -.-> A1
    F2 -.-> A2
    F3 -.-> D2
```

### 8.2 Data Flow for a "Bring Water" Task — End-to-End Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Elder
    participant HRI as Layer 5 HRI
    participant Cog as Layer 3 Cognition
    participant Plan as Layer 4 Planner
    participant Sens as Layer 2 Perception
    participant HW as Layer 1 Hardware

    Elder->>HRI: "I am thirsty please bring water"
    HRI->>Cog: Transcribed intent and entities
    Cog->>Cog: NLU parses request, queries Knowledge Base
    Cog->>Plan: Task "navigate to kitchen, find glass, fetch water, return"
    Plan->>Sens: Request current pose and map
    Sens-->>Plan: Pose estimate and occupancy grid
    Plan->>HW: Mobile base wheel velocity commands
    HW-->>Sens: Encoder feedback and IMU updates
    Sens-->>Plan: Updated pose
    Plan->>HW: Manipulator trajectory to grasp glass
    Plan->>HW: Lower force threshold for contact
    HW-->>Plan: Grasp success confirmation
    Plan->>HW: Return navigation to elder
    Plan->>HRI: Speak "Here is your water"
    HRI->>Elder: TTS greeting and visual confirmation
```

### 8.3 Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architectural Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Slow["DELIBERATIVE LAYER - Slow"]
        S1[World Model]
        S2[Task Planner]
        S3[Global Path Planner]
        S4[Knowledge Base]
    end

    subgraph Fast["REACTIVE LAYER - Fast 10 to 50 ms"]
        R1[Obstacle Avoidance]
        R2[Safety Stop Reflex]
        R3[Force Limit Reflex]
        R4[Voice Reflex Wake Word]
    end

    subgraph Arb["ARBITRATOR"]
        ARB{Behavior Priority Logic}
    end

    Slow --> ARB
    Fast --> ARB
    ARB --> ACT[Actuator Commands to Motors]
    ARB -->|Override on emergency| Fast
```

### 8.4 Communication Bus Topology (Internal)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[Main Computer Jetson] -->|Ethernet GigE| CAM1[Front RGB-D Camera]
    CPU -->|USB 3.0| LIDAR[2D LiDAR]
    CPU -->|SPI I2C| IMU[9 Axis IMU]
    CPU -->|CAN Bus| MC1[Motor Controller Base]
    CPU -->|EtherCAT| MC2[Motor Controller Arm]
    CPU -->|USB Audio| MIC[Microphone Array]
    CPU -->|GPIO| SAFE[Safety E Stop Circuit]
    MC1 --> M1[Left Wheel]
    MC1 --> M2[Right Wheel]
    MC2 --> J1[Joint 1]
    MC2 --> J2[Joint 2]
    MC2 --> J3[Joint 3]
    MC2 --> J4[Joint 4]
    MC2 --> J5[Joint 5]
    MC2 --> J6[Joint 6]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 9. Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct, definitional KTU Part A style. Answers should be **3–4 lines**, precise, and use syllabus terminology.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Define a service robot for elders. List any four functional subsystems that its architecture must include.**

**Model Answer:**
A service robot for elders is a personal-care robot (as per ISO 13482) that autonomously assists aged users in daily living, healthcare monitoring, mobility, and companionship within domestic or assisted-living environments. Its architecture must include:
1. **Mobile base** for indoor navigation
2. **Manipulator arm** for object handling
3. **Perception module** (cameras, LiDAR, IMU) for environment and person sensing
4. **Cognition/planning module** for decision-making and task planning
5. **HRI module** (speech, display) for natural interaction

*(Any four suffice for full marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between deliberative and reactive architectural paradigms for a service robot.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Deliberative | Reactive |
|---|---|---|
| Reasoning | Sense → Plan → Act sequence | Stimulus → Reflex response |
| Response time | Slow (seconds) | Fast (milliseconds) |
| Robustness | Brittle to world changes | Robust to dynamic environment |
| Use case | Task planning, long-horizon goals | Obstacle avoidance, safety stops |
| Example module | Global path planner | Bumper-triggered emergency stop |

---

## 10. Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Draw and explain the five-layer system architecture of a service robot for elders. State the role of each layer.

**Model Solution:**

The five-layer architecture is the industry-standard organization for elder-care robots. Each layer abstracts and serves the layer above it.

**Layer 1 — Hardware/Physical Layer:**
This is the physical body of the robot. It includes the **mobile base** (differential or omnidirectional drive for stable indoor navigation), the **manipulator arm** (6–7 DOF for human-scale object handling), the **sensor suite** (RGB-D cameras, 2D/3D LiDAR, IMU, microphone array, force/torque sensors, tactile skin), the **on-board computer** (GPU-accelerated edge AI), the **battery pack with BMS**, and **safety hardware** (E-stop, bumpers, cliff sensors). It provides the actuation and sensing primitives.

**Layer 2 — Perception Layer:**
Transforms raw sensor data into semantic understanding. Modules include **sensor fusion** (EKF/UKF combining LiDAR + camera + IMU), **SLAM** for mapping and localization, **object detection** (deep CNNs) to recognize household items, **face recognition** to identify the elder, **pose estimation** to detect falls, **speech recognition** (ASR) for voice input, and **affective computing** to detect emotion or pain. This layer answers the question: *"What is happening around me and with the user?"*

**Layer 3 — Cognition / Decision Layer:**
Implements the "brain." Contains the **task planner** (PDDL or HTN), which decomposes high-level goals (e.g., "bring water") into ordered subtasks; the **behavior coordinator**, which arbitrates concurrent behaviors using priority logic; the **knowledge base** (user routines, medication schedule, room semantics); the **NLU** module for intent extraction; and the **anomaly detection engine** for health and safety alerts. This layer answers: *"What should I do now?"*

**Layer 4 — Motion Planning & Control Layer:**
Translates plans into actuator commands. Includes the **global path planner** (A*, Dijkstra), the **local planner** (DWA, TEB) for dynamic obstacle avoidance, the **motion controller** (PID, MPC), the **force-compliant controller** for safe physical contact, and the **reactive safety reflex** for sub-50 ms emergency responses.

**Layer 5 — HRI / Application Layer:**
The user-facing surface. Implements the **dialogue manager** (multi-turn conversation), **TTS** (text-to-speech), **touch screen** and **LED expressive face** for multimodal feedback, **cloud sync** for uploading health reports to family/caregivers, and **telepresence** for doctor video calls. This layer answers: *"How do I interact with the human in a natural, trustworthy way?"*

**Cross-cutting Safety Subsystem (ISO 13482):** Wraps all layers with **emergency stop**, **power and force limiting**, and **safety-rated monitored stop** to ensure no single point of failure can cause harm.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often *omit* the cross-cutting safety layer. Always mention it. A neat labeled diagram is worth 3 of the 7 marks.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A service robot for elders has the following power-consuming subsystems: Mobile Base 50 W, Manipulator 35 W, On-board Computer 30 W, Sensors 12 W, HRI 8 W, Communication 4 W. Determine the **minimum battery capacity in Wh** required for **10 hours** of continuous operation. Assume **80% depth-of-discharge** and a **24 V nominal pack**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Power:**
$$
P_{total} = 50 + 35 + 30 + 12 + 8 + 4 = 139 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Energy:**
$$
E_{theoretical} = P_{total} \times t = 139 \times 10 = 1390 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply DoD Derating:**
$$
E_{battery} = \dfrac{1390}{0.80} = 1737.5 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ah at 24 V:**
$$
C_{pack} = \dfrac{1737.5}{24} = 72.4 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Pack:**
Using 7S Li-Ion ($7 \times 3.6 = 25.2$ V nominal, close to 24 V) with 3.0 Ah 18650 cells:
- 25 parallel strings needed: $72.4 / 3.0 = 24.13$ → round to **25P**
- Final pack: **7S25P**, capacity = $25 \times 3.0 = 75$ Ah, energy = $75 \times 25.2 = 1890$ Wh

**Final Answer:** Minimum theoretical capacity = **1737.5 Wh**, practical pack **7S25P @ 1890 Wh**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Do **not** forget the DoD derating step. It costs **2 marks**.
> - Do **not** write the answer only in Wh *or* only in Ah. Mention both.
> - Do **not** skip the practical cell-pack statement. Examiners reward engineering pragmatism.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare the three classical architectural paradigms (Deliberative, Reactive, Hybrid) for elder-care robots. Justify which is most suitable.

**Model Solution:**

**Deliberative (Hierarchical) Architecture:**
Implements a strict *Sense → Plan → Act* loop. A world model is maintained and updated, a planner reasons over it to generate a sequence of actions, and the executor carries them out. Strength: logically consistent, explainable, suitable for long-horizon tasks like "prepare morning routine." Weakness: slow (planning takes seconds), brittle if the world model is incomplete or stale, and cannot react in real time to sudden events like a person falling.

**Reactive (Behavior-Based) Architecture:**
Decomposes control into many parallel stimulus–response behaviors (e.g., "if obstacle close → stop"). No global world model; each behavior directly maps sensors to actuators. Strength: extremely fast (millisecond cycle), robust to dynamic environments, fault-tolerant (loss of one behavior does not crash the system). Weakness: no long-horizon planning, hard to coordinate many behaviors, and no learning of complex goals.

**Hybrid Deliberative/Reactive Architecture:**
Combines both: a **slow deliberative layer** handles planning, knowledge, and task sequencing; a **fast reactive layer** handles safety, obstacle avoidance, and reflexes. An **arbitrator** merges their outputs, with the reactive layer able to *override* the deliberative layer in emergencies.

**Justification for Elder-Care Robots:**
Elder-care demands **both** long-horizon reasoning (medication schedule, daily routine) **and** real-time safety (fall detection, obstacle avoidance). Neither pure deliberative nor pure reactive suffices. Therefore, the **Hybrid Architecture** is the most suitable. Industry examples include the *LAAS architecture* and modern *ROS 2 + Behavior Trees* stacks. The ISO 13482 safety requirements effectively *mandate* a hybrid approach.

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the **HRI (Human-Robot Interaction) subsystem** of an elder-care robot. List **four** safety considerations specific to elderly users.

**Model Solution:**

**HRI Subsystem Block Diagram:**

```
+----------------------------------------+
|   HRI SUBSYSTEM (Layer 5)             |
+----------------------------------------+
| Multimodal Input:                      |
|   - Far-field Microphone Array (ASR)   |
|   - Touch Screen (GUI menu)            |
|   - Vision-based gesture recognition   |
+----------------------------------------+
| Dialogue Manager:                      |
|   - Intent classifier (NLU)            |
|   - Context tracker                    |
|   - Response generator                 |
+----------------------------------------+
| Multimodal Output:                     |
|   - TTS (Text-to-Speech, multilingual) |
|   - LED expressive face (emojis)       |
|   - Touch screen UI                    |
+----------------------------------------+
| External Interfaces:                   |
|   - Cloud sync (caregiver app)         |
|   - Telepresence (video call doctor)   |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Four Safety Considerations Specific to Elderly Users:**

1. **Cognitive Load Minimization** — UI must use large fonts, high-contrast colors, and simple iconography. Voice prompts should be slow and clear. Elderly users with mild cognitive impairment cannot process dense information.

2. **Voice Clarity in Hearing Deficits** — TTS must use **lower pitch and slower rate**; microphone must be far-field and noise-cancelling because many elders have presbycusis (age-related hearing loss).

3. **Physical Safety during HRI** — Force-limited contact (per ISO/TS 15066) when handing over objects. The robot must never approach faster than **0.25 m/s** and must yield if the elder pushes it.

4. **Privacy & Data Security** — Continuous video/audio monitoring in a home raises serious privacy concerns. All data must be encrypted, on-device processing preferred, and explicit consent required from the elder and family.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Generic safety points (e.g., "the robot should be safe") earn **0 marks**. Use **specific, elderly-focused** considerations.
> - Always cite **ISO 13482** or **ISO/TS 15066** in safety answers.
> - A **neat labeled diagram** is worth 2–3 marks. Do not skip it.

---

## 11. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision sheet — memorize before the exam:**

- [x] **Service robot for elders** is a *personal care robot* per **ISO 13482**.
- [x] **System Architecture** = structured organization of hardware, software, perception, cognition, control, and HRI.
- [x] **Five Layers:** Hardware → Perception → Cognition → Motion Control → HRI/Application.
- [x] **Cross-cutting Safety Layer** (ISO 13482) wraps all layers with E-stop, PFL, SRMS.
- [x] **Three Paradigms:** Deliberative (slow, logical) / Reactive (fast, reflexes) / **Hybrid (industry standard for elder care)**.
- [x] **Battery sizing formula:** $E_{bat} = (P_{total} \times t) / \eta_{DoD}$, then $C = E_{bat} / V_{pack}$.
- [x] **Typical speed:** 0.25–0.6 m/s indoors. **HRI latency:** < 500 ms. **Battery life:** 8–12 h.
- [x] **Manipulator:** 6–7 DOF, force-limited compliant control.
- [x] **Perception tools:** SLAM (Cartographer), Object detection (YOLO), ASR (Whisper), Face recognition.
- [x] **Elder-specific HRI:** large fonts, slow clear speech, low-pitch TTS, high-contrast UI.
- [x] **Privacy:** on-device processing, encryption, explicit consent — non-negotiable.
- [x] **Key standards to cite:** ISO 13482, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 12100.
- [x] **In numericals:** always apply **DoD derating** (typically 0.80) — most common 2-mark loss.
- [x] **In diagrams:** include the **safety cross-cutting layer** and label all data flows.
- [x] **In comparisons:** present in a **table** with at least 4 attributes (speed, robustness, use case, example).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hardware Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Field and Service Robotics (FSR)**, the **Hardware Structure** refers to the integrated electromechanical architecture that physically constitutes a robot operating in unstructured, semi-structured, or human-populated environments. It encompasses all physical sub-systems — the **mechanical chassis (frame)**, **locomotion platform**, **manipulator and end-effector**, **actuation system**, **sensor suite**, **power and energy management subsystem**, **embedded computation unit**, and **communication interface** — arranged in a modular, rugged, and often weather-resistant topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Hardware Structure in Module 4 specifically addresses the *physical embodiment* of field/service robots — the components students must identify, classify, and justify when designing a robot for applications such as **agriculture, mining, defence, healthcare, underwater exploration, and disaster response**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a field/service robot exactly like the **human body**:

| Body Part | Robot Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Skeleton & Muscles | Chassis, frame, actuators | Provides structure and movement |
| Brain & Nerves | Microcontroller / SBC + wiring | Processes decisions and signals |
| Eyes, Ears, Skin | Sensors (cameras, LIDAR, IMU, tactile) | Perceives the environment |
| Heart & Lungs | Battery, power management | Supplies energy |
| Hands & Arms | Manipulator, gripper, end-effector | Interacts with objects |
| Mouth & Ears (talking) | Radio, Wi-Fi, 5G, CAN bus | Communicates with operator/other robots |

A *humanoid helper robot* walking through a hospital, a *rover* navigating a Mars-like terrain, and an *autonomous tractor* in a paddy field all share the same hardware "anatomy" — only the proportions and ruggedisation differ.

## 1.3 Core Hardware Metrics in Field Robotics

The following standard metrics are universally used to compare robot hardware structures (highlighted as required by the KTU 2024 rubric):

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — number of independent motions a robot can perform.
- **Payload-to-Weight Ratio (PWR)** — a dimensionless figure of merit.
- **Ingress Protection (IP) Rating** — e.g., **IP67**, **IP68** for dust/water resistance.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — typically **> 2,000 hours** for field-grade systems.
- **Operating Temperature Range** — often **−20 °C to +60 °C** for outdoor robots.
- **Power Density (W/kg)** — battery mass normalised output.
- **Wheel/Track Ground Pressure (kPa)** — critical for soft-terrain traversal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ruggedisation matters in FSR:** Unlike industrial robots that work inside cages in climate-controlled factories, field robots must survive dust, rain, mud, vibration, EMI, and temperature shocks. Hence *hardware structure* is **not just a frame** — it is an **engineered system of protection, power, sensing, and actuation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sub-Systems of Robot Hardware Structure

The hardware structure of any field or service robot can be decomposed into **five tightly coupled sub-systems**. The KTU examiner expects students to be able to *draw, label, and explain* these layers.

### A. Mechanical Chassis & Locomotion Platform
- **Wheeled:** differential-drive, Ackermann steering, omnidirectional (Mecanum / Swedish).
- **Tracked:** skid-steered, used for rough/soft terrain (e.g., military EOD robots).
- **Legged:** 2-, 4-, 6-, 8-legged; high mobility, high control complexity.
- **Aerial (UAV/Drone):** multirotor, fixed-wing, hybrid VTOL.
- **Marine/AUV/ROV:** thruster-based, ballast-controlled.

### B. Actuation System
- **DC Geared Motors** — cheap, high torque, easy to control.
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motors** — high efficiency, low maintenance, used in drones and modern AGVs.
- **Servo Motors** — closed-loop position control (e.g., Dynamixel, MG996R).
- **Stepper Motors** — open-loop precision, lower speed.
- **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Actuators** — for high-force service robots.
- **Series Elastic Actuators (SEA)** — compliant, safe for human interaction.

### C. Sensor Suite (Proprioceptive + Exteroceptive)
- **Proprioceptive:** wheel encoders, IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), motor current sensors, joint encoders.
- **Exteroceptive:** GPS, RGB-D cameras, stereo cameras, LIDAR (2D/3D), ultrasonic, infrared, thermal/IR cameras, force/torque sensors, tactile skins, gas/chemical sensors.

### D. Power & Energy Subsystem
- **Li-Po / Li-Ion batteries** — most common.
- **Lead-acid** — used in heavy AGVs and mining robots.
- **Solar panels + MPPT** — long-endurance outdoor robots.
- **Fuel cells (Hydrogen)** — emerging in large field robots.
- **Super-capacitors** — for regenerative braking / burst power.

### E. Embedded Computation & Communication
- **Microcontroller (MCU):** STM32, Arduino, ESP32 — low-level real-time control.
- **Single-Board Computer (SBC):** Raspberry Pi 4/5, NVIDIA Jetson Orin, Google Coral — perception, SLAM, AI.
- **Industrial PC (IPC):** for heavy computational payloads (autonomous trucks).
- **Buses:** CAN, RS-485, Ethernet, EtherCAT.
- **Wireless:** Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 4G/5G, LoRa, ZigBee, satellite (Iridium).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested** in KTU University ESE (End Semester Examination) for the Robotics paper.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Traction force | $F_t = \mu \cdot N$ | N | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $N$ = normal force |
| 2 | Hill-climbing force | $F_c = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ = slope angle |
| 3 | Total tractive force | $F_{tot} = m a + m g \sin\theta + \mu_r m g \cos\theta$ | N | $a$ = linear acceleration, $\mu_r$ = rolling resistance |
| 4 | Motor torque required | $\tau_{req} = \dfrac{F_{tot} \cdot r}{n \cdot \eta_g}$ | N·m | $r$ = wheel radius, $n$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = gear efficiency |
| 5 | Motor power | $P_m = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 6 | Battery energy | $E = V \cdot C$ | Wh | $V$ = nominal voltage, $C$ = capacity in Ah |
| 7 | Endurance time | $t = \dfrac{E \cdot \eta_{sys}}{P_{avg}}$ | h | $\eta_{sys}$ = system efficiency |
| 8 | Power density | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P_{out}}{m_{bat}}$ | W/kg | Battery-specific |
| 9 | Payload-to-weight ratio | $PWR = \dfrac{m_{payload}}{m_{robot}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Wheel ground pressure | $p_g = \dfrac{m g}{A_{contact}}$ | Pa | Critical in soft soil |
| 11 | DOF of manipulator | $M = 6(n-1) - \text{redundant joints}$ | — | For open kinematic chain |
| 12 | Workspace volume (sphere) | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | m³ | If spherical reach envelope |
| 13 | Static stability margin | $SSM = \dfrac{d_{CoM\ to\ edge}}{h_{CoM}}$ | — | For legged robots |
| 14 | Reynolds number (AUVs) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | — | Predicts flow regime |
| 15 | Specific Energy | $SE = \dfrac{E}{m \cdot d}$ | Wh/(kg·km) | Energy per unit distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas directly drive decisions like *"Can this 4-wheel differential robot climb a 30° slope?"* or *"Will a 24 V, 20 Ah Li-Po last an 8-hour shift on the farm?"* — exactly the kind of design-justification questions asked in KTU Module 4.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Motor Torque for a Wheeled Field Robot Climbing a Slope

This is a **standard KTU 14-mark derivation** and is virtually guaranteed to appear once in every cohort.

### Given
- Robot mass $m = 50$ kg
- Wheel radius $r = 0.10$ m
- Gear ratio $n = 30$ (gearhead reduction)
- Gear efficiency $\eta_g = 0.85$
- Slope angle $\theta = 20°$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.05$
- Desired acceleration $a = 0.5$ m/s²
- Take $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Two driven wheels (rear)

### Step 1 — Identify the resisting forces on a slope
On an incline, three forces resist the motion of the robot:
1. **Inertial force** (for acceleration): $F_{acc} = m a$
2. **Gravitational component along slope**: $F_{grav} = m g \sin\theta$
3. **Rolling resistance**: $F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m g \cos\theta$

### Step 2 — Sum the resisting forces

$$
F_{tot} = F_{acc} + F_{grav} + F_{roll}
$$

$$
F_{tot} = m a + m g \sin\theta + \mu_r m g \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

First compute each term:

$$
F_{acc} = 50 \times 0.5 = 25.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{grav} = 50 \times 9.81 \times \sin 20°
$$

Since $\sin 20° \approx 0.3420$:

$$
F_{grav} = 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.3420 = 167.761 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 0.05 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times \cos 20°
$$

Since $\cos 20° \approx 0.9397$:

$$
F_{roll} = 0.05 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times 0.9397 = 23.050 \text{ N}
$$

Adding them:

$$
F_{tot} = 25.0 + 167.761 + 23.050 = 215.811 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert wheel force to per-wheel torque

Because there are **two driven wheels**, each wheel must supply half the total force:

$$
F_{wheel} = \dfrac{F_{tot}}{2} = \dfrac{215.811}{2} = 107.906 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 5 — Wheel torque (before gearbox)

$$
\tau_{wheel} = F_{wheel} \cdot r = 107.906 \times 0.10 = 10.7906 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 6 — Motor torque (after accounting for gear ratio and efficiency)

The gearbox multiplies the motor torque by $n$ and reduces efficiency by $\eta_g$:

$$
\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{n \cdot \eta_g} = \dfrac{10.7906}{30 \times 0.85}
$$

$$
\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{10.7906}{25.5} = 0.4232 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Answer & Sanity Check

$$
\boxed{\tau_{motor} \approx 0.42 \text{ N·m per motor}}
$$

> A standard 12 V DC geared motor rated at **3–5 N·m** stall torque easily handles this load, with a healthy safety margin of **> 7×** — a typical KTU design-validity check.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Locomotion Sub-System

| Application | Terrain | Recommended Locomotion | Why (Justification) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor service robot (hospital) | Flat, tiled | Differential / omnidirectional | Energy-efficient, simple control |
| Agricultural field robot | Soft soil, mud | Tracked / 4-WD Ackermann | Low ground pressure, high traction |
| Search & rescue rubble | Mixed debris | Tracked + flipper arms | Self-righting, high obstacle clearance |
| Planetary rover (Mars) | Rocky, sandy | 6-wheel rocker-bogie | Passive suspension, all-terrain |
| Warehouse AGV | Flat, marked floor | Differential drive | Cost-effective, SLAM-friendly |
| Underwater inspection | Aquatic | Thrusters (vectored) | 3D manoeuvrability |
| Aerial mapping | Open sky | Quadcopter (multirotor) | Hover, vertical take-off |
| Pipe inspection | Confined tube | In-pipe caterpillar /履带 | Small diameter adaptability |
| Defence EOD | Variable | Tracked + manipulator | Rugged, explosive-safe |
| Legged humanoid | Stairs, uneven | Bipedal (2-leg) | Anthropomorphic, human environments |

## 3.3 Sensor Fusion Snapshot (Analytical)

In a typical outdoor field robot, the **sensor fusion equation** for pose estimation is a weighted sum of independent estimators:

$$
\hat{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, \hat{x}_i, \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

The weights are often derived from the inverse of the **covariance** of each sensor (Kalman Filter framework). The KTU board typically awards 1–2 marks for simply stating this form, and another 1–2 marks for naming **at least two sensor modalities** (e.g., GPS + IMU).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Field/Service Robot

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **layered hardware architecture** — from power input to task output — with all interconnections labelled. This diagram, when reproduced in the exam, is worth **3–4 marks** by itself.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR1[Power Source Li-Ion Battery Pack] --> PWR2[Power Management BMS with DC DC Converters]
    PWR2 --> ACT[Actuation Layer DC BLDC Servo Motors]
    PWR2 --> SENS[Sensor Layer GPS IMU LIDAR Camera Encoders]
    PWR2 --> COMP[Compute Layer MCU plus SBC]
    PWR2 --> COMM[Comms Layer WiFi 4G CAN Bus]
    PWR2 --> MAN[Manipulator Layer Arm plus Gripper]
    ACT --> MECH[Mechanical Chassis Wheels Tracks Frame]
    MAN --> MECH
    SENS --> COMP
    ACT --> COMP
    COMP --> ACT
    COMP --> MAN
    COMM --> COMP
    COMP --> OP[Operator Interface GUI plus Telemetry]
    SENS --> FUS[Sensor Fusion Module EKF]
    FUS --> COMP
```

## 4.2 Hardware-Software Co-Design Flow (Mermaid)

This second diagram clarifies **how the hardware structure interacts with the software stack** — a frequent "design question" in KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Field Environment] --> B[Exteroceptive Sensors]
    C[Robot Internal State] --> D[Proprioceptive Sensors]
    B --> E[Perception Stack]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Decision and Planning AI Module]
    F --> G[Motion Controller]
    G --> H[Low Level Motor Driver]
    H --> I[Actuators and Wheels]
    I --> J[Physical World Effect]
    J --> A
    K[Remote Operator] --> L[Communication Link]
    L --> F
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Layout of a Typical Wheeled Service Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP[Top Deck Payloads Camera LIDAR Antenna] --> MID
    MID[Mid Deck Compute and Power SBC Battery BMS] --> BASE
    BASE[Base Deck Chassis Motors Wheels Encoders]
    BASE --> TRACK[Tracks or Wheels Contact with Ground]
    MID --> ARM[Side Mounted Manipulator 5 DOF Arm]
    ARM --> GRIP[End Effector Gripper]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams matter in KTU exams:** The 2024 Scheme's CO4 (Design) requires students to "represent and justify the hardware architecture using schematics." A well-labelled Mermaid-style block diagram typically fetches **3–4 out of 14 marks** in a design question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hardware Structure Questions**
