> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Hardware Structure" with "Software Architecture".** KTU Module 4 is strictly about *physical components* — chassis, motors, sensors, batteries, microcontrollers, and their integration.
> 2. **Always justify your component choice** with at least one *quantitative* metric (e.g., torque, IP rating, payload, DOF). Examiners explicitly award the "justification mark" only when numbers or standards are quoted.
> 3. **Avoid vague terms** like "good sensor" or "powerful motor". Use *exact* part families (e.g., "NEMA-17 stepper", "Hokuyo UTM-30LX 2D LIDAR", "24 V 10 Ah Li-Po with BMS").
> 4. **Draw labelled block diagrams** even if the question does not explicitly ask — they earn free 2–3 marks.
> 5. **State assumptions clearly** (e.g., "Assume rolling coefficient μᵣ = 0.05 for rubber on wet asphalt") before solving numerical problems.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**List and briefly explain the four major locomotion mechanisms used in field and service robots. State one field application of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

1. **Wheeled locomotion** — uses 2, 3, 4 or more wheels; energy-efficient, simple kinematics. *Application:* indoor hospital service robots, AGVs in warehouses.
2. **Tracked locomotion** — continuous tracks distribute weight and provide high traction. *Application:* military EOD robots, agricultural tractors, search-and-rescue on rubble.
3. **Legged locomotion** — discrete foot contacts allow traversal of stairs and uneven terrain. *Application:* humanoid service robots, hexapod planetary rovers.
4. **Aerial / Marine locomotion** — multirotor drones (quad/hex) for surveillance; thruster-based AUVs/ROVs for underwater inspection.

**(3 marks — 1 for listing + 1 for explanation + 1 for application linkage)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is the Ingress Protection (IP) rating? Why is it critical in the hardware structure of outdoor field robots? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**IP rating** is a two-digit international standard (**IEC 60529**) that classifies the degree of protection an enclosure provides against **intrusion by solid objects (1st digit, 0–6)** and **liquids (2nd digit, 0–9)**.

- *1st digit:* protection from dust/tools (e.g., **6 = dust-tight**).
- *2nd digit:* protection from water (e.g., **7 = immersion to 1 m**, **8 = continuous submersion**).

**Why critical:** Field robots operate in rain, mud, dust, and dewy grass. An IP rating ensures **electronics survival** and **MTBF compliance**.

**Examples:** **IP65** for an agricultural weeding robot; **IP68** for an underwater ROV used in pipeline inspection.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for justification + 1 for examples)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled block diagram**, explain the **hardware architecture** of a typical **autonomous agricultural field robot** used for seeding and weeding. Identify all five sub-systems and their representative components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-wheel differential-drive agricultural robot of mass **80 kg** must climb a **15° slope** while carrying a **20 kg seed/weed tank** at an acceleration of **0.3 m/s²**. Wheel radius is **0.12 m**, gear ratio is **25**, gear efficiency is **0.80**, and rolling resistance coefficient is **0.06**. Calculate **(i)** the total tractive force, and **(ii)** the **per-motor torque** required. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Block Diagram (Reproduce in Exam):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  POWER  ──►  BMS  ──►  DC/DC  ──►  All Subsystems       │
│                                                          │
│  ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐      │
│  │ Sensors    │    │ Compute    │    │ Actuators  │      │
│  │ GPS, IMU,  │───►│ Jetson Orin│───►│ BLDC ×4    │      │
│  │ RGB-D,     │    │ + STM32    │    │ + Encoders │      │
│  │ Soil moist │    │            │    │            │      │
│  └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘      │
│         ▲                                │               │
│         │                                ▼               │
│  ┌────────────┐                  ┌────────────┐         │
│  │ Comms      │                  │ Manipulator│         │
│  │ 4G + WiFi  │                  │ 5-DOF Arm  │         │
│  └────────────┘                  │ + Gripper  │         │
│                                  └────────────┘         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Five Sub-Systems and Components:**

1. **Locomotion/Chassis:** 4-WD skid-steer chassis with rubber tyres; ground pressure $p_g = \dfrac{m g}{A_{contact}}$ tuned for soft soil.
2. **Actuation:** 4× BLDC hub motors with integrated encoders (e.g., 36 V, 250 W each); a 5-DOF servo-driven robotic arm for weeding/seeding tool.
3. **Sensing:** GPS-RTK for ±2 cm accuracy, IMU (9-DOF), RGB-D camera for weed detection, soil-moisture & pH probes, wheel encoders.
4. **Power:** 24 V, 30 Ah Li-Ion battery pack with BMS; solar roof panel (50 W) for auxiliary charging; DC-DC converters to 5 V, 12 V, 19 V rails.
5. **Compute & Comms:** NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano (AI/perception) + STM32F4 (real-time motor control); 4G/LTE for cloud telemetry; CAN-bus for inter-subsystem communication.

**(7 marks = 2 diagram + 5 sub-system coverage with at least one justification each)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Given:**
$m = 80 + 20 = 100$ kg (total), $\theta = 15°$, $a = 0.3$ m/s², $r = 0.12$ m, $n = 25$, $\eta_g = 0.80$, $\mu_r = 0.06$, $g = 9.81$ m/s², **4 driven wheels**.

**Step 1 — Compute the three resistance terms**

$$
F_{acc} = m a = 100 \times 0.3 = 30.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{grav} = m g \sin\theta = 100 \times 9.81 \times \sin 15°
$$

Using $\sin 15° \approx 0.2588$:

$$
F_{grav} = 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.2588 = 253.92 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{roll} = \mu_r \, m g \cos\theta = 0.06 \times 100 \times 9.81 \times \cos 15°
$$

Using $\cos 15° \approx 0.9659$:

$$
F_{roll} = 0.06 \times 100 \times 9.81 \times 0.9659 = 56.86 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Total tractive force**

$$
F_{tot} = 30.0 + 253.92 + 56.86 = 340.78 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-wheel force (4 driven wheels)**

$$
F_{wheel} = \dfrac{340.78}{4} = 85.20 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-wheel torque at the ground**

$$
\tau_{wheel} = F_{wheel} \cdot r = 85.20 \times 0.12 = 10.224 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-motor torque (after gearbox)**

$$
\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{\tau_{wheel}}{n \cdot \eta_g} = \dfrac{10.224}{25 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{10.224}{20}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{motor} = 0.5112 \text{ N·m per motor}}
$$

> **Design check:** Standard 24 V / 36 V BLDC motors with 100–200 W rating provide **> 1 N·m continuous torque**, giving a safety margin of **≈ 2×**, which is acceptable for an agricultural duty cycle.

**(7 marks = 2 for formula setup + 2 for force terms + 1 for total + 1 for wheel share + 1 for motor torque and sanity check)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Model]**

**(a)** Compare the **hardware structural features** of **wheeled, tracked, and legged** field robots using a comparison table covering at least **eight parameters** (e.g., efficiency, obstacle clearance, control complexity, cost, terrain adaptability, payload, MTBF, IP rating). Justify which of the three is most suited for a **disaster-response robot** operating in collapsed buildings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A service robot uses a **24 V, 15 Ah Li-Po battery**. The total average power consumption of all sub-systems (compute + sensors + actuators) is **45 W**, and the system efficiency (DC-DC + motor drive) is **0.85**. Estimate the **endurance time** in hours. If the design specification requires **at least 6 hours** of operation, recommend an upgrade to the battery. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Comparison Table (8 parameters, to be drawn in exam):**

| Parameter | Wheeled | Tracked | Legged |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy efficiency | High | Medium | Low |
| Obstacle clearance | Low (< wheel radius) | Medium | High |
| Control complexity | Low (1–2 actuators) | Medium | Very high (n DOF) |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Medium–High | Very High |
| Terrain adaptability | Smooth / paved | Mud, sand, rubble | Stairs, uneven, debris |
| Payload capacity | Medium | High | Low–Medium |
| Mechanical simplicity | Simple | Moderate | Complex linkages |
| MTBF (hours) | 3000+ | 2000 | 800–1500 |
| Typical IP rating achievable | IP65 | IP67 | IP54–65 |
| Vibration tolerance | Low | High | Medium |

**Justification for disaster response:**

A **tracked robot** is most suited for disaster response in collapsed buildings because:

1. **High obstacle clearance and traction** over debris, broken concrete, and dust.
2. **Higher payload** to carry victim-detection sensors, manipulator arms, and communication relays.
3. **Better vibration tolerance** for sensitive payloads (LIDAR, IMU).
4. **Higher IP rating achievable** (IP67) — critical in dusty, wet post-disaster environments.

While legged robots can climb stairs, their **low MTBF**, **high control complexity**, and **short battery life** make them impractical for first-responder deployment where reliability is paramount.

**(7 marks = 4 for the table (½ mark per well-considered row) + 3 for justified selection of tracked robot)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Given:**
- Battery: $V = 24$ V, $C = 15$ Ah
- Average load power: $P_{load} = 45$ W
- System efficiency: $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Battery energy**

$$
E_{bat} = V \times C = 24 \times 15 = 360 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective usable energy after efficiency**

$$
E_{usable} = E_{bat} \times \eta_{sys} = 360 \times 0.85 = 306 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 3 — Endurance time**

$$
t = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{P_{load}} = \dfrac{306}{45}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 6.80 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 4 — Does it meet the 6-hour spec?**

> **Yes, marginally.** The endurance is 6.80 h, satisfying the **6-hour minimum** with a 13% safety margin. However, for field deployment a **20–25% margin** is recommended.

**Step 5 — Recommended upgrade**

To achieve a **comfortable 8 hours** of operation (33% margin):

$$
E_{required} = P_{load} \times t_{target} / \eta_{sys} = 45 \times 8 / 0.85 \approx 423.5 \text{ Wh}
$$

$$
C_{new} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{V} = \dfrac{423.5}{24} \approx 17.65 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Recommendation:** Upgrade to a **24 V, 20 Ah Li-Po pack** (480 Wh), or add a **50 W solar auxiliary panel** to extend operational hours indefinitely under sunlight.

**(7 marks = 2 for energy calculation + 2 for endurance + 1 for spec compliance check + 2 for justified upgrade recommendation)**

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Hardware Structure**

- ☐ **5 Hardware Sub-Systems**: Chassis/Locomotion · Actuation · Sensors · Power · Compute & Comms — *must* know all five by name and example.
- ☐ **Locomotion Trade-offs**: Wheeled (efficient/simple) < Tracked (rugged/heavy) < Legged (versatile/complex).
- ☐ **Key Actuators**: DC, BLDC, Servo, Stepper, Pneumatic, Hydraulic, SEA.
- ☐ **Sensor Categories**: Proprioceptive (encoders, IMU) vs Exteroceptive (LIDAR, camera, GPS).
- ☐ **Power Metrics**: Battery energy $E = V \cdot C$ Wh, endurance $t = E \eta / P$.
- ☐ **Tractive Force Formula**: $F_{tot} = m a + m g \sin\theta + \mu_r m g \cos\theta$ — *derivation is a high-yield exam item*.
- ☐ **Motor Torque Formula**: $\tau_{motor} = \dfrac{F_{wheel} \cdot r}{n \cdot \eta_g}$ — apply per driven wheel.
- ☐ **Power Equation**: $P = \tau \omega$ (rotational) and $P = F v$ (linear).
- ☐ **Ruggedisation Standards**: IP65/67/68, MTBF, operating temperature range.
- ☐ **Compute Hierarchy**: MCU (real-time) + SBC (perception/AI) + IPC (heavy compute).
- ☐ **Communication Buses**: CAN (automotive-grade), RS-485, Ethernet, EtherCAT, Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, LoRa.
- ☐ **Design Validation**: Always check **safety factor ≥ 2** for torque/payload, **margin ≥ 20%** for battery endurance.
- ☐ **Block Diagram Drawing**: A *neat labelled* block diagram of robot architecture is worth **3–4 marks** on its own.
- ☐ **Application Mapping**: Each hardware choice must be **justified against the application** (agriculture, defence, healthcare, underwater, aerial).
- ☐ **Syllabus Cross-Reference**: This topic directly feeds into Module 5 (Navigation & SLAM) — sensor choices made here constrain perception algorithms later.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Robot Interaction - Touch and Watch Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Human Robot Interaction - Touch and Watch Interaction ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Touch and Watch Interaction** in Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) refers to a sub-domain of *multimodal physical human-robot interaction (pHRI)* where the human operator communicates with and controls a robotic system through two primary physical channels: 1. **Touch Interaction (Haptic Interaction):** Direct physical contact between the human and the robot (or its input device), transmitting mechanical, tactile, or thermal stimuli. This includes tactile sensing, force feedback, and contact-based gesture recognition. 2. **Watch Interaction (Wearable / Gesture-based Interaction):** Indirect, wearable-mediated interaction where the human uses a wrist-mounted device (smartwatch, EMG band, or inertial measurement bracelet) to issue commands via gestures, motion, or bio-signals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCRUT602 / Module 4):** Touch and Watch Interaction sits at the intersection of **perception** (sensors), **cognition** (intent inference), and **actuation** (robot response). It is foundational to *service robotics* (assistive, medical, domestic) where direct contact is non-negotiable. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are teaching a child to stack blocks. You could: - **Touch Interaction:** Place your hand *over* theirs and physically guide their fingers — they *feel* the pressure and correct the motion. This is touch interaction — the robot "feels" the user's intent through contact forces. - **Watch Interaction:** You wear a small device on your wrist, and when you rotate your wrist clockwise, the child instantly rotates the block. The wrist motion is a remote *command*. This is watch interaction — the robot infers intent from wrist kinematics or bio-signals without any physical contact between the user and the robot. > [!NOTE] > **Core Distinction:** *Touch* = **contact-mediated**, **closed-loop feedback**. *Watch* = **non-contact (often)**, **open-loop command** through wearable inertial/EMG sensors. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield) - **Touch Resolution:** Number of independent sensing cells per unit area, typically **16–64 taxels/cm²** for high-resolution tactile skins. - **Force Sensitivity Threshold:** Minimum detectable normal force, typically **0.01 N to 0.1 N** for capacitive tactile sensors. - **Sampling Rate of Wearable IMU:** Standard **50 Hz to 200 Hz** for gesture classification. - **Gesture Recognition Latency:** **< 100 ms** is considered real-time for HRI under KTU industrial usability benchmarks. - **Degrees of Freedom (DoF) of a Wrist:** Anatomically **2 DoF** (flexion/extension, radial/ulnar deviation) + **pronation/supination** ≈ 3 DoF usable for gesture control. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Touch Interaction — Operational Stack Touch interaction in service robotics is decomposed into **four functional layers**: #### Layer 1: Tactile Sensing (Transduction) Converts physical contact into electrical signals. - **Capacitive Sensors:** Detect change in capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ when a finger or tool alters the electrode gap $d$ or dielectric $\varepsilon_r$. - **Piezoelectric Sensors:** Generate charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ proportional to applied force $F$ via the piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33}$ (units: pC/N). - **Resistive (FSR – Force Sensitive Resistor):** Resistance $R \propto \dfrac{1}{F}$. #### Layer 2: Signal Processing Raw signals are conditioned via: - **Low-pass filtering** to remove mechanical vibration noise. - **Normalization:** $x_{norm} = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ mapping sensor output to $[0, 1]$. - **Feature extraction:** Pressure centroid, contact area, force vector magnitude. #### Layer 3: Intent Inference (Classification) The processed tactile vector is mapped to a robot action — typically via: - **Threshold-based triggers** (light tap = stop, firm press = continue). - **Machine Learning classifiers** (SVM, Random Forest, CNN over tactile image arrays). #### Layer 4: Haptic Feedback (Closed Loop) The robot transmits force/thermal cues back to the user via: - **Vibrotactile actuators** (LRA / ERM motors, 100–250 Hz). - **Force-feedback exoskeletons** applying counter-torque. ### 2.2 Watch Interaction — Operational Stack A wrist-worn module performs **gesture recognition** through the following pipeline: 1. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) Capture:** Accelerometer ($a_x, a_y, a_z$), gyroscope ($\omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z$), magnetometer. 2. **Bio-signal Capture (Optional):** Surface Electromyography (sEMG) reading muscle electrical activity at $\mu V$ scale. 3. **Segmentation:** A gesture is windowed — typically **40 ms to 200 ms sliding windows** with 50% overlap. 4. **Classification:** Gesture $g_i$ is selected from a learned set $\mathcal{G} = \{g_1, g_2, \dots, g_n\}$ via maximum likelihood. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Capacitance (touch) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Farads (F) | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | | 2 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Coulombs (C) | $d_{33}$ in pC/N | | 3 | FSR conductance | $G = k \cdot F$ | Siemens (S) | $k$ is sensor constant | | 4 | Normalized signal | $x_{norm} = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | dimensionless | Range $[0, 1]$ | | 5 | Force vector magnitude | $\vert \mathbf{F} \vert = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | Newtons (N) | Used in 3-axis tactile arrays | | 6 | IMU orientation (quaternion update) | $q_{t+1} = q_t + \dfrac{1}{2} \Omega(\omega) q_t \, \Delta t$ | unit quaternion | Madgwick / Mahony fusion | | 7 | Gyroscope angular displacement | $\theta = \int_{0}^{t} \omega(\tau) \, d\tau$ | radians (rad) | Drift accumulates | | 8 | sEMG RMS envelope | $EMG_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | $\mu$V | Gesture activation indicator | | 9 | Bayes gesture posterior | $P(g \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid g) P(g)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | dimensionless | MAP classification | | 10 | Reaction time bound | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{infer} + t_{actuate}$ | seconds (s) | KTU: $< 100$ ms for real-time | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Table Convention:** Wherever absolute value or magnitude appears, **never** use literal `|` characters inside the table — they are written as `\vert` or explicit squared-root form to preserve Markdown table syntax. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci Surgical System):** Force-sensing instruments give surgeons tactile feel during minimally invasive surgery. - **Domestic Service Robots (iRobot, Pepper):** Capacitive skin prevents collision injuries during human-robot coexistence. - **Disaster Response Field Robots:** Operators wearing smartwatches can teleoperate search-and-rescue drones without freeing both hands. - **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Touch-based therapy robots guide stroke patients through kinesthetic feedback exercises. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Centroid on a Tactile Array A tactile sensor matrix has $M \times N$ taxels. Each taxel $(i, j)$ reports a normalized pressure $p_{i,j} \in [0, 1]$. **Step 1 — Total applied force (digital integration):** $$ F_{total} = k \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} p_{i,j} $$ where $k$ is the per-taxel force calibration constant (N/taxel). **Step 2 — Compute first moments (weighted centroid coordinates):** $$ x_{c} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} j \cdot p_{i,j}}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} p_{i,j}} $$ $$ y_{c} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} i \cdot p_{i,j}}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} p_{i,j}} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** The point $(x_c, y_c)$ represents the *center of pressure* (CoP), analogous to the biomechanical CoP used in human postural studies. The robot uses $(x_c, y_c)$ to determine **where** the user is touching and infers an intent like "move in this direction." **Step 4 — Velocity-style rate (optional):** $$ \mathbf{v}_{CoP} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\begin{pmatrix} x_c \\ y_c \end{pmatrix} $$ This drift velocity of the contact point can drive a *proportional joystick-like* robot motion: faster swipe = faster robot motion. ### 3.2 Derivation: Watch-Based Gesture Classification Using MAP Rule Given an observation window of IMU data $\mathbf{x} = (a_x, a_y, a_z, \omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z)$, the gesture $\hat{g}$ is selected by **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)**: $$ \hat{g} = \arg\max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} P(g \mid \mathbf{x}) $$ **Step 1 — Apply Bayes' theorem:** $$ \hat{g} = \arg\max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid g) P(g)}{P(\mathbf{x})} $$ **Step 2 — Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $g$, drop it:** $$ \hat{g} = \arg\max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} P(\mathbf{x} \mid g) P(g) $$ **Step 3 — Assume Gaussian likelihood per class:** $$ P(\mathbf{x} \mid g) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert \Sigma_g \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g)^{\top} \Sigma_g^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g)\right) $$ where $d = 6$ (six IMU channels), $\boldsymbol{\mu}_g$ is the class mean, $\Sigma_g$ is the covariance. **Step 4 — Take log-likelihood for numerical stability:** $$ \hat{g} = \arg\max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} \left[-\dfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g)^{\top} \Sigma_g^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g) - \dfrac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_g\vert + \ln P(g)\right] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Mahalanobis distance $(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g)^{\top} \Sigma_g^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_g)$ generalizes Euclidean distance by accounting for sensor noise correlations — critical because accelerometer and gyroscope axes share mechanical coupling. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Touch vs Watch Comparison | Parameter | Touch Interaction | Watch Interaction | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | Contact required | **Yes** | **No (often)** | | Primary sensor | Tactile / force array | IMU + sEMG | | Range | Robot's body surface | Wearer's wrist | | Bandwidth | High (kHz tactile) | Medium (≤ 200 Hz IMU) | | Feedback to user | Direct haptic | Vibration / visual | | Latency budget | < 30 ms | < 100 ms | | Best for | Precision tasks | Hands-free commands | | Failure mode | Sensor saturation | Gesture misclassification | | Energy budget | Passive possible | Active (battery) | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example A $4 \times 4$ tactile array returns normalized pressure matrix: $$ P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.0 \\ 0.2 & 0.6 & 0.4 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.5 & 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.1 & 0.3 & 0.1 \end{pmatrix} $$ With calibration $k = 0.5$ N/taxel. **Step 1 — Total force:** $$ F_{total} = 0.5 \times \sum P = 0.5 \times 3.9 = 1.95 \;\text{N} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\sum j \cdot p_{i,j}$ row-wise:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Row 1:} & \quad 1(0.1)+2(0.2)+3(0.3)+4(0.0) = 1.4 \\ \text{Row 2:} & \quad 1(0.2)+2(0.6)+3(0.4)+4(0.1) = 3.0 \\ \text{Row 3:} & \quad 1(0.1)+2(0.5)+3(0.7)+4(0.2) = 3.8 \\ \text{Row 4:} & \quad 1(0.0)+2(0.1)+3(0.3)+4(0.1) = 1.5 \\ \text{Total } \Sigma j \cdot p: & \quad 9.7 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Centroid x-coordinate:** $$ x_c = \dfrac{9.7}{3.9} \approx 2.487 $$ **Step 4 — Repeat for y (by symmetry of calculation):** $$ y_c \approx 2.41 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The center of pressure lies slightly right of array center — the robot interprets this as a directional nudge toward the positive X axis, generating a small lateral velocity command. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Touch Interaction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Human User] --> B[Robot Skin / Tactile Array] B --> C[Signal Conditioning] C --> D[Feature Extraction] D --> E[Gesture / Intent Classifier] E --> F[Robot Controller] F --> G[Robot Actuator] G --> H[Physical Motion] H -->|Haptic Feedback| A E -->|Vibration Alert| I[Wearable Watch Module] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Watch Interaction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD U[User Wrist Motion] --> S1[IMU Accelerometer] U --> S2[IMU Gyroscope] U --> S3[sEMG Sensor Optional] S1 --> F[Sensor Fusion Filter] S2 --> F S3 --> F F --> W[Sliding Window Buffer] W --> CL[Gesture Classifier MAP] CL --> TX[Wireless Transmitter BLE] TX --> RX[Robot Receiver] RX --> RC[Robot Command Decoder] RC --> RA[Robot Actuator] RA --> RV[Robot Visual Feedback] RV --> U ``` ### 4.3 Combined HRI Multimodal Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HUMAN[Human Operator Layer] H1[Intent] H2[Touch Action] H3[Wrist Gesture] end subgraph SENSING[Sensing Layer] T1[Tactile Array] T2[IMU on Wrist] T3[sEMG Band] end subgraph PROCESSING[Processing Layer] P1[Feature Vector] P2[Classifier CNN SVM] P3[Fusion Module] end subgraph ROBOT[Robot Layer] R1[Motion Planner] R2[Servo Driver] R3[Haptic Actuator] end H2 --> T1 H3 --> T2 H3 --> T3 T1 --> P1 T2 --> P1 T3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R3 --> H1 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flow — When Touch vs Watch is Activated ```mermaid flowchart TD ST[Start HRI Session] --> Q1{User Hands
Free?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Task Requires
High Precision?} Q1 -->|No| WT[Activate Watch Interaction] Q2 -->|Yes| TC[Activate Touch Interaction] Q2 -->|No| HM[Hybrid Mode Both Active] WT --> END[Stream Commands to Robot] TC --> END HM --> END ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define Touch Interaction in Human-Robot Interaction. List any two tactile sensor technologies used in service robots.** **Model Answer:** Touch Interaction is a mode of physical Human-Robot Interaction (pHRI) in which the user communicates intent to a robot through **direct physical contact**, with the robot transducing mechanical stimuli (force, pressure, vibration, temperature) into actionable commands and providing haptic feedback back to the user. Two tactile sensor technologies: 1. **Capacitive tactile sensors** — detect change in capacitance $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ caused by finger/proximity altering the electrode gap. 2. **Piezoelectric sensors** — generate charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ proportional to applied force, useful for dynamic contact detection. *(Other acceptable answers: FSR – Force Sensitive Resistor, MEMS barometer-based skin, optical tactile sensors like GelSight.)* --- #### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is Watch Interaction? Mention two advantages of using a smartwatch-based interface for service robot teleoperation.** **Model Answer:** Watch Interaction is a wearable-mediated HRI modality where the human operator uses a wrist-mounted device (smartwatch, IMU band, or EMG bracelet) to issue commands to a robot through **gestures, motion patterns, or bio-signals**, without requiring direct contact with the robot's body. Two advantages: 1. **Hands-free operation** — critical when the user is already carrying tools or assisting a patient, e.g., a field rescue worker teleoperating a drone. 2. **Mobility and range** — the user can control the robot from a safe distance, up to **10–30 m with BLE 5.0**, enabling exploration of hazardous environments. *(Other acceptable: low cognitive load, discreet interaction, multi-modal fusion with voice.)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (1) skipping the *closed-loop* aspect of touch interaction, (2) confusing *capacitive* with *resistive* principles, (3) forgetting units in numerical problems, and (4) omitting the *Mahalanobis* distance when explaining gesture classifiers. Always explicitly write units and class assumptions. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice A]** **(a)** Explain the operational pipeline of a **touch-based interaction system** in a service robot, with reference to capacitive and piezoelectric sensing. Discuss how the **center of pressure (CoP)** is computed and used for robot navigation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **MAP-based gesture classification rule** for a watch-based interaction system using a 6-channel IMU. State clearly the role of the covariance matrix $\Sigma_g$ and the Mahalanobis distance. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. **Pipeline (4 marks):** Present the four-layer stack — *Transduction → Signal Conditioning → Feature Extraction → Intent Inference → Haptic Feedback*. Specify that capacitive sensors measure change in $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ while piezoelectric sensors output $Q = d_{33} F$. Both feed into an ADC and a microcontroller. 2. **CoP computation (2 marks):** Use the summation formulas for $x_c$ and $y_c$ as derived in Section 3.1, with explicit summation limits and the calibration constant $k$. 3. **Navigation utility (1 mark):** Explain that drift velocity $\mathbf{v}_{CoP}$ of the CoP can drive a *proportional robot velocity command*, enabling "swipe-to-steer" interfaces on robot skin. #### Model Solution — Part (b) 1. **MAP derivation (4 marks):** Begin with $\hat{g} = \arg\max P(g \mid \mathbf{x})$, apply Bayes' theorem, drop the denominator, then substitute the multivariate Gaussian likelihood. 2. **Role of $\Sigma_g$ (2 marks):** Explain that $\Sigma_g$ encodes per-class sensor noise and inter-axis coupling. A larger $\Sigma_g$ implies a more "spread-out" gesture cluster in feature space, reducing over-confidence. 3. **Mahalanobis distance (1 mark):** Emphasize that $D_M^2 = (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_g)^{\top} \Sigma_g^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_g)$ is the natural distance metric under Gaussian noise — *Euclidean distance is only a special case when $\Sigma_g = \sigma^2 I$.* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Internal Choice B]** **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe a **smartwatch-based gesture interface** for controlling a field robot. List any four gestures and their mapped robot actions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A $3 \times 3$ tactile array reports: $$ P = \begin{pmatrix} 0.0 & 0.2 & 0.0 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.0 & 0.3 & 0.0 \end{pmatrix} $$ With calibration $k = 0.4$ N/taxel, compute the **total force** and the **center of pressure** $(x_c, y_c)$. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Show the chain — *Wrist → IMU → Sensor Fusion → Sliding Window → Classifier → BLE → Robot → Servo*. Use a Mermaid or labelled block diagram. **Four Gestures & Actions (4 marks):** | Gesture | Robot Action | |---------|--------------| | Single flick right | Move forward | | Double tap (wrist) | Stop all motion | | Clockwise rotation | Rotate base clockwise | | Pronate-supinate | Open/close gripper | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Total normalized sum:** $$ S = 0.0+0.2+0.0+0.1+0.9+0.1+0.0+0.3+0.0 = 1.6 $$ **Step 2 — Total force:** $$ F_{total} = 0.4 \times 1.6 = 0.64 \;\text{N} $$ **Step 3 — Compute weighted sums:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum j \cdot p & = 1(0.0)+2(0.2)+3(0.0)+1(0.1)+2(0.9)+3(0.1)+1(0.0)+2(0.3)+3(0.0) \\ & = 0.0 + 0.4 + 0.0 + 0.1 + 1.8 + 0.3 + 0.0 + 0.6 + 0.0 = 3.2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum i \cdot p & = 1(0.0)+1(0.2)+1(0.0)+2(0.1)+2(0.9)+2(0.1)+3(0.0)+3(0.3)+3(0.0) \\ & = 0.0+0.2+0.0+0.2+1.8+0.2+0.0+0.9+0.0 = 3.3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Centroid:** $$ x_c = \dfrac{3.2}{1.6} = 2.0, \qquad y_c = \dfrac{3.3}{1.6} \approx 2.0625 $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** The contact is centred on the middle taxel $(2,2)$ with mass $0.9$ (peak pressure). Centroid is **at the geometric centre**, indicating a *firm central press*, not a swipe — the robot would interpret this as a "confirm" or "hold position" command rather than directional motion. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Touch Interaction** is **contact-mediated**, **closed-loop**, and uses tactile/force sensors (capacitive, piezoelectric, FSR). - **Watch Interaction** is **wearable-mediated**, **often non-contact**, using IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope ± magnetometer) and optional **sEMG**. - The **center of pressure (CoP)** $x_c = \dfrac{\sum j p_{i,j}}{\sum p_{i,j}}$, $y_c = \dfrac{\sum i p_{i,j}}{\sum p_{i,j}}$ is the spatial indicator of user intent on a tactile array. - **MAP rule** governs gesture classification: $\hat{g} = \arg\max_g P(\mathbf{x} \mid g) P(g)$ — implemented via **Mahalanobis distance** under Gaussian assumption. - **Latency budget:** Touch < 30 ms (closed-loop); Watch < 100 ms (open-loop command). - **Wrist biomechanics:** ~3 DoF available for gesture encoding (flexion/extension, deviation, pronation/supination). - **Standard tactile resolutions:** 16–64 taxels/cm² for high-fidelity service robot skins. - **Failure modes to mention in exams:** Sensor saturation (touch), gesture confusion / class imbalance (watch), IMU drift, occlusion. - **Always state units** (N, F, Hz, $\mu$V, s) — KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for units. - **Multimodal fusion** (touch + watch + vision) is the **current research trend** and a frequent 14-mark question theme. ---

topics.title: Speech Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speech Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speech Interaction** in the context of Field and Service Robotics refers to the bidirectional auditory communication modality between a human operator and an autonomous robotic agent. It is realized as a tightly coupled loop of two complementary sub-systems: **Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)**, which transcribes spoken human language into machine-readable symbolic representations, and **Text-to-Speech Synthesis (TTS)**, which converts internal symbolic responses into natural-sounding spoken utterances.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Speech Interaction is classified under **Human-Robot Interaction (HRI)** modalities and is considered a *high-bandwidth*, *hands-free*, *eyes-free* interaction channel — critical for service robots deployed in domestic, healthcare, hospitality, and field environments where touchscreens and joysticks are impractical.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bilingual Waiter

Imagine a robotic waiter in a noisy Kerala restaurant. A customer speaks in Malayalam or English, and the robot must:
1. **Hear** the sound waves through its microphones (just like your ears).
2. **Filter** out the clatter of plates, fans, and other voices (like your brain's "cocktail party effect").
3. **Recognize** the words from the sound (like translating sound to meaning).
4. **Understand** the intent — "give me one masala dosa" (like comprehending meaning).
5. **Decide** what to do — fetch the dosa (like planning a response).
6. **Speak back** — "One masala dosa, coming right up!" (like voicing a reply).

This entire loop is **Speech Interaction**, and the robotic waiter is performing the same pipeline that your smartphone's Google Assistant or Alexa performs.

### 1.3 The Two Halves of Speech Interaction

| Sub-system | Direction | Input | Output | KTU Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASR** (Automatic Speech Recognition) | Human → Robot | Acoustic waveform $x(t)$ | Text transcript $W = \{w_1, w_2, ..., w_n\}$ | **WER** (Word Error Rate) |
| **TTS** (Text-to-Speech Synthesis) | Robot → Human | Text response $R$ | Acoustic waveform $y(t)$ | **MOS** (Mean Opinion Score, 1–5) |
| **Speaker ID / Verification** | Biometric | Voice sample | Identity claim / rejection | **EER** (Equal Error Rate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Speech Interaction is *not* just speech recognition. It is a complete **perception–cognition–action** loop. Examiners frequently test whether students understand the *bidirectional* nature and the role of **dialogue management** in the middle.

### 1.4 Why Speech Interaction Matters for Field & Service Robots

- **Hands-busy operations**: A surgeon needs to command a surgical robot verbally while holding instruments.
- **Visually-impaired users**: Guide robots for the blind rely almost exclusively on speech.
- **Noisy field environments**: Search-and-rescue robots in disaster zones must understand shouting operators over rubble noise.
- **Social acceptance**: A robot that *speaks naturally* is perceived as more intelligent and trustworthy (the **uncanny valley of voice**).
- **Multi-modal redundancy**: Speech acts as a fallback when vision is occluded (smoke, dust, darkness).

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Acoustic Metrics

The following standards are universally used in speech engineering and frequently appear in KTU numerical problems:

- **Speed of sound in air** at 20°C: $c = 343 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard sampling rate** for wideband speech: $f_s = 16 \text{ kHz}$ (telephony uses 8 kHz)
- **Nyquist frequency** for 16 kHz: $f_N = 8 \text{ kHz}$
- **Typical frame length**: 25 ms with 10 ms hop
- **Standard MFCC count**: 12–13 coefficients + delta + delta-delta = 39-dimensional feature vector
- **Reference SNR for clean speech**: $\text{SNR} \geq 30 \text{ dB}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Speech Interaction Pipeline (End-to-End)

The complete Speech Interaction loop in a service robot follows a 10-stage pipeline:

1. **Acoustic Capture** — Microphone array (often 2–8 mics) records sound pressure $p(t)$.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion** — Sampling at $f_s = 16$ kHz, quantization to 16-bit PCM.
3. **Pre-processing** — Pre-emphasis filtering, DC removal, silence detection via energy threshold.
4. **Beamforming / Source Separation** — Spatial filtering to isolate the target speaker using **Delay-and-Sum** or **MVDR** beamformers.
5. **Feature Extraction** — Conversion from time-domain waveform to a compact spectral representation (**MFCC**, **FBANK**, or **wav2vec embeddings**).
6. **Acoustic Modeling** — Mapping acoustic features to phonetic units using **HMM**, **DNN-HMM**, or end-to-end **Transformer/CTC** models.
7. **Language Modeling** — Estimating probability of word sequences using **n-gram** or **neural LMs**.
8. **Decoding** — Searching the best word sequence using **Viterbi** algorithm or **beam search**.
9. **Natural Language Understanding (NLU)** — Intent classification + entity extraction (often BERT-based).
10. **Dialogue Management + Response Generation** — State tracking, action selection.
11. **Text-to-Speech (TTS)** — Synthesizing the robot's spoken reply.
12. **Post-processing & Playback** — Volume normalization, speaker output.

### 2.2 The Bayes' Theorem Foundation of ASR

The mathematical heart of every ASR system is Bayes' theorem. Given an observed acoustic feature sequence $X = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_T\}$, we want to find the most probable word sequence $W^*$:

$$W^* = \arg\max_{W} P(W \mid X)$$

Applying Bayes' rule:

$$W^* = \arg\max_{W} \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$$

Since $P(X)$ is independent of $W$, it can be dropped:

$$W^* = \arg\max_{W} P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$$

The two terms correspond directly to the two main components:

| Term | Meaning | Model Type |
|---|---|---|
| $P(X \mid W)$ | Acoustic Model — how likely are these sounds given the words? | HMM, DNN-HMM, CTC |
| $P(W)$ | Language Model — how likely is this word sequence in the language? | n-gram, RNN, Transformer |

### 2.3 Hidden Markov Model (HMM) for Acoustic Modeling

An HMM models speech as a doubly-stochastic process:
- **Hidden states** $S = \{s_1, ..., s_N\}$ represent phonemes or sub-phonetic units (tied-states, senones).
- **Observations** $O = \{o_1, ..., o_T\}$ are acoustic feature vectors (e.g., 39-dim MFCCs).
- Each state emits an observation with probability $b_j(o_t)$ (the *emission probability*, now modeled by a DNN).
- Transitions between states follow probabilities $a_{ij}$.

An HMM is fully specified by the triplet $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$:
- $A = \{a_{ij}\}$ — state transition probability matrix
- $B = \{b_j(o)\}$ — observation probability distribution
- $\pi = \{\pi_i\}$ — initial state distribution

**Three fundamental HMM problems:**
1. **Evaluation** — $P(O \mid \lambda)$? Solved by the **Forward algorithm**.
2. **Decoding** — Best state sequence? Solved by the **Viterbi algorithm**.
3. **Training** — Adjust $\lambda$ to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$? Solved by the **Baum-Welch (Forward-Backward)** algorithm.

### 2.4 The Forward Algorithm (Key Derivation)

The forward variable $\alpha_t(i)$ = probability of being in state $i$ at time $t$ after observing $o_1, o_2, ..., o_t$:

$$\alpha_t(i) = P(o_1, o_2, ..., o_t, q_t = s_i \mid \lambda)$$

**Recursion:**

$$\alpha_1(i) = \pi_i \, b_i(o_1)$$

$$\alpha_t(j) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij} \right] b_j(o_t)$$

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_T(i)$$

### 2.5 MFCC — The Canonical Feature Extractor

**MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients)** are the de-facto standard features for ASR. The pipeline has 7 stages:

1. **Pre-emphasis**: $y[n] = x[n] - \alpha \cdot x[n-1]$, typically $\alpha = 0.97$
2. **Framing**: Split signal into 25 ms frames with 10 ms shift
3. **Windowing**: Multiply each frame by Hamming window $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$
4. **FFT**: Convert each frame to magnitude spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert$
5. **Mel filterbank**: Apply triangular filters spaced on the **Mel scale**:
   $$m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$
6. **Log compression**: Take log of filterbank energies (mimics human loudness perception)
7. **DCT**: Discrete Cosine Transform to decorrelate → 12–13 MFCCs + log energy = 13 dims
8. **Dynamic features**: Add delta ($\Delta$) and delta-delta ($\Delta\Delta$) → final 39-dim vector

### 2.6 Text-to-Speech (TTS) Synthesis Techniques

| Technique | Principle | Quality (MOS) | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formant Synthesis** | Rule-based, models vocal tract resonances | 2.5–3.5 | Very low |
| **Concatenative (Unit Selection)** | Stitches pre-recorded diphone/phone units | 3.8–4.2 | Low |
| **Parametric (HTS, HMM-based)** | Statistical parametric synthesis | 3.5–4.0 | Low |
| **Neural (Tacotron 2 + WaveNet/HiFi-GAN)** | End-to-end deep learning, mel-spectrogram → waveform | **4.5–4.7** | Higher |

Modern service robots (e.g., SoftBank Pepper, Temi) use **Neural TTS** for naturalness, often leveraging cloud APIs (Google WaveNet, Amazon Polly).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W^* = \arg\max_W P(X\mid W) P(W)$ | Bayes-optimal ASR decoding | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{WER} = \dfrac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$ | Word Error Rate | % |
| 3 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 4 | $m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$ | Hz → Mel scale | mels |
| 5 | $f = 700 \left(10^{m/2595} - 1\right)$ | Mel → Hz scale | Hz |
| 6 | $\alpha_t(j) = \left[\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(o_t)$ | HMM Forward recursion | probability |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{d \sin\theta}{c}$ | Time Delay of Arrival for beamforming | seconds |
| 8 | $y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \, x[n-1]$ | Pre-emphasis filter | sample index |
| 9 | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_i \alpha_T(i)$ | Total observation likelihood | probability |
| 10 | $\text{EER} = \text{FRR} = \text{FAR}$ | Speaker verification operating point | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Many students confuse **Word Accuracy** with **Word Error Rate**. The correct relation is:
> $$\text{Word Accuracy} = 100\% - \text{WER}$$
> WER can exceed 100% if there are many insertions — this is valid!

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare Robots (e.g., Moxi, Tug)**: Voice command for supply delivery in hospitals where staff wear gloves.
- **Domestic Robots (e.g., Temi, Amazon Astro)**: Wake-word detection ("Alexa", "Hey Temi") → ASR pipeline.
- **Agricultural Field Robots**: Voice-controlled drone piloting in large farms.
- **Assistive Robots for Elderly**: Reminders, medication alerts via TTS in local languages (Malayalam, Hindi).
- **Industrial Cobots**: Voice programming for repetitive tasks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — WER Calculation (Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A service robot's ASR system produces the following output for the reference sentence *"bring me a glass of water please"* (7 words). The hypothesis (recognized) sentence is *"bring me glass water please"* (5 words). Calculate the WER using the edit distance (Levenshtein) alignment.

**Step 1 — Identify the three edit operations needed to align Hypothesis to Reference:**

Comparing reference and hypothesis word-by-word:

| Position | Reference | Hypothesis | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | bring | bring | Match ✓ |
| 2 | me | me | Match ✓ |
| 3 | a | (missing) | **Deletion** (D) |
| 4 | glass | glass | Match ✓ |
| 5 | of | (missing) | **Deletion** (D) |
| 6 | water | water | Match ✓ |
| 7 | please | please | Match ✓ |

**Step 2 — Count the three error types:**

$$S = 0 \quad (\text{no word substituted})$$

$$D = 2 \quad (\text{"a" and "of" were deleted in hypothesis})$$

$$I = 0 \quad (\text{no extra word inserted})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the WER formula:**

$$\text{WER} = \frac{S + D + I}{N} \times 100\%$$

where $N$ = number of words in reference = 7.

$$\text{WER} = \frac{0 + 2 + 0}{7} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{7} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{WER} \approx 28.57\%$$

**Step 4 — Word Accuracy:**

$$\text{WAcc} = 100\% - 28.57\% = 71.43\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A WER of ~29% is **unacceptable** for a deployed service robot (industry standard: WER < 5% for clean speech, < 15% for noisy field conditions). This indicates the acoustic model is failing on short function words like "a" and "of" — a classic problem requiring better language model training data or a sub-word tokenizer.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Bayes' Decision Rule for ASR

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Goal:** Show that minimizing WER is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the posterior $P(W \mid X)$.

Let $X$ be the acoustic observation and $W$ be the true word sequence. The expected loss (risk) of choosing $\hat{W}$ is:

$$R(\hat{W} \mid X) = \sum_W L(\hat{W}, W) \, P(W \mid X)$$

For the **0–1 loss function** ($L = 0$ if $\hat{W} = W$, else $L = 1$):

$$R(\hat{W} \mid X) = 1 - P(\hat{W} \mid X)$$

Minimizing the risk is therefore equivalent to maximizing the posterior:

$$\hat{W}^* = \arg\min_{\hat{W}} R(\hat{W} \mid X) = \arg\max_{\hat{W}} P(\hat{W} \mid X)$$

Substituting Bayes' theorem:

$$\hat{W}^* = \arg\max_{\hat{W}} \frac{P(X \mid \hat{W}) \, P(\hat{W})}{P(X)}$$

Since $P(X)$ is constant for a given utterance, we drop it:

$$\hat{W}^* = \arg\max_{\hat{W}} \underbrace{P(X \mid \hat{W})}_{\text{Acoustic Model}} \cdot \underbrace{P(\hat{W})}_{\text{Language Model}}$$

This is the **Fundamental Equation of Statistical Speech Recognition** — every KTU examiner expects students to be able to derive and interpret this.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Mel Scale Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Convert 1000 Hz to Mel scale, and back to verify.

**Forward conversion (Hz → Mel):**

$$m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

Substituting $f = 1000$ Hz:

$$m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{1000}{700}\right)$$

$$m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + 1.4286\right)$$

$$m = 2595 \log_{10}(2.4286)$$

$$m = 2595 \times 0.3854$$

$$m \approx 999.99 \text{ mels} \approx 1000 \text{ mels}$$

*(This is by design — 1000 Hz corresponds approximately to 1000 mels.)*

**Inverse conversion (Mel → Hz):**

$$f = 700 \left(10^{m/2595} - 1\right)$$

Substituting $m = 1000$ mels:

$$f = 700 \left(10^{1000/2595} - 1\right)$$

$$f = 700 \left(10^{0.3854} - 1\right)$$

$$f = 700 \left(2.4286 - 1\right)$$

$$f = 700 \times 1.4286$$

$$f \approx 1000 \text{ Hz} \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation — HMM Forward Algorithm (Toy Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Toy Problem:** 2-state HMM, 3 time steps. Compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ where $O = (o_1, o_2, o_3)$.
> Given: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$, $B = \begin{pmatrix} b_1(o_1)=0.5, b_1(o_2)=0.4, b_1(o_3)=0.1 \\ b_2(o_1)=0.1, b_2(o_2)=0.3, b_2(o_3)=0.6 \end{pmatrix}$, $\pi = (0.6, 0.4)$.

**Initialization (t=1):**

$$\alpha_1(1) = \pi_1 \cdot b_1(o_1) = 0.6 \times 0.5 = 0.30$$

$$\alpha_1(2) = \pi_2 \cdot b_2(o_1) = 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.04$$

**Recursion (t=2):**

$$\alpha_2(1) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{11} + \alpha_1(2) a_{21}] \, b_1(o_2)$$

$$\alpha_2(1) = [0.30 \times 0.7 + 0.04 \times 0.4] \times 0.4$$

$$\alpha_2(1) = [0.210 + 0.016] \times 0.4 = 0.226 \times 0.4 = 0.0904$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = [\alpha_1(1) a_{12} + \alpha_1(2) a_{22}] \, b_2(o_2)$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = [0.30 \times 0.3 + 0.04 \times 0.6] \times 0.3$$

$$\alpha_2(2) = [0.090 + 0.024] \times 0.3 = 0.114 \times 0.3 = 0.0342$$

**Recursion (t=3):**

$$\alpha_3(1) = [\alpha_2(1) a_{11} + \alpha_2(2) a_{21}] \, b_1(o_3)$$

$$\alpha_3(1) = [0.0904 \times 0.7 + 0.0342 \times 0.4] \times 0.1$$

$$\alpha_3(1) = [0.06328 + 0.01368] \times 0.1 = 0.07696 \times 0.1 = 0.007696$$

$$\alpha_3(2) = [\alpha_2(1) a_{12} + \alpha_2(2) a_{22}] \, b_2(o_3)$$

$$\alpha_3(2) = [0.0904 \times 0.3 + 0.0342 \times 0.6] \times 0.6$$

$$\alpha_3(2) = [0.02712 + 0.02052] \times 0.6 = 0.04764 \times 0.6 = 0.028584$$

**Termination:**

$$P(O \mid \lambda) = \alpha_3(1) + \alpha_3(2) = 0.007696 + 0.028584 = 0.03628$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ASR Techniques Comparison

| Technique | Acoustic Model | LM | Strengths | Weaknesses | WER (TIMIT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GMM-HMM | Gaussian Mixture | n-gram | Fast, interpretable | Limited accuracy | ~25% |
| DNN-HMM | Deep Neural Network | n-gram | Big jump in accuracy | Hybrid complexity | ~18% |
| End-to-End CTC | BiLSTM/Transformer | External or implicit | Simpler pipeline | Independence assumption | ~14% |
| Attention Seq2Seq | Encoder-Decoder + Attention | Implicit | Best single-model accuracy | Slow training | ~12% |
| Whisper (Modern) | Transformer Encoder-Decoder | Built-in | Multilingual, robust | Large model size | ~6% (English) |
| wav2vec 2.0 | Self-supervised Transformer | Fine-tuned | Low-resource friendly | Needs pretraining | ~5–8% |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Beamforming Algorithms

| Beamformer | Principle | Computational Cost | Robustness to Noise |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delay-and-Sum | Align delays, average | $O(M)$ per frequency bin | Low |
| MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response) | Minimize noise, preserve target | $O(M^3)$ | High |
| GSC (Generalized Sidelobe Canceller) | Adaptive, MVDR variant | Medium | Medium-High |
| MVDR + Post-filter | MVDR + spectral subtraction | High | Very High |

where $M$ = number of microphones.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Speech Interaction Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH[Human Speaker] --> nodeM1[Mic 1]
    nodeM2[Mic 2] --> nodeBF
    nodeM3[Mic 3] --> nodeBF
    nodeM1 --> nodeBF[Beamformer Array]
    nodeBF --> nodePP[Pre-processing: Pre-emphasis + VAD]
    nodePP --> nodeFE[Feature Extraction: MFCC or wav2vec]
    nodeFE --> nodeAM[Acoustic Model DNN-HMM or CTC]
    nodeLM[Language Model n-gram or Transformer] --> nodeDEC
    nodeAM --> nodeDEC[Decoder Viterbi or Beam Search]
    nodeDEC --> nodeNLU[Natural Language Understanding BERT]
    nodeNLU --> nodeDM[Dialogue Manager State Tracker]
    nodeDM --> nodeACT[Action Planner]
    nodeACT --> nodeTTS[TTS Synthesizer Neural or Concatenative]
    nodeTTS --> nodeSP[Robot Speaker Output]
    nodeSP --> nodeH
```

### 4.2 Detailed ASR Sub-System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AcousticFrontEnd[Acoustic Front End]
        sigA[Raw Waveform 16 kHz] --> sigB[Pre-emphasis Filter]
        sigB --> sigC[Framing 25 ms / 10 ms hop]
        sigC --> sigD[Hamming Window]
        sigD --> sigE[FFT 512 point]
        sigE --> sigF[Mel Filterbank 40 filters]
        sigF --> sigG[Log Compression]
        sigG --> sigH[DCT]
        sigH --> sigI[MFCC 13 dim + Delta + Delta-Delta = 39 dim]
    end
    sigI --> acousticModel{Acoustic Model}
    acousticModel --> decoderCore
    lmModel{Language Model} --> decoderCore
    decoderCore[Decoder Viterbi Beam Search] --> textOut[Word Sequence W]
    textOut --> nluStage[NLU Intent + Entity]
```

### 4.3 HMM State Topology for a Word

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart([Start]) --> nodeS1[State 1: Beginning phoneme]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[State 2: Middle phoneme]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[State 3: End phoneme]
    nodeS3 --> nodeEnd([End])
    nodeS1 -.->|self-loop a_ii| nodeS1
    nodeS2 -.->|self-loop a_jj| nodeS2
    nodeS3 -.->|self-loop a_kk| nodeS3
```

### 4.4 Dialogue State Machine for a Service Robot

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Listening: Wake word detected
    Listening --> Processing: End of speech detected
    Processing --> Understanding: ASR complete
    Understanding --> Responding: Intent classified
    Responding --> Acting: TTS + Action triggered
    Acting --> Idle: Action complete
    Listening --> Listening: Re-prompt on no input
    Processing --> Listening: Low confidence fallback
```

### 4.5 TTS Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeText[Input Text Response] --> nodeTP[Text Processing Tokenization + Normalization]
    nodeTP --> nodePA[Phoneme Analyzer]
    nodePA --> nodePM[Prosody Model Duration + F0 + Energy]
    nodePM --> nodeAC[Acoustic Model Tacotron 2 / FastSpeech 2]
    nodeAC --> nodeMS[Mel Spectrogram]
    nodeMS --> nodeVC[Vocoder WaveNet / HiFi-GAN]
    nodeVC --> nodeWAV[Output Waveform]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and explain the role of acoustic and language models in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) is the process by which a machine converts an acoustic speech signal $X$ into the corresponding sequence of words $W$ that the speaker uttered.

**Mathematical formulation (1 mark):** Using Bayes' theorem, the optimal word sequence is:

$$W^* = \arg\max_W P(W \mid X) = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$$

**Role of components (1 mark):**
- **Acoustic Model** $P(X \mid W)$ — captures the relationship between the audio signal and phonetic units. Modern implementations use DNN-HMM hybrids or end-to-end Transformers (e.g., Whisper).
- **Language Model** $P(W)$ — assigns probability to word sequences based on linguistic structure, ensuring the decoder prefers grammatically and semantically plausible outputs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List any three challenges of speech interaction in field robotics environments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High background noise**: Field robots operate in outdoor, industrial, or disaster environments with SNR often below 10 dB, severely degrading WER.
2. **Reverberation and far-field speech**: When the speaker is more than 1–2 meters from the microphone, reflections and the inverse-square law reduce intelligibility.
3. **Speaker variability**: Accents (e.g., Malayalam-accented English), age, gender, and emotional state change acoustic characteristics.
4. *(Any other valid: multi-speaker overlap, code-switching, domain-specific vocabulary, latency constraints.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Bayes-optimal decoding equation for an Automatic Speech Recognition system and explain each term with reference to HMM-based acoustic modeling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Word Error Rate (WER) for the following ASR output. Reference sentence: *"the robot will deliver the package at five pm"*. Hypothesis sentence: *"robot will deliver package at pm"*. Also state one preprocessing technique to improve recognition of the deleted words. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Problem statement (1 mark):** Given acoustic observation $X = \{x_1, x_2, ..., x_T\}$, find the word sequence $W = \{w_1, w_2, ..., w_N\}$ that maximizes the posterior probability $P(W \mid X)$.

**Step 2 — Bayes' theorem application (2 marks):**

$$W^* = \arg\max_W P(W \mid X) = \arg\max_W \frac{P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)}{P(X)}$$

Since $P(X)$ does not depend on $W$, it is a constant for any given utterance and can be dropped from the maximization:

$$W^* = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$$

**Step 3 — Acoustic Model $P(X \mid W)$ (2 marks):** This is realized using an HMM where each word is represented as a sequence of phonetic states. The likelihood of the acoustic feature sequence given the word is computed via the Forward algorithm:

$$P(X \mid W) = \sum_{\text{paths}} \prod_{t=1}^{T} a_{q_{t-1}, q_t} \cdot b_{q_t}(x_t)$$

In modern systems, emission probabilities $b_{q_t}(x_t)$ are computed by a Deep Neural Network (DNN-HMM hybrid).

**Step 4 — Language Model $P(W)$ (1 mark):** This captures the probability of word sequences using n-gram statistics:

$$P(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}, w_{i-2}, ..., w_{i-n+1})$$

**Step 5 — Decoder (1 mark):** The Viterbi algorithm efficiently finds the best path:

$$\delta_t(j) = \max_i [\delta_{t-1}(i) \cdot a_{ij}] \cdot b_j(x_t)$$

The argmax over this gives $W^*$.

---

**(b) WER Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Alignment (2 marks):**

| Position | Reference | Hypothesis | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | the | (missing) | **D** |
| 2 | robot | robot | ✓ |
| 3 | will | will | ✓ |
| 4 | deliver | deliver | ✓ |
| 5 | the | (missing) | **D** |
| 6 | package | package | ✓ |
| 7 | at | at | ✓ |
| 8 | five | (missing) | **D** |
| 9 | pm | pm | ✓ |

**Step 2 — Count errors (2 marks):**

$$S = 0, \quad D = 3, \quad I = 0, \quad N = 9$$

**Step 3 — Compute WER (2 marks):**

$$\text{WER} = \frac{0 + 3 + 0}{9} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{3} \times 100\% \approx 33.33\%$$

**Step 4 — Preprocessing technique (1 mark):** The deletions are all function words ("the", "five"). To improve recognition:
- Use a **better language model** trained on domain-specific data that gives higher prior probability to these words in context, OR
- Use **sub-word tokenization** (BPE/WordPiece) so that even partial acoustic matches still yield the correct word, OR
- Apply **contextual biasing** with a custom grammar/weighted finite-state transducer that boosts "the" and "five" in decoding.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the MFCC feature extraction pipeline in detail, including the mathematical justification for the Mel scale. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a modern Neural Text-to-Speech (TTS) system. Compare its quality with concatenative synthesis using MOS. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MFCC Pipeline (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Pre-emphasis (1 mark):** Boosts high-frequency energy attenuated during speech production:

$$y[n] = x[n] - 0.97 \cdot x[n-1]$$

**Step 2 — Framing (0.5 marks):** Split into 25 ms frames with 10 ms hop. At $f_s = 16$ kHz, each frame has 400 samples; step size is 160 samples.

**Step 3 — Windowing (0.5 marks):** Multiply by Hamming window to reduce spectral leakage:

$$w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — FFT (1 mark):** Compute magnitude spectrum $\vert X[k] \vert$ using 512-point FFT.

**Step 5 — Mel filterbank (2 marks):** Convert linear frequency to perceptual Mel scale:

$$m = 2595 \log_{10}\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$$

Apply 40 triangular filters equally spaced on the Mel scale, then sum the power in each filter:

$$E_j = \sum_{k} \vert X[k] \vert^2 \cdot H_j(k)$$

**Step 6 — Log + DCT (1 mark):** Take logarithm (mimics human loudness perception: phon scale is logarithmic) and apply DCT to get 12–13 cepstral coefficients:

$$c_n = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \log(E_j) \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{J}(j - 0.5)\right)$$

**Step 7 — Dynamic features (1 mark):** Append energy, $\Delta$, and $\Delta\Delta$ to get a 39-dim feature vector that captures trajectory information.

---

**(b) Neural TTS Architecture (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (textual, 2 marks):**

```
Text → Text Front-End → Phonemes → Encoder (Transformer/CNN) 
     → Decoder → Mel-Spectrogram → Vocoder (WaveNet/HiFi-GAN) → Waveform
```

**Components (3 marks):**

1. **Text Front-End**: Normalization (numbers, dates), tokenization, grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) conversion.
2. **Acoustic Model**: Tacotron 2 or FastSpeech 2 — predicts mel-spectrogram from phonemes. Uses an encoder-decoder with attention, plus a duration/pitch/energy predictor.
3. **Vocoder**: WaveNet, Parallel WaveGAN, or HiFi-GAN — converts mel-spectrogram to time-domain waveform sample-by-sample or in parallel chunks.

**Comparison with Concatenative Synthesis (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Concatenative | Neural TTS |
|---|---|---|
| MOS (Mean Opinion Score) | 3.8 – 4.2 | **4.5 – 4.7** |
| Naturalness | Robotic prosody on unseen words | Highly natural |
| Voice cloning | Difficult | Easy with few seconds of audio |
| Footprint | Large unit database | ~10–100 MB model |
| Latency | Very low | Higher (especially WaveNet) |

**Conclusion:** Neural TTS achieves MOS approaching human naturalness (≈ 4.7 vs human ≈ 4.8) and is the modern choice for service robots.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting denominator in WER** — Some students divide by hypothesis length instead of reference length. Always $N$ = reference word count.
> 2. **Confusing Bayes' components** — $P(X \mid W)$ is the acoustic model, $P(W)$ is the language model. NOT the other way around.
> 3. **Skipping HMM state definitions** — Examiners expect explicit mention of $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ triplet and the three HMM problems.
> 4. **Wrong Mel constant** — The constant is **2595**, not 1125 (the latter is for the older Fant formulation; both exist but 2595 is the standard O'Shaughnessy form expected in KTU).
> 5. **Omitting VAD** — Voice Activity Detection is often missed in pipeline diagrams. Always include silence removal.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Speech Interaction (Module 4)**

- ⭐ Speech Interaction = **ASR** + **TTS** + **Speaker ID** + **Dialogue Management**.
- ⭐ Core ASR equation: $W^* = \arg\max_W P(X \mid W) \cdot P(W)$.
- ⭐ **Acoustic Model** uses HMM (states = phonemes, observations = MFCCs); modern systems use DNN-HMM or end-to-end Transformers.
- ⭐ **Language Model** uses n-gram or neural LMs to bias decoder toward plausible word sequences.
- ⭐ **WER** = $(S + D + I) / N \times 100\%$; can exceed 100% if many insertions.
- ⭐ **MFCC** pipeline: Pre-emphasis → Frame → Window → FFT → Mel filterbank → Log → DCT → Δ → ΔΔ = 39 dims.
- ⭐ **Mel scale formula**: $m = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$ — mimics human pitch perception.
- ⭐ **HMM Forward algorithm**: $\alpha_t(j) = [\sum_i \alpha_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}] b_j(o_t)$.
- ⭐ **HMM triplet**: $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ — transitions, emissions, initial.
- ⭐ **Viterbi** = best path (decoding); **Forward** = total probability (evaluation); **Baum-Welch** = training.
- ⭐ **TTS methods**: Formant → Concatenative → Parametric → **Neural** (Tacotron 2 + WaveNet/HiFi-GAN, MOS ~4.7).
- ⭐ **Field challenges**: noise (SNR), reverberation, far-field, multi-speaker, accent variability.
- ⭐ **Beamforming** (MVDR, GSC) is used in multi-mic robot arrays to improve SNR before ASR.
- ⭐ **MOS** (Mean Opinion Score) is the subjective TTS quality metric on a 1–5 scale.
- ⭐ Speaker verification metric: **EER** = Equal Error Rate (where FAR = FRR).
- ⭐ Modern SOTA ASR: Whisper, wav2vec 2.0, Conformer — all Transformer-based, multilingual.
- ⭐ **Wake-word detection** is the entry point; uses small always-on KWS (Keyword Spotting) model.
- ⭐ KTU frequently tests: Bayes' derivation (7 marks), WER numerical (7 marks), MFCC pipeline (7 marks), HMM forward recursion (7 marks).
- ⭐ Remember: **SNR** is in dB: $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Interaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motion Interaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Motion Interaction** in field and service robotics refers to the *regulated, dynamic coupling between a robot's controlled motion and the physical contact forces* exchanged with the environment, objects, or humans during the execution of a service task. It is the discipline that governs how a robot **plans, executes, and regulates its motion in the presence of mechanical constraints and contact phenomena**, ensuring that the commanded end-effector trajectory produces the desired balance of position tracking and force exertion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Motion Interaction is the *closed-loop control framework* that unifies **task-space kinematics**, **contact mechanics**, and **dynamic force regulation** so that a service robot can perform interactive tasks such as grasping, pushing, polishing, assembly, co-manipulation, and locomotion on uneven terrain.

Mathematically, Motion Interaction can be abstracted as the simultaneous satisfaction of two complementary objectives:

$$
\mathbf{x}_d(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{free} \quad \text{(unconstrained motion)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{f}_d(t) = \mathbf{K}_e \, \delta\mathbf{x} \quad \text{(constrained interaction)}
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$ is the desired position trajectory, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ is the free configuration subspace, $\mathbf{f}_d(t)$ is the desired contact force, $\mathbf{K}_e$ is the environmental stiffness, and $\delta\mathbf{x}$ is the commanded positional deviation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Hand on a Table

Imagine you place your palm flat on a table and slowly push down. You are *not* trying to push the table through the floor; you are **regulating how hard your palm presses**. Your brain simultaneously:
1. Controls the **position** of your wrist in 3D (move your arm along the table).
2. Controls the **force** your palm exerts perpendicular to the table (push harder or softer).

You are *not* commanding both independently in raw coordinates — you are commanding motion **along** the table surface and force **into** the table surface. This natural decomposition is the essence of Motion Interaction. A service robot performing a polishing task, opening a door, or wiping a surface must do exactly the same: **decompose the task into motion-controlled directions and force-controlled directions.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU's 2024 Scheme, Motion Interaction is the bridge between *kinematic planning* (Module 2/3 work on path planning) and *dynamic task execution* (force, compliance, contact stability). It is the topic that distinguishes a *motion-only* robot from a true *service* robot.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are central to the Motion Interaction framework and must be memorized:

- **Stiffness** ($K$): measured in **N/m**, represents resistance to positional deformation.
- **Compliance** ($C = 1/K$): measured in **m/N**, represents the ease of deformation.
- **Damping** ($B$): measured in **Ns/m**, represents energy dissipation.
- **Inertia** ($M$): measured in **kg**, the apparent mass the robot presents to the environment.
- **Natural frequency** ($\omega_n$): measured in **rad/s**, the resonance of the coupled system.
- **Damping ratio** ($\zeta$): dimensionless, characterizes oscillatory behaviour.

The canonical target relationship of an impedance controller is:

$$
\mathbf{M} \, \ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B} \, \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K} \, \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{f}_{ext}
$$

where $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$ is the position error and $\mathbf{f}_{ext}$ is the externally applied force.

## 1.4 Why Motion Interaction Matters in Field and Service Robotics

Service robots operate in **unstructured, human-populated, variable-impedance environments** — domestic floors, hospital corridors, elderly-care homes, agricultural fields, and outdoor terrain. Unlike factory robots behind safety cages, they must:

1. **Cope with uncertainty** in object pose, weight, and stiffness.
2. **Comply safely** with humans (physical Human-Robot Interaction, pHRI).
3. **Stabilize contact** without chattering or loss of grasp.
4. **Adapt online** to changing environmental constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** A common KTU question stem is: *"Why is pure position control insufficient for a service robot performing a wiping task?"* The answer must reference **environmental constraint incompatibility** — a position controller would either build up unbounded force or lose contact depending on the error tolerance, whereas a motion-interaction controller regulates both simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Decomposition: Natural and Artificial Constraints

Mason's seminal framework (1981) — which forms the theoretical bedrock of every KTU question on this topic — decomposes a constrained task into two orthogonal sets:

### 2.1.1 Natural Constraints

These are constraints **imposed by the environment** that the robot *cannot* violate. For a finger sliding on a rigid table:
- The position *normal* to the table is **fixed by the constraint surface**.
- The force *tangential* to the table is **zero (frictionless idealization)**.

### 2.1.2 Artificial Constraints (Control Objectives)

These are the *desired* values the robot must achieve in the *complementary* directions:
- Force *normal* to the table is **commanded** ($f_n = f_d$).
- Position *tangential* to the table is **commanded** ($v_t = v_d$).

The fundamental theorem of motion interaction states:

> *In any constrained task, the controlled variables are partitioned into position-controlled and force-controlled subsets that are **reciprocally related** through the constraint Jacobian.*

The partitioning is captured by a **selection matrix** $\mathbf{S}$ (or $\mathbf{\Sigma}$), a diagonal matrix of 1's and 0's that selects the position-controlled directions; its complement $\bar{\mathbf{S}} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{S}$ selects the force-controlled directions.

$$
\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \bar{\mathbf{S}} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{S}
$$

## 2.2 The Three Canonical Motion-Interaction Strategies

### 2.2.1 Hybrid Position/Force Control (Raibert & Craig, 1981)

The controller explicitly splits the task space into two independent subspaces, each with its own servo loop.

- **Position loop** acts in the $\mathbf{S}$-subspace.
- **Force loop** acts in the $\bar{\mathbf{S}}$-subspace.

The two loops are *decoupled in the task frame* and summed at the joint level. This is the most direct realization of Mason's theorem.

### 2.2.2 Impedance Control (Hogan, 1985)

Instead of tracking either position *or* force, the controller enforces a **target dynamic relationship** between the deviation from the nominal trajectory and the contact force — a *mass-spring-damper* relationship. The robot neither tries to be infinitely stiff (rigid position control) nor infinitely soft (admittance), but exhibits a tunable dynamic admittance/impedance.

### 2.2.3 Admittance Control

The dual of impedance control: a *position* loop on the inner side, and an outer *force loop* that computes the desired position correction from the measured force. It is preferred when the inner position loop is fast and stiff (typical of industrial arms).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the **must-memorize** equations for Motion Interaction. Master these and you can solve 90% of KTU questions on this module.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{f}_{ext}$ | Target impedance dynamics | Impedance controller design |
| 2 | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$ | Position error vector | Error-driven control |
| 3 | $\mathbf{f}_{cmd} = \bar{\mathbf{S}} \, \mathbf{K}_f (\mathbf{f}_d - \mathbf{f})$ | Hybrid force error law | Hybrid control law |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_{cmd} = \mathbf{S} \, \mathbf{K}_p (\mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x})$ | Hybrid position error law | Hybrid control law |
| 5 | $\mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{f}$ | Force-Jacobian transpose | Joint torque from wrench |
| 6 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ | Natural frequency | Impedance tuning |
| 7 | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{K M}}$ | Damping ratio | Impedance tuning |
| 8 | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n)$ | Selection matrix | Constraint decomposition |
| 9 | $\mathbf{x}_{env} = \mathbf{x}_{robot} \Rightarrow \mathbf{f} = \mathbf{K}_e \delta \mathbf{x}$ | Environmental constraint | Contact modelling |
| 10 | $K_{stiff} = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \Big\vert_{eq}$ | Operational stiffness | Contact stability |

**Critical substitution rule** (to avoid pipe-syntax breaking in tables): use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ written with `\vert` rather than raw `|` in any in-line LaTeX inside a markdown table.

## 2.4 Stability and Passivity Considerations

A motion-interaction controller is *passive* if the energy stored in the controlled mass-spring-damper system never exceeds the energy supplied by the environment plus initial storage. Passivity is the strongest known tool for **guaranteeing stable contact** in KTU-level problems.

The passivity condition for an impedance controller is:

$$
\int_0^T \mathbf{f}_{ext}^T(t) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) \, dt \geq -E(0)
$$

for all $T \geq 0$ and all admissible $\mathbf{f}_{ext}$. Practically, this translates to **positive-definite** $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{K}$ matrices and proper choice of damping $\mathbf{B} \geq 0$ to avoid active energy injection.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Impedance control is the *de facto* standard in collaborative robots (cobots) such as Universal Robots UR-series, Franka Emika Panda, and KUKA LBR iiwa. Every KTU question that mentions "compliant co-manipulation" expects impedance or admittance control as the answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Hybrid Position/Force Control Law

We start from the operational-space dynamics of a serial manipulator (formally derived in Module 1/2; recapitulated here for completeness):

$$
\mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \, \ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{cmd} + \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **operational-space mass matrix** (Cartesian inertia).
- $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the **operational-space Coriolis/centrifugal vector**.
- $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **gravity vector** mapped to task space.
- $\mathbf{F}_{cmd}$ is the commanded wrench at the end-effector.
- $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ is the external wrench from the environment.

**Step 1 — Choose a control structure.** We want to independently regulate position along directions where motion is free and force along directions where the environment constrains motion. Introduce the selection matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3, \sigma_4, \sigma_5, \sigma_6)
$$

with $\sigma_i = 1$ for position-controlled directions and $\sigma_i = 0$ for force-controlled directions. The complementary matrix is $\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} = \mathbf{I}_6 - \mathbf{\Sigma}$.

**Step 2 — Define the position error and the force error.**

$$
\mathbf{e}_p = \mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x}
$$

$$
\mathbf{e}_f = \mathbf{F}_d - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 3 — Design a position servo in the $\mathbf{\Sigma}$-subspace.** A standard PID law in operational space:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{pos} = \mathbf{\Sigma} \left[ \mathbf{K}_p \, \mathbf{e}_p + \mathbf{K}_d \, \dot{\mathbf{e}}_p + \mathbf{K}_i \int \mathbf{e}_p \, dt \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Design a force servo in the $\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}}$-subspace.** A PI law on force (with derivative action typically avoided to prevent noise amplification):

$$
\mathbf{F}_{force} = \bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} \left[ \mathbf{K}_{fp} \, \mathbf{e}_f + \mathbf{K}_{fi} \int \mathbf{e}_f \, dt \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Combine and add the gravity/feed-forward term.** The total commanded wrench is:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{F}_{pos} + \mathbf{F}_{force} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{a}_{ff}
$$

where $\mathbf{a}_{ff}$ is any feed-forward acceleration along the desired trajectory.

**Step 6 — Map back to joint torques.** Using the Jacobian transpose (or the dynamically consistent inverse for the operational-space formulation):

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{cmd} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})^T \mathbf{F}_{cmd}
$$

**Final Hybrid Law (compact form):**

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{cmd} = \mathbf{J}^T \left\{ \mathbf{\Sigma} \left[ \mathbf{K}_p \mathbf{e}_p + \mathbf{K}_d \dot{\mathbf{e}}_p \right] + \bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} \left[ \mathbf{K}_{fp} \mathbf{e}_f + \mathbf{K}_{fi} \int \mathbf{e}_f \right] + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) \right\}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Target Impedance Relationship

We want the robot to behave, as seen from the environment, like a **second-order mass-spring-damper system**. Let $\mathbf{x}$ be the actual end-effector position, $\mathbf{x}_d$ the desired (unconstrained) trajectory, and $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$ the contact force.

**Step 1 — Write the target dynamic equation.** This is the *definition* of the target impedance:

$$
