\mathbf{M} (\ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d) + \mathbf{B} (\dot{\mathbf{x}} - \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d) + \mathbf{K} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d) = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the position error.** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$, so $\dot{\mathbf{e}} = \dot{\mathbf{x}} - \dot{\mathbf{x}}_d$ and $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}} - \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d$. Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{M} \ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the commanded acceleration.** Treating this as the desired closed-loop dynamics:

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{cmd} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{M}^{-1} \left[ \mathbf{F}_{ext} - \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Generate the corresponding commanded wrench.** Plug the commanded acceleration into the operational-space dynamics. Defining $\mathbf{u} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{cmd}$, the computed-torque command is:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{u} + \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.** After substitution and cancellation of terms:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{M}^{-1} (\mathbf{F}_{ext} - \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e}) + \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{g} - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

This is the **impedance control law** in operational space. Its meaning: the controller does *not* track position rigidly in free space; it deliberately *admits* a position deviation $\mathbf{e}$ proportional to the contact force, with the proportionality tuned by $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio of the Impedance

Consider a 1-DOF target impedance $M \ddot{e} + B \dot{e} + K e = 0$ (free response).

**Step 1 — Take the Laplace transform.** Assuming zero initial conditions:

$$
M s^2 E(s) + B s E(s) + K E(s) = 0
$$

$$
E(s) \left[ M s^2 + B s + K \right] = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Write the characteristic polynomial.**

$$
s^2 + \frac{B}{M} s + \frac{K}{M} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the canonical second-order form.**

$$
s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Match coefficients.**

$$
\omega_n^2 = \frac{K}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}
$$

$$
2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{B}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \zeta = \frac{B}{2 \sqrt{K M}}
$$

These two equations are the **principal tuning laws** for an impedance controller and are routinely asked in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common exam trap:** Students often forget that for *critical damping* ($\zeta = 1$), the damping must be $B = 2\sqrt{KM}$, *not* $B = KM$ or $B = K/M$. This is a one-mark trap in Part A and a frequent 3-mark trap in Part B.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Controller Comparison

| Property | Hybrid P/F | Impedance | Admittance |
|----------|------------|-----------|------------|
| Controlled variable | Position *and* force (decoupled) | Force-to-motion relation | Motion-to-force relation |
| Requires force sensor | Yes | Yes (for outer loop) | Yes (for outer loop) |
| Robust to stiffness uncertainty | Low | High | Medium |
| Switching logic needed | Yes (for $\mathbf{\Sigma}$) | No | No |
| Stable on stiff contact | Yes | Tunable | Tunable |
| Stable on free motion | Yes | Tunable | Tunable |
| Best for | Polishing, deburring | Co-manipulation, walking | Industrial stiff arms |
| KTU typical question | "Design a hybrid controller for door opening" | "Tune an impedance for safe HRI" | "Implement admittance on a UR5" |

## 3.5 Constraint Frame Transformation

A frequently tested sub-skill is transforming a wrench/velocity specification from a *task frame* $\{T\}$ (e.g., aligned with the table) to the *base frame* $\{B\}$ of the robot. If $\mathbf{R}_{T}^{B}$ is the rotation matrix of $\{T\}$ expressed in $\{B\}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{ext}^{B} = \mathbf{R}_{T}^{B} \, \mathbf{F}_{ext}^{T}
$$

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}^{B} = \mathbf{R}_{T}^{B} \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}^{T}
$$

Similarly, the selection matrix in the base frame becomes:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}^{B} = \mathbf{R}_{T}^{B} \, \mathbf{\Sigma}^{T} \, \left( \mathbf{R}_{T}^{B} \right)^T
$$

This block-diagonalizes the selection in the task frame and rotates it into the base frame in which the robot's controller natively operates.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid Position/Force Control

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **closed-loop architecture** of a Raibert–Craig hybrid position/force controller applied to a service-robot manipulation task (e.g., polishing a surface).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Task Planner] --> B[Selection Matrix S]
    A --> C[Complement I-S]
    B --> D[Position Controller Kp Kd]
    C --> E[Force Controller Kfp Kfi]
    D --> F[Sum Node: Wrench Command]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Inverse Dynamics + Gravity Comp]
    G --> H[Robot Manipulator]
    H --> I[End Effector + Environment]
    I --> J[Force Torque Sensor]
    I --> K[Position Encoder via FK]
    J --> L[Force Error e_f]
    K --> M[Position Error e_p]
    L --> E
    M --> D
    H --> N[Jacobian Transpose J transpose]
    N --> O[Joint Torque Command]
    O --> H
```

### 4.1.1 Reading the Diagram

- **Top row:** Task planner issues *desired position* $\mathbf{x}_d$ and *desired force* $\mathbf{F}_d$ into the two branches.
- **Selection matrix** $\mathbf{S}$ (block B) routes position to the **position servo** (block D), and $\bar{\mathbf{S}}$ (block C) routes force to the **force servo** (block E).
- **Sum node F** adds the two control contributions and a feed-forward gravity term.
- **Inverse dynamics block G** converts the commanded Cartesian wrench to joint torques via $\mathbf{J}^T$.
- The **robot H** acts on the environment **I**.
- **Sensors J, K** close the loops: force sensor feeds the force error; encoders + forward kinematics feed the position error.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam hint:** When asked to draw a "block diagram of a hybrid controller", the **selection matrix** is the *single most important* block. KTU examiners award full marks only if $\mathbf{S}$ and $\bar{\mathbf{S}}$ are explicitly drawn and labeled.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Impedance Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Trajectory xd] --> B[Error e = x - xd]
    C[Measured Position x] --> B
    B --> D[Stiffness Term K e]
    B --> E[Damping Term B de dt]
    B --> F[Inertia Term M d2e dt2]
    D --> G[Sum Node: Impedance Force]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    H[External Force Fext] --> G
    G --> I[Operational Space Dynamics]
    I --> J[Robot + Environment]
    J --> K[Force Sensor]
    K --> L[Admittance Outer Loop]
    L --> A
    J --> C
```

### 4.2.1 Impedance Reading Notes

- The **impedance block** (sum node G) is a *virtual* mass-spring-damper: it computes the contact force expected for the measured position error and adds it to $\mathbf{F}_{ext}$.
- The robot is *not* told "achieve this force" — it is told "behave like this mass-spring-damper", and the resulting force emerges naturally from the interaction.
- The **admittance outer loop** (block L) is optional: in pure impedance control, no outer loop is needed; in admittance control, force measurement is fed back to modify the desired trajectory.

## 4.3 Constraint Frame Selection Tree

A common KTU sub-question: *"For a service robot wiping a whiteboard, write the natural and artificial constraints in the task frame."* The decision tree:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Task: Wipe Whiteboard] --> B{Is the direction free or constrained?}
    B --> C[Along board plane: free motion]
    B --> D[Normal to board: contact constraint]
    C --> E[Natural: f parallel = 0]
    C --> F[Artificial: v parallel = v desired]
    D --> G[Natural: x normal = constant]
    D --> H[Artificial: f normal = f desired]
    E --> I[Selection Matrix S parallel = I]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Selection Matrix S normal = 0]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Hybrid Controller]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Student pitfall:** The whiteboard is rigid ⇒ *natural constraint* on normal position. The whiteboard exerts whatever force is needed ⇒ *artificial control objective* on normal force. Reversing these is a common 2-mark error.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define Motion Interaction as applicable to a field/service robot. State two real-world tasks that mandate motion-interaction control. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> *Motion Interaction is the disciplined, closed-loop coupling of a robot's commanded motion with the contact forces exchanged with the environment such that both **position tracking** and **force regulation** objectives are simultaneously satisfied in orthogonal subspaces of the task space.*
>
> *It is formally realized by partitioning the task into position-controlled and force-controlled directions using a **selection matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ and implementing either a **hybrid position/force law** or an **impedance/admittance law**.*
>
> *Two real-world service tasks requiring motion interaction:*
> 1. *A domestic service robot **wiping a kitchen counter** — must track motion tangentially and regulate normal contact force.*
> 2. *A rehabilitation robot **assisting a patient to stand** — must remain compliant under variable human effort (physical Human-Robot Interaction).*

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for examples with reasoning.)*

### Question 2. State Mason's theorem of natural and artificial constraints. For a robot finger sliding on a frictionless horizontal table, list the natural and artificial constraints. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> *Mason's Theorem (1981): In any constrained task, the **position-controlled** and **force-controlled** directions are *reciprocally* related through the task's constraint geometry; the environment dictates the natural (position or force) constraints, and the controller specifies the artificial (force or position) objectives in the complementary directions.*
>
> *For a finger on a frictionless horizontal table (with $z$ vertical, $x$-$y$ horizontal):*
>
> | Direction | Natural Constraint | Artificial Constraint (Control Objective) |
> |-----------|--------------------|-------------------------------------------|
> | $x$ (tangential) | $f_x = 0$ | $v_x = v_{x,d}$ (track velocity) |
> | $y$ (tangential) | $f_y = 0$ | $v_y = v_{y,d}$ (track velocity) |
> | $z$ (normal) | $z = z_0$ (constant) | $f_z = f_{z,d}$ (regulate force) |
>
> *Selection matrix in the table frame:*
>
> $$
> \mathbf{\Sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

*(3 marks: 1 for theorem, 2 for the correctly populated constraint table.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1: Design a Hybrid Position/Force Controller for a Polishing Task `[14 Marks]`

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

A 6-DOF service robot manipulator is required to **polish a curved metal surface** of unknown but moderate stiffness. The end-effector holds a polishing pad that must maintain a **constant normal force of 5 N** while moving along a **pre-planned path on the surface** at 0.05 m/s.

**(a) Formulate the natural and artificial constraints in the appropriate task frame. Derive the structure of the selection matrix. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the task frame $\{T\}$.** Let the $z_T$ axis be the *outward surface normal* and the $x_T, y_T$ axes lie in the tangent plane. The frame moves with the end-effector so that it always remains aligned with the local surface.

**Step 2 — Natural constraints (imposed by the environment).** Because the surface is rigid and the pad is non-deformable in the limit:
- Position along $z_T$ is fixed: $\dot{x}_{T,z} = 0$.
- Force along $x_T, y_T$ is zero (ideal frictionless contact for the constraint): $f_{T,x} = f_{T,y} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Artificial constraints (controller objectives).**
- Velocity along $x_T, y_T$ is commanded: $\dot{x}_{T,x} = v_d = 0.05$ m/s (and a small $y_T$ component to cover the surface).
- Force along $z_T$ is commanded: $f_{T,z} = 5$ N.

**Step 4 — Write the selection matrix in $\{T\}$.**

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_T = \mathbf{I}_3 - \mathbf{\Sigma}_T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Transform to base frame $\{B\}$ for implementation.** If $\mathbf{R}_T^B$ is the rotation matrix of $\{T\}$ in $\{B\}$:

$$
\mathbf{\Sigma}_B = \mathbf{R}_T^B \, \mathbf{\Sigma}_T \left( \mathbf{R}_T^B \right)^T
$$

This is the matrix the controller will use in real time as the tool frame moves.

**(7 marks: 2 for constraints, 2 for selection matrix, 1 for transformation, 2 for clarity of exposition.)**

---

**(b) Derive the complete hybrid control law mapping the commanded Cartesian wrench to joint torques. Briefly discuss the role of gravity compensation. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Position control law (in the $\mathbf{\Sigma}_T$ subspace).**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{pos} = \mathbf{\Sigma}_T \left[ \mathbf{K}_p (\mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{K}_d (\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{x}}) \right]
$$

with $\mathbf{K}_p = \text{diag}(200, 200, 0)$ N/m and $\mathbf{K}_d = \text{diag}(40, 40, 0)$ Ns/m for a critically damped tangential response.

**Step 2 — Force control law (in the $\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_T$ subspace).** Use a PI law (no derivative to avoid sensor-noise amplification):

$$
\mathbf{F}_{force} = \bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}}_T \left[ \mathbf{K}_{fp} (\mathbf{F}_d - \mathbf{F}_{ext}) + \mathbf{K}_{fi} \int (\mathbf{F}_d - \mathbf{F}_{ext}) \, dt \right]
$$

with $\mathbf{F}_d = [0, 0, 5]^T$ N, $\mathbf{K}_{fp} = \text{diag}(0, 0, 0.5)$ (dimensionless gain), $\mathbf{K}_{fi} = \text{diag}(0, 0, 5)$ (1/s gain).

**Step 3 — Sum the wrench command and add gravity compensation.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{F}_{pos} + \mathbf{F}_{force} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 4 — Map to joint torques using the Jacobian transpose.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{cmd} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})^T \, \mathbf{F}_{cmd}
$$

**Step 5 — Discuss gravity compensation.** *The polishing task is performed by a manipulator arm in Earth's gravity. Without explicit compensation, gravity would (i) bias the force measurement at the wrist sensor, (ii) cause sag of the manipulator and disturb the contact normal direction, and (iii) produce a steady-state position error in the tangent plane. The gravity term $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ pre-emptively cancels the gravitational wrench at the end-effector, allowing the force sensor to read the **true contact force** and the position loop to track the **true geometry** of the surface. For a 6-DOF polishing task with a heavy tool (e.g., 2 kg pad), neglecting gravity can cause force errors of 20 N or more — a 400% error on a 5 N setpoint.*

**(7 marks: 3 for control law derivation, 2 for Jacobian mapping, 2 for gravity discussion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hybrid Control:**
> 1. Forgetting the $\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}}$ on the force branch is a **-3 mark** error — it means the position and force loops fight each other.
> 2. Writing $\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{F}_{pos} + \mathbf{F}_{force}$ *without* gravity $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ costs **-2 marks** in any practical design.
> 3. Using $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ instead of $\mathbf{J}^T$ for torque mapping loses **-1 mark** because $\mathbf{J}^T$ is the natural adjoint for force/torque duality; $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ is for velocity mapping.

---

### Question B — Option 2: Impedance Controller Design for a Co-manipulation Task `[14 Marks]`

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

A rehabilitation service robot is to assist a human patient in **lifting a 3 kg object from a table**. The robot must exhibit a **compliant, spring-like behaviour** with a target stiffness of 200 N/m and a damping ratio of $\zeta = 0.7$ (to avoid oscillation while remaining responsive). The apparent inertia of the robot arm is 5 kg.

**(a) Determine the impedance parameters $M$, $B$, $K$ and derive the target impedance equation in the vertical direction. Justify the choice of damping ratio. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data.**
- $K = 200$ N/m
- $M = 5$ kg
- $\zeta = 0.7$

**Step 2 — Compute the natural frequency.**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{5}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the damping coefficient using the damping-ratio relation.**

$$
\zeta = \frac{B}{2 \sqrt{K M}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 2 \zeta \sqrt{K M}
$$

$$
B = 2 \times 0.7 \times \sqrt{200 \times 5} = 1.4 \times \sqrt{1000} = 1.4 \times 31.62 \approx 44.27 \text{ Ns/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inertia if not given (alternative).** The problem states $M = 5$ kg. We retain the given value.

**Step 5 — Write the target impedance in the vertical ($z$) direction.**

$$
M \ddot{e}_z + B \dot{e}_z + K e_z = F_{ext,z}
$$

Substituting:

$$
5 \, \ddot{e}_z + 44.27 \, \dot{e}_z + 200 \, e_z = F_{ext,z}
$$

**Step 6 — Justify the choice of $\zeta = 0.7$.** A damping ratio in the range $0.6 \leq \zeta \leq 0.8$ is the engineering standard for **underdamped-yet-near-critically-damped** systems intended for human interaction. *Why?*

- $\zeta < 0.6$ leads to perceptible oscillation, which is uncomfortable and unsettling for the human partner and may destabilize the grasped object.
- $\zeta = 1$ (critical) gives the fastest non-oscillatory response but is often perceived as *sluggish* and *dead* — the robot feels heavy and unresponsive.
- $\zeta \approx 0.7$ is a classical **butterworth** choice that minimizes the combined overshoot and settling time for a second-order system, giving the most *natural-feeling* compliant response during co-manipulation.

**Step 7 — Verify the natural frequency against human neuromuscular bandwidth.** Human arm bandwidth is typically 2–5 Hz (12.6–31.4 rad/s); with $\omega_n = 6.32$ rad/s $\approx 1$ Hz, the robot's apparent stiffness will be perceived as **comfortable and stable** by the patient.

**(7 marks: 2 for parameter calculation, 2 for impedance equation, 3 for justification.)*

---

**(b) Derive the full operational-space impedance control law and discuss the stability of contact with a stiff human limb (effective environmental stiffness $K_e = 5000$ N/m). (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the operational-space dynamics from the hybrid derivation.**

$$
\mathbf{\Lambda}(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{x}} + \boldsymbol{\mu}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{F}_{cmd} + \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose a desired trajectory $\mathbf{x}_d(t)$ and define the position error.** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_d$.

**Step 3 — Specify the desired target impedance (vector form).**

$$
\mathbf{M} \ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the commanded acceleration.**

$$
\ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{cmd} = \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{M}^{-1} \left[ \mathbf{F}_{ext} - \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into operational-space computed-torque control.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{\Lambda} \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_{cmd} + \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{g} - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \mathbf{\Lambda} \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{M}^{-1} \left( \mathbf{F}_{ext} - \mathbf{B} \dot{\mathbf{e}} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{e} \right) + \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{g} - \mathbf{F}_{ext}
$$

**Step 7 — Group terms.**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{cmd} = \underbrace{\mathbf{\Lambda} \ddot{\mathbf{x}}_d + \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{g}}_{\text{feed-forward}} - \underbrace{\mathbf{F}_{ext}}_{\text{rejection}} + \underbrace{\mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{M}^{-1} \mathbf{K}}_{\text{position stiffness}} (\mathbf{x}_d - \mathbf{x}) + \underbrace{\mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{M}^{-1} \mathbf{B}}_{\text{damping}} (\dot{\mathbf{x}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{x}}) + \underbrace{\mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{M}^{-1} \mathbf{F}_{ext}}_{\text{force feed-forward}}
$$

**Step 8 — Map to joint torques.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}_{cmd} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})^T \, \mathbf{F}_{cmd}
$$

**Step 9 — Stability analysis against the stiff environment.** The coupled robot–environment system has effective stiffness $K_{eff}$ where the robot and environment act in *series* in the contact direction:

$$
\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = \frac{1}{K} + \frac{1}{K_e} = \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{5000}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{K_{eff}} = 0.005 + 0.0002 = 0.0052 \text{ m/N}
$$

$$
K_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.0052} \approx 192.3 \text{ N/m}
$$

The coupled natural frequency:

$$
\omega_{n,coupled} = \sqrt{\frac{K_{eff}}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{192.3}{5}} \approx 6.20 \text{ rad/s}
$$

This is a **1.9% reduction** from the unconstrained $\omega_n = 6.32$ rad/s. The system remains stable because:

- The damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$ is preserved (the environment is approximately massless, so it does not add inertia).
- The **passivity condition** is satisfied: $K, B, M$ are all positive definite, and the controller is a passive one-port network.
- A common failure mode — *contact instability* — would occur if the environmental stiffness $K_e$ dominated and the controller damping were too low. Here, $K_e = 5000$ N/m is moderate, and $B = 44.27$ Ns/m is well above the critical value $2\sqrt{K_{eff} M} \approx 2\sqrt{192.3 \times 5} \approx 62.1$? *Wait — recheck:* critical damping is $2\sqrt{K_{eff} M} = 2\sqrt{192.3 \times 5} = 2 \times 31.0 = 62.0$ Ns/m. With $B = 44.27$ Ns/m, the coupled damping ratio is $\zeta_{coupled} = B / 2\sqrt{K_{eff} M} = 44.27 / 62.0 \approx 0.71$ — still in the comfortable range.

> *Correction insight:* The slight reduction in natural frequency and the negligible change in damping confirm that the design is **robust to this level of environmental stiffness**.

**(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for stability, 1 for concluding statement.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Impedance Control:**
> 1. **Critical-damping formula** must use $B = 2\sqrt{KM}$ — students writing $B = KM$ or $B = K/M$ lose **2 marks immediately**.
> 2. **Series stiffness** for the coupled system is $\tfrac{1}{K_{eff}} = \tfrac{1}{K} + \tfrac{1}{K_e}$, *not* $K_{eff} = K + K_e$ (that would be parallel). Confusing the two is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Damping ratio justification** must be tied to human-perception or contact-stability reasoning — generic statements like "it is a good value" earn **0 of 3 marks** in the justification step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — Motion Interaction, Module 4**

- **Definition:** Motion Interaction is the **joint regulation of position and force** in orthogonal task-space directions during contact tasks.
- **Mason's Theorem:** Natural constraints come from the environment; artificial constraints come from the controller; they are *complementary* in the task frame.
- **Selection matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$:** Diagonal of 1's (position-controlled) and 0's (force-controlled); complement is $\bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{\Sigma}$.
- **Hybrid Control Law:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T [\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{F}_{pos} + \bar{\mathbf{\Sigma}} \mathbf{F}_{force} + \mathbf{g}]$.
- **Impedance Equation:** $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{B}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{F}_{ext}$.
- **Tuning formulas:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$, $\zeta = B / (2\sqrt{KM})$.
- **Critical damping:** $B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{KM}$, achieved at $\zeta = 1$.
- **Recommended damping for HRI:** $\zeta \approx 0.7$ (Butterworth-optimal).
- **Force-torque duality:** Use $\mathbf{J}^T$ (Jacobian transpose) to map wrenches to joint torques; **never** $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ for force.
- **Passivity is stability insurance:** keep $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{K}$ positive-definite; never inject active energy through feedback.
- **Task frame convention:** $z$ along the contact normal; $x, y$ in the tangent plane.
- **Stability against stiff environment:** Use series stiffness $\tfrac{1}{K_{eff}} = \tfrac{1}{K} + \tfrac{1}{K_e}$; verify coupled $\zeta$ remains $\geq 0.5$.
- **Hybrid vs Impedance:** Hybrid = explicit dual loops, needs switching; Impedance = single dynamic relationship, robust to stiffness uncertainty.
- **Cobots and pHRI:** Always prefer impedance/admittance for human-shared workspaces; prefer hybrid for deterministic industrial tasks.
- **Force-sensor noise:** Use PI (not PID) on the force loop; derivative amplifies sensor noise and causes chattering.
- **Gravity compensation** $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q})$ is **mandatory** for any practical hybrid/impedance implementation on a non-horizontal arm.
- **Constraint transformation:** $\mathbf{\Sigma}_B = \mathbf{R}_T^B \, \mathbf{\Sigma}_T (\mathbf{R}_T^B)^T$ — exam-favorite.
- **KTU-favorite real-world examples:** *door opening* (hybrid), *wiping a surface* (hybrid), *co-manipulation* (impedance), *legged locomotion on uneven ground* (impedance/admittance per leg), *fruit picking* (hybrid with soft contact).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical and Healthcare Robots: Key Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Medical and Healthcare Robots: Key Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Medical and Healthcare Robots** are a specialized class of service robots designed to operate in clinical, surgical, rehabilitative, and assistive healthcare environments. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Field and Service Robotics, these are mechatronic systems that augment, assist, or autonomously execute tasks traditionally performed by medical professionals, thereby enhancing precision, safety, sterility, and accessibility of healthcare delivery.

The **"Key Benefits"** subtopic within Module 4 systematically categorizes the qualitative and quantitative advantages of deploying robotic systems in medical workflows, ranging from intra-operative surgical assistance to post-operative rehabilitation and hospital-wide logistical automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Medical robots are NOT autonomous decision-makers in the legal/ethical KTU context. They function as **surgeon-master, robot-slave** teleoperated systems (with partial task-level autonomy), where the cognitive responsibility remains with the certified human practitioner.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a master painter (the surgeon) holding the handle of an extremely long, perfectly steady brush (the robotic manipulator). The artist's hand may shake due to fatigue, age, or prolonged concentration — but the *tip* of the brush, held by a mechanical linkage, remains absolutely still and translates the artist's intent with **scaled-down, tremor-free precision**.

**Real-world mapping:**
- The **painter's hand** = Surgeon's console input device
- The **long brush** = Robotic manipulator (e.g., da Vinci's EndoWrist instruments)
- The **steady brush-tip** = The 8 mm end-effector inside the patient's body
- The **steady hand** = Active tremor filtration, motion scaling (e.g., 3:1 ratio)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Medical robots do not replace the surgeon — they **amplify** the surgeon's best qualities (precision, steadiness, endurance) while **filtering out** the human limitations (fatigue, tremor, restricted dexterity inside the body).

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Medical Robots (Context for Benefits)

| Sub-Domain | Example System | Primary Benefit Class |
|---|---|---|
| Surgical Robots | da Vinci Xi, Mako, Rosa | Precision, Minimally Invasive Access |
| Rehabilitation Robots | Lokomat, Armeo Power | Repetitive, measurable therapy |
| Assistive/Social Robots | PARO, NAO | Companionship, cognitive engagement |
| Hospital Logistics | TUG, HelpMate | 24/7 supply transport |
| Pharmacy Automation | McKesson Robot-Rx | Error-free medication dispensing |
| Disinfection Robots | Xenex LightStrike | UV-based pathogen neutralization |
| Telepresence/Consultation | InTouch Vici, RP-VITA | Remote specialist access |
| Diagnostic/Imaging | CyberKnife, MAKO | Sub-millimeter targeting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the exam:** When KTU asks "Discuss the key benefits of medical robots," examiners expect students to **categorize** benefits according to sub-domains rather than producing a single undifferentiated list. Structure = marks.

### 1.4 Core Quantitative Metrics (KTU Board-Standard)

The following empirical metrics are standard benchmarks in KTU 2024 Scheme valuation:

- **Dexterity (DOF):** 6–7 DOF per instrument (da Vinci) vs. 4 DOF of human wrist inside a port
- **Motion scaling ratio:** typically **3:1** (3 cm hand movement = 1 cm instrument tip movement)
- **Tremor filtration threshold:** **< 50 Hz** physiological hand tremor filtered
- **Surgical incision size:** **8–12 mm** ports vs. **150–250 mm** open laparotomy
- **Magnification:** Up to **10–15×** high-definition 3D visualization
- **Recovery time reduction:** **30–50%** shorter hospital stay for robotic vs. open procedures
- **Positioning accuracy:** Sub-millimeter (**< 0.5 mm**) in image-guided systems like CyberKnife

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Triple-A" Benefit Framework (KTU Favourite Model)

For analytical clarity, the benefits of medical and healthcare robots are best organized under the **Triple-A Framework**, which examiners frequently test:

#### A1. **Augmentation** of Human Capability
- **Motion Scaling:** Hand tremor (typically 50–100 µm RMS in surgeons) is mechanically filtered. The input-to-output relationship is governed by a linear gain:

$$G_{scale} = \frac{\Delta x_{instrument}}{\Delta x_{hand}}$$

Typical value for da Vinci = **1/3** (3:1 scaling).

- **Force Scaling:** Surgical force at the tip can be scaled down to allow delicate tissue handling.

$$F_{tip} = G_{force} \cdot F_{hand}, \quad G_{force} \ll 1$$

- **DOF Enhancement:** The EndoWrist mechanism provides a **virtual pivot** at the port, restoring intracorporeal wrist dexterity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Augmentation benefits address the **physical limitations** of the surgeon — the robot is a *force, motion, and dexterity multiplier*.

#### A2. **Accessibility** (Minimally Invasive & Telepresence)
- **Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS):** Port-based access reduces incision from ~200 mm to ~10 mm.
- **Tele-surgery:** The latency constraint for safe teleoperation is:

$$L_{max} \approx 200 \text{ ms (round-trip)}$$

The famous **Lindbergh Operation (2001)** by Prof. Marescaux (NY → Strasbourg, ~7,000 km) demonstrated feasibility.

#### A3. **Automation** (Repetitive, High-Volume, Error-Sensitive Tasks)
- Hospital logistics, pharmacy dispensing, disinfection cycles.
- Automation index:

$$A_{index} = \frac{N_{automated\_tasks}}{N_{total\_tasks}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2 Detailed Benefits Breakdown (KTU 14-Mark Ready)

#### Benefit 1: **Enhanced Precision and Accuracy**
- **How:** High-resolution encoders, sub-mm backlash, image-guided registration (CT/MRI fusion).
- **KTU Expected Points:** 
  - Elimination of physiological hand tremor
  - Motion scaling (1:3 typical)
  - 3D high-definition magnified visualization
  - Registration with pre-operative imaging

#### Benefit 2: **Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS)**
- **How:** Small ports replace large incisions.
- **Engineering benefit:** Reduced blood loss, lower infection risk, smaller scars.
- **Quantitative gain:** Hospital stay reduction from **7–10 days** (open) to **2–4 days** (robotic).

#### Benefit 3: **Improved Patient Outcomes**
- Lower post-operative pain (reduced analgesic requirement).
- Reduced complication rates (lower wound infection, less blood transfusion need).
- Faster return to daily activities.

#### Benefit 4: **Surgeon Ergonomics & Reduced Fatigue**
- Surgeon sits at a console with **ergonomic posture** instead of standing for hours.
- **Eliminates** neck/back strain from leaning over an open surgical field.

#### Benefit 5: **Remote Surgery & Telepresence**
- Brings specialist expertise to underserved/rural areas.
- Critical for military battlefield scenarios and pandemics (e.g., COVID-19 tele-ICU robots).

#### Benefit 6: **Enhanced 3D Visualization**
- **High-definition stereoscopic 3D** camera with **10–15× magnification** vs. naked-eye laparoscopy.
- Integration with **fluorescence imaging** (indocyanine green — ICG) for vascular mapping.

#### Benefit 7: **Tremor Filtration & Motion Compensation**
- **Active tremor filtering** uses high-frequency cutoff filters:

$$H_{filter}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s/\omega_c}, \quad \omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 6 \text{ Hz (typical cutoff)}$$

This eliminates physiological tremor (8–12 Hz) while preserving intentional motion (< 2 Hz).

#### Benefit 8: **Scalability of Motion (Micro-macro Manipulation)**
- Enables **micro-surgery** (e.g., supermicrosurgery at 0.3–0.8 mm vessel diameter).
- Enables **macro-surgery** (multi-quadrant abdominal access via patient cart repositioning).

#### Benefit 9: **Reproducibility and Consistency**
- Robots execute pre-programmed trajectories with **deterministic repeatability**.
- Critical in radiosurgery (CyberKnife delivers **± 0.5 mm** accuracy over 100+ sessions).

#### Benefit 10: **Reduced Radiation Exposure (For Surgical Team)**
- In interventional radiology, the surgeon controls the C-arm robot from a shielded console.
- Operator exposure reduced from **mSv/h** to near-background.

#### Benefit 11: **Data Integration & Surgical Analytics**
- Modern systems log every kinematic event → enables **post-operative review, AI-based skill assessment, and medico-legal documentation**.

#### Benefit 12: **Rehabilitation Continuity**
- Robots like **Lokomat** deliver **10⁴–10⁵** gait cycles per session vs. ~10�² cycles in manual therapy.

#### Benefit 13: **Cognitive & Social Assistance**
- Companion robots reduce agitation in dementia patients (PARO seal trials show 30–40% reduction in behavioral symptoms).

#### Benefit 14: **Infection Control**
- UV-C disinfection robots (Xenex) achieve **> 99.9%** pathogen reduction in 5–10 minute cycles, reducing Hospital-Acquired Infections (HAIs).

#### Benefit 15: **24/7 Operational Availability**
- Logistics robots work continuously, freeing nursing staff for direct patient care.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Motion Scaling Gain | $G_{scale} = \Delta x_{tip} / \Delta x_{hand}$ | Hand-to-tip displacement ratio | 1/3 to 1/5 |
| Force Scaling Gain | $F_{tip} = G_{force} \cdot F_{hand}$ | Force at instrument tip | 0.1 to 0.5 |
| Tele-op Latency Budget | $L_{round-trip} \le L_{max}$ | Maximum tolerable delay | $\le$ 200 ms |
| Tremor Filter Cutoff | $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ | Low-pass filter cutoff | 6–10 Hz |
| Repeatability (Radiosurgery) | $\sigma_{pos} \le 0.5$ mm | Standard deviation of target | mm |
| Automation Index | $A_i = N_{auto}/N_{total}$ | Fraction of automated steps | 0–1 (dimensionless) |
| HAI Reduction Rate | $R_{HAI} = (HAI_{pre} - HAI_{post})/HAI_{pre}$ | Infection rate drop | $\ge$ 30% |
| Therapist-to-Patient Cycle | $N_{cyc} = f \cdot t$ | Rehab repetitions per session | 10² manual vs. 10⁴–10⁵ robotic |
| DOF Enhancement | $DOF_{eff} \ge 6$ | Intracorporeal dexterity | 6–7 |
| Visualization Magnification | $M = f_{cam}/f_{eye}$ | Optical magnification factor | 10–15× |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never use the absolute-value pipe symbol $\vert$ inside any markdown table cell. Use $\mid$ or write "absolute value of x" instead — broken table syntax is a guaranteed mark-deduction trigger in PDF/board evaluation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Space Medicine:** NASA is developing surgical robots for deep-space missions where real-time tele-surgery from Earth is impossible (latency > 20 s) — driving **autonomous surgical AI** research.
- **Pandemic Response:** During COVID-19, telepresence robots reduced clinician PPE consumption by **~80%** per patient round.
- **Rural India:** Telerobotic ultrasound (e.g., **ARTHUR**) brings specialist diagnosis to primary health centres in Kerala, Goa, and Tamil Nadu — directly relevant to KTU's Kerala healthcare context.
- **Defence:** **MAARS (Modular Advanced Armed Robotic System)** has medical evacuation variants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-effector Position Error with Tremor Filtration

Let us derive the residual position error at the surgical instrument tip when a surgeon's hand tremor is filtered by a robotic motion-scaling system.

**Step 1: Decompose the hand motion signal.**

The surgeon's hand motion $x_{hand}(t)$ is composed of two components:
- **Intentional low-frequency motion:** $x_{low}(t)$ with spectral content $\le 2$ Hz
- **Physiological tremor:** $x_{tremor}(t)$ with spectral content 8–12 Hz

We can write:

$$x_{hand}(t) = x_{low}(t) + x_{tremor}(t)$$

**Step 2: Apply the Tremor Filter Transfer Function.**

The robotic controller applies a low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c$ and scaling gain $G_{scale}$:

$$H_{filter}(s) = G_{scale} \cdot \frac{\omega_c^2}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_c s + \omega_c^2}$$

where:
- $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c$ is the cutoff angular frequency
- $\zeta$ is the damping ratio (typically 0.7 for critical damping)
- $G_{scale}$ is the motion scaling factor

**Step 3: Substitute typical KTU-standard values.**

For a da Vinci-class system: $G_{scale} = 1/3$, $f_c = 6$ Hz.

Therefore:

$$\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 6 = 12\pi \approx 37.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate filter response at intentional and tremor frequencies.**

At $f = 1$ Hz (intentional motion):

$$H(j2\pi \cdot 1) = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{(37.7)^2}{(j2\pi)^2 + 2(0.7)(37.7)(j2\pi) + (37.7)^2}$$

At low frequency ($\omega \ll \omega_c$), the filter passes the signal with gain $G_{scale} = 1/3$.

At $f = 10$ Hz (tremor band), well above cutoff ($\omega \gg \omega_c$):

$$H(j2\pi \cdot 10) \approx \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{\omega_c}{\omega}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{37.7}{62.8}\right)^2 \approx \frac{1}{3} \cdot 0.36 \approx 0.12$$

**Step 5: Calculate the residual tip error.**

If hand tremor amplitude is **100 µm** peak, the residual tremor at the tip is:

$$x_{tip,tremor} = 0.12 \times 100 \text{ µm} = 12 \text{ µm}$$

This is **sub-cellular scale precision** — far beyond human capability.

**Step 6: Conclude the benefit.**

$$\boxed{\text{Tremor Amplitude Reduction} = \left(1 - 0.12\right) \times 100\% \approx 88\%}$$

This explains why robotic microsurgery (e.g., **supermicrosurgical lymphaticovenular anastomosis** at 0.3–0.5 mm vessels) is feasible.

### 3.2 Derivation: Telemedicine Latency Constraint for Safe Tele-surgery

**Step 1: Define the closed-loop delay.**

The round-trip delay $L_{RT}$ is composed of:

$$L_{RT} = L_{prop} + L_{encode} + L_{decode} + L_{net} + L_{control}$$

where:
- $L_{prop}$: signal propagation (geosynchronous satellite: ~250 ms one-way)
- $L_{encode}$: video encoding (~20 ms)
- $L_{decode}$: video decoding (~20 ms)
- $L_{net}$: network jitter (~30 ms)
- $L_{control}$: robotic controller computation (~10 ms)

**Step 2: Sum the components.**

$$L_{RT} \approx 250 + 20 + 20 + 30 + 10 = 330 \text{ ms (satellite-based)}$$

$$L_{RT} \approx 30 + 20 + 20 + 30 + 10 = 110 \text{ ms (fiber-based, 7,000 km)}$$

**Step 3: Compare against stability threshold.**

For haptic teleoperation, the **Lawrence stability criterion** (1993) states:

$$L_{RT} \le L_{max} = \frac{\pi}{2 \omega_c} \approx 200 \text{ ms (for }\omega_c = 8 \text{ rad/s)}$$

**Step 4: Draw the engineering conclusion.**

- **Fiber-based tele-surgery (Lindbergh, 2001):** $L_{RT} \approx 110$ ms < 200 ms ✓
- **Satellite-based tele-surgery:** $L_{RT} \approx 330$ ms > 200 ms ✗ (degrades haptics)

**Conclusion:** Modern tele-surgery is geographically constrained to **fiber-connected distances** (~10,000 km) unless **predictive haptic displays** or **event-based control** are introduced.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Benefit-to-Stakeholder Mapping

This matrix is the **single most important KTU 14-mark answer framework**. Use it for any "Discuss the key benefits of medical robots" question.

| Benefit | Surgeon | Patient | Hospital | Society |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Precision / Tremor Filtering | ✓✓✓ (surgical quality) | ✓✓ (safety) | ✓ (reputation) | — |
| Minimally Invasive Access | ✓ (visualization) | ✓✓✓ (less pain, faster recovery) | ✓✓ (shorter stay) | ✓ (productivity) |
| Tele-surgery | ✓✓ (reach) | ✓✓✓ (rural access) | ✓ (specialist leverage) | ✓✓✓ (health equity) |
| Ergonomics | ✓✓✓ (career longevity) | — | ✓ (fewer errors) | ✓ (workforce health) |
| 3D HD Visualization | ✓✓✓ (confidence) | ✓ (outcomes) | — | — |
| Data Logging & Analytics | ✓ (training) | — | ✓✓ (QA, legal) | — |
| 24/7 Logistics | — | ✓ (faster service) | ✓✓✓ (cost saving) | — |
| Infection Control | — | ✓✓✓ (safety) | ✓✓✓ (HAI cost ↓) | ✓ (public health) |
| Pharmacy Automation | — | ✓✓✓ (zero dispensing error) | ✓✓ (efficiency) | — |
| Rehabilitation Continuity | — | ✓✓✓ (recovery) | ✓ (throughput) | ✓ (disability burden ↓) |

> **Legend:** ✓ = indirect benefit, ✓✓ = direct benefit, ✓✓✓ = critical/transformative benefit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Benefit-Cascade Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** mapping how the engineering features of a medical robot cascade into clinical, operational, and societal benefits. This diagram directly supports 14-mark answers in KTU ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[Surgeon Hand Motion]:::in
    Input2[Pre-operative Imaging CT MRI]:::in
    Input3[Patient Anatomy Real-time]:::in
    
    subgraph Core1[Engineered Feature Layer]
        F1[Tremor Filtration LPF]:::feature
        F2[Motion Scaling 1 colon 3]:::feature
        F3[Force Scaling and Haptics]:::feature
        F4[3D HD Visualization]:::feature
        F5[Image Registration and Fusion]:::feature
    end
    
    subgraph Core2[Robotic Functional Layer]
        R1[Miniaturized End Effector 8 mm]:::robot
        R2[6 to 7 DOF Wrist]:::robot
        R3[Sub mm Position Accuracy]:::robot
        R4[AI based Path Planning]:::robot
    end
    
    subgraph Core3[Clinical Benefit Layer]
        C1[Enhanced Precision]:::benefit
        C2[Minimally Invasive Access]:::benefit

        C5[Faster Recovery]:::benefit
    end
    
    subgraph Core4[Operational Benefit Layer]
        O1[Surgeon Ergonomics and Less Fatigue]:::ops

    end
    
    subgraph Core5[Societal Benefit Layer]
        S1[Rural Access via Tele Surgery]:::soc
        S2[Reduced Healthcare Cost Long Term]:::soc

    end
    
    Input1 --> F1

    F1 --> R3
    F2 --> R1
    F3 --> R2
    F4 --> R1
    F5 --> R3
    
    R1 --> C1
    R2 --> C1
    R3 --> C1
    R3 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    
    R1 --> O1
    R3 --> O2
    R4 --> O3
    R4 --> O4

    O1 --> S3
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> S4
    
    classDef in fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
