```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Generation 1 AESOP 1994 Voice Controlled] -->|Lessons Learned| G2[Generation 2 EndoAssist 2000 Head Controlled]
    G2 -->|Lessons Learned| G3[Generation 3 ViKY 2007 Compact Endoscopic]
    G3 -->|Lessons Learned| G4[Generation 4 FreeHand 2010 Foot Controlled]
    G4 -->|Lessons Learned| G5[Generation 5 SoloAssist 2018 Hands Free Modular]
    G1 -.Metric.- M1[DOF 5 to 6 Mass 14 kg]
    G2 -.Metric.- M2[DOF 5 Mass 8 kg]
    G3 -.Metric.- M3[DOF 5 Mass 0.6 kg]
    G4 -.Metric.- M4[DOF 5 Mass 4 kg]
    G5 -.Metric.- M5[DOF 5 to 7 Mass 3.5 kg]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 (Model)]**  
*Define Auxiliary Surgical Support Robot. How is it functionally different from a telesurgical robot like the Da Vinci?*

**Model Answer:**  
An Auxiliary Surgical Support Robot is a medical robot that operates within the sterile field of the operating room to hold and position surgical instruments or cameras under the direct supervisory control of the surgeon, without performing the actual surgical act. It is functionally distinct from a telesurgical system (e.g., Da Vinci) in three aspects: **(i)** it works in *cooperative autonomy* with the surgeon present in the OR, not via a remote console; **(ii)** its task is *holding and repositioning*, not *manipulation and excision*; **(iii)** it requires only **5–7 DOF** versus the **12+ DOF** of a bimanual teleoperator. Examples include AESOP, EndoAssist, and ViKY.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]**  
*What is a Remote Center of Motion (RCM) and why is it critical for laparoscope-holding robots?*

**Model Answer:**  
The **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** is a kinematic constraint where a robot's end-effector pivots about a fixed point in space that is *not* located on any of its joints. In laparoscopic surgery, this point coincides with the **trocar insertion site** on the patient's abdominal wall. The RCM is critical because: **(i)** the trocar seals the incision; the instrument must rotate *about* it, never translate *through* it; **(ii)** it minimizes tissue trauma at the entry point; **(iii)** it allows the instrument shaft to sweep through a wide angular range while keeping the entry wound stationary. Mathematically, the constraint is:

$$
(x-x_0)^2 + (y-y_0)^2 + (z-z_0)^2 = r^2
$$

where $r$ is the fixed insertion depth, typically 0.05–0.15 m.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The internal choice gives the student the freedom to *attempt either Option A or Option B*.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of an AESOP-type voice-controlled laparoscope-positioning robot. Explain the role of the speech recognition engine, the inverse kinematics module, and the RCM constraint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF planar RCM arm has link lengths $L_1 = 0.25$ m and $L_2 = 0.20$ m. The trocar is at the origin, and the desired camera position is at $r_{RCM} = 0.15$ m, $\theta = 45°$, with the elbow joint at $90°$ relative to the upper arm. Calculate the required joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, and verify the RCM constraint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**  
The architecture has five layers (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram above):

- **Input Layer:** Directional microphone array captures surgeon's voice commands (e.g., "AESOP, move up").
- **Signal Processing Layer:** DSP filters OR noise (suction, cautery, ventilation); speech recognition engine converts voice to text using a small grammar of ~30 commands.
- **Decision Layer:** Command validator checks safety (e.g., "move down 10 cm" might be rejected if the camera is already 2 cm from tissue). The trajectory planner generates a smooth Cartesian path, and the inverse kinematics module computes joint angles satisfying the RCM constraint.
- **Servo Layer:** PID controllers drive each joint motor; current limiters enforce $F \leq 2$ N to prevent tissue injury.
- **Actuator Layer:** Five servo motors actuate the arm holding the laparoscope.

The RCM constraint is enforced by the inverse kinematics module: the distance from the trocar to the camera tip is held constant.

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step:**

Given $L_1 = 0.25$ m, $L_2 = 0.20$ m, $r_{RCM} = 0.15$ m.

**Step 1: Yaw angle (azimuth):**  
The azimuth angle equals the desired camera angle:
$$
\theta_1 = 45°
$$

**Step 2: Radial reach equation:**  
The planar reach is:
$$
r = L_1 \cos\theta_2 + L_2 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)
$$

Given the elbow joint is at $90°$ relative to the upper arm, $\theta_3 = 90°$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.15 &= 0.25\cos\theta_2 + 0.20\cos(\theta_2 + 90°) \\
0.15 &= 0.25\cos\theta_2 - 0.20\sin\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve using substitution** — let $A = 0.25$, $B = -0.20$:

$$
A\cos\theta_2 + B\sin\theta_2 = C
$$

where $C = 0.15$, $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.1025} = 0.3202$ m.

$$
\sin(\theta_2 + \phi) = \dfrac{C}{R} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.3202} = 0.4685
$$

where $\phi = \arctan(A/B) = \arctan(0.25/(-0.20)) = \arctan(-1.25) = -51.34°$ (second quadrant correction: $\phi = 128.66°$).

$$
\theta_2 + 128.66° = \arcsin(0.4685) = 27.94° \text{ or } 152.06°
$$

**Step 4: Two valid solutions:**

- **Solution 1 (elbow up):** $\theta_2 = 27.94° - 128.66° = -100.72°$ — *rejected* (mechanically infeasible).
- **Solution 2 (elbow down):** $\theta_2 = 152.06° - 128.66° = 23.40°$ — *accepted*.

$$
\boxed{\theta_1 = 45°, \quad \theta_2 = 23.40°, \quad \theta_3 = 90°}
$$

**Step 5: RCM verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= 0.25\cos(23.40°) + 0.20\cos(23.40° + 90°) \\
&= 0.25(0.9176) + 0.20\cos(113.40°) \\
&= 0.2294 + 0.20(-0.3927) \\
&= 0.2294 - 0.0785 \\
&= 0.1509 \text{ m} \approx 0.15 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The RCM constraint is satisfied to within rounding error (0.6% deviation, well within the 0.5 mm tolerance for a 150 mm reach).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a)** Compare voice, head-motion, and foot-pedal control modes for surgical support robots. List two clinical advantages and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the maximum safe interaction force on bowel tissue for a circular instrument tip of radius **2 mm**, given the tissue yield stress $\sigma = 0.2$ N/mm². If the robot uses a current-limited DC motor with torque constant $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A$ and a 50:1 gear reduction, what is the maximum allowable motor current to keep the joint torque below 5 N·m? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Table:**

| Control Mode | Clinical Advantage 1 | Clinical Advantage 2 | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voice** | Hands-free, ergonomic | Surgeon's natural modality | OR noise causes false triggers |
| **Head-motion** | Silent, very fast | No microphone needed | Surgeon neck fatigue after 2–3 h |
| **Foot-pedal** | High reliability, robust | Decoupled from voice | Surgeon hands still on instruments |

**Part (b) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Contact area for circular tip of radius $r = 2$ mm:**

$$
A_{contact} = \pi r^2 = \pi (2)^2 = 4\pi = 12.566 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Maximum safe force:**

$$
F_{max} = \sigma \cdot A_{contact} = 0.2 \times 12.566 = 2.513 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Joint torque constraint (after gear reduction):**

Given the maximum allowable joint torque $\tau_{max} = 5$ N·m and gear ratio $N = 50$:

$$
\tau_{motor,max} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{N} = \dfrac{5}{50} = 0.1 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Motor current limit:**

$$
I_{max} = \dfrac{\tau_{motor,max}}{K_t} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.05} = 2.0 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{max} \approx 2.51 \text{ N}, \quad I_{max} = 2.0 \text{ A}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students frequently forget to convert gear ratio (dimensionless) into torque division (multiplicative). *Remember: $\tau_{load} = N \times \tau_{motor}$, not the other way around.*
> 2. **Contact area formula:** A circular tip uses $A = \pi r^2$, not $A = \pi d^2$ (this is a frequent 1-mark error).
> 3. **RCM constraint violation:** When solving inverse kinematics, students often solve for joint angles *without* verifying the RCM equation. The 1-mark verification step is the most commonly skipped.
> 4. **Voice vs head vs pedal:** Students frequently describe one mode in detail and the others in a single line. KTU requires *balanced treatment* for full marks.
> 5. **Missing units:** Numerical answers without units lose 0.5 to 1 mark each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Auxiliary Surgical Support Robots**

- **Definition:** Robots that hold/position instruments *inside* the sterile field, under surgeon supervision; **NOT** telesurgical.
- **Primary clinical role:** Laparoscope holding — the "third hand" of the surgeon.
- **Key design constraint:** **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** at the trocar — non-negotiable.
- **Standard DOF count:** **5** (3 position + 2 orientation) for camera holders; up to **7** for instrument positioners.
- **Three control modes:** **Voice** (e.g., AESOP), **Head-motion** (e.g., EndoAssist), **Foot-pedal** (e.g., FreeHand).
- **Force safety limit:** $F_{max} \leq$ **2 N** on soft tissue (yield stress $\sigma \approx 0.2$ N/mm², $A \approx 10$ mm²).
- **Repeatability:** $\leq$ **0.5 mm**.
- **Voice latency:** $\leq$ **300 ms**.
- **Sterilization:** Must survive **134 °C** autoclave, hence use of drapes and motor-base placement.
- **IK form:** RCM-enforced inverse kinematics with constant $r_{RCM}$ — solved via cosine law.
- **Key examples:** **AESOP, EndoAssist, ViKY, FreeHand, SoloAssist, Socrates.**
- **MTBF requirement:** $\geq$ **1000 hours** (FDA-grade).
- **Steadiness ratio:** $S = \theta_{actual}/\theta_{commanded} \geq 0.95$ for camera stability.
- **Killer formula to memorize:** $F_{max} = \sigma A$ and $\tau_{motor} = \tau_{joint}/N$.
- **Clinical impact:** Reduces surgeon fatigue, eliminates camera shake, enables solo laparoscopic surgery.
- **Engineering trade-off:** More DOF = more dexterity, but heavier distal mass and higher error.
- **Future trend:** AI-driven intent prediction + miniaturization → sub-1 kg modular camera holders.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Paradigms in Surgical Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Paradigms in Surgical Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Control Paradigms in Surgical Robots

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Control Paradigms in Surgical Robots):**
> A *control paradigm* in surgical robotics is the formal mathematical and architectural framework that governs how a surgical manipulator acquires, processes, and translates surgeon intent (haptic, visual, cognitive) into precise, safe, and stable end-effector motion inside the patient's anatomy. The dominant paradigms include **teleoperation (bilateral) control**, **impedance/admittance control**, **visual servoing**, **constraint-based / Virtual Fixture (VF) control**, and **supervisory / shared autonomy control**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine driving a heavy truck by holding a small toy steering wheel at home. You turn the toy wheel, and a powerful hydraulic mechanism turns the actual truck wheels on the road. This is **teleoperation**. Now imagine the truck had a "force-feedback steering wheel" — when the truck hits a pothole, your hands at home feel the jolt. That jolt is **haptic feedback**, and the scheme that *mathematically couples* your hand to the road is the **bilateral control paradigm**. Surgical robots are exactly this — but the "truck" is a 5–8 mm instrument inside a beating heart, and the "pothole" is living tissue. So the control paradigm must guarantee **sub-millimetre positional accuracy**, **bounded interaction forces (≤ 2 N for soft tissue)**, **tremor cancellation (~8 Hz biomechanical tremor, 50 µm amplitude)**, and **scaled motion (typically 3:1 to 10:1)**.

### 1.2 Why Control Paradigms Matter (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industrial position control (PID on joint encoders) is **unsafe and insufficient** for surgery because:
> 1. The surgeon is **in the loop** → control must be *compliant*, not rigid.
> 2. The environment (tissue) is **deformable and uncertain** → no fixed model exists.
> 3. Safety standards (e.g., **ISO 14971**, **IEC 80601-2-77**) require **hardware + control-layer fault tolerance**.
> 4. The **Remote Center of Motion (RCM)** constraint demands a non-holonomic kinematic lock that classic joint-PID cannot enforce.

The standard quantitative targets accepted across KTU-level case studies (e.g., **da Vinci Si/Xi**, **MAKO**, **ROSA Spine**) are:
- **Position accuracy:** 0.1–0.5 mm RMS
- **Force resolution:** 0.05 N
- **Communication latency budget:** **≤ 1 ms** (one-way) for stable bilateral teleoperation
- **Master-to-slave motion scaling:** typically 3:1 to 5:1
- **Maximum safe tissue interaction force:** **2 N** (soft tissue), **10 N** (bone)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Surgical Robot Control

A surgical robot's control architecture is built on four orthogonal pillars. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *map a given clinical task (e.g., suturing, ablation) onto the right pillar*.

### Pillar I — Teleoperation (Bilateral) Control

- **Architecture:** Master console (input device, often a 7-DOF haptic interface) ↔ Communication Channel ↔ Patient-side slave manipulator.
- **Why needed:** Surgeon must *feel* the tissue; pure open-loop position command is dangerous.
- **Two canonical schemes:**
  1. **Position-Position (PP) Architecture** — both sides track each other's position; force is inferred.
  2. **Position-Force (PF) / Force-Position (FP) Architecture** — one side commands position, the other commands force (asymmetric).
- **The Lawrence Four-Channel (4-Ch) Architecture** is the gold standard benchmark:
  - $C_1$ — Position feedforward (master → slave)
  - $C_2$ — Force feedforward (slave → master)
  - $C_3$ — Position feedback (slave → master, with $\hat{Z}_t$ environment)
  - $C_4$ — Force feedback (master → slave, with $\hat{Z}_h$ hand impedance)

### Pillar II — Impedance / Admittance Control

- **Core idea (Hogan, 1985):** Instead of controlling position OR force, control the *dynamic relationship* between them.
- **Mechanical analogy:** The robot tip behaves like a programmable **mass-spring-damper**:
$$F_{tip} = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx$$
  where $M$ is virtual mass, $B$ is virtual damping, $K$ is virtual stiffness, all **tunable by the surgeon** in real time.
- **Admittance** = inverse of impedance: command *motion in response to measured force*. Used when environment is stiff (bone).
- **Impedance** = command *force in response to motion error*. Used when environment is compliant (soft tissue).

### Pillar III — Visual Servoing (VS)

- **Definition:** A control loop closed through a vision sensor (endoscope / microscope) rather than joint encoders.
- **Two flavours:**
  1. **Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS):** Error defined in 2D image space $s = (u, v)$ pixel coordinates.
  2. **Position-Based Visual Servoing (PBVS):** Error defined in 3D Cartesian space, reconstructed from images.
- **Why critical in surgery:** Occlusion, specular reflection from wet tissue, and the narrow field-of-view of the endoscope make VS uniquely challenging — hence **hybrid VS** and **deep-learning-augmented VS** are active research areas.

### Pillar IV — Constraint-Based Control (Virtual Fixtures & RCM)

- **Virtual Fixture (VF):** Software-generated forbidden regions or guide surfaces overlaid on the surgeon's motion — like *invisible rails*.
- **RCM (Remote Center of Motion):** A geometric constraint forcing the surgical tool to pivot about a fixed entry port on the patient's body wall, preventing any lateral force on the incision.
- **Two VF subtypes:**
  1. **Guidance VF** — attract the tool toward an ideal path.
  2. **Forbidden-Region VF (Stop VF)** — repel the tool from a hazardous zone (e.g., a nerve or major vessel).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` (not `|`) inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown parsing.**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Engineering Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bilateral position tracking error | $e_p = x_m - \alpha x_s$ | $x_m$ = master pos, $x_s$ = slave pos, $\alpha$ = motion scaling | $\alpha = 1/3$ to $1/10$ |
| 2 | Force reflection gain | $F_m = \beta F_s$ | $F_m$ = master feedback force, $F_s$ = slave–tissue force, $\beta$ = force scale | $\beta = 1$ to $3$ |
| 3 | Mechanical impedance | $Z(s) = M s^2 + B s + K$ | $M$ virtual mass, $B$ damping, $K$ stiffness | $K = 50$–$500$ N/m in soft tissue |
| 4 | Admittance model | $X(s) = Y(s) \cdot F(s)$ | $Y(s) = 1/Z(s)$ | — |
| 5 | Stability condition (simplified) | $\tau \leq \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu)}{\omega_c}$ | $\tau$ comms delay, $\mu$ gain margin, $\omega_c$ crossover freq | $\tau \leq 1$ ms |
| 6 | Image-Based VS velocity | $\dot{q} = -\lambda \, L^+ \, e$ | $L^+$ Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse of interaction matrix, $e = s - s^*$ pixel error | $\lambda = 0.1$–$1.0$ |
| 7 | RCM kinematic constraint | $(p_{tool} - p_{port}) \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | $p_{port}$ = entry port, $\hat{n}$ = trocar axis | Constraint enforced in IK |
| 8 | Critical damping for admittance | $B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{M K}$ | — | Use $B = 0.7 \, B_{crit}$ in practice |
| 9 | Surgical motion scaling | $\dot{x}_{slave} = (1/\alpha) \dot{x}_{master}$ | — | $\alpha \in [3, 10]$ |
| 10 | Interaction matrix (IBVS) | $L = \begin{bmatrix} -1/Z & 0 & x/Z & xy & -(1+x^2) & y \\ 0 & -1/Z & y/Z & 1+y^2 & -xy & -x \end{bmatrix}$ | $(x,y)$ = normalized image coords, $Z$ = depth | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **da Vinci Surgical System (Intuitive Surgical)** — uses PP bilateral teleoperation with **3:1 motion scaling** and **~50 Hz** force-reflecting update; surgeon master commands slave via CAN-bus / proprietary fiber link.
- **MAKO RIO (Stryker)** — orthopedic, uses **impedance control** for haptic bone-cutting; surgeon "feels" the virtual bone boundary.
- **ROSA Spine / ROSA Brain (Zimmer Biomet)** — **constraint-based + virtual fixtures** to lock trajectory to pre-op plan.
- **CyberKnife** — uses **supervisory / shared control** with image-guided beam steering — robot repositions autonomously while physician validates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When KTU asks *"Which control paradigm is best for bone-milling vs. soft-tissue suturing?"* — answer is **impedance control for bone** (high stiffness, position-correct) and **admittance / force control for soft tissue** (compliance-aware).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Mechanical Impedance Relationship (Hogan 1985)

### Step 1 — Define the disturbance

Let the surgeon's tool tip have a commanded (desired) position $x_d(t)$ and an actual position $x(t)$. The deviation is the position error:

$$
e(t) = x_d(t) - x(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Define the environment force

The tissue exerts a contact force $F_e(t)$ on the tool tip. By Newton's second law applied to the (rigid) tool, the control law must produce an *equivalent tip force* $F_c$ to balance $F_e$ plus any desired dynamics.

### Step 3 — Write the impedance equation

We require the tool tip to behave as a second-order mass-spring-damper. Equating the control force $F_c$ to the desired dynamic law:

$$
F_c(t) = M \, \ddot{e}(t) + B \, \dot{e}(t) + K \, e(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Laplace domain (zero initial conditions)

$$
F_c(s) = \left( M s^2 + B s + K \right) E(s)
$$

### Step 5 — Define mechanical impedance

The mechanical impedance $Z(s)$ is the ratio of force to motion in the Laplace domain:

$$
Z(s) \; \stackrel{\text{def}}{=} \; \frac{F_c(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; M s^2 + B s + K
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for natural frequency and damping ratio

Match $Z(s)$ to the canonical second-order form $M(s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2)$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{M K}}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** If $M = 1$ kg, $K = 400$ N/m, and we desire $\zeta = 0.7$ (critically-damped feel for suturing):

$$
B = 2 \zeta \sqrt{M K} = 2(0.7)\sqrt{1 \cdot 400} = 1.4 \times 20 = 28 \; \text{N·s/m}
$$

**Logic of the conversion:** The canonical second-order ODE form is a universal benchmark across all control engineering; matching the impedance to it lets the surgeon tune *feel* using physically meaningful parameters $\omega_n$ (responsiveness) and $\zeta$ (smoothness).

## 3.2 Derivation II — Lawrence Four-Channel Architecture Block-Algebra

### Step 1 — Define hybrid matrices

For a two-port system (master $m$ and slave $s$), define the hybrid matrix $H$ relating velocities ($V_m, V_s$) and forces ($F_m, F_s$):

$$
\begin{bmatrix} F_m \\ V_s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_m \\ -F_s \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Insert controllers $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ and impedances

The closed-loop hybrid matrix becomes:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} -C_3 & C_2 \\ C_1 & -C_4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -\hat{Z}_h & 0 \\ 0 & \hat{Z}_e \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Impedance matching (perfect transparency)

For the surgeon to "feel" the tissue as if his hand were directly on it, the **ideal hybrid matrix** is:

$$
H_{ideal} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This means $F_m = F_s$ (force matching) and $V_s = V_m$ (velocity tracking), i.e., the system is **perfectly transparent**.

### Step 4 — Solve for required controller gains

Setting the difference $H - H_{ideal} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3 &= \hat{Z}_h \\
C_4 &= \hat{Z}_e \\
C_1 &= 1 \\
C_2 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of the conversion:** In real systems, $C_1 < 1$ (motion scaling) and $C_2 < 1$ (force scaling). This *breaks* perfect transparency but improves **stability under time delay** $\tau$. The well-known **Hannaford stability bound** for delay-limited teleoperation is derived by requiring the closed-loop poles remain in the left-half-plane despite $\tau > 0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Mandated for "Compare & Contrast" Questions)

| Property | Impedance Control | Admittance Control | Position-Position Teleop | IBVS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Measurement needed** | Position (encoder) | Force (sensor) | Both encoders | Camera pixels |
| **Best tissue type** | Soft / compliant | Stiff / hard (bone) | Soft + stiff | Surface tracking |
| **Bandwidth** | High (1 kHz) | Medium (100 Hz) | Medium (50–100 Hz) | Low (10–30 Hz) |
| **Computational load** | Low | Medium | Low | High (image proc.) |
| **Latency sensitivity** | Low | Medium | **High** (stability) | Medium |
| **Typical KTU example task** | Suturing | Drilling | Generic MIS | Tumor targeting |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Visual Servoing Convergence

A surgical microscope-mounted camera observes a 2 mm tumour at normalized image coords $s^* = (0.1, -0.05)$. The current image feature is $s = (0.4, 0.2)$. The image Jacobian determinant is $\det(L) = 0.05$ (well-conditioned). With $\lambda = 0.5$, compute one step of $\dot{q}$.

$$
e = s - s^* = (0.4 - 0.1, \; 0.2 - (-0.05)) = (0.3, \; 0.25)
$$

$$
\dot{q} = -\lambda \, L^+ e
$$

Assuming a simplified 2-DOF camera with $L^+ = (1/\det L) \cdot I$:

$$
\dot{q} = -0.5 \times \frac{1}{0.05} \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix} = -10 \times \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 \\ 0.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.0 \\ -2.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Logic of the conversion:** The negative sign indicates the camera must *rotate to reduce the error*, driving $s \to s^*$. The pseudo-inverse $L^+$ converts image-space error into joint-space velocity command.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master-Slave Teleoperation Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    surgeon[Surgeon Hand\nF_h, V_h] --> master[Master Haptic Device\n7-DOF]
    master -->|position V_m| c1[Controller C1\nPosition Feedforward]
    c1 --> comm[Communication Channel\nDelay tau]
    comm --> slave[Slave Manipulator\nPatient Side]
    slave -->|tip force F_s| tissue[Living Tissue\nF_e]
    tissue -->|reaction| slave
    slave -->|measured F_s| c2[Controller C2\nForce Feedforward]
    c2 --> commback[Return Channel]
    commback --> c3[Controller C3\nForce Feedback]
    c3 -->|reflected F_m| master
    master -->|impedance Z_h| surgeon
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Surgical-Robot Control Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer4[Layer 4: Supervisory Autonomy\nTask-level: suturing sequence]
    layer3[Layer 3: Shared Control\nBlending autonomy and surgeon]
    layer2[Layer 2: Virtual Fixtures and RCM\nConstraint enforcement]
    layer1[Layer 1: Impedance / Admittance\nTip-level dynamics]
    layer0[Layer 0: Joint Servo\nPID on encoder]
    layer4 --> layer3 --> layer2 --> layer1 --> layer0
```

## 4.3 IBVS Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    desired[Desired Feature s*\ntumor pixel coord] --> subtractor1((+ -))
    measured[Current Feature s\nfrom endoscope image]
    measured --> subtractor1
    subtractor1 -->|error e| lplus[Interaction Matrix L+\nImage Jacobian Inverse]
    lplus --> gain[Gain lambda]
    gain --> jointspeed[Joint Velocity dot q]
    jointspeed --> robot[Surgical Manipulator]
    robot --> scene[Patient Anatomy]
    scene --> camera[Endoscope Camera]
    camera -->|image processing| measured
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Paradigm to Pick

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Identify Surgical Task] --> q1{Is tissue\nstiff or compliant?}
    q1 -->|Stiff bone| admittance[Use Admittance Control\nForce-sensor based]
    q1 -->|Compliant soft tissue| q2{Need direct\nvisual feedback?}
    q2 -->|Yes| ibvs[Use IBVS / PBVS\nVisual Servoing]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Surgeon physically\npresent at console?}
    q3 -->|Yes| teleop[Use Bilateral Teleoperation\nwith haptic feedback]
    q3 -->|No, autonomous| super[Use Supervisory\nShared Autonomy]
```

## 4.5 RCM (Remote Center of Motion) Constraint Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    port[Trocar Port on Body Wall\nFixed Point p_port] --> rcm[Geometric Constraint\nDot product equals zero]
    tool[Tool Tip p_tool] --> rcm
    slave[Slave Arm IK Solver] -->|joint angles| rcm
    rcm -->|enforce pivot only| slave
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the Remote Center of Motion (RCM) constraint in surgical robotics. Why is it a non-holonomic constraint at the joint level?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The RCM constraint enforces that a surgical tool pivots about a fixed entry port on the patient's body wall, preventing any lateral force on the incision. Mathematically, $(p_{tool} - p_{port}) \cdot \hat{n} = 0$, where $\hat{n}$ is the trocar axis. At the joint level it is non-holonomic because it constrains velocities (not just positions) — the tool tip must move on a sphere around $p_{port}$ while the shaft angle changes, requiring the manipulator's joint-rate vector $\dot{q}$ to lie in a constrained null-space, i.e., $\dot{q} \in \ker(J_{RCM})$. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for non-holonomic justification.)

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Impedance and Admittance control. State one clinical task where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Impedance control:** Tool tip behaves as a programmable mass-spring-damper; motion error drives a control *force*. Measurement = position. Best for **soft tissue suturing** (2 marks for 1-line each distinction).
- **Admittance control:** Tool tip behaves as the *inverse*; measured *force* drives a control *velocity*. Measurement = force. Best for **bone milling / drilling** (1 mark for clinical example).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Reference]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the **Lawrence Four-Channel (4-Ch) bilateral teleoperation architecture** for surgical robots. Define each controller $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ and write the closed-loop hybrid matrix equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Lawrence 4-Ch architecture is a two-port network model of master–slave teleoperation. The four controllers are:
- $C_1$ — Position feedforward from master to slave (drives slave motion).
- $C_2$ — Force feedforward from slave to master (reflects tissue force).
- $C_3$ — Position feedback (compensates for hand impedance $\hat{Z}_h$).
- $C_4$ — Force feedback (compensates for environment impedance $\hat{Z}_e$).

The closed-loop hybrid matrix is:

$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} -C_3 & C_2 \\ C_1 & -C_4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -\hat{Z}_h & 0 \\ 0 & \hat{Z}_e \end{bmatrix}
$$

For perfect transparency, $H = H_{ideal}$ where $H_{ideal} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. This gives $C_1 = C_2 = 1$ and $C_3 = \hat{Z}_h$, $C_4 = \hat{Z}_e$. In practice, the gains are reduced below unity to maintain stability under communication delay $\tau$. **(3 marks for block diagram description, 2 marks for definitions, 2 marks for the matrix equation and transparency condition.)**

**(b)** A teleoperated surgical system operates with a communication delay of $\tau = 0.5$ ms. The crossover frequency of the master controller is $\omega_c = 50$ rad/s, and the gain margin is $\mu = 0.5$. Verify whether the system satisfies the Hannaford–Raju stability bound for safe bilateral operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Hannaford stability bound (simplified form for position-position teleoperation) requires:

$$
\tau \leq \frac{\ln(1+\mu)}{\omega_c}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\ln(1+0.5)}{50} = \frac{\ln(1.5)}{50} = \frac{0.4055}{50} = 8.11 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} \approx 8.1 \; \text{ms}
$$

Comparison with actual delay:

$$
\tau_{actual} = 0.5 \; \text{ms} \;\leq\; 8.1 \; \text{ms} \; \checkmark
$$

Since $\tau_{actual} \ll \tau_{max}$, the system **satisfies the stability bound** and bilateral teleoperation will remain stable. **(2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for numerical answer, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain **Hogan's impedance control** framework as applied to surgical robots. Derive the relationship between virtual stiffness $K$, virtual mass $M$, and damping ratio $\zeta$. Why is critical damping preferred for suturing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Hogan's framework treats the tool tip as a programmable second-order mechanical system. The control force at the tip is:

$$
F_c(s) = M s^2 X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s)
$$

where $X(s) = x_d(s) - x(s)$ is the position error. The mechanical impedance is $Z(s) = M s^2 + B s + K$.

Matching to canonical form:

$$
Z(s) = M \left( s^2 + \frac{B}{M} s + \frac{K}{M} \right) = M \left( s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 \right)
$$

This gives:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}, \quad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{M K}}
$$

For suturing, critical damping ($\zeta = 1$) is preferred to:
1. Eliminate tool overshoot (which could tear tissue).
2. Provide the fastest non-oscillatory response.
3. Give the surgeon a predictable, smooth "feel."

**(2 marks derivation, 2 marks for $\omega_n$/$\zeta$ expressions, 3 marks for clinical justification.)**

**(b)** A surgical tool is controlled by an impedance law with $M = 0.5$ kg and $K = 200$ N/m. Compute the **critical damping coefficient** $B_{crit}$, and the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$. If the surgeon requests a softer "feel" by halving $K$, recompute both for the new stiffness. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1 — Original stiffness $K = 200$ N/m:**

$$
B_{crit} = 2\sqrt{M K} = 2\sqrt{0.5 \times 200} = 2\sqrt{100} = 20 \; \text{N·s/m}
$$

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \; \text{rad/s} \approx 3.18 \; \text{Hz}
$$

**Case 2 — Reduced stiffness $K_{new} = 100$ N/m:**

$$
B_{crit,new} = 2\sqrt{0.5 \times 100} = 2\sqrt{50} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14 \; \text{N·s/m}
$$

$$
\omega_{n,new} = \sqrt{\frac{100}{0.5}} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14 \; \text{rad/s} \approx 2.25 \; \text{Hz}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Halving $K$ reduces the natural frequency by $\sqrt{2}$ and reduces $B_{crit}$ by $\sqrt{2}$, giving a "softer, slower" feel — appropriate for delicate work near nerves. **(2 marks formula, 3 marks calculation case 1, 2 marks calculation case 2.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Very Common Mark Loss):**
> 1. **Confusing impedance and admittance direction** — Remember: *Impedance* = Force response to **motion** error; *Admittance* = **Motion** response to force input. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_1/C_2$ and $C_3/C_4$** in Lawrence 4-Ch — $C_1, C_2$ are *forward paths*; $C_3, C_4$ are *feedback paths*.
> 3. **Stating motion scaling as $1:3$ vs $3:1$** incorrectly. Convention: master moves 3 cm → slave moves 1 cm → scaling factor is **3:1 (master:slave)**.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\ln(1+\mu)$** in the Hannaford bound and writing just $\mu/\omega_c$ — instant 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers — KTU strictly penalises unit-less numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Four pillars** = Teleoperation, Impedance/Admittance, Visual Servoing, Constraint-based (VF + RCM).
- ✅ **Lawrence 4-Ch** = $C_1, C_2$ forward; $C_3, C_4$ feedback; ideal transparency needs $C_1 = C_2 = 1$.
- ✅ **Hannaford bound** = $\tau \le \ln(1+\mu)/\omega_c$; keep $\tau \le 1$ ms for safe surgery.
- ✅ **Impedance** $Z(s) = M s^2 + B s + K$; $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$, $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{MK})$.
- ✅ **RCM** = pivot about fixed port → non-holonomic joint constraint $(p_{tool} - p_{port}) \cdot \hat{n} = 0$.
- ✅ **Virtual Fixtures** = *Guidance VF* (attract) + *Forbidden-Region VF* (repel).
- ✅ **IBVS** = error in 2D image space, uses interaction matrix $L$, pseudo-inverse $L^+$.
- ✅ **PBVS** = error in 3D Cartesian space, requires camera calibration & depth.
- ✅ **Soft tissue** → **Impedance** (use position sensor); **Bone** → **Admittance** (use force sensor).
- ✅ **Motion scaling** typical **3:1 to 10:1** (master : slave).
- ✅ **Critical damping** $\zeta = 1$ is preferred for suturing to avoid overshoot and tissue damage.
- ✅ **Safety force limit** ≈ **2 N (soft tissue)**, **10 N (bone)** — ISO-standardised thresholds.
- ✅ **Communication latency budget** ≤ **1 ms one-way** for stable haptic feedback.
- ✅ **da Vinci uses Position-Position** bilateral teleoperation with 3:1 scaling and ~50 Hz update.
- ✅ **MAKO uses impedance control** for haptic bone-shaping; **CyberKnife** uses **supervisory** shared autonomy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Applications of Robots in Healthcare
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT602 - FIELD AND SERVICE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other Applications of Robots in Healthcare

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Healthcare Robotics (Extended Applications)** refers to the deployment of autonomous, semi-autonomous, or teleoperated robotic systems across clinical, rehabilitative, assistive, pharmaceutical, and patient-care environments — **excluding** conventional industrial or primary surgical manipulator platforms. These systems leverage **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, **end-effector precision**, **haptic feedback**, **machine vision**, and **closed-loop control** to augment, substitute, or enhance human capabilities in medical workflows.

In the context of KTU PCRUT602 (Field and Service Robotics), the **"Other Applications"** umbrella specifically addresses service-oriented healthcare robots that operate *outside* the conventional master–slave surgical console paradigm. They are categorized under **ISO 13482** (Personal Care Robots) and **ISO 80601** (Medical Electrical Equipment) compliance frameworks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital as a Smart City**
> Imagine a hospital as a miniature city. A **rehabilitation robot** is like a *personal trainer* who never tires. A **pharmacy dispensing robot** is the *warehouse inventory clerk* who never miscounts. A **disinfection robot** is the *sanitation crew* working through the night with UV light. A **telepresence robot** is the *remote doctor* who "walks" the ward from another city. Each robot takes over a specific human role with greater **precision**, **repeatability**, and **fatigue-immunity**.

### 1.3 Classification of Healthcare Robots (Other Applications)

Healthcare robots under the "Other Applications" category can be taxonomized into the following functional clusters:

| # | Class | Primary Function | Typical DOF | Environment |
|---|-------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Rehabilitation Robots | Restore motor function post-stroke/spinal injury | 1–7 | Clinic / Home |
| 2 | Assistive / Companion Robots | Aid elderly & disabled in daily living | 4–6 | Home / Care facility |
| 3 | Exoskeletons | Augment human locomotion | 6–12 (joints) | Outdoor / Rehab |
| 4 | Hospital Service Robots | Logistics, delivery, disinfection | 3–6 | Hospital corridors |
| 5 | Pharmacy Automation Robots | Dispense, sort, package drugs | 3–4 (gantry) | Pharmacy labs |
| 6 | Telepresence Robots | Remote doctor–patient consultation | 2 (pan-tilt) + mobile base | Hospital / Home |
| 7 | Robotic Prosthetics | Replace lost limbs (myoelectric) | 5–7 | Wearable |
| 8 | Diagnostic / Lab Robots | Sample handling, pipetting, RT-PCR | 3–6 (SCARA) | Diagnostic labs |
| 9 | Disinfection Robots | UV-C or vaporized H₂O₂ sterilization | Mobile + UV array | Patient rooms |
| 10 | Surgical Training Simulators | Haptic VR rehearsal of procedures | 6–7 DOF + haptics | Training lab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Questions on this topic frequently test the student's ability to *map a robot type to its compliance standard, DOF requirement, and end-effector specification* — not deep mathematical derivations.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Healthcare Robotics

The following measurable quantities define the operating envelope of any healthcare robot and are **high-yield** for KTU exams:

- **Positioning Accuracy:** Typically **±0.1 mm** for surgical-grade, **±1 mm** for rehabilitation, **±5 mm** for mobile service robots.
- **Payload Capacity:** Surgical ~ **5 N**, Rehabilitation ~ **20–50 N**, Service ~ **50–200 N** (delivery carts).
- **Reach / Workspace:** Exoskeleton reach = full body articulation; manipulator reach = **0.5–1.2 m**.
- **Repeatability:** Better than **±0.05 mm** for haptic surgical trainers.
- **Cycle Time:** Pharmacy dispenser — **< 8 seconds per prescription**.
- **Battery Autonomy:** Mobile hospital robots — **8–12 hours** continuous operation.

These metrics are collectively governed by the kinematic relationship:

$$
\mathbf{T}_{end}^{base} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_{i}^{i-1}(q_i)
$$

where $n$ is the total **Degrees of Freedom**, $\mathbf{A}_i^{i-1}$ is the homogeneous transformation matrix for joint $i$, and $q_i$ is the joint variable (revolute or prismatic).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsystem-by-Subsystem Operational Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Rehabilitation Robots (e.g., MIT-Manus, InMotion ARM, Lokomat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle:** A rehabilitation robot applies **assisted-as-needed (AAN)** control, where the controller senses patient intent through residual EMG signals or force sensors and provides only the *minimum torque deficit* required to complete the motion.

**Why and How it works — Stepwise Logic:**

1. **Sensing Layer:** Surface EMG electrodes or force-torque sensors capture patient's residual neuromuscular signals.
2. **Intent Decoder:** A neural network or threshold-based algorithm maps EMG amplitude to a desired velocity/position reference $v_d$.
3. **Controller:** A PID or impedance controller generates the assistive torque $\tau_{assist}$.
4. **Actuation:** Brushless DC motors (or pneumatic muscle actuators) deliver $\tau_{assist}$ at the joint.
5. **Feedback:** Position encoders close the loop and continuously adapt $\tau_{assist}$.

The governing impedance control equation is:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}_{assist} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{patient} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{env}
$$

where $\mathbf{M}$ is the inertia matrix, $\mathbf{C}$ is the Coriolis term, $\mathbf{G}$ is gravity, and the right-hand side sums the assistive, patient-generated, and environmental torques.

#### 2.1.2 Exoskeletons (e.g., ReWalk, Ekso GT, HAL by Cyberdyne)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Exoskeletons are *wearable* robots with actuators placed in **series** with the human joint. They operate under the **Zero-Moment Point (ZMP)** stability criterion for bipedal locomotion.

The ZMP condition for static/dynamic stability is:

$$
x_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i x_i (\ddot{z}_i + g) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i (\ddot{z}_i + g)}
$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of link $i$, $g = 9.81$ m/s² is gravitational acceleration, and $(x_i, z_i)$ are the planar coordinates of the center of mass of link $i$.

**Stability Rule:** The ZMP must lie strictly **inside** the foot-support polygon.

#### 2.1.3 Pharmacy Automation Robots

Pharmacy robots (e.g., **ScriptPro**, **Omnicell**) operate on a **gantry + SCARA** configuration. The core equations governing throughput are:

$$
T_{cycle} = t_{pick} + t_{verify} + t_{dispense} + t_{label}
$$

$$
\eta_{throughput} = \frac{N_{prescriptions}}{T_{total}} \quad \text{[prescriptions/hour]}
$$

The error rate $E$ follows the Six-Sigma quality model:

$$
E = \frac{N_{errors}}{N_{total}} \times 10^6 \quad \text{[defects per million opportunities — DPMO]}
$$

#### 2.1.4 Disinfection Robots (UV-C based, e.g., Xenex LightStrike)

The microbial kill efficacy follows the **UV dose–response kinetics** (exponential decay):

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot D(t)}
$$

where $N(t)$ is the surviving colony count, $N_0$ is the initial count, $k$ is the microbial susceptibility constant, and $D(t)$ is the accumulated UV-C dose in **mJ/cm²**.

A typical disinfection threshold is **D ≥ 40 mJ/cm²** for *C. difficile* spores and **D ≥ 22 mJ/cm²** for *MRSA*.

#### 2.1.5 Telepresence Robots

Telepresence robots (e.g., **Double Robotics**, **InTouch Vita**) operate as **non-holonomic** mobile bases with a pan-tilt camera head. The kinematic model is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $v$ is linear velocity, $\omega$ is angular velocity, and $\theta$ is heading. This is a **unicycle model** with non-holonomic constraint $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics | $\mathbf{T}_{end}^{base} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_i(q_i)$ | $n$ = number of joints |
| 2 | Jacobian-based Velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\mathbf{J}$ must be non-singular |
| 3 | Impedance Control | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \tau_{total}$ | All vectors in joint space |
| 4 | ZMP (X-coordinate) | $x_{ZMP} = \frac{\sum m_i x_i(\ddot{z}_i+g) - \sum m_i z_i \ddot{x}_i}{\sum m_i(\ddot{z}_i+g)}$ | Stability: inside foot polygon |
| 5 | UV Microbial Kill | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-kD(t)}$ | $D \geq 40$ mJ/cm² (C. diff) |
| 6 | Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = N_{presc}/T_{total}$ | Unit: prescriptions/hr |
| 6 | Six-Sigma Error Rate | $E = (N_{err}/N_{total}) \times 10^6$ | Unit: DPMO |
| 7 | Non-holonomic Base | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | Cannot move sideways |
| 8 | Workspace Volume | $V = \pi r^2 h$ (cylindrical); $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ (spherical) | Reach $r$, height $h$ |
| 9 | DOF Count (Grübler) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $j_1$: 1-DOF joints, $j_2$: 2-DOF joints |
| 10 | Payload-Torque | $\tau = m g r \cos\theta$ | $m$ in kg, $r$ in m, $\theta$ in rad |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation:** Reduces therapist workload by **60–70%** and enables data-driven recovery tracking.
- **Exoskeletons:** Used in paraplegic gait rehabilitation — FDA-approved **ReWalk** enables 1.0 m/s walking speed.
- **Pharmacy Automation:** Achieves error rates of **< 0.0005%** vs. **1–3%** for manual dispensing.
- **Disinfection:** Cuts hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) by **up to 50%** when deployed in ICUs.
- **Telepresence:** Reduces physician travel time by **> 80%** in multi-hospital networks.
- **Lab Automation:** Increases RT-PCR throughput from **96 samples/8 hr** (manual) to **> 1000 samples/8 hr** (robotic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The common thread across all these applications is the *replacement of human variability with deterministic, sensor-driven repeatability* — a fundamental principle that KTU examiners love to highlight.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Rehabilitation Wrist Module

Consider a planar 2-DOF rehabilitation wrist with revolute joints — a foundational subsystem used in devices like the **InMotion ARM**. Let joint angles be $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.

**Step 1 — Define the homogeneous transformation matrix for a single revolute joint (DH-convention):**

$$
\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For a planar (2D) manipulator, $\alpha_i = 0$, $a_i = L_i$, $d_i = 0$. Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{A}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & L_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & L_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{A}_1$ (for joint 1):**

$$
\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & L_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & L_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c_1 = \cos\theta_1$, $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{A}_2$ (for joint 2):**

$$
\mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & L_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & L_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{A}_1 \cdot \mathbf{A}_2$:**

We compute the rotation and translation blocks separately. The top-left 2×2 rotation block:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 & -(c_1 s_2 + s_1 c_2) \\ s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2 & c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Using the angle-sum identities, $c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2 = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

The translation (position) vector:

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 c_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1 s_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Final End-Effector Pose:**

$$
\mathbf{T}_{end}^{base} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The end-effector position in the base frame is:

$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

**Step 6 — Workspace Boundary (Reachability Check):**

The reachable workspace is an annulus of inner radius $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.

$$
R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert, \quad R_{max} = L_1 + L_2
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Jacobian and Velocity Mapping for the 2-DOF Wrist

**Step 1 — Compute the Jacobian from the position vector $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$:**

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} = -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Assemble the Jacobian:**

$$
\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Mapping:**

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

where $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}]^T$ is the Cartesian end-effector velocity and $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2]^T$ is the joint velocity vector.

**Step 4 — Singularity Condition:**

The manipulator is at a singularity when $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0$. Computing:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = [-L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12}][L_2 c_{12}] - [-L_2 s_{12}][L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12}]
$$

Expanding:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = -L_1 L_2 s_1 c_{12} - L_2^2 s_{12} c_{12} + L_1 L_2 s_{12} c_1 + L_2^2 s_{12} c_{12}
$$

The middle terms cancel:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = -L_1 L_2 s_1 c_{12} + L_1 L_2 s_{12} c_1
$$

Using $s_{12} = s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2$:

$$
\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2
$$

**Result — Singularity occurs when $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = \pi$** (i.e., when the arm is fully stretched or fully folded — exactly the boundary of the workspace).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This singularity analysis is *identical* for surgical manipulators and rehabilitation robots — it is one of the most-tested KTU questions.

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Pharmacy Robot Throughput

A pharmacy robot dispenses an average of **240 prescriptions in a 6-hour shift**. Out of these, **3 prescriptions** had incorrect dosage. Compute throughput efficiency and Six-Sigma defect rate.

**Given:**
- $N_{presc} = 240$ prescriptions
- $T_{total} = 6$ hours
- $N_{errors} = 3$

**Throughput:**

$$
\eta = \frac{240}{6} = 40 \;\text{prescriptions/hour}
$$

**Six-Sigma Error Rate:**

$$
E = \frac{3}{240} \times 10^6 = 12{,}500 \;\text{DPMO}
$$

**Sigma Level Conversion:** A DPMO of 12,500 corresponds to approximately **3.8σ** — *below* the Six-Sigma threshold (3.4 DPMO, 6σ). The pharmacy needs a process improvement to upgrade its dispensing workflow.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: UV Disinfection Dose Verification

A UV-C robot emits **30 mJ/cm²** per pass at 1 m distance. The room requires **40 mJ/cm²** for *C. difficile* kill threshold. Compute the number of passes $n$.

**Dose accumulation:**

$$
D_{total} = n \cdot D_{per\,pass}
$$

**Setting $D_{total} \geq D_{required}$:**

$$
n \geq \frac{40}{30} = 1.333
$$

**Result:** The robot must execute **at least 2 full passes** to exceed the *C. difficile* inactivation threshold.

| Robot Type | Primary Control Strategy | Key Sensors | DOF Range | KTU 2024 Significance |
|------------|-------------------------|-------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Rehabilitation Arm | Impedance / AAN | EMG, F/T, Encoders | 5–7 | High |
| Lower-limb Exoskeleton | ZMP-based gait | IMU, Force plates | 6–12 | Very High |
| Pharmacy Robot | Pick-and-place (PLC) | Barcode, Vision | 3–4 | Medium |
| UV Disinfection Mobile | Coverage path planning | LIDAR, IR | 2 (mobile) | Medium |

| Surgical Simulator | Haptic admittance | Encoders, F/T, Haptic glove | 6–7 | High |
| Robotic Prosthetic | Myoelectric pattern recognition | Surface EMG | 5–7 | High |

| Hospital Delivery | SLAM + AMCL | LIDAR, Wheel odometry | 2 | Medium |
| Companion Robot | Behavioral AI + face tracking | Camera, Mic, ToF | 4–6 | Low–Medium |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Healthcare Robot Subsystem Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *generic* block-level architecture shared by nearly all healthcare service robots. Each block represents a functional subsystem, and arrows indicate signal/data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HMI[User Interface / Clinician Console] --> CTRL[Central Controller / SBC]
    TELE[Telemetry and Cloud Link] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT[Actuator Drivers BLDC Servo]
    ACT --> MECH[Mechanical Linkages and Joints]
    MECH --> END[End Effector or Tool]
    END --> ENV[Patient or Hospital Environment]
    ENV --> SENS[Sensor Array Cameras Force IMU EMG]
    SENS --> FUSE[Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter]
    FUSE --> CTRL
    SAF[Supervisory Safety Watchdog ISO 13482] --> CTRL
    PWR[Power Management Li-ion BMS] --> ACT
    PWR --> CTRL
    CTRL --> LOG[Data Logger and Audit Trail]
    CTRL --> FB[Haptic or Visual Feedback to User]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **CTRL (Central Controller):** The brain — typically an embedded SBC like NVIDIA Jetson or Intel NUC.
- **SENS → FUSE → CTRL:** Forms the perception loop.
- **CTRL → ACT → MECH → END:** Forms the actuation path.
- **SAF (Safety Watchdog):** Independent hardware layer — compliant with **ISO 13482** and **ISO 13849 (PL d)**.
- **LOG:** Mandatory for medical-grade auditability under **FDA 21 CFR Part 11**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Flow for a Rehabilitation Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PAT[Patient Intent EMG or Residual Force] --> DEC[Intent Decoder NN]
    DEC --> REF[Reference Trajectory Generator]
    REF --> PID[PID or Impedance Controller]
    PID --> DRV[Motor Driver PWM]
    DRV --> MOT[BLDC Motor at Joint]
    MOT --> LINK[Robot Link and End Effector]
    LINK --> PAT2[Patient Interaction and Feedback]
    PAT2 --> SENS[Encoders and F T Sensors]
    SENS --> PID
    SENS --> LOG[Therapy Data Logger]
```

### 4.4 Mobile Disinfection Robot — Coverage Path Planning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Power On and Self Test] --> MAP[Build Room Map via LIDAR SLAM]
    MAP --> IDENT[Identify High Touch Zones Door Handles Bed Rails]
    IDENT --> PLAN[Generate Coverage Path A Star or Boustrophedon]
    PLAN --> MOVE[Navigate to First Waypoint]
    MOVE --> UV[Activate UV C Lamps]
    UV --> DOSE[Accumulate UV Dose Sensor]
    DOSE --> CHK{Dose Threshold Met}
    CHK --> NO[MOVE: Continue Coverage]
    CHK --> YES[Move to Next Zone]
    YES --> DONE{All Zones Covered}
    DONE --> NO2[MOVE: Next Zone]
    DONE --> YES2[Shutdown and Return to Dock]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU 2024:** Block diagrams like the above are *expected* in 14-mark answers. Examiners reward clarity of signal flow, identification of feedback loops, and correct labeling of standards.
